1 Transcript of the Oral Commentary  
to Lama Tsongkhapa’s 
Middle Length Lam-Rim 
by 
Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Istituto Lama Tzong Khapa, 2005-2007 
 
  
Monday afternoon, 17 January 2005 
 
Note: Lama Tsongkhapa’s Middle Length Lam-Rm,  t r a n s l a t e d  b y  P h i l i p  Q u a r c o o ,  ©  F P M T,  I n c . 2 0 05 ,  i s  i n d e n t e d . Th e  
numbers in brackets { } correspond to the Tibetan text of the Middle Lam-Rim  published by Sera Je Computer Project, Sera 
Je Monastic University, Bylakuppe, 1999. Footnotes added by Joan Nicell begin with the initials JN. 
 
Today as we begin the Basic Program everyone should be relaxed and not worried. Okay? We 
should try to develop the motivation wishing to  attain enlightenment for the benefit of all 
sentient beings thinking, “I will study and practice  an inner path in order to be able to lead all 
sentient beings to enlightenment.” 
 
The text we will study – Middle Stages of the Path to Enlightenment  or Middle Lam-Rim  – is by 
Lama Tsongkhapa but later on some more outlines were added by the younger tutor of His 
Holiness the Dalai Lama, Kyabje Trijang Rinpoche . We should try to understand this text 
deeply. At the beginning I will read ahead a littl e bit so as to give the transmission of the 
Tibetan text and after that I will try to explain what I have read. If there are problems due to 
my English being broken and some of you mayb e not being able to understand me, you can 
ask the two teaching assistants, Olivier and Birgit, to clarify what was said.  
 The main point is to develop renunciation. Re nunciation means to not be attached to the 
t h i n g s  o f  t h i s  l i f e  –  w e a l t h ,  n a m e ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  T o  d e v e l o p  r e n u n c i a t i o n  w e  n e e d  t o  understand that cyclic existe nce is the nature of suffering . Wherever we are born we 
experience suffering. Sometimes we experience  some enjoyment, but after that again we 
suffer. Therefore, we need to think about the fact that the na ture of cyclic existence is 
suffering and thereby generate the wish to aban don the qualities of cyclic existence or the 
qualities of the desire realm, and develop our inner qualities and happiness. This is 
important. This is the main point.   In addition, we should try to have an open he art, not a closed heart but an open heart, and 
try to understand what we need to do. We need to develop our inner qualities and knowledge so that we can become more confident of ourselves. Sometimes we are not confident, however that is not right. We need to take care of ourselves, others cannot take care of us.
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
2  Other people cannot give us their mind of en lightenment or knowledge, like giving someone 
some clothing, a watch, and other things. When  someone teaches you something, listen to 
what he or she says, try to understand it, an d put it into practice. In this way our inner 
qualities will grow. Other people cannot give them to us. It would be a mistake to wait for this 
to happen. In the mind of th e Buddha there is great compassi on, wisdom, and power, and if 
he could have given them to us he would have , but he cannot. In our world we see that if 
someone does not study and instead of going to school hides and plays, that he or she cannot 
learn. We know this very well. We need to think about this. We ourselves need to study. If a 
university teacher is good but we do not listen to him or her, we cannot attain qualities. Even 
to become a good cook we need to study, we need someone to teach us how to cook. I know someone who was a very learned ge she but when he was at home his family did everything for 
him and even when he was at th e monastery his brothers took ca re of him. It then happened 
that one day when he needed to cook for hims elf, he could not. He did not know how to 
cook! So if we need someone to teach us ev en how to cook, then in order to go to 
enlightenment we definitely need someone to teac h us how to go there. We need to listen to 
teachings on this, try to keep the teachings in  our mind, contemplate their meaning, and then 
put that into meditation practice. This is important.  
 What I just said is my idea. It does not mention this here in the Middle Lam-Rim . Please try 
to do as I said.  
 The title of this text   is:  
Middle Length Lam-Rim 
 
Lam in Tibetan, marga  in Sanskrit, is translated into En glish as “road” or “path,” meaning 
that which takes one somewhere.  There exist small narrow paths, middling-size roads, and 
large wide roads, like the highways in Europe . Similarly Lama Tsongkhapa wrote a large or 
extensive presentation of the st ages of the path called the Great Stages of the Path  (Lam rim chen 
mo), a middling-length presentation called the Middle Stages of the Path  (Lam rim ‘bring ba ) 
which is also known as the “Small,” and a brief presentation called A Song of Experience (Lam 
rim nyams mgur ).  
 The lam-rim teachings were given to us by the Buddha. He taught the three baskets – the 
vinaya basket, sutra basket, and abhidharma basket. The lam-rim teachings form part of the 
abhidharma basket. Abhidharma  means “higher knowledge.” The subjects of the three baskets 
are the three higher trainings in morality, concentration, and wisdom. Buddha Shakyamuni 
taught that it was important to develop these th ree trainings within us. Morality in general is 
defined as a mind of safeguarding or a mind of abandonment. In other words, it is a mind that wishes to abandon something. Concentration is a mind single-pointedly set on an object 
without distraction. Wisdom is the correct discri mination of what to is to be abandoned, for 
example, killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, lyin g and so forth, and what is to be practiced, 
not killing any being, not stealing anything, not engaging in sexual misconduct, not lying, and 
so forth. It is also the discri mination of the categories of phenomena The basis of the other 
two qualities is morality. In other words, on th e basis of morality we develop concentration, 
and on the basis of concentration we develop wisd om. This is a brief explanation of the three.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
3  Morality in this case refers to the thr ee vows: the vows of individual liberation, the 
bodhisattva vows, and the tantra  vows. Most monks and nuns have these three sets of vows. 
However, in general morality means to practice the ten virtuous actions. This should be the 
basis of our practice in daily life. In addition, we should try to concentrate our mind. For 
example, when reciting prayers we should try to keep the mind on the prayer. It should not be 
that the mouth is verbally saying prayers but the mind is going here and there thinking “I will buy this,” “I will sell this,” “I will go to the disco, the cinema,” and so forth. We should not 
do like this. We need to concentrate single-poi ntedly on what we are doing. Then wisdom is 
based on this concentration.   In your everyday life try to have less desire and more satisfaction regarding material things, 
okay? In this way you will be more relaxed. On the other hand, if you want more and more 
material things and, not satisf ied with what you have, continua lly think “I need more, I need 
more,” then the mind will become more an d more disturbed and unhappy. Whatever you 
have think “This is enough,” and try to be satisfied. This is ve ry important. The main obstacle 
to a happy life is a strong desire for the materi al things of the world and dissatisfaction. They 
make our mind unhappy and can even induce phys ical sickness. This is because our physical 
illnesses are caused by the mental afflictions.  According to Tibetan medicine there are 400 
types of illnesses and their main root is the af flictions. Thus it is said that ignorance, 
attachment, hatred, and a combination of all three each produce 10 0 types of physical 
illnesses. More precisely, attachment produces 10 0 types of physical illnesses, anger or hatred 
produces another 100, ignorance produces anot her 100, and a combination of all attachment, 
hatred, and ignorance in equal parts produces  another 100. In this way there are 400 
illnesses, and these four are the main roots.   This means that physical illness can be caus ed by attachment to eating. It can also be 
caused by attachment to drinking, for example, al cohol, or attachment to smoking. It can also 
be caused by ignorance, for example, thinki ng that drinking a lot will produce joy or 
happiness. While it temporarily appears that dr inking brings happiness, in the end it only 
harms one’s body and mind. For example, if one becomes drunk one m ay even lie down in 
the middle of a road. We need to understand that the main cause of our problems is inside us, not outside. We tend to think that if we  have a problem someone did something to us. 
We point to the source of the problem as be ing outside ourselves. However, this is not 
correct. We need to understand this. To do so we need to understand that the mental 
poisons are the main problem-maker, that they are the cause of our sufferings, not something 
outside us. For example, when a man and woman have attachment for each other, they stay 
together, go everywhere together, and do ever ything together. However, later on something 
happens, they start to fight and eventually sepa rate. Why does this happen? It happens due to 
attachment and dissatisfaction. They each came to think that their part ner was no longer the 
right one and developed a wish to have another partner. When this happens however, people 
often end up remaining alone! Check whether this is how things happen. All this is caused by 
attachment. Attachment accompanies us ever yday, wherever we go. And then it makes 
problems for us. Hatred is not like this. Someti mes we have hatred and sometimes not, it is 
not always present. When it comes, it is bad bu t it is not always present. On the other hand, 
attachment is always present, when walking,  talking, or sleeping. Therefore, the most 
dangerous affliction is attachment. We should try to recognize the negative emotions and to 
understand that they are our main enemy, and then try to destroy them. A foe destroyer is 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
4 someone who has destroyed the mental afflictions, it is not that he has destroyed the foes that 
are other people.  
 In short, we need morality, concentration,  and wisdom. These three are important. We 
definitely need these qualities in our life. Theref ore, we should try to develop them as in this 
way we will become happy. We should try to do this.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
Middle length explanation of the stag es of the path to enlightenment  
to be practiced by the three [types of] beings 
with supplementary insertions and a thematic structure 
The word “middle” implies that there is also a big and a small. Sometimes the word “middle” 
refers to center but in this context it refers to the length of the expl anation. When condensed 
there are five spiritual paths – th e path of accumulation, path of  preparation, path of seeing, 
path of meditation, and path of no-more-learning – that we need to develop. In this context a 
path is an inner quality. It is defined as: a cl ear knower conjoined with definite emergence. A 
path is therefore that which bring us to enlightenment or buddhahood.  
 “Stages” refers to the different stages: the st age of beings of small capacity, the stage of 
beings of middling capacity, and the stage of beings  of great capacity. It also refers to the small 
path, the middle path, and the gr eat path, so-called because the practitioners of these paths 
are respectively small beings, middling beings , and great beings. Small beings are further 
divided into ordinary and special small beings. Ordinary small beings include dogs, cats, 
horses, and birds. They too are small ordinary beings. Likewise, human beings who do not think to practice a religion or spiritual path  but only think of this life, whose minds are 
occupied with obtaining food, clothing, wealth , and so forth, are small ordinary beings. A 
special small being, on the other hand, is some one who thinks that the next life is more 
important than this life and, wishing to obtain  a good human rebirth in the future, practices 
pure morality. While this person is considered to be a small being, since he or she practices 
the Dharma for the success of his or her future  life, that is, to obtain a precious human 
rebirth with the eighteen qualities, he or she is a “special” small being.   A middling being is someone who wishes to atta in nirvana, the state of a foe-destroyer. In 
other words, he or she wishes to eliminate his or her own suffering, all the sufferings of cyclic 
existence, and to go beyond cyclic existence and to achieve nirvana, a state of solitary peace. A 
great being is someone who wishes to attain enlightenment himself or herself but also wishes that all other sentient beings attain enli ghtenment, and therefore strives to attain 
enlightenment in order to be of benefit to all sentient beings. Thus, a great being is a being 
with a big mind.   These three types of beings, the small, middli ng, and great, do not have anything to do 
with the height of the body but are in relati on to their inner mental qualities. Thus, if 
someone’s mental qualities are small he or she is  a small being, if they are middling then he 
or she is middling being, and if they are great then he or she is a great being. There was a 
Tibetan in the past who though t that higher members of the government were great beings, 
that businessmen and rich men were middling beings, and that monks and nuns and those 
who practice with humility were small beings ! However, he was mistaken as, not knowing 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
5 their correct meaning, he thou ght that due to their possessi ng great power the members of 
the government were great beings; that due to their possessing wealth that can buy the 
support of others but not possessing power that businessmen and rich men were middling 
beings; [and that due to lacking both wealth and power monks and nuns and those who 
practice with humility were small beings ]. It is not like this. 
 We need to develop our inner qualities, and for this reason in our everyday life when we 
w a k e  u p  w e  s h o u l d  t h i n k ,  “ T o d a y ,  w h a t e v e r  I  d o ,  I  w i l l  d o  i t  t o  s e r v e  a n d  b e n e f i t  o t h e r  sentient beings,” for example, to benefit one’s friends and family members. In this way we should develop a good motivation wishing to serve others. Then when we are at work we 
should check whether we are doing what we pr omised that morning, and in the evening we 
should dedicate whatever we did with this g ood attitude that it becomes the cause for all 
sentient beings to attain enlightenment. In th is way, everyday one will collect virtue and merit 
and one’s inner qualities will grow. At the same time our bad energy and non-meritorious actions will slowly decrease. Therefore, in orde r to attain enlightenment we need to practice. 
Enlightenment is a state in which all obscurations and all sufferings have been abandoned, and all knowledge, the omniscience that can s ee all phenomena directly, has been attained. 
When this knowledge has been realized, one ha s attained enlightenment. For this aim, we 
need to practice whatever morality we can and to develop a good heart.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
  
Respectfully I prostrate at the feet of the venerable saints who are full of great 
compassion. 
 
We too need to develop respect for holy be ings such as Buddha Shakyamuni and then to 
show this by prostrating to  them. This is important. Je tsun  (rje btsun ) is translated here as 
“venerable.” Tsun implies that this person has good morality and practices well, does not 
perform mental, verbal, or physical bad actions and does not harm other people, and tries to 
take care of his or her body, speech, and mind. This is a simple way of explaining the term 
tsun. Dam pa ,  h e r e  t r a n s l a t e d  a s  s a i n t ,  r e f e r s  t o  o n e  w h o  i s  a  h o l y  b e i n g ,  t h a t  i s ,  o n e  w h o  
engages in virtuous actions and does not engage  in non-virtuous actions. “Great compassion” 
(thugs rje chen po ) is the mental attitude wishing that a ll sentient beings be free from suffering 
and the causes of suffering. Great love, on the other hand, is the thought, “How wonderful it 
would be if all sentient beings were to have  happiness and the causes of happiness.” Based on 
this thought, one tries to avoid causing th em suffering and to avoid harming them.  
 Compassion is the main cause of bodhichitta, the mind of enlightenmen t. There is a small, 
a middling, and a great compassion. In Chandrakirti’s Supplement to the Middle Way  there is an 
explanation of three types of compassion: compassion observing sentient beings, compassion 
observing phenomena, and compassion observing th e unobservable. This text also says that in 
t h e  b e g i n n i n g  c o m p a s s i o n  i s  l i k e  a  s e e d  t h a t  o n e  s o w s  i n  a  g o o d  f i e l d ;  i n  t h e  m i d d l e  compassion is like fertilizer, water, and heat; an d at the end compassion is like the fruit of the 
seed that one sowed that one can then use or en joy. Compassion is likened to a seed because 
it is from this that the mind of enlightenm ent develops in one’s mental continuum. Then 
having generated the mind of enlightenment, if later on one loses one’s compassion, also 
one’s mind of enlightenment will degenerate an d disappear. Also if at the end one achieves 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
6 enlightenment but does not have compassion on e will not teach the Dharma to other beings. 
Therefore, compassion is important in the be ginning, during the middle, and at the end of 
the spiritual path. For this reas on in our everyday life we need  to try to develop compassion 
and love.  
 Then the text says “I prostrate.” Prostratio ns are done with the body, speech, and mind. 
Bodily prostrations involve bowi ng the body and prostrating it on the ground or just putting 
the hands together in a gesture of respect. A verbal prostration involves the recitation of 
praise. A mental prostration is to go for refuge and to generate respect for all the holy beings 
from the depth of one’s heart. Hence, it is not enough to do just a physical movement of the 
body. In Tibetan the word for prostration or homage is chag tsel  (phyag ‘tshal ). Chag  refers to 
the qualities of great wisdom or omniscience,  great compassion, and great energy or power 
that exist in the mind s of the holy beings. Tsel includes the wish “May I develop these 
qualities,” and the request “Please bless me to de velop them within myself.” If one prostrates 
the body while thinking in this way it becomes a real prostration. When one prostrates to the 
holy beings, one prostrates to all those abiding in the ten directions, in the infinite buddha 
lands, and in the billion world systems, wh ile developing respect for them, as well as 
confidence and faith in them. This is what is  meant by prostrating to the holy beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
  
This here is the middling of three lam-rim texts – the great, the middle length, and the small – which the gentle 
protector, the Dharmaraja1
, the great Tsongkhapa, composed. It is also widely known as the “small lam-rim”.  
 
This text is also known as the Small Lam-Rim . Who is the great Tsongkhapa? He is considered 
to be a manifestation of Manjushri and therefor e of the omniscient mind. He was born in the 
region of Amdo. Tibetans often call the people  f r o m  t h i s  a r e a  “ b i g  n o s e . ”  L a t e r  o n  L a m a  
Tsongkhapa went to central Tibet. His first lama was from the Drikung Kagyu tradition, 
Kyopa Rinpoche, whose name was Jigten Sumgon. Jigten is usually translated as “world” 
(literally, support of the transitory), sum means “three” and gon, short for gon po  means 
protector. Therefore his name translated into  English would be “Protector of the Three 
Worlds.” Later on Lama Tsongkhapa studied th e Sakya tradition in a Sakya monastery. One 
day one of the monastery’s benefactors offere d all the monks a red hat, but when Lama 
Tsongkhapa arrived the red cloth needed to make  him a hat had come to an end and all that 
remained was some yellow cloth. Due to this, a yellow hat was quickly ma de and given to him. 
For this reason his followers later on came to be called the Yellow Hats. It was not that he 
started a new tradition of wearing yellow hats, it  just happened because of what had occurred 
at the Sakya monastery. In the past all the Sa kya monks had red hats but now it seems that 
everyone wears a yellow hat, maybe they are nicer! A hat of this particular shape is called a 
pandit hat from the Indian tradition in which pandits wore a hat shaped like this.   We will go ahead with the explanation of th is text like a turtle, not like a rabbit! 
 
                                             
 
1 Dharma King 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
7 Tuesday morning, 18 January 2005 
 Try to develop a loving-kind mind and the mind  of enlightenment, and then meditate on 
this.  
 Lama Tsongkhapa is the compa ssionate Dharma king. He compos ed three lam-rim texts, the 
great, the middling, and the small. Th e text that we are studying is the Middle Lam-Rim  but it 
is also renowned as the small.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
Its source text has three sections: 
1 The introductory elements of the explanations 
2 Composition of the explanations themselves 
3 Their completion 
1 The introductory elements of the explanation 
A Expression of veneration 
B Vow of composition 
C Identification of the Dharma to be explained in the context of what the title means 
Expression of veneration an d the vow of composition 
 
“The introductory elements of the explanatio n s ”  o r  “ t h e  b r a n c h e s  f o r  e n g a g i n g  i n  t h e  
explanation” include the two main points that occur at the beginning of any text: prostration 
or veneration and the promise to compose the text.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
1A  Expression of veneration 
Bhagavan, forbearing Lord of this world system, 
Conqueror’s regent, Jamgön, lord of the Dharma, Manjughosha, single father of all the sugatas, 
Nagarjuna and Asanga, foretold by the Conqueror,  
bowing to you with respect, 
 First Lama Tsongkhapa sets out a verse of pr ostration or veneration. To whom does he 
express veneration? First he expresses venera tion to the Bhagavan, that is, to Buddha 
Shakyamuni, and then to Jamgön, the king of Tushita, that is , Maitreya. Then he expresses 
veneration to Manjugosha or Manjushri, who all wisdom of the sugatas manifested in the 
form of a deity. Then he venerates Nagarjuna and Asanga who were predicted by the Conqueror.   Buddha Shakyamuni is the founder of the doctrine , that is, it is he who taught the Dharma 
in this world, Jambudvipa. In Sanskrit he is called bhagavan , which is translated into Tibetan 
as chom den (bcom ldan ). Chom  (to destroy) indicates he has co mpletely destroyed or abandoned 
both the afflictive obscurations and the obscurations to knowledge and so forth. Den (to 
possess) indicates that he possesses the six qualit ies of knowledges. Since the word “bhagavan” 
was also used in the Indian culture to refer to Mahadeva, Indra, Brahma, and so forth, in 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
8 order to show that the Buddha wa s not a mundane god the syllable de (‘das), meaning gone 
beyond or transcended, was added to chom den  in Tibetan to indicate that the Buddha was 
one who had gone beyond cyclic existence.  
 Buddha Shakyamuni teachings are composed of two types of teachings, the profound and 
the vast. The Buddha taught the vast to Maitreya, who then ta ught Asanga, who then taught 
his brother, Vasubandhu, and so forth. In this  way they were passed down to us without 
interruption from the Buddha himself. Buddha  Shakyamuni taught the profound teachings 
on emptiness to Manjushri, who then taught them to Nagarjuna. Asanga and Nagarjuna are 
very important in that Nagarjuna clarified the Middle Way point of view while Asanga 
clarified the Chittamatra or Mind-Only point of vi ew. Due to this they are said to be the trail-
blazers or innovators respectively of these tw o schools. For this re ason, Lama Tsongkhapa 
includes them among his objects of veneration . The text says “foretold by the Conqueror” 
because Buddha Shakyamuni gave many teachings on sutra and tantra in which he predicted 
the coming of Nagarjuna who would be born in  such-and-such a place and would clarify his 
doctrine, and also likewise predicted the coming of Asanga. I will not go into this in detail. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
1B  Vow of composition 
I shall once more 
give explanations here in a summary fashion 
of the stages of the path of the most profound view 
and of vast conduct, making it easy to access. 
 
The “profound” refers to emptiness. There are si xteen or twenty emptinesses. In this context 
“emptiness” refers to the emptiness of inhe rent existence of all phenomena. Why are 
phenomena empty? They are empty of inhere nt existence because they are dependent-
relations. All functioning things depend on causes and conditions. While permanent 
phenomena, also called non-things or non- functioning things, such as space and non-
analytical and analytical cessations, do not depe nd on causes and conditions, they do depend 
on their parts. In addition, all phenomena are dependent-relations in the sense of their being 
dependent on imputation by name and thou ght. In other words, in dependence on 
something being given a name it comes into exis tence. For example, when a nation chooses a 
president, while previously the person who won the election was not the president, after being 
voted for he came to be called “president,” to  think of himself as president, and to perform 
the function of a president.   In a similar way, everything is created by th e mind. For example, scie ntists create many new 
machines. First these machines were  just thoughts or projects in  the minds of their inventors, 
only later on were they actualiz ed. All these machines that make it easy to work and so forth 
came about from thought. It is not that they ha ve existed from primordial time. Rather in the 
beginning they did not exist and only later on were they created by people. For this reason, 
from the Buddhist point of view all things are created by the mind and not by a god such as 
Ishvara, as is believed in India. Hindus thin k that first Ishvara thinks about something and 
then it arises. If it were like this, then no on e would ever need to suffer as Ishvara would have 
the power to make everyone happy and relaxed. He would just have to think “How nice it would be if all sentient beings had happiness and the causes of happi ness” and then this 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
9 would occur. However, it is not like this. Happiness and suffering are created by our mind. 
We should examine this.  
 In short, all phenomena are only imputed by  thought and name. Ther efore, they do not 
exist at all from their own side, not even the smallest atom. Hence, all phenomena are empty 
of inherent existence. This is what is referred to by the word “profound.” 
 
The “vast” refers to many things. It includes the five paths, the ten bodhisattva grounds, the 
six or ten perfections, and the four means of ga thering disciples. All th ese were taught by the 
Buddha and set out by Maitreya in his Ornament for Clear Realizations , in relation to which 
many Indian pandits wrote commentaries. In fact, the main root text for the lam-rim 
teachings is the Ornament for Clear Realizations  (Abhisamayalamkara ).  
 We need to understand which are ordinary  paths and which are arya paths. Then we 
should try to develop these qualities in our me ntal continuum. The main quality to develop is 
renunciation or definite emergence – the thou ght to definitively emerge even from the 
pleasures of cyclic existence. Th is thought is based on the unders tanding that even the best of 
pleasures will eventually bring suffering since it  is caused by afflictions and contaminated 
actions. To avoid this suffering  we must strive to commit less  non-virtuous actions and to 
reduce the mental afflictions, such as anger or  hatred, jealousy, miserliness, and so forth. 
There are many afflictions, there being six root and twenty secondary afflictions. We need to 
study them and then check whethe r or not they exist in our mind . We will probably find that 
everyone of them exists in our mind! Therefore, our main aim should be to eliminate the mental afflictions. If we eliminate them, we will experience real happiness and joy. Since 
everyone wishes to be happy and no one wants to suffer, we ourselves need to create the 
causes of happiness. For this reason we need to practice the Dharma in daily life. This is said 
here very briefly.   Here Lama Tsongkhapa says that he will give  a very brief explanation of the profound and 
v a s t  s o  a s  t o  m a k e  i t  e a s y  t o  p u t  t h e m  i n t o  practice. By continuous ly engaging in study, 
thinking, and meditation on them, it will definitely bring a result.   
Middle Lam-Rim:   
General introductory explanation of the instructions being taught here 
1C Identification of the Dharma to be explained in the context of what the title means 
The Dharma to be explained here consists  in how the fortunate are lead to the 
level of buddhahood by mean s of the stages of the pa th to enlightenment. It 
summarizes all the points of the Conque ror’s excellent speech, [follows] the 
tradition of the two great trail-blazers Nagarjuna and Asanga, {2} constitutes the 
Dharma system of the supreme beings wh o progress to the state of omniscience,2 
and contains all the stages without omission that the three [types of] beings need 
to practice. 
 
                                              
2 JN: “who progress to the ground  of a knower of all aspects” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
10 The name of the text, Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment , sets out what is to be explained – the 
path to enlightenment. Budd ha Shakyamuni’s teachings incl ude 101 volumes of sutra and 
tantra that have been translated into Tibetan.  The essence of these teachings is condensed in 
the teachings of Venerable Nagarjuna and Vene rable Asanga, thus their teachings show the 
entire path. All sentient beings wish to attain omniscience, that is, to become holy beings. For 
this it is necessary to practice  the paths of the three beings: th e great, middle, and small paths 
of the beings of great, middling, and small capaci ty. All the stages necessary to accomplish this 
are contained in their entirety in this text, no thing is missing. This is because if one being 
wishes to go to the state of omniscience one needs to practice the complete path without 
anything missing.  
 How are sentient beings led to enlightenment?  They are led to enlightenment by teaching 
them the stages of the path, af ter which they must put them in to practice. In this way the 
fortunate ones will gradually go to enlightenment,  that is, to the pure land of a buddha, such 
as Akanishta, Tushita, and Sukhavati, or  to buddhahood. Once there one will always 
experience enjoyment.   Thus, that which is to be explained is the th ree paths of the beings of great, middle, and 
small capacity. These are then to be practice d. First one practices the common small path, 
then the common middle path, then the common great path, and then the uncommon great 
path, that of tantra. The fortunate are thos e who have taken a precious human rebirth 
complete with the eighteen qualities, are en dowed with intelligence, understand whatever 
they hear or study, have met the doctrine, an d have interest in the Buddha’s teachings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2 Composition of the explanations themselves 
A Correspondence with the manner of explanation proposed by the scholars 
B The actual explanations in accordance with that 
2A  Correspondence with the manner of explanation proposed by the scholars 
The scholars of glorious Vikramashila [Mon astery] considered it vital to start off 
with three preliminaries, i.e., the greatn ess of the authors of  the teachings, the 
greatness of the teachings, and the way to explain and listen to them. 
 
Vikramashila Monastery was a great monastery th at once existed in eastern India. How did 
the pandits or scholars of this monastery teach?  They were very learned in Buddha’s teachings 
as well as in what was not Buddha’s teachings, that is, the five sciences. They understood all 
of these clearly and therefore possessed very hi gh qualities. They also greatly benefited the 
Dharma.   It is important to understand how did to te ach the Dharma and how to listen to it. The 
teachings of Buddha are of great benefit becaus e they come down to us from great holy 
beings. We need to try to integrate the instru ctions on how to elimin ate and solve problems, 
how to develop realizations, and so forth with  our mind. We need to learn this. For this 
reason, these holy being first explained how to  listen to the Dharma. They said that the 
teacher is like a doctor, whereas the Dharma, th e teaching or explanation, is like medicine. 
The doctor diagnoses our illness and, if we ta ke the medicine, our physical sickness can be 
cured. Similarly, if we put th em into practice, the Buddha’s teachings can cure our mental 
illness. Thus, if we listen to them, think abou t them, and meditate on them, they can cure our 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
11 mental illness. We should not think that what he  taught is not right, no r should we listen to 
the Dharma while looking for faults in it.  
 Whenever someone teaches he or she should  do so motivated by compassion and love, 
wishing to benefit the listeners. He should not do so for name or fame or to gain material 
things. This is not the right motivation on the part of the teacher. Both teacher and listener 
need a good motivation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B  The actual explanations in accordance with that 
1 The greatness of the author for the sake of showing the authenticity of the teachings 
2 The greatness of the teachings for the sake of generating respect towards the instructions 
3 How the teachings characterized by these two greatnesses should be listened to and 
explained 
4 The stages by which a disciple should be guided by means of the actual instructions  
In accordance with that, the guide to the stages of the path to enlightenment has 
four [parts]: The greatness of the author fo r the sake of showing the authenticity of 
the teachings, the greatness of the teachi ngs for the sake of generating respect 
towards the instructions, how the teaching s characterized by these two greatnesses 
should be listened to and explained, and the stages by which a disciple should be 
guided by means of the actual instructions. 
 
The Dharma comes from an authentic sour ce – Buddha Shakyamuni. Since all these 
instructions and scriptures come from a holy being, it possesses all his qualities. For this 
reason we should generate respect for these explanations and instructions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
Showing the greatness of the auth or for the sake of showing  
the authenticity of the teachings 
2B1 The greatness of the author for the sake of showing the authenticity of the teachings 
A The root and actual author 
B His greatness 
2B1A The root and actual author 
First, the author: Generally speaking, the present instructions are the instructions 
that the venerable protector Maitreya gives in his Ornament for Clear Realizations . In 
particular, they are based on the Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment , therefore its 
author is also the author of this presen t text. Other than under the name of Great 
Master Dipamkara Shrijñana, he is al so widely known as Glorious Atisha. 
 
From whom does the Dharma comes? Who comp osed the Dharma? It was composed by the 
Buddha, for example, the Perfection  of Wisdom Sutras. Although the Ornament for Clear 
Realizations  was written by Buddha Maitreyanath, thes e instructions come from the teachings 
of the Buddha. Later on Atisha wrote the Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment . Is the Buddha or 
Atisha the author of this text? Although they are not the same people, their qualities and what 
t h e y  w r o t e  a r e  v e r y  s i m i l a r .  A s  A t i s h a ’ s  t e x t  c o n t a i n s  i n s t r u c t i o n s  t h a t  c o m e  d o w n  f r o m  Buddha Shakyamuni, these instru ctions are useful. Why is this? It is because the text 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
12 composed by Atisha is easier to put into practi ce due to being short and concise, yet it still 
contains all the practices of sutra and tantra in a complete way. For this reason it is easier to 
study, to memorize, and to hold in mind so as to then be able to integrate the meaning with 
one’s mind.  
 An “instruction” is an explanation of what to do and what not to do, for example, an 
explanation of how to clean. At the beginning ou r parents taught us how to walk, to eat, to 
get dressed, and so forth, all of these are called instructions.  
 
Dipamkara Shrijñana is his full name in Sanskr it, whereas in Tibetan he is commonly known 
by the shorter name, Atisha. Dipam  means “lamp,” kara means “illuminating,” shri means 
“glorious,” and jñana  means “wisdom.” The letter a in Sanskrit represents the essence of all 
the Buddha’s teachings. A-tisha was so-called because he possessed the essence of Buddha’s 
teachings in his mind and then gave others these teachings.   There is an explanation below of  were Atisha was born, the deed s he did, and so forth, but, 
in short, he wrote the text Lamp for the Path to Enlightenment , which even nowadays remains 
very useful for us. This text is entitled “lam p” because just as an ordinary light dispels 
darkness, similarly this text dispels our mental ig norance, which is like darkness. In fact it is 
the light of wisdom, not normal light, that  eliminates the darkness of ignorance and 
confusion. Due to this, he called his text a “lamp.”  I explained the word “path” yesterday. It means a road that when traveled brings one to one’s destination. In our case in order to travel  to enlightenment, we need a path. A path is 
defined as a clear knower conjoined with defi nite emergence or renunciation. This clear 
knower is called a path because it is what brings us to enlightenment.   The word “enlightenment” refers buddhahood . There are three types of enlightenment: 
small enlightenment, middle enlightenment, an d great enlightenment. Small enlightenment 
is the enlightenment of hearer foe-destroyers ( shravekas ) which is achieved by practicing their 
particular path; middle enlightenment is the enlightenment of solitary realizers 
(pratyekabuddhas ) which is achieved by practicing  their particular path; and great 
enlightenment is buddhahood which is achieved by practicing the bodh isattva path. From the 
point of view of sutra, the bodhisattva path is  divided into five paths and ten grounds or ten 
perfections, by means of which one can attain  enlightenment. In this particular context 
“enlightenment” refers to great enlightenment or buddhahood.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B1B [Atisha’s ] greatness 
1 How [Atisha] took birth in an excellent family 
2 How [Atisha] achieved his good qualities on that basis 
3 Having achieved them, how [Atisha] performed deeds for the sake of the teachings 
His greatness is threefold: How he took  birth in an excellent family, how he 
achieved his good qualities on that ba sis, and having achieved them, how he 
performed deeds for the sake of the teachings. 
 
Then Lama Tsongkhapa set out the excellent fa mily into which Atisha took birth; then, 
having taken a human body as a basis, how he attained his good qualities; and then, having 
attained these qualities, how he performed d eeds or worked for the Buddha’s teachings in 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
13 India and Tibet such that his teachings still ex ist in the world and that there still exist his 
followers who put them into practice.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B1B-1 How [Atisha] took birth in an excellent family 
{3} According to the [great] translator’s Praise : 
 
Due east in the excellent country of Sahor 
lies a city, big and tall, 
namely Vikramanipura. In its midst: the royal court, an extremely spacious palace 
called “the one with golden banners.” 
Its pleasures, might, and wealth resemble those of King Tongkhun of China. King of that land is Kalyanashri  and his queen is called Shriprabha. The royal couple have three sons: Padmagarbha, Chandragarbha, 
and Shrigarbha they are called. 
Again, Prince Padmagarbha has five royal consorts and nine sons – the oldest one, named Shriguna, is a mahapandita widely known as Dhanashri. His youngest brother, called Shrigarbha, is the Bhikshu Viryachandra. The middle one is Chandragarbha – he is our exalted guru. 
 Atisha was born in eastern India in the area of Sahor, now in East Bengal. Even now near 
Kushbihar one can see the palace  of Sahor, and, although no  one lives there, everyday at 
midday someone still distributes food from there to the poor.   Atisha was born in what was in the great ci ty that was then called Vikramanipura. In its 
center was a large and vast palace possessing golden victory banners and so forth. It was complete in wealth, like the we alth of King Tongkhun of Chin a. This Chinese king was said 
to be very rich and each day, although he woul d be given thousands of foods, still he was not 
satisfied. For this reason he was called Tongkh un, meaning “not satisfied by a thousand foods 
or tastes” (literally, a thousand rumblings). Just  as the Chinese king was rich, so too was the 
family of Atisha. Atisha’s father was called Ka lyanashri, and his mother, Shriprabha (Glorious 
Light). They had three sons called Padmagar bha (Lotus Essence), Chandragarbha (Moon 
Essence), and Shrigarbha (Glory Essence). Pa dmagarbha had five wives and nine sons. The 
oldest was Shriguna (Glorious Merit). He was a great pandit known as Dhanashri (Glorious 
Giving). His youngest brother was a fully-or dained monk called Viryachandra. The middle 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
14 one, Chandragarbha (Moon Essence)  is our venerable guru, Atisha. For this reason, Atisha is 
known as the son of a king.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B1B-2 How [Atisha] achieved his good qualities on that basis 
A How [Atisha] attained the positive qualities of comprehensive scriptural knowledge 
B How [Atisha] attained the realizational qualities of its correct accomplishment 
How he attained his positive qualities [is presented under] two [headings]: how he 
attained the positive qualities of compre hensive scriptural knowledge and how he 
attained the realizational qualities of its correct accomplishment. 
2B1B-2A How [Atisha] attained the positive qualities of comprehensive scriptural knowledge 
1 How [Atisha] learnt the areas of general knowledge 
First: By the age of 21, having studie d the four areas of knowledge common to 
Buddhists and non-Buddhists – language,3 logic, crafts, and medicine – he had 
become an excellent scholar. In particular, the great Drolungpa relates how at the 
age of 15, having heard the4 Lines of Reasoning  just once, he debated with a non-
Buddhist intellectual renowned for his er udition and defeated him, which spread 
his fame far and wide. 
 
Atisha possesses the quality of having much kn owledge of the scriptures. In fact, how he 
gained inner realizations was that he first studied many scriptures. He studied the common 
fields of knowledge or sciences: linguistics (t his includes poetry and so forth), logic or 
reasoning or dialectics, crafts (how to make tangkas, tables, and so forth), and medicine, 
including the different kinds of illness, how to  recognize them, their treatment, and so forth. 
Then at the age of fifteen, having li stened one time to teachings on the Lines of Reasoning , a 
text written by Chandrakirti on logic, he debated with a well-known non-Buddhist scholar 
and defeated him.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2 How [Atisha] learnt Mantra 
Then he requested complete empowermen t from the master yogi of the main 
temple on Black Mountain, the glorious  Rahulagupta who had received direct 
visions of Hevajra and a prophecy from Va jradakini, {4} receiving the secret name 
Jñana-guhya-vajra. Up to the age of 29 he studied the Vajrayana under numerous 
highly realized gurus due to which he became knowledgeable in all texts and 
instructions. When the thought “I am kn owledgeable in secret mantra“ occurred 
to him, dakinis pointed out, in his drea m, several volumes of mantra he had not 
seen before. That abated his pride. 
 
                                              
3 JN: sgra could also be translated as  ‘grammar’ or ‘linguistics’ 
4 Dharmakirti’s

15 Atisha also studied tantra in a temple on Blac k Mountain with a yogi who had had a vision of 
Hevajra and received a prophecy from Vajradakin i. He received empowerment from this yogi, 
Rahulagupta, and was conferred the name Jñan a-guhya-vajra (Vajra of Secret Wisdom).  
 
This is all similar to a story, it is not so important. What we need to do is to try to take care of 
our own mind, to try to keep it happy and rela xed, to not worried or agitated, and to enjoy 
ourselves.  
 
Tuesday afternoon, 18 January 2005  Atisha requested his guru Rahulagupta to be stow upon him the complete empowerment, 
whereby he was conferred empowerment and given the secret name of Jñana-guhya-vajra. Up 
to the age of twenty-nine he received teaching s and studied the vajrayana in the presence of 
many realized holy beings due to which he  became knowledge in the scriptures and 
instructions. When he came to th ink that he was the best of tant ra practitioners or the best in 
understanding of tantra, the dakinis came to him in a dream and told him that there were 
many tantra texts that he had not yet seen an d therefore could not say this. Due to this his 
pride was abated.   
Middle Lam-Rim:   
3 How [Atisha] was ordained and learnt the collections of inner knowledge 
Both in his dreams and directly, the gurus and protectors5 then made known to 
him that vast benefit would accrue for th e teachings and many migrating beings, if 
he took monastic vows as appropriate. Upon their encouragement he had himself 
ordained. The great upholder of monastic di scipline, Shilarakshita, an elder of the 
Mahasamghika tradition who had attained the concentration in which you single-
pointedly enter [ultimate] reality on the pa th of preparation, officiated as abbot. 
Chandragarbha received the name Shri Dipamkara-jñana. 
 Atisha had visions of some lamas and meditati onal deities and also dreamt of them. He was 
told by them that if he took ordination he  would benefit the teachings of the Buddha and 
many migrating beings. Due to this, he took  the vows of a monk in the Mahasamghika 
tradition. According to the Vaibhashikas th ere are four different traditions of vows: 
Sarvastavadin, Mahasamghika , Sthavira, and Sammitiya.  
 Shilarakshita, Atisha’s lama from Indonesia who acted as abbot, had achieved the path of 
preparation. This is the second of the five  paths – the path of accumulation, path of 
preparation, path of seeing, path of meditation , and path of no-more-learning. On the path of 
accumulation, one accumulates merit and the hear ing of many teachings in order to develop 
faith in the teachings of the Buddha. Having studied and contemplated them, one puts what 
one has learned into meditation practice wh ereby one develops the wisdom arisen from 
meditation. At this time, one attains the path of preparation, which is divided into four levels: 
heat, peak, tolerance, and supreme mundane qualit ies. From the point of view of realization 
                                             
 
5 JN: yi dam , also translated as ‘meditational deity’ or ‘tutelary deity’ 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
16 Shilarakshita was a being on the path of preparat ion, and from the point of view of tenets he 
was a Vaibhashika. Shilarakshita (Protecting Mo rality) was one of Atisha’s main teachers. 
Upon receiving ordination, Atisha came to be called Shri Dipamkara-jñana and then 
practiced in accordance with the vinaya.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
 
 Afterwards, up to the age of 31, he studied the upper and lower divisions of 
inner philosophical knowledge, in particular, for 12 years, the Great Detailed 
Explanation6 under Guru Dharmarakshita in Od antapuri. As he was very well 
versed in the scriptures of the four origin al traditions, he had an infallible grasp of 
the tiniest details in modes of behavior that should be cultivated and rejected 
according to the various philosophical school s, like [for instance the monastic rules 
concerning] accepting gifts. 
 
Up to the age of 31, Atisha studied the vehicl e of philosophy, that is, he studied the upper 
tenets, the Madhyamaka and Chittamatra, an d the lower tenets, the Sautrantika and 
Vaibhashika. These tenets have different assertions  or points of view. In particular, he studied 
all four tenet systems under the guidance of  Dharmarakshita (Protecting the Dharma) for 
twelve years in Odantapuri. For example, he  studied the five bases which are the main 
subjects of the Treasury of Manifest Dharma , as well as the two subjects of the Supplement to the 
Middle Way , the profound and the vast. He gained kn owledge of the scriptures and then the 
realization of their meanings. He came to unders tand all the subtle details of the vinaya, for 
example, that a fully-ordained monk cannot take things if they have no t been offered to him. 
If he needs to take food, for example, because ther e is no one to offer it to him, he must first 
ask permission from Buddha Shakyamuni to do this . Atisha understood all of this clearly and 
completely without any mixing. Thus, from the po int of view of scripture Atisha understood 
all of sutra and tantra, the th ree baskets and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B1B-2B How [Atisha] attained the realizational qualities of its correct accomplishment 
1 How all realizational qualities are contained in the three trainings 
2 How [Atisha] was accomplished in the three trainings 
2B1B-2B1 How all realizational qualities are contained in the three trainings 
Second: As generally speaking all the sc riptural teachings by the Conqueror are 
contained in the three precious collection s, the realizational teachings are also 
contained in three precious trainings. 
 
All the scriptural teachings of the Buddha are included in the three baskets – the vinaya, 
sutra, and abhidharma baskets. The realizationa l teachings – the higher trainings of morality, 
concentration, and wisdom – are the subject of these three baskets. The main subject of the 
vinaya basket is the higher trai ning in morality, the main subj ect of the sutra basket is the 
higher training in concentration, and the main subject of the abhidharma basket is the higher 
                                              
6 Mahavibhasha 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
17 training in wisdom. Likewise the doctrine of  realization are included in three. A more 
detailed explanation of this subject is given by Maitreya in his Ornament for the Mahayana 
Sutras  (Sutra-alamkara). In short, the main object  to be practiced is the three: morality, 
concentration, and wisdom. We need to study them and then try to develop them in our 
mind.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B1B-2B2 How [Atisha] was accomplished in the three trainings 
A Ethical discipline 
B Accomplishments in the training of concentration 
C Accomplishments in the training of wisdom 
2B1B-2B2A [Ethical discipline] 
1 Transition to ethical discipline 2 How he was actually accomplished in ethical discipline 
3 Proper protection [of ethical discipline] 
2B1B-2B2A-1 Transition to ethical discipline 
{5} In this regard, the training in ethi cal discipline is often praised in the 
[Buddha’s] excellent teachings and in th e commentaries as the foundation of all 
good qualities such as the trainings in co ncentration and wisdom. Therefore, it is 
necessary, first of all, to have realizationa l qualities in terms of ethical discipline. 
 
First one accomplishes the training in morality ( abhishikya ), then one develops concentration, 
and then one generates wisdom. This is the orde r in which they are necessarily accomplished. 
The training in morality or ethical discipline is  often praised in sutra and tantra as well as in 
the commentaries by the Indian pandits because in order to achieve the good qualities of 
realization one first needs morality.  
 Morality is a mind of safeguarding. While mora lity can also be physic al, it is mainly the 
mental attitude wishing to safeguard not killing,  not stealing, not engaging sexual misconduct, 
not lying, not engaging in divisive talk, not enga ging in harsh words, and not engaging in idle 
talk. It is also a mind of abandonment, the wish to abandon bad ideas and bad actions.  
 Morality includes the five vows or precepts of lay people, who are called upasikas  if they are 
men and upasikanis  if they are women. Upa means “close, whereas sika means becoming, 
therefore this word means “becoming close,” that  is, approaching, nirvana. A lay vow-holder 
has five precepts – those of not killing, not st ealing, not engaging in sexual misconduct, not 
l y i n g ,  a n d  n o t  t a k i n g  i n t o x i c a n t s .  S u c h  a  p e r s o n  s h o u l d  g u a r d  t h e s e  f i v e  v o w s .  T o  k e e p  morality means just this. One should think, fo r example, “I will not harm or kill even an 
insect”; this is a mind of safeguarding or a mind of abandonment.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B1B-2B2A-2 How [Atisha] was actually accomplished in ethical discipline 
A How [Atisha] possessed the highest vows of individual liberation 
In this regard there are three [kinds]: Ho w he held the first of those, the highest 
vows of individual liberation: 
Having received the bhikshu vows, he protected them the way a yak protects its coat; a yak is so attached to its coat that it rather risks its life trying to save a strand 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
18 of tail hair that has got caught  on a tree than to part with it, even if it sees its life is 
being threatened by a hunter. That is how Atisha protected every detail of the 
foundation of trainings, not to speak of  the major [rules of] the foundation of 
trainings which he had undertaken, so that (therefore) he came to be called the 
elder who is a great upholder of discipline. 
 In the case of the vows of indivi dual liberation, one first takes the lay vows, then the vows of a 
novice monk or nun ( shramanera ), and then the vows of full ordination of a monk or nun 
(bhikshu  or bhikshuni ). They are necessarily taken in this or der. Atisha kept his vows even if his 
life was in danger, meaning that he did not tr ansgress even the smallest of abandonments but 
strove to keep all his vows very precisely. It is said  that if a yak gets its tail caught in a bush, it 
will not pull away from the bush even if a hunter  were to threaten his li fe because it does not 
want to lose even a single strand of tail hair. Just as Atisha took care of his vows in this way, so too do we, his followers, need to take ca re of our vows. He became an elder who was a 
great upholder of the vinaya or discipline. The word “elder” in Tibetan is ne ten  (gnas brtan ), ne 
meaning “abiding,” that is, abiding in the vows, and ten meaning “stable,” that is, stable in the 
vows like a mountain that ca nnot be moved by anything.  
 There are eight types of individual liberation vows: 
(1) the vows that are taken for one full day called one-day precepts (Tib. bsnyen gnas ; Skt. 
upavasa ; literally, approacher-abider); 
(2-3) the vows of laymen (Tib. dge bsnyen ; Skt. upasaka ; literally, male virtue-approachers)  and 
laywoman (Tib. dge bsnyen ma ; Skt. upasika ; literally female virtue-approachers);  
(4-5) the vows of novice monks (Tib. dge tshul ; Skt. shramanera ; literally, male virtue-mode) and 
novice nuns (Tib. dge tshul ma ; Skt. shramanerika ; literally, female virtue-mode); 
(6) the vows of probationary nuns (Tib. dge slob ma ; Skt. shikshamana ; literally, female virtue-
trainees); and (7-8) the vows of fully- ordained monks (Tib. dge slong ; Skt. bhikshu ; literally, male virtue-
beggars) and fully-ordained nuns (Tib. dge slong ma ; Skt. bhikshuni ; literally female virtue-
beggars). 
 
There are eight one-day precepts. [Lay vow-holders have five vows ], novices have thirty-six 
vows, probationary nuns have forty-eight vows, fully-ordained monks have 253 vows, and fully-
ordained nuns have 364 vows. These vows of indivi dual liberation are wh at liberate one from 
cyclic existence and set one in nirvana.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
B How [Atisha] possessed the bodhisattva vows 
Bodhisattva vows: He practiced numerous in structions for training in the mind of 
enlightenment which is rooted in love and compassion, and in particular, in 
attendance upon Serlingpa, he trained for a long time in the highest instructions 
passed on by Venerable Maitreya and Manjugosha through Asanga and 
Shantideva. Thereby the mind of enlighte nment that cherishes others more than 
oneself, arose in his heart. This aspiration al mind gave rise to the mind of active 
involvement and due to the good behavior of training in the trainings following 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
19 the promise to train in the vast conduct of bodhisattvas, he never transgressed the 
rules of Buddha’s children. 
 
Love is the mental attitude wishing all sentie nt beings to have happiness and the causes of 
happiness. Compassion is the mental attitude wishing all sentient beings to be free from 
suffering and the causes of suffering. These tw o attitudes are the roots or bases upon which 
one develops bodhichitta, the mind of enlightenm ent. Atisha received many instructions on 
how to develop the mind of enlightenment, in  particular he received instructions from 
Serlingpa, a lama from Sumatra, who is also  called Dharmakirti. Buddha taught Maitreya who 
then taught Asanga, the author of five texts known collectively as the Five Treatises on the 
Grounds. Buddha also taught Manjushri who then taught Shantideva, the author of Engaging 
in the Bodhisattva Deeds  (Bodhisattvacharyavatara ), the Compendium of Trainings  
(Shiksasammucchaya ), and so forth. Atisha received al l these teachings from Dharmakirti and 
then trained in them for a long time until he developed the mind of enlightenment that 
cherishes all sentient beings and gives up cheris hing oneself. One comes to see that oneself is 
just one whereas others are infinite, whereby one comes to see that oneself is unimportant 
and thereby generates the mind of enlightenmen t. One exchanges oneself for others, meaning 
that one changes the mental attitude of ordina ry beings who wish only to take care of 
themselves for the attitude that wishes to only take care of and cherish other sentient beings.  
 There are two kinds of mind of enlightenmen t. The first is the wishing or aspirational 
mind of enlightenment which is the mere wish to attain enlightenment for the benefit of all 
sentient beings. The second is the engaging mind of enlightenment (translated above as 
“mind of active involvement”) which involves ge nerating the mind of enlightenment and also 
engaging in the vast conduct of  a bodhisattva. This conduct co nsists of training in the six 
perfections of generosity, morality, patience, e ffort, concentration, and wisdom or in the ten 
perfections. One engages in these perfecti ons for the purpose of ripening one’s own 
continuum. In addition, engages in the four me ans of gathering disciples for the purpose of 
ripening others’ continua. Similarly, Christians al so first give others material things, and then 
they talk nicely to them, whereby people genera te confidence in them. They then teach others 
how to do the practices related to  Christianity, and, as this is not enough, they also practice 
what they preach. These are the ways of ripeni ng or maturing others’ minds that are also 
practiced by bodhisattvas. Thus, they are called the conduct of a bodhisattva.  
 Generosity is a mind of giving. One can give material things, the Dharma or one’s 
knowledge, and protection. From the point of view of sutra there are these three types of 
generosity. From the point of view of tantra th ere are four types with the addition of giving 
love. The generosity of material things includes giving food to those without food, clothing to 
those without clothes, bedding to  those without bedding, and so forth. The giving of Dharma 
is to teach without miserliness what one knows of the three baskets. One can also teach other 
things that one knows, for example, one can te ach how to paint those who want to know how 
to paint. The generosity of protection is to pr otect those whose life is in danger. For example, 
although in general monks are not permitted to lie,  if it is for a good purpose they can tell a 
lie. When a hunter, for example, has lost his prey  in the forest and asks a monk if he saw the 
animal, even if he did see it he  can say instead, “I saw a man pa ss by,” even though in actual 
fact he saw, for example, the rabbit. Also if someone is trying to murder a person who has 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
20 taken shelter in one’s home and one has hidden him in a closet in order to protect him, one 
can say to the murderer that the other person has left and gone elsewhere.  
 There are also different kinds of morality: th e morality of safeguarding the vows that one 
has taken, the morality of benefiting all sentient  beings, and the morality of practicing virtue. 
In addition, we need to practice patience. Just as even a small fire ca n burn down an entire 
forest, similarly even one instant of anger or hatred toward someone can destroy many eons 
of the collection of merit or virtue. Anger is therefore the most dang erous of minds as it 
harms oneself as well as others. For this reason , we need to practice patience. Patience means 
that when someone acts against us, deprecates us, criticizes us, or yells at us, at this time our 
mind should be relaxed and as unmoved as a mountain. Also when someone beats us we 
should not retaliate by beating the person. We n eed this patience of non-retaliation. We also 
need patience with respect to the Dharma. For example, when it is difficult to understand 
something, we should put great effort into ou r studies, bearing the ensuing difficulties with 
patience. This is called the patience of practicing the Dharma. Chandrakirti in his Supplement 
to the Middle Way  says: 
 
The Sugata mainly praise d these three practices  
Of generosity, morality, and patience for lay people.  
These are also the collection of merit, the cause  
Of a buddha body, the nature of which is form.    [ 3 . 1 2 ]  
 
The Buddha said that lay people should mainly  practice the three perfections of generosity, 
morality, and patience. For example, when we become angry with our wife or husband, we 
need patience. We also need to avoid getting angry and instead practice patience with our 
c h i l d r e n  w h e n  t h e y  c r y  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  I n  f a c t  we need to continually practice patience with 
respect to our wife, husband, children, and other family members.   The practice of effort, on the other hand, is necessary for everyone, whether lay or 
ordained. The main practices of the ordained, monks and nuns, are the last two perfections, 
those of concentration and wisdom. Because th ey do not need to care for a partner or 
children, it is easier for them to meditate and develop concentration.  
 However, whether one is lay or ordained it  is important to try to develop one’s inner 
qualities. We should try to practice the bodhisa ttva conduct or activities as much as we can in 
the same way that bodhisattvas do. To do this, we should train in the morality or vows of 
bodhisattvas. The bodhisattva vows consist of eighteen root vows and forty-six secondary 
vows. We should learn them and then try to keep  them well, just as Atisha trained in keeping 
these vows.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
C How [Atisha] possessed vajrayana vows 
Vajrayana vows: Owing to the fact that he had the meditative stabilizations of the 
generation stage in which one’s own body is seen as that of a deity and of the 
completion stage of the vajra mind, {6} he became chief among yogis. In particular 
he protected his tantric commitments properly by not transgressing the prescribed 
rules. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
21 In order to enter the vajrayana, one must first receive an empowerment. And prior to taking 
the four empowerments of highest yoga tantra, on e must first take the tantra vows. In highest 
yoga tantra there are in general the vase, se cret, wisdom, and word empowerments, however 
in the case of Kalachakra they are slightly different. Having taken an empowerment, one is 
permitted to visualize that one’s body of bones, flesh, and skin, this presently impure body, is 
completely purified and that one arises in the body of deity, the nature of which is wisdom. 
T h i s  i s  c a l l e d  “ t h e  g e n e r a t i o n  s t a g e ”  ( bskyed rim ) .  W h a t  i s  i t  t h a t  i s  g e n e r a t e d ?  T h e  f i v e  
aggregates – form or body, feelings, di scriminations, compositional factors and 
consciousnesses aggregates – are transformed and generated into the tathagatas of the five 
lineages. Thus, the body or form aggregate is transformed into Vairochana, the feeling 
aggregate into Ratnasambhava, the discri mination aggregate into Amitabha, the 
compositional factors aggregat e into Amoghasiddhi, and the consciousness aggregate into 
Akshobhya. In this way the five aggregates take  on the aspect of the five buddha lineages. In 
short, this is the generation stage in which on e generates one’s body as the body of a deity. 
 After that there is the completion stage ( rdzogs rim ). It generally consists of five stages, 
although there are slight differences between the different tantras. From the point of view of 
Guhyasamaja there are either five or six stages. The six are: the isolation of body, isolation of 
speech, isolation of mind, impure illusory body, actual clear light, and the union of the pure 
illusory body and actual clear light. In order to achieve these, we first need to meditate on the 
generation stage, in particular on the practice of taking death into the path to the truth body, 
the intermediate state into the path to the en joyment body, and birth into the path to the 
emanation body. This is a very general description of tantra. 
 In short, Atisha practiced tantra and obtained  the realizations. He also had the realizations 
or qualities of the vows of individual liberati on, the bodhisattva vows, and the tantra vows. 
He had both the scriptural qualities and the realizational qualities. We should generate 
interest in attaining these qualities and th en generate the intention to develop them. 
 In our everyday life we should try to do a littl e bit of meditation, for example, the meditation 
on the breath. When the mind is  unclear, agitated, or disturbe d by many thoughts, at that 
time one should try to meditate a bit on the breath. Imagine that one exhales from the right 
nostril, thinking that all one’s negative emot ions, agitation, nervousn ess, and the pains of 
body and mind are completely exhaled in the as pect of black smoke an d go out into space. 
Then one inhales through one’s left nostril all the compassion, love, omniscience, and energy 
of the holy beings in the form of white ligh t. Think that one’s body becomes completely 
transparent, like crystal, and filled with this wh ite light and qualities, and that one’ mind also 
becomes clear and very relaxed. One should do this for three, four, or five minutes, while 
inhaling and exhaling. This meditation is not difficult. One thinks “Now I am exhaling. Now 
I am inhaling.” It is a method for making one’s  mind more relaxed. One should try to keep 
one’s mind happy by reducing thoughts in this way. Many different conceptions arise in our mind that disturb us and make us unhappy, fo r example, the thought “I cannot do it. I better 
give up.” This way of thinking is a mistake. We  are all able to become a buddha, a holy being. 
We should think “Why not I? I too have been born in the human race.” We should think in this way.   From the Christian point of view, in Italy and in other Western countries there are many 
saints, both male and female. Likewise, also fr om the Buddhist point of view there are many 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
22 foe-destroyers and buddhas. In the different cult ures there are different ways of teaching but 
the final aim is the same. For example, if there are four roads that all lead to this building, 
whichever road one travels to get here, when one arrives the building is the same. Because 
there are different mentalities and different cultures, there is a need to teach in different ways 
in order for human beings to gain realizations . On the other hand, animals in general cannot 
be taught the Dharma or a religion. However, it is said that Saint Francis, for example, gave teachings to a wolf that had killed many people , due to which it stoppe d killing, became calm, 
and like a dog stayed near Saint Francis’s medi tation place. While it is possible to teach 
animals some things, for example, some animals are trained how to kill, in general we cannot 
teach them the Dharma. For example, although  we can teach a parrot to recite OM MANI 
PADME HUM, we cannot teach it to understand the meaning.  
 Wednesday morning, 19 January 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B2B-2B2A-3 Proper protection [of ethical discipline] 
Not only did he courageously undertake th e trainings in ethical discipline of the 
three vows, he also upheld them as he  had promised. He kept the vows without 
transgressing the rules associated with them and even if he transgressed them ever 
so slightly, he quickly, very quickly purifi ed this by means of a suitable ritual for 
restoring the given vow. 
 
Atisha safeguarded the three moralities comple tely without transgression. However, when he 
did transgress them, he immediately confessed it in  order to purify the transgression. It is said 
that he always carried a statue of Buddha Sh akyamuni with him wherever he went, and when 
he committed even a very slight transgression he would take out the statue, set it in front of 
him, and perform three prostrations to it. In th is way he strove to keep proper morality or 
ethical discipline. On the other hand, we ordinary  beings tend to keep our vows well after we 
first take ordination but then, as time goes by , we become distracted and no longer keep our 
vows so purely. Atisha, who was a holy being, never did this. Ther efore, we should rejoice in 
his purity and try to follow his example by k eeping pure the three vows or three moralities: 
the vows of individual liberation, the bodhisa ttva vows, and the tantra vows. Atisha also 
possessed the three types of vows of individual li beration: the five lay precepts, the thirty-six 
novice vows, and the 253 vows of a fully-ordain e d  m o n k .  H e  a l s o  k e p t  a l l  o f  t h e m  w e l l .  
Whenever we transgress any of our vows, we should confess it immediately.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B1B-2B2B [Atisha’s ] accomplishments in the training in concentration 
1 Common 
His accomplishments in the training of concentration are twofold. Common 
concentration: he had achieved the servicea bility of the mind of calm abiding.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
23 2 Uncommon 
As regards uncommon concentration, he had the extreme stability of the 
generation stage. Furthermore, he comple ted the three or six years of conduct of 
yogic realization discipline.7 
 
There are common and uncommon concentrations . The common concentration is the union 
of calm abiding (samatha) and special insigh t (vipassana), which makes the body and mind 
serviceable. “Common” in this case means commo n to or shared by both sutra and tantra, or 
common to both Buddhists and non-Buddhists. They are common because calm abiding and 
special insight are also practi ced by the saddhus in the Hind u tradition for the purpose of 
achieving higher realizations su ch as clairvoyance. However, their calm abiding and special 
insight are not conjoined with the thought of definite emergence or renunciation.  
 Uncommon in this case refers to the context of tantra. In ta ntra there are the two stages, 
the generation stage and the completion stage. On e must first gain stability in the generation 
stage. In this stage, one carries death into th e path to the truth body, the intermediate state 
into the path to the enjoyment body, and birth into the path to the emanation body. In this 
way death is no longer an ordinary death, the intermediate state is no longer an ordinary 
intermediate state, and birth is no longer an ordinary birth; instead they become special due 
to the power of one’s meditation. When one’s  meditation on the inestimable mansion and 
the deities in it becomes stable, one is able to visualize them as no bigger than a tiny mustard 
seed. In short, the generation stage is the generation of death into the truth body, the 
intermediate state into the enjoyment body, and birth into the emanation body. When one 
does a self-generation or a sadhana and arises as a deity with one face and two arms, this is the 
practice of the generation stage. In the comple tion stage one purifies the channels, winds, and 
drops, whereby one can attain the actual concentration of the completion stage.  
 Atisha also performed three or six years of conduct of yogic realization discipline.   
Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B1B-2B2C His accomplishments in the training of wisdom 
1 Common 
His accomplishments in the training of wisdom has two aspects. The common 
one: he had attained the concentration of special insight that is a union of calm 
abiding and special insight. 
 
2 Uncommon 
The uncommon one: he had attained the sp ecial concentration of the completion 
stage. 
 The common wisdom is from the point of view  of sutra; it refers to the meditative 
stabilization of special insight that is a union of calm abiding and special insight. This is 
common to both sutra and tantra. The unco mmon wisdom of tantra is the special 
concentration of the completion stage. The comp letion stage includes the isolation of body, 
isolation of speech, isolation of mind, metaphor ic clear light, impure illusory body, actual
