Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
24 clear light, and the union of the pure illusory bo dy and actual clear light,  that is, the union of 
the exalted body and exalted mind.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B1B-3 The deeds he did for the sake of the teachings, having attained those positive qualities  
A Deeds  in India 
B Deeds in Tibet 
The deeds he did for the sake of the teachings  are of two [types]: his deeds in India 
and his deeds in Tibet. 
2B1B-3A Deeds  in India 
First: At the palace of great enlightenm ent in Bodhgaya he defeated three times 
the proponents of inferior non-Buddhist doctrines8 by means of the Dharma, thus 
maintaining the Buddha’s teachings. El iminating the taints of ignorance,9 
misconception, and doubt also in regard  to the teachings of his own upper and 
lower systems, he spread the teachings, so  that he was regarded as a crown jewel 
beyond partiality by [proponents of] all factions.  
 
This section describes Atisha’s deeds in India. Bodhgaya ( bodhi  meaning “mind”), called Vajra 
Seat ( rdo rje’i gdan ) in Tibetan, is the place where the Buddha attained enlightenment under a 
bodhi tree. It is said that in the evening he destroyed the maras or demons. While the 
Buddha was in meditation, the maras manifested very beautiful girls who danced in front of 
him, trying to disturb his mind by making hi m generate attachment. However, by the power 
of the Buddha’s meditation, they  were transformed into ugly ol d hag who, out of shame, then 
disappeared. The maras then set an army of so ldiers armed with bows and arrows who shot at 
the Buddha. However, when the arrows reached his body they became beautiful flowers that 
could not harm him. In this way he defeated  and destroyed the demons. Then at dawn, he 
achieved enlightenment, the actual clear ligh t, and became a buddha. Presently there is a 
stupa in Bodhgaya behind which is a bodhi tree that is a descendent of  the tree under which 
the Buddha attained enlightenment.  
 Sometimes people come to me and say that demons are disturbing their meditation and 
ask for my help. However, in general it is not that their minds are disturbed by outer demons, 
more often they are disturbed by the inner demons  of the afflictions. It  is these demons that 
we need to destroy. However, it can happen that when we are in particular trying to do good things that there arise interferences caused by external demons.   The Buddhist teachings describes four ty pes of maras or demons: the mara of the 
aggregates, the mara of the afflictions, the mara  of the Lord of Death, and the mara of the 
sons-of-the-gods. The sons-of-the-gods are a type of being included among the six types of gods 
o f  t h e  d e s i r e  r e a l m ,  k n o w n  a s  L o r d  o f  D e s i r e .  T h e y  a r e  s a i d  t o  s h o o t  f i v e  t y p e s  o f  a r r o w s  
related to the energies of attachment, hatred, ig norance, pride, and jealousy. When we are hit 
by one of these arrows, our mind is influenced by  that particular affliction right from the time 
                                             
 
8 JN: mu stegs kyi smra ba ngan pa , the evil Forder s or Tirthikas  
9 JN: ma rtogs , literally ‘non-realization’

Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
25 we wake up in the morning. This is a sign that we have been influenced by the negative energy 
of these beings. 
 Our inner mental afflictions are also consider ed to be a mara; in fact they are the main 
m a r a  o r  d e m o n .  D e a t h  i s  a l s o  c o n s i d e r e d  t o  b e  a  m a r a  b e c a u s e ,  e v e n  t h o u g h  w e  w a n t  t o  
remain alive, when death comes we have no choice but to die. The contaminated body 
aggregate is also considered to be a mara because it harms us due to being the base of many types of sufferings. Because this body needs f ood, clothing, shelter, and so forth, we work 
hard in the service of our body, yet in spite of this our body still becomes sick. Presently there 
are illnesses in the world such as AIDS and cancer  that did not exist in the past or were very 
rare, whereas nowadays they have become very  common. All of these sicknesses are based on 
the body. Thus, the body aggregat e is considered to be a mara because it prevents us from 
practicing Dharma and developing our inner qualities.   When the demon or mara of th e mental afflictions – attachme nt, hatred, ignorance, pride, 
afflicted doubt, wrong view, an d  s o  fo r t h – har m  u s , w e n eed  t o  keep o u r  m i n d  calm  an d 
relaxed. In the past in India there were many  demons, but perhaps nowadays there are even 
more! The Dharma as taught by the Buddha degene rated in India, as did some of the ancient 
Hindu schools such as the Samkya and Vaisheshika. In the past brahmins were careful not to 
eat black foods but I think that th ey now eat them in hiding. Simi larly, all the major religions, 
a l t h o u g h  t h e y  a p p e a r  t o  b e  g o o d ,  h a v e  i n  f a ct degenerated. This is because the time has 
worsened in the sense that our minds have wo rsened, given the fact that we have more 
afflictions such as jealousy and pride.   In short, due to Atisha holding the completely pure doctrine, all the different sects in India respected him as an elder.   
Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B1B-3B Deeds in Tibet 
1 Clearing up misconceptions about the Dharma and restoring the tradition 
2 Identifying the three causes of excellence in the composition of commentaries  
3 The ability to ascertain the Conqueror’s intentions due to these three causes 
4 The special qualities of his disciples 
2B1B-3B1 Clearing up misconceptions about the Dharma and restoring the tradition 
Deeds in Tibet: Lha Lama10 {7} and his nephew sent th e two great translators Gya-
tson-seng and Nagtso Tsultrim Gyalwa to India one after the other. As they took 
great pains again and again to invite [Atish a], he came to Upper Ngari during [the 
reign of] Jangchub O11 and was requested to purify the Buddha’s teachings. 
Thereupon, he spread the teachings by composing the Lamp on the Path to 
Enlightenment , a text bringing together all the points of sutra and tantra and 
condensing them into applicable stages,12 as well as other texts. Thus, he spent 
three years in Ngari, nine years in Nyetha ng, and five years elsewhere in Tsang and 
Central Tibet teaching the fortunate all th e scriptures and instructions of sutra 
and tantra without exception. Thus, he rest ored the tradition of teachings that had 
                                              
10 King of Guge 
11 The nephew of Lha Lama 
12 JN: nyams su len pa’i rim pa , stages of practice  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
26 declined, made those that remained flourish, corrected13 those that had been 
soiled by the errors of misconception, en suring that the precious teachings were 
freed from all flaws. 
 
Part of the area of Ngari is now included in La dhak, India. In the past there lived in Ngari the 
son of a king called Lha La ma Yeshe O and his nephew Lh a Lama Jangchub O. It is 
recounted that Lha Lama Yeshe O set out to India to invite Atisha to come to Tibet but that 
at the border he was ca ught and imprisoned, due to which he could not complete his mission. 
Jangchub O made attempts to get him freed from  prison but the minister of the area agreed 
to free him only if Jangchub O were to bring him an amount of gold equal in size to the body of Yeshe O. Through a window in the pris on cell, Jangchub O told Yeshe O what the 
minister had agreed to, and told him that he had been able to find gold equal in size to Yeshe 
O’s body minus the size of his head. He reques ted Yeshe O to be patient while he found the 
rest and to continue to live. However, Yeshe O replied that he had no problem giving up his 
body for the Dharma, and asked Jangchub O to no t give the minister the gold but instead to 
go to India and invite Atis ha, while offering him the gold, to come to Tibet.  
 Jangchub O did this and Atisha’s abbot, Ra tnakara, upon receiving instructions from a 
naturally-occurring image of Tara on the Bodhga ya stupa to send Atisha to Tibet, granted 
Atisha permission to go to Tibet for not mo re than three years. Atisha brought the two 
translators Gya-tson-seng and Nagtso Tsultrim Gyalwa to the Ngari area of Tibet where he 
gave teachings and composed the Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment . He also wrote a version of 
this text in Sanskrit and sent it back to his teacher, Ratnakara. When  Ratnakara read it, he 
thought if was extremely useful , being short and precise yet co ntaining all the practices of 
sutra and tantra without omission. He realized th at if he had not permitted Atisha to go to 
Tibet, Atisha would never have written this text. He then requested Atisha to write a 
commentary on the Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment  and granted him permission to stay as 
long as he wanted in Tibet. Due to this, Atisha remained in  the Ngari area of upper Tibet 
(Toling) for three years, after which he went to Nyethang in Central Tibet. He liked this place 
very much and built a monastery called Nyetha ng Dewachen (Nyethang Sukavati Monastery). 
Beside it, he built a Tara temple and then staye d there for nine years. He also visited other 
places in Tibet over a five-year period. Ther efore, he remained in Tibet for a total of 
seventeen years before passing away, I think in Nyethang. His body was cremated and his ashes were put in a vase and kept in his mo nastery in Nyethang. When the Chinese invaded 
that area, they found the vase on which was in scribed Atisha’s name and place of origin due 
to which they returned the vase to Bangladesh in India. In Tibet there still remain small clay 
tsa-tsas (relieves of deities) and clay stupas made by Atisha. When I visited his monastery, I 
asked an old ex-monk who was taking care of the monastery to give some to me. He gave 
them to me but advised me to keep them hi dden as otherwise the Chinese military could 
cause problems. Atisha had made 100,000 tsa-tsas , which had been placed in a stupa that was 
later destroyed by the Chinese.   Atisha practiced both sutra and tantra. In  Tibet he restored the teachings that had 
degenerated due to differing ideas and wrong conceptions. In this way Atisha made the Buddha’s doctrine once again pure in Tibet.  
                                             
 
13 JN: legs par bsal , excellently clarified 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
27  Atisha had many disciples in Tibet but the main or best among them was Drom Tonpa, a 
lay vow-holder (upasika), who is said to have  been a manifestation of Avalokiteshvara. His 
appearance was that of an extremely poor be ggar and it is recounted that one day while 
traveling he met a rich man who asked him wher e he was going. When he said that he was 
going to a particular monastery, the rich man,  who was going to the sa me place, gave him a 
pair of shoes to carry for him. When they arri ved near the monastery, there were many monks 
waiting, playing music and holding banners. The rich man asked Drom Tonpa what was 
happening and Drom Tonpa told him that they were waiting for him! The rich man was very 
ashamed that he had made Drom Tonpa carr y his shoes and ran off without his shoes! 
 Drom Tonpa’s incarnation is Reting Rinpoche, who is said to be a manifestation of Four-
Arm Avalokiteshvara (Tib. Chenrezig) whereas Hi s Holiness the Dalai Lama is said to be a 
manifestation of Thousand-Arm Avalokiteshvara.  Although these aspects of Avalokiteshvara 
are different, the qualities of their mind is the same. A previous incarnation of Reting 
Rinpoche, Ngawang Choden, was a well-known, l ay lama of the Nyingma tradition who was a 
teacher of the V Dalai Lama, who wore his long hair tied  up at the back. One day when 
traveling with the V Dalai Lama, the Dalai Lama told him it would be better to cut off his hair. From this, he understood that in his next life he should take the aspect of a monk. In f a c t ,  i n  h i s  n e x t  l i f e  h e  b e c a m e  a  m o n k  a n d entered the Samlo Kangtsen (Geshe Tenzin 
Tenphel’s kangtsen) of Sera Je Monastery an d became the Ganden throne-holder and the 
tutor of the VII Dalai Lama.  
 His next incarnation, Reting Trichen Tenp a Rabgye was well known as he acted as the 
regent of Tibet. It is recounted that when th e XIII Dalai Lama went to  Reting Monastery with 
him by car, a lot of dust flew up into the air due to which he earned the nickname Tala Lung 
Karpo (White Ashes Blowing in the Wind). When the XIII Dalai Lama arrived at the 
monastery, he instructed the monks to set out two thrones of similar height and width and to 
offer himself and the regent a long life puja.  During the puja, the XIII Dalai Lama suddenly 
stood up and prostrated in front of Reting Ri npoche. He offered a mandala to him and asked 
him to act as his regent or substitute when he passed away. Reting Rinpoche agreed to do 
this. Later on, as the regent of Tibet, he se t out to find the XIV Dalai Lama. He visited 
Lhamo Lha Tso, a lake related to the female protector Palden Lhamo,  in which he had a 
vision of a house in Amdo and the first letters of the father’s and moth er’s names. When he 
returned to Lhasa, he found that in the mean time some members of the Tibetan government 
had recognized the son of the XIII Dalai Lama ’s brother as the incarnation of XIII Dalai 
L a m a .  T h e  n e x t  d a y  t h e y  w e r e  t o  o f f e r  t h i s  c h i l d  k a t a s  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  T o  s t o p  t h i s  f r o m  
happening, Reting Rinpoche wrote all Tibetan go vernment workers telling them to come to a 
meeting the next day at 9:00 AM and saying that if they did not come they would lose their 
positions. Due to this, no one went to the home  of XIII Dalai Lama’s brother, who waited in 
vain with his son for their arrival. Reting Ri npoche then sent some lamas together with 
members of the Tibetan government in search of  the XIII Dalai Lama’s incarnation to Amdo, 
some to Tsang, some to the south, and some to Kham. In this way Keutsang Rinpoche and 
his party arrived in Amdo. When they were abou t to go to the house that Reting Rinpoche 
had seen in the lake, Keutsang Rinpoche dre ssed himself as a servant, whereas his servant 
dressed himself in very fine clot hes. When they arrived at the house, a young boy, only four 
or five years old, immediately embraced Keutsang Rinpoche, greatly surprising this lama. The search party had a mala, bell, damaru, and othe r items that had belonged to the previous

28 Dalai Lama together with similar items that ha d not belonged to him. They set them all out 
on a table, whereupon the young body immediatel y took up all those that had belonged to the 
XIII Dalai Lama. Keutsang Rinpoche was comple tely convinced that the young boy was the 
XIV Dalai Lama. Before he could be taken to Lhasa, the Chinese aske d that the child be 
brought to them and asked that a payment of go ld and so forth be made to them in order 
that they grant permission to allow him to travel  to Lhasa. This was done and then, by horse, 
he was slowly taken to Lhasa. When they arrived in Ngagchu, near where my family lived, I 
went to meet him. I remember he was wearing a yellow hat. In this way, I met the Dalai Lama 
for the first time when I was about seven or eight years old. Everyone there was extremely 
happy to see the incarnation of the Dalai La ma, whom the Tibetans call Gyelwa Rinpoche 
(Precious Conqueror). Thus, mainly due to Re ting Rinpoche the incarnation of the XIII 
Dalai Lama was found.  
 In short, Drom Tonpa is the manifestat ion of Four-Armed Avalokiteshvara and his 
incarnation is called Reting Rinpoche. Nowadays  there are many political problems in Tibet 
in relation to him, about which books have  been written by some Western authors.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B1B-3B2 Identifying the three causes of excellence in the composition of commentaries 
There are three causes of excellence in the composition of a text clarifying the 
Muni’s intentions: (1) being learned in th e five areas of knowledge, (2) having 
received instructions for the applicatio n of their meaning, which transmit the 
i n s t r u c t i o n s  o f  t h e  p e r f e c t l y  c o m p l e t e  B u d d h a  i n  a n  u n b r o k e n  l i n e  o f  e x a l t e d  
[masters], and (3) having received the perm ission to teach through the vision of a 
protector deity.14 If a text can be composed with any one of these [causes of 
excellence], it will turn out greatly excell ent if all three are complete. This Great 
Master, [Atisha,] possessed all three. 
 Atisha possessed all three qualities needed to co mpose a text or scripture clarifying the Muni’s 
intentions. First of all he was learned in the five outer sciences or areas of knowledge – 
linguistics ( sgra rig pa ), medicine ( gso ba rig pa ), dialectics ( gtan tshig rig pa ), crafts ( bzo rig pa ), 
and valid cognition ( tshad ma rig pa ). He was also learned in the five inner sciences – the five 
texts studied in monasteries – Perfection ( Ornament for Clear Realizations, Abhisamayalamkara ), 
Supplement to the Middle Way  (Madhyamakavatara ), Treasury of Manifest Dharma  
(Abhidharmakosha ), Compendium of Valid Cognition  (Pramanavarttikakarika ), and Discipline 
(Vinaya ). Atisha wrote the lam-rim text Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment  and other texts. He 
also had experience of putting the instructions into practice and could teach them to others. 
He had received the uninterrupted lineage of these teachings passed down from Buddha 
Shakyamuni down through other holy beings to the present day. Holy beings give the oral 
transmission and explanation based on their ex perience. He had also received permission 
from his meditational deity, in this case Tara , to write the text. Ther efore, he had all three 
qualities complete.

29 Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B1B-3B3 The ability to ascertain th e Conqueror’s intentions due to [Atisha] possessing these three causes  
A The care of protector deities 
As for being cared for by the protector deities it says in the Praise : 
 
Of the glorious Hevajra, 
of the King of Array of Samayas, of heroic Lokeshvara, noble, venerable Tara, and so forth  you had visions and permissions, therefore, in dreams and directly 
you perpetually heard teachings
15 
both the ones profound and vast. 
 
Atisha received visions of Hevajra; the King of  Array of Samayas, a deity similar in aspect to 
Buddha Shakyamuni (the empowerment of which is included in the Rinchung Gyatsa ); heroic 
Lokeshvara (Avalokiteshvara);  and noble, venerable Tara. Jo mo , translated here as “noble,” is 
a title used to for nuns in some parts of Tibet.  Tara is the manifestation in the aspect of a 
deity of the enlightened activities of all th e buddhas. In other words, all the enlightened 
activities of all the buddhas of th e three times, all those that exist, are revealed in the aspect of 
a deity called “Tara.” There are many aspects of Ta ra, such as the twenty-one Taras. There is a 
prayer to Tara that says:  
Deity whom I have practiced in previous lives,  Enlightened activity of all the buddhas of the three times,  
Green, one-faced, two-armed, swift pacifier;  Mother who holds an utpa la, I prostrate to you. 
 Since every virtuous action that is created by anyo ne is called an “enlightened activity,” we are 
close to Tara who is the enlightened activity of  all the buddhas of the three times. For this 
reason, if we pray to her and ask her to help us , we will easily receive blessings from her and 
thereby be able to solve our problems. The story of  Tara is set out in a text by Taranatha that 
has been translated into English ( Origin of Tara Tantra , Library of Tibetan Works and 
Archives, Dharamsala, 1995). If you read this, yo u will understand things more clearly. There 
is a story related to her that happened more recently in a Theravadin monastery in south 
India. In this monastery there were many yo ung monks. Each night one disappeared due to 
having been captured and eaten by demons. Th e monks did not know what to do until one 
young monk reminded the others that the Maha yanists have a deity called Tara who protects 
them. He suggested that maybe they should ta ke refuge in her and recite her mantra. The 
other monks agreed to do this, even though th e practice came from the Mahayana tantras. 
They set an image of Tara in their temple and began to prayed to her. Due to this, the 
                                             
 
15 JN: dam chos , the holy Dharma 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
30 interferers went away and no other monks disa ppeared. This story shows that Tara has the 
power to help us.  
 
In short, whatever happens we should keep our mind quiet and relaxed, and not worry. Try 
to meditate a little bit. Do breathing meditation  while exhaling negative energy and inhaling 
all the positive energy of all the holy bein gs in the universe. Imagine that one becomes 
stronger, more relaxed, and more happy . Try to do this and do not worry!  
 
Wednesday afternoon, 19 January 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim:   
B How [Atisha’s ] lineage is uninterrupted 
[Atisha] held numerous lineages of master s such as the two lineages of the shared 
vehicle and of the great vehicle, with regard to which there are the two – the 
perfection and secret mantra [vehicles]. In the perfection vehicle he held three 
lineages: the lineages of the view and of conduct, the latte r of which has the 
lineage from Maitreya and the lineage from Manjugosha. In the secret mantra he 
had received five types of transmission16 as well as numerous lineages such as the 
lineage of teaching systems,17 the lineage of blessings, and the lineage of various 
instructions. The gurus from whom he hear d [instructions] directly are mentioned 
in the Praise : 
 
The gurus you always relied on were 
Shintipa, Serlingpa,  Bhadrabodhi, Jñanashri – 
many with high accomplishments. 
In particular you held the profound and vast instructions that were passed on from one to another, [all the way] from Nagarjuna. 
 
It is well known that he had twelve gu rus who had attained high accomplishments 
as well as many others. 
 
There is a lineage common to the Hinayana an d Mahayana. The lineage of the Mahayana has 
two: the perfection vehicle and the vajra vehicle.  In the perfection vehicle there is also two: 
the lineage of the view and the lineage of the conduct or the lineage of  the vast. One lineage 
was passed on from Buddha Shakyamuni to Mait reya, to Asanga, to Vasubandhu, to Arya 
Vimuktisena, and so forth. The other was pa ssed from Buddha Shakyamuni to Manjushri and 
so forth. In the secret mantra lineage there are fi ve lineages. There is also the lineage of tenets, 
the lineage of blessings, and the lineage of instructions.  
                                             
 
16 JN: brgyud pa lugs lnga , could also be translated as five lineage traditions 
17 JN: grub mtha’ , tenets 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
31  Atisha listened to teachings from many different gurus including Shintipa, Serlingpa, 
Bhadrabodhi, Jñanashri. Shintipa was also know n by the Sanskrit name  Ratnakara (Source of 
Jewels). Serlingpa, who was from Indonesia, is also known as Dharmakirti. Atisha also 
received teachings from Bhadrabodhi (Good En lightenment) and from Jñanashri (Glorious 
Wisdom). These lamas had attained the common and uncommon attainments (high 
accomplishment). Atisha also received th e special lineage that passed from Buddha 
Shakyamuni to Manjushri, to Nagarjuna, an d then to Buddhapalita and so forth. As 
Nagarjuna held the lineages of both the vast an d profound, Atisha also received teachings on 
the vast and profound. For example, Nagarjuna’s Fundamental Wisdom  is mainly on the 
profound, that is, on the subject of emptiness, his Precious Rosary contains both the profound 
and vast, and in other texts he mainly focuses on the vast – the five path s, the six perfections, 
and the ten grounds. It is renowned that Atisha  had twelve teachers with high attainments.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
C The fact that he was learned in the five areas of knowledge 
The fact that he was learned in the five  areas of knowledge has [already] been 
explained. For all these reasons this mast er was able to ascertain the Conqueror’s 
intentions skillfully. 
 
That Atisha was learned in the five sciences  or areas of knowledge was explained before. 
These five: linguistics, medicine, dialectics, craf ts, and valid cognition. Dialectics or logic 
involves the application of reasonings such as “H e is angry because his face is red” or “He is 
happy because he is smiling.” Likewise, it invo lves the deduction that someone possesses great 
compassion because of the person’s external as pect, that fire exists because there is the 
presence of smoke, and so fort h. For example, from someone’s external expression it can be 
deduced that he or she is happy, unhappy, nervous, and so forth.  
 Atisha had also studied the inner sciences, for example, the Vinaya , which explains how to 
subdue or discipline the mind. He had also studied the Supplement to the Middle Way , which 
teaches the middle way free from the extremes of permanence and annihilation. This text 
eliminates the extreme of permanence by the reasoning that ph enomena are empty of 
inherent existence because they are dependent- arisings and also eliminates the extreme of 
annihilation. This text also teaches that compassi on is like a mother because just as a mother 
gives birth to a child, compassion gives birth to the mind of enlighte nment from which come 
buddhas and bodhisattvas. We need to develop love and compassion for all sentient beings 
beginning with our friends, relatives, and the pe ople around us and then extending it to all 
sentient beings.  
 In addition, Atisha studied the Ornament for Clear Realizations  in which, for example, the 
mind generation (mind of enlightenment) is di vided into aspirational mind generation and 
engaging mind generation. It shows the different ways in which this mind can be generated by 
means of twenty-two analogies. For example, th e mind generation like the earth means that 
just as when a seed is sown in good earth it wi ll grow into a healthy plant, so to when this 
mind exists all other qualities will grow. This mind generation exists from the small level of 
the path of accumulation on up ward. Then the mind generation like refined gold is so-called 
b e c a u s e  j u s t  a s  g o l d c a n  b e  m a de  i n t o  r i n g s ,  e a r r i n g s ,  n e c k l a c e s ,  c u p s ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h  b u t  i t s  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
32 nature does not change and always remains gold , so too does this mind generation continue 
until enlightenment is attained without chan ging due to conditions. In this way, the 
Ornament for Clear Realizations  sets out the vast teachings.  
 Due to having studied these five fields of kn owledge, Atisha was able to understand all the 
intentions of the Buddha’s teachings. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B1B-3B4 The special qualities of his disciples 
A His disciples in India, Ngari and Tibet 
{9} The master who was like this had an in credible number of disciples in India, 
Kashmir, Oddiyana, Nepal, and Tibet, ho wever, the main ones were four great 
scholars in India who matched the master  himself in their knowledge: Pitopa, 
Dharma-akaramati, Madhyasinha, and Ksitig arbha. Some also add Mitraguhya as 
the fifth. In Ngari there were the great translators Rinchen Sangpo and Nagtso as 
well as the ordained King Jangchup O18; in the province of Tsang there were 
Gargewa and Gokugpa Lhaytsay; in Lhod rak there were Chagpa Trichog and 
Gewa Kyong; in Kham there were Naljorpa Chenpo, Gonpawa, Sherab Dorje and 
Chadar Tonpa; and in Central Tibet there were the three, Khu, Ngog, and Drom.  
 
India is called Gyakar ( rgya gar ) in Tibetan, perhaps because Indians generally wear white 
clothes, whereas when a member of the family dies they wear black to show they are in 
mourning. In India there were six great cities in  the past, together they were referred to as 
Gyakar. China is called Gyanag perhaps becaus e the Chinese generally wore black or blue 
clothes, whereas when a member of the family di es they wear white. This is the explanation 
that Tibetans commonly give regarding these two names.  
 Atisha had many disciples in India, Kashmir, Oddiyana (part of  Orissa), Nepal, and Tibet. 
However, his four main disciples were Pitopa, Dharma-akaramati, Madhyasinha, and 
Ksitigarbha. Dharma-akaramati means “Dharm a coming to the mind,” Madhyasinha means 
“middle way lion,” and Ksitigarbha means “essenc e of the earth.” In addition, Atisha had a 
fifth disciple called Mitraguhya.   Ngari is in the western part of Tibet wher e Mount Kailash is found. In Ngari Atisha’s 
disciples included the great translators Rinche n Sangpo and Nagtso, who invited Atisha to 
Tibet, and King Jangchub O, who was respon sible for Atisha coming to Tibet. Rinchen 
Sangpo was a famous translator of the 11
th century who translated many scriptures into a 
more modern Tibetan. His present incarnation is  no longer a monk and as a lay person he 
now works for the Tibetan gove rnment in Delhi. Jangchup O’s incarnation is known as 
Tatsun Rinpoche who is presently living in Se ra Me Monastery. His previous incarnation, 
who lived till he was 85 or 90 years old, gave  many empowerments during his lifetime. When 
the Chinese invaded Tibet, they put him in prison but a Nepalese  family offered to take care 
of him and the Nepalese government intervened  and asked that he be given to them. The 
Chinese agreed and the Nepalese family took ca re of him until he died. His next incarnation 
was then reborn in this family.

33  Naljorpa Chenpo was a great yogi. Gonpawa,  Sherab Dorje and Chadar Tonpa were all 
from the area of Pembo in Tibet. In this area there where there are many monasteries, 
especially Kadampa monasteries, where the mo nks were renowned for keeping such pure 
vows that they did not even look at a woman’ s face. Later on someth ing happened, perhaps 
karma, and all the Kadampa monasteries in Pembo became nunneries with good nuns! 
Perhaps this was because th e monks had not allowed any woman to even visit their 
monasteries.  
 Atisha’s three main disciples in Central Tibet were Khuton, Ngog or Dog, and Drom 
Tonpa.

Chungnay who had been prophesied by Tara and further spread the exalted 
activities of the master.  This was a summary description of the au thor’s greatness. You should come to 
understand it more extensively in  the great biographical texts.  
 Drom Tonpa spread the enlightened activities  of Atisha. He was ca lled Gyelway Chungnay 
(Source of Conquerors).   This is a short explanation of the greatn ess of the author, Atisha. A more extensive 
explanation is found in the great biographical texts, for example, in the text composed by 
Yongdzin Tsechog Ling Yeshe Gyal tsen in the context of the li neage lamas of the lam-rim.

Middle Lam-Rim:   
Showing the greatness of the teachings 
for the sake of generating respect for the instructions 
2B2 Showing the greatness of the teachings 
A Identifying the teachings 
B Presentation [of this text] as the three: complete, easily applicable, and a particularly noble system C Presentation of the four greatnesses 
2B2A Identifying the teachings 
Second: As for the teachings, the source text of the present instructions is the 
Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment . 
2B2B Presentation [of this text] as the three: complete, easily applicable, and a particularly noble system 
Although Jowo [Atisha] composed numerous texts, the Lamp on the Path to 
Enlightenment  is their perfect completion, like a root. As it indicates all the points 
of both sutra and tantra in summary fashion,  it is complete in terms of content. As 
it makes the gradual taming of the mind it s main topic, it is easy to put into 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
34 practice. As it is ornamented by the inst ructions of the two masters {10} who were 
skilled in the systems of the two trail-blazers19, it is a particularly noble system 
compared to others.  
 
The Dharma of which the grea tness is shown here is the Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment . 
Atisha composed this text that contains the th oroughly complete path of the small, middling, 
and great beings and in the end also sets out the subject of tantra. With respect to inferior or 
small beings, the root that is set out at the beginning is the relian ce on a teacher (guru 
devotion). Then we need to recognize that pr esently we have a precious human rebirth with 
the eighteen qualities but that in the future it  will be lost. This body is better than a wish-
fulfilling jewel because, even though such a jewel can give us as much wealth as we want, it 
cannot bring about inner realizations or a good rebirth in the next life. Only the precious 
human rebirth, endowed with an intelligent mind , that we have right now can fulfill our aim, 
whether small, middle, or great. The small aim i s  t o  t a k e  i n  o u r  n e x t  l i f e  a  g o o d  h u m a n  
rebirth possessing all necessary conditions such as a health body with intact sense powers, 
wealth, and so forth. A person with this aim has the intention to practice the Dharma in 
order to attain realizations through having unde rstood that it is more important to develop 
one’s good qualities rather than accumulate wealth.   The middle aim is to abandon the afflictions an d thereby attain the state of a foe-destroyer, 
whereby one will no longer be reborn in cyclic existence by the force of actions and afflictions. 
Instead one will be completely free from all sufferings and problems such as loneliness. One’s mind will be continually happy and always in meditation.   The great aim is to achieve buddhahood in  order to be able to emanate different 
emanations so as to be of help and benefit to other sentient beings. For example, if they need a bridge one will be able to manifest a bridge to enable them to cross to the other side of river, one will be able to manifest a ship in or der that they can travel, and so forth. Whatever 
is needed, a buddha can emanate. In the past  the Buddha emanated many seemingly strange 
things, but this was done only in order to be nefit other sentient beings. If they would not 
have been of benefit, he would not have ma nifested such things. Since a buddha can help 
s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  i n  d i f f e r e n t  w a y s ,  b y  a t t a i n i n g  b u d d h a h o o d  o n e  w i l l  b e  a b l e  t o  b e n e f i t  t h e  infinite sentient beings in the infinite worlds. One will be able to take an emanation body in 
order to help them. On the other hand, if it is  not possible to benefit particular beings in a 
particular place, a buddha will not emanate there because a buddha knows the dispositions 
and so forth of sentient beings.   Thus, there are the three paths of the three beings that bring three different results. In 
general this covers the sutra way. In tantra th ere are four classes: action tantra, performance 
tantra, yoga tantra, and highest yoga tantra. Th ese tantras involve different practices by means 
of which one can attain enlightenment.  
 In short, in this context th e Dharma, or the scripture contai ning these instructions, refers 
to the Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment . We should try to read this  text which is very short, 
yet because it is precise and contains all the subject matter of sutra and tantra in a concise 
way, it is very precious. It is easy to understand in that one does not need to read many other texts in order to understand it. Since it cond enses the important points of all the sutra and 
                                             
 
19 i.e., Nagarjuna and Asanga 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
35 tantra teachings, it is complete. It is easy to  put it into practice and mainly enables us to 
subdue our mind. Our mind is like a crazy elephant  that is difficult to tame, but just as a crazy 
elephant can be tamed so too can our mind be  tamed through knowing what to do and what 
not to do.  
 Since the instructions are complete, this text  is like an ornament for other teachings or 
instructions. Therefore, it is said to  be an excellent and special text.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B2C Presentation of [this text ] as possessing the four greatnesses 
1 The greatness of recognizing all teachings as non-contradictory 
2 The greatness of all of the Buddha’s teachings coming into view as useful instructions 
3 The greatness of allowing you easily to find the meaning intended by the Buddha 
4 The greatness of a grave misdeed stopping by itself 
2B2C-1 The greatness of recognizing all teachings as non-contradictory 
The greatness of the instructions of this text  has four [aspects]. The first of these is 
the greatness of recognizing all teachings as non-contradictory. 
A Identifying the teachings 
Everything the Conqueror said. 
 
Due to the instructions of this text, the doctrine or teachings ( bstan pa ) are realized as being 
without contradiction. This is because all th e Buddha’s teachings were given for the same 
purpose, that of subduing the minds of sentie nt beings, in particular subduing the minds of 
human beings. In other words, the Buddha gave  many different teachings – 101 volumes in 
Tibetan – for the one purpose of benefiting sentient beings through enabling them to 
eliminate the mental afflictions and achieve inne r happiness. For this reason, the teachings of 
the Buddha are not contradictory. However, from the point of view of tenets, different words 
were used due to which there may appear to be  contradictions. But in the end they are not 
contradictory as they were all given as instruct ions in order to benefit sentient beings with 
different mentalities so that they can elim inate obscurations and suffering and achieve 
happiness. Therefore, one should  not think that they are cont radictory, although the words 
may at times appear to contradictory. For example, the Buddha once said, “You who are a 
good son, father and mother are objects to be killed.” However, he said this to someone who 
was very depressed and filled with regret for ha ving killed his father. Due to what the Buddha 
said, his sadness was eliminated. He repeated this  line over and over again, contemplating its 
meaning. Later, he came to understand that  “father” and “mother” did not refer to the 
parents who had given birth to him but rather to the father who is the afflictions and the 
mother who is contaminated actions. These two are to be eliminated or abandoned, whereby 
one becomes pure. The Buddha said these seemingly unusual words, but his main purpose 
was that it be an instruction for this particular  person. Similarly, when we are sick a doctor 
may say not to eat meat or fatty foods but at an other time he may advise us to eat meat and 
fatty foods. There is a purpose to this, even th ough these two different pieces of advice may 
appear to be contradictory. The purpose is that in the case of certain sicknesses meat or fatty 
foods can make one even more sick, whereas when  one’s body is weak and the winds in one’s 
body are disturbed it is beneficial to one’s health to eat meat and fatty foods.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
36  
Thursday morning, 20 January 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim:   
B How all the teachings are recognized as non-contradictory 
They all come to be understood as one person’s path to buddhahood. That is to 
say some are primary points of the path, others are appropriate secondary points. 
 
Some of Buddha Shakyamuni’s teachings set out th e practice the main pa ths, whereas others 
set out the branches of the path . Although they have different subjects, the teachings are not 
contradictory. They were all given for the sa me purpose, that of subduing the different 
disciples.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
C How it is demonstrated through scripture and logic that it would be contradictory not to accept this 
In this regard, it is the bodhisattva’s wish  to work for the welfare of the world. For 
t h a t  p u r p o s e ,  i t  i s  a l s o  n e c e s s a r y  t o  care for all three types of disciples20 and 
therefore train in their respective paths.  This is because it is explained by 
Venerable Maitreya that the knower of path s of the three vehicles constitutes the 
means of realizing the bodhisattvas’ goals. 
 
The Buddha’s teachings are to benefit sent ient beings. There are three main lineage-
possessors: those who possess the lineage of hearer s, those who possess the lineage of solitary 
realizers, and those who possess the lineage of bodh isattvas. In order to take care of them, the 
Buddha gave different teachings. For example, for the hearers, selflessness refers to the lack of 
self-supporting substantial existence. This is their main object of meditation by means of 
which they abandon the afflictions. Another type  of selflessness is the non-duality of object 
and object-possessor (subject), that is, the empt iness of their being different entities. This 
assertion says that the object, for example, a form, and the object-possessor, the consciousness 
cognizing the form, are one enti ty; they are not different enti ties. Thus, they are empty of 
existing dually. This is the main object of medi tation of solitary realizers by means of which 
they abandon the coarse objects of knowledge.  For bodhisattvas all phenomena are empty of 
true existence, this selflessness or emptiness is their main object of meditation. By meditating 
on it, they abandon the obscurations to knowle dge. For this reason, the Buddha taught these 
three paths.  
 In the Ornament for Clear Realizations  three knowers – a knower of bases, a knower of paths, 
and a knower of all aspects – are set out. The verse of homage in this text says: 
 
By the knower of all, hearers seeking pacification are led to peace; By the knower of paths, those benefiting mi grating beings accomplish the welfare of the 
world; 
By the perfect possession of it, the subduers  teach the varieties possessing all aspects; 
                                             
 
20 JN: rigs chan sum ga , all three lineage-possessors 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
37 To these mothers of the buddh as, together with the host of  hearers and bodhisattvas – 
homage. 
 
For those who are seeking solitary  peace, the pacification of al l sufferings, the Buddha taught 
the path of a knower of all, also called a know er of bases, which realizes that the sixteen 
attributes of the four noble truths are empt y of self-supporting substantial existence.  
 Here the text says “the world,” referring to sentient beings. The Budd ha taught the knower 
of paths so that bodhisattvas would medita te on the emptiness of objects and object-
possessors being different substances so that th e y  w o u l d  b e  a b l e  t o  t a k e  c a r e  o f  s o l i t a r y  
realizers. The Buddha taught all phenomena as being empty of true existence for the sake of 
bodhisattvas. In short, Maitreya in his Ornament for Clear Realizations  says that a bodhisattva 
has to know all three paths in order to be able  to take care of the followers of the three 
vehicles.   
Middle Lam-Rim:   
D Establishing that the teachings are non-contradictory 
In the Mahayana path there is the co mmon and the uncommon. The former are 
those that originate from the Hinayana coll ection of scriptures, except for some 
peculiarities that are not common to do with  the thought of aspiring to individual 
liberation21 and the like and to do with the pres cribed rules. Apart from that, it is 
not that faults are partially ceased and qu alities are partially completed in perfectly 
complete buddhahood, but rather all kinds of faults have been eliminated and all 
types of good qualities have been comple ted. Consequently, as the Mahayana that 
achieves this also brings about the cessation  of all faults and the development of all 
good qualities, the path of the Mahayana comprises all the various aspects of the 
good qualities of abandonment and realization of all other vehicles. {11} That is why the sections of the Mahayana pa th, which brings about buddhahood, 
comprise all the Buddha’s teachings, for th ere is no utterance of the Muni that 
does not cause the cessation of some fa ult or the development of some good 
quality, and because it is not the case that  all of this is not brought about by the 
Mahayana. 
 
All the Buddha’s teachings are established to not be contradictory because the Mahayana 
contains both a common and an uncommon path. The commo n path is that which is 
common to or shared by both the Hinayan a and Mahayana. The Hinayana also has a 
common and an uncommon path. The uncommon Hinayana path is the wish to achieve 
peace or liberation for oneself alone. It is uncommon because Mahayanists do not aspire to 
achieve liberation for themselves alone. In othe r words, because a bodhis attva never thinks to 
do this, it is uncommon or unique to Hin ayanists. A common Hinayana path, on the other 
hand, is the realization of the four noble trut hs and their sixteen attributes because this 
realization is common to both the Hinayana an d Mahayana. The four noble truths are true 
sufferings, true origins, true paths, and true ce ssations, each of which has four attributes. For 
                                             
 
21 JN: rang gcig bu zhi bde don gnyer , aspiring for one’s own peace and happiness alone 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
38 example, true sufferings have the four attribut es of being impermanent, suffering, empty, and 
selfless. Impermanence is further divided in to the coarse and subtle. We see coarse 
impermanence in our everyday life, for example, a broken cup. It is coarse because we directly 
s ee t he cha n ge fro m  an  un bro ken  t o  a broken  cup. Si m ilarly, w e can  direct ly s ee s o m eo n e  
who was alive and then died, such as our parents,  friends, and so forth. Subtle impermanence 
refers to momentarily changing. For example, during one day of twenty-four hours, in each short instant there is a change in the body and mind. The duration of a fingersnap is divided 
into 65 parts, each of these parts is called ‘subtle impermanence.’  There are different types of suffering: the su ffering of suffering, suffering of change, and 
pervasive suffering. The suffering of suffering re fers to, for example, the pains in our body 
which are the nature of suffering – a toothache, headache, and so forth.  
 The suffering of change is, for example, when  we eat an exaggerated amount of delicious 
food it is followed by a stomach ache. Orig inally we experienced happiness and then the 
experience changed into suffering. Also when we go to the beach at first there is the pleasure 
of the warmth of the hot sun but soon it turns to a feeling of burning and pain. Also we see in relationships that at the beginning a couple is very happy and enjoy being with each other. 
Then gradually they begin to argue and to fi ght, and then they eventually the end up 
separating due to both of them having become unhappy. Cyclic existence is like this; what is 
originally happiness eventually changes into suffering. This is the suffering of change. 
 Pervasive suffering means that in a nation, fo r example, everyone – from the person in the 
highest position, the presiden t, down to the lowest, a beggar who sleeps on the road – 
experiences suffering. From the point of view of our world, all people ex perience suffering. In 
other words, we are pervaded by suffering. Alternatively, it can be said that pervasive suffering means that from the crown of our head down to the soles of our feet there is the experience 
suffering if we are, for example, pricked with a thorn. Our entire body is pervaded by pain. The cause of this is the afflic tions, therefore we need to abandon them by applying their 
antidotes. This is not easy. If we  have a thorn in our finger we can pull it out, but we cannot 
do this with the afflictions. While we can wash  dirt away with soap and water, the afflictions 
cannot be washed away like this. Nor can th e Buddha transfer his realizations to us. 
Therefore, in order to eliminate the afflictions we need to apply their antidotes. By doing so, 
our inner qualities will grow and we wi ll become a buddha or a holy being.  
 A buddha is not one who has not yet abandoned sl ight faults or has not yet attained slight 
qualities, rather he has abandoned all faults and completed all the qualities of body, speech, 
and mind that exist. We should develop the wish to achieve this  state of having abandoned all 
faults and achieved all qualities. The Mahayana  teachings have the ability to eliminate all 
faults and to generate all good qualities in ou r continuum. While hearers and solitary realizers 
have abandoned some objects of abandonment,  they have not abandoned all faults and 
gained all good qualities. Because all good qu alities are included in  the Mahayana, it is 
supreme. For this reason, we need to study and practice the Mahayana. However, as the 
Hinayana teachings also have branches that are to be practiced by Mahayanists, we need to 
study the scriptures of hearers and solitary realizer s as well as those of bo dhisattvas, that is, we 
need to study both the scriptures of the Hinayana and Mahayana. Even one word of the 
Buddha’s teachings can eliminate some faults and can bring about the achievement of some 
qualities. In other words, there are no teachi ngs given by the Buddha that do not eliminate 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
39 some faults or bring about some qualities. Even  a single word can do this. Therefore, these 
teachings are excellent and not contradictory.  
 A Thub pa  in Tibetan or Muni  in Sanskrit is one who is able to destroy the non-virtues of 
body, speech, and mind. Such a person is also ab le to destroy the afflictions, our main enemy. 
Due to this a buddha is called a Muni. We often recite the name mantra of Buddha 
Shakyamuni: TAYATHA OM MUNI MUNI MAHA MUNIYE SVAHA. This mantra sets out 
the three paths: the first MUNI sets out the small path, the seco nd MUNI sets out the middle 
path, and MAHAMUNI sets out the great path.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
E Clearing up doubts 
If you think: “That may be true for the paramitayana, but not for those entering 
the vajrayana.” Although the way of tr aining in the paramitayana – in the 
countless clear distinctions that clarify gi ving etc. – is different from mantra, the 
behavioral basis22 – the generation of the mind23 – and the coarse form of the path 
of training in the six perfections, the behavior, is nevertheless approximately the 
same and therefore something shared by them. In Vajra Peak  it says: 
 
Not even to sustain one’s life, 
should the bodhi mind be abandoned. 
 
and: 
 
The practice of the six perfections, 
must not be abandoned ever. 
 
This is also taught in numerous other mantra texts.  
 
Someone thinks that since the practice of th e paramitayana or perfection vehicle leads to 
enlightenment, it not necessary to practice th e vajra vehicle. This kind of doubt must be 
eliminated. The perfection vehicle consists of ex tensive teachings on generosity and so forth, 
and sets them out in many different ways. On the other hand, these practices are not 
extensively explained in tantra. However, it is no t that if one engages in the perfection vehicle, 
it is not necessary to engage in the tantra vehi cle. The perfection vehicle or sutra teaching has 
many divisions, for example, the six perfection s which are each further divided into six for a 
total of thirty-six. They are not explained in vajra vehicle because it is not the case that one 
can avoid studying the perfection vehicle and i mmediately engage in tantra. The base of all 
the Buddha’s teachings is the mind of enlightenm ent, therefore it is necessary both in the 
perfection vehicle and the mantra  vehicle. Likewise, the practices of the six perfections are 
                                              
22 JN: spyod pa’i rtan , support of the conduct 
23 sems skyed , lit. mind-generate(d) is a very common ellipsis for " byang chub mchog tu sems skyed " "generation of a 
mind or mental attitude [aiming] fo r supreme enlightenment" or "generated  mind/mental attitude [aiming] for 
supreme enlightenment" 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
40 also necessary in tantra. Therefore, the mind of enlightenment and the practice of the six 
perfections are common to both sutra and tantra. In  other words, it is no t that one can think, 
“Now that I am a tantric practitioner I do not need to develop the mind of enlightenment or 
practice generosity, morality, patience, effort, co ncentration, and wisdom.” If one thinks like 
this, one will not gain the realizations of tantra . Similarly, in the past in Tibet there were 
scholars who mistakenly thought that if one were  to take the vows of individual liberation and 
then take the bodhisattva vows, that the vows of individual liberation would be given up. 
They also thought that if one were to then take the tantra vows, that the bodhisattva vows 
would also be given up, whereby one would posse ss only one type of vow. Others mistakenly 
thought that the individual liberation vows transformed into the bo dhisattva vows, and the 
bodhisattva vows into the tantra vows, implying  that the three vows are not three different 
entities. This would mean that when one engages in tantra, sutra is no longer necessary. 
However, this is not the case.   The mind of enlightenment is a primary mi nd wishing to attain enlightenment for the 
benefit of all sentient beings. This mind of en lightenment is of two types: aspirational mind 
of enlightenment and the engaging mind of  enlightenment. The engaging mind of 
enlightenment is the actual mind of enlightenm ent; with it one enga ges in the conduct or 
behavior of a bodhisattva, the six perfections and so forth. 
 The Vajra Peak Tantra  says that one should not give up the mind of enlightenment even at 
the cost of one’s life. The development of mi nd of enlightenment is fundamental to the 
Mahayana, both sutra and tantra. This text also says that the six perfections should never be 
given up, meaning that they must be practiced. Fr om the point of view of tantra, generosity is 
divided into four types, whereas in sutra there ar e only three types. This is because in tantra 
the giving of love is added to the three generosi ties of sutra: giving material things, giving 
Dharma, and giving protection. The generosity of gi ving love is to wish that all sentient beings 
have happiness and the causes of happiness and th en to try to make them happy by giving 
them happiness and the causes of happiness. This shows that the pr actice of generosity is also 
very important in tantra. In fact, many tant ra or mantra texts say that the mind of 
enlightenment, the six perfections, and so forth are essential. Therefore, one should not give 
them up, thinking that one is a tantric practiti oner. The practice of sutra is necessary as a 
basis for one’s practice of tantra.

 Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B2C-2 The  greatness of all of the Buddha’s teachings coming into view as useful instructions  
Second, the greatness of all of the Buddha’ s teachings coming into view as useful 
instructions 
A The distinction of pure and impure manners of comprehension 
Whoever construing the great scriptures to be explanatory teachings that do not 
contain the points of prac tice and that the guidance26 indicating the meaning of 
the essential points of practice exists sepa rately from them, holds that even in the 
holy Dharma the explanatory and accomplishing teachings27 are found in two 
different places {12}, will create an obstacle for the arisal of deep respect towards the immaculate sutras and tantras as well as the treatises clarifying their intent. 
One should be aware that the karmic ob scuration of abandoning the Dharma is 
accumulated by despising them saying that  they only outline external knowledge 
without presenting the inner meaning. He nce, for those who want liberation, the 
infallible, supreme instructions are indeed the great scriptures. Nevertheless, it is 
possible that due to poor intelligence etc. one cannot reach certainty through 
relying only on these scriptures as supr eme instructions, whereby one should seek 
certainty with respect to them thinking “I will seek certainty regarding them by 
relying on the excellent [practical] guidan ce,” but one should not think that the 
great scriptures do not contain the heart of the matter because they only outline 
external knowledge while the practical guid ance is supreme because it presents the 
inner meaning. 
 All the Buddha’s teachings can be taken as inst ructions for oneself. Therefore, those who 
think that the great scriptures on philosophy are to be learne d and explained but are not to 
be practiced, are mistaken. It is also incorrect to think that the essential points to be practiced 
are to be found somewhere else. There is a story with respect to this id ea about a teacher from 
Drepung who was very learned and well known. He  had many disciples and taught more than 
a hundred monks every day. Then one day he disappeared. Where did he go? When even the 
Tibetan government took part in the search fo r the missing teacher, it was learned that he 
had gone to Choden Rinpoche’s hermitage to practice. At this place there was a monk who 
had studied very little philosophy but had st udied some lam-rim. The teacher was in the 
hermitage and was taking instructions from the monk who has studied very little philosophy. 
He stayed there for a year or two. Although he was very learned he thought that what he had 
                                             
 
26 JN: man ngag , could also be translated as advice 
27 JN: bshad pa dang sgrub pa’i chos , could also be translated as the Dharma of explanation and the Dharma of 
practice or accomplishment 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
42 studied was mere knowledge, like studying geography or history. He had not been able to see 
that the great texts were instructions to su bdue one’s own mind. Some monks requested the 
teacher to return to Drepung and take his geshe degree, but he refused saying that wanted to 
stay in the hermitage. Then the Tibetan govern ment sent a messenger by horse to tell him 
that the Dalai Lama wanted him to return to Drepung to take the geshe degree. He did this 
and came out the best in the cl ass. All this happened because he  had been unable to see that 
the great texts, the Ornament for Clear Realizations , Supplement to the Middle Way , Treasury of 
Manifest Dharma , Vinaya, and Valid Cognition , contained the objects to be practiced. This is 
similar to what is Lama Tsongkhapa means here. 
 In short, some people think there is a Dh arma to be explained and a Dharma to be 
practiced, however it is not like this. All the Dh arma is both, that is, it can both be explained 
and put into practice. Therefore, we should un derstand all the sutra and tantra teachings and 
their commentaries to be inst ructions for our practice.  
 Thinking that the great texts are mere dry word s is a great obstacle to  one’s practice. It is 
wrong to think that the great texts are only to  be understood intellectually and explained. 
Rather they are both to be understood and to  be put into practice. This doubt must be 
eliminated. If instead one thinks  that the great texts are just words and not for practice, one 
abandons the Dharma. To abandon the Dharma is th e heaviest negative action. It is said that 
even by practicing the Guhyasamaja Tantra, medi tating on the generation stage and attaining 
clear light, one cannot purify the negativity of  abandoning the Dharma. On the other hand, if 
one commits any of the five actions of immediat e retributions – killing one’s mother, killing 
one’s father, killing a foe-destroyer, drawing bl ood from a buddha, or causing a schism in the 
sangha, it can be purified by practicing the Guhyasamaja Tantra . This is mentioned in the 
Guhyasamaja Root Tantra . Therefore, the heaviest negative action is that of abandoning the 
Dharma in that it prevents one’s inner qualit ies and realizations from developing. On the 
other hand, having killed one’s mother, father, and so forth, if one later meditates one can still gain higher realizations.  
 Thus, if one desires to achieve liberation , one should see the great texts as supreme 
instructions. They are infallible or non-de ceptive. For example, if one studies the Ornament for 
Clear Realizations  one will see that everything in it is  to be practiced. Even the homage or 
prostration contains the entire path. Therefore, one should study the great texts and treatises.  
 On the other hand, those who have weak in telligence or whose minds are obscured or 
confused will not be able to understand that th e great texts are instructions. Such people need 
to study, contemplate, and meditate on the lam- rim. It is like having many ingredients to 
make food and then using only some to cook  some food and putting the rest away in a 
storeroom. The great texts are like a storeroom  of instructions. The lam-rim, on the other 
hand, is like pizza from Naples or spaghetti from Bologna in that it is easy to digest since it is 
already prepared.  
 Just the homage in the Treasury of Manifest Dharma , for example, says:  
 
To the one who has utterly destroyed the darkness with respect to all, 
Extricates migrating beings from th e mire of cyclic existence, and 
Teaches in accordance with  the meaning – homage.   [1.1abc] 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
43 If one tries to understand just this single vers e, it will eliminate some obscuration. Our mind 
is as if covered with ignorance, like a dark room. Just as a light illuminates the darkness and 
allows one to see what is in the room, the dark ness of ignorance that obscures our mind can 
be eliminated by understanding selflessness or the four noble truths. The main thing is to 
recognize sufferings as suffering and to recogniz e their causes or origins – the afflictions and 
contaminated actions of body, speech, and mi nd. There are ten non-virtuous actions: the 
three of body are killing, stealing, and sexual mi sconduct; the four of speech are lying, divisive 
speech, harsh words, and idle talk; and the thr ee of mind are covetousness, that thought “I 
want” that mentally grasping to something, malice or harmful mind , the thought to harm 
someone, and wrong view, the thought that the la w of cause and result does not exist, past 
and future lives do not exist, and so forth. Th ese ten non-virtues are the main cause of all our 
sufferings. When this cause ripens, it brings four  results: a fruitional result, an empowering or 
dominant result, and so forth. The fruitional result  of a non-virtuous action  is rebirth as a hell 
being, hungry ghost, or animal. The non-virt uous actions are motivated by afflictions.  
 When we take a good human rebirth in cyclic existence it is due to the causal motivation 
of ignorance and a virtuous motivation of time that brings about a contaminated action. Such 
actions bring about rebirth as a human, anti-god, or  god. However, it is still a rebirth in cyclic 
existence and therefore one will still experience suffering. We need to eliminate the causes of 
suffering. The main root or cause is ignoranc e, and another two causes are attachment and 
hatred. These three are called the “mental poison s.” They are the main causes of creating 
virtuous and non-virtuous actions. While one can create virtue moti vated by attachment, 
anger cannot motivate a virtuous action. The root  of all is ignorance, not knowing the nature 
of phenomena, the emptiness or selflessness that is the lack of  inherent existence. Having 
recognized the causes of suffering, we need to me ditate true paths. By doing this one will be 
able to eliminate the causes, afflictions an d contaminated actions, whereby the result, 
suffering, is also eliminated. This brings about tr ue cessations, the cessation of the afflictions, 
which are coarse and subtle. 
 In short, it is important to understand the four noble truths. This understand can be applied to our daily life. For example, when in  a family the members criticize each other and 
fight, this is a true suffering. The cause is one of the mental afflictions, anger, jealousy, pride 
and so forth. This is the true origin of the su ffering. Motivated by this mental affliction, we 
engage in verbal and physical actions, such as  talking badly to or beating the other person. 
These afflictions bring suffering. Therefore, we need to develop respect for each other, trust 
i n  e a c h  o t h e r ,  a n d  a  l o v i n g - k i n d  m i n d  f o r  e a c h  o t h e r .  T h i s  c a n  b e  l i k e n e d  t o  a  t r u e  p a t h .  Then the result, all the members of  the family living together in  harmony, can be likened to a 
true cessation, the cessation of fighting and disharmony.   However, the four noble truths are like an on ion, which can be peeled away in layers, as 
they can be understood on a more and more subtle level.  Thursday afternoon, 20 January 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim:   
B How all the Buddha’s teachings come into view as useful instructions  
The great yogi Jangchub Rinchen said: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
44  
Someone who has reached certainty concerning a handy little volume, 
cannot be said to know all the topics of practical guidance, this is said 
about someone who understands all of the Buddha’s teachings as 
instructions. 
 We need the kind of understanding Gr eat Jowo [Atisha]’s disciple Gompa 
Rinchen Lama expressed saying he had gr ound his body, speech and mind to dust 
in a single meditation session on Atis ha’s instructions and that thereby the 
understanding had now arisen in him th at all scriptures are instructions.  
 To fortunate beings all the Buddha’s teaching s appear as instructions. The great yogi 
Jangchub Rinchen was a Tibetan lama of the Kadam tradition. He said that the recognition 
or understanding that all the teachings are in structions for subduing one’s own mind does 
not come from studying a small volume.   Great Jowo is Atisha. His disciple Gompa Rinchen also said that all the Buddha’s teachings without exception are to be understood clearly as instructions. This is the best kind of 
understanding. If one does not understand this , then the great texts will appear to one as 
similar to texts on history or geography. In the Tibetan language there are 101 volumes of 
Buddha Shakyamuni’s teachings and more than 200 volumes of commentaries by the Indian 
pandits. Although they contain different ways of  teaching, in the end th ey are all instructions. 
An instruction is that which subdues the mind, th at is, it makes the mind, that was very hard, 
soft and smooth as well as joyful.   
Middle Lam-Rim:   
 
In the words of the precious [Drom] Tönpa: 
 
If after studying the Dharma a lot yo u feel the need to search elsewhere 
for a way of applying the Dharma, you are mistaken. 
 
Likewise those who study the Dharma a lo t over a long period and, completely 
ignorant as to how to set about it, deve lop the wish to practice Dharma, do not 
understand and therefore err in  the manner described above.  
 Precious Tonpa or Tonpa Rinpoche is Drom Tonpa. He said that if one studies many 
scriptures and then, when about to practice, looks elsewhere, thinki ng that this is not 
explained in these scriptures, this is a mistake. In other words, if one studies many scriptures 
over a long time but still does not understand how to practice the Dharma, this would be very 
strange. Having studied the great texts, one should practice as they teach.
