Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
45 {13} As it says in the Treasury28: 
 
The Buddha’s teachings have two aspects: 
their nature is scripture and realization. 
 In accordance with this dictum, the teachi ng is nothing apart from the scriptural 
teaching and the realizational teaching. 
 T h e  B u d d h a ’ s  t e a c h i n g  o r  d o c t r i n e  o r  i s  o f  t w o  t y p e s :  t h e  s c r i p t u r a l  d o c t r i n e  a n d  t h e  realizational doctrine. The scriptural doctrine  is that which shows us how to develop our 
inner qualities, whereas the inner qualities that  have been developed are the realizational or 
realized doctrine. The scriptures include the thr ee baskets, the twelve sets or categories of 
scriptures, and the 84,000 aggr egates or heaps of Dharma. However, all the others are 
included in the three baskets – the sutra, vi naya, and abhidharma baskets – that teach the 
higher trainings in morality, concentration, and wisdom. The purpose of the vinaya is to 
subdue the body and speech, that is, to elim inate bad speech and bad bodily movements. Due 
to studying the vinaya, we can change the be havior of our body and speech. For the mind, 
there are the two other trainings, those of concentration and wisdom. Concentration is a 
s t a b l e  m i n d  t h a t  i s  a b l e  t o  r e m a i n  s i n g l e - p o i n t e d l y  o n  i t s  o b j e c t .  O n  t h e  b a s i s  o f  t h i s  concentration, one engages in analytical meditation . It is also said that on the basis of having 
achieved calm abiding, one develops special insight. The mind having become stable, the 
wisdom knowing the different entities and obje cts of meditation can eliminate the mental 
afflictions. For this reason, first one needs to study the scriptures, then gain an understanding 
of the subject matter, and then put the knowledge gained into practice. In other words, first 
one should study, then think or contemplate, an d then meditate. In this way one will gain the 
wisdom arisen from hearing, the wisdom arisen  from thinking, and the wisdom arisen from 
meditation. Due to gaining these three kinds of wisdom one will realize one’s aim.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
 
The scriptural teaching is how the Dharma  should be practiced and establishes the 
manner of accomplishment, while the realiz ational teaching is what is [thus] 
established. Since one practices in accordance  with what is to be established, these 
two act as cause and effect. To give an analogy: when there is a horse race, the 
horse is first shown the course and afterwar ds, likewise, it runs there. It would be 
ridiculous to show it a course and then ha ve the race elsewhere. When it comes to 
attainments, how could it be appropriate to accomplish something after having 
determined something else through listenin g and reflecting? That is also expressed 
in the last volume of Stages of Meditation :  
 
Whatever has been realized by the wisdoms arising from listening and reflecting is precisely what should be  cultivated by means of the wisdom 
                                             
 
28 i.e. the Treasury of Manifest Knowledge - Abhidharmakosha  by Vasubandhu 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
46 arising from meditation, and not anothe r; just as a horse runs along the 
course it has been shown. 
 
When there is a horse race, first the trainer shows the horse the racecourse by leading it 
around it a couple of times so that it gets to know the track. After that when the horse races, 
it will be able to go quickly and easily due to already knowing the ro ad and not having any 
doubts. On the other hand, if this is not done, the horse may have doubts  that there is danger 
and will therefore not run as quickly.  
 Similarly, we should listen to teachings fr om our teacher, or discuss the Dharma with 
friends, or study on one’s own until one gain s an understanding of the meaning. Then one 
should think about the meaning and then meditate  on it. In this way, one will gain the three 
types of wisdom: wisdom arisen from hearing, wisdom arisen from thinking, and wisdom arisen from meditation. These three types of wi sdom are not direct realizations. Only later 
will the wisdom directly realizing emptiness ar ise whereby one will achieve the path of seeing. 
The path of seeing is so-called because for th e first time one directly sees the four noble 
truths. The continual meditation on them that follows that direct realization is called “the 
path of meditation.” After that  one attains the path of no-mor e-learning, at which point there 
is no longer a need to train or study.  
 The word “path” or “road” is marga  in Sanskrit and lam in Tibetan. An ordinary path or 
road is one that takes us to a certain destinat ion, there being small, middling, and big roads. 
In this context a path it what leads us to a realization. For example, when one attains an 
uncontrived thought of definite emergence or re nunciation one enters a path. This first path 
is the path of accumulation on which one accu mulates the hearing of many teachings as well 
as merit. Thereby, one develops more and more faith in the Buddha’s teachings. One 
gradually progresses through the small, middle, and great paths of accumulation. On the great 
path of accumulation one gains the concentration called “the stream of Dharma,” at which 
time one can receive teachings even from a stat ue of the Buddha. One sees statues as actual 
emanation bodies and can receive teachings from  them. Right now we cannot do this, but in 
the future we will be able to receive teachings in this way.  
 Having received many teachings and meditate d on them, one then achieves the path of 
preparation. This path is divided into four  levels: heat, peak, tolerance, and supreme 
mundane qualities. At the end of the supreme mu ndane qualities level, one achieves the path 
of seeing. The path of seeing is also divided in to three: a path of seeing that is meditative 
equipoise, a path of seeing that is subsequent a ttainment, and a path of seeing that is neither 
meditative equipoise or subsequent attainment . The path of seeing that is meditative 
equipoise is divided into sixteen divisions: th e eight forbearances whic h are paths of seeing 
that are antidotes, and the eigh t knowledges which are paths of seeing that are liberated. One 
first applies the antidotes to the acquired afflicti on that is the conception  of a self, after which 
one abandons the innate afflicti on that is the conception of true existence. The path of 
meditation is divided into nine rounds. This, in brief, is how one progresses on the path.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
47 C Indication of analytical meditation as a means of making all teachings appear as instructions 
Thus, through summarizing all the topics of the paths set ou t in the excellent 
teachings and commentaries – starting from how to rely on a spiritual teacher up 
to calm abiding and special insight, th ey are all condensed into the steps of 
practical application in terms of perf orming stabilizing meditation where 
stabilizing meditation is required, and an alyzing with the wisdom of individual 
investigation where analytical meditation is required. Thereby, these instructions 
guide one so that all the excellent teachi ngs present themselves as instructions. 
Then the certainty develops that they  s h o u l d  b e  u n d e r s t o o d  a s  s u p r e m e  
instructions, and the misconception that they should be understood as mere 
background [knowledge] for the Dharma rather  than as actual instructions {14} is 
completely reversed. 
 
The instructions begin with an explanation of how to rely on a spiritual teacher or lama and 
progress through to calm abiding and special insight. The Buddha’s teachings contain all 
these subjects in a complete way without anyt hing missing. For example, in the context of 
relying on a guru there is a discussion of the teacher’s qualities, the student’s qualities, and 
how to rely on a lama in thought and action. Fi rst of all, one should examine whether or not 
the teacher is valid as well as examining whether or not what he teaches is valid. One does not 
need to immediately take someone as one’s te acher. One can determine whether or not one 
will be able to rely on him completely by ex amining whether or not his teachings are valid in 
terms of, for example, the four types of validiti es set out in the Sakya tradition. One should 
also check whether the person who is teaching can be trusted or not. For example, some so-
called “teachers” have been know n to claim that they have realiz ations of clairvoyance and so 
f o r t h  w h e n  t h e y  d o  n o t .  T h i s  is their way to cheat others in order to gain something for 
themselves such as material things. That is not right. Even His Holiness the XIV Dalai Lama 
says that he does not have any realizations bu t is just a normal ordi nary monk. He does not 
say “I am THE DALAI LAMA!”, but instead sp eaks about himself and teaches in a very 
humble way.  
 It is usually said that the teacher should have  ten qualities from the point of view of sutra, 
another ten from the point of view of vinaya, another ten from the point of view of Mahayana, and another ten from th e point of view of tantra. The ten qualities that he should 
possess from the point of view of sutra are taught in the Ornament of the Mahayana Sutras . 
However, his mind and behavior should, in shor t, be subdued. One should rely on such a 
guru and one should put whatever he teaches into practice as much as possible, even if it is difficult. One should treat all his teachings as a means to subdue one’s minds, and to reduce 
one’s afflictions and wrong ideas. In this way one’s mind will improve.  
 Calm abiding is single-pointed meditation. It is achieved by means of nine stages of mental abiding, setting, continuous setting, resetting , close setting, and so forth. One chooses an 
object of meditation and sets the mind on it, tr ying to remain focused on it. In this way, one 
gradually progresses through the nine stages. Then , when one attains a special pliancy of body 
and mind that induces bliss, one attains calm abiding. On this basis one does analytical 
meditation. One analyses whether a table, for ex ample, is empty of inherent existence using 
the reasoning: the table is empty of inherent exis tence because it is a dependent-relation. It is 
a dependent-relation because it depends on part s, or it is a dependent-relation because it 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
48 depends on causes and conditions. By analyzing in this way, one will come to understand that 
it is not inherently existent or concrete, an d for this reason can be  destroyed. While all 
phenomena do not exist inherently, we see all ph enomena as if they exist from their own side 
or inherently. This is a mistake.  
 We also see all phenomena as if they were perm anent, for example, as if this hall were the 
same now as it was ten years ago. The hall is  not like that, yet we apprehend it to be 
permanent while in fact it is impermanent an d constantly changing. When one understands 
subtle impermanence, one will understand that th is hall is constantly changing. For example, 
if we check ourselves we will see that we gr ew taller and taller until one day we stopped 
growing, yet continued to get older and older. Every day, every month, we get older. In the 
past when we were children, we did not have wh ite hair or wrinkles, rather our skin was soft 
and nice. However, gradually our skin becomes wrinkled, our hair becomes white, our teeth begin to fall out… This is because we ar e impermanent and constantly changing.  
 When we have a toothache we can get out t ooth pulled. On the other hand, if teeth were 
permanent phenomena they could neither be remo ved nor cause pain. Likewise if they were 
inherently existent they could not be remove d or cause pain. One should do analytical 
meditation on this. Five great reasonings proving the lack of inherent existence are 
mentioned in the texts. For example, one set of reasonings is that the table is not produced 
from self, not from other, not from both self  and other, and not without causes. In other 
words, the table is not produced ultimately, in herently, or truly because it is not produced 
from self, from other, from both, or without caus es. In fact, the table is produced from causes 
and conditions and is therefore not produced in herently or truly. This kind of reasoning 
establishes that it does not exist either as an inherently existent cause or as an inherently 
existent result. It is not produced from self or  other or both. Neither is it produced without a 
cause. A thing cannot be produced without a ca use. Therefore, the ta ble is not produced 
inherently or truly.   Another reasoning is: the table is not truly exis tent because it is not a truly existent one or 
a truly existent many. It is not a truly existe nt one because it has parts. With parts and 
without parts are directly contradictory and part less phenomena do not ex ist. The table is not 
a truly existent many because it is not a trul y existent one. Many is dependent on one, 
therefore if one does not exist,  many cannot exist. Thus, all ph enomena are not truly existent 
because they do not exist either as a trul y existent one or a truly existent many.  
 Another reasoning is: the table does not exist inherently because it is a dependent-arising. 
All these reasonings are examples of analytical  meditation on emptiness. With wisdom one 
analyzes the nature of phenomena. Having do ne this, one does stabilizing meditation on 
emptiness. In this way one will be able to abandon the afflictions.  
 In this way, all the Buddha’s teachings wi ll arise as instructions for oneself.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B2C-3 The greatness of allowing you to easily discover the Conqueror’s intentions  
Third: The greatness of allowing you to ea sily discover the Conqueror’s intentions. 
A For beginners, the Conqueror’s intention is not easy to find without a guru’s oral instructions 
Although the great texts – the teachings along with their commentaries – form the 
supreme instructions, completely uneducat ed beginners cannot find the intended 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
49 meaning, even though delving into th em, without relying on excellent oral 
instructions, and even if they find it they  spend a lot of time and enormous effort. 
 
It is not easy to see the intention of the grea t texts even though one reads and studies them. 
Although these texts contain the supreme instruct ions, they are not easy to understand. Thus, 
even if one studies them for a long time, even spending many years reading them while 
undergoing many difficulties, it still remains di fficult to identify the instructions. For this 
reason, we need to rely on a lama, a holy being, who can explain how these texts are instructions.   
Middle Lam-Rim:   
B [The Conqueror’s intention ] is found through a guru’s oral instructions 
I f  t h e y  r e l y  o n  a  g u r u ’ s  o r a l  i n s t r u c t i o n s  a n d  t h e  l i k e ,  i t  b e c o m e s  e a s y  t o  
understand. 
 
O n  t h e  o t h e r  h a n d ,  b y  m e a n s  o f  t h e  g u r u ’ s  o r a l  i n s t r u c t i o n s  o r  a d v i c e  o n e  c a n  c o m e  t o  understand the Conqueror’s intention quickly an d easily. For example, the lama identifies 
something as an instruction, and then one thin ks about it and puts it into meditational 
practice. If one does this, one will quickly and ea sily gain realizations. This is because having 
understood the instructions, one can easily practice them.   When we practice the Dharma, at times we experience difficulties. Why is this? It is 
because there arise many interferences and obstac les. For example, we have many afflictions; 
sometimes we are lazy, sometimes we are ti red, sometimes the mind becomes dark and 
confused. When we practice Dharma distractions tend to arise. For example, if while an older 
monk is doing retreat he has the responsibility  to delegate work to the younger monks in 
order to care for the monastery, when he does sessions instead of medita ting on the deity he 
may be thinking of the work that needs to be done. When one tries to meditate or do 
recitations, there often come thoughts of wh at one needs to do next. In addition, one 
remembers one’s dear friends and wishes to be with them and to enjoy their company. These 
thoughts disturb one’s mind. Many distractions ar ise, making it difficult to meditate. In spite 
of this, we need to try every day to put effort into meditation even just for a short time, as a 
long time may be difficult. In this way slowly our concentration will improve.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B2C-4 The  greatness of a grave misdeed stopping by itself 
Fourth: the greatness of a grave misdeed stopping by itself. 
A Showing the abandonment of the Dharma as the biggest misdeed 
As explained in the White Lotus [Sutra]  and the Chapter of the Truthful One , it 
amounts to abandoning the Dharma if yo u hold that some utterances of the 
Buddha are means of attaining buddhah ood while others are obstacles to 
buddhahood, dividing them into good and bad, appropriate and inappropriate, or 
Mahayana and Hinayana, and thereupon hold ing that a bodhisattva needs to train 
in some and not in others because you fail  to understand that all the words of the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
50 Buddha directly or indirectly teach th e means of attaining buddhahood. In the 
Sutra Gathering All the Threads  it says that the karmic obscuration due to 
abandoning the Dharma is so subtle that it is hard to recognize.  
 
Abandoning the Dharma is the greatest of faults. For example, while all the Buddha’s 
teachings either directly or indirectly show  the methods for attain ing buddhahood, if one 
does not understand this but instead thinks th at some teachings are to be abandoned because 
they are not instructions that are methods fo r attaining buddhahood but are rather obstacles 
to attaining buddhahood, one co mmits the negative action of  abandoning the Dharma. For 
example, if I were to say “I am a practitioner  of the Mahayana, therefore I do not need to 
study or practice the Hinayana scriptures bu t instead must abandon them,” I would commit 
the fault of abandoning the Dharma. This is a huge obstacle to our Dharma practice and a 
great mistake because all the Buddha’s teac hings, whether Hinayana or Mahayana, are 
methods for attaining enlightenment. For example,  we need to learn the subject matter of the 
Hinayana teachings and put them into practice be cause definite emergence or renunciation is 
the object of meditation that is the basis fo r entering a path. These teachings emphasize the 
four noble truths and their sixteen attributes , and we also need to meditate on them. 
therefore, thinking that we do not need to medi tate on them and that th ey are an obstacle to 
practicing the Dharma is a mistake and the faul t of abandoning the Dharma. It is important 
to understand from sutra what it means to aban don the Dharma as it would be a great fault.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
 
Concerning the grave disadvantages that ensue if you abandon the Dharma, it says 
in the King of Concentration Sutra : 
 
Someone here in Jambudvipa 
tears down all the stupas found, 
someone abandons a class of sutras; 
the latter’s misdeed is far more grave. 
 Someone even murders arhats as many as the sand grains in the Ganges, someone abandons a class of sutras; {15} the latter’s misdeed is far more grave. 
 Although there generally appear to be ma ny ways in which the Dharma might be 
abandoned, the one described previously se ems to be the greatest, which is why we 
should try hard to give it up. 
 If someone were to destroy all the stupas here  in the southern continent of Jambudvipa he 
would create a big negativity and big fault, but if  one were to abandon a su tra saying that it is 
not to be practiced, one would create a much greater and heavier negativity. If someone were 
to kill foe-destroyers equal in number to the ma ny sand grains of the Ganges River, he would 
create a big negativity that is an action of im mediate retribution, but a person who says that 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
51 one of the Buddha’s teachings is not to be studied or practiced or that it is an obstacle to the 
Mahayana creates a negativity that  is much greater and heavier than this. This is because the 
abandonment of the Dharma is the heaviest of ne gativities. Having recognized this, we should 
strive to not abandon the Dharma.  
 Although there are different doors or ways in which to abandon the Dharma, the heaviest 
of them all is to say, for example, that a Hi nayana sutra is not to be practiced and is an 
obstacle to the Mahayana and sh ould therefore be given up.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
B When there is a realization along the lines of the first two greatnesses, the misdeed stops by itself 
That is to say, since this is reversed si mply by gaining certainty regarding what was 
shown above, the misdeed stops by itself. 
 
There are four greatnesses. When one realizes the first two, then faults or bad behavior 
naturally cease. In other words, one does not n eed to apply any other antidote to cease them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
C You should come to realize such certainty from the White Lotus Sutra  and other texts 
This certainty should be sought by studying the Chapter of the  Truthful One  as well 
as the [Sutra of the] Excellent Dharma’s White Lotus . The other ways of abandoning 
the Dharma should be learnt from the Sutra Gathering All the Threads . 
 
By reading the two texts, the Chapter of the  Truthful One  and the Sutra of the Excellent Dharma’s 
White Lotus , one will come to understand how the Dh arma is abandoned and one will then be 
able to easily abandon engaging in this kind of  action. The other doors or ways of abandoning 
the Dharma can be understood from the Sutra Gathering All the Threads .  
 
I have tried to explain this subj ect as much as I am able in my  broken English. You can also 
discuss it and thereby slowly come to gain more and more understanding. Do not worry! Really, do not worry! Be very relaxed! Study in a very relaxed way. Keep your mind relaxed and also try to keep your body relaxed! Do not force or push yourself an d become tight. If one 
forces, one will come to think that  learning the Dharma is difficult. Per favore ! Please! Try to 
learn it all very slowly, while remaining happy and relaxed. Do not worry, thinking, “I cannot 
study. I cannot pass the exams.” DO NOT WORRY . I’ll tell you a story. There was once a 
monk called Dawa whose family lived in Lhasa. They were from a noble family and while his father had passed away, his mother remained in th e house with many servants to take care of 
her. She would go around each day, giving them orders. When Dawa came home to visit her, she would say, “You are a monk. You have an easy  and happy life. It is not a difficult life. You 
can sit on your cushion all day, reading books. It  is very easy.” Then one day he said to her, 
“Okay, mamma. Tomorrow it is your turn to not move. You just sit on this cushion and read 
these prayers while I bring you breakfast, lunch,  and dinner. I will also clean the house and 
make the offerings of water and butterlamps on  the alter. So tomorrow, please mamma, stay 
without moving.” She agreed and tried to do this, but soon beca me very tired as she was not 
used to sitting still. At the end of the day, she said that it had been very hard and difficult. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
52 Her son said to her, “Do you understand now that  it is not so easy?” And the mother said, “It 
is not so easy.” After that, he would go home from time to time, but his mother never again 
said that his life was easy. Instead she to ld him to sit while she brought him food!  
 
Friday morning, 21 January 2005  
I have been requested to give the oral transmission of the Guru Yoga of Lama Tsongkhapa . 
According to the Sera Je tradition, one should first go for refuge, then generate the mind of 
enlightenment, then do the continuous confe ssion, and then the four immeasurables. After 
that one should recite the verses of prostration or homage from Fundamental Wisdom , the 
Ornament for Clear Realizations , and Compendium of Valid Cognition . Then one should make 
requests to the holy beings to protect all sent ient beings, do the offering of ablution, and 
make a mandala offering. Only then should  one begin the actual  practice of the Guru Yoga of 
Lama Tsongkhapa . 
 This practice contains the seven limbs of prostration, offering, confession, rejoicing, 
exhorting to turn the wheel of Dharma, reques ting to not pass away, and dedication. First 
there is the invocation followed by the request to  r e m a i n  a s  l o n g  a s  c y c l i c  e x i s t e n c e  i s  n o t  
emptied and so forth. This practice also includ es the recitation of migtsema, whether in nine 
lines, six lines, five lines, or four lines.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
Explanation of how the Dharma possessing the two greatnesses 
should be listened to and explained 
2B3 How the Dharma possessing the two greatnesses should be listened to and explained 
A How to listen 
B How to explain 
C Generally, how to proceed at the end 
The third one has three points: how to list en, how to explain, and generally how to 
proceed at the end. 
2B3A How to listen 
1 Thinking about the advantages of listening to the Dharma  
2 Developing respect towards the Dharma and those who teach it 
3 The actual way to listen 
The first has three points: thinking about the advantages of listening29, developing 
respect towards the Dharma and who teaches it, and the actual way to listen. 
2B3A-1 Thinking about the advantages of listening 
First: from the Verses on Listening30:  
 
Through listening you come  to know the dharmas, 
                                              
29 The word "hear" ( thos pa ) includes reading texts. " thos pa mang po " lit.: "hear/having he ard a lot" means "well 
read", "educated" which is why, depending on the context " thos pa " may also be rendered as "study".  
30 Udanavarga 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
53 through listening misdeeds are turned away, 
through listening all trivia are abandoned, through listening nirvana is achieved. 
 
These four [lines] say that in dependence  on listening, an understanding of the 
points that should be adopted and abandoned gradually develops. From this knowledge arises the ethical discipline that counteracts wrongdoing. Then, once 
you have turned away from meaningless purs uits, the concentration that is a mind 
that can stay on a wholesome object as  long you wish arises. Then, through 
training in the wisdom realizing the such ness that is selflessn ess, the root of 
samsara’s fetters is cut and you attain liberation.  
 Now Lama Tsongkhapa sets out how to listen an d how to explain the Dharma and what to do 
at their conclusion. If one listens to the holy Dharma, there will arise many benefits. If one 
listens to the holy Dharma taug ht by the Buddha, one’s knowledge will increase. If one listens 
to or studies the Dharma, one will turn away fr om non-virtuous actions.  If one listens to or 
studies the Dharma, one will come to understa nd the meaning of the Buddha’s teachings and 
wisdom will arise in one’s mind . If one listens to and studies the Dharma, one will abandon 
bad and meaningless actions, whereby one will gradually come to achieve nirvana, the state 
beyond cyclic existence. Therefore, there are many  benefits of listening to the Dharma that we 
need to contemplate.   By listening to and studying the Dharma, on e will come to understand what is to be 
practiced or adopted and what is to be abandone d. In this way one will abandon negative or 
non-virtuous actions. One will understand what is meaningful and what is meaningless, and 
one will naturally give up the meaningless. If  one listens well, one will slowly generate 
concentration and wisdom and eventually achieve the state of liberation that is free from all 
suffering. With the wish to achieve concentratio n, one should set one’s mind single-pointedly 
on a virtuous object of meditation. If one is able to remain on the object, one will come to 
understand and then realize the thusness or se lflessness of persons or  phenomena. Thereby, 
one’s mind will have become trained in wisdom.   Therefore, first, based on understanding, on e trains in the base that is morality by 
abandoning non-virtuous actions. On this  base, one trains in the development of 
concentration. Then one engages in analyz ing the suchness of phenomena and persons, 
examining their lack of inherent existence or lack of self-suppo rting substantial existence. In 
this way, one can cut the root of cyclic existenc e and attain the state of liberation or nirvana. 
I n  s h o r t ,  w e  n e e d  t o  p u t  e f f o r t  i n t o  t r a i n i ng in the three higher trainings of morality, 
concentration, and wisdom, whereby we will become better and better.   
Middle Lam-Rim:   
 
In the Jataka Stories  it too says: 
 
He who through listening fills his mind with faith, 
{16} grows firm in rejoicing in that which is excellent.  
Wisdom is born and bewilderment vanishes; 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
54 this is well worth paying for with one’s own flesh. 
Listening is the lamp against mental darkness; the greatest wealth no robber can carry away; 
the weapon destroying the enemy – ignorance; 
the best of friends, giving guidance in skilful means, near and dear, if you are poor or not; inoffensive cure against ills of sorrow, 
the foremost army destroying an army of faults, 
it is the best fame, and treasure, and splendor as well. If you meet noble beings, the best of gifts; and, in assemblies, it is the delight of the wise. 
 as well as:  
Having listened, make the attainment the essence [of practice ], 
and you will be released from the stronghold of births with ease. 
 
Keep thinking again and again about these and other advantages of listening, and 
generate resolute faith
31 from the bottom of your heart. 
 
By listening to teachings one’s generates fait h in the Dharma and becomes very happy and 
joyful. Wisdom is generated, and ignorance or confusion is eliminated. Listening or hearing, 
like a lamp that illuminates the darkness of the world, brings about the wisdom arisen from hearing that can eliminate the ignorance or conf usion in our minds. This  type of quality is 
real wealth because if one has knowledge, it cannot be taken away by thieves or robbers. On 
the other hand, money, even a wish-fulfilling je wel, can be stolen by  thieves. Because our 
inner qualities, called “arya jewels” or “arya we alth,” cannot be taken away by anyone, they are 
the best wealth. Listening or hearing, specifical ly the wisdom arisen from hearing, destroys 
our inner enemy, ignorance. Therefore, we need  to generate the wealth of inner qualities – 
the seven jewels of arya beings – in our me ntal continuum, and then develop them. Then, 
even if we do not have any money, we will not have any problems! I have a photo in my house 
of a baby boy under which it says “No money. No  job. No girl. No problem”! I once knew an 
Indian pandit called Upadaya who said that ev en if one goes to another country without 
anything, if one can teach something to others one will be able to make money. On the other hand, if one has money but no knowledge and th en loses the money, one will not be able to 
gain it back. Upadaya shared this advice with th e students of the Tibeta n Institute of Higher 
Studies in Varanasi.   If one is rich, one will have many friends, bu t if one day one were to lose all one’s wealth 
and become as poor as a beggar, all those so -called friends would suddenly disappear. On the 
other hand, if we possess knowledge gained fr om hearing the Dharma, the wisdom arisen 
from hearing and the wisdom arisen from thinki ng, even if we become poor we will not lose 
it, just like the body is always  accompanied by its shadows. Ther efore, we need to study so as 
to develop our inner qualities as they will always be of help to us.  
                                             
 
31 JN: mos pa , could also be translated as admiration or aspiration 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
55  Listening to the Dharma is also like medici ne. When our body is sick, medicine can cures 
the illness without harming our body. Similarly,  knowledge always benefits us and does not 
harm our mind. Listening to or studying the Dhar ma is also like a powerful army that enables 
us to destroy our great faults. It also brings us fame, makes us supreme, and makes us into a 
t r e a s u r e  o f  k n o w l e d g e .  I t  f r e e s  u s  f r o m  w o r r y .  I t  a l s o  b r i n g s  a b o u t  a  g o o d  r e b i r t h  i n  c y c l i c  
existence. Cyclic existence, kor wa  in Tibetan (‘ khor ba ), means “to circle.” How do we circle? 
We are born and die, born an d die, born and die, continuo usly circling through the six 
realms – the three lower and three higher real ms. Sometimes we are born in the hell realm, 
sometimes in the hungry ghost realm, sometime s in the animal realm, and sometimes we are 
born in the happy migrations as a human being,  anti-god, or god. Up to now we have taken 
rebirth in every one of these realms. In fact, we  cannot say that there is a realm in which we 
have not been born. We have been born from  the lowest hell of Unrelenting Torment up 
through the Peak of Existence. All these rebirths were due to  afflictions and contaminated 
actions. Even now, once our virtuous actions have been exhausted, we will once again take 
rebirth in the lower realms. Everyday we create virtuous and non-virtuous  actions. A virtuous 
action acts as a projecting karma that brings rebirth in the upper realms, whereas a non-
virtuous actions acts as a projecting karma that  brings rebirth in the lower realms. Therefore, 
we need to check what kind of ac tions we create. This is not deceptive talk, it really works like 
this. If we create a very great negative action, it  is said that we can ev en experience the hell 
realm in this very life. There is a story about this regarding an Indian king. We need to understand this and to understand the benefits of listening to the Dharma. It is not enough 
to think about what one has heard just one time , one has to think about it again and again so 
as to keep it in one’s heart. Due to this one will develop the wish to practice more and more. 
To do this we need to li sten to many teachings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:   
2B3A-2 Developing respect towards the Dharma and those who teach it 
Second: from the Ksitigarbha Sutra : 
 
Listen filled with one-sided faith and respect, 
without any mockery or disrespect towards them, 
worship the exponents of the Dharma, producing the notion of them as resembling buddhas. 
 
In accordance with this quotation, you should view them as resembling a buddha, 
offer them service with things like lion th rones, worship them with gifts, and give 
up disrespect. In the Bodhisattva Levels  it says that you should be free from 
arrogance and disregard towards the teac hings and those who expound them, and 
that you should respect those two. And in the Jataka Stories  it says: 
 
Sit on a seat that is very low, 
develop the glory of discipline, 
see with eyes imbued with joy, while drinking the nectar of the words. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path   ILTK 17-21 January 2005 (01) 
 
56  
Bow down in respect and concentration, with a mind that is pure and free of stains 
like one who is ill {17} heeds the doctor’s words, 
respectfully listen to the Dharma. 
 How should we listen to teachings? We need to  develop faith and respect and then listen to 
the teachings with this attitude . One should also respect the teacher, and if possible also 
develop faith in him. At the very least, one sh ould not criticize him. If able to do so, one 
should think that of the Dhar ma teacher as being similar to  the Buddha. There are many 
buddhas but due to our karma we are unable to  see them directly. For this reason, they 
appear in a normal body of flesh and bones that like us belongs to the human race. One 
should think that buddhas manifest in this aspe ct in order to teach us and therefore respect 
the teacher as if he were an actual buddha.  
 One should set out a throne for the teacher su pported by four snowlions, representing the 
four fearlessnesses, or one can set out a high cu shion. Then one should request the teacher to 
give teachings, make offerings to him of food, tea, and so forth, and abandon disrespect.  
 Are there any doubts or questions? If not, okay!  I’ll stop here today so you can relax a bit 
more.  
 
Before I mentioned the Guru Yoga of Lama Tsongkhapa . There are many commentaries on this 
practice which you can read as the explanation is not difficult. This practice is also called the 
Hundred Deities of the Joyful Land . The Joyful Land is called Tushita in Sanskrit. Where is it? It 
is one of six levels of the gods of the desire realm. The first is the Fo ur Great Kings which is 
located mid-way up Mount Meru; the second is the Thirty-three, which is on top of Mount 
Meru; above that is Free from Combat; and above th at is Joyful Land or Tushita. The last two 
levels, Free from Combat and Joyful Land, ar e not based on the ground. Above them is 
Enjoying Emanations and above that is Cont rolling Others’ Emanations. Above these six 
desire realm gods are the seventeen levels of the form realm gods. In addition, there are the 
four formless realms. These are beings of our universe, even though we cannot see them.

FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
57 Monday afternoon, 23 January 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 17): 
2B3A-3 The actual way to listen 
A Indication of how to listen in general 
B The need to listen, having related this to one’s mind 
C How to keep in mind the purpose of attainments 
2B3A-3A Indication of how to listen in general 
1 Giving up the three faults of a vessel 
2 Relying on the six discriminations32
 
The third one has three [points]: Giving up the three faults of a vessel and relying 
on the six discriminations. 
2B3A-3A1 Giving up the three faults of a vessel 
First: If a vessel is turned upside down, or held the right way round but unclean, or 
clean but with the bottom leaking, then even if rain is made to fall by the gods on 
it, it cannot go in; or even if it does go  in, it will be polluted by the dirt and 
therefore will not fulfill the purpose of bein g drunk and the like; or  even if it is not 
spoiled by dirt, it will not stay but will leak  out. In the same way there will be no 
great purpose to hearing the Dharma, if you sit in a place where it is being 
explained, but do not listen well; or list en, but take it wrong or with a faulty 
motivation and the like; or even if these faults are not present, the words and the 
meaning you grasped at the time of hearing are not consolidated and get lost 
through forgetfulness, etc. Therefore, one should be free from these [faults]. The 
antidotes to these three are set forth in the sutras in three words: “Listen well, 
intensely, and keep it in mind!” And in the Bodhisattva Levels  we are told to listen 
with the desire to know everything, sing le-pointedness, attention, a humble mind, 
and the thought of all sentient beings.  
 
There are three types of inappropriat e vessels for Dharma teachings:  
1. an upside down vessel: similar to one who atte nds teachings but either does not listen to 
them or falls asleep, 
2. a dirty vessel, such that even when  nectar is put into it it can no longer be drunk: similar to 
one who listens to the teachings while looking for faults in them, and 
3. a vessel that is upright but has a hole in the bottom; similar to one wh o does not retain or 
remember the teachings but immediately fo rgets whatever he or she has heard.  
Someone who is free from these three faults is  an appropriate vessel to receive the Dharma 
teachings. Hence, we should list en to the Dharma carefully, with  attention, with respect, and 
with a good motivation wishing to understand more in order to develop our inner wisdom, and then try to remember or keep in mind what  we have heard without forgetfulness. On the 
other hand, if, for example, one has a good moti vation but forgets what was said there will 
not be any benefit. In order for the teachings to be of benefit, we should listen to them well 
and carefully and keep the teachings in  our mind. This is the main point. 
                                             
 
32 JN: the word ‘notion’ (‘du shes ) has been changed to ‘discrimination’ as it is a more familiar translation to the 
students at Lama Tzong Khapa Institute 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
58  The Bodhisattva Levels  says that one should listen atte ntively and without distraction. In 
other words, one should not listen to the Dhar ma while thinking of something else such as 
going shopping, for a picnic, or a trip to the mountains or the beach. One should concentrate 
and listen carefully.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3A-3A2 Relying on the six discriminations 
A Contemplating the discrimination of oneself as a patient 
B Contemplating the discrimination of the one expounding the Dharma as a doctor 
C Generating the discrimination of what has been taught as medicine 
D Generating the discrimination of persistent practice as the cure  
E Contemplating the discrimination of the Tathagata as an exalted being 
F Generating the discrimination of the Dharma tradition lasting long 
The second one has six points: contemplat ing the discrimination of oneself as a 
patient, of the one expounding the Dharma  as a doctor, subsequently generating 
the discrimination of what has been taug ht as medicine, the discrimination of 
persistent practice as the cure, contemplating the discri mination of the tathagatas 
as exalted beings, and generating the di scrimination of th e Dharma tradition 
lasting long. 
2B3A-3A2A Contemplating the discrimination of oneself as a patient 
First: In the Guide33 it says: 
 
Even when stricken with ordinary illness 
{18} one has to follow the words of the doctor, how much more so, if a hundred ills like lust strike you down all the time! 
 As is mentioned here, owing to mental affl ictions like attachment, etc. we always 
suffer from diseases causing long-lasting and severe suffering that is difficult to cure. Therefore we first need to diagnose them as such. Kamawa said:   
If it is not in accord with the actu al situation, our meditation goes off 
in the wrong direction. We have been  stricken with a serious illness due 
to the three poisons, the illness is ve ry grave, yet we do not know that 
we are ill at all. 
 We should first think of or discriminate ourselve s as a sick person or patient, whereby we will 
recognize the need to depend on a doctor. The do ctor, having examined us, tells us what kind 
of treatment is necessary, that is, what kind of  medicine to take, for our illness. The Buddha 
is similar to such a doctor.  The Dharma, the teachings of th e Buddha, is like medicine. If we are sick with an ordinary 
illness, we need to take certain medicine and fo llow the instructions of the doctor to not eat 
certain foods and so forth. By doing so, we ca n be cured of the illness. Similarly, if we 
                                             
 
33 Guide to a Bodhisattva’s Way of Life  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
59 consider the Dharma to be an actual medicine an d put it into practice, it can heal the mental 
illness of the afflictions.  
 Thus, we need to discriminate the Buddha, who is a holy being, as being like a doctor, and 
to discriminate the Buddha’s te achings as the medicine that can cure us. Then, we should 
think, “How nice it would be if the Buddha’s  teachings were to last for many eons.” All 
sentient beings, just like us, are sick. Like us, they too will be cured by following the advice of 
the doctor as we all have the same mental sickness of cyclic existence. To cure this sickness we 
need to put effort into practicing what the Buddha taught, since even to cure an ordinary 
illness we need to follow the prescribed treatment of a doctor. Here Engaging in the Bodhisattva 
Deeds  is cited as saying “100 ills,” whereas us ually there are said to be 84,000 mental 
afflictions to which we need to  apply 84,000 antidotes. On the other hand, 400 illnesses are 
mentioned in the Tibetan medical texts (not 424 il lnesses as is translated by some translators 
– literally, the Tibetan reads “four sets of 100”) . There are said to be 100 illnesses caused by 
attachment (translated above as “lust”), 100 caused by hatred, 100 caused by ignorance, and 
100 caused by a combination of equal parts attachment, hatred, and ignorance.  
 People often say to me that they have lung. In fact 100 sicknesses are related to the inner 
winds ( lung), 100 to the bile, and so forth. However, the root of all these physical illness is the 
mental afflictions. For example, some particular  illnesses arise due to attachment to sweets. 
While many illnesses are difficult to cure, still they can be cured over varying periods of time. 
H o w e v e r ,  w h e n  s i c k n e s s  o c c u r s  d u e  t o  k a r m a ,  i t  i s  v e r y  d i f f i c u l t  t o  c u r e .  B u t  e v e n  m o r e  
difficult to cure is the illness of  the afflictions. This illness is not at all easy to cure. This is 
because even though we have this illness, we do  not appear to be sick, but consider ourselves 
to be very healthy. In fact, if someone were to say to us “You are crazy,” we would become 
angry. This is due to not recognizing that we are in fact “mentally ill.” Mental illnesses in 
general are very difficult to cure. Although ps ychologists and psychotherapists can help some 
people with mental illness, in general they ca nnot cure them. To cure our mental illness we 
need to apply the appropriate antidote. The r oot of all mental illness is ignorance – the 
conception of a self or the conception of a self -supporting substantially existent self. It is the 
thought “I.” This illness is not easy to cure because it is not easy to eliminate this idea. Yet 
when it is eliminated, both our physical an d mental illnesses are cured. We need to 
understand that if we are to meditate, we need to  do it in a correct and perfect way. If we do 
not meditate in the right way, instead of brin ging benefit the mind may become even more 
disturbed. In this way our meditation will not be able to cure our mental illnesses of attachment, hatred, and ignorance.   Therefore, we first need to recognize that ou r main sickness is the mental afflictions within 
us. It is not that someone external creates pr oblems for us. Please be careful, our main 
problem-maker is inside our mind. We need to recognize this, after which we can work to eliminate it. If we want to blame someone or something for our problems, we should blame our own mental attitude. We are sick but we  do not recognize ourselves as sick. While 
physically we are not ill, mentally we are ill:  sometimes due to attachment, sometimes due to 
hatred, and sometimes due to ignorance. Amon g these, the main mental problem-maker is 
attachment. Attachment can make us really craz y. Therefore, we need to take care of our 
mind and apply the antidote to our mental illne ss. The antidote is the Buddha’s teachings, in 
particular the teaching that all phenomena ar e empty of true existence. All phenomena do 
not exist truly, but exist as dependent-relations.  By meditating on dependent-relation, we can 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
60 eliminate our ignorance. If ignorance is eliminated , since it is the root of the other afflictions, 
they too will be eliminated. Thus, meditation is the real antidote.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3A-3A2B Contemplating the discrimination of the one expounding the Dharma as a doctor 
Second: If we are stricken with a serious illness like, for example, a wind or gall 
disease, we look for a specialist in medicine and, meeting one, we are extremely 
happy, listen to what he says, treat him with respect and acknowledge him. In the 
same way one looks for a spiritual guid e who teaches the Dharma and, having 
found one, accomplishes what he says, taking it not as a burden but as an 
ornament, and acts with respec t and reverence towards him. 
 
If we have wind disease ( lung) we need a doctor, mainly a psyc hiatrist or psychologist who will 
ask us for a lot of money per hour! Also if we have  a gall or bile disease, such as jaundice, we 
need to take a particular type of medicine and need to find someone who can cure our illness. 
In short, if we are sick we need a doctor. The Ti betans say that eating a lot of fatty foods such 
as butter is the cause of bile disease, whereas ea ting food that lacks fat or oil is the cause of 
wind disease. One monk from the Masters Progra m, for example, often said to me, “Geshe-la, 
I have lung.” I would tell him not to worry and to eat some meat with oil and nutmeg. This 
would help him to sleep.  
 The Tibetans say that all the illnesses of hu man beings are caused by food, whereas all the 
illnesses in horses are caused by their running t oo fast. It is 100% sure that all our illnesses 
are caused by food. Therefore, we need to take care of our body. Presently we have a precious 
human rebirth endowed with the eighteen qualit ies, therefore we should be careful not to 
waste it. Our mind is also intelligent and clea r and capable of understanding, therefore we 
need to use it to develop our wisdom and inner qualities. To do this, we need a health body and a healthy mind. Therefore, we need to take care of them. For this reason, we need a good 
doctor. When we find such a good doctor, we become very happy. Then we need to listen to him. Similarly, in terms of the Dharma, we shou ld generate respect for our teacher and try to 
serve him. The best service is trying to put into practice what he has taug ht. This is important.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3A-3A2C Generating the discrimination of what has been taught as medicine 
Just as a patient greatly appreciates the medicine put together by the doctor, one 
should regard the instructions and teac hings taught by someone expounding the 
Dharma as the most importan t thing, and then make an effort to hold t hem in  
respect and to not waste them th rough forgetfulness and the like. 
 
When a doctor or nurse gives us medicine, we should take care of the medicine and consume 
it. Similarly, whatever the teacher has taught us  and whatever instructions he has given us, we 
should think of them as important, and put effort  into taking care of them and not forgetting 
them. If, on the other hand, we forget them, the teachings will be a waste. Therefore, 
whatever teachings we receive we should try to  keep them in our mind without forgetting 
them. This is not easy but we need to try.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
61  Perhaps we do not forget the teachings but co me to think that we do not need them and 
therefore set them aside. Sometimes when we ha ve listened to many teaching there comes a 
point where we think, “That is enough. I don’t need to listen to anym ore teachings. I don’t 
need to practice anymore. The teachings have  not worked for me, therefore I should give 
them all up.” However, this is not right.  
 The practice of sutra is said to take a long  time, in fact by this means it takes three 
countless eons to achieve buddhahood. Due to th is, we may think that it is better not to 
practice sutra but to immediately enter tantra  whereby we can achieve buddhahood in this 
life, in the short period of thre e years and forty-five days. Howeve r, if we do this, we will not 
have the necessary basis for the practice of tant ra and our inner realizations will not grow. As 
a basis we need the lam-rim or the common path. Only when this basis is strong and stable 
can we really practice the higher teachings.  
 Someone from Vicenza last week said to me , “May I ask you a question?” I joked with him 
saying, “I will not reply!” Then he said, “Please,  only one word,” so I said “Okay, tell me.” 
This person had originally been a student of Geshe Yeshe Tobden but later on he became a 
Dzogchen practitioner. He said that he had heard that, according to the mahamudra 
teachings, it was not necessary to do meditation on emptiness, on the deity, or anything else, 
nor to do recitations or visualizations, that it  was enough to meditate on the mind alone. I 
asked him, “How does one meditate on the mi nd? How does one recognize the nature of the 
mind? There is a conventional nature of mind and an ultimate nature of mind. Which one should one meditate on?” He said, “It is enough to know the mind. That is all. This was said 
by Tilopa.” I asked him if he had met Tilopa, bu t he said that he had not. I said, “But you 
said that Tilopa said that.” He said that this  is what had been translated by the translator. 
Some people think that there is no work to do, that just by sitting still in meditation that 
realizations will spontaneously develop and one will be able to fly in the sky! However, I think 
that it is not like this. We need the pres ence of two mental factors, mindfulness and 
introspection, at all times, whatever practice we  are doing. If there is no mindfulness we will 
forget everything. Without mindfulness even if someone says something to us, as soon as we 
move our body to another position we will immediately forget what he said. We should not be like this, but should cherish and put effort  into whatever teachings we have received, 
thinking that we are a patient who has been to ld what medicine to take by the doctor. We 
should act in accordance with whatever instructions he has given us.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3A-3A2D Generating the discrimination of persistent practice as the cure 
1 The contempt of striving for words without practicing them 
Fourth: An ill person sees that his illness cannot be alleviated if he does not take 
the medicine the doctor put together, an d takes it. Likewise, once you see that 
attachment etc. cannot be eliminated withou t practicing the instructions taught by 
the Dharma teacher, you should practice di ligently and not make a great effort at 
the words of various classifications and so forth without putting them into 
practice. As a matter of fact, what good would it be to a leper whose hands and legs are coming off to resort to a dose of medicine once or twice? {19} Likewise, for 
us who have been stricken since beginning less time by the wicked basis of illness 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
62 that is the mental afflictions, it is no t sufficient to practice the meaning of the 
instructions only a couple of times. This is why we shou ld examine all the 
complete parts of the path with discriminating wisdom34 and be as diligent as a 
flowing river. It is like it says in the Praise of Confessing : 
 
...That is to say our minds are always obscured, 
for long we have served the causes of our ills. How can a leper whose hands and feet have fallen off, and who takes his medicine but rarely, derive benefit? 
 Since we are a patient or a sick person, when a doctor prepares and gives us medicine we 
should think that if we do not take this medi cine our sickness will not be cured. Rather we 
should accept the medicine and then consume it. Similarly, if a Dharma teacher gives us 
teachings or instructions but we do not practice  them, we will be not be able to reduce or 
destroy our mental afflictions. Understanding this, we need to put effort into practice. This is 
important.  
 If someone does not practice what he has b een taught but instead s ays, “I have received 
numberless teachings from this-and-that lama an d from such-and such lama,” it just becomes 
meaningless talk that is of no benefit. There are people who say that they received lam-rim 
teachings from the Dalai Lama and from this-a nd-that lama, who name many lamas yet do not 
put effort into practice. They only put effort into talking about the te achings that they have 
received! This is of absolutely no benefit.  
 If someone with leprosy, who has lost his hands or feet, were to take medicine only once or 
twice, he would not be cured of his illness. In fact everyone is well aware that when one has a 
serious physical illness, one has to take medici ne for a long time, not just once or twice. 
Similarly, since we have been sick with the afflictions from beginningless time, we cannot be 
cured by receiving one or two teachings and doing a bit of practice. This is not enough to cure 
a sickness that is beginningless. Our rebirths  are beginningless and likewise our mental 
afflictions are beginningless, therefore they ca nnot be cured by merely receiving one or two 
instructions. Just hearing one or  two words and then putting th em into practice, it is not 
possible to be cured of our mental illness. We n eed to do analytical meditation that examines 
the different mental afflictions and the appropriat e antidotes that must be applied in order to 
stop them. One antidote cannot eliminate all th e mental afflictions, each one has its own 
specific antidote. For this reason, we need to practice the complete path.  
 We need to do analytical meditation and to  study like a flowing river, for example, the 
Ganges River, which flows continually without stopping. Similarly, our study and meditation 
should be continuous, never stopping. We sh ould not do it for a short time but then 
interrupt it, then again after some years do a bit more and then stop once  again. This will be a 
bit of benefit but it cannot eliminate or weaken our mental afflictions, the sickness of cyclic 
existence. To do this, we need to practice medi tation continually. For example, as an antidote 
to our attachment to a very beautiful object ther e is the meditation on ugliness. If we consider 
someone else’s body to be attractive, we should meditate on it as being repulsive, for example, 
as being a skeleton. If we think in this way our attachment will be reduced. On the other 
                                             
 
34 JN: so sor rtog pa’i shes rab , the wisdom of individual investigation 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
63 hand, if we have strong anger, we should apply the antidote of meditating  on love, that is, we 
should develop love for the object of our anger. Love is very important as it can reduce anger. 
While anger is a mental attitude wishing to harm  and cause suffering, love  is a mental attitude 
wishing to help or benefit and to make ha ppy. Therefore, these two are opposites.  
 Then there are people who have a lot of prid e. In this case we should also meditate, 
examining our body. Imagine if we were to cut our head in half would it be nice? If we were to 
cut our body open would it be nice? We would see that in fact we are very ugly. This will 
reduce the pride of thinking that we are beautifu l or the best in the world. If we check where 
it is that we are beautiful we will not be able to find the beautiful. There is a story regarding 
this about Saint Claire of Assisi. It is recounte d that when someone told  her that she had very 
beautiful eyes, she took out an eye and gave it to him! The eyeball in her hand was not at all 
beautiful! Therefore, we should check where is the beautiful. If we were to open our body, we 
would see that it looks like a sewer! Does it ha ve a good smell?! No, not at all. Therefore, we 
should try to reduce our pride, thinking that we are in fact not so beautiful after all, as we are 
unable to find any beauty.  
 In short, we need to practice as much as we can and continually. If we meditate, we need to 
d o  t h i s  c o n t i n u a l l y .  W e  s h o u l d  n o t  d o  i t  a t  times, and then not do it at other times. 
Sometimes we may be happy and so read a lot of texts, but at other times when we are 
unhappy we may stop completely. However, when it comes to meditation it is better not to 
stop, but to try every day to do even just a littl e bit. Many of us are busy, therefore we do not 
have much time to practice so this can be difficu lt. However, we have time to listen to a lot of 
gossip and to talk a lot! In fact , when engaging in meaningless talk, we can talk for three or 
four hours, even late into the evening. On the other hand, when it comes to practice, we 
either think that we have no ti me or that we will do it tomorro w as today we are too tired.  
 We should try to always have a mind of love  and compassion toward all sentient beings. If 
we cannot do this, we should at least try to  develop such minds toward our family and 
friends. Having done this, we can gradually exte nd this attitude to others, making it bigger 
and bigger. Everyone wishes for happiness and no one wants suffering. From this point of 
view we are all the same, whether learned or fool ish, rich or poor. All sentient beings are the 
same, even a cat wants to be happy and does not want suffering. However, animals cannot do 
much to take care of themselves. On the othe r hand, since we human beings are intelligent, 
we should try to be happy and to enjoy life, th inking that we have a good rebirth and a good 
family, have met the Buddha's teachings, and are able to study, to practice, and to meditate. 
We should think, “I am really very lucky.” We should be joyful and not depressed. We should 
not think we have problems. If we think we have problems, more and more problems will 
come. Because we have been born in cyclic existence, everyone has some problems and 
suffering. There is almost no one in this worl d who does not have some suffering. We have 
big sufferings and small sufferings, but thinking that we are suffering and unlucky is not useful. Instead we should think that we are ve ry lucky. We have a precious human rebirth 
with the eighteen qualities, ha ve met the Dharma, a virtuous spiritual friend, and friends with 
whom we can study and meditate. In this way we should relax!   Tuesday morning, 24 January 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim (Tibetan text page 19): 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
64 2 The importance of the discrimination of oneself as someone ill 
That being so, the discrimination of oneself as someone ill is very important. If it 
is present, the other ones also arise. However, if this remains merely words, 
without the meaning of the instructions  being accomplished for the sake of 
eliminating the mental afflictions you will only be a listener. You will resemble an 
ill person who is not rid of his illness but,  having sought a doctor, only cares about 
the medicinal composition and does not take the medicine. This is said in the King 
of Concentration [Sutra] . It also says: 
 
If, after I have explained the excellent Dharma, you do not apply it well, although you heard it, it is like with patients whose pouches are full of medicine, but who still cannot cure their own diseases. 
 
And in the Guide : 
 
These should be put into practice physically, 
what would be gained by uttering just the words? Would it be beneficial to the patients, if they just read how the medicine is meant to be used? 
 Here Lama Tsongkhapa sets out the importance of discriminating or recognizing oneself as being like a sick person. Thinking “I am sick,” we need to then rely on a good doctor, for 
example, a surgeon. Then we should try to fo llow whatever treatment he or she recommends 
a c c o r d i n g l y .  W e  a r e  s i c k  w i t h  t h e  a c t u a l  i l l n e s s  o f  c y c l i c  e x i s t e n c e .  T h e  D h a r m a  i s  l i k e  t h e  
medicine or treatment for this illness. In addi tion, we need someone who is like a doctor who 
can show us how to rely on the Dharma or ho w to take the medicine of the Dharma. Having 
received teachings on the Dharma, like receiv ing and taking medicine, we should put them 
into meditation practice. In this way our mental sickness, the illness of cy clic existence, can be 
cured. This is important.   On the other hand, if someone teaches us what to do but we do not put it into practice, it 
will not be very useful. If we understand the te achings but just listen to them as mere words, 
our afflictions cannot be eliminated. Therefore, we  need to practice care fully and to take care 
of ourselves. In this way, just as a patient or sick person can be cured and freed from his or her sickness by taking the medicine given to hi m or her by the doctor in accordance with his 
advice, so too if we practice the Dharma in accordance with the inst ructions given by the 
Buddha our mental illness can be cured and we ca n achieve the state of liberation or nirvana. 
We need to take care of ourselves because just as  if we do not take the medicine prescribed by 
the doctor we cannot be cured, similarly if havi ng received teachings we do not put them into 
practice our mental illness cannot be cured. 
 Here the King of Meditative Stabilizations  says that if someone, for example the Buddha, 
gives excellent and profound teachings and we listen to them but do not practice them 
perfectly, they cannot help us. Likewise, if a do ctor gives us many medicines but we just carry 
them around in a pouch without taking them, th ey cannot cure us. Therefore, we need to put 
effort into practice just as a sick person does whatever the doctor advises, even going so far as 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
65 to allow part of his body to be cut off in order to be cured. We need to put the Buddha’s 
teachings into practice in order to develop th e mind of enlightenment, love and compassion, 
and definite emergence in our minds. This is important.  
 If someone reads in a book that such-and-such a medicine can cure his illness, this reading 
alone will not cure him. He needs to take the medicine. Similarly, if one reads the lam-rim 
but does not practice it, it will not have any resu lt. Perhaps in this case it would be better to 
put effort into making money! For example, if  one learns how to make pizza, one can open a 
small restaurant and make money! If one just  reads books without doing any practice, the 
mind may become even more confused. I’m no t joking. We see people who ask for pujas, 
such as the Medicine Buddha Puja, when they are sick. Although this can help eliminate 
some external interferences, just reading medical texts cannot cure anyone’s illness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
3 The instruction to act [accordingly], having identified [the meaning of] “persistence” 
Therefore, the discrimination  that persistence eliminat es the disease should be 
developed. “Persistence” in this statement signifies putting into practice the topics 
of adopting and abandoning [of modes of  conduct] from the spiritual teacher’s 
instructions. {20} Now, to put them into practice you need to know them, and for 
that you need to listen and study. Again,  after studying and knowing, you need to 
practice, and therefore it is crucial to engage in the meaning of what has been 
studied to the best of one’s abilities. Othe rwise, at the time of death you will have 
regrets with regard to what you have not accomplished. You will resemble an actor 
imitating others or someone interested in sugar who eats only the cane. In 
Exhortation to the Supreme Intention35 it says: 
 
“I failed in my attainments, now what should I do?” 
that is how the childish wail while dying. 
They have not delved deep and the suffering is great; 
note that these are the faults of delighting in words. 
 
And: 
Like someone amidst the crowd observing a play, 
discussing the virtues of another hero, and failing with regard to his own persistence; note that these are the faults of delighting in words. 
 And: 
In the bark of sugarcane there is no essence,  the delightful flavor is inside; someone eating the bark is unable to find the delicious flavor of molasses.  
                                             
 
35 JN: lhag bsam skul ba , could also be translated as Exhortation to the Special Thought  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
66 Similarly, words are like the bark, 
the meaning of those words is like the taste. Consequently abandon delighting in words, 
always be conscientious, reflecting their meaning! 
 
We need to recognize what is important, for example, the medi cine of the Dharma teachings. 
Why is this important? It is important because ju st as in order to eliminate a physical sickness 
we need to take medicine and follow the treatme nt prescribed by the doctor, so too in order 
to eliminate our mental illness we need to put the Dharma into practice. To do so, we need 
to rely on a spiritual friend, a teacher, who te lls us what to abandon or discard and what to 
adopt or practice. Having been told this, we need  to understand what kind of actions of body, 
speech, and mind need to be abandoned, these be ing the non-virtuous acti ons of body such as 
k i l l i n g ,  t h e  n o n - v i r t u o u s  a c t i o n s  o f  s p e e c h  s u c h  a s  l y i n g ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  W e  a l s o  n e e d  t o  abandon bad behavior. We need to try to ab andon all these objects of abandonment. The 
objects to be practiced are the virtuous actions of not killing, not stealing, not engaging in 
sexual misconduct, not lying, and so forth. We  need to perform as many of these virtuous 
actions as we can, as in this way we will de velop the mind of enlightenment and correct view, 
whereby our inner qualities will develop and th e mental afflictions can be eliminated. For 
this, we need to put effort into the instructions that we have received or the knowledge that we have gained from reading texts such as th ose on the lam-rim. We should not read these 
texts as if they were books on history or geogra phy, this would not be ri ght. Rather we should 
read them and then put them into practice.  What is the purpose of listening to teachings?  Initially it is to gain knowledge. Having 
gained knowledge, what is the purpose of listen ing to teachings? It is to put effort into 
practicing whatever one has heard and understood. It is important to list en to teachings, to 
think about the meaning, and to put the meaning that has been understood into practice as 
m u c h  a s  o n e  c a n .  T h i s  i s  c o r r e c t .  I f  w e  d o  t h i s ,  a t  t h e  t i m e  o f  d e a t h  w e  w i l l  b e  f r e e  f r o m  regrets and sadness. We will be happy and joyf ul, like someone who having gone to another 
country returns home, happy to meet their fami ly and to once again stay with them. At the 
time of our death, the only thing that is of  any benefit is the Dharma. The non-virtuous 
actions that we have created will only bring us suffering and regret for what we have done. At 
the time of death, they may even cause us to have  a vision that we will go to the hell realm for 
example. We may see the Lord of Death in front of us, stacking up white stones for the virtue 
and black stones for the non-virtue that we di d, the black being much more than the white. 
Then, we may hear the Lord of Death telling us th at we must now go to the hell realm, to the 
hungry ghost realm, or to the animal realm. If  we are born in the hell realm we will not be 
happy. If we suddenly changed into a cat or a horse would we be happy?! There is a story 
about such an event. I won’t te ll it now. Or maybe I will! 
 In ancient times in India, a monk and a lay tantric practitioner were traveling together. 
One day the tantric practitioner asked the monk to go collect wood, as the monk was younger 
and stronger, while he remained behind. The mo nk suspected that he was plotting something 
but agreed and went off to get the wood. When  the monk had left, the tantric practitioner 
switched their pots of food and cooked and at e the monk’s food. Due to having eaten the 
monk’s food, the tantric practitioner was tran sformed into a horse. The monk returned, put 
his bags on the horse, and continued his journe y leading it behind him. When he arrived at 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
67 Mount Kailash, there were many saddhus there and he asked them if they had anything to 
transform the horse into a human. They made some dough balls, recited mantras on them, 
and fed them to the horse, which became a hu man being but with many sores on its back.  
 In  s ho rt , we n eed t o  be careful. We s ho u ld not wear a mask, like monks when they do 
ritual dances ( cham ) or like actors in a play, as there will be no benefit. Also we should not be 
like magicians who do tricks and make things  disappear. This means that we should not 
pretend to be a practitioner whil e in actuality not practicing.  
 We should take the essence or sweetness of ac tual sugar, which is inside the cane, rather 
than eating the outside cane or  bark, which has very little sweet ness. If one reads but does not 
practice or does not integrate one’s understandin g with one’s mind, it will be like eating the 
bark. We need to practice well and perfectly, putting it into our mind and not leaving it to be 
mere words on our lips. We need to extract the meaning in order to change our mind. It is 
important to do this.   In short, we need to extract the essence of  the Dharma. For example, the essence of the 
lam-rim consists of the three principal aspects of  the path to enlightenment: renunciation or 
definite emergence, the mind of enlightenment,  and correct view. These three are important. 
We need to try to develop the renunciation that is not just the wish to give up suffering, but is also the wish to give up the pleasures of cy clic existence, which appear to be happiness but 
in reality are the nature of suffering. In fact the more we enjoy the pleasures of cyclic existence, the more suffering and problems we will experience. For this reason, we need to 
think about the fact that these pleasures are essenceless and thereby give up the mundane 
happiness of this life and strive to achieve th e final happiness of nirvana or liberation. In 
other words, we need to develop the mind wi shing to attain either  nirvana or buddhahood 
that has renounced the pleasures of cyclic existe nce, wealth, name, and so forth. This is the 
meaning of renunciation or definite emergence put in a simple way.  The mind of enlightenment is the mind th at wishes to achieve enlightenment for the 
benefit of all sentient beings. We need to genera te this kind of mind. At first it will only be 
contrived, but gradually with familiarity it can become uncontrived. This mind is the cause of 
attaining buddhahood.   We also need correct view: the understand ing that all phenomena are empty of true 
existence or inherent existence. These three mind s are important. If one wishes to attain the 
state of nirvana and become a hearer or solita ry realizer foe-destroyer, one needs to have 
correct view. If one wishes to attain budd hahood, one needs the mind of enlightenment 
together with correct view. With the develo pment of these three minds, one can attain 
enlightenment and become free from cyclic ex istence and achieve the state of happiness. 
Practicing these three is like taki ng the essence of the sugarcane.  
 We also need to abandon talking too much, and to try to think more. We need to have 
conscientiousness at all times and to take care  of our own mind. This is mentioned in the 
lam-rim and is important in our daily life. Ha ving understood this, we need to develop a 
happy mind. In this way we can gain realizations.   Another example is an apple. If we eat the sk in there is not much ta ste, due to which we 
usually peel it, throw away the skin, and eat the fruit inside. We should practice the Dharma 
in a similar way, that is, we should take its essence. However, perhaps some people eat the 
skin and throw away the apple! But, as this wo uld not be intelligent, we should not do like 
this!
