Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
68  
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3A-3A2E Contemplating the discrimination of the Tathagata as exalted being 
The fifth: having brought to mind the teacher of the Dharma , the Bhagavan, one 
should develop respect. 
 
The Bhagavan is our teac her, Buddha Shakyamuni. Bhagavan  is a Sanskrit word, which is 
translated into Tibetan as chom den . Chom , meaning destroyed, indicates that he has destroyed 
the four maras, and den, meaning possess, indicates that he possesses the six qualities. I 
mentioned before that there are four maras or demons: the mara of the aggregates, the mara 
of the afflictions, the mara of the Lord of De ath, and the mara of th e sons-of-the-gods, the 
Lord of Desire. These are all demons in the sense that they harm us. Having taken this 
human body, when its relationship  with the mind is cut we die. Since this harms us, death is 
considered to be a demon.  
 The afflictions are demons because due to them we cannot achieve everlasting happiness 
and instead experience suffering. The aggregat es, especially the body, are demons because 
e v e r y  p a r t  o f  i t  c a n  b e c o m e  s i c k  o r  e x p e r i e n c e  p a i n  s u c h  t h a t  w e  h a v e  t o  g o  t o  a  h o s p i t a l ,  spend money, and so forth. The sons-of-the- gods are a type of god. Buddha Shakyamuni 
destroyed all these demons. He abandoned the afflictions and obscurations, abandoned the 
contaminated body and attained a wisdom or mental body, attained omniscient mind, and 
destroyed the root of the afflictions, due to wh ich he could no longer be overcome by death. 
He also destroyed the sons-of-the-gods because,  when he was sitting under the bodhi tree in 
meditation, the maras emanated beautiful wome n who danced around him and soldiers with 
weapons who shot arrows at him, yet he dest royed all of them. In this way the Buddha 
completely destroyed all four maras.   Buddha Shakyamuni came from a good family and had a son, Rahula, who later became a 
foe-destroyer. According to the Vinayasutra , he had everything, good knowledge, good 
wisdom, a good body, a good family, a good line age, and so forth. His body had the qualities 
of the 112 signs and exemplifications, his sp eech had the 64 melodious qualities, and his 
mind had the qualities of the 21 types of wisdom . These qualities, together with the cause of 
each of the signs and exemplifications, ar e explained in the ei ghth chapter of the Ornament for 
Clear Realizations .  
 I n  s h o r t ,  “ b h a g a v a n ”  r e f e r s  t o  B u d d h a  S h a k y a m u n i ,  w h o  i s  v e r y  k i n d  t o  u s  i n  t h a t  h e  taught the Dharma that we can put into practi ce. Thinking in this way and remembering his 
qualities, we should generate faith in him.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3A-3A2F Generating the discrimination of the Dharma tradition lasting long 
The sixth consists in the thought: “How ni ce it would be if, based on listening to 
such a Dharma, the Conqueror’ s teachings were to remain in the world for a long 
time.” 
 
Having listened to and practiced the Dharma, one should think “May the Buddha’s teachings 
abide for a long time in this world.” Having list ened to teachings, if one then teaches others 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
69 and then they teach others and so on and so on , the Buddha’s teachings will in fact last long. 
As it is important for this to happen, every day we should develop this wish.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3A-3B The way in which, having related this to one’s mind, it is necessary to listen 
Apart from that, if while explaining or li stening to the Dharma you keep your own 
mind to one side, the Dharma remaining sepa rate from it, then this will result in 
whatever is explained missing the point. Therefore, it is necessary to listen in order 
for one’s own continuum to reach certainty.  To illustrate this: {21} wanting to 
check whether or not there is dirt or some  other impurity on your face, one looks 
in a mirror and, understanding that there is dirt, removes it; likewise, when you 
hear the Dharma, your faulty behavior  appears in the mirror of the Dharma, 
whereupon you feel anguish thinking: “That is what my mind has come to.” Then, 
as you engage in eliminating faults and a ttaining qualities, you necessarily train in 
accordance with the Dhar ma. It is like in the Jataka Stories  [where] the son of 
Sutasa asks Prince Chandra for Dharma teachings in these terms:  
Beholding the forms of my bad conduct, in the dazzling mirror of Dharma, 
anguish arises forcefully in me, 
and I turn towards the Dharma. 
 Knowing this to be the thinking of a [s uitable] vessel for hearing the Dharma, the 
Bodhisattva gave him teachings. 
 We need to listen to teaching s, contemplate them, and then me ditate on them in order to 
integrate them with our own mind. This is important. If, on the other hand, we listen to teachings but do not apply them to our mind as an tidotes to our mental afflictions, it will be 
as if our mind is on one side and our Dharma on the other. It would be like buying a book in 
a shop and, without reading it, putting it on  a shelf! If the Dharma remains something 
external to us and unmixed with our mind, this would not be right. Whatever Dharma 
anyone teaches us, whatever Dharma we read in a book, or whatever Dharma we listen to on 
a tape recorder, video cassette, and so forth, we sh ould try to put it insi de our mind such that 
it becomes mixed with our mind. Just as milk put into tea mixes with the tea and it becomes 
milk tea, so too should we mix our mind and the Dharma in a similar way. In other words, we should not allow them to remain separate, like biscuits put into tea. If  we do this with the 
Dharma, it will not function well and will not b e  b e n e f i c i a l .  W e  s h o u l d  e n s u r e  t h a t  t h e  
instructions and our mind become inseparable. 
 We need to recognize the many mental af flictions that exist in our continuum. For 
example, if we have the affliction of laziness, we need to recognize it and apply its antidote. 
What is the antidote to laziness? It is joyous effort. On the othe r hand, if we see that there is 
miserliness in our mind, what antidote do we n eed to apply? We need to develop the mind of 
giving, the mind that wishes to give. We need to practice in this way.  
 In the morning when we first wake up, we look at our face in the mirror and, if we see that 
it is not clean, we clean it. If we see that our hair is messed, we comb it. Similarly, when our 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
70 faults appear in the mirror of Dharma, we n eed to clean them with wisdom. The Dharma 
mirror is wisdom. With wisdom, we can recognize our faults. When these faults appear in the 
Dharma mirror of wisdom, we should apply thei r antidotes. When we look in the Dharma 
mirror and see our many faults, it may bring us  mental suffering. What should we do then? 
We should determine to eliminate these faults. Seeing the qualities and benefits of the 
Dharma, we should put effort into practicing the Dharma. In this way our life will become 
useful. If we do not practice th e Dharma, it will not become usef ul but will be like the study 
of geography or history, which do not involve any practice. Acting in this way is not right. The 
Dharma of the lam-rim is the main tool for cleaning out our inner faults – our negative 
emotions, our bad behavior, and our bad actions. With it we can clean the three doors of our 
body, speech, and mind. Practicing in this way will benefit us as well as other people. This is very good. Therefore, we should try to do this.   In short, we need to study the lam-rim care fully, to read books about it, and to try to 
understand it. If we do not understand something, we should ask the people around us so as 
to eliminate our doubts. Virtuous friends are those who help each other to develop good 
attitudes, good actions, and a good mind, and to improve their practice of the Dharma. We 
should try in daily life to think, “I will help everyone as much as I can.” Even if we cannot 
help them, we can still develop the wish to help  them. In this way, our inner qualities will 
grow.   It is important to try as much as possible to  develop our inner qualities, mainly love and 
compassion. These two are important for everyone, for every living being. They are a universal 
Dharma or universal religion, whereas the diffe rent religions have different practices and 
different scriptures. There are also those wh o do not accept a religion, for example, 
Communists. However, if we mention love an d compassion to them, everyone will accept 
them. Love and compassion are a universal reli gion, no one will argue that we do not need 
them. Everyone knows that everyone needs love . Communists also have love and compassion, 
for example, they try to take care of the poor and to eliminate their poverty. They say this and 
wish to do this, however it is difficult to el iminate everyone’s poverty. But this shows that 
inside they have love and compassion. We also see that even mother cats, dogs, and birds try 
to take care of their offspring. Therefore, in daily life we should develop the minds of love 
and compassion.   We should also be more satisfied and have le ss desire for material things. We should think, 
“I have enough. I have all the necessary conditions.” If instead we think, “I already have a car but I want a better and more expensive one,” we will not be satisfied and will not be mentally 
happy. We need a satisfied and content mind, with less desire. On the other hand, we need 
more desire for inner attainments, for enligh tenment, and for Dharma practice. From the 
side of Dharma or from the side of developing inner qualities, we need more desire. We need a strong desire to attain enlightenment. We also  need dissatisfaction with what we have done 
so far. Dissatisfaction and more desire for mate rial things, on the other hand, is an obstacle 
and only brings suffering, therefore we need to give them up.   We also need to help others as much as possi ble. If we cannot benefit them at the very least 
we should try not to harm them. This is importan t. This is the main pr actice of Buddhism, it 
is the very basis. We also need to understand what pure Dharma is, to do this we need to study. This is not easy as we cannot understand  everything immediately, we need to put effort 
into gaining understanding. Therefore, we need  to eliminate the obstacle of laziness. There 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
71 are many types of laziness and we need to elim inate them all. We should not think, “Today I 
a m  t i r e d .  I  w o n ’ t  d o  i t  t o d a y ,  I ’ l l  d o  i t  tomorrow.” Then again tomorrow we think, “Not 
today, tomorrow.” Until we die, there will al ways be a tomorrow! For example, one of my 
students who is not well mentally often asks me, “Geshe-la, can I get better?” I say to him, 
“Maybe tomorrow you will be better!” I told him to  go to India or Nepal as he cannot work in 
Italy. He asked me if by going to India his ment al instability could be cured. I told him that 
this would depend on him. 
 W e  n e e d  t o  t a k e  c a r e  o f  o u r s e l v e s .  O t h e r s  c a n  h e l p  u s  f i n a n c i a l l y ,  b u t  i f  o u r  m i n d  i s  
unhappy they cannot help us. We need to apply the antidotes and develop our inner qualities. We need to apply the Dharma to our mind, as in this way we can eliminate faults. 
W e  s h o u l d  h a v e  a  g o o d  m i n d ,  a l w a y s  o n  t h e  s i d e  o f  t h e  D h a r m a .  I f  w e  d o  t h i s ,  w e  w i l l  become a vessel for listening to and contemplatin g the Dharma. In this case the bodhisattvas 
and buddhas will give us teachings. They know the mental attitudes of others.  
 In the past a man went to see a well-known guru in Tibet and asked him for instructions. 
The guru was busy with something else and said  to him “Get out” in the Indian language. 
The man thought that this was an instruction fo r him and that he should recite these words. 
Therefore, he continuously reci ted “Get out, get out, get out…” One day, thinking about the 
meaning of “Get out,” he came to understand th at he should get out of  cyclic existence. Due 
to this, he developed the wish to get out of cy clic existence, renuncia tion. In this way, he 
received an instruction, even though the gu ru had not had any intention to give him a 
teaching, he had just wanted him to leave!   There is another story about a lama who was a tantric practitioner. A man with many pock 
marks on his face and particularly on his no se went to see him and asked the lama for a 
mantra. This happened in upper Tibet near Ladhak. The lama said to him, “Your nose is like 
rudraksha ,” a large round seed with many protruding  bumps on it. The man, satisfied that he 
had received the transmission of a mantra, reti red to a cave and recited these words over and 
over again, like a mantra. He eventually beca me a healer and benefited many people. One day 
the lama became sick, his throat swollen and blocked. He saw many doctors and took many 
different types of medicine, but nothing cured hi s ailment. One day one of his attendants told 
him about the famous healer who lived in the ar ea and asked whether he could invite him to 
see the lama. The lama agreed. When the healer  arrived, the lama was lying down in great 
pain. The healer began to recite his “mantra, ” at which point the lama remembered saying 
this to him and burst out laughing. Due to this , an abscess filled with pus burst and he was 
cured! This shows that if there is  faith there will be benefit. If there is no faith, there will not. 
Therefore, when practicing the Dharma we need strong faith. This is important.  
 
Tuesday afternoon, 24 January 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 21):  
2B3A-3C How to keep in mind the purpose of attainments 
In short, thinking, “I sha ll attain buddhahood for the sa ke of all sentient beings. 
To attain that, I need to train in its caus es. Apparently for that it is necessary to 
listen to the Dharma, therefore I shall list en to the Dharma,” you should generate 
the mind of enlightenment, consider the be nefits of study, and listen with joy, 
abandoning the faults of a vessel. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
72  
In brief, we need to develop the mind wishin g to attain enlightenment or buddhahood. Then, 
in order to achieve buddhahood, it is necessary to train in its causes. Since a result cannot 
arise without its causes and conditions, we need to train in the necessary causes and 
conditions for enlightenment. For this, we need to listen to the Dharma in order to achieve 
buddhahood for the benefit of all sentient beings. In this way we should generate the mind of 
enlightenment. We should think about the many  advantages of hearing the Dharma, try to 
remember them, and listen to th e Dharma with joy. In this way, we will listen to the 
teachings, having abandoned the th ree faults of being like an upside down vessel, like a dirty 
vessel, and like a leaky vessel. Just as if one we re to pour water into an upside down vessel it 
cannot go inside, similarly if while listening to the Dharma we fall asleep, the teachings 
cannot go inside. Just as even nect ar that has been poured into a vessel that is dirty and smelly 
becomes undrinkable, similarly if we listen wh ile looking for faults in the teachings, the 
Dharma will not be of any benefit.  Just as when a vessel has a hole in it, whatever is put into it 
will leak out, similarly if we listen to teachings but forget what we heard as soon as they finish, 
that is, they go in one ear and out the other, this would not be useful. We should listen well 
and carefully, with a single-pointed mind. One wh o does this is the best vessel for the Dharma 
or the best disciple. In this way receiving teaching s will be of benefit. Similarly, if we forget 
what we have read as soon as we close the book, there will not be much result. On the other hand, if we remember what we have read of the Buddhas teachings, it will be of great benefit. By means of mindfulness we should try to keep  whatever we have heard or studied in our 
mind.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3B How to explain 
1 Considering the benefits of explaining the Dharma 
2 Generating respect for the teacher and the teachings 
3 What kind of attitude and activities to explain with 
4 Distinction between who the explanations should and should not be given to 
The second one has four points: Consid ering the benefits of explaining the 
Dharma,. generating respect for the teac her and the teachings, what kind of 
attitude and activities to explain wi th, and the distinction between who the 
explanations should and should not be given to. 
2B3B-1 Considering the benefits of explaining the Dharma 
First: In the Treasury36 it says: 
 
Giving Dharma free of mental afflictions, 
teach according to sutra and other true texts. 
 In its auto-commentary it says:  
                                             
 
36 cf. 10 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
73 Therefore, those who ex plain the Dharma incorrectly and those who 
explain it with an afflicted mind craving for material gain, respect, and 
fame, {22} corrupt their own great merits.  
 
Therefore, a pure motivation to teach the Dharma is extremely important, and just 
like past meditators said, “I have neve r explained the Dharma without meditating 
upon impermanence before the session,” it  is crucial to review it beforehand.  
 In Exhortation to the Superior Intention  two groups of 20 advantages of giving the 
gift of Dharma in the absence of material  things and the concern for gain, respect, 
and the like are set forth. Also, in Drag Shul Chan37 it says that the merit of a 
householder giving immeasurable material things is exceeded by an ordained 
person giving a single verse of Dharma. 
 There are many benefits of giving or explai ning the Dharma. Howeve r, the motivation for 
giving teachings should be that  of wishing to benefit, it sh ould not be motivated by the 
mental afflictions. We should think to offer teachings, without being attached to gaining 
material things, wishing to become famous, an d so forth. We should not teach with an 
afflicted mind, but should have a pure motivati on wishing to give teachings to benefit other 
sentient beings. With this motivation we should  teach sutra, such as the twelve branches of 
scripture, and so forth. This then will be perfect.   If we give a mistaken explanation this will also  not be of any benefit. In addition, if we are 
motivated by the wish to gain material things, this would not be correct. Also teaching in 
order to have others act as our servants is no t correct, nor is teaching  with the thought to 
become famous. All of these will only cause ou r previously accumulated merit to degenerate. 
Therefore, if we wish to give teachings it is im portant to do so with a pure motivation, not an 
afflicted motivation. An example of a pure moti vation is the mind of enlightenment, as well 
as compassion and love. If we give teachings with such a motivation, we will teach in an 
excellent way. On the other hand, if we teac h the Dharma or the scriptures with a mind 
afflicted by attachment, pride, and so forth, then it just becomes idle talk, which is non-
virtuous. In fact the Treasury of Manifest Dharma  says that if one teaches even the twelve 
branches of scriptures motivate d by attachment, pride, and so forth, although one’s speech 
will appear to be Dharma, in actuality it will be  idle talk, one of the ten non-virtuous actions. 
Therefore, when we teach the Dharma we should develop a g ood motivation thinking to 
benefit our listeners.   Some meditators of the past said that  they never teach wi thout meditating on 
impermanence in a previous session. This impl ies that they meditated on the certainty of 
death, the uncertainty of when death will come , and the fact that it cannot be turned away. 
We too can do a short meditation on impermanen ce before giving a teaching. We could also 
meditate on love, compassion, or emptiness, yet impermanence is the best. This is because if 
we remember death-impermanence, we will rememb er to practice the Dharma. Therefore, we 
need to keep in mind, “I am impermanent. I am changing momentarily. Now, even though it seems that I am healthy, when I lie down to  sleep I may not wake up tomorrow. I may die 
before then.” Recently someone called me to s ay that the evening before her partner had been 
                                             
 
37 Sutra Requested by  Drag Shul Chan  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
74 fine, but the next morning when she went to wa ke him, he did not move, he was dead. This 
happens because it is uncertain when death will come. 
 When we do recitations or prayers and make offerings, for example, of water, light, or 
flowers to the Buddha, we should first develop a good motivation, thinking to do so in order 
to benefit all sentient beings. In this way, we will create a lot of merit and all our actions will 
be successful and all interferences will be eliminated. Therefore, at the beginning our 
motivation is important. There are two types of motivation: causal motivation and time 
motivation. Of these, the most important is th e causal motivation. Then, if the motivation 
were also good at the time of the action that would be the best . However, if we are unable to 
generate a good motivation at that time, then  we should at least keep a neutral mind. For 
example, if one’s mother were to have a lot of  suffering and pain, out of love for her one may 
come to think, “It would be better for my mother  to die.” If one were to then kill her, while 
the causal motivation would be compassion an d love, the motivation at the time of the 
action, such as giving her poison, woul d be negative. This is taught in the Hundred Actions 
Sutra . On the other hand, to kill one’s mother ou t of anger or hatred is an action of 
immediate retribution.  If we give teachings without seeking wealth , respect, service, fame, and so forth, but 
instead, having completely abandoned these, give teachings out of love, this will be of benefit. 
This is the generosity of  giving the Dharma. The Exhortation to the Superior Intention  describes 
two sets of twenty advantages to  giving Dharma in this way.  
 Drag shul chan  means Wrathful Track. This text says th at if a lay person gives many material 
t h i n g s  s u c h  a s  f o o d ,  d r i n k ,  m o n e y ,  c l o t h i n g ,  a n y  k i n d  o f  g o o d  t h i n g ,  t h e r e  w i l l  b e  g r e a t  
benefit. However, if an ordained monk or nun gives teachings, even just one verse of Dharma, 
it is of much greater benefi t and creates more merit.  
 In short, if we teach Dharma with a good motivation, we will create a lot of merit. On the 
other hand, if we have an afflicted or faulty motivation, even though we teach the Dharma it 
does not become meritorious, rather it become s non-meritorious or non-virtuous. Therefore, 
we have to be careful how we give teachings. If with a good motivation we give even a few 
words of explanation it will be of great benefit, whereas if we teach a lot with an afflicted motivation it will not even be virtuous. In addi tion, if we work for a Dharma center such as 
Lama Tzong Khapa Institute with a good motivation, not minding whether it is hot or cold and so forth, we can purify non-virtuous action s and create merit. All of this depends on our 
motivation.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3B-2 Generating respect for the Teacher and the teachings 
Second: When uttering the Mother of the Buddhas,38 the Teacher39 himself arranged 
a seat and so on. Accordingly, since the Dharma is a field of veneration even for 
buddhas, one should bring to mind the positive qualities and kindness of the 
Dharma and the Teacher, and generate respect for them. 
 
                                              
38 A common epithet of the Perfection of Wisdom Sutras 
39 i.e., the Buddha himself 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
75 We should have respect for the Teacher and the teachings. Even when Buddha Shakyamuni 
himself gave teachings, he began by arranging a pr oper seat and prostrating. He did this out of 
respect for the Dharma. The Mother of the Conquerors  refers to the Perfection of Wisdom 
Sutras, whether the Heart Sutra , or the Perfection of Wisdom  in 8,000, 25,000, or 100,000 
verses. They are called “mother” because, just as a mother gives birth to sons, these sutras, 
which contain explicit teachings on emptine ss and implicit teachings on the mind of 
enlightenment and so forth, are like mothers because they give rise to the Conqueror’s 
children, the bodhisattvas, and then these bodhisattvas give rise to buddhas.  
 Therefore, we too should pay respect to th e Dharma. For example, we should treat even 
Dharma texts with respect. We should not thro w them on the ground, sit on them, or walk 
o n  o r  o v e r  t h e m  a s  t h i s  w o u l d  b e  a  g r e a t  n e gativity. We should generate respect for the 
teachings through remembering their good qual ities, and generate respect for the Teacher 
through remembering the kindness of the Buddh a .  T h e n  w e  s h o u l d  d e v e l o p  t h e  m i n d  o f  
enlightenment or a good heart. In this way our practice of the Dharma will become excellent.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3B-3 What kind of attitude and activities to explain with 
A Attitude 
B Activities 
The third one has two points: attitude and activities.40 
2B3B-3A Attitude 
First, [the Sutra] Requested by Sagaramati  puts forward five discriminations – 
generating the discrimination of oneself as a doctor, of the Dharma as medicine, 
of the one listening to the Dharma as a pa tient, of the Tathagata as a holy being, 
and of the mode of the Dharma as abiding for a long time – as well as the 
development of loving kindness towards surrounding people. The envy [born from] anxiety that others are superior, the laziness of putting  things off, the 
disenchantment of fatigue due to explai ning things over and over again, the 
praising of oneself and chit-chatting abou t other people’s faults, the concealment 
of examples regarding the Dharma,
41 as well as concern for material things such as 
food and clothing, should be abandoned,  thinking, “Those very merits from 
teaching for the sake of my own and othe rs’ enlightenment are the provisions of 
my happiness.” 
 
W h a t  k i n d  o f  a t t i t u d e  d o  w e  n eed when teaching the Dharma? The Sutra Requested by 
Sagaramati  says that we need five discriminations:  
(1) the discrimination of onesel f, the teacher, as a doctor,  
(2) the discrimination of the teachi ngs that one gives as medicine,  
(3) the discrimination of the listeners as patients,  
(4) the discrimination of the Ta thagata as a holy being, and  
(5) the discrimination of the wish for the Buddha's teaching to last for a long time.  
                                              
40 JN: bsam pa dang sbyor ba , also translated as “thought and action” 
41 JN: chos la dpe mkhyud byed pa  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
76 In addition, we should also develop love for th e people around us. We should not be jealous 
or envious of people who are superior to us. We  should not be lazy, th inking to do something 
tomorrow. We should be tireless even when givi ng teachings again and again. We should not 
praise ourselves saying, “I am a good teacher. I am a marvelous person,” and so forth, while 
criticizing others. We should instead reveal ou r faults and praise ot hers’ excellences. We 
should not be like, for example, Berlusconi, th e Italian prime minister, and George W. Bush, 
the American president, who say that that th ey are compassionate men and good Christians! 
Although many people do this , we should not. When someon e says that we did something 
bad, it is not right to immediately get angry. Instead, when others point out our faults, we 
should accept that we have made a mistake,  promise not to do it again, and ask for 
forgiveness. 
 “The concealment of examples ” means that we should not be  miserly with the Dharma, for 
example, wanting to keep some meditation techniques to oursel ves. I have met some painters 
who know special painting techniques but do no t teach them to their disciples. We should 
not do this.  We should also not teach the Dharma out of a wish to gain material things such as food 
and clothing. So what kind of motivation shou ld we have? We should have the motivation 
wishing that oneself and others attain enlightenm ent. In this way, we will accumulate merit, 
which will bring about happiness for ourselves. This is the correct and perfect motivation.   When a doctor examines a patient, he should be motivated by the wish to cure the person 
through recognizing the sickness and prescribin g the right treatment. Likewise, the Buddha 
gave teachings motivated by the wish to eliminat e the afflictions of sentient beings. Therefore, 
these teachings are real medicine. If Buddha  Shakyamuni were to come here, we would 
g e n e r a t e  f a i t h  t h a t  h e  i s  a  h o l y  b e i n g ,  j u s t  as when the Pope visits a place, thousands of 
people, out of faith that he is a holy being, come together to listen to him with respect. 
Similarly, the Buddha is also a holy being, a ta thagata, therefore we should have faith in him. 
“Tathagata” means “thus gone.” Where has he gone? He has gone to a pure land or to 
buddhahood, that is, he has gone beyond cyclic existence. However, his teachings still remain 
in this world, Jambudvipa, the southern continent.   In addition, we should try to develop love  and compassion for the people around us, 
wishing to help them and to gi ve them happiness and the causes of happiness. In this way we 
will create only excellence. We should also not develop jealousy for those who have higher 
qualities than ourselves, like, for example, tw o beautiful girls who, looking at each other, 
become jealous of each other. We do not de velop jealousy toward poor people such as 
beggars, but develop it for those with power and beauty.  W e  s h o u l d  n o t  b e  l a z y ,  t h i n k i n g  “ I  w i l l  d o  i t  t o m o r r o w . ”  W e  s h o u l d  n o t  p r o c r a s t i n a t e .  When we give teachings, we shou ld not think, “I am tired. I will do it tomorrow.” Instead we 
should be tireless. We should also not think, “I  am a great teacher. I am the best meditator,” 
and then criticize others saying that they are not good meditators and so forth. Also, when 
someone asks us to teach something, we should  not say that we do not know this subject 
when in fact we do know it. We should not be  miserly with the Dharma . We should also not 
teach in order to gain food, clothing, and other material things. Nor should we teach to gain 
money. We should abandon teaching with an a fflicted motivation. Rather we should teach, 
thinking to do so in order that we ourselve s and all sentient beings may achieve buddhahood. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
77 If we teach with this motivation , we will create a lot of merit. With this merit, we will gain 
happiness and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3B-3B Activities 
{23} Second: Having washed and dressed in  immaculately clean clothes, you sit on 
a cushion on a Dharma throne in a clean and pleasant place. If you then chant a 
mantra for subjugating demons, demons an d the gods of the demonic category will 
not come closer to you than a perimeter of 100 yojanas42, and even if they do 
come, they will not be able to create  any obstacles, as it says in [the Sutra] Requested 
by Sagaramati.  Therefore, chant that mantra and,  with an extremely radiant facial 
complexion, give your explanations conjoined with the conditions for ascertaining 
their meaning – examples, proofs, and quotations. 
 When giving teachings, previously we should  have washed our body and dressed in clean 
clothes. The place where they will be given should also be clean and pleasant. We should place a Dharma throne there and si t on a cushion. Then the text says that we should recite a 
mantra for subjugating demons, however we usually recite the Heart Sutra  in order to 
eliminate interferences. The Sutra Requested by Sagaramati  says that due to reciting this mantra, 
demons and the gods of the cla ss of demons will not come clos er than 100 yojanas to us, and 
even if they do come near us they will be unable to harm us.    We should teach with a nice expression, a smiling face. We should also teach by setting 
out analogies, by giving reasonings, and by ci ting scriptures that clarify the meaning. An 
example of reasoning is the syllogism: a table is impermanent because it is produced by causes 
and conditions. For example, human beings ar e impermanent. Why? Because they change 
momentarily; their mind changes, their attitu de changes, and their body changes, even 
though we cannot see these changes which occu r in each instant. In this way, we are 
impermanent. Our body is also suffering; for exam ple, if due to suffering from the heat in a 
hot room we go out into the cold, we will expe rience the suffering of cold. This body is the 
nature of suffering. In addition, to giving analogies and reasonings, we can quote the Buddha's teachings or the texts of the Indian pand its. In this way our teachings will be easier 
for others to understand.   There was a famous Kadam lama from Pemb o called “Potowa,” who wrote a text called 
Precious Heap of Jewels of Analogies . In this text he gave a teachi ng and then set out an analogy 
to clarify it. He did this because analogies or examples make it easier for us to understand the meaning. However, at times examples can be an obstacle because we only remember the 
example and not the meaning!  In our everyday life we should develop love for others and remember impermanence. Because 
wealth is impermanent, at times we may be ri ch and other times we may lose everything and 
become extremely poor. Such things happen.  In the West there have been a lot of 
technological and scientific developments due to  which we presently have a high standard of 
                                             
 
42 one dpag tshad  (yojana in Sanskrit) is equivalent to ab out 4.5 miles (7.4 km) or 9 miles (14.8 km)  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
78 life, but everything can change. Things develo p and then degenerate. We were born a small 
baby, then grew into a youth who was healthy and strong both mentally and physically, and 
then things gradually began to worsen due to becoming old. When we are old, we may even 
become unable to sit down on a chair, go to the toilet alone, and so forth. Why is this? It is 
because of impermanence. Things change. If thin gs were permanent, or existed inherently or 
truly, they would not change, they would alw ays remain the same. Space, for example, is 
always empty, it never changes. Why? Becaus e it is permanent. While our world can be 
completely destroyed, empty space will still remain. That which is permanent remains, 
whereas the impermanent arises and disappears. For this reason, we need to meditate on 
death-impermanence. We should remember that everything is changing momentarily, even 
our own mind. For example, maybe we wish to do something, and then in the next instant change our mind. This is because we are impe rmanent and always chan ging. Therefore, we 
need to meditate on impermanence. We also need  to develop love and compassion as well as 
humility.  
 
Wednesday morning, 25 January 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 23): 
2B3B-4 Distinction between who the explanations should and should not be given to  
Fourth: In the Sutra on Discipline  it says: “Do not act without being asked to.” 
Accordingly, do not teach without having been asked, and even when someone 
asks, you should examine the vessel. Howeve r, knowing someone to be a vessel, it 
is acceptable to teach even without ha ving been asked, as it says in the King of 
Concentration [Sutra] . Further modes of conduct are set forth in the Sutra on 
Discipline . 
 
The Sutra on Discipline  (Vinayasutra ) says that one should not give teaching unless one has 
been specifically asked to do so. In other word s, in order for us to give teachings, someone 
should have requested us to do so. If, on the other hand, no one has made such a request, 
one should not teach. Then, when someone reques ts us to teach, we should examine whether 
or not he or she is a proper vessel for the teachi ngs. For example, just as in the past in India 
when someone wanted to enter a monastery an d receive vows, he had to live near the 
monastery for four months and do some work so that his behavior and discipline could be 
examined. Having received the first vows, the vows of a novice monk ( shramanera ), later on 
one can take the vows of full ordination. Simi larly, when someone re quests teachings, we 
should first examine whether he or she is a suit able person to receive these teachings. If we 
find that he or she is a proper vessel, we ca n then give him or her the teachings. However, 
there is an exception to this general rule. If we  already know that the person is an appropriate 
vessel, we can give teachings to him or her ev en if he or she does not ask for them. The Sutra 
on Discipline  explains the correct behavior of a teacher and that of a listener. For example, this 
text says that one should not teach someone who is  eating, for example, a piece of chocolate; 
one should not teach someone who is lying do wn or sleeping; one should not teach while 
oneself is standing and the disciple is lying do wn; and one should not te ach if oneself is lying 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
79 d o w n  a n d  t h e  d i s c i p l e  i s  s t a n d i n g .  T h i s  t e x t  s a y s  m a n y  t h i n g s  w i t h  r e g a r d  t o  p r o p e r  a n d  
improper behaviors.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B3C Generally, how to proceed at the end 
1 What to do afterwards – the actual things to do 
The roots of virtue of teaching and li stening in this way should be sealed43 by 
means of pure aspirational prayers such as the Prayer of Good Conduct . 
 
The roots of virtue created by explaining or li stening to the Dharma should be sealed with a 
prayer such as the Prayer of Good Conduct  (Prayer of Samantabhadra ). In other words, whenever 
we create virtuous actions, we should immediat ely dedicate that they become the cause of all 
sentient beings achieving enlightenment. In this way, our virtuous actions will not be 
exhausted until all sentient beings achieve enli ghtenment. For example, if we were to put a 
single drop of water into the ocea n, that drop of water will rema in as long as the ocean itself 
does not dry up. Likewise, when we practice, stud y, or do recitations, we should immediately 
say a prayer of dedication, whereby our virtuous  actions will not go to waste but will remain 
until enlightenment is achieved. The P r a y e r  o f  G o o d  C o n d u c t  i s  t h e  b e s t  o f  p r a y e r s ,  d u e  t o  
which it is also known as the King of Prayers  (or in Italian as the Queen of Prayers !).  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2 The benefits of this 
There is no doubt that, if th e Dharma is explained and listened to in this manner, 
in just one session the advantages stated  above will arise. Due to the act of 
listening and explaining the Dharma hitting home, all the karmic obscurations 
accumulated previously of not respecting  the Dharma or the one expounding it, 
etc. are purified, and all the ones being newly accumulated are nipped in the bud.44 
 
There was a citation above that said that ther e is benefit from giving even one word of 
teachings and from listening to even one word . It is important that at the beginning of 
explaining or listening that our motivation is good. Even if one gives teachings or listens to 
teachings for just one session, there will be many benefits. It can be of help to other sentient 
beings. Therefore, when giving or  listening to teachings, the esse ntial point is to have a good 
motivation. At the beginning one should develop the mind of love, the mind of 
enlightenment, or a good heart, and then at th e end one should dedicate the virtuous actions 
that one has created. In this way, there will be great benefit. For example, if in other lives we 
did not respect the teacher or the teachings, the Dharma, due to which we created karmic 
obscurations, these can be purified and those that have been newly created will be stopped 
from increasing. In this way, giving or listening to teachings has much benefit.  
 In the past we created karmic obscurations that  need to be purified. For example, why is it 
that some people try to understand the teachi ngs and think a lot about them, but still cannot 
                                              
43 lit.: planted (to) thrive 
44 JN: ‘ phrod ‘chad , literally, “are stopped from increasing” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
80 understand them? Or why is it that some people  who wish to memorize them are unable to do 
so? It is due to their karmic obscurations. Thes e karmic obscurations are the main obstacle to 
developing inner knowledge, Dharma knowledge.  There are many types of obscurations, for 
example, karmic obscurations, fruitional obscurat ions, afflictive obscurations, obscurations to 
knowledge, and so forth. We have many obscur ations, some of which are heavier than others. 
Fruitional or maturation obscurations are, for ex ample, rebirth as a anim al such as a cat or 
dog. One’s body itself is a fruitional result and a fruitional obscuration because it is an 
obscuration that prevents one from understandin g the Dharma as well as  other things. As an 
animal one only knows how to eat, drink, and fight with each other. One remains stupid and 
has neither knowledge or culture, nor a religion.  
 Because we have karmic obscurations, we n eed to purify them. How can we do this? By 
applying the four opponent forces and reciting mantras, doing prostrations, making offerings, 
practicing generosity to animals and poor hu man beings, and so forth. However, the best 
method of purification is meditation on emptin ess, the lack of inhere nt existence. This is 
because the root of all the many  negative actions that we have created is ignorance or the 
conception of a self, and the main antidote to this ignorance is the wisdom realizing 
emptiness. For this reason, meditation on emptin ess is the best of all purification methods. 
However, one can also recite the Confession to the Thirty-Five Buddhas  or the hundred-syllable 
mantra of Vajrasattva, or do  the Samayavajra practice or th e burnt offering ritual of 
Vajradaka, which involves burning black sesame seeds in a fire. All of these practices purify 
our negativities. By doing them, we can become completely pure.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
3 The general mode of behavior of saints 
Due to the manner of listening hitting home, the instructions set forth bring 
benefit in the mind. Seeing this, all the earlier saints in general {24} were diligent 
in this, and especially the former gurus of  this instruction persevered at it with 
utmost diligence. 
 
If we listen correctly and perfec tly when someone gives us instruct ions, this will be of benefit. 
The past holy beings saw this and put them in to practice, therefore we too need to follow 
their example. We should check what they did,  and then emulate them. Like them, we should 
develop a good attitude and good behavior. Th e lamas or gurus of the past, understanding 
that it was very important, were very careful to do this. We too should try to follow the 
example of holy beings such as Buddha Sh akyamuni, Nagarjuna, Asanga, and the other 
Indian pandits, as well as Tibetan lamas such  as Lama Tsongkhapa, the founder of the Gelug 
tradition, and the founders of the other sects. We need to understand how they practiced and 
try to do the same.  
 In our daily life that which is of great benefi t and easy to practice is the lam-rim. Tantra 
may appear to be easy, but at times it can be of great benefit and at other times it can be 
dangerous, therefore we need to take care. For example, when practicing  action tantra (kriya 
tantra), it is important not to eat black food but to eat white food, to keep the body clean, 
and to practice in a clean place. When practi cing tantra, one takes pledges (samayas) that if 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
81 broken can cause one to fall into the hell realms ( naraks ) or the other lower realms. Therefore, 
we need to be careful.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
4 The advice to cherish them, since it is a great fault to turn away from them 
These are evidently great instructions. If one does not reach certainty about this 
and one’s attitude does not change, as is  often the case, no matter how much the 
Dharma, profound and vast, is explained, one will become like a god fallen to [the 
rank of] a demon, and that very Dharma will become an aid to the afflictions. 
Therefore, since it is like they say, “If yo u err on the first of the month, [you will 
do so] until the fifteenth,45” the wise strive at this method of transforming what 
has been heard and explained into the path, and make every opportunity to 
explain and listen into something worthwhi le. For this is the best preparation for 
teaching the instructions. 
 
If we practice in a way that is opposite to the instructions, we will accumulate many faults. 
Therefore, we need to take care of or cheris h the correct and perfect way of practicing. That 
the instructions appear to be great means that wh atever we practice we should try to do it in 
the correct way. We should not do the opposite as this would be dangerous. Therefore, these 
instructions are very great.   If we teach the vast, the six perfections an d so forth, or the profound, emptiness, to 
someone who does not understand the meanin g of the Dharma or whose mind quickly 
changes, first thinking “yes” and then “no,” ther e will not be any benefit, only danger. Here 
the text says that they will be like “a god wh o falls to a demon,” why is this? If we rely on 
mundane gods, they may be helpful for some time  but later on, if they become unhappy with 
us because of some mistake, they  may transform into demons that harm us. There is this risk. 
Our practice of Dharma is the antidote to the mental afflic tions and the antidote to the 
conditions for the generation of the mental a fflictions. While many people listen to the 
teachings and understand what is said, it can happen that their knowledge of Dharma 
becomes a condition for the generation of the afflictions. For example, they may develop 
pride, thinking that they are very knowledgea ble. Or they may teach out of attachment to 
material things or fame. In either case, teaching  becomes a cause for the afflictions to increase 
rather than to lessen. Since there is this danger, we need to be careful. Whatever 
understanding or knowledge we have, we should teach it to others motivated by love, and teach with humility, in a subdued way, withou t pride. This is what is meant here. For 
example, the Dalai Lama always reiterates that h e  i s  n o t  s p e c i a l ,  t h a t  h e  i s  j u s t  a  n o r m a l  
simple monk who is trying to practice bodhichi tta but does not yet have any realization of it. 
Just this shows us something. Hence, whatever knowledge we have, we sh ould humbly share it 
with others without pride. Our knowledge of the Dharma must become an antidote to our 
mental afflictions, it should not become a cond ition for our mental afflictions to grow! This 
would not be the right way to practice.  
                                             
 
45 i.e., full moon day 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
82  The text gives the example that beginning the month by thinking “Today is the first day of 
the month” when it is in fact the second, re sults in this mistake continuing until the full 
moon on the fifteenth. In other words, one begins with a mist ake and continues to think in 
this way until the full moon. Therefore, to avoi d mistakes, we should try to understand how 
to listen to and teach the Dharma. A good moti vation – the mind of enlightenment, love and 
compassion, renunciation, and so forth – are ne cessary for both listening and explaining. If 
we teach or listen with this motivation, our te aching or listening will become a condition for 
our inner spiritual path. In other words, we will come to achieve a path. A path is defined as: 
a clear knower conjoined with uncontrived renunc iation. A path is a road, and if it is good 
road we can travel in any kind of vehicle on it. Similarly, to get to buddhahood we need a 
good path or road. Whether listening or ex plaining we need intelligence and a good 
motivation. They will bring success in and benefit to listening and explaining, and this in turn 
will bring about a good result. Thus, a good mo tivation and good behavior are the necessary 
preparation for giving teachings and listening to teachings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 24): 
Explanation of the stages by which a student of  the actual instructions should be guided 
2B4 The stages by which a student should be  lead through the actual instructions 
A The root of the path: how to rely on a spiritual teacher 
B The stages of how to train the mind once you have relied 
The fourth one has two points: the root of  the path: how to rely on a spiritual 
teacher, and the stages of how to train the mind once you have relied. 
 
This section set out how to guide a disciple in the path. We first need to rely on a spiritual 
friend, a teacher. How should we rely on him, the root of the path? Be cause the generation of 
realizations in our mental continuum depends upon  a teacher, we need to rely on a teacher. 
However, this teacher must be qualified. If he is not qualified, we will not be able to develop 
inner realizations. In fact if we rely on a teac her with inferior qualities, our qualities will 
degenerate. If we rely on a te acher with equal qualities to ourselves, our qualities will not 
develop but will remain the same. If we rely on a teacher with superior qualities, our qualities 
and realizations will increase. Therefore, we need to rely on a virtuous spiritual friend or guru 
who is excellent.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B4A The root of the path: how to rely on a spiritual teacher 
1 The somewhat elaborate explanation for generating certainty 
2 The condensed presentation of how to sustain it 
The first one has two points: the somewhat elaborate explanation for generating 
certainty and the condensed presen tation of how to sustain it. 
 
How should we rely on a guru? Having found a guru, we should train in relying on him. This 
is explained elaborately, and then how to sustain the certainty is briefly explained. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
83 2B4A-1 The somewhat elaborate explanation for generating certainty 
A The need for relying on a spiritual master since all good qualities depend on him 
B The actual way to rely on him 
2B4A-1A The need for relying on a spiritual master since all good qualities depend on him 
First: As all goodness, starting off from th e development of a single positive quality 
and the decrease of a single fault in the mind of the disciple, has its root in the 
sublime friend, the way to rely on him is important in the beginning. 
 
Since all good qualities and knowledge develop in dependence upon relying on a teacher, 
right from the beginning we need a teacher. For example, in order to learn how to write or to 
draw or to paint, we first need a teacher to teach us this. By relying upon him, we come to 
gain such knowledge. Similarly, with respect to  the Dharma it is extr emely important to rely 
on a teacher. Why? Even a single quality that arises in the mind of disciples, those to be 
s u b d u e d ,  d o e s  s o  i n  d e p e n d e n c e  o n  a  t e a c h e r .  I n  s h o r t ,  t o  d e v e l o p  e v e n  o n e  s i n g l e  b i t  o f  
knowledge and to eliminate even one single fault, it is important in the beginning to rely on a 
teacher.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B4A-1B The actual way to rely on him 
1 Characteristics of the one to be relied on, the virtuous spiritual friend 
2 Characteristics of the one who relies, the disciple 
3 The way he relies on him 
4 The benefits of reliance 
5 The drawbacks of not relying 
6 Summary of the meaning 
In this regard there are six [points]: characteristics of the one to be relied on, the 
virtuous spiritual friend; characteristics of  the one who relies, the disciple; the way 
he relies on him; the benefits of reliance; the drawbacks of not relying; {25} and a 
summary of the meaning. 
2B4A-1B1 Characteristics of the one to be relied on, the virtuous spiritual friend 
A Identification of a “guru” 
B His characteristics 
2B4A-1B1A Identification of a “guru” 
First: Although, generally speaking, in terms of the individual vehicles, a lot is said 
[about this] in the [Buddha’s] excellent words along with the commentaries, here 
only a virtuous spiritual teacher who guid es one on the path to buddhahood, the 
Mahayana, by gradually guiding on the path of the three [types of] beings, will be 
presented. 
2B4A-1B1B His characteristics 
1 Highest characteristics 
2 Middling characteristics 
3 Minimum characteristics 
2B4A-1B1B-1 Highest characteristics 
A Good qualities he himself needs 
In the Ornament of the Sutras  it says that the disciple needs to rely on a spiritual 
teacher endowed with ten properties: 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
84 A spiritual teacher with discipline, calm, and complete peace, 
more qualities, with effort, rich in scriptures, who has thoroughly realized suchness, is eloquent, 
loving, and never disheartened – on that one rely! 
 
We need to rely on a qualified guru or virt uous spiritual friend, that is, one who possesses 
certain qualities or characteristics. In genera l in the Buddha’s teachings and commentaries it 
is said that different qualities are necessary for a Hinayana and for a Mahayana teacher. For 
example, ten qualities are set out in the Sutra of Discipline , another ten qualities are set out for 
a Mahayana teacher, and another ten qualities are set out for a tantra teacher. However, in general a teacher needs to possess the good qualit y of knowledge, that is, he should know the 
subject that he is teaching. If someone is calle d a teacher but does not know the subject, he 
will not be able to help or benefit others. Th erefore, it is important that a teacher possess 
good knowledge.  
 In this context, we are talking about the st ages of the path to enlightenment. Among the 
three vehicles, the great, middling, and small, we  are discussing a Mahayana teacher as in this 
context we are talking about the great path , the Mahayana, that wishes to achieve 
buddhahood. In order to follow this  path we need a qualified teacher. Here it says that there 
are three types of teachers, the superior, the mi ddling, and the inferior. A superior teacher is 
the best, a middling teacher is one who possesses half of the ten qualities, and an inferior 
teacher is one who possesses less than five of the qualities but still has a minimum of 
qualities. These will be explained later. 
 The verse cited here from the Ornament of the Sutras  is slightly different from the same verse 
that I memorized in the past. The teacher sh ould be subdued, pacified, and completely 
pacified. In addition, he should have more qualities than the disciple. He should have joyous 
effort in teaching, therefore some one who is lazy and lacking effo rt is not a qualified teacher. 
He should have a wealth of scriptures as an i nner quality. He should have realized suchness, 
or thusness, or emptiness, or the selflessness of persons and phenomena. He should also be 
skilled in teaching, that is, able to talk eloquently, as in this way people will want to listen to 
him. He should also have compassion, and not be teaching in order to gain money and so 
forth. He should also have the quality of being tireless with respect to teaching. He should not 
think, “I am tired today, therefore I cannot te ach.” He should have abandoned all fatigue in 
teaching. If a teacher possesses all ten of these qu alities, he is the best and most qualified of 
teachers.   “Subdued” or “with discipline” means that hi s mind is subdued by morality or ethical 
discipline. “Pacified” or “calm”  means that he has pacified distractions, that is, he has 
achieved calm abiding and his mind is able t o  r e m a i n  s i n g l e - p o i n t e d l y  o n  a n  o b j e c t .  
“Thoroughly pacified” or “great peace” means that he possesses the quality of wisdom. In 
other words, these three mean that he has achi eved the three higher trainings of morality, 
concentration, and wisdom.   Morality includes vows – the vows of individual  liberation, the bodhisattva vows in the case 
of the great vehicle, and the tantra vows in the ca se of the mantra vehicle. We must first try to 
eliminate or abandon external bad behavior and ba d conduct; this is the practice of morality. 
We should become very honest and stop cheating  other people. We need to train the mind in 
this. How to do this is taught in the vinaya.  The Buddha said that vows can be given with 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
85 respect to physical and verbal actions, as they  can be stopped by vows. Actions of the mind, 
on the other hand, cannot be stopped by vo ws or anything else. For example, if one 
constructs a dam in front of flowing water, the flow of water will come to a stop. Similarly, 
morality or vows are a type of form that stop negative actions of body and speech. There are 
many types of form, such as revelatory and non- revelatory form. External forms are colors and 
shapes, odors, sounds, tastes, and tangible object s, which include the elements of earth, water, 
fire, and wind. Inner non-revelatory forms are di fferent from these. They are a special form 
that cannot be seen by the eyes, but can stop us from engaging in non-virtuous actions of 
body and speech. Therefore, vows or morality st op our bad behavior and bad conduct. In this 
way, our mind becomes more subdued. This is the meaning of “subdued.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
 
Furthermore it says that someone who has not disciplined himself is not in a 
position to discipline someone else. A sp iritual master who disciplines others has 
to be someone who has first disciplined his own mind. You may wonder: “Well 
then, in what way must one discipline it?”  Having accomplished whatever occurs to 
you, to label it as a quality of realizatio n due to existing in the mind is of no 
benefit. This is why a system of mental  discipline compatible with the general 
teachings of the Conquerors is necessary. That has been ascertained as the three 
precious trainings, and therefore the three,  ethical discipline and so forth, have 
been set forth. In this regard “discipl ined” refers to the training in ethical 
discipline.  
 If our own mind is not subdued or disciplined bu t instead is as hard as a rock, we will not be 
able to subdue others’ minds. How can we subd ue our mind? If we accomplish many things 
even though they exist in our mind, they are not of much benefit. For example, if someone 
teaches us how to dance, how to  move the body and make the ri ght gestures and steps, some 
quality arises in our continuum. Although there is  some benefit, there is no benefit at all in 
terms of becoming enlightened. As here we  are mainly talking about how to go to 
enlightenment, we need something else. In ge neral if someone teaches us something and we 
learn it, it will be of some benefit, that is, it  will benefit this life but not the next. For this 
reason it is not of much benefit.   What kind of benefit is needed? In accord ance with the general teachings of the Buddha 
we should learn how to become more subdued.  The general teachings consist of the three 
higher trainings in morality, concentration, an d wisdom, which in Sanskrit are respectively 
adishila , adisamatha , and adiprajña . Shila literally means “cool”; morality is said to be 
“coolness” because due to it one experiences fres hness and lightness, rather than the pain or 
suffering of heat. If we are hot and feel a cool breeze, we experience joy. Morality is therefore 
called “coolness” as it is pleasant or refreshing to the mind, in that it does not bring suffering or pain. This is the meaning of morality. We need to understand this.   Wednesday afternoon, 25 January 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 25):  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
86  
In the Individual Liberation46 it says:  
 
Individual liberation is 
a bridle with a hundred sharp nails that accords with the horse of the mind hard to steer that always strays willfully away. 
 Just like someone skilled at breaking in wi ld horses tames them by means of a good 
bridle, someone trained in ethical discipli ne – which subjugates the sense powers 
that pursue wrong objects, like a wild  horse, {26} and the engagement in 
inappropriate activities, and which thro ugh many efforts makes one engage in 
what ought to be done – has tamed the horse of the mind. 
 “Calm” means having developed the trai ning of concentration where, based on 
mindfulness and introspection with regard to engaging in positive and counteracting unwholesome behavior, the mind abides in a state of inner calm. “Complete peace” means that, based on calm abiding of a serviceable mind, the training of wisdom has been developed th rough individual analysis of the correct 
meaning [of reality]. 
 
The Sutra of Individual Liberation , the root text of the vows of the individual liberation, says 
that our mind is as difficult to subdue as a wild horse. Our mind is also often likened in the 
scriptures to a crazed elephant that is difficult to control. Our mind is like this because when 
our eye sense power sees something attractive, thin king that it is good, the mind goes toward 
it; when we hear pleasant music, thinking that it is good, our mind goes toward it; when we 
s m e l l  a  g o o d  f r a g r a n c e ,  t h i n k i n g  t h a t  i t  i s  g o o d ,  o u r  m i n d  g o e s  t o w a r d  i t ;  w h e n  w e  t a s t e  delicious food or drink, such as alcohol, thinking that it is good, our mind goes toward, and so forth. In this way the five sense powers to gether with their respective minds follow their 
objects. For this reason, it is difficult to bring the mind under control. In fact, it is not easy. 
Thus, we need to put effort into stopping ourselves from engaging in bad actions. In other words, we need to apply strong effort to subduing our mind as we need to bring it to the side 
of enlightenment. If we train well in morality, our mind will be subdued. To be subdued  or to 
be disciplined , that is, to possess the higher training in morality, is the first quality of a virtuous 
spiritual friend.   The second quality of a virtuous spiritual friend is to be pacified  (“calm”) that is, his mind is 
pacified due to having eliminated distractio ns and laziness, and having developed single-
pointed concentration on the object of meditati on, that is, having achieved calm abiding. 
When obstacles to meditation arise we have to eliminate them by training in concentration. 
To achieve calm abiding we need many good conditions – an appropriate place, the right 
company, a healthy body, and so forth. If all th ese conditions are present, we will be able to 
meditate single-pointedly, whereby the mind will be pacified, that is, freed from distractions 
and laziness. To achieve this we need the ment al factors of mindfulness and introspection. 
Mindfulness keeps the object of meditation in mind without forgetfulness. When one part of 
                                             
 
46 Sutra on the Vows of Individual Liberation  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
87 the mind remains on the object but another part  is distracted outward, introspection notices 
this and brings the mind back to the object. These are the main two mental factors that are 
needed for the development of concentration or ca lm abiding. A mind that is pacified abides 
on its object without going outside. This is  the achievement of the higher training of 
concentration, – a single-pointed mind free from distraction and laziness.  
 The third quality of a virtuous spiritual friend is to be completely pacified  (“complete peace”). 
At this point one has achieved calm abid ing and one’s body and mind have become 
serviceable. The mind is easily  brought inside. Then, when medi tating on an object, one part 
of the mind is set on it and another part of th e mind examines its qualities. For example, if 
this cup is the object of meditation, one sh ould focus on the cup and with wisdom one 
should examine whether or not the cup is impermanent or permanent, suffering or 
happiness, pure or impure, and self-supporting substantially existent or selfless, that is, 
inherently existent or not. One should investigat e the cup in this way so as to differentiate its 
characteristics. When this wisd om makes the mind blissful, light, and pliant, one has achieved 
the higher training of wisdom.   
 These three higher trainings are important for practice and meditation. All the Buddha's 
teachings can be included in them. Morality ma kes the mind disciplined and eliminates faulty 
actions of body and speech, whereas concen tration and wisdom eliminate the mental 
afflictions. By means of these three combined , we can abandon all objects of abandonment 
and achieve all good qualities. Therefore, the hi gher trainings in morality, concentration, and 
wisdom ( adishila , adisamatha , and adiprajna ) are the main objects to be practiced.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (translated by Philip Quarcoo, ©FPMT 2005): 
 
However, it is not enough merely to have developed, by means of the three 
trainings in this manner, the qualities of realization that act to tame the mind. As 
the quality of scriptural knowledge is also needed, [the text says] “rich in 
scriptures,” meaning that the three collections47 and so forth have been studied 
extensively.  
 
Are the three qualities of the higher trainings su fficient for someone to be a qualified teacher? 
They are not. In addition, a qualified guru shou ld also possess the quality of being rich in the 
scriptures. This means that he should have memo rized at least parts of the vinaya basket, sutra 
basket, and abhidharma basket, and should ha ve a good understanding of the Buddha's 
teachings and their commentaries and be able to  explain them. Thus, the teacher should be 
rich in hearing, meaning that he should have heard many teachings from many teachers. To 
hear one time is not enough, he should have heard many teachings many times. In addition, 
he should have read and studied many texts. This is the quality of scriptural knowledge. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (translated by Philip Quarcoo, ©FPMT 2005): 
 
According to Geshe [Drom] Tonpa: 
 
                                              
47 the three collections or baskets or pitakas  of scripture, i.e., the su tra, vinaya, and abhidharma 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
88 For someone to be called a Mahayana guru, when he explains he must 
generate boundless understanding, and when he practices, he must 
show what is of benefit and direct si gnificance in the last days of the 
teachings. 
 
The virtuous spiritual friend Drom Tonpa, Atis ha’s main disciple, was a lay vow-holder, not 
an ordained monk. Although he had studied very well, he was extremely humble, and is 
considered to be a manifestation of Avalokit eshvara, the Buddha of Compassion. Here Drom 
Tonpa is cited as saying that a Mahayana guru should be able to give explanations in many 
ways, such that by listening to him a student ca n generate a complete understanding. He also 
says that a Mahayana guru should practice wh at he teaches. Nowadays, when the Buddha’s 
teachings are about to vanish, a guru should teach what is of benefit to his listeners, not 
necessarily philosophy. In this way, he will achi eve his aims and be of benefit. In other words, 
as we approach the end of the Buddha's doctrine , a lot of talk is not always useful, so a guru 
should limit himself to teaching what is of bene fit. He should teach people in different ways 
according to their needs, for example, teachi ng some extensively with many citations and 
reasonings, and teaching others in a very simp le and straightforward manner, which in certain 
cases can be of more benefit.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
 
“Having realized suchness” refers to the special training in wisdom – the 
realization of the selflessness of phenomena. Alternatively, it is mainly held to be 
manifest suchness. However, even if this is not present, it is said that this 
[criterion] is also fulfilled by a realization through scriptures and reasonings. 
 The teacher should have realized thusness or su chness, that is, he should have the wisdom 
realizing the selflessness of phenomena. He should have realized in general that all 
phenomena are empty of inherent existence, and should have realized in particular that the 
form aggregate, feeling aggregate, discriminati on aggregate, compositional factors aggregate, 
and consciousness aggregate are empty of inherent  existence. The best is that he possesses a 
direct realization of suchness. Alternatively he should have understood this subject well. An 
understanding of selflessness that is not yet direct is also considered to be a type of realization. 
Thus, if the teacher has not yet directly realized  the selflessness of phenomena, he should at 
least have understood emptiness well by means of scriptures and reasonings so that he is able 
to explain the emptiness of phenomena to the student.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
 
 That being so, even if he possesses script ural knowledge and realizations, it is not 
enough for them to be inferior or equal to those of the disciple, therefore someone 
with “more qualities” is needed. In the Verses
48 it says: 
 
                                              
48 cf. Note 12 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
89 People relying on someone inferior wither, 
those relying on one of their peers stay the same, those relying on leaders achieve excellence. 
Therefore, rely on those ahead of you 
 
endowed with discipline, perfect peace, and the most exalted wisdom; 
if you rely on any such a lord, 
{27} you even end up ahead of him. 
 
That is why Puchungpa said: “As I listen to the accounts
49 of the saints, I look up 
to them” and Tashi said: “I take the old men at Radreng as a model50.” 
Accordingly, we need someone with more qualities as a model to look up to.  
 Those six properties like this are qualitie s that you yourself attain [for your own 
welfare]. 
 It is important that the teacher be superior to the disciple in terms of his qualities. This is 
because if the disciple has superior realizations or superior knowledge to the teacher, although 
the teacher teaches him, his realizations will no t increase and they may even degenerate. Also 
if the teacher and disciple are similar in terms of  their knowledge, the di sciple’s qualities will 
not increase but will remain the same. But if we rely on a teacher with superior qualities, we 
will learn many things and our knowledge and wisdom will increase. Thus, we need such a 
teacher.   I will tell you a story. In Tibet, in the ar ea of Kham, there was a monk with very little 
knowledge who had not studied much philosophy  and mainly performed rituals in people’s 
homes. However, he had a disc iple who was a tulku, a reinca rnate lama, who would prostrate 
to him after which the teacher would tr ansmit the words of a text (give a lung). One day 
someone asked the tulku why he prostrated in fron t of the old monk as if he were his disciple, 
since the tulku was very intelligent whereas hi s teacher did not have much knowledge. The 
tulku responded saying that, even though the old monk did not have much knowledge, by 
going to him and listening to the transmissions his own memory became very stable whereby he would not forget what he had heard, an d his mind became more happy and joyful. 
Therefore, he said that he received a lot of benefit from this old monk who was his teacher. 
The other person thought this was very strange, however, I think that it was due to the tulku’s 
faith in the old monk.   With regard to the importance of faith, a st ory is often recounted of an old Tibetan woman 
whose son was going to India on business. She asked him to bring back  the tooth of a holy 
being so that she could make offerings to it. Th e son went to India but forgot about the tooth 
until he was almost back home. He decided that  he would have to lie to his mother. Nearby 
him, lying on the side of the street, was the corpse of a dog whose body had been half eaten 
by other animals. He took out on e of its teeth, cleaned it, and wrapped it in a piece of cloth. 
Arriving home, he gave the tooth to his mother saying that he had brought her an object of

Buddha Shakyamuni it would not be of any benefit.  
 If we rely on a teacher who is superior to ourselves, we can attain higher realizations, 
subdue and pacify our mind, and attain higher  wisdom. Here the text mentions Puchungwa 
who was a Kadampa geshe from the Pembo area of Tibet. He said that listening to the
