beings, we should aspire to attain their qualit ies and try to emulate them with the wish to 
become a holy being ourselves. 
 Tashi (literally, Four Extremes), another Kada mpa geshe, said that he looked at what the 
old men of Radreng were doing. Radreng is a well -known place in Tibet as the regent of this 
place was poisoned by the Tibetan government . However, because it was he who found the 
incarnation of the XIII Dalai Lama in Amdo, he was actually very kind to the Tibetans. This 
is recounted in several books that make it appe ar that Tibet was not a Buddhist country but a 
barbaric place in which lamas an d monks were killed. However, politics is similar everywhere, 
although perhaps things are a bit better nowadays  due to the existence of democracy in many 
countries.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 27):  
B Positive qualities necessary for the welfare of others 
The remaining ones are qualities for ta king care of others. As they say: 
 
The Capable Ones51 do not wash away evil with water, 
they do not remove the suffering of beings by hand, 
they do not graft their realizations on others; they liberate, teaching the truth of reality’s nature. 
 
As is expressed here, apart from taking care [of others] through teaching them the 
path in an unmistaken manner, nothing can be done such as washing away their 
negativities with water and the like. Amon g the four characteristics regarding this, 
“eloquent” means experienced with regard to  the stages of guidance and skilled in 
getting the meaning across to the minds of  those to be tamed. “Loving” means 
having a pure motivation to teach th e Dharma and teaching motivated by 
compassion without regard for material gain or respect. 
 
The Buddha said that negativities cannot be wa shed away with water since if this could be 
done they would be very easy to eliminate. He  said this because in some Hindu traditions 
                                             
 
51 "capable" as in the "muni" of Shakyamuni 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
91 there is the belief that by bath ing in and drinking the water of  the Ganges River negativities 
and non-virtues can be cleansed. Nor can suffer ings be removed with the hands. Nor can the 
realizations of the Buddha be transferred to ot hers’ continua. The best method to liberate 
sentient beings is to teach them thusness, emptiness, reality, or ultimate truth. This is because 
by meditating on emptiness, they can slowly ab andon the root of cyclic existence – ignorance 
or the conception of phenomena as inherently ex istent or truly existent. This ignorance is the 
root of all afflictions and all acti ons. Therefore, if this root is abandoned, all its branches will 
be eliminated. Therefore, the best solution is to meditate on emptiness or thusness or 
ultimate truth. Among the two truths, the conv entional and the ultimate, by meditating on 
the ultimate truth we can abandon the afflic tions in our mind and attain liberation.  
 Regarding the possibility of removing suffe ring with the hands there is story about 
Devadatta, the Buddha’s cousin, who, having ea ten something bad, was experiencing severe 
pain. He was lying on the bed groaning in ag ony but when the Buddha placed his hand on 
his head the pain was relieved. However, when he opened his eyes and saw that the Buddha 
had his hand on his head, he became angr y and asked the Buddha what he was doing 
touching him! Some people are like this, but th is story shows that just by the touch of the 
Buddha’s hand he was able to eliminate very strong pain. There are other stories to this 
regard but I will not tell them now. The Budd ha had completely abandoned all obstacles and 
all sufferings so no one could harm him, yet stil l there were people like Devadatta who wished 
to harm him.  
 In order to take care of sentient beings the Buddha taught a perfect and unmistaken path 
as washing and so forth cannot help them.   Previously six of the ten qualities were mentione d, therefore four remain. One of these is to 
be skilled in talking, that is, to be eloquent. A teacher should be able to talk very nicely so as to lead others into the path more easily and cause them to easily understand the subject. A 
teacher also needs to have mercy or compassi on (“loving”). If a teacher does not have 
compassion either he will not teach at all or will teach out of a wish to gain something for 
himself such as wealth, material things, or ho nor. Thus, without seekin g material things, and 
motivated by compassion and compassion, a teac her should teach others so that they can 
change their minds, gain wisdom, and develop love.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (translated by Philip Quarcoo, ©FPMT 2005): 
 
 We need someone like Potowa [who] said to Chenawa: “Son of Lima, however 
many explanations of the Dharma I have given, not once have I claimed the 
applause as my own. There is no migratin g being who is not pitiable.” “Energetic” 
means constantly delighting in the welf are of others. “Never disheartened” means 
not to become weary due to explaining thin gs over and over again, but rather to 
bear the hardships of explaining. 
 Geshe Potowa was a well-known la ma who wrote many texts on mind training. He spoke to 
Chenawa Lodro Gyaltsen calling him “Son of Li ma,” Lima perhaps being his family name. He 
gave teachings on mind training because sentient beings are pitiable or miserable, in order to 
help them subdue their minds.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
92  If with energy or perseverance a teacher takes care of other sentient beings, his mind will 
become happy and stable in enacting the welf are of others. Therefore, joyous effort in 
necessary for a teacher. He should also have abandoned weariness or tiredness when teaching 
others, instead he should be tireless. This mean s that even when he needs to teach the same 
thing again and again due to many people reques ting him to do so, he should not think, “I 
cannot do this now because I am tired.” Rather  a teacher needs to be tireless in giving 
teachings. This is an important quality. We t oo should bear the difficulties or hardships of 
teaching others.  
 Therefore, the best of teachers  possesses ten qualities. In the Ornament for the Sutras  
Maitreya says that one should rely on an exce llent teacher who possesses the ten qualities.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B4A-1B1B-2 Middling characteristics 
Potowa said: 
 
The three trainings, the realization of suchness, and a loving heart; 
these five are the main ones. My master Shangtsun is not very {28} learned in all and since he cannot be ar disenchantment either, he does 
not impress on his mind the kindness he received; however, as he has 
the above[-mentioned] five, whoever is in his presence benefits. Nyanton is by no means eloquent an d each time he makes a dedication 
the only thing he knows is that, ag ain, nobody understood anything, 
but as he has those five, those who are near him benefit. 
 If a teacher does not have all ten qualities it is sufficient for him to have five of them. Potowa said that the main ones are to possess (1-3 ) the three higher trainings in morality, 
concentration, and wisdom, (4) a realization or understanding of emptiness or the selflessness 
of phenomena, and (5) compassion for his disciple s. Therefore, if a teacher has these five, he 
is a qualified teacher having  middling characteristics.  
 Potowa’s words in Tibetan are difficult to understand. Potowa said that his master Shangtsun had not studied very much, was unable  to bear tiredness, and did not repay debts 
of kindness (i.e., he was not grateful). In othe r words, although he ha d trained in the three 
trainings, had some realization of suchness, and possessed compassion, he was not learned in 
the scriptures, was not able to bear difficulties,  and was not tireless in teaching. Thus, he did 
not possess all ten qualities, but because he po ssessed the five main ones those who listened 
to his teachings benefited. In addition, even wi thout teaching, he was of benefit to others due 
to the blessings.   
There was an Indian monk named Legpek arma who stayed together with Buddha 
Shakyamuni for about twenty years, yet still said that he had not seen any qualities, not even 
one, in the Buddha. He had memori zed and could recite the entire 100,000 Verse Perfection of 
Wisdom Sutra  but said that the Buddha was a trickste r who, when someone made offerings to 
him, would lie, predicting that in such-and-such a life this pers on would generate the mind of 
enlightenment and become enlightened. When Lekpekarma died he was reborn in the hell 
realm where he remembered criticizing his teache r, Gautama, and understood that this karma 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
93 had now ripened due to which he was experien cing suffering day and night for eons. This 
came to his mind but it was too late for him to do anything about it.  
 
Thursday morning, 26 January 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 28): 
2B4A-1B1B-3 Minimum characteristics 
A The actual minimum of characteristics 
Even if, due to the times, it is difficult to find someone who has a complete set of 
such characteristics, it is said that you should not rely  on anyone whose faults are 
greater or whose faults and positive qu alities are equal, but on someone whose 
positive qualities exceed his faults. 
 
This section sets out the minimum characteristic s of an inferior teacher who does not even 
have half of the ten char acteristics. Since due to this degene rated time it can be difficult to 
find a teacher who has all the necessary characteri stics, we should rely on someone who at the 
very least has more good qualities than faults . Thus, we should not rely on a teacher whose 
faults are greater than or equal to his good qualit ies. This is because if we were to rely on a 
teacher with more faults than qualities there would be no benefit and due to his influence we 
might even acquire the same faults ourselves. Likewise, if his faults and qualities are equal 
there will not be much benefit from relying on him. Thus, in the context of a Dharma teacher 
we will learn much more if we study with and rely upon a qualified teacher.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
B The expediency of association with the characteristics 
Such a spiritual master who brings about co mplete liberation is a perpetual root of 
aspiration. Therefore, those who wish to rely on a spiritual teacher should be 
aware of and make an effort to look fo r someone who possesses the characteristics. 
Those wishing to have disciples rely on th em should also strive to acquire these 
characteristics. 
 
One who wishes to achieve libera tion should rely on a lama or  spiritual teacher who is the 
root of aspiration. Therefore, we should seek a qualified teacher possessing as many of the ten 
characteristics as possible. On the other hand, if  someone wishes to benefit others by teaching 
them, he or she should either already possess the characteristics or else should put effort into 
attaining and completing them. This is importan t because then when one teaches, one will be 
of benefit to one’s disciples. In short, the di sciples should seek a qualified teacher and the 
teacher should try to develop th e necessary characteristics.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B4A-1B2 Characteristics of the one who relies, the disciple 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
94 A Faults of someone who does not have and benefits of someone who does have the five 
characteristics 
Second: From the Four Hundred52:  
 
One who is impartial, intelligent, and endeavors; 
thus is explained a vessel for teachings. Otherwise qualities of the teacher have no effect; in the listener, there will be no change. 
 As it says in the commentary: someone en dowed with the three characteristics is a 
suitable vessel for listening. If all three are complete, the positive qualities of the 
one expounding the Dharma appear as po sitive qualities and do not appear as 
faults. Not only that, the positive qualities of the listeners also appear as positive 
qualities to that person and do not appear as  faults. If the characteristics of a vessel 
are not complete, the listener, under the power of his faults, will classify even a perfectly pure and great spiritual teacher who expounds the Dharma among those who have faults, and perceive an exponent’s faults as qualities. 
 Here the qualities or characteristics that make one a suitable disciple are set out. We should 
examine whether or not we possess the three charac teristics. A suitable disciple is one who is 
impartial, intelligent, and has a sincere intere st in the teachings. We ourselves possess such 
qualities in that: (1) we are honest or impartial or unbiased;  (2) we are intelligent and possess wisdom and can therefore understa n d  w h a t  i s  t o  b e  
discarded or abandoned and what is to be adopted or practiced; and  (3) we possess the interest or aspiration or en deavor to achieve good qualities and wish to 
attain liberation or buddhahood for the benefit of all sentient beings.  These are the three main charac teristics that a disciple shou ld possess. If we possess these 
three qualities, when we listen to a teacher we wi ll not look at his or her faults but will look at 
his or her qualities. On the other hand, if we  do not possess these qualities we will wonder 
whether or not he is a good teacher and whet her or not the teachings are correct. We may 
come to think that he is not a skillful teacher, that he is not kind, and so forth. Such doubts 
will not bring us any benefit. Instead, it is better for a listener to see the one who is teaching as possessing qualities, whereby one will not see many faults.  The text says that if we are not impartial, ev en though from the teacher’s side he is pure, 
since from our own side our mind is not pure, there will not be any benefit. Instead we will engage in even more faulty behavior. On the other hand, if we see the teacher as possessing 
faults but think of the faults as qualities there wi ll be benefit. It should be noted that from the 
side of the teacher there is no benefit, it is fr om the disciple’s side that there will be benefit. 
Contrarily, if we think that the teacher is faul ty, then when we listen to Dharma teachings 
from him or her there will be no benefit. In other words, the teachings will not help our mind 
since we are continually looking at  the faults of the teacher.  
                                             
 
52 Aryadeva’s Four Hundred Verses  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
95  The Compendium of Valid Cognition  also sets out the qualities and faults of a disciple, saying 
that one who possesses the faults is not qualifie d to be a disciple. For example, those who are 
attached to the mundane activities of daily life, as well as to wealth, and consequently always 
act in order to gain something, even going so far as to cheat others, are not suitable to be 
disciples. Also those who are always jealous or  envious of others’ qualities, wealth, and so 
forth are not suitable to be disciples because they  will not reap benefit from the teachings. In 
fact jealousy is included among the twenty se condary mental afflictions. Also those who are 
not intelligent and cannot understand what th ey are taught or what they study are not 
suitable to be disciples. With respect to this  the Tibetans have the expression: “Whether one 
puts a piece of gold or a stone in the ear of a donkey, there will be no difference!” While gold 
is generally considered good, for a stupid donk ey it makes no difference. Similarly, when 
someone is unintelligent, whether one teaches him or her the Dharma or merely talks gossip 
there will be no difference.  
 So who is the discip le described in the Compendium of Valid Cognition ? He or she should be 
one who does not cling to mundane activities, who is not jealous and is compassionate, and 
who possesses the intelligence that can understa n d  w h a t e v e r  h e  o r  s h e  i s  t a u g h t  j u s t  b y  
mentioning a little bit. Thus, this description is slightly different from that given here in the 
Middle Lam-Rim .  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
B Explaining the specifics of the [various] characteristics in detail 
{29} In this regard “impartial” means bein g without bias. If you have bias, it will 
make you obscured and you will not perceive  positive qualities, due to which the 
meaning of excellent explanations will not be found. It is just as it says in the 
Essence of the Middle Way :  
 
With a biased – thus anguished – mind,53  
peace can never be realized. 
 
To be biased means to be attached to one’s own approach and to hate others’ approach. Having examined for something like this in your own continuum, you 
should give it up. Is that by itself sufficient? Even though impartial, someone to whom good ways of explaining appear as faulty ones due to not having the 
intelligence to distinguish the two, is not a suitable vessel. Therefore, one needs have the intelligence that understands those two.  
 Are those two sufficient? Even if one has the two, [impartiality and intelligence ], 
someone who is just like the listener to th e Dharma in a [lifeless] picture is not a 
suitable vessel. Therefore, one [also] needs to have great endeavor. The 
commentary states that by adding the two – respect for the Dharma and the 
expounder of Dharma and an attentive mind, five [characteristics] are set forth. 
 
                                             
 
53 to be more precise: "with a mind in anguish due to bias" 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
96 To be biased ( phyogs lhung , literally, fallen to one side) means to be attached to one’s own side, 
thinking, for example, “ my friend,” and to have hatred for the other side. One makes a 
division between those on one’s own side and th ose on the other side, being attached to and 
thinking to take care of those on one’s side, and neglecting or abandoning those on the other 
side. This is an obstacle to receiving Dharma te achings and it is not a good quality because it 
obscures one from seeing others’ qualities and ca uses one to see only their faults. Due to this, 
even if someone gives an excellent explanation, due to being obscured by one’s bias one will 
not find anything good in it. The Essence of the Middle Way  by Acharya Bhavaviveka says that 
due to being biased, the mind will be tormen ted by suffering, whereby one will never realize 
peace, implying that instead one will be consta ntly disturbed and suffer. Therefore, we need 
to abandon this kind of attitude.  
 We should examine our own continuum, checking whether or not we have this bias that is 
attached to the people on our si de and hates those on the other si de. If we find that we have 
this attitude, we must give it up as otherwise we will not be able to gain any good qualities. 
However, is it enough to be impartial? It is not enough. What else do we need? If we are not intelligent, when someone explains a correct an d perfect path while so meone else engages in 
faulty speech that explains a wrong or imperf ect path, we will not be able to distinguish 
between the two paths. Therefore, only th ose who can distinguish these due to being 
intelligent are suitable to be disciples. Th ose who, on the other hand, are not able to 
distinguish them are not. In short, we need the intelligence that is able to distinguish qualities 
and faults.   If someone is impartial and inte lligent is that enough to be a disciple? It is not. One should 
also have the interest or aspiration or be en deavoring to attain inner qualities and for this 
reason listen to and study the Dharma. Someone wh o is a like a listener in a drawing is not a 
suitable disciple; one should wish to attain the path, develop inner qualities, and attain 
enlightenment.   Is it enough to possess these three qualities?  Here it is not specified what commentary is 
referred to, but perhaps it is the commentary on the Essence of the Middle Way . This text says 
that in addition the disciple should have re spect for the Dharma an d the one teaching the 
Dharma, and should listen to th e teachings with single-pointed attention. With the addition 
of these two qualities, there are five characterist ics that a disciple should have: (1) impartiality, 
(2) intelligence, (3) interest or endeavor, (4) respect for the Dharma an d the teacher, and (5) 
an attentive mind while listening to, studying, and meditating on the holy Dharma. A person 
who has these five characteristics is a qualifie d disciple. We should examine whether or not 
we also have them. If we have them, we can think of ourselves as  a good disciple. If we do not 
have all of them, for example, if we are biased or partial, we will not be a good disciple. I’m 
not joking.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
C Specifics of achieving the conducive conditions an d eliminating the adverse conditions to having the 
four characteristics 
That being so, there are four: (1) great ende avor with regard to the Dharma, (2) an 
excellently attentive mind while listening,  (3) great respect fo r the Dharma and the 
expounder of Dharma, and (4) holding to g ood explanations having rejected faulty 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
97 ones. Intelligence is a conducive circumst ance for those four, and impartiality is 
what eliminates adverse circumstances. 
 
There are four characteristics: (1) having great interest in or endeavoring in the Dharma, (2) 
listening attentively to the Dharma, (3) having respect for the holy Dharma and the teacher, 
and (4) having abandoned faulty explanatio ns and holding to excellent explanations. 
Intelligence is a conducive condition for th e previous four. Impartiality eliminates 
unfavorable conditions. A disciple needs these fo ur conducive conditions in order to receive 
t e a c h i n g s .  I n  a d d i t i o n ,  i t  i s  n e c e s s a r y  t o  b e  intelligent, and to be impartial in order to 
abandon adverse conditions. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
D The need to strive for those characteristics 
You should examine whether all the attribut es that would make you suitable to be 
lead by a spiritual teacher are complete or not, and cultivate joy if they are 
complete. If they are not complete, you should strive henceforth in future [lives] in 
the causes for making them complete. 
 
In order to be led to enlightenment, all four  or five characteristics should be complete. 
Therefore, we should examine whether or not we have all of them. If th ese characteristics are 
complete, we should rejoice, thinking “I am very lucky. I am really very lucky.” If we find that they are not complete, we should try to make them complete in our future lives. In other 
words, if we cannot develop the four characteristics in this life, we need to create the causes to 
d e v e l o p  t h e m  i n  f u t u r e  l i v e s .  T h u s ,  w e  n e e d  t o  put effort into attaining a good life in the 
future with all the necessary conditions, and for this we need to create the causes. What are 
the causes? They are virtue, that is, we n eed virtuous projecting karma and virtuous 
actualizing karma. If, on the other hand, our pr ojecting karma is non-virtuous it will project 
or throw us into a bad rebirth. If both our pr ojecting and actualizing karmas are good, it is 
100% certain that we will take rebirth in the happy migrations, for example, as a human 
being and have all conducive circ umstances. In that life we will once again strive to achieve 
the four or five characteristics and therefore will be an excellent vessel for receiving the 
teachings of the Buddha. In this way, we will become better and better until we achieve 
buddhahood. No one can stop this from happenin g. If we ourselves practice, we can achieve 
this state. No one else can do this for us.   We can examine how people become successful politicians or wealthy businessmen. However, even if we study these methods well, it is not guaranteed that we will become, for 
example, a politician with a hi gher position, as our success ca n be blocked by other people. 
This is what happens from the side of the world. However, from the side of Dharma if we 
practice well, no one  can throw us into the hell realm, hungry ghost realm, or animal realm. 
We will only go up, that is, we will definitely a ttain a good rebirth. Therefore, we should try at 
all times to be honest or impart ial, to have perfect behavior, to develop our intelligence, and 
to endeavor to increase our inner qualities, whereby our mind will gradually become more 
and more happy.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
98  The Tibetan nomads, such as  my family, commonly say, “I f one does not make oneself 
happy, others will only make one suffer.” In other words, in order to be happy, we need to 
make our own mind happy. Others cannot make us happy, they can only make problems for 
us! And even if they do not make problems, we  will think that they are making problems. 
Therefore, from our own side we need to take  care of ourselves. This is important.  
 
If someone has financial problems and asks us for help or asks to borrow money from us, out 
of compassion, love, and generosity we can give or lend that person some money. We can give 
others material things. Some people do this to help others. Even if someone wants an eyeball, 
w e  c a n  g i v e  i t  t o  h i m  o r  h e r  j u s t  a s  A r y a d e v a  g a v e  o n e  o f  h i s  e y e s  t o  a  b l i n d  m a n .  I t  i s  recounted that the monks at Nalanda Monastery in India performed a puja during which they 
offered a torma to Mahakala. This torma contai ned an invitation to Aryadeva asking him to 
come to Nalanda Monastery to debate a non-Bu ddhist scholar who could not be defeated by 
any of the Buddhist scholars. The torma was put outside and a crow, a manifestation of 
Mahakala, came and ate the lette r contained in the torma. It flew to where Aryadeva was 
staying in a cave and gave him the letter. Aryadeva read the letter and asked his teacher Nagarjuna what to do. Nagarjuna told him that they should practice debating together in 
preparation for debating the non-Buddhist. Du ring their debate Nagarjuna held the Samkya 
point of view so well that Aryadeva forgot that  he was only pretending to be a Samkya. When 
Aryadeva finally defeated Nagarjuna, he picked up his shoes and circled them around Nagarjuna’s head to emphasize his victory. Naga rjuna told him that he would win the debate 
but that on his way to Nalanda he would expe rience some interference that could not be 
avoided because of Aryadeva’s inauspicious gest ure of circling his teacher’s head with his 
shoes! Because he had created this negative ka rma, he would have to experience its result. 
And so it happened that when Aryadeva was trav eling to Nalanda, he came across an old and 
blind Indian saddhu leaning on a staff. The old man asked Aryadeva for one of his eyes, 
whereupon Aryadeva immediately took one of his eyes out and gave it to him. Thus, minus 
an eye, Aryadeva arrived at Nalanda. When Aryadeva arrived at Nalanda, the gong was 
ringing and the monks were going into the temple. At the door Ashvagosha, the non-
Buddhist, waited with a stick that he used to tap each monk on the he ad in recognition as 
they entered. When Aryadeva arri ved at the door, he realized that another monk had arrived 
and said, “Where has this previously non-existe nt bald head come from?” Aryadeva replied, 
“It has come from the neck.” The non-Buddhist, left speechless, was unable to reply. Then, 
while standing on the doorsill, he said to Aryadeva, “Am I going inside or outside?” Aryadeva replied, “It depends on your motivation.” Again he was left speechless. Then, in front of the 
king, the ministers, the pandits, and so forth,  they debated on philosophical points. When 
Aryadeva asked him questions Ashvagosha was un able to reply, but when he asked Aryadeva 
questions Aryadeva immediately replied. Prior to the debate they had made an agreement that 
if Aryadeva won, Ashvagosha would become a Buddhist, whereas if  Ashvagosha won, 
Aryadeva would become a non-Buddhist. However, when Ashvagosha saw that he was sure to 
lose, he suddenly flew up into the sky, his long hair flowing behind him. Aryadeva followed behind him and when they had reached a certain height told Ashvagosha that he should not 
go any further as otherwise he would be cut to pieces. He advised Ashvagosha that, if he did 
not believe him, to throw his long hair up ab ove him. Ashvagosha did this and immediately 
his hair was cut off. Thus, they returned to  the ground where the Nalanda monks locked 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
99 Ashvagosha in the library. He began to read th e many texts contained in the library and came 
across one in which he read the Buddha’s prop hesy regarding his debate with Aryadeva. 
Seeing this, his mind changed and he develope d faith in the Buddhist teachings. He later 
became a well-known Buddhist pandit. While he wrote many texts, in order to purify his 
having criticized the Buddha he wrote the Life Stories  (Jatakamala), which recounts the 
previous lives of the Buddha. 
 Thursday afternoon, 26 January 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 29):  
2B4A-1B3 The way he relies on him 
A Explanation of the need to rely on someone with the characteristics 
B The actual manner of relying on someone who has the characteristics 
2B4A-1B3A Explanation of the need to rely on someone with the characteristics 
{30} Third: Thus someone endowed with [the characteristics of] a vessel should 
examine in the way explained above wh ether or not the guru possesses the 
characteristics, and if so, obtain th e kindness of the Dharma from him. 
 
If we possess the necessary characteristics for a disciple, we should examine whether or not 
the lama or guru has the characteristics needed to  act as a teacher. If we find that he has the 
necessary qualities or characteristics, we should  request him to kindly give us teachings or 
instructions. Thus, a qualified disciple who ha s found a qualified teacher should request him 
to bestow Dharma teaching s. If we do this, the lama will most likely accept our request! If he 
does not accept, saying that he is not qualified to teach, we may have to ask him several times 
u n t i l  h e  a c c e p t s .  H o w e v e r ,  o t h e r s  w i l l  a c c e p t  r i g h t  a w a y !  T h i s  i s  b e c a u s e  t e a c h e r s  h a v e  different kinds of personalities.  
Middle Lam-Rim:  
 2B4A-1B3B The actual manner of relying on someone who has the characteristics 
With regard to the manner of relying on a spiritual teacher from whom in general 
you obtain the kindness [of the Dharma ] and who, in particular, guides your mind 
well by means of perfectly complete instructions, there are two points: the manner 
of relying in thought and the ma nner of relying through actions. 
 
1 The manner of relying in thought 
2 The manner of relying through actions 
 
Having found a qualified teacher possessing  either the previously mentioned ten 
characteristics, or five of the ten characterist ics, or a minimum of tw o characteristics, we 
should rely on him. The best teacher is one wh o has all ten characteristics, a middling teacher 
is one who has five, and the lowest teacher is one who has two. Whicheve r kind of teacher we 
have found, we should rely on him or her. There is a difference between a teacher who gives 
us teachings in general and personal advice in pa rticular. If whatever instructions he gives us 
are for the purpose of guiding us to enlightenmen t, we can rely on him. How should we rely 
on him? We should rely on him in thou ght and rely on him through actions.

Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
100  
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B4A-1B3B-1 The manner of relying in thought 
A The root, training in confidence 
B Through remembering his kindness, developing respect 
The first has two points: the root, tr aining in confidence, and through 
remembering his kindness, developing respect. 
 
In terms of relying in thought we  need to train in developing the faith or confidence that this 
l a m a  c a n  h e l p  u s  b y  g i v i n g  u s  i n s t r u c t i o n s  o r  t e a c h i n g s  a n d  c a n  l e a d  u s  i n  a  c o r r e c t  p a t h .  
Having kindly acted to help us, we should re member his kindness and in this way generate 
respect for him. Therefore, the fi rst point is to generate faith in  the teacher and the second is 
to remember his kindness and thereby generate respect for him. We ca n show respect with 
our body and speech; for example, we should  be polite and humble when requesting 
teachings and not say “Give me teachi ngs!” This would not be right.  
 There was a classmate of mine who initially did not study but was rather a wild monk who 
was part of the group of monks referred to as dob-dobs. Such monks had long hair, put black 
coal around their eyes, and walked around very  arrogantly. One day he was told to study and 
threatened that, if he did not, his hair would be  cut off. Due to this he developed a respectful 
aspect and came to my teacher, Geshe Tashi Bu m, and said “Gen-la, teach me!” He did not 
come to teachings with a text but with paper, a pen made of bamboo, and a pot of ink, which 
he gave to Geshe Tashi Bum to write down some  teachings, as he himself could not write. 
Geshe Tashi Bum would write a few words, and every day the monk tried to read and study them. When most monks ask a lama for teaching s they make three prostrations and offer him 
a mandala, but this monk basically said, “Hey, teach me!” I remember him well. From then onward, he studied well and became a good debater, surprising the other monks. However, although the bad can become good, the good can once again become bad. This monk 
remained in Tibetan and, following the orders of the Chinese, beat other monks. He later 
disrobed, married, and had three or four children, forgetting all that he studied. Now he is old, almost eighty, and is very sad that he di d not escape with me to India and that he is 
unable to take good care of his children. Th ere are many people who disrobe but later on 
want to take ordination again, why is this? It  is due to following attachment. We need to 
understand that this fault is inside  us and strive to eliminate it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2B4A-1B3B-1A The root, training in confidence 
1 Explanation of confidence as the basis of all qualities 
First: In the Jewel Tala Dharani  it says: 
 
The preparatory practice of confiden ce, like a mother, gives birth to 
all positive qualities, protects  them, and makes them increase. 
 
This says that confidence gives birth to po sitive qualities that have not yet arisen, 
and once they have arisen it sustains and increases them.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
101 First we need the quality of faith or confidence ( dad pa ). Just as the ground is the basis in 
which all plants can grow, similarly faith is th e basis on which all inner qualities can grow. 
Faith is also likened to a mother who gives birth to a child in the sense that faith gives birth to 
all good qualities. Faith also does not allow th e good qualities we already have to degenerate 
but maintains or protects them, and faith also increases the good qualities that we already 
possess. Faith means, for example, faith in Bu ddha Shakyamuni, our teac her. With faith in 
him, we should try to follow his instructions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (translated by Philip Quarcoo, ©FPMT 2005): 
 
In the Ten Dharmas
54 it also says: 
 
That which guides to liberation,55 
the foremost yana is confidence. 
Therefore, the intelligent, rely on pursuing confidence.  In those who are without confidence 
wholesome qualities do not arise. 
Likewise, in seeds that were burnt by fire, no green sprout can germinate. 
 
In terms of forward and reverse pervasion,
56 c o n f i d e n c e  i s  s a i d  t o  b e  t h e  
foundation of all positive qualities. 
 
If we have faith, our good qualities will grow. I f  w e  d o  n o t  h a v e  f a i t h ,  t h e y  w i l l  n o t .  “ T h e  
foremost yana” refers to the great vehicle, the Mahayana. A “vehicle” is that which can carry a 
load; the great vehicle is that  which can carry us to libera tion and enlightenment. Human 
beings who are intelligent should rely on faith. Wh ite qualities, that is, vi rtues, do not grow or 
arise in people who do not have faith in the teacher and the Dharma. Virtuous qualities are 
called “white” because they bring a white or posi tive result. If a seed, for example, has been 
burnt by fire, even if it is planted in good soil it will not produce a sprout. Similarly, if we do 
not have faith, our continuum will be as if burned in the sense that qualities will not be 
produced in our mind. Although our intelligen ce can increase without faith, our Dharma 
qualities cannot.   I n  s h o r t ,  i f  w e  h a v e  f a i t h  o r  c o n f i d e n c e  in the Buddha’s teachings, white or virtuous 
qualities will be develop within us. Without fa ith, they cannot grow, only the opposite will 
happen. Because of this, faith is the basis of all good qualities. In this context “good qualities” 
refers to inner qualities – the path, the mind  of enlightenment, compassion, renunciation, 
and so forth. Since the basis of such qualities is  faith, we need faith. If we rely on someone 
with complete confidence, that person will th ink that he needs to help us. On the other 
                                             
 
54 Sutra of the Ten Dharmas  
55 JN: nges ‘byung , often translated as “definite emergence” or “renunciation” 
56 JN: rjes su ‘gro ldog

102 hand, if we ask someone for help but do not have confidence in that person, when he or she 
gives us advice we will not follow it. Then when  we once again ask that person for help in 
solving our problems, he will no t likely accept because he understands that we do not trust 
him. We can verify and understand this based on  our experiences in daily life. This is also 
true in terms of achieving enlightenment as th ere are causes and conditions that bring this 
about. Hence, we need to strive to develo p our good qualities and abandon our faults.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim:  
2 Identification of confidence 
Generally speaking there are many types of confidence: in the Jewels, actions and 
effects, and the four noble truths, however, here it is confidence in the spiritual master. 
 
What kind of faith or confidence is to be iden tified or understood in this context? There are 
different kinds of faith: faith or confidence in the Three Jewels, in the law of cause and effect, 
in the four noble truths, and so forth. However,  in this context faith refers to faith in the 
lama, the spiritual master. However, if we also lack faith in the existe nce of Three Jewels, the 
Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, or in the law of ca use and effect, or in the four noble truths, 
our inner qualities will not grow and our roots of vi rtue will be cut. We need to have faith in 
the Buddha, our teacher. In addition, we need faith in the Dharma, this mainly being true 
paths and true cessations. True cessations are ce ssations of afflictions and suffering that come 
about through meditating true paths. We also  need faith in the Sangha, those who have 
meditated true paths and actualized true cessati ons. A single person, whether man or woman, 
who has realized emptiness directly is an actu al Sangha Jewel. The conventional sangha, on 
the other hand, is a group of four fully-ordained monks or nuns.   The four noble truths also exist. In our da ily life we experience many sufferings. These 
sufferings come from afflictions and action s. These actions were created by our own 
continuum in previous lives. They come from  afflictions. However, some people do not 
accept this and think that karma, or actions and results, does not ex ist. They think that 
everything has been created by God. However, a God who creates suffering and problems could not exist, and those who teach this and th ose who follow this idea do so because they 
have not examined this well. According to Buddh ism, we, that is, our non-virtuous actions of 
body, speech, and mind, create our sufferings. Th erefore, we need to try to understand what 
is good and what is bad. For example, our body, which is contaminated matter made of flesh and bones, is a true suffering. It is caused by  actions, and the main cause of actions is the 
afflictions. We should try to understand that th ese four noble truths exist as in this way we 
will develop more faith in them.   We also need faith in the la ma, the guru. The Sanskrit word  “guru” is composed of two 
syllables, which when broken up are gun and rup. Gun means “qualities,” whereas rup means 
“heavy.” Therefore, a guru is “one who is heavy in qualities.”  In short, we should develop faith in the la ma, the Three Jewels, actions and results, and 
the four noble truths. We s hould also have fa ith in our father and mother as they too are 
objects of veneration.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
103 3 The need to discriminate a buddha with regard to the spiritual master 
Furthermore, as for how to see the spiritual master, it says in the Vajrapani 
Empowerment Tantra : 
 
Lord of Secrets, how should a disciple see the master? He should see 
him just like one would see the Buddha Bhagavan. 
 
{31} Similar statements can be found in th e collection of Mahayana sutras and in 
the vinaya. Their meaning is this. If you un derstand that someone is like a buddha, 
a mind conceiving faults in him will not arise, but rather a mind thinking about 
his positive qualities will arise. Likewise , with regard to a spiritual master, you 
should purposely abandon any concepts of fa ults in every respect while training in 
the mind conceiving positive qualities. 
 
Why do we need to discriminate the lama as a buddha? It is impo rtant as it will bring us many 
good qualities. Why is this? In the Vajrapani Empowerment Tantra , which sets out the 
empowerment of Vajrapani, the Buddha asks V ajrapani, “How should a disciple see the 
master?” Vajrapani replies, “One should see hi m in the same way that one sees the Buddha 
Bhagavan.”  
 The word “buddha” is sang gye  (sangs rgyas ) in Tibetan, whereas “bhagavan” is chom den de  
(bcom ldan ‘das ) Chom  means “destroyed” and indicates that  he has destroyed the four maras. 
Den means “to possess” and indicates that he po ssesses all the good qualities of body, speech, 
and mind. In Tibetan the syllable de is added to chom den  to make chom den de  because the 
term chom den  or bhagavan is also used by Hindus to refer to mundane gods such as Brahma, 
Vishnu, Ram, Mahadeva, and so forth. In fact, even Rajneesh or Osho was referred to by his 
disciples as Bhagavan Rajneesh,  perhaps because he sometimes claimed to be a buddha! The 
additional syllable de means “gone beyond” or “transcendent.”  
 It is also mentioned in the Mahayana sutras and in the vinaya that we should see the guru 
as a buddha. This is because if we think that  the teacher is a buddha, meaning one who has 
purified all faults and possesses all qualities, ther e will not arise a mind conceiving of faults in 
him. In other words, if it is clear to one’s mind that a buddha has abandoned all objects of 
abandonment and has attained all qualities, ther e will not arise the conception of faults in a 
buddha. Likewise, if we think that our lama is  a buddha, meaning that he has exhausted all 
faults and achieved all qualities, then we will  also abandon looking for faults in him and we 
will conceive of him as possessing all the qualitie s of a buddha. In this way, we will be able to 
train our mind in the right way. In our every day life, one should train in seeing the qualities 
of the teacher and refrain from seeking out his fa ults. We should rely on the teacher in this 
way. To do so, we need to develop faith or confidence in him.   
Middle Lam-Rim:  
4 The disadvantages of conceiving faults 
In addition, the above tantra says: 
 
Apprehend the master’s positive qualities, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
104 do not ever apprehend his faults. 
Apprehending his qualities you will gain siddhis, apprehending his faults they cannot be gained. 
 
You should act accordingly. Thus, if posi tive qualities predominate in a spiritual 
master but you think about him in terms of the few faults he has, this will become 
an obstacle to your own accomplishments.
57 Even if faults predominate, if you 
train in confidence from the perspective of  his positive qualities without thinking 
about him in terms of his faults, this wi ll become a cause for accomplishments to 
come about. Therefore, when it comes to  your own spiritual master, whether his 
faults are big or small, contemplate the disadvantages of thinking about him from 
that perspective, repeatedly generate a mind to abandon this, and make it stop. 
 
“The above tantra” refers to the Vajrapani Empowerment Tantra . This text says that we should 
recognize or apprehend the qualities of the lama  or master. On the other hand, we should not 
seek out his faults. If we apprehend his qual ities, we will attain siddhis or spiritual 
attainments. On the other hand, if we only seek out faults in our spiritual master, specifically 
in our tantric teacher, we will not attain any sp iritual attainments. In other words, realizations 
will not grow in our continuum. Therefore, this  tantra says that we should think about and 
understand this.  
 If our lama has more qualities than faults, but we  look only at his faults this will become an 
interference to achieving attainments, realizat ions, or inner qualities. When we see some 
faults in our lama, we should remember that he mostly has good qualities, whereby, by 
focusing on these good qualities, we will be ab le to attain the attain ments. If our lama has 
more faults than qualities, from our own side we  should not look at the side of the faults but 
should try to see his qualities, whereby, by trai ning in faith in him, th is will become a cause 
for us to attain the attainments or realizations. In short, whatever the faults of our lama or 
teacher, big or small, we should not focus on them but instead we should look at the other 
side. In this way, we should abandon the though t that our lama has faults, even slight ones. 
We should reject and try to stop this mind. Inst ead we should always try to see his qualities.  
 For example, if a child always sees his father or mother as being hard, as not taking care of 
him, as talking badly to him, and so forth, that is , if he always looks for faults in his father or 
mother, he will become more and more unhappy, will not trust his parents, and will think to 
escape from home. This is not a useful way to think. If a father or mother sometimes appear 
to be wrathful and use harsh words, it is in order to eliminate their child’s mistakes and to 
make him more humble. It shows that a father an d mother have real love  for their child. On 
the other hand, if day and night they are always kind and sweet to their children and let them 
do whatever they want, this will not be of any benefit. Sometimes parents need to be wrathful. 
Likewise, in Buddhism there are many wrathful deit ies. Why is this? It is because some people 
cannot be subdued by the other types of actions of pacification, increase, and control, due to 
which there is a need to manifest in a wrathf ul aspect. Sometimes parents also need to do 
this. When they yell at us, it should not make us  sad, instead we should understand that they 
                                              
57 JN: dngos grub , in Sanskrit siddhi , is also translated as “attainment,” “spiritual attainment,” “higher 
attainment,” etc. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 23-26 January 2005 (2) 
105 are trying to take care of us and are trying to eliminate our mistakes. For this reason, we 
should try to look at the qualities in our fa mily members and not look at their faults. We 
should also do the same with our friends.

Monday afternoon, 31 January 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 31) says:  
5 The way of restraining concepts of faults 
If concepts of faults arise due to carelessness,58 a lot of afflictions, and so forth, you 
should apply yourself to restraint through confession.59 If you accustom yourself in 
this manner, you may see that there are a few faults, but since your mind holds on 
to the side of positive qualities, it will not become an obstacle to your confidence. 
For instance, the great Jowo upheld the view of the Madhyamaka, while Serlingpa 
upheld the view of the True Aspectarian Chittamatra, which is why, in terms of 
view, one was higher than the other. Howeve r, as he discovered the general stages 
of the Mahayana path and the mind of en lightenment through relying on him, he 
regarded Serlingpa as unequalled among spiritual teachers. 
 
Due to non-conscientiousness, many affliction s, and so forth, we may see faults in our 
spiritual teacher. When this happe ns, we need to confess it in order to eliminate it by means 
of purification. Since we are ordinary beings, we  have many mental afflictions, due to which 
negative thoughts that see faults can arise. However, by looking at the side of the good 
qualities of the guru and familiarizing with this, when we see slight faults there will not arise 
non-faith, that is, we will be able to stop this  from happening. If we practice looking not at his 
faults of others but at his qualities, we will co me to see more and more good qualities. On the 
other hand, if we look at the side of faults an d do not look at his good qualities, this will 
become an obstacle for us and will create many mental problems for us.   If we think bad of someone, that person does not become bad. Likewise, if we think good of someone, that person does not become good. However, if we intentio nally seek the bad in 
him or her, we will only see faults, due to wh ich our mind will become more agitated and 
nervous, and we will experience more problems. Therefore, with respect to all beings we need 
to practice looking at the side of their good qualit ies, as in this way our life will become easier.  
 Atisha held the view of the Madhyamaka or  Middle Way tenet system, whereas his teacher, 
Serlingpa, held the view of the Chittamatra or  Mind-Only tenet system. Among these two, the 
view of the Madhyamaka is superior to that of the Chittamatra. In spite of holding a higher 
view than his teacher, Atisha ga ined many good qualities, in particular he generated the mind 
of enlightenment, by relying on  Serlingpa. While from the poin t of view of tenets Serlingpa 
was a Chittamatrin, he had generated the conventi onal mind of enlightenment, that is, out of 
great compassion he wished to attain highest enlightenment or buddhah ood for the benefit of 
all sentient beings. Thus, he possessed the two as pirations: the aspiration to benefit or take 
care of other sentient beings and the aspi ration to attain enlightenment. These two 
aspirations acted as the cause for him to de velop the complete mind of enlightenment, 
whereby his mind became very soft and good, unlike our minds which are rough. In short, 
                                             
 
58 JN: bag med , could also be translated as “non-conscientiousness” 
59 JN: bshags bsdam , could be translated as “to confession and restraint” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
107 why did Atisha regarded Serlingpa as the best  among his many gurus? It was because he 
developed the mind of enlightenmen t through relying on Serlingpa.  
 Hence, even though our teacher or guru may po ssess faults (given that as long as one is not 
a buddha one will have faults as even high bodhisattvas have some faults), we should look at 
him from the side of his good qualities, that is, at his positive side. We should cultivate the 
thought that he has qualities and should avoi d looking for faults, such as inappropriate 
behavior, in him. By looking only at the guru’s qualities, we will also train in blocking the 
arisal of bad thoughts toward other people.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-1B3B-1B Remembering the kindness and being respectful 
Second: {32}  
In the [ Sutra of the ] Ten Dharmas  it says: 
 
He looks for me who has roamed in cy clic existence for a long time; he 
awakes me from a long time of  obscuration and torpor due to 
ignorance; he pulls me out as I sink in the ocean of existence; he shows 
good paths to me who has entered ba d ones; he frees me who has been 
bound in the prison of existence; he  i s  a  d o c t o r  t o  m e  w h o  h a s  l o n g  
been tormented by illness; I should generate the discrimination of him 
as rainclouds pacifying me, who ha s been ablaze with the fire of 
passion60 and so on. 
 
We have been roaming or wandering in cyclic  existence since beginningless time. Even now 
we are still wandering in cyclic existence, howe ver we are now being sought by a guru who can 
free us from cyclic existence. We have been ob scured by ignorance and as though asleep for a 
long time, but now we have been awakened by the guru. Having found the Dharma and put it 
into practice, the guru shows us what to do , whereby we can eliminate the obscuration of 
ignorance, which is like darkness obscuring our mind.  
 We have been drowning or sinking in the ocean of cyclic existence, unable to get out, for a 
long time. The guru who teache s the Dharma enables us to become free from this ocean. 
Having taken a bad path, the gu ru shows us which path to ta ke, whereby we can travel the 
right path and avoid wrong path s. All of this depends on th e guru. When he shows us the 
good path, we should follow it. When we are traveling a wrong path, we should turn away 
from it.   We are bound in the prison of cyclic existence by the mental affliction s. It is the guru who 
unties the knots of cyclic existence, whereby we can become free from cyclic existence.  
 When we have been ill for a long time and our body is very weak, a doctor can tell us what kind of treatment to take, what kind of food to eat and what kind of food to avoid. If we follow his advice we can be cured of our illness.  Similarly, to us who have been sick with the 
mental sickness of cyclic existence for a long time, the guru gives the Dharma, which is like 
medicine. By practicing it, we can be cured of this illness.  
                                             
 
60 JN: ‘dod chags , “attachment" 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
108  The attachment, hatred, jealousy, and so forth in our mind are like burning fires. The guru 
who teaches us the Dharma comp letely extinguishes this fire. For example, when we have 
strong attachment, it is like a fire burning in  our mind, making us unhappy. If we are unable 
to obtain the object of our attachment, we be come completely unhappy, due to which we may 
even need to take medicine for mental illness, and, if it cannot be cured, we may even commit 
suicide. Our mental afflictions su ch as attachment are very strong , but they can be pacified by 
the guru, like a forest fire can be extinguishe d by strong rain. The Buddha’s teachings are 
likened to rain because, just as rain makes pl ants grow, keeps the dust down, and so forth, 
similarly by receiving the rain of Buddha’s teac hings our mind become more positive and the 
dust-like negativities are stopped. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
The Array of Stalks61 [Sutra]  also says: 
 
“These are my spiritual friends, expounders of Dharma, 
Exhaustively teaching the qualities of all that is, Thoroughly teaching the conduct of bodhisattvas,” With these thoughts in mind, come here.  As they give birth to all th at, they are like my mother. 
As they give the milk of virtues, they are like nurses. They cleanse me completely by me ans of enlightenment’s branches. 
 Our spiritual friends who expound the Dharma te ach us the qualities of all phenomena or of 
all dharmas, that is, they th oroughly teach us the conduct of bodhisattvas. What is the 
bodhisattva conduct? It is the mind of enli ghtenment and the six or ten perfections: the 
perfections of generosity, morality or ethical di scipline, patience, joyous effort, concentration, 
wisdom, and so forth. A bodhisattva constantly ex erts effort to take care of other sentient 
beings and, to do this, examines what is the best way to help them. Sometimes bodhisattvas 
even manifest as a ship or a bridge in order to help sentient beings. They can also manifest as a house for those who do not have shelter and so forth. Bodhisattv as strive to benefit sentient 
beings in whatever way they can. Their only desire is to help others. Even to help just one single sentient being they are prepared to go to the hell realm and stay there for a long time in order to extract this being from the hell realm.  T h e y  c o n t i n u a l l y  s t r i v e  t o  h e l p  o t h e r s  a n d  
never think to harm or act against others.   Recognizing the behavior and qualities of bodh isattvas as being the very best and thinking 
that our teacher also possesses these qualities, we should generate the wish to develop them 
also. By thinking in this way, we will be able to eventually develop them.   The teacher is likened to a mother because, just  as a mother gives birth to a child, similarly 
due to the teacher good qualities are born in  our continuum. Then, even after a mother has 
given birth to her child, she does not abandon it but continues to nourish it with her milk 
and so forth so that it grows better and better.  Similarly a Dharma teacher nourishes our good 
                                             
 
61 Gandavyuha  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
109 qualities of the mind of enlightenment, love, compassion, the mind of safeguarding, patience, 
joyous effort, concentration, and wisdom, and helps them grow, whereby our mind becomes 
better and better.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
These spiritual friends completely exorcise harm. 
They are like doctors releasing from death and aging. Showering a rain of nectar, they are like Indra, the lord of the gods. 
 The teacher, by teaching us the Dharma, turns away or elimin ates harm. For example, by 
teaching us how to develop the mind of enligh tenment, even if someone wants to harm us 
they will not be able to do so.   The teacher, like a doctor, teaches us the Dh arma, which is like medicine. When a doctor 
g i v e s  u s  m e d i c i n e  i t  c a n  s a v e  o u r  l i f e  a n d  prevent us from dying. While aging cannot be 
avoided, death can be turned away for a while.   The teacher who sends down a rain of nectar of Dharma is like Indra, who causes rain to fall from the clouds upon the earth. Indra is th e king of the Realm of the Thirty-Three. It was 
he together with Brahma who first asked Buddh a Shakyamuni to turn the wheel of Dharma 
i n  t h e  w o r l d .  F o r  t h i s  r e a s o n ,  I n d r a  i s  c o n s i d e r e d  t o  b e  D h a r m a  k i n g  w h o  f o l l o w s  t h e  Buddha’s teachings. Because he made the reques t, “Buddha, now that you are enlightened, 
please turn the wheel of Dharma,” Buddha Shak yamuni let fall a rain of Dharma teachings. 
Present at this first teaching, the wheel of Dharma of the four  noble truths, were five human 
beings, who became Buddha’s main disciples, as  well as among the gods  Brahma and Indra, 
and nagas and anti-gods. Although these beings were present at the teaching, they could not 
be seen by the human beings.   It is recounted that once when Buddha Shakya muni was teaching, a na ga came in the form 
of a king surrounded by his many ministers. However, seeing this, the Buddha told him to 
come to teachings the next day in his real form. Thus, the next day a huge five-headed snake 
came to the teachings, creating fear in everyo ne. They wondered why a snake had come to the 
teachings and Buddha Shakyamuni  explained that the elegant maharaja who had come to the 
teachings the previous day was in fact this same king of the nagas. He to ld his disciples not to 
worry as even though his body was that of a sn ake, the naga king unde rstood the Dharma and 
had generated the mind of enlightenment. For this reason, it is said that one with a naga body 
can generate the mind of enlighte nment, this being an exception to the general rule that it is 
not possible to generate the mind of enlightenm ent on the basis of an animal body. Nagas are 
included in the animal realm because they ar e not gods or anti-gods or human beings or 
hungry ghosts or hell beings. Thus, in the anim al realm there are some beings, for example, 
nagas, who have sharp an d intelligent minds.  
 In fact it was the nagas who invited Nagarj una to their land, where the Perfection of 
W i s d o m  S u t r a s  h a d  b e e n  t a k e n .  N a g a r j u n a ,  h a v i n g  b e e n  u n a b l e  t o  f i n d  t h e  P e r f e c t i o n  o f  W i s d o m  S u t r a s  i n  I n d i a ,  w e n t  t o  N e p a l  w h e r e he visited the naga land. At the back of 
Swayambunath stupa there is a building in whic h there is a door through which Nagarjuna is 
said to have gone to the naga land and then  returned back to the human realm with these 
texts. One of the texts that Nagarjuna brought back  with him can still be seen in Nepal. It is
