Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
110 kept locked up in a special place because some one once stole some pages and sold them. This 
text is written in the lentsa  letters of the Sanskrit language. I once saw this text; the paper is 
black and the letters are written in gold. Therefore,  the Dharma also exists  in the naga lands.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Like the full moon they flou rish with white qualities. 
They show the direction of peace, like bright sunlight. 
Regarding friends and foes they are like mountains. They have minds as un disturbed as the sea. 
They give perfect support, some say: “like boatmen.” With this in mind, Sudhana, come here. 
 
The full moon, being completely white, fills the sk y with white light. The teacher is likened to 
the moon in that he makes the white dharmas or  virtuous qualities increase in our mind. An 
example of a white or virtuous dharma is not ki lling, whereas killing is a black or non-virtuous 
dharma.  
 The teacher is also likened to sunlight, fo r example, the autumn sun which is clear and 
bright, not hot but rather cool, due to the sky no t being hazy or foggy as it is in summertime. 
 The teacher is also likened to a mountain. Just as a mountain cannot be moved by enemies or friends, but treats everyone the same without difference, similarly, the teacher is stable like 
a mountain in that he does not look to friends and enemies.  
 The mind of the teacher is like an ocean, in that just as an ocean cannot easily be disturbed 
even though boats travel on its surface and fi sh swim in its depths, likewise, his mind is 
undisturbed, being similar to the mind s of the buddhas and bodhisattvas.  
 The teacher thoroughly supports or nourishes us , like a boatman, one who drives a boat. It 
is said that there are three type s of mind of enlightenment: th e mind of enlightenment like a 
king, the mind of enlightenment like a boa tman, and the mind of enlightenment like a 
shepherd. Just as a shepherd first puts his anim als in their stalls and only then goes home, 
similarly someone with this mind of enlightenmen t wishes to first set all other sentient beings 
in buddhahood and then attain buddhahood himsel f. Just as a boatman travels together with 
the people in his ship, similarly someone with this mind of enlightenment thinks to travel 
together with all other sentient beings to enligh tenment, that is, he do es not think to go to 
enlightenment first and then to bring them to  enlightenment, nor does he think to bring 
them to enlightenment first and then at the en d to attain enlightenment himself. Just as a 
king thinks to become king in order to take ca re of his subjects (or someone thinks to become 
president in order to take care of the populati on), someone with this mind of enlightenment 
thinks to attain enlightenment first in order to then set all sentient beings in enlightenment.  
 Tuesday morning, 1 February 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 32) says: 
 
“Bodhisattvas bring forth my understanding. 
Buddha’s children cause enlightenment. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
111 These friends of mine are praised by the Buddha”; 
With such wholesome thoughts in mind, come here. 
 
That the Buddha’s children or th e bodhisattvas cause enlightenmen t means that they strive to 
help their disciples to generate the mind of enlightenment so that they themselves become 
children of the Buddha and achieve buddhahood. It is usually said that the Buddha gave 
teachings, and that, by studying them, his disc iples generate the mind of definite emergence 
or renunciation, the mind of enlightenment,  and the perfect view realizing emptiness, 
whereby they come to be called “bodhisattvas” or “children of the Buddha.” Thus, if we too 
practice in this way we will become bodhisa ttvas. The three principal aspects of the path – 
renunciation, the mind of enlightenment, and co rrect view – are the main causes of ordinary 
bodhisattvas. Even though they are not yet un contrived renunciation, an uncontrived mind 
of enlightenment, and uncontrived correct view, they are the causes th at make one enter the 
path. Then, having generated an uncontrived mind  of enlightenment, for example, one enters 
the Mahayana path and becomes a child of th e Buddha, whereby we will come to achieve 
enlightenment or buddhahood. The “wholesome thoughts”, that is , the virtuous thoughts, of 
this context refers to the mind of enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
“As they save the world,  they are like heroes. 
They have become the captains , protectors, and refuges.  
They are {33} the eye bestowing happiness on me,” 
With thoughts like these ho nor your spiritual friends. 
 [The thoughts] expressed here should be remembered through a tune to go with 
the verses, substituting oneself for “Sudhana.” 
 The spiritual friends or teachers are also hero es who protect worldly beings. They are also 
captains, protectors, and refuges, as well as eyes  bestowing happiness. What kind of eye is it 
that bestows happiness? It is the wisdom eye, sinc e it is wisdom that enables us to understand 
everything. Therefore, we need to venerate or honor our virtuous spiritual friends.  
 We should memorize or remember these verses  and chant them with a pleasant tune while 
remembering the kindness of our teacher. “S udhana” is the Sanskrit for the Tibetan nor bzang , 
meaning “fine wealth.” We should su bstitute his name with our own.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-1B3B-2 The manner of relying through actions 
A The need for reliance through actions 
B Condensed explanation of the three means of pleasing him 
C The extensive explanation of those three 
 
Now the text sets out how to rely on the guru th rough actions. However, first the need to rely 
on him through actions is explained.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
112 2B4A-1B3B-2A The need for reliance through actions 
The second: 
In the Fifty [Verses About the] Guru  it says: 
 
What need is there to say a lot of words here? 
Do everything62 to make your guru happy. 
Give up anything that does not please him. 
Apply yourself to that. Examine that! 
 
“Spiritual attainments follow from the guru”; This was said by Vajradhara himself. Aware of this, use anything there is, and 
Make your guru totally, [utterly] happy. 
 
In brief, try hard to please him and to give up whatever he does not like. 
 
Ashvagosha’s Fifty Verses on the Guru  says that there is no need to  say a lot here. In short, we 
should try to act in such a way that the guru will be happy with us. On the other hand, if 
some action we do does not make him happy, we should abandon it. We should put effort 
into this and examine what kinds of actions make the guru happy or delighted and what 
kinds of actions make him unhappy. We should use our wisdom to analyze this, and then we 
should put effort into accomp lishing the actions that make him happy and abandoning the 
actions that do not please him. Thus, what is meant by relying on the guru through action is 
to abandon that which does not please him and to do that which pleases him. 
 That all spiritual attainments (siddhis) are ge nerated in dependence on the guru or master 
was said by Vajradhara himself. Having underst ood this, we should try to do whatever makes 
the guru happy and try to avoid whatever make s him unhappy. If we are unable to make him 
happy, we should at least avoid bad actions and do only neutral actions. For example, 
sometimes no matter what we do, our parents ar e not happy! Still we should try as much as 
possible to act in accordance with  that taught by the Buddha.  
 In brief, we should put effort into doing th at which pleases the guru and abandoning that 
which does not please him. We need to put st rong effort into this. We need to encourage 
ourselves and feel strong and able to do this. We should not get depressed. If our body and 
mind are strong, we will be able to do much. On the other hand, if our body is not strong 
and our mind is weak and lacking in courage, we will not be able to do anything. We will constantly think, “I cannot do it.” People sometimes ask me what they to do when this 
happens, but as I do not know I tell them to eat well and sleep well, whereby their energy may 
return. We can also do the breathing meditation , imagining that all our negative energy of 
body, speech, and mind, all our sadness, depression , and so forth, are exhaled in the aspect of 
black smoke. As we inhale, we should imagine th at all the qualities, knowledge, and positive 
energy of the buddhas and of the entire world en ter into us in the aspe ct of white light. Our 
body becomes clean and clear, as transparent as  crystal, and our mind becomes clear and able 
to understand everything. This breathing medi tation is not difficult, therefore we should 
                                             
 
62 lit. “everything should be done” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
113 concentrate on the breath whil e sending out all negative energy and inhaling all positive 
energy. In this way our mind will become more relaxed.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-1B3B-2B Condensed explanation of the three means of pleasing him 
The three means of pleasing [the guru] ar e (1) offering him material things, (2) 
serving him and paying respect to him with  body and speech, and (3) practicing in 
accordance with his advice. In this regard, it says in the Ornament of the Sutras : 
 
Through homage and pure offerings and service 
and practice, you should rely on a spiritual friend. 
 
There are three ways of pleasing the guru:  
making offerings of material things to him, serving him in whatever way he needs, and putting into practice whatever he has taught. Among the three, the latter is the best way of relying on him by means of actions. Maitreya’s 
Ornament of the Sutras  says that we should rely on a spir itual friend by means of offering him 
material things, serving him and paying resp ect to him with our body and speech, and 
practicing. “Serving him with our body” includes  cleaning the guru’s room, putting his things 
in order, and so forth. “Serving him with our sp eech” is, for example, if the guru asks us to 
give someone a message we pass it on to that  person, thereby substituting his speech. In 
addition, we should put whatever the guru has ta ught us into practice. The latter is the best 
way to rely on the guru, the main point of relyin g on a guru being to practice whatever he has 
taught us as purely as possible. However, we need please him in all three ways. These three 
will now be explained extensively.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-1B3B-2C The extensive explanation of those three 
1 Offering him ma terial things 
As regards the first, it says in the Fifty [Verses] : 
 
Through things not normally gi ven – your son and your wife, 
And through your life, you should forever rely 
On the master of your sacred commitments; Needless to say, through fickle possessions [as well]. 
 
And: 
 
Giving is what amounts to always Offering gifts to all the buddhas. Offering is what accumulates merit, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
114 Which turns into the highest attainments63. 
 
We should give to the guru even the things th at we would not generally give to others. For 
example, if the guru wants our son, we should  give him our son; if he wants our wife, we 
should give him our wife; if he wants our husband, we should give him our husband, and so 
forth. We should even be prepared to offer our life to our guru. In addition, we should 
mentally  offer whatever possessions we own to our gu ru, there being no need to actually give 
them to him. Just by mentally offering them to him, we will accumulate merit. Thus, we can 
also imagine all the forests, lakes, and so fort h in the world and then mentally offer them to 
the guru or to the Three Jewels  or to the entire merit fiel d. This will bring about the 
accumulation of a great collection of merit or virtue that will be very strong and stable. To 
give our life to the guru means to spend our life  in serving him, like, for example, Ven. Roger 
who constantly serves Lama Zo pa Rinpoche and accompanies hi m wherever he goes. Perhaps 
we can assume that he has also given his life to his guru. To follow the guru like a shadow, 
constantly trying to help him, is what it mean s “to offer our life to the guru.” It does not 
mean to give him our life in the sense of sacrificing our life and dying for his sake. Rather it means to dedicate our life to serving him, gi ving him whatever possessions we have, and so 
forth. If we offer these things to the guru, it is like offering them to the buddhas of the ten 
directions. In this fortunate eons  1,000 buddhas will visit this wo rld; if we make offerings to 
the guru we will gain the same amount of  merit as offering to the 1,000 buddhas.  
 To offer all our material things to all th e buddhas always means to offer them in the 
morning, at midday, in the even ing, and at night. Offering in  this way we will accumulate 
many merits, due to which we will eventually ac hieve spiritual attainments or realizations, that 
is, buddhahood or, from the point of view of ta ntra, the state of Vajradhara. For this reason, 
we should offer material things to the guru.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Serving and paying respect to him with body and speech 
The second [involves] bathing him, massaging him, cleaning him, nursing him 
when he is ill, etc., and talkin g about his positive qualities. 
 
We can serve the guru by bathing him, massag ing his body, cleaning him by anointing him 
with oils, and nursing and serving him when he is  ill. We should serve the guru in this way 
when he is not well and unable to move.  
 We should also express the guru’s qualities wi th our speech, saying, for example, that he is 
excellent, possesses all ten qualities or fi ve of them or the minimum two mentioned 
previously, that he is compassionate and tr ies to help his disciples, and has certain 
knowledges, for example, that he knows how to paint, how to work with computers, how to 
build houses, and so forth. This advice refers  not only to Dharma teachers but to all our 
normal worldly teachers. We should praise all of them as we have gained knowledge from their teachings, and try to help them. We should  praise them by revealing their good qualities 
and refrain from pointing out their faults. For ex ample, we should not say that on the outside 
our teacher appears to be good but in reality he or she cheats others and so forth. Atisha said 
                                             
 
63 lit. “this accumulation turns into the highest attainments” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
115 to Jangchub O, “Do not expose others’ faults, but conceal them. Talk about others’ qualities 
and hide your own qualities. Reveal your own fa ults.” This means that even if we are unable 
to point out others’ qualities, we should at least refrain from pointing out their faults. We too 
need to act like this in our daily life.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 Practicing in accordance with his advice 
The third [consists in] {34} to practice wi thout contradicting the instructions, this 
being the main one. In the Jataka Stories  it says: 
 
The offering to be made in return for his help 
Is to practice in accordance with his instructions. 
 We should practice whatever the guru has taug ht us without contradicting his instructions, 
that is, we should not do the opposite to what he has advised us to do. This is the main thing 
to be done in the context of relying on the guru. The Jataka  or Life Stories  say that in order to 
repay the benefit we have received from our guru , we should practice in accordance with what 
he has advised, doing what he ha s said to do and avoiding what he has said not to do. This is 
the best of offerings. Thus, whatever advice we have been given, we should try to do as we 
have been told and to not do the opposite. This is the best way of relying on the guru, and is 
very useful for our practice.  
 This subject is explained in much more detail in Lama Tsongkhapa’s Great Exposition of the 
Stages of the Path . However, in short, we should do what the guru says to do and avoid doing 
w h a t  h e  s a y s  s h o u l d  n o t  b e  d o n e .  T h i s  i s  s i m i l a r ,  f o r  e x a m p l e ,  t o  t h e  f a c t  t h a t  i t  i s  g o o d ,  
whether from the Dharma point of view or the wo rldly point of view, to follow the advice of 
our grandparents and parents and to try not to go against their advice but to respect them. Likewise, whether we are in a Dharma center, sch ool, or university we should try to abide by 
the rules set out by the director or principle. This is correct according to both the world and 
the Dharma as it will result in good behavior . On the other hand, by not abiding by these 
rules, our behavior will become bad and negative  and may harm our society, the center, or the 
school.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-1B4 The benefits of reliance 
A Benefits such as approaching the state of a buddha 
Fourth:  
It says [in the scriptures] that you will approach the state of  a buddha, that the 
Conquerors will rejoice, that you will not be  deprived of virtuous spiritual friends, 
that you will not fall to the lower realms, and that you will not easily succumb to 
bad karma and afflictions. Since yo u will not transgress the conduct of 
bodhisattvas and will be mindful of it, yo ur accumulation of good qualities will 
grow higher and higher and all your temporary and ultimate goals will be 
accomplished. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
116 By relying on a guru in the right and perfect way, we will come closer to buddhahood. In 
addition, all the conquerors will be delighted, an d in our future lives we will not be bereft of 
teachers, that is, we will always find virtuous sp iritual friends. We will also not fall into the 
lower realms – the hell realm, hungry ghost realm,  or animal realm. Instead we will be reborn 
as a human being in a good family with all our sense powers complete and an intelligent 
mind. In addition, we will not be easily overco me by bad actions and afflictions, that is, we 
will not be defeated by them. These are some of  the benefits of correctly relying on a guru.  
 Also we will not transgress the conduct of bodh isattvas, that is, we will not do actions that 
are opposite to them. We will also possess mi ndfulness, whereby our collection of good 
qualities will grow higher and higher. Temporally  we will achieve happiness and finally we will 
achieve the state of enlightenment.  
 In short, we will come closer to buddhahood, the conquerors will be delighted with us, we 
will not be deprived of gurus in the future, and so forth. If, on the other hand, we do the opposite of what the guru advises, we will become more distant from the state of a buddha, 
the conquerors will be unhappy with us, we will be  bereft of virtuous spir itual friends, we will 
fall into the hell realm, hungry ghost realm, or animal realm, we will be overcome or defeated 
by evil actions and the mental afflictions, we  will transgress and degenerate the bodhisattva 
conduct, we will have forgetfulness instead of mindfulness, and our bad qualities will increase 
instead of our good qualities, as a result of which temporally we will experience problems and 
unhappiness and finally we will be reborn in the hells.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Bad karma is exhausted and [the benefit] exceeds even that of offerings to numerous buddhas 
Furthermore, through serving and paying respect to the spiritual teacher, the 
karmas for experiencing the lower realms are exhausted directly in this lifetime 
t h r o u g h  e x p e r i e n c i n g  o n l y  s l i g h t  h a r m  t o  b o d y  a n d  m i n d  o r  e l s e  t h r o u g h  b e i n g  
experienced in dreams. It is said that th ere are enormous benefits that outshine 
the roots of virtue of making offeri ngs, etc. to innumerable buddhas.  
 
Through making offerings to all the buddhas,  our negative or non-virtuous actions are 
exhausted and a great amount of merit is accu mulated. However, through serving the guru 
and making him happy with offerings and so fort h, instead of experiencing the suffering of 
the lower realms for which we had created the cause, we will  either experience a slight 
physical sickness or mental unhappiness in this li fe or even just some suffering in a dream. 
For example, we might dream that we have been reborn in the hell, hungry ghost, or animal 
realm and in the dream experience their resp ective sufferings. In the hell realm one 
experiences the suffering of heat and cold, in  the hungry ghost realm one experiences the 
suffering of hunger and thirst, and in the animal realm one experiences the suffering of being 
stupid, of eating one another, and so forth. Inst ead of actually experiencing these sufferings in 
the future, we will experience them in a drea m. In this way, our negative actions are 
completely exhausted by serving the guru.  
 If we make offerings of material things to  immeasurable buddhas, we accumulate a lot of 
virtue, it is of great benefit. However by serv ing and respecting the guru we accumulate even 
more, that is, the benefit is greater. Therefore, we need to practice what the guru teaches.

117 In our every day life we need to develop a good heart toward all sentient beings and try to

should be our main practice in daily life, since it is quite easy.  
 
Tuesday afternoon, 1 February 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 34) says: 
2B4A-1B5 The drawbacks of not relying 
A The way it ruins this  life and future ones 
Fifth: If you take someone as your spir itual teacher and then back off in your 
reliance on him, a lot of illnesses and negative forces64 will harm you in this life, 
and in future lives you will have to experience the immeasurable sufferings of the 
lower realms.  
 We should try our very best to rely on the gu ru. To initially think that someone is our guru 
but then later on to act against him or br eak the relationship with him would be a 
transgression and not right. As a result of not relying properly on him, we will experience 
many illnesses, both physical and mental, as well as harm from evil spirits in this life. Then in 
future lives we will be reborn in the bad migrat ions where we will experience infinite types of 
sufferings. This is mentioned below in the Fifty Verses on the Guru . Thus, we need to try to rely 
on the guru properly and not transgress his advice or instructions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In the Fifty [Verses]  it says:  
 
Do not ever agitate the minds 
Of your masters – not in any way. If you do so due to some delusion You will necessarily roast in hell.  
It has been explained authentically, 
That all those who slander their masters Will come to dwell in the horrific hells, Such as the Avici, that were taught. 
 We should never do actions that disturb the guru ’s mind. This is because if, out of ignorance, 
we commit actions that disturb his mind we will  burn in a hell realm, for example, being 
boiled in molten iron, unable to  do anything to avoid this suffering. The worst of the hells is 
Avici or Unrelenting Torment where the suffering does not stop even fo r an instant and lasts 
for eons. As we could take rebirth there, we mu st be very careful not to disturb the mind of 
the guru or the minds of other holy beings, su ch as bodhisattvas and foe-destroyers, or the 
                                             
 
64 JN: gdon, could also be translated as “evil spirits” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
118 minds of our parents. Since all of them are obje cts of veneration, if we disturb their minds we 
will later experience the sufferings of the he ll realms. In fact th e fourth chapter of Treasury of 
Manifest Dharma  says that, among human beings, our pa rents are very precious because they 
gave us this precious human rebirth endowed with  the eighteen qualities.  Therefore, we also 
need to respect them and to be careful with respect to them. 
 The Buddha explained that the hells are fearfu l, horrific places, and that one reason for 
being reborn there is criticizing or slandering our guru. Since this  explanation is not faulty, we 
must be careful to avoid committing such actions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
{35} In a passage from the Commentary on the Difficult Points of the Black Opponent [of 
Yama]  it also says: 
 
Whoever hears a single verse [of Dharma ], 
And does not perceive the spiritual teacher
65, 
Will be born among dogs a hundred times, 
And afterwards as someone of low cast.66 
 
It needs to be clarified that if we just hear a single verse of Dharma from someone, it is not necessary to take him or her as our teacher. We can, for example, listen to a Dharma teaching 
while thinking that we are merely listening to  a lecture, independent of whether the person 
teaching is a monk, nun, or lay person. In othe r words, we can just treat the teaching as a 
general talk, due to which this person does no t become our teacher. For someone to become 
our teacher we have to think that  we want to take him as our te acher, that is, we must have 
this specific motivation. However,  having taken him as our spiritua l teacher, if we then listen 
to teachings from him but lack respect for hi m, we will be reborn a hundred times among 
dogs and even when we are reborn as a human bein g we will be born in a lower caste, such as 
that of butchers. In Nepal th e butchers who kill animals and sell the meat are all women, 
whereas in India they are all men.   In short, if with the motivati on that is the thought to listen to a Dharma teaching from our 
teacher, we listen to even a single verse but later on deprecate our teacher, the consequence 
will be to be reborn again and again as a do g one hundred times, and then eventually when 
we are once again born as a human being to be a butcher who kills other beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B No positive qualities are attained and earlier ones deteriorate 
Apart from that, positive qualities that have  not arisen do not arise, and those that 
have arisen, deteriorate and vanish. 
 
                                              
65 gang gis bla mar mi ’dzin pa  – lit. “and does not hold/understand/apprehend as a spiritual teacher” 
66 JN: sme sha can , butcher 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
119 If we break our relationship with our guru we will not attain any good qualities that have not 
yet been generated, and whatever good qualitie s we already have will degenerate. Our mind 
will become disturbed, due to which we will  not remember our commitments with our guru. 
As a result of this, any good qualities we deve loped in the past will degenerate and we will not 
generate any new qualities because of lack of interest and so forth. This is the danger of not 
relying properly on our guru.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C The disadvantages of relying on unwholesome spiritual friends and bad companions 
If you rely on unwholesome spiritual friends and bad companions, again your 
positive qualities will diminish and your faults will increase, whereby there will be 
undesirable results. Therefore, it was taug ht that this should be avoided in every 
way. 
 
If we rely on an improper or non-virtuous teacher or on bad companions or negative friends, 
there will arise many shortcomings similar to th e previous ones. In other words, the qualities 
that we generated in the past will degenerate  and our faults will increase, due to which the 
undesirable will occur. Hence, we must strive to abandon the actions of relying on non-virtuous teachers and negative friends. For exam ple, we see children who were originally well 
behaved but then, having become friends with ne gative boys or girls, began to use marijuana 
and other drugs. They then slowly became habitu ated to taking stronger and stronger drugs, 
such as heroine, began to steal money even  from their own families, and sold their 
possessions and so forth. This is all due to nega tive friends who cause our behavior to change 
from good to bad. A similar thing can happen due to following negative teachers. There exist 
such teachers in the world, for example, thos e who teach black magic, who teach others how 
to steal, and so forth. If we follow them and practice what they  teach, we too will engage in 
these negative actions. Thus , what is meant by non-virt uous teachers and negative 
companions is those who lead us to engage in negativities. We should give up or abandon such teachers and friends.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-1B6 Summary of the meaning of these [points] 
A The importance of proper reliance on the root of perpetual striving67

understood according to what has been ex plained before, it not being enough to 
do the visualizations in just one medi tation session. When you do one Dharma

teacher who guides you unmistakenly.  
 When doing guru yoga, having been given inst ructions as mentioned previously, it is not

120 we should try to put them into practice every day. If from the depths of our heart we wish to 
practice the Dharma, we must rely for a long time  on a virtuous spiritual friend who guides us 
unmistakenly to enlightenment. Al though in general it is not en ough to rely on a guru for a 
short time in order to become enlightened, there are some people who created a lot of 
potential in their past lives due to which they  can quickly attain enlightenment. However, 
such people are exceptions; in general we need to rely on a guru for many years. 
 An example of guru yoga is the Hundred Deities of the Joyous Land  (Ganden Lha Gyema ), 
which is also called the G u r u  Y o g a  o f  L a m a  T s o n g k h a p a . In addition, there is the Guru Puja  
(Lama Chopa ), the Six-Session Guru Yoga , and so forth. Thus, there are many types of guru yoga. 
However, in each case, it is not enough to do su ch visualizations just one time, rather we must 
do them over and over again. Similarly, we must rely on a guru for a long time. If we rely on 
him for a short time and then abandon him, we will experience many interferences and will not gain any realizations.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
As Chekawa also said about that time sp an: “When you rely on a guru, there will 
be doubtful questions whether to give him up.” Accordingly, since there will only 
be loss without profit if you do not know how to rely on him, it becomes evident 
that the cycles of teachings about relying on a spiritual teacher are more important 
than any other ones and the root of perpetual striving.
68 
 
Geshe Chekawa wrote the Seven Point Mind Training , a well-known text on mind training. He 
said that if we rely for on a guru a short ti me, there may arise the doubt that wonders whether 
to give him up. For this reason, we need to understand how to rely on a guru. If we do not 
know how to rely on him, there will not be any profit and only loss. Therefore, we must think 
a lot about relying on a guru. Because the root of all the good things of this life, all our good

understood this, we should study how to rely on him in thought – th rough developing faith 
in him and through remembering his kindness de veloping respect for him – and then how to 
rely on him by means of actions. Thus, we need  to spend time learning the points of guru 
devotion, then trying to understand them, an d then doing the visualizations in order to 
develop faith and confidence in him, thinking that, like a mother who gives birth to a child 
and nourishes it, whatever qualities and knowle dge we generate depend on and are nourished

121 We should think about the disadvantages of  not relying properly on a guru and the 
advantages of relying properly on a him.  We should study this subject well.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The need for confession and a vow because, if that proper reliance does not come about, it is a great 
fault 
With our coarse mental afflictions – not knowing how to rely or knowing how to 
but not doing so – we have often allowed many faults69 of reliance on a spiritual 
teacher to occur in regard to listening to  the Dharma. Since it is difficult for an 
awareness of that to develop through conf ession and a vow, having understood the 
advantages and {36} disadvantages as explained previously, sincerely confess any 
occurrences that are not in accordance with proper reliance and develop many 
resolute minds. 
 If we do not rely on our teacher, that is, if we break the relationship with him, many faults 
will arise. For example, if there were four do ors open, one on each side of a room, dogs and 
cats as well as thieves could enter, dust would come in with the wind, and so forth. Similarly, 
if we have a lot of afflictions, are non-conscienti ous, are ignorant, and lack respect, we will not 
rely on the guru properly. Also if we do not know how to rely on him, or know how to rely on him but do not do it, when we listen to him teach,  negativities will arise. In this case, we need 
to do confession and make a vow to not do allo w this to happen again in the future. In other 
words, we should regret what we have done in  the past and make a vow or promise not to do 
it again in the future. In order to do so, we n eed to remember the benefits of relying on the 
guru and the faults of not relying on him corre ctly, which were mentioned previously. Then if 
we do not rely on him correctly, we should confess this from our heart and promise or resolve not to do it again. To confess our negativities thoroughly, we need to apply the four opponent 
powers: (1) the opponent force of the base – going for refuge to the Three Jewels, the Buddha, 
Dharma, and Sangha, and generating  the mind of enlightenment,  
(2) the opponent force of regret – developing regret for negative actions committed, (3) the opponent force of determination – th e thought or mental promise not to do the 
negative action again, and (4) the opponent force of applying an anti dote, for example, reciting mantras, doing 
prostrations, making images of buddhas such as tsa-tsas, offering water bowls, and reciting the 
Confession to the Thirty-Five Buddhas , the mantra of Vajrasattva, or sutras. However, the best 
means of purification is to meditate on the empt iness of inherent existence, thinking that the 
negative action, the creator of th e negative action, and the recipi ent of the negative action are 
empty of inherent existence.   Thus, at the beginning of confession, we sh ould recite the refuge formula three times 
together with the generation of the mind of enlightenment as we do before teachings saying:  
 
I go for refuge until I am enlightened  To the Buddha, Dharma, and Supreme Assembly.  
                                             
 
69 JN: kha na mo tho ba , could also be translated as “misdeed”

122 By my merit from practicing generosity and the other perfections,  
May I attain buddhahood for the benefit of all sentient beings. 
 
Then we should develop sincere regret for our negative actions, thinking that we did them 
out of ignorance, and then determine not to re peat them again in the future. Then we could 
recite the hundred-syllable mantra of Vajrasattv a or his short mantra or do any of the other 
antidotes. At the end of the confession, we  should think that we have succeeded in 
completely purifying our negative actions and then  dedicate all the virtue  that we created to 
the enlightenment of all sentient beings. If we do this everyday before going to sleep, it would 
be excellent and will bring a good result. Therefore, we should try to do this.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How, if you act in that manner, the realization of reliance is not far away 
If you act in that manner, before long  you will become like the bodhisattva 
Sadaprarudita and the young Sudhana who was insatiable in his quest for a 
spiritual teacher.  
 If we act in this way, we will quickly find a qualified teacher and thereby quickly achieve our 
aims, such as buddhahood. There are many st ories about the two, Sadaprarudita (Always 
Crying) and Sudhana. It is recounted, for ex ample, that Sadaprarudita set out on a long 
search for a mahayana teacher who could teach him the perfection of wisdom. Having heard 
that the guru Dharmodgata, who was living in  the east, could teach him the perfection of 
wisdom, he set out to find him. During his journey, Sadaprarudita developed the wish to 
make an offering to this in order to create merit. However as he did not have any money or 
possessions and was living like a beggar, he though t to sell his body in the marketplace and to 
buy offerings with the money earned. Sakra, the ki ng of the gods, in order to test his resolve, 
manifested as a young man who offered to buy his heart, blood, and marrow. Overjoyed, Sadaprarudita used a sharp sword to pierce his flesh to make the blood flow. He also cut off 
the flesh from his thigh and prepared to break the bone to extract th e marrow. However, the 
daughter of a merchant, seeing what was happeni ng, offered to give him whatever he needed 
and to accompany him to meet Dharmodgata. They  went together to the city of Ghandavati 
where Dharmodgata was teaching the perfection  of wisdom. They made offerings to him, 
received teachings on the perfection of wisdom, and then Dharmodgata entered into 
concentration for a period of seven years. Toward  the end of this period, having been told by 
a voice that Dharmodgata would soon rise from  his concentration, Sa daprarudita prepared a 
throne for his teacher. Unable to find water to settle the dust around the throne, he again 
pierced his body in order to cause his blood to drip to the ground and settle the dust. This is 
an example of perfect guru devotion that  is recounted in the last chapter of Eight Thousand 
Perfection of Wisdom Sutra  (see The Perfection of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines  translated by 
Edward Conze, Sri Satguru Publications, Delhi, 1973).   Try to relax. Do not worry. Slowly, slowly you wi ll come to understand all these things. In the 
meantime, try not to worry. Think instead, “I will understand. It is not difficult. It is easy to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
123 understand. There is no problem.” Thinking in this  way, try in your daily life to be happy, to 
not be agitated, to not get nervous, to be very relaxed, and to enjoy yourselves with delight! 
 
Wednesday morning, 2 February 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 36) says: 
2B4A-2 The condensed presentation of how to sustain it 
A The actual way to sustain it 
B The reason why it needs to be sustained by means of two modes of sustaining 
The second one has two points: the actual way to sustain it and the reason why it 
needs to be sustained by means of two modes of sustaining. 
 
These are actual outlines by Lama Tsongkhapa , whereas those written in smaller print were 
added later on by Trijang Rinpoche as notes. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A The actual way to sustain it 
1 What to do during the meditation session itself 
2 What to do during the periods between sessions 
The first one has two points: what to do during the meditation session itself and 
what to do during the periods between sessions. 
2B4A-2A1 What to do during the meditation session itself 
A What to do in preparation 
B What to do during the actual [meditation session ] 
C What to do at the end 
The first one has three points: what to do in preparation, during the actual, and at 
the end. 
2B4A-2A1A What to do in preparation 
1 [Cleaning your dwelling and setting up re presentations of body , speech, and mind] 
2 [Laying out offerings] 
3 [Sitting posture and mental attitude] 
4 [Visualization of the merit field] 
5 [Accumulation of merit and purification of negativities] 
6 [Mandala offering and request for blessings] 
First: There are six preparatory practices. 
2B4A-2A1A-1 [Cleaning your dwe lling and setting up representati ons of body, speech, and mind] 
Following the life story of Serlingpa, you should clean your dwelling and arrange 
representations of the exalted body, speech, and mind. 
 
We should first clean our meditation place and then set out a statue, text, and stupa 
representing the body, speech, and mind of the Buddha on an altar.  
 While we externally clean our room with a broom or a vacuum cleaner, internally we 
should have the motivation thinking to clean ou t the dirt of our mental afflictions, such as 
attachment, with the broom of  wisdom. Thus, while sweeping the floor, for example, we 
should be wishing to clean our mind of th e mental poisons, attachment, hatred, and 
ignorance. While doing so, we ca n recite, “Get rid of the dust. Purify the stains” (in Tibetan, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
124 “Dul pang. Dri ma dag .”). When we say “Get rid of the dust ,” it does not refer to the external 
dust in the room but to the internal dust in our mind of the mental afflictions – attachment, 
hatred, and ignorance. While cleaning our room, we should think that we are simultaneously 
abandoning these mental afflictions. Then, “Purify the stains” means to clean out the 
imprints left by attachment, hatred, and ignoranc e. Thinking in this way, cleaning our room 
becomes a practice of purification.  
 At the time of the Buddha ther e was a practitioner called “S mall Path,” who initially was 
unable to remember even a single word of Dh arma. Therefore, the Buddha told him to clean 
the temple while reciting “Get rid of the dust . Purify the stains.” But by the time he had 
cleaned one side of the temple, the other was du sty again and he could never finish cleaning 
it. At first when he recited these two phrases, by  the time he got to “Purify the stains,” he had 
forgotten the words “Get rid of the dust,” and by the time he  had recited “Get rid of the 
dust,” he had forgotten the words “Purify the stains .” This went on for a long time as he tried 
to memorize the phrases. However he persevered  and one day came to realize that “dust” did 
not refer to the external dust bu t to the internal dust of the a fflictions, and that “stains” did 
not refer to external dirt but to the imprints le f t  b y  t h e  a f f l i c t i o n s .  H e  r e a l i z e d  t h a t  i t  w a s  
these that needed to be cleaned. He then slow ly came to understand that the broom he was 
using to clean the dust represented the wisdom realizing the emptiness of inherent existence 
of all phenomena. In this way, one day whil e cleaning the temple, he directly realized 
emptiness or the four noble truths and became an arya being. If we clean in the same way 
with a good motivation, we too can become noble or arya beings!  
 There are different ways of cleaning: we can do so with a small motivation, with a middling 
motivation, or with a great motivation. If we  clean our room while thinking to clean our 
mind of the negative karma that will bring rebi rth in the lower realms and thinking that the 
next life is more important than this, we ha ve the small motivation. If we clean our room 
while thinking that if we continue to take re birth in cyclic existence there will always be 
suffering and generate the wish to clean the contaminated actions and the afflictions that 
motivate them in order to achieve the stat e of a foe-destroyer, we have the middling 
motivation. If we clean our room while thinking  to clean all the obscurations in general and 
the obscuration of the self-cheri shing mind in particular by me ans of method and wisdom, we 
have the motivation of the maha yana or great vehicle. If we are practicing tantra, while 
cleaning our room we should thin k that we are cleaning the main obstacle to deity yoga – the 
appearance of and adherence to ordinariness – so that whatever we see a ppears as the body of 
the deity, whatever we hear appears as the sp eech of the deity, and whatever thought arises 
appears as the mind of the deity, whereby we hold the clear appearance and divine pride of 
being the deity. 
 With one of these motivations we should clea n our meditation room as well as our sitting 
room, bedroom, bathroom, and so forth.   Here Lama Tsongkhapa says that we should clean as did Serlingpa, whose actual name is 
Dharmakirti. He is called Serlingpa by Tibeta ns because he came from Serling (Island of 
Gold), which is the Tibetan for Sumatra, Indonesia.   In short, with a good motiva tion we should try to clean ou r room of external dirt while 
also cleaning our internal mental dirt.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
125 2B4A-2A1A-2 [Laying out offerings] 
Beautifully lay out an arrangement of offerings that were honestly acquired. 
 
Having cleaned the meditation place, we should  set out a pleasing and beautiful arrangement 
of offerings. These offerings should be unde ceiving. This is mentioned because sometimes 
when we make offerings we are motivated by  the eight worldly dharmas or eight worldly 
concerns, for example, the though t: “Today my benefactor will come to visit me, so I must 
have a nice altar.” This is not right. Also of ferings obtained by cheating or deceiving other 
people are not correct objects to be offered as they are not pure. Also sacrificial offerings of 
dead animals or their blood are not correct offerings. We should only offer beautiful things.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-3 [Sitting posture and mental attitude] 
On a comfortable seat straighten up your body. Assume a suitable position in 
cross-legged or half cross-legged posture, and make sure that your mind is fused 
with going for refuge and bodhicitta. 
 
We should sit on a comfortable seat, our bottom slig htly raised so that our back is straight as 
this straightens the right and left channels and makes meditation easier. If possible we can sit 
in the complete vajra position with the legs cro ssed or in half vajra position. We should sit in 
the seven-point posture of Vair ochana as this leaves imprints to achieve buddhahood.  
 Place the right hand, palm upward, on the le ft palm, representing the union of method 
(male energy) and wisdom (female energy). Put the tips of the thumbs together, forming a 
triangle that represents the fire of inner heat  (tummo). This posture deposits a potential to 
ignite the fire of inner heat that melts the wh ite drop, the nature of water, at the crown, 
which then descends in the central channel to th e navel and secret place, producing joy, with 
which one realizes emptiness. Th is is from the point of view of tantra, therefore this hand 
position has great significance.  Your eyes should be directed toward the ti p of the nose. They should not be completely 
closed as this can cause mental sinking, laxity, and they should not be too wide open as this 
can cause excitement. Since laxity and exciteme nt are the two main obstacles to meditative 
stabilization, the eyes should be  half open and directed toward  the tip of the nose. However, 
there may be other traditions regarding this. For example, I have a friend, a lama of the Kagyu 
tradition, who is perhaps 80 years old now. We stayed in the refugee camp in Buxa together 
many years ago. Once he came to my room, sa t on the bed beside me, and told me that he 
had done the three-year retreat six times. Ba sed on this experience, he advised me not 
meditate with the eyes closed, but rather to ti lt the head slightly backward with the eyes 
looking up into space. I asked him why and he said that this position helps stop the mind 
from wandering. I mentioned to him that the texts, including the Kagyu lam-rim ( Ornament of 
Liberation ), do not say this. He said that this is an instruction based on his, Lama Ugyen’s, 
personal experience that if the eyes are almo st closed, sleep will come due to which one 
cannot meditate. Perhaps because there are differe nt mentalities, there need to be different 
meditation techniques.   
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
126 Having sat down in the correct position, we shou ld mentally go for refuge to the Three Jewels, 
the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, and generate the mind of enlightenm ent that wishes to 
attain enlightenment for the benefit of all se ntient beings. We need to develop such an 
attitude and then recite the prayer of refuge: 
 
I go for refuge to the Guru.   ( lama la kyab su chi o ) 
I go for refuge to the Buddha.   ( sang gye la kyab su chi o ) 
I go for refuge to the Dharma.   ( cho la kyab su chi o ) 
I go for the refuge to the Sangha.  ( ge dun la kyab su chi o ) 
 
Alternatively, we can recite the short version:  
I go for refuge to the guru, the meditational deities, and the Three Jewels. ( lama 
yidam kon chog sum la kyab su chi o ) 
 There is a verse of refuge in the Nyingma tradit ion that instead goes for refuge to the lama, 
the root of blessing; the meditati onal deities, the root of attain ments; and the viras, dakinis, 
and Dharma protectors, the root of enlightened activities.  We should also generate the mind of enlightenment saying:  
I go for refuge until I am enlightened  To the Buddha, Dharma, and Supreme Assembly. By my merit from practicing generosity and the other perfections, May I attain buddhahood for the benefit of all migrating beings. 
 We need to generate the causes to go for refuge . The first cause for going for refuge is fear of 
the suffering of the lower realms, while the second  is faith or confidence in the Three Jewels, 
the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, based on the un derstanding that they can actually help us. 
These are the general causes of going for refuge , whereas the special cause of Mahayana refuge 
is fear of the self-cherishing mind. Seeing that this attitude harms us, we generate the wish to 
give it up and to develop the mind that cher ishes others. Thus, we ask the Buddha, Dharma, 
and Sangha to bless us to be able to do this. Wi th this cause, which should be very strong, we 
go for refuge from the depths of our heart to the Three Jewels.   There are different ways of going for refuge. For example, we can think that the Buddha is 
similar to a doctor, the Dharma is similar to medicine, and the Sangha are similar to nurses, 
while we ourselves are like sick people who stay in  the hospital of cyclic  existence. The doctor 
examines us, identifies our illness, and prescribes the treatment. The nurses help us by giving us injections and so forth. By taking the medi cine, we can be cured and leave the hospital. In 
a similar way, to we who are presently sick in th e hospital of cyclic ex istence, the Buddha gives 
teachings, the Dharma. If we practice it and inte grate it with our mind, purify our negativities 
and accumulate merit with the help of the Sa ngha, our merit and wisdom will increase and 
our mental sickness of the afflictions will dimi nish and gradually be abandoned. Thereby we 
w i l l  b e  c u r e d  a n d  w e  w i l l  l e a v e  c y c l i c  e x i s t e n c e  a n d  g o  t o  n i r v a n a ,  t h e  c i t y  o f  l i b e r a t i o n .  Therefore, wishing to get out of  cyclic existence we should pu t effort into our practice. We 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
127 should not be like someone in hospital who, instead of taking the medicine given by the 
doctor, hides it in a drawer or puts it unde r the mattress! For example, when our venerable 
kind guru, Geshe Yeshe Tobden, was here in  Italy he was given many medicines for 
tuberculosis, but after he left for India I saw that he had put all these medicines under his 
mattress. Doing this, he could not be cured of his illness. If we are sick, we need to follow the 
doctor’s advice and take the medicine he pres cribes. I myself was in hospital for two years 
after I arrived in India in 1959. Whatever medici nes or injections were given me, I took them, 
whereby I was cured. Of course, a complete cure is not always possible as long as we possess a 
contaminated body and have not yet attained a mental body. However, we need to take the 
proper medicine at the right time as advised by  a doctor. Dharma practice is similar in that 
going for refuge protects us from suffering. Th erefore, we need to recognize the Buddha as 
the doctor, the Dharma as the medici ne, and the Sangha as the nurses.  
 Another analogy is to think of the Buddha as being like a university professor, the Dharma 
as being like the subject matter that one studies,  and the Sangha as being like classmates who 
help us study. If we study whatever the profe ssor teaches us and put it into practice, we can 
pass the exams and eventually become a universi ty professor with a Ph.D. Similarly, if we 
practice the Dharma we too will become a buddh a in the future. We should not think that it 
is impossible to achieve buddhahood. Just as by studying well in university we can become a 
qualified professor, so too can we become a buddh a. Thinking in this way, we should go for 
refuge to the Three Jewels.  
 In addition, we should generate the mind of enlightenment that wishes to benefit all 
sentient beings. We can generate  any of the different kinds of mind of enlightenment, either 
that like a king, that like a captain, or that li ke a shepherd or cowboy! Just as a shepherd puts 
all his animals in their stalls and only then goes home himself, similarly someone with the 
mind of enlightenment like a shepherd thinks to set all sentient beings in buddhahood and 
then attain enlightenment himself. Just as a captain or boatman travels across the ocean 
together with all the other people in the boat (it not being that they go first and then he 
f o l l o w s ,  o r  t h a t  h e  g o e s  f i r s t  a n d  t h e n  t h ey go), similarly someone with the mind of 
enlightenment like a captain thinks to attain en lightenment together with all sentient beings. 
Just as one first becomes a king or president in order to make one’s subjects happy, similarly someone with the mind of enlightenment like a king thinks to first attain enlightenment 
himself after which he takes care of all sentie nt beings by leading them to buddhahood. We 
should generate whichever type of mind generation is more comfortable for us.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-4 [Visualisation of the merit field] 
In the space in front of you imagine that  there abide the line age lamas of vast 
conduct and profound view and countles s buddhas, bodhisattvas, arya hearers and 
solitary realizers along with their attend ants; [thus,] visualising the merit field. 
 
Next we should visualize the merit field. There are two types of merit fields, one with Buddha Shakyamuni in the center and one with Lama Ts ongkhapa in the center. In this context we 
should visualize Buddha Shakyamuni surroun ded by the lineage lamas of the vast and 
profound to either side of him as well as the lineage lamas of the blessing behind him. We 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
128 should try to make this visualization clear, su ch that we see each guru with his respective 
mudra, implements, and so forth.  
 In terms of “buddhas,” we can visualize the thirty-five buddhas of confession and the eight 
medicine buddhas, given that it is difficult to visualize all 1,000 buddhas. In terms of 
“bodhisattvas,” there are the eight bodhisattvas  who were the close di sciples of the Buddha 
such as Manjushri, Vajrapani, Kshitigarbha, and so forth. Then there are the sixteen elders or 
foe-destroyers who show the aspect of hearers, although they are not he arers, and the twelve 
solitary realizers. In addition, there are Dh arma protectors such as Mahakala, Kalarupa, 
Shridevi (Palden Lhamo), Vaishravana, and so fo rth. All the Dharma protectors included in 
the merit field are supramundane beings. Those that are mundane beings, such as the Tibetan 
g o v e r n m e n t  o r a c l e  N e c h u n g ,  s h o u l d  n o t  b e  visualized in the merit field. Nechung is 
considered to be a mundane being because he enters others’ bodies and then leaves them 
once again, after which the medium appears to be in a faint. In fact he even refers to himself 
as a spirit. On the other hand, Khadroma, anot her oracle who came to the Institute some 
months ago, who is also called Tsering Che Nga (Five Long Life Sisters), appears as a normal 
human being when she gives predictions. Sh e does not appear to be someone who is 
possessed by a spirit as is the case when Ne chung enters someone’s body, whereupon that 
person foams at the mouth, shakes, and bares his teeth.  
 In short, the Dharma protecto rs in the merit field are supramundane. After visualizing the 
merit field, we should make offerings to it.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-5 [Accumulation of merit and purification of negativities] 
A The reason why it is necessary to accumulate [merit] and purify [negativities] 
B Explanation of accumulation and purification in detail 
C Categorization of the individual [elements] 
2B4A-2A1A-5A The reason why it is necessary to accumulate [merit] and purify [negativities]  
It is exceedingly difficult for paths to ar ise if the favourable conditions for their 
arisal in your continuum, the accumula tions, have not been accumulated, and 
their adverse conditions, the obscurations , have not been purified. Therefore you 
should purify the mind by means of the seven-limb70 practice that brings together 
the key points of accumulation and purification. 
 
We need to purify our negativities and accu mulate merit. We can accumulate merit by 
meditating, doing prostrations, making offerings of water bowls, food, ablution, garments, perfume, and so forth. We can purify our ma ny negative actions by remembering them and 
confessing them. The Tibetan word jang (sbyang ) means to purify, that is, to purify negativities, 
and the word sag (bsag) means to accumulate, that is, accumulate merit.  
 After this we should offer a mandala and make the request:   
I pray that all mistaken attitudes towards the spiritual teacher such as disrespect 
may cease and that all the non-mistaken  attitudes towards him may come about

FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
129 with ease, and I request blessings for a complete pacification of all outer and inner 
obstacles. 
 
We ask that all obstacles or adverse conditions , both the outer and inner, be pacified or 
eliminated, and that all qualities be generate d in our continuum. This will be explained in 
more detail later on.

2B4A-2A1A-5B1A [The limb of prostration] 
1 [Prostration combining the three doors] 
2 [Prostrations of the three individual doors] 
2B4A-2A1A-5B1A-1 [Prostration combining the three doors] 
{37} With regard to the limb of prostr ation, the prostration combining the three 
doors71 is [set out in] the single stanza “Whoever...” This is not [prostration ] to the 
realms of the world of one direction an d to the buddhas of one time. Rather, 
focusing on all the Conquerors residing in the ten directions and three times, you 
prostrate body, speech and mind to them  with respect and from the bottom of 
your heart. 
 We prostrate with the body by completely ex tending the body on the ground in a “long” 
prostration, by touching the five points to the ground (knees, hands, and forehead) in a 
“short” prostration, or by just putting the two hands together in prayer. We prostrate with the 
speech by verbally praising the qualities of the holy beings, the merit field, and so forth, for 
example, saying “I prostrate to tathagata…” We prostrate with th e mind by developing respect. 
This is explained in detail in relati on to the verse cited above from the King of Prayers  
(translated by Jesse Fenton) which says:  
You lions among humans, Gone to freedom in the present, past and future In the worlds of ten directions, To all of you, with body, speech, and sincere mind, I bow down.  
Ji nye su dag chog chü jig ten na 
                                             
 
71 JN: sgo, this has been changed from “gate” to “door” only because this is a more familiar translation at ILTK 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
130 Dü sum sheg pa mi yi seng ge kün 
Dag gi ma lü de dag tam che la Lü dang ngag yi dang we chag gyi o 
 
We should not prostrate to buddhas of the worl ds in just one direction but to the buddha of 
all the worlds in the ten directions. Likewise, we should not prostrate to the buddha of one 
time alone, for example, Buddha Shakyamuni, but to all the buddhas of the three times – 
past, present, and future. For example, we shou ld not prostrate to the buddhas in Tuscany or 
to those in Milan alone! Instead we should pr ostrate to all the buddhas residing in the ten 
directions – those in the east, south, west, nort h, southeast, northeast,  northwest, southwest, 
above, and below. From the bottom of our heart we should recite “To all of you, with body, 
speech, and sincere mind, I bow down,” generating  respect for the conquerors residing in the 
ten directions and three times. In this way, we prostrate resp ectfully with our body, speech, 
and mind.  
 The Tibetan word chag tsel  (phyag ‘tshal ), usually translated as “prostrate” or “ homage,” in 
this context means “I desire to attain all the qualities of knowledge, compassion, and energy 
possessed by all the buddhas of the three times. Therefore, please bestow the qualities of your 
exalted body, speech, and mind upon me.” The qualities of a buddha’s body, speech, and 
mind as well as the quality of a buddha’s enli ghtened activities are set out in the eighth 
chapter of the Ornament for Clear Realizations . Since the buddhas have these qualities and we 
wish to attain them, we request that they be bestowed on us. This is the meaning of 
“prostration” ( chag tsel ).  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-5B1A-2 [Prostrations of the three individual doors] 
As regards the prostrations of the three individual doors, there are three points: 
 
A [Physical prostration] 
Physical prostration [is set out in ] the single stanza “Excellent behaviour...” Focus 
on all the Conquerors belonging to the dire ctions and times as the objects of your 
mind, as though you directly perceive  them. Emanating your own body in 
manifestations as numerous as fine particles, you should prostrate. Furthermore, 
having generated the power of confidence in the excellent conduct of the objects, 
be motivated by that. 
 
The verse cited above from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton) says: 
 
With the energy of aspiration for the bodhisattva way, 
With a sense of deep respect, And with as many bodies as atoms of the world, To all you buddhas visualized as real, I bow down.  
Zang po chö pe mön lam tob dag gi 
Gyel wa tam che yi kyi ngön sum du Zhing gi dül nye lü rab tü pa yi 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
131 Gyel wa kün la rab tu chag tsel lo 
 
We should imagine all the buddhas of the thr ee times – the past, present, and future – and 
the ten directions as if seeing them directly. Th en emanate infinite bodies equal in number to 
the fine particles of the earth. We imagine our bo dy to be huge and prostrate it on the ground 
while imagining that all the manifestations also prostrate with very stro ng faith. In this way 
huge benefit will come to us even though we ourselves only performed one prostration. Thus, 
by the power of our mind, we create infinite me rit by touching our five points, the legs, arms, 
and head, to the ground. Imagining that our body  covers the particles below us down to the 
golden base of this world system, will also  bring about a great collection of merit.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B [Mental prostration] 
Mental prostration [is set out in ] the single stanza “On one fine particle...” On top 
of each minute particle, buddhas as innume rable as dust particles reside encircled 
by bodhisattvas. Generate the resolute faith72 that recollects their positive qualities. 
 
Mentally imagine that on the top of each an d every subtle particle reside many buddhas 
surrounded by numberless bodhisattvas. Reme mbering the qualities of the buddhas and 
bodhisattvas and wishing to attain them, genera te strong faith in them and recite the verse 
cited above from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton): 
 
On every atom are buddhas numberless as atoms, Each amidst a host of bodhisattvas, And I am confident the sphere of all phenomena Is entirely filled with buddhas in this way.  
Dül chig teng na dü l nye sang gye nam 
Sang gye se kyi ü na zhug pa dag 
De tar chö kyi ying nam ma lü pa 
Tam che gyel wa dag gi gang war mö 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C [Verbal prostration] 
Verbal prostration [is set out in ] the single stanza “Praising those...” From each of 
the bodies innumerable heads emanate and also from each of the heads emanate 
innumerable tongues that sing inexhausti ble praises of the objects’ positive 
qualities to sweet melodies. Here “melodies”  are praises, and their “branches,” that 
is, their causes, are the tongues. The word “oceans” refers to a lot. 
 
The verse cited above from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton) says: 
 
                                              
72 JN: mos pa , often translated as “aspiration” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
132 With infinite oceans of praise for you, 
And oceans of sound from the aspects of my voice, I sing the breathtaking excellence of buddhas, 
And celebrate all of you gone to bliss. 
 
De dag ngag pa mi ze gya tso nam 
Yang kyi yen lag gya tsö dra kün gyi Gyel wa kün gyi yön ten rab jö ching De war sheg pa tam che dag gi tö 
 Remember all the qualities of the infinite buddha s and bodhisattvas and recite this verse from 
the King of Prayers . While doing so, we should emanate infinite bodies, each of which has 
infinite heads, and each of which has many tongues, all of which recite praise with a 
melodious tune.   By prostrating in this we way, we create hu ge amount of merit with our body, speech, and 
mind, whereby our qualities and realizations wi ll increase. Also by collecting merit, our 
negative or non-virtuous energy diminishes.   “Branch” can mean “cause” in some contexts, as  it does here. In this case, the cause is the 
tongues, by means of which we offer melodious praises. In this context the word “oceans” 
means “many,” it does not refer to the oceans made of water.   In short, we prostrate with our body, speec h, and mind to all the buddhas of the three 
times who abide in the ten directions, while gene rating faith in them and praising them. In 
such a way, we create a lot of merit.  This is the limb of prostration.

2 [Unsurpassable offerings] 
 
There are two types of offering s: the surpassable and the unsu rpassable. While surpassable 
offerings are good offerings, they can be su rpassed by something that is even better.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-5B1B-1 [Surpassable offerings] 
Surpassable offerings [are set out in ] the two stanzas “The best flowers...” The 
“best flowers” {38} are marvellous specimens of divine and human flowers. 
“Garlands” are many different kinds of flowers mixed and strung together. Both 
together are all actual and imaginary flowers.  
 “Cymbals”73 are the sounds of traditional instruments and the like. “Scented 
unguent”74 is a mixture of fragrant perfumes. “Sublime parasols” are the finest of 
parasols. “Butter lamps” are fragrant, radian t lights of butter or incense as well as 
                                              
73 JN: sil snyan , translated by Jesse Fenton in the verse below as “sweet music” 
74 JN: mar me , translated by Jesse Fenton in the verse below as “sparkling lights” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
133 radiant jewel lights. “Incense” refers to co mbinations of fragrant substances and to 
a single one.  
 
The verse cited above from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton) says: 
 
Beautiful flowers and regal garlands, 
Sweet music, scented oils, and parasols, 
Sparkling lights and sublime incense, 
I offer to you victorious ones.  
Me tog dam pa treng wa dam pa dang 
Sil nyen nam dang jug pa dug chog dang Mar me chog dang dug pö dam pa yi Gyel wa de dag la ni chö par gyi 
 While reciting this verse, we imagine offering beautiful or supreme flowers, for example, in 
the form of flower petals scattered about. We can offer all the flowers of this world, these 
being a type of contaminated offering. We also offer flower garlands, that is, flowers strung 
together on a string that can be hung around  the neck. In India we also see women with 
garlands tied in their hair. We imagine offering  a garland of flowers to each of the infinite 
buddhas in the ten directions. We can offer bo th actual and imaginary flowers, as well as 
artificial flowers made of cloth or plastic.   We can also offer music made with cymbals. In the case of the Vajrayogini practice, for example, there are many different offerings of music. In the West there are many different 
types of musical instruments and Tibetans also have drums, horns, guitars, and cymbals of 
various sizes and shapes. We offer all this musi c to the buddhas of the three times and the ten 
directions, as well as all the bodhisattvas and holy beings, who are delighted and as a result 
generate wisdom in their minds.   We offer unguents, oil, or ointments for the body that are made from fragrant flowers and 
so forth combined with water or oil. In India,  women anoint their chil d’s body with fragrant 
oil and then rub it off, whereby the remaining oi l blocks the pores of the body, this being said 
to make the body healthy.   We also offer the best of parasols or umbr ellas adorned with ornaments and jewels, with a 
staff made from a precious tree, and so fort h. We should not offer normal umbrellas!  
 We offer butter lamps made with melted butte r combined with a fragrance that when lit 
makes light and emits a pleasant fragrance. Ho wever, nowadays there are not many lights 
made from butter! Instead we can offer the ligh t from oil lamps or electric lamps, which are 
less dirty and messy than butterlamps which can also make an unpleasant smell. However, 
because butter is the essence of milk and we ar e nourished by our mother’s milk when we are 
y o u n g ,  b u t t e r  i s  s t i l l  c o n s i d e r e d  t o  b e  a  g o o d  o f f e r i n g .  W e  c a n  a l s o  o f f e r  t h e  l i g h t  f r o m  sparkling jewels, it being said that some part icular jewels can illuminate a dark room.  
 There is a story about such a jewel. In Indi a in the past there was a king who had a wish-
fulfilling jewel that he kept hidden away. Howe ver, one day one of his ministers advised him 
to put the jewel on the top of a hill so that its strong light would illuminate the area and 
people, seeing this, would be amazed whereby he would become even more famous. The king 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
134 agreed to this and the minister took the jewel, but instead of pu tting it on top of the hill he 
put a big block of ice in its plac e. When the sun shone, the ice sparkled, lighting up the sky. 
The king, seeing this, was pleased with his jewe l. However, with the heat of the sun the ice 
slowly melted and the light disa ppeared. The king then sent hi s minister to check what had 
happened. The minister returned and told him, “Your jewel has urinated and escaped! If you 
do not believe me, go and check it out for yourse lf.” In this way he cheated the king of his 
jewel. It is easy to cheat th ose who want to become famous! 
 We also offer incense, that is, the smoke of burning incense. The incense can be made 
from sandalwood, different kinds of herbs, differe nt types of flowers, and so forth, which are 
crushed into a powder and then made into ince nse. Nowadays there are many different types 
of Tibetan incense as well as incense from Chin a, India, and so forth. There is also incense 
that comes from Lawdo (Mountain Stone), Lama Zopa Rinpoche’s birthplace in Nepal where 
in his previous life he meditated in a cave for thirty years. In that life he was a layman who 
told his wife and child that, having done a lot of meditation, he had dedicated all the virtue 
he had created so that in his next life he wo uld be able to make the Buddha-Dharma flourish 
in the world, particularly in the West. In that  life he had been called “Lama Yeshe,” and in 
this life his teacher was “Lama Yeshe.” The La wdo incense, when burned, can stop pollutions 
such as epidemics like SARS. At the time of th is epidemic all this incense was bought by the 
Chinese, such that not a stic k could be found in Lhasa.  
 Incense can also be made of just one subs tance such as sandalwood, which when crushed 
and burnt makes a pleasant smell. However, it can harm those with high blood pressure. Also 
other kinds of incense made from flowers make a pleasant fragrance when burnt.

Since surpassable ones are the offerings of worldly beings, this here is everything

Chö pa gang nam la me gya che wa

 With this single stanza we offer unsurpassable offerings. These offerings mainly consist of the

fifteen days after the new year in order to defe at six well-known Indian teachers of the non-
Buddhist Tirthika school. We can also offer all that  is mentally created such as the earth filled 
with jewels, the eight auspicious signs, the seve n royal emblems, the five objects of the senses, 
and so forth. We can imagine the whole earth as  filled with these offerings and then offer 
them.   We usually put offerings of water, flowers, wh ether real or artificial , and so forth on the 
alter. These offerings are good but the best offe ring is to offer our virtues transformed into
