Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The motivation for prostrations and offerings 
The last two lines are to be joined to all the previous [sets of] two in order to 
complete them. They indicate  the motivation for prostrations and offerings as well 
as their objects. 
 
The last two lines of this verse from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton) say: 
 
With strength of conviction in the bodhisattva way,76 
I offer and bow down to all victorious ones. 
 
Zang po chö la de pe tob dag gi 
Gyel wa kün la chag  tsel chö par gyi 
 In short, we make offerings to all the holy be ings, the conquerors, who exist in all the billion 
world systems. Thus, the object of offering is the conquerors and so fo rth. The motivation for 
prostrating and making offering to them is the wi sh to benefit other sentient beings such that 
their minds change and they develop love, co mpassion, and the mind of enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-5B1C [Confession of negative actions] 
The confession of negative actions [is set out in ] the one stanza “Desire...” In 
dependence on the cause, the three poison s, and by means of the three bases, the 
body etc., their nature is that I have done them (that is, that I have actually created 
them), that I have made others create them, and that I {39} rejoiced in those 
created by others; all of which are gene rally subsumed under “whatever I have 
done.” If through recollecting their faul ts, you regret those done previously and 
sincerely confess them with a mind of re straining from them in the future, the 
increase of deeds done earlier and the continuity of future occurrences will be 
severed. 
 
The verse cited above from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton) says: 
 
Every harmful action I have done 
With my body, speech, and mind 
Overwhelmed by attachment, anger, and confusion, 
All these I openly lay bare before you. 
                                              
76 JN: bzang po spyod pa , literally, “good conduct” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
137  
Dö chag zhe dang ti mug wang gi ni 
Lü dang ngag dang de  zhin yi kyi kyang 
Dig pa dag gi gyi pa chi chi pa 
De dag tam che dag gi so sor shag 
 
We should confess whatever actions of body, sp eech, and mind that we created motivated by 
attachment, hatred, and ignorance. In addition to  confessing those that we ourselves created, 
we should also confess the negative actions that others created due to us ordering them to do 
them. Out of attachment, for example, we actual ly create negative actions but we can also 
cause others to create them. An example of th is is, out of attachment to meat, to ask a 
butcher to kill a particular animal. Although the butcher does the actu al act of killing the 
animal, it is we who ordered him to do so. Anot her example is that of war, for example, the 
war in Iraq in which the American military has killed many people. While an individual 
soldier kills a certain number of people, Pres ident Bush, who gave the order to kill them, 
creates the action of killing all the people who have been killed by the American army in Iraq. 
In fact the Treasury of Manifest Dharma  says that when one country is at war with another one, 
the whole population, even though  they do not all actually figh t and kill, is involved in the 
war due to wishing that their country win and th e other lose. This action of ordering others 
to kill and so forth is very bad negative karma. Therefore, we need to confess all the actions of 
body, speech, and mind motivated by attachme nt, hatred, and ignorance that we ordered 
others to do.   We also need to confess the negative actions of body that we have done out of hatred. For 
example, out of anger we may also have appropriated others belongings, raped their wives, a n d  s o  f o r t h .  W e  h a v e  a l s o  d o n e  n e g a t i v e  a c t i o n s  o f  b o d y  o u t  o f  i g n o r a n c e .  W e  h a v e  a l s o  created negative actions of speech such as lying, ordering others to lie, and so forth. We have also created negative actions of mind, however, these can only be created by ourselves alone as 
we cannot order others to develop covetousness,  malice, or wrong views. In short, we can 
order others to commit negative actions of bo dy and speech, but we cannot order them to 
commit those of mind.   In addition, when others killed many people, we  may have rejoiced in this action, thinking 
t h a t  i t  i s  v e r y  g o o d  t h a t  o u r  e n e m i e s  h a v e  b e e n  k i l l e d .  B y  d o i n g  s o ,  w e  c r e a t e d  n e g a t i v e  actions. On the other hand, if we rejoice in th e good things others do, we create virtue.  
 We need to confess whatever negative acti ons we have created by means of our body, 
speech, and mind. We should remember them, re collect their many disadvantages and faults, 
and develop regret for having done them. Then we should determine to try not to do them 
again in the future. We should develop this re solve from the depths of our heart. Then, we 
should apply a virtuous antidote such as medita tion on love, compassion, a deity, emptiness, 
the selflessness of persons, the selflessness of phenomena, and so forth. We can also recite 
mantras such as OM MANI PADME HUM or the names of holy beings. Physically we can do prostrations or other actions. In this way th e negative karma that we have created can be 
purified and stopped from increasing, and gradually we will be able to stop creating more negative actions.   When we lack conscientiousness and mindfuln ess, we create many non-virtuous actions 
and do not even remember that we did them! Therefore, we should do purification every 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
138 evening before going to bed. As a minimum we should meditate for ten minutes, trying to 
place the mind single-pointedly on this practice . Mentally we should regret whatever negative 
actions we did that day, determine not to do  t h e m  a g a i n ,  g o  f o r  r e f u g e  t o  t h e  B u d d h a ,  
Dharma, and Sangha, generate the mind of enli ghtenment, and recite some mantras and so 
forth. Then at the end we should dedicate. If we do this, we will be able to purify the negative 
karma that we create every day, and, even if we  cannot purify it completely, we will be able to 
stop it from increasing. In this way our virtuo us energy will increase, and our non-virtuous 
energy will decrease.   Attachment, hatred, and ignorance are called “the three mental poisons.” Just as regular 
poison when consumed takes our life, similarly due to our mind being filled with the three 
poisons of attachment, hatred, and ignorance we experience many problems and the of 
liberation is cut.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-5B1D [Rejoicing] 
Rejoicing [is set out in ] the single stanza “The Conquerors of the ten directions...” 
As you recall the benefits of the five persons’ virtue, develop joy. 
 
The verse cited above from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton) says: 
 
I lift up my heart and rejoice in all positive potential 
Of the buddhas and bodhisattvas in ten directions, 
Of solitary realizers, hearers still training and those beyond, 
And of all ordinary beings.  
Chog chü gyel wa kün dang sang gye se 
Rang gyel nam dang lob dang mi lob dang Dro wa kün gyi sö nam gang la yang 
De dag kün gyi je su dag yi rang 
 
We rejoice in the virtue of all the conquerors and their children, the bodhisattvas, abiding in 
the ten directions. We also rejoice in the virtue of  solitary realizers, as well as the virtue of 
hearer learners and no-more-learners. Thus, we rejoice in the virtues of these five types of 
persons in particular, and in the virtues of all ordinary beings in gene ral. While doing so, our 
mind should be happy and joyful, thinking that  their actions are very good and wishing to 
emulate them even though we are not yet able to do so.  
 The five persons are: (1) the buddhas or the conquerors, (2) th eir spiritual children or sons, 
the bodhisattvas, (3) the solitary  realizers, (4) the hearer lear ners, and (5) the hearer no-more-
learners. In addition, we rejoice in the virtue of ordinary sentient beings. In short, we should rejoice in whatever good things others do, and should not be jealous of their good actions. 
Jealousy arises in different ways, but it always disturbs our mind. Theref ore, it is not right. 
Instead we should try to rejoice. For example,  if someone cleans the meditation hall well and 
makes nice offerings on the altar, we should th ink “How wonderful,” and rejoice. In this way, 
we too will create virtue or merit. If we thin k, on the other hand, “He did that because it is 
his job and he must do it,” this  would not be good, although it is what we tend to think.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
139  We should rejoice in whatever good things others do, and should not rejoice in their bad 
actions but instead plead with them not to do such actions again in the future. If we talk 
pleasantly and humbly to them, they may listen  to our advice. However, some people’s ears 
are as if made of rock, nothing goes inside! The Tibetans nomads say that some people are 
like rocks in that, even though a rock is cons tantly immersed in water, it does not become 
soft, and similarly some people who supposedly pr actice the Dharma, stay in a place such as a 
monastery and do recitations and so forth, but their minds remain hard. In other words, even 
though they try to practice th e Dharma they are unable to subdue their mind, which remains 
hard. This is due to not practicing in the right way. Such people are also likened to a leather 
pouch which, even though it is used to hold butte r, is not softened by th e butter. In short, we 
should reduce our afflictions and develop our inner qualities, especially the mind of 
enlightenment, love and compassion, and cherishing others.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-5B1E [Requesting for the wheel of Dharma to be turned] 
Requesting for the wheel of Dharma to be turned [is set out in ] the single stanza 
“In the ten directions...” You exhort those who have awoken to complete 
buddhahood in the fields of the ten dire ctions, who are detached and have found 
unobstructed exalted knowledge, to te ach the Dharma through emanating that 
same number of bodies for a long time. 
 
The verse cited above from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton) says: 
 
You who are the bright lights of worlds in ten directions, 
Who have attained a buddha’s omniscienc e through the stages of awakening, 
All you who are my guides, Please turn the supreme wheel of Dharma.  
Gang nam chog chü jig ten drön ma dag 
Jang chub rim par sang gye ma chag nye Gön po de dag dag gi tam che la Kor lo la na me pa kor war kül 
 The fifth limb is to request the buddhas to turn  the wheel of Dharma. More precisely, we ask 
the enlightened buddhas abiding in the pure lands of the ten directions, who are without 
obscurations and have achieved omniscience, to  turn the wheel of Dh arma since they know 
the mentalities of the different sentient beings  and whether or not they are ripe, and whether 
they are ripe but are not yet liberated. In short,  we ask them to turn the wheel of Dharma in 
different ways to suit the needs of these differe nt sentient beings. We ask them to manifest 
many emanation bodies in order to appear to the different sentient beings in the different 
worlds and give them different teachings.   It is said that Buddha Shakyamuni turned the wheel of Dharma for the first time in 
Sarnath where he taught the four noble truths, he turned the second wheel on Vulture Peak 
Mountain where he taught the Perfection of Wisdom according to the Middle Way, and he 
turned the third wheel in Vaishali. This presen tation is from the point of view of sutra, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
140 however from the point of view of tantra he al so appeared in other places in other forms in 
order to teach the various tantras.  
 At the end of the first teachi ng on the four noble truths, due to the power of the Buddha, 
the five ascetics spontaneously achieved ordination and simultaneously achieved the path of 
seeing, that is, they realized true paths. When Prince Siddhartha first left the palace and went 
to meditate by the Narayana River, he spent six years in meditation and strict ascetic practices 
in the company of five other ascetics. However, as a result of this he became very weak and 
eventually when a young woman offered him yo gurt and fruit he ate it, whereby his body 
regained its strength. Seeing this, the five ascetics thought that his determination had 
weakened and, leaving him behind, they went from Bodhgaya to Sarnath, where they stayed 
in a kind of cave under an overhanging rock. After Buddha Shakyamuni had attained 
enlightenment under the bodhi tree, he remained in meditation for a further forty-nine days. 
He t hen  t rav eled fro m  Bo dhgay a t o  Sarn ath. When the five ascetics saw him coming, they 
agreed among themselves not to stand up as a sign of respect when he approached, even 
though one of them protested saying that sinc e Siddhartha was the son of a king they should 
stand up, even though he had not attained hi gher realizations. However, when the Buddha 
arrived nearby, they immediately stood up desp ite themselves. He spoke with them for a bit 
and then they went together to Deer Park, to  the spot where there is now a stupa. As the 
Buddha was willing to teach, the ascetics built him a kind of throne out of rocks. There, 
Brahma of the Thirty-three and Indra of the First Concentration requested the Buddha to 
turn the wheel of Dharma. He chose a special day to teach and on that particular day gave the 
teaching on the four noble truths. Thus, the Buddh a first taught true sufferings, then the true 
origins of suffering, the true cessations of suff ering, and the true path s to the cessation of 
sufferings. For example, with re spect to true sufferings he ta ught the four attributes of 
impermanence, suffering, empty, and selfless. Du e to this, some of his listeners realized 
selflessness, some realized empty (not the emptin ess of inherent existence), and some realized 
impermanence, that things ch ange momentarily. An example of impermanence is that 
sometimes we are born in the Peak of Exis tence and sometimes in the lowest hell of 
Unrelenting Torment, that is, we go up and do wn, up and down. This is  the very nature of 
cyclic existence. It is impermanent, always changing. In terms of suffering, there is the 
suffering of suffering, the suffering of change , and pervasive suffering. Due to the Buddha’s 
teaching on true sufferings, some of his listen ers recognized sufferings as sufferings. He then 
taught their cause, this being true origins – th e two, actions and afflictions. Since the root 
cause is the afflictions, we need to abandon th em. By doing so, we can achieve true cessations. 
How can we do this? By meditating true paths, fo r example, meditating on selflessness. In this 
way the root of cyclic existence, ignorance or the view of the transitory collection of I and 
mine, can be eliminated. The Buddha gave this as  his first teaching in this world, and we too 
can still study and realize it.  
 The second wheel of Dharma was that of the perfection of  wisdom, in which the Buddha 
taught the emptiness of true existence or inherent existenc e of the 108 phenomena ranging 
from form through a knower of all aspects. Being empty of true  existence, they do not exist 
independently or concretely. Rather, they exist dependently because they are dependent-relations. This is the teaching in which the Bu ddha explicitly set out emptiness and implicitly 
set out the activities of bodhisa ttvas, the five paths, ten grounds,  the eight categories of clear 
realizations, and so forth. In this way, taught the profound explicitly and the vast implicitly. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
141 This teaching, from the point of view of te nets, is according to the Madhyamika (Middle 
Way) school.  
 The Buddha taught the third wheel of Dharma  from the point of view of the Chittamatra 
tenets, mainly the emptiness of duality of ob jects and object-possessors. This was for the 
purpose of leading disciples who, from the point of view of their tenets , were Chittamatrins. 
This school belongs to Mahayan a whereas the Vaibhashika and Sa utrantika schools belong to 
the Hinayana. Although from the point of view  of tenets someone is a Hinayanist, that 
person can still develop the mind of enlightenm ent. Likewise, there are people who from the 
point of view of tenets are Mahayanists but th ey develop renunciation and do not develop the 
mind of enlightenment, whereby they become hearers.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-5B1F [Supplication] 
Supplication [is set out in ] the single stanza “Showing the passage into nirvana...” 
You supplicate those who show how to pass be yond sorrow in the fields of the ten 
directions to stay, not passing into nirvan a for as many eons as there are particles 
of dust in the universe in order to bring about the benefit and happiness of 
migrating beings. 
 The verse cited above from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton) says: 
 
With palms together I earnestly request: 
You who may actualize parinirvana, Please stay with us for eons numberless as atoms of the world, For the happiness and well-being of all wanderers in samsara  
Nya ngen da tön gang zhe de dag la 
Dro wa kün la pen zhing de we chir 
Kel pa zhing gi dül nye zhug par yang 
Dag gi tel mo rab jar söl war gyi 
 We request all the holy beings, the buddhas and so forth, who abide in the ten directions to 
not show the aspect of passing beyond sorrow, th at is, of entering nirvana, but to remain in 
this world for the happiness and benefit of all mi grating beings. In short, we plead with them 
saying, “Please, please do not pass beyond cycl ic existence into nirvana, but remain for eons 
equal to the particles of dust of the universe.” In other words, we request them to not die but to remain here with us. While “nirvana” refe rs to the state of complete abandonment of 
suffering, in normal everyday language to ask so meone to not pass into  nirvana is to say, 
“Please do not die. Please remain here.” For example, we can request our parents, the pope, 
and so forth to not die but to continue to live. However, here we specifically request the holy 
beings to remain for a long time in order to benefit sentient beings. Here we are not talking 
about the holy beings of Buddhism, Christiani ty, Islam, and so forth, we are requesting 
whatever holy beings exist to remain. Thus, we request all the people with good qualities to 
remain, that is, to not pass away. Similarly, as an auspicious gesture when our teacher is sick, 
we ask him to remain for eons, even though in fact no one can live for eons!  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
142  
Thursday afternoon, 3 February 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-5B1G [Dedication] 
The limb of dedication [is set out in ] the single stanza “Prostrations and...” All the 
roots of virtue represented by the virt ues of the previous six limbs are made 
common [property] of all sentient beings an d never come to an end since they have 
been dedicated with strong determin ation to complete enlightenment. 
 
The verse cited above from the King of Prayers  (translated by Jesse Fenton) says: 
 
Whatever slight positive po tential I may have created, 
By paying homage, offering, and acknowledging my faults, 
Rejoicing, and requesting that  the buddhas stay and teach, 
I now dedicate all this for full awakening.  
Chag tsel wa dang chö ching shag pa dang 
Je su yi rang kül zhing söl wa yi Ge wa chung ze dag gi chi sag pa Tam che dag gi jang chub chir ngo o 
 By means of the first six limbs of prostration, offering, confession, rejoicing, the exhortation 
to turn the wheel of Dharma, and the request or supplication to remain, we have created 
virtue. In addition, we have created virtues from  beginningless time in cy clic existence. All of 
these we now dedicate that they may become the cause for all sentient beings to attain 
buddhahood. This is the right way to dedicate our virtue.   There are three possibilities regarding that whic h is a dedication and that which is a prayer:  
1. that which is a prayer but not a dedication, 
2. that which is both a prayer and a dedication, and 
3. that which is neither a prayer nor a dedication.  
This is because when we say a prayer it is not necessary to have accumulated virtue. We 
merely make a request to be bestowed blessings,  for obstacles to be eliminated, and so forth. 
Such requests are prayers but not dedications since there is no substance (virtue) that has 
been created and is dedicated for a particular purpose. Thus, in order for it to become a dedication there must exist a substance, that is , virtue, that can be dedicated. When someone 
makes an offering for a puja, for example, and we  dedicate for their success and so forth, this 
is both a dedication and a prayer. There is no dedication that is not a prayer, however there 
are prayers that are not dedications, for exam ple the prayer “May all sentient beings achieve 
enlightenment.”  When we dedicate our virtue to enlightenment, the virtue will not be exhausted as long as all sentient beings have not attained enlighte nment. In other words, when we make such a 
dedication the virtue is never exhausted but continues to remain. For this reason, with a 
strong motivation or aspiration we should dedi cate whatever virtuous actions we create that 
all sentient beings may achieve buddhahood.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
143  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4A-2A1A-5B2 The need to take the meaning of the words to heart 
If you thus develop an understanding of the meaning of these words and gently do 
as was taught without allowing your mind  to wander, you will hold immeasurable 
heaps of merit. 
 
We should understand the me aning of the words of the King of Prayers  from the depths of our 
heart and keep that meaning in mind without fo rgetting it. Thus, with an undistracted mind 
we should quietly and slowly try to recall the meaning of the words while reading and reciting 
them. When doing prostrations, remember the ob jects to whom we are prostrating, the merit 
field; when making offerings, remember to offer both the surpassable and unsurpassable 
offerings; when confessing, remember to puri fy one’s actions of body, speech, and mind 
created out of attachment, hatred, and ignora nce; when rejoicing, remember the virtues 
created by others; when requesting to turn th e wheel of Dharma and supplicating to remain 
in the world and not pass into nirvana, but to remain as long as cyclic existence lasts, 
remember the meaning of these verses; and then dedicate the merit or virtue accumulated by 
doing the previous six limbs. In this way, reci te all the words slowly while remembering their 
meaning.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-5C Categorization of the individual [elements] 
Accordingly the five limbs of prostrating,  offering, requesting, {40} supplicating, 
and rejoicing constitute the collection of  accumulations. Confessing purifies 
obscurations. One aspect of rejoicing, th e cultivation of joy in one’s own virtue, 
multiplies the virtue. By means of dedicati ng, the virtues of collecting, purifying, 
and multiplying, however small, are multip lied manifold, and what would come to 
an end after producing a temporary effect is  made inexhaustible. In brief, there are 
the three: (1) accumulation, (2) purifica tion, and (3) multiplication and making 
inexhaustible.  
 
The limbs of prostrating, offering, rejoicing, requesting, and supplicating are the collection of 
merit. The limb of confession purifies obscurat ions. Rejoicing in the virtues that we created, 
thinking “I did good,” as well as in those created by others, multiplies our virtue. Dedication increases our virtue and makes it inexhaustible, th at is, makes it such that it will not come to 
an end but will continue to remain as long  as all sentient beings have not attained 
enlightenment, that is, as long as cyclic existence is not emptied.  
 In brief, by means of the seven limbs we colle ct or accumulate virtues, purify, and increase 
our virtues such that they cannot be exhausted. In fact, whenever we engage in any virtuous 
activity we should dedicate the virtue created as this causes ou r virtue to not be exhausted, 
that is, to not be stolen by the thieves of an ger and wrong view. Thus, when we dedicate our 
virtue for the benefit of all sentient beings, it is like putting money in the bank in order to 
protect it from being stolen by thieves.   
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
144 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1A-6 [Mandala offering and request for blessings] 
Then, with a clear visualisation of the ob jects, offer a mandala and with strong 
aspiration supplicate many times: “I pray that all mistaken attitudes towards the 
spiritual teacher such as disrespect m ay cease and that all the non-mistaken 
attitudes towards him may come about with ease, and I request blessings for a 
complete pacification of all outer and inner obstacles.” 
 The object of the mandala offering is the merit field of the lam-rim, th e stages of the path, 
which was visualized previously. We should visual ize this merit field clearly. In the center is 
Buddha Shakyamuni surrounded by the lamas, medi tational deities, buddhas, bodhisattvas, 
solitary realizers, hearer foe-destroyers, daka s and dakinis, and Dharma protectors. Try to 
imagine all of them in front of you. You can think of the merit field as being like a busy 
market filled with people in which someone is standing on a table selling things! This is 
because Buddha Shakyamuni is on a throne that is higher than  the others and is surrounded 
by the other holy beings of the merit field. We  should try to imagine all of them, but if we 
cannot do that we can just visualize Buddha Shak yamuni, thinking that he is the embodiment 
of the lamas, meditational deities, sangha, dakas and dakinis, and Dharma protectors. We 
should visualize the image of Buddh a Shakyamuni very clearly, not like a statue but as a living 
buddha. Then we should offer a mandala.  There are many different mandala offerings. The short mandala offering consists of the 
seven which are located on the foundation: Mo unt Meru, the four cont inents, the sun, and 
the moon. We should visualize them and offe r them to the merit field with the verse: 
 
This ground anointed with perfume and strewn with flowers,  Adorned with Mount Meru, the four continents, sun, and moon,  I offer, visualized as a buddha field.  May all beings enjoy this completely pure realm.  
Sa zhi pö kyi jug shing me tog tram 
Ri rab ling zhi nyi de gyen pa di Sang gye zhing du mig te ul war gyi Dro kun nam dag zhing la chö par shog 
 We dedicate this offering that all migrating beings may achieve a pure land. Then we offer the 
mandala with: IDAM GURU RATNA MANDALAKAM NIRYATAYAMI.  In order to actually do a mandala offering, take  up the mandala base in the left hand while 
holding a bit of rice. With the right hand, put a small heap of rice in the center of the base 
and then rub it in a clockwise circle three time s with the inside of the wrist. When rubbing 
the base the first time, think that you are cleani ng your negative actions of body, killing and 
so forth, motivated by the three mental poisons.  When rubbing it the second time, think that 
you are cleaning your negative actions of speec h, lying and so forth, motivated by the three 
mental poisons. When rubbing it the third time , think that you are cleaning your negative 
actions of mind, covetousness and so forth, motivated by the three mental poisons.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
145  Then do the opposite. Clean the base three ti mes while rubbing it in anticlockwise circles, 
meanwhile thinking that you respectively attain  the virtuous actions of body, not killing and 
so forth; the virtuous actions of speech, not ly ing and so forth; and the virtuous actions of 
mind, non-covetousness and so forth, motiva ted by non-attachment, non-hatred, and non-
ignorance. Then put the larger ring on the gold en base, representing the iron fence. As you 
recite the long or short mandala offering, pile up heaps of rice representing the various 
offerings. For example, when doing the long mandal a, put a pile in each of the four directions 
for the continents, then put a pile to each side  of the continents for the sub-continents, then 
put a pile on top of the continents for the qualit ies of the four continents, and so forth. I will 
not go into detail about this now. In short, we should try to imagine offering the entire 
mandala on the base.   Then, if you do not have a proper base, you can merely make the mudra of offering a mandala, with the fingers representing Mount Me ru and the four continents, as we usually do 
during pujas. The word “mandala” means “taking the essence,” that is, taking the essence of 
the Dharma.   After the mandala offering, make the request:  “I pray that all mistaken attitudes towards 
the spiritual teacher such as disrespect may cease and that all the non-mistaken attitudes 
towards him may come about with ease, and I requ est blessings for a complete pacification of 
all outer and inner obstacles.” We should re cite this request many times with fervent 
aspiration. In this way, we ask that all the mistak en attitudes, such as disrespect, that arise in 
our mind toward the spiritual friend may cease,  that is, that they may completely stop. We 
should always generate respect in our mind for our virtuous spiritual friends, our mother and 
father, and sick people. Then we request blessi ngs that all interferences inner and outer may 
be pacified. This last request is general.   Those who, having received an empowerment,  practice tantra must offer a mandala three 
times during the day and three times at ni ght. This can be done by reciting the Six Session 
Guru Yoga  three times in the morning and three times in the evening (that is, in the morning, 
at midday, in the afternoon, at evening, at nigh t, and before dawn). However, because this is 
difficult to do, we can just do it three times in the morning and three times in the evening. 
Alternatively, those who are very busy can do all six times together. This is just my idea, I have 
not heard it from anyone else. I think this w ay because our modern life is very busy. We 
always have something that needs to be done and,  if we do not do it, we will not be able to 
buy food or clothing and may even die of hunger!   
Previously the Middle Lam-Rim said “In Exhortation to the Superior Intention  two groups of 
twenty advantages of giving the Dharma in th e absence of worldly things and the concern for 
gain, respect, and the like are set forth.” I want to return to this subject as I found the twenty 
in the Great Exposition of th e Stages of the Path  (Snow Lion Publications , Ithaca, 2000, page 63) 
which says: 
 
For, as the Exhortation to Wholehearted Resolve  says:  
 
Maitreya, there are twenty benefits of the generosity with which you 
give a gift of the teaching without co ncern for material things, profit, or 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
146 honor. What are the twenty? They are as follows. You will come to 
have: 1) recollection, 
2)intelligence, 
3) understanding, 
4) stability, 5) wisdom, 
6) supramundane wisdom, 
7) little desire, 8) little hatred, and 9) little ignorance. Also, 10) demons will not find an opportunity to harm you; 11) the bhagavan buddhas will think of you as precious, only child; 
12) non-humans will guard you; 
13) deities will bestow charisma and power on you; 14) enemies will find no opportunity to harm you; 
15) friends will remain steadfast; 16) your words will be trusted; 17) you will attain fearlessness; 18) you will have abundant happiness; 19) you will be praised by the wise; and 20) you will gift of the teaching will be worthy of remembrance.  
 When we teach we should abandon doing so fo r fame, wealth, and so forth and instead we 
should have the motivation wishing to benefit other beings. In this way we will acquire these 
twenty qualities. For example, we will come to  recollect or remember everything, that is, we 
will possess mindfulness. We will be intelligent, that is, we will be able to discriminate what 
we should do and what we should not do, whereb y we will be able to do what is important 
and abandon the unimportant. In addition, we will have less attachment, less hatred, and less 
ignorance, this being the opposi te of their increase. These tw enty qualities are important. 
 I also wanted to say something more about the nine attitudes necessary for relying on a guru 
which are mentioned in the Great Exposition of the Stages of the Path  (see Snow Lion 
Publications, Ithaca, 2000, page 79). These nine are: 1. the attitude which is like a dutiful child, 
2. the attitude which is like diamond, 
3. the attitude which is like the earth, 
4. the attitude which is like foothills,  
5. the attitude which is like a worldly servant, 
6. the attitude which is like a sweeper, 
7. the attitude which is like a foundation, 
8. the attitude which is like a dog, and 
9. the attitude which is like a ferry.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 31 January-3 February 2005 (03) 
147 A diamond or vajra has the characteristic of not changing. We, like a diamond or vajra, 
should be able to bear whatever the guru give s us to do. The earth ha s the characteristic of 
not complaining no matter what we do to it. We too should be like this. A dog has the 
characteristic of still following us even after we beat it. We too should be like this, that is, if 
the teacher beats us we should escape for a bit and then return! You can read this section in 
the Great Exposition of the Stages of the Path .

Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
148 Tuesday morning, 22 February 2005 
 (No teachings 7-21 February 2005 due to th e annual Losar break and group retreat) 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 40) says: 
2B4A-2A1B What to do during the main part 
1 How to sustain [meditation] in general 
2 How to sustain [the meditation] of this context 
The second one has two points: how to sust ain [meditation] in general and in this 
context. 
2B4A-2A1B-1 How to sustain [meditation] in general 
A Identification of and need for meditation 
First: Meditation on the path as it will be  explained serves to make you fit to make 
use of any virtuous object you wish.  
 
First of all we should choose an object of medi tation that is virtuous, that is, one that by 
meditating single-pointedly on it, we will deve lop more virtuous energy and our mind will 
become stronger in the practice of virtuous ac tivities. This what is meant by identifying the 
object of meditation.  
 Meditation on the path will be explained la ter on. There are different types of paths such 
as the five: the path of accumula tion, path of preparation, path of seeing, path of meditation, 
and path of no-more-learning. There is also the pa th of a being of small capacity, the path of a 
being of middling capacity, and the path of a bein g of great capacity. In the context of the text 
we are studying, “meditation on the path ” mainly refers to the latter three.  
 When meditating the path of a being of small capacity, we first need to recognize that this 
human rebirth endowed with the eighteen qualities is precious, that we have presently 
obtained such a rebirth, and that it is very us eful. We should identify each of the eighteen 
qualities, the eight freedoms and ten endowments , with respect to ourselves, whereby we will 
become very happy thinking, “I am very fortunat e. I can achieve whatever I desire.” However, 
while we have presently found this precious hu man rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities, 
i t  w i l l  b e  d i f f i c u l t  t o  f i n d  s u c h  a  r e b i r t h  a g a i n .  F o r  e x a m p l e ,  e v e n  t h o u g h  t h e r e  a r e  m a n y  
human beings in this world, those who have a precious human rebirth are very few. And 
among them, those who are interested in the next life are even fewer, as most people are only 
interested in obtaining the happinesses of th is life, wealth, fame, and so forth. Out of 
attachment to such things, we constantly strive to obtain them but due to interferences we do 
not always manage to attain them, whereby we become sad and even mad. Some people even 
jump to their death from a bridge into a river or from the upper window of a high building. 
This is caused by dissatisfaction. Therefore, we need to try to have less thought for this life, 
and to think more of the future life. To do th is we need to prove the existence of past and 
future lives by thinking, for example, about the fact that this flower comes from a seed, which 
came from a flower, which came from a seed, and so forth. Without the seed, the flower 
could not have grown. We need to practice vi rtuous actions that will bring the result of 
happiness.  
 So what is the object of meditation? We shou ld examine this. In our daily life, even if our 
practice is not perfect due to ou r mental afflictions, we should to try to place our mind single-
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
149 pointedly on our object of practice. For example, when reciting a prayer, sadhana, or mantra, 
we should try to focus our mind on it, without thinking about other things. This in general is 
important but in our case when we try to medita te or visualize, one part of our mind has the 
tendency to be thinking, “I need to do this. I fo rgo t t o do  t hat .” This  is  n o t s o  goo d. We 
should give up such thoughts.  
 
B The faults of bad meditation 
Moreover, if you attend to this and that  object, thinking to take merely this 
number of virtuous objects and in any orde r you wish, nothing will come of it and 
having gone wrong from the beginning, your spiritual practice77 will continue to be 
faulty throughout your life.  
C How to sustain good meditation 
Therefore, you should first of all deve lop certainty concerning the number and 
order of objects to be sustained. Then, you should unleash {41} a strong driving 
force that does not allow thoughts to aris e other than what has been ascertained, 
and sustain with mindfulness and alertness78 what has been ascertained without 
addition or omission. 
 When we meditate, first we need to recognize th e objects to be meditated, and then ascertain 
the number of objects to be meditated and th e order in which they are to be meditated. 
Having completely understood and remembering this, we should think to do a particular 
meditation or practice without allowing our mind  to go to other objects. At the beginning we 
should make a strong determination or develop a strong motivation thinking, for example, “I 
will only think of this precious human rebirth. I will focus on this alone. I will not allow my 
mind to go to other objects.”   Two mental factors are necessary for medita tion, one is mindfulness and the other is 
introspection. We need to try to have both of them present in our mind at all times. Instead 
when forgetfulness, distraction, and so forth arise, we should st op them. In this way we will be 
able to do whatever we need to do.

150 B How to train in faith by means of thinking about his positive qualities 
Then, repeatedly resolve never to allow your  mind to get lost in conceiving faults 
in the guru and think of the qualities like  discipline and meditative stabilization79 
that you yourself have perceived in him, cu ltivating the faith that is a mind having 
the aspect of faith until it arises.  
 
If you rely on a guru there will be benefits or good results, and if you do not rely on him, for example, on Buddha Shakyamuni, there will be faults or bad results. We should think about this and then contemplate the benefits of re lying on a guru, for example, the gaining of 
knowledge. Whether we rely on a Dharma teac her or on a mundane teacher who teaches us, 
for example, how a particular machine is made, how to use a computer, how electricity works, 
and so forth, we can gain knowledge. Thus, by relying on a teacher we can gain knowledge, which we can then use to achieve our financia l aims, fame, and so forth. Alternatively by 
relying on a Dharma teacher and thereby de veloping the mind of enlightenment or 
compassion, we can continue in future lives to benefit others by means of the virtue we have 
accumulated. Therefore, reliance on a teacher brings benefit. If, on the other hand, without 
relying on a teacher we try to learn things alon e, most likely many mistakes will occur.  
 I n  s h o r t ,  w e  n e e d  t o  t h i n k ,  i n  t h i s  p a r t i c ular context, of the qu alities of the guru. The 
Sanskrit word guru is composed of two syllables: gun, which means good qualities, and rup, 
which means heavy. Therefore the word guru means “heavy in good qualities.” By relying on 
s u c h  a  g u r u ,  w e  t o o  w i l l  b e  a b l e  t o  g a i n  t h e  s a m e  g o o d  q u a l i t i e s .  T h e r e f o r e ,  w e  n e e d  t o  develop faith and confidence in the guru, un derstanding that what he teaches us is 
unmistaken and that, by acting in accordance wi th what he says, we too will develop similar 
qualities.   On the other hand, we should not look for faults and mistakes in the guru. Instead we 
should look at his positive side and not chec k his negative side, thinking, for example, “He 
has such-and-such a fault. He does not meditate perfectly. He acts badly. He takes care of 
some people and not others.” It is best not to do this. Instead we should think that the guru 
has good qualities such as morality, concentrat ion, and wisdom. In other words, he possesses 
the three higher trainings in morality, concentr ation, and wisdom. The base of the latter two 
is morality or ethical discipline. Morality in Sanskrit is shila, which means coolness . The name 
indicates that by practicing morality it brin g coolness, that is, freedom from mental and 
physical suffering. The practice of morality me ans to discipline or subdue ourselves. Even 
politicians talk about the need for morality. It  should be the main basis of our practice. 
Morality implies a natural rule, not a set of rules created by someone. We need to have 
respect for such natural rules.  Concentration means to set our mind single-p ointedly on whatever we are doing, whether 
it is working or meditating. If we set our mind  on what we are doing, we will do it well, 
whereas if we are distracted we will make many  mistakes. Thus, concentr ation is helpful at all 
times. We need to understand that the guru  possesses the quality of concentration. Then, 
wishing to develop it in our ow n continuum, we should follow hi s advice and meditate on the

151 stages of the path. Thinking about his qualities,  we should make them clearly appear to our 
mind and then develop faith or confidence in him. We should do continuous meditation on 
this until we develop this faith. For exampl e, we can meditate on Buddha Shakyamuni as 
being our teacher who, since he gave us the teac hings that we are now trying to follow, is a 
perfect guru. We should try to understand his qualities of body, speech, and mind and 
develop faith or confidence that he can lead us to enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Training in the respect that remembers kindness 
Then you should consider, in accordance with the above quotations from the sutra 
collection, the [instances of] beneficial kindness that you received and are still 
receiving and sincerely cultivate respect until it arises. 
 
We should remember the kindness of the gu ru and thereby develop respect for him by 
thinking that he has benefited us in the past, is  benefiting us now, and will benefit us in the 
future, due to which he is very kind. Similarly, our mother has been kind to us in the past, is 
kind to us now, and will be kind to us in th e future. We should remember this kindness, not 
thinking that she is bad and against us and th erefore not wanting to see her. This is a bad 
attitude and a fault. Our mother has benefited us , is benefiting us, and will benefit us. She is 
constantly thinking of her son or daughter. Howe ver, because she is also an ordinary being, 
sometimes she may get angry and talk badly to us or beat us a bit. But even so it would not 
right to think that she is bad. If she says bad words to us it is becaus e she wants to teach us 
how to change our attitude. Therefore, we should  think of her words as instructions. This is 
important in our daily life.   Buddhism teaches that all sentient beings ha ve been our mothers in the past. This is 
because having taken rebirth from beginningless time until now, for example, having been 
born infinite times as human beings, in each of these rebirths we needed a mother. Therefore, there is no one whom  we can point to and say that  he or she has not been our 
mother. Thus, all sentient beings have been our mother. At that time th ey were very kind to 
us. In addition, even when they were not our mo ther they were very kind to us. We should 
remember their kindness and then develop the wish  to repay it. In a similar way, our teacher 
has been kind to us, therefore we need to de velop respect for him and the wish to repay his 
kindness by helping him with our body and speech. This is good to do.  
 We should think about the previous citation s from sutras and meditate continually on 
them until respect arises in our mind throug h remembering the kindness of the guru. It is 
said that the best offering to the guru is ou r practice or meditation. Offering him money, 
food, clothing, and so forth is an offering, but the best is to try to practice and meditate as he 
has explained. We can imagine that our virtues are transformed into precious wish-fulfilling 
gems and then offer them to the guru.

152 Third: By means of the Prayer of Good Behaviour  and the Seventy Aspirations  the 
accumulated virtue should be dedicate d with strong determination to the 
temporary and ultimate aspirations. 
 
When we begin to do a practice we should genera te a good motivation and at the end of it we 
should dedicate the virtue accumulated. Th e dedication can be done by reciting the Prayer of 
Good Behavior , also called the Samantabhadra Prayer  and the King of Prayers , or the Seventy 
Aspirations , a prayer written by an Indian that ha s seventy verses. There are also short 
dedication prayers like:  
Just as the brave Manjushri and Samantabhadra, too, Realized things as they are, I, too, dedicate all these merits in the best way, 
That I may follow their perfect example.  I dedicate all these roots of virtue With the dedication praised as the best By the victorious ones thus gone of the three times, So I might perform good works. 
 It is necessary to make such dedications because by dedicating the virtue  that we have created 
to become the cause for all sentient beings to achieve buddhahood, then later on, even if 
hatred or anger arise in our mind, they will not be able to destroy this virtue. Instead, by dedicating our virtue for this cause, it will re main until all sentient be ings achieve nirvana or 
enlightenment. Therefore, it is very good to  dedicate. If, on the other hand, we do not 
dedicate our virtue, when we get angry our ange r will destroy our virtues such that they no 
longer exist. Anger is likened to a fire. Even the tiny fire at the end of a burning cigarette butt 
can burn down thousands of acres of fields and forests and many buildings. It burns whatever 
it comes into contact with. Similarly, anger is like  fire in that even one single instant of anger 
can destroy a great amount of virtue. Just as a good seed that has been burnt by fire will not 
bring the result of a sprout, similarly anger bu rns our virtues such that they cannot bring a 
result. For this reason, we need to immediately dedicate our virtue. However, if we cannot do 
that, then in order to purify negativities comm itted during that day, we should before going 
to bed set aside ten or twenty minutes (or even just five minutes) to recite, for example, OM 
MANI PADME HUM or OM TARE TUTTARE  TURE SVAHA seven times, or OM 
VAJRASATTVA HUM twenty-one times, combined  with the determination to not do those 
negativities again and the dedication of our virt ue. If we do this, it will definitely bring the 
result of happiness. When, I don’t know! However, it will really bring happiness.   For example, it is like having two bags, one filled with our black negativities that is very heavy to carry and the other that is very light as it contains only a few virtues. There is a story 
of a meditator who put a heap of black stones and a heap of white stones in front of him. When a non-virtuous mind arose,  he would set aside a black st one and when a virtuous mind 
arose, a white stone. At first there were many  black stones and few white stones, but then 
gradually he became able to set aside more wh ite stones until they were equal to the black 
stones, and then by continuing to practice he  eventually came to have many more white 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
153 stones than black stones. We too in our every day life need to generate a good motivation 
when we get up, thinking that whatever we do  that day we will do for the benefit of all 
sentient beings, that is, in the service of all other human beings. In other words we will work 
for them even if we do not receive a salary for doing this! If whatever we do is done with a 
motivation thinking of others, then even whil e we are at work we will create virtue.  
 Two things are said to be important, one at  the beginning and one at the end. It is the 
motivation that is important at the beginning. Our motivation should be that of a mind of 
enlightenment, compassion, and renunciation or definite emergence. At the end it is 
important to dedicate. Whatever we do, whethe r practice or work, we should dedicate the 
virtue that has been created. Even when cleaning , if we do so with the motivation to clean our 
mental afflictions, we will create virtue. Th ere is a story about a monk who joined my 
kangtsen in Sera some two hundred years ago. It  is said that he did not study but spent his 
time cleaning the area outside the monks’ ho uses where the monks would go to urinate. 
When he became old, he returned to his birt h place, which was called Manghi, and built a 
small house where he meditated and did retrea t for some years. He eventually passed away 
and when he was cremated there appeared a stat ue of Chakrasamvara, about a meter high, in 
the ashes! After that, he was called him “M anghi Dorje Chang” (Vajradhara of Manghi). 
Although he had never studied but had only cl eaned day and night, having returned home, 
done retreat, and died, he came to be calle d Manghi Dorje Chang. When his incarnation 
died, there also appeared a perfect statue, perhap s of Kalachakra, as if it had been made by 
someone. One of his later incarnations came to Sera when I was a young boy. I remember 
him as a very tall man who in th e evening would recite texts in a loud booming voice. He later 
returned to his birthplace but was caught by the Chinese, beaten badly, and imprisoned. He 
died and was cremated and again there arose a sp ecial small image in the ashes. Such stories 
show that we cannot tell from the outer appear ance who is a practitioner and who is not.  
 In short, even when we clean, work, and so forth we should have a good motivation. This 
is important in our daily life.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A1C-2 Time and duration of meditation 
A Indication of the time of meditation 
Thus you should meditate in four sessi ons – at dawn, in the morning, in the 
afternoon, and in the evening.  
 
We should do four sessions of meditation every day. The time to meditate is at dawn, in the 
morning, in the afternoon, and in the evening.  On the other hand, we should not begin to 
meditate at midday, midnight, sunset, and sunrise as at these times interferences to our 
meditation can arise. Dawn is when the sky be comes clear; morning is 9:00, 10:00, or 11:00 
AM; afternoon is 1:00, 2:00, 3:00, or 4:00 PM; an d evening is when it begins to get dark, at 
the moment about 6:00 PM.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
154 B How to meditate in the beginning 
Moreover, if [your sessions are] long in the beginning it is easy to fall under the 
power of laxity and excitement. As it is di fficult to correct this state of mind once 
you have got used to it, you should do many short sessions. If you break off the 
session when there is still a desire to meditate, you will really want to enter 
meditation the next time too. They say that otherwise you will be overcome by 
nausea at the sight of your practice place. 
 
In initially we should not do very long session s. This is because if the sessions are too long, 
laxity and excitement, which are the main obstacles to meditation , will arise. If we meditate 
too long the mind becomes tired and laxity or mental sinking can easily occur. Or if we 
meditate too long we may begin to think of th ings we need to do, remember meeting such 
and such a person, and so forth. In this way the meditation will not go well. Also if laxity and 
excitement arise and overwhelm our mind, it becomes difficult to change the mind. Therefore, at the beginning we should do short sessions of meditation, for example, one hour 
followed by a break, or even just twenty or thirty minutes followed by a break. In fact we 
should stop meditating when we still want to me ditate because it will then be easy for us to 
face and do the next session. On  the other hand, if we become very tired during meditation, 
d u e  t o  w h i c h  o u r  m i n d  b e c o m e s  u n h a p p y  o r  d i s c o u r a g e d ,  i t  w i l l  b e  d i f f i c u l t  t o  e n g a g e  i n  meditation in the next session. Just thinking about meditation, the mind will once again feel 
tired. Here the text says that when just by s eeing our meditation cushion we will feel nauseous 
and will not want to go there. Perhaps this happens to some pe ople. I myself get nauseous and 
sometimes even vomit when traveling by car, an d then the next time I need to travel I get 
nauseous just by seeing the car!   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How to sustain [the meditation] once it has stabilized a bit and adv ice on how to avoid exhaustion 
When it has stabilized a bit, you should prolong the sessions, sustaining them all 
{42} free from the faults of being excessi vely tense or relaxed. Thereby, small 
obstacles as well as over-exhaustion and drowsiness will be pacified. 
 
Having trained in meditation, we will come to be able to meditate for six or seven hours 
without our mind being disturbed by laxity an d excitement. Instead the mind will remain 
clear and able to abide single-pointedly on th e  o b j e c t .  A t  t h i s  p o i n t  w e  c a n  d o  v e r y  l o n g  
meditation sessions, whereas at the beginni ng we should keep our sessions short.  
 However, when our meditation first begins to  become a little bit more stable, we should 
make the session only a little bit longer, not much  longer. If we put very strong energy into 
our meditation or are too relaxed, faults can ar ise. We need to do the middle way, whereby 
there will be less interferences, less tiredness, and less dullness or sinking mind. When we meditate and become tired, laxity tends to aris e. At first the object becomes unclear yet we 
can still see it. Then, gradually more darkness arises until we can no longer see the object of 
meditation. This is the main interference to or fault of meditation. It needs to be eliminated. For this reason, it is important to do medita tion at the right time and without too much 
pushing. By keeping the mind quiet and calm, it will be easier to meditate and it will be easier for the mind to engage in meditation the next time.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
155  Although when we try to meditate we tend to  fall asleep, when we watch a movie, a show, 
dancing, and so forth we do not fall asleep bu t instead are able to watch the whole thing with 
wide-open eyes. On the other hand, when we try to do something virtuous, we become sleepy. 
We can verify this based on our own experience . Therefore, we should meditate for the right 
amount of time, neither too short nor too long, trying to stabilize the mind, and then 
gradually prolong the session but not by much.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A2 What to do during the periods between sessions 
A General practices enhancing the object [of meditation] 
B The causes of calm abiding and special insight 
2B4A-2A2A General practices enhancing the object [of meditation] 
Generally speaking, there are numerous pr actices enhancing the object such as 
prostrations, circumambulation, and recita tion, however, the main point is that, 
having made an effort at the essence of the session, if, during the intervals where 
you leave the session, you let go carelessly without relying on mindfulness and 
introspection with respect to the observed object and aspect – which is the cause of 
sustaining [the meditation], the outcome wi ll be very little. Therefore, in between 
sessions you should also be concerned wi th the Dharma [texts] that teach that 
[object], and recall it again and again. By  various means you should accumulate the 
collection of conditions favour able to the arisal of positi ve qualities, and by various 
means purify the adverse conditions – th e obscurations. Your determination with 
respect to the discipline you have accept ed, the basis of everything, should be 
reaffirmed a lot. 
 Between sessions, that is, after the meditation sessions, we should try to keep our mind in 
virtue. In general it is said to do prostrat ions, circumambulate stupas, recite one’s daily 
commitments, and so forth between sessions. We should also try to remember the object of 
meditation again and again, bringing it to mi nd and not allowing the mind to roam about. 
With mindfulness and introspection, we should  read Dharma texts on the object of our 
meditation. Since we meditate in order to gain hi gher qualities, we also need to collect merit 
in different ways and we need to purify unfavora ble conditions. In this way, our mind will on 
one side collect merit and on the other side purify negativities and non-virtues, whereby 
unfavorable conditions will be purified.   “The basis of everything” is morality, in this  context the taking of vows, for example, those 
of a monk, nun, or the layperson, the bodhisa ttva vows, and so forth. We need to keep and 
safeguard the vows that we have taken. This is important in our daily life. To do so, we need 
to close the five doors of our senses as they are the cause of many faults. Why is that? We 
should examine this subject. For example, if we look at everything around us, we will see 
attractive objects toward which attachment will  arise as well as ugly objects toward which 
anger will arise. If we hear pleasant music or  singing, attachment wi ll arise. On the other 
h a n d ,  i f  w e  h e a r  s o m e o n e  m a k i n g  a  l o t  o f  u n p l e a s a n t  n o i s e ,  a n g e r  w i l l  a r i s e .  I n  t h i s  w a y ,  different object produce attachment, hatred, and ignorance. Therefore, we need to close or 
bind our sense doors, like covering them with our hands!  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
156 Tuesday afternoon, 22 February 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A2B The causes of calm abiding and special insight 
Apart from that, train in the collection of four causes for the easy arisal of the 
paths of calm abiding and special insight. 
 
Here there is a discussion of calm abiding (s hi ne) and special insigh t. Shi means to have 
pacified the distractions and to abide single-p ointedly on the object. Special insight (lhag 
tong) means to achieve the concen tration on the basis of which ar ises special insight. First one 
does analytical meditation whereby we can achi eve calm abiding and then special insight. We 
need the causes for these, the four accumulation s or collections, in order to quickly generate 
calm abiding and special insight in our mind . We need to train in the four causes.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A2B-1 Controlling the sense doors 
In this regard, when the six consciousn esses arise in dependence upon their 
objects and sense powers, after that attach ment and aversion arise toward the six 
pleasant and the six unpleasant objects of mental consciousness. [Controlling the 
sense doors ] guards against that arisal. 
 
The first of the four causes of calm abiding and special insight is  to restrain the doors of the 
senses. In order for a consciousness to arise, an observed object condition, an empowering 
condition, and an immediately preceding conditio n are necessary. For example, in the case of 
attachment, a pleasant object and a physical se nse power, which is lucid form, are necessary 
whereby a consciousness arises. That consciousness,  for example, an eye consciousness, acts as 
the immediate preceding condition for the arisal of a mental consciousness.   When we see an attractive object, attachme nt arises. When we see a repulsive or ugly 
object, hatred or anger arises. Therefore, we need to protect our mind from the arisal of 
attachment and hatred by controlling the ey e sense power through not looking around too 
much. We should also not listen to too many sounds, smell too many odors, taste too many 
tastes, or touch too many tangible objects. In order for some consciousnesses to arise, their 
object needs to come into contact with their se nse power, whereas, in the case of the eye, 
objects at a great distance from the eye sense power can be seen. Sound, on the other hand, 
must be a bit closer in order to be heard. Od ors must actually contac t the nose although the 
source of the odor can be at a distance from  the nose. Tastes, whether sweet, sour, and so 
forth, must come into actual contact with ou r tongue. Likewise, tangible objects must come 
into contact with our body in order to be ex perienced. If these objects are pleasant they 
produce attachment in our mind. If they are un pleasant, they produce anger. Therefore, we 
should try to avoid generating attachment and hatred by closing our sense doors.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A2B-2 Vigilant behavior 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
157 Vigilant behavior: in  Engaging in Bodhisattva Behaviour80 it says:  
 
To examine time and time again, 
The situation of one’s mind and body; Put concisely, that and only that is 
The sign of protective vigilance.
81 
 
Following this, whenever the body etc. engages in such and such an action, 
through understanding whether or not it should be done, behave accordingly. 
 In addition, we need introspective or vigilant  behavior. Introspection, like a policeman who 
checks what drivers are doing on the road and so  forth, constantly examines the situation of 
our body and mind as to what they are doing.  We need to examine them again and again, 
asking, for example, “What is my  body doing?” There is a Tibetan expression that says that 
when we are alone in someone else’s house we need to check what our hands are doing, that 
is, we need to make sure that they are not taking  what does not belong to us. As there is this 
risk, we need to constantly check the situatio n of our body. We should also check our mind, 
looking at how is it thinking. Is it thinking in a good or bad way? What kind of objects is it 
going to? We should examine whether the mind  is going to an object that generates 
attachment, or to one that generates anger, or ig norance, or pride, or doubt, or afflicted view. 
This is important.   The Tibetan expression says that when we are alone we should check our body, and when 
we are together with many people we should check our mouth. In other words, we should 
check what we are saying, whether it is good or ba d, whether it is lying, divisive speech, and so 
forth. Thus, when we are in the company of many people we should check our mouth. In addition, we should at all times check our mind . In this way we should constantly examine 
the three doors of body, speech, and mind, and when we see with introspection that one of 
them is engaging in a negative action we should immediately stop it.   In short, when our body engages in an action  we should check whether it is a good action 
or a bad action. If it is good, we should do it. If it is not, we should stop it. For example, if we 
are doing prostrations, this is good, no? If we are beating someone, that is not good! Here the words “the body etc.” include the mind.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2A2B-3 Knowing the right measure of food 
Knowing the right measure of food means to give up eating too much or too little 
food and to eat only the amount that does not do damage to one’s virtuous 
activities. Furthermore, having meditated upon the drawbacks of craving for food, 
you should also remember what is said in the scriptures about eating, thinking that 
it should be without mental afflictions, th at it should be of benefit to the giver, 
{43} that while you now bring together th e micro-organisms of your body by means 
                                              
80 by Shantideva, better known as Guide to a Bodhisattva’s Way of Life   
81 JN: shes bzhin , also translated as “introspection” and “alertness” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
158 of material things, in the future you wi ll bring them together by means of the 
Dharma, and that you will bring about th e welfare of all sentient beings.  
 
The third cause of calm abiding and special insi ght is to know the right measure of food. We 
need to eat the right amount of food, that is, to  not eat either too much or too little. To eat 
too little is not good as it causes our body to become thin and weak. It causes the energy in 
our body to lessen, whereby we easily fall si ck. Our stomach also gradually becomes smaller 
and smaller until it is no longer able to accept  food, whereby this can even lead to death. On 
the other hand, by eating too much we will become sleepy wh en, for example, we try to 
meditate. Therefore, neither too much food or too little is good for us. We need to eat the 
right amount.  
 We should also not be attached  to good food. This is a fault as it causes strong attachment 
to arise for food due to which we eat too much and constantly think of eating food. Since it 
can bring about the arisal of faults, we should  contemplate the disadvantages of attachment. 
Although attachment appears to be a close friend , it only makes problems for us. Just as when 
we have a very close friend we do not recognize his or her faults, similarly we do not recognize 
the faults of attachment. For this reason, we need to put effort into thinking about the 
disadvantages of attachment to good food. In fact no one has attachment to bad food! We 
can see that if the Institute’s c ook makes bad food for us, we turn up our nose and do not eat 
it whereas if we consider it to be good we  generate attachment to it and eat a lot!  
 In short, when we eat we should do so wi thout an afflicted mind. When someone gives us 
food we should think to eat in order to benefit that person and generate love for him or her. 
We should also think to eat for the sake of the many tiny micro-organisms in our body, who 
also need to consume food in order to survive.  We should think that while we now give them 
material things, we will give them Dharma te achings in the future when they take human 
form. In this way we should eat with the wish to be able to benefit sentient beings through 
having a strong and healthy body as if our body  is not strong we will not be able to help 
others. Thinking like this, we should eat out of generosity, not out of the wish to fill our 
stomachs. Thus, we eat while giving food to th e micro-organisms in our body, thinking that 
now we give them food but that  when they die and are reborn we will give them the Dharma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In the Letter to a Friend  it also says: 
 
Reasoning that foodstuffs are like medicine, 
Depend on them without attachment or hatred.  
They are not meant for conceit nor for arrogance, 
They are not meant to fatten you; but just to keep you going82.” 
 
Food can be considered to be like medicine since it cures the suffering of hunger and thirst. 
We should depend on it without attachment or  hatred. For example, we should not become 
angry when we do not receive good food. Nor should we should not eat out of haughtiness

159 (rgyags ) or conceit ( bsnyems pa ) ,  w h i c h  a r e  a  b i t  d i f f e r e n t  f r o m  p r i d e .  N o r  s h o u l d  w e  e a t  i n  
order to have unwrinkled and firm flesh. Why should we eat? We should do so only in order 
to remain alive. In short, we should eat food thinking of it as a medicine that can cure illness; for example, certain foods can cure illnesses related to the inner winds. We should not 
generate attachment or anger in relation to food.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4A-2A2B-4 Exerting oneself in yoga without sleeping, and what to do when lying down to sleep 
A What to do when lying down to sleep 
B How to apply [all this] to the intervals between all the meditations 
Exerting oneself in yoga without sleeping,  and what to do when lying down to 
sleep.  
2B4A-2A2B-4A What to do when lying down to sleep 
1 The time to lie down 
In the Letter to a Friend  it says:  
 
Sensible one, great being, after all the day’s sessions, 
As well as the first and last ones of the night, 
Also sleep with mindfulness in between these 
So that during sleep, too, fruit will not be absent.83 
 
The essence of the sessions of both the enti re day and the first and last parts of the 
night as well as what should be done in between sessions is explained. Therefore, 
having walked about and sat down, you shou ld completely purify your mind of the 
five obscurations, and thus make [whatever you do] meaningful. 
 The fourth cause of calm abiding and special insi ght is the yoga of sleep . What is the yoga of 
sleep? It means to sleep in a good way and includ es when to lie down an d so forth. We should 
not sleep during the entire day, and at night we should not sleep during the early and later 
parts of the three parts of the ni ght, that is, we should only sleep in the middle part of the 
three parts of the night.  This is mentioned in Letter to a Friend  saying that if one does not 
sleep during these times the there will be a good result. In short, this implies that we should 
only sleep for three or four hours in the middle  of the night. Why? Because at other times we 
should meditate.   We should walk about a bit and then sit on our meditation cushion and try to purify the 
mind of the five obscurations, which are not set out here. The five obscurations are: (1) 
excitement and regret, (2) malice , (3) longing desire, (4) doubt, and (5) lethargy and sleep. We 
need to try to recognize them and to elimin ate them. Then we should act meaningfully 
between sessions.  
                                             
 
83 JN: An alternative translation is: 
 Learned one, the entire day and the first and 
 Last periods of the night having passed, 
 That even the time of sleep not be fruitless, 
 Sleep mindfully between them. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
160  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The behavior of sleep 
As the behavior of sleep occurs between sessions, it should also not be wasted 
meaninglessly. In this regard, the conduc t of the body consists in lying down, 
during the middle part of the three parts of  the night, on your right side, placing 
the left leg on top of the right one, and sleeping like a lion. 
 
Between sessions we should do the yoga of sleep  whereby we will not allow sleep to become 
meaningless. What is the correct conduct of the bo dy? It is to lie down on our right side, with 
the left leg placed on top of the right, and th e right hand against the right cheek. We should 
sleep like a lion which, unafraid of other anim als, sleeps relaxed and comfortably. It is also 
advised to block the right nostril with the tip of the right finger as this stops the flow of wind 
related to attachment, whereby our sleep can be come more virtuous. In the teachings on the 
S t a g e s  o f  t h e  P a t h  i t  s a y s  t h a t  w h e n  w e  g o  t o  s l e e p  w e  s h o u l d  g e n e r a t e  t h e  m i n d  o f  enlightenment, thinking to attain enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient beings. We can 
also generate the thought “How wonderful it would be if all sentient beings had happiness 
and the causes of happiness. May they have them . I will make them have them.” We can also 
think that all phenomena are empty of inhere nt existence (the obje ct clear light), and 
meditate on this whereby we go to sleep in th is clear light. In this way we will practice 
Dharma or create virtue during the entire duration of sleep.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 Mindfulness 
As for mindfulness, you should rely on the fact that, until you fall asleep, it 
pursues the predominant virtuous action th at you have cultivated during the day. 
Therefore, you can sustain your spiritual pr actice, such as meditative stabilization, 
even while asleep, similar to when you are not asleep. 
 
In our every day life, remembering the virtuous activities that we have cultivated during the day, we should go to sleep. In this way, our sleep is certain not to become either non-virtuous 
or neutral. Instead, while we sleep, we will stil l be meditating, that is, still doing a virtuous 
action due to which our sleep will become mean ingful and serve to increase our virtuous 
qualities. Therefore, when going to sleep, reca ll the virtues with which you have familiarized 
and generate a good motivation thinking to bene fit all sentient beings. In this way, we will 
practice meditative stabilization even while sleep ing, whereby the virtuous energy or potential 
in our continuum will increase and the negati ve energy or potential will diminish. By 
increasing our positive potential and reducing our negative potential, we can gradually come 
to create only virtue and thereby realize our final aim.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
161 4 Vigilance84
 
Vigilance: If any mental affliction aris es while you cultivate mindfulness, {44} 
having become aware of it, rather than grasping it, eagerly abandon it. 
 
If any one of the different mental afflictions aris es, it is necessary to understand or recognize 
it and then try to abandon it. This implies that we need to examine with introspection 
whether or not an affliction has arisen. If we find that one of them has arisen, we should 
immediately try to eliminate or abandon it. So metimes attachment arises, sometimes anger or 
hatred arises, sometimes ignorance arises, sometimes pride arises, sometimes doubt arises, and 
sometimes one of the afflicted views arises. If an y of the six root afflictions arises, we should 
immediately apply its antidote in order to stop it. At other times, there arise belligerence, at 
times laziness, at times jealousy, at times ha ughtiness, and so forth, as there are many 
afflictions that are mental factors. Therefore, we  need to check what kind of mental afflictions 
arise in our mind and immediately try to put a stop to them. For example, if having left a 
door to a room open, dogs, cats, and so forth en ter, we need to send them out and close the 
door as otherwise they will make a mess in the room. Similarly, with mindfulness and introspection we should examine whether attachment, anger, and so forth arise and, if they 
have arisen, immediately try to abandon them. We should not accept the presence of, for example, attachment, thinking that it is nice or that it can bring us pleasure. If we allow 
attachment to remain without sto pping it, it will only become stronger. Therefore, we need to 
try to stop it immediately.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
5 The discrimination of getting up 
The discrimination of getting up consists in  projecting the thought “I will get up at 
this and that time.” 
 
Before we go to sleep we should make a dete rmination to wake up at a certain time, for 
example, at dawn. If we do this, we will be able to wake up at that time. In other words, in 
o r d e r  t o  g e t  u p  a t  5 : 0 0  A M  i n  o r d e r  t o  p r a c tice meditation, do prostrations, or do any 
virtuous activity whatsoever, we need to ha ve made a strong determination the previous 
evening to get up early the next morning.   In our every day life we should generate th e mind of enlightenment, do some meditation 
on the breath combined with taking-and-giving, or do the Six Yogas of Naropa, the six magical 
wheels. We should also try to be relaxed, t hinking that we are very lucky, that we have no problems. If we think that something is a pr oblem, then many problems will arise and our 
mind will become disturbed. This is not of any benefit whatsoever.  
 
Wednesday morning, 23 February 2005

2B4A-2A2B-4B How to apply this to the intervals between all meditations 
                                              
84 JN: shes bzhin , “introspection”
