162 That being so, apart from the uncommon ways  of sustaining meditation during the 
actual meditation, [these indications as to ] what should be done in preparation, 
during the actual meditation, at the en d, and in between sessions should be 
applied to all observed objects and aspects being sustained from here up to special 
insight. 
 
In order to meditate we need to do the preparation, the actual meditation, and the 
conclusion. In fact every action has a preparat ion, an actual action, and a conclusion. For 
example, to cook food and put it on a plat e, set out the cutlery, and so forth are the 
preparatory actions; eating the food is the actual  action; and to wash the dishes and put things 
away are the concluding actions.   When we do the meditations ranging from re lying on a guru through to special insight, 
that is, when we do the meditations of beings of  lower, middling, and great capacities, in each 
case there should be a preparation, an actual meditation, and a conclusion. Thus, we need to 
recall that first we need to do this, then we need to do that, and so on. When we do any 
analytical meditation we should briefly rememb er all the preceding meditations. In all cases 
we should focus our mind on a virtuous object and then try to single-pointedly do analytical meditation on it.

Second: In Ornament of the Sutras  it says:  
 
Here, first of all, proper mental attention85 arises in dependence on 
study,86 and from proper mental attention, again, the exalted wisdom 
with the meaning of pure reality87 as its object arises. 
 
That is to say that from proper mental attention, by means of the wisdom arisen 
from reflecting on the meaning of what ha s been studied, the wisdom arisen from 
meditation directly realizing the meaning of pure reality arises.  
 We need to do both analytical meditation and placement meditation as they are both 
important. First we should do analytical medita tion. However, before this we need to listen 
properly to many teachings or read many books in order to develop understanding such that 
                                             
 
85 JN: yid byed  
86 JN: tos, “hearing” or “listening”

163 the wisdom arisen from hearing arises. Then we  need to think about what we have heard or 
read, that is, the mind needs to engage the obje ct. In this way we can slowly come to realize 
the meaning of emptiness and achieve the path of seeing.  
 On the path of accumulation we accumulate  merit and the hearing of many teachings. 
Then we think about their meaning, whereby we  achieve the wisdom arisen from thinking 
and attain the path of preparation. Then, when  we realize the meaning of emptiness directly, 
we achieve the arya path, the supramundane path , the path of seeing. Through familiarizing 
with this on the path of meditation, we even tually attain the path of no-more-learning.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In the Ornament of Direct Realization , too, it says:  
 
For the four aspects of ascertainment,88  
The paths of seeing and meditation,  
Repeated reflection, assessment, and 
Certainty are the path of meditation.89 
 
That is to say there are [types of] repe ated reflection, assessment, and certain 
understanding (“certainty”) that constitu te the path of meditation of mahayana 
aryas. 
 
There are four branches of definite discrimina tion or the four branches of the path of 
preparation are heat, peak, tolerance, and su preme mundane qualities. After the supreme 
mundane qualities level, upon seeing emptiness di rectly one achieves the path of seeing. Then 
one meditates on the emptiness that has bee s een again and again, whereby one achieves the 
path of meditation.   There are ten grounds of arya bodhisattvas. Th e first ground is divided into two parts, the 
first part being included in the path of seeing and the second part being included in the path 
of meditation. The second through the tenth grounds are all included in the path of 
meditation. Because the object of abandonment, the conception of true existence, is divided 
into nine (the great of the grea t, middle of the great, small of the great, great of the middle, 
middle of the middle, small of th e middle, great of the small, middle of the small, and small 
of the small), so too is the path of meditation , their antidote, divided into nine rounds that 
abandon the nine degrees of the conception of  true existence. The vajra-like meditative 
stabilization is the final antidote and the last un interrupted path of the path of meditation. In 
the very next instant one attains th e buddha ground or enlightenment.

164 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 In the Compendium [of Trainings ] it also says: 
 
Thus, you should practice meditation, continuously giving away, 
guarding, purifying, and increasing your body, possessions, and merit in 
all kinds of ways. 
 With regard to the meditations that should  be done according to the statement for 
all four activities – giving away, guarding , purifying, and increasing – with respect 
to each of the three – body, possessions,  and roots of virtue, there are both 
analytical meditations, which are sust ained through analysis by means of 
discriminating wisdom,
90 and placement meditations,  which are to place [the 
mind] single-pointedly without analysis. 
 
There are two texts known by the abbreviated title of Compendium  (bslab btus ), one by Asanga 
and one by Shantideva. Here Compendium  probably refers to the text by Shantideva, the 
Compendium of Trainings . This text says that we should meditate on continuously giving away, 
safeguarding, purifying, and increasing our body, possessions, and merit.  
 If we give our body away we will die! So how can we give it away? We can give away parts of it, such as our legs, our kidneys, or our ey eballs, to others. To give away our possessions 
means to practice generosity by  g i v i n g  t h e m  t o  o t h e r s .  f o r  e x a m p l e ,  w e  c a n  d o  l i k e  S a i n t  
Francis who gave away all his possessions and went  to meditate in a cave. To give away our 
merit means to dedicate it for the sake of other sentient beings.  
 We can also safeguard or take care of our body. Likewise, we can purify it by washing it and so forth, both externally and internally. In  this way the body becomes stronger. Here 
“increasing the body” must refer to increasing the energy of the body, since the body itself 
cannot be increased. Although when we are youn g the body increases in size, when we get old 
it decreases!  We can also guard our possessions. In addition, we can purify them, meaning that we should not obtain them by means of any of the five wrong livelihoods, that is, by cheating 
others, stealing them, and so forth. We should not act like the Italian Mafia whose wealth is 
made up of “dirty money,” due to which they have to keep it hidden! We should increase our possessions or wealth, for example, by investing our money, or by putting it in a good bank 
whereby the interest causes it to incr ease, or by doing business with it.  
 We should guard our merit by dedicating it, as soon as it is created, that it may become the 
cause for all sentient beings to achieve enligh tenment. We should keep our merit pure by 
ensuring that it does not become contaminated by the mental afflictions. In other words, we 
should not allow it to become mixed with no n-virtue but keep it completely white and 
virtuous. That which is completely white or vi rtuous definitely brings a white or pleasant 
result, whereas if we create merit that is mixed with afflictions it will not be completely pure. 
We should also increase our merit by generati ng a good motivation. The motivation at the 
time of the preparation for an action should be  virtuous, the motivation at the time of the 
                                             
 
90 JN: so sor rtog pa’i shes rab , “wisdom of individual investigation” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
165 actual action should be virtuous, and the motiva tion at the time of the conclusion should be 
virtuous. In this way our merit will increase. Just  as a tiny seed, for example, an apple seed, 
when sowed in a fertile field ca n bring a tree with many branches laden with fruit, so to can 
our inner virtuous energy increase.  
 There is a type of meditation in which we think about and analyze an object, this being 
called “analytical meditation.” Then, there is another type of meditation in which we place 
the mind on an object, this being called “placement meditation.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B Identification of analytical and placement meditation  
{45} Now if you ask what are the paths that  are analytical meditation and what are 
the paths that are placement meditation: fo r meditations such as the cultivation of 
confidence in the spiritual teacher, the great meaning of the freedoms and 
endowments and the difficulty of find them, death-imperman ence, karma and its 
effect, the faults of cyclic existence, an d the cultivation of bodhicitta, analytical 
meditation is necessary. For each of them , minds of great strength and the ability 
to subdue the mind for a long time are ne cessary, because, if they are absent, their 
counterparts, disrespect and the like cannot be prevented, and because the 
generation of these minds depends on no thing but the meditation analyzing them 
by means of investigating them individually time and again.  
 How are analytical meditation and placement me ditation to be done? First we should do 
analytical meditation and then placement medita tion. For example, in order to develop faith 
or confidence in the virtuous spiritual friend it  is necessary to do analytical meditation that 
analyzes the qualities that he possesses. We shou ld not do this analysis for just one or two 
days, rather we may need to analyze him for many years. In this way, we will come to be able 
to rely on him with complete faith and confid ence. Similarly with respect to the precious 
human rebirth endowed with the eighteen qualities, we should analyze its great meaningfulness and the difficulty of find it again. Then, we need to analyze the fact that, having found it, we will definitely lose it be cause of the certainty of  death-impermanence.  
 Then we also need to analyze actions and their results. For example, among the four results, the main one is the fruitional or matura tion result, that is, due to a negative action 
one will be reborn in the hell, animal, or hung ry ghost realm. Due to the action being black 
or non-virtuous, the result will definitely be  black or suffering. On the other hand, the 
fruitional result of a white or virtuous action  that is not mixed with non-virtue or the 
afflictions is to be reborn as a human being, an anti-god, or a god. There are six realms and 
when we die we will necessarily be reborn  in one of them. Whic h one depends on our 
a c t i o n s .  I f  w e  c r e a t e  v i r t u o u s  a c t i o n s  w e  c a n ,  f o r  e x a m p l e ,  b e  r e b o r n  a s  a  h u m a n  b e i n g .  
However, even among human beings there are those who have a precious human rebirth and 
those who do not have such a rebirth. The latte r are human beings but they lack Dharma and 
the possibility of developing wisdom and a g ood-heart. Thus, even among human beings there 
are different types of rebirths. There are also different types of anti-gods, and among gods there are the six types of gods of the desire re alm, the seventeen of the form realm, and the 
four of the formless realm. To take rebirth as a human being, anti-god, or god it is necessary 
to have created virtuous or meritorious actions. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
166  We should also analyze the disadvantages or sh ortcomings of cyclic existence. For example, 
there are many disadvantages of a human rebirt h since human beings experience the three, 
six, and eight types of sufferings. Thus, even  b e i n g s  w h o  h a v e  b e e n  b o r n  i n  t h e  h a p p y  
migrations experience suffering; we can see this in our daily life. We experience the suffering 
of suffering, suffering of change, and pervasive su ffering. We should analyz e this. To analyze it 
means to check whether or not it is so. 
 When cultivating the mind of enlightenmen t, one meditates in or der to generate the 
conventional mind of enlightenment. There are two lineages for developing the conventional 
mind of enlightenment, one of which is “the  six causes and one result,” which was passed 
down from Buddha Shakyamuni to Maitreya, to Asanga, and so on down to us. In this 
context we do analytical meditation that analyzes  the fact that all sentient beings have been 
our mother, just like our mother of this life. Wh y is this? Because we have been taking rebirth 
since beginningless time and whenever we were  born as, for example, a human being we 
needed a mother. Therefore, our mothers are infinite. In this way, we do analytical 
meditation that applies the reas oning that when we were born as a human being we needed a 
mother. Because we have had so many rebirths, even one sentient being has been our mother 
countless times. Since they have  also been our father, grandfat her, grandmother, uncle, aunt, 
and so forth, why do we emphasize the fact that  they have been our mother? This is because 
we have the closest relationship with our mother  due to the fact that she carried us in her 
womb for 39 or 40 weeks. During this entire  time our body was connected to her body, 
whereas this is not the case for the father. For this reason , in Buddhism the mother is 
emphasized. Also while we were in her womb she took care of us, and when we were born she 
continued to take care of us. However, perhap s there are some exceptions as some mothers 
even abandon their children in a garbage can! There is a story that even in ancient times a 
mother seeing the placenta and not recognizing that it contained a ba by threw the placenta 
into a field. However, due to a crow (an mani festation of Six-Armed Ma hakala) pecking at it, 
a beautiful baby was revealed. Someone then took care of the baby and when he grew up he became a famous spiritual master.  
 After recognizing that all sentient beings ha ve been our mother, we need to remember the 
their kindness based on the many reasonings re lated to this. Then we need to develop the 
thought to repay their kindness. How can we  d o  t h i s ?  W e  n e e d  t o  d e v e l o p  l o v e  a n d  
compassion for them, followed by the extraordinary thought that is  determined to take care of 
them – to eliminate their suffering and set them in the state of happiness. However, realizing 
that we are not yet able to actually do this, we should analyze who can do this, whereby we 
come to understand that only a buddha can do this. We then generate the mind of 
enlightenment. This is of two types: an aspira tional mind of enlightenment and an engaging 
mind of enlightenment. We should analyze all these points.  
 Alternatively, in order to generate the mi nd of enlightenment we can think about the 
many disadvantages of the self-cherishing mind . Then we can think about the advantages of 
cherishing others. Based on this, we generate th e attitude wishing to exchange ourselves with 
others, and in order to do this we meditate on  taking-and-giving. This meditation is called 
tong-len  in Tibetan, literally giving-and-taking, howe ver we first take the sufferings of sentient 
beings upon ourselves in combination with th e inhalation of the breath. We visualize all 
sentient beings in front of us in the aspect of human beings, and, motivated by compassion, 
take all their sufferings upon ourselves. We imag ine that from their right nostrils come their 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
167 sufferings, non-virtuous actions, and mental affl ictions in the aspect of black smoke, which we 
inhale through our left nostril. The black smoke descends to our heart where we have 
visualized a heap of black dust in the center of  the central channel, whic h is the nature of our 
self-cherishing mind. The black smoke touches the heap of black dust and completely destroys 
it. In this way our self-cherishing mind is de stroyed and all other sentient beings are freed 
from their suffering. Then, we do the meditation  on giving. Motivated by love, we generate 
the wish to give others our virtues and merit,  our precious human rebirth, our possessions, 
that is, every good thing that we  possess. We imagine that they take the aspect of white light 
which leaves through our right nostril and enters  the left nostrils of the sentient beings 
visualized in front of us. They achieve everythi ng they want and are de lighted. The meditation 
on taking-and-giving is a way of developing the mind of enlightenment by means of 
exchanging ourselves with others. In this way, we do analytical meditation on the mind of 
enlightenment.   We should do analytical meditation with a st rong motivation and for a long time until our 
mind moves. If this does not happen, the opposi te may occur, that is, interferences may arise. 
These interferences can be the self-cherishing mi nd, the thought that we  cannot generate love 
for some beings because they act ag ainst us, a lack of respect, and so forth. It is said that we 
need, for example, to respect human rights, howe ver some people are convinced that there are 
certain people who must be tortured. If we do  not generate love, compassion, and the mind 
of enlightenment continually and strongly for other people, there is the danger that such 
thought may arise. For this reason, we need to  do analytical meditation again and again.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
For example, just as intense attachment ar ises if you meditate on an object of 
attachment a lot while exaggerating its pl easant features, in the same way intense 
aversion arises if you think of many unplea sant features with regard to an enemy. 
Hence, in regard to meditating these path s, these processes are similar whether or 
not the aspect of the object appears clearl y, but the mode of apprehension of the 
mind must be intense and long-lasting, which is why you should do analytical 
meditation. In the context of practicing calm abiding, which enables one to place 
[the mind] on whatever single object  one wishes, since someone whose mind 
cannot stay on a single object will not be fit for mental quiescence
91 if he analyses 
again and again, it is necessary to pr actice placement meditation on that. 
 
When a man or woman is our object of attachme nt, we think over and over again that he or 
she is very nice, exaggerating the niceness than  actually exists. In this way such strong 
attachment arises that it may even prevent us fr om sleeping and eating. If we cannot meet that 
person, we continuously think about him or he r, whereby the attachment becomes stronger 
and stronger. If we are unable to enter a relati onship with that person, we experience many 
difficulties – we cannot sleep, cannot eat, cannot concentrate, cannot work…   Similarly, in the case of our enemy we exa ggerate the unpleasant aspects of that person, 
due to which strong anger or hatred arises that  wishes to harm or destroy him or her. Our 
                                             
 
91 JN: sems gnas  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
168 mind is continually disturbed by  this anger or hatred, whereby our mind will not be able to 
relax, will not be quiet and peaceful, and will not be happy. Instead the mind will constantly 
be unhappy. When this happens, we need to meditate continually and strongly on love and 
compassion. 
 On the other hand, if we have attachment  for someone, we should meditate on the 
ugliness of the object. In  the eighth chapter of Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  Shantideva 
sets out the fact that the body is composed of th irty-six impure substances and is therefore like 
a sack of filth. If a sack of filth were placed in front of us, we would hold our nose and 
immediately try to get rid of it! However, we think that our own and others’ bodies, which are 
composed of impure substances, are pleasant. We  can also meditate on skeletons in order to 
overcome attachment, this medi tation being set out in the Treasury of Manifest Dharma . The 
Vinayasutra  also sets out nine antidotes of various types of ugliness to counteract attachment. 
Thinking in this way, we can reduce our attachment.  
 When meditating, whether the observed object  and its aspect appear clearly or not, we 
should try to hold the object very strongly. Fo r example, we should continually do analytical 
meditation on the precious human rebirth and th e difficulty of finding it again. We should 
also meditate on the fact that this rebirth will  s o o n  b e lo s t  d u e to  d eat h, w hi c h i s  cer t ai n , 
whereas the time of death is uncertain, and that  at the time of death, our body, possessions, 
and relatives will not be able to help us, we will have to go on alone.  
 However, at other times we should not do analysis, rather we should do calm abiding 
meditation. In this case, without analyzing we should set our mind single-pointedly on its 
object. In this way, we do placement meditation.  
 There is calm abiding and special insight. Calm abiding  in Tibetan is shi-ne  (zhi gnas ), shi 
meaning the pacification of distractions, and ne meaning to abide on the object. Special insight  
in Tibetan is lhag-tong  (lhag mthong ), literally supreme or higher s eeing. First we need to achieve 
calm abiding, after which we develop special insigh t. This is according to sutra, that is, they 
are developed sequentially, first calm abiding an d then special insight. However, according to 
tantra, calm abiding and special insight can be developed simultaneously. This is because in 
those with higher realizations, special insigh t (vipassana) can actually induce calm abiding 
(samatha).  
 We should first do analytical meditation in  order to understand the nature of phenomena 
– the nature of our body, feelings, discrimination s, and so forth. We have five aggregates: the 
form aggregate, feeling aggregate, discriminati on aggregate, compositional factors aggregate, 
and the consciousness aggregate. We can do analytical meditation on the body, asking 
ourselves, “What is this body? What is its natu re?” We should try to meditate in this way. 
Externally the body is covered with skin, wherea s inside it are muscles, bones, organs, nerves, 
and so forth. We can ask ourselves, “Is this body  pure or impure in nature? It is permanent or 
impermanent in nature? Is it suffering or happiness in nature? Is it self-supporting 
substantially existent self or empty of self-suppo rting substantially existent self in nature?” We 
should do analytical meditation with respect to the body in this  way. We should also analyze 
how our body depends on its different parts, the two arms, two legs, the head, the internal 
organs, and so forth, for its existence. The entirety of parts is what is called “a human body.” Therefore, does it exist independently from its ow n side? It does not; it exists in dependence 
on its parts. Thus, the body is empty of in herent existence because it does not exist 
independently or from its own side. We should  analyze the emptiness or selflessness of the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
169 body, its impermanence, its nature of suffering, and its impurity in this way. Like this, we 
should try to do analytical meditation on  the body and the othe r four aggregates.  
 
Wednesday afternoon, 23 February 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4A-2B2 Refutation of the misconception that although there are both analytical and placement meditations, 
one single person does not practice them 
Although some who do not know this appr oach claim that if you are a scholar you 
should only do analytical meditation while saddhus should only do placement 
meditation, this is not so because it is nece ssary to do the two, one by one. This is 
because the learned, too, need to achieve calm abiding, and saddhus, too, need to 
achieve strong confidence in the spiritual teacher {46} and the like. Therefore, the 
understanding that repeated analysis by means of discriminating wisdom belongs 
to the context of listening and reflecting , and not to the context of practice, is 
inadmissible. 
 
There are those who think that a single person does not practice both placement meditation 
and analytical meditation, however this is a misc onception that must be refuted. Such people 
think that well-studied scholars should only do analytical meditation, whereas kusali  
(translated above as “saddhus”) should only do placement meditation. This is not correct 
because scholars who have studied much philosop hy also need to do placement meditation at 
times, and kusali or saddhus also need to do an alytical meditation at times. Because scholars 
also need to achieve calm abiding, they need to do placement meditation. The kusali, on the 
other hand, need to do analytical meditation be cause they need, for example, to develop faith 
in the virtuous spiritual friend. To do this, th ey must determine whether or not someone is a 
qualified teacher by examining wh ether he or she possesses all ten qualities, or the five of the 
ten that are necessary in this degenerate ti me, or the minimum of two (that he has more 
understanding than the disciple and wishes to be nefit the disciple). In other words, the kusali 
also need to examine whether a person possesses these qualities before accepting him or her 
as their teacher.   It is also a mistaken conception that the wi sdom of individual investigation, which checks 
again and again, belongs to the occasions of list ening and thinking but not to that of practice. 
This is because in order to practice correctly, we need to develop all three wisdoms: the wisdom arisen from hearing, the wisdom arisen  from thinking, and the wisdom arisen from 
meditation. First we need to listen to or hear many teachings, then we need to contemplate or 
think about their meaning, and then we need to  meditate single pointedly on the meaning. 
Therefore, both scholars and kusali need anal ytical meditation and placement meditation.  
 “Kusali” is a title given to someone who ea ts, defecates and urinates, and sleeps. Perhaps 
this word has been translated here as “saddhu”  because it appears that saddhus only do these 
things. However, although someone may show this aspect, he may actually possess high 
realizations, for example, Shantideva. It is re counted that the monks of Nalanda Monastery in 
India thought that all Shantideva did was eat, defecate, and sleep. Thus it was that when it 
came his turn to teach in a nearby nunnery  on the day of sojong, the nuns, under the 
direction of the monks, set up a very high thro ne without any stairs in order to mock him. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
170 When Shantideva arrived in the temple, he pr ostrated in front of the throne and then, 
without anyone seeing how it happened, he suddenly appeared sitting on the throne. The 
audience was very surprised. Shantideva then asked them whether he should teach something 
that had already been taught or something new,  and the answer was that he should give a new 
teaching. He said that while there did not exist a teaching that had not already been taught by 
the holy beings of the past and that he was no t learned in grammar and poetry, he would try. 
He then taught the text Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  to an audience that included foe-
destroyers with clairvoyance, retentions (dharani s), and so forth. As he  was teaching, he began 
to float upward until he went out through a wi ndow in the roof and flew to the south of 
India. Everyone present deeply regretted how they had treated him due to being ignorant of 
his qualities. The same can happen to us nowad ays as we do not know what qualities others 
possess. A kusali is thus someone who does not reveal his or her inner qualities.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4A-2B3 Refutation of the misconception that asserts that all conceptions apprehend signs 
The understanding that all conceptions apprehend signs92 and therefore create 
obstacles to enlightenment has the fault of not discriminating between improper 
mental attention, the conc eption of true existence,93 and proper mental attention, 
the conception of the perfect.94 
 
There are those who think that all conceptions a pprehend signs, that is, the sign of inherent 
existence or true existence. Su ch people think that all concep tions are meaning generalities 
and are objects of abandonment because they cr eate obstacles to attaining enlightenment. 
This view mostly stems from Chinese scholars  called Hashang who me ditated in order to 
abandon all conceptualization. For this reason th ey tried to stop the mind from engaging in 
objects. However, if it were like this, tabl es and trees would also meditate and attain 
enlightenment since they do not conceptualize. Thus, this is a wrong view.   While the mind of enlightenment, compassion, love, and so forth in the minds of ordinary 
beings are conceptions, they are good concep tions that are not objects of abandonment. 
Likewise, the realization of emptiness on th e path of accumulation and the path of 
preparation is a conception, since it is not yet a di rect perceiver, but it is not an object to be 
abandoned. It is recounted that King Trisong De tsen of Tibet invited Kamalashila to debate a 
Chinese Hashang who propounded that all conc eptions are to be abandoned. Prior to the 
debate, Kamalashila and Hashang made an agreem ent that the one who lost the debate would 
become a follower of the winner. During the de bate Hashang was unable to respond to the 
points raised by Kamalashila and, because he  did not want to become his follower, he 
escaped, leaving behind one of his shoes. This fact was taken to be sign that the Hashang’s 
view would continue to remain in Tibet. However,  this view that both the conception of true 
existence and the conception of emptiness ar e to be abandoned is not right. The three 
principal aspects of the path – renunciation , conventional mind of enlightenment, and 
                                             
 
92 JN: mtshan mar ‘dzin pa  
93 JN: bden ‘dzin gyi rtog pa  
94 JN: yang dag pa’i rtog pa  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
171 correct view – are all conceptions that need to be developed. Right now we should strive to 
develop at least a contrived or fabricated form  of renunciation, the conventional mind of 
enlightenment, and the understanding of the la ck of inherent existence of all phenomena. 
These are objects to be practiced, not to be aban doned. This is because in order to attain the 
small enlightenment of the Hinayana, renunciati on is necessary on th e side of method and 
the correct view of emptiness is necessary on  the side of wisdom, while to attain the 
enlightenment of the Mahayana, the mind of enlightenment is necessary on the side of 
method and the correct view of emptiness is ne cessary on the side of view. Therefore, since 
the correct view of emptiness is necessary  to achieve the small, middle, and great 
enlightenment, it is not that concepti ons are obstacles to enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4A-2B4 Refutation of the misconception that asserts that if a lot of analytical meditation is done prior to the 
arisal of meditative stabiliz ation it becomes an obstacle 
The idea “It is necessary to achieve a non-conceptual meditative stabilization 
capable of placing the mind on  a single object in whatever  way it pleases, whereas, 
if you do a lot of analytical meditation be fore, it will become an obstacle to the 
arisal of that meditative stabilization,” is not upheld in the present instructions. 
For example, when a smith burns gold or si lver in the fire and washes it in water 
again and again, all the impurities are removed and it becomes very supple and 
workable, whereby it can subsequently be made into any jewellery like earrings at 
will. Likewise, just as at first the mental afflictions, the secondary afflictions, and 
faulty conduct emerge on the occasion of  meditating on black actions and their 
results and the disadvantages of cyclic  existence, by meditating on such 
disadvantages again and again with the wisdom of individual investigation, the mind will become completely tormented or disenchanted; therefore it is like burning gold in a fire. Similarly, just as  the mind turning away from the black side 
and the defilements being purified emerges on the occasion of [meditating on ] the 
positive qualities of the spiritual frie nd, the great meaning of the freedoms and 
endowments, the positive qualities of th e Three Jewels, white actions and their 
results, and {47} the benefits of the mind of enlightenment, by meditating on these 
positive qualities again and again with the wisdom of individual investigation, the 
mental attention of the mind becomes moistened or sincere, whereby it is like washing gold with water. Through the mind approaching the white side and 
delighting in it, the mind is moistened by virtuous dharmas. When it has been 
transformed in this way, whatever you wi sh to achieve, whether calm abiding or 
special insight, will be achieved without difficulty when the mind is focused on it. 
That is why such an analytical meditati on is the best means of achieving a non-
conceptual meditative stabilization.  
 There are people who have the mi sconception that analytical me ditation is an obstacle to 
achieving calm abiding. However, this is not correct since analytical meditation is not an 
interference to the generation of calm abiding.  For example, a smith wh o makes gold or silver 
ornaments first puts the gold or silver in the fire and washes it again and again in order to 
remove the impurities. The more he does this, the better the qual ity of the earrings, bracelets, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
172 necklaces, and so forth that he will make as by this process the gold or silver is completely 
purified. The pure gold or silver can also be us ed to make offering bowls, butterlamps, and so 
forth. Similarly, by doing analytical medita tion again and again, our root and secondary 
afflictions will more easily be removed or puri fied. There are six root afflictions: attachment, 
anger, ignorance, pride, doubt, and afflicted view, the latter being further divided into five. 
There are twenty secondary afflictions that in clude belligerence, resentment, spite, and so 
forth. All these afflictions exist in our mind. We are therefore rich in mental afflictions, 
whereas we are poor in the virtuous mental fact ors. By thinking about this with analytical 
meditation, we can purify our afflictions.  
 By means of analytical meditation we will also come to see that black or non-virtuous 
actions bring a black or bad result, that of suffe ring, and that cyclic existence is filled with 
f a u l t s  o r  d i s a d v a n t a g e s .  I n  t h i s  w a y ,  i t  w i l l  b e come easy to give negative actions and cyclic 
existence up. We need to contemplate these faul ts again and again by means of the wisdom of 
individual investigation. In this way our mind will become tormented or disenchanted and we 
will develop the wish to turn away from cyclic existence. Just as if we put gold into the fire, although it initially seems to be burnt, in realit y the fire cleans it, similarly by thinking of the 
disadvantages of cyclic existence we can purify the impurities of the mind and turn away from 
the black side.   We should also analyze the positive qual ities of the virtuous spiritual friend, the 
meaningfulness of a precious human rebirth posse ssing the eighteen qualities, the positive 
qualities of the Three Jewels, wh ite actions and their results, an d the benefits of the mind of 
enlightenment. For example, if the mind ba sed on this body generates the mind of 
e n l i g h t e n m e n t ,  w e  w i l l  b e c o m e  a n  o b j e c t  o f  veneration of humans and gods, because even 
though the body did not change the mind did.  The mind of enlightenment brings infinite 
b e n e f i t s  i n  t h a t  d u e  t o  i t  w e  c a n  a c h i e v e  b u d d h a h o o d  a n d  b e  a b l e  t o  l e a d  o t h e r  s e n t i e n t  beings into nirvana or enlightenment.   We should think in this way and do analytical  meditation regarding wh at is good and what 
i s  b a d .  I f  w e  d o  n o t  d o  a n a l y t i c a l  m e d i t a t i o n  w e  w i l l  n o t  b e  a b l e  t o  u n d e r s t a n d  t h i s .  
Therefore, analytical meditation is important, it  is vital. Also in order to develop the wisdom 
of individual investigation we need to medita te again and again, meditating once is not 
enough. If we do this, our mind will be moistene d by the mind of enlightenment, that is, our 
hard mind will become softer or moister, no longer as hard as a stone. Our mind will also 
become more clear, like putting gold in the fi re and washing it with water whereby the gold 
becomes more flexible. Similarly, we should always turn our mind toward the white or 
virtuous side, or the side of enlightenment, or  the side of complete purity, and should avoid 
the black or thoroughly afflicte d side. In this way, our mind will become happier and more 
virtuous, no longer hard or bad. By meditati ng, we can transform our mind in this way.  
 If we want to achieve calm abiding (samatha ) or special insight (vipassana), we need to 
focus our mind on it as in this way we will ea sily achieve it. This means that by meditating 
every day, we will be able attain them. On th e other hand, if we do a bit of meditation and 
then stop for some time and later on start again, this will not happen. We need to do 
meditation continually, without in terruption. In this way we ca n bring about a change in our 
mind and we will easily achieve realizations. Th us, first we should do analytical meditation 
again and again in order to recognize the faults , whereby it will become easy to do single-
pointed meditation and achieve calm abiding.

Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
173  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
That is how Arya Asanga also put it: 
  
By way of analogy, when a smith or hi s skillful apprentice burns gold or 
silver in a fire and washes it with water a couple of times in order to 
discard all impurities and slag, it becomes supple and workable for this and that ornament, this being dire ctly perceived. In this regard, 
through the know-how that accords with  this of the smith or his skilful 
apprentice, any manner of ornament desired is then made with their smith’s tools. Likewise, when a yogi does not turn toward impurities and slag such as covetousness and so forth, he thereby becomes disenchanted and turns away from unhappy afflicted minds, and 
approaches manifest joy in the virtuous side, thereby becoming 
manifestly joyful. In that case, that yogi definitely trains his mind in either the side of calm abiding or the side of special insight, such that it 
is thoroughly applied to this and th at, rests in its natural state, is 
immovable and unwavering, and {48}  also moves for the sake of 
perfectly achieving whatever purpose he has contemplated. 
 
When a smith burns gold or silver in a fire and then washes it with wate r, the gold or silver 
becomes more flexible and can be easily made in to whatever kind of ornament he wants, such 
as earrings, amulets, bracelets, anklets, and so fo rth. They can also be ma de into studs that are 
to be put into the lip, the brea st, the navel, and so forth! In a similar way, a yogi purifies 
covetousness, malice, and wrong view – the non-virtuous actions of mind.  
 When an affliction arises in our mind it functions to bring unhappiness and never brings 
happiness. However, some people think that if they did not have attachment they would be 
missing out on some type of useful energy. Such  thoughts are not right. We need to increase 
our positive energy and diminish our negative energy. In our everyday life we should try to 
practice virtue more and more precisely and avoi d engaging in non-virtuous actions, that is, 
we need to try to reduce non-virtue and increase virtue. It is possible to change our mind, our behavior, and our habits, whereby we can improve more and more.   When our mind is manifestly joyful, we will think about what we wish  to achieve, whether 
calm abiding or special insight, and will be able to quickly achieve these qualities. Due to this, 
our mind will remain stable on its object and wi ll not move to other objects as it does at the 
moment. We will be able to perfectly achiev e our purpose and our mind will improve more 
and more.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Furthermore, the main adverse conditions to the mind staying on an object of 
meditation continuously are twofold: laxi ty and excitement. If you have a strong 
and constant awareness that sees the positive  qualities of the Three Jewels, etc., it is 
quite easy to overcome laxity, for many va lid sources say that its antidote is to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
174 uplift the mind by looking at positive qual ities. If you have a strong and constant 
awareness that sees the disadvantages of impermanence, suffering, and so forth, it 
is quite easy to overcome excitement, be cause, since excitement is a distracted 
mind belonging to the category of attachme nt, many texts praise disillusionment as 
its antidote.  
 
The two main interferences or adverse conditio ns to achieving calm abiding are laxity and 
excitement. Laxity ( bying ba ) literally means sinking , like an object dropped into a cup of tea 
sinks to the bottom of the cup. Excitement (rgod pa ) is a mind that goes out to objects of 
attachment. These two mental factors are the main  obstacles to our meditation, especially to 
the achievement of calm abiding. When we try to  meditate single-pointedly on or visualize the 
Buddha’s body or face, Tara’s face, and so forth, we should try to get a clear image of its aspect and then focus the mind on this. However,  we will notice that after some time that 
image no longer appears to our mind, but has been replaced by something else, perhaps the 
face of our lover! In fact the VI Dalai Lama wrote in one of his poems that when he tried to meditate on his guru’s face it did not appear clearly to him, bu t that even without meditating 
on his lover’s face it clearly appeared to him! We are like this, although it was probably not true in his case. Similarly, when we meditate on  a deity or do a sadhana, we remember all the 
worldly activities that we need to do. This is distraction. We need to eliminate it. By seeing 
the qualities of the Three Jewels and developing strong faith or confidence in them and meditating on them for a long time, we can cut off the sinking mind due to clearly seeing the 
eight qualities each of the Buddha, Dhar ma, and Sangha, which are taught in the Sublime 
Continuum  (Uttaratantra ). The more we understand these qualities, the more faith we will 
develop in the Three Jewels that  they are able to protect us from all the different types of 
sufferings. While the Three Jewels can protect us  from all sufferings, mundane beings such as 
gods and powerful human beings such as kings,  judges, and so forth cannot. Seeing this, we 
should develop strong faith in them, believing th at they can help us. In this way our mind will 
be lifted up and freed from laxity.  On the other hand, if excitement arises in ou r mind, for example, due to being attached to 
a past lover we think of him or her and wish to be with him or her, at this time we need to 
meditate on death-impermanence or suffering. We can think about the fact that we will 
definitely die, that the time of death is unce rtain, and that at the time of death our body, 
possessions and wealth, as well as our friends and rela tives, will not be able to help us, as only 
our Dharma practice will be of help. Thinking in  this way, we should try to remember that we 
are impermanent and therefore could die tomorro w, that those who are our friends today can 
change and tomorrow become our enemies, and so forth. Due to impermanence, things are 
always changing. In fact in cyclic existence th ere is nothing that is stable, everything is 
constantly changing, moving, and deceptive. By th inking in this way, our mind will not go to 
objects of attachment and will therefore not generate excitement.   Alternatively to counteract excitement we can think about suffering. For example, we can 
think about the fact that if we were to be reborn in our next life in the hell realm, in one of the eight hot hells, eight cold hells, four neighb oring hells, or the occasional hells, we would 
experience intense suffering. We can also think ab out what it would be like to be reborn in 
the hungry ghost realm unable to find food, or i f  a b l e  t o  f i n d  i t  w h e n  e a t e n  i t  b u r n s  o u r  
stomach. Similarly, if we were to find somethin g to drink, it would become molten iron that 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
175 burns our throat and stomach. If we were to see a tree laden with delicious fruit, when we 
arrive close to it we would no longer see the fruit but only dry leafless branches. If we were to 
see a river in the distance, when we got close to it, we would see only a dry riverbed. The 
hungry ghosts have such karma. Could we oursel ves bear to be reborn there? Since we could 
not bear it, we must avoid crea ting non-virtuous actions.  
 Similarly, if we were to be reborn as an an imal while it might be a little better, since some 
animals are taken care of by human beings, many others kill and eat each other, many are 
used as beasts of burden, and so forth. Therefore, even if we were to be reborn as an animal 
we would experience suffering. Thinking abou t such suffering, we will generate a strong 
determination to continue to meditate will be able to easily cut off excitement.   H e r e  L a m a  T s o n g k h a p a  s a y s  t h a t  e x c i t e m e n t  i s  d i s t r a c t e d  m i n d  t h a t  i s  a  f a c t o r  o f  attachment. The best antidote to it is renunciation or disillusion ment with cyclic existence.  
 Thursday morning, 24 February 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B The stages of how to train the mind once you have relied 
1 The basis of leisure – an exhortation to take its essence 
2 How to take the essence 
The second has two points: with regard to the basis of leisure – an exhortation to 
take its essence and how to take the essence. 
 
We are first exhorted to take the essence of this precious hu man rebirth with the eighteen 
qualities and then it is explained how to do so. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-1 The basis of leisure – an exhortation to take its essence 
A Identification of the leisures and endowments 
B Considering their great value 
C Considering the difficulty in finding them 
The first has three points: identificati on of the leisures and endowments, 
considering their great value, and cons idering the difficulty in finding them. 
 
In order to take the essence of  this precious human rebirth we first need to recognize the 
eight leisures and ten endowments. Then we should think about their great meaning or 
usefulness and the difficulty of finding them. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-1A Identification of the leisures and endowments 
1 Leisures 
2 Endowments 
The first one has two points: leisures and endowments. 
 
There are eight leisures and ten endowments. Am ong the eight leisures, four are related to 
human beings and four to non-human beings.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
176 2B4B-1A1 Leisures 
A Identification of the leisures 
First: In the Summary95 it says: 
 
Through rules the entity of the many worlds of animals 
And eight unfree states are renoun ced; thus leisure won forever. 
 Accordingly, the leisures consist in freedom from the eight unfree states.  
B The eight unfree states: 
(1) [Living in] a border remote country where the four types of followers [of the 
Buddha] do not roam, (2) being stupid or dumb and having incomplete faculties – 
missing a limb, an ear etc., (3) holding wr ong views that regard past and future 
lives, actions and their effects, and the Three Jewels as non-existent, (4) being 
without the word of a conqueror, with no  buddha having appear ed [in the world], 
are the four unfree states of humans. {49}  (1-3) The three bad migrations and (4) 
being a long-lived god are the non-human un free states. As for the long-lived gods, 
the Commentary on the Letter to a Friend  discusses two types: discriminationless ones 
and formless ones. The former live in on e region of the fourth concentration, 
Great Result, as if in a solitary place fa r away from a village. The latter are ordinary 
beings born in the formless realm. In Discourse on the Ei ght Unfree States  gods of the 
desire realm who are constantly distracted by  activities of desire are explained to be 
long-lived gods. 
 
The four non-leisures as a human being are: 
1. to be born as a human being in a barbar ic land without religion and culture, 
2. to be born as a human being who is stupid, that is, with a mind that is unable to 
understand, or to be deaf and dumb, that is ,  u n a b l e  t o  s p e a k ,  o r  t o  b e  b o r n  w i t h  
incomplete faculties or limbs, 
3. to be born as a human being but to hold wrong views thinking that past and future lives, 
the law of actions and results, or  the Three Jewels do not exist; 
4. to be born as a human being in a dark eon when no buddha has come to the world due to 
which the Dharma does not exis t. Such a period is called “dark” because due to lacking 
the Dharma there is no light of wisdom. 
The four non-leisures as a non-human are: 1. to be born as a hell being, 
2. to be born as a hungry ghost, 
3. to be born as an animal, and 
4. to be born as a long-lived god. 
Long-lived gods are the gods called “Without Di scrimination” who are born in a part of the 
fourth concentration called Great Result. Great Result can be likened to Vatican City, which 
is located inside the city of Ro me. Such gods only discriminate that they have been born in 
this state, thinking “Now I have been born,”  and then they remain for eons without any 
discrimination until the time of death when th ey discriminate, “Now I will die.” In between 
                                             
 
95 Summary of the Perfection of Wisdom in 80,000 Verses  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
177 their birth and death their faculties do not fu nction and they do not have any manifest 
minds, due to which it is impossible for them to  practice Dharma. They are also said to be 
“mindless,” meaning that they do not have any ma nifest minds, that is, although they have a 
mind they do not have any manifest minds.  
 The Commentary on the Letter to a Friend  also says that ordinary beings born in the formless 
realm in general cannot practice the Dharma as  they cannot hear teachings. However, there 
are also arya beings, called non-returners, who can be born in Infinite Space through the Peak 
of Existence, who can practice Dharma in the formless realm. 
 The Discourse on the Eight Unfree States  also considers gods of the desire realm to be long-
lived gods. They cannot practice the Dharma  because they are distracted by the many 
enjoyments of the desire realm and therefor e do not think to practice the Dharma.  
 According to the Christian religion wh en someone transgresses any of the Ten 
Commandments, he or she after death will be bo rn in the hell realm and will remain there 
forever. Why do Christians say this? Probably because one remains in the hell realm for so 
many eons that it appears as if one will never get out.  
 There are animals such as some insects that only  live for one day, others that only live for a 
few days, and others that live for a long time. Hungry ghosts live up to 500 years and during 
this time mostly suffer, due to which they are unable to practice the Dharma. However, there 
are some exceptions to the general rule that animals and hungry ghosts cannot practice the 
Dharma. Among nagas, who are included in the an imal realm, some are said to practice the 
Dharma, and some hungry ghosts are said in sutra to be able to practice the Dharma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-1A2 Endowments 
A The five personal endowments 
The second one has two points of which the five personal endowments are said to 
be:  
 Human nature, birth in a central region, complete faculties,  
No extremely perverted deeds, and faith in the foundation. 
 Here “birth in a central region” is to be born in a place where the fourfold 
followers roam. “Complete faculties” cons ist in not being stupid and dumb and 
h a v i n g  c o m p l e t e  l i m b s ,  m i n o r  b o d y  p a r t s ,  a n d  e y e s ,  e a r s ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  “ N o  extremely perverted deeds” implies not having committed or caused to commit an action of immediate retribution. “Faith  in the foundation” is faith in the 
foundation from which all the mundan e and supramundane white dharmas are 
born – discipline. Here “discipline” should be applied to all three collections [of scriptures]. 
 As those five are brought together in one’s own mental continuum and 
constitute circumstances for the accompli shment of the Dharma, they are called 
“personal endowments.” 
 There are five endowments that are personal and five that are related to the society. The five 
personal endowments are: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
178 1. to be born as a human being, 
2. to be born in a central land where the Dharma  teachings exist (in some contexts “center” 
refers to Bodhgaya, which is the center of Indi a, but here it refers to a place in which the 
Dharma exists); 
3. to have all faculties, the mental faculty and the five sense faculties, complete without any 
defect, 
4. to not have committed any of the five action s of immediate retribution – killing one’s 
father, killing one’s mother, killing a foe-de stroyer, drawing blood from a buddha, or 
creating a schism in the Sangha, the result of which ripens immediately after this life; 
5. having faith in the foundation or basis of  white or virtuous qualities – discipline. 
“Discipline” (‘ dul ba ) refers to a pacified mind, or to  the three scriptural baskets or 
collections by means of which we can subdue our mind. By morality our coarse actions of 
body and speech are subdued, by concentratio n our distracted mind is subdued, and by 
wisdom the root of cyclic exis tence – the view of the transito ry collection or the ignorance 
holding all phenomena to be inherently or truly existent – is subdued and uprooted. 
Thus, by means of these three our body, speech,  and mind are subdued, and we can attain 
calm abiding, the basis, and then special insi ght, on the basis of wh ich we can achieve all 
higher realizations and attain buddhahood.  
Since these five endowments are included in our own continuum and enable us to practice 
the Dharma, they are called “personal endowments.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The five endowments conditioned by others 
The five endowments conditioned by others are said to be: 
 
The arrival of a buddha, the holy  Dharma having been taught,  
The teachings abiding and being followed,  
As well as hearts of mercy for others. 
 Here the “arrival or the arisal of a bu ddha” means that, after accumulating the 
collections for three countless eons, he manifestly attained complete buddhahood. 
“The holy Dharma having been taught” means that a buddha or  his hearers have 
taught the Dharma. “The Dharma teaching s abiding” means that the Dharma that 
is accomplished by means of actualizin g the Dharma of the ultimate from the 
Buddha and the Dharma teachings up to no t passing into nirvana {50} have not 
degenerated. “The Dharma teachings th at abide being followed” means that 
through that very realization of the Dharma  there is an awareness that beings have 
the power to directly perceive the holy  Dharma and then there are those who 
follow the teachings in accordance with th at realization. “A heart of mercy for 
others” means that there are alms-givers and benefactors who grant monastic robes 
and the like.   As these five are present in the mental continua of others and constitute circumstances for the accomplishment  of the Dharma, they are called 
“endowments conditioned by others.” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
179  
The five circumstantial endowments are: 1. a buddha has visited the world, 
2. he has taught the Dharma,  
3. his teachings remain in the world, 
4. there are those who follow or practice his teachings, 
5. there are those who out of mercy or compassi on support practitioners by offering them 
food, clothing, and so forth. A fully-ordained monk, a bhikshu, is one who begs for alms. 
The Tibetan translation of bhikshu as gelong ( dge slong ) literally means “virtue-beggar,” 
that is, one who, carrying a bowl, begs for al ms without directly asking for food whereby 
whoever gives him food creates virtue.  
According to sutra, we must accumulate merit for three countless eons in order to become a 
buddha. During this fortunate eon, 1,000 buddhas will visit this world. Among them, 
Buddha Shakyamuni is the fourth. When his teac hings no longer exist, Buddha Maitreya will 
come to this world and teach. For this reason , we need to practice the teachings of Buddha 
Shakyamuni and study the Five Treatise s of Buddha Maitreya, such as the Ornament for Clear 
Realizations  and so fort h. In  t his way, even i f we do not achieve enlightenment in this life, 
when Buddha Maitreya comes to this world we will be among his foremost disciples and will 
definitely attain enlightenment. This promise was made by Buddha Maitreya himself. In fact, 
the reason that Lama Zopa Rinpoche wants to make an enormous statue of Buddha Maitreya 
is to create the cause that Maitreya arrive in th is world, without any delay whatsoever, as soon 
as the teachings of Buddha Shakyamuni disappear. By establishing a connection with Maitreya in this life, we will be born in his retinue and will attain enlightenment.   Buddha Shakyamuni gave teachings and then sh owed the aspect of passing away. However, 
his teachings were memorized and then writte n down by his disciples such as Venerable 
Kashyapa, whereby they were preserved so th at even now there are those who teach the 
Dharma and those who listen to it. Because th ese teachings were transmitted down to us 
without interruption, the Buddha’s doctrine stil l remains in this world. Since the scriptures, 
both sutra and tantra, still remain in their entire ty, it is still possible to achieve their result, 
nirvana and buddhahood.  
 In addition, there exist in the world those who follow the teachings and strive to realize 
them. There exists the Dharma of scripture an d the Dharma of realiz ation. By means of 
hearing and thinking about the Dh arma of scripture it is possible to achieve the Dharma of 
realization, that is, a direct realization of th e four noble truths and their sixteen attributes. 
The four noble truths are true sufferings, true or igins, true cessations, an d true paths, each of 
which has four attributes. True sufferings have the four attributes of being impermanent, 
suffering, empty, and selfless. True origins have  the four attributes of being cause, origin, 
strong producer, and condition. True cessations have the four attributes of being cessation, 
peace, auspicious highness, and definite emerge nce. True paths have the four attributes of 
being path, suitable, achievement, and definite deliverance. Buddha Sh akyamuni gave his first 
teaching on the subject of the four noble trut hs at Sarnath in India. These teachings still 
remain in that we can still hear teachings on this subject, think about them, and meditate on 
them. Some people think that all phenomena ar e created by the mind of Ishvara or by God 
and so forth. However, since this would mean that God also created suffering and problems, 
he could not be compassionate. This is the main point of difference between Buddhism and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
180 other religions. There are also different points  of view regarding self lessness. One view or 
tenet is that there is no self-suppo rting substantially existent self.  
 Attachment, for example, is a cause. Since it brings many results, it is an origin. A cause 
produces a result. For example, an action is an origin or source that gives rise to a result. 
When human beings work, this functions as a cause or origin of money. Without working, 
money does not fall from the sky! If a farmer does not sow any seeds, there will not be the 
result of crops. Therefore, first we need a ca use or origin, the seed, from which can come the 
result, crops, that can be used as food and so forth. The farmer needs to work in order to 
experience the result. Without an action, a result will not arise.   Attachment is also a strong producer that give s rise to a result – mental pain and suffering. 
It also brings the result of babies!   Attachment is also a condition. For example, when we sow a rice seed in field, the seed 
itself is the main cause of a rice seedling, wher eas the condition for the rice seedling is water, 
sunshine, and so forth. If there is the cause but not the necessary conditions, the result will 
not be produced. Attachment ca n also act as a condition.  
 A true cessation is the cessation of the affl ictions and sufferings. How can they cease? By 
meditating true paths and applying their antidote s. By eliminating the cause, the afflictions, 
the result, suffering, will cease. If there is no cause, a result will not arise. For example, if we do not want a tree in our garden but only cut off its branches it will continue to grow. As long as the roots are not dug up, it will continue to  bring a result. Likewise, even if we stop 
engaging in negative actions of body and speech, as long as we do not uproot the view of the transitory collection they will continue to bring results. If we eliminate the afflictions, we will 
have peace and have auspicious highness. Thus, the root is eliminated by means of true paths.  
 We have a negative side and a positive side wi thin our mind. We need to try to increase the 
strength of the positive side an d diminish the strength  of the negative side and thereby slowly 
eliminate it. In this way, the positive side will  increase like the waxing moon and the negative 
side will decrease like the waning moon. The Kalachakra Tantra  calls the waxing lunar phase 
from the first to the fifteenth of the month the white side, and the waning phase from the 
sixteenth to the thirtieth, the black side. In sh ort, we need to understand that black actions 
produce the result of suffering, difficulties, and problems. On the other hand, white actions 
bring the result of happiness and enjoyment, a lack of problems, and success in everything we 
do. Therefore, we need to put effort into eliminating our ne gative emotions, and, if we 
cannot eliminate them, we should at least try to  reduce them. We can do this. Everyone can 
do this. We should keep our mind happy.   The Buddha’s teachings remain in the world and there also exist people who help and 
support practitioners. The doctrine is that of  scriptures and realization. We have some 
knowledge of scriptures and some knowledge of realizations. For example, if we practice the 
ten virtuous actions by safeguarding morality, this  is also called a realization. A realization is 
not necessarily the ability to fly! Someone once  asked me whether the ability to fly indicates 
clairvoyance. I said that it did not, as birds ca n fly yet they are not clairvoyant. They can fly 
due to the power of their karma. Human beings cannot fly because they do not have wings!  
 Thus, the scriptures and realizations exist in the world, followers of the teachings exist, and 
those who help practitioners motivated by compa ssion also exist. The latter have the wish to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
181 give, that is, they have a mind of generosity, due to which they give food, clothing, and so 
forth to Dharma practitioners.  
 These are the five endowments from the side  o f  o t h e r s  i n  t h a t  t h e y  e x i s t  i n  o t h e r s ’  
continua. Therefore, we presently have all eight een qualities, both the eight leisures and ten 
endowments. We are extremely lucky. With these we can practice the Dharma. We can 
achieve higher realizations in this life, or, if we  cannot do so in this life, we will be able to 
leave imprints on our mind that will ripen in our next life so that we will once again be able 
to practice strongly and achieve higher and high er realizations. In this way we will continually 
go upward. 
 We should try to generate more compassion in ou r mind and to relax, relax, relax, and to not 
worry but to be more relaxed. In this way it will be easier to understand the teachings. Slowly we will be able to understand more, whereby more knowledge will arise in our mind. This will be helpful for ourselves and others, our family members and so forth. In our society we need 
t o  hav e a go o d-hear t  fo r  each o t her , t o  hel p eac h o t her , an d t o  li v e i n  har m o n y w i t h eac h  
other. We should have a subdued mind and resp ect for others. We need to try to do this.

Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 50) says:  
2B4B-1B Considering their great value 
1 Considering the inappropriateness of someone with this special base acting like an animal 
Second: If no practice of the completely  p u r e  D h a r m a  f o r  t h e  s a k e  o f  l a s t i n g  
happiness comes about at all, you are like an  animal despite your birth in a higher 
realm,96 since animals also endeavor merely  to attain happiness and eliminate 
suffering until they die. 
 
Since we presently have this special body or base , it would not be correct to act like an animal. 
However, if we think only of this life (of obta ining things like food, clothing, and so forth) 
and do not do anything for our future lives, th at is, do not practice Dharma, even though we 
have the aspect of a human being, we would be like animals since they too strive to eliminate 
suffering and obtain happiness. In other words, although we are born as a human being, by 
behaving in this way we would be not different from an animal. To walk upright like a human being but to behave like a dog or cat, thin king only to obtain temporal happiness and 
eliminate suffering, would not be right. For exam ple, even animals instinctively act to protect 
their babies from harm. If we, who have been born as human beings, were to act like them it would not be good. We should do something more than animals. Since we have a human 
b o d y  w e  s h o u l d  m a k e  o u r  l i f e  m e a n i n g f u l  b y  w o r k i n g  f o r  t h e  s a k e  o f  o u r  f u t u r e  l i v e s .  W e  should not have a meaningless li fe like that of animals.
