FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
182 2 How a body of leisures and endowments is the best 
For the accomplishment of the Mahayana path a base like the one explained 
earlier is necessary. In the Letter to a Disciple  it says: 
 
The base of the path of those gone to  bliss, the tool for leading beings, 
That very strong mind, a strength that human beings have gained,  
That path has not been found by go ds or nagas, nor by anti-gods, 
Not by garudas, vidyadharas, kinnaras, or snakes. 
 
Furthermore, although some gods of th e desire realm with strong habitual 
tendencies97 f r o m  h a v i n g  p r e v i o u s l y  t r a i n e d  o n  t h e  p a t h  o n  a  h u m a n  b a s i s ,  a r e  
suitable as a basis for seeing the truths for the first time, it is impossible to attain 
the arya path for the first time on an upper realm98 basis. However, as explained 
before, the desire realm gods are said to  mostly be an unfree state and therefore 
this is not the best as a basis for atta ining the path for the first time. Again, 
inhabitants of the continent of Uttaraku ru are unsuitable as a basis for vows, 
which is why the bases of [the other] three continents are praised; and among 
them, [especially] the inhabitants of Jambudvipa. 
 
We have presently obtained a precious human rebirth possessing the eight leisures and ten 
endowments. This body is the best of bodies  because on this basis we can achieve the 
mahayana path. In other words, this body can act as the basis of a mind that generates the 
mind of enlightenment, the ma hayana path of seeing, and so forth. The human body is the 
best basis for this and, among human beings, those of the southern continent, Jambudvipa, 
have the very best of human bodies. Since we  have the eight leisures and ten endowments 
that were explained before, we need to  use them to go to higher states.  
 Nagarjuna composed the text cited here, Letter to a Disciple . He says, in short, that we have 
a human body that can act as the base or suppo rt of a path. We also have a powerful mind, 
therefore we can achieve a path more easily th an other beings. Gods, nagas, and anti-gods do 
not have a body that is the su pport of the mahayana path. Whil e some of these beings have 
bodies that are larger and stronger than ours, our mind is stronger an d more powerful than 
theirs in terms of accomplishing the path. Fo r example, the desire realm gods have much 
taller bodies than ours, and those of the form realm gods are even taller, but their minds are 
not as powerful. Also nagas can be very large, thei r body is not suitable as a basis or support of 
the mahayana path. Likewise, the bodies of an ti-gods, garudas, vidh yadharas, kinnaras, and 
snakes are not suitable supports.   Due to having trained in the path in the pr evious life as a human being, when someone 
takes rebirth in the next life as a desire realm god, he can come to directly see the four noble 
truths or directly realize emptiness due to th e latencies he deposited earlier on as a human 
being. However, in general gods of the desire realm cannot come to see emptiness directly. In 
addition, the gods of the form realm, although th ey have a large body, have achieved an actual 
                                             
 
97 JN: bag chags , also translated as “latencies” and “imprints” 
98 i.e., form and formless realms – as opposed to “higher realm” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
183 concentration, and possess clairvoyance and other qualities, cannot generate the path of 
seeing for the first time.   Thus, it is mainly human beings of the desire  realm who can achieve the path of seeing for 
the first time, and among all human beings those of the southern continent have the very best 
of conditions. The human beings of the sout hern, eastern, and western continents can 
generate the mind of enlightenment, and ca n enter the path of accumulation, path of 
preparation, and path of seeing. However, th e human beings of the northern continent, 
Unpleasant Sound, are not a suitable basis even for the vows of individual liberation. Why is 
the support of a human being of the southern  continent best? Because a buddha has visited 
this world and because the time of our death is not fixed, given that some humans die in the 
womb, some soon after they are born, some as young children, some as adolescents, and so 
forth. Our lifespan is so uncertain that a gran dfather or grandmother can still be alive while 
his or her grandchild has already died. Since th e lifespan of human beings in Jambudvipa is 
not fixed, it is easier to generate renunciati on or definite emergence, due to which it is 
possible to quickly achieve a path. In addition, the support of a human being of the southern 
continent is the best because the Buddha, in the form of a supreme emanation body, visited 
this world and in the aspect of a human being, a fully-ordained monk, gave teachings. He did so because in this way it was easier to subdue beings and bring about a result.  
 On the other hand, the human beings of the ea stern continent, Tall Body, have a lifespan 
that is fixed, thus they do not die at variou s ages like the human be ings of the southern 
continent. Also the human beings of the wester n continent have a fixed lifespan. Here “west” 
does not refer to Europe and so forth as our whole world, according to Buddhist cosmology, 
is included in the “southern continent.” Theref ore, the eastern continent and so forth that 
are mentioned in the Buddhist scriptures refer to places that are not included in our world, the planet Earth. All the human beings of th e southern continent can practice the Dharma, 
generate renunciation, enter a path, and achieve the state of a foe-destroyer, bodhisattva, or 
b u d d h a .  T h e r e  i s  n o  o n e  a b o u t  w h o m  i t  c a n  b e  s a i d  t h a t  h e  o r  s h e  c a n n o t  a t t a i n  buddhahood. We cannot see the inner qualities of  others, therefore it is not the case that 
because the outer body is beautiful, the person also has many qualities inside. Someone’s body can be beautiful while his or her mind can be very hard, or filled with attachment, 
anger, pride, and so forth. On the other hand, someone can have a very ugly body but inside have a mind that is very good and kind. Nor can it be said that because someone has a tall and good-looking body that he has a lot of knowledge. We cannot measure inner knowledge 
by the size or beauty of the body.  The human beings of the northern continent, Unpleasant Sound, have  a fixed lifespan of 
1,000 years. They cannot die before this, that is , there is no untimely death. Also they are 
naturally all well-off, easily obtain crops withou t need to sow seeds, and do not need to do 
business. Because they easily obtain whatever th ey need, they are very happy. For this reason 
they do not think about Dharma and do no t feel a need for Dharma. Their minds are 
distracted by their happiness. Similarly, we see people who always listen to music in order to 
distract their mind and make themselves happy. Maybe some of them also drink a bit at the 
same time in order to relax even more!   In short, our human body is the best, best , best. We have obtained this body now, 
therefore it is necessary to utilize it to practi ce Dharma, to develop our inner qualities so that 
our realizations will grow. This is  our job! We need to try to do  this. We should not just think 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
184 about how to make money and how to do busi ness, trying one way and then another and 
thinking only of the financial concerns of this life wishing to increase our wealth and 
possessions. This is not our main job! We n eed to do some work because we need food, 
clothing, a house, bedding, and so forth. Wi thout working, money does not spontaneously 
arise. Therefore, we do need to work but at  the same time we should put effort into 
developing our inner qualities.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim says:  
3 How to train in the wish to take the essence, having brought to mind those [two] objectives  
“Consequently, why would I act in such a way that an excellent base like this one I 
have gained {51} does not have a result ? If I do not make it meaningful, could 
there be a greater self-betrayal and a bi gger stupidity? Having passed through the 
many perilous places of unfreedom, such as  the bad migrations, again and again, if 
I were to go back to those places, forsaking this one-off liberation as meaningless, it 
would be like turning mindless as though stupefied by a magic spell99.” By way of 
thoughts like these you should meditate again and again. In Engaging in Bodhisattva 
Behaviour it says: 
 
Having found this kind of leisure, 
Not to cultivate what is virtuous, No betrayal could be a greater, 
Nothing more deluded than that. 
 
And: 
Having found this place so rare, 
Hard to find and beneficial, 
If I, well aware of it, Were to be taken to that hell again 
 
As if deluded by magic spells, Has my mind here wasted away?  Through what delusion I do not know either What has become of my mind, my feelings? 
 Since we have presently found this precious huma n rebirth, we should not waste it. If we do 
not put effort into takings its essence, it will be come meaningless, in the sense that it will not 
bring a result. In this way we will only cheat ours elves, no one else will cheat us. It would be a 
big delusion, done out of ignorance. Because we are intelligent we sometimes try to cheat 
others by not acting honestly and with a good he art, but in reality by not treating others well 
we only end up cheating ourselves.  We should not do like this.  
 If having traveled a very narrow and perilous  road, we have now achieved this precious 
human rebirth we must use it well. Right now we have escaped from the non-leisures, rebirth 
                                             
 
99 JN: rig sngags , literally “knowledge-mantra” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK 22-24 February 2005 (04) 
185 in the hell realm, hungry ghost realm, and animal realm, and have just this one time been 
born as a human being. Therefore, we need to  think about its usefulness and not waste it. For 
example, if wishing to buy jewels or nice thin gs in a certain place we go there but return 
empty-handed, this would be very foolish. We  would be like someone under the power of 
black magic or black mantras, which make us as though unconsciousness or intoxicated. 
Sometimes when traveling in India people are offe red an orange to eat and, having eating it, 
f a l l  i n t o  a  k i n d  o f  t r a n c e  i n  w h i c h  t h e y  c a n see that the other person is stealing their 
belongings but they are as though petrified an d cannot do anything to stop them. We should 
not act like this! There were two monks wh o went to Assam from Buxa. One was Geshe 
Tsering, Lama Yeshe’s half-brother who lives in Tushita, Dharamsala. The other is an old 
monk, now maybe 89 or 90 years old, who lives in Sera. When traveling to Assam they were 
offered an orange to eat, became intoxicated,  and everything, including all their documents, 
were stolen from them. However, later on the documents were given to the police who 
returned them to them.

FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
186 Monday afternoon, 28 February 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 51) says: 
4 The inappropriateness of returning empty-handed given that not only ultimate but also temporary 
objectives are easily accomplished 
Thus you should also consider the method  that allows you to easily attain the 
causes of an excellent higher realm body100, resources, and a retinue, which are not 
only greatly meaningful from an ultimate but also from a temporary point of view, 
by means of the basis: giving, ethical disc ipline, patience, and so on. If you do not 
apply yourself day and night to the causes  of those two by me ans of this greatly 
meaningful basis, it would seem to be like returning empty-handed from an island of precious jewels. 
 We should not be like someone who returns empty-handed from an island where wish-
fulfilling gems can be found. Now that we have obtained th is precious human rebirth we 
need to take its essence so as to obtain our final or ultimate objectives or aims as well as all our temporal aims. To do this, we need to practi ce generosity and so forth in order to obtain 
a body of high status, plenty of resources, and to be surrounded by good people. In short, we 
need to practice generosity, safeguard morality, and develop patience as these three are important in order to attain a good human bo dy. Monks and nuns who have less work than 
lay people should study and meditate more so as to also develop concentration and wisdom. 
Everyone, whether lay or ordained, needs effort  in order to achieve their aims. In other 
words, we all need to eliminate laziness and develop joyous effort.   Generosity is the cause of wealth. Thus, if we want to have all the conditions that are 
needed in order to study and meditate in the futu re, we should practice generosity. If we want 
a human rebirth in the future, we should practi ce morality by abandoning killing, stealing, 
sexual misconduct, lying, divisive speech, harsh words which hurt others’ minds, and idle talk 
or gossip. We also need to try mentally to ab andon covetousness, malice, and wrong views. In 
other words, we need to eliminate the ten non- virtuous actions and practice their opposites, 
the ten virtuous actions. Morality is the wish to abandon negative actions, that is, it is a mind 
of abandonment. Alternatively, it is the wish to abandon bad behavior, bad attitudes, and bad 
habits. Morality is also a mind of safeguarding, that is, the wish to guard or protect virtuous 
actions of body, speech, and mind. It includes ta king vows, such as the five lay precepts, the 
vows of novice monks and nuns, and so forth. Ha ving taken these vows, we need to safeguard 
them.   In addition, we need to practice the diff erent types of patience. For example, when 
someone says something bad to us  we should try not to respond with bad words or actions. 
We should not retaliate, but instead ask to be  forgiven for any mistakes we have made. We 
will not lose anything by doing this. We also need patience when studying and meditating on 
the Dharma. In particularity, we need the patience with respect to understanding and 
meditating on emptiness. We also need the pa tience of voluntarily accepting suffering that 
thinks, for example, “This negative karma has ripe ned upon me now, therefore it will come to 
an end and afterward I will be happy.” 
                                             
 
100 JN: mngon mtho’i lus , “body of high status” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
187  We also need joyous effort, that is, we n eed to develop effort and eliminate laziness. 
Sometimes we tend to think “I am tired today, I will do it tomorrow,” and then again the next 
day we think, “I will do it tomorrow.” Until we  die there will always be a tomorrow. We need 
to eliminate this attitude as well as the othe r kinds of laziness. Even when, due to having 
worked hard, the body is tired, we should thin k that this tiredness is but a small suffering, 
and that if we do not practice the Dharma we will  continue to create negative actions that will 
bring rebirth in the lower realms where there wi ll be much greater suffering. Therefore, why 
are we not able to bear some small suffering now? We should try to do bear it. 
 Concentration can be difficult as we have ma ny distractions. However, we should try to 
meditate every day even for five minutes, or even  one, two, or three minutes, or a bit longer 
on an object; it does not matter which one. In  this way our mind will gradually become a bit 
more stable on the object. We can also do anal ytical meditation. Previously it was said that 
analytical meditation is very important when  we meditate on the stages of the path. 
 In this way we should practice the six perfections, the main causes of achieving 
enlightenment. Therefore, we sh ould think, “Now I can do this . It is easy. Therefore, now 
that I have a human rebirth, I will put effort  into practicing the six perfections day and 
n i g h t . ”  I f  w e  w e r e  t o  g o  t o  a n  i s l a n d  w h e r e there were many jewels but returned empty-
handed, without bringing anything back with us, this would be foolish. Similarly, as we now 
have this precious human rebirt h that can be used to attain both our temporary and ultimate 
aims, it would be foolish not to use it well . With this precious human rebirth we can 
temporarily attain a higher rebirth and avoid a lo wer rebirth, and finally or ultimately we can 
achieve nirvana or omniscience. Thus, we need to put effort into doing this.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-1C Considering the difficulty in finding it 
1 Illustration of the difficulty of finding it 
2 Reasons why it is difficult to find 
3 The rarity of those obtaining the causes of leisure and endowments 
4 The result of sustaining those objectives 5 The method of meditation with four special features 
6 Differentiated, sustained mode [of practice] 
2B4B-1C1 Illustration of the difficulty in finding it 
Third: In the Basis of Scriptural Transmission  it says that the beings who die from the 
bad and happy realms and migrate to the ba d realms are like the dust of the great 
earth, and that the beings who are born fr om there into the happy realms are like 
the dust taken up with the tip of a fingernail. {52} Therefore, [a precious human 
rebirth] is difficult to find from both the happy and the bad realms. 
 
It is difficult not only to find a precious human rebirth in particular but even just to find a 
human rebirth in general. The text cited here s ays that those who die from a bad migration or 
from a happy migration and are reborn in the lo wer realms are like the dust that composes 
the entire earth. On the other hand, thos e who die from a bad migration or a happy 
migration and are reborn in a happy migration are like the amount of dust that sits on the tip 
of a fingernail, that is, it happens very rarely. Th us, to take a lower rebirth, like a fingersnap, is 
very easy. On the other hand, to take rebirth in the upper realms is very difficult. Why is this? 
To discover the answer we can examine our own mind. Having done so, we will see that we 
continually create many causes for taking rebirt h in the lower realms while creating very few 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
188 causes – virtue – for taking rebirth in the upper realms. Most of our actions are non-virtuous, 
the result of which is suffering, that is, rebirt h in the lower realms. There are different lower 
realms where different degrees of suffering are experienced: the hell realms, hungry ghost 
realm, and animal realm. However, in all of them there is suffering. Because we create the 
causes of suffering, we experience the result. On the contrary, our virtuous actions are very 
small. We mostly create non-virtuous actions ou t of anger, attachment, jealousy, doubt, wrong 
view, belligerence, resentment, an d the like. Thus, our mind is rich in non-virtuous mental 
factors, in fact it is filled with them, like a rich person’s treasure house. Our virtuous minds, 
on the other hand, are very few. We need to check our own mind to verify this, not other 
people’s minds! If we were paid to be a spy it  would be appropriate to check others’ faults as 
in this way we would earn our salary. However, since we are not paid to do this, why should 
we do it, given that it only causes us to crea te negative karma that brings us unhappiness and 
suffering? We need to understand this and then  make a choice about what is better to do. 
With this understanding, we should do as much as we can to eliminate non-virtuous actions 
and to not allow non-virtuous minds to arise, th at is, to try to stop them by applying their 
antidotes. This may be difficult, but we should  at least try to stop thinking non-virtuous 
thoughts and to be more relaxed. When doing Zen meditation we try to stop thoughts from 
arising, sometimes this can be useful. We need to take care of ourselves as others cannot do 
this for us. And even though the buddhas try to take care of us, we are not able to perceive 
this.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-1C2 Reasons why it is difficult to find 
If you ask just why it is so di fficult to find, it says in the Four Hundred [Verses] : 
 
For the most part people fix 
On the side that is unholy. 
Consequently common beings  Mostly surely go to bad realms. 
 
Accordingly, there are many humans and ot her beings who mostly fix on the side 
of the ten non-virtues and that is also why, consequently, they go to the bad 
realms. Furthermore, if, for each mome nt of anger and the like towards a 
bodhisattva, you have to spend one eon in  the hell of Incessant Torment (Avichi), 
the negativities accumulated over many li fetimes which have not come to fruition 
nor been overcome by antidotes, and wh ich are present in your own continuum, 
will doubtlessly cause you to spend many eons in the bad realms.  
 
Why is this precious human rebirth difficult to find? In his Four Hundred Verses  Aryadeva says 
that it is difficult to find because most human beings hold to the side of the unholy rather 
than holding to the side of the holy. That is, be cause they look to the bad side and not to the 
good side, most ordinary beings definitely go  to the bad migrations. In other words, they 
mostly take rebirth in the lower realms due to creating non-virtuous actions and not creating 
virtuous actions. An ordinary being is someone who is not an arya being, a superior being. 
Such ordinary beings hold to the unholy, that is , not being interested in the holy they hold to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
189 its opposite. “Holy” refers to that which is g ood, for example, the positive actions in which 
arya beings engage. Therefore, the “unholy” is that which arya beings avoid, for example, bad 
actions. Thus, the reason that it is difficult for us to obtain a precious human rebirth is 
because we hold to the unwholesome or non-vi rtuous and thereby create many non-virtuous 
actions due to which we will not be  reborn in the ha ppy migrations.  
 In short, we mostly engage in the ten non-vi rtuous actions because our mind is mostly fixed 
on the side of non-virtue. Due to the cause, non-virtue, we will take rebirth in the lower 
realms, meaning that having died we will go to the lower realms. It is not that we are walking 
toward them right now!  
 Even one instant of anger toward a bodhisa ttva, a being who has generated the mind of 
enlightenment , can destroy hundreds of eons of  virtue, as a result of which we will take 
rebirth in the hell of Unrelenting Torment (Avici ) for eons. But we have created non-virtuous 
actions over many lifetimes, the results of wh ich have not yet ripened nor have they been 
destroyed by their antidote, due to which this non-virtuous energy or imprint abides within 
us. This is what acts as the ca use for us to take rebirth in the lower realms, and not in the 
higher realms. Due to all the non-virtuous ac tions that we have created and the lack of 
virtuous actions, it becomes difficult to be reborn in the upper realms.  
 If we were to spend one day sitting on our meditation cushion and checking our body in 
terms of how we move, checking our speech in te rms of what we say, and checking our mind 
in terms of what we think, we would come to understand that we mostly create non-virtuous 
actions, and not virtuous actions. For this reason , it is difficult to find  a rebirth in the happy 
realms, especially as a human being with a pr ecious human rebirth possessing the eighteen 
qualities, and instead we mostly take rebirth in  the lower realms. Since we have obtained this 
present opportunity just this one time, we have to be careful not to waste it but to take its 
essence by practicing the Dharma, developing  love for all sentient beings, a mind of 
enlightenment, and a good heart. We should de velop these attitudes toward everyone, it not 
being the case to develop them toward our frie nds but not toward people who we consider to 
be neutral and not toward our enemies, who in stead we want to be unhappy and to suffer. 
We should give up such attitudes. Developing love and so forth for others is said to be the best way that we can take care of ourselves.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-1C3 The rarity of those obtaining the causes of leisure and endowments 
Although, if you thus made sure to clear away and purify the causes of bad 
migrations that were accumulated earlier and closed the door to new engagement 
in them, happy migrations would not be rare, as it is, they are very rare indeed. If 
you fail to do so, you will go to bad migrations, and, once you have gone to bad 
migrations, you do not create virtue, but always commit negativities, and therefore 
do not even hear the names of the happy realms for many eons, as it says in 
Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior.  
 This is similar to what was said before. In short,  why is it difficult to obtain a happy or higher 
rebirth? It is because we do not create the ca uses of the eight leisures and ten endowments. 
Since we rarely do this, we do not obtain a precious human rebirth. In addition, we have not purified the non-virtuous actions that we created both in past lives and earlier on in this life, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
190 which are the causes of taking rebirth in the lo wer realms. We also continue to newly engage 
in non-virtuous actions. In other words, we have not blocked or closed the door on them. 
Having done non-virtuous actions we need to purify them. We also need to abandon newly 
creating them, that is, we should try to block th e door of engaging in them. If we do this, it 
will not be difficult to obtain a higher rebirth.  However, since we do not do this, it will be 
difficult to obtain such a rebirth.  
 We constantly engage in many new non-virt uous actions and, because we do not purify 
them, they continually increase in strength. Th is will cause us to take rebirth in the bad 
migrations for many, many eons to come. Having  been born there, we will not even hear the 
name “happy migration,” and we will only expe rience suffering. In this way, we will not 
achieve a higher rebirth again.   Therefore, right now we need to create the causes of a precious human rebirth possessing 
the eighteen qualities. In other words, we need  to create virtuous actions, purify the non-
virtuous actions that we have already created, and avoid creating new non-virtuous actions. By 
doing this, we will once again obtain a higher rebirth, that is, in the next life we will once again obtain a precious human rebirth possessing  the eighteen qualities. If we create the 
causes for this, no one can stop it from happen ing. No one else can throw us into the lower 
realms. Even if someone were to throw us into th e ocean or we were to throw ourselves into it 
thinking to commit suicide, if we do not have th e karma to die we will be able to get out. On 
the other hand, if we have created non-virtuous actions we will helplessly jump into the lower 
realms, like someone wishing to commit suicide who jumps off a high bridge into a river. Even though mentally we do not want to commi t suicide, our actions will be similar in that 
we voluntarily jump into the lower realms. Havi ng been reborn there, we will not even hear 
the names of the happy migratio ns – “human being,” “anti-god, ” or “god,” nor will we hear 
the name “buddha,” “foe-destroyer,” or “holy be ing.” The beings born in the hell realm wail 
out of pity for themselves due to their suffering . Therefore, we should try not to create non-
virtuous actions in the future if we wish to take rebirth in th e happy migrations.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-1C4 The result of sustaining those objectives 
Potowa said:  
 
In the region of Phan there was a soli d fort by the name of Ma'i Chakar 
that had been seized by an enemy. For a long time the people had been 
their poor servants. One old man used to be in anguish about this fort 
and when, one day, he heard people saying that it had been won back, 
he grabbed his spear and, not being able to walk, dragged himself along 
saying, “If only the capture of Ma’i Chakar is not a dream.” Like this, 
having won leisure and endowments, one must also derive such joy from it and practice the Dharma. 
 {53} In accordance with this statement, yo u should meditate in order to gain this 
kind of attitude. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
191 This passage cited from Potowa is difficult to understand in the Tibetan. “Potowa,” the name 
of place, is the name of a well-known Kadam geshe whose present incarnation is Dra-gyab 
(Behind the Rock) Rinpoche who live s in Sera Monastery, India.  
 In Tibet there was a palace or fortress called Ma’i Chakar that had been seized by an 
enemy. One old man, having heard that this palace had been won back, was overjoyed. We 
should be like him in that we should be extremely happy that we have obtained a precious 
human rebirth possessing the eight leisures an d ten endowments. Based on this, we should 
make a determination to practice the Dharma an d to use this life we ll. Thus, we need to 
develop a strong motivation to put effort into developing this attitude. By doing this, we will 
slowly obtain inner qualities and experience inner happiness. If, on the other hand, it is useless to be sad and discouraged, thinking “I cannot do anything. Poor me!” Instead we 
s h o u l d  t h i n k ,  “ I  a m  c a p a b l e .  W h y  c a n  I  n o t  d o  i t ?  I n  t h i s  v e r y  l i f e  I  c a n  a t t a i n  h i g h e r  realizations and even buddhahood.” Encouraging ou rselves in this way, we should happily put 
effort into practicing the Dharma whereby we will experience the result of happiness. 
Contrarily, if we think badly about ourselves, we will only become mentally depressed and incapable of doing anything. We will not be able to sleep, nor to digest or enjoy our food and 
perhaps will even feel nauseous at the mere si ght of food! All this is created by ourselves 
alone. If we think everyone is against us, that ev eryone is looking at us badly, and that no one 
wants to communicate with us, this is only our mental projection. Perhaps someone else 
wants to talk to us but it is us who look away! This is not right. Therefore, it is better to think, 
“Now I have obtained this precious human rebirt h. I am very lucky. Since I want to obtain 
such a rebirth again in the future, I will prac tice Dharma day and night in order to develop 
my inner concentration, generosity, morality, pati ence, and so forth.” If we try to do this, we 
will be able to. For example, if we do not know something, by studying it we will come to know it. For example, if we want to learn Tibetan, first we have to learn the alphabet. Perhaps it may appear to be difficult at the beginning bu t, by putting effort into it, one day we will be 
able to read Tibetan. I myself have not stud ied English much but due to listening to many 
translators I can now speak some English. Even though it is not perfect, other people can 
understand me and I can say many things. I did not study much, but I learned a bit. Therefore, even to learn a language we need to  endure some difficulties as the pronunciation 
may be difficult, the grammar is different, and so  forth. For example, in Italian this cup is 
called “ tazza ,” whereas in Tibetan ta means horse and tsa means salt, therefore tazza  would be 
horse-salt! In English we say A, B, C, D ( aye, bee, see, dee ), whereas in Italian we say a, be, chee, 
dee a n d  s o  f o r t h .  I t  i s  n o t  e a s y  t o  p r o n o u n c e even one letter properly! For example, the 
Tibetan sound nga is difficult for non-Tibetans to say. To  add to this difficulty, there are even 
different pronunciations among the Tibetans themselves!  In short, when we put effort into learning something new, we will slowly, slowly come to 
understand it. We cannot expect to learn something new immediately.   In our everyday life, we need to try to keep our mind happy and relaxed, to be less worried. 
We should try this! Think, “Everything will im prove. One day I will know everything. I will 
also be able to fly by myself. I will no longer need to take planes!” for example, there is a 
realization called “fast-walking,” which we can ob tain. With this attainment, we could go to 
and return from Pisa (about 40 kms. away) in ten minutes. I am not joking. Some people can 
do this. There was once a monk named Geshe Jampel Senggye. In one day someone met him while walking in a particular place, and on the same day another person saw him another 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
192 places, these two places being as far apart as Ro me and Milan. Although this geshe seemed to 
walk at a normal speed, others could not keep up with him. This was due to his having the 
realization of fast-walking. We t oo can attain such qualities.  
 
Tuesday morning, 1 March 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 53) says:  
2B4B-1C5 The method of meditation with four special features 
Thus, four thoughts regarding the Dharma  are necessary for the generation of a 
fully qualified wish to take the e ssence of this basis of leisure: 
1. The need to accomplish [the Dharma] – because all sentient beings only want 
happiness and do not want suffering, and attaining happiness and eliminating 
suffering only depends on the holy Dharma. 
2. The ability to accomplish [the Dhar ma] – because you have the external 
condition, a spiritual teacher, and the internal condition, the leisures and 
endowments. 
3. The need, also, to accomplish  [the Dharma] in this life  – because if you do not 
accomplish it within this life, it will be difficult to find the leisures and 
endowments in many [future] rebirths. 
4. The need to accomplish [the Dharma] ri ght now – because the time of death is 
uncertain. 
The third one counteracts the laziness of ab andoning it due to thinking “I will 
practice the Dharma in later lives,” and the fourth one counteracts the laziness of 
not engaging in it due to thinking “It n eeds to be accomplished in this life, but 
although it was not accomplished in earlie r years, months, and days, it is enough 
to accomplish it much later on.” It is admissible to make them into three through 
subsuming these two under “ [the need to ] accomplish it quickly.” Like that, 
remembering death can also be linked to them. In any case, as this is a lengthy 
topic it will be explained below. 
 If we wish to take the essence of this precious  human rebirth that we have obtained, we need 
to generate four thoughts in our mind. First of all we need to practice Dharma . Why is this? 
Because all sentient beings want to be happy and do not want to suffer, we need to help them to achieve happiness and to eliminate suffering  and this depends on practicing the holy 
Dharma or a spiritual path. We cannot do th is in any other way. We cannot buy their 
happiness with money, that is, no matter how ri ch we are we cannot buy happiness for them. 
Perhaps our money could give them some physic al happiness, but it cannot give them mental 
happiness. Therefore, the only thing for us to do is to practice the holy Dharma.  
 In addition, we are able to practice the Dharma . Why is this? We possess the two favorable 
conditions for this. First of all we possess th e external favorable condition of having met a 
virtuous spiritual friend or teacher, and seco ndly we possess the internal favorable condition 
of having a precious human rebirth possessing th e eighteen qualities. Due to these conditions, 
we are able to practice the Dharma. “To prac tice Dharma” means to strive to abandon all 
negative actions of body, speech, and mind and to strive to collect all virtuous actions of body, 
speech, and mind. By doing this, we can a ttain happiness in the future and eliminate 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
193 s u f f e r i n g .  W e  c a n n o t  d o  t h i s  i n  a n y  o t h e r  w a y .  T h e r e f o r e ,  w e  n e e d  t o  p u t  e f f o r t  i n t o  
practicing the Dharma. We should not fix our mind on external things such as making 
money, doing business, and so forth. We tend to  fix on external projects but as they often 
cannot be accomplished we become sad and do no t know what to do. It is not easy to have 
success in business and it is not easy to make money.  
 Then, we need to practice the Dharma in this life . Why is this? Because we have obtained the 
favorable conditions of the eight leisures and ten endowments in this life but it will be 
difficult to find such an opportunity again in our next life.  
 Then, we need to practice the Dharma right now . Why is this? Because the time of our death is 
uncertain. We might die tomorrow, or someth ing may happen later on today and we might 
even die tonight. When we will die is not sure but it is sure that death will definitely come. 
This will be explained more extensively later on.  
 We may think to practice Dharma later on in th is life but, since it is not definite that we 
will be able to do so, we need to eliminate such  laziness. Why is this? Because it is not certain 
when we will die, it could happen even in thes e very days. Therefore, we need to eliminate 
our laziness and practice the Dharma right now.  W e  n e e d  t o  p r a c t i c e  D h a r m a  i n  t h i s  l i f e  
quickly. We should not think, “Today I will  not practice Dharma, maybe I will do it 
tomorrow,” or “This year I will not practice Dh arma, maybe I will do it next year,” or “This 
month I will not practice Dharma, maybe I will do  it next month.” Thinking in this way, we 
will not practice the Dharma now and later on it will also be difficult to practice the Dharma. 
Therefore, we need to eliminate such laziness and just now, quickly, quickly, practice the 
Dharma. To do this we should remember that de ath will definitely come but we do not know 
when. Having been reborn in cyclic existence wh erever, we will definitely die. There is no one 
who will not die. And because the time of our death is uncertain we need to practice the Dharma now. Perhaps today we feel very healthy and are not sick but even this evening we could experience a strong pain in our head or  stomach and very early that next morning we 
may need to be taken to the hospital. We cannot  say that because we are not sick now, we will 
never get sick! Sometimes due to cold, due to food, or due to other conditions, we become 
sick. Due to an imbalance in the elements – eart h, water, fire, and wind – of our body, we can 
lose our life. There are many conditions that ca n take our life, and few conditions that sustain 
it and help us remain healthy. Therefore, we n eed to think, “I must pr actice the Dharma right 
now.” Thinking about death-impermanence in this  way, we will generate a stronger wish to 
practice Dharma so that at the time of death we can die happy. In short, since we accept the 
existence of future lives and wish to take a happy  rebirth, we need to practice Dharma and to 
practice it right now, not later on.  Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-1C6 Differentiated, sustained mode [of practice] 
A How to contemplate from various points of view 
B How to contemplate in particular 
2B4B-1C6A How to contemplate from various points of view 
As the mind is more significantly subdue d if you contemplate from various points 
of view, you should contemplate as explai ned above. If you cannot do that much, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
194 you should subsume them in the three101: (1) what the entity of the leisures and 
endowments is, (2) the manner in which they are greatly meaningful from a 
temporary and ultimate point of view, and (3) how difficult they are to find from 
the point of view of cause and effect. Yo u should take up each of the points 
explained above that suit you and meditate on it. 
 
We should check how to make our mind strong  in the practice of Dharma from various 
points of view. We should think about the enti ty of the leisures and endowments. With this 
precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qu alities we can attain both our temporal and 
ultimate aims. But is it easy to obtain the eigh teen qualities? It is not because we need to 
create the perfect cause for each one of them. Even if we are reborn again as a human being 
we will not necessarily have a precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities. The 
basis of the practice of Dharma is morality or  ethical discipline: the abandonment of killing, 
stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisive sp eech, harsh words, idle talk (which becomes 
heavy due to doing it often), covetousness (a mind that wants this and that), malice (the 
thought to harm), and wrong views (these in gene ral do not arise very much). This is what to 
practice Dharma means, therefore we should try to do practice as  was explained above. In this 
way we will come to consider the success of ou r future lives, which are many, more important 
than the success of this one single life. After that we will come to develop renunciation or 
definite emergence.   With this body we can achieve many temp oral things. We can set up a business, go 
dancing, climb snow mountains, swim in the oc ean… We can do whatever we want. However, 
we can also achieve our final aims, that is, we can ensure the happiness of future lives and can 
also achieve liberation or enlightenment. To do  this, we need to put effort into practicing 
Dharma whereby we will achieve our aims.   This human rebirth is difficult to find because it is difficult to create its cause. Since its 
cause is difficult to create, the result is also difficult to obtain. Without cause a result cannot 
arise. Therefore, we should practice the Dh arma. We should meditate on this precious 
human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities wh ich is difficult to find, but when found is 
very meaningful and useful. Then, having found it, we will quickly lose it. Thus, we should 
meditate on death-impermanence, thinking about the fact that death is certain, the time of 
death is uncertain, and at the time of death noth ing except the Dharma will be of any help to 
us. All our wealth, the many friends and relative s around us whose crying makes us even more 
sad, and our precious body that we have taken so much care of and have cherished, none of 
this can help us. When we die, this body becomes a corpse and even those who once liked our body will hold their nose against the bad odor and experience a sense of repulsion when 
they come near it. In the Indian tradition wh en a family member dies, many wailing woman 
gather around and follow the corpse as it is ta ken to the funeral pyre to be cremated. There is 
no benefit from this. Perhaps in the past the purpose was to show that the person had had a 
good relationship with the ot her people in the village, however nowadays these women are 
paid to cry!

101 “three” – dang gsum  is an insertion by Trijang Rinpoche

195 2B4B-1C6B How to contemplate in particular 
1 The difficulty in finding [the leisures and endowments] from the point of view of their cause 
First: The difficulty in finding [the leis ures and endowments] from the point of 
view of their cause. 
Generally speaking, {54} even for the attain ment of a mere happy migration, it is 
necessary to perform one of the pure virtue s such as discipline. In particular, for 
the attainment of the complete leisures and endowments, you need a lot of roots 
of virtue such as getting hold of a base through pure ethical conduct, to reinforce 
that with giving and the like, and to make connections by means of stainless 
prayers. In that case, it becomes apparent  that those who acco mplish such causes 
are very few, which is why one should th ink that, proportionately, the result – a 
general and specific base of the happy realms – is difficult to find. 
 A precious human rebirth is difficult to find from  the point of view of its cause. In order to 
take a rebirth in the happy migrations in genera l, whether as a human being, an anti-god, or a 
god, it is necessary to have created pure virtue. The basis of taking such a higher rebirth is the 
practice of morality or discipline, which is very difficult to do. Because the cause is difficult to 
create, it is difficult to find a human rebirt h. We can examine this. For example, presently 
there are about 6 billion human beings in our world but if we look at the animals, for 
example, we will see that even the ants in a sm all area of ground are more numerous. Then, if 
we look at the animals living in the ocean we will see that there are even more. Then, there 
are more hungry ghosts than animals, and more  hell beings than hungry ghosts. Therefore, 
even though human beings appear to us to be many in reality there are not so many.  
 It is recounted that one time when a lama  was teaching the lam-rim and explaining how 
difficult it is to obtain a human being, a man in the audience said to those around him that 
obviously the lama had never been to China, where there were many, many human beings, as 
otherwise he would have realized that it is not so difficult to be reborn as a human being. While China is the most populated country in our world, the Chinese people are not very 
happy. Their life is difficult and they are always worried about doing something wrong 
because they could be accused of a crime, be aten, and even killed. Although they are not 
happy, they cannot show this to others. Instead th ey must always appear to be happy. This is 
because human rights are not respected in Chin a. Therefore, even though the Chinese have 
been born as human beings, they are no bette r off than animals. Animals kill and eat each 
other. For example, as soon as the cats that li ve here at the Institute see an insect moving, 
they pounce on it and kill it, even without any wi sh or need to eat it. In  this way they create 
non-virtue. Nor do birds create virtue. However, we as human beings can create virtue, although it is may not be completely perfect.  We can make a determination, for example, 
thinking, “I will try never to kill a single sentient  being,” and then strive to keep this promise. 
This is one morality. We can also make the resolu tions “I will not steal a single thing,” “I will 
try to never engage in sexual misconduct,” “I wi ll try to never lie or cheat other people,” and 
so forth. Even if we cannot practice all ten virt ues, we should at least try to practice one of 
them. This will become the practice of morality th at acts as a cause for us to take rebirth in 
the happy migrations.   Having achieved a precious human rebirth po ssessing all eighteen qualities in a complete 
way, we need to practice morality as the basis. On top of that, we need to practice generosity, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
196 patience, and the other perfections. However,  among them we mainly need to practice 
generosity: the generosity of giving material th ings, the generosity of giving protection, and 
the generosity of giving the Dharma or our knowle dge. We need to do this from a good heart, 
not as a business or as a way to make money.  
 In addition to the practice of morality and generosity, we need to make pure or stainless 
prayers in order to obtain a precious human rebi rth. However, it is not easy to create such 
virtuous actions. Having taken vows, for exampl e, even though we m ay not transgress a root 
vow, we often transgress the smaller vows. It is  also difficult to abandon the ten non-virtues 
and practice the ten virtues perfectly. Therefore, it  is difficult to create the cause of a precious 
human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities. However, because we now have this rebirth, 
we need to make sure that we obtain another one in our next life so that in this way we can slowly improve and gain realizations that le ad to attaining the small enlightenment, the 
middling enlightenment, or the great enlighte nment of buddhahood. We need to practice 
creating the causes of such a precious human re birth. If we do not create the causes, it is 
logical that we will not obtain the result. The re sult is difficult to obtain because in order to 
obtain it we need to create causes of similar ty pe. It is not that non-virtuous actions bring the 
result of happiness, nor that virtuous actions br ing suffering. Therefore, if we want to have a 
happy result we need to create its specific caus e. Because the cause is difficult to create, the 
result, a human rebirth, is difficult to obtain , and a precious human rebirth is even more 
difficult to find. In fact it is rare, only  a very few people have such a rebirth.  
 In short, among those who take a higher rebi rth, those with a precious human rebirth are 
very few. For example, in a family of ten childr en maybe only one or two will be interested in 
the Dharma or spiritual practice, most of them will not be interested in it and will only be 
interested in mundane, material things. We see can this with our own eyes. We need to 
examine our own world as in this way we will  come to understand this subject better.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The difficulty of finding [the leisures and endowments] from the point of view of the result 
The difficulty of finding [the leisures and endowments] from the point of view of 
the result. Meditate on the fact that, from the po int of view of the beings in the bad 
migrations, different in rank from us, ev en the mere happy realms seem hard to 
attain, and from the point of view of bein gs in happy migrations, of the same rank, 
too, those with the distinguished leisures
102 are extremely rare. Geshe Dolpa laid 
great emphasis on this. He said that all other teachings come about brought on by 
this. As it is like this, you should strive accordingly. 
 
In general a higher rebirth and in particular a precious human rebirth are very difficult to 
obtain because we need to create causes that are similar to the result, and we create such c a u s e s  o n l y  v e r y  r a r e l y .  I f  w e  t h i n k  i n  t h i s  w a y ,  w e  w i l l  d e v e l o p  t h e  w i s h  t o  p r a c t i c e  t h e  
Dharma. For example, we should meditate on de ath-impermanence thinki ng: death is certain, 
the time of death is uncertain, and at the ti m e  o f  d e a t h  o n l y  t h e  D h a r m a  w i l l  b e  o f  a n y  
                                             
 
102 i.e., the leisures complemented by the endowments 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
197 benefit to us. We should also meditate on th is precious human rebirth and its cause, pure 
morality, on the basis of which we also need to  practice generosity and make stainless prayers. 
By thinking in this way, we will generate the wish to practice the Dh arma. If we think about 
these subjects as well as the meditations of middl ing beings and great beings, we will want to 
practice the Dharma even more.  
 Due to seeing the benefits of practicing the Dharma, we will want to practice it. For example, if we understand the benefits of inve sting our money, for example, the interest it 
will bring, we will want to invest it and will  engage in investing it in the stock market. 
Likewise, understanding that if we sow seeds in the ground we will later reap the result of a good harvest, we will sow the seeds, do the nece ssary work to make them sprout, and reap the 
result. Likewise, if we use this body well, we will again obtain the result of another precious 
human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualitie s in the future. Understanding this, we will 
want to practice Dharma and will actual ly engage in practicing the Dharma.

2B4B-2 How to take the essence: the three beings’ mode of applying the path103
 
A Generating certainty with regard to the general presentation of the path 
B The actual way of taking its essence

2B4B-2A Generating certainty with regard to the general presentation of the path 
1 How all the Buddha’s teachings are contained in the paths of the three [types of] beings 
2 Giving reasons for gradual guidance from the point of view of the three [types of] beings  
The first one has two points: How all the Buddha’s teachings are contained in the 
paths of the three [types of] beings and giving reasons for gradual guidance from 
the point of view of the three [types of] beings. 
 
We have obtained this precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities, therefore we 
need to take it essence. How should we do th is? There are three ways of practicing the path 
according to the three types of beings.  
 “The general presentation of the path” refers to the path of small beings, path of middling 
beings, and path of great beings. It does not refer to the path of accumulation, path of 
preparation, path of seeing, path of meditation, and path of no-more-learning.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A1 How all the Buddha’s teachings are contained in the paths of the three [types of] 
beings 
A How the meaning of all the excellent teachings [of the Buddha] is subsumed under the 
accomplishment of two objectives 
First: As everything the Buddha did, in the beginning the generation of the mind 
[of enlightenment], in the middle the accumu lation of the collections, and at the 
end manifest, complete buddhahood, was done  for the welfare of sentient beings 
alone, all his Dharma teachings also on ly accomplish the welfare of sentient 
                                              
103 JN: Is this correct? It seems like two outlines have been merged into one. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
198 beings. That being so, the goal to be acco mplished, the welfare of sentient beings, 
is twofold: temporarily high status and ultimately certain goodness.  
 
All the Buddha’s teachings are included in the pa th of small beings, path of middling beings, 
and path of great beings. The pa th of small beings includes th e recognition of the precious 
human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities, that  is it very meaningful, that by means of it 
all temporal and final aims can be attained, and that it is quickly lost and difficult to find 
again. We should think about th e precious human rebirth from th e point of view of its cause 
and from the point of view of the result. From the point of view of its cause, it is necessary to 
practice pure morality and so forth. That fact that it is difficult to find can also be understood 
by means of examples or analogies, many of  which are mentioned in the teachings on the 
stages of the path.   While we have obtained such a rebirth presen tly, it will quickly be lost. Therefore, we 
should think about death-impermanence in relati on to the three points: death is certain, the 
time of death is uncertain, and at the time of death only the Dharma can be of any help. Each 
of these three points is further divided into thr ee for a total of nine points or rounds that are 
to be meditated. We should also meditate on the law of cause and results, for example, that 
non-virtuous actions bring the result of rebi rth in the lower realms. The hell realms are 
composed of eight hot hells, eight cold hells,  various neighboring hells, and the occasional 
hells. There are also different types of hungry  ghosts: those with outer obscurations, those 
with inner obscurations, and those with both outer and inner obscurations. When those with 
outer obscurations look for water and, seeing a ri ver in the distance, actually arrive at there, 
all they see is a dry river bed. Likewise, if th ey see a tree laden with delicious fruit in the 
distance, when they get there all they see is a withered tree without any leaves or fruit. Because 
of these outer obscurations they cannot find  either food or drink. Those with inner 
obscurations, on the other hand, are able to fi nd some food but when they put it in their 
mouth and swallow it, it becomes like molten ir on that burns their throat and stomach. Some 
other hungry ghosts have both outer and inner obscurations. In general, hungry ghosts have 
stomachs that are as big as mountains but their throats are tiny and narrow. If we were to be 
reborn as a hungry ghost we would not be able to bear this suffering. In spite of this, we can be reborn in this state due to th e negativities we have committed. 
 Then we should examine the situation of anim als. Those that are taken care of by human 
beings have a little better life, whereas those that  live in the ocean and th ose that live wild on 
the ground eat each other, are stupid, and so fo rth. This is all the result of non-virtuous 
actions. Seeing this, we must abandon creating  negative actions and seek protection from 
such suffering. Looking around, we will see th at only the Three Jewels, the Buddha, Dharma, 
and Sangha, can help us. Theref ore, we need to develop fait h and confidence in them and 
request them to protect us from the suffering  of the lower realms. Right now we have not 
been born in the lower realms, but fearing that we will fall into them into the future and 
experience great suffering there, we ask the Buddh a to help us. The Buddha helps us by giving 
us teachings. Then, by practicing the Dharma, the actual refuge, we will be protected from 
suffering by actualizing true cessations throug h meditating true paths. This, the Dharma 
Jewel, is the actual refuge that can protect us from all suffering. The Sangha are like our 
helpers. For example, by following the example of how they practice the Dharma, we too will 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
199 be able to stop committing non-virtuous action s, whereby we will be protected from falling 
into the lower realms. Hence, we go for re fuge to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.  
 When we go for refuge to the Three Jewels , we need to abandon something specific in 
relation to each one of them. Thus, if we go for refuge to the Buddha as our final refuge, we 
should abandon taking mundane gods as our final refuge. If we go for refuge to the Dharma, 
w e  s h o u l d  a b a n d o n  h a r m i n g  e v e n  o n e  s i n g l e  b e i n g .  I f  w e  g o  f o r  r e f u g e  t o  t h e  S a n g h a ,  w e  should follow virtuous friends and abandon non-vi rtuous friends, such as Tirthikas, Samkyas, 
Vaishashikas, Charvakas, and other followers of the ancient Hindu traditions.   I n  a d d i t i o n ,  w h e n  w e  g o  f o r  r e f u g e  t o  t h e  Three Jewels, we need to practice something 
specific in relation to each one of them. Th us, with respect to the Buddha, we need to 
consider any image of a buddha as if it were an actual living buddha and treat it with respect. 
With respect to the Dharma, because they teac h us how to meditate and cease suffering we 
should treat Dharma texts with  respect by not walking on them or over them and by not 
sitting on them, and instead put them in a high place. With respect to the Sangha, we should 
respect all monks and nuns by th inking that they are our spiritual friends who help us in our 
practice. There are also general precepts of refu ge that need to be maintained which can be 
understood by reading about this subject. If we go for refuge to the Three Jewels there will be 
many benefits, as was mentioned before. On the other hand, if we do not go for refuge, there 
will be only bad results.   If we create a non-virtuous action we must develop regret for it and make a determination to not commit it again. Then we should go for refuge to the Budd ha, Dharma, and Sangha, 
and generate the mind of enlightenment toward all sentient beings. Then we should apply an 
actual antidote by reciting mantras or sutras, doing prostrations, or doing some other type of 
virtuous action. For example, we could recite the hundred-syllable mantra of Vajrasattva or 
just his short mantra OM VAJRASATTVA AH or OM VAJRASATTVA HUM. We should 
try to do some purification every evening before going to bed. Thinking that we have purified 
all our negative actions, we sh ould feel happy and then lie down while trying to generate a 
good heart, the mind of enlightenment, compa ssion, or love, and then slowly fall asleep. In 
this way, while we sleep, we will continue to pr actice the Dharma. This is of great benefit, so 
we should try to do it.   If I have said anything useful, please try to put it  into practice. If not, just leave it aside! In 
either case, try to keep your mind relaxed and to not be agitated or worried. Instead, try to 
relax! Try to have less desire for material things  and try to be more cont ent and satisfied. This 
is one way to practice the Dharma in daily life.   Tuesday afternoon, 1 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 54-55) says:  
B How lesser beings attain high status  
{55} As for the undertaking to attain the former: everything that has been set forth 
is included in the actual cycle of teachi ngs for lesser beings or in the cycle of 
teachings common to lesser beings. Special lesser beings do not mostly act for the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
200 sake of this life, pursuing [instead] the excellence of high status in future lives and 
engaging in achieving the causes for that. In the Lamp on the Path104 it says: 
 
Whoever pursues by any means, 
The happiness of samsara only, Just for his own benefit, Should be known as the least of beings. 
 
“High status” ( mngon mtho ) refers to rebirth in a higher realm.  
 All the many different kinds of teaching s that were given by Buddha Shakyamuni are 
included in the actual cycle of teachings of le sser beings or in cycle of teachings that is 
common to lesser beings, that is, which is shared by middling and great beings.  
 Lesser or small beings are of two types: mere  small beings and special small beings. Mere 
small beings are those who work for the happiness of this life, whereas sp ecial small beings are 
those who do not take care of this life but inst ead emphasize the happiness of future lives, as 
they wish to improve in each successive life.   There is an event related to this that happe ned sometime in the past in Tibet. A man in 
the Tibetan government, who had not studied much  lam-rim but had heard just a little bit of 
teachings, claimed that great beings are poli ticians who belong to the government, that 
middling beings are the rich business men of Lhas a, and that small beings are the poor monks 
of Sera, Ganden, and Drepung Monasteries who had poor clothing, food, and so forth. The 
person to whom he said this knew the lam-rim well and only laughed at him, saying that it 
would follow that the debating partner (Tsensha b) of His Holiness the Dalai Lama was a small 
being! Another minister, who knew the Dharma we ll, said that politicians are not great beings 
but that monks are because they are rich inside . We cannot see others’ inner realizations. It 
can happen that externally someone has an attrac tive body, wears fine clothing, and so forth, 
but this does not mean that he or she is rich in inner qualities. In fact, a beggar living on the road might be richer in qualities, even though his clothing is poor and his body unattractive.  
 In short, mere small beings th ink only of the needs of this li fe, food, money, and so forth, 
whereas special small beings think to practice  Dharma by observing morality, practicing 
generosity, and making prayers in order to crea te the causes of obtaining a rebirth of high 
status, that is, a precious human rebirth possessi ng the eighteen qualities. Without creating 
the causes, it is not possible to obtain the result. 
 Atisha in his text Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment  said that someone who strives only to 
obtain the happiness of cyclic existence and to take  care of him or herself alone is the least of 
beings. Such people only think of themselves and the happiness of this  life. However, even 
thinking of future lives and practicing in order to create the causes for happiness in the future 
is not of great benefit as it is still only a rebirt h in cyclic existence. As long as we are born in 
cyclic existence, we will have to experience  the different types of sufferings. Therefore, 
t h i n k i n g  i n  t h i s  w a y ,  w e  n e e d  t o  g e n e r a t e  a  s t r o n g  i n t e r e s t  i n  a n d  a s p i r a t i o n  t o  a c h i e v e  
nirvana, the state passed beyond cyclic existence,  and for this reason to engage in practicing 
the Dharma. This motivation is better, therefore we need to generate it.

FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
201 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How intermediate beings achieve the certain goodness of liberation 
There are two kinds of certain goodness: (1 ) the liberation that is merely release 
from samsara and (2) omniscience. In this regard everything that has been set forth 
beginning from the vehicles of hearers and solitary realizers is included in the 
actual cycle of teachings for intermediate  beings or in the cycle of teachings 
common to intermediate beings. Intermed iate beings develop disenchantment 
with existence as a whole and, having ma de the liberation that is release from 
existence for their own welfare their object  of attainment, they engage in method 
for that, the three trainings. In the Lamp on the Path  it says: 
 
Indifferent105 to the pleasures of existence, 
One desists from evil deeds –  
Whoever pursues only personal peace 
Is called “an intermediate being.” 
 
Middling or intermediate beings strive to attain definite or certain goodness, which is of two types. The first type of definite goodness is libe ration from cyclic existence or the achievement 
o f  n i r v a n a .  T h e  s e c o n d  t y p e  o f  d e f i n i t e  g o odness is the omniscient state of buddhahood. 
Those who abandon cyclic existence and achieve ni rvana are the hearers and solitary realizers. 
All of the Buddha’s teachings were given in order to take care of the different types of beings, 
such as the Hinayana hearers and solitary realizer s. For example, he gave the first teaching on 
the four noble truths in order to take care of  Hinayana disciples. Some of these teachings are 
the actual path of middling beings and some are common to the path of small beings and 
some to the path of great beings.  
 How can we achieve the path of middling beings ? To do so, we need to generate a wish to 
abandon the entirety of cyclic ex istence. “Cyclic existence” refe rs to the constant joining by 
the force of afflictions and actions. By joining again and again [with aggregates ], we circle, like 
a prayer wheel, in cyclic existence. In other wo rds, we are born again and again in the six 
realms: the three bad migrations  and the three happy migrations. The three bad migrations 
are the hell realm, hungry ghost realm, and an imal realm, whereas the three happy migrations 
are the human realm, anti-god realm, and god realm. We circle in these realms, going up and 
down, but not yet getting out of them.   When the Buddha taught  the four noble truths, he first said that it is necessary to 
recognize sufferings, that it is then necessary to  abandon the causes of suffering, that they are 
to be abandoned by meditating the path, and that  we thereby actualize cessation. In short, we 
need to recognize suffering as suffering, to ab andon the causes, to actualize cessations, and to 
meditate paths. Where do true sufferings come from? They come from true origins. We need 
to recognize that these origins, the source of true sufferings, are contaminated actions and the 
mental afflictions. Due to the afflictions, we enga ge in negative actions that bring the result of 
suffering. We need to abandon th e causes of suffering – actions and afflictions. If we abandon 
them, what will we achieve? We will achieve tr ue cessations. How we do this? We do so by 
                                             
 
105 srid pa’i bde la rgyab phyogs shing  – lit. : “having turned one’s back on  the happiness/pleasures of existence” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
202 meditating true paths. What ar e the true paths to be meditated? Mainly we meditate on the 
selflessness of persons and the se lflessness of phenomena. In th e context of the Hinayana, the 
selflessness of persons refers to the lack of a self-supporting su bstantially existent self, this 
being the object of negation. The realization of the selflessness of the person or the being acts 
as the antidote to the conception of a self (self- grasping), that is, to the conception of a self-
supporting substantially existent self. This real ization has the opposite mode of apprehension 
to the conception of a self, therefore it can aban don it. This conception of a self is the first 
link, ignorance, among the twel ve links. When this is aban doned, we abandon creating 
karma, the second link of karmic formations. When we no longer create karmic formations, 
we will not deposit imprints on the consciousness,  the third link. In this  way, we will be able 
to cease the suffering results: consciousness, na me and form, the six sources, contact, feeling, 
birth, and aging-death. Thus, we need to elimin ate the causes. The first link is ignorance, the 
second is karmic formations, the third is cons ciousness, the fourth is name and form, the 
fifth is the six sources, the sixth is contact (t he coming together of a consciousness, a sense 
power, and an object), and the seventh is feelin g (which is produced by contact – for example, 
contact with something soft produces happy f eeling, whereas contact with a thorn produces 
suffering and contact with something between these two produces neutral feeling). This 
produces the eighth link, craving. When this in creases there is the ninth link, grasping. This 
produces the tenth link, becoming or existenc e (the increase in strength of the karmic 
formations deposited due to ignorance on the co nsciousness). This produces birth (birth in 
this context refers to conception in the womb as a human being). This then gives rise to the 
twelfth link, aging-death. “Aging” includes the fa ct that sometimes, having been conceived, we 
die without ever being born, and that sometime s a grandfather or grandmother remain alive 
while their grandchild dies, there being no fixe d order with respect to death, that first old 
people die and then younger people. “Death” means that having taken this body, the life-
faculty or life-power, which keeps the connec tion between the body and mind, ceases to 
function whereby the mind leaves the body and it is no longer conjoined with feeling. Even if 
we wish to live for 80 years, 90 years, or 100 years, or even for 110 or 115 years, it is unlikely 
that this will actually happen.  On the other hand, some people do not want to grow old 
because they recognize the problems of old age (for example, that some old people become 
like small children) and do not wish to live longer than 70 or 75 years. Someone once asked me to pray that he die before he reached the ag e of 75 because at this age most people cannot 
go to the toilet alone, wash th emselves, get out of bed, and so forth. I asked him whether he 
was sure that having died he would be reborn as a human being, but he was not worried about this!   In short, we should try to improve. We should  practice the path of middling beings as in 
this way we will achieve liberation from cyclic existence, that is, nirvana. What is the method 
or cause for achieving this? It is to practice th e three higher trainings: the higher training in 
morality, the higher training in concentration,  and the higher training in wisdom. These are 
the main objects to be practiced. Atisha’s Lamp on the Path of Enlightenment  says that we need 
to give up attachment to or turn away from the happiness of cyclic existe nce. We also need to 
avoid non-virtuous actions and strive to achieve personal peace, that is, the cessation of all 
sufferings. Those who are interested in achieving their own personal peace or nirvana are called “middling beings,” since they are neither small nor great beings.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
203  We, on the other hand, are attached to the pl easures of cyclic existence. For example, we 
are attached to the five desirable objects. We  are attached to attractive forms (shapes and 
colors) which are objects of the eye consciousne ss. When we see such forms, we feel happy. 
On the other hand, when we see unattractive forms, which are di fficult to bear, we experience 
unhappiness and become angry. Because of atta chment and anger we engage in actions that 
bring suffering. We are also attached to pleasa nt sounds, for example, singing or chanting, 
which are objects of the ear consciousness. We  are attached to pleasant odors, such as 
perfumes, which are objects of the nose consci ousness. We are attached to good tastes, 
delicious foods, which are objects of the tongue  consciousness. We are attached to pleasant 
tangible objects, which are objects of the body  consciousness. Due to these attachments, we 
engage in negative actions that bring the result of suffering. For this reason, we need to give 
up attachment to attractive forms, pleasant sounds, fragrant odors, delicious tastes, and 
pleasant tangible objects. We need to turn away from them. In our everyday life, the sense 
power first engages an object, then this produc es consciousness, due to which different ideas 
arise. We need to give up all such attachment to the pleasures of cyclic existence, whereby we 
will be able to achieve nirvana or  personal or individual peace.  
 To do this, we need to practice morality as a basis. For example, we  can take the five lay 
precepts, or even just one, two, three, or fo ur of them. Having taken these vows, we should 
then safeguard them. In this way, we practice th e base of all that is good – morality. If the 
base is good, something good will rise from it. For example, if we build a house on a stable base or foundation, the house itself will be stable , whereas if the base is not strong and stable 
whatever is built upon it will collapse. Therefor e, we need to practice morality. In addition, 
we also need to practice concentration, for ex ample, calm abiding. We can take any object 
that we like as an object. Some people even ta ke the image of the flame of a candle and try to 
focus on it. However, the best object is a holy image, thus we should visualize such an image 
and then try to focus our mind on it. We ca n also meditate and develop concentration on 
emptiness or selflessness, however this is not very easy. In short, first we should develop 
concentration so as to achieve calm abiding and then on this basis achieve special insight. 
This is discussed in detail in the context of the path of great beings. 
 When attachment arises it is because we see an  object as attractive. To counteract this we 
should think that it is very ugly. By meditating in this way, our mental image of the object will 
change and our attachment will diminish. If we have anger, the mental  attitude wishing to 
harm ourselves or others, we should apply the antidote of love, the mental attitude wishing 
ourselves and others happiness and the causes of happiness. Love is the opposite of anger, 
therefore by trying to develop love for ours elves and other sentient beings our anger will 
diminish.   When strong pride arises, we should exam ine where the I is. We can ask ourselves, 
“Among the five aggregates which one is the I?  Is the form aggregate the I? Is the feeling 
aggregate the I? Is the discrimination aggregat e the I? Is the compositional factors aggregate 
the I? Is the consciousness aggregate the I?” Then , among the form aggregate, “Is the eye the I? 
Is the ear the I? Is the nose the I? Is the to ngue the I? Is the body the I?” We should do 
analytical meditation in this way, trying to find  the I. However, we will not be able to find it. 
Our body is composed of the four elements, ther efore we can also ask ourselves: “Is the earth 
element, the solidity of our body, the I? Is the water element, the liquids in our body, the I? Is 
the fire element, the heat in our body, the I? Is the wind element, the movement in our body, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
204 the I?” If we check in this way, we will not be able to find the I, whereby the pride thinking 
“I” will diminish.  
 In addition, ignorance arises in our mind, for example, the ignorance conceiving a self-
supporting substantially existent I. When this  happens, we should meditate on dependent-
relation. The I or self or person or being is im puted on the basis of any of the five aggregates. 
If we examine this, we will find that the I depe nds on the five aggregates, that the person is 
imputed by thought on the basis of any of the five aggregates. Therefore, the person depends 
on the five aggregates. The person also depend s on causes and conditions, for example, on 
karma. The person also depends on the fath er and mother as our body comes from the 
combination of the semen and blood of our pa rents into which our mind entered and then 
the embryo developed through various stages. Th e person is also dependent on parts. Thus, 
the person is only an imputati on by thought on the basis of any of the five aggregates. We 
should meditate on dependent-relation in this way as doing so will el iminate ignorance or the
