conception of a self or the view of the transitory collection.  
 At times the doubt that thinks “Maybe this, maybe that, maybe yes, maybe no” arises. In 
order to counteract it we need to unde rstand the nature of all phenomena.  
 In short, we need to meditate on ugliness as  the antidote to attachment, on love as the 
antidote to anger, on dependent-relation as the antidote to ignora nce, on which of the 
elements, sources, and so forth is the I as th e antidote to pride, and so forth. Tantric 
practitioners can also imagine that all phenom ena including their own body become empty, 
that from the empty state arises  a seed-syllable which is the nature of mind, and that this 
transforms into oneself as a deity such as  Tara, Yamantaka, Chakrasamvara, Chenrezig 
Gyalwa Gyatso, Avalokiteshvara,  and so forth. Having receiv ed an empowerment, we can 
visualize our body as a deity and so forth. Ho wever, even without an empowerment, we can 
meditate on emptiness, love, compassion, and impermanence. For exampl e, we can think “All 
compounded phenomena are impermanent becaus e they change momentarily.” They change 
even in the instant of a fingersnap. A fingersnap can be further divided into sixty-five parts 
and things change even in one of these parts, th ey do not abide for even this length of time 
but continually mutate. For the reason that they change momentarily, all compounded 
phenomena are impermanent.  We can also meditate on the uncertainty of cyclic existence. Sometimes we are in high 
positions, sometimes we are even in prison. So metimes we are rich, then later on we lose 
everything and become poor. Similarly, the poor sometimes become rich. In this way, there is 
no certainty. Things are constantly changing . Today someone is our good friend, tomorrow 
something may happen and he or she becomes ou r worst enemy. Things are always changing, 
thus they are impermanent. There is no certaint y at all in cyclic existence. Even our friends 
are uncertain, for a while they are our good fr iends and then they change. We often see in 
our world that, having happily given birth to a chil d, a couple later begins to argue, then they 
separate, and then they no longer even want to  see each other. This happens more and more 
often. Why? This shows that the nature of cyclic existence is un certainty, nothing is fixed or 
certain. We should meditate th is way on true sufferings.  
 A l s o  i n  r e l a t i o n  t o  t r u e  s u f f e r i n g s ,  w e  s h o u l d  e x a m i n e  t h e  f a c t  t h a t  t h i s  b o d y  i s  a  contaminated, appropriated aggregate. It is an  illustration of a true suffering. We should 
check in what way it is a true suffering. This body is the nature of suffering. Due to it, we 
experience the suffering of suffering, the suff ering of change, and pervasive suffering. The 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
205 suffering of suffering is the experience of actu al pain, for example, kidney pain, pain in the 
liver, pain in the lungs, and so forth. Thus, to that which is already the nature of suffering is 
added pain. The suffering of change refers to th at which is for a time pleasure. For example, 
when we lie in the sun on the beach, initially we  experience pleasure but the longer we stay in 
the sun the more we begin to burn and to experi ence suffering. Therefore, “change” refers to 
the fact that something is initially happiness and then transforms into suffering. Another 
example of this suffering is if we eat delicious f ood and keep on eating it, because it is gives us 
pleasure, eventually we will come to have a stomach ache and experience pain. Pervasive 
suffering is the fact that our body is pervaded by suffering in that from the crown of our head 
to the soles of our feet we experience pain, for example, when pricked with a needle. This 
experience shows that our entire body is perv aded by suffering. This is the explanation of 
pervasive suffering in relation to our body, howe ver it can also be understand in terms of all 
cyclic existence in that suffering pervades the lo west of hot hells up to the Peak of Existence. 
All beings experience suffering. For example,  human beings, whether those in the highest 
positions, those in middling positions, or those in  the lowest positions, all of them experience 
physical and mental suffering. The worst is ment al suffering, for example, worry about losing 
one’s possessions. This leads us to thinking ho w we can cheat others while at the same time 
maintaining our reputation of being honest an d so forth. All of these sufferings are true 
sufferings.   The causes of true sufferings are actions and afflictions. “Actions” refers to all types of 
actions. Bad actions bring problems.   In brief, because the nature of cyclic existenc e is suffering we should wish to give it up. 
Even the things that appear to bring happine ss in the end only bring suffering. They too are 
the nature of suffering. In fact, all the plea sures and happiness of cyclic existence are the 
nature of suffering and bring suffering. By thin king about this, we will generate the wish to 
get out of cyclic existence and to achieve libera tion or nirvana. Hinayan a hearers and solitary 
realizers take the mental afflictions as their objects of abandonment and thereby also abandon 
contaminated actions. In this way, they achiev e their own happiness or peace. This is the aim 
of middling beings.   True origins are actions and afflictions. How many actions and afflictions are there? There 
are many. The root afflictions are attachment, an ger, ignorance, pride, doubt, and afflicted 
views. The latter are also further divided into th e five: the view of the transitory collection, 
extreme view, the view holding a bad view as a supreme view, the view holding bad conduct as 
good conduct, and the wrong view that the law of  actions and results, past and future lives, 
and the Three Jewels do not exist.  In this way there are ten afflic tions or ten subtle-increasers. 
Then, from the point of view of the Madh yamaka School there are twenty secondary 
afflictions. They have different  natures and functions, but they  always bring a bad result. We 
should try to learn about all of them.  Then, in terms of actions we often talk about “karma.” There is karma that is both created 
and accumulated, karma that is accumulated bu t not created, karma that is created but not 
accumulated, and karma that is neither create d nor accumulated. An action that is both 
created and accumulated is one for which we en gage in the preparation. For example, going 
to war wishing to kill our enemies (the preparat ion) and then killing someone and rejoicing in 
it. An example of an action that is accumulate d but not created is not actually killing other 
people but sending others to kill, this being the type of action that Geor ge W. Bush creates by 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
206 sending soldiers to Iraq. Although he does not ac tually do the action of killing with his body, 
mentally he accumulates the action of killing . On the other hand, a soldier who does not 
want to kill but is ordered to do so and then kills someone even though he does not want to 
kill commits an action that is created but not accumulated. An action that is neither 
accumulated nor created is, for example, to acci dentally step on and kill an insect. In this 
case, we did not have the motivation wishing to kill and therefore lack the preparation. There 
is an action, a karma, but it is neither accumulated nor created.  
 Then there are black actions,  white actions, and mixed black and white actions. There are 
actions of body, speech, and mind. Then from th e point of view of cyclic existence there are 
projecting actions and actualizing actions. For example, we take rebirth due to a projecting 
action, this being an action that projects a result. There are many, many different types of 
actions. Karma is thus very deep and not easy to understand.  
 In short, everyday we should try to collect virtuous actions and avoid committing non-
virtuous actions. This is our job in everyday life. It will bring the result of a higher rebirth in 
the future, one in which we will experience en joyment and be able to practice Dharma, and 
perhaps even attain enlightenment or buddhahood  and then manifest in the many different 
world systems in order to teach and benefit sent ient beings in all these places. We need to 
achieve the state of happiness.  
 Wednesday morning, 2 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D How great beings attain omniscience 
There are two means of attaining omniscienc e: the great vehicle of secret mantra 
and of the perfections. These two are cont ained in the cycle of teachings of great 
beings. Since great beings are under the power of great compassion, having made 
enlightenment their object of attainment in order to bring all the sufferings of 
sentient beings to an end, they train in the six perfections, the two stages, etc. In 
the Lamp on the Path  it says: 
 
He whose own mind is affected by suffering, 
Who thus yearns to bring to an end All the suffering of others completely, {56} Such a one is a superior being. 
 As for the means by which th ese beings attain enlightenment, they are set forth 
under two [headings]: the perfections and mantra. 
 The buddhas are omniscient. There are two ways of attaining omniscience: (1) by means of 
the secret mantra vehicle and (2) by means of the perfection vehicle. The word “mantra” is 
composed of the syllable man, meaning mind, and tra, meaning protection. Therefore, mantra 
is “that which protects the mind .” The word “tantra” means continuum; it includes the tantra 
of base, tantra of path, and tantra of result.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
207   Both secret mantra and the perfection vehi cle are included in the cycle of teachings of 
great beings. A great being is one whose mind is  controlled by compassion. Wishing to free all 
sentient beings from suffering, they take enlightenment or buddhahood as their object of 
attainment. Then, in order to do this state, they  engage in the practice of the six perfections 
and the two stages of learners. The six perfecti ons are the perfections of generosity, morality, 
patience, joyous effort, concentration, and wi sdom. The two stages are the generation stage 
and completion stage. In order to attain enlighte nment, we need to practice all of these. Great 
beings understand that all sentient beings, like themselves, are suffering, and therefore, 
wishing to put an end to all their suffering, de termine to attain enlightenment. Such beings 
do not think of themselves alone.  
 The word “being,” kye bu (skys bu ) in Tibetan and parusha  in Sanskrit, means “one who is 
able,” that is, one who is able to attain enligh tenment. To attain this state, beings practice 
sutra or the mahayana perfection vehicle, and then the tantra or mantra vehicle. It is said that 
it is possible to reach up to the tenth grou nd by means of the sutra path, but that a 
bodhisattva on the tenth ground must necessaril y enter the tantra path and achieve the actual 
clear light, whereby he attain s buddhahood, the union of the actual clear light and pure 
illusory body, or the state of Vajradhara. We n eed to understand this and to try to practice 
accordingly.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
E Elucidating the sources of the term “three beings” 
The term “three beings” is mentioned in many texts such as the Compendium of 
Ascertainments  and the Commentary on the Treasury .106 
 
“The three beings” are mentioned in the Compendium of Ascertainments  by Asanga, which sets 
out many different types of beings and the diffe rent names for them. They are also mentioned 
in the commentary on the Treasury of Manifest Dharma . Since we are beings, parusha , we are 
capable. Capable of what? We are capable of learning and studying and of attaining inner 
realizations and qualities such as  calm abiding and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
F Clearing up doubt 
Although, with regard to small beings, th ere are both those wh o devote themselves 
to this life and those who devo te themselves to future lives, this is about the latter. 
One should consider them to be engaging in the unmistaken means to high status. 
 
Small beings or beings of small capacity are of two types. One type thinks of and puts effort 
only into this life, whereas the other type puts  effort into achieving the happiness of future 
lives. Among these two, in this context we are discussing the latter, i. e., those who think of 
and emphasize the next life. In order to attain a higher rebirth in the next life it is necessary 
to practice an unmistaken or perfect method, th is being meditation on relying on a guru, the 
precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen  qualities, its entity and qualities, its 
                                              
106 Vasubandhu’s Auto- Commentary on the Treasury of Manifest Knowledge  cf. Note 18 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
208 meaningfulness, the difficulty of finding it from  the point of view of cause, examples, and 
number, the fact that having been found it do es not last long due to death-impermanence, 
and the need to abandon non-virtuous actions an d create virtuous actions, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim says: 
2B4B-2A2 Giving reasons for gradual guidance from the point of view of the three [types of] 
beings 
A The purpose of guiding someone by way of the paths of the three beings B Giving reasons for such gradual guidance 
The second one has two points: what is th e purpose of guiding someone by way of 
the paths of the three beings and givi ng reasons for such gradual guidance. 
2B4B-2A2A The purpose of guiding someone by means of the paths of the three beings 
First: Thus, although it is explained in te rms of the three beings, the path of great 
beings contains within it the paths of the two other [beings], which is why Master 
Ashvagosha says that these two are bran ches of the Mahayana path. Consequently, 
here, they are not guidance  o n  t h e  p a t h  o f  s m a l l  b e ings who make only the 
happiness of existence their object of atta inment, nor on the path of intermediate 
beings who make only liberation from sams ara for their own benefit their object of 
attainment. Rather, through considering some paths common to those two to be 
preparatory practices leading to the path of great beings, they are taken to be 
branches for training in the path of great beings.  
 
Why is it necessary to lead someone in the path s of the three beings, the small, middling, and 
great? Although there are many teachings on th e objects of practice of the three beings, the 
path of small beings and the path  of middling beings are included  in the path of great beings. 
In fact Ashvagosha said that these two paths are branches of the path of the great vehicle, that 
is, they are causes of it. Due to this, prior to practicing the Mahayana path it is necessary to 
practice the paths of the small and middling bein gs as a cause for the Mahayana path. This is 
because in some contexts “branch” can mean “cau se.” Thus, we need to practice the path of 
small beings followed by the path of middling be ings and only then should we gradually enter 
into the path of great beings. Fo r example, in the case of getting  an education, we must first 
go to elementary school and then to middle or  high school, and only having finished these 
two schools can we go to university. Just as  without completing th e first two schools we 
cannot go to university, similarly we must firs t complete the paths of small and middle beings 
before entering the Mahayana path.   The beings who wish to obtain the happiness of cyclic existence, that is, who take this as 
their object of attainment, are small beings or  beings of small capaci ty. Those who wish to 
mainly achieve freedom from cyclic existence are middling beings or beings of middling capacity. They strive to achieve nirvana for th emselves, without thinking of others. Thinking 
only of their own purpose, they wish to achieve liberation from cyclic existence. To do this 
they must engage in the path of middling beings.   Thus, the path of small beings and the path of middling beings can be considered to be 
like preliminaries to the path of great beings . Thus, we first go for refuge to the Buddha, 
Dharma, and Sangha, and try to safeguard the pr ecepts of refuge. Then we guard the law of 
cause and result and act accordingly. Then we try to understand the four noble truths and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
209 train in the three higher trainings of morality , concentration, and wisdom. All this must be 
done prior to engaging in th e Mahayana path. It is similar to the need to do the six 
preparatory practices prior to engagi ng in meditation on the lam-rim. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B Giving reasons for such gradual guidance 
1 The actual reasons 
2 The purpose 
The second one has two points: the actual reasons and the purpose. 
2B4B-2A2B-1 The actual reasons 
A The entry gate to the Mahayana 
B The need to consider its benefits and allow one’s delight to increase before generating the mind [of 
enlightenment ] along with the [relevant] sources 
C The benefits of striving for this mind 
D How the lesser and intermediary [paths] are necessary for laying the basis of those benefits E How you need the lesser and intermediate when training in the actual mind 
F How the lesser and intermediate are necessary for the ritual[s of] accumulation and purification of this 
mental attitude G Advice to cherish the way that the teaching cycles of lesser and intermediate beings become 
branches of that of great beings 
H [The order on the path] I Summary of all paths in the three trainings 
J For the sake of skilful classification: summary of all paths in the two collections 
K The need to enter the Mantrayana 
L How to pursue the perfection only path 
M The stages of engagement in mantra 
N Explanation that the intention of Lamp on the Path  etc. is the engagement in both sutra and mantra 
[vehicles] 
2B4B-2A2B-1A The entry gate to the Mahayana 
First: The entry gate for entering the Mahayana consists in forming the mind 
toward supreme enlightenment. Once it has arisen in the mind, according to 
Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior : 
 
When bodhichitta has arisen, at once, 
The wretched bound in the prison of cyclic existence 
Are to be called “children of those gone to bliss.” 
 
{57} For in accordance with this statement, [an individual ] who has obtained the 
name of “bodhisattva” is admitted among Mahayana beings. If it107 deteriorates, he 
will leave the midst of Mahayana beings.  
 
The entry gate or door to the Mahayana is th e mind of enlightenment or bodhichitta. When 
we generate this in our mind, we become a bo dhisattva and a Mahayana being or Mahayanist. 
If we lack the mind of enlightenment, the aspi ration to attain highes t enlightenment, we are 
not Mahayanists. There is a different between  Mahayanists and Mahayana practitioners. 
Mahayana practitioners are those who have not yet generated the mind of enlightenment but 
are practicing cherishing other sentient beings, are developing great love and compassion, and 
have a contrived or artificial mind of enligh tenment, and thus are tr ying to practice the 
Mahayana path. However, they are not Mahayana beings or Mahayanists.  
                                              
107 i.e. “bodhichitta” or “the mind toward  supreme enlightenment” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
210  Shantideva says that in the very instant that we generate the mind of enlightenment, we 
become an object of veneration of human beings and gods and come to be called “a child of 
t h o s e  g o n e  t o  b l i s s ”  o r  “ a  c h i l d  o f  t h e  c o n q uerors.” Such a person who has generated the 
mind of enlightenment is a bodhisattva. Although our body remains a miserable, 
contaminated phenomenon that is included in cy clic existence, having generated the mind of 
enlightenment we become an object of venera tion. There are also those who generate the 
mind of enlightenment on the basis of a body of  the hell realm or the body of an animal, that 
is, the body of a naga. It is said that due to receiving teachings from Buddha Shakyamuni, 
some nagas generated the mind of enlightenmen t while their body remained that of a naga. 
Such a being is called a “bodhi sattva” and a “Mahayana being.” 
 If at first we generate bodhichitta or the mind of enlightenment, but then later on it 
degenerates, we will be expelled from the ra nk of Mahayana beings. There are people who 
initially generate bodhichitta but later on, wh en engaging in the bodhisattva activities, 
become discouraged and generate the thought th at it would be better to attain their own 
liberation from cyclic existence, due to wh ich they enter the Hinayana path and leave the 
Mahayana path. This can happen to bodhisattvas  on the small path of accumulation. Having 
degenerated their mind of enlightenment, th ey enter the Hinayana path of hearers.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1B The need to consider its benefits and allow one’s delight to increase before generating the 
mind [of enlightenment ] along with the [relevant] sources 
This being the case, those who wish to enter the Mahayana need to exert 
themselves in many ways to arouse that mind. However, before arousing it, they 
need to familiarize with the benefits of ar ousing that mind and then increase their 
delight with regard to those benefits an d do the seven-limb practice and go for 
refuge, which has been set out in the Compendium of Trainings  and Engaging in 
Bodhisattva Behavior . 
 
If we wish to generate the mind of enlightenm ent, we must first contemplate its benefits or 
advantages. In this way, our mind will become  very happy. Then we  can enter the Mahayana 
path.  
 There are many different method s for generating the mind of enlightenment, thus we need 
to create its causes. To do this, we need to contemplate or familiarize with the benefits of 
mind of enlightenment, whereby we will genera te strong interest in it. We should do 
analytical meditation, contemplating the ma ny reasons for generating the mind of 
enlightenment and entering the Mahayana path. Af ter that, what do we need to do? We need 
to do the seven-limb prayer and go for refuge as is taught in the Compendium of Trainings  and 
Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior . The first chapter of the latter text sets out the benefits of 
generating the mind of enlightenment and in co njunction with this there is the practice of 
the seven-limbs. The first of the seven limbs is  prostration. We can prostrate with all three 
doors – our body, speech, and mind. However, prostration mainly refers to extending the 
body on the ground, that is, touching one’s he ad, hands, and knees to the ground. However, 
if this is difficult due to physical problems, one can also just fold one’s hands together.  
 We imagine the merit field with Buddha Shak yamuni in front of us. To his right is the 
lineage of the vast, to his left is the lineage of  the profound, and behind him is the lineage of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
211 blessing. He is surrounded by the gurus and the meditational deities of action, performance, 
yoga, and highest yoga tantra. In addition, th ere are all the buddhas, the eight bodhisattvas, 
the twelve solitary realizers, and the sixteen el ders or foe-destroyers. There are the dakas and 
dakinis and Dharma protectors. Externally there are the four Great Kings in the four 
directions. Having visualized them, we should prostrate and make offerings to them. We can 
offer them actual substances, such as water, ligh t, flowers, and so forth, as well as offering 
them all the things that do not belong to us, su ch as snow mountains, gardens of flowers, and 
so forth, by mentally taking them to mind.  
 Then we should confess all our non-virtuo u s  a c t i o n s  b y  d e v e l o p i n g  r e g r e t  f o r  t h e m ,  
determining not to do them again, going for refuge to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha and 
generating the mind of enlightenment, and appl ying an actual antidote. Then, we rejoice in 
the good or virtuous actions done by oursel ves and others, without being jealous of them. 
Then we request the teachers to turn the wheel  of Dharma and then supplicate them to not 
pass way until all sentient beings are enlightened, that is, until cy clic existence is empty. Then 
we dedicate all the virtue that was created by the previous six limbs for the sake of 
enlightenment. We also dedicate all the virtue that we have created in  the three times – that 
created in the past, that which we are creating  now, and that which we will create in the 
future – that all sentient beings may achieve en lightenment. This is a very short explanation 
of the seven limbs.  Then the text says that we should go for refuge . We can do so by reciting the verse “I go for 
refuge to the Buddha. I go for refuge to the Dh arma. I go for refuge to the Sangha.” In the 
context of tantra we also go for refuge to  the guru. However, th e Buddha, Dharma, and 
Sangha include all final objects of refuge. In te rms of temporal refuges even this house is a 
refuge as it protects us from heat, cold, rain, snow, and so forth. If someone is trying to kill us and we hide in a forest, the forest also become s a temporal object of refuge. There are even 
small guesthouses high up in the mountains that  are called “refuges” because they protect us 
from suffering, relieve our hunger, and so forth. However, the final refuges are only three: the 
Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.  
  We can go for refuge with the short verse: 
 
I go for refuge until I am enlightened To the Buddha, Dharma, and Supreme Assembly. By the merit from practicing generosity and the other perfections,  May I attain buddhahood in order to  benefit all migrating beings.  
 Or:   
NAMO BUDDHAYA NAMO DHARMAYA NAMO SANGHAYA  
 Or: 
BUDDHAM SARANAM GACCHAMI  DHARMAM SARANAM GACCHAMI SANGHAM SARANAM GACCHAMI 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
212  
We should recite these words while generating  faith in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.  
 The Compendium  refers to the Compendium of Trainings  by Shantideva. It is also mentioned 
in his Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds . We should try to study these two texts.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1C The benefits of striving for this mind  
If we summarise the benefits set forth in this way, there are two: temporary and 
ultimate benefits. The former, again, are twofold: not falling into the lower realms and being born in the happy realms. Once  this mind has arisen, the causes of 
miserable existences that were accumu lated previously are purified and the 
continuity of those accumulated later on is interrupted. The causes of happy 
realms that were accumulated previously, due to being conjoined with it, increase 
extensively, and those that are newly create d, due to being motivated by it, become 
endless. When it comes to attaining the ultimate objectives of liberation and 
omniscience with ease through relying on this mind: if an uncontrived wish to 
achieve the temporary or ultimate benefits is not present, one may say “Since those 
benefits arise from generating that mind, I shall endeavor to generate that mind,” but it will only be words. This is very clear if we examine our own mind.  
 W e  n e e d  t o  d e v e l o p  i n t e r e s t  i n  g e n e r a t i n g  t h e mind of enlightenment. If we generate the 
mind of enlightenment there will be two types of benefits: temporary benefits and ultimate or final benefits. The temporary benefits of genera ting the mind of enlightenment are the two: 
we will no longer fall into the lower realms and we will be reborn in the happy migrations. In terms of rebirth, there are only two possibilit ies, either rebirth in the bad migrations or 
rebirth in the happy migrations. However, if we generate the mind of enlightenment that 
wishes to achieve enlightenment for the benefit of  all sentient beings, we will no longer take 
rebirth in the lower realms and will only take re birth in the higher realms. This is because by 
generating the mind of enlighte nment, the causes of rebirth in  the lower realm – non-virtuous 
actions – will cease or be purified. In addition, we will no longer collect new causes for such 
r e b i r t h ,  m o r e  n o n - v i r t u o u s  a c t i o n s .  T h e y  w i l l  b e  s t o p p e d ,  t h a t  i s ,  w e  w i l l  n o t  c r e a t e  t h e m  anymore. Also the collection of virtue in ou r continuum for taking a high rebirth, due to 
being conjoined with or held by the mind of enli ghtenment, will increase greatly, that is, it 
will become stronger. In addition, our newly cr eated virtuous actions, due to being motivated 
by the mind of enlightenment, will become endl ess. Thus, our virtues will only increase and 
will never be exhausted. These are the temp orary benefits of generating the mind of 
enlightenment.   The ultimate or final benefit of generating the mind of enlightenment is that we will 
achieve omniscience. Thus, those who wish to attain liberation and those who wish to attain 
omniscience will be able to do so. Omniscience is “the great liberation” – liberation from both cyclic existence and solitary  peace. Because a buddha thinks only to take care of others 
and never thinks of himself, he is free from both extremes, the extreme of existence and the 
extreme of peace. He has completely abandoned them, that is, he is free from them. Because 
of this, he attains the enlightenment of buddhahood or great liberation.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
213  By generating the mind of enlightenmen t, we can quickly achieve enlightenment. 
Therefore, we need to understand both the temporary and final benefits of the mind of 
enlightenment. Due to it we can achieve our temporary and ultimate aims or the temporary 
and ultimate results. Because all these benefi ts only arise from generating the mind of 
enlightenment, we need to put effort into ge nerating it in our continuum. However, just 
reciting mere words is not enough. We need to  examine our continuum as to whether or not 
we have generated the mind of enlightenment. If we find that we have generated the mind 
wishing to attain liberation or the state of a bu ddha, we should maintain and increase it. If we 
have not yet generated it, we should put effort into generating the contrived or artificial mind 
of enlightenment. To generate the mind of en lightenment we need many causes, however the 
main one is great compassion. This is th e main cause for generating the mind of 
enlightenment because seeing the suffering of ot her sentient beings, and recognizing that they 
are being tormented by the three, six, and eigh t types of suffering, we generate the wish to 
liberate them from suffering and the causes of suffering. Then, with this attitude, we generate 
the extraordinary thought to do this ourselves. Then realizing that we cannot yet do this, we 
sees that only a buddha can do this, and, wish ing to take care of all sentient beings, we 
generate the wish to attain enlightenment for th eir benefit. In this way, we need to always 
generate the mind of love and compassion and the mind of enlightenment toward all sentient 
beings.   Wednesday afternoon, 2 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 57) says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1D How the lesser and intermediate [branches of the path] are necessary for laying the basis of 
those benefits 
In order to develop the wish to attain th ose two benefits, high status and certain 
goodness, it is necessary to develo p the thoughts common to lesser and 
intermediate beings. {58} 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1E How the lesser and intermediate [branches of the path] are necessary when training in the 
actual mind 
Having thus developed the wish to achiev e the two benefits, when you cultivate the 
beneficial mind it is necessary to de velop the root of that mind, love and 
c o m p a s s i o n .  I n  t h i s  r e g a r d ,  i f  y o u r  b o d y  hair does not shiver and quiver as you 
think about the way you yourself wander in  cyclic existence deprived of happiness 
and oppressed by suffering, there is no way the inability to bear how other sentient 
beings are deprived of happiness and oppr essed by suffering can come about. In 
Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior  it says: 
 
In beings to whom previously, 
Such a thought, even for their own sake, Did not even appear in their dreams, How could it arise for the sake of others? 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
214 For that reason, having thought about how,  in the context of lesser beings, the 
harm of the sufferings of bad migrations fa lls upon you and how, in the context of 
intermediate beings, even in [rebirths of ] high status there is suffering and no 
peace or happiness, meditating – in acco rd with your own experience – on the 
s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  w h o  h a v e  b e c o m e  d e a r  t o  y o u ,  b e c o m e s  t h e  c a u s e s  f o r  l o v e  a n d  
compassion to arise. As the mind of enli ghtenment is born from them, training in 
the thoughts shared with lesser and in termediate [beings] is the means of 
generating an uncontrived mind of enlightenment. 
 If we wish to attain high status and certain or  definite goodness, we must first generate the 
thoughts that are common to small and middling beings. In order to generate the mind of 
enlightenment, we first need to think about its benefits – that we ourselves can attain 
enlightenment and that we can then lead others  to high status and liberation. But prior to 
generating the mind of enlightenment, we n eed to develop love and compassion. And prior 
to generating love and compassion, we need to train in the path of small beings and the path 
of middling beings.   In short, in order to develop the mind of  enlightenment, we first need to generate 
affectionate love and compassion. Such love sees  all sentient beings in an attractive aspect, 
and not as repulsive and somethin g to be avoided. Love is the wish that all sentient beings 
have happiness and the causes of happiness. Just as we wish to have happiness and the causes 
of happiness, so too do all sentient beings want the same. However,  in order to obtain 
happiness, we must create its causes. In other word s, if we want to obtain a certain result, we 
first need to create its causes. We should think about the fact that all sentient beings are presently tormented by the suffering of suffering , suffering of change, and pervasive suffering, 
and then generate the mental attitude that wish es them to be free from such sufferings. In 
particular we need to develop th e wish that they be free from pervasive suffering. In this way 
we generate compassion toward all sentient be ings, wishing them to be free from suffering 
and the causes of suffering. Then we take upon ourselves the responsibility to free all sentient 
beings from suffering and the causes of sufferi ng and to set them in the state of happiness; 
this is the extraordinary thou ght or attitude. We make this promise but presently we are 
unable to achieve it. Who can achieve it? On ly the buddhas, the enlightened beings, or 
omniscient ones, are able to do this. Therefor e, we generate the mind of enlightenment, 
which consists of two aspirations. The causal aspiration  is the wish to benefit all sentient 
beings, that is, to achieve the welfare of all sentient beings. The concomitant aspiration  is the 
aspiration to achieve enlightenment. With thes e two aspirations we can generate the mind of 
enlightenment. Bodhichitta or the mind of en lightenment is a primary mind, thus when the 
texts say that it is an aspiration, an aspiration to  achieve enlightenment, this is a case of giving 
the name of the result to the cause.   We should think about the fact that we ou rselves are devoid of happiness and tormented 
by suffering. Just as we wander in cyclic existenc e so too do other sentient beings. If we do not 
think of how we ourselves lack happiness and are tormented by suffering, it will be difficult to 
develop the thought to take care of other sentie nt beings. All sentient beings lack happiness 
and the causes of happiness and all of them are tormented by sufferings. Although these 
sufferings are difficult to bear, still we have not choice but to experience them. Engaging in the 
Bodhisattva Deeds  says that is difficult to generate such  thoughts even with respect to oneself, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
215 that we do not generate a good heart, love , compassion, or the mind of enlightenment 
thinking to take care of others even in dream.  We do not generate such thoughts even toward 
ourselves. However, since sentient beings are more important than ourselves, we need to 
develop the thought to take care  of them. We should not think to take care of some and not 
others, but instead we should gene rate the wish to take equal care of them all. To do this, we 
first need to generate the thoughts of small be ings and middling beings. We need to think, “If 
I myself were to experience the suffering of the lower realms, my body burning in molten 
iron, would I be able to bear it? I would not.” As just now we are free from such suffering, we 
need to ensure that in our next life we will not be reborn in the lower realms by practicing morality and so forth. We should check whethe r or not we could bear such horrific suffering. 
Finding that we would not be able to bear it, we  must take care not to be born in the lower 
realms. The means to do this is  the practice of morality.  
 However, even if we are not born in the lo wer realms but are born in the higher realms, 
the happy migrations, as a human being, anti-god , or god, we would still experience suffering. 
Human beings in general experience more suffer ing than gods, but when gods experience the 
sign of death they experience intense mental suffering for seven days of the god realm which 
is longer than our entire lifespan as a human bein g. It is even longer than many lifetimes as a 
human being, perhaps being as long as 1,000 years.   The anti-gods also have suffering in that they  continually fight with the gods of the Thirty-
Three over the jambu  tree, which has its roots in their la nd whereas its fruit is found in the 
god realm. Due to this, the gods pick and enjo y the fruit, making the anti-gods very jealous. 
Also the female anti-gods, who are extremely beautiful, are carried away by the male gods, making the anti-gods even more jealous. In this  way, they fight and are killed and experience 
suffering.   Since we have suffering even in our ordinary daily life, there is no need to mention that 
human beings suffer. Just being conceived an d being born is suffering. Sickness is also 
suffering, as is death. Due to being unable to ob tain the objects that we desire, we experience 
suffering. Having obtained something we desi re, we then worry about losing it through 
others’ power and so forth. In this way, we  experience much suffe ring, all of which we 
ourselves can see. In addition, our body experiences many different sufferings. Since it is a contaminated, appropriated aggreg ate caused by mental afflicti ons and contaminated actions, 
it is the very nature of suffering. Someti mes we suffer from hunger, sometimes thirst, 
sometimes heat, sometimes cold, and so forth. For example, there are many human beings in 
our world who are unable to find food, and ever y day thousands of children die in the third 
world. These are all sufferings of human beings . Thinking about this, we need to generate 
compassion and love. We need to develop the wi sh to eliminate or relieve this suffering. How 
c a n  w e  d o  t h i s ?  F o r  e x a m p l e ,  i f  w e  h a v e  a  h e adache we can ask a doctor for medicine to 
relieve the pain. There are many different medicines for our physical and mental sufferings. There are also different therapies and healing methods such as pranatherapy, chromotherapy, 
reiki, and so forth. There are many such me thods for relieving the pain and sickness of 
human beings. However, we are more limited in  our ability to help other kinds of beings. 
Although a dog, for example, cannot do much to  relieve its pain other than lick its wounds, 
human beings can take it to a veterinarian who gives it medicine and so forth. But still there 
are many animals that live in forests and other places where no one takes care of them. Since 
human beings are intelligent, they can eliminate their suffering. For this reason, we need to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
216 understand how we ourselves suffer and generate the wish to become free from this suffering. 
How can we do this? Only by practicing the Dharma. Perhaps we cannot prevent the suffering 
of this life but we can ensure that in the future  we will take better rebirths with less suffering.  
 Middling beings understand that even higher rebirths are the nature of suffering. Just to be 
a sentient being is to experience suffering. Theref ore, it is necessary to generate love for all 
sentient beings, the thought to take care of all our mother sentient beings. In order to do this 
we need to generate the mind of enlightenment.  Prior to this, we need to train in the paths 
common to small and middling beings. In this  way we can generate an uncontrived or 
genuine mind of enlightenment. For this, we need its causes, love and compassion. It was 
mentioned before that prior to developing love and compassion, we must generate the 
thought to repay the kindness of  sentient beings, before that we must remember their 
kindness, and before that we must recognize all sentient beings as having been our mother. If 
we do not have a good relationship with our mo ther and are convinced that she was very hard 
and did not treat us well, it can be more benefici al to think that all sentient beings have been 
our father, brother, sister, uncle, aunt, or even  a very close friend whom we love very much. 
We should think about how this person is very good to us and treats us very well, and then 
think that all sentient beings have done the same when they were our very close friend. Then 
remember that as our close friend they were very  kind to us. Then generate the wish to repay 
their kindness. Then develop love for them, then compassion, and then the extraordinary 
attitude. After this, we generate the mind of enlightenment that wishes  to attain buddhahood 
in order to be able to benefit all sentient beings. Thinking in this way, we should strive to develop the mind of enlightenment.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1F How the lesser and intermediate [branches of the path] are also necessary for the ritual 
accumulation and purification of this mental attitude 
Efforts to purify and accumulate by many  means through the thoughts of going for 
refuge, actions and results, and so forth on the occasion of the two paths, also 
constitute methods for training the ment al continuum – preliminary practices for 
the generation of the mind [of enlightenm ent]. As they become [part of] the seven-
limb practice along with going for refuge, these [thoughts common to lesser and 
intermediate beings ] should also be understood as  methods for the arisal of the 
mind [of enlightenment ]. 
 
In order to generate the mind of enlightenment we need to engage in its preliminaries. Here 
in the outline the word “ritual” does not refe r to prayers and pujas but to the practices of 
purifying negative actions and accumulating positi ve energy in order to generate the mind of 
enlightenment. There are different methods of purifying our negativities, for example, those 
that are common to both sutra and tantra. Howe ver, the best means of purification is to 
meditate on emptiness, thinking that the thr ee spheres – the agent, the negative action, and 
the recipient – are all empty of inherent existence.   The seven limbs are, as mentioned before, pr ostration, offering, confession, rejoicing, 
requesting to turn the wheel of Dharma, suppl icating the teachers to not pass beyond sorrow 
(to not enter nirvana) but to remain until a ll sentient beings achieve enlightenment, and 
dedication. Together with these seven limbs, we  go for refuge. These methods are constantly 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
217 mentioned in terms of how to collect merit. We should visualize the merit field in any one of 
the different ways as depicted in various tang kas. We can meditate the members of the merit 
field as stacked one above the other or like  a market place – the Buddha in the center 
surrounded by the meditational deities, bu ddhas, bodhisattvas, dakas and dakinis, and 
Dharma protectors. Or we can imagine th e Buddha alone and think of him as the 
embodiment of all the Buddha, Dharma, and Sang ha. Thus, in order to collect merit we need 
to do one of these visualizations, either the easy visualization of the Buddha alone or the 
more complete visualization of him surrounded by the other deities and so forth. We then 
prostrate to the merit field, make offerings, co nfess our negativities, rejoice, and so forth. 
There are many lam-rim texts translated into English and various other languages, which you 
can read so as to understand more about the merit field.  To generate the mind of enlightenment we need to accumulate a lot of merit and purify negativities. This is our main practice. If ha ving generated the mind of enlightenment or a 
good heart, we give a handful of food to a dog,  cat, or pigeon, we create a huge amount of 
merit. On the other hand, if without the moti vation of love, compassion, or the mind of 
enlightenment we were to give a lot of money to  others in order that they think we are very 
gen er o u s , so  as  t o gai n  fam e o r  a go o d  r epu tation and to be admired by everyone, we will 
create only a very small amount of merit. Cont rarily, if motivated by love, compassion, or the 
mind of enlightenment, even if we only give away something small we will create a lot of 
merit. Therefore, how much merit we cr eate is dependent on our motivation.  
 When Buddha Shakyamuni was on the learner path of a bodhisattva,  he circumambulated 
a Brahmin meditator-sage, who was absorbed in concentration, nine times while hopping on 
one foot and generating a very good motivation to attain enlightenment,  whereby he collected 
merit equal to that collected over nine eons. Br ahmins avoid eating blac k foods, such as meat, 
eggs, and so forth, and eat mainly butter, yogu rt, and milk. In addition, some brahmins only 
eat food prepared by themselves, or food prep ared by their wife, and so forth. Some avoid 
drinking tea, some only eat vegetables, some o n l y  e a t  f r u i t ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  T h e y  h a v e  m a n y  
ritual practices as there are different lineages, so me of which are very strict. While they try to 
avoid making the slightest mistakes, we do not do the same. In short, it is possible to create a 
lot of merit in a short time.  Milarepa went from upper Tibet to a place near the border with Nepal and then to a place south of Lhasa, looking for Marpa the Transl ator. Eventually someone pointed out Marpa, 
who was working in a field. Milarepa asked Marpa to accept him as his disciple. Marpa 
offered Milarepa some food and drink. They at e together and then Marpa told him to eat all 
that remained, to finish the work that still remain ed to be done in the fi eld, and then to come 
to his home. Milarepa finished the food and the work, and then went to report this to Marpa. 
Marpa thought to himself that it was very auspic ious and indicated that Milarepa would finish 
whatever work he was given to do. However, he  refused to teach Milarepa and instead gave 
him physical work to do. He told Milarepa to build a tall house, using stones that were to be carried on his back. Watching Milarepa, Marpa’s wife felt so sorry for him that one day she 
brought a stone to help him. When the work  was almost done, Marpa came and scolded 
Milarepa, saying that the work he had done was not good as one of the stones had been 
brought there by his wife. He told Milarepa to take down the tower and return the stones to 
their original places. He then told Milarepa to  make another tower at another location. This 
happened eight or nine times. Only after Milarepa  had finished this did Marpa accept to give 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
218 him a special teaching on Mahamudra but told hi m that he would have to make an offering 
in order to request Dharma teachings. Ho wever, Milarepa did not own anything and 
although Marpa’s wife gave Milarepa somethin g to offer to Marpa, he did not accept it. 
Eventually he accepted to teach Milarepa for a short time, perhaps just one or two hours. 
However, Milarepa completely understood what  he had been taught and meditated for one 
week immovable in meditative equipoise. When  Marpa asked where Milarepa was, he was 
told that he was sitting in meditation as still as a statue. He said to call Milarepa to come to 
him. This all came about because, by doing what Marpa had said, Milarepa had been able to 
purify all his negativities and obscurations and achieve enlightenment in that very life. This 
can happen.   Prior to meeting Marpa, Milarepa had been begged by his sister, who did not have a good 
relationship with the villagers as they had taken away all thei r belongings when their father 
died, to do something to return the harm caus ed to them by the villa gers. Because of this, 
Milarepa went to study black magic. Having done  so, his sister insistently asked him to use it 
to kill all the villagers and eventually Milarepa gave in to her badgering. Using black magic, he 
caused a black cloud to gather above the villag e from which huge hailstones fell. He then 
caused lightening to set the village on fire, unti l all that remained was his sister’s house. In 
this way he created heavy negative karma which he later needed to purify. For this reason 
Marpa gave him the difficult job of making ma ny tall houses or towers. This story is 
commonly recounted by Tibetans and is also me ntioned in Milarepa’s biography. It shows 
that it is possible to attain enlightenment in th is very life. Because we have a precious human 
rebirth, have met and can practi ce the holy Dharma, we should not waste time in developing 
the mind of enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1G Advice to cherish the way that the teaching cycles of lesser and intermediate beings become 
branches of great beings 
Here the spiritual master should indicate  how the teaching cycles of lesser and 
intermediate beings become branches for th e arisal of the mind of enlightenment, 
{59} and the disciple should also gains ce rtainty in this rega rd. Through recalling 
those [points] with each practice, you shou ld thoroughly cherish this training. If 
this is not done, the path of great be ings and the individual paths become 
unconnected. As you do not gain cert ainty with regard to the mind of 
enlightenment until you reach the actual pa th of great beings, this would be an 
obstacle to the arisal of that mind or, meanwhile, the degeneration of that great 
objective. Therefore, you should exert yourself in it. 
 
We need to understand more clearly that th e practices of the paths of small and middling 
beings are branches of the path of great beings. In other words, they are necessary in order to 
develop the mind of enlightenment. It is import ant that this be taught  by the lama and that 
the disciple definitely understands this.  
 We should apply mindfulness in order to tr ain our mind in the important points of the 
paths of small and middling beings – the method  for generating the mind of enlightenment. 
We should remember this and cherish it. If we do not do this, we will come to think that the 
three paths are unrelated. In short, we will come  to think that the path of great beings is not 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
219 related to the paths of small and middling beings . However, this is not correct in that the 
paths of small and middling beings are branches or causes for developing the path of great 
beings. Since they are very important, we should  do a glance meditation on all the points of 
these paths, like looking at a painting on a wa ll. In other words, everyday we should briefly 
review the path of small beings and then the path of middling beings, before going on to 
meditate on the fact that all sentient bein gs have been our mothers, remembering their 
kindness, generating the wish to repay this kindness, developing love, compassion, and the 
extraordinary attitude, and generati ng the mind of enlightenment.  
 Since we need to have all these points clea r in our mind, we should try to emphasize the 
practices of all three paths. In this way, we will  achieve the actual path of great beings without 
much effort. On the other hand, if we do no t meditate on the paths of small and middling 
paths, we will not be able to achieve the path of  great beings. We will experience interferences 
and will lose the great aim or objective. Our mind of enlightenment will degenerate and we 
will not be able to achieve the aim of attainin g enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient 
beings. Therefore, it is definitely import ant to meditate on th e two common paths.  
 
Thursday morning, 3 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 59) says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1H [The order on the path] 
1 The order of the two minds etc. and how to train [in them]  
Having trained in this manner, you sh ould make the uncontrived mind of 
enlightenment arise in your mental cont inuum as much as possible. Then, in 
order to make it stable, first go for un common refuge and then perform the ritual 
of the aspiring [mind of enlightenment ]. Having taken the aspiring [mind of 
enlightenment ] through by means of the ritual, pr actice its trainings. Then, time 
and again develop the wish to train in the trainings – the six perfections, the four 
ways of gathering, etc. Once the wish to  train in them has come forth from the 
bottom of your heart, take the pure vow of the engaging [mind of enlightenment ]. 
 
“The two minds” in this context refer to the aspiring or wishing mind of enlightenment and 
the engaging mind of enlightenment. Thus, here  they do not refer to the conventional and 
ultimate minds of enlightenment.   We need to train in the mind of enlighte nment by meditating until we generate an 
uncontrived mind of enlightenment. We need to  put effort into meditating on it. Having 
generated the mind of enlightenmen t, we then need to make it stable by going for refuge in a 
way that is not common to or shared by sm all and middling beings. In the case of the 
uncommon refuge of the Great Vehicle, the Bu ddha Jewel is the enjoyment body, which is 
one of the form bodies; the Dharma Jewel is th e true cessations and true paths related to the 
abandonment of the obscurations  to knowledge; and the Sangha Jewel is the bodhisattvas 
who have achieved the path of  meditation and so forth.  
 Then we should take the aspiring mind of enlightenment by means of the ritual. Having 
done so, we must abandon the four black dhar mas and rely on the four white dharmas. In 
other words, what is to be aban doned is the four black dharmas an d what is to be practiced or 
adopted is the four white dharmas. It is explai ned in some texts that by giving up even one 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
220 sentient being with the though t “I cannot develop love and co mpassion for him or her,” we 
lose the vow of the aspiring mind of enlightenment and accrue many faults.  
 Having taken the aspiring mind of enlightenmen t, we then need to practice in accordance 
with that to be practiced or trained in. We sh ould develop the wish again and again to train 
in the six perfections and four means of gather ing disciples. The six pe rfections are the usual 
ones: generosity, morality, patience, joyous effort, concentration, and wisdom. The four 
means of gathering disciples are to give material things in order to bring others close to one, 
to talk nicely to them, to te ach them the Dharma, and to prac tice in accordance with what 
one teaches. We can see that Christian practi tioners also engage in the four means of 
gathering disciples. First they give material th ings to those who are poor and talk nicely to 
them, then they teach them the beliefs of Christ ianity, and then they also act in accordance 
with what they have taught. Sinc e these four are also practices of bodhisattvas, we should not 
criticize their behavior.  
 T h e n ,  i f  w e  r e a l l y  w i s h  t o  p r a c t i c e  t h e  s i x perfections and the four means of gathering 
disciples, we should take the vo w of the engaging mind of enli ghtenment, that is, the actual 
bodhisattva vows. Having taken them, what do we  need to practice? We need to practice the 
bodhisattva conduct, the six perfections and so fo rth, as much as possible in our everyday life. 
In addition, having taken the bodhisattva vo ws, we should study and then safeguard the 
eighteen root downfalls and fo rty-six secondary infractions. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The order of explanations 
Then, even risk your life in order not to be tainted by a root downfall. Strive not 
to be tainted by small and medium contamin ations and by infractions. However, if 
you get tainted, purify them well by means of what has been expounded with 
regard to restoring downfalls. Then, trai n in general in the six perfections and 
especially in concentration, the essence of calm abiding, in order to make the 
mind serviceable with respect to whatever  virtuous object you wish. The statement 
in Lamp on the Path  that you train in calm abiding in order to develop the 
superknowledges108 is only an illustration. Atis ha himself has stated on other 
occasions that it also serves to develo p special insight. Therefore you should 
develop calm abiding for that purpose too. Then, {60} in order to cut the bonds of 
grasping at the two selves, decide on the meaning of emptiness by means of the 
view. Through sustaining this mode of medi tation flawlessly, accomplish the entity 
of wisdom, special insight. 
 
Having taken the bodhisattva vows, we need to av oid being stained by a root downfall even at 
the risk of losing our life. We should also  avoid by stained by the contaminations and 
infractions. There are four cont aminations that need to be abandoned. If we commit one of 
them, it is considered to be a small contaminatio n; if we commit two or three of them, it is a 
middling contamination; and if we commit all four  it is a great contam ination These four are 
mentioned in the Six Session Guru Yoga: (1) no t regarding the downfall as detrimental, (2) 
                                              
108 JN: mngon shes , can also be translated as “clairvoyance” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
221 not forsaking the wish to repeat such behavior, (3) delighting and taking pleasure in it, and 
(4) having no shame or embarrassment. By commi tting all four, we lose the bodhisattva vows. 
If, on the other hand, we commit a small or middling contamination, we can purify and 
restore the bodhisattva vows by retaking them. In fact, even after having committed a great 
contamination whereby the vows are completely lost, they can still be retaken again. The 
bodhisattva and tantra vows are such that even  if one commits a root downfall they can be 
retaken. For this reason they are likened to gold or silver which, when broken, can be melted 
down and newly refashioned into the same obje ct. On the other hand, the vows of individual 
liberation are likened to a clay pot which, once broken, cannot be put back together again. In 
spite of this, since there is always the danger of degenerating the bodh isattva vows we should 
be careful with respect to them. 
 We should practice the six perfections and in  particular we should try to achieve calm 
a b i d i n g .  W e  s h o u l d  m e d i t a t e  s i n g l e - p o i n t e d l y  o n  a n  o b j e c t  a s  m u c h  a s  p o s s i b l e .  T h u s ,  w e  should first try to develop calm abiding by trai ning in it again and again. Having attained 
calm abiding, the body and mind become complete ly serviceable, that is, we can do whatever 
we want with them. Then we should develop special insight in relation to emptiness. 
Meditation on emptiness is the main antidote  to all the afflictions because it directly 
counteracts the root of all the afflictions – the conception of a self of persons or the 
conception of all phenomena as truly or inherent ly existent. In fact, “emptiness” refers to the 
fact that all phenomena la ck inherent existence.  
 We need to abandon the two conceptions of  a self: a self of persons and a self of 
phenomena. “Phenomena” in this context refers to the five aggregates: the form aggregate, 
feeling aggregate, discrimination aggregat e, compositional factors aggregate, and 
consciousness aggregate. On the basis of these aggregates (phenomena), we impute the self, 
the person, or the being. In th is way, the person depends on  the aggregates. Likewise, the 
body aggregate does not exist inherently or truly, that is, it is empty of inherent or true existence. We should take this la ck of inherent or true existence as our object of the mode of 
apprehension and strive to develop special insi ght with respect to it. The ultimate truth is 
emptiness. Conventional truths include whatever  we see with our eyes, hear with our ears, 
smell with our nose, taste with our tongue, and f eel with our body, as well as all the objects of 
the mind, such as space, dreams, visions, and so forth. Such conventional truths are also 
called “deceptive truths.”   In order to meditate on emptiness, we first need to establish it by means of many reasonings. An example of one of these reasonings is:   
This table does not exist inherently because it is not an inherently existent one or 
an inherently existent many. It is not an  inherently existent one because it has 
parts. Having parts and being partless are direct contradictions, and partless 
phenomena do not exist. The table is also not an inherently existent many because 
it is not an inherently existent one. Ma ny cannot exist without one. Therefore, the 
table is not an inherently existent on e or an inherently existent many.  
 We should try to get a mental image, a meaning generality, of what inherent existence is like. 
For example, even though we do not have horn s on our head, we can imagine what we would 
look like if we did have horns on our head. Or  we can imagine what we would look like with 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
222 a tail. Similarly, even though all phenomena do  not exist inherently, we can imagine how they 
would exist if they were inherently existent. Fo r example, they would be completely stable and 
immutable. On the other hand, because phenomena are not inherently existent they change, 
can move, and so forth.  
 
Another reasoning is: a flower is not inherently existent because it is not inherently produced 
from self, other, both, or without causes. In other words, all compounded phenomena are not 
inherently produced from themselves, they are not inherently produced from other, they are n o t  i n h e r e n t l y  p r o d u c e d  f r o m  b o t h  s e l f  a n d  other, and they are no t produced without a 
cause; therefore, they are not inherently prod uced. Nagarjuna sets out this reasoning in his 
Fundamental Wisdom  in order to refute the views of schools other than the Prasangika 
Madhyamaka. For example, one non-Buddhist school asserts that all phenomena are 
inherently produced because they are produced from self, whereas another such school asserts 
that they are inherently produced from both. The Vaibhashika, Sautrant ika, Chittamatra, and 
Svatantrika Madhyamaka, which are Buddhist schools, assert, on the other hand, that 
phenomena are inherently produced from ot her. The Jains assert that phenomena are 
produced either from self or without a cause.  The Charvakas also assert that phenomena are 
produced without a cause.   All these views existed in ancient India. The Samkya view, for example, is that all 
phenomena are included in twenty-five categori es. Among them, the most important cause is 
the Primordial Principle, which is partless, uncaused, and permanent. The being, self, or 
knower are neither cause nor result, that is, it is  not a cause nor does it have a cause. Some of 
the twenty-five are both cause and result. They a ssert that when the being looks at all results 
and asks who created them, it will find that it is the Primordial Principle that created them. Having been seen, the Primordial Principle be comes ashamed and disappears, whereby all its 
results – forms, sounds, odors, tastes, and tang ible objects, and the four elements – also 
disappear, leaving only the being. At this time the being attains liberati on and cyclic existence 
comes to an end. Other schools say that everyt hing was created by Ishvara, that is, by his 
thinking of something, it arises. Since there were these many different tenet systems of the 
ancient Indian schools, Nagarjuna had to refute all such views. 
 In Tibet, when I was a small child, my grandmother once told me how human beings were created. She said that human beings were fash ioned out of butter by the Old Man, who then 
gave them life by blowing into their nose. Ho wever, when he called out “Human being,” his 
creation did not respond. Due to this, he then fashioned eyes, ears, nose, and so forth out of 
ashes, blew on the figure again, and once again called out “Human being!” This time the 
being responded and came alive! Wondering whether or not this was true, I too made some small figures out of butter and called out “D o g !  C a t !  S h e e p ! ”  a n d  s o  f o r t h ,  b u t  n o t h i n g  
happened! When I asked my grandmother why none of them had responded, she only 
laughed at me! There are similar stories in ma ny cultures. However, Buddha Shakyamuni, the 
Omniscient One, taught that everything is cr eated by the mind. In other words, we create 
actions motivated by the mind, just as we firs t mentally develop a project and only later on 
actualize it. For example, architects first make drawings of a house, indicating where to put 
the bathroom, bedrooms, kitchen, veranda, and so fo rth, and then later on it is actually built. 
Similarly, electronic things, such as computer s, are first created mentally by someone and 
then later on they are physica lly constructed. Cell phones, wh ich we can now use not only to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
223 talk to others but also to send written messages,  take photos, see who we are talking to, and so 
forth, are the same. They are all created by the mind, that is, first the mind thinks to make 
something and then later on it is actualized.  
 We should make a mental plan to medita te and achieve calm abiding, and then to 
meditate on emptiness or ultimate truth in or der to attain the spec ial insight that takes 
emptiness as its object. This wisdom realizing em ptiness destroys the conception of a self of 
persons and the conception of a self of phen omena, and thereby cuts the root of cyclic 
existence. Then, even if we wish  to take rebirth for the benefit of others, we can do so. Thus, 
at the moment we should put ef fort into trying to understand emptiness more deeply. This 
will be discussed later on in th e context of special insight.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1I All paths are contained in the three trainings  
Accordingly, except for the accomplishment  of calm abiding and special insight, 
up to and including the training in the vow of the engaging [mind of 
enlightenment ] constitutes the training in ethical discipline; calm abiding is the 
training in the mind; and special insight is  training in wisdom. So it says in the 
Commentary on the Lamp on the Path . 
 
All the paths are included or contained in the th ree higher trainings: the higher training in 
morality, the higher training in concentration or  the mind, and the higher training in wisdom 
or special insight. Up to the generation of the aspiring and engaging mind of enlightenment 
and the taking of the bodhisattva vows is all incl uded in the higher training in morality. Calm 
abiding is the higher training in concentration or in the mind. Special insight is the higher 
training in wisdom. Wisdom itself is in fa ct special insight. This is taught in the Commentary 
on the Lamp on the Path  by Atisha.  
 In short, the vows of individual liberation , the bodhisattva vows, and the tantra vows are 
included in the higher training in morality. Th e training in setting the mind single-pointedly 
on an object is included in the higher traini ng in the mind. The anal ytical meditation on 
emptiness or analytical meditation on the four  noble truths and their sixteen attainments in 
included in the higher training in wisdom.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1J For the sake of skill in classification, all paths are contained in the two collections 
Furthermore, up to and including calm abiding is method, the collection of merit, 
the path based on conventional truth, an d the stages of the vast path, while the 
generation of the three sp ecial wisdoms is wisdom, the collection of wisdom, [the 
path] based on ultimate truth, and the stages  of the profound pa th. Therefore, you 
should generate great certainty with respec t to their order and set number, as well 
as the fact that enlightenment cannot be attained by means of method and 
wisdom that are separate from one another. 
 
All six perfections are included in the two co llections – the collection of merit and the 
collection of wisdom. The first five – genero sity, morality, patience, joyous effort, and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
224 concentration – are, among method and wisdom , included in method, and, among the two 
collections, they are included in the collection of merit. In addition, th ey are paths that rely 
on or are based on the conventional truth. They are also the stages of the vast path. The three 
special wisdoms, on the other hand, are, am ong method and wisdom, included in wisdom, 
and, among the two collections, are included in the collection of wisdom. They rely on or are 
based on the ultimate truth. They are al so the stages of the profound path.  
 The three wisdoms could be: (1) the wisdom  analyzing for the conventional, (2) the 
wisdom analyzing for the ultimate, and (3) the wisdom analyzing how to cherish and benefit 
sentient beings. They could also be something else but I do not remember this subject right 
now.  
 If we collect merit but not wisdom, or wisdom  but not merit, or collect merit and wisdom 
separately, we will not be able to achieve bu ddhahood. Therefore, we need to collect both 
merit and wisdom. In other words, we need to practice generosity and so forth in order to 
collect merit, and we need to meditate on empt iness in order to collect wisdom. Alternatively, 
it can be said that we need both method and wisdom. Just as a bird with its two wings intact can fly whereas if one of them is broken it cannot, similarly due to having both merit and 
wisdom we can go to enlightenment.   Thursday afternoon, 3 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 60) says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1K The need to enter the Mantrayana 
Having trained your mental continuum th us by means of the common paths, you 
must enter the Mantrayana, for, once you have entered it, you will quickly 
complete the two collections. 
 
Having trained in the common pa th of the perfection vehicle,  we should then enter the 
mantra or tantra vehicle. Why is this? Becaus e by means of entering the tantra vehicle and 
practicing it, it is possible to quickly  complete the two collections, the collection of merit and 
the collection of wisdom. For this reason, it is important to enter the path of tantra.  
 For example, if we travel somewhere by foot we  will not get to our destination very quickly, 
whereas if we go there by bicycl e we will get there a bit quicker, if we go there by car we will 
get there even quicker, and if we go there by plane we will get there much quicker. Traveling 
by foot is like the small path, by  bicycle is like the middling path , by car is like the bodhisattva 
vehicle or the mahayana, and by plane is like th e tantra path. Therefore, in order to quickly 
complete the two collections we need to enter the path of tantra and practice it. From the 
point of view of tantra, it is possible to simultaneously collect merit and wisdom, whereas this is not possible from the point of view of sutra,  according to which one first collects a lot of 
merit and only then collects wisdom. Because these two can be achieve simultaneously by 
means of tantra, it becomes quicker to achieve buddhahood.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1L How to pursue the perfection path alone 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
225 If you cannot manage more than [the perfection path ] or due to the small power 
of your lineage you do not want to, you shou ld just expand on these very stages of 
the path more and more.  
 
If we travel the perfection path alone we will not achieve enlightenment very quickly. 
However, some people are only able to practi ce the perfection path and some, due to small 
power of their lineage, do not want to enter th e tantra path. For such people it will not be 
easy to attain enlightenment as it will be necessa ry to practice the stages of the path again and 
again, increasing them more and more.   Thus, if we wish to attain complete buddh ahood quickly, we should first practice the 
perfection vehicle and then at a certain point en ter the practice of tantra. Wishing to practice 
tantra, we first need to receive an initiation or empowerment. In action tantra (kriya tantra) 
there are two empowerments – the water em powerment and the crown empowerment. In 
performance tantra (charya tant ra) there are the five empowe rments of the five buddha 
lineages – the water, crown, vajra, bell, and na me empowerments. In yoga tantra there are the 
five empowerments of the five buddha lineag es plus the vajra-master empowerment. In 
highest yoga tantra there is the common vase empowerment of the five buddha lineages as 
well as the vajra-master empowerment which is included in the vase empowerment, together 
with the secret empowerment, wisdom empowerm ent, and word empowerment. In the case of 
the vase empowerment, the empowering substanc e is the water in the vase. The disciple is 
either given this water to drink, or he or she is given it to put on his or her forehead, or the vase itself is place on the crown of his or her head. This is followed by the secret, wisdom, and 
word empowerments. Having received these four  empowerments, we can enter the practice of 
highest yoga tantra. Empowerment is therefore sa id to be the door to the tantra vehicle.  
 To summarize, it is not possible to achiev e enlightenment by means of the sutra path 
alone.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1M The stages of engagement in mantra 
As for engaging in mantra, since proper re liance on a spiritual teacher is generally 
given great emphasis in all the vehicles and particularly in mantra, you should 
make it [even] more pre-eminent [in th i s  c o n t e x t ]  t h a n  b e fore. Then, having 
ripened your mental continuum by means of an empowerment that comes from a 
pure tantra class, you should risk your life for, that is, guard, the commitments and vows that you obtained at that time. {61}. Es pecially, if a root downfall occurs, even 
though you take them again, this will spoil your mind and it will be difficult for 
positive qualities to develop. Therefore,  do not be tainted by them. Do not get 
tainted even by branch downfall and, if  you do get tainted, you should not be 
unconcerned about it, but purify it thro ugh confession and restraint. Then you 
should be guided as appropriate in the yoga with signs in accordance with the 
lower tantra classes or in the yoga of th e generation stage in accordance with the 
higher tantras. On that basis you should train as appropriate in the yoga without 
signs in accordance with the lower tantra classes or in the yoga of the completion 
stage according to the higher tantras.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
226 What is the order of the stages for entering in to mantra? In the case of all the vehicles in 
general, whether the hinayana or mahayana, or wh ether the small, middle, or great vehicles, it 
is necessary to rely on a guru, teacher, or virt uous spiritual friend. Then, in particular if we 
wish to engage in the practice of tantra or mant ra, we need to rely on a teacher who is able to 
confer empowerment and so fort h. To confer empowerment, the guru himself must have 
received that particular empowerment, have comp leted the “suitable activities” retreat of that 
deity, and have performed the conc luding burnt offering or fire puja to the deity. Only if he 
has done all this is he qualified to confer that particular empowerment.
