 A perfect or pure practice of tantra arises from receiving an empowerment that arises from 
a pure source. Empowerment ripens our contin uum. On the occasion of an empowerment, 
we receive vows and commitments that we must then  safeguard even at the risk of losing our 
life. Thus, it is very important to keep thes e vows and commitments well. If we commit a root 
downfall, we should take the tantra vows once again. However, when this happens our mental 
continuum is spoiled and the vows are no longer as good as they used to be. It is like leaving 
an object out in the rain whereby it gets dama ged and is not as useful as before. Thus when 
this happens, it becomes difficult to generate good qualities in our continuum.  
 In addition, we should take care to also  not be stained by the branch or secondary 
downfalls. With respect to the tantra vows, ther e are defeats and gross infractions. We should 
avoid such defeats and gross infractions, but if one happens, instead of thinking that it does 
not matter, we should engage in purifying it by  confessing the transgression. If we do not do 
this, our continuum will be spoiled or damaged.   From the point of view of the lower tantras, we need to engage in the yoga with signs and 
then, when that becomes stable, in the yoga wi thout signs. From the point of view of the 
higher tantras we need to engage in the yoga  of the generation stage and then, when that 
becomes stable, in the yoga of the completion st age. There are some differences in these with 
respect to the various deities but the different st ages can be set out as the isolation of body, 
the isolation of speech, the isolation of mind, th e metaphoric clear light, the impure illusory 
body, the actual clear li ght, the pure illusory body, the unio n of a learner of the actual clear 
light and pure illusory body, and the union of no-more-learner and the achievement of enlightenment or the state of Vajradhara. We need  to train in these stages in order to become 
better and better in them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2A2B-1N Explanation that the intention of the Lamp on the Path  etc. is the engagement in both sutra 
and mantra [vehicles] 
The body of the presentation of the path is set forth in Lamp on the Path , and the 
lam-rim also guides [students] in the same way.  
 
The body of the presentation of  the path is set out in the Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment , 
and the lam-rim also guides us in the path in a similar way. First we engage in the sutra path 
and then, having trained in this, we are led into  the tantra path. This is similar in Atisha’s 
text and in the teachings on the stages of th e path. Therefore, we too need to follow this 
procedure. In the Lamp on the Path , Atisha first explains the vehicles of the three beings and 
then toward the end sets out the n eed to enter the mantra vehicle.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
227 Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2A2B-2 The purpose 
A The actual purpose 
B The sources 
2B4B-2A2B-2A The actual purpose 
1 Question 
Second: “If the Dharma-cycles of lesse r and intermediate beings are the 
preparatory practices of great beings, it shou ld indeed be all right to consider them 
the stages of the path of great beings. But what is the use of saying ‘the stages of 
the path that are common to lesser and intermediate [beings]’?” 
 
Here a question is posed which is then answered in the next section. Wh y is it necessary to 
practice the paths of small and middle beings prio r to engaging in the practices of great beings 
since it should be enough to immediately engage  in the practices of great beings? Given this, 
why say “the stages of the path that are common  to small and middle beings”? In other words, 
since we are great beings, that is, mahayana prac titioners, why should we  engage in the paths 
that are common to small and middl ing beings? This person thinks that this is not necessary, 
rather it is enough to engage in  the path of great beings alone. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Answer 
There are two major purposes for classifyin g or guiding three kinds of beings. In 
this way, (1) the conceit of claiming ones elf to be a great being, while the mental 
attitudes that are common to lesser or intermediate beings have not even arisen, is 
overcome, and (2) all three – the highest, intermediate, and least mental attitudes 
– have great benefits. The way in which th ey are great is that since the two more 
advanced beings also need to strive for high status and liberation, there is no fault in teaching the great or intermediate indi viduals who are to be guided how to train 
in the thoughts of those two, and positive  qualities are developed. However, if a 
lesser person trains beginning with the more advanced ones, the more advanced 
thoughts will not arise and the lesser ones will  be cast away, {62} so nothing will be 
developed. Moreover, through being taught the common path and through 
training, those with the good fortune of superior [capacity] will soon develop 
positive qualities that had or had not aris en before, whereby they can be guided to 
higher and higher [levels], once the lower ones have arisen. Since this is correct, 
their path will not be prolonged. 
 The path is divided into three in terms of some beings being led into the path of small beings, 
some into the path of middling beings, and some into the path of great beings. This division 
is made in order to reduce or eliminate the pr ide of those who, withou t having trained in the 
paths of small and middling beings, think of them selves as great beings. Since some beings are 
very intelligent, some are middling intelligent, an d some are less intelligent, it is necessary to 
teach the three paths to respectively those with sharp faculties, middling faculties, and small 
faculties. In other words, there are beings wh o have great capacity, beings who have middling 
capacity, and beings who have small capacity. Those with small capacity should be taught the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
228 small path, whereupon, by training in it, thei r mental faculty improves and they can be led 
into the middling path. Similarly, those with mi ddling capacity should be taught the middling 
path, whereupon by training in it their mental faculty improves  and they can be led into the 
great path, the Mahayana.  
 How does there arise great benefit? Since sm all beings are more interested in obtaining 
higher rebirth, they should be taught the path  of small beings. Those who are interested in 
attaining liberation or nirvana should be taught the path of middling beings. However, both 
those who wish to attain the happiness of higher rebirth, that is, the success of future lives, 
and those who wish to attain liberation, must fi rst enter the path. Initia lly we are all ordinary 
beings who are not interested in  the Dharma or meditation, ther efore interest in the Dharma 
must first be induced before they can be led in to the small path. When this interest has been 
developed, they should e taught about the fact that future lives are mo re important than this 
life through the explanation that when this life ceases we do not cease to exist, rather our 
mental continuum continues. Then, because this  life is only one and our future lives are 
many, future lives are more important. For this reason it is necessary to practice the Dharma 
in order to obtain a good rebirth in the future . To do this, then need to be taught how to 
practice the morality of the ten virtues. They  should also be taught how to meditate, for 
example, how to do the breathing meditation. Th ey need to be told that they should focus 
their mind on the breath, following the inhalati on and exhalation of the breath while trying 
not to allow their mind to be distracted to othe r things. It is said that when many conceptions 
arise, we should stop them by meditating on th e breath. To do this we think, “Now I want to 
exhale,” while focusing one part of the mind  on the breath and an other on the wish to 
exhale. Then we think, “Now I want to inhale” and so forth.  In this way, the breathing 
meditation can reduce conceptions. Purification  can also be done in concomitance with the 
breathing meditation by imagining that with each  exhalation we exhale all the negative energy 
created by our body, speech, and mind since beginningless time. For example, when we are 
very agitated or nervous or the mind is complete ly dark, sinking, or filled with laxity, we can 
imagine exhaling all this negative energy to a gr eat distance in the form of black smoke. Then 
when we inhale we can imagine inhaling, in the form of white light, all the good qualities of 
the holy beings, their compassion, love, and positi ve energy, as well as all the positive energy 
of the entire universe. Thereby we achieve what ever we need and become very relaxed. We 
can meditate in this way, training our mind. This  is a simple meditation but it is necessary to 
do it. Our mind is like a crazy elephant. However, while a crazy elephant can be tamed by 
being hooked with iron hooks and beaten with sticks, our mind cannot be controlled by such 
things. Our mind can only be controlled by meditation.   In this way beings of small capacity can be led into the path of small beings. On the other 
hand, if the great path is taught to them, even th ough they try to train in  it they will be unable 
to generate its qualities and realizations. Therefor e, it is better to initially lead them in the 
small path, whereby they can achieve realizatio ns and improve more and more. Contrarily, if 
someone who has given up the small path is led in  the path of great beings, he or she will be 
unable to generate either the lower or higher path, due to which realizations will not arise, 
only ignorance will arise. Therefore, everyone needs to first travel the small path, then the 
middling path, and then the great path, progressive ly going higher and higher. In this way we 
will be able to gradually develop the entire path to enlightenment.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 28 February-3 March 2005 (05) 
229  If those who are very intelligent or who have  good fortune, having already generated the 
common paths, are taught the high er path, they will quickly achieve these qualities. Thus, the 
best is to first be taught the small path, then the middling path, and then  the great path. If we 
are led gradually in the paths, we will be able to generate all of them. In this way we will 
quickly generate the Mahayana path. Therefore, in  your everyday life try to develop love and 
compassion toward all sentient beings. This is the main base for the increase of the Mahayana 
qualities and the cause of generating the conventional mind of enlightenment.  
 First we generate the aspiring mind of en lightenment and then the engaging mind of 
enlightenment. In this way we accumulate meri t on the path of accu mulation and enter the 
path of preparation. Then we achieve the path of seeing and gradually attain the forbearances 
and knowledges. Then we achieve the path of me ditation. In this way all the obscurations or 
the objects of abandonment gradually diminish . Then, by means of the Hinayana path, we 
can attain the state of a foe-de stroyer in which all the afflic tive obscurations have been 
abandoned, although we will still have the ob scurations to knowledge. Having attained the 
state of a foe-destroyer, we can then en ter the Mahayana path and gradually attain 
buddhahood. However, we can also, right from  the beginning, enter the Mahayana path 
without engaging in the Hinayana path of hearer s or solitary realizers. Some people, on the 
other hand, engage in the Hin ayana path for a while and then due to some circumstances 
meet a mahayana spiritual friend, listen to Mahaya na teachings and, generating interest in it, 
give up the Hinayana path and enter the Maha yana path. There are these different ways of 
traveling the path. 
 In short, we need to familiarize our mind with the path of small beings, the path of 
middling beings, and the path of great beings. We need to generate definite emergence or 
renunciation in our mind. It is ea sy to wish to give up suffering but not as easy to give up the 
pleasures and excellences of this world! Ho wever, by giving them up, we can achieve 
enlightenment. Buddha Shakyamuni was at one ti me a prince, the son of a king, who had a 
wife and a son, but then, due to generating renunciation, he left his family and together with five servants meditated for six years while engagi ng in ascetic practices, eating and drinking 
very little. He gained concentration but his bo dy became like a skeleton, just bones covered 
with skin. When he was eventua lly offered a bowl of butter an d honey, he ate it whereby the 
strength of his body improved, but the other five ascetics thought that he was no longer a true 
practitioner and so abandoned him. The future  buddha then sat down under a bodhi tree to 
meditate. He destroyed the interferences or demo ns by the power of his meditation on love. 
First they manifested many beautiful woman wh o danced and sang around him, but, by the 
power of his meditation, he was able to transform them into ugly old hags with wrinkled skin. In this way, they could not harm him. The demons  then sent an army of  soldiers who shot at 
the Buddha with bows and arrows, but when th e arrows touched his body they transformed 
into flowers whereby they did not harm him. Li ke this, he destroyed the demons and then at 
sunrise attained enlightenment and manifested  omniscient mind, becoming a buddha. We 
will stop here on an auspicious subject that we too will go to buddhahood!  
END 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
230 Monday afternoon, 7 March 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 62) says:  
2B4B-2A2B-2B The sources 
The need to develop mental states gradually is proclaimed in [the Sutra ] Requested 
by the King Lord of Dharanis  through applying the analogy of a skilled jeweller 
cleaning precious stones gradually to th e meaning. The protector Nagarjuna also 
taught to gradually lead in the path of high status and certain goodness:  
 
First the Dharma of high status, 
Then that of certain goodness comes forth. 
Therefore, once you achieve high status, 
Certain goodness will excellently come by stages. 
 It is necessary to train the mind gradually with out jumping here and there. Just as a skilled 
jeweler gradually cleans and refines precious ston es, so too do sentient beings need to be led 
gradually according to their dispositions and imprints. Thus, the same teachings should not 
be given to all beings. In fact, if certain teachi ngs are given to certain beings they will not be 
o f  a n y  b e n e f i t  a n d  m a y  e v e n  b e  o f  h a r m .  T h e r e f o r e ,  i t  i s  n e c e s s a r y  t o  b e  s k i l l f u l  i n  g i v i n g  teachings in accordance with particular people’s mental dispositions. Just as a skilled jeweler purifies and cleans precious stems gradually, simi larly it is necessary to give different kinds of 
teachings in order to clean for sentient be ings to clean their mental afflictions.  
 In this context three levels of teachings ar e mentioned: easy Dharma teachings, middling 
Dharma teachings, and profound Dharma teachi ngs. The Buddha, for example, first taught 
the wheel of the four noble truths, then he taug ht the wheel of emptiness or signlessness, and 
then he taught the wheel of the differentiation  of characteristics. Th e beings for whom the 
first wheel was taught were, from the point of view of tenets, Vaibhashikas and Sautrantikas, 
and from the point of view of path, mostly hearers. The beings  for whom the second wheel of 
Dharma was taught were, from the point of view of tenets, Svatantrika Madhyamikas and 
Prasangika Madhyamikas. The beings for whom  the third wheel was taught were, from the 
point of view of tene ts, Chittamatrins. The King of Dharanis  Sutra  also sets out three wheels of 
Dharma but explains them a bit differently. This  subject can be learned from other texts.  
 Nagarjuna said that first it is necessary to give teachings on the path of small beings in 
order for sentient beings to generate more inte rest in attaining a rebi rth of high status, for 
example, as a human being, in their next life. In other words, first we must stop thinking only of this life. Because this life is just one, whereas future lives are many, we need to give emphasis to future lives, that is, to obtaining rebirth as human being or god. Therefore, it is 
necessary to first teach sentient beings morali ty – the abandonment of the ten non-virtuous 
actions and the practice of the ten virtuous acti ons. Having been taught morality, they should 
be taught the need to strive for certain or definite goodness. Hearers should be taught 
middling teachings, that is, the four noble truths  and that the pleasures of cyclic existence of 
are not complete and even bring suffering, in order to generate interest in them regarding the state of liberation or nirvana. Then they sh ould be slowly led in the higher path, the 
Mahayana. After that they should slowly be in troduced to the Prasan gika Madhyamaka point 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
231 of view – the profound and vast teachings of  the Mahayana. Nagarjun a said that sentient 
beings should be gradually taught high st atus and definite goodness in this way.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Arya Asanga also said: 
 
A l s o ,  i n  o r d e r  t h a t  t h e  s i d e  o f  v i r t u e  b e  g r a d u a l l y  a c c o m p l i s h e d  properly, the bodhisattva initially te aches those who have the wisdom of 
childish sentient beings easy Dharma, and causes them to follow easy directions and instructions. Real izing that they have developed 
middling wisdom, he causes them to follow intermediate Dharma teachings and intermediate directions  and instructions. Realizing that 
they have developed vast wisdom , he teaches them the profound 
Dharma and causes them to follow subtle directions and instructions. His beneficial activities descend on those sentient beings progressively. 
 Asanga also said that in order to gradually increase virtues on the side of enlightenment, the bodhisattva first gives easy teachings to childish beings with an ordinary mind as well as makes 
t h e m  f o l l o w  e a s y  a d v i c e  o r  d i r e c t i o n s  a n d  i n structions. Then he slowly teaches them the 
middling level of Dharma teachings, that is, the path of middling beings, and makes them 
follow middling level of advice and instructio ns, that is, the Hinayana path to nirvana. He 
teaches them that cyclic existenc e is the nature of suffering due to which it is necessary to get 
out of cyclic existence and achieve nirvana. For this purpose, the main objects to be 
abandoned are the afflictive obscurations – the afflictions such as attachment, anger, 
ignorance, pride, doubt, and afflicted view. Th en he teaches them how to abandon them by 
means of applying their specific antidotes. By being taught the middling Dharma in this way, 
they become vessels for vast or great wisdom. Then the bodhisattva teaches them emptiness, 
the profound. This refers to the Perfection of  Wisdom Sutras in 100 ,000, 25,000 and 8,000 
verses, which teach emptiness explicitly and teach the path, the eight topics and seventy 
categories, implicitly. All of these are nece ssary to develop a deeper understanding of 
emptiness. Then the bodhisattva teaches that emptiness is divided into twenty, sixteen, and 
four types, and that there two types of self lessness, the selflessness of persons and the 
selflessness of phenomena. Then he teaches th at the realization of emptiness should be 
conjoined with great compassion and love, that is, with the conventional mind of 
enlightenment that wishes to attain buddhahood for the benefit of all sentient beings. Then 
he teaches that based on this, sentient beings should practice  the activities or conduct of 
bodhisattvas, the six perfections and the four means of gathering disciples (generosity, talking 
nicely, giving teachings, and practicing what one teaches).   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Aryadeva, in his Lamp of Summarized Behavior , establishes that first the training in 
the contemplations of the perfection vehicle and after that the mode of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
232 engagement in mantra {63} needs to be gradual. Summarizing the meaning of this 
he says: 
 
In order for beings who are beginners 
To get involved in ultimate truth 
The means the perfect Buddha expounded Resemble the steps of a flight of stairs. 
 
In his text Summarized Behavior , Aryadeva says that first it is ne cessary to train the mind in the 
thoughts or contemplations of the Mahayana, that is, of the perfection vehicle. This means 
that first one should train in the vast and profound, whereby one comes to develop the mind 
of enlightenment, as well as great compassion and love, and the realizat ion of emptiness. Only 
after that should one enter the mantra vehicle. To practice tantra one first needs to receive 
empowerment. Without receiving an empowermen t, one cannot practice tantra. In fact the 
door to tantra is the receiving of an em powerment. If someone, without receiving 
empowerment, practices tantra by reading books and so forth, he or she will not obtain its 
result because in order to achieve the result it  is necessary to receive empowerment. There are 
four classes of tantra: action tantra (kriya tant ra), performance tantra (charya tantra), yoga 
tantra, and highest yoga tantra (mahanuttarayoga ). It is necessary to receive empowerment in 
these different classes of tantra. Having re ceived empowerment, when one practices the 
sadhana of the deity one should try to visualiz e oneself as that particular deity. First one 
transforms into emptiness, then with respect to  this mind realizing emptiness one thinks “I 
am the truth body.” From emptiness one arises as, for example, a cubit of light which is the 
nature of one’s mind, with respect to which one thinks “I am the enjoym ent body.” Then this 
transforms and one arise as the deity with one face and two arms, with respect to which one 
thinks “I am the emanation body.” When practici ng tantra, it is necessary to carry death into 
the path to the truth body, the intermediate state into the path to the enjoyment body, and 
birth into the path to the emanation body. By do ng this visualization ag ain and again, it will 
slowly become more familiar. In this way, one should try to practice tantra.  In order for beginners to engage in the ultima te truth, the method ta ught by the Buddha is 
like a  flight of stairs that is to be climbed one by one.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B  The actual way of taking its essence 
1 Training the mind in the stages of the path common to lesser beings 
2 Training the mind in the stages of the path common to intermediate beings 
3 Training the mind in the stages of the path of great beings 
The second one has three points: Training  the mind in the stages of the path 
common to lesser beings, training the mind  in the stages of the path common to 
intermediate beings, and training the mind  in the stages of the path of great 
beings. 
 
The path common to small or lesser beings be gins with the meditation recognizing the 
precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qu alities, the difficulty of finding it, and the 
fact that it can easily be lost due to death- impermanence. Thus, the subsequent meditation is 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
233 that of the nine-round meditation on death, of which the three main points are: death is 
certain, the time of death is uncertain, and th at at the time of death nothing, no worldly 
activities nor our wealth or body, can help us . Next there is the meditation on the lower 
realms: the sufferings of the he ll realms, the sufferings of th e hungry ghost realm, and the 
sufferings of the animal realms. One examines whether or not one could bear such rebirths. 
Since one could not bear them, one meditates on the need to abandon creating the projecting 
causes that throw one into the lower realms and strive to create the projecting actions that 
will throw one into the higher realms. Look ing for something to protect one from such 
sufferings, one will discover that only the Th ree Jewels, the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, are 
capable of doing this. Thus, one contemplates th eir qualities and, having generated faith in 
them, goes for refuge to them . Having done so, one must re spect the general and specific 
advice concerning what to abandon and what to  practice with respect to each one of them. 
Then one meditates on the need to guard the law of actions and results. One contemplates 
the nature of karma, for example, that karma is definite, that karma increases, that if karma is 
c r e a t e d  i t  w i l l  n e v e r  b e  l o s t ,  a n d  t h a t  i f  k a rma has not been created its result will not be 
experienced. One also meditates on the fact that  there is karma that is both accumulated and 
created, karma that is accumulated but no t created, karma that is created but not 
accumulated, and karma that is neither accumula ted or created. This can be understood from 
the Hundred Actions Sutra , one of the Buddha’s teachings, as well as from the fourth chapter of 
the Treasury of Manifest Dharma .  
 Having understood how actions function, that is, how they bring a result, one will come to 
abandon non-virtuous actions and engage in vi rtuous actions. However, due to the many 
different afflictions that arise in  the mind of ordinary beings, th ey create non-virtuous actions. 
These actions need to be purified by means of applying the four opponent forces: the force of 
going for refuge and generating the mind of enli ghtenment, the force of generating regret for 
the negative actions that have been done, the fo rce of resolving not to do them again, and the 
force of applying an actual antidote, such as  reciting mantras, doing prostrations, and so 
forth. By purifying negative actions, one will go higher and higher.   Then on the path common to middling or in termediate beings, one meditates on true 
sufferings. For example, one examines the many different sufferings that are experienced even 
as a human being. Then one looks for the cause, wh ereby one will find that  it is true origins – 
actions and afflictions (karma and klesha). Ha ving understood this, one will apply their 
antidotes by meditating true paths, that is, on emptiness, whereby one will actualize true 
cessations, the cessation of all the afflictions.  When the causes are purified, one no longer 
creates actions to be reborn again in cyclic existence. One will also realize the result – 
liberation or nirvana.  Then on the path of great beings, one contem plates the fact that even if one achieves 
nirvana or freedom from cyclic existence, still all one’s mother sentient beings will continue 
to be tormented in cyclic existe nce. Thereby, one will generate the wish to take care of them 
and thereby enter the Mahayana path. Then, one strives to generate bodhichitta either by 
means of the meditation on the six causes an d one result, which come s down from Buddha 
Shakyamuni to Maitreya, from him to Asanga, and so forth, or by means of the meditation on 
equalizing and exchanging self and others, which comes down from Buddha Shakyamuni to 
M a n j u s h r i ,  f r o m  h i m  t o  N a g a r j u n a ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  T h u s ,  i n  a c c o r d a n c e  w i t h  t h e  l i n e a g e  o f  Asanga one meditates on the six causes and one result, and in accordance with the lineage of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
234 Nagarjuna one meditates on exchanging self and others through thinking of the disadvantages 
of cherishing oneself and the advantages of cherishing others. One should do whichever 
meditation is more comfortable and easier for one’s mind. After that one should receive 
empowerment and enter the path of tantra.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 63) says:  
PART II: 
SMALL SCOPE 
Explanation of the stages of the path common to lesser beings  
2B4B-2B1 The stages of the path common to lesser beings 
A The actual training in the contemplations of lesser beings  
B The gauge of those contemplations having arisen C Clearing up misconceptions about them  
{63} The first one has three points: the ac tual training in the contemplations of 
lesser beings, the gauge of those contempl ations having arisen, and clearing up 
misconceptions about them. 
 
First there is the topic of training in the actu al path of small beings, then the measure of 
having trained in this, and then the clea ring up of misconceptions about this. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A The actual training in the contemplations of lesser beings 
1 Developing a mental attitude of interest in future worlds 
2 Relying on the methods for the happiness of future worlds 
The first of those has two points: developing a mental attitude of interest in future 
worlds and relying on the methods fo r the happiness of future worlds. 
 
We need to develop a mind that is interested in future worlds or future lives and then rely on 
the methods for achieving the happiness of  future worlds or future lives.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1 Developing a mental attitude of interest in future worlds 
A Being mindful of death: the thought that one is not going to stay in this world for long 
B What things will be like in future existences: thinking of the happiness and suffering of the 
two kinds of migrating beings 
The first one has two points: being mindful of death – the thought that one is not 
going to stay in this world for long; and w h a t  t h i n g s  w i l l  b e  l i k e  i n  f u t u r e  
existences – thinking of the happiness an d suffering of the two kinds of migrating 
beings. 
 
We will generate interest in our future lives by  remembering that we will not remain in this 
world for long. Why is this? Because we will s oon die. No matter how much we wish to stay 
alive and do not want to die, still we  will die. One day death will come.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
235  Having thought about this, we next examine what will happen if  we die. We will find that 
we will either go to a happy migration or to a bad migration. However, which one is not. If we 
practice well, we can perhaps be  confident of not being reborn in the lower realms and of 
being reborn in the higher realms, however for the most part we cannot be sure of this. We 
need to think about this.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
Explanation of how to be mindful of death –  
that one will not stay in this world for long 
2B4B-2B1A-1A Being mindful of death – the thought that one is not going to stay in this world for 
long  
1 The disadvantages of not cultivating mindfulness of death 
2 The advantages of cultivating it 
3 What kind of mind that is mindful of death is generated 
4 How to cultivate mindfulness of death  
The first of those has four points: the disadvantages of not cultivating mindfulness 
of death, the advantages of cultivating it ,  w h a t  k i n d  o f  m i n d  t h a t  i s  m i n d f u l  o f  
death is generated, and how to cultivate mindfulness of death.  
 There are disadvantages of not being mindful of or remembering death. On the other hand, there are advantages to remembering death. What  kind of mind that is mindful of death is 
generated? The kind of mind that remember s death is an analytical mind. How is the 
mindfulness of death cultivated? By examinin g the fact that death will definitely come. 
Everyday we need to think of death, how th e elements cease to function, how the body 
gradually becomes unable to move, how the mind slowly becomes unable to remember 
anything, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A1 The disadvantages of not cultivating mindfulness of death  
A Demonstration that the mind’s bias towards the idea that one is not going to die is the gateway of 
harm 
First: Although in all of us there is the thought that at the end of our days death 
will come, every day we think “I will no t die today,” “Today I will also not die.” 
That way, even right until we are about to die, the mind holds on to the idea that 
we are not going to die.  
 
We tend to think, “I will not die today.” However, this kind of mind is harmful to us in that, due to thinking that we will not die today, we  d o  n o t  e n g a g e  i n  t h e  p r a c t i c e  o f  D h a r m a .  
Instead we think, “I am not going to die today.  Since I will live for a long time, I can do it 
later on. Right now I need to engage in worldl y activities, such as business and so forth in 
order to make money and become wealthy.” Thin k i n g  i n  t h i s  w a y ,  s o m e  p e o p l e  e n g a g e  i n  
harmful actions such as stealing, drinking, smok ing, and so forth. Other people do not do 
negative actions but lazily spend much of their time sleeping. These things happen if we do 
not remember death.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
236  Because there are people who live to be more  than 100 years old, we  too might think that 
we will also live that long. The mind that thinks , “I will die when I am 80, or 90, or 100 years 
old,” is a mind that thinks “I will die in the future, but I will not die today.” Then we think 
the same way the next day and then again the ne xt day, so that, even when we are about to 
die, we still think, “I will not die.” However,  this is not the case. For example, sometimes 
someone buys a motorcycle and soon after has an  accident and dies. That person also thought 
that he or she would not die, but suddenly due to  an accident he or she died. Likewise, every 
weekend young people go out to dance in di scotheques and enjoy themselves until early 
morning, but when they are driving home they become sleepy, have a car accident, and die. 
They had only thought to enjoy themselves by singing and dancing, th ey had not thought to 
die yet still they died. Also it happens that sometimes a thief breaks into a house and shoots 
the people in it, due to which they die. Such things happen every day yet still we continue to 
think “I will not die.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B How this prevents us from even taking an interest in future lives 
{64} If you do not take to heart an antido te to this and you are obscured by such 
an idea, and then develop the idea that yo u will remain in this life, you will keep 
thinking about ways to achieve happiness an d eliminate suffering in this life only 
thinking, “I need this and I need that…” As a mind investigating meaningful goals, 
such as future lives, liberation, and omnisc ience, does not arise, no allowances are 
made for engaging in Dharma.  
 
Due the mind thinking that we will not die, we do not think of future li ves. We think only of 
today, “Today I want to be happy. Today I will do this and that.” Due to not thinking of 
death, our mind is completely blocked from thinking of the future.  
 We do not think of and apply the antidote to not remembering death because we are obscured by the thought “I will not die. I will not die.” We hold to th e fact that we will not 
die, due to which we become obscured and think th at we will remain in this life and therefore 
need this and that. We think to do business, wash  the car, take care of our husband, wife, or 
children, and so forth. Some people engage in the business of selling jewels, some in selling 
cars, some in selling clothing, some in selling make-up, some in running beauty salons, and so 
forth. Why do they do this? Because they need money in order to buy a house and so forth as 
without money they will no t be able to do this.  
 I remember a man in Nepal who married a Tibe tan girl. She would go shopping every week 
and would always buy a new pair of shoes. They had a small house but it was full of shoes! In 
just one or two years, she had bought as many as 45 pairs of shoes! Her husband became tired 
of this and escaped to Drepung Monastery in so uth India where he asked one geshe to ordain 
him as a monk. The geshe said that he could not do this because the man was married. The man explained to him that he could not bear being married and told him not only about the 
shoes his wife had bought but also about the ma ny clothes, make-up, and so forth she would 
regularly buy. Prior to his marriage, this same  geshe had told the man to become a monk but 
he had not accepted this and now regretted his mistake. The geshe advi sed him to remain for 
a couple of months, to study Buddhist philosop h y ,  a n d  t h e n  t o  d o  a n  e x a m  i n  o r d e r  t o  
become a monk. Since the man had been a school  teacher he was able to do this and soon 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
237 became a monk. He then studied very well and later on became a lear ned geshe who was also 
a good and humble practitioner. All this happe ned because he had generated some kind of 
renunciation! On the other hand, there are peop le who first generate renunciation and then 
later on change their mind, give up ordination, look for a partner, find one for a while, and 
then change partners over and over again. This is all due to thinking of this life – thinking to 
obtain the happiness of this life and to eliminate the suffering of this life. This does not refer 
to thinking to eliminate the suffering of agin g and so forth, but only  to the suffering of 
poverty and so forth which we want to  eliminate by accumulating wealth.  
 Since we do not even think of our future lives,  there is no need to mention that we do not 
think of liberation and omniscience. We only think of obtaining happiness in this life. Due 
to this, we do not generate the wish to practice  the Dharma in this life. We do not think to 
attain the highest aim of omnisc ience, the middling aim of liberation, or the small aim of a 
higher rebirth in the future. Our mind is unab le to engage in the practice of Dharma, but 
remains completely blocked. For this reason, we  n e e d  t o  t h i n k  t h a t  i f  w e  h a v e  t o  c h o o s e  
between this life and our future lives, the most  important one is our future lives, these being 
many while this life is only one. If we compar e one to many, the majority is more important.  
 Then we need to think that even if we take a higher rebirth in future life it will still be a rebirth in cyclic existence, and therefore gene rate the wish to attain freedom from cyclic 
existence, that is, liberation or nirvana. Altern atively, we should generate the wish to attain 
enlightenment or buddhahood for the benefit of all sentient beings. We need to try to train 
in these attitudes in our everyday life and to increase their stre ngth in our mind.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How this results in meaningful endeavors also having little power 
Even if you engage in study, co ntemplation and meditation for 100 [years], 
whatever virtue you do will have little power as it is done for the sake of this life 
alone. As you will engage in them linked with faulty behavior, negativities, and 
downfalls, it will be rare for them not to be mixed with causes of bad migrations.  
 
Even if we engage in hearing, thinking, or meditating a lot, due to not remembering death 
most of it will be done for the sake of this life . Due to this, whatever virtues we create will not 
be very powerful. Why is this? For example, we m ay study in order to gain qualities so as to be 
able to teach and gain wealth, a high salary, an d so forth. Alternatively, we may meditate in 
order to make other people think that we are a great meditator due to which we will receive 
offerings, be taken care of by others, and not have to do anything else. Although we engage in 
a bit of meditation, the motivation will not be to achieve higher realizations but to collect 
wealth and to be respected. This is not good  and if we create virtues motivated by such 
thoughts, they will not be powerful. These action s will be mixed with or linked to non-virtue 
and faulty conduct. Actions that are a mixture of  virtue and non-virtue, rather than bringing 
rebirth in the higher realms, are more likely to become the cause of rebirth in the lower 
realms. Therefore, whatever we practice, we need  to be careful not to mix virtuous or white 
actions with non-virtuous or black actions. Instea d we should strive to create actions that are 
only white or virtuous, however this is very ra re. Even when we listening to the Dharma, in 
one part of our mind attachment , anger, ignorance, pride, jealousy, resentment, and so forth 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
238 can arise. Our listening can also be mixed with sleep. In short, whatever little virtue we do, we 
should ensure that it is not mixed with non-virtue but remains completely white.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D How this results in long-term goals also being postponed 
Even if you aim at future worlds and allow for their attainment, you will not be 
able to cease the laziness of postponement th at thinks, “I shall do  it later.” A major 
effort at accomplishing it properly will  not come about, as you while away your 
time with sleepiness, pointless chatter, an d the distractions of food, drink, and the 
like.  
 
Due to laziness we think, “I cannot do it today.  I will do it tomorrow .” Then again the next 
day we think the same and one again postpone it till later. This laziness of postponement 
results in us not engaging in Dharma practice. Even if we want to do something for the next 
world or life and try to practice, out of laziness we will continually postpone it till later. Instead we will pass our time in sleep and leth argy, engaging in meaningless talk, enjoying 
eating and drinking, and so forth.  Thus, not only do we waste time  out of laziness, but we also 
pass our time in a state of sleepiness and lethar gy. We also spend our time chattering about 
t hi s  an d t hat  per s o n , w hat  he o r  s he i s  do in g, an d s o  fo r t h. As  w e n eed t o eat  an d dr i nk, 
sometimes we go to a restaurant where we need to  wait even one hour just to get the aperitif! 
Then again no more food comes for a while and time passes in useless talk until the actual 
meal comes. After that we pass more time drin king coffee or a drink and eating a desert. In 
this way, we continually waste our time.  
 Tuesday morning, 8 March 2005 (Happy Women’s Day!)  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 64) says:  
E How holding to permanence induces attachment, aversion, and so forth and how they induce faulty 
behavior 
Not only that, through putting great effort  into the excellences of this life, the 
mental afflictions and the faulty behavior  resulting from them increase and then 
you turn your back on the nectar of  Dharma. Since that leads to the bad 
migrations, what could be more  inappropriate than that?  
 
Due to holding ourselves and others – co mpounded phenomena – to be permanent, 
attachment, hatred, ignorance, an d other mental afflictions are induced. Due to thinking that 
we will not die and holding our body and mind to  be permanent, we generate attachment to 
our relatives, wealth, fame, and so forth; to th ose who interfere with ge tting all the things we 
want, we generate hatred, thinking that they are acting against us; and towards others, we 
generate confusion. This attachment, hatred, an d  s o  f o r t h ,  t h e n  i n d u c e  f a u l t y  b e h a v i o r  o r  
conduct, that is, inappropriate or incorrect behavior.  
 In other words, thinking mostly of ourselves,  we put effort into achieving the happinesses 
and excellences of this life. This then produces  the mental afflictions of attachment and so 
forth, which in turn produce many bad activities  or faulty behavior. Due to their increasing 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
239 more and more, we then turn away from the ne ctar of the Dharma, th e Buddha’s teachings. 
Then what happens? Our bad attitude and faulty ac tivities lead us to the lower realms in our 
next life. Since this would be a mistake and ve ry bad, what could be more unsuitable than 
that?  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
From the Four Hundred Verses :  
 
If someone sleeps as if at ease, 
As if the ruler of the three worlds, The lord of his death did not exist – What else could be more awful? 
 
And in Engaging in [Bodhisattva] Behavior  it says: 
 
Everything has to be abandoned, 
Unaware that this is so, I performed all kinds of negativities, For the sake of friend and foe. 
 
“The three worlds” mentioned above in the Four Hundred Verses  refer to the worlds above, on, 
and under the ground. Due to not thinking about death, we sleep as ease. Although we think 
that we are “the ruler of the three worlds” and that we are very powerful, one day we will fall 
d o w n .  T h e r e f o r e ,  t h i s  k i n d  o f  b e h a v i o r  i s  v e r y  u n s u i t a b l e .  W e  w i l l  d i e  a n d ,  w h e n  w e  d i e ,  
whatever we own or rule, even the three worlds , must be left behind. We also leave behind 
our sons and daughters, our ministers, our friend s, and, going on alone, our mind leaves even 
o u r  b o d y  b e h i n d .  A l t h o u g h  w e  d o  n o t  k n o w  w h e r e  w e  w i l l  b e  r e b o r n ,  i f  w e  c r e a t e d  m a n y  negativities we will definitely only take rebirth without choice in the lower realms.  
 Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  says that unaware of death we  take care of our friends and 
harm those who are not are friends, that is, our foes or enemies. In other words, not 
understanding and not recognizing that we will have to die and that when this happens we 
will have to give everything up and go on alone, we take care of our friends and act against t h o s e  w e  d o  n o t  l i k e  o r  w h o  w e  c o n s i d e r  t o  b e  o u r  e n e m i e s .  I n  t h i s  w a y  w e  c r e a t e  m a n y  negativities. The result of this will be that, wi thout choice, we will go on alone to the lower 
realms.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A2 The advantages of cultivating it 
A The great value 
Second: {65} Once a genuine awareness mindful of death has developed, for 
instance if you are convinced that you wi ll die tomorrow and have just a little 
understanding of the Dharma, you will see th at henceforth you will be without the 
company of relatives and friends and will av ert craving for them. In the majority of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
240 people the wish to take the essence of this  life by means of giving things away and 
the like will naturally arise. In that way, s eeing that all effort for the sake of worldly 
goals such as gain and fame are futile, you will turn away from faulty behavior, 
accumulate the good karma of going for refuge, ethical discipline, and the like. 
What could be more meaningful than asce nding to sublime states oneself by those 
means and guiding migrating beings there too?  That is why it is also praised with 
many analogies.  
 
Having found a precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities, we need to take its 
essence. Thinking that it is very useful and me aningful, we need to put effort into thinking 
about death-impermanence because if we do not re member death it will be difficult for us to 
take its essence. On the other hand, if we re member that we will definitely die today or 
tomorrow, we will naturally strive to gain some  understanding and experience of the Dharma. 
By thinking of the fact that, when we die, we will not be accompanied by our friends and 
relatives, we should give up attachment to them. Even though we might stay together with 
them for a while, one day something will happe n and we will separate or we will die. It 
happens that one day we are close friends with someone and then the very next day we argue with him or her and no longer want to stay to gether, work together, or eat together. Because 
this happens, we should try not to be too attached to our friends and relatives.  
 In addition, if we have accumulated wealth, we  should try to give it away to needy sentient 
beings. If we understand when we die we will no t be able to keep our wealth, effortlessly and 
naturally the thought to give away our money and possessions, as a donation to schools, 
hospitals, temples, and so forth, will arise. We  will spontaneously want to give them away. We 
will also understand that the thought to gain wealth, respect, and so forth is a mundane or 
worldly concern. Does it have any essence? It do es not. Discovering that  such things have no 
essence, it will be easy to turn away from an d eliminate faulty behavior and bad conduct. We 
will also naturally go for refuge to the Th ree Jewels, the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, and 
try to practice morality, as well as generosi ty and patience. Buddha Shakyamuni said that for 
lay people it was most suitable to practice ge nerosity, morality, and patience. Chandrakirti’s 
Supplement to the Middle Way  also says: 
 
The Sugata mainly praise d these three practices  
Of giving, ethics, and patience for householders.  These are also the collection of merit, the cause  Of a buddha body the nature of which is form.    [3.12] 
 Therefore, lay people should put effort into these three perfections. While everyone needs 
effort, including the ordained, the ordained also need to practice the perfections of 
concentration and wisdom. Lay people or householders, on the other hand, do not in general have time to develop concentration and wisdom due to being busy with their everyday lives. 
However, they can practice morality. Morality includes the morality of abandoning the ten 
non-virtuous actions. It also includes the mo rality of keeping vows such as the vows of 
individual liberation – the one-day precepts an d the five precepts of lay men and lay women, 
which include not taking intoxicants.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
241  In addition, lay people should practice gene rosity by giving away whatever they can to 
others, for example, giving food to dogs, cats, birds, and so forth with a good motivation of 
compassion and love. Even giving one morsel of food to another sentient being is the practice 
of generosity. In addition, lay people need patience in their daily life. This is important 
especially with respect to other family members.  Patience is not something outside ourselves, 
rather it is a mind that remains undisturbed when others say or do something against us. 
Thus, it does not mean that externally we a ppear to be patient while inside our mind is 
churning! This is still better than  reacting negatively, but patience  is something that occurs in 
the mind and in the heart. If we try not to disturb others, our own mind will not be 
disturbed. We need to develop respect for an d patience toward the members of our family. 
For example, when our husband or wife is a bi t naughty we need patience. When there is a 
lack of patience, a couple will eventually separa te. Since we tend to separate again and again, 
it is better to stay with our first partner and instead practice patience. 
 By collecting good actions through practicing  morality and so forth, we will climb to a 
higher state. In addition, we will also be able to lead other migrating beings in the path, that 
is, in the understanding that worldly activities  are essenceless and that they too need to 
practice the Dharma by way of generosity, mora lity, and patience. By doing so, we will render 
our precious human rebirth very meaningful. Such  actions are praised with many examples or 
analogies.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In the Parinirvana [Sutra]  it says: 
 
Among all plowing of fields, that in autumn is the best. Among all 
footprints, that of the elephant is the best. Among all discriminations, 
those of impermanence and death are the best: they eliminate all the 
desire, ignorance, and pride of the three realms. 
 
Similarly, it is praised, among other things, as being a hammer that instantly 
destroys all mental afflictions and all faulty  behaviors as well as being a gate to the 
simultaneous accomplishment of all that is virtuous and excellent. 
 With respect to plowing fields, the best is to  plow them in the autumn because when seeds 
are later sown in that field they will grow well. The best of footprints is  that of the elephant 
since it is bigger than other animals. Am ong all discriminations, the best are the 
discriminations of impermanence and death. These discriminations eliminate the desire, 
ignorance, and pride of the three realms – the desire realm, form realm, and formless realm. 
They eliminate desire because, through seeing th at we will die, we will no longer be attached 
to the good qualities of this world. Instead we will understand that they only bring a deceptive 
happiness and not final or absolute happiness.   There are different types of ignorance, for example, the ignorance not knowing thusness, 
selflessness, or emptiness and the ignorance not knowing actions and results. In this context 
the ignorance not knowing actions and results is the main ignorance that is eliminated by the 
discriminations of death and impermanence. The la w of actions and results refers to the fact 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
242 that a certain kind of action brings a certain ki nd of result. This is a natural law that was not 
made by any nation or country.  
 The discriminations of death and impermanence also eliminate the pride that causes us to 
be swollen or puffed up with ourselves. Thus, the discriminations of death and 
impermanence eliminate these three mental afflictions.  
 Impermanence refers to the fact that a ll phenomena change momentarily and do not 
abide. Instead they are like a flowing river that never stops. For example,  with respect to this 
year 2005, the first and second months have al ready finished and we have now arrived at the 
third month, which every day, every hour, every minute becomes less and less. Our life passes in this way but we do not think about or recognize this reality. Life goes by very quickly. Even 
if we have a lifespan of 100 years, everyday it lessens. Thus, there is impermanence in general, 
as well as the specific impermanence of death,  both our own and others. If we examine this 
well, we will see that everyday many people die du e to the many conditions that take our life – 
being killed, sickness, a heart attack, car accide nts, a building collapsi ng, and so forth. We 
should think about this.   If we think about death-impermanence, th e afflictions and faulty conduct will be 
spontaneously destroyed as if by  a hammer. Simultaneously, the d oor to all virtuous actions – 
going for refuge, practicing generosity, morali ty, patience, and so fo rth – will be opened.  
 Middle Lam-Rim says:  
B It is the door to all excellence 
In brief, the time for achieving the goals of beings is only this time of having 
gained a special basis. We mostly remain in  bad migrations and even if we make it 
to the happy migrations just once, they  are predominantly places of unfreedom, 
due to which we do not find the opport unity to accomplish the Dharma. If we 
gain a basis suited to accomplishing the teachings and still do not accomplish the 
Dharma properly, it is due to  this thought of “I’m not go ing to die yet.” Therefore, 
this mind that holds to the position of no t dying {66} is the door to all manner of 
degeneration and mindfulness of death is the door to all excellence.  
 The discrimination of death-impermanence an d the going for refuge are the door to all 
excellence. In brief, we can accomplish all the aims of beings at this time when we have 
achieved this human body, a special basis. Alth ough we have had beginningless lives, in most 
of them we were reborn in the lower realms and then remained there for a long time. We 
only achieved a happy realm, for example, as a human being, every once in a while, and then 
once again we returned to the lower realms. Now that we have a human rebirth we are still busy with worldly activities, do not have much ti me to practice the Dharma, and it is difficult 
for us to practice it. Why is this? It is beca use in our world everyone is busy and there are 
always many things to do. Although we coul d practice the Dharma well with this body, 
because we have no time and are busy we do not find time to meditate, recite prayers ,and so 
forth. We should check why this happens.   Some people, having gained such a special body, try to practice but are unable to do it perfectly and correctly. For example, having taken an empowerment and received commitments such as the commitment to do the S i x  S e s s i o n  G u r u  Y o g a ,  t h e y  d o  i t  v e r y  
quickly while the mind is distracted to other th ings such as preparing dinner. Therefore, even 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
243 when we practice, we do not do it very well or very perfectly. Why is this? It all stems from the 
root that is the thought, “I am not going to die yet.” We hold ourselves to be permanent and 
this grasping to not dying is the cause for all g ood things to degenerate. However, even if we 
have not practiced the Dharma we will still die. Th erefore, we need to try to think, “I will die, 
maybe today, maybe tomorrow.” We should meditate on death-impermanence and 
continually remember death as this is the door to all excellence. This is because if we are 
worried about dying tomorrow, if we feel the re ality of death-impermanence, we will put effort 
into our practice of the Dharma. This will lead to virtuous actions, which are the cause of all 
excellences and perfections.   Hence, we should try in everyday life, as much as possible, to do some analytical 
meditation on whether or not we  will die. We will definitely die. When? We do not know. 
What can help us at the time of death? If we examine this we will find that only our Dharma practice will be of help at the time of death, nothing else can help us. Therefore, every day we 
should try to do a bit of meditation on de ath-impermanence. By familiarizing with this 
thought, we will engage in the practice of the Dharma. Thus, meditation on death-
impermanence is a good method for inducing us to practice the Dharma.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How it is necessary to give up misunderstandings and meditate from the bottom of one’s heart  
Therefore, you should not think: “This is  a practice for those who do not have 
other profound Dharma on which to medita te” and “You should meditate on this 
object of meditation but only a bit in the beginning, it is unsuitable to practice it 
continually.” Rather you should meditate upon it, until you gain certainty from 
the bottom of your heart that it is necessary in the beginn ing, in the middle, and at 
the end.  
 
We need to abandon misunderstandings or mi staken conceptions regarding meditation on 
death and then meditate on it from the depths  of our heart. For ex ample, we should not 
think that meditation on death-impermanence is only for those who do not meditate on a 
more profound Dharma; this would be a mistake. Nor is it correct that it is an object of 
meditation for a bit in the beginning, but then la ter on there is no need to meditate on death-
impermanence. Instead we need to meditate on  death-impermanence at the beginning, in the 
middle, and at the end. We should try to gain  certainty from the bo ttom of our heart that 
such meditation is necessary at all times. We ca n do it at the beginning , in the middle, and at 
the end of the day, or at the beginning, in the middle, and at the end of our Dharma practice. 
In short, everyday we should try to meditate a bit on death-impermanence as this will induce 
going for refuge, the practice of morality, and so forth. It will also induce an understanding of 
the four noble truths and meditation on the Mahayana path, for exampl e, the recognition of 
all sentient beings as having been our mother, their kindness, the need to repay this kindness, 
love, compassion, the extraordinary thought, and the mind of enlightenment. Everyday we 
need to do a brief glance meditation or brie f review meditation by means of analytical 
meditation, not placement meditation, on a ll the points of the stages of the path. 
 
Tuesday afternoon, 8 March 2005
