Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
244 Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 66) says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A3 What kind of mind that is mindful of death is generated 
Third: The fear born from wo rries about separation from relatives etc. is the kind 
of fear of death of those who are not trai ned in the path. Therefore, here this is 
not was it to be generated. Well then, what [kind of fear is to be generated ]? All 
bodies that are taken under the power of karma and mental afflictions do not pass 
beyond death, which is why – even though this may give rise to fear – it cannot be 
prevented for the time being. However,  without blocking the causes for bad 
migrations and without accomplishing th e causes for high status and certain 
goodness, you will fear death. With respect to that, if you think about the resulting 
fear, this can be accomplished, whereby yo u will not be afraid on the verge of 
dying. If you do not achieve this object ive, through fear of not being liberated 
from the cyclic existence in general and of falling into the bad migrations in 
particular you will be tormented by remorse at the time of death.  
 
The fear of being separated from relatives, friend s, wealth, and so forth at the time of death is 
the kind of fear that is generated by those who have not trained in the path. However, this is 
not the fear that we should generate in this context. Why is that? Because we are people who 
have trained in the path. On the other hand, if we were dying right now and feared separation from our relatives, friends, wealth, and our belongings, it would show that we have 
not trained in the stages of th e  p a t h .  S o  w h a t  k i n d  o f  f e a r  s h o u l d  w e  g e n e r a t e ?  W e  t a k e  
rebirth by the force of afflictions and contamin ated actions. Whatever kind of rebirth we 
take, whether as a human being, anti-god, god, animal, and so forth, we will definitely die. 
We cannot escape death. Therefore, if we fear death but cannot yet avoid it, what do we need 
to do? We need to try to stop creating the caus es for taking rebirth in the lower realms. If we 
have a precious human rebirth but do not use it to create the causes of higher rebirth in the 
future, then when we die we will necessarily ex perience fear. What can we do to avoid taking 
rebirth in the lower realms? We can practice  virtuous dharmas and abandon non-virtuous 
dharmas, whereby we will no longer need to worry about being reborn in the lower realms. 
Thus, we should practice this no w so as to avoid experiencing fear at the time of death. As 
long as we are not free from cyclic existence we can fall into the lower realms. If we do not 
practice Dharma henceforth until we die, at the time of death we will regret not having done 
this and will experience the fear of taking rebirt h in the lower realms. However, at that time it 
will be too late. Therefore, we should not do  t h i s .  W h a t  s h o u l d  w e  d o ?  W e  s h o u l d  t r y  t o  
practice well, due to which we can be free from the worry of falling into the lower realms.   In addition, by practicing the path of middl ing beings, we can even achieve liberation or 
nirvana due to which there will be no need for any more fear. In short, due to generating a 
strong determination to practice Dharma, we ca n avoid taking rebirth in the lower realms and 
take rebirth in the higher realms. In addition, we can practice and achieve high status or nirvana or even complete enlightenment or  buddhahood. Now we can practice: we are 
healthy, we are intelligent, all our faculties are complete, and we have all the necessary conditions. Therefore, right now we need to practice the Dharma. Therefore, we should 
develop the mind of renunciation or definite  emergence that thinks to become free from 
cyclic existence and strives to attain the state of liberation. To do this, we need to abandon 
creating non-virtuous actions.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
245  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A4 How to cultivate mindfulness of death 
A It is certain that we will die 
B It is uncertain when we will die  
C At the time of death nothing except for the Dharma will be of benefit 
Fourth: You should meditate on this by way of the three root  thoughts, the nine 
reasons, and the three resolutions. In this  regard, the three [root thoughts] are: (1) 
it is certain that we will die, (2) it is uncertain when we will die, and (3) at the time 
of death nothing except for the Dharma will be of benefit. 
2B4B-2B1A-1A4A It is certain that we will die 
1 Actual reasons 
2 Resolution 
2B4B-2B1A-1A4A-1 Actual reasons 
A The Lord of Death will certainly come and there are no conditions for warding him off  
B One’s lifespan cannot be increased and diminishes incessantly 
C You die without having had time to practice Dharma while alive  
The first one has three points: (1) the Lord  of Death will certainly come and there 
are no conditions for warding him off, (2 ) one’s lifespan cannot be increased and 
diminishes incessantly and (3) you die without having had time to practice 
Dharma while alive {67}.  
A The Lord of Death will certainly come and there are no conditions for warding him off 
First: In the Udanavarga  it says that whatever body you have taken, whatever 
country you reside in, and whatever the time , you will be defeated by death. It will 
come to pass that way and cannot be turn ed back by fleeing swiftly away or by 
force, wealth, material substances, mantra s, or medicines, as it says in [the] Advice 
to a King [Sutra] . Kamaba said: “Now some fear of death is necessary. At the 
moment of death you must not be appreh ensive. We do it the other way round: 
now we are not afraid [but] in the moment of death we dig our fingernails into our 
chests.” 
 
There are three roots, nine reasons, and thr ee resolutions or determinations to make. The 
first of the root thoughts is that death is defi nite, that is, we will definitely die. This root 
thought is further divided into three reasons.  (1) Even powerful people, kings, teachers, 
politicians, famous singers and writers, and so forth, will die; there is no one who does not 
die. There are many conditions that take our li fe. We cannot ward off or turn away death. (2) 
Even if our lifespan is 100 years, nothing can be  added to it, instead it continually diminishes 
day after day. (3) Even if we want to practice  the Dharma but think th at right now we do not 
have time, still we will die. Therefore, the conclusi on is that we will definitely die. We need to 
meditate on this.   How can we turn away the conditions for de ath? We cannot. Our body can become sick, 
for example, due to the fact that our world is very polluted by chemicals that can cause us 
cancer and so forth. This pollution becomes a condition that takes our life. There are also 
some conditions for life that transform into co nditions for death. We cannot add anything to 
our life, everyday our life becomes shorter and shorter. In one day and night, each minute our 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
246 lifespan becomes shorter and shorter. One day finishes quickly, then one week finishes 
quickly, and then one month finishes quickly. In  this way time goes quickly and our lifespan 
finishes. We cannot do anything to increase it.  
 Because we are presently busy, we think that  while we cannot practi ce the Dharma today, 
we will do it later on. However, thinking like this, one day death will arrive and we will no 
longer be able to practice the Dharma. Th inking about this, we need to make the 
determination: “I will practice the Dharma. I will really practice the Dharma. Everyday I will 
practice the Dharma.”  
 Whether we take a human body, anti-god body, god body, animal body, hungry ghost b o d y ,  o r  h e l l  b o d y ,  w e  w i l l  d i e .  T h e r e  i s  n o  b o d y  t h a t  o n c e  o b t a i n e d  w i l l  n o t  d i e .  A l s o  wherever we reside, there is no place in whic h we will not die. Wherever we go, under the 
ground, inside a mountain, on top of a mountain , up into space, to other planets or to the 
moon, there is no place where we will not die. Wherever we go we will die. There is no one 
who can say, “I will never die.” Everyone will di e when the time arrives. Therefore, we cannot 
escape death. It will come. Even if we swiftly run away from it, we will not be able to escape 
death. Even if we are very powerful, we will not be able to avoid death. Even if we are very rich, we will not be able to pay death to no t come. Perhaps if we are rich, we can pay a 
hospital a lot of money whereby we can postpo ne death for a bit longer, but still one day 
death will come. Sometimes it happens that do ctors make a mistake and the patient dies 
during surgery. Sometimes they even leave some  instrument or a piece of cotton gauze inside 
the patient’s body, due to which he or she dies . Even though they do their best, the patient 
becomes worst and then dies.  When we take medicine, sometimes it can cure  the body of sickness but it cannot eliminate 
death. There is also no substance or  mantra that can turn death away.  
 Kamaba said that it is necessa ry to generate the fear of deat h now. Due to fearing death, we 
will engage in practice whereby at the time of death we will be free of fear and worry. We will 
be able to die happily, like someone who return s home after having visited another country. 
Thinking that we have practiced well, we can go to our next life free of  worries. Thus, we need 
to think about death now. On the other hand, if  we do not worry about or fear death while 
we are alive, at the time of death we will tear our chest with our fingerna ils. However, then it 
will be too late to do anything. There are peop le who at the time of death experience great 
fear and call out for help, wailing in fear, but no  one is able to help them. At the time of 
death some people say “Take this weight off me. I am sinking under the earth,” this shows 
that after death they will be born in the hell or hungry ghost realms. When others experience 
cold and beg to be covered with more blankets, this is a sign that af ter death they will be 
reborn in the cold hell. When others experience great heat an d ask for the blankets to be 
removed, this is a sign that af ter death they will be reborn in  the hot hells. These experiences 
at the time of death show that  the person did not practice we ll during his or her life by 
engaging in virtue so as to be reborn in the higher realms, but instead continually engaged in 
non-virtuous actions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B One’s lifespan cannot be increased and diminishes incessantly 
Second: As it says in [the Sutra of] Entering the Womb , attaining a lifespan of a 
hundred years seems to be the limit of what  is possible. However, even if you reach 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
247 that, meanwhile, the years until then wa ste away by the passage of month after 
month, day after day, and day and night. As they too waste away with the passage 
of morning and so on, it seems that the lifespan in general has already degenerated 
from before and that the remainder also diminishes incessantly without anything 
being added.  
 
The Sutra of Nanda’s Entering the Womb  says that very few human beings have a lifespan of 100 
years. However, even if we have such a life span, still one year passes away month by month, 
one month passes away day by day, and one day passes away in a day and night. Then in one 
day there is morning, then afternoon, then ev ening, then night, and once again another 
morning. Like this time passes quickly and our lifespan, even 100 years, is quickly exhausted. 
While our life is quickly exhausted, we cannot add any time to it. In this way our life quickly, 
quickly, quickly, quickly finish es. What can we do? We cannot do  anything about it. It will 
finish. This is what is said here.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In Engaging in [Bodhisattva] Behavior  it says: 
 
This life decreases constantly, 
Day and night, without respite, If from nowhere there is increase, Either, how could I not die? 
 Day and night without staying still, our life span constantly diminishes. Nor can we add 
anything to it. If, on the other hand, our lifespan decreased but we could add some time to it, things would be fine, but we cannot. However, th ere are some exceptions to this general rule 
as by means of certain long-life tantra practices, such as the recitation of a long-life mantra, we 
can eliminate one particular cause of death due to which we can remain alive longer. 
However, this does not imply that we can add ti me to our lifespan. Every day it continues to 
decrease. Therefore, how could we not die? We will die!  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Furthermore, you should consider this by way of numerous analogies such as a weaver weaving cloth, [animals] to be slaughtered approaching the slaughter place 
with each step, cattle being lead without any control to their place by a herdsman, 
and so forth. This is also taught in [the Sutra of] Extensive Display  by means of many 
analogies: 
 
The three existences are impermanent like autumn clouds; The birth and death of migrating beings resemble a show, The passage of life of migrating beings is like a flash of lightning in the sky; It passes fast like a waterfall from a steep mountain. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
248  
Just as when a weaver weaves his cloth quickly,  he will soon finish his work, so too does our 
life quickly come to an end. Like animals being brought to the slaughter place or like 
prisoners with a life sentence who are told to s ay their last words to their family and then put 
in an electric chair or laid on a bed and inje cted with a lethal drug, we too are approaching 
death. Similarly, just as a herdsman puts his ca ttle without their control in a stall at night and 
closes the door behind them, so too do we not have the power to escape death.  
 The three existences are as impermanent as an autumn cloud, which is rare and quickly 
disappears. The birth and death of sentient beings  is like a show or a play in which an actor 
puts on one mask after the other, disguises hims elf as a woman, and so forth, in that we too 
take different rebirths one after the other. The li fe of migrating beings is quickly over, like a 
flash of lightening in the sky. It also passes as quickly as a waterfall falling down the side of a 
steep mountain. It is also like a flash flood that  occurs when there has been a lot of snow and 
rain and that destroy roads, houses, and so forth.  Our life ends as quickly as this. Therefore, 
we should think, “I will not remain long. I cannot escape death. Wherever I go, I will die,” 
and develop a strong determination in our mind to practice the Dharma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 For someone with the resolve of inner co ntemplation {68} there are no external 
objects that do not point out impermanence to him. Since this is taught, certainty 
arises if you consider it often from many  angles. There is no benefit in thinking 
one has [considered] something several ti mes, but still no certainty arose. As 
Kamaba said: “You say nothing has come about through contemplation, but when 
did you contemplate? During the day you indulge in distractions, at night you 
sleep – don’t lie!”  
 
All functioning things teach impermanence, however we do not understand this. Our mind remains very tight and we continue to think “I  will not die.” We hold to our permanence. 
However, external things show us impermanence , for example, when the wind blows, leaves 
fall from the trees and blow away, buildings are de stroyed, and so forth. We also see that if we 
paint a wall outside white, even after just a few days it will once again be dirty due to wind and rain. Also if a man shaves in the morning, again the next there will be hair on his chin. 
These are all signs of impermanence, that things change.   Some people, like the Italian prime minister Silvio Berlusconi, lose more and more hair 
every day and think to undergo a hair transplant . People who have money pay a lot to do this, 
however it rarely works. We cannot make ours elves younger. Even though we may undergo 
cosmetic surgery to remove our wrinkles and afte rward we may in fact look younger, in reality 
we are not any younger! Other people use variou s types of creams, trying to make themselves 
look younger, but in reality we cannot become  younger, instead everyday we get older. We 
cannot escape from aging, nor can we turn it away. It is a sign showing us the reality of 
impermanence.   We should check this subject from differe nt points of view, whereby we will gain 
understanding and certainty that death is definite.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
249  Kamaba says that if we say that nothing ha s happened even though we have contemplated 
death-impermanence, when did we actually cont emplate it? During the day we are distracted 
and at night we sleep, therefore we should not lie about having done something that we have 
not done.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 It is not just at the end of our lives when death overcomes us that we proceed 
towards a another world, for up until th en there is not a moment in which our 
lifespan does not diminish. Therefore, ev er since we entered the womb, we have 
been going straight towards a future life without remaining still even for a mere 
instant. In the meantime life also wastes  away as we go towards death led by the 
messengers of disease and aging. Therefore,  do not rejoice in the view that you 
abide during your life without approaching fu ture worlds. It is li ke the analogy that 
at the time of having fallen from a high  mountain peak, the occasion of falling 
through the sky before crashing to the grou n d  i s  n o t  w o r t h y  o f  j o y .  A l s o  i n  t h e  
Commentary on the Four Hundred Verses  it says:  
 
Hero of men, in that night at the very beginning, When someone comes to dwell in a womb in the world – Starting from that point he moves with each day, 
Without the pause of a step, towards the Lord of Death. 
 At the end of our life, the Lord of Death will come and take away our very life. At this time 
we will go on to another life. Therefore, we no w need to think about the fact that our lifespan 
quickly diminishes, that it does not remain immu table. In fact, from the very moment that we 
are conceived in our mother’s womb, our life begins to diminish. In the first instant our 
mind, which is that of an intermediate state be ing, enters the mixture of red and white fluids 
of our parents and we are conceived. Then, in  the second instant, the liquids begin to 
coagulate a little bit and become more curd-like,  then the embryo gradually forms, the head 
and limbs begin to develop, and so forth. Du e to the movement of different inner winds, 
development in the fetus occurs until it becomes a fully developed baby with all its organs 
complete at about thirty-six or thirty-seven weeks of gestation. At that time the baby begins to 
experience the womb as a dirty and smelly plac e, narrow and dark, in which it is not possible 
to move its arms and legs, due to which it de velops the wish to leave the womb. The baby 
then turns upside down and is born. At this time  it is normal to say, “Today a child has been 
born,” whereas according to the Buddhist texts the instant of conception corresponds to the 
time of birth. Sometimes the baby only remain s for a few weeks and then dies, sometimes it 
dies when it is almost ready to be born, someti mes it dies just after birth, and sometimes it 
dies as a small child. Therefore,  the time of death is not cert ain and from the second instant 
onward in the womb we approa ch death. Therefore, we need to think a lot about death-
impermanence. Since death will definitely come , we should develop a very strong wish to 
practice the Dharma and make the decisi on: “I will practice the Dharma.”  
 We like to think that we will remain for a long  time, but it is not like this. Our life is not 
stable, rather it constantly changes. For exampl e, while a white lotus fl ower is very beautiful 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
250 when it first blossoms, after but a few days it wi thers and dies due to wind, rain, and so forth. 
We should think of such analogies regarding th e reality of impermanence. All the things in 
our world, the beautiful flowers, the snow mounta ins, and so forth, chan ge due to causes and 
conditions. Therefore, we must contemplate impermanence. We should also meditate on 
impermanence, thinking that no thing except empty space is permanent. While empty space 
remains the same, everything else changes. Howe ver, empty space allows us to move, to build 
things, and so forth. Without empty space we co uld not do this. In short, functioning things 
change constantly and quickly. Our lifespan is like a play in which there is a king, queen, and 
so forth but after it is over there is no longer a king, queen, and so forth.  
 We should constantly strive to remember de ath by thinking again and again “I will die.” 
Even if we live for 100 years, death will still de finitely come. Until then we do not know when 
death will come. It is uncertain. Also we cannot say that we will not die because at the 
moment we are healthy. We may go to sleep tonight and tomorrow people will refer to us 
saying, “Oh, he/she died last night due to a he art attack.” On the evening before he died a 
well-known Italian journalist was seen by millions  of people on a talk-show, but the very next 
morning they were saying about him “He died la st night.” Even those wh o think that they are 
powerful and will not die, still die. One lama even died while he was sitting on a throne 
giving teachings. We do not know when we will die.  
 I knew a geshe from Sera Monastery who was not very old, perhaps 56 or 57, who wanted 
to come to Italy, but one day while out for a wa lk, slipped, hit his head, and died before he 
arrived at a hospital that was only two or th ree kilometers away. Some people have been 
known to do gymnastics in the bathroom, slip and break their spine, and die. Such things 
happen everywhere. When impermanence arrives, no one can escape. We cannot say, “Death 
will not happen to me today or tomorrow.” Theref ore, we must try to practice Dharma. Only 
this can help us.  
 While we constantly approaching death and our future life, we do not think about or 
remember this. Instead we think that we are go ing to the market, the doctor, and so forth, 
forgetting that we are also going toward death.  We should think that our days and nights will 
quickly finish and that death will come to us.   Wednesday morning, 9 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C You die without having had time to practice Dharma while alive.  
Third: Even if you can live for that long, it is inappropriate to  think you have time. 
As is stated in [the Sutra on] Entering the Womb , initially, during the first ten years of 
childhood, it does not occur to you to practice the Dharma, and at the end, during 
the 20 years of old age, you do not have the power to practice  the Dharma. Of the 
time in between, sleep having snatched aw ay one half and illness and the like also 
having wasted much time, no more than a few opportunities remain to accomplish 
the Dharma.  
 
The Sutra on Entering the Womb  says that even if we live for 100 years, not much of it will be 
spent in practicing the Dharma. For the first ten years of our life, we are too young to develop 
the wish to practice Dharma. Be cause small children are not ab le to understand why and how

Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
251 to practice the Dharma, they do not do it. At the end of our life , for the last twenty years of 
our old age, we are also unable to practice the Dharma. Between the age of ten and eighty 
w h a t  d o  w e  d o ?  I n  e a c h  t w e n t y - f o u r  h o u r  p e r i o d ,  w e  s l e e p  a b o u t  e i g h t  h o u r s .  I n  a d d i t i o n ,  
sometimes we are sick and unable to practice. In this way time passes and the occasions in 
which we practice Dharma are short and far be tween. If we examine our day to day life, we 
will see that at night we sleep, then when we wake up, we spend time washing our face and so

weather, or in the summertime go to the seasid e, relax for a while, go home, and again go to 
sleep. In this way the time in which to practi ce Dharma becomes very short. Also sometimes 
we are so busy that we forget to practice the Dharma. Then when we are sick, we need to go 
to the hospital and so forth. Thus, there are many conditions that take time away from our 
Dharma practice. In fact, from the point of view of Dharma a lot of our time goes to waste. In 
this way, time becomes very short and our life fi nishes. On top of this, even when we practice 
the Dharma, we do not do it very well! Therefor e, we need to make the decision to practice 
Dharma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A4A-2 The decision to practice the Dharma 
{69} Since it is like this, at the time of de ath all the excellences of this life will only 
be an object of recollection – like the recollection of a happy experience in a 
dream after you have woken up. Why would you be delighted by being beguiled by 
this life, if the enemy who is death will ce rtainly come? With this thought in mind 
you should make the decision that you defi nitely need to practice the Dharma and 
reiterate this innermost feeling109 many times. From the Jataka Stories :  
 
Alas, afflicted worldly beings, 
Your stay is not stable, there is no joy; Even this water lily’s splendor Will soon be but a memory. 
 We mostly think of the excellences of this life, wealth and so forth, du e to wanting to have a 
happy life. Even in our dreams we want to be happy. We recount to others the happiness we experienced in a dream, even though it is just a dream. We do not think of the time of death 
and thinking that we will not die, we think of  the things of this life, such as wealth, 
possessions, money, cars, and so forth. We want to have many things, but to obtain them we 
first need to collect money, therefore we thin k only of this. While the enemy who is death 
will definitely come, we do not think of this an d only think of the things of this life. Then, 
even if we wish to practice Dharma and make th e decision to do so, still we will not do it.  
 The Jataka Stories  say that worldly beings whose minds are filled with afflictions are not 
stable due to which they are unable to be happy.  Even if they are happy for a day or two, once 
again they experience mental unhappiness. A kumu da flower, a white lotus flower, is very nice 
while it lasts, but it soon wilts.  Also the flowers on this table ar e very nice but if we were to

FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
252 put them outside for even one hour in the cold and wind, they would soon dry up and 
become ugly. Our life is like this, it quickly finishes like falling leaves.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A4B The time of one’s death is uncertain  
1 The need to generate a mental state mindful of death every day 
[2 The actual reasons 
3 The decision that from now on you need to practice the Dharma]  
2B4B-2B1A-1A4B-1 The need to generate a mental state mindful of death every day 
Second: It is certain that death will come between now and 100 years [from now] 
and it is not certain on which day in be tween it will come. Therefore, it is not 
definite on any day like today whether yo u will die or not. Nonetheless, the mind 
siding to the idea of death, you must gene rate the awareness that thinks “I will die 
today,” for if the mind sides with the idea of not dying with the thought “I will not 
die today,” you will constantly prepare for staying in this life and will not prepare 
for future lives. Due to this, if you then  get seized by the Lord of Death in the 
meantime, you will have to die in anguish. However, if you prepare to die every 
day, you will accomplish a lot that is mean ingful for the future, so that even if you 
do not die it is good to have done it, and if you die, it was all the more necessary.  
 We need to think about the fact that death w i l l  r e a l l y  h a p p e n .  W e  s h o u l d  g e n e r a t e  t h e  
awareness of death-impermanence every day by thinking, “Before the next 100 years pass by, 
death will definitely come to me. When will it ha ppen? I do not know.” Perhaps we will die at 
the age of 99, 98, 97 , 96, 95 and so forth or perhaps we wi ll die tomorrow, or even tonight, or 
even this afternoon. Due to there being many ci rcumstances that can ta ke our life, we could 
even die today. Therefore, we do not know wh en death will come, but definitely we will die 
before the next 100 pass by. We  cannot decide “Today I will not die.” Why? Because when 
the conditions arise, we will die. It can happen that when walking along the street that we are 
hit by a crazy driver in a car and die. Therefore,  we do not know when we will die. Because of 
this, we need to think, “I will die. Perhaps I will even die today.”   Death is suffering because at death the conne ction between the mind and body ceases. Our 
family members and friends who cry out of sadne ss at out death also suffer, even though this 
is of absolutely no benefit. Hence, we need to think, “Since I will definitely die, I need to  practice the Dharma. My practice  of the Dharma can help me when I am dying. Nothing else, 
no condition whatsoever, nothing at all, can help me at the time of death.  
 On the other hand, if we continue to think “Today we will not die,” we will be constantly 
occupied with making preparations to obtain th e happiness of this life and will not practice 
the Dharma. Only our practice of the Dharma is a preparation for our future lives, collecting 
money, wealth, and so forth, which is only for the sake of this life, are not. Therefore, we 
need to make preparations for our future life by  practicing the Dharma. Even if we think that 
we will not die and do not make preparations for our future life, one day the Lord of Death 
will arrive, whereupon we will experience sadne ss and regret. This itself becomes a cause for a 
bad rebirth. While we have imprints of both vi rtuous and non-virtuous minds, if we die with 
sadness, worry, and so on, or with attachment for our body, belongings, relatives, and so on, 
this will cause the imprint of a non-virtuous action  to ripen. This will cause us to take rebirth 
in the next life in the hell realm, hungry ghos t realm, or animal realm. Which of these three 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
253 is not sure, but due to the ripening of a non-virt uous action we will definitely be reborn in 
one of them.  
 Therefore, everyday we need to think “Today  I will die,” and make preparations for our 
future lives. In this way, we will achieve our aim of happiness in future lives. When we die, 
things are not finished. We just give up this body and take another one. We need to make 
sure that we take a good body, for example a human body possessing the eighteen qualities. To do this, we must practice the Dharma. This means that we must meditate everyday on the 
lam-rim, the stages of the path , by meditating on death-impermanence or any of the other 
lam-rim meditations. First we should do analytic al meditation and then from time to time do 
single-pointed or placement meditation while trying to keep our mind fixed on the object.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A4B-2 The actual reasons 
A There is no certainty with regard to the lifespan in Jambudvipa, which is why the time of 
death is uncertain  
B There are many conditions that lead to death and few conditions that promote life  
C The body is extremely vulnerable and the time of death therefore uncertain  
This has three points: (1) there is no ce rtainty with regard to the lifespan in 
Jambudvipa, which is why the time of de ath is uncertain, (2) there are many 
conditions that lead to death and few that promote life, and (3) the body is 
extremely vulnerable and the time of death therefore uncertain. 
A There is no certainty with regard to the lifespan in Jambudvipa which is why the time of 
death is uncertain 
First, in general the lifespan  of the beings of Uttarakuru110 is set, and for most 
beings of the other [continents ] there is more certainty, even though it may not be 
s u r e  t h a t  t h e  f u l l  e x t e n t  o f  t h e  l i f e s p a n  o f  e a c h  i n d i v i d u a l  b e  r e a c h e d .  F o r  t h e  
beings of Jambudvipa the lifespan is ex tremely uncertain. In the beginning, they 
even reached immeasurable years, {70} afterward there is a maximum duration of 
ten years and even nowadays it does not s eem certain whether they die in their old 
age, in their youth, or in the middle. In the Treasury  it says:  
 
Here it is uncertain: in the end, 
Ten years; at first, immeasurable.111 
 
Through taking to heart the fact that your  gurus or friends and so forth have died 
through sudden internal or external condit ions for death before reaching the end 
of their lives, you should think over and ov er again, “I too have a similar quality.” 
 The lifespan of human beings in Jambudvipa is  uncertain, we can die at any age. This is 
because there are many conditions that can take  our life, and few condit ions that support our 
life. Then, even among the condit ions that usually sustain life, there are those that at times

254 transform into the cause of death. Our body appear s to be strong, but it is not, rather it is 
very fragile. In fact it is said to be simila r to a water bubble, which is extremely fragile.  
 On the other hand, the human beings of the northern continent, Unpleasant Sound 
(Uttarakuru), have a fixed lifespan of 1000 years. They cannot die before reaching that age 
and so will definitely live for 1000 years. Whil e the human beings of the western and eastern 
continents have a long lifespan, its duration is  not completely fixed. However, for the most 
part, they can be certain not to die before their lifespan is exhausted. Contrarily, the lifespan 
of the human beings of Jambudvipa, the southe rn continent, is very uncertain. At the 
beginning of the eon, their lifespan was immeas urable, after which it gradually decreases until 
their maximum lifespan will be ten years. Thus, at  that point many of them will not even live 
to the age of 10.  
 When Buddha Shakyamuni came to this wo rld, the lifespan of human beings was 100 
years. Perhaps it is somewhat less now but we ca n still say that it is 100 years, even though 
very few people actually reach 100 and most di e before. Some even die shortly after they are 
conceived in the womb, others die after five or  six months in the womb, and some die while 
they are still a small child. Therefore, our life span is very uncertain. While the grandparents, 
parents, uncles, and aunts remain alive, a youn g child can die. This is because the time of 
death is not certain in Jambudvipa. We need to contemplate this fact.   It is said that there are five periods of development in the womb, each of which has a 
particular name, and after birth there are furt her divisions in relation to the periods of 
infancy and so forth. In short, at the be ginning of the eon, human beings had an 
immeasurable lifespan and at the end they will only live for a maximum of ten years. We see 
that, due to outer and inner conditions for deat h, our teachers and friends die before their 
life is complete. For example, our guru, Lama Thubten Yeshe, died even though he was not 
yet old. We also had friends who have already died. We also had relatives who died before they got old. This is because there are many co nditions that can take life. If we think about 
this, we will realize that we are in the same situ ation. Therefore, since it  is not sure when we 
will die but it is sure that we will die, we need to practice the Dharma well. We need to make 
a strong decision to do this and then put effort into it. 
 There are many external conditions that can take our life, for exampl e, fire, water, wind, 
earth, and so forth. There are also inner conditions, for exampl e, eating bad food, which can 
cause us to die. Damage to our inner organs ca n also be a condition for our death. Therefore, 
we should think, “Death will also come to me. I have seen my teachers and friends die due to 
various conditions. This will also happen to me.”  We are the same as them. Hence, we should 
think, “Death will definitely come. But when it  will come, I do not know. Therefore, I need 
to practice the Dharma.” By thin king in this way, we will be inspired to practice the Dharma 
now.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B There are many conditions that lead to death and few conditions that promote life 
Second: Since there are many harmful infl uences – animate as well as inanimate – 
to this life, you should thoroughly consid er how it is threatened by human beings 
and non-human malevolent influences, how there are types of animals that 
endanger one’s body and life through manifold harm, and likewise how both 
internal illnesses and external elements cause harm. Furthermore, one’s body is 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
255 necessarily formed from the four elements  and they too harm each other if their 
domains expand or diminish out of ba lance, thereby causing illnesses and 
ravishing one’s life. Since they exist simult aneous with oneself, even though body 
and life appear to be stable they cannot be relied on.  
 
This is another reason for practicing the Dhar ma. There are many conditions that take or 
harm our life, for example, both human beings  and non-human beings, such as spirits. In 
addition, there are various types of animals that can harm us and take our life. An example of 
a human being taking our life is a thief who, wanting to rob our house, kills us. Another example is being killed in a place of war, due to  a bomb thrown by a soldier. We can also be 
killed by spirits as well as by ferocious animals such as tigers, lions, el ephants, and so forth. 
We can even be killed by small insects, such as bees. Once a monk was walking from McLeod 
Ganj down to Dharamsala together with the oracle of the Tibetan government, Ka Dong, 
when they were attacked by angry bees. The monk was stung nine or ten times and, even 
though he was brought to hospital, died from the bites. There are also small snakes whose 
poison is so strong that, having been bitten by one, one will die before one can take seven 
steps. Even a small mosquito can bite us and give us an illness that takes our life.   Internally there are various illnesses that ca n kill us. According to Tibetan medicine there 
are 400 types of illness that can harm us and ta ke our lives. However, there are also many new 
illnesses that have arisen in this past century wh ich, even if they do not kill us, weaken us so 
much that we cannot do anything.   Externally there are the four elements that can also harm us. For example, a strong wind, 
such as a tornado, can uproot a tree and crus h us to death. An ea rthquake can cause the 
death of many people. Water, such as floods or tsunamis, also kill many people. Fire can also kill us as well as burn down entire forests and many houses.  
 We also have the four elements – earth, wa ter, fire, and wind – inside our body. When 
they are out of balance, we become sick and can even lose our life. For example, when the fire 
element is strong, we experience fever or hi gh blood pressure. When the wind element is 
disturbed, the mind is also disturbed. In fact  there are many different  sicknesses related to 
each of the four elements. We need to think about this.   While this body and lifespan appear to be st able, they are not. They are unstable. Nor is 
our mind stable, it quickly changes. One day we  want something, and the next day we do not 
want it anymore!  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In the Parinirvana [Sutra] it says:  
 
The discrimination of death is that this life, constantly surrounded by 
hostile enemies, weakens in each moment and that there is nothing 
that increase it. 
 The discrimination of death should be that ou r life is surrounded by many hateful enemies 
and that in each instant it degenerates or weak ens and that there is no thing that increases it. 
In each moment our life degenerates, that is, it  becomes shorter. Even if our lifespan is 100 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
256 years, after one year it is no longer 100 years. Since one day pa sses quickly, so does one month 
and one year. Our life is like a flowing river, it never stops.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And in the Precious Garland : 
 
We live amidst the conditions for the Lord of Death, 
Like a butter lamp in a gale. 
 The Lord of Death abides as an inner condit ion within us, like a butter lamp that is 
immediately extinguished in a gale (literally, a red wind). Death is strong and powerful, like a strong wind that can easily extinguish a fragile flame.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Besides life itself permeates the reality of death, since many conditions for life are 
unreliable. From the Precious Garland : 
 
There are many conditions for death. 
Those for living are only few. Even they may well be deadly. 
Therefore, always practice the Dharma. 
 
L i f e  i t s e l f  g o e s  t o w a r d  d e a t h .  T h e r e  a r e  m a n y  c o n d i t i o n s  t h a t  t a k e  o u r  l i f e  a n d  t h o s e  t h a t  sustain it are very few. In addition, even the fe w conditions that normally sustain life can at 
times transform into conditions that bring abou t our death. Therefore, everyday we need to 
try to practice the Dharma. To practice the Dharma, put very simply, means to abandon non-
virtuous actions and to create or collect virtuo us actions. Thus, we should abandon creating 
any of the ten non-virtuous actions and try to create even one of the ten virtuous actions. 
Alternatively to practice the Dharma can refe r to the recitation of mantras, prayers, and 
sadhanas, to doing prostrations, to building temples, stupas, and statues, to working to help the poor, and to taking care of the sick. All of  these can be included in the practice of the 
Dharma. For example, we can do a sadhana, ge nerate ourselves as a deity, and carry death 
into the path to the truth body (dharmakaya), carry the intermedia te state into the path to the 
enjoyment body, and carry birth into the path  to the emanation body. If we are tantra 
practitioners we should practice the generation st age in this way. We should also eat, drink, 
dress, wash, and so forth motivated by the wish to transform them all into virtue. In this way 
these daily activities also become Dharma practice.   Everyday we should try to keep mindfulness and introspection present in our mind. In this way, we should try to make our life useful and meaningful day after day.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
257 C The body is extremely vulnerable and the time of death therefore uncertain 
{71} The body is as vulnerable as a water bubble, therefore this and that, such as a 
thorn piercing it, may occasion the destru ction of life without the need for major 
h a r m .  T h u s  i t  i s  v e r y  e a s i l y  o v e r c o m e  b y  c o n d i t i o n s  o f  d e a t h .  I n  t h e  Letter to a 
Friend  it says:  
 
If physical things – the ground, Mount Meru, and oceans – 
Are burnt up by the seven blazing suns 
And even ashes are not left behind,112 
What need is there to mention exceedingly feeble human beings? 
 
Our body is very fragile, like a water bubble that  can easily be destroyed. For example, even a 
simple thorn that piercing the sole of our f oot can become a condition that takes our life. 
Thus, there are many conditions th at can take or destroy our life. Since such conditions easily 
destroy our body without any difficulty, even th ough it appears to be strong, it is not.  
 Nagarjuna’s Letter to a Friend  says that even the ground, Mount Meru, and the oceans can 
be destroyed by the seven blazing suns. This refe rs to the end of the eon at which time seven 
suns will appear simultaneously and burn the ground, Mount Meru, and the oceans, such that not even ashes will remain . All will be completely destro yed. Therefore, what need is 
there to mention that our body will be easily destroyed? Since our human body is weak and 
fragile, we can quickly and easily lose our life.   In short, there are many external conditions  that can take our life. Although our body 
appears to be strong and healthy, in reality it is not. Rather it is very soft and fragile, and can 
easily be destroyed.   We should do the nine-round meditation on death-impermanence. That  death is certain has 
three reasons, that the time of death is uncert ain has three reasons, and that at the time of 
death only our Dharma practice and nothing el se can b e of help h a s three reasons. When 
dying none of our relatives, such as our father , mother, son, or daughter, can help us. Nor is 
our wealth of any help to us then. Even this body, which we have cherished so much, cannot 
help us as we need to leave it  behind and go on alone. When  death comes we cannot take our 
guru, father, mother, a very dear friend, or anyo ne else with us. We cannot use our wealth to 
stop our death nor can we bring it with us. We  cannot even bring our body with us, we must 
leave it behind whereupon it comes to be called a “corpse.”  
Wednesday afternoon, 9 March 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A4B-3 The decision that it is necessary to practice Dharma from now on 
At the end of thoughts like these, you should reiterate many times the innermost 
decision to practice the Dharma from this  very moment, for it is uncertain when 
                                              
112 According to the cosmogony alluded to here, everything  physical will be destroyed by fire at the end of a 
cycle of eons. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
258 t h e  b o d y  a n d  l i f e  w i l l  b e  o v e r c o m e  b y  t h e  L o r d  o f  D e a t h  a n d  i t  s h o u l d  n o t  b e  
assumed that there is still time. As Shri Jagatamitra said:  
Earth Lord, as long as this borrowed body 
Abides in pleasure – not sick, not decayed, 
At that time take its essence. Without the fears of illness, death, decay, 
In times of illness, old age, and decay,  
You may be concerned, but to what avail? 
 
Among the three root [thoughts], the thought about the uncertainty of the time of 
death is the most important, which is why you should make effort in it.  
 
We need to make the decision to practice the Dharma right now, starting immediately. In the 
end we will die, but it is not certain when our body and life will be destroyed. Therefore, we 
should put effort into practicing the Dharma right now. We should make a decision to do 
this from the depths of our heart, thin king “I will practice the Dharma now!”  
 “Earth Lord” refers to our present body which we have borrowed for the time being. 
However, one day it will be taken back by its ow ner, like a tenant sent away by the owner of a 
house. Since our body is neither ill nor dec ayed, and we presently abide in happiness, we 
need to take the essence of this life right now.  While our body will become ill and old in the 
future, and its bones, blood, and so forth will degenerate, and we will die, right now this has 
not yet happened, due to which we can practi ce the Dharma. On the other hand, when we 
have become old and our body has degenerated, even if we think that it is necessary to 
practice the Dharma and want to do so, at that time it will be difficult.  
 There are three roots: (1) death is certain, (2) the time of deat h is uncertain, and (3) at the 
time of death all that can help us is our practi ce of the Dharma, nothin g else and no one else, 
whether our friends, our wealth, or our body, ca n help us. At death our mind leaves our body 
and we have to leave all these behind. This wi ll happen to us one day. Among the three root 
thoughts, the most important is the thought that the time of de ath is uncertain. Therefore, 
we need to put effort into thinking about this, whereby we will be inspired to put more effort 
into practicing the Dharma.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A4C At the time of death nothing except for the Dharma will be of benefit 
1 The actual reasons 
2 Decision 
2B4B-2B1A-1A4C-1 The actual reasons 
A [You cannot take friends along] 
Third: Thus when you see that you have to go another world, even if at that time 
you are surrounded by loving and grieving friends and relatives, you cannot take 
even one of them along.  
 
At the time of death, when we go to another wo rld, that is, to our next life, nothing except 
the Dharma will be of any benefit to us. Even  though our close relatives and friends stand 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
259 around us, they cannot help us. We cannot bring even one of them with  us. Due to this we 
suffer much. In fact, their crying and wailing will only make us sadder, yet still we will not be 
able to bring even one of them with us.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B [You cannot take  possessions along] 
Whatever heaps of attractive riches you have obtained, you cannot even take along 
even a small particle of it.  
 
No matter how much money and wealth we have accumulated, even a treasure house full of 
jewels, clothing, shoes, and so forth, when we di e we will not be able to bring along even one 
small particle of it. However, perhaps our re latives will be happy about this! It happens 
sometimes that when a person is alive his or he r relatives are not very close to him, but when 
he is dying suddenly they show up claiming to be  his brother or sister, uncle or aunt, father or 
mother, and so forth. These relatives then begin to argue amongst themselves about who 
should get what, saying that a pa rt of the inheritance is due to  them. In the worst cases they 
even end up in court. Thus, our wealth and belongings are not very useful to us at the time of 
our death and they may cause serious problems between our relatives.  
 There is a story about an old monk who lived in a monastery near Lhasa, Tibet. He owned 
a piece of turquoise, which he guarded very care fully and did not allow any of his disciples to 
touch for fear that they would break it. One day when he was close to death, one of his 
disciples said to him that he must bring the tu rquoise with him. The dying monk said that he 
could not, but his disciple insisted, saying that he had always kept it with him. He told his 
disciple to bring him the turquoise, whereupon he crushed it into powder and swallowed it together with a glass of water. He then told hi s disciple not to worry as  in this way he would 
bring the turquoise with him!  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C [You cannot take your body along] 
If you have to give up even the flesh and bones that you were born with, what need 
to talk of anything else?  
 
Our friends and relatives also cannot help us, given that we cannot bring even one of them 
with us. Also no matter how much money, clot hing, animals, possessions we have, we cannot 
bring even the tiniest particle with us. Even th is body of flesh and bones that we were born 
with, must be given up, that is, it must be left behind when we die. We cannot bring it with 
us. If we think in this way, we will come to re alize that at the time of death we must go on 
alone.  
 Tibetan lay people commonly say that when we die, we will have visions of beings who come to take us seven layers below the earth to the hungry ghost re alm where the Lord of 
Death lives. They will bring us in front of th e Lord of Death who will check in a mirror how 
many virtues we have and for each one will se t aside a white stone, and how many non-virtues 
we have and for each one will set aside a blac k stone. When he is finished, he will compare 
the number of white and black and decide where we are to be reborn. Usually there are only a 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
260 few white stones for every 100 black ones, so then  the Lord of Death will tell us in which of 
the lower or higher realms we are to be reborn , whereby we will be immediately born there.  
 At the time of death, most of us will experien ce fear. Only those who have lived a good life, 
and can be confident of not being reborn in th e lower realms and of being reborn as a human 
being, are relaxed and calm at the time of death. When some people die, even though 
externally they appear to be de ad, their mind still abides togeth er with the subtle wind in the 
indestructible drop at the center of their hear t chakra. They can remain  in meditation on the 
clear light for as long as a week or even for an entire month.  
 When the mind of an ordinary being leaves th e body, if it goes out through the top of the 
head it indicates that the person will take a g ood rebirth, whereas if it goes out through a 
lower part of the body it indicates that the pe rson will be reborn in the lower realms. Usually 
there is also some red liquid that exits from th e nostril and some white liquid that exists from 
the lower orifice, unless the person has been sick  for a long time before dying. The body then 
becomes thinner and begins to smell. Everyone ca lls this body a “corpse”  and it is brought to 
the cemetery to be buried.   There is a story about a Nepalese man who fell  into a coma, but his family, convinced that 
he was dead, brought his body to the river in Pashupath to wash it prior to the cremation. 
When his feet were put in the river, the man woke up and asked where he was. He was told 
that he had died, but he argued that he had no t! Someone, thinking he was a zombie, wanted 
to beat him, but some other people stopped hi m. As the man became a bit better he became 
able to sit up. However, according to Nepalese tradition, because he had “died” he could not 
go back to live in his home. For this reason , his family made a bed for him on the veranda 
and gave him food to eat, but did not allow hi m to go inside the house. This made the old 
man very sad but he could not do anything about it.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1A4C-2 The decision to practice nothing but Dharma  
A The actual [decision] 
Then think: “At that time, it  will certainly come to pass that all the excellences of 
this life leaves me, just as I leave them, and I will proceed to another world. This 
will happen today.” {72} You should make the decision many times to practice the 
Dharma without submitting to the power of  conditions like the body, friends, and 
possessions.  
 
The first decision is “I will practice the Dhar ma.” The second decision “I will practice the 
Dharma right now.” The third decision is “I  will practice the Dharma purely.” Without 
submitting to the power of our friends and rela tives, possessions, and body, we should make 
the decision to practice the Dharma purely. Fo r example, our venerable teacher, Geshe Yeshe 
Tobden, when he was a teacher earning a good salary at the Tibetan Institute of Higher 
Studies in Sarnath, suddenly decided to give ev erything up and go to meditate in the hills 
behind Dharamsala. He remained there unti l His Holiness the Dalai Lama asked him to 
come to teach here in Italy. Some people are able  to give up everything and spend their life in 
meditation, for example, Saint Francis of Assisi.   One day we too will have to give up all our excellences, our wealth as well as our body, and go to another world, that is, to another life and another place. Since this could happen even 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
261 today, we should not think to pass our time in taking care of our friends and relatives, our 
belongings, and our body. Instead, not allowing ourselves to come under their influence, we 
should practice the Dharma right now and in a pure way.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The need to try hard since it is the foundation of the path  
Although this arises with difficulty, you sh ould try hard since it is the foundation 
of the path. In the words of Potowa: 
 
These are my meditations on impermanence illuminating my 
appearances. Through eliminating all th e appearances of this life – close 
relationships, possessions, and the like – and aware that I will go here and there to another life alone wi thout anything, wishing to not be 
attached to this life I think: “The r e  i s  n o t h i n g  t o  d o  o t h e r  t h a n  t o  
practice the Dharma.” As long as th is [attitude] has not arisen in my 
mind, I will sit with the path s to all the Dharma blocked. 
 The understanding of death-impermanence is th e base or foundation of the path, therefore 
we need to put effort into doing the nine-round meditation on death-impermanence, which is composed of the three roots that result in the three decisions. In other words, because this 
meditation is the basis of Dharma  practice, we should strive in it. Although it is not easy to 
generate the realization of this meditation, we n eed to put effort into it  since this meditation 
is the base of the entire path.   For this reason, Geshe Potowa said that his me ditation on impermanence is like a light or 
illumination that allows us to clearly see in the darkness. It is like a lamp or candle in a dark room, which enables us to see what is in the room. We need to eliminate attachment to the 
appearances of this life, our relatives, posse ssions and so forth, by meditating on death-
impermanence. We must go on alone to our next life; no one else can come with us. 
Therefore, the one thing that can help us at  the time of death is our Dharma practice. 
Nothing else can help us. By thinking of this, we  will give up attachment to this life, to our 
wealth, relatives, and so forth. If we do not ge nerate this understanding in our mind, the path 
to all the Dharma will remain blocked. In other words, if we do not think about death-
impermanence, we will not think to practice th e Dharma. Therefore, it is important to think 
about death-impermanence. In fact in the Ny ingma tradition the first meditation to be 
practiced is that on impermanence, the fact that everything changes. Having done this 
meditation, all other realizations will come. On  the other hand, if th is meditation is not 
done, no progress will be made on  the path. Thus, their way of practicing is different from the 
Gelug tradition in which the first meditation is  that on relying on a guru, followed by the 
meditation on recognizing the precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities, its 
causes, and the difficulty of finding it. After th at in the Gelug tradition, we think about the 
f a c t  t h a t  w e  h a v e  f o u n d  t h i s  g o o d  f o r t u n e  just this one time but that there are many 
conditions that can take it away from us. In ot her words, this precious human rebirth quickly 
comes to an end because there are many condit ions that bring about death. Then, we think 
that death is certain, that the time of death is uncertain, and that at the time of death nothing 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
262 other than the practice of the Dharma will be of any help to us. We can meditate in 
whichever of these ways suits us since we all have different mentalities and dispositions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And Dolpa said: 
 
Incidentally, if you accumulate the collections and purify obscurations, 
make requests to the deities an d [your] guru, and contemplate 
insistently together with perseveran ce, even that which you think will 
not come about for 100 years will come about effortlessly, for compounded phenomena do not remain static as they are. 
 When someone asked Kamaba about moving on to another object [of meditation] 
he said: “Repeat the previous one!” When asked about the remaining ones, he 
said: “There is nothing beyond it.” 
 In order to attain calm abiding, we need to accumulate merit and purify our negativities or 
obscurations. How can we do this? While mainly  doing meditation, at other times we should 
accumulate merit and purify obscurations (in th is context mainly the karmic obscurations as 
opposed to the afflictive obscurations and the obscurations to knowledge). In addition, we 
should make strong prayers to the meditational deities and to our own gurus. If we do this, 
even without meditating for one hundred years it will not be difficult to generate realizations 
in our mind. Since all compounded phenomena do  not remain static, but are fluctuating and 
transitory, even if we think that we will no t obtain realizations, we will obtain them.  
 When someone asked Kamaba whether he coul d change his object of meditation, Kamawa 
told him that he should do th e same meditation again and again. The rest of the Tibetan 
sentence is difficult to understand but it seems to mean that there is no thing else to meditate 
on.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How to carry out the practice of all the excellent scriptures [of the Buddha] 
Thus, if you know how to practice reliance  on a spiritual teacher, the leisures and 
endowments, as well as impermanence in this and that context of the excellent 
scriptures and their commentaries, and sustain them, you will easily find the 
intention of the Conqueror. You will also become aware of them in other 
contexts. 
 When we meditate on guru devotion or reliance on a guru, we should first examine how 
many qualities the guru needs. Then, having found a qualified teacher, we should rely on him 
properly in thought and action . Then, we should meditate on  the eight leisures and ten 
endowments of a precious human rebirth. Then , we should meditate on impermanence by 
means of the nine-round meditation on death. In this way we will be able to easily understand 
the intention of all the Buddha’s script ures as well as the commentaries.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
263  
In short, in our every day life we should do so me analytical meditation on relying on the guru, 
the precious human rebirth, death-impermanence , the objects of refuge and how to go for 
refuge to them, the law of cause and result an d the need to safeguard it, and so forth.
