Thursday morning, 10 March 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
Explanation of what will become  of you in future worlds: 
How to think about the happiness and suffering  
of the two [types of] migrating beings 
2B4B-2B1A-1B The happiness and suffering of the two [types of] migrating beings 
1 Thinking about what will become of you in future worlds 
[2 From among the happiness and suffering of the two migrations, considerations about the suffering ] 
2B4B-2B1A-1B1 Thinking about what will become of you in future worlds 
Second: That being so, since it is certain that you will die soon, {73} you have no 
time to spend in this life and, after deat h, it is not that you become non-existent; 
you must take rebirth. Since apart from th e two there is no other place of birth, 
you will be reborn in either the happ y migrations or the bad migrations. In 
addition, since you are not under your ow n control but are under the control of 
other – karma, you will be reborn acco rding to the wholesome and unwholesome 
karma that impels you.  
 
Up to now we have talked about this life, but now we will examine what will happen in our 
next life. Since we will soon die, that is, we wi ll not remain in this life for long, what will 
happen to us next? It is not that we become non-existent, rather we must once again take 
rebirth. There are two types of rebirth: rebirth in the happy migrations and rebirth in the bad 
migrations. We are not able to choose in which of these we will be reborn, but will be projected there by our actions. If we have crea ted virtuous actions we will be born in a happy 
migration, whereas if we have created non- virtuous actions we will be born in a bad 
migration. Of course, if we could choose, we wo uld choose to be reborn in a happy migration! 
However, due to our mind being under the contro l of afflictions, we are unable to make a 
choice and completely follow the afflictions, du e to which we create negative actions and do 
not create good actions. Therefore, we are no t free, but are under the control of our karma, 
that is, our actions.   With respect to rebirth, there are two types of actions: projecting or throwing karma and 
actualizing karma. We mainly create projecting karma. When we create an action it does not go to waste but instead leaves an imprint on th e consciousness, which at the time of death is 
activated and becomes projecting karma. Due to  the power of this karma we are projected 
into either a happy migration or a bad migration.  For this reason, we need to avoid creating 
non-virtuous actions and create virtuous actions.  This is our job and our responsibility. With 
mindfulness we need to be careful to avoid en gaging in negative actions and to engage in 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
264 good actions. If we create black or unwholes ome karma it will only bring the result of 
suffering, whereas white or wholesome karma will  bring the result of happiness. Thus, “white 
karma” is karma that brings the result of ha ppiness or pleasure, wher eas “black karma” is 
karma that brings suffering and unhappiness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1B2 From among the happiness and suffering of the two migrations, considerations about the 
suffering 
A The need to think about suffering 
[B How thinking about suffering is a vital point C How to think about suffering] 
2B4B-2B1A-1B2A The need to think about suffering 
Think about the suffering of the bad migr ations considering, “What will become 
of me if I am reborn in the bad migrations?” Nagarjuna said: 
 
Every day remember the hells  
Which are fiercely hot and cold. Also remember the hungry ghosts, Emaciated by hunger and thirst 
 
Remember and look at animals Beset with the sufferings of dullness. Give up their causes; enjoy the causes for happiness. Once you have gained that hard to gain,  A human body in Jambudvipa, 
Endeavor to cut the causes of the bad migrations. 
 
Since there is karma that brings the result of happiness and karma that brings the result of 
suffering, they can be called respectively “hap py karma” and “suffering karma.” We need to 
examine suffering by thinking, “If I were to be reborn in the bad migrations what would 
happen to me?” If we examine this, we will find that we will experience suffering, as set out by 
Nagarjuna.   If we are reborn in the hell realm, we will experience the suffering of either intense heat or intense cold. Later on there will be more details about this suffering and its duration.   When we meditate on the hell realm and so  forth, we should not imagine that someone 
else is experiencing the suffering of heat or co ld. Instead we should imagine that we ourselves 
are in hell; for example, our body being surrounded by fire and being burnt. If we imagine ourselves experiencing this suffering, we will deve lop the wish to avoid such suffering and will 
therefore give up creating its cause. We should also imagine our body frozen, like ice, in the 
cold hell and unable to do anything to reli eve the suffering through remembering that even 
when the weather is cold here in this world we are unable to bear it and do not want to go outside. Therefore, we should not imagine someone else as experiencing this suffering but think of ourselves in this situation. In this way we will generate a strong determination to 
never take such a rebirth again in the future. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
265  We should also think about the suffering of  hungry ghosts. They experience hunger and 
thirst due to being unable to find food and drink. Their bodies are shriveled up and their 
necks are thin, but their bellies are huge. We should  try to imagine what it would be like to be 
r e b o r n  a s  s u c h  a  b e i n g  a n d  w h a t  w e  w o u l d  f e e l  l i k e  i f  w e  c o u l d  n o t  f i n d  f o o d  a n d  d r i n k .  
Based on this understanding, we should determin e to avoid creating the causes to be reborn 
as a hungry ghost. 
 We should also think about what an animal rebirth would be like. An imals are very stupid. 
They also eat each other and experience many other sufferings that we can see. We should 
imagine ourselves born as an animal, for example,  as a dog. While there are dogs that have a 
good owner and are given food and even clothing, we should imagine ourselves reborn as an abandoned dog and examine the suffering we would experience. We should also imagine 
what it would be like to be a wild animal living in a forest where there is the constant danger of being eaten. Some animals do not kill and ea t other animals, for example, elephants, cows, 
and so forth, but they are killed and eaten by ot her animals. They can even be eaten by small 
animals that enter the body. Since we could not bear such a rebirth, we should determine to 
give up creating its causes, non-virtuous actions.  Some say that very heavy negative actions br ing the result of rebirth in the hell realm, 
middling negative actions as a hungry ghost, and small or light negative actions as an animal. 
Others say that heavy negative actions bring re birth in the hell realm, middling negative 
actions as an animal, and small negative action s as a hungry ghost. We should examine why 
there are these different assertions. However, in short, we should try to abandon the cause of 
rebirth in such realms, that is, we should ab andon non-virtuous actions and create virtuous 
actions.   It is difficult to achieve rebirth as a human be ing in Jambudvipa because it is very precious. 
A l t h o u g h  t h e  l i f e s p a n  h e r e  i s  u n c e r t a i n ,  w e  possess the inner conditions for practicing the 
Dharma – the six elements due to having ta ken birth in a womb. We can transform our 
energy into good energy and achieve enlightenmen t even in this life, even in three years and 
forty-five days. This is because in Jambudvipa it is easier to generate renunciation than in the 
other continents. It is difficult to develop renunciation in Unpleasant Sound due to the 
lifespan being fixed at 1000 years and due to being wealthy. Since these human beings are 
happy, they do not think to practice the Dhar ma and cannot generate renunciation. Also in 
the eastern and western continents it is difficult  to generate renunciation. This does not refer 
to the eastern and western parts of this world, but to different worlds. In Tall Body, the human beings’ bodies are very tall. In the west ern continent, there are very special cows that 
are a source of precious jewels, whereby th e human beings there are very rich. In our 
continent, Jambudvipa, the lifespan is not fixe d, the rich become poor , and the poor become 
rich. Because things change very quickly here, it is easier to generate the mind of renunciation 
or definite emergence. Since we have obtained  such a human rebirth now, we need to make 
our mind very strong in cutting off the causes of rebirth in the lower realms and we need to put effort and work into this. It is not easy to cut the causes, the main one being karma, for 
such a rebirth. Karma is produced by the a fflictions and the root of the afflictions is 
ignorance. In fact, in the contex t of the twelve links, the first link is ignorance. Due to the 
ignorance of not knowing the selflessness of pers ons acting as a causal motivation, we engage 
in different types of actions. Thus, the root of  all the virtuous and no n-virtuous actions that 
we create is ignorance. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
266  There is a causal motivation and a simultaneous  or time motivation. If  the time motivation 
is virtuous, the action will be virtuous and will bring the result of rebirth in the higher realms 
in the future. If the time or simultaneous moti vation is non-virtuous, the action will be non-
virtuous and will bring the result of rebirth in the bad migrations in th e future. Therefore, we 
need to cut the root of cyclic existence – igno rance or the conception of a self. However, for 
the moment we need to at least avoid creating non-virtuous actions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1B2B How thinking about suffering is a vital point 
Meditation about the sufferings of cyclic  existence in general and of the bad 
migrations in particular is a very vital point, for if you thus consider how you 
yourself have fallen into the ocean of suffering, despair will repel pride and 
arrogance. Having seen that suffering is a result of non-virtue, you will be very 
careful to avoid negativities and downfalls. Since you do not want suffering but 
want happiness, once you see that [happiness ] is the result of virtue, you will also 
enjoy accomplishing virtue. In proportion to your own experience, compassion for 
others will arise, and out of aversion to cyclic existence you will strive for 
liberation. For fear of suffering, yo u will take refuge forcefully.  
 
We need to meditate on the sufferings of cyclic  existence in general and the sufferings of the 
lower realms in particular. In general as long as  we are reborn in cyclic existence, we will not 
be completely free from suffering. There are six realms or three realms – the desire realm, form realm, and formless realm. The desire real m consists of six types of migrating beings: 
gods, anti-gods, human beings, animals, hungry ghosts, and hell beings. However, even if we 
are reborn in the Peak of Existence of the formless realm, one day the karma created by 
having meditated in the past will be exhausted,  and we will fall once again into the desire 
realm, perhaps even into the hell, hungry ghost,  or animal realm. Therefore, there is nowhere 
in cyclic existence that is completely free of suffering.   The happier states of cyclic existence include,  for example, the seventeen levels of the form 
realm, thirteen of which are levels in which or dinary beings are reborn and five of which are 
pure lands in which arya beings are reborn. Then there are the six types of desire realm gods. 
Then there are the human beings of the four continents [and eight sub-continents ]. In all 
these realms there is suffering. In the desire re alm suffering is manifest, whereas in the form 
realm physical suffering is not manifest. Ho wever, these gods experience great mental 
suffering when death approaches and they see with  their clairvoyance that they will fall into a 
lower realm and experience suffering there. They experience particular signs of their 
impending death for seven days before their de ath but these seven days are like a thousand 
human years. Therefore, they experience inte nse suffering for a long period of time.  
 If we think about the fact that we have falle n into the ocean of suffering, it becomes easier 
to generate renunciation. It will also reduce ou r haughtiness and pride since this suffering is 
the result of non-virtue. Therefore, we need to very carefully try not to create negativities and 
downfalls. It is important to try not to do this . Why is that? Because we do not want suffering, 
but want to be happy, to enjoy ourselves, and to  experience pleasure. Happiness or pleasure is 
the result of virtuous actions. Understanding this, we will put effort into creating virtuous 
actions. We will also enjoy creating virtuous ac tions, such as meditating, reciting prayers, and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
267 so forth. On the other hand, if we do not un derstand the good result that will come about 
due to meditation, prayer, and so forth, we will th ink that it is a waste of time to sit unmoving 
like a statue and that it would be better to  spend our time working and making money.  
 Realizing that we suffer, we should also gene rate compassion for all other sentient beings, 
who are suffering in the same way. Thinking in this way, we will generate compassion. 
Through understanding that there is no essenc e in cyclic existence and that it has many 
disadvantages, we will generate the mind of re nunciation and the wish to achieve liberation, 
the state of everlasting happiness. We should generate compassion and think about how we 
can lead other sentient beings into the libera ted state and free them from the sufferings of 
cyclic existence. We should generate strong fear  regarding such sufferings and go for refuge to 
the objects of refuge who are Buddha, Dhar ma, and Sangha. A buddha is someone who has 
abandoned all objects of abandonment and is free from obscurations, and has achieved all 
knowledge and is omniscient, knowing all that ex ists. The real Dharma is true paths and true 
cessations. The cessation of suffering and the ca uses of suffering is a true cessation. It is 
actualized by meditating true paths, whereby all afflictions and their results, contaminated 
phenomena such as this body, are abandoned.  The real Sangha are arya beings, those who 
have achieved the path of seeing, path of me ditation, or path of no-more-learning. They 
meditated true paths and achieved true cessat ions. Those who achieve the actual refuge of 
true paths and true cessations are the Sangha. They help us in our practice of Dharma.  
 There are two types of objects of refuge, the causal and the resultant. The causal Buddha 
refuge is, for example, our Teacher, Buddha Shakyamuni, who taught us what to practice, 
whereby we can attain liberation from cyclic existence. The resultant B u d d h a  r e f u g e  i s  t h e  
buddha we will become in the future. The causal  Dharma refuge is the true paths and true 
cessations in others’ continua. The resultant Dharma refuge is the true paths and true 
cessations in our own continuum. The causal Sangha  refuge is other arya beings such as foe-
destroyers and so forth. The resultant Sangha refuge is the arya that we will become in the future when we achieve true paths and true cessations. We should develop the wish to 
meditate true paths and actualize true cessations and with this motivation take refuge in the 
Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
These positive qualities of suffering th at comprise many important points of 
practice {74} are stated in Engaging in [Bodhisattva] Behavior  i n  t e r m s  o f  s u f f e r i n g  
[already] arisen in one’s own continuum, ho wever, they are equal[ly applicable] to 
suffering one is yet to experience.  
 
Suffering has one good quality – it can be el iminated! This is mentioned in Shantideva’s 
Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds . In addition, the suffering in our own continuum makes us 
generate the wish to attain liberation and en lightenment. It also makes us develop great 
compassion and the mind of enlightenment,  due to which we will attain buddhahood. 
Therefore, the suffering in our continuum is of benefit to us. If we recognize our own 
suffering, for example, that today we have a head ache or a toothache, we will go to the doctor 
who perhaps will then send us to  the hospital for X-rays and so forth. On the other hand, if 
we have sickness in our body but it is not manife st we will not go to the doctor or hospital to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
268 find out what is wrong. Sometimes when someon e only experiences a bit of pain they put off 
going to the hospital but, when they finally do go , the doctors say that it is already too late to 
do anything. There was a geshe, Geshe Thubte n Tsering from Ganden Jangtse Monastery, 
who lived in Madrid, Spain. He had had a pain in his neck for a long time and had been in 
hospital in Singapore, Malaysia, and Bangkok, but even after he arrived in Spain he 
continued to have pain. However, the root of  the pain could not be found. The doctors 
always looked at his neck but could not find anything. Some years later his stomach became 
swollen but he did not experience pain for a long  time. One day when he had a bit of pain he 
went to the hospital where he was told that it  was necessary to undergo an operation. When 
the surgeons opened him up, they found that hi s stomach was completely rotten and that it 
was to late to do anything for him. They told  him he would die in five or six days. Someone 
called me from Spain asking me to do some pu jas for Geshe Thubten Tsering. I also asked his 
monastery and friends to pray for him as he wa s very sick and perhaps would quickly die. 
Some days later I received a call that he had no t yet died and in fact he did not die until two 
months later. Therefore, sometimes when a sickne ss is not manifest, in that there is no pain 
and so forth, it becomes too late to cure it and the person dies. On the other hand, when there is pain there is the benefit that  the illness can be found and cured. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1B2C How to think about suffering 
1 The actual [considerations concerning the suffering of the bad migrations] 
2 How it is necessary to meditate on this until the experience is produced  
2B4B-2B1A-1B2C-1 The actual [considerations concerning the suffering in bad migrations] 
A Thinking about the sufferings of hell beings 
B Thinking about the sufferings of animals 
C Thinking about the sufferings of hungry ghosts 
Thus, the considerations about the sufferi ng of the bad migrations have three 
points: thinking about the sufferings of hell beings, animals, and hungry ghosts. 
2B4B-2B1A-1B2C-1A Thinking about the sufferings of hell beings 
1 The actual [considerations] 
2  The need for fear at the thought that you have already accumulated numerous causes for being born there 
2B4B-2B1A-1B2C-1A1 The actual [considerations] 
A The great hells of sentient beings 
B The neighboring hells 
C The cold hells 
D The occasional hells 
 
The first one has four points: the great he lls of sentient beings, the neighboring 
hells, the cold hells, and the occasional hells.  
A The great hells of sentient beings 
First: 32,000 yojanas113 below here there is Continual Revival, and below it are 
seven more hells each sepa rated by 4,000 yojanas. 
 In the first among the eight, Continua l Revival, the sentient beings come 
together and stab each other with various weapons that arise by the power of their 
                                              
113 See introduction above, note 27 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
269 karma. Whenever they faint and collapse on  the ground, a voice rings out from the 
sky, “You, come back to life!” Upon that, they stand up again and start stabbing as 
before, due to which they experience immeasurable suffering. 
 In Black Line, the hell guardians put bl ack lines in [the shape of] many squares 
and the like on the [bodies of] the sentient  beings born there and then the latter 
experience the suffering of being cut into  pieces by weapons along those lines.  
 In Crushing Hell,114 the hell guardians pack the se ntient beings, once they have 
been rounded up, between two goat-faced or similar iron mountains. Then, they 
are immediately crushed by the two, whereupon rivers of blood spurt out from all their orifices.  
 In Howling Hell, the sentient beings in se arch of an abode enter iron houses. {75} 
As soon as they enter, fires blaze all around and they burn.   The Great Howling Hell is like the prev ious one, the difference being that the 
iron house has two layers.   In Hot Hell, the hell guardians put the sentient beings into very hot and blazing iron cauldrons of several yojanas, where th ey are burnt like fish. They are impaled 
through their buttocks on blazing iron st akes that emerge at the crown of the 
heads. Fire blazes out of their mouths, eye sockets, nostrils, and ears as well as all 
their pores. They are laid out, on their back or face down, on the blazing iron 
foundation and pounded and tormen ted with blazing iron hammers.  
 In Very Hot Hell, an iron trident is stuc k into their buttocks so that the right and 
left prongs emerge from the two should ers and the middle one from the crown of 
the head. Owing to that, tongues of fire blaze from their orifices such as the 
mouth. Their bodies are wrapped in blazin g, hot iron sheets and placed headlong 
into an iron cauldron full of boiling li quid, whereupon they are tossed up, down, 
and about as they boil. Then, once their skin, flesh, and blood have been destroyed and nothing but the skeleton is left, they are pulled out again and spread out on the iron foundation. After that, when the skin, flesh and blood have 
developed again, they are put in once again. Apart from that, it is like the Hot 
Hell.  
 I cannot say much about this as I haven’t been there! I am joking. There are different hells. 
The “great hells” are the hot hells. Then ther e are the neighboring hells, cold hells, and 
occasional hells. The occasional hells also exis t in the human realm, there being stories about 
this in the Hundred Actions Sutra (Karmashatam ).  
 The hot hells are the main hells. The first of  these is Continual Reviving, in which one 
faints and revives, again and again. In Black Line Hell the guardians mark one’s body with 
black lines and then cut it into pieces along the lines. In Crushing Hell, the guardians put 
one between two iron mountains with the shape of an animal’s head that come together, 
crushing one between them.   There are different causes for such rebirths. Fo r example, if, without a strong motivation to 
kill, we carelessly set grass on fire due to whic h many insects, such as ants, die, this can 
become the cause for taking rebirth in the hell  where there is least suffering. Among the eight 
                                             
 
114 bsdus ‘'joms  – lit. “(hell of) bringing to gether (and) dest roying”, Skt. samghata  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
270 hot hells, the worst suffering is in Unrelent ing Torment (Avichi). In the others there is 
gradually less suffering, therefore rebirth in them  depends respectively on the heaviness of the 
action. This in turn depends on the presence  of a motivation, an actual action, and a 
conclusion. For example, if, with a motivation th at is very negative, one tortures other human 
beings very badly by breaking their bones and so forth, one will be reborn in Unrelenting 
Torment. The cause for taking rebirth in the Bl ack Line is cutting up animals, for example, 
fish, that are not yet dead or putting them live  into boiling oil and so forth. The cause of 
rebirth in Crushing Hell is said to be crushing , for example, lice between one’s thumb nails in 
order to kill them.  
 In Howling Hell, the beings wail and howl due to their suffering. Rebirth there is caused by beating and torturing others. In this hell, on e is forced by one’s ka rma into an iron house 
surrounded on all sides by blazing fire, due to which the body is burned. Suffering greatly, 
one wails or cries loudly.   In Great Howling Hell, there is even more su ffering because the iron house is made of two 
layers, which are again surrounded by blazing fire in all directions. Due to this, the beings there wail even more loudly than in Howling Hell.  In Hot Hell, the hell guardians put the bein gs into cauldrons of boiling liquid, due to 
which intense suffering is experienced as their organs and so forth melt. Fire comes out the 
orifices of their eyes and so forth and the pore s of their body. The hell guardians stretch them 
out on the burning iron ground on their backs or on their stomachs. They also beaten them 
with incandescent hammers. In these ways these sentient beings experience the suffering of 
intense heat. Therefore, we should be careful not to harm others with fire or beat them. 
 In Very Hot Hell, the sentient beings are pierced with burning incandescent tridents, the points of which come out through their head and shoulders. Due to this, they experience 
great suffering and flames of fire issue forth from their mouths and orifices. They are also 
wrapped in burning iron sheets and put upside down into molten iron where they bob up 
and down as they boil and burn. Although thei r skin, flesh, and blood vanish, they are then 
restored once again due to their karma. In this  way, they experience the suffering of great 
heat. Could we bear such a rebirth? Since we co uld not, we must stop creating its cause – non-
virtuous actions – and strive to en gage only in virtuous actions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 In Without Respite, the foundation is completely ablaze for hundreds of yojanas 
from the east. As it spreads, the force of the fire increases, gradually destroying the 
skin, flesh, sinews, and bones of the sentie nt beings to the marrow. Like the wick 
of a butter lamp, their whole body is pervad ed by blazing tongues of fire. {76} In 
the other three directions it is similar.  
 In Unrelenting Torment (Avichi) there is not on e instant in which ther e is no suffering. In 
other words, there is continuous, unrelenting su ffering. The foundation blazes with fire for 
hundreds of yojanas in all directions. The fire  burns one’s skin, flesh, sinews, and bones to 
the marrow. Like the wick of a butter lamp whic h still remains even while being burnt by fire, 
their bodies are completely consumed by fire yet still remain.

271 Thursday afternoon, 10 March 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 76) says:  
 
 As the fire coming from the four directio ns mixes there, [these beings] experience 
incessant suffering and only by their path etic howling are they understood to be 
sentient beings. In iron baskets they are made to jump on burning iron embers. 
They are m ade t o  fall do w n  o n the iro n  fo un dat io n  and t o  clim b up huge iro n 
mountains. Their tongues are pulled out from their mouths and spread out flat with a hundred pegs, then made to be without a wrinkle or crease, as though preparing cow hides. Then again they are placed on the iron foundation face up, 
their mouths are opened with iron tongs,  and blazing lumps of iron are put into 
their mouths. Boiling copper is poured in to their mouths and burns their mouth, 
throat, and entrails before issuing from th eir lower bodies. Apart from that it is 
similar to the Very Hot Hell.  
 The fire comes from all sides from a distance of  many yojanas until it meets together and is 
mixed, whereby the beings there experience uninterrupted suffering. Since their burning 
bodies appear to be the very nature of fire, they can only be understood to be sentient beings from the sound of their wailing.   Their sufferings are quite easy to understa nd from the description given above. The 
guardians of the hell realm torture the hell bein gs in many ways, due to which they experience 
suffering. We t o o  ha ve been bo rn in such pl aces and experienced such suffering. We have 
also been born in the form and formless real ms and experienced great pleasure there, but 
similarly we have also forgotten this experien ce. We should think about whether or not we 
could bear the suffering of the hell realm. Since we could not, we must stop engaging in non-virtuous actions, the cause of taking rebirth in the lower realms, right now. If we do not stop 
engaging in such actions, we will once again ta ke rebirth there and experience great suffering.  
 In short, the hell beings are made to hop about on the incandescent iron ground and to 
climb up and down burning iron mountains. Thei r tongues are pulled out and spread out flat 
with pegs. They are made to lie on their back s on the burning iron foundation, while blazing 
lumps of molten iron are put in their mouths . Boiling copper is also poured into their 
mouths, burning their insides and then leaving through the lower parts of their bodies. These 
are the particular sufferings of Unrelenting Torm ent, which in other ways are similar to those 
of the Very Hot Hell.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
How long those sufferings are experienced is as stated in the Letter to a Friend :  
 
Even if, for a billion of years, you experience 
Completely unbearable sufferings in that way, As long as the non-virtue  has not been exhausted, 
Equally long you will not get rid of your life. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
272 Moreover, in the Treasury and Actuality of the Stages  it is stated that 50 human years 
are one day in the [god realm of the] Four  Great Kings, thirty of those one month, 
and twelve of those one year. 500 such year s is the lifespan of  the beings of the 
Four Great Kings. All that is one single d ay in the hell of Continual Revival, 30 of 
those a month, twelve of those a year, an d 500 such years a lifespan. Likewise 100, 
200, 400, 800, and 1,600 human years resp ectively form a day [in the other five 
desire realm gods ranging] from the Thirty-Three up to Controlling Others’ 
Emanations, and their life-spans can be 1,000, 2,000, 4,000, {77} 8,000, and 
16,000 god years. These respectively fo rm a day in Black Line through the Hot 
Hell, and, they can reach from 1,000 through 16,000 of their own years. In the hell of Great Heat they can reach half an intermediate eon and in Without Respite, one intermediate eon.  
 How long does the suffering of the hells la st? Even though there may seem to be the 
conditions for death, as long as we have not exhausted the non-virtuous  actions that are the 
cause for rebirth there, we will not die and wi ll continue to experience this suffering.  
 Fifty human years are one day in the Four Gr eat Kings, thirty such days are one month, 
and twelve such months are one year. The lifesp an of the beings in this realm is 500 such 
years. They are equal to a single day in the Re viving Hell, thirty such days are one month, 
twelve such months are one year , and 500 such years is the life span of those in the Reviving 
Hell. In each of the other hot hells the lifesp an increases in correspondence with the length 
of the days for the other gods of the desire real m. In Great Hot Hell the beings live for half an 
intermediate eon, and in Unrelenting Torm ent or Without Recite they live for an 
intermediate eon. This is mentioned in the Treasury of Manifest Dharma  and Actuality of the 
Grounds .  
 We cannot even begin to imagine the length of these lifespans. If we could, we might have 
a heart attack!   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The neighboring hells 
Second: Each one of the eight hells has fo ur side walls and four gates. Outside, 
they are surrounded by an iron fence, and that fence also has four gates. By each 
gate there are the four remaining hells of  sentient beings: (1) the Pit of Burning 
Embers, (2) the Bog of Putrid Corpses or the Bog of Excrement Reeking of 
Corpses, (3) the Razor Road, etc., and (4) the River Without a Ford.  
 In the first, there are hot embers up to the knees and when sentient beings go to 
look for a place [to stand], as they set th eir feet down their skin, flesh, and blood 
are destroyed, and as they lift them up they regenerate. 
 
I do not need to say much about this as you can understand it just by reading the above. 
There are four surrounding or neighboring hell s located around each side of the eight hot 
hells. The hot hells are surrounded by four iron wa lls with a door or gate  in each of the four 
directions. Outside that, they are surrounded by a n  i r o n  f e n c e .  O n  o n e side there is first 
neighboring hell, the Pit of Burning Embers. The sentient beings born there look for a place 
to relax a bit, but when they make a step their f oot is burned, and when they lift it up it is 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
273 restored to normal while the other foot is burn ed. This suffering is experienced due to having 
created its cause – carelessly burning and killing other sentient beings with fire. On the other 
s i d e  o f  t h i s  h e l l  i s  t h e  S w a m p  o f  D e c o m p o s e d  Corpses, a swamp or bog from which it is 
difficult to extract the legs and is filled with inse cts that bite the flesh. On the other side there 
is the Razor-filled Plain, a road or path made of razors that cuts the feet and legs. On the 
other side there is the River Without a Ford, a river that cannot be crossed except by going 
into it whereupon the body is badly burned.  
 Sentient beings are initially born in one of th e main hot hells, then, when they die from it, 
they are reborn in one of the surrounding or ne ighboring hells. It is di fficult to leave them 
and great sufferings that cannot be eliminated are experienced there.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 Nearby, there is the second one, the Bo g of Excrement Reeking of Corpses. As 
they cross it in search of an  abode, the sentient beings fall into it and sink up to 
their head. In this bog there are worms called “sharp-beaks” that pierce the skin, 
flesh, sinews, and bones and dig through them all the way to the marrow.  
 In this hell sentient beings fall into a swamp  or bog in which insects pierce and bore into 
their flesh and so forth right up to the marrow of their bones.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 Near the bog there is the third one, a pa th filled with razor-blade teeth. When 
sentient beings walk along it in search of an abode, as they put their foot down, 
their skin, flesh, and blood are cut, and as they lift it up they grow back again.  
 
In this hell, the sentient beings, looking for a pl ace in which to rest, walk on a road made of 
razors that cuts the skin, flesh, and bones of their feet, causing them much suffering. But as 
they lift their feet up, they are once again rest ored. In this way the same suffering is repeated 
over and over again.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 N e a r b y  t h e r e  i s  t h e  F o r e s t  o f  S w o r d - l e a v e s .  A s  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  w a l k  a r o u n d  i n  
search of an abode, when they make use of its shade and settle down, {78} swords 
emerge from the trees and chop up their limbs and other body parts. When they 
faint there, reddish-brown dogs come, grab them from behind and eat them. 
 In this forest the leaves of the trees are like swords. The sentient beings look for a place to rest and sit down in the shadow of these trees, whereupon swords come from the trees and cut their limbs to pieces. When they fall down in a faint, dogs come and grab them from behind 
and eat them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

274  
 N e a r b y  i t  i s  t h e  F o r e s t  o f  I r o n - S p i k e d Trees. Sentient beings walking around 
there in search of an abode set about clim bing up the trees. As they climb up, the 
tips of the thorns point downwards and as  they climb, down they point upwards, 
chopping up the limbs and other body part s. The birds with iron beaks that exist 
in that place alight on their shoulders or heads, peck out their eyeballs, and eat 
them.  
 In the forest or grove of shalmali or iron-spo ked trees, the sentient beings, looking for a place 
to rest, climb up the trees, whereupon their th orns point downward and pierce them. Then, 
when they climb back down, their thorns poin t upward and once again pierce and destroy 
their limbs and body parts. Bird s with iron beaks land on their shoulders or head, and peck 
out and eat their eyeballs.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 These places are considered to be one du e to the harm by weapons being similar. 
 
Because in these three neighboring hells – the Razor-fille d Plain, Grove of Sword Leaves, and Forest of Iron-
Spoked Trees, the sentient beings  are harmed by weapons, they are considered to be one.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 The fourth, near the Shalmali Forest, is the so-called River Without a Ford filled 
with fast moving, boiling water. Sentient beings in search of an abode fall into it 
and are cooked as they bob up and down like peas thrown into a cauldron filled with water over a great fire. On either ba nk of the river stand [guardians] holding 
sticks, hooks, and nets in their hands who do not allow anyone to get out. Having 
pulled them out with  the hooks or nets, [the guardians ] place them face upward 
on the blazing great foundation and ask th em what they want. When they say, “I 
don’t know and cannot see anything, however I am hungry and thirsty,” [the 
guardians ] put fiercely blazing lumps of iron into their mouths and pour boiling 
copper into their mouths. 
 
Near the Iron-spoked Forest is the River Withou t a Ford that cannot be crossed at any point. 
In it, the water flows very fast and is like mo lten iron. The hell beings, looking for a place to 
rest, fall into this river where th ey experience the great suffering of being cooked like peas in a 
cauldron of boiling water. However, in spite of  the intense suffering, these beings do not die 
and when they try to get out of the river, guardi ans on either bank of the river beat back them 
back with sticks. They pull the hell beings ou t of the river with hooks and nets, lie them on 
the burning foundation, and ask them what they want. When the hell beings reply, “I do not 
know anything. I cannot see anything. I am hung ry and thirsty,” the guardians put lumps of 
burning iron and boiling copper in their mouths an d tell them to eat and drink. In this way, 
the beings there experience unbearable suffering due to their karma.
