Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
275 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 The Actuality of the Stages  explains that the lifespan in the neighbouring and 
occasional hells is uncertain. However, suffe ring will be experienced as long as the 
potency of the karma for experiencing th ose sufferings has not been exhausted. 
 
The beings in the neighboring and occasion al hells do not have a fixed lifespan.  
 There is a story in a sutra about an occasion al hell in the human realm. At night it would 
t r a n s f o r m  i n t o  a n  o c c a s i o n a l  h e l l  i n  w h i c h  t h e  b e i n g s  f o u g h t  a n d  k i l l e d  e a c h  o t h e r ,  a n d  during the day it would return to its usual form  of a monastery where all the monks were very 
subdued and kind to each other. This was seen  by a bodhisattva who, when traveling, came 
across a monastery and asked to be allowed to  remain there for a few days. He was given 
permission to stay but was told to remain lock ed up in his room and not to look out at a 
certain time. However, he did not follow this ad vice and, looking out, saw that the rooms of 
the monastery had become iron houses that were  being burnt on all sides and that the beings 
inside where fighting and killing each other. He  was amazed by this, but a short time later it 
once again became a quiet monastery.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C The cold hells 
The third – the cold hells – are directly opposite the eight great [hot] hells of sentient 
beings each at a distance of 10,000 yojanas . {79} 32,000 yojanas below that there is 
the hell of Blisters and from there, se parated by 2,000 yojanas each, the other 
seven hells. Among those eight, in the on e of Blisters you are hit by strong icy 
wind and your body shrivels, while blisters appear on it everywhere. In the hell of 
Bursting Blisters you shrivel as the bl isters burst open. Chattering Teeth, 
Lamentation, and “Brrr” are named after th e sounds that are uttered. In Splitting 
Apart Like an Utpala Flower, due to being hit by strong icy wind, you turn blue in 
color and split apart into five or six pieces. In Splitting Apart Like a Lotus you pass beyond blue and become red, splitting apart into ten or more pieces. In Great Splitting Apart Like a Lotus your skin becomes deep red and you split apart into 
100 or even more pieces. All this is explained in Actuality of the Stages .  
 
The cold hells are described here much more br iefly than were the hot hells. The cold hells 
are located 10,000 yojanas from the hot hells. A yojana
115 is composed of 8 krosha, a krosha is 
composed of 500 armspans, an armspan is compos ed of four cubits, a cubit is composed of 24 
fingerwidths, and so forth, as explained in the Treasury of Manifest Dharma . 32,000 yojanas 
below that is the Blistering Hell. The other se ven cold hells are each located 2,000 yojanas 
below the previous one. In the Blistering Hell the body is struck by an extremely cold wind 
and blisters break out on the body. In Bursting Blisters, the blisters burst open into pieces.  
 In Chattering Teeth the beings cannot open their mouths, the teeth being clenched tight 
together due to the cold. In Lamentation or Gr oaning, the beings wail “What can we do?” In 
                                              
115 JN: approximately 7.2 kms 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
276 Brr or Moaning, they complain continually of the cold. These three he lls are named after the 
sounds the beings make.  
 In Splitting Like an Utpala a great wind blows,  turning the body blue and causing it to split 
into five or six pieces. In Splitting Like a Lotu s, the body becomes red and splits into ten or 
more pieces. In Great Splitting Like a Lotus, the body becomes dark red and splits into one 
hundred or more pieces. These facts are explained in Actuality of the Stages . Could we bear 
such suffering? Since we could not, we must av oid creating the cause of such rebirths – non-
virtuous actions. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 As for the lifespan, it is stated that if you were to fill a storage container up to the 
brim with eighty kharis of sesame seed s from Magadha and take out one sesame 
seed every hundred years, the lifespan in th e hell of Blisters would last longer than 
for the sesame to be completely exhausted,  and the lifespan in each hell below is 
twenty times that of the previous one. 
 
The lifespan increases by twenty in each of the successive cold hells. Because of this, having 
been born in the hell realm, it can seem that  one will never again be reborn in the higher 
realms. For this reason, we need to strive in our daily life to eliminate the causes of such 
rebirths. A cause of taking rebirt h in the cold hells is, for exampl e, to put small insects outside 
in the cold due to which they immediately die, to  throw animals such as cats or dogs in very 
cold water even as a joke, and so forth. These acts  will act as a cause for us to be reborn in the 
cold hells where we will be struck by very cold wi nd. If we experience such suffering, it is only 
because we have created the ka rma to be reborn there.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D The occasional hells 
The fourth: These exist in the vicini ty of the hot and cold hells. In Actuality of the 
Stages  it says that they also ex ist in human places, and in Basis of [Vinaya] Scriptures  
it is stated that they exist near the shores of great lakes, as it occurs in Expression of 
the Realizations of Sangharakshita .  
 
The occasional hells are located near the hot an d cold hells, but others also exist in the 
human realm. Another text says that they can be found on the shores of the ocean in the 
human realm. Sangharakshita was the monk who visited a the monastery and saw how it 
transformed into an occasional hell.  
 In our everyday life we create and collect many negative actions. However, it would be better to try to avoid creating negative actions and to try to create only virtuous actions, whereby we 
will come to experience happiness. On the other hand, if we only create negativities, we will experience suffering again and again. For this reason, we need to choose what we want to 
experience in the future and if  we wish to experience happi ness we must definitely stop 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 7-10 March 2005 (06) 
277 creating non-virtuous actions and strive to crea te virtuous actions. Ho wever, if you want to 
experience sufferings, you can do whatever you want! 
 
END 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
278 Monday afternoon, 14 March 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 79) says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1B2C-1A2 The need for fear at the thought that you have already accumulated numerous causes 
for being born there 
Since it is very easy for the causes of bi rth there, as will be explained below, to 
come about, whereby each day you also a ccumulate many of them, and since also 
those that you accumulated before are i mmeasurable, it is inappropriate to sit 
around happily. {80} Therefore, thinking  about them, you should be afraid, for 
nothing other than this unsteady resp iration separates you from them. From 
Engaging in [Bodhisattva] Behavior : 
 
How can you sit there happily, 
Having created the karma for hell? 
 Every day we accumulate negative and non-virtuous  actions – the causes fo r rebirth in the hell 
realm. Due to this, it is not right to sit abou t happily. Instead we should think about the fact 
that we have accumulated non-virtuous actions and generate the wish to purify them. Fearing 
their results when our breath comes to a stop, we  must refrain from engaging in actions that 
will bring us rebirth in the lower realms and crea te only the virtuous actions that will bring us 
rebirth in the upper realms.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And in the Letter to a Friend  it also says: 
 
Evildoers separated only 
By the time it takes for the breathing to stop, Hearing about the boundless torments of hell And not scared in thousand ways, are like vajras. 
 
If seeing, hearing, remembering, and reading Descriptions of hell as well as their depictions 
Generates fear, what need is there to speak 
Of experiencing the unbearable fully ripened effects. 
 
The intense suffering is also stated in the Letter to a Friend : 
 
Just as the bliss of extinguishment of craving Is supreme among all kinds of bliss, Likewise among all kinds of sufferings –  Those of Avici hell are the most unbearable. 
 
Here, if in one day three hundred spears Were to hit you brutally, the suffering 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
279 Would be small compared to the sufferings of hell, 
It would not even be a fraction of them. 
 
Having created heavy non-virtuous actions, as s oon as our breathing stops we will immediately 
be reborn in the hell realm. Even upon hearing about the sufferings of hells or seeing 
drawings, paintings, or statues of them, we gene rate fear, like, for example, when seeing the 
relieves of the eight cemeteries in the Nyingma Golden Temple in south India. Therefore, we 
should definitely generate fear when thinking  about the non-virtuous actions that we have 
done and strive to purify them. If we do this, we will not need to fear taking a bad rebirth since we will be reborn as a human being, anti-god, or god.   However, as long as we are born in cyclic ex istence we will experience suffering. We can see 
that even as human beings we experience wo rry, for example, about getting cancer, even 
though this pain is very small compared to th e much greater suffering of the hells where there 
is suffering all the time, day and night. Even th e gods of the form realm experience suffering, 
especially the suffering of the signs of death. Si nce wherever we are born in cyclic existence, 
we will experience suffering, we need to genera te the wish to pass beyond cyclic existence and 
go to the other shore of nirvana or the highest st ate of enlightenment. To do this we need to 
purify our negativities. Sufferings and negativities  have one quality: respectively they can be 
eliminated and purified.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
You should come to understand that the cause giving rise to such suffering is only 
your own faulty behavior and strive with all your might not to be tainted even by 
minor faulty behavior. From the same [text]: 
 
The seed of these non-virtuous effects consists In faulty behavior of body, speech, and mind. Strive with such resourcefulness that nothing, Not even a speck of them, is done by you. 
 Why do we experience such sufferings? It is due to having committed faulty or non-virtuous 
actions of body, speech, and mind in the past and not having purified them. Since these seeds 
abide in our mind, when the conditions arise they ripen and bring forth their result of 
suffering. Therefore, while we are intelligent and physically powerful we must put effort into 
purifying our negativities.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1B2C-1B Thinking about the sufferings of animals 
Second: The weaker animals are killed by more powerful ones. They become the 
chattels of gods and humans and lackin g freedom and being under the control of 
others, they are killed, beaten, an d harmed. It is explained in the  Actuality of the 
Stages  that owing to {81} accompanying humans and gods, they do not have 
another abode. The Commentary on the Treasury explains that their main abode is 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
280 the great ocean and that the others spread  out from there. Furthermore, they are 
born in darkness and in water, age ther e and die. They are worn out by heavy 
burdens, put to the plow, shorn, driven , and killed miserably by means of many 
different killing methods. They are torm ented by hunger, thirst, sun, and wind. 
They are persecuted by hunters and the like in many ways, whereby they are always 
terrified. Therefore, you should feel sadde ned and disquieted thinking of the many 
ways in which they suffer.  
 With respect to their lifespan, in the Treasury  it says that “the longest animal 
lifespan is one eon” and it is explained th at the long-lived ones attain a mere eon 
while the short lived ones are unspecified.  
 
Among the animals, the bigger ones kill and eat the smaller ones. For example, if we examine 
the animals we see around us in our daily life , such as the pigeons, swallows, cats, and so 
forth, we will see that the cats are constantly trying to catch the pigeons and other birds. Some 
animals, such as sheep, cows, horses, donkeys, buffaloes, elephants and so forth, are utilized 
by human beings as if they were things. Lack ing freedom and under others’ power, they are 
killed, beaten, and harmed by human beings. Animals live in the same places as gods and 
human beings, that is, they do not have a separa te abode. There are some that take rebirth in 
very dark places and some that are born in th e water, where they age and die. Some animals 
are used by human beings to carry heavy burdens. Others are made to pull plows, others have their wool shorn, and so forth. They are killed in many different ways, for example, by cutting 
off their head, cutting open their stomachs, being shot, and so forth. They also experience the 
sufferings of thirst, hunger, great heat, strong  wind, of being hunted, and so forth. Human 
beings often rejoice, like crazy people, in having killed a helpless animal.   In short, animals suffer in many ways every day. We should think, “If I were born as an 
animal, would I be able to bear it?” We should meditate on what it would be like to actually 
be born in this state. Since we could not bear it, we need to abandon creating its cause – non-
virtuous actions.  
 Some animals live for one eon while others  only live for one day. Therefore, the Treasury of 
Manifest Dharma  says that the lifespan of animals is uncertain.  
 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1B2C-1C Thinking about the sufferings of hungry ghosts 
Third: Those who predominan tly attend to miserliness will be born as hungry 
ghosts. They too have hunger and thirst . Since their skin, flesh, and blood are 
dried up, they are like burnt logs. Their faces are covered with hair. Their mouths 
are exceedingly dry and they lick them with their tongues.  
 
A Those with external obstructions to food and drink 
B Those with internal obstructions to food and drink 
C Those with obstructions to food and drink itself  
There are three of them: those with external  obstructions to food and drink, those 
with internal obstructions to food and dr ink, and those with obstructions to food 
and drink itself.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
281 Here the text clearly explains the suffering of  hungry ghosts. The cause of such rebirth is 
miserliness – being tight-fisted and unable to give to others. Hungry ghosts experience the 
suffering of hunger and thirst. Their skin is dr ied up, like a burnt log. Their faces are covered 
with hair, blocking their vision, and they are not intelligent enou gh to tie it back out of the 
way. They are very thirsty. There are three types of hungry ghosts:  
4. those with external obstructions to food and drink,  
5. those with internal obstructions to food and drink, and  
6. those with obstructions to food and drink itself. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim says:  
A Those with external obstructions to food and drink  
As for the first ones, if they wander towa rds springs, lakes, and ponds, they are 
hindered externally by sentient beings holding swords, spears, and lances and, 
seeing the water as pus and blood, they do not want to drink it.  
 When the hungry ghosts with external obstructions see water, such as a lake and so forth, in the distance, they go toward it, but when they arrive nearby they see beings holding swords, 
spears, and so forth who block their approach. Also  what appeared to be large river or lake in 
the distance is only a dry river bed or lake bed when they arrive there. Likewise, if they see trees laden with fruit in the distance and approa ch them, when they get there all they see are 
dried up trees without any leaves or fruit. Th is is all due to their specific karma.  
 It is said that when a human being sees a cu p of water, a god sees a cup of nectar and a 
hungry ghost sees a cup of pus and blood. Likewise, if the human beings drinks it, he experiences water; if the god drinks it, he expe riences nectar; and if the hungry ghost drinks 
it, he experiences blood and pus. This is also due to their karma.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Those with internal obstructions to food and drink  
The second ones have mouths like the me re eye of a needle. Their mouths blaze 
with fire, and they have goiters and big be llies. Even if they end up finding food 
and drink without others creating obstruct ions, they are not able to eat and drink 
them. 
 
The hungry ghosts with internal  obstructions have mouths that  are the size of the eye of a 
needle. Flames of fire come from their mouths . They have huge goit ers on their necks and 
enormous bellies. Even though they are not hi ndered by other beings, due to their karma 
even when they find food they cannot enjoy it  because of their small mouths, the fire coming 
from their mouths, or their goiters.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Those with obstructions to food and drink itself 
As for the third ones, the so-called “fire- garlanded ones” get burned due to {82} 
everything they eat and drink catching fire. The so-called “filth-eaters” eat 
excrement and drink urine. They only eat and drink that which is unclean, smelly, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
282 harmful, and despicable. Some cut their own flesh and eat it. They cannot make 
use of clean and good food or drink.  
 
Whatever these hungry ghosts eat or drink, it  catches fire and burns them. Others eat food 
that has transformed into excrement, and dr ink that has transformed into urine. This 
happens due to their having created certain karm a in the past, whereby they experience such 
suffering without any choice.  
 Whatever they eat and drink, it becomes uncl ean and filthy. Not being able to find food, 
some of them cut their own flesh and eat it. Th ey are unable to enjoy or use clean or good 
food and drink due to the heavy negative karm a they created in the past, mainly out of 
miserliness. Presently we are human beings who have many enjoyments, but if we are miserly 
and, instead of practicing generosity, keep ever ything for ourselves, we too will be born as 
hungry ghosts. Could we bear this? We could no t, therefore we need to purify its causes. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 Their abode, as explained in the Commentary on the  Treasury , is below the city of 
Rajagriha in Jambudvipa, separated by a distance of 500 yojan as, and from there 
the other hungry ghosts have spread out. As for their life span, it says in the 
Actuality of the Stages  that one human month is a da y and that they reach 500 of 
their years. In the Letter to a Friend  is says: 
 
Attending to suffering uninterruptedly, 
Fettered by the thick rope Of karma of faulty behavior, some embodied beings  Do not die for five or even ten thousand years. 
 
The commentary explains that the lifespan of some hungry ghosts is 5,000 years 
and of some 10,000 years. 
 In all three bad migrations th e body size is indefinite. In Actuality of the Stages  it 
says that through the power of the non- virtues there are all kinds of sizes. 
 
Where do the hungry ghosts live? They are said  to live 500 yojanas below Rajagriha. In the 
Torma Offering to Kalarupa  i t  s a y s  t h a t  t h e  L o r d  o f  D e a t h  a b i d e s  i n  t h e  l a n d  o f  t h e  h u n g r y 
ghosts, seven levels below the ground. This is the main place in whic h hungry ghosts live, 
others spread out from there. How long do they live for? Some hungry ghosts live for 5,000 years and others live for 10,000 years.   The body size of the hell beings, hungry ghos ts, and animals is indefinite, there being some 
that are extremely tall and some that are very sm all. We see, for example, big animals such as 
elephants, middling animals such as cows and bu ffaloes, and small animals like cats and so 
forth. Similarly, there are hungry ghosts that are very big and those that are very small.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-1B2C-2 How it is necessary to meditate on this until the experience is produced 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
283 In accordance with that, when you think about the sufferings of bad migrations 
reflect: “If it would be difficult for me to sit for one day with my hands stuck in 
embers, or to just stay in a freezing cave during a winter storm without clothes, or 
to be without food and drink for a few days, or for my body to be bitten by 
something like a bee, how could I bear the sufferings of the hot hells, the cold 
hells, the hungry ghosts, and the animal s who devour one another alive?” You 
should meditate with respect to such experiences until a change of mind of intense 
{83} fear and terror occurs. Through thin king about this now with a good base, 
what has been accumulated previously is pu rified and what you will engage in later 
on lessens. Through intense aspirational pr ayers, direct the virtue that has been 
accumulated previously and thereby increa se it manifold. Since this opens up 
many avenues for engaging in them newly,  every day one will make the leisures 
meaningful. If you do not think about this  now, once you have fallen into the bad 
migrations, even if you look for it, you will not find a refu ge that protects you from 
those fears. At that time, you will lack the power of mind to take on what should 
be done and reject what should no t be done. This is mentioned in Engaging in 
[Bodhisattva] Behavior . 
 
We should think about such sufferings, trying to  imagine what it would be like to experience 
them ourselves and asking ourselves whether or not we could bear to be reborn in the bad 
migrations. For example, could we bear the suffering of putting our hands in embers for one day? We could not. Could we bear staying outsid e during the winter in a strong wind without 
any clothes on even for a single night? We coul d not. If we had nothin g to eat or drink even 
just for three or four days, could be bear it? Most of us could not. If we were to be stung by insects such as bees, mosquitoes, and so forth, could we bear it? We could not. Could we bear 
being eaten alive by another animal? We could not. This being so, how could we bear the 
sufferings of the hell beings, hungry ghosts, an d animals? Thus, if we examine the sufferings 
of the beings of the three lower realms and as k ourselves whether we could bear them, we will 
find that we could not. Therefore, now that we have this opportunity, this precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities, we should not waste it. Generating fear of the 
sufferings of the bad migrations and realizing that  we could not bear them, a change occurs in 
our mind. Due to this, we will engage in purify ing the negativities that we accumulated in the 
past and will not engage much less in creati ng non-virtuous actions in the future. For 
example, if today we develop an experience of  this strong suffering in our meditation, the 
next day we will try to refrain from creating  non-virtuous actions and to purify the non-
virtuous actions that we have already created in the past. We will also make strong prayers 
that direct and increase whatever virtue we have  already accumulated. This will open up many 
doors to making our life meaningful by practici ng virtue every day again and again. Since this 
is what will make our precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities meaningful, 
we must do it. If instead we do not think about or  do this, in our next life we will fall into the 
hell realm, hungry ghost realm, or animal real m where we will experience intense sufferings. 
At that time, even if we search for a refuge we wi ll not find it. In other words, if we waste this 
present opportunity we will fall into the bad migr ations where we will not find an object of 
refuge even if we look for it. Right now we have the power of mind to be able to examine what kind of actions are to be done and what kind of actions are not to be done, but if we 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
284 were reborn in the animal realm, for example, we would not be able to do this. Having been 
born there, it will be too late to engage in su ch practices. Therefore, right now we need to put 
effort into it.  
 
This concludes the subject of th e sufferings of the lower realms. Next we must make ensure 
that we find happiness in our future lives. 
 
(Oral transmission  started 7:10 PM) 
 
Tuesday morning, 15 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 83) says:  
Explanation of how to rely on the meth ods for the happiness of future worlds 
2B4B-2B1A-2 Explanation of how to rely on the methods for the happiness of future worlds 
A Training in going for refuge: the holy gate for entering the teachings  
B The root of all well-being: generating the confidence of firm conviction in actions and [their] 
effects  
The second one, [how to rely on the methods for the happiness of future existences]  has two 
points: training to go for refuge: the holy  gate for entering the teachings, and the 
root of all well-being116: generating the confidence of  firm conviction in actions 
and [their] effects. 
 
This section sets out how to rely on the method s for achieving happiness in future lives. The 
first of these is the door of entry into Buddh ism – going for refuge to the Three Jewels and 
training in this. To enter into Buddhism we must take the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha as 
our final refuge, while still being able to take temporary refuge in those gods, human beings, 
and so forth who can protect us in the meantime if we ask them for help. In other words, we 
can ask worldly gods such as Brahma, Indra, an d others for some kind of temporary help, but 
we cannot take them as our final refuge. 
 Since all happiness and excellence (well-be ing) comes from generating belief and 
confidence in the law of cause and result, that  is, actions and their results, we need to 
generate faith in it and then abide by this law.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A Training in going for refuge: the holy gate for entering the teachings 
                                              
116 The Tibetan expression, which is consistently translated  as “well-being” here, consists of a combination of 
the two syllables bde and legs (lit. “happy–good”), bde referring to the happiness of happy, i.e., higher, realms, 
and legs referring to “certain goodness,” i.e., liberation fr om cyclic existence or enlightenment. Thus “well-
being” implies both temporary and ultimate happiness. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
285 1 The causes on which going for refuge depends  
2 Depending on them, the objects to which to go for refuge 
3 The way in which to go for refuge as much as one can 
4 The stages of training after having gone for refuge 
The first one has four points: the causes  on which going for refuge depends; 
depending on that, the objects in which to  go for refuge; the way in which to go 
for refuge as much as one can; and the st ages of training after having gone for 
refuge. 
 
First, what are the main causes for going for refuge? Then in dependence on these causes, 
what are the objects of refuge? Then, how do we go for refuge to them? Then, having gone for 
refuge, what are the precepts or trainings of refuge that are to be kept?  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A1 The causes on which going for refuge depends 
First: Although, in general, there are many  causes, here it is the fact that, as 
explained above, we do not stay in this li fe but soon die and that after death we 
will not have control over where we will be reborn but will be controlled by our 
karma. Also karma, according to Engaging in [Bodhisattva ] Behavior , is [like this]: 
 
As a flash of lightning at night amidst the clouds 
Illuminates the darkness for a moment, 
Likewise on the road, through the power of buddhas 
A rare understanding of merit comes forth in the worldly. Therefore, that which is virtuous is always weak, While that which is evil is extremely strong and unbearable. 
 {84} Thus, by thinking about how you will fall into the bad migrations due to the 
power of wholesome karma being small and that of unwholesome karma being 
extremely strong, the two arise: fear of th e bad migrations and the conviction that 
that which is rare and precious,
117 [the Jewels ], can protect you from them. 
Consequently, if these two remain but words, going for refuge will be similar. 
However, if they are present intensely and fi rmly, going for refuge will also come to 
tame the mind. Therefore, you should strive in those two causes. 
 Presently we have obtained a good human rebirth due to having created its causes in our past lives. However, we will not remain alive for long , but will soon die. When we die, where will 
we go? We will not have any control or choice over where we will be reborn, but will be completely under the control of our karma. Ther efore, we should carefully examine our mind 
in terms of what kind of motivations are directing our actions of body, speech, and mind. If our motivation is always good, we will create virtuous actions of body, speech, and mind, whereby our future rebirth will also be good. On the other hand, if we create non-virtuous 
actions of body, speech, and mind, without choice  we will take rebirth in the lower realms.  
                                             
 
117 dkon mchog , “rare (and) precious/most excellent” is the word  used for “jewel” in the context of going for 
refuge. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
286  Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  says that just as when lightning flashes at night it only 
briefly illuminates the surroundings, similarly by the power of the buddhas we occasionally 
generate a short-lived virtuous mind, whereby we create merit and gain some wisdom. In 
general our virtuous actions are quite small an d weak, whereas when we  create non-virtuous 
actions even in our daily life they are very heav y. Thus, our white or virtuous actions are very 
weak, whereas our non-virtuous or  black actions are very strong. Due to this we will fall into 
the lower realms. Thinking about this, we should  generate fear of the sufferings of the lower 
realms. Then what should we do? We should  look for someone who can protect us. The 
Three Jewels, the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, ha ve the power to protect us. Therefore, we 
should generate complete belief or conviction in them.  
 Thus, there are two main causes of going for re fuge. The first one is fear, mainly fear of the 
sufferings of the lower realms. The second is fa ith or confidence that  the Three Jewels can 
protect us from these sufferings. Thus, if we recite “I go for refuge to the Buddha. I go for refuge to the Dharma. I go for refuge to the Sangha,” without fear of  the sufferings of the 
lower realms and without faith that the Three Jewels can protect us from them, they will 
remain mere words without much power. On the other hand, if our fear and faith is very strong, so too will our going for refuge be strong  and come from our heart. Therefore, initially 
we need to develop these two causes.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A2 Depending on them, the objects to which to go for refuge 
A Identifying the objects [of refuge]  
B The reasons why they are worthy to be refuges 
The second one has two points: identifying the objects [of refuge] and the reasons 
why they are worthy to be refuges. 
 
We need to recognize the entity of the objects of refuge and understand why they are correct 
objects of refuge.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A2A Identifying the objects [of refuge] 
First: In the Praise in One Hundred Fifty Verses  it says:  
 
To one in whom perpetually 
All faults are definitely absent, To one in whom in every manner, All good qualities abide;  If someone like that exists, It is fit to go for refuge to him, To praise him, to attend on him, To abide in what he teaches. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
287 Thus, if someone has the intelligence to di fferentiate between what is a refuge and 
what is not a refuge, it is appropriate fo r him to go for refuge to the undeceiving 
protector, the Buddha Bhagavan. This also illustrates the Dharma Jewel and 
Sangha Jewel. From the Seventy Verses on Going for Refuge : 
 
Buddha, Dharma, Sangha are 
The refuge of those who want liberation. 
 If there exists someone in whom all faults are absent and all good qualities are present, we 
should take refuge in him. Therefore, we shou ld examine the qualities of a buddha, eight of 
which are mentioned in Sublime Continuum . In short, a buddha is one who has abandoned all 
the objects of abandonment and has comp leted great compassion (the mind of 
enlightenment), wisdom, and ability or power. Du e to this, a buddha is able to help us. For 
this reason, the Buddha is one of the main obje cts of refuge. Thus, it is suitable for human 
beings who have a mind and are intelligent to go  for refuge to him, praise and respect him, 
and try to abide by his teachings.   Those who have wisdom should differentiate what  is an object of refuge and what is not, 
meaning what is and is not a final object of re fuge. The Buddha is an object of refuge because 
he never deceives us. We need to go for refu ge to him if we want to achieve buddhahood. 
 Similarly, we should also go for refuge to  the Dharma Jewel and Sangha Jewel. Those who 
wish to attain liberation should take the Bu ddha, Dharma, and Sangha as their objects of 
refuge. An example of the Buddha Jewel is Bu ddha Shakyamuni, the Dharma Jewel is true 
paths and true cessations, and the Sangha Jewe l is arya beings. That other objects are not 
objects of refuge means that they are not fi nal objects of refuge, although they can be 
temporary objects of refuge.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A2B The reasons why they are worthy to be refuges 
1 The actual reasons  
Second: Someone who is free from all fear , skilled in the means of liberating 
others from fear, steeped in great compa ssion toward everyone without closeness 
and distance, and who works for the welfare of all those who help and do not help 
h i m ,  s u c h  a  o n e  i s  w o r t h y  t o  b e  a  r e f u g e .  A s  o n l y  t h e  B u d d h a  h a s  t h o s e  
[qualifications], while Ishvara and the like do not, he is a refuge. For that reason, 
the Dharma taught by him as well as the Sa ngha of hearers are also {85} worthy to 
be refuges. 
 A buddha possesses four qualities. First of all, he  himself is free from all fear. In other words, 
a buddha is completely free from all fears due to having attained the quality of the four 
fearlessnesses. He is also free from fear because he has abandoned all the objects of 
abandonment – the afflictive obscurations, obsc urations to knowledge, the obscuration to 
absorption, karmic obscurations, and fruitional obscurations.   A buddha is also skilled in the means or methods for liberating others from fear. He knows all the different methods necessary for people of different mentalities to become free from 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
288 fear and then teaches them these methods. Fear  cannot be removed with the hand, nor can it 
be washed away with water; it can only be  removed by giving teachings to others. 
 A buddha also has great compassion for all sentient beings. He does not hold some 
sentient beings as near and some as far but has the same compassion for all of them. For 
example, in Dharmakirti’s Commentary on Dignaga’s ‘Com pendium of Valid Cognition’  it says that 
a buddha has the same attitude toward someone who is cutting the flesh of one side of his 
body and to someone else who is massaging the other side of his body with fragrant 
sandalwood oil. We, on the other hand, who do  not have great compassion, like those who 
help us, our friends, and do not like those wh o do not help us. A buddha does not have such 
partiality.  
 A buddha also acts for the welfare of both those who help him and those who do not help 
him. It is not that he only benefits those who help him and does not benefit those who do 
not help him.  Only a buddha possesses these four qualities;  they are not possessed by the eight mundane 
gods such as Ishvara, Mahadeva , Brahma and so forth, who ar e described in various texts. 
Although these gods are very proud, they bo w down before a buddha because they do not 
have such qualities.   Thus, we go for refuge to a buddha who has thes e four qualities. We also go for refuge to 
the Dharma that he teaches and that we deve lop in our mental continuum. We also go for 
refuge to his disciples, such as the hearers,  who are the Sangha. Th ere are eight types of 
sangha, ranging from approachers to stream enterers through ab iders in foe-destroyer, that 
can be further divided into twenty. We should go for refuge to all of them.  The actual refuge is the Dharma. If we list en to teachings, memorize them, and meditate 
on them, we will develop the path, for exampl e, the mind of renunciation or definite 
emergence that wishes to emerge from cyclic exis tence. In this way, we will achieve the path of 
accumulation, then the path of preparation, path  of seeing, path of meditation, and path of 
no-more-learning. This is how we need to practice.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Advice to be persistent in conviction, the principal cause  
That being so, once you have derived cert ainty with regard to these [objects of 
refuge] set forth in the Compendium , since when you are able to rely on them with a 
single-pointed mind, it is impossible for yo u not to be protected, generate certainty 
from the bottom of your heart. Of the two causes for being protected, there is no 
incompleteness in the external cause since our Teacher, [the Buddha,] has already 
achieved it, however since the internal cause,  taking hold of that refuge trustingly, 
has not come about, that is why we suffer.  
 
We should set our mind single-pointedly on  the three objects of refuge – the Buddha, 
Dharma, and Sangha – as in this way they will be  able to protect us. In other words, if we, 
from our own side, deeply go for refuge, there is no doubt that the Bu ddha and so forth will 
definitely protect us. Therefore, we need to gene rate confidence or certainty in them from the 
bottom of our heart.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
289  For three countless eons Buddha Shakyamuni accumulated and completed the collections 
of merit and wisdom and attained buddhahood. Th erefore, there is no lack in the external 
object or condition for refuge. However, if ther e the internal cause for refuge is absent, the 
Buddha will not be able to help us. On the ot her hand, if we have completely confidence 
from the depths of our heart in the three obje cts of refuge, they can help us. However, we 
tend not to be completely confident in the Three Jewels, due to which we continue to 
experience physical, mental, an d both physical and mental su ffering. There are many causes 
and conditions for experiencing unhappiness and we have created all of them. There is no 
point to thinking that our unhappiness comes fr om other people – our parents or our friends 
and so forth, and not from our own mind. This  is wrong. While some mothers even blame 
their daughters for their suffering and physical  sicknesses, the real cause is the mental 
afflictions, – attachment, anger, pride, jealou sy, and so forth. These are what cause us to 
engage in bad or negative actions, wh ich ripen in the result of suffering.  
 In short, we suffer because we do not have co mplete confidence in the Three Jewels. In this 
way we cheat ourselves. We should thin k, “I will never do that again!”

2B4B-2B1A-2A3A Understanding their positive qualities 
1 The positive qualities of the Buddha 
2 The positive qualities of the Dharma 
3 The positive qualities of the sangha 
First: Since it is necessary to be mindful of  the positive qualities of the refuges, this 
has three points: the positive qualities of the Buddha, the positi ve qualities of the 
Dharma, and the positive qualities of the Sangha. 
 
First we need to recognize the qualities of the ob jects of refuge. The qualities of the body of a 
buddha consist of the 112 signs and exemplif ications: the thirty-two signs and eight 
exemplifications. The qualities of his speech cons ist of the sixty-four melodious qualities. The 
qualities of his mind consist of the twenty-one groups of exalted wisdoms. The qualities of his 
enlightened activities consist of the twenty -seven enlightened activities. We need to 
understand each one of these qualities.

2B4B-2B1A-2A3A-1 The positive qualities of the Buddha

290 1 The positive qualities of [the Buddha’s] body 
2 The positive qualities of [the Buddha’s] speech 
3 The positive qualities of [the Buddha’s] mind

The first one has four points: the positive qualities of [the Buddha’s] body, speech, 
mind, and exalted activities.  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3A-1A1 The positive qualities of [the Buddha’s] body 
First: You should be mindful of the signs and exemplifications of a buddha. In the 
Praise in Analogies  it says:  
 
Your body is adorned with the signs, 
Beautiful, nectar for the eyes, Like a cloudless sky in autumn Ornamented with clusters of stars. 
 
O Muni, endowed with the color of gold, 
Beautiful, dressed in monastic robes, 
Like a golden mountain peak Wrapped in clouds at dusk or dawn.  O Protector, the splendor of your facial orb, 
Although unadorned by jewelry, 
Is beyond that of the moon disc, Although vast and clear of clouds.  If the lotus of your mouth And a lotus in bloom in the sun  Are beheld by a bee, it Will have its doubts about the lotus.  Your face endowed with the color of gold, White teeth making it [more] beautiful, {86} Like immaculate autumn moonbeams Filtering through golden mounts. 
 
Venerable one, your right hand, Embellished by the sign of the wheel Gives breath to the people Frightened by cycle of existence. 
 
O Muni, as you walk, your feet Draw what looks like lotuses of Glory on the earth [you tread]. With lotuses how would it be lovelier? 
 You should remember them in ac cordance with this statement. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
291  
A buddha’s body is adorned with the signs and exemplifications. He is beautiful, nectar for the eyes, like a cloudless autumn sky filled with stars; his body gold in color, adorned with the 
three robes of a monk, like a golden mountain peak enveloped in clouds; his face naturally 
beautiful though unadorned with jewelry, sple ndid like the full moon free from clouds; his 
face making whoever sees it happy, like a lotus th at opens at sunrise allowing the bees to take 
the pollen inside; golden face with beautiful t eeth, like a golden mountain splendid in the 
autumn moonlight; his right hand marked with  a Dharma wheel relieving sentient beings 
from the fear of the suffering of cyclic existe nce; his feet not touchi ng the ground when he 
walks yet leaving behind the imprint of a lotus in  the ground and rocks; his body like a lake 
beautified with lotus flowers.  
 The Buddha had a disciple called Legpe Karma who said that even th ough he had stayed 
with the Buddha for many years he had not seen an y qualities in him. Instead he said that the 
Buddha only cheated thos e who paid respect and made offeri ngs to him by prophesying that 
they would achieve certain qualities and so fort h. However, he too said that the Buddha’s 
body was beautiful!  
 The main qualities of a buddha are se t out in the eighth  chapter of the Ornament for Clear 
Realizations . You should try to understand them more deeply.  
 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3A-1A2 The positive qualities of [the Buddha’s] speech 
Second: You should think about the very marvelous way in which when sentient 
beings in the realms of the world ask di fferent questions at the same time, he 
apprehends them with wisdom that possesse s a single instant of mind and answers 
all the questions with one utterance that  they understand in their respective 
languages. In the Chapter of the Truthful One  it says:  
 
If thus all beings, in a single moment, 
Ask a question with many terms, His mind comprehends them in a single moment And answers them all with one melodious [statement].  Thus know that the melodious Brahma voice Of him who gives instructions, rings forth in this world. He thoroughly set in moti on the wheel of Dharma 
That eradicates the sufferings of gods and men. 
 You should reflect along the lines of this statement. 
 When many people ask the Buddha different questions at the same time, the Buddha can reply to all of them at the same time in a comp lete way with even just one phrase. This is one 
of the particular qualities of a buddha’s sp eech. For example, when the Buddha taught 
“Compounded phenomena are impermanent,” some of the listeners heard “All contaminated 
aggregates are suffering,” some heard “All contaminated aggregates  are empty of being 
permanent, partless, and independent,” and so me heard “All contaminated aggregates are 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
292 selfless.” In this way, with one phrase the Buddha was able to teach different things to 
different sentient beings in accordance with their needs. It is said that even one word can be 
understood in different ways by different disciples who thereby receive a particular 
instruction. Due to this, everyone is able to understand the Buddha’s speech very easily. Also 
those who know human language s hear the response in thei r human languages, those who 
know the gods’ languages hear it in their god la nguages, and so forth with respect to the anti-
gods and hungry ghosts. Therefore, even though  the Buddha only teaches in one language, all 
those around him understand him in their own language. Thus, the Chapter of the Truthful 
One says that if 100 people, for example, were to  simultaneously ask 100 different questions, 
the Buddha could respond to all of  them with a single phrase.   
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3A-1A3 The positive qualities of [the Buddha’s] mind 
A The positive quality of knowledge  
B The positive quality of mercy 
The third one has two points: the positive  quality of knowledge and the positive 
quality of mercy. 
 
In this context, two qualities of the mind of  a buddha are mentioned: the quality of his 
exalted knowledge and the quality of his mercy. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
A The positive quality of knowledge 
First: Since all knowable objects – modes and varieties – are engaged by the Muni’s 
unobstructed knowledge, like a dhatri  berry118 placed on the palm of his hand, the 
diverse knowable objects are pervaded by the Muni’s knowledge. However, they are 
not pervaded by others’ [knowledge ] as knowable objects are vast and their 
consciousnesses are narrow.  
 
The buddha knows all objects of knowledge – both  ultimate truths (modes) and conventional 
truths (varieties). These two truths are differe nt, yet a buddha sees them simultaneously. He 
directly and simultaneously sees the ultimate tr uth and the varieties of objects, for example, 
the different human beings sitting here in this room, like seeing a flower in the palm of his hand. His knowledge is not obstructed, whereas we, for example, cannot see what is beyond 
the walls of this room. A buddha’s knowledge also pervades all the phenomena in all the world systems. In other words, he knows ev erything. The minds of human beings, on the 
other hand, are not like this as, due to ou r minds being narrow and small, they do not 
pervade all phenomena. If we examine our mind , we will realize that we do not even know 
what is behind us! We also do not know what is inside us, our inner organs, and whether they 
are healthy or damaged, big or small, and so fort h. Since we are obscured even as to what is 
inside our body, there is no need to ment ion that our knowledge does not pervade all 
phenomena!  
                                             
 
118 A berry with a somewhat transparent skin that allows for the veins, seeds, and pulp to shine through. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
293  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In this regard it says in Praise of the Praiseworthy :  
 
Only your exalted wisdom, 
{87} Encompasses all the knowable objects; 
For all the others apart from you, 
Some knowable objects remain to be known. 
 And:  
Bhagavan, the phenomena of all times, 
All aspects, all places of origin, 
Are the sphere of activity of your mind, 
Like a dhatri  berry in the palm of your hand. 
 
With regard to phenomena, static or mobile, Unitary or various and distinct, There is nothing to which your mind is obstructed – Just like wind moving to and fro in space. 
 You should reflect in accordance with this statement. 
 A buddha’s knowledge pervades all objects of kn owledge, that is, all phenomena, whereas the 
knowledge of other beings does not. The phenom ena of all times, all aspects, and all places 
can be directly seen by a buddha, for example, like seeing a myrobalan fruit in the palm of his hand. This fruit is sometimes said to be kind of transparent fruit, the inside of which can be 
seen from the outside, however I do not think th is is true as I saw this fruit one time in 
Kopan Monastery.  
 Whether phenomena move or not and whether th ey are single or various, a buddha sees all 
of them individually. Like the wind moving in  space, a buddha’s mind is not obstructed by 
anything. Thus, even though we try to hide so mething from them, they can see it, whereas we, 
on the contrary, cannot see something even if it is  just covered with a piece of cloth. When we 
become a buddha, we too will be able to see things in this way.  
 Tuesday afternoon, 15 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 87) says:  
B The positive quality of mercy 
Second: Just as sentient beings are boun d by the afflictions without any control, 
the Muni is bound by great compassion  without control and therefore great 
compassion continuously arises in him when he perceives suffering migrating 
beings. In the One Hundred and Fifty Verses  it says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
294  
All of these migrating beings 
Are equally bound by the afflictions. 
In order to untie their afflictions 
You have long been bound by compassion. 
 Should I first prostrate to you 
Or to that which causes you  
To stay in samsara for long, aware Of its faults – great compassion? 
 
In the Chapter of the Truthful One  it also says: 
 
When he sees the many beings always 
Obscured by ignorance and mental darkness 
Who are imprisoned in the prison of samsara, Compassion is born in the holy sage. 
 
You should remember [mercy] in accordance with these statements.  
 
A buddha possesses the quality of mercy. Beca use all sentient beings are bound by the 
afflictions without any power, they experience suffering. Similarly, Buddha Shakyamuni’s 
continuum is bound without any power by great compassion and mercy, that is, his mind is 
overcome by compassion and mercy, due to which he wishes to liberate all the sentient beings who, bound by their afflictions,  are experiencing various types of suffering. Thus, a buddha 
continually possesses great compassion in his co ntinuum, due to which he looks at all times 
upon sentient beings, wishing to help and take care of them.  
 One Hundred and Fifty Verses  says that the buddhas, seeing that all migrating beings are 
bound by the afflictions, wish to  liberate them. In this sense, their minds have been bound by 
compassion for a long time. Due to this, they wish  to take care of sentient beings by enabling 
them to eliminate their sufferings and the causes  of sufferings, and to create the virtue and 
generate the good minds that bring good results.  
 Then the question is posed as to whom or to what to prostrat e, that is, to the buddhas or 
to the great compassion in their continuum. Wh en the buddhas see sentient beings who have 
faults, negativities, and bad qualities, they gene rate the great compassion  that wishes to take 
care of all sentient beings. This is the jo b of all buddhas since they have taken upon 
themselves the responsibility to free sentient be ings from suffering and set them in liberation 
or omniscience. Sentient beings are imprisoned  in the prison of cyclic existence by the 
ignorance or darkness that obscures their mi nd. Thus, the main cause of the suffering of 
birth, death, things getting better, things getting worse, and so forth is ignorance or 
confusion. For this reason, sentient beings  need to be liberated from ignorance.  
 The main cause of rebirth in cyclic existence is  ignorance, this being the first of the twelve 
links. It acts as the cause of projecting or th rowing karma. This karma leaves an imprint on 
the consciousness which remains, without going to  waste, in the form of a seed or imprint. 
When someone is approaching death, the afflic tions – craving and gras ping – arise. They 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
295 cause an imprint that was left on the consciou sness to be activated. This link is called 
“existence” or “becoming”; it is an actualizing karma. Among the twelve links, there are three 
actualizing causes for taking rebirth: cravin g, grasping, and becomi ng. The three links of 
ignorance, karmic formations, and consciousne ss are projecting causes. The four links of 
name and form, six sources, contact, and feelin g are the projected results. The two links of 
birth and aging-death are actualized results. In th i s  w a y ,  w e  t r a v e l  o r  t a k e  r e b i r t h  i n  c y c l i c  
existence over and over again. Ignorance is always  at the root of this, th erefore it is the root 
that causes us to wander in cyclic existence.  Therefore, we need to  eliminate it. How? By 
understanding the nature of phenomena – the se lflessness of persons and the selflessness of 
phenomena. From the point of vi ew of the lower tenets, the self lessness of persons is the lack 
of a self-supporting substantially existent se lf. However, from the point of view of the 
Prasangika Madhyamaka school the selflessness of persons is the lack of inherent existence, or 
existence from its own side, of the person, wh ereas the lack of inherent existence of the 
aggregates is the selflessness of phenomena.  Selflessness is also called “emptiness,” the 
realization of which can cut the root of cy clic existence – ignora nce or confusion.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3A-1A4 The positive qualitie s of [the Buddha’s] exalted activities 
Fourth: By means of both the spontaneous and uninterrupted exalted activities of 
his body, speech, and mind, he benefits all sentient beings. In fact, insofar as a 
trainee is fit for guidance, it is impossible for the Muni not to give that trainee 
what is excellent and pull him out of his de generation. That is why it is stated that 
they certainly perform all the appropriate actions. {88}  
 
The enlightened activities of the buddha s’ exalted body, speech, and mind arise 
spontaneously and uninterruptedly, whereby a buddha strives constantly to benefit all 
sentient beings. Thus, from the side of the bu ddhas they are constantly checking what they 
can do to ripen and help sentient beings. Th ey are always ready to act for our benefit. 
However, if we from our side are not ripe, that is, are not yet ready to be brought to the side 
of liberation and are still heading toward the side of cyclic existence with a mind 
overwhelmed by afflictions, they will not be able to help us. In this case, we would be like, for example, a child who does not want to study an d go to school, college, or university, and, 
even though the family tries to help him, he does not listen and perhaps even leaves home 
and starts to smoke, steal, and so forth. In this way, the child’s mind cannot be subdued and 
so it is difficult to bring him to the side of go od. Similarly, only if someone is mentally ready 
o r  r i p e  c a n  a  b u d d h a  h e l p  h i m  o r  h e r  a n d  b r i n g  t h a t  p e r s o n  t o w a r d  t h e  s i d e  o f  enlightenment. Thus, although th e buddhas do whatever they can to help sentient beings, if 
we are not ripe they will not be able to do anything to help us. In short, if sentient beings can be helped the buddhas help them, but if th ey cannot be helped the buddhas cannot do 
anything. For example, just as the orange tree outside in the courtyard appears to be laden 
with delicious fruit, but in reality the oranges ar e very bitter and inedible, similarly, there are 
b e i n g s  w h o  e x t e r n a l l y  a p p e a r  t o  b e  v e r y  n i c e  but inside their minds are like a rock and 
nothing can be done to change them. Thus, ev en though the buddhas are always ready to 
perform enlightened activities for the benefit of all sentient beings, they are not able to help 
all sentient beings.
