Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
296  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3A-1B Supplementary points 
1 Gaining certainty regarding the conviction: the cause of going for refuge purely 
Potowa said this: “If you keep contempl ating again and again, the firmer your 
conviction and the purer your mental co ntinuum becomes, the more blessings will 
arise. As you gain certain knowledge in this  regard, you will go for refuge from the 
bottom of your heart and then, if you mere ly train in the precepts, everything you 
do will become an instance of practicing the Buddha’s Dharma.”  
 
As a cause of going for refuge to the Three Jewe ls, we need to develop the faith or confidence 
that they can eliminate our sufferings and brin g us into the path to happiness, whereby we 
too will be able to achieve the result. Potowa said that if we think about the Three Jewels 
again and again, we will gain greater and grea ter faith in them whereby our continuum will 
become more and more pure, due to which we will receive their blessings. If we gain a definite understanding of this, we will go for refuge and will train in the precepts of going for refuge. 
In this way, whatever we do will become  the practice of the Buddha’s Dharma.  
 Therefore, initially we need to do analytic al meditation on the qualities of the Buddha 
Jewel, Dharma Jewel, and Sangha Jewel, whereby more confidence in them will arise. This will 
act as a condition that cleans or purifies ou r mental continuum, whereby we will receive the 
blessings of the Three Jewels. As we gain certaint y regarding this, we will go for refuge to them 
from the depths of our heart and train in what to adopt and what to discard with respect to 
the precepts of going for refuge. For example, ha ving gone for refuge to the Buddha, we must 
abandon taking refuge in non-Buddhist teachers. At the same time, we must train in seeing 
any representation of the Buddha, whether a drawin g, painting, or statue, as if they were the 
actual Buddha Jewel. Having gone for refuge to the Dharma Jewel, we must abandon harming 
any being and develop respect for all the scriptures , big or small, as if they were the actual 
Dharma Jewel. Having gone for refuge to the Sangha Jewel, we must abandon associating and 
staying with, for longer than one week, non-Bu ddhists who criticize the Buddhist teachings. 
In other words, we must abandon friends whose company makes our mind degenerate due to their influence. In addition, we must respect al l practitioners, monks, and nuns, as if they 
were the actual Sangha Jewel. We should deve lop respect for all those who wear the robes of 
monks and nuns because they definitely have some  qualities. In addition, there are lay people 
who are the actual sangha, for example, Marp a the Translator who had high realizations. 
When I was living in India, I heard about a monk in Tibet who remained at home and 
worked the fields under the control of the Chinese. One day he sent a message to his 
classmates, Khensur Jampa Tegchog, my teache r Geshe Tashi Bum, and others, saying that 
even though he lived at home like a householder and did the same work as lay people, his 
mind was completely liberated. I think he had be come an arya being. Therefore, by looking at 
someone from the outside, we cannot tell what he or she is like on the inside. 
 In short, every day we need to generate fa ith from the bottom of our heart in the Buddha, 
Dharma, and Sangha and to try to put the Buddha’s teachings into practice.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
297 2 Not gaining the conviction 
We do not value the Buddha’s exalted knowle dge even as much as that of a keen 
woman fortune-teller. If a keen fortune-tell er tells you, “I know there will be no 
bad luck for you this year,” you walk aw ay happy and comfortable. If she says, 
“This year [there will be] great misfortu ne. Do this. Don’t do that!”, you will try 
hard to accomplish it, and, if you do not manage, you will think, “I did not 
accomplish what she told me to do” and yo ur mind will be anxious. If the Buddha 
says, “Do this and that. Accomplish this and that,” do you take it on? If you do not 
accomplish it, does your mind become anxi ous? Explained from [the point of view 
of] Dharma, to say, “Due to place and time  that is inconvenient now. I’ll must do 
this,” is to ignore the Buddha’s statemen ts and proceed on the basis of your own 
understanding. If you turn the mind within  and investigate this in detail, rather 
than being unquestioningly happy and confus ed with respect to the mere words, it 
will strike you as very true.  
 
We tend to not respect or value the exalted knowledge of a buddha as much as we do the 
knowledge of a woman fortune-teller, that is, a woman who makes divinations. Due to this, 
when she makes divinations and pr edicts that we will not have any problems this year, we 
become very comfortable, like sitting on a soft  cushion! We do not worry any longer because a 
fortune-teller, like Khadroma, for example, who is much respected in Dharamsala, has told us 
this good news. On the other hand, if she tells us that we will experience many problems and 
difficulties, when they happen we will think th at it is because we did something wrong and 
will blame ourselves for the problem. On the ot her hand, when the Buddha says to this kind 
of action and not to do that kind of action , that is, to abandon non-virtuous actions and 
adopt virtuous actions, to do meditation an d so forth, do we confidently follow these 
instructions or not? In general, we do not believe and have confidence in what the Buddha 
said, nor do we always do what he has advised.  Instead we tend to ignore his teachings and 
turn to fortune-tellers. For example, in India there are people who make predictions based on 
a parrot choosing a certain card from a pile of cards!   If we examine our own mind, we will probably find that we do not have strong faith or confidence in the buddhas or holy beings. For example, it is said that the Virgin Mary 
appeared and gave prophecies in certain places to certain people, but some people believe in 
this and others do not. We tend to believe people only when they tell us nice things or give us good news. For example, if we have to chose between following the advice received from two 
people, one who is very humble and honest while the other is very intelligent but not so honest, we will more likely believe the intellig ent one who talks deceptively. There are also 
many stories of men who, bragging about th eir money and possessions, deceive women by 
promising them everything they want. This ha ppens, not just here but everywhere! There are 
also cases of older women who talk very nicely to young men in order to catch them. In short, 
it is easier to form a relationship with some one who talks pleasantly and sweetly, rather than 
with someone who has a rough way of talking. But the buddhas are not like this, they never 
cheat us. Therefore,, if we do not have faith and confidence in them, everything will only 
become worse.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
298 3 Insistent advice to achieve certainty 
Therefore, think about the Buddha’s positi ve qualities again and again, and strive 
to achieve as much certainty as possible. If it develops, you have reached the main 
point of going for refuge, because a similar certainty will come with respect to the 
Dharma that arose from him and the Sa ngha of those who accomplished that 
Dharma. Without it, there is no way that going for refuge, a tamed mind, let alone 
other paths, will come either.  
 
We need to gain certainty and understanding regarding the qualities of body, speech, and 
mind of the Buddha, as well as his enlightene d activities, by thinking about them again and 
again. We need to put strong effort into achieving this certainty, whereupon confidence and 
certainty will also arise with respect to the Dhar ma Jewel and with respec t to the Sangha Jewel 
who have accomplished the Dharma. This is the e ssential point of going for refuge; it is real 
going for refuge. Without this, other paths will no t come about. Since th e path taught by the 
Buddha alone leads to enlightenment, we need to  go for refuge from the depths of our heart 
to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.  
 In short, there are three objects of refu ge, the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, each of 
which has the eight qualit ies that are taught in Sublime Continuum  (Uttaratantra ). Thinking 
about these qualities, we should develop certaint y with respect to the fa ct that they alone are 
final or absolute objects of refuge, whereas other refuges are not final objects of refuge.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3A-2 Positive qualities of the Dharma 
Second: On account of your respect for the Buddha, you should be mindful 
thinking: “The fact that the Buddha has {8 9} limitless positive qualities arose from 
manifesting and meditating the Dharma of scriptures and realization, true 
cessations and true paths, the epitome of the abandonment of faults and the 
achievement of positive qualities. That is the meaning of the Compendium of the 
Dharma [Sutra ].  
 
We can generate respect for the Buddha Jewe l because the Buddha has many qualities. 
Thinking about this, we can also  generate respect for the Dharma . For example, if we examine 
where all the infinite qualities of the Buddha come from, we will come to understand that 
they come from the Dharma or doctrine of scriptures and the Dhar ma or doctrine of 
realization. The doctrine of realizations cons ists in the true paths and true cessations. 
Meditation on true paths brings about th e abandonment of all faults, that is, the 
abandonment of all objects of abandonment, whereby true cessations are achieved. 
Meditation on true paths also brings about the generation of all good qualities, which are the 
entity of realization. Thus, all this – the ab andonment of all objects of abandonment and the 
achievement of true cessations, as well as  the achievement of higher attainments or 
realizations such as omniscience – comes from meditating true paths.  The doctrine of the 
scriptures refers to studying, reading, and list ening to teachings, then thinking about them, 
and then meditating them, whereby we graduall y achieve the path of accumulation, path of 
preparation, and the path of seeing, at whic h time we attain true paths and then true 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
299 cessations. Then, at the end, we achieve the st ate of omniscience, the achievement of all the 
good qualities of a buddha of both the abandonments and realizations.  
 Therefore, first we need to study the script ures, think about them, and meditate on them, 
whereby we can generate renunciation in our mi nd and a clear knower, such as the path of 
accumulation and so forth, and in the end a ttain omniscience. All this comes from the 
practice of the Dharma. We need to remember this.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3A-3 Positive qualities of the Sangha 
Third: This mainly refers to persons who are aryas. Recalling the positive qualities 
of the Dharma, be mindful of those who accomplish it correctly. That is the 
meaning of Compendium of the Dharma . 
 
The Sangha Jewel consists mainly of arya person s. We should remember the qualities of the 
Dharma Jewel, which is what is accomplished by the Sangha Jewel, arya beings. The Sangha 
Jewel has two main qualities: the quality of kn owledge and the quality of freedom. The quality 
of knowledge includes the knowledge of ulti mate truth or thusness, the knowledge of 
conventional truth, and the knowledge of inner sciences. The quality of freedom includes the 
three freedoms from the thr ee types of obscurations.  
 In short, the Buddha, Dharma , and Sangha are the objects of refuge, and we need to 
develop confidence in them by thinking about their good qualities as this will bring a good 
result to us.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3B Understanding their distinctions 
Second: As is apparent from the Compendium , go for refuge aware of the mutual 
distinctions between the Three Jewels.  
 
We need to understand the distinctions betw een the Three Jewels. For example, the Buddha 
is the teacher that shows the pa th, the Dharma is the subject th at he teaches, and the Sangha 
are those who practice it. Another analogy is th at the Buddha is like a university professor; 
the Dharma is like the subject we study at univ ersity such as Politica l Science, Geography, 
Medicine, Electronics, and so forth; and the Sa ngha is like the classmates with whom we 
study. If we study a particular subject well, we will be able to obtain a high degree and become a professor ourselves. Similarly, if we practice  in accordance with the doctrines of scripture 
and realization taught by the Buddha, we will first become sangha, and then at the end, by 
practicing more and more, we will even become a buddha.   Another way to see the Three Jewels is that th e Buddha is like a doctor, the Dharma is like 
medicine, the Sangha is like nurses, we ourselves are like patients, and cyclic existence is like a 
hospital. If the doctor diagnoses our illness and prescribes medicine, and we take it with the 
help of the nurses, then one day we will be able to leave the hospital. Similarly, if we practice 
the Dharma taught by the Buddha with the help  of the Sangha, one day we can leave cyclic 
existence and attain libera tion or even buddhahood.  
 Thus, when going for refuge we should think about the Three Jewels in one of these ways.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
300  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3C Assertion 
Third: As is apparent from the Extensive Commentary on the Vinaya , we should go 
for refuge in terms of considering the Bu ddha to be the one who teaches refuge; 
the Dharma to be nirvana, which is the ac tual refuge; and to the Sangha to be the 
companions who accomplish refuge.  
 
The Buddha is the one who teaches refuge, th e Dharma is the state gone beyond cyclic 
existence, nirvana, and the Sangha are the co mpanions who help us. This is way going for 
refuge is explained in the Extensive Commentary on the Vinaya .  
 Wednesday morning, 16 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 89) says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A3D Not advocating other ones  
The fourth: Through understanding the su periority and inferiority of Buddhist 
and non-Buddhist teachers, teachings, and students, you should consider the 
Three Jewels alone to be a refuge, and sh ould not consider teachers and so forth 
who are incompatible with them to be a refuge. With respect to the difference 
between the two [sets], the difference between  the teachers is that the Buddha is 
without faults and has completed the positi ve qualities while other teachers are the 
opposite to that. In the Praise of the Particularly Exalted  [One] it says:  
 
Having abandoned the other teachers, 
Bhagavan, I take refuge in you. If you ask “For what purpose?” Because You have no faults but have qualities. 
 And: 
 
The more I’ve thought about the scriptural traditions 
Of others, the Tirthikas
119, the more 
My confidence in you, Protector, has grown. 
Those whose minds are wasted by the 
Faulty tenets of the non-omniscient, 
Whose thinking is wasted, do not see {90} Faultless teachers – not even you.  
 
                                             
 
119 Six principal Indian tenet systems at the time of the Buddha, literally “Forders”, so called [?] because they 
were pointing out fords for crossing the river of suffer ing – paths to liberation – which they did not achieve 
due to ignorance about the truly appropriate means. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
301 We need to understand between what are and wh at are not objects of refuge. In other words, 
we need to differentiate between the Buddhist  teachers, Buddhist teachings or doctrine, and 
followers or students of the Buddhist doctrine  and the non-Buddhist teachers, non-Buddhist 
teachings, and their followers, such as the Ti rthikas. Since the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha 
– the Three Jewels – are real objects of refuge, we should not consider teachers and so forth 
who are incompatible or contradictory with them  to be a refuge. For example, we should not 
take refuge in the six well-known teachers of  Hindu tenets in ancient India, who were not 
Buddhists.   The Buddha is without even subtle faults and has completed all the qualities of body, 
speech, mind, and enlightened activities. Other te achers are the opposite to that in the sense 
that they are not without faults and have not completed the qualities of body, speech, mind, 
and enlightened activities.  
 The writer of the text Praise of the Particularly Exalted  One was originally a Hindu 
practitioner who later on converted to Buddhism. He says that having abandoned other 
teachers, he goes for refuge to the Bhagavan because the Bhagavan has eliminated all faults 
and possesses all good qualities. He saw the di fference between the Tirthikas, many of whom, 
for example, would often fight with their husb and or wife, whereas the Buddha, who had had 
a wife and had been surrounded by many beautifu l women, gave them all up and went to the 
forest to meditate. The Buddha has completed al l good qualities, therefore he alone is a 
worthy object of refuge, while other teachers ar e not. For this reason, we should not look for 
other objects of refuge.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4 The stages of training after having gone for refuge 
A The actual content  
B Eliminating inaccuracies [with respect to the precepts of going for refuge] 
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A The actual content  
1 Specific precepts [for the individual objects of refuge] 
2 Common precepts [for all three objects of refuge] 
The fourth one has two points: specific pr ecepts [for the individual objects of 
refuge] and common precepts [for all three objects of refuge]. 
 
Having gone for refuge to the Buddha, Dharma , and Sangha, there are specific precepts or 
trainings to be kept with respect to each of the individual objects of refuge. For example, 
having gone for refuge to the Buddha, we must  not go for refuge to non-Buddhist teachers; 
having gone for refuge to the Dharma, we must not go for re fuge to other teachings; and 
having gone for refuge to the Sangha, we must not go for refuge to non-Buddhist friends and 
so forth. In addition, having gone for refuge to the Buddha, we must consider any paintings 
and so forth of the Buddha as an actual livi ng buddha and therefore an object of refuge. 
Having gone for refuge to the Dharma, we must respect the texts or scriptures of the teachings 
by not throwing them on the ground, putting th em under our bed, using them as a cushion, 
and so forth. Having gone for refuge to the Sa ngha, we must not criticize or disparage monks 
and nuns and other virtuous spiritual friends bu t respect the conventional sangha as if they 
were the actual Sangha. These are the specific precepts or trainings with respect to the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
302 individual objects of refuge – Buddha, Dh arma, and Sangha. The common precepts or 
trainings will be explained below.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-1 Specific precepts [for the individual objects of refuge]  
A Precepts of what is to be stopped 
B Precepts of what is to be accomplished 
The first one has two points: inhibitory precepts and precepts of accomplishment. 
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-1A Precepts of what is to be stopped 
First: From the  [Sutra of Great Final ] Nirvana : 
 
One who goes for refuge to the three 
Is a perfect approacher to virtue. 
He does not ever go for refuge To some other gods.  
One who goes for refuge to the holy Dharma 
Is free from the mind to harm or kill. One who goes for refuge to the Sangha 
Does not mingle with the Tirthikas. 
 
In accordance with this statement there ar e three [inhibitory precepts]: (1) to not 
go for refuge to other gods, (2) to abando n causing harm or difficulty to sentient 
beings, and (3) to not associate with Tirthikas.  
 Those who go for refuge from their heart to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha are considered 
to be “virtue approachers,” a title normally rese rved for male or female lay vow-holders. Those 
who take refuge in the Buddha should never go  for refuge to other gods, for example, to 
Mahadeva, Brahma, Indra, the Sun (Surya), the M oon (Chandra), and so forth. While we can 
take temporary refuge in them and ask them for their help, we should not go for final refuge to these gods. This building is also a type of temporary refuge since it protects us from rain 
and snow. A forest can also be a refuge if we hi de in it to escape from someone who wants to 
kill us. There are also places to stay in the mountains that are called “refuges” because they 
can protect us from the weather and serve food and drink.   In relation to going for refuge to the Dharma, we should never harm any sentient being. In relation to going for refuge to the Sangha, we should not associate with Tirthikas.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
1 Not to go for refuge to other gods 
The first of these: If one should not rega rd even worldly gods, Rudra, Vishnu and 
the like, as a long-term refuge, there is no  need to speak of nagas and local spirits 
who belong to the hungry ghosts. In this re gard, while it is not appropriate to rely 
on them from a lack of conviction in the three objects of refuge, it is not 
inappropriate to seek their assistance for some action of temporary character, for 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
303 example, like seeking assistance from a benefactor for one’s livelihood or like 
relying on a doctor in hope of a cure for an illness.  
 
If worldly gods such as Rudra, Vishnu, and so forth, were final objects of refuge, so too would 
nagas and local spirits be final objects of refuge; however, they are not. If we were to go for 
final refuge to Rudra, Vishnu, Ram, Lakshmi, and so forth due to lacking faith in the three 
objects of refuge, this would not be suitable. On  the other hand, it is suitable to ask Rudra, 
Vishnu, Ram, Lakshmi, and so forth for tempor ary help or benefit, in the same way that, 
confident that he or she can help us, we ask a benefactor for money in order to buy food, to 
pay our rent, and so forth, or the way in which we rely on a doctor for help when we are sick.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 To give up causing harm or difficulty to sentient beings 
The second one consists in giving up causing harm or difficulty to humans, 
animals, and so forth through thought or actions like beating them, binding them, 
ensnaring them, piercing their noses, and loading them unbearably.  
 
If we go for refuge to the Dharma, we mu st abandon harming animals and other human 
beings by beating them, binding them, trapping  them, putting a ring through their noses, 
loading them with heavy burdens, and so forth. In short, we must give up harming others by 
thought and by action, meaning th at we need to give up even  the thought to harm another 
person or an animal, as well as giving up actually physically harming them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 Not to associate with non-Buddhists 
The third one consists in not conforming  to those who are unconvinced of the 
protection of the Three Jewels and denigrate them.  
 
If we go for refuge to the Sangha, we should abandon associating with those who criticize the 
Three Jewels, do not believe in their existence, and so forth. There are those who say that if 
the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha exists, it shou ld be possible to point them out. There are 
also many people, especially the younger genera tion, who do not believe in past and future 
lives because they say that they cannot see them directly. One time I was invited to give a talk 
in a Catholic school in Sardinia. While the professor who had invited me did not criticize 
Buddhism, some of his students said to me, “I f you believe in past and future lives, show 
them to us! Also where are the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha that you talk about?” I said to 
them “If something cannot be pointed out, does that mean that it does not exist?” Someone 
answered that it was like that, whereupon I said, “Since you believe in God, show him to me!” 
The person remained open mouthed and unable to respond. 
 While we do not need to follow or practice  other religions or doctrines, we should not 
criticize them because they are suitable for di fferent people, different cultures, and different 
places. For particular people these other reli gions are more suitable than is Buddhism. 
However, we ourselves should take the Buddha,  Dharma, and Sangha as our final objects of 
refuge. Having taken the Buddha as our teacher, realizations and qualities will develop in our 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
304 continuum whereby we will become Sangha and eventually become a buddha. This is 
possible. Okay? 
 
(Oral transmission 11:55 AM, pp. 90-101) 
 
Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-1B Precepts of what is to be accomplished 
1 How to train with respect to the representations of the Buddha 
2 How to train with respect to the representations of the Dharma 
3 How to train with respect to the representations of the Sangha 
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-1B1 How to train with respect to the representations of the Buddha 
A The actual way to train with respect to the representations of the Buddha  
{91} Second: However excellent or flawed painted pictures of the Buddha’s body 
may be, do not disparage them and do  not put them on the ground when 
inconvenienced. Having given up the i rreverence of pawning them etc. and 
defaming them, regard them as fields of respect like the Teacher [himself]. In the 
Letter to a Friend  it says:  
 
Do as the wise who honor any form 
Of the Sugata – even made from wood. 
 Representations of the Buddha are statues and so  forth, representations of the Dharma are 
texts and so forth, and representations of th e Sangha are monks and nuns, including their 
robes. Whether a statue, painting, and so forth of the Buddha is of good or bad quality, we 
should not put it in a bad place, nor should we pa wn it, that is, give it as a guarantee in order 
to borrow money. Whether or not the painter or  sculptor was skilled, we must respect all 
representations of the Buddha. Thus, we shou ld not differentiate between representations 
that are well made and those that are not. Likewise, whether a statue is made of gold, silver, wood, or clay, we should treat it with respect. Th us, it is not that we sh ould respect those that 
are made of valuable materials but not respect those that are made of poor materials. Nor 
should we respect statues and so forth that are well-made but not respect those that are not. If 
we do not respect them, we will experience th e ripened results that are explained below. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Examples of the fully ripened effects of irreverence that occurred in the past  
In the Scripture on Discernment  it  is  s t at ed t hat  M an av aka pila abus ed s an gha w h o  
were learners and non-learners in 18 di fferent ways saying things like: “You 
elephant heads! How do you know what is  Dharma and what is not?” Because of 
that, he was reborn as a sea monster, a spec ies of fish, with 18 different heads, and 
remained an animal from the time of th e Teacher Kashyapa to that of the Shakya 
King [Shakyamuni]. 
 
Manavakapila, for example, spoke badly to the sangha calling them “E lephant head” and so 
forth in eighteen different ways in order to  put them down and win debates with them. 
However, due to this, in his next life he was born as a sea monster, a kind of huge fish, with 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
305 eighteen heads. He remained in this form from  the time of the Teacher Kashyapa to that of 
Shakyamuni, and everyone who saw him would ex perience fear. For this reason, we should 
avoid calling others by derogatory names, sayi ng they are like animals and so forth, as 
otherwise we too will experience the unplea sant fruitional or fully-ripened result.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim says:   
 In Minor Precepts  [of the Vinaya ] it says that after the teacher Krakucchanda had 
passed into nirvana, when King Carumat had a big stupa built, a hired workman 
twice scoffed at it saying: “Who knows when such a huge stupa will ever get finished.” Later on, when the stupa had been  nicely finished, he felt sorry and with 
his wages had a golden bell made and tied it  to the stupa. Due to that, he was born 
as “The Well-sounding One” with an ugly  complexion and a small body but a very 
pleasant voice. Therefore, do not talk ab out representations saying “This is like 
that.” It is inappropriate to disparage th e establishment of a great representation 
from good materials and to dissuade [other s] from it through disruptive remarks. 
 At the time of the Buddha there was an extremely ugly monk wh o had a very pleasant voice, 
such that when others heard him singing or chan ting they were amazed at the beauty of his 
voice and repulsed by the ugliness of his bo dy. When the Buddha wa s asked why this had 
happened, he replied that in th e past, when King Carumat was b uilding a large stupa, one of 
the hired workers remarked, “When will this big stupa ever be finished?” Later on when the 
stupa was finished, the worker repented his word s and offered the stupa a golden bell bought 
with his wages. Due to this, he was reborn as  a human being whose body was small and face 
very ugly, but with a beautiful voice. His ugly  small body was the result of denigrating the 
stupa, whereas his beautiful voice was the result of offering the stupa a golden bell. Therefore, 
we too should be careful of disparaging representations of the Three Jewels.   Wednesday afternoon, 16 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 The Great Yogi [Jangchub Rinchen] gave At isha a statue of Manjushri to look at 
and asked: “What is the quality like? If it is  good, I’ll take it and give the four gold 
coins Rongwa Garge has given me”. {92} Atis ha said: “There is nothing that is not 
good about the Venerable One’s body. As fo r the sculptor, he is average.” Having 
said this, he placed the statue  on the crown of his head.  
 
The Great Yogi gave Atisha a statue of Manjus hri and asked him to look at it and tell him 
whether it was excellent or faulty. He said that if  it was good, he would give four gold coins in 
exchange for it. Atisha responded that while th e body of Manjushri is always excellent, the 
sculptor was just average, and then placed the statue on his head. This story indicates that 
with respect to statues and so forth we should not think, “This one is good. That one is not 
good,” and thereby respect those that are well made and not respect those that are not. By 
doing this, we would create negative karma. Th us, whether a statue is made of gold, silver, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
306 clay or any other material, for the mere fact that it is an image of a holy being we should 
respect it.   
Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-1B2 Precepts associated with the Dharma  
A The actual [precepts]  
You should abandon irreverence to as little as four lines of Dharma. You should 
abandon pawning volumes of scripture, tr ading them, putting them on the bare 
ground or in inconvenient places, carry ing them around toge ther with shoes, 
stepping over them and so forth. Instead tr eat them with respect as if they were the 
Dharma Jewel. 
 
Here the text says that we should abandon a lack of respect even toward a mere four lines, 
that is, one verse, of Dharma. However, it is of ten said that we should respect even a single 
word of Dharma. We should also abandon pa wning volumes of scripture, selling them, 
putting them on the bare ground, and so forth. Instead we should  put them in high places or, 
at the very least, cover the ground with a shaw l, a coat, and so forth and then place them on 
it. We should not carry them together with sh oes, for example, by tying shoes together and 
then draping them with a text over our shoulder s. We should also not allow the lower parts of 
our cloths to pass over the texts. Instead we sh ould respect these texts as if they were the 
actual Dharma Jewel.  
 Middle Lam-Rim says:  
B How the masters of former times acted 
It is said that Geshe Chengawa used to pu t his palms together an d rise up when he 
saw scriptures [of the Buddha’s teachings]  being brought to him. Later on, when 
he could not get up any more, he used to [simply] put his palms together. 
 It is said that when Atisha came to Ng ari, one tantric practitioner did not listen 
to Dharma [teachings] from him. However, when one scribe smeared residue from 
his teeth on a Dharma text and Atisha, seeing  it and unable to bear it, said: “Alas! 
That’s no good. That’s no good!”, the ta ntrika gained faith and listened to his 
teachings.  
 Sharawa said: “We play around with the Dharma in all sorts of ways. Irreverence 
for the Dharma and expounders of the Dharma is a cause for distorted wisdom. 
We are already confused enough now. What are we going to do if we become more 
confused?”  
 Geshe Chengawa was Ge she Lodro Gyatso from Chengawa who wrote many texts on mind 
training. He acted with great respect toward  the scriptures and would stand up when 
scriptures were brought to him. Even when he was older and could no longer get up, he 
would still put his hands together in a gesture of respect. We too should act like this.  
 When Atisha was first invited to Tibet by Ki ng Jangchub O, he stayed some time in the 
area of Ngari, most of which is now included in  Lhadak, India. It is recounted that at that 
time one tantric practitioner initially did not attend his teachings. However, upon hearing 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
307 that Atisha had scolded a scribe whom he had s een take some dirt from his teeth and smear it 
on the text in order to mark hi s place, the tantric practitioner developed faith in Atisha and 
after that attended his teachings. 
 Geshe Sharawa, who was from  the monastery of Shar Bump o in the Pembo region of 
Tibet, said that we tend to play with the Dhar ma and that our disrespect for the teachers and 
teachings becomes the cause of corrupt or distorted wisdom, meaning that we become more 
ignorant instead of wiser, so what could be more  stupid than this? For this reason, we should 
not joke about or play with the Dharma or th e teachers. Nor should we deprecate them in 
any way, for example, by saying that a certain text  is not good, as such behavior will only cause 
us to generate distorted or faulty wisdom wh ereby we will become even more ignorant and 
confused than we already are. Instead we sh ould treat the Dharma and those who teach the 
Dharma with respect.   Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-1B3 Precepts with regard to the Sangha  
A The actual [precepts] 
You should not abuse or despise the Sangha  as well as anyone merely bearing the 
marks of ordination. Nor should you look at them as opponents in any way having 
made [arbitrary] divisions in terms of “your group” and “my group.” Instead you 
should treat them with respect as if  they were the Sangha Jewel. In Exhortation to 
the Special Attitude  it says: 
 
Those who, dwelling in forests, wish for qualities Should not look for faults in other [people], 
They should not produce the attitude: 
“I’m especially noble, I’m the best.”  
{93} All carelessness grows
120 out of this complacence.121 
Don’t let contempt for lesser monks arise. 
You won’t reach liberation for one eon. This is the succession
122 of this teaching.  
 
We should not deprecate or criticize the Sang ha, even those who just wear the robes of a 
monk or nun and have the mere name “monk” or “nun.” Nor should we divide them into 
groups of friends and enemies. Rather we shou ld think that of them as if they were the 
Sangha Jewel and generate respect for them.   W e  s h o u l d  a l s o  n o t  t h i n k ,  “ I  a m  v e r y  l e a r n e d .  I  a m  n o b l e , ”  w h i l e  l o o k i n g  f o r  f a u l t s  i n  
other people. We should not allow such attitude s to arise at any time as this arrogance or 
haughtiness, which is similar to pride, is the r oot of all non-conscientiousness. It leads us to 
criticize others thinking, for example, “He is an  inferior monk, I am superior.” However, such

Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B How the masters of former times acted and the benefits [of that] 
Geshe [Drom] Tonpa and the Great Yogi, instead of stepping over a piece of 
yellow material on the ground, would shak e it and carry it to a clean place. You 
should train in accordance with their practice, for others will respect you as much 
as you respect the [Three] Jewels. In the King of Concentration  it says:  
 
What kind of actions you have done, 
That kind of effects you will obtain. 
 When Geshe Drom Tonpa and the Great Yogi wo uld see even a small piece of yellow cloth 
on the ground, they would not walk over it but wo uld pick it up, shake it clean, and put it in 
high and clean place so that others would also not step over it. We too should train in this 
way because by doing so we will collect merit – the cause of achieving higher realizations 
quickly.  
 The King of Meditative Stabilizations  says that we obtain a result similar to the cause. In 
other words, if we create a virtuous action it will bring the result of happiness whereas if we 
create a non-virtuous actions, such as disrespe ct, it will bring the result of suffering. For 
example, if we respect our pare nts, then when we ourselves become parents our children will 
also respect us. Likewise, if we harm others or  destroy their belongings, we too will be beaten 
or even killed and our belongings will be dest royed. Since we all want happiness and pleasure 
and do not want suffering or unhappiness, we need to create good causes, that is, virtuous 
actions, whereby we will experience good result s, that is, happiness. If , on the other hand, we 
do the opposite and create bad actions, we will experience only bad results.  
 Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-2 Common precepts [f or all three objects of refuge] 
A Remember the differences and positive qualities of the Three Jewels and, due to that, go 
for refuge to them again and again 
 
We must go for refuge three times during the day and three times at night while remembering 
the good qualities of the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.  
 
B Remember their great kindness and, due to that, exert oneself at all times to make offerings 
to them and offer them even the first portion of one’s food and drink 
 
We need to remember the kindness of the Three Je wels in taking care of us and at all times 
worship them by making offerings to them. Even when we are about to eat or drink 
something, we should first offe r it to the Buddha or the Three Jewels and after that eat or 
drink it. If, in addition, we think that by doing so our food or drink has been blessed by 
them, this practice will become even more effective.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
309  
C Remember their great compassion and, due to that, set other migrating beings in such a 
mode [of behavior]  
 
We need to remember the great compassion of th e Three Jewels in taking care of us and then 
strive to make other sentient beings go for refuge to them.  
 
D Whatever one does and whatever one needs, having worshipped the Three Jewels, 
supplicate them and abandon other worldly methods 
 
Whenever we do activity, we should remember the Three Jewels and make requests to them 
and at the same time abandon relying on worl dly gods for whatever we need. Instead we 
should rely on the Three Jewe ls, the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, alone. Likewise, whatever 
we need, we should ask the Buddha, Dharma , and Sangha for help in obtaining it.  
 
E Aware of the benefits, go for refuge three times a day and three times at night  
 
Seeing the benefits of going for refuge to th e Three Jewels, we should go for refuge three 
times during the day and three times at night. In other words, we should recite one of the 
refuge prayers three times during the day and three times at night while remembering the 
Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.   
F Do not give up the Three Jewels for one’s life nor for a laugh but guard against this 
 
We should not give up the Three Jewels even if our life is in danger nor should we do so to make others laugh. Instead we must guard ours elves against such behavior, as otherwise we 
will commit a downfall – the cause of rebirth in the lower realms.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
The second one has six points: Remember th e differences and positive qualities of 
the Three Jewels and, due to that, go fo r refuge again and again; remember their 
great kindness and, due to that, exert onesel f at all times to make offerings to them 
and offer them even the first portion of  one’s food and drink; remember their 
great compassion and, due to that, set ot her migrating beings in such a mode [of 
behavior]; whatever one does and what ever one needs, having worshipped the 
Three Jewels, supplicate them and abandon other worldly methods; aware of the 
benefits, go for refuge three times a day an d three times at night; do not give up 
the Three Jewels for one’s life nor for a laugh but guard against this. 
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-2A Remember the differences and positive qualities of the Three Jewels and, due 
to that, go for refuge again and again 
First: As explained above, think abou t the differences between things non-
Buddhist and Buddhist as well as among the Three Jewels and their positive 
qualities.  
 
Non-Buddhists in Tibetan are literally “outer beings,” whereas Buddhists are “inner beings.” 
We should think about the differences between them. We should also think again and again 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
310 about the good qualities of the Three Jewels. If we do this, we ourselves will become improve 
more quickly.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-2B Remember their great kindness and,  due to that, exert oneself at all times to 
make offerings to them and offer them even  the first portion of one’s food and drink 
1 Constant offerings 
Second: You should understand all the well-being you experience as the kindness 
of the Three Jewels and make offeri ng to them by acknowledging that. 
 
We should make offerings to the Three Jewels daily. In other words,  through understanding 
that whatever happiness and excellence comes to us is from the kindness of the Three Jewels, 
we should generate the wish to repay their kind ness by making offerings to them of flowers, 
bowls of water, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The peculiarity that the collections are completed with little trouble  
Since you constantly need to eat and dr ink, {94} if you continuously offer123 the 
first portion of them you will complete a great amount of collections with little 
trouble. Therefore, you should offer th e first portion of whatever you enjoy, 
including even water, from the bottom of your heart. 
 
Since we need to eat and drink every day, we ca n easily collect merit by  offering them without 
making much effort. Thus, whenever we eat or drink, we should first offer it to the Three 
Jewels by reciting an offering prayer. By doing so, we will collect a lot of merit without much 
difficulty and effort. Even when we are about to drink water, we should first offer it to the 
Three Jewels from the depths of our heart. In this way we will create much merit.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 Impure offerings  
Moreover Sharawa said: “You should not ma ke [offerings] from the blue [mold] on 
coagulated milk and yellow leaves, but make them but from something good. 
[Worship] comes about each time through of fering the first portion of tea, but not 
through something like cleaning out the dust.” 
 
T i b e t a n s  m a k e  a  k i n d  o f  c h e e s e  m i x e d  w i t h  butter that gets moldy and rotten when it 
becomes damp. We should not offer this, nor should we offer yellowed, withered, or rotten 
vegetables. Rather we should ma ke offerings of good things. We  can offer even a small bit of 
tea but should not offer that which has been dusted off.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

311 4 The manner of respect when offering 
In a sutra it says:  
 
For instance, if at the time of planting seeds in a fertile field something 
remains unplanted, it cannot just be  left. Likewise, by planting the 
seeds of well-being continually th roughout all four seasons in the 
sublime field from which all future well-being springs, may the field of 
merit be tilled with the plow of confidence in them.  
 If that which needs to be done does no t occur, it would be a very great loss. 
 If we have fertile land, we should not leave it  empty but instead should plow it and sow good 
seeds, such as barley, wheat, vegetables, and so  f o r t h ,  i n  i t .  S i m i l a r l y ,  w e  s h o u l d  p l o w  t h e  
merit field with confidence or faith in the Th ree Jewels and plant the seeds of buddhahood in 
it. If we do not do that, it would be a great pity and a great loss.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 The need to exert oneself in making offerings 
T o  n o t  h a v e  e v e n  a s  m u c h  r e g a r d  f o r  t h e  b e s t  o f  f i e l d s  a s  f o r  a n  o r d i n a r y  f i e l d  
would not a good attitude. Therefore, we should strive, at all times, to make 
offerings to the [Three] Jewels.  
 
Since the Three Jewels are a the best of fields, if we were to look upon them as if they were an 
ordinary field this would not be a good a ttitude but would be arrogant on our part. 
Therefore, we should strive to make offerings at all times to the Three Jewels. We can even 
make offerings and create a great amount of meri t by merely imagining beautiful flowers, nice 
clothes, and so forth and mentally offering them to the Three Jewels.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
6 High status, paths, etc. are attained through making offerings in that way 
If you do that, through the power of the roots of virtue that  grow in the most 
excellent field, the power of your intelligenc e concerning the stages of the path will 
increase. When your intelligence is very feeble such that you do not retain the 
words while listening, do not understand  the meaning while contemplating, and 
nothing arises in the mind while meditati ng, the quintessential instruction is to 
rely on the power of the field.  
 
In order to achieve high status (high rebirth) and definite goodness (liberation or 
omniscience), we need to create the cause by co llecting merit with respect to a holy field. In 
other words, we need to engage in virtuous action s with respect to a holy or excellent field. If 
we do this, when we try to meditate on the stag es of the path our mind will be strong and our 
intelligence will increase. Thus, if, due to the po wer of our mind being weak, we are unable to 
understand something at the time of listen ing and unable to memorize it, unable to 
understand the meaning while thinking about it, and unable to gain the realization of it by 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
312 meditating, we should rely on a holy field wher eby we will accumulate merit. In this way, we 
will be able to understand and memorize that  which we previously could not understand and 
could not memorize; we will be able to unde rstand that which we previously could not 
understand by thinking about it; and we will be able to realize that which we previously could 
not meditate on and realize. In this way, our qu alities, such as concen tration and wisdom, will 
increase more and more. For this reason, the instruction is to rely on such a field.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
7 How the size of the offerings and so forth is a distinction to do with the mind 
Also the offering is not confined by the thing [offered], it is confined by your 
confidence. As it is stated [in the scriptures ], if you have faith it is enough to offer 
mandalas, water, and offerings that are not considered by anyone [to be their 
property], therefore you should act accordingly even if you do not have any 
material things [to offer]. {95} Not to b e  a b l e  t o  g i v e ,  e v e n  t h o u g h  y o u  h a v e  
something, saying: “I, a wretched pauper  without any merits, do not have any 
other possessions to offer,”124 would be, in the words of Potowa, like a blind 
person putting some cheap aromatic plant125 into a smelly conch-shell container in 
order to fool someone with eyesight by calling it “sandalwood and camphor perfume.”  
 Whether something becomes a great or small offe ring depends on our mind, that is, on our 
motivation. For example, we can offer even th ings that we do not possess with faith and 
confidence in the Three Jewels. For example, we can offer a mandala of the entire universe by 
making a particular gesture with our hands. We can also offer water and other things that are 
not owned by anyone. Thus, we can offer the en tire universe, forests, beautiful mountains, 
lakes, oceans, and so forth by mentally taking them to mind and imagining offering them to 
the Three Jewels. In this way we can make offerings of many thin gs rather than thinking that 
we are poor and do not have any belongings to offer. On the other hand, if we do in fact possess material things but are unable to offer them even mentally, this would not be right. 
We should not think of ourselves as wretch ed paupers without any merits and without 
anything to offer. This would be like a blind person trying to fool someone with good eyesight by putting an aromatic plant into a smelly po t and calling it “perfume.” In general it is 
difficult for the blind to deceive those with eyesight, but perhaps sometimes they try!   Thursday morning, 17 March 2005 (Happy Saint Patrick’s Day!) 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 95) says:  
8 How to train in upright[ness with regard to] offering substances, following the masters of former times  
Puchungwa said: “At first I used to offer a pungent aromatic plant. Then I had the 
means to offer sweet incense prepared from  four substances. Now I offer the most

you do not give anything out of contempt for small offerings, you will go on like 
that throughout your entire life. Howeve r, if you develop perseverance from a

[worth] twenty-two gold coins.  
 
We should train following the example of past ma sters by not making offerings of things that 
have been obtained by deceitful means. Puchun gwa initially offered a pungent type of incense 
uncomfortable to the nose. Then , when he had more money, he offered incense having a 
sweet smell and composed of four substances. Af ter that, when he became  wealthier, he made 
offerings of costly incense made of aloe wood  (akaru), duruka, and so forth that have an 
excellent fragrance. Similarly, we too should o ffer whatever we have, even something small. If, 
on the other hand, we continually think that so mething is too small to offer, we will never 
make any offerings during our entire life. Therefore, we should offer whatever we can obtain, even the very small, while generating the mind wi shing to make offerings. In this way we will 
gradually improve.  In short, we should not deprecate small offe rings, but offer whatever we have. We should 
also mentally offer whatever food or drink we have before eating or drinking it. We can even offer our clothing to the holy beings. Likewise, when we wash our face, take a shower, and so 
forth, we can think that we are offering ablution  to the holy beings. In this way whatever we 
do will be a virtuous action and collect merit.  Since we can make offe rings mentally by means 
of visualization, we should never think, “I don’t have anything to offer.”   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
9 Refutation of the manners of speaking [to the effect] that the way bodhisattvas make offerings, having 
gained control over material things, is impure 
If great bodhisattvas, having gained cont rol over material things, emanate many 
hundreds of thousands of bodies and for each body emanate hundreds of 
thousands of hands, etc., that appear in all the fields and make offerings to the 
Conquerors for many eons, the statement by those who know on ly the superficial 
appearance of positive qualities that “As for me, I do not hope [to reach] 
enlightenment in that way,” is the idle t a l k  o f  [ p e o p l e ]  w h o  h a v e  v e r y  l i t t l e  
familiarity with the Dharma.  
 
The great bodhisattvas, who have attained control over material things, can emanate 
hundreds of thousands of bodies, each of wh ich has a hundred thousand hands, and with 
each hand they present offering s to the merit field or Conquerors of the ten directions for 
many eons. Thus, if someone, thinking that it is  not possible to emanate so many bodies with 
so many hands, were to say “I do not hope to achieve enlightenment in this way,” it would show that this person has very little un derstanding of the Dharma. Not seeing the 
bodhisattvas’ ability to emanate ma ny bodies even in a single atom , such people th ink it is not 
t r u e .  H o w e v e r ,  w e  s h o u l d  b e  a b l e  t o  u n d e r s t a n d  t h i s  b a s e d  o n  t h e  f a c t  t h a t  s c i e n t i s t s  h a v e  proven that even tiny atoms,  which cannot be seen with the eyes, are very powerful. 
Therefore, we should be refrai n from saying such things.

314  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
10 How to make offerings with other vast things  
In the Jewel Cloud  [Sutra ] it says:  
 
From the sutra collections you should study what are very vast offerings 
and honors and from the bottom of yo ur heart dedicate them with the 
most excellent special attitude to the buddhas and bodhisattvas. 
 
You should do like that. 
 How can we offer other vast things? The sutras s ay that we should listen  to explanations of 
how to make offerings and act respectfully and then dedicate them from our heart. In other 
words, we should mentally offer all the good things that we see in the world, the oceans, 
mountains, lakes, forests, flowers, and so fo rth, to the buddhas and bodhisattvas from the 
depths of our heart. In this way we can make offerings with vast things, there being no need 
to hold them in our hands in order to offer th em. Instead merely take them to mind and then 
mentally offer or dedicate them to the Three Jewels, the buddhas, and bodhisattvas. In this 
way we will first become bodhisattvas and then buddhas, whereupon we will be able to take 
care of all sentient beings. Thus , we should manifest a big mind that wishes to make offerings 
of all the vast things in the world.   Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-2C Remember their great compassion and, due to that, establish other beings in 
such a mode [of behavior]  
Third: Out of loving concern126 establish other sentient beings in going for refuge 
as much as you can. {96} 
 
Out of great compassion or mercy, we should try to set other sentient beings in going for refuge. We should do this by talking to people nicely and then, motivated by great compassion, leading them as much as possible in going for refuge to the Buddha, Dharma, 
and Sangha. If we talk nicely to people we can bring about a change in their minds, whereby they too can come to think about going for refu ge. In this way we should set as many human 
beings in the practice of Buddhism as possible,  that is, in going for refuge to the Buddha, 
Dharma, and Sangha, whereby they become Buddhists.  
 However, we, in short, should explain to ot hers the fact that every human being needs to 
develop love and compassion for others. I think all religions and all people would accept the 
truth of this fact. On the other hand, if we  were to say to Christians, Moslems, and 
practitioners of other religions that they shou ld practice Buddhism, they would reject this. 
Therefore, to avoid this, we should explain to th em that it is necessary for us to develop love 
and compassion for all sentient beings because a ll sentient beings want to be happy and none 
of them want to suffer, and it is our respon sibility to set them in happiness and free them 
                                             
 
126 JN: brtse ba , can also be translated as “mercy” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
315 from suffering. It would be best to talk to th e people we meet in this way since love and 
compassion are not rejected by any religion. Since everyone has the seed of innate love and 
compassion, such teachings are easy for them to understand. Therefore, this is what we 
should teach them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-2D Whatever one does and what ever one needs, having worshipped the Three 
Jewels, supplicate them and abandon other worldly methods 
Whatever you do and whatever requirements  you see, rely on the Three Jewels and 
do what is in agreement with them such as  making offerings to them and so forth. 
However, it is inappropriate to rely in al l ways on that which is not in agreement 
with them such as Bon. Consequently, yo u should trust the Three Jewels at all 
times.  
 
Whatever we do and for whatever purpose, we should first rely on the Three Jewels, Buddha, 
Dharma, and Sangha, that is, we should re quest them for help in accomplishing and 
succeeding in whatever it is th at we want to do or achieve. Relying on the Buddha, Dharma, 
and Sangha, we should make offerings to them, prostrate to them, make praises to them, and 
so forth. We should generate confidence and belief in the Three Jewels and not rely on that which is not compatible with them, for example, the Bon tradition.  
 Why is Bon specifically mentioned here? It is  because the Bon tradition says that when our 
relatives die, they can help us achieve success if  we respect them, make offerings to them, and 
so forth. Some say that the Bon tradition is a fo rm of shamanism, whereas others say that it is 
a form of Buddhism that was founded by  their teacher 1000 years before Buddha 
Shakyamuni visited the world. Therefore, they s ay that although it is not based on Buddha 
Shakyamuni’s doctrine, it is based on the doct rine of a buddha. However, I myself do not 
think that this is true. I think th at it is a story that was invented by someone. In the past the 
Bonpo also made animal sacrifices, offered th e blood of animals whose necks had been cut, 
and so forth, whereas such rituals were never part of Buddhism. There are three types of Bon 
– Black Bon, White Bon, and Mixed Bon. The Bo npos took the Buddhist texts, such as the 
Ornament for Clear Realizations , and created their own scriptures by replacing the word 
“Dharma” with the word “Bon.” There are sti ll many Bon practitioners in southern Tibet 
near the border with Burma and Assam. I have heard that some of them do pujas in which 
they offer live chickens, sheep, goats, buffaloes, and so forth, and that if  they do not have any 
animals to offer, they offer an egg by breaking  it open. It is for these reasons that Lama 
Tsongkhapa says that we should not rely on the Bon tradition.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-2E Aware of the benefits, go for refuge three times a day and three times at night 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
316 1 It is the measure of being a Buddhist 
2 It is the basis of all vows  
3 The karmic obscurations accumulated previously are reduced and exhausted 
4 One accumulates vast merit 
5 One will not fall into the bad migrations 
6 One will not be harmed by human and non-human obstacles 
7 One will attain everything one wishes for 
8 One will swiftly attain buddhahood 
The fifth one has eight benefits : It is the measure of being a Buddhist, it is the basis 
of all vows, the karmic obscurations accumulated previously are reduced and 
exhausted, one accumulates vast merit, on e does not fall into the bad migrations, 
one is not harmed by human and non-huma n obstacles, one attains everything one 
wishes for, and one swiftly attains buddhahood. 
 
There are eight benefits of going for refuge  to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. Among 
them, the first is that one becomes a Buddhist as going for refuge to the Buddha, Dharma, 
and Sangha is considered to be the boundary or measure or door that defines a Buddhist. On the other hand, if we do not go for refuge to  the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha as our final 
refuge, we will not be considered to be a Buddhist practitioner.   Another benefit of going for refuge is that it  serves as a basis for taking vows because in 
order to take vows we need to have previously  gone for refuge to the Buddha, Dharma, and 
Sangha. In other words, in order to take any of  the eight vows of individual liberation it is 
necessary to first go for refuge to the Three Je wels. The same is true of the bodhisattva vows 
and so forth.   Also due to going for refuge, the karmic obsc urations that we accumulated previously will 
become thinner, that is, they will lessen, and ev entually they will be exhausted. Also due to 
going for refuge, we accumulate vast merit. We will also not fall into the bad migrations, that 
is, we will no longer take rebirth in the lower realms. In addition, interferences, both human 
and non-human, will not be able to harm us. Th ere exist many different types of interferences 
including human beings who try to harm us by, for example, black magic, casting spells, and 
so forth. “Non-humans” include spirits, of wh ich there are many different types, that cause 
harm to sentient beings. Also inanimate ob jects, such as wind and fire, can cause 
interferences. If we go for refuge, such interferences will not be able to harm us. 
 In addition, due to going for refuge, we will  be able to achieve all our wishes, including 
enlightenment, as well as our temporary aims . And, by going for refuge properly, we will 
quickly attain buddhahood.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
1 It is the measure of being a Buddhist 
First: Although, generally speaking, there a ppear to be many ways of positing non-
Buddhists and Buddhists, it is widely known that Jowo [Atisha] and Shantipa 
distinguished them by [the fact of] going for refuge. Therefore, [Buddhists ] should 
be understood to be those who have achieved going for refuge and have not given 
it up. Hence, as for the measure of being a Buddhist, it is necessary to consider the 
Three Jewels as teacher and so forth from  the bottom of your heart. Without that, 
even though you do virtuous deeds, yo u will not be included among Buddhists.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
317 In general there are many different ways of posi ting “outer beings” and “inner beings,” that is, 
non-Buddhists and Buddhists, but Atisha and Sh antipa (perhaps Ratnakara Shantipa who was 
one of Atisha’s teachers) said that they are di stinguished by the fact that Buddhists are those 
who go for refuge from the depths of their he art to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha and do 
not give up the precepts of refuge, whereas no n-Buddhists do not do th is. However, Buddhists 
and non-Buddhists can also be distinguished from the point of view of method or conduct 
and from the point of view of wisdom or vi ew. For example, Buddhists are those who hold 
the views that: 5. all compounded phenomena are impermanent,  
6. all contaminated phenomena are suffering,  
7. all phenomena are empty and selfless (for exam ple, they are empty of being permanent, 
partless, and independent), 
8. nirvana, the state passed beyond sorro w or cyclic existence, is peace.  
If we accept or assert these four seals, we are considered to be Buddhists from the point of 
view of wisdom. I do not remember the four from the side of method, which are mentioned 
in a text by Khedrub Je, one of Lama Tsongkha pa’s two main disciples, and in some Sakya 
texts. Because of this, there are people, for ex ample, some Vaibhashikas, who from the point 
of view of wisdom are Buddhists but are not Buddhists from the point of view of method, 
and vice versa.  
 In short, in order to be a Buddhist it is necessary to go for refuge to the Three Jewels from the bottom of our hearts while thinking that th e Buddha is the teacher, the Dharma is his 
teachings, and the Sangha are those who follow hi s teachings. If we do not go for refuge in 
this way, even though we accumulate virtue we will not be included among Buddhists.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 It is the basis of all vows 
Second: In the Commentary on the Treasury  it says:  
 
Those who go for refuge have gained  access to taking all the vows 
correctly. 
 And in Seventy Verses on Going for Refuge  it also says: 
 
Upasakas go for refuge to the three; 
That is the root of the eight vows. 
 The intention is that, through going for refuge, the thought of passing beyond 
suffering
127 is stabilized and that the vows spring from that.  
 
In order to take vows it is necessa ry to have the basis of having gone for refuge. In this context 
“vows” refers to the vows of individual liberati on. In order to take them perfectly, we must 
first go for refuge to the Three Jewels. Because of this, going for refuge is like “a door” to 
                                              
127 “myang ’das kyi bsam pa ” – the literal meaning of “ myang ’das ”, a contraction of “ mya ngan las ’das pa ” is 
“passing beyond suffering.” It is the Tibeta n rendering of the Sanskrit term “nirvana”. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
318 taking these vows. For example, if we wish to go into a temple, we must first open the door, 
whereas if the door remains closed, we cannot enter it. Likewise, if we do not go for refuge, 
we cannot take the eight vows of individual liberation:  
(1) the one-day precepts (eight vows that ar e similar to the Eight Mahayana Precepts),  
(2-3) the vows of laymen an d laywomen (five vows),  
(4-5) the vows of novice monks and nuns (thirty-six vows),  
(6) the vows of probationary nuns (thirty-six pl us twelve additional vows that are taken when 
training for two years prior to taking the 364 vows of full-ordination), and  
(7-8) the vows of fully-ordained monks an d nuns (253 and 364 vows respectively).  
Thus, two of the eight vows are vows taken by lay people and six are vows taken by ordained people. The basis for taking any of these vows is refuge.   Having gone for refuge, the thought to atta in nirvana, the state passed beyond sorrow, 
becomes stable and this makes the generation of  vows more stable. On the other hand, if we 
take vows without the proper motivation and with out thinking at the time of the ordination 
that we have received the vows, we will not actu ally possess the vows but will merely have the 
aspect of someone who has taken vows. Vows can be taken but degenerated, or taken but not 
achieved. Having received the vows of a novice monk or nun, for example, we need to abide in them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The karmic obscurations accumulated previously are reduced and exhausted 
Third: In the Compendium of Trainings  it says: 
 
Here, in the context of showing that negativities are purified by going 
for refuge, the story of the pig should be presented as an illustration. 
 
For instance, a son of a god who was going t o  b e  r e b o rn  a s  a  p i g ,  { 9 7 }  t h r o u g h  
going for refuge was not born as one. It is stated that: 
 
Those who have gone for refuge to the Buddha, Will not go to the bad migrations. Having given up their human body They obtain the body of a god. 
 It is stated similarly with respect to the Dharma and Sangha.  
 The son of a god was to be reborn in his next li fe as a pig but, due to going for refuge to the 
Three Jewels, he was not born as a pig because in  this way he purified the negative karma to 
be born as a pig. This example shows that th ose who take the Buddha and so forth as their 
objects of refuge will not be reborn in the lowe r realms. Instead those who go for refuge to the 
Buddha while they are human beings will, when  they die, give up their human body and 
obtain the body of a god. The same result come s from going for refuge to the Dharma and the 
Sangha. Thus, those who go for refuge to the Dh arma Jewel – true paths and true cessations – 
will not be reborn in the bad migrations and when they die and give up their human body 
they will take the body of a god in their next li fe. It is similar for those who go for refuge to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
319 the Sangha Jewel. In short, going for refuge to  the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha is the source 
of increasing all our good qualities.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 One accumulates vast merit 
Fourth: In the Summary of the Perfections  it says:  
 
If the merit of refuge were to take shape 
Even the three realms would be small as a container. Great oceans, foremost of water bodies, Could not hold or measure it. 
 That is how [vast] it is. 
 If the merit or benefit of going for refuge were  to take form, it could not fit in the three 
realms: above, on, and below the ground. Just as  the water in the oceans cannot be measured 
by taking out one handful at a time, similarly the merit of going for refuge to the Three Jewels cannot be measured.   Thursday afternoon, 17 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 One will not fall into the bad migrations 
Fifth: You should understand this from the above.  
 
By going for refuge to the Three Jewels, we will  not go to the lower realms after our death. 
This is because by going for refuge, not only wi ll we not fall into the lower realms, but, having 
given up this human body, we will attain the body of a god.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
6 We will not be harmed by human and non-human obstacles 
Sixth: This is easy to understand. 
 
It is easy to understand that if we go for refuge we cannot be harmed by human and non-
human interferences because, having gone for re fuge to the Three Jewels, we will come under 
their protection.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
7 One will attain everything one wishes for 
Seventh: Whatever Dharma activity you do , if at the beginning you make offerings 
to the Three Jewels, go for refuge, an d supplicate them, it will easily be 
accomplished.
