320 At the beginning of accomplishing a Dharma acti vity, we should make offerings to the Three 
Jewels, recite a verse of refuge, and ask them fo r their help as in this way we will easily and 
quickly accomplish it. In other words, whenever  we are about to do a virtuous action, we 
should go for refuge, make offerings of water, flowers, incense, and so  forth, and request the 
Three Jewels for help, whereby we will easily achieve success in accomplishing the action.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
8 One will swiftly attain buddhahood 
In [the Sutra ] Requested by Singha  it says:  
 
Through faith unfreedom is abandoned. 
 
In accordance with that, you will obtain  the distinguished leisures and, having 
encountered refuge, train in the special path, whereby you will become a buddha 
without a long delay. Mindful of these kind s of benefits, you should go for refuge 
three times a day and three times at night. 
 If we have faith or confidence in the Three Jewels, we will be able to abandon states of non-leisure and obtain the special or distinguished leis ures, that is, a good rebirth, meet objects of 
refuge such as holy beings, and train in the special path. In this way, we will soon be able to attain our objective of buddhahood or enligh tenment. Thus, remembering the benefits of 
going for refuge, we should go for refuge thr ee times a day and three ti mes a night. In this 
way, things will go better in this life and in our next life. On the other hand, if, having gone for refuge, we do not recite a verse of refuge three times a day and thr ee times at night to the 
Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, we will create a down fall or fault. Due to this, we will not take 
a good rebirth for eons whereby it will become even more and more difficult to take a higher rebirth. Therefore, we should make sure to go  for refuge three times a day and three times at 
night.   Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4A-2F Do not give up the Three Jewels for one’s life nor for a laugh but guard against 
this 
There is no doubt that we will separate fr om this body, life, and possessions. If we 
give up the Three Jewels for their sake, there will be continuous suffering in all 
successive lives [to come], therefore we should vow not to abandon [our] refuge 
come what may, and not even utter words ab out giving up [our] refuge for a laugh. 
 
We must not give up the Three Jewels, in other words we must not give up going for refuge to the Three Jewels. Since we will have to separa te from our body, life, and belongings, we 
should not create negative karma for their sake by  giving up the Three Jewels as due to this in 
many future lives we will experience contin uous suffering. Therefore, we should make a 
promise to never give up going for refuge to the Three Jewels even if at the cost of losing our 
body, life, or belongings. We also should not give them up even as a joke in order to make o t h e r s  l a u g h .  E v e n  t h i s  w o u l d  c r e a t e  v e r y  n e g a t i v e  k a r m a ,  t h e  r e s u l t  o f  w h i c h  w o u l d  b e  t o  experience suffering and a lack of happiness. Th erefore, we should always examine what kind 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
321 of result such-and-such an action will bring in th e future. Then, if we find that certain actions 
will bring the result of suffering, we must strive to give them up. We need to try to do this; if 
we do not even try, nothing will happen. Theref ore, we must think about the advantages of 
going for refuge and the disadvantages of not go ing for refuge and then make a choice as to 
what to do. This is up to us. However, since sometimes our choices can be wrong and later on 
we come to regret them, we need to be careful, even as joke, not to say “I give up the Buddha. 
I  g i v e  u p  t h e  D h a r m a .  I  g i v e  u p  t h e  S a n g h a .  I  g i v e  u p  m y  v o w s , ”  a s  w e l l  a s  o t h e r  s u c h  
statements related to abandoning the Three Jewels.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2A4B Eliminating inaccuracies [with respect to the precepts of going for refuge] 
1 Refutation of that which is not a precept 
Former masters stated a precept to the e ffect that you should train in going for 
refuge to the tathagata of whatever dire ction you go in, but I have not seen a 
source [for that].  
 
Some past masters said that there is a precept of  refuge that when we are about to walk or go 
in a particular direction, we should first go for refuge to the tathagata of that particular 
direction, however Lama Tsongkhapa says that he has not seen a scriptural source for this 
precept. Wherever we go, we necessarily go in one of the ten directions – east, south, west, 
north, southeast, southwest, northeast, northwes t, up, or down. Thus, th ese masters said that 
we need to go for refuge to the tathagata of  that direction, but La ma Tsongkhapa has not 
been able to verify that there is a valid source for this precept.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The sources of the specific precepts 
Thus, the six common precepts [for all the objects of refuge] are just like they 
appear in the Commentary on the Lamp on the Path . {98} The first three specific 
precepts are explained in the sutra collec tion, whereas the latter three appear in 
the Six Branches of Going for Refuge . 
 The six common precepts or trainings, which are explained by Atisha in his Commentary on the 
Lamp on the Path , are: 
1. to remember the differences and positive qualit ies of the Three Jewels and, due to that, to 
go for refuge to them again and again, 
2. to remember their great kindness and, due to th at, to exert oneself at all times in making 
offerings to them and offering them even the first portion of one’s food and drink, 
3. to remember their great compassion and, due to  that, to set other migrating beings in such 
a mode [of behavior], 
4. whatever one does and whatever one needs,  to worship the Three Jewels and then 
supplicate them and abandon other worldly methods, 
5. to go for refuge three times a day and thr ee times at night while being aware of the 
benefits, and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
322 6. to not give up the Three Jewels even at the cost of one’s life nor as a joke and to guard 
against this. 
 
The first three specific precepts, which have their source in the sutra collection, are: 
1. having gone for refuge to the Buddha, to abandon going for refuge to mundane gods and 
so forth;  
2. having gone for refuge to the Dharma, to abandon harming any sentient being; and  
3. having gone for refuge to the Sangha, to ab andon associating with non-virtuous or non-
Buddhist companions such as Tirthikas.  
 
The latter three precepts, which are set out in Six Branches of Going for Refuge , are: 
1. having gone for refuge to the Buddha, to resp ect any statue of a buddha, whether made of 
gold, silver, wood, clay, and so forth, as well as any painting and so forth, as if it were an 
actual buddha;  
2. having gone for refuge to the Dharma, to resp ect even one word or phrase of the texts of 
Buddha’s teachings as if it were the actual Dh arma Jewel by putting texts in high places, 
remembering that they represent true cessation s and true paths and that by means of them 
we can attain the realization of the actual Dharma; and  
3. having gone for refuge to the Sangha, to resp ect even a scrap of red or yellow cloth as 
though it belonged to the robe s of the sangha, as well as to respect monks and nuns as 
being representatives of th e actual Sangha Jewel.  
 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The difference between degenerating and relinquishing [the precepts] 
As for the way in which the transgression  of these precepts becomes a cause of 
degenerating and relinquishing them, the transgression of [the precept ] to not give 
up your refuge even for the sake of your life actually constitutes relinquishing it. 
Similarly, even if you do not give up th e Three Jewels, but dualistically uphold the 
three – teachers and so forth – that ar e incompatible with them, you transgress 
[the precept to ] not advocate another refuge, and by not having confidence in the 
refuges you also relinquish it. I think that , if these do not occur, it is a mere 
transgression of the precepts but not a cause for relinquishing them. 
 
The transgression of the precepts of refuge beco mes a cause for us to degenerate our precepts 
and can also become a cause for completely relinq uishing them. Therefore, even if our life is 
in danger, we must not give our refuge up by saying, “I give up going for refuge to the Three Jewels.” Also, even if we do not directly ab andon the Three Jewels, by upholding discordant 
teachers, that is, by going for refuge to them, we  transgress the precepts of refuge. If we think, 
for example, “I can also go for refuge to othe r teachers, teachings, and communities that are 
not the Buddha, Dharma, and Sang ha,” then this becomes a cause for transgressing the refuge 
precepts. However, as long as we do not go for refuge to other teachers  and so forth that are 
incompatible with the Buddha, Dharma, and Sang ha from the depths of our heart, while we 
transgress our refuge precepts we will not relinquish them.   
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
323 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 The advice not to transgress the precepts because of how great the benefits are, such as being the 
entrance gate to the teachings etc. 
Thus, going for refuge is the great entry gate to the Buddha’s teachings. And, if 
going for refuge does not consist in mere  words, you will not be thwarted by 
external or internal obstacles due to your relying on a superior power, and by the 
distinction of the good qualities coming about easily and deteriorating with 
difficulty, they will increase more and more. Therefore, it is vital that you uphold 
your refuge by means of being mindful of the fear and the positive qualities 
explained above, and that you put effort  into not transgressing its precepts.  
 Going for refuge is the great door or entranceway to the Buddha’s teachings or doctrine. In other words, we become Buddhist practitioners by going for refuge from the depths of our 
heart to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. If we go for refuge to them sincerely, we will not 
be overcome by inner and outer interferences.  O n  t h e  o t h e r  h a n d ,  w e  s h o u l d  n o t  g o  f o r  
refuge by just reciting the mere words “I go fo r refuge to the Buddha. I go for refuge to the 
Dharma. I go for refuge to the Sangha.” If we go for refuge from the depths of our heart, we 
will generate all good qualities and they will no t degenerate, whereby all our white actions will 
increase more and more. If we remember fe ar and the qualities of Buddha, Dharma, and 
Sangha, we will uphold our refuge and will not transgress its precepts. Therefore, in our daily 
life we need to go for refuge to the Three Je wels from our heart while reciting the words of 
refuge and recalling their qualities. In this way we will not be overcome by outer and inner 
interferences and our good qualities will increa se more and more, just as the waxing moon 
gradually becomes a full moon, unti l we attain complete buddhahood.  
 Having gone for refuge to the Three Jewels, we also need to guard the law of actions and 
results. Since this is like a precept of going for refuge, next we need to develop conviction in this law.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B Generating the faith of conviction in karma and its results 
A Considering karma and its results in general 
B Considering karma and its results in particular 
C Having considered them, how to engage [in virtuous actions] and counteract [the non-
virtuous] 
The second one – generating the faith of conviction in karma and its results – has 
three points: considerations about karma an d its results in general, considerations 
about actions and their results in particul ar, and having considered them, how to 
engage [in virtuous actions] and counteract [the non-virtuous].  
 
First we need to think about actions and their re sults in general, then about them in specific, 
and then about how to engage in certain actions and avoid others.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

324 2B4B-2B1A-2B1 Considering karma128
 and its results in general 
A The actual way to consider karma and its results in general 
B Considering the specific divisions 
The first one has two points: the actual w ay to consider karma and its results in 
general and considering the specific divisions. 
 
First there is the way to think about actions an d their results in general and then thinking 
about the specific divisions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1A The actual way to consider karma and its results in general 
1 How karma is certain 
2 The great increase of karma 
3 You do not meet with karma you have not created 
4 Karma you have created does not dissipate 
The first one has four points: how karma is  certain, the great increase of karma, 
you do not meet with karma you have not created, and karma you have created 
does not dissipate. 
 
The four points here are: the results of karma are certain, karma increases, karma that has not 
been created does not bring a result, and the karm a that has been created is not lost or does 
not go to waste.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1A-1 How karma is certain 
1 The actual way in which karma is certain 
All happiness having the aspect of pleasant feeling, whether in ordinary beings or 
aryas – even as little as that which is gene rated in a sentient being born in hell in 
dependence on a cool wind springing up – arises from virtuous karma 
accumulated previously. {99} Happiness ar ising from non-virtuous karma is a non-
source. All suffering having the aspect of painful feelings – even as little at that 
which is generated in the mind of an arhat – arises from non-virtuous karma 
accumulated previously. Suffering arising fr om virtuous karma is  a non-source. In 
the Precious Garland  it says:  
 
From the non-virtuous, all suffering; 
Likewise, all the bad migrations. From the virtuous, all happy realms and Happiness in all rebirths. 
 
                                             
 
128 In Tibetan, the word for “action” and “karma” is the same (“ las”). It will be useful to keep this in mind 
throughout the following explanations  on karma. The word “karma” has b een chosen in cases where the term 
“action” failed to put across the implied relation with an  effect, e.g., “karma that is  certainly experienced” or 
“accumulate karma”. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
325 A n y  e x p e r i e n c e  o f  h a p p i n e s s ,  w h e t h e r  b y  o r d i n a r y  b e i n g s  o r  a r y a  b e i n g s ,  i s  t h e  r e s u l t  o f  a  
virtuous action accumulated in the past. Even th e experience of a cool breeze in the hell realm 
that relieves the suffering a bit is due to having  accumulated virtuous actions in the past, that 
is, in a previous life. Without having create d a virtuous action, happiness cannot arise. 
Similarly, all suffering feeling that arises, even that in the mind of a foe-destroyer, is due to 
having created a non-virtuous action in the past  and not having purified it. Virtuous actions 
are not a source of suffering, nor are non- virtuous actions a source of happiness.  
 Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland  says that all suffering comes from non-virtuous actions, as 
does rebirth in the bad migrations. Similarly, all happiness, as well as rebirth in the happy 
migrations, comes from virtuous actions. Thus, both suffering and happiness come from a 
specific source. Suffering comes from the source  that is non-virtuous actions, and happiness 
comes from the source that is virtuous actions. We need to understand this clearly. Then we 
must try as much as possible in our daily life to abandon creating non- virtuous actions and to 
try as much as possible to create virtuous action s. In this way we will make good use of this 
precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qu alities that we have obtained presently. If 
we do not do this but instead use it to engage  in non-virtuous actions, our precious human 
rebirth will be a complete waste.   Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2 Explanations [of this] as the basis of all white dharmas 
Therefore, happiness and suffering do no t arise without a cause or from an 
incongruous cause such as a primordial prin cipal, Ishvara, and the like. In general 
happiness and suffering arise in general fr om virtuous and non-virtuous karma. 
Also the various specifics of happiness an d suffering arise individually from the 
various specifics of the two [t ypes of] of karma without any error. To gain certainty 
with regard to actions and their results bein g certain and infallible is said to be the 
correct view of all Buddhists and is praise d as the foundation of all white dharmas. 
 The law of actions and their results is that happiness comes from virtue and suffering comes 
from non-virtue. However, some people assert that happiness and suffering arise without a cause. For example, the Charvakas do not acce pt past and future lives and say that the 
roundness of peas is without a cause, as is the sh arpness of thorns. Similarly, they also say that 
the colors in the eyes of a peacock’s feathers ar ise without cause. In this way they assert that 
results arise without needing to depend on causes and conditions.  
 The Samkyas, on the other hand, say that a ll results arise from a primordial or general 
principal, that is, it is the creator of all resu lts. They divide all objects of knowledge into 
twenty-five categories and say that, among them , the primordial principal is a cause alone, 
seven are both cause and result, sixteen are results alone, and one – the self, knower, 
consciousness, or person – is neither a cause nor a result. Thus, they assert that the person is 
permanent and that when that person, self, kn ower, or consciousness, which is permanent 
and neither a cause nor a result, searches for the creator of all results and discovers that they 
are created by the primordial principal, the pr imordial principal becomes ashamed of having 
been recognized, whereby all results absorb in to it and it too disappears, like a rainbow 
vanishing in the sky. At this ti me the self, knower, or consciou sness achieves nirvana, and it 
alone remains.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
326  Other schools assert that the creator of everyt hing is Ishvara. They say that when Ishvara 
merely thinks of something, it immediately comes into existence. This a ssertion as well as that 
of the Samkyas are cases of asserting that things  arise from a discordant or incongruent cause, 
however results cannot arise from a discordant cause. We need to think about and 
understand this.  
 One time there was a meeting in Sarnath of pe ople knowledgeable in these different tenets. 
One man strongly asserted that thinks arise wi thout a cause. Another man asserted that if 
something exists it must be able to be seen wi th the eyes, due to which past and future lives 
do not exist. In response, someone asked him, “Does that mean that the back of your head 
does not exist since you cannot see it? Do organs such as lungs, livers, kidneys, and so forth 
not exist in your body since you cannot see them ?” When the man replied that they do exist, 
his challenger asked, “Is your liver big or smal l?” The man refused to answer saying he did not 
want to debate with a child. However, the next day he did not return to the meeting. 
 In short, all happinesses and sufferings arise from a particular and unmistaken cause. 
Therefore, we need to understand that a certain kind of result arises from a certain kind of 
cause. To gain certainty with respect to this is to have a correct view of the law of actions and 
results, that is, that results accords with their causes. Since this understanding is praised as the 
foundation of all white dharmas, that is, all good qualities and al l happiness, we need to try to 
gain certainty with respect to the relation ship between actions and their results.  
 
Friday morning, 18 March 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 99) says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1A-2 The great increase of karma 
Second: There is nothing among external causes and results that resembles the 
increase of internal causes and results, for even from a small virtuous karma there 
arises the result of enormous happiness while even from a small non-virtuous 
karma there arises the result of enormous suffering.  
 
Karma or action increases such that even a sma ll virtuous action can bring the result of great 
happiness, and even a small non-virtuous acti on can bring the result of great suffering. 
Similarly, we see that in our external world wh en a tiny seed, for example, an apple seed, is 
sown in the ground it gives rise to a large tr ee with many branches laden with many apples. 
Also just as if an external seed is planted in the ground but is then ea ten by a bird or insects 
or rots in the ground or is burnt by fire, it wi ll not bring a result, similarly if we confess and 
purify our non-virtuous actions by applying th e four opponent forces, they will not bring a 
result. Also, just as fire destroys a seed, the vi rtuous actions that we created in the past are 
destroyed by very strong anger, particularly that developed toward a holy being or a holy 
object, whereby they will not bring the result of happiness. In this way, we should examine 
external analogies in order to understand that ju st as from a small seed comes a big result, so 
too from a small action comes a big result. For ex ample, if we were to kill an ant or mosquito 
today and did not purify it, tomorrow it would be  twice as great in strength, the next day it 
would be four times as great in strength, and so  forth, that is, each day the strength of the 
action would be multiplied exponentially.   
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
327 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In the Udanavarga  it says:  
 
Even committing a small negativity 
Will bring about great fear and great Disaster in future worlds, 
Like poison that seeps through to the core. 
 Even committing small merit Will bring about great happiness in future worlds, Causing greatly meaningful things, Like grains that ripen in the excellent. 
 
If we ingest even a small amount of poison it can be very dangerous since it can make us very 
sick and even take our life. Likewise, even a sm all negativity can bring us great fear and great 
disaster in the next world (literally, the next tr ansitory basis). Therefore, we should strive not 
to create such actions in the sa me way that we try to avoid inge sting poison (unless we want to 
commit suicide!) through fearing that it will kill us . Our greatest fear is that of losing our life, 
it being much greater than the fear of losing ou r belongings or even our friends and relatives, 
due to which we are careful not to in gest the smallest bit of poison.  
 On the other hand, even small merit or virtue  can bring great happiness in our future lives 
and enable us to achieve great aims, just like so wing a small barley seed in the ground brings 
the excellent result of a barley plant. For this reason, we should avoid thinking that it is not 
important to create small virtuous actions, an d since they will bring a great and meaningful 
result we need to strive to create them. In sh ort, in our daily life we must strive to avoid 
creating even small negativities and strive to  create even small virt uous actions, without 
thinking that they are not important or do not matter.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1A-3 You do not meet with karma you have not created 
Third: {100} If you have not accumulate d karma that will become the cause for 
experiencing happiness or suffering, you wi ll never experience the happiness or 
suffering that is its result. Also, thos e who enjoy the effects of the Teacher’s 
accumulation of an incalculable collection [of merit] do not need to accumulate 
all its causes, however they need to accumulate a part of them129.  
 
If we do not accumulate an action, virtuous or non-virtuous, we will not experience its respective result of happiness or suffering. Thus , without creating an action, there will never 
                                             
 
129 Geshe Thubten Ngawang (Tibetan Centre, Hamburg) offered the following explanation for this passage: 
Buddha Shakyamuni, apart from accumulating the coll ection of merit necessary for attaining buddhahood, 
also spent some time accumulating fu rther merit which he dedicated to the goal that his followers meet with 
the necessary living conditions. However, according to  the laws of karma, they can only experience the 
corresponding results if they themselv es accumulate a part of the cause. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
328 be the result of happiness or suffering. It is similar to the fact that if a farmer does not sow 
seeds in his field, he will not experience the result of crops, and if a gardener does not plant flower seeds, he will not experience the result o f  f l o w e r s .  S i m i l a r l y ,  i f  w e  h a v e  n o t  c r e a t e d  
virtuous or non-virtuous actions, we will not ex perience the result of happiness or suffering.  
 The Teacher, that is, Buddha Shakyamuni, a ccumulated merit for three countless eons and 
thereby achieved buddhahood. He then gave te achings or turned the wheel of Dharma. We 
fortunate beings can enjoy or utilize the result of his having turned the wheel of Dharma by 
studying his teachings and meditating on them. However, to do this, we too need to have 
created merit in the past since if we did not create merit in the past, we will not meet the 
teachings. Due to having accumulated merit,  we can study the Buddha’s scriptures, think 
about them, and meditate on them. In this w ay we are able to generate the path in our 
continuum: the path of accumulation, path of  preparation, path of seeing, path of 
meditation, and path of no-more-learning. We can also enjoy or train in the higher training in 
morality, higher training in concentration, and higher training in wisdom. We can also enjoy, 
that is, we can develop, the th ree principal aspects of the path  – renunciation or definite 
emergence, the uncontrived conventional mind  of enlightenment or bodhichitta, and the 
correct view of emptiness – in our mind whereb y we can attain buddhahood . All this is due to 
being able to enjoy the Buddha’s teachings du e to having created merit in the past and 
continuing to create merit in the present. By doing this, even when the doctrine of this 
buddha vanishes, we will be able to meet with  the teachings of Buddha Maitreya and achieve 
the final result. This is because Buddha Maitre ya made a promise that those who follow the 
teachings of Buddha Shakyamuni but do not at that time attain higher realizations, will attain 
the state of a foe-destroyer at the time of his doctrine and pass into nirvana. Thus, if we do 
not attain nirvana or enlightenm ent in this lifetime, we should pray to be able to achieve 
enlightenment within sixteen lifetimes. We shou ld make a determination to practice, whereby 
we will be able to achieve this result.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1A-4 Karma you have created does not dissipate 
Fourth: The virtuous and non-virtuous ac tions that have been done issue forth 
pleasant and unpleasant results. In the Praise to the Particularly Noble  it says:  
 
Brahmins say they can transfer 
Virtues and negativities as though giving and taking them. You said, what is done won’t vanish, What is not will not be met. 
 A virtuous action brings a pleasant result, wher eas a non-virtuous action brings an unpleasant 
result. This is a natural rule. It can also be said  that they respectively bring forth the result of 
happiness and the result of suffering.  
 The Praise to the Particularly Noble  says that while brahmins say that the results of actions 
can be transferred to someone else, the Buddha sa id that if we have cr eated virtuous or non-
virtuous actions they will not go to waste but will bring a result, and that if we have not 
created a particular action we will not meet it s result. Thus, although there are brahmins who 
assert that our actions can be given to someon e else, like giving them our rosary, watch, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
329 clothing, and so forth, this is not correct. We cannot give our happiness or suffering to 
others. If this were possible, the holy beings  would give us happiness and take away our 
suffering. But since we cannot give others our happiness nor take away their suffering, we 
cannot transfer our karma to others. If we have  created karma we will experience its result, 
and if we have not created karma we will not ex perience its result. If we create a negative 
action, we should purify it so that it cannot brin g a result. If we do not, at some point we will 
have to experience its result since it will not go  to waste. While it may not bring a result either 
in this life or in our next life, in some di stant future life the result will eventually be 
experienced.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And from the King of Concentration  [Sutra ]: 
 
Plus you cannot but touch your deeds, 
What others did you will not feel. 
 
And from the Foundation of  [Vinaya ] Scriptures : 
 
Karmas do not go to waste 
Even in hundreds of eons; when 
They find the host [of conditions ] and time, 
They will become effects for beings130. 
 
If we create an action, it is we ourselves who wi ll come into contact or m eet with its result. On 
the other hand, we will not experience the result  of happiness or suffering of actions that have 
been done by others.   E v e n  i f  w e  c r e a t e d  a n  a c t i o n  a  h u n d r e d  e o n s  a g o ,  i t  w i l l  n o t  g o  t o  w a s t e .  W h e n  t h e  conditions and time come together, we will experience its result. In other words, when 
conditions come together and the time is right, a karma created even a hundred eons 
previously will bring its result. However, in order for a result to ripen certain conditions are necessary and the time must be right. In terms of  time, if, for example, we were to sow a seed 
in the ground during the winter wh en it is very cold and the wind  is strong, it will not bring a 
result. On the other hand, if we plant a seed in  the summer and water it and so forth, it will 
bring the result of a sprout. Likewise, although a seed is healthy and not rotten, if we plant it 
in the ground but do not water it, it will not br ing a result. Therefore, just as the conditions 
and the time must be right to bring the result of  a sprout, so too is it similar in the case of 
karma.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B Considering the specific divisions 
                                              
130 Lit. “ lus can rnams ” – “those endowed with bodies” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
330 1 Indicating that there are mainly ten paths of action 
2 Establishing actions and effects 
The second one has two points: indicating  that there are mainly ten paths of 
action and establishing actions and effects. 
 
Ten paths of actions – ten virtuous paths of ac tions and ten non-virtuous paths of actions – 
are presented in the Buddha’s sutras as well as in the commentaries written by Indian pandits. 
The ten non-virtuous pa ths of actions are: 
7. the three created by the body – killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct, 
8. the four created by the speech – lying, divisi ve speech, harsh words, and idle talk, and 
9. the three created by mind – covetousness, malice, and wrong view.  
Similarly, there are ten virtuous paths of ac tions that are the abandonments of killing, 
stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisive speech, harsh words, idle talk, covetousness, 
malice, and wrong view. These ten actions include most of the coarse actions of body, speech, 
and mind.  
 Then in the next section actions and their resu lts are settled, there being four main results.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-1 Indicating that there are mainly ten paths of action  
A Explanation that there are ten in the sutras and treatises 
First: Thus you should first generate cert ainty concerning the ways of actions and 
results through realizing that happiness an d suffering results are certain to have 
their individual causes, that karma increa ses greatly, that you do not meet with 
anything you have not created, and that karma does not dissipate. Now, if you ask 
about what [actions] to adopt and what ones to reject, [it is as follows ]. In general, 
the gates for engaging in good conduct an d faulty conduct are determined to be 
three. Although not all virtues and non-vi rtues of the three doors are included in 
the ten paths of action, the more obviou s or coarser virtues and non-virtues are 
[included in them ]. The Bhagavan, summing up the essential points, stated that 
those that are the great foundation of mi sdeeds are the ten black paths of action. 
Having seen that, when they are given up , the most significant points are also 
included in ten, he taught them to be  the ten white paths of actions {101}.  
 
The two results are happiness and suffering, the two causes are virtue and non-virtue. Thus, 
the result of a karma is definite, karma increase s, if we have not created a karma we will not 
experience its result, and if we have created a karma it will not go to waste. Understanding 
how actions and results function, we should ne xt examine which actions are to be abandoned 
and which actions are to be adopted.   There are three doors for engaging in good conduct and faulty conduct – the door of the 
body, the door of the speech, and the door of the mind. By means of these three doors, we 
engage in all the different kinds of actions. In  other words, all virtuous actions and all non-
virtuous actions are created by our body, speech,  and mind. Are all these actions included in 
the either the ten non-virtuous paths of action or the ten virtuous path s of action? Although 
they are not, most of them, especially the coarser ones, are included in them. Buddha 
Shakyamuni set out ten black paths of action, and their opposites or abandonments, the ten 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
331 white paths of action. By abando ning the ten black paths of ac tion, we create the ten white 
paths of action.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In the Treasury  it says:  
 
Through summarizing the most obvious among them, 
The virtuous and non-virtuous  Were stated as ten paths of action. 
 
Also, in the Scripture on Discernment  it says:  
 
Guarding one’s speech and completely restraining one’s mind, 
Refraining from non-virtuous deeds with the body, 
Once you have purified the three paths of action, You will attain the path that was taught by the seers. 
 
The Treasury of Manifest Dharma  says that there are ten virtuous  and ten non-virtuous paths of 
actions: 
 
The non-virtues of the body and so forth,  
Are asserted to be the three faulty conducts.  
Although covetousness and so forth are not actions,  They are the three types of faul ty conduct of mind.  [4.65] 
 Their opposites are good conduct. Through condensing the mo st obvious among them,  
The paths of actions, the virtuous and  Non-virtuous in kind, were taught to be ten.   [4.66] 
 We need to guard or protect our speech by no t saying much. In other words, we should 
control our mouth as otherwise we tend to say things that make others unhappy, cause them 
pain, or disturb them. Therefore,  by controlling our mind we n eed to protect our speech. We 
also need to strongly restrain our mind. In addition, we need to stop creating non-virtuous actions of body by controlling our mind. Whenever we beat others or say bad words to them, these actions originate from our mind. Therefore,  we need to make a strong promise to not 
speak bad words, to not lie, and so forth and to not kill and so forth. By controlling our 
mind, we will be able to engage in the ten wh ite paths of actions. Having purified the three 
paths of actions of the three doors,  we will attain the path taught by seers or sages, that is, by 
holy beings.   We should try to listen to Dharma teaching s and read Dharma books, to keep our mind 
quiet, and to generate the motivation to not enga ge in non-virtuous actions of body by killing, 
stealing, and sexual misconduct and to not engage  in non-virtuous actions of speech by lying, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
332 using divisive speech, saying harsh words, and engaging in idle talk or gossip. By making this 
promise and not engaging in non-virtuous action s, we can purify our negativities of body, 
speech, and mind and achieve an actual path.  
 
QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS 
  
STUDENT : When we die, how does the I continue such that in the next life it can become the 
experiencer of our past actions? Since it seems that the person of the next life can be very 
different, perhaps the future person is not even I.  
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : In this life we can be a man and in the next life a woman! If we do not 
experience the result of a particular karma in this  life, we can experience it in our next life or 
in any other of our future lives. Although it is  not the same person who experiences the result 
of the past action, it is the same  continuum. There are said to be  four types of “I”: the I that 
comes from the previous life to this life, the I of  this life, the I of the next life, and the I that 
continues throughout all these lives. This latter I is the I on which all the karmic imprints of 
this and previous lives has been deposited and th at travels to future lives. Presently we are 
studying the Dharma, making offerings, doing pr ostrations, making mandala offerings, and so 
forth, the results of which we may experience in this life with this body, or in our next life with another body, or in a more distant future li fe with another body. In either case, there is 
an I that continues and that experiences the result.   
STUDENT : What is the difference between merit an d virtue? When a cat, for example, is 
carried around a stupa, although it probably receives some merit does it also create virtue even 
though it has no intention to go around the stupa? 
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : I think that merit and virtue are the same. The cat does not have any 
motivation or thought to go around the stupa,  but the human being who carries it around the 
stupa will create both merit and virtue through wi shing to benefit the cat.  In short, virtue is 
merit. This is my opinion! 
 
STUDENT :  W h e n  i s  i t  p r o p e r  t o  t a k e  d o w n  f o o d  o f f e r i n g s  f r o m  t h e  a l t a r  a n d  w i t h  w h a t  
attitude should they be taken down so that it does not become stealing? 
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : Taking down food offerings from one’s altar is not stealing. Having 
offered food on the altar, we can take it away  even after one day. We  should think that the 
holy beings have taken the offerings and ble ssed them, and then we can then ask them to 
return them to us. Likewise, if it is our job to take care of a temple, clean it, and make 
offerings on the altar, when we remove the o fferings it is not stealing. However, we can 
mentally ask the holy beings to return the offerings to us after having blessed them.  
 
STUDENT : We have some questions about the pure lands:  
• What does it mean that a place like Akanisht a is located in the fourth concentration?  
• Is it a place in cyclic existence? 
• Is it a true suffering?  
• Is it a place in samsara for a being in samsara but a place in nirvana for a being who has 
attained nirvana?  
• Can ordinary beings see it? 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 14-18 March 2005 (07) 
333 GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : If, for example, a being of nirvana were present in this assembly hall, 
he would see it as a pure land, whereas for us beings who are not of ni rvana it is a place of 
suffering. There are five pure lands included in  the fourth concentration. The word “pure” 
indicates that only arya beings can take rebirt h there, that is, ordinary beings do not take 
rebirth there. Therefore, Akanishta or Not Low is not a place of cyclic existence or samsara, so 
perhaps it can be considered to be a place of ni rvana. Previously I said that Akanishta is like 
the Vatican City which is located inside Rome; Vatican City being like a pure land and Rome 
being a city of suffering where there are many demonstrations, a lot of fighting, cheating, and 
so forth.  
 
STUDENT : How does a buddha know all phenomena of the three times directly , if all 
phenomena are imputed by the minds of sentient beings?  
• If he knows them by knowing the minds of sentient beings , he would not know them directly. 
• If he knows them by directly perceiving the object , does that mean he sees the water, pus, and 
nectar which are imputed by a human, a hungry  ghost, and a god at the same time on the 
same basis? 
• How does he see future phenomena , which are not even imputed by anyone yet?  
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : A buddha, without doubt, sees all phenomena directly. He can also see 
others’ minds directly, what they are thinking an d so forth. It is said that a human being who 
has created virtuous actions, a god who has crea ted virtuous actions, and a hungry ghost who 
has created non-virtuous actions, will see a cup of water differently. Why is this? If a hungry 
ghost takes up the cup, by the force of his karm a the liquid will transform into pus. This is 
not a mere imputation but is due to the hungry  ghost’s karma. If a go d takes it up, by the 
force of his karma the liquid will transform into  nectar, since he always drinks nectar. If a 
human being takes it up, it will remain water. Th is is all due to karma; it is not due to a god 
imputing nectar, a hungry ghost imputing pus,  and a human being imputing water. Karma is 
very strange. It is even possible that a woman can transform into a man and vice versa. While 
such events happened in the past, I don’t kn ow whether they still happen spontaneously. 
Perhaps this only happens these days by way of an operation!  
 
END

334 Monday afternoon, 4 April 2005 
 (Note: There were no teaching s from 19 March to 3 April du e to the BP retreat and exam 
followed by the Easter holidays.) 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 101) says:  
B Explanation of cherishing the adoption and abandonment of the ten [paths of action]  
Therefore, the meaning of the praise to the ethical discipline of abandoning the 
ten non-virtuous [paths of action] from the Sutra on the Ten Grounds  is summarized 
in Entry to the Middle Way :  
 
For certain goodness and the high status 
Of ordinary beings, those born from [the Buddha’s ] speech,131 
Of those with the definite nature of self-enlightenment132, 
The Jinas’ children133 – the cause is not other than discipline. 
 
We need to take care of and cherish the practi ce of the morality of abandoning the ten non-
virtuous paths of actions and practice the ten virtuous paths of actions. Ordinary beings, 
those born from Buddha’s speech, that is, the hearers, those who achieve self-enlightenment, 
that is, the solitary realizers, and the children  of the Conquerors attain definite goodness and 
high status based on the cause that is morali ty. Therefore, we too need to practice the 
morality of abandoning the ten non-virtuous pa ths of actions. In ot her words, whether we 
wish to attain the small enlightenment of th e hearers, the middling enlightenment of the 
solitary realizers, or the great enlightenment of  the buddhas we must do so on the basis of 
keeping morality. Therefore, morality is very impo rtant. In short, we need to practice or adopt 
the ten virtuous paths of actions and abando n or discard the ten non-virtuous paths of 
actions. In our daily life we need to try to keep  our mind more disciplined, that is, to generate 
the mind of abandonment that wishes to abandon all non-virtuous actions. We need to 
abandon the three non-virtuous paths of actions of the body – killing, stealing, and sexual 
misconduct, and the four of speech – lying, di visive speech, harsh words, and idle talk. We 
also need to abandon the three non-virtuous paths of actions of mind. Thus, we need to 
abandon the covetousness that is attached to others’ excellences and wishes to possess them. 
We also need to abandon malice or harmful intent that wishes to harm others. We ordinary beings usually cherish our friends and relative s but wish to harm and create problems for 
those whom we consider to be our enemies and rejoice when they experience difficulties. We 
need to abandon this kind of attitude. We also need to abandon wrong views, although in general we do not have them. In conclusion, we must strive to abandon the ten non-virtues as 
much as possible.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
                                             
 
131 Synonym for “shravaka”, the “hea rers” of [Buddha’s teachings]  
132 Synonym for pratyekabuddha – solitary realizer 
133 Synonym for bodhisattva 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
335 C Giving up hypocrisy 
That being so, it would be quite disgrace ful if someone who does not guard even a 
bit of ethical discipline through relying on  the mind of restraint again and again, 
were to say: “As for me, I am a Mahayanist.” In the Kshitigarbha Sutra  it says:  
 
By means of these ten virtuous paths [of actions] you become a buddha. 
On the other hand, if one who does not guard even a single virtuous 
path of action throughout his life and yet says: “I am a Mahayanist. I 
seek unsurpassed, perfectly complete  enlightenment,” that person is a 
thorough hypocrite and a great liar, deceiving the world in front of all the bhagavans and propounding nihilism . At the time of death, he will 
be deluded and go to the wrongly fallen. 
 “Wrongly fallen” should be understood as a synonym for the bad migrations in 
their entirety. 
 We need to give up hypocrisy. Otherwise, if we do not even try to abandon the seven 
abandonments, the three of body and four of sp eech, by thinking not to commit them again 
and generating the wish to keep morality, yet say “I am a Mahayanist,” this would be 
hypocritical. There are even people who do ne gative actions claiming to do them for the 
benefit of others! They think that  i t  i s  f i n e  t o  e v e n  k i l l  s o m e o n e  i f  i t  i s  f o r  t h e  b e n e f i t  o f  
others! However, this is not right and it woul d be better for them to say that they are not 
religious practitioners but that they are, for example, Communists and therefore against 
religion. In short, making such cl aim is utterly mistaken since it will cause others to engage in 
criticizing the Mahayana by saying that it is no t a teaching of the Buddha. In fact, there are 
people who say that the Buddha only taught Hi nayana and not Mahayana and that tantra is 
not part of Buddhism but comes from Hinduism.  
 The Kshitigarbha Sutra says that if we practice the ten virtuous paths of actions we will 
become a buddha in the future. On the other hand, if we do not even guard a single virtuous 
action while we are alive yet boastfully say, “I  am a Mahayanist. I seek unsurpassed, perfectly 
complete enlightenment,” we will be a hypocrite and a great liar. For example, if we were to 
receive an empowerment and claim to be a Mahayan ist but still go on creating non-virtue we 
would be a liar and would deceive the Buddha an d the entire world. Such behavior is very 
ignorant or deluded and will caus e us to take rebirth in the lower realms. At the time of death 
we will not be able to do anything about to pr event this and will fall into the bad migrations. 
Therefore, we should avoid such lies and practice as much as we can in our daily life. If, on 
the other hand, we tell such lies we will definitely fall into the lower realms. Monks and nuns who have received different levels of vows need to safeguard their vows, while everyone needs to try to abide by the natural rule of abandoning  the ten non-virtuous paths of actions, or at 
least one, two, three, and so forth of them. To  do this, we need to understand the different 
types of killing and so forth and then abandon them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2 Determination of actions and their effects 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
336 A Negative actions and their effects 
B Positive actions and their effects 
C Indication of other divisions of actions 
The second one – determination of actions and their effects – has three points: {102} 
negative actions and their effects, positive  actions and their effects, and indication 
of other divisions of actions. 
 
Black or negative actions are non-virtuous acti ons. They are called “bla ck” because they bring 
the bad result of suffering and difficulties. White or positive actions are virtuous actions. They 
are called “white” because they bring the good  result of happiness. There are also other 
divisions of actions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A Negative actions and their effects 
1 The actual negative paths of action 
2 Differences in weight 
3 Indication of their effects 
The first one has three points: the actual negative paths of ac tion, differences in 
weight, and indication of their effects. 
 
Black actions and their results are discussed unde r three points: actual black paths of actions, 
the differences in lightness and heaviness, and their results.   Black actions consist in the ten non-virtuous paths of actions: 
1. the three of body – killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct, 
2. the four of speech – lying, divisive sp eech, harsh words, and idle talk, and 
3. the three of mind – covetousness, malice, and wrong views.  
 White actions are their respective abandonments: 1. the abandonment of killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct, 
2. the abandonment of lying, divisive speech, harsh words, and idle talk, and 
3. the abandonment of covetousness, malice, and wrong views. 
In the Treasury of Manifest Dharma  and in the Hundred Actions Sutra  many other divisions of 
actions are set forth.   With respect to each non-virt uous path of action, there are those that are lighter and those 
that are heavier. For example, with respect to killing, the action of killing one’s father or 
mother is very heavy while that of killing an insect, such as an ant,  is much lighter. The 
heaviness of the action depends on the complete ness of the four branches – basis, attitude, 
performance, and completion.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1 The actual negative paths of action 
A The actual paths of action 
B Distinction between action and path 
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A The actual paths of action

2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-1 Killing 
A Basis134
 
B Attitude 
C Performance135
 
D Completion 
 
These are the four branches of killing.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-1A Basis 
As for the first one, the basis of killing is another sentient being. 
 
The basis of killing is a being with mind (a sent ient being) who is other than oneself. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-1B Attitude 
1 Discrimination 
The attitude has three points, the first of  which is discrimination, which [again] 
has four points:  
1. With regard to the basis, a sentient bein g, the discrimination of it as such,  
2. the discrimination of it is as not a sentient being, 
3. the discrimination of what is not a sentie nt being as not a sentient being, and  
4. the discrimination of it as a sentient being. 
The first and third discriminations are unmistaken, while the second and fourth 
are mistaken. That is, if you have the motivation, for instance, thinking to kill 
Devadatta alone but, when it comes to the performance, you mistakenly kill 
Yajnadatta there is no negativity to do with the actual basis since, for that, an 
unmistaken discrimination is necessary . However, if, at the time of the 
performance, you have the general motivation  thinking to kill whoever is at hand, 
an unmistaken discrimination is not nece ssary. You should be aware of this rule 
also with regard to the remaining nine as appropriate.  
 
The first division of th e attitude or thought ( bsam pa ) is discrimination. With respect to the 
basis that is a sentient being, one can discriminate  it as a sentient being or discriminate it as 
not a sentient being. With respect to a basis that  is not a sentient being, one can discriminate 
                                              
134 In this context the term “basis” ( gzhi) refers to the object of the action. 
135 JN: sbyor ba , also translated as “preparation” or “execution” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
338 it as not a sentient being or as a sentient being.  To discriminate what is not a sentient being as 
a sentient being is mistaken, for example, to disc riminate a scarecrow in a field that appears to 
be a human being as a sentient being or when  walking in the forest to imagine hearing 
footsteps behind one and to discriminate the pres ence of a sentient being although no one is 
there.  
 This implies that if we wanted to kill Sa dam Hussein, for example, and tried to kill him 
but instead killed someone else, it would not be a complete non-virtuous path of action. This 
is because in order to complete the action, we n eed to correctly discriminate and then actually 
kill the person we intended to kill. On the ot her hand, if we want to kill Devadatta but are 
also happy to kill anyone at all and then actual ly kill someone else, we would create an actual 
non-virtuous path of action of killing. Likewise, if a hunter wants to kill a specific animal but then kills whatever moves nearby, such as a bi rd or rabbit, he commits an actual path of 
action of killing.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Mental affliction 
The mental affliction is any of the three poisons.  
 
The mental affliction behind the action of ki lling can be any of the three mental poisons. 
Thus, we can kill out of attachment, for exampl e, to the skin, bones, and so forth of an 
animal, we can kill out of anger, and we can kill out of ignorance. In this way, any of the three 
mental poisons can induce the action of killing.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 Motivation 
The motivation consists in the wish to kill. 
 
The motivation is the wish to kill that thinks “I want to kill…”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-1C Performance 
1 The performer 
It is the same whether you yourself do  the performance or  whether you cause 
someone else to do it.  
 
In terms of the performance or preparation of killing, there is no difference whether we do 
the performance ourselves our make someone else do it. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The entity of the performance 
The entity of the performance is that it can be done by means of either a weapon, 
poison, or knowledge-mantra.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
339 The performance can involve kill ing with a weapon, poison, knowledge-mantra (black magic), 
and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-1D Completion 
The completion consists in the death [of the other being] due to the condition of 
the performance, at that ti me or at another time but before you yourself die.  
 
The completion or conclusion is that the other bein g dies, that is, his or her life is cut, before 
we ourselves die. Thus, if we shoot someone wi th the intention to kill but that person shoots 
and injures us due to which we die first, all fo ur branches will not be complete because we 
d i e d  b e f o r e  o u r  v i c t i m .  I n  t h i s  w a y ,  w e  w i l l  n o t achieve an actual path  of action of killing 
because for this to happen the completion is nece ssary, that is, the other being must die first.  
 In short, all four branches are necessary in or der for the action of killing to be complete – 
the basis, attitude, performance, and completion. Whether we kill a being ourselves or cause 
others to kill, if all four branches are present, we achieve an actual path of action of killing. 
On the other hand, if one or more branches is  missing, while we will not achieve the actual 
path of action of killing, we still create some negativity and will experience its result. However, 
if we do purification by means of relying on the four opponent powers, we will not need to 
experience its result. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-2 Stealing 
A Basis 
B Attitude 
C Performance 
D Completion 
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-2A Basis 
The basis of stealing is any substance that  is thoroughly held by someone else.  
 
A complete action of stealing also consists  of the four branches of basis, attitude, 
performance, and completion. The basis of steali ng is anything that belongs to someone else. 
Therefore, if we take anything that belong s to others without permission, for example, 
clothing, a watch, a car, an anim al, and so forth, it is stealing. In addition, if we take that 
which is prohibited by law and punishable by law,  we engage in stealing. Thus, even if we just 
take a handful of rice but do so day after day, wh en it comes to have a certain value it will be 
stealing. Likewise, if we steal one cent today, th en take another cent the next day, and so on, 
when the total comes to have a certain value it will be considered stealing.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-2B Attitude 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
340 As for the attitude, among the three, the discrimination and mental affliction are 
as above136.  
{103} The motivation consists in the wi sh to take something away from another 
[person] without it having been given. 
 
There are four discriminations as before:  1. discriminating that which is another’s possession as being another’s possession, 
2. discriminating that which is another’s po ssession as not being another’s possession, 
3. discriminating that which is not another’s possession as being another’s possession, and 
4. discriminating that which is not another’s po ssession as not being another’s possession, 
 The discrimination must be correct: that is the discrimination of another’s possession as 
another’s possession. The affliction can be the a ttachment that wants to have something. It 
can also be anger, for example, the wish to ha rm someone by taking his belongings, including 
his wife, child, horse, car, and so forth. So metimes when we are not able to harm someone 
directly, we steal something from him in order to  cause harm indirectly. We can also steal out 
of ignorance, this happens when  we steal without intending to steal. The motivation is the 
wish to separate an owner from his possessions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-2C Performance 
1 The performer 
As regards the performance, the perfor mer is as [was explained] above.  
 
We can do the action of stealing ourselves or ma ke someone else do it by ordering him or her 
to steal. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The entity of the performance 
As for the entity of the performance, it is the same whether you take something by 
force or steal it sneakily. In addition, being deceitful with respect to a debt or a 
trust and using other fraudulent methods also constitutes stealing. Also whether it 
is done for one’s own or someone else’s purpose or to harm someone etc., it is 
stealing. 
 
The entity of the performa nce can be either to take something by force or to take it by stealth 
or ambush. For example, there are those who ro b others by holding a gun to their heads and 
threatening, “If you do not give me your belong ings, I will kill you,” whereas others steal by 
stealth, for example, robbers who enter a house at night. Then there are those who steal by 
deceit saying, for example, “You have a very nice  watch. Can I see it?” The thief then puts it 
                                              
136 That is, as regards the discrimination , the basis must be correctly discriminated. As regards the mental 
affliction, one or several of the three poisons may be pres ent. This is how the following references (“as above”) 
should also be understood. (Oral explanation by Geshe Soepa.) 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso

341 on his arm and runs off with it. There are also pickpockets who distract someone by bumping 
into him or her and then taking off with his or  her wallet or purse. This often happens in 
Connaught Place in Delhi as well as  here in Italy. Once when I was taking the train to Venice, 
I saw a transvestite run off with someone’s purse.  There are many ways to steal, for example, 
we can ask someone to borrow money but then  not return it. It can also happen that 
someone asks us to take care of his things but when he returns we do not want to give them 
back! There are different ways of cheating others. However, if we take things in any of these 
ways, we steal. Thus, there are different ways of stealing, for example, sometimes banks fail 
and do not return people’s money, and someti mes companies go bankrupt and do not return 
the investors’ money.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-2D Completion 
The completion consists in the arisal of  the thought that one has obtained it. 
However, if you have caused someone else to rob or steal, it is en ough [for t hat  
thought] to arise in him. This is like wh en someone kills someone else due to one’s 
order and, even without one’s knowledge, when the latter dies, the negativity of 
the actual [path of action ] befalls the one who gave the order to kill.  
 
When we think “Now it is mine,” this completes the path of action of stealing. Also if we 
order someone else to steal something, when th e thief obtains the object and thinks “Now it 
is mine,” simultaneously we too achieve a comple te action of stealing even though we do not 
think “Now it is mine.” Similarly, if we order someone else to kill, when that person kills the victim, we simultaneously achiev e a complete action of killing even though we do not know 
that the victim has died.   Tuesday morning, 5 April 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 103) says:

2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-3A Basis 
1 Persons you should not have intercourse with  
Among the four bases of sexual misconduct , the first is those you should not have 
intercourse with: your mother etc.137, women under the care of their mothers 
etc.138, all men139, eunuchs, and ordained women.  
                                              
137 i.e., your mother or other close relatives 
138 In the corresponding passage of his Great Treatise of the Stages of the Path , Je Tsongkhapa also mentions 
women under the care of their families in general, as well as the wives of other men.

point out that to women, the opposites apply.

342  
There are four bases in relation to sexual  misconduct: an inappropriate partner, an 
inappropriate bodily part, an inappropriate place, and an inappropriate time. 
 The first, an inappropriate partner, is, for ex ample, for a man to have intercourse with his 
mother, sister, or daughter, with women who ar e still under the care of their mothers, and 
with men, eunuchs, and ordained women. Fo r women, an inappropriate partner would be 
her father, brother, or son, monks, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Inappropriate body parts 
Inappropriate body parts are those other th an the entrance to the womb, such as 
the mouth.  
 
An inappropriate body part is anything other than the vagina, such as the mouth, anus, and 
so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 Inappropriate places 
Inappropriate places are the vicinity of gu rus, places with stupas, and the like.  
 
An inappropriate place is near one’s guru, near st upas, in temples, in front of statues, and so 
forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 Inappropriate times 
Inappropriate times are pregnancy, ab iding in the one-day vows, etc.  
 
An inappropriate time is to have intercourse with a pregnant woman even if she is one’s wife, 
and when one has taken the one-day lay vows or the eight mahayana precepts.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-3B  Attitude 
1 Three positions of scholars on discrimination 
Among the three [aspects] of attitude , with respect to discrimination: 
1. the first [position] is that the Compendium states  that the discrimination of 
that as that must be unmistaken, 
2. the second [position] is that the Vinaya states that with regard to the defeat of 
impure conduct, it is the same whether the discrimination is mistaken or non-
mistaken, 
3. the third [position] is that the Commentary on the Treasury explains that if 
you approach someone else’s wife140 discriminating her to be yours, this will not 
                                              
140 chung ma  – wife, mate, consort, partner 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
343 constitute the path of action [under disc ussion], and that there are two ways in 
which getting involved with someone’s wi fe through discriminating her to be 
the wife of someone else may or m ay not [constitute such a path].  
 
The attitude or thought consists, as usual, of : the discrimination, affliction, and motivation. 
The Compendium  s a y s  t h a t  i n  t h e  c a s e  o f  s e x u a l  m i s c o n d u c t  i t  i s  n o t  n e c e s s a r y  t h a t  t h e  
discrimination be unmistaken with respect to one’s partner, the body part, and so forth. 
However, the Vinaya says that in the case of impure conduct a correct discrimination is not 
necessary, whereby if a monk has sexual inte rcourse with someone he breaks a root vow 
regardless of whether or not he has correctly identified the object. The Commentary on the  
Treasury of Manifest Dharma  s a y s  t h a t  h a v i n g  s e x u a l  i n t e r c ourse with a friend’s wife, for 
example, while discriminating her to be someon e else’s wife, may or may not constitute the 
path of action of sexual misconduct.
