{104} The mental affliction can be any of the three poisons.  
 
The affliction can be attachment, hatred, or ignorance.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 Motivation 
The motivation is the wish for interc ourse by means of impure conduct.  
 
The motivation is the wish to have sexual intercourse.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-3C Performance 
The performance consists in ende avoring for the sake of that. 
 
The performance is to strive or make efforts to engage in sexual intercourse, for example, by 
embracing someone and so forth, but to not ye t actually engage in sexual intercourse.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-3D Completion 
The completion consists in the coming  together of the two by the two.  
 
The completion is the union of the two organs, for example, the union of the male organ and 
an anus, the union of the male or gan and a mouth, and so forth.  
 There is much more detail in the Vinaya Su tra about sexual misconduct. For example, it 
says that even if a monk does not engage in un ion with one of the organs (a vagina, anus, or 
mouth), he commits a remainder just by ejaculating.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
344 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-4 Lying 
A Basis 
B Attitude 
C Performance 
D Completion 
 
The action of lying also consists  in the four branches: basis, a ttitude or thought, performance 
or execution, and the completion or conclusion.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-4A Basis 
1 Basis of expression 
Among the two bases of lying, the basis of expression is the fo ur – that which one 
has seen, heard, distinguished, or become fully aware of – as well as their four 
opposites.  
 
In the case of lying, the basis is that having se en something, to say that one has not seen it or 
has seen something else; having heard somethin g, to say that one has not heard it or has 
heard something else; having distinguished someth ing, to say that one has not distinguished it 
or has distinguished something else; and having fully understood something, to say that one 
has not fully understood it or has fully underst ood something else. For example, it would be a 
lie if someone were to ask us whether we saw a ma n and, in spite of having seen him, we were 
to say “I saw a dog.” In general, if we say that  we did not see what we have seen, did not hear 
what we have heard, and so forth, it is a lie. However, in the case of a hunter who is chasing a boar, if having seen the boar we were to say to him “I saw a man,” it would only be a little lie. 
 In short, to lie is also to say “I saw” when we did not see; to say “I heard” when we have 
not heard; to say “I distinguished” when we ha ve not distinguished; and to say “I understand” 
when we have not understood. These are the four possible bases of expression.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Object that  comprehends 
The object that comprehends is the othe r person who understands the meaning.  
 
In order for it to be a lie, it is necessary for the other person to understand the meaning. If he 
does not, for example, if someone tells a lie in  Tibetan to an Italian who does not understand 
Tibetan, it does not become a lie.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-4B Attitude 
Among the three [aspects associated with ] the attitude, the discrimination consists 
in making something that one has seen into something that one has not been 
seen, etc. The mental affliction is any of the three poisons. The motivation consists 
in the wish to say something thro ugh changing the discrimination. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
345  
The discrimination consists in changing the disc rimination of the seen to the not seen and so 
forth. The affliction is any of the three poison s. The motivation is th e wish to change the 
discrimination of the other person regarding what is true even if this is not done directly with 
words.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-4C Performance 
1 The actual performance 
2 Clearing up doubts 
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-4C1 The actual performance 
The performance consists in indicating some thing by saying it, by making sure not 
to say anything, or through body language. 
 
The performance can be done by saying someth ing, by not saying something, or by one’s 
expression or a gesture of the body such as a no d or shake of the head, a raising of the hands, 
and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-4C2 Clearing up doubts 
A Clearing up the doubt that it depends on the purpose 
[B Three approaches concerning the distinction between the path of action being complete or not if you 
enjoin someone else to lie]  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-4C2A Clearing up the doubt that it depends on the purpose 
Moreover, [in the scriptures] it says that it  is the same whether you say it for your 
own or someone else’s purposes. 
 
There is no difference whether we li e for our own or others’ purpose.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-4C2B Three approaches concerning the distinction between the actual path of action 
being incomplete if you enjoin someone else to do it 
1 The approach of the Treatise on the Grounds  
Here it is stated that with respect to ly ing, divisive speech, and harsh words, even 
through you enjoin [someone else to do them], these three will come about.  
 
According to the Vinaya we must do actions of  speech ourselves in order for them to be 
complete, whereas they are not complete if we order others to do them. However, according 
to the Treatise on the Grounds by Asanga one achieves a complete  action even when one orders 
or makes others engage in lying, divisive speech, and harsh words.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
346 2 Vasubandhu’s approach 
In the Treasury , the root text and its commentary it is explained that all four 
associated with speech become paths of ac tion through enjoining [someone else to 
perform them].  
 
According to Vasubandhu’s Treasury of Manifest Dharma  and its commentary all four actions 
of speech – lying, divisive speech, harsh words,  and idle talk – become complete paths of 
action when one orders others to do them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The approach of the Vinaya is to join them to the downfalls  
In the Vinaya it is stated that in or der to produce a complete downfall, you 
yourself must speak.  
 
T h e  V i n a y a  s a y s  t h a t  i n  o r d e r  t o  i n c u r  a  c o m p l e t e  d o w n f a l l  o f  l y i n g  a n d  s o  f o r t h ,  i t  i s  
necessary to express the words oneself. Theref ore, this does not happen when one orders 
others to lie and so forth. Thus, there are different assertions in different texts.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-4D Completion 
1 The actual completion 
The completion is that it is understood by the other person. 
 
The completion is that the other pers on understands what one has said.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Clearing up doubts 
The Commentary on the Treasury  explains that, if it is not understood, it becomes 
merely idle talk. This is also the same  for divisive speech and harsh words.  
 
The Commentary on the  Treasury  says that if one lies to some one but the other person does not 
understand what one has said, it becomes merely idle talk. It is the same for divisive speech 
and harsh words. Divisive speech is, for example, to say to one person “He said this,” and to 
another person “She said that,” motivated by the wish to create di sharmony between these 
two people. However, if we speak in order to divide people but the other person does not 
understand the meaning of what we  said, then it does not become an actual path of action of 
divisive speech. The same is true of harsh words.  Similarly, if we engage in divisive speech or 
harsh words with respect to a big rock or in an empty room, it does not become an actual 
path of action of divisive speech or harsh words.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-5 Divisive speech 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
347 A Basis 
The basis of divisive speech are sentient beings who are in agreement and those 
who are in disagreement with each other.  
 
The basis of divisive speech can be people who are in harmony or agreement, such a wife and 
husband. With respect to them, one says to the husband “Your wi fe did this” and then to the 
wife “Your husband did this,” in order to crea te disharmony between them. In this case one 
creates disharmony where initially there was ha rmony. However, the ba sis of divisive speech 
can also be those who are already in disharmony if one seeks to create further disharmony by 
saying to them “He or she did this and that.”   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Attitude 
Among the three [aspects associated with ] the attitude, the discrimination and 
affliction are as above. {105} The motivation  consists in the wish to divide sentient 
beings in agreement and the wish that th ose in disagreement not to be reconciled. 
 
The discrimination is as above. The affliction can be either a ttachment, hatred, or ignorance. 
The motivation is the wish to divide those in  harmony or agreement or the wish that those 
already in disharmony not be reconciled. For ex ample, one can strive to create a division 
between a husband and wife who are in harmony or to create a further division between a 
husband and wife who are already in disharmony. Likewise, one can do the same between a mother and her daughter or a father and his son,  saying, for example, “You have this problem 
because your mother did not take care of you. She did not love you enough,” and so forth, or 
“You have this problem because your father  did not treat you well” and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Performance 
The performance consists in expressing either pleasant or unpleasant subject 
matter with either true or untrue word s and to say them for either one’s own 
others’ purpose.  
 
The performance is to speak either pleasantly or  unpleasantly, and either  truly or falsely, for 
one’s own or others’ purpose. For example, if  a man who has fallen in love with someone 
else’s wife, in order to obtain her, says to he r husband “Your wife no longer believes in you. 
She wants to leave you,” and so forth, he enga ges in divisive speech for his own purpose – to 
create a separation between husband and wife in order to obtain the wife for himself. Likewise, it is possible to create a division for others’ purpose.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D Completion 
The completion consists in the divisi ve words that were expressed being 
understood.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
348 The completion is that the other people unders tand the meaning of the divisive speech. Thus, 
in order to complete the action it is not nece ssary that an actual division occurs. When all 
four branches are complete, one achieves a comp lete path of action of divisive speech.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-6 Harsh words 
A Basis 
The basis of harsh words is a sentient bein g who has become the basis for the arisal 
of a hostile attitude.  
 
Harsh words come from malice, the wish to ha rm someone. In other words, out of malice, 
one says something in order to hurt someone else. The basis of harsh words is a sentient 
being, specifically a human bein g who can understand one’s words that one does not like. On 
the other hand, an insect, for example, would not understand our words.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Attitude 
 [Among the three aspects associated wi th] the attitude, the discrimination and 
affliction are as above. The motivation is  the wish to speak with harsh speech.  
 
The discrimination can be either mistaken or  unmistaken. The affliction can be either 
attachment, hatred, or ignorance.  The motivation is the wish to speak harsh words that can 
hurt others’ minds.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Performance  
The performance consists in speaking unplea santly by means of either the truth or 
untruth about [the person’s] faults of extraction, body, ethical discipline, or 
behavior.  
 
The performance is to speak unpleasantly eith er truthfully or not about the faults of 
someone’s lineage or extraction, body, morality , or behavior. For example, one can say that 
someone’s lineage is bad, that his or her body is  ugly, that his or her morality is not good, or 
that his or her behavior is not correct. For ex ample, harsh words can be  to say something to 
someone who comes from the lower caste of cleane rs, washers, and so forth that hurts his or 
her mind.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D Completion 
The completion consists in the one to  whom it was said understanding the 
meaning.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
349 The completion is that the other person unders tands the meaning of on e’s words. If all four 
branches are complete, one achieves a complete path of action of ha rsh words. Instead of 
engaging in harsh words, we shou ld always speak nicely to others. If we do this, saying “You 
are very nice. Your body is very beautiful,” an d so forth, their minds will be happy. On the 
other hand, if we say to someone “You are very bad. You are very ugly. You are very hard and 
unkind,” they will feel pain in their mind and be disturbed.   
STUDENT : Is writing harsh words also included in harsh words of speech?  
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : I think that harsh words refers to actual speech, not to writing. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-7 Idle talk 
A Basis 
The basis of idle talk is a meaningless topic.  
 
Idle talk is to blah-blah-blah about nothing in particular. However, if motivated by attachment 
to wealth or fame, we recite the Sutra of Golden Light  or a sadhana of Yamantaka, 
Guhyasamaja, Chakrasamvara, Vajrayogini, and so  forth, it too becomes idle talk. This is 
because the Treasury of Manifest Dharma  says that if we recite a Dharma text motivated by 
attachment to worldly things it becomes idle talk.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Attitude 
Among the three [aspects associated with] the attitude, although with regard to 
discrimination, nothing has been stated othe r than “It is the discrimination of that 
as that,” here it is to talk having disc riminated the very topic that one wishes to 
express as that, because for this there is no need for someone’s understanding. The 
mental affliction is any of the poisons.  The motivation is the wish to talk 
disconnectedly and randomly.  
 
The discrimination is to discrimi nate that which is meaningless as  meaningless. In the case of 
idle talk, there is no need fo r someone to understand one, it  is enough to just talk. The 
affliction can be either attachment, hatred, or ignorance. The motivation  is the wish to talk 
unrelatedly and randomly.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Performance 
The performance consists in sett ing about uttering idle talk. 
 
The performance is to speak blah-blah-blah. Fo r example, we can sigh to ourselves, “What 
should I do? I am so sad…” and so forth,  without actually talking to someone.  
 
Tuesday afternoon, 5 April 2005  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
350 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D Completion 
The completion is to finish uttering the idle talk.  
 
The completion is to finish saying that which is meaningless. There is no need for someone 
else to have listened or underst ood, it is enough for oneself to ta lk nonsense. This is similar to 
someone talking in their sleep! 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-8 Covetousness 
A Basis 
The basis of covetousness is a possession or provision of someone else.  
 
The basis of covetousness is something – wealth or property – that belongs to someone else. 
With respect to that which belongs to someone else, one thinks “I too want to have such 
clothes” and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Attitude 
Among the three [aspects associated with] the attitude, the discrimination consists 
in the discrimination of that basis as that. The affliction is any of the three 
poisons. The motivation is the wish to make it one’s own.  
 
The attitude or thought consists of the three: 1. discrimination – the discrimination of th e basis as that basis, for example, the 
discrimination of a watch owned by someone else as that very watch owned by someone 
else,  
2. affliction – either attachment, hatred, and ignorance, but mainly the attachment that, 
thinking that something is nice, wants to obtain it; and 
3. motivation – the wish to make something one’s  own, for example, to make something my 
wife, my husband, my child, my wa tch, my country, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Performance 
The performance consists in making an e ffort for the object of that thought.  
 
The performance is to make a mental  effort to obtain the object.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D Completion 
The completion is the thought: “May that possession etc. become mine!” 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
351 The completion is the thought “May this thing that belongs to someone else become mine,” 
without actually doing anything to obtain it. One just thinks, “How nice it would be if it were 
mine!”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-9 Harmful intent 
A/B Basis and attitude 
The basis, discrimination, and affliction of  harmful intent are similar to those of 
harsh words. The motivation is the wish to  hit etc., {106} thinking “How nice it 
would be if [this person] were to killed or  bound or if his resources were to decline 
by themselves or through someone else” and the like.  
 
The basis of harmful intent or malice is in ge neral another sentient being. However, in some 
cases it can be a thing, for example, sometimes we get angry and beat our car or furniture, 
throw things on the floor, and so forth. Howe v e r ,  t h e  m a i n  o b j e c t  o f  h a r m f u l  i n t e n t  i s  
another sentient being. The discrimination is to  correctly identify the object of the harmful 
intent, for example, to correctly identify Tashi as Tashi. The affliction is mainly hatred or 
anger, although it can in general be any of th e three mental poisons – attachment, hatred, or 
ignorance. The motivation is the wish to beat someone, the wish  to kill someone, the wish to 
tie someone up, and so forth. It can also be the wish that others’ wealth or resources be taken 
away by others or that they degenerate naturall y so that the person becomes poor. One thinks, 
“How nice it would be if this were to happen.” Harmful intent is a mental action, therefore it 
does not need to be accompanied by a physical action.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Performance 
The performance consists in applyi ng oneself to this thought.  
 
The performance is to think about how to ha rm others. Due to such thoughts, we later 
actually engage in harming others.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D Completion 
The completion consists in the decision to hit and the like.  
 
The completion is the mental decision to beat someone, kill someone, bind someone, and so 
forth. In China at the time of Mao Tse Tung there were many problems and it was difficult 
for people to find food for their children. Du e to this, sometimes people would take their 
children for a dangerous hike in the mountain s in search of food, whereupon one of them 
would fall from a cliff and die. This was seen to be preferable to allowing their children to die 
of starvation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
352 2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-10 Wrong views 
A Basis 
B Attitude 
C Performance 
D Completion 
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-10A Basis 
The basis of wrong views is an object that exists.  
 
The basis of wrong views is some thing that exists, for example, the law of actions and results, 
that one then holds to not exist. Likewise, whil e past and future lives exist, one holds that 
they do not exist. Or one holds that the Three Jewe ls do not exist. In short, a wrong view is to 
think that something that exists does not exist.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-10B Attitude 
Among the three [aspects associated with] the attitude, the discrimination consists 
in discriminating the object that is denied as true.  
 
The discrimination is the discrimination of an ob ject that is denied is true. In other words, 
one thinks that it is true that some thing that exists does not exist.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
The affliction is any of the three poisons.  
 
The affliction can be either attachment, hatred , and ignorance. Attach ment is the wish to 
have something, hatred is the wi sh to harm, ignorance is of di fferent types, for example, the 
ignorance of the nature of phenomena, the lack of true existenc e, due to which one 
apprehends true existence; the ignorance of actions and result s, the ignorance of past and 
future lives; the ignorance of th e Three Jewels, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
The motivation is the wish to deny [the object].  
 
The motivation is the wish to deny th e existence of something that exists.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-10C Performance 
1 The actual performance 
[2 Divisions] 
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-10C1 The actual performance 
The performance consists in setting abou t applying oneself to such a thought.  
 
The performance is to apply on eself to such a thought.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
353  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-10C2 Divisions 
In this regard there are four [divisions]:  
A Denying causes 
Denying causes consists in [the view]: “Excellent conduct and faulty conduct do 
not exist.” 
 
This wrong view denies the existence of causes by thinking that excellent and faulty conduct, 
which are causes, do not exist. In other words,  one thinks that virtuous and non-virtuous 
actions do not exist.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Denying effects 
Denying effects consists in [the view]: “F ully ripened effects of those two do not 
exist.”  
 
This wrong view denies the existence of results by  thinking that there is no result of engaging 
in excellent conduct and faulty conduct.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Denying activities 
Denying activities has three points:  
1. Denying the activity of planting and reta ining the seed consists in [the view]: 
“There is no planting of a seed by the father and no retaining of a seed by the 
mother.141” 
2. Denying the activity of coming and going consists in [the view]: “There is no 
coming from a former existence to this one and no going from this to a future 
one.”  
3. Denying the activity of being born cons ists in [the view]: “There is no 
intermediate state miraculously  born sentient beings.”  
 A child is produced through the mind enters th e mixture of the white seed from the father 
and the red seed from the mother. However, there are those who deny  that the seed is 
planted by the father and held by the mother.  To deny coming and going is to deny that we do not come from a past life to this life and 
do not go from this life to a future live, that is , to deny the existence of past and future lives.  
 T o  d e n y  t h e  a c t i v i t y  o f  b e i n g  b o r n  i s  t o  deny the existence of miraculously born 
intermediate state beings. Birth can be from heat  and moisture, from an egg, from a womb, or 
                                             
 
141 In this regard, Geshe Thubten Soepa explains that all these aspects of denying existent phenomena are 
associated with the problem of nihilism, the refusal to a ccept certain conventional tr uths. In this context each 
of them has its special significance. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
354 miraculous. The intermediate state being is said  to be miraculously born. If one does not 
accept the existence of past and future lives, one would not accept the intermediate state.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D Denying existing things 
Denying existent things consists in [the vi ew]: “Arhats and so forth do not exist.”  
 
To deny things that exist is to think that foe-destroyers and so forth do not exist. Foe-
destroyers are those who have destroyed the foe or enemy that  is the afflictions such as 
attachment and hatred. This view also consists in denying the existence of complete buddhas.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1A-10D Completion 
The completion is certainty with respect to one’s denial.  
 
The completion is to be certain with respect to  what one denies, that is, one is certain that 
something that exists does no t exist. There are many such wrong views, however they are 
dangerous in that they cut our roots of virtue. While most of us do not have wrong views, we 
do engage in the other non-virtuous actions.  
 
(Oral transmission 7:05-7:30 PM) 
 Wednesday morning, 6 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 106) says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A1B Distinction between actions and paths of action  
Among them, the intention in the mind is an action but not a path of action. The 
seven actions of body and speech are actions as well as paths of action since they 
are also the basis of operation for the in tention. The three, covetousness and so 
on, are paths of actions but not actions142.  
 
Among the ten, the seven actions of body and speech – killing, steali ng, sexual misconduct, 
lying, divisive speech, harsh words, and idle talk – are both actions and paths of actions 
because they are the basis of operation for the intention. The three of mind – covetousness, 
harmful intent, and wrong view – are paths of action but not actions. Thus those done by 
body and speech are actions whereas those done by the mind are not. Vows, for example, are 
made in relation to actions of body and speec h but are not made in relation to the mind 
because they are unable to block the mind. Acti ons of body and speech are considered to be 
                                              
142 Despite the precise distinction drawn here, the general term “ten non-virtuous  actions” has become 
common in English. Tibetan texts also frequently refe r to “actions” in less specific contexts, although, 
according to the definition proposed here , the last three of the “ten non-virtues ” do not constitute actions. 
Thus “action” should often be understood more loosely as a term that only requires specification in certain 
contexts. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
355 form due to which, just as a dam can stop the flow of a river, in a similar way vows can stop 
non-virtuous actions of body and speech. Different types of form are set forth in the Treasury 
of Manifest Dharma  including revelatory form and non-reve latory form. Non-revelatory form is 
a form that is not matter which is composed of  eight particles of eart h, water, fire, wind, 
form, odor, taste, and tangibility.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A2 Differences in weight 
A The weight of the ten paths of action 
B Incidentally, a summarized indication of the gates of strong karma  
The second one – differences in weight – has two points: the weight of the ten paths of 
action and incidentally, {107} a summary indi cation of the gates of strong karma.  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A2A The weight of the ten paths of action  
First: In Actual Basis of the Levels  six ways of being weighty are stated.  
 
The ten paths of actions can be of  different weights or gravity, that is, heavier or lighter. The 
Actual Basis of the Level  sets out six ways in an action becomes heavy.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
1 Intensive action 
The strongly compounding are actions mo tivated by the strong presence or 
absence of the three poisons.  
 
Non-virtuous actions motivated by strong attachment, hatred, and ignorance are heavy, and 
likewise virtuous actions motivated by non- attachment, non-hatred, and non-ignorance are 
heavy. Virtuous actions motivated by strong compassion or the mind of enlightenment are 
also heavy.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Habituation 
Habituation is to have resorted to virt uous and non-virtuous actions for a long 
time and to have familiarized with them many times.  
 
Virtuous and non-virtuous actions become heavy due to having familiarized with them for a 
long time and done them frequently. In general due to having familiarized with non-virtuous 
actions in our past lives and still continuing to engage in them in this life, they are heavy. 
However, if we familiarized with meditation on  love, compassion, the mind of enlightenment, 
emptiness, a deity, and so forth in  the past and continue to medita te on them in this life, they 
too become very powerful.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
356 3 Entity [of the action] 
As for their entity, each of the former of the seven of body and speech is weightier 
than the later, whereas for th e three actions of mind the later is weightier than the 
former. 
 
Among the seven actions of body and speech the former are heavier than the later. Hence, 
among killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct, killing is the heaviest, especially killing a 
human being; stealing, taking others’ belongin gs without permission, is the less heavy; and 
sexual misconduct is the least heaviest. “Heavy” means that it brings a great result. Among the 
actions of speech, the heaviest is lying, follow ed by divisive speech, followed by harsh words, 
followed by idle talk. The heaviest of lies is to say that one has realized emptiness although 
one has not, that one has realized impermanence  although one has not, that one has attained 
calm abiding although one has not, and that one has attained the clairvoyance that sees 
everything or knows others’ minds although one ha d not. Divisive speech is to speak in such a 
way as to create a division between people. Because harsh words do not happen very often 
and do not last long, divisive speech is heavier th an them. Idle talk is by nature not very heavy 
but, because we often engage in it, it becomes heavy.  Among the paths of action of mind, wrong view  is the heaviest, follow ed by harmful intent, 
followed by covetousness. Wrong view is the heav iest because it cuts the roots of virtue, that 
is, it makes them unable to bring a result. Beca use harmful intent or malice is the wish to 
harm others and thoughts about how to do this , whereby it leads to non-virtuous actions of 
body and speech, it is the next heaviest. Cove tousness is the mind that wants something and 
is therefore the lightest of th e paths of action of mind.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 Basis 
The basis is benefit and harm to, for instance, the Buddha, the Dharma, the 
Sangha, and the gurus. 
 
Due to their being special objects, if one harms or benefits the Buddha, Dharma, Sangha, the 
gurus, and so forth, the action becomes heavy. In this way, the action becomes heavy due to 
the basis being heavy. Thus, even  though we do not harm an actual buddha, if we destroy a 
statue of a buddha it becomes a heavy action. Also if we destroy texts or criticize the teachings, 
the action becomes heavy. Likewise if we harm the sangha, it becomes a heavy action. On the other contrary, if we think of images of buddha s as actual buddhas and generate faith in and 
respect for them, it becomes a heavy virtuous action.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 One-sided fixation on the discordant side  
A one-sided fixation on the discordant side  consists in spending one’s whole life 
adopting non-virtuous actions with one- sided determination and not even doing 
one single virtue.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
357 I f  w e  c r e a t e  o n l y  n o n - v i r t u o u s  a c t i o n s  i n  o u r  d a i l y  l i f e ,  f o r  e x a m p l e ,  b y  t a k i n g  a  n o n - v o w  
through making the promise “I will be a butche r and kill animals for my entire life,” it 
becomes a heavy non-virtuous action. Thus, if we constantly engage in non-virtue due to 
taking such a non-vow and do not create any virt ue, we will create extremely heavy non-virtue. 
Similarly, if one swears to be a soldier, which implies killing other people, one creates very 
heavy non-virtue.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
6 Elimination of the discordant side 
To eliminate the discordant side is to give up the side of non-virtue, to become 
free from attachment, and to trai n in virtuous actions. In the Letter to a Friend  it 
also says: 
 
Persistence, strong adherence, no antidote,  A basis with foremost qualities – actions that spring from [Them] are the five great types of virtue and non-virtue. 
From them, endeavor in virtuous behavior! 
 
Moreover, there are five because of the division [of the basis ] into two: (1) a basis 
endowed with good qualities, such as the Three Jewels, and (2) a basis of benefit, 
such as one’s parents. 
 If eliminate the discordant side that is the si de of non-virtue, separate from attachment, and 
train in virtuous actions, our actions will become heavy virtue. Nagarjuna’s Letter to a Friend  
says that an action becomes heavy by doing it constantly, by doing it with strong adherence, 
by not applying its antidote, and by doing it wi th respect to a basis that possesses qualities. 
This basis is divided into a basis endowed with  good qualities, this being the Three Jewels, 
and a basis of benefit, this being one’s parents. In this way, there are five factors that make 
actions, whether virtuous and non-virtuous, heav y. Thus, we should no t act against the Three 
Jewels and our parents but instead generate re spect for them; for example, when our mother 
asks us to do something we should not angrily refuse but should try to do as she asked.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A2B Incidentally: a summarized indication of the gates of strong karma 
1 Great strength by way of the field 
2 Great strength by way of the support 
3 Great strength by way of the thing 4 Great strength by way of the attitude 
The second one has four points: great strength by way of the field, great strength 
by way of the support, great strength by w ay of the thing, and gr eat strength by way 
of the attitude 
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A2B-1 Great strength by way of the field 
A The field of the Three Jewels  
First: the Three Jewels, gurus, those who ar e like a guru, parents, and so forth [are 
gates of strong karma] because even if the thoughts toward them are not intense 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
358 and the benefit or harm done to them is  small, the merit {108} and misdeed are 
great. In this regard, it says in Establishment of Mindfulness  that the karma of stealing 
even a little thing from the Three Jewels is  purified – in the case of the Buddha 
and the Dharma – if it is returned. 
 
With respect to the Three Jewels – the Buddh a, Dharma, and Sangha, gurus, those who are 
similar to a guru, one’s father and mother, and so forth, even if one’s motivation is not 
strong, when one performs a slight benefit one creates great merit and when one does slight 
harm one creates a great misdeed.  
 The Sutra of the Close Placements of Mindfulness  says that if we take a small object belonging 
to the Three Jewels without permission but late r on return it or give something similar to 
them, we can purify the non-virtue created wi th respect to the Buddha and Dharma. In other 
words, if, having stolen something from the Thre e Jewels, later on we ma ke an offering to an 
image of a buddha or to a Dharma text, we can purify the negative action. However, this is 
not the case with the Sangha.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The field of the Sangha 
However, in the case of the sangha, it does not get purified until feeling is 
experienced. Furthermore it is stated that  if it was a means of providing for the 
mouths of the sangha, one will be  born in the great hell and,  if it is other, one will 
born in the great darkness surrounding Without Respite.  
 
If one takes something belonging to the Sangha, one will necessarily experience the feeling of 
suffering. In other words, if one takes the po ssessions or belongings of the Sangha without 
permission, the action will be very heavy and ca nnot be purified such that no result will be 
experienced. In other words, one will necessarily  have to experience some result. If without 
asking one takes food belonging to the Sangha, ve getables, rice, bread, fruit, and so forth, it 
becomes a very heavy negative karma that acts as  a cause for taking rebirth in the great hell, 
the hell of Unrelenting Torment. If one takes ot her things that are not food from the Sangha, 
one will be reborn in a dark place surrounding  the hell of Unrelenting Torment. Because of 
this, we need to be careful in our daily life. Since the community of a Dharma center is 
similar to the Sangha, we need to be carefu l not to take the property of the community 
without asking permission, as this too would beco me a heavy non-virtue. Stealing in general is 
non-virtuous, but when it is done with resp ect to the Sangha it becomes even more non-
virtuous.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C The field of bodhisattvas 
Bodhisattvas are an extremely powerful field of virtue and non-virtue. In the Sutra 
of the Seal of Engageme nt in the Generation of the Power of Confidence  it says that 
compared to someone who, out of anger, puts all the sentient beings of the ten 
directions into a dark prison, if someone else, out of anger, turns his back on a 
bodhisattva and says “I will not look upon this horrible person [again],” he 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
359 generates incalculably greater negativity. Also compared to someone who has 
destroyed and burnt down stupas as numero us as the sand [grains] of the Ganges 
River, if someone else generates anger and harmful intent towards a bodhisattva 
a n d  s a y s  u n p l e a s a n t  t h i n g s  t o  h i m ,  i t  i s  l i k e  b e f o r e .  I n  t h e  Sutra of the Seal of 
Entering Certainty and Uncertainty  it says that compared to someone who with a 
mind of love makes eyes develop again in the sentient beings of  the ten directions 
whose eyes were plucked out, and who rele ases the previous sentient beings from 
prison and establishes them in th e happiness of a universal monarch143 or Brahma, 
if someone else with clear faith looks at and with clear faith wishes to look at a 
bodhisattva who aspires to the Mahayana and proclaims his praise, he generates 
incalculably greater merit. Also in the Sutra of the Magic of th e Complete Certainty of 
Total Peace  it says that compared to killing all the sentient beings of Jambudvipa or 
seizing all their possessions, someone who obstructs even as little as the virtue of a 
bodhisattva of giving a handful of food to  an animal, generates incalculably greater 
negativity. {109} Therefore, you should be ve ry careful with respect to these objects.  
 Bodhisattvas are objects or fields of virtue an d non-virtue. Therefore, if one generates very 
strong faith in a bodhisattva, it becomes very vi rtuous whereas if we generate even slight anger 
toward a bodhisattva it destroys a great amount of virtue. For example, if we were to put all 
the sentient beings of the ten directions in pr ison we would create heavy non-virtue, but the 
non-virtue created by generating anger even fo r a short moment toward a bodhisattva is much 
heavier. Likewise, generating anger at a bodhisattva and speaking unpleasantly to him is heavier than destroying stupas equal in number to the grains of sand of the Ganges River. 
Likewise, if we destroyed and burnt many temple s this would be very bad, but the negative 
karma of generating anger toward a bodhisattva is much heavier.  
 If all the eyes of the sentient beings of the ten directions had been plucked out and motivated by the mind of love we were to rest ore them, this would be very virtuous. Likewise, 
if we released all the sentient beings of the ten directions who had been put in prison and 
established them in the happiness of a wheel-turni ng king or Brahma, this too would be very 
virtuous. However, we would create much more  merit by generating clear faith toward a 
bodhisattva, wishing to look at him,  and proclaiming praise to him.  
 Also if we were to kill all the sentient be ings of Jambudvipa, the southern continent, or 
steal all their wealth and belongings, this would be a very heavy non-virtuous karma. However, 
if we were to obstruct even one bodhisattva from giving a single handful of food to an animal, this would be much heavier. Therefore, we need to be very careful with respect to 
bodhisattvas as just by obstructing their ac tions we create very heavy negative karma. 
However, we do not even know who is a bodhisattva and who is not.  
 There is a story about several monks in Ti bet that happened some years ago. One monk, 
now called Langdol Lama Rinpoche but previously called Crazy Langdol, went from Sera Monastery to Lhasa with some other monks. Al ong the way they stopped to rest and in the 
course of the conversation asked each othe r, “Among the four of us, which one is a 
bodhisattva?” One said that it definitely was not Crazy Langdol and that perhaps it was the 
                                             
 
143 ’khor sgyur  (’khor los sgyur ba’i rgyal po ) – lit. “wheel-turning king” – lege ndary universal monarchs ruling by 
means of a wheel made from go ld, silver, copper, or iron. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
360 monk who was richer and had ni cer robes called Chodzin (Dha rma-Holder). Another of the 
monks agreed with him that it was not Langdo l. While Langdol did not say anything, he was 
in fact the bodhisattva. It is recounted that some time after he had entered the Lower Tantric 
College, a family asked for a monk to be sent to their home to do the Offering in Three Parts . 
The disciplinarian sent Langdol to do this. Howe ver, instead of performing that puja, he just 
made the torma, recited the Heart Sutra , and told someone to thro w the torma outside. One 
monk told the disciplinarian about what ha d happened, due to which he expelled Langdol 
from the monastery. Langdol then went to Sera Monastery to study. Later on the same family 
asked the Lower Tantric College to send the same  monk back to them to once again perform 
a puja, saying that after he had done the prev ious puja all their problems, their daughter’s 
sickness and so forth, had been resolved. However, they were told that in the meantime he 
had been sent out of the monastery. He was then found to be staying in Lowa Kangtsen in a 
very dark room. One time when he was foun d to have some flowers in his room, the 
disciplinarian told him that he could not keep  them and threw them onto the land of the 
kangtsen next door. This was later said to be an auspicious sign that there would be learned 
monks from Gelu Kangtsen, and in fact some  became the Ganden throne-holder. Another 
time when Langdol Lama defecated in the debate  courtyard of Sera Me Monastery, the monk 
who found it threw the excrement at the wall of the neighboring kangtsen. From it there grew 
a very fragrant tree similar to sandalwood with beautiful flowers. Later on another lama said 
that if the excrement had been left in the deba te courtyard, the monks of Sera Me would have 
been very learned. This story shows that we do not know who is a bodhisattva and therefore 
need to be very careful in our actions!  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A2B-2 Great strength by way of the support 
A The difference between the skillful and the unskillful  
Second: Even a small lump of iron sinks to the bottom, but a container made from 
it floats on the surface. Similarly it has been set out that the negativities committed 
by the unskillful and the skillful are heavy an d light. In fact, it is said that for the 
skillful who regret previous negativities, restrain themselves in the future, do not 
keep them secret, and apply their virtuous  antidotes, they are light, whereas for 
those who arrogantly assume they are sk illful and do not do this but out of 
contempt deliberately engage in them, they are heavy.  
 
Actions become heavy or light due to the skill of the person who committed them. For 
example, if even a small lump of iron is thrown  into the ocean it sink s to the bottom, whereas 
a pot made out of iron floats on  its surface. Similarly, if some one who is skillful creates non-
virtue it becomes light, whereas if someone who is  stupid creates non-virtue , even if initially it 
is not heavy, it becomes heavy because he or sh e does not apply its antidote. Thus, a skillful 
person who creates non-virtue is one who immedi ately regrets it by thinking that it was not 
right, determines to not to do it again in the fu ture, does not keep it secret but reveals it to 
others, and purifies it by relying on its antidote . In this way the negative  karma created by this 
person becomes light. On the other hand, if someone does not do this and instead thinks 
that it does not matter because he is skillful or  learned, the action becomes very heavy. Thus, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
361 for someone who knows that it is a negative acti on but, thinking proudly that he is learned, 
deliberately engages in it, the action becomes very heavy.  
 
In short, whenever we create a non-virtuous  action, we should generate regret for it, 
determine not to do it again, go for refuge and generate the mind of enlightenment, and 
recite a mantra such as the hundred-syllable mantra of Vajrasattva, the Sutra of the Confession of 
Downfalls of a Bodhisattva , the General Confession , and so forth. In this way we can immediately 
purify the negativities we create in our daily life. In fact, every evening we should examine 
what kind of actions we did duri ng the day and, if we find that  we did non-virtuous actions, 
we should develop regret for them, resolve to no t do them again, go for refuge and generate 
the mind of enlightenment, and meditate on em ptiness and so forth. This will make our life 
meaningful and ensure that in the next life we will have a good rebirth and be a qualified 
practitioner.  
 
Wednesday afternoon, 6 April 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 109) says:  
B The actual strength of the support 
In the Jewel Heap Sutra  it also says that if all the sentient beings of the billion 
universes, having entered the Mahayana, were to possess the kingdom of universal 
monarchs and each of them were to offe r butterlamps in vessels the size of an 
ocean with wicks the size of Mount Meru to a stupa of a buddha, this would not 
match even the hundredth part of the me rit a bodhisattva who has left his home 
and who holds up a little bit of wick smeare d with butter in front of a stupa. This 
makes clear the strength of the support144 even though there is no difference in the 
attitude and the field and even if the thing [offered] is very different. This 
reasoning also makes clear that depend ing on whether or not the support has 
vows, and, if it does, whether one has one,  two, or three, th e cultivation of the 
path will be the more effective the more he  has. There is a great difference in the 
strength of the roots of virtue whether, fo r instance, a householder, at the time of 
giving donations etc., acts on the basis of  keeping vows, such as the one-day vows, 
or whether he acts without any vows.  
 The heaviness in this case does not depend on th e strength of the object or the strength of the 
field but depends on the support, that is, the body  of the being. If the sentient beings of the 
billion worlds were to enter the Mahayana pa th and become wheel-turning kings and offer 
butterlamps the size of an ocea n with wicks the size of Mount Meru to a stupa of a buddha, 
they would create a huge amount of merit. Howe ver, if an ordained bodhisattva were to offer 
even a tiny thing, such as a me re wick, in front of a stupa, th e merit would be much greater. 
Thus, there is a great difference in the merit created by one who has vows and one who does 
not, given that the merit create d by one with vows is much more . This is due to the body or 
support possessing vows. For example, having taken the one-day vows, whatever virtuous 
                                             
 
144 In this context “support” refers to the vows of ethical discipline.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
362 actions are done that day one creates much greate r merit than on those days that one does the 
same actions but does not have the vows. Even by  just offering a single butterlamp one creates 
much more merit when one has vows than when one does not. For this reason, it is also very 
good to take the Eight Mahayana Precepts, wh ich are also taken for twenty-four hours, as 
much as possible.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C The faults of discipline being adulterated 
In the Sutra of Annihilating Adulterations of Discipline  it says that {110} compared to a 
human being possessing the ten non-vi rtues who, for a hundred years, 
continuously and uninterruptedly accumula tes negativity, a fully-ordained monk 
who has [completely] broken his [vows of] discipline and who for one day dresses 
in the victory banner of sages145 and enjoys that which has been offered to him 
with faith, [accumulates] far more nega tivity. Again by way of the support, 
negativity has greater strength. In the Scripture on Discernment  it also says: 
 
It is better to eat lumps of iron 
Blazing with tongues of fire Than, unrestrained and undisciplined, 
To eat the alms-food of the area. 
 
This has been stated with regard to both  adulterated discipline and slackness in 
training. The Teacher’s statements that compared to negativities associated with the Dharma, the negativities of the ten non-virtues are small appear to be true.  
 
If someone possessing all ten no n-virtuous actions were to co ntinuously do them for one 
hundred years, he would create much negativi ty. However, a fully-ordained monk who has 
broken his vows by receiving a defeat due to killing a human being, stealing, engaging in 
sexual misconduct, or telling a great lie, yet co ntinues to wear the robes of a monk and enjoy 
the offerings made with faith to the sangha, crea tes far more negativity. Why is this? It is due 
to the support, in that negativities become grea ter due to the support or body. In other words, 
if a fully-ordained monk who has broken his vows still dresses in robes, gives teachings, and 
e n j o y s  t h e  o f f e r i n g s  m a d e  t o  h i m ,  h e  w i l l  c r eate great negativity. This was taught by the 
Buddha in the sutra that says that it is better to eat lumps of blazing iron, which only burn 
the stomach, rather than not safeguarding one’s  vows and still going for alms and eating the 
food that one is offered. This is heavier in th at, although the food do es not immediately burn 
one’s stomach, later on one will be reborn in hell where one’s body and the burning fire will 
be inseparable in nature. This will happen to both those who have broken their vows and 
those who are slack in guarding their vows. Th e Buddha said that negativities created in 
relation to the holy Dharma become very heavy, whereby co mpared to them the ten non-
virtues appear to be small and insignificant.  
 
                                             
 
145 Synonym for monastic robes. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
363 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A2B-3 Great strength by way of the thing 
Third: With regard to giving gifts to se ntient beings, giving the Dharma [is far 
more excellent], and with regard to making  offerings to the buddhas, offering one’s 
realizations is far more excellent than gi fts and offerings of material things. From 
that example, you should also understand the [differing strength of] other [things ].  
 
In terms of the giving of gifts of material thin gs to sentient beings and the giving of Dharma 
to sentient beings, much more merit is accu mulated through giving them the Dharma. In 
terms of making offerings to the buddhas, the offe ring of our attainments or realizations (the 
mind of enlightenment, love, compassion, renu nciation, emptiness, and so forth) is far 
superior in strength to offering them butterlam ps, biscuits, flowers, and so forth. This is 
because the object offered – one’s realizations – are far better than the offering of material 
things.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A2B-4 Great strength by way of the attitude  
A Greatness by way of the intensity of the attitude, its duration, etc.  
Fourth: In the Heap of Jewels Sutra  it says that compared to each one of the sentient 
beings of the billion universes creating a buddha stupa as massive as Mount Meru 
and all those who are objects of respect paying respect to them for ten million 
eons, a bodhisattva undivorced from th e mind of omniscience who throws one 
single flower [generates] more merit. That  being so, you should understand this in 
terms of the differences in attitude – whethe r the object to be attained is superior 
or inferior, whether you observe your own be nefit or that of others, and so forth – 
as well as the intensity and duration. When  it comes to faulty conduct, a strong 
afflicted attitude {111} and long du ration also have more strength.  
 
An action becomes heavier in dependence on the strength of the attitude  or thought, whether 
one has the attitude continually or not, whethe r one has it for a long time or a short time, 
and so forth. If each of the sentient beings of the billion universes were to build a stupa to the 
Buddha as large as Mount Meru and were to take  care of it, clean it, and so forth for ten 
million eons, they would create much merit. Ho wever, if with the mind of enlightenment not 
separated from the mind of omniscience, one were  to offer a single flower to the image of a 
buddha, one would create much more merit. In this case, the action becomes heavier by way 
of the attitude – the mind of enlightenment or the wish to attain enlightenment, which is a 
very powerful mind. In addition, the action becomes heavier in dependence on whether the objective is superior or inferior, whether one thin ks of only oneself or of all sentient beings, 
and so forth. Thus, if one does an action thinking only of oneself, it will be weaker than if one does the same action in order to benefit a ll sentient beings. The strength of the action 
also depends on the intensity of the mind of en lightenment, whether it is strong or weak, and 
for how long one cultivates it. For example, the merit created with the aspiring mind of 
enlightenment is less than that created with th e engaging mind of enlightenment. Also one 
creates more merit when one continually thinks  to attain enlightenment for the benefit of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
364 other sentient beings than when one only medi tates on the mind of enlightenment from time 
to time.  
 Non-virtuous actions are similar in that they  als o  beco m e heav ier  in  depen den ce o n  t h e  
intensity of the afflictions and the duration of  the action, that is, those done with strong 
afflictions and those done for a long time are heavier than those done with weak afflictions 
and only briefly or infrequently.  
 In short, when doing any action we should continually cultivate love, compassion, and the 
mind of enlightenment, as well as the unde rstanding of emptiness, impermanence, and so 
forth. We should not meditate very occasionally  and very briefly and then give it up! There 
are many people who meditate with great effo rt for a few days and then become tired and 
completely stop. This is a faul t of many human beings. Whatever  practice we do, we should 
try to do it continually and with a strong virtuous mind. We should avoid having an afflicted attitude, that is, having a primary mind that  is concomitant with attachment, hatred, or 
ignorance. A virtuous primary mind is one that is concomitant with virtuous mental factors, 
whereas a non-virtuous primary mind is one that  is concomitant with any one of the six root 
afflictions – attachment, anger, ignorance, pride, doubt, or wrong view. There are afflicted 
minds that are stronger than others; for exampl e, a mind that is concomitant with anger or 
hatred is very strong, whereas a mind that is concomitant with ignorance is less strong. 
However, the strength also depend s on the duration of the mind.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B How anger is particularly strong 
Also among [the mental afflictions], anger is particularly strong. In Engaging in 
[Bodhisattva ] Behavior  it says:  
 
Whatever good conduct was amassed 
In a thousand eons by giving and worship To the sugatas and the like, All is destroyed by one [flash of] anger. 
 In this regard, anger towards those whose conduct accords with purity weighs very 
heavily, and anger towards the conquero rs’ children more heavily still.  
 Among the three mental poisons, anger is the st rongest. Why is this? Because even an instant 
of anger can destroy the merit created by practi cing generosity and making offerings to the 
tathagatas, practicing morality, and meditating for a thousand eons. In other words, the merit 
that arises by way of generosity and morality ca n be destroyed by an in stant of anger toward a 
bodhisattva. That it is “destroyed” means that it becomes unable to produce its good result.  
 While becoming angry with our companions who are striving to become a buddha is heavy, 
it is even heavier to become angry at a bodhisattva because ju st a single instant of anger 
toward a bodhisattva destroys all the merit created by practi cing generosity and morality. 
Therefore, anger is very heavy and for this reason  we need to strive to avoid getting angry at 
bodhisattvas and at our Dharma friends with whom  we study, meditate, and so forth. If we do 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
365 get angry at them, we should ask their forgiv eness and determine not to get angry at them 
again. In this way, especially if the person accepts our apology, we can purify the anger.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In the King of Concentration  [Sutra ] it says:  
 
If one has harbored harmful intent for another, 
Discipline and hearing cannot save one, Nor can concentration or an isolated place, Nor can gifts or worship of the Buddha. 
 Someone who has harmful intent, anger being the nature of a mind of harm, toward another 
being cannot be protected by his morality, by his wisdom arisen from hearing, by 
concentration, by abiding or hiding in an isol ated place such as a hermitage, by generosity, 
and by making offerings to the buddhas. In ot her words, if one has mind of harm, one will 
not be protected by making offerings to a statue  of the Buddha and so forth as virtue will not 
arise in one’s mind. There are those who, when involved in an angry court case, ask that a 
puja be done in order to help them win it! Howeve r, it is unlikely that they will receive this 
help.  This concludes the discussion of karma from the po int of view of the cause. Next is the result.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2A3 Indication of their effects 
A The fully ripened effect  
B The effect in conformity with the cause  
C The conditioning or empowering effect  
The third one –  the effects of these [actions] – has three points: the fully ripened effect, 
the effect in conformity with the caus e, and the conditioning or empowering 
effect. 
A The fully ripened effect 
First: As each of the ten pa ths of action depends on th e basis, the three poisons, 
and whether it is great, intermediate, or small, the fully ripened effect is also 
threefold. In Actual Basis of the Levels  it says that through each of the ten, a great 
[act of] killing and so on, one is reborn as a hell being, through each of the ten 
intermediate ones one is reborn as a hu ngry ghost, and thro ugh each of the ten 
small ones you are reborn  as an animal. In the Sutra on the Ten Levels  the effects of 
small and intermediate ones are said to be the other way round.  
 
The fully ripened or fruitional result refers to th e type of rebirth one takes due to a particular 
action. An action also has a result similar to the cause or in conformity with the cause. The 
empowering or environmental result refers to th e type of place in which one is born or in 
which one lives. Each of the te n non-virtuous paths of action s has these three results. The 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
366 result is correspondingly small, middling, or great in dependence on the basis, the three 
mental poisons, and the strength of the action.  
 Thus, if killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, ly ing, divisive speech, harsh words, idle talk, 
covetousness, malice, and wrong views are great or  strong, the fully ripened result will be to 
take rebirth in one of the hell realms – th e hot hells, cold hells, neighboring hells, or 
occasional hells. If they are middling in streng th, the fully ripened result will be to take 
rebirth in the hungry ghost realm as a hu ngry ghost with outer obstructions, inner 
obstructions, or obstructions to food and drink. If they are small in strength, the fully ripened 
result will be to take rebirth in the animal realm as one of the many animals found in the 
depths of the oceans or one of the animals that  live amongst human beings. In this case, even 
if one is cared for by human beings, one will sti ll be unable to practice and will think only of 
eating, killing other animals, and so forth. The Sutra on the Ten Levels , on the other hand, says 
that great non-virtue brings the result of re birth in the hell realm, middling in the animal 
realm, and small in the hungry ghost realm. The reason for the latter exposition is that it 
asserts that the animal realm is  worse than the hungry ghost realm because animals are almost 
always stupid, whereas hungry ghosts are more intelligent and can therefore learn and even practice the teachings of the Buddha and purify  their negative action s. On the other hand, 
the Actual Basis of the Levels  says that the hungry ghost realm is worse because the suffering 
there can last even for eons whereas the life span as an animal can be very short.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The effect in conformity with the cause 
Second: The effects in conformity with the cause are that even having left the bad 
migrations and taken rebirth as a human bein g, respectively your life will be short, 
you will be lacking in possessions, you will  not keep your partner, there will be a 
lot of slander, you will be separated from loved ones, you will hear unpleasant 
sounds, others will not listen to your wo rds, and the three, attachment, hatred, 
and delusion will predominate.  
 
The result similar to the cause is  that, after having been reborn  in the lower realms when one 
once again takes rebirth as a human being: 
1. due to killing, one will have a short life; 
2. due to stealing, one will be poor and deprived of necessities such as food, shelter, and so 
forth; 
3. due to sexual misconduct, one’s partner, wife or husband, will not remain with one;  
4. due to lying, one will be criticized and will not have success;  
5. due to divisive speech, one will be separated from dear ones;  
6. due to harsh words, one will hear unpleasant sounds;  
7. due to idle talk, others will not listen to one’s words;  
8. due to covetousness, one will have strong attachment;  
9. due to malice, one will have strong anger and hatred; and 
10. due to wrong view, one will have confusion and be unable to understand.  
 
Thursday morning, 7 April 2005
