Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
367 Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 111) says:  
C The conditioning or empowering effect 
Third: The conditioning effect:  
1. of killing is that in future worlds, food and drink, medicine, harvests, and the 
like will have little power;  
2. that of stealing, great droughts and rains and {112} poor harvests etc.;  
3. that of sexual misconduct, swamps, much filth, unpleasant experiences, etc.;  
4. that of lying, that field work and work to do with boats will not succeed, etc.;  
5. that of divisive speech, that the loca tion [where you live] will be bumpy and 
uneven, it will be difficult  to travel, and so on;  
6. t h a t  o f  h a r s h  w o r d s ,  t h a t  t h e  l o c a t i o n  [ w h e r e  y o u  l i v e ]  w i l l  b e  f u l l  o f  l o g s ,  
brambles, rocks, pebbles, potsherds, etc.;  
7. that of idle talk, that fruit trees do not be ar fruit, bear fruit out of season, etc.;  
8. that of covetousness, that everything ex cellent deteriorates year by year, month 
by month, and day by day, etc.;  
9. that of harmful intent, a lot of epidemic s, injuries, infections, disputes, wars 
with enemy troops, etc.; and  
10. that of wrong views, that in the vessel wo rld the best resources will decline, etc.  
 Each of the ten non-virtuous paths of acti ons has its own empowering or environmental 
result: 1. that of killing is that external things such as  food, drink, medicines, harvests, and so forth 
will have little power; 
2. that of stealing is that in th e place in which one lives, there will droughts, strong winds and 
rains, poor harvests, and so forth – due to a la ck of rain, the trees and plants will die, and 
due to strong wind and rain, the blossoms on trees will be destroyed and will not bear fruit; 
3. that of sexual misconduct is that in the place in which one lives there will be many 
swamps, it will be very filthy, one will have unpleasant experiences, and so forth due to 
which one will not be happy there but due to karma one will have to stay there; 
4. that of lying is that one will not be successf ul in whatever one does whether one sows seeds 
in a field or works on boats; 
5. that of divisive speech is that the place in wh ich one lives will be bumpy and uneven due to 
which it will be difficult to walk and so forth; 
6. that of harsh words is that the place in which one lives will have many thorn bushes, 
brambles, stones, broken tiles and clay pots, and so forth; 
7. that of idle talk is that fruit trees, such as ol ive trees, will not bear fruit or will not bear fruit 
at the right time but instead will bear it at the wrong time and it will not be good due to which it will not be useful;  
8. that of covetousness is that all one’s excellences will deteriorate year by year, month by 
month, and day by day, whereby one will eventu ally come to lose everything and becomes 
poor; 
9. t h a t  o f  m a l i c e  o r  h a r m f u l  i n t e n t  i s  t h a t  o n e  w i l l  b e  a f f l i c t e d  b y  e p i d e m i c s  s u c h  a s  t h e  
plague, injuries, infectious dise ases, conflict in one’s family and with neighbors, wars, and 
so forth, whereby one’s mind will always be  worried and uncomfortable, like the people 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
368 living in Iraq who can never be happy or re laxed due to the fear caused by constant 
bombing; 
10. that of wrong view is that the res ources, su ch as jewels and minerals, in the country in 
which one lives will decline.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2B Positive actions and their effects 
1 White actions 
2 Their effects 
The second one has two points: the actions and the effects.  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2B1 White actions 
A Brief indication by means of scripture 
F i r s t :  T h e y  a r e  t h e  a c t i o n s  o f  b o d y  o f  c o r r e c t l y  a b s t a i n i n g  f r o m  k i l l i n g ,  s t e a l i n g ,  
and sexual misconduct due to possessing a virtuous mind having thought about 
their disadvantages and of carrying this abst ention to its conclusion. Similarly, this 
is to be applied to the four of speech and the three of mind, the difference being 
t h a t  t h e y  a r e  c a l l e d  “ a c t i o n s  o f  s p e e c h ”  a n d  “ a c t i o n s  o f  m i n d . ”  T h i s  i s  w h a t  i s  
stated in Actual Basis of the Levels .  
 
White actions are virtuous actions. If we ha ve a virtuous mind that thinks about the 
disadvantages of killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct, we will try to avoid committing 
these non-virtuous actions by having a mind of abstaining from them. Likewise, thinking 
about the many disadvantages of lying, divisive speech, harsh words, and idle talk, we try to 
abstain from these actions. Similarly, coveto usness, harmful intent, and wrong views have 
many disadvantages, thinking about them we turn away from and abstain from them. 
Thinking about the disadvantages of the ten no n-virtuous paths of actions, we will refrain 
from engaging in them  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Extensive explanation 
This should therefore be related to the basis, attitude, performance, and 
completion as appropriate. When applied to the path of action of abandoning 
killing, the basis is another sentient being.  The attitude is the wish to give it up 
through seeing its disadvanta ges. The performance is to put effort into completely 
abstaining from killing. {113} The comple tion is the action of body of correct 
abstention having been completed. The ot her [paths of action] should also be 
understood in this manner.  
 
The virtuous paths of actions also have a basi s, attitude, performance, and completion. In 
relation to the path of action of abandoning killing, the basis is another sentient being, this 
being mainly a human being or an animal as we are not able to perceive other types of 
sentient beings. However, while some beings canno t be killed by way of the body, they can be 
killed by black magic and so forth. The attitude  is through seeing the disadvantages of killing 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
369 to wish to abandon it. The performance is to pu t effort into abandoning the action of killing. 
The completion is to completely abstain oneself from killing.  
 The other nine paths of action s should be understood in the same way. For example, with 
respect to the white action of  abandoning stealing, the basi s is another’s belonging. The 
attitude is through seeing the disadvantages of stealing to wish to abandon stealing others’ 
belongings. The performance is to put effort in to completely abstaining from stealing, for 
example, taking others’ things with our hands. The completion is to mentally resolve to not 
steal others’ things. Likewise, with respect to sexual misconduct the basis is a man or woman 
who belongs to someone else and so forth. The a ttitude is through seeing the disadvantages of 
sexual misconduct to mentally abandon sexual misconduct, thinking “I will never engage in 
sexual misconduct.” The performance is to put e ffort into completely abstaining from sexual 
misconduct. The completion is to completely ab stain from it both mentally and physically.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2B2 The effects 
Second: From among the three [effects of virtuous paths of action], the fully 
ripened effect is to be born as a human, a desire realm god, and a god of the two 
upper realms due to a small, intermediate , and great virtuous action. As for the 
effect in conformity with the cause and the conditioning effect, the opposites to 
the [respective effects from] non-virtuous actions apply.  
 
The fully ripened or fruitional result of a small virtuous action is rebirth as a human being, 
that of a middling virtuous action is rebirth as a desire realm god, and that of a great virtuous 
action is rebirth as a god of the form or form less realms. With respect to human beings, there 
is rebirth as a human being in any one of the four continents. With respect to desire realm 
gods, there is rebirth in one of the six levels of the Four Great Kings, the Thirty-Three Gods, 
Free from Combat, Joyous Land, Enjoying Eman ations, and Controlling  Others’ Emanations. 
The form realm is divided into four concentrat ions which are further divided into seventeen 
levels – the first, second, and third concentratio ns are each further divided into three, and the 
fourth is divided into three places of ordinary  beings and five pure lands. The formless realm 
consists in Infinite Space, Infinite Consciou sness, Nothingness, and Without Discrimination 
Not Without Discrimination or the Peak of Ex istence. In order to achieve rebirth in the 
upper realms it is necessary to achieve the ca lm abiding included in the preparation of a 
concentration or formless absorption, which then acts as the projecting karma to be reborn 
there.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2C Indication of other classifications of actions146
  
1 The classification of throwing and completing ones  
2 The classification of what will definitely be experienced and what will not  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2C1 The classification of throwing and completing ones  
                                              
146 cf. note 10 (see separate root text) 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
370 1 [Presentation of the first classification]147
 
The third one has two points, the first of which is the classification of throwing 
and completing [actions].  
Throwing actions for [rebirth in] the happy  migrations are virtuous [actions] and 
throwing actions for [rebirth in] the bad migrations are non-virtuous [actions]. The 
completing ones are uncertain, however, even in the happy migrations, incomplete 
limbs, minor body parts, or sense powers , hideous looks, a short life span, many 
illnesses, poverty, and so forth are caus ed by non-virtue. Also for animals and 
hungry ghosts, perfect prosperity is caused by virtue.  
 Among the classifications of actions there is  a l s o  t h r o w i n g  o r  p r o j e c t i n g  k a r m a  a n d  
completing karma. When the throwing karma is virtuous it will cause rebirth in the happy 
migrations, whereas when it is non-virtuous it  will cause rebirth in the bad migrations. In 
other words, virtuous actions ar e throwing karma that throw one into the higher realms – the 
human, antigod, and god realms – and non-virt uous actions are throwing karma that throw 
one into the lower realms – the hell, hungry ghost, and animal realms.   With respect to completing karma, having taken rebirth as a human being, for example, 
due to non-virtuous completing karma one coul d have an imperfect bo dy such as lacking a 
limb, a minor body part such as  a finger, o r a sense power, one’s body could be very ugly, one’s lifespan could be short, one could be affl icted by many illness, one could be poor and 
unable to find the necessities of life, and so fo rth. All of these are due to having created non-
virtuous actions that act as completing karmas . On  t he o t her han d , h av in g be en  bo rn  as  a 
hungry ghost or animal due to non-virtuous th rowing karma, one could be very wealthy or 
prosperous due to virtuous completing karma.  For example, due to virtuous completing 
karma even though a naga rebirth is included  among the animals many of them are rich, 
likewise there are hungry ghosts who are rich. We  also see, for example, dogs and cats that 
belong to rich people who have good food, clot hing, a pleasant place to live, and so forth. 
Their throwing karma is non-virt uous due to which they have ta ken rebirth as an animal such 
as a dog, but their completing karma is virtuous  due to which they obtain good food and so 
forth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The four possible combinations of virtuous and non-virtuous throwing and completing actions  
Thus, there are four alternatives:  
- projected by virtuous throwing [action] and also completed by virtuous 
completing [action], 
- projected by virtuous throwing [action] and completed by non-virtuous 
completing [action], 
- projected by non-virtuous throwing [action] and completed by non-virtuous 
completing [action] 
                                              
147 Trijang Rinpoche is proposing an addi tional classification of Je Tsongkhapa’s first item into five sections 
some of which however overlap with Je Tsongkhapa’s se cond item. Trijang Rinpoche’s points three to five 
belong to Je Tsongkhapa’s second item and thus do not form a sub-division of the first one. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
371 - projected by non-virtuous throwing [action] and completed by virtuous 
completing [action].  
 
An example of one with virtuous throwing karma and virtuous completing karma is we 
ourselves who have been born as a human bein g and have sufficient wealth, all necessities, 
and a good body endowed with the eighteen qualities.  
An example of one with virtuous throwing karma but non-virtuous completing karma is 
someone who has been reborn as a human being but is very poor and unable to find food, clothing, shelter, and so forth. This is caused by having practiced morality but not generosity, 
that is, having been unable to gi ve others what they asked for. 
An example of one with non-virtuous throwing  karma and non-virtuous completing karma is 
a being born as an animal that is also unable to find food, shelter, and so forth due to which 
it is in risk of dying of hunger and thirst.  An example of one with non-virtuous throwing  karma but virtuous completing karma is a 
being born as an animal, such as  a dog or cat, that has good f ood, a good place to live in, is 
taken care of by a human being like a son or daughter, and so forth. This is due to having practiced generosity in past lives but not having created virtuous throwing karma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B1B-2C2 The classification of what w ill definitely be experienced and what will not  
3 [Presentation of the second classification] 
With respect to what will definitely be ex perienced and what wi ll not, what will 
definitely be experienced is what has been done intentionally and accumulated. 
What will not definitely be experienced is what has [not] been done intentionally 
and has not accumulated. 
 
There is definite karma and indefinite karma. An action that is definite is one that will 
definitely bring a result. A karma that will defini tely be experienced is an action that was done 
intentionally and was accumulated. An indefinite  karma to one that is not done intentionally 
and is not accumulated; for example, to kill an ant accidentally by stepping on it while 
walking, or to kill a dog or cat accidentally when driving a car. In both cases we did not have the wish to kill, we did not mentally intend to kill, and we did not accumulate the action. 
Similarly, when a dog is brought around a stup a, the dog does not have the intention wishing 
to circumambulate the stupa but still it crea tes some virtue. The commentary on the twelve 
links in the Ocean of Sport  has a more detailed explanation ab out karma that will definitely be 
experienced and divides it into that which will definitely be experienced in this life, that 
which will definitely be experienced in the ne xt life, and that which will definitely be 
experienced in some future life.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 Classification of what has been done and what has been accumulated 
The difference between what has been done and what has been accumulated [is as 
follows]. What has been done is what has b een intended or what has manifested 
through the body or speech after having been intended. What has been 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
372 accumulated are actions apart from the ten [exceptions] such as those done in a 
dream. What has not been accumulated are the ten types such as those done in a 
dream.148 
 
There is karma that has been done and karma th at has been accumulated. An action that has 
been done means that one mentally intended or  thought to do an action or, having thought 
about it, did it with the body or speech. In ot her words, one mentally wishes to do something 
and then actually does it by body or speech, whereby this becomes an action that has been 
done. Actions that have been accumulated excl ude the actions of killing, stealing, sexual 
misconduct, and so forth and the abstention from  killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, and so 
forth that are done in a dream and so forth. In other words, the actions, virtuous or non-
virtuous, done in a dream and so forth are not done and not accumulated. All other such actions, the three of body and four of speec h, done during waking are accumulated.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 [Further classification of that which will definitely be experienced] 
{114} That which will definitely be experien ced is [divided into] three [categories] 
in terms of the time when th e result is experienced:  
1. That which will be experienced as a vi sible phenomenon is an action whose 
effect will be experienced in this life. 
2. That which will be experienced after taking rebirth is [an action] whose effect 
will be experienced in the following149 life.  
3. T h a t  w h i c h  w i l l  b e  e x p e r i e n c e d  a t  a n o t h e r  t i m e  i s  w h a t  w i l l  b e  e x p e r i e n c e d  
from the next but one life onwards. 
 Actions whose results will definitely to be expe rienced can be divided into three in terms of 
time. The first is actions whose results will be experienced in this life, for example, 
Angulimala who killed 999 people after his teacher told him to kill 1000 people and to cut off one of their fingers and string it on a rosary in order to purify. It is recounted that after 
Angulimala had killed 999 people and could not fi nd anyone else to kill, given that, terrified, 
everyone had disappeared, he thought to kill hi s mother even though he did not want to kill 
her. However, while he was contemplating th is, the Buddha appeared in front of him and 
walking slowly away from him,. Angulimala call ed out to him to wait, to which the Buddha 
replied, “You are always going. I am always st aying still.” The Buddha then gave Angulimala 
teachings whereby his mind changed. However, because of the heavy negative karma he had 
created previously, when he was alone in his room he experienced the suffering of being 
burned by fire. In fact, when the Buddha told someone to stick an iron rod through a hole in the wall in order to verify this, the rod melt ed! Angulimala experienced this suffering as a

forgetfully, (7) without meaning to, (8) actions that are naturally neutral, (9) that have been purified through 
regret, or (10) through antidotes.

Thursday afternoon, 7 April 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 114) says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B2 Specific considerations on karma  
A The way in which it is necessary to achieve a fully qualified basis 
[B Divisions] 
2B4B-2B1A-2B2A The way in which it is necessary to achieve a fully qualified basis  
Second: Through abandoning the ten non-virtues you do indeed obtain a good 
basis. However, if you achieve a fully qualified basis for accomplishing 
omniscience,150 it will be quite unlike others [with respect to] covering ground in 
meditating on the path. Therefore, you should attain that basis.  
 
By abandoning the ten non-virtuous actions, we will obtain a good basis or good rebirth, that 
is, a good body. However, it is necessary to achieve the particular basis for accomplishing 
omniscience – a precious human rebirth posse ssing the eighteen qualities of the eight 
freedoms and ten endowments. With such a body it  is easier to attain buddhahood since, by 
meditating on the path, one will quickly achieve enlightenment. With any other kind of body, 
even that of a god of the desire realm, form realm, or formless realm, it will take longer and 
not be easy to achieve omniscienc e. Although they are good rebirt hs, it takes longer for one to 
achieve buddhahood on this support because these beings have more enjoyments that distract 
them and make them have little in terest in achieving buddhahood.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

There are eight fruitional or fully ripened qualities: 
• having an excellent life span, 
• having an excellent complexion, 
• having an excellent extraction, 
• having excellent power, 
• others respecting one’s words, 
• being renowned as very powerful, 
• being male, and 
• having strength. 
Each of these qualities ha s its respective cause.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
A Excellent life span 
The first one is that due to an earlier thro wing [action], a long life has been thrown 
and one lives accordingly. Due to that, you undertake actions for the purpose of 
yourself and others and accumulate a lot of virtue for a long period of time.  
 
The cause of a long life is having created virtuous actions in previous lives by safeguarding 
others lives and so forth. Due to having a long  life, we can undertake actions for our own and 
others benefit and accumulate a lot of virtue for a long period of time.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Excellent complexion 
The second one refers to good complexion  and shape, complete sense powers, and 
balanced proportions. Due to this, disciple s, having been delighted at one’s very 
sight, are gathered and listen to one’s words.  
 
Due to having a good complexion and shape, complete sense powers, and good proportions, when other people see us they will like us, wh ereby we will be able to gather disciples and 
when we talk or teach them they will listen well.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Excellent extraction 
The third one is to be born into an exal ted and well-known family respected by the 
world. Due to this, one’s commands will be accomplished and not transgressed.  
 
Due to being born into a noble family that is respected by and renowned in the world, when 
we give commands to others they will not tr ansgress them but will accept them and carry 
them out.

You will not be afraid among people, and no one will prevent you from going together with all sentient bein gs or living in seclusion. 
                                             
 
152 As has been noted above in the context of the teacher-disciple relation ship, the term vessel ( snod) refers to

associated with this quality.

of sutra, however according to tantra men and women have the same possibility to practice 
and achieve enlightenment in this very life. Howe ver, from the point of view of sutra there are 
differences that are related to the culture that existed in India at the time of the Buddha’s 
teaching. So don’t worry, okay?!

2B4B-2B1A-2B2B-2 The causes of  the fully ripened [effects]

2B4B-2B1A-2B2B-2A Division of the causes in eight 
The second one has eight points, the first of  which is to not harm to any sentient 
being and to cultivate an attitude of non-harm. Furthermore, it says: 
 
Through freeing those caught in places of slaughter 
And likewise helping through [giving] life, Through reversing harming creatures You will gain longevity. 
 
From a doctor’s respect for the ill, From the administration of medicine, From not harming creatures with sticks Or lumps of earth, you will be without illness. 
 
                                             
 
153 JN: mngon shes , clairvoyance

377 There are eight causes of the ei ght fruitional or fully ripened results. The cause of the first, 
having an excellent life span, is  to not harm any sentient being and to rely on a mind of non-
harmfulness. Due to saving animals that a butc her is leading to slaughter, through helping 
others in danger, through turning away harm to others’ lives, and so forth, we will have a long 
life. Likewise, if we take care of the sick, give  them medicines, and so forth, we will have a 
long life. Similarly, through not harming others with lumps of earth, sticks, and so forth, we 
will be free from illness and thereby have a lo ng life. Due to having a long life and good 
health, we can achieve whatever we wish, whereas if we are often sick and have a short life or 
are mentally depressed we will not be able to achieve much.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
The second one is to offer light, such as butter lamps, and new clothing. Apart 
from that:  
 
From the absence of anger as basis 
And offering jewelry – a good physique. From lack of envy [you will gain] Good fortune as fruit, it is explained. 
 If we eliminate the darkness with candles, butte rlamps, and so forth in order to help others 
and give them new clothing, shoes, cushions , and so forth, we will obtain the second 
fruitional results – a good complexion and a clea r mind. As the verse says, by relying on non-
anger and giving others ornaments or jewelry such as earrings, necklaces, and so forth, we will 
obtain a good body. As a result of being free fr om jealousy, we will have good fortune or good 
luck, that is, everything will go well for us, both mentally and physically.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

servant.  The fourth one is to give food, clothing , and so forth to those who ask for it and 
to also benefit [others] without being asked, and, while lacking necessities, to give 
away to the suffering and to those who are fields of positive qualities.

The cause of the third fruitional result, having an  excellent extraction, is to destroy our pride 
and to respect our gurus and other beings like  a servant. The cause of the fourth, having

need help even though they have not asked for it, to give to those who are suffering, and to

 The cause of the fifth, others respecting one’s  words, is to abandon the four non-virtues of 
speech – lying and so forth. The cause of the sixth, being renowned as very powerful, is to 
make prayers to accomplish various good qualitie s in our future lives and to make offerings to 
the Three Jewels, our parents, hearers, solita ry realizers, abbots, masters, and gurus.  
 The cause of the seventh, being male, is to de light in the good qualities of men and to not 
delight in women but to see the disadvantages of  doing so (for example, attachment to her 
body, which is soft to th e touch and so forth, that brings th e bad result of committing a defeat 
and so forth), to oppose the aspiration of th ose who desire a woman’s body, and to free those 
whose male organs are to be castrated. In In dia there were people who were castrated and 
turned into eunuchs, whereas here in Italy male  dogs and cats are taken to a veterinarian to 
be castrated as a form of birth control. In Ti bet the nomads would castrate the male goats, 
sheep, and so forth in the springti me as this would make their meat  taste better. In the past in 
Tibet there was a king who castra ted several men like animals bu t, unlike the animals, they 
died from the operation due to which a law wa s passed forbidding the castration of human 
males!

do, to help others by adding our strength to th eir strength, and to give others food and drink. 
In other words, we can either do others’ work for them if they are unable to do it, or we can help them by putting our strength  together with theirs in order,  for example, to move a table. 
If they ask for our help but we are unable to help them, we should at least apologize for our 
inability to help them. We should not get an gry when others ask for our help and make 
excuses that we are too busy or need to go somewhere!  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B2B-2B Threefold division  
When those eight causes are conjoined with three [more] causes, their fully 
ripened effects will be outstanding. 
2B4B-2B1A-2B2B-2B1 The first cause: a perfectly pure mind 
A Aspects related to oneself 
B Aspects related to others 
As for the three causes, [the first one,] a perfectly pure mind, has two aspects 
related to oneself. 
A Aspects related to oneself 
They are: 1. to dedicate the virtues of accompli shing those causes to unsurpassed 
enlightenment without hoping for a fully ripened effect, and 
2. to accomplish the causes intensely from the bottom of one’s heart.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
379  
The first of the three causes is a completely pu re mind. It has two aspects related to oneself: 
1. to dedicate virtues to enlighte nment without hope of reward, 
2. to make a strong determination from the botto m of one’s heart to achieve these causes and 
to achieve enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Aspects related to others 
The two aspects related to others are: 
1. to give up envy, competitiveness, an d scorn at the sight of the highest, 
intermediate, and least [practitioners] in agreement with the Dharma and to 
rejoice,  
2. even if you do not manage to do that  [completely], to examine many times a 
day what to do.  
 
There are two aspects related to others. With re spect to those who are superior, middling, and 
inferior, we should respectively abandon jeal ousy, competitiveness, and scorn and instead 
rejoice in whatever they do. In addition, we sh ould examine ourselves ma ny times a day to see 
whether when we see people who are superior je alousy arises, when we see people who are our 
peers whether competitiveness arises, and when we see people who are inferior whether scorn 
or disdain arises. If we see that any of these atti tudes arise, we should try to eliminate it since 
in this way we will gradually improve.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B2B-2B2 The second cause: pure performance 
A Related to oneself 
Pure performance related to oneself implies long duration, continuity and 
intensity. 
 
Pure performance related to oneself means to do  it for a long time, without interruption, and 
very intensely.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Related to others 
Related to others, [pure performance ] is to cause someone who has not correctly 
taken [the rules of ethical discipline] to do  so, to express praise for those who have 
taken them so that they gene rate delight, to make them do so continuously, and to 
make them not abandon them. 
 
Pure performance related to others is that when others have not correctly taken vows to 
encourage them to take vows such as the Eigh t Mahayana precepts, the one-day lay vows, and 
so forth. Then when others have taken them, we should praise them in order to make them delight in this, make them do  so continuously, and make them not give them up. For 
example, if we know someone who once a week on the weekend engages in some practice, we 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
380 should encourage that person to try to do th at practice everyday. On the other hand, if 
someone is thinking to give up practicing even  just once a week on the weekend, we should 
encourage him or her to keep doing it every weekend and to not give it up.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B2B-2B3 The third cause: a pure field 
Since the very two – the attitude and perf ormance – produce plenty of good fruit, 
they resemble a field. 
 
Because our attitude and performance are pure they will bring many good results, due to 
which they are like a field in which we can sow seeds that bring the result of plants. 
 
Friday morning, 8 April 2005 
 
(Oral transmission 10:50-11:10 page 117-124)  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 117) says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3 After these considerations how to engage [in virtuous actions] and oppose [the 
non-virtuous] 
A General presentation 
B Specifically how to purify by means of the four powers 
{117} The third one – after these considerations how to engage [in virtuous actions] and 
oppose– has two points: general presentation and specifically how to purify by 
means of the four powers. 
2B4B-2B1A-2B3A General presentation 
1 How to bring forth a mind cultivating this perpetually, day and night  
First: In Engaging in [Bodhisattva ] Behavior it says:  
 
Suffering springs from what is non-virtuous. 
‘How to reach certain freedom from it?’ 
It is appropriate, day and night, 
Always to think about nothing but this. 
 
And: 
 
The root of everything that’s virtuous Is aspiration, the Muni said. Again, the root of that is always To meditate on fully ripened effects. 
 
In accordance with this statement, you should come to understand positive and 
negative actions and their effects and then meditate on them over and over again, 
for [the karmic process] is a very hidden phenomenon which is why it is extremely difficult to gain certainty about it.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
381  
Due to having created many non-virtuous action s, specifically the ten non-virtues, suffering 
will definitely arise. Therefore, we need to think day and night about how to become free 
from these non-virtuous actions, in other words,  how to purify them. In short, we need t o 
think again and again about the fact that havi ng created negative actions they will bring 
suffering, until a clear understanding of this arises and we generate the mind determined to 
abandon or stop creating non-virtuous actions.  
 Buddha Shakyamuni said that the root of all that  is virtuous is belief or confidence in the 
law of actions and their results. We need to  think about what the Buddha taught and to 
generate faith in it, whereby we will engage in virtuous actions and strive to avoid non-
virtuous actions. Thus, we need to think about the fruitional or fully ripened result of non-
virtuous actions, which is rebirt h in the bad migrations, and that of virtuous results, which is 
rebirth in the happy migrations . Understanding this clearly, we should strive to abandon 
creating non-virtuous actions and strive at all times to create virtuous actions. We need to 
think again and again about white actions an d their results and black actions and their 
results, and to do analytical me ditation on this. The law of acti ons and their results is not easy 
t o  u n d e r s t a n d  n o r  i s  i t  e a s y  t o  g a i n  c e r t a i n t y  with respect to it because it is a very hidden 
phenomenon. In fact, the law of actions and re sults cannot be understood on the basis of 
logic or reasoning but only on the basis of what  the Buddha taught it, that is, on the basis of 
scriptural authority. Unlike fire, which is no t a hidden phenomenon, it cannot be understood 
by inference based on reasoning such as “There is fire because there is smoke.” Also unlike 
impermanence, it cannot be understood base d on reasoning such “Things are impermanent 
because they change momentarily.” The detail s of the law of cause and result cannot be 
understood by reasoning but only by relying on the scriptures of the Buddha. In other words, 
only by having faith in the Buddha’s teachings ca n we understand the law of cause and result.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The vital importance of gaining conviction concerning the Buddha’s statements about how actions 
[bring about] effects 
In this regard the King of Concentration  [Sutra ] says:  
 
Even if the moon and stars fell down, 
The earth collapsed with mountains and with towns, And if the heaven’s aspect was transformed – Still no word you said would be untrue. 
 You should thus develop conviction in th e words of the Tathagata and sustain it. 
Unless you have gained uncontrived certai nty regarding them, you will not gain 
the certainty with regard to any Dhar ma that gladdens the Conqueror.  
 We need to develop conviction in the Buddha’s teachings about actions and their results. The 
King of Meditative Stabilizations Sutra  says that even if the moon  and stars were to fall down, 
even if the earth together with the mountains an d villages were to be destroyed, and even if 
the aspect of space were to change into so mething else, still Buddha Shakyamuni words would 
not become untrue. In other words, since he neve r lies but always tells the truth, we need to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
382 develop belief in him and in his teachings. If we do not understand this correctly, we will not 
gain conviction in the Tathagata’s teachings.  O n  t h e  o t h e r  h a n d ,  i f  w e  g a i n  b e l i e f  a n d  
certainty in them, we will delight the Conque ror. In other words, by gaining faith and 
certainty in the Dharma, the Conquerors, who ar e omniscient and know everything, will be 
delighted. If, on the other hand, we fight with  others, steal from them , speak harsh words to 
them, and so forth, while the buddhas will not become unhappy, neither will they delight. 
Rather they will think th at they cannot help us because our mi nd is not yet ripe since we only 
engage in negative actions. Although the Buddh a has given teachings, only if we, from our 
own side, put them into practice can our inner realizations grow. If we do not have much 
confidence in the Buddha’s teachings, do not understand them deeply, and do not practice 
them much, there will still be some result but it will be much less. Therefore, we need to 
understand the meaning of the Buddha’s scriptures.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 Meditation on emptiness becomes helpful for gaining certainty with respect to actions and their 
results  
Some profess to have gained certainty with respect to emptiness and do not have 
certainty with respect to actions and their results nor care about it, however they have arrived at a mistaken understandin g of emptiness for if you understand 
emptiness, having seen it to be the me aning of dependent relation it becomes 
helpful for generating certainty with re spect to actions and their results.  
 
By meditating on emptiness we can gain cert ainty with respect to the law of actions and 
results. This is because in order to understand  emptiness, we need to understand dependent-
arising or dependent-relation. If we understand  dependent-relation, we will understand that 
in dependence on good causes good results will arise, and that in dependence on bad causes 
bad results will arise. Thus, non-virtuous action s, which are bad, bring about the bad result of 
suffering, whereas virtuous actions, which are g ood, bring about the good result of happiness. 
The more we meditate on emptiness, the more we will come to understand this because in 
order to meditate on emptiness it is necessary to meditate on dependent-arising. In this way 
we will come to understand the law of cause and result and gain certainty with respect to it.  
 If someone, on the other hand, says that they have gained cert ainty with respect to 
emptiness but do not take care of the law of ac tions and their results, this shows that they 
have not understood the law of actions and re sults and have a mistaken understanding of 
emptiness. Such people think that they have realized emptiness but in fact they have a 
completely mistaken understanding of it. This is because if someone understands emptiness, 
he or she necessarily understands dependent-aris ing. In fact, emptiness is understood by way 
of reasonings such as: the table is empty of inherent existence because it is a dependent-
arising. In other words, because it is empty,  it is a dependent-arising. By understanding 
emptiness, we understand dependent-relation. Thus, meditation on emptiness is a favorable 
condition for understanding the law of actions and their results. Here we are not talking 
about meditating on an empty stomach or an empty room, we are talking about the emptiness 
of true existence or inherent ex istence. Why is something empty of inherent existence? It is 
empty of inherent existence because it depends on causes and conditions, because it depends 
on parts, and so forth. By means of such re asonings we meditate on emptiness. There are 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
383 those who call themselves Mahayanists but, not understanding the meaning of emptiness, 
contemplate a nothingness, a lack of all phenom ena, and try to stop thoughts from arising. 
This, however, is mistaken.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
From the same sutra:  
 
Like an illusion, a bubble, a mirage, or lightning, All phenomena are like the moon in water. Although human beings who pass to another world  After death don’t perceive this,  {118} Actions once done do not disappear; 
White or black they cause like effects to ripen. 
The logical approach is good, but the Jina’s  Object is subtle and hard to see. 
 All phenomena are like illusions, bubbles, mirage s, lightening, and the reflection of the moon 
in water in the sense that they  are not inherently or truly existent. Illusions or magical 
emanations are not true, they arise due to the power of substance and mantra. However, even 
though they are not true, they appear to be true. Bubbles, on the other hand, appear to be 
solid but are not; they easily disappear. A mirage is a vision  of water on a summer day in a 
location where no body of water exists. Phenom ena are also like lightning. After sentient 
beings die they pass to another world, that is, th ey do not go to another country in this world 
but go to another realm. When we die, we do  not cease to exist. While our contaminated 
body of flesh and bones which came from our parents is left behind, our feeling, discrimination, compositional factors, and cons ciousness aggregates remain and seek another 
body.   Actions that were done before do not become non-existent. White and black karma 
respectively bring white or happy and black or  suffering fruitional results. This way of 
reasoning is good, but the law of actions and th eir results can only be understood slightly 
from reasonings since the way in  which actions bring results is very subtle and difficult to 
understand. It cannot be seen by the eyes, and is only an object that is seen directly by the 
Conqueror.   In short, the results of actions are difficult to understand. Karma needs conditions in order 
to bring its result, only when they come together will it bring a result. Thus, if the karma exists but the conditions do not come together, it will not bring its re sult. Both causes and 
conditions are necessary in order to bring fort h a result. For example, if a flower seed, the 
cause, exists and we sow it in a field but the conditions of water, heat, and fertilizer are lacking, even though the seed is healthy it wi ll not grow into a flower due to lacking the 
necessary conditions. On the other hand, when th e conditions are presen t the seed will give 
rise to a flower. Thus, a cause alone without co nditions is not sufficient to bring a result. 
Likewise, the many seeds or imprints on our co nsciousness will ripen and produce their result 
only upon particular conditions are met.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
384  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 The uselessness of mere knowledge if you do not think about actions and their results 
Therefore, you should generate certainty wi th respect to the two [types of] karma, 
the causes, along with their effects, and constantly, day and night, cut yourself off 
from the bad migrations by means of examining the three gates. To not be 
proficient at first in the distinctions of cause and effect and to know a bit but to 
still act carefree with regard to the thr ee gates only opens the gate to the bad 
migrations.  
 
If we know only a bit about the law of cause an d result but do not think much about it there 
will not be much benefit. There are two causes , white or virtuous actions and black or non-
virtuous actions, that have two results, happi ness and suffering. We need to understand this 
and gain certainty with respect to it, and then  constantly, day and night, examine the three 
doors of our body, speech, and mind. In other words, we should examine day and night what 
our body is doing, what our speech is saying, an d what our mind is thinking. In this way, we 
will come to understand that by not engaging in non-virtuous actions with our body, speech, 
and mind, we can cut the root of rebirth in  the bad migrations, meaning that we can 
interrupt the taking of rebirth in the lower real ms. On the other hand, if we do not think like 
this, it will be difficult to do.  
 Hence, if someone only understands the law of  cause and result a little bit, is not very 
skillful with respect to it, and does not put much  effort into guarding the three doors but acts 
without conscientiousness, he or she will not be able to block the door to rebirth in the lower 
realms. In other words, such a person will be unable to put a stop to rebirth in the lower realms due to being careless and thereby creating non-virtuous actions, ev en though he or she 
has some understanding of actions and their result s. Because no effort is needed in order to 
create non-virtuous actions, we need to be ca reful regarding the actions of our body, speech, 
and mind. In particular, we need to develop the intention to not engage in non-virtuous 
actions. We should not allow our mind to mo ve toward a motivation to engage in non-
virtuous actions of body and speech. For action s of body and speech a mo tivation is necessary. 
It is intention that moves the m i n d  t o  n o n - v i r t u e  o r  v i r t u e  d u e  t o  w h i c h  w e  c r e a t e  n o n -
virtuous and virtuous actions. “Intention” is co nsidered to be action of mind. We should not 
allow our mind to move toward non-virtuous  actions and instead make it move toward 
virtuous actions of body and speech. If we do th is, we will experience good results, whereas if 
we do not, we will experience bad results. Just as people cannot come into a room if the door is closed, we need to close the door to the lower realms.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In the Sutra Requested by  [the Naga King ] Sagara  it says:  
 
Lord of Nagas, one Dharma of th e bodhisattvas cuts right through 
falling down and being born in ba d migrations. What is that one 
Dharma? It is this: the individual investigation regarding virtuous 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
385 phenomena thinking, “What am I do ing? Am I straying away from 
them or abiding in them night and day?” 
 
That which cuts falling into and being reborn in the bad migrations is one Dharma of the 
bodhisattvas. What is that one Dharma? It is at all times, day and night, to understand that if 
we create non-virtue we will take a bad rebirth wh ereas if we create virtue we will take a good 
rebirth. Not wishing to go to the bad migratio ns, we need to practice virtue. We need to 
understand this by means of doing analytical me ditation on it. Hence, we need to think day 
and night, “What will happen if I create non-virtuous actions? I will go to the bad migrations. 
On the other hand, if I create virtuous actions, I will not.” We need to understand and think 
about this and stop engaging in non-virtuous acti ons. In this way, we will close the door to 
rebirth in the lower realms, that is, we will interrupt or cut rebirth in the lower realms.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 How to turn away from faulty conduct through such considerations  
As for how to turn away from faulty cond uct through such considerations, it says 
in the Chapter of the Truthful One :  
 
O King, do not commit the act of killing. 
For every being life is a great loss, Therefore, we all wish to keep it long. Even deep down you should not consider killing. 
 Like this, you should familiarize with rely ing on the mind of restraint a lot without 
your motivation even moving towards mi sdeeds like the ten non-virtues and the 
like. 
 With respect to how to turn away from faulty conduct through such th oughts, the Buddha in 
this sutra tells the king to not kill, saying that for all beings it is a great loss to be killed since 
everyone cherishes their life, that therefore ever yone wishes to remain alive for a long time, 
and that he should not have the slightest mental thought wi shing to kill. We too should 
abandon engaging in misdeeds such as killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisive 
speech, harsh words, idle talk, covetousness, malice, and wrong view. We should not allow 
even the slightest motivation or intention to aris e in our mind to kill, to  steal, to engage in 
sexual misconduct, to lie, and so forth. Mentally  we should not allow such thoughts to arise, 
b u t  i f  t h e y  d o  w e  s h o u l d  s t o p  t h e m  i m m e d i ately and familiarize with the thought to not 
engage in any non-virtuous actions. By doin g this, we will become able to abstain from 
creating non-virtuous actions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
6 The principle of action in accordance with statements of the former Kadampas  
Khamlungpa said to Puchungpa: “Geshe [Drom Ton]pa taught that only actions 
and their results are of vital importance , however, nowadays explaining this, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 4-8 April 2005 (08) 
386 listening to it, and meditating on it are ma de out to be worthless. I wonder is this 
just because it is difficult to practice?” and Puchungpa said: {119} “It is just so.” 
 
Khamlungpa, a Kadam geshe whose present incarn ation is a layman with a wife and child 
who stays together with Geshe So pa in the United States, said  to Puchungpa “The virtuous 
friend said that the law of cause and result is very important but nowadays there are those 
who think that explaining it, list ening to it, and meditating on it are worthless. Is that just 
because it is difficult to practice?” Puch ungpa replied saying, “It is like that.”

FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
387 Monday afternoon, 11 April 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 119) says:  
 
 Dromtonpa said: “Followers of Jowo [Atisha], audacity is totally inappropriate, 
dependent origination is subtle.” Puchun gpa said: “In my old age I am [at last] 
getting on with the Sutra of the Wise and the Foolish155.” And Sharawa said: “The 
Buddha taught that whatever errors and fa ults come about, they should not be 
blamed on a bad direction or a bricklayer’s  work; it is simply that ‘This action 
having been done, this is produced.’” 
 
The way in which karma – a dependent-arising – functions is very subtle, therefore we must 
be careful to avoid creating even very slight  negative actions. Puchungpa, a Kadampa lama, 
said that in his old age he would read and familiarize himself with the Sutra of the Wise and the 
Foolish . We too should study this sutra that se t out how karma works. Geshe Sharawa said 
that the Buddha taught that every error and faul t that arises, come from our actions. Having 
done a non-virtuous action, the result of bad rebirth in the lower realms and so forth will 
come about. For this reason, we should not bl ame other people, saying that our problems are 
their fault. We need to understand that they  are our fault since it is we who did the non-
virtuous actions of killing, stealing, sexual mi sconduct, and so forth. Due to the many non-
virtuous actions that we have created, suffering is produced. Why do beings take rebirth in the bad migrations? Does someone throw them  there? No, only their own non-virtuous 
actions throw them there. In other words, such rebirths are the result of their own actions. 
Understanding this, we must strive as much as possible not to create negative actions. We should not think that small non-virtuous acti ons do not matter since they too will bring the 
unpleasant result of suffering. Therefore, we need to be careful at all times.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

statements of the Teacher Buddha 
[2 How to redress downfalls 
3 How to redress negative actions 
4 How the four powers are complete with all the antidotes

2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-1 How negativities and downfalls need to be purified by various means in accordance with 
the statements of the Teacher Buddha 
Second: If despite your effort not to be ta inted by such faulty conduct, some fault 
occurs under the influence of non-consci entiousness or the multitude of mental 
afflictions, etc., it is inappropriate to let the matter rest without giving it any 
consideration. It is, therefore, necessary  t o  m a k e  a n  e f f o r t  a t  t h e  m e a n s  f o r  
redressing it taught by the compassionate Teacher.

388 We need to put effort into not being stained by  faulty conduct, but when faults arise due to
