powers, that were taught by Buddha Shakyamuni.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-2 How to redress downfalls  
More specifically, as regards redressing down falls, this should be done as explained 
according to the three individual [types of] vows. 
 
While “the three vows” could refer to the lay vo ws, novice vows, and vows of full ordination, 
here they probably refer to the vows of indivi dual liberation, the bodhisattva vows, and the 
tantra vows. When, for example, we transgress one the eight types of vows of individual 
liberation, we should purify them in accordan ce with the explanations of how to redress 
them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3 How to redress negativities 
A The power of remorse  
B The power of thorough application of antidotes 
C The power of preventing faults in future 
D The power of the support 
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3A The power of remorse 
Redressing negativities should be done by means of the four powers. Among them, 
the first power is the power of remorse,156 which is much regret for the non-
virtuous deeds one has done since beginningless time. For it to arise, it is necessary 
to meditate on how the three [types of] effects, such as the fully ripened ones, are produced. When applying this [power], you should do it through the confession in 
the Sacred Golden Light Sutra  and the confession by means of the thirty-five 
buddhas. 
 To purify our negativities we need to apply the four opponent powers: 1. the opponent power of regret, 
2. the opponent power of the resolve not to do it again, 
3. the opponent power of applying an antidote, 
4. the opponent power of relying on the basis – going for refuge and generating the mind of 
enlightenment that wishes to attain enlightenm ent for the benefit of all sentient beings.  
The opponent power of repudiation or regret is  generated by thinking about the fact that 
from beginningless time until now we have cr eated such non-virtuous actions and thereby 
generating strong regret or remo rse for them. In order to genera te sincere regret, we need to 
understand that every action ha s three types of results: a fully  ripened or fruitional result, a 
result similar to the cause, and an environmen tal or empowering result. The fruitional result 
of non-virtuous actions is to be reborn in the lower realms as a hell being, hungry ghost, or 
                                             
 
156 JN: sun ‘byin pa’i stobs , more literally “power of repudiation” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
389 animal. When this karma has been somewhat ex hausted, we once again take rebirth in the 
human realm where we will still have to experi ence the result similar to the cause and the 
environmental result. We need to think about and meditate on how this happens. Then we 
need to do the confession found in the Sutra of Golden Light  or the confession in relation to 
the thirty-five buddhas ( Confession of the Downfalls of a Bodhisattva ). 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3B The power of thorough application of antidotes  
1 Based on the profound sets of sutras 
2 Aspiration for emptiness 
3 Based on recitation 4 Based on images 
5 Based on making offerings 
6 Based on names 
The second one, the power of thorough ap plication of antidotes, has six points: 
based on the profound sutras, aspiration for emptiness, based on recitation, based 
on images, based on making offerings, and based on names.  
 
The power of thorough application of antidotes is of six types: 
1. the power of the antidote based on  the profound sets of sutras, 
2. the power of the antidote of aspiration for emptiness, 
3. the power of the antidote based on recitation, 
4. the power of the antidote based on images, 
5. the power of the antidote based on making offerings, and 
6. the power of the antidote based on names.  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3B1 Based on the profound sets of sutras 
The first one is to absorb the words of th e sets of sutras like the Perfection of 
Wisdom Sutras, retain them, read them, and so forth.  
 
With respect to the power of the antidote based on the profound sets of sutras, in order to 
purify negativities we can recite, for example, from among the Perfection of Wisdom Sutras, 
the Diamond Cutter Sutra , retain or memorize it, and read it. We can also recite other sutras, 
for example, the Eight Thousand Verse Perf ection of Wisdom Sutra , memorize parts of it, and read 
it. We can also recite, memorize, and read the Heart Sutra . The subject matter of all these 
sutras is emptiness, and we need to understand and meditate on emptiness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3B2 Aspiration for emptiness 
The second one is to engage in the realit y that is selflessness and clear light {120} 
and to be convinced with respect to primordial purity.  
 
W e  c a n  a l s o  p u r i f y  n e g a t i v i t i e s  b y  d e v e l o p i n g  a n  a s p i r a t i o n  f o r  o r  a n  a p p r e c i a t i o n  o f  t h e  
reality that emptiness or selflessness or clear light.  There is a both a clear light that is an object 
and a clear light that is an object-possessor or su bject (that is, a mind). The clear light that is 
an object is the emptiness of all phenomena of  true or inherent existence, that is, the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
390 emptiness of phenomena of existing from  their own side. Since all phenomena are 
dependent-arisings, they are empty of inherent existence. We can meditate on the selflessness 
of persons thinking that the self does not exis t independently, permanently, or without parts, 
or that it is not self-sufficient substantially ex istent, or that it is not truly or inherently 
existent. We can also meditate on the selflessne ss of phenomena in relation to the aggregates. 
We have five aggregates that are the basis on wh ich the person or self is imputed. These five 
are the form aggregate, feeling aggregate, di scrimination aggregate, compositional factors 
aggregate, and consciousnesses aggregate. If we  examine whether or not these five aggregates 
exist from their own side, we will find that the form aggregate does not exist from its own side 
but is a dependent phenomenon. The same is true  of the other four a ggregates. For example, 
the compositional factors aggregate is composed of  forty-nine of the fifty-one mental factors by 
excluding feeling and discrimination which are re spectively included in the feeling aggregate 
and discrimination aggregate. We should examin e each one of the forty-nine mental factors, 
thinking about how they exist and how they functi on. By doing so, we will find that none of 
them exists inherently. Therefore, neither the person, the aggregates, on e’s belongings, and so 
forth exist truly or inherently. Meditating in this  way, we should strive to see the object of 
negation – existence from its own side – and then negate it. Phenomena are empty of true 
existence; this particular absence is what is called “emptiness.” We should focus the mind on 
this emptiness and develop confidence in the fa ct that all phenomena do not exist inherently. 
From the beginning all phenomena are free from inherent existence, th us they never existed 
independently or inherently due to which they are said to be primordially pure. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3B3 Based on recitation 
A The need to make an effort at recitation until signs appear that the negativities have been purified  
The third one is the recitation of special dharanis like the hundred-syllable mantra 
according to [the respective] ritual. In [the Sutra ] Requested by Subahu  it says: 
 
As the flames of forest fires in spring, 
Uncontained, burn all the underbrush, Discipline’s wind fans fires of recitation, Burning negativities with the flames of great joyous effort.  
 
As the sun strikes snow with rays of light Which, overwhelmed, does no t withstand its brilliance, 
When discipline’s sun and rays of recitation strike The snow of negativities, it wastes away.  As bringing out a butter lamp in darkness, 
Dispels the darkness so there’s nothing left, 
The darkness of negativities collected in a thousand lives Is quickly dispelled by the lamp of recitation. 
 Moreover, [you should continue the recitati on] until you see signs that negativities 
have been purified.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
391  
We should purify our negativities by reciting  the hundred-syllable mantra and so forth until 
signs that they have been purified arise. One of  the main ways of purifying negativities is to 
recite the hundred-syllable mantra of Vajrasattva,  for which reason it is  often included at the 
beginning of a long sadhana. We should do this purification practice every day together with 
the development of regret for whatever nega tivities we have committed and the firm 
resolution to not commit them again.  
 The Sutra  Requested by Subahu says that in the spring when it is hot and has not rained, even 
a tiny flame can start a huge fire that destroys  an entire forest. Similarly, the fire of our 
recitation burns all our negativities when acco mpanied by the wind of morality, which makes 
our recitation strong and effective. Likewise, just  as the heat of the sun melts the snow on the 
mountains whereby they lose their magnificence as rocks and soil are revealed, likewise the 
sun of our morality and the rays of our recitation purify our negativities. Also, just as a lamp 
dispels darkness, the recitation of the Vajras attva mantra dispels the many negativities 
accumulated since beginningless time. We should engage in purification until signs of purification arise.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The signs 
In the Dharani of Exhortation  the signs are said to be dreaming in dreams of 
vomiting bad food, drinking and vomiting things like yogurt or milk, seeing the 
sun and moon, traveling in the sky, burnin g fire, withstanding buffaloes and black 
people, seeing a sangha of fully-ordaine d monks and nuns, climbing a tree from 
which milk emerges, an elephant, a bull , a mountain, a lion throne, or a fine 
house, and hearing the Dharma.  
 
Signs of purification include dreaming of vomiti ng bad food, drinking and vomiting yogurt or 
milk, seeing the sun or moon rise, traveling in  the sky, burning fire, subduing a black buffalo 
or a black man, seeing a sangha of monks an d nuns, climbing a tree from which white milk-
like sap arises, climbing on an elephant or a bull, climbing a mountain, a lion-throne, or a 
fine house or palace, and listening to the Dhar ma. When we have purified our negativities, 
such signs occur in dreams. On the other hand, if bad dreams ar ise when purifying, it shows 
that we are not doing the purification well  and that we are making some mistake.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3B4 Based on images 
The fourth one is, having gained confiden ce in the Buddha, to create images of 
him. 
 
Another antidote is, having generated faith in th e Buddha, to make images, such as tsa-tsas, of 
buddhas. For example, we can make images of  Buddha Shakyamuni, the thirty-five buddhas, 
Vajrasattva, as well as any other image of buddh as. Previously the word “tsa-tsa” in Tibetan 
was used to refer only to small, round clay stupas made from a metal mould. However, 
nowadays all small clay images of buddhas are called tsa-tsas.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
392  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3B5 Based on making offerings 
The fifth one is to make various types of  offerings to the Buddha and his stupas. 
 
Another antidote is to make various types of o fferings, such as those usually set out on an 
altar, to the Buddha, to images of the buddhas, and to stupas.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3B6 Based on names 
The sixth one is to listen to and retain  the names of the buddhas and the great 
sons of the conquerors.  
 
These are the ones {121} that ar e explicitly mentioned in the Compendium of 
Instructions , however there are many others.  
 
Another antidote that purifies our negativities is to listen to and retain or memorize the 
names of the buddhas and great bodhisattvas an d then recite them. For example, we could 
listen to or recite the names of the thirty -five buddhas, the mantra of Buddha Shakyamuni 
OM MUNI MUNI MAHA MUNIYE SVAHA, or th e names of the eight close bodhisattvas 
such as Manjushri, Vajrapani, Maitreya, and so forth.  
 While these are the antidotes that are taught in the Compendium of Trainings , there are 
many more. For example, we can recite th e mantra of Kalachak ra (OM AH HUM HOH 
HAM KSHA MALAVARAYA HUM PHAT), the mantra of Vajrapani (OM VAJRAPANI 
HUM), the mantra of Manjushri (OM A RA PA CHA NA DHIH), and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 121) says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3C The power of turning away from faults in the future 
1 The actual [power] and its benefits  
The third one – the power of turning away  from faults in the future – is to 
completely restrain yourself fr om the ten non-virtues. In the Essence of the  Sun 
[Sutra ] it says that thereby the actions of the three doors [committed ] by means of 
killing and so on, as well as the afflicti ve obscurations and obscurations to the 
Dharma,157 whatever you have done, caused others to do, or rejoiced in before, are 
destroyed. 
 
We need to restrain ourselves from committing th e ten non-virtuous actions, that is, we need 
t o  s t o p  e n g a g i n g  i n  t h e m  a g a i n .  T h e r e f o r e ,  w e  s h o u l d  g e n e r a t e  a  s t r o n g  r e s o l u t i o n  i n  o u r  
mind not to do them again. For example, we  should resolve never to do again whatever 
negativities we have committed by means of our three doors – killing, stealing, and sexual 
                                              
157 The somewhat unusual term “ chos kyi sgrib pa ” is unclear. According to Geshe Soepa’s oral commentary it is 
either a synonym for “ shes sgrib ” i.e., “obscuration of objects of knowledge” or “ chos spong gi sgrib pa ”, i.e., 
“obscurations [caused] through abandoning the Dharma”. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
393 misconduct by means of the body, lying, divisive  speech, harsh words, an d idle talk by means 
of the speech, and covetousness, malice, and wr ong view by means the mind. We must also 
resolve not to abandon the Dharma again. In a ddition, we must purify those actions that we 
have not done ourselves but have caused others to do and those in which we have rejoiced by 
resolving not to do this again. Even by rejoicin g in the negative actions of others, although we 
did not actually do the action we too accumulate negative karma. Therefore, if we did that in 
the past, we need to purify it by generating re gret for it and making a strong determination or 
promise not to do it again in the future.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The importance of sincere restraint 
The Extensive Commentary  [on Vinaya ] explains that the inte ntion of the question 
asked in the passage “Is there restraint henc eforth?” is that a confession without a 
sincere mind of restraint is nothing but wo rds. Therefore the mind of restraint of 
not doing it henceforth is important. Its development also depends on the first 
power.  
 If we just recite the names of the thirty-f ive buddhas without regret and so forth, our 
confession becomes mere words and does not puri fy our negativities. This is why we need to 
first develop strong regret for our past negative  actions, then a strong  determination not to 
commit them again, and then to strongly apply an antidote. Only by doing this will we be able 
to purify our negativities. Thus, a strong resolu tion to not commit the ne gative action again is 
very important. Likewise, when monks and nuns do the Restoring and Purifying Rite (so-jong) 
in order to restore their virtues and purify their downfalls and faults, they need to make such 
a determination. As a basis for this resolution we  need the regret that thinks, “It is very bad 
that I did such an action. Therefore, I will not do it again.” Following the development of this 
mind, we should recite mantras such as the hund red-syllable mantra, recite sutras such as the 
Golden Light Sutra , the Perfection of Wisdom Sutras, the Diamond Cutter Sutra , and so forth, 
or meditate on emptiness.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-3D The power of the support 
The fourth one – the power of the support – is to go for refuge and cultivate the 
mind of enlightenment.  
 
In order to purify our negativities, we also n eed to go for refuge to the Three Jewels – the 
Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. To do this proper ly we need to generate the two causes of 
going for refuge: (1) fear of the suffering of cycl ic existence in general and fear of the suffering 
of the three lower realms in particular and (2) the confidence or faith that the Three Jewels 
can protect us from this suffering. Having gene rated these two attitudes, we should recite a 
refuge formula such as “I go for refuge to the Buddha. I go for refuge to the Dharma. I go for 
refuge to the Sangha.” Alternatively we can also recite this or similar formulas in Sanskrit or 
Tibetan.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
394  Then we need to generate the mind of enli ghtenment – the wish to attain enlightenment 
in order to be able to take care of all sentient beings.  
 By means of the four opponent powers, we can purify all our negative karma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-4 How all the antidotes to harmful actions are complete within the four powers  
The Conqueror said that in general there is a diversity of ways for beginners to 
purify negativities, however the thoroughly complete antidote is the four powers in 
their entirety. 
 
The Conqueror said that in general there ar e different ways for beginners to purify 
negativities but that the thoroughly complete an tidote is the four powers in their entirety. 
This is important to note because if one or  more of the four powers is missing, our 
purification will not be complete due to which we will not be able to purify all four results of 
our negative actions. For this reason all fo ur opponent powers need to be complete.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-5 The manner of purifying 
A How the karma is not  categorically definite [in dependence on] whether the antidotes are complete or 
incomplete etc. in the one doing the purification  
The manner in which negativities are purified is as follows. Having purified, 
whether the causes for the arisal of th e great suffering of the bad migrations 
become causes for the arisal of minor suffe ring, or despite being reborn in the bad 
realms their sufferings are not experienced,  or they become a mere headache in 
one’s present form, and likewise whethe r something that would need to be 
experienced for a long time becomes some thing short or does not need to be 
experienced at all, is not categorically defi nite since it is also depends on the power 
of the one doing the purification being grea t or small, as well as the antidote – the 
four powers – being complete or incomple te, intense or not intense, the duration 
being long or short, and so on.  
 
Depending on the power of the on e doing the purification being great or small, as well as the 
antidote – the four powers – being complete or incomplete, intense or not intense, the 
duration being long or short, and so on, the ca uses for taking rebirth in the lower realms and 
experiencing great suffering can become the caus e of minor suffering. Alternatively, they can 
be experienced as a small suffering, for exampl e, as a bad headache, a stomach ache, and so 
forth in this very body.   Likewise, although we have created the cause to experience suffering for a very long time, having purified its cause we might experience su ffering only for a very short time or not have 
to experience it at all. In short, we human beings can apply all four opponent powers very strongly, whereby we can purify our negative karm a. On the other hand, if we do not do apply 
four opponent powers very strongly  or do not apply all of them, the result will not be definite.  
 Tuesday morning, 12 April 2005   
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
395 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B How even karma that is definite [to be experienced] is purified from the root  
As for the statement in sutra and vinaya “A ctions are not lost even in a hundred 
eons,” {122} the intention is  that that is without cultivating the antidote of the 
four powers. However, in the commentary on the Eight Thousand  [Verse Perfection of 
Wisdom Sutra ] it says that through the antidote of the four powers, if purified in 
accordance with the explanations, even karm a that is definite to be experienced is 
purified.  
 
Both sutra and vinaya say that having created acti ons they do not go to waste even in hundred 
eons. However, this is in the specific case that we do not apply the antidote of the four 
opponent powers. In other words, if the four opponent powers are not applied, actions done 
even a hundred eons ago will not go to waste but will eventually bring their result, whereas 
when they are applied, they will not br ing their result. The commentary on the Eight Thousand 
Verse Perfection of Wisdom Sutra  says that if one applies th e four opponent powers in 
accordance with the explanations, even karma that is definite to be experienced will be 
purified. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Even if the one doing the purification does not completely purify karma of little strength, its effect will 
not come forth due to the full ripening having been weakened  
In Blaze of Reasoning  it says that once you have thus weakened the potential to 
bring forth a fully ripened effect by means of confession, [the resolve to exercise] 
restraint, and the like, the fully ripened e ffect will not come about even if other 
conditions are met. Likewise, the destructio n of roots of virtue by wrong views and 
anger is also similar.  
 
Even if by applying the four opponent powers we are unable to completely purify a negative 
karma, still its fully ripened or fruitional result will be weakened. In other words, by applying 
the antidotes but not doing so very strongly, th e potential of an action to produce a fully 
ripened result will be weakened and it will not be able to bring the full result. For example, 
when a barley or rice seed has been damaged due to rotting or being eaten by insects, it will 
not bring its result even though, having been planted in the ground, it meets the necessary 
conditions. Likewise, the roots of virtue that ha ve been destroyed by wrong view or anger will 
not bring their results. This is mentioned by Bhavaviveka in his Blaze of Reasoning .  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1A-2B3B-6 How it is necessary to make an effort from the start not to be tainted by faults 
Although through confession and [the re solve to exercise] restraint you may be 
able to purify [karma] so that nothing at all remains, there is [still] a great 
difference between the purity of not bein g tainted by faults from the beginning 
and the purity after confession. For example, in the Bodhisattva Levels  it says that 
w hen  a ro o t  do w n fall has  o c curre d, it  can  b e res t o red by t akin g t he bo dhis a t t v a 
vow [again], however it will be impossible to reach the first ground in this life. Also 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
396 in the Sutra Gathering All the Threads  it says that once the negativity of abandoning 
the Dharma, which is explained in that su tra, has occurred, if one confesses it 
three times a day over seven years the negativity will be purified, but still it will 
take ten eons even at the quickest to atta in [the level of] tolerance [of the path of 
preparation]. Therefore, purification with out exception means that the production 
of unpleasant effects is purified without ex ception. However, since the arisal of the 
realizations of the path and so forth wi ll be much delayed, you should endeavor 
not to be tainted from the beginning. Also fo r that reason, it says that aryas do not 
knowingly engage even in subtle negativities  or downfalls even for the sake of their 
lives, otherwise, if there was no di fference between purifying them through 
confession and their non-arisal from the beginning, there would be no need to act 
like that. In the world, {123} too, even if  a wounded arm, leg, etc. heals again, it 
still will not be as if from the start it had never been wounded.  
 From the beginning it is best to try not to commit any negativities or downfalls. On the other hand, if we do commit them we can purify them by applying the four opponent powers very strongly, but still it would be better not to have  committed them in the first place rather than 
to commit them and then purify them. These tw o cases are in fact very different. To not 
commit any faults would be the best since, even having purified those that have been created, 
it is not the same as not having  committed them at all. It is li ke, for example, having broken 
an arm or leg, even though the bone is put back  together with metal plates and nails, the arm 
or leg is still not the same as it wa s previous to having been broken.  
 The Bodhisattva Levels  says that we ordinary beings can restore the root downfalls of 
bodhisattva vows by purifying them and retaki ng them, however due to having committed a 
r o o t  d o w n f a l l  w e  w i l l  n o t  b e  a b l e  t o  a c h i eve the first ground in this life. Also the Sutra 
Gathering All the Threads  says that, having abandoned the Dh arma, even if one purifies this 
negativity three times a day over a period of seve n years, at the very quickest it will take ten 
eons to reach the tolerance level of the path of  preparation. Therefore, we must not abandon 
the Dharma by criticizing other religions and so  forth. Although we do not need to follow 
other religions, we must at all cost avoid criticizing them.   “Purification without exception” means that th e action will no longer bring a fully ripened 
or fruitional result, however it will still take longer and be more difficult to generate the 
realizations of the path. Theref ore, from the very start we sh ould try not to commit negative 
actions. Aryas, due to understanding their result , do not engage even in subtle negativities 
and downfalls even at the cost of their life. We , on the other hand, thinking that it does not 
matter, continue to engage in them. However, this  is like, having broken one’s arm or leg, the 
arm or leg does not return to how it was prior to breaking it. Therefore, we need to avoid 
creating downfalls and faults from the very beginning.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 123) says:  
2B4B-2B1B The measure of the attitude [of lesser beings] having been developed  
Second: the measure of those thoughts [of lesser beings] having developed. Previously you 
had an uncontrived interest in this world only, and your interest in future worlds 
was just a [vague] understanding fascinat ed with the words.  Having exchanged 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
397 them, when future worlds have become the main interest and this world is nothing 
but a by-product, [the attitude of lesser beings ] has developed. As this nevertheless 
needs to be made stable, you should strive, that is, train, in it even after it has 
arisen.  
 
The measure of the attitude of a small being ha ving arisen is that one comes to think that 
future lives are more important than this life. Since this life is one whereas future lives are 
many, future lives are more important. At the moment we have an uncontrived aspiration for 
this life, whereas when we think of future live s our aspiration is only mere words and not at 
all deep. Therefore, we need to exchange these two attitudes by coming to think that, between 
this and future lives, our future lives are more important. Then, thinking that this life is not 
important, we need to practice keeping pure morality, generosity, patience, and so forth for 
the sake of future lives. The th ought or attitude striving for fu ture lives is the generation of 
the actual path of special small beings. On the other hand, middling beings are those who, 
understanding that all of cyclic existence is the nature of suffering, generate the wish to 
achieve liberation or nirvana. Then great beings  are those who, seeing that it is not enough 
for themselves to achieve liberation since all sent ient beings are suffering in cyclic existence, 
generate the mind that wishes to take care  of all sentient beings and develop love, 
compassion, and the mind of enlightenment.   In short, small beings are those who mainly enga ge in practice for the sake of future lives. 
When this thought is stab le, they should still cont inue to train in it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1C  Eliminating misconceptions about [the attitude of lesser beings ] 
1 Setting out the assertion 
[2 Its refutation] 
2B4B-2B1C-1 Setting out the assertion 
In the scriptures it says th at it is necessary to turn the mind away from all the 
excellence of cyclic existence, whereby, having become a basis of confusion, some 
[people] think it is inappropriate to deve lop interest in the high status of an 
excellent body, possessions, and so forth beca use they are [part of] cyclic existence.  
 
Here a wrong conception regarding the attitude of lesser beings are set out and then refuted. 
There are those who think that wi th respect to the renunciation  of cyclic existence it is 
mistaken to wish for a high rebirth in cyclic existence with a good body, possessions, and so 
forth. However, this is not right. The thought to attain an excellent body, excellent wealth, 
and so forth in the next life is good since they are necessary in order to be able to practice the Dharma in future lives. In other words, we also need a healthy body and other favorable 
conditions in our future lives as this will make  it easier for us to practice the Dharma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B1C-2 Its refutation 
A How a continuous succession of excellent bodies is necessary 
With respect to that which is to be st riven for, there are both temporary and 
ultimate goals to strive for. Even th ose who strive for liberation from the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
398 excellences of a body and so forth of cyclic  existence need to temporarily strive for 
them since definite goodness is to be  gained in the end from a continuous 
succession of such supports.  
 
There are both temporary and final objects to aspire and strive for. The temporary aim is an 
excellent body of cyclic existence because through relying on such a body we can achieve nirvana and so forth. On the other hand, wi thout obtaining such a body, it will not be 
possible to achieve nirvana. Therefore, initially we need to aspire for a high rebirth as by 
obtaining this we will be able in the end to achieve the definite goodness of nirvana. In other words, temporarily we need to achieve a precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities in order to continue our Dharma practice, whereby we will finally attain the definite 
goodness of liberation.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Refutation looking at ultimately high status  
Not all that is the high status of an excellent body, possessions, and retinue are 
included in cyclic existence, for the culm ination of an excellent body, etc. is the 
form body [of a buddha], the wealth of its field, and its retinue. It is with this 
intention that the Ornament of the Sutras  says that the first four perfections 
accomplish the high status of an ex cellent body, possessions, retinue, and 
undertakings, {124} and that many [other] sc riptures state that they accomplish the 
form body. 
 
Not all that exists in high status, an excellen t body, resources, and retinue, is included in 
cyclic existence because the culmination of an excellent body and so forth is the form body, 
the wealth of its field, for example, that of Akanishta, and its retinue. For this reason the 
Ornament of the Sutras  by Maitreya says that the first fo ur perfections, generosity, morality, 
patience, and effort, are for the purpose of achi eving an excellent body and so forth, whereas 
other texts say that they are the cause of a buddha’s form body.  
 Middle Lam-Rim says:   
This concludes the explanation of mental  training in the stages of the path 
common to lesser beings. 
 This concludes the explanation of the training of the mind in the stages of the path common 
to small beings.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 124) says:  
PART III: INTERMEDIATE SCOPE 
Explanation of the stages of the path common to intermediate beings 
2B4B-2B2 Training the mind in the stages of the path common to intermediate beings 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
399 A Transition 
[B The actual training] 
2B4B-2B2A Transition 
Thus, having become mindful of death and reflected on the way in which you will 
fall into the bad migrations after death, you have turned your mind away from this 
world and developed a striving for th e happy migrations. Then, through the 
common going for refuge and certainty rega rding positive and negative karma and 
its effects, you will give up negativities, and by putting effort into accomplishing 
virtue you will indeed attain the state of  the happy migrations. However, that is 
not held to be sufficient here. Rather, once you have developed the attitude 
common to lesser beings and have develope d the attitude common to intermediate 
beings, which counteracts the adherence to cyclic existence as a whole, in 
dependence on them you will develop the mind of enlightenment and be led into 
the [path of] great beings. The purpose of tr aining in the attitude of intermediate 
beings is that, even though you attain th e state of a god or human being in this 
manner, you will not have passed beyond compounding suffering. Therefore, 
holding to that as happy by nature is mist aken, since in reality they are devoid of 
happiness and in the end they are bad beca use you are certain to fall into the bad 
migrations again. 
 At all times we need to remember that we will die. In other words, we need to constantly think “I am going to die, but I do not know when.” All the sentient beings who have taken 
rebirth will definitely die, however the time of their death is uncertain. Also, at the time of 
death, our relatives, wealth, and body will not be  of any help to us, only the Dharma will be 
of help. Having died, we will probably fall into  the bad migrations. For these reasons, we need 
to give up thoughts of this li fe and generate the th ought wishing to attain a happy rebirth in 
our next life, for example, a precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities, in 
order to be able to continue our Dharma practice . To obtain this, we need to go for refuge to 
the Three Jewels and to understand that white actions and black actions respectively bring a 
good or happy result and a bad or suffering result. Thinking about this, we should then put effort into abandoning non-virtuous actions and accumulating virtuous actions so that in our 
next life we can obtain a high rebirth.  
 H o w e v e r ,  i s  i t  s u f f i c i e n t  t o  a c h i e v e  a  h i g h  r e b i r t h ?  I t  i s  n o t .  T h e r e f o r e ,  w e  a l s o  n e e d  t o  
generate the path common to middling beings thro ugh thinking about the fact that if we take 
rebirth again in cyclic existence we will once again have to experience many problems. From 
the depths of our heart we need to understand th at any rebirth at all in cyclic existence is bad 
and, based on this understanding, gene rate the wish to attain nirvana.  
 Having generated the thought of intermediate beings, we then need to enter the path of 
great beings and develop the mind of enlightenm ent. However, in order to do this, we first 
need to train in the path of middling beings by contemplating the fact  that even if we are 
reborn as a god or human being, still it is the nature of suffering. To grasp at or adhere to the happiness of cyclic existence as being true is inappropriate because it is not real happiness. 
This is because, even having achieved a human rebirth, sometimes we are happy and 
sometimes not. We experience the suffering of change which is that, while initially something 
appears to be happiness and pleasure, by contin uing to enjoy it, it gradually transforms into 
suffering. Thus, even a high rebi rth as a human being, a god of the desire realm, a god of the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
400 form realm, or a god of the formless realm is not real happiness because, having taken such a 
rebirth, eventually we will fall once again in to the lower realms. Therefore, we need to 
generate the wish to strive for nirvana.  
 
(Oral transmission 12:05-12:25)  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 124) says:  
2B4B-2B2B The actual [training] 
1 The actual training in that attitude 
2 The measure of its development 
3 The elimination of misconceptions about it 
4 Determining the nature of the path leading to liberation 
Therefore, the training of the mind in the stages of the path common to 
intermediate beings has four points: the actual training in that attitude, the 
measure of its development, the elimin ation of misconceptions about it, and 
determining the nature of the path leading to liberation.  
Explanation of how to develop the mind striving for liberation  
2B4B-2B2B-1 The actual training in that attitude 
A Identification of the mind striving for liberation 
B The methods for generating [the mind striving for liberation ] 
The first one has two points: identification of the mind striving for liberation and 
the methods for generating it.  
 
In order to train in the mind striving for cycl ic existence, we first need to understand and 
identify the state of liberation whereby we wi ll generate the aspiration to achieve it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1A Identification of the mind striving for liberation 
1 Identification of liberation 
First: “Liberation” is freedom from bond age. Specifically, there are two things 
binding one in cyclic existence, karma an d the afflictions. Through the power of 
these two, from the point of view of real ms, there are three realms, those of desire 
etc.; when divided by way of migrat ing beings, there are five or six,158 such as gods, 
etc.; and in terms of birth-sources, ther e are four, such as a womb. This joining 
with such aggregates is the entity of bondage, therefore freedom from them is 
liberation. 
 
We cannot achieve liberation presently because we are bound in cyclic existence by contaminated actions and afflictions. Therefore, in order to achieve liberation or nirvana, the 
state free from cyclic existence, we need to be come free from these two bonds. To do this, we 
first need to understand the four noble truths . The first one is true sufferings. Rebirth in 
cyclic existence is itself suffering. Sometimes we experience the suffering of suffering and 
                                             
 
158 depending on whether gods and demigods are listed separately or grouped together 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
401 sometimes the suffering of change, whereas at all times and in all places in cyclic existence we 
experience pervasive compounding suffering. Thus, we need to recognize suffering as 
suffering. Then we can ask “Where does suffering come from?” The answer is that it comes 
from true origins, that is, suffering comes fr om contaminated actions, which in turn come 
from the afflictions. In short, the origins or causes of suffering are actions and afflictions. Can 
suffering cease? It can, but for this to happen we need to achieve true cessations. How can we 
achieve them? We can do so by meditating true paths.  
 
Tuesday afternoon, 12 April 2005  Liberation is freedom from actions and affliction s, which are what presently bind us in cyclic 
existence and cause us to be unable to beco me free from it. On the other hand, when we 
become free from contaminated actions and afflicti ons, we will attain the state of liberation.  
 Cyclic existence or samsara as a place is divi ded into three realms in which we take rebirth 
by the power of actions and afflictions. The th ree realms are the desire realm, form realm, 
and formless realm. All sentient beings in cyclic  existence are born in these three realms. In 
terms of migrating beings, there are five kinds: gods or devas, human beings, animals, hungry 
ghosts, and hell beings. In this case the anti-g ods or asuras are included in the god realm. 
Alternatively there are six kinds of migrating be ings: the animals, hungry ghosts, and animals 
of the three suffering migrations and the huma n beings, anti-gods, and gods of the three 
happy migrations. In terms of the abode of birt h or birth-source there are four: birth from 
heat and moisture, birth from an egg, bi rth from a womb, and miraculous birth.  
 This joining with the aggregates of the next life due to actions and afflictions is called 
“bondage.” Similarly, cyclic ex istence is the joining with the aggregates of the next life. 
Freedom from this is called “liberation.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
2 The mind striving for [liberation ] 
The desire to attain that [liberation]  is the mind striving for liberation. 
 
The desire or wish to attain liberation  is the mind striving for liberation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B The methods for generating [the mind striving for liberation ] 
1 Transition 
2 The actual methods 
2B4B-2B2B-1B1 Transition 
Second: Just as the development of the de sire to obtain the pacification of the 
suffering of thirst, for instance, depends on seeing the torment due to thirst as 
undesirable, likewise the development of the desire to attain the liberation that is 
the thorough pacification of the suffering  of the appropriated aggregates, also 
depends on seeing the shortcomings of th e appropriated aggregates of having the 
nature of suffering. Therefore, if, havi ng meditated on the shortcomings of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
402 existence, the mind that desires to reject them does not arise, the desire to attain 
the pacification of suffering will not arise either, because in the Four Hundred Verses  
it says:  
 
One who is not disenchanted here, 
How could he have respect for peace? 
 
Only someone who is suffering from thirst wish es to eliminate or pacify this suffering by 
finding something to drink, such as water, juic e, or any other potable liquid. Similarly, in 
order to generate the wish to pacify the suff ering of the appropriate aggregates and achieve 
liberation, it is necessary to first understand th at the aggregates are the nature of suffering and 
to see their shortcomings, since on ly after that will the desire to  discard them arise. In other 
words, by seeing the faults of the appropriated aggregates by doing analytical meditation on 
them, we will generate the wish to give up such a body or aggregates. If we do not generate 
this mind, the mind wishing to achieve the pacification of suffering will not arise.  
 Likewise, Aryadeva’s Four Hundred Verses  says that if we do not generate disenchantment 
with cyclic existence, we will not generate respect for peace. In  other words, we first need to 
see the faults of something in order to generate the wish to abandon or give it up. Only if we 
see something as a problem will we want to give it up. On the other hand, if we do not think 
about its faults, we will not generate the mind wishing to give it up.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2 The actual methods 
A Thinking about the shortcomings of cyclic existence – true sufferings 
B Thinking about the stages of entering cyclic existence – origins 
This being so there are two points with  respect to that: thinking about the 
shortcomings of cyclic  existence – true sufferings; an d thinking about the stages of 
involvement in cyclic existence – the origins. 
 
We first need to understand true sufferings – the shortcomings of cyclic existence, that is, we 
need to understand the nature of suffering. Th en we need to understand how we enter or 
engage in cyclic existence, this being by means of true origins.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A Thinking about the shortcomi ngs of cyclic existence – true sufferings 
1 Indicating the intention of stating true sufferings as the first of the four truths  
2 The actual meditation on suffering 
The first one has two points: indicating the intention of stating true sufferings as 
the first of the four truths, and th e actual meditation on suffering. 
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-1 Indicating the intention of stating true sufferings as the first of the four truths  
A Setting out a doubt 
B Reply 
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-1A Setting out a doubt 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
403 First: If you ask: “If origins are earlier and sufferings later since origins are causes 
and {126} true sufferings are their effect , why is it that the Bhagavan taught: 
‘Monks, this is suffering, truth for an arya. This is the origin, truth for an arya’?”  
 
Among the four truths of arya beings, the Buddha first taught true sufferings. However, there 
can be a doubt as to why he first taught true su fferings and, only after that, true origins since, 
in terms of the order of their arisal, first there arises true origins and then true sufferings. In 
other words, while first there arises the cause (origin) and then the result (suffering), the 
Buddha first taught the result and then the cause,  saying “Monks, this is sufferings, truth for 
an arya. This is origin, truth for an arya.” Th erefore, why did he do this? The reply is as 
follows.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-1B  Reply 
1 Summarized indication in reply 
2 Extensive explanation 
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-1B1 Summarized indication in reply 
There is no fault in that since the fact th at the Teacher stated cause and effect in 
reverse order has great significance for practice. 
 
The Buddha taught the result first because, ha ving understood the result, we will seek its 
cause. For example, if we want  to achieve success in business in the future, we first think of 
the result, success, and then enga ge in the activities, the causes , necessary for the achievement 
of this result. Likewise, in order to reap the resu lt of crops, we first need to plant the seeds. 
But only through understanding the result, crops,  do we put effort into creating its cause, 
planting seeds. Therefore, althou gh in our daily life we experience many sufferings, we first 
need to recognize them as suffering. Only then  will we ask ourselves “From where do these 
sufferings come?” and look for their cause – true  origins. For this reason, the Buddha taught 
true sufferings and then true origins, as well as  true cessations and then true paths. In other 
words, the Buddha first taught th e result and then the cause, al though the cause arises before 
the result, for the purpose of practice. Therefore,  there is no fault in the Buddha having first 
taught true sufferings and, only after that, true origins.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-1B2 Extensive explanation 
A The need to teach true sufferings, origins, cessations, and paths one after the other in accordance 
with the scriptures 
If you go on to ask: “How so?” It is like this: If the unerring wish for liberation 
from cyclic existence is not initially gene rated in the disciple himself, how should 
he be guided to liberation while lacking its root? In fact, initially disciples are 
shrouded by the darkness of ignorance and deceived by the misconception that apprehends the suffering excellences of cyclic existence as happiness.  
 There is a purpose to teaching the four noble trut hs – first true sufferings, then true origins, 
then true cessations, and then true paths – in  this order in the scriptures. It is because 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
404 without generating the wish to become free from  cyclic existence and attain liberation, we 
cannot be guided to liberation. Therefore, init ially disciples need to understand how cyclic 
existence is the nature of suffering. However,  due to being obscured by the darkness of 
ignorance, we are deceived by our misconceptio n that thinks that the excellences of cyclic 
existence are happiness.  
 Middle Lam-Rim says:  
 
In the Four Hundred  [Verses ] it says:  
 
If the ocean of this suffering 
Has no limits in any respect, Child, you who are sinking in it, Why are you not gripped with fear? 
 Thus, many aspects of suffering having been taught saying “This, in reality, is not 
happiness but rather suffering,” it is nece ssary to develop disenchantment, due to 
which true suffering were stated first. Th en, having seen that you yourself have 
fallen into the ocean of suffering, if you want to be liberated from it, you see the 
need to ward off suffering. Having come to  understand that if you do not ward off 
its cause you will not avert it, thinking  “What is its cause?” you come to 
understand true origins. That is why true  origins were stated next. Then, once an 
understanding of true origins has arisen – that the suffering of cyclic existence is 
produced by contaminated actions, that  those actions are produced by mental 
afflictions, and that the root of [afflictions ] is the apprehension of a self – {127} 
and you see that the apprehension of a se lf can be opposed, you vow to actualize 
the cessation that puts a stop to suffering . Therefore, true cessations were taught 
next. 
 
Aryadeva’s Four Hundred Verses  sets out the question: “Since the suffering of cyclic existence is 
like an ocean in that it is has no limits, why do we who are sinking in this ocean not generate 
fear?” In fact, we should generate fear of cyclic  existence. While cyclic existence appears to be 
pleasant and many things, such as our body, appe ar to be happiness, in reality they are all the 
nature of suffering. Because we first need to un derstand the nature of suffering, that is, true 
sufferings, the Buddha taught them first. Th rough understand suffering, we will generate 
disenchantment or sadness with respect to it. Understanding that we have fallen into the 
ocean of cyclic existence, we  will generate the wish to become free from this suffering. 
However, since without eliminating its cause we  will never become free from suffering, we 
then pose the question: “What is the cause of these many sufferings?” We will then come to 
see that their cause is true origins. For this reason, the Buddha taught the true origins of 
suffering next. Through understanding that the su ffering of cyclic existence is produced by 
contaminated actions, that contaminated action s are produced by afflictions, and that the 
root of the afflictions is the conception of a self, we will understand the true origins of 
suffering. Then, through seeing that the concep tion of a self can be eliminated, we will 
generate the wish to actualize the cessation of sufferings – true cessations. The way to actualize 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
405 true cessations is to meditate on the antidote to  the conception of a self – the realization of 
selflessness or emptiness. This is  the actual antidote that elim inates the afflictions and their 
cause, actions, whereby the result, suffering, is ceased. For this reason, the Buddha first taught 
true sufferings, then true origins, then true cessations, and then true paths.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim says:  
B Giving up objections to presenting [the truths] in this order 
You may wonder: “Well then, as the wish for liberation arises when true sufferings 
are presented, it would make sense to present true cessations after true sufferings.” [However,] there is no fault, for, even though, at that point, the wish for liberation 
thinking “If only I achieved the cessation that pacifies suffering” is present, since 
you have not identified the cause of sufferi ng and do not see that the cause can be 
averted, you do not apprehend liberation as  something to be attained thinking “I 
should actualize cessation.” In that manner, once you apprehend liberation, 
thinking “I should actualize cessation,” you will engage in true paths, thinking 
“What is the path leading there?” That is why true paths were stated at the end.  
 Someone might think that as the wish for li beration arises from understanding true 
sufferings, true cessations should be taught after true sufferings. However, there is no fault in 
the Buddha not having done that. This is becaus e without recognizing the causes of suffering, 
one will not apprehend the pacification of su ffering as something that can be achieved. 
Therefore, first it is necessary to understand th e cause of sufferings, true origins, that then 
need to be eliminated in order to achieve true  cessations. Therefore, we must first recognize 
true sufferings and then their causes, true origin s. Then, through seeing that true origins can 
be eliminated, we will come to understand that  we can achieve liberation. Otherwise, without 
understanding true origins, we will not unders tand the possibility of achieving liberation. 
Then, through thinking “I will actualize true ce ssations,” we will come to meditate true paths, 
the cause of true cessations. Since in order to ac hieve true cessations, we must first create its 
cause – meditation on true paths, the Buddha taug ht true paths as the last of the four noble 
truths.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Establishing by means of logic that such an order is necessary 
The four truths are taught many times in this manner throughout the Mahayana 
and Hinayana. Since the Sugata condensed in  them the crucial points of entry into 
cyclic existence and reversal from it, th ey are extremely important for achieving 
complete liberation. Since it is the encapsulation159 of practice, students need to be 
guided in this order. If th e adherence to cyclic existenc e is not reversed and put to 
rest at all by way of reflecting on true su fferings, the wish to achieve liberation will 
become mere words, since whatever you do will become an origin [of new 
                                              
159 Geshe Thubten Ngawang’s explanation of the phrase “ ’gag bsdoms ” (lit. “stop – bind”): imagine a central 
station where all the various tracks come together and where tracks in certain directions can be blocked or 
joined. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
406 suffering]. If you do not come to a good  understanding of the root of cyclic 
existence – karma and the afflictions – by reflecting on origins, you will be like 
someone shooting an arrow who does not see the target, for you will miss the 
crucial point of the path and will hold wh at it is not a path to freedom from 
existence to be that, whereupon your hardship s will be fruitless. And, if you do not 
know what is to be abandoned, sufferings and origins, you will not even identify 
the liberation that is their thorough pacification, whereby your striving for 
liberation {128} will also end up as nothing but an [arrogant] presumption. 
 
The four noble truths are taught many time s in both the Hinayana and Mahayana. Why is 
this? It is because if we wish to achieve the state of complete liberation, we first need to 
understand how we engage in cyclic existence an d how we reverse from it. This is the essential 
point or the condensed meaning of the four noble truths for leading disciples to liberation. If, 
on the other hand, our adherence to cyclic exis tence is not reversed by thinking about the 
nature of suffering, then our wish for liberation  will become mere words and whatever we do 
will become a true origin of suffering. Thus, if we do not understand that the root of cyclic 
existence is actions and afflictions (true origins) , we will be like someone who shoots an arrow 
at a target without seeing the target. For this reason, we need to clearly understand that the 
root of cyclic existence is contaminated actions and afflictions. The root of these in turn is 
ignorance – the apprehension of all phenomen a as existing truly or inherently or the 
a p p r e h e n s i o n  o f  a n  i n h e r e n t l y  e x i s t e n t  s e l f  o f  persons. Hence, in order to cut the root of 
cyclic existence, we need to first identify its ro ot. In other words, we need to understand that 
the object of apprehension of ignorance does  not exist and then we need to develop its 
antidote, the realization of emptiness or selflessne s s ,  a s  t h i s  i s  w h a t  c u t s  t h e  r o o t  o f  c y c l i c  
existence. In short, the r oot of actions is the afflictions, the root of the afflictions is ignorance, 
and ignorance is the root of cyclic existence. It can be cut, but without cutting it we cannot 
attain liberation from cyclic existence.   If we mistake what is not a path to liberation  to be a path to liberation, all our effort and 
hardships to meditate will be useless and will no t bring any result. In other words, if we do 
not understand what is the object of abandonmen t – true sufferings and true origins, we will 
not be able to recognize the liberation that is th eir pacification and our striving for it will only 
be an arrogant presumption. Thus, we need to un derstand that the root of cyclic existence is 
ignorance, and how this ignora nce apprehends persons and phen omena to be inherently or 
truly existent, that is, how it apprehends a se lf of persons and a self of phenomena. This 
apprehension is the very root of  cyclic existence. However, the object of this apprehension is 
non-existent. Therefore, by eliminating this  apprehension we can achieve liberation.  
 Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2 The actual meditation on suffering  
A Considering the suffering of cyclic existence in general 
B Considering the suffering of individual [realms]  
The second one has two points: considerin g the suffering of cyclic existence in 
general and considering the suffering of individual [realms].

407 First we need to think about the suffering of cy clic existence in general and then the specific
