Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A Considering the suffering of cyclic existence in general  
1 Considering the eight [types of] suffering 
2 Considering the six [types of] suffering 
The first one has two points: considerin g the eight [types of] suffering and 
considering the six [types of] suffering. 
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1 Considering the eight [types of] suffering 
A How to meditate by the level of the faculty 
[B How it is necessary to be free from laxity and excitement  C How all the qualities of the three vehicles are accomplished through such meditation 
D The eight types of suffering 
E Advice to cherish [this] manner of meditating on the essence ] 
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1A How to meditate by the level of the faculty 
First: With regard to sustaining the ro und of meditation common to intermediate 
beings, here you should also take up th e common points explained in the context 
of lesser beings. With regard to sustaining  those that are not common [to both], if 
you have the mental strength, you shou ld sustain them [in meditation] as 
explained [here], whereas if your mental st rength is small, you should discard the 
scriptural citations and sustain only the en tity of the meaning of whatever falls in 
this context.  
 
With respect to the meditation s that are common to middling be ings, those of sharp faculty 
should also think about the sufferings that are mentioned in the context of the path of small 
beings. On the other hand, those of weak facult y should set aside the scriptural quotations 
and meditate only on the entity, that is, on th e various types of suffering. For example, in the 
context of the eight types of suffering, those of weak faculty should concentrate on trying to 
recognize and meditate on each one of these sufferings without taking into consideration 
quotations from the sutras, Shantideva, Aryadeva, and so forth, but just try to understand the 
main points.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1B How it is necessary to be free from laxity and excitement 
Although these are analytical meditations,  focusing the mind on the object of 
meditation without allowing it to go to  virtuous, non-virtuous, or unspecified 
objects other than it, you should cease exci tement and so forth. Not allowing [the 
mind] to fall under the influence of sleepy drowsiness or laxity, you should 
meditate continuously from a state of extr eme clarity and lucidity, for it was taught 
that the results of all spiritual practice in which the mind is distracted to other 
things are small.  
 
In order to meditate, we need to eliminate the two mental factors of laxity and excitement in 
order to achieve a single-pointed mind. When la xity arises, the object gradually becomes more 
and more unclear until actual sleepiness occurs . When excitement arises, the mind is no 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
408 longer focused on the object but goes to anothe r object, such as a friend with whom we enjoy 
spending time and so forth. These two, laxi ty and excitement, are the main obstacles to 
meditation.  
 In the context of the stages of the path, we  generally do analytical meditation and not 
stabilizing meditation. However, we still need to keep the mind on the ob ject of the analytical 
meditation and not allow it to go to other vi rtuous, non-virtuous, and neutral or unspecified 
objects. Instead we need to focus on the object  and eliminate excitement. We should also not 
allow the mind to come under the power of sleepy drowsiness and laxity, whereby we will 
have a clear and strong mind.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
  
In Engaging in  [Bodhisattva ] Conduct  it says:  
 
Recitation, austerities, and things, 
Even though long engaged in, If done with one’s mind distracted elsewhere Are meaningless, the seer taught. 
 When we recite mantras, sadhanas, or prayers, we need to keep our mind on what we are 
doing and not allow ourselves to be distracted . Also whatever practices or austerities we 
engage in, we should do so without allowing our mind to be distracted to other things. This is 
because if we recite something but our mind is distracted even to other sadhanas or mantras, 
there will not be much result, that is, it will become meaningless. Therefore, whenever we do 
anything, we need to so while applying intros pection. If, on the other hand, we allow our 
m in d t o  go t o  an o t her o bject  w he n  w e are doing prostrations, prayers, circumambulations, 
mantras, pujas, and so forth, since the action of body or speech is virtuous there will be some 
good result but it will not be very mu ch due to the mind being distracted.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And in the Sutra on the Development of Confidence in the Mahayana  it says: 
 
Son of the lineage, from this enumer ation it should be understood that, 
in this manner, confidence in the Mahayana of the bodhisattvas and 
anything that arises from the Maha yana, comes from having correctly 
thought about the meaning and the Dhar ma with a mind that is utterly 
undistracted. 
 
In this regard {129} “a mind  that is utterly undistracted ” is a mind that does not 
stray away from a virtuous observed obje ct to something else; “the meaning and 
the Dharma” refers to the meaning and the words; and “correctly thought” is to 
analyze and think with an awareness of in dividual investigation. That shows that 
for all accomplishment of the Dharma of g ood qualities both [a lack of distraction 
and investigation] are necessary. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
409  
The Sutra  on Cultivating Faith in the Mahayana  says that whatever practice we do, we should try 
to not allow our mind to be distracted an d to think about the meaning and the Dharma 
correctly. If we do this, our practice will be ve ry good and perfect. If, on the other hand, we 
read Mahayana texts such as the Heart Sutra  while our mind goes to other things, even though 
there will be some result it will not be perfect.  
 In short, we need a mind that is not distract ed to other virtuous objects. In the context of 
“the meaning and the Dharma,” “the Dharma” re fers to words, that is, to the scriptures. 
“Correct thought” refers to individual inve stigation. For example, the words from the Heart 
Sutra  “Form is empty” are words that are called “t he Dharma.” Their mean ing is that the base 
–  f o r m  –  i s  e m p t y  o f  i n h e r e n t  e x i s t e n c e .  W e  n e e d  t o  u n d e r s t a n d  t h i s  c l e a r l y  b y  d o i n g  analytical meditation that investigates, for exampl e, what is form, how does it abide, what is 
its nature, what does it come from, what is the form aggregate, and so forth. Thus, if we wish 
to achieve the Dharma of good qualities we need to think about the two – the meaning and 
the Dharma, that is, we need to understand the meaning and the words. For example, when 
we say the word “empty,” what kind of emptine ss does it refer to? It mainly refers to the 
emptiness of true existence; it does not refer to an empty cup, empty house, and so forth. 
Hence, we need understand the meaning.   In this context, we are specifically discussi ng true sufferings. For example, the word “true 
suffering” is the Dharma, the meaning is the nature of suffering. How do we suffer? There are many types of suffering, the eight and so forth,  which will be explained below. The eight types 
of suffering are: the suffering of birth, suffering  of aging, suffering of sickness, suffering of 
death, suffering of not finding the desirable, suffering of meeting the undesirable, and suffering of losing the desirable.   Wednesday morning, 13 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 129) says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1C How all the positive qualities of the three vehicles are accomplished through such 
meditation 
This being so, it was taught that for the accomplishment of all the positive 
qualities of the three vehicles, two [things] are necessary:  1. actual calm abiding – a single-pointed mind that abides on a virtuous observed 
object without being distracted to anythi ng else – or a facsimile of that, and  
2. actual special insight, which individually  analyzes a virtuous observed object 
and differentiates modes and varieties, or a facsimile of that.  
 
All the good qualities of the three vehicles ar e achieved by such meditation, that is, by 
analytical meditation. Therefore, we need to  put effort into keeping our mind single 
pointedly on one virtuous object. Since it is no t good for our mind to become distracted to 
other objects, when this happens we should try to bring the mind back to the virtuous object 
of meditation, not allowing it to be distracted but trying single-pointedly to do calm abiding 
or a facsimile of calm abiding. In order to ac hieve actual calm abiding it is necessary to 
traverse nine states of mental abiding. We sh ould try to set the mind on an object and to 
focus single-pointedly on it. The virtuous obse rved object of analytical meditation can be 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
410 either modes (thusness) or varieties, that is, ul timate truth or conventional truth. We should 
differentiate between them and try to develop ac tual special insight. If we are unable to 
develop actual special insight, we should try to develop at least a facsimile of it. It is important 
to develop the two, calm abiding and special insight, however if we cannot develop actual 
calm abiding and actual special insight we should  try to develop at least something similar to 
them. Calm abiding is achieved when, by focusing  single-pointedly on an object, we achieve a 
pliancy of body and mind that induces bliss, and special insight is achieved when, by doing 
analytical meditation, we achieve a pliancy of body and mind that induces bliss. These are 
actual calm abiding and special insight, but we sh ould try to at least develop a state similar to 
them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In accordance with that it also says in the [ Sutra ] Unraveling the Intention :  
 
Maitreya, you should understand that  all virtuous qualities, whether 
mundane or supramundane, of hearers,  bodhisattvas, or tathagatas are 
the result of calm abiding and special insight. 
 
If calm abiding and special insight were not divided in the two – the actual and a facsimile to it, there would be no certaint y that all the positive qualities of the 
three vehicles come forth as effects of actual calm abiding and special insight. 
 All the virtuous qualities, whether mundane or  supramundane, of hearers, bodhisattvas, and 
tathagatas arise from or are the result of actu al calm abiding and special insight. Therefore, 
these two are important and we need to meditate in order to achieve them.   Calm abiding and special insight are divided into actual calm abiding and actual special insight and a facsimile of calm abiding and a facsimile of special in sight. While all good 
qualities are not the direct result of calm abid ing and special insight, they are their indirect 
results. Therefore, while it cannot be said that all the good qualities, the mundane and 
supramundane, of hearers, bodhisattvas, and tathag atas are a direct result of these two, they 
are their results.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D The eight types of suffering 
1 The suffering of birth 
2 The suffering of aging 
3 The suffering of illness 
4 The suffering of death 
5 The suffering of meeting with the unpleasant 
6 The suffering of separation from the pleasant 
7 The suffering of seeking what you desire and not getting it 
8 “In brief: the five appropriated aggregates are suffering” 
 
These are the eight sufferings that are often mentioned: 1 the suffering of birth, 
2 the suffering of aging, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
411 3 the suffering of illness, 
4 the suffering of death, 5 the suffering of meeting with the unpleasant, 
6 the suffering of separation from the pleasant, 
7 the suffering of seeking what one desires and not getting it, and 
8 the suffering of the five appropriated aggregates.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-1 The suffering of birth 
Here the considerations on the suffering of birth, the first of eight types of 
suffering, have five points: 
A [Suffering] 
Birth is suffering because it is associat ed with suffering. When the four – hell 
sentient beings, perpetually suffering hungry ghosts, beings born from wombs, and 
beings born from eggs – are born, they are born together with a lot of violent 
suffering feeling.  
 
There are five types of suffering associated with the suffering of birth. For as long as we take 
birth, there will be suffering. For example, havi ng taken birth in the hell realm, there is 
continuous suffering and never any happiness. Al so, having taken birth in the hungry ghost 
realm, there is perpetual suffering. Whether we are born from a womb or born from an egg, 
there is suffering. It is not easy to take birth, for example, when a mother gives birth to a child 
she experiences great pain, may need to be cut op en, and so forth. The baby is also squeezed, 
its bones crushed, as it leaves the womb. Although we no longer remember it, we have experienced this pain. Many animals are born from the womb while others are born from 
eggs.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B [Connection with taking on bad states] 
Birth is associated with taking on bad stat es, therefore, due to connecting with the 
seed for the arisal, abidance, and increase of  mental afflictions, {130} it is unsuited 
for actions that instigate virtue or unsuited to being controlled at will.  
 
Birth has many problems, for example, since it  connects one to the seeds for the generation, 
abiding, and increase of mental afflictions, it is not suitable for engaging in virtue.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C [The basis of suffering] 
Birth becomes the basis of suffering, therefore, in dependence on having taken 
birth in the three realms, the suffering of aging, illness, death, etc. increases.  
 
Birth is the source or basis of suffering because, having been born in any of the three realms – 
desire realm, form realm, and formless realm, th ere will be the experience of the suffering of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
412 a g i n g ,  i l l n e s s ,  d e a t h ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  I n  s h o r t ,  h a v i n g  b e e n  b o r n ,  o n e  w i l l  e x p e r i e n c e  t h e s e  
sufferings. In other words, all these sufferings arise due to having been born, whereas if we 
had not been born we would not have to expe rience the sufferings of aging, sickness, or 
death. In the formless realm there is aging, no t in the sense of the body becoming wrinkled, 
losing its hair and teeth, and so forth, but in  the sense of the lifespan diminishing, due to 
which one will eventually die.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D [The basis of mental afflictions] 
Birth becomes the basis of the mental affl ictions, therefore, once you have been 
born in cyclic existence, the three pois ons of attachment, hatred, and ignorance 
arise with regard to objects. Also due to that, body and mind being thoroughly 
unpacified, they suffer and consequently do not abide in happiness. Thus, body 
and mind are tormented in various ways by the mental afflictions. 
 
Birth is the source or basis for the arisal of th e mental afflictions. This is because, having 
taken birth in cyclic existence,  the three mental poisons – a ttachment, hatred, and ignorance 
– will arise, as well as other afflictions such as  jealousy, miserliness, pride, doubt, afflicted 
view, and so forth. Many afflictions arise in us due to having been born in cyclic existence. 
The afflictions make our body and mind unpaci fied and rough, due to which we experience 
suffering. Physically we experience the suffering s of pain, aging, sickness, and so forth, and 
mentally we experience the suffering of not ob taining an object of attachment, and, having 
found it, the suffering of the fear  of losing it, and so forth. In  this way the body and mind, the 
basis of suffering, are tormented in a variety of  ways by the mental afflictions. Sometimes the 
body suffers, sometimes the mind, sometimes bo th. In this way we constantly experience 
suffering. Since the mental afflictions produce suffering in different ways, there are many 
types of suffering.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
E [Involuntary separation] 
Birth is suffering due to the reality of in voluntary separation, in that death, the 
end of everything born, has not been transc ended and is undesirable. It also only 
makes you experience suffering.  
 
You should think about these [points] again and again. 
 Having been born, even though we do not wish to separate from our body, this will naturally 
happen due to which we will experience suffering . Having taken this body, we think of it as 
excellent and do not want to separate from it . However, even though we do not want to 
separate from it and want to keep it, we will defi nitely have to separate from it at the time of 
death. Death is the separation of the body and mind, that is, the cutting off or interruption of 
their relationship. At this time our life comes to an end. This will happen to everyone. We 
need to think about this and to examine how a ll the beings of the past, whether holy beings, 
rich people, kings, or powerful leaders, have died. All that remains now is their names. For 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
413 example, Buddha Shakyamuni passed away, Jesu s passed away, and likewise Mohammed no 
longer exists. All these religious leaders are gone. Likewise, all our paternal and maternal 
ancestors have passed away. Thus, having been born, we will have to die.  
 In short, birth is suffering. There is suffering  at the time of conception, and there is also 
suffering while in the womb due to it being da rk, narrow, and filled with liquid, and even 
though there is the wish to leave, one cannot.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-2 The suffering of aging  
A Extensive explanation 
B Summary of the essence  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-2A Extensive explanation 
Second: The considerations on the suffe ring of aging have five points:  
 
The five sufferings associated with aging are: 
1. the deterioration of the body, 
2. the deterioration of power and strength, 
3. the deterioration of the senses, 
4. the decline in sense pleasures, and 
5. the complete deterioration of life. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
1 [Deterioration of the body] 
The complete deterioration of the fine body is such that your spine becomes 
curved like a bow, your head becomes wh ite like moss flowers, your forehead 
becomes covered in wrinkles like a cutting  board, and so on, whereby the splendor 
of youth deteriorates and you become ugly. 
 
In our youth our body is very fine, but as we  age it degenerates. Our spine becomes curved 
like a bow. Our hair gradually becomes white,  first becoming a mixture of black and then 
becoming entirely white. Our forehead become s covered in wrinkles. Although some people, 
not wanting to get old, undergo plastic surgery such  as facelifts, hair transplants, and so forth 
to hide their age, still they cannot stop the aging process. In spite of all our efforts, our 
excellent body deteriorates and becomes ugly.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 [Deterioration of power and strength] 
The deterioration of power and strength is such that when you sit down, you 
resemble a sack of soil when its rope is  cut; when you get up, you resemble tree 
roots being pulled up; when you speak, you babble unintelligibly; when you walk, 
you are completely bent over and so on. 
 
Due to aging, our power and strength deteriorates . When we sit down, it is like the dropping 
of a heavy sack. When we stand up , it is like pulling up the r oots of a tree. Our speech is no 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
414 longer clear and we babble or mumble. When we walk, we do so bent over. All this is due to 
the body losing its strength.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 [Deterioration of the senses] 
The deterioration of the senses  is such that your eyes and so forth do not clearly 
perceive forms and so forth any more, and your mindfulness and so forth diminish 
as you grow forgetful and so forth.  
 
The sense powers deteriorate whereby, for exampl e, the eyes can no longer clearly see colors 
and shapes. Similarly, the mind also loses its power and we are no longer able to remember 
many things.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 [Decline in sense pleasures] 
The enjoyment of [sense] objects declin e s  s u c h  t h a t  f o o d  a n d  d r i n k  b e c o m e  
difficult to digest, and you can no longer enjoy other pleasures {131}. 
 
Also our enjoyment of objects declines. We no longer enjoy our food and drink and we find 
it difficult to digest, such that we may even vomit. This is due to a decline in the inner energy of the body, whereby even if we want to eat and drink we are unable to enjoy food and drink.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 [Complete deterioration of the lifespan] 
The suffering of the lifespan having dete riorated completely is that, due to the 
lifespan having been largely spent, you face death.  
 
You should think about these [points] again and again. 
 
There is suffering as our lifespan diminishes. Due to being old, we cannot remain for long. 
While some people live to 110, 112, or even 115 years, it is not possible to live longer than 
that. In fact most of us are unable to live fo r 100 years and many do not reach even into our 
90s. In addition, it is the rare exception for peop le at this age to still be independent, to be 
able to make their own food, make their own be d, go to the toilet alone, and so forth. 
Nowadays because most people are busy with thei r work and families, they are no longer able 
to take care of their aging parents and are forced  to put them into a senior citizens home for 
which they pay a lot of money, even though th e care might not be ve ry good. As they get 
older, some people have great difficulty eating, standing, walking, and so  forth, and, thinking 
that they are a burden on their children or gran dchildren, wish to die.  However, in spite of 
this, some have to wait a long time for death to come.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-2B Summary of the essence  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
415 Chennga[wa] said: “The suffering of death is  bad, but brief. This aging [process] is 
also bad.” Kamapa said: “It’s good that old age comes gradually. If it came in a 
single moment, it would be unbearable”. 
 
The suffering of death is great but it only last s for a short time. Aging, on the other hand, is 
of long duration, due to which it causes intense suffering. Kamapa says that it is a good thing 
that aging happens gradually as if it were to ha ppen suddenly, for example, in a single day, the 
pain would be unbearable. We might even have  a heart attack from the shock and die! For 
example, if one day we have a beautiful young fa ce and then the next da y, when looking in a 
mirror, we were to discover that overnigh t we have become old, our complexion has 
deteriorated, our face has become covered in wr inkles, our hair is completely white, and our 
teeth have fallen out, we really might have a heart attack! However, because aging happens 
gradually, it is a little more bearable. However,  we should examine whether we would be able 
to bear it if old age were to arrive suddenly an d we were no longer able to see and hear well, 
unable to remember things, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-3  The suffering of illness 
Third: The considerations on the suffe ring of illness have five points:  
 
The five sufferings associated with illness are: 
• the nature of the body changes, 
• suffering and mental unhappiness increase, 
• the restriction of enjoyments, 
• the need for unpleasant measures, and 
• the separation from one’s life. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
A [Bodily changes] 
The nature of the body changes in that the body flesh becomes loose, the skin 
dries up, etc. 
 
Due to illness, the nature of the body changes and the flesh becomes loose, the skin dries up, 
and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B [Suffering and mental unhappiness increase] 
Suffering and mental unhappiness increase and you predominantly abide in them 
in that the bodily constituents, water and so  forth, become disturbed in strength as 
they lose their proportional balance, pr oducing pain in the body which in turn 
brings mental unhappiness that lasts day and night.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
416 Due to illness, physical suffering and mental  unhappiness increase. The constituents or 
elements in our body become unbalanced as some become stronger and others weaker. For 
example, due to the water element predominatin g, the liver, stomach, kidney, legs, and so 
forth retain liquid that then needs to be drained off. Due to the fire element predominating, 
we experience high fevers and so forth. Due to  the wind element predominating, the mind is 
disturbed and agitated. Due to the earth elem ent predominating, the body becomes heavy 
and it becomes difficult to move. In this way, due to an imbalance in the elements, physically 
there is pain and discomfort, which in turn give s rise to mental unhappiness. Unable to sleep 
and so forth, the mind becomes more and more unhappy.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C [Restriction of enjoyments] 
You lack desire to enjoy pleasant things. Ha ving been told that pleasant things are 
harmful when you are ill, you do not have  the power to indulge in them as you 
wish and, likewise, you cannot do whatever you want.  
 
A l t h o u g h  w e  w i s h  t o  e n j o y  d e l i c i o u s  f o o d  o r  d r i n k ,  h a v i n g  b e e n  t o l d  b y  a  d o c t o r  t h a t  w e  should not eat them as they will  harm our body and worsen our illness, we must refrain from 
eating and drinking them. Therefore, even thou gh we wish to enjoy the desirable we cannot. 
We are also unable to do whatever we want as, otherwise, our illness will worsen. For example, we might be advised not to walk alon e because we could fall and hurt ourselves, we 
might be advised not to drive, not to fly, and so forth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D [Need for unpleasant measures] 
You need to undergo the unpleasant against your will. You have to employ 
unpleasant medicine, food, drink, and th e like. Likewise, you need to rely on 
rough [means of] examination such as be ing burnt with fire, pierced with sharp 
instruments, and the like.  
 
Due to illness, we have to undergo unpleasant things even though we do not want to do so. For example, we need to eat things  that we do not want to eat. We also have to take medicine 
that we do not like. We may need to be treated with moksta  (the heat of burning fire), 
undergo an operation, and so forth even thou gh such treatments are not pleasant and cause 
ulterior suffering. The Chinese often treat illn esses with moksabustion, whereas in the West 
doctors often recommend an operation. Even thou gh we do not want an operation, we accept 
to undergo it due to being worried that the illness may take our life. However, after the 
operation, we once again experience pain and other problems. Also treatments such as 
chemotherapy causes various sufferings includin g the loss of one’s hair and so forth, but 
people are willing to bear such unpleasant experi ences in order to be cured of their illness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
417 E [Separation from life] 
You will be separated from your life. Having seen that the illness is incurable, 
suffering arises.  
You should consider these points in detail. 
 
Due to illness, there is the suffering of separati ng from life. In other words, having seen that 
our illness cannot be cured, we come to realize that we will soon die. 
 
We should do analytical meditation on the suffe ring of illness, imagining what it would be 
like to be sick and to experience such sufferings.   Wednesday afternoon, 13 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 131) says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-4  The suffering of death 
Fourth: {132} The consideratio ns on the suffering of de ath have five points: You 
are separated from your excellent possession s, relatives, companions, and splendid 
body,160 and at the time of death you experience suffering and intense mental 
unhappiness. As long as you have not be come mentally disenchanted with these 
[sufferings], you should think about them  again and again. The way in which the 
first four become suffering is that, havi ng seen that you will be separated from 
those four, you suffer. 
 
The suffering of death is presented here very briefly. At  the time of death, we will have to 
separate from our possessions, our relatives, surrounding people, and our body, and we will 
experience strong suffering and mental unhappi ness. Thus, without choice, we will have to 
separate from our family, our parents, brothers, sisters, and so forth, as well as our dear friends and partner. We should think about th is again and again. The first four become 
suffering because, having seen that we will  have to separate from them, we suffer.  
 We need to understand how we suffer: we suffer due to birth, aging, sickness, and death. 
These are our principal sufferings. Among them, the strongest is that of death. Seeing this, we 
should meditate on it again and again, thinking “I will die, and at that time I will have to 
separate from everyone and everything.” By reco gnizing the suffering of death, our mind will 
be moved to practice the Dharma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-5 The suffering of meeting with the unpleasant 
Fifth: The suffering of meeting with the unpleasant has five points:  
                                              
160 Longs spyod rtsa ’jing ’khor lus sdug pa phun sum tshogs  – it is not quite clear whether the two qualifiers 
(“excellent ” and “splendid”) at the end of the phrase refer to the noun they follow – “body” – or indeed to all 
four nouns 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
418 1. by simply meeting, for example, an en emy, suffering and mental unhappiness 
arise, and,  
2. due to that, you are beset by fear of being punished, 
3. fear of bad talk, 
4. fear of a dreadful death, and 
5. fear due to the misgiving that you wi ll go to a bad migration after death 
because of transgressing the Dharma.  
You should think about these [types of suffering]. 
 
When we meet our enemies, we experience suffering and our mind becomes unhappy. We 
experience fear of being punished by them, fear of bad talk, fear of a dreadful death, and fear 
of going to a bad migration after death due to transgressing the Dharma. Enemies are an 
example of undesirable or unpleasant objects that  we do not wish to meet, but are forced to 
meet against our will. For example, when we meet  powerful enemies, we fear that they might 
kill us, that they will cause us to have a bad reputation while we wish for a good reputation, 
and so forth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-6 The suffering of separation from the pleasant 
Sixth: The suffering of separation from th e pleasant has five points. Due to being 
separated say from close ones who are very dear to you: 
1. in the mind, sorrow arises, 
2. in the speech, there is lamentation, 
3. in the body, harm arises, 
4. through remembering the good qualities of  the object and longing for it, there 
is mental torment, 
5. you miss the pleasure of it.  
You should think about these [types of suffering]. 
 
We also experience the suffering of being sepa rated from the pleasant. For example, when we 
are separated from a very dear friend, we experience much suffering. Even when we have not yet separated from this person, we worry about separating from him or her. All this causes 
sorrow to arise in the mind, verbally we lament  about this suffering, and our body is harmed. 
Remembering the qualities of our dear friend, th e mind is tormented by sadness. We miss the 
enjoyment of being with that person. In this we , we suffer when separated from that which is 
dear to us. However, this will continue to  happen due to the nature of impermanence. 
Sometimes we have good relati onships, sometimes bad relati onships, sometimes there is 
separation… This is the very natu re of cyclic existence. We shou ld think about the fact that we 
will separate from our dear friends. Just beca use we are the nature of impermanence, at some 
point we will have to separate from them. When we die, our mind leaves everything behind, even our body. Thinking in this way, when such su ffering arises, it will be easier to bear it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-7 The suffering of seeking what you desire and not getting it 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
419 Seventh: The suffering of seeking what you desire and not getting it has five points 
that are similar to [those associated with] separation from what is pleasant. Seeking 
what you desire but not getting it is the depressing suffering of not getting what 
you have been hoping for despite your effort  in seeking it, such as doing farm work 
but not reaping a harvest and doing business but not accruing a profit. 
 
The suffering of seeking the desirable but not obtaining it is similar to the suffering of 
separating from the pleasant. Se eking the desirable but not finding it is, for example, to have 
sown seeds in the field but to not reap the harvest or to do business but to not achieve 
success. Having put effort into obtaining some thing but not having obtained it, the mind 
suffers and we become sad and depressed. We ex perience mental suffering which in turn can 
produce physical suffering, since body and mind ar e related. In fact most of our sicknesses are 
created by our mind. Despite being careful about what we eat and drink, how we walk, and so 
forth, we still experience suffering, both mental  and physical. Therefore, it is important to 
keep our mind quiet and happy. We should not have much desire for material things or friends. Instead we should try to have less desire  and more satisfaction. In this way, our life 
will be easier. On the other hand, if we ha ve strong desire and little satisfaction or 
contentment, we will continually experience ment al suffering that will also induce physical 
suffering. In this way, we will experience many sufferings.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1D-8 “In brief, the five appropriated aggregates are suffering” 
A Brief presentation 
B Extensive explanation  
 
That the five appropriated aggregates are suff ering mainly means that our body is suffering. 
There is a brief presentation and an  extensive explanation of this.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
A Brief presentation  
Eighth: Thinking about the meaning of the statement: “In brief, the five 
appropriated aggregates are suffering” has five points:  
1. they are a vessel of suffering that will become manifest, 
2. they are a vessel of suffering that is the base for that which has [already] become 
manifest, 
3. they are a vessel for the suffering of suffering, 
4. they are a vessel for the suffering of change, and 
5. they are the nature of compounding suffering.  
You should think about these [points] again and again. 
 The five appropriated aggregates are the form aggregate, feeling aggregate, discrimination 
aggregate, compositional factors aggregate, and consciousness aggregate. All of them are the 
nature of suffering. Our body is a vessel of sufferi ng that will become manifest, that is, it is 
vessel for many sufferings. In other words, our bo dy is like a container or vessel into which 
many sufferings can be put, for example, the su ffering caused by the four hundred types of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
420 illnesses. The body is also a vessel for the suffer ing that is the base fo r that which has already 
become manifest, such as kidney pain, a stomac h ache, pain in the shou lder, in the back, in 
the feet, in the bones, and so fo rth. The body is also a vessel fo r the suffering of suffering, that 
is, our body is the very nature of suffering and on top of that there is the additional suffering 
of pain in the kidneys, head, ears, and so forth.  The body is also a vessel for the suffering of 
change. It is also a vessel for pervasive comp ounding sufferings since, from the crown of our 
head to the soles of our feet, the body is pe rvaded by suffering. This way of explaining 
compounding suffering is in relation to oneself. Another way of explaining this suffering is 
that everywhere, from the Peak of Existence down to the hell  of Unrelenting Torment, is 
pervaded by suffering. In short, our body is the very nature of compounding suffering.   We need to think about the fact that our bo dy aggregate is the nature of suffering. While 
f e e l i n g s  c a n  a l s o  b e  s a i d  t o  b e  t h e  n a t u r e  of suffering, among them there are feelings of 
happiness, suffering, and neutrality. Also while discriminations are the nature of suffering, 
some discriminations make the mind happy an d others make it suffer. Among the many 
compositional factors, many of them are suffe ring. Likewise, the consciousness aggregate is 
often the nature of suffering, but sometimes this  suffering is manifest and sometimes it is 
unmanifest. However, it is still the nature of suffering.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Extensive explanation 
{133} Here the first [point] is that, in dependence on taking these appropriated 
aggregates, the sufferings of the next rebirth onward are induced.  
The second one: the aggregates that have already been established become the 
basis of the illness, aging, etc. that depend on them.  
The third and fourth ones: [the aggregates] give rise to these two types of suffering 
due to their relationship with the negative tendencies161 of the two [sufferings ].  
The fifth one: due to the mere establishm ent of the appropriated aggregates they 
arise in the nature of compounding su ffering, for all compounded phenomena 
under the control of other – previous  karma and mental afflictions – are 
compounding suffering.  
 In dependence on taking the appropriate aggregates  of this life, the suffering of future lives is 
induced. Then, based on the aggregates – the body – that has already been achieved, we experience the sufferings of illness, aging, and death, as well as the many different types of 
sufferings such as heat, cold, hu nger, thirst, and so forth. For example, when it is hot we 
immediately take off some layers of clothing, and when it is cold we immediately put on 
more; we do this only because the body is suffering.   The third, that the aggregates are a vessel for the suffering of suffering, and the fourth, that 
they are a vessel for the suffering of change, ar e related to the negative tendencies. “Negative 
tendencies” are mental imprints deposited at the time of being an ordinary being that cause, 
                                             
 
161 The term implies that the elements of one’s presen t existence have arisen under the power of karma and 
mental afflictions which is why they entail correspon ding tendencies tainting them (cf. Geshe Thubten 
Ngawang in Systematic Study of Buddhism , semester XII, transcript 4/5, p. 8). See also the fourth aspect of the 
suffering of birth. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
421 for example, even foe-destroyers, who have ab andoned the afflictions, to speak badly from 
time to time, to swing from trees like monkeys, and so forth. The fifth, that the aggregates are 
the nature of compounding suffering, mean s that, due to being under the power of 
contaminated actions and afflictions, the aggreg ates are the nature of compounding suffering. 
Our aggregates are not under our control, they ar e under the control of other, that is, actions 
and afflictions. Because beings take rebirth in cy clic existence, that is, they take appropriate 
aggregates by the power of actions and affliction s, they are the nature of suffering and are not 
happiness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A1E Advice to cherish this manner of meditating on the essence 
If no genuine disenchantment develops to wards cyclic existence, which is of the 
nature of the appropriated aggregates, there is no way for a genuine awareness 
striving for liberation to develop nor for great compassion for the sentient beings 
roaming about in cyclic existence to deve lop. Therefore these considerations are 
extremely important whichever vehicle you enter, the Mahayana or Hinayana. 
 
Cyclic existence is the nature of  the appropriated aggregates. It is the constant circling over 
and over again. We need to generate disencha ntment with respect to cyclic existence since 
without this we will not be able to generate a genuine mind striving for liberation. If we do 
not think about suffering, we will also not be able to generate great compassion for all the 
sentient beings wandering in cyclic existence and experiencing suffering. In other words, in 
order to generate compassion for them, we need  to understand that the nature of cyclic 
existence is suffering. Only through understandin g their suffering will we generate the wish 
for all sentient beings to become free from th e suffering of cyclic existence. Therefore, 
whether we enter the Hinayana or Mahayana, it  is important to recognize suffering as 
suffering and generate the wish to become free from this suffering. In addition, if we have 
entered the Hinayana we need to generate the wish  to attain liberation or nirvana, and if we 
have entered the Mahayana we need to generate the wish to attain enlightenment in order to 
be able to liberate all sentient beings from  suffering. Through generating great compassion, 
we can generate the mind of enlightenment that wi shes to take care of all sentient beings. For 
this purpose, we need to meditate again and ag ain on love and compassion. Thus, in our daily 
life it is important to meditate regularly on love and compassion.   (Oral transmission 7:19-7:34, pp. 133-139)  Thursday morning, 14 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 133) says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A2 Considering the six [types of] suffering 
A Summarized presentation of the six [types of] suffering 
B Presentation of them condensed in three groups  
C How there is nothing in contaminated feelings that is of the entity of happiness  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A2A Summarized presentation of the six [types of] suffering 
Second: The Letter to a Friend  sets out the faults [of cyclic existence]: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
422 1. lack of certainty, 
2. insatiability, 
3. discarding one’s body again and again, 
4. joining again and again, 
5. changing from high to low [states] again and again, and 
1. lacking companions. 
 
Nagarjuna’s Letter to a Friend  sets out the six sufferings: 
• a lack of certainty, 
• a lack of satisfaction, 
• discarding the body again and again, 
• joining with a body again and again, 
• changing from high to low again and again, and 
• loneliness or lack of companions. 
We can see how things change, for example, on e day we are happy and the next day we are 
unhappy. This is because there is no certainty in  cyclic existence. There is also a lack of 
satisfaction, which mentally causes us many prob lems. Then there is the suffering of having to 
give up or discard our body over and over again. We are born and die again and again, 
continually forced to give up our body. There is also the suffering of joining with or taking 
another body again and again, seemingly without end. Then there is the suffering of attaining 
a high position but later on once again falling down. for example, we become wealthy, have a 
high social position, power, and fame but th en something happens and we lose everything 
and even become poor and so forth. We see that sometime very rich people suddenly become poor, and that sometimes those who were very p oor become very rich. We know this reality 
very well. Then there is the suffering of loneli ness. Although there is the suffering of dying 
alone without anyone to accompany us to our next life, here this suffering refers in general to 
loneliness, which can be experienced even wh en we are surrounded by loving family and 
friends.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A2B Presentation of them included in three groups  
To sum them up in three [groups]:  
1. in cyclic existence there is nothing reliable, 
2. no matter how much of its happiness one enjoys, there is no final point of 
satisfaction, and 
3. one has been entering into it since beginningless times. 
 
There is nothing in cyclic existence that is  reliable. No matter how much we enjoy the 
apparent pleasures of cyclic existence, they do not bring us satisfaction, but only make us want 
more and more. In addition, we have been suffe ring in cyclic existence since beginningless 
time and it also appears that this suffering will  be endless in the future . However, while cyclic 
existence is beginningless, there is an end to th e cyclic existence of an individual being. In 
other words, by practicing well, we can attain the state of a foe-destroyer or a buddha and put 
an end to our cyclic existence.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
423  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
The first one has four points: 
1. the inappropriateness of relying on the body you have attained since you will have to discard your body again and again; 
2. the inappropriateness of relying on benefit and harm [done to you] since there 
is no certainty as to whether your father will become your son; your mother, 
your wife; and your enemy, someone very dear to you; and the like; 
3. the inappropriateness of relying the exce llences you have achieved since {134} 
you fall from high places to low ones; and 
4. the inappropriateness of relying on co mpanions since you have to go on 
without them [in the end].  
 
The third one is that, since you join again and again, an end to the continuity of 
births is not evident.  
 You should reflect in this manner again and again. 
 Even though we have attained a good body, it wo uld be inappropriate to rely on it since it is 
not stable in that we will have to give it up once  again. Also it is not appropriate to rely on the 
benefit and harm done to us because when our father dies he can be reborn as our son, our mother can be reborn as our wife, our enemy can be reborn as a dear friend, and so forth. In 
this way, cyclic existence is unreliable in th at everything constantly changes. It is also 
inappropriate to rely on our excellences, such as our wealth and power, because we can fall 
from the high to the low. For example, there ha ve been presidents who were the dictator of a 
country for many years but later on ended up in prison, alone and without anyone to take care of them. There have also been people who originally were in a low position but later on became prime ministers, ministers, and so fort h. However, they too can once again fall to 
such a state that they even need to escape fr om their country. There are also those who were 
famous and wealthy who later on were taken to court and put in prison.  In addition, it is inappropriate to rely on our companions or friends because, after some 
time, their mind may change and they might say to us, “At the moment I need to think about 
our relationship. For now it would be better for me not to have contact with you.” At first 
such people were our dear friends, but then th eir mind changed and they either gave us up 
entirely or distanced themselves from us. Th is shows that even our dear friends are not 
reliable as they too can change.   We take rebirth, give up our body and take rebirth again. Because after death our existence 
does not finish, we need to take another rebirt h. In this way an end to our future rebirths 
cannot be seen. However, if we realize selfle ssness directly, especially the selflessness of 
persons, we will be able to understand that it is possible to abandon the conception of a self, the root of cyclic existence, an d thereby put an end to rebirth and to cyclic existence. While 
some people can see an end to their rebirths, for most of us it is not possible to see even our 
next life. Because the existence of past and futu re lives is a hidden phenomenon, they must be 
proven by reasonings. For this reason, it is necessary to study logic.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
424  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2A2C How there is nothing in contaminated feelings that is of the entity of happiness 
Furthermore, at present most of the happy  feelings that increase our attachment 
are minds of happiness that arise with resp ect to a relief of suffering; they are not 
happiness by way of entity that is not re lated to suffering. For instance, if you 
suffer due to having walked too much, th e mind of happiness that arises due to 
sitting down appears to be a happiness that arises gradually while the previous 
great suffering gradually ceases. However, it is not happiness by way of entity, for if you sit too much, once again suffering will arise as before. If they were causes of 
happiness by way of their entity, just as su ffering increases to the same extent as 
you are subject to the causes of suffering , so too should walking, sitting, lying, 
eating and drinking, as well as sun and sh ade, etc. increase your happiness to the 
same extent that you indulge in them. Howe ver, it appears that in the long term 
only suffering is produced.  
 These points are also set out in [the Sutra of Nanda’s ] Entry into the Womb  and the 
root text of the Four Hundred  [Verses] and its commentary.  
 
Out of attachment we generate happy feeling, and even though it is not happiness in entity 
we still perceive it to be happiness. For exampl e, when we eat deliciou s food we experience 
happy feeling that increases our attachment to ea ting but in the end, due to eating too much, 
we experience the suffering of an overfull stomac h. Likewise, if due to liking coffee, we drink 
too much, suffering will arise. Although these things appear to bring happiness, due to our 
overindulging in them we come to experience  suffering, for example, that of high blood 
pressure. There are people who, seeking happin ess, smoke marijuana or hashish, convinced 
that it will bring them happiness, such as the se nsation of flying in the sky, but in the end it 
only brings them suffering and sickness. Whatever we enjoy in cyclic existence, even though it 
appear to be happiness, in reality it is not happiness and only brings us more suffering and 
unhappiness. There is nothing at all that is happine ss by way of entity in cyclic existence. Only 
if we eliminate all sufferings will we naturall y experience happiness. On the other hand, as 
long as we have not eliminated suffering, there is  nothing in cyclic existe nce that is the entity 
of happiness.  For example, if we walk for a long time and, becoming tired, sit down, upon sitting for a long time in a chair, once again there will be suffering. This happen s naturally. Although 
when we first sit down, there is the feeling of ha ppiness, in actuality it is only a reduction or 
relief of the previous suffering that appears to be happiness. Having remained sitting for a 
long time, once again we become uncomforta ble and want to again walk. Then, when we 
once again begin to walk, it too appears to be  happiness due to a reduction of the previous 
suffering of sitting. However, as we walk, the suffering of walking gradually returns. On the 
other hand, just as the longer we experience the causes of suffering, the more our suffering 
increases, similarly, if walking, sitting, and so forth were the nature or entity of happiness, the 
more we engage in them, the more our happiness would increase. However, this does not happen. Likewise, if eating and drinking were the nature of happiness, the more we eat and 
drink, the more happiness we would experience. Si milarly, if lying in the sun were the nature 
of happiness, the more we lie in the sun,  the more happiness we would experience, but 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
425 instead we soon become hot and want to move into the shade. Howeve r, if staying in the 
shade were the nature of happiness, the more we stay in the shade, the more happiness we 
would experience, but as before we soon beco me cool and want to return to the sun.  
 Therefore, because cyclic existence is the natu re of suffering, we should generate the wish 
t o  b e c o m e  f r e e  f r o m  i t  a n d  t o  g o  t o  a  p u r e  l a n d  s u c h  a s  S u k h a v a t i .  H a v i n g  g o n e  t o  s u c h  
places, we will always be happy and never experience suffering. Otherwise, we will not be able to avoid suffering. There exist many conditions for suffering and we also have many internal 
causes for the experience of suffering, the main  cause being the contaminated or non-virtuous 
actions that we have created.  
 This lack of happiness is set out in the Sutra  of Nanda’s  Entry into the Womb  and the root 
text and commentary on the Four Hundred  Verses . The sutra sets out the fact that, having died, 
we are once again conceived in a womb, after which the fetus gradually develops due to the 
movement of various winds over a period of th irty-seven weeks. When the body is complete 
and the mental consciousness is established, th e baby begins to suffer from the darkness and 
tightness of the womb and gene rates the wish to leave it. 
 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B Considering the suffering of the individual [realms]  
1 The suffering of the three bad migrations 
2 The suffering of humans 
3 The suffering of demigods 
4 The suffering of gods 
The second one has four points: the suffe ring of the three bad migrations, the 
suffering of humans, the suffering of demigods, and the suffering of gods. 
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B1 The suffering of the three bad migrations 
The first has already been explained. 
 
The sufferings of the bad migrations – the hell s, hungry ghosts, and animals – were already 
explained in the context of the path of small beings. Having contemplated them, we should 
go on to think about the sufferings of human beings, demigods, and gods.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B2 The suffering of humans 
The second one [consists in] the suffering s of hunger and thirst, the unpleasant 
contact of heat and cold, frantic activity , and fatigue. In addition, you should 
understand the seven [types of suffering], bi rth, aging, illness, death, and so forth162 
by means of the previous explanations.  
 
Human beings experience the sufferings of hunger due to a lack of food, thirst due to a lack 
of drink, heat and cold due to unpleasant contact with heat or cold. They also experience the 
suffering of frantically seeking something and not finding it, for example, a boyfriend or 
girlfriend, a job, and so forth. They also suffer  from tiredness or fatigue. In addition, human 
                                              
162 and the suffering of meeting with the unpleasant, the se paration from what is pleasant and seeking what you 
desire and not getting it

Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
426 beings experience the seven types of suffering  mentioned before: the suffering of birth, 
suffering of aging, suffering of illness, suffe r i n g  o f  d e a t h ,  s u f f e r i n g  o f  m e e t i n g  w i t h  t h e  
unpleasant, suffering of separating from the pl easant, and suffering of seeking the desirable 
and not obtaining it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Furthermore, in the Discourses of the Collections  it says:  
 
All the sufferings of bad migrations, 
Also appear to exist for human beings. {135} Troubled by suffering like beings in hell, Subjects of Yama by poverty, 
They also have the suffering of animals: 
Powerful ones oppress the weak 
By force, inflicting harm on them. It is like a river [engulfing you]. 
 It appears that human beings also have similar sufferings to those existing in the bad migrations. For example, at times human beings  are oppressed or intoxicated by suffering, 
similar to the beings in the hell realm. However, since the suffering in an actual hell realm is 
much greater, the suffering of human beings is small compared to it.   In terms of poverty, human beings are at time s similar to the beings of the world of Yama, 
that is, to the hungry ghosts. When some hungry  ghosts, wishing to eat, approach a tree laden 
with fruit, upon arriving there the tree is found to be dried up and without any fruit at all. 
For others, when, wishing to drink, they pick up  a vessel of liquid, it appears to them to be 
filled with pus and blood. For others, when they want to drink or eat, the beverage transforms 
into molten iron and the food in to lumps of incandescent iron.  
 Human beings also experience suffering simila r to that of animals who are overpowered by 
other animals or used without choice by huma n beings to carry heavy loads, work in the 
fields, and so forth. For example, some huma n beings, without freedom, are forced to work 
day and night by others who have such power ov er them that they are not permitted even to 
express their suffering. Powerful human beings also oppress those who are weak and inflict 
harm on them.   This experience of suffering arises continuous ly, like the uninterrupted flow of a river.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And in Four Hundred Verses : 
 
Higher ones have mental suffering, 
Lower ones get physical pain. These two types of suffering  Overwhelm the world each day. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
427 You should reflect in accordance with these statements. 
 
Higher human beings, the rich and so forth, ex perience much mental suffering. Why is this? 
It is because they constantly worry about losing their power,  wealth, and so forth, while 
simultaneously aspiring to increase their powe r, wealth, and so forth. On the other hand, 
while the poor experience less mental sufferin g, they experience more physical suffering 
b e c a u s e  t h e y  n e e d  t o  w o r k  h a r d  p h y s i c a l l y  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  T h e y  d o  n o t  h a v e  m u c h  m e n t a l  
suffering because they do not have worries ab out losing their wealth, power, fame, and so 
forth, and they are satisfied with merely findin g enough food and drink and a bed to sleep on. 
However, because they often need to do heav y manual labor, they experience physical 
suffering.  
 When the text says that “The world is destro yed each day by the two types of suffering,” the 
word translated as “world,” in Tibetan jig ten  (‘jig rten ) ,  l i t e r a l l y  m e a n s  “ t h e  s u p p o r t  o f  t h e  
transitory” and refers to our body and mind, which are unstable, constantly changing, and 
harmed by the two types of suffering, mental and physical.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B3 The suffering of demigods 
Third: It is said that the demigods ar e mentally tormented by envy that cannot 
bear the wealth of the gods. When based on that they fight with the gods, they 
experience many sufferings of having their bodies chopped up, split, etc. Although 
they possess wisdom, they have the fully- ripened obscurations and consequently 
cannot see the truth in that basis. 
 
The demigods or antigods, upon seeing the weal th and so forth of the gods, experience the 
mental suffering of great jealousy. Due to this, th ey fight with the gods, that is, they wage war 
against them. However, the gods being more powerful, the demigods then experience the 
intense suffering of having their bodies cut to  pieces with weapons. Also, because they are 
unable to win the battle, they experience su ffering. Although the demigods possess wisdom, 
due to having fruitional or fully-ripened obscuratio ns they are unable to see the truth, that is, 
to directly realize the four noble truths, with that basis or body. In other words, due to the 
obscurations of their body they are unable to achieve the path of seeing. Similarly, due to 
fruitional obscurations, animals, such as horses, donkeys, sheep, goats, pigeons, and so forth, 
cannot achieve the path of seeing, generate bodh ichitta, and so forth due to being stupid and 
only thinking about eating and drinking.   It is also said in sutra that because the youn g female demigods are very beautiful, the gods 
take them away by force to their land, for whic h reason the demigods also wage war against 
the gods but are unable to defeat them.   Thursday afternoon, 14 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 135) says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B4 The suffering of gods 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
428 A The suffering of the desire [realm] gods 
B The suffering of the gods of the uppermost realms 
The fourth one has two points: the suffering of desire [realm] gods and the 
suffering of gods of the uppermost realms. 
 
Next there is a discussion of the sufferings of the gods of the desire realm and those of the 
gods of the upper realms. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B4A The suffering of the desire [realm] gods 
1 The suffering of death-transference and falling 
2 The suffering of despair  
3 The suffering of being chopped up, split, killed, and banished 
The first one has three points: the suffering  of death-transference and falling, the 
suffering of despair, and the suffering of being chopped up, split, killed, and 
banished. 
 
The gods of the desire realm experience great su ffering at the time of death-transference and 
for the fact that after death they will once again fall into the lower realms. Being unable to do 
anything about this, they experience the great su ffering of despair. They also experience the 
suffering of having their bodies chopped to piec es, of their legs, arms, and so forth being cut 
off, of being killed, and of being banish ed when they fight with the demigods.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B4A-1 The suffering of death-transference and falling 
A The suffering of death-transference 
B The suffering of falling to a lower place 
The first one has two points: the suffering of death-transference and the suffering 
of falling to a lower place.  
A The suffering of death-transference 
First: At the time of death-transference wh en a god sees the five signs of death, the 
suffering that springs from this is far greater than the happi ness that previously 
arose from indulging in the desirable obje cts of the gods. The five signs of death 
are: (1) a hideous bodily complexion, (2) a dislike for one’s seat, (3) the wilting of 
one’s flower garlands, (4) one’s clothes be coming smelly, and (5) sweat, which did 
not exist previously, appearing on one’s body. 
 
The gods of the desire realm suffer from death-tr ansference and suffer due to seeing that they 
will fall to a lower place. Although they prev iously enjoyed many desirable object, when the 
five signs of their impending death arise they  experience the mental suffering of intense 
unhappiness that is far greater than their prev ious happiness. The five signs of death are:  
1. the complexion or color of their body becomes ugly,  
2. they come to dislike their cushion,  
3. their flower garlands wilt whereas previously they never withered,  
4. their clothes begin to smell, and  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
429 5. sweat arises on their bodies wherea s previously this never happened.  
At that time of approaching death, they experience much more suffering than that 
experienced by human beings, yet they cannot do an ything to avoid it. In this way, the gods of 
the desire realm also experience great suffering.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The suffering of falling 
The second one is just like it says in Letter to a Friend : 
 
As they transfer from the world of the gods 
When there is no remainder of anything virtuous, {136} They change without control into situations  Of animals, hungry ghosts, and hell beings. 
 When the gods of the desire re alm do not have any remaining virtue, they fall after their 
death into the hell, hungry ghost, or animal re alms. In other words, they take rebirth as one 
of the different kinds of animals (those in the oceans and those in the places of humans and 
gods), hungry ghosts (those with external obst ructions, those with internal obstructions, and 
those with obstructions to food and drink), and hell beings (those of the eight hot hells, eight 
cold hells, four neighboring hells, and the occa sional hells) due to their karma. Having taken 
rebirth in any one of these places, they will ex perience much suffering. We should meditate 
on the suffering that arises when the desire re alm gods see that they will fall down into the 
lower realms and that they are powerless to change this.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B4A-2  The suffering of despair 
Second: For the gods with very extensive heaps of merit the most exquisite sense 
pleasures arise. When the sons of the gods  with small merit see that, they despair 
and experience great suffering and mental unhappiness on that basis.  
 
Among the desire realm gods there are those who,  due to having vast heaps of merit, possess 
great wealth and excellences. When the sons of  the gods with small merit see the gods who 
possess great wealth and excellences, they desp air and experience great suffering and mental 
unhappiness. Even when poor human beings see rich people they do not despair in the same way, but see that the rich created the merit to have this wealth. In other words, they 
understand that if someone is rich it is beca use in a previous life that person practiced 
generosity, whereas those who are poor did not. However, if th ose who experience the result 
of generosity in this life do not continue to practice generosity, they may come to experience 
poverty and problems.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B4A-3 The suffering of being chopped up, split, killed, and banished  
Third: When the gods fight with the demi gods, they experience the suffering of 
having their limbs and minor body parts chopped off, the suffering of their bodies 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
430 being split, and the suffering of being ki lled. If their heads are chopped off they 
die, but if their limbs and minor body pa rts are chopped off or split they grow 
back and they survive. As for banishme nt, when there is conflict the more 
powerful gods drive the weaker sons  of the gods out of their abodes. 
 
When the gods fight with the de migods, they experience the suffering of having their limbs 
and minor body parts cut off, the suffering of ha ving their bodies split, and the suffering of 
being killed. When their head is  cut off by the demigods, the go ds die, but when their limbs 
or minor body parts are chopped off, they grow back and they do not die, but go on living. When there is conflict, the more powerful gods  banish or expel the weaker ones from their 
homes. We can only imagine the fighting between  the gods and demigods since we are unable 
to actually see them. We can only see the suffering of human beings and animals, and not 
that of other sentient beings. They can only be seen by omniscient beings and those with 
higher realizations, such as clairvoyance. When we achieve the clairvoyance of divine eye and 
so forth we will be able to see very distant phen omena clearly, just like we presently see things 
placed in front of us. We will also be able to see the different kinds of rebirth that sentient beings, including ourselves, will take and so forth.   Although there is fighting between the gods  and demigods, there is even more fighting 
among human beings, as well as more suffering and mental unhappiness.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2A-2B4B  The suffering of  the gods in the uppermost realms 
1 [Lack of power to avoid future suffering] 
Although the gods in the two uppermost realms do not have the suffering of 
suffering, in possessing mental afflictions and obscurations, they lack control over 
their death-transference and the [next] state, which is why they suffer through 
taking on bad states. 
 
The gods of the two upper realms also experien ce suffering but not the suffering of suffering. 
What kind of suffering do they experience? Am ong the three types of suffering – the suffering 
of suffering, suffering of change, and pervasiv e suffering, these gods experience pervasive 
suffering. Why is this? It is because they stil l have the mental afflictions and obscurations. 
Therefore, when they die, without power or freedom but due to karma created in the past 
that left an imprint on their consciousness, they will once again experience suffering.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 How they have the suffering of fa lling to bad migrations once again  
Furthermore it says in Discourses of the Collections :  
 
Someone in the form or formless realms, 
Having transcended the suffering of suffering, 
Possessed of the bliss of meditative stabilization, Who dwells immovable for eons,  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
431 Is still not certain of freedom at all; 
He will still fall from there again. If he has emerged somewhere 
Beyond the whirlpool of pain of bad realms, 
 
Even with effort he won’t last long there. Like a bird in flight up high, 
Like an arrow shot by a child, 
That one also ends up falling –  Like butterlamps that blaze for long {137} While [actually] perishing moment by moment, He is completely subject to the suffering Called ‘compounding, thoroughly changing.’ 
 The gods of the upper realms also experience the suffering of falling back into the lower 
realms. Having been born as a god of the form realm or formless realm, even though they do 
not have the suffering of suffering, due to possessi ng the bliss of meditative stabilization, and 
do not move from this state for eons, they ar e still not liberated from suffering, from the 
mental afflictions, and from the obscurations. Be cause of this, they will fall once again into 
the bad migrations and experience suffering. It is  not easy to go beyond such bad migrations, 
and even though we apply effort we cannot rema in in the upper realms. Just as a bird cannot 
remain forever in the sky but must once again return to the ground, so too do these gods 
return to the bad migrations. Likewise, like an a rrow shot by a child that goes up into the sky 
but then quickly falls back to the ground, so too do the gods who have  taken rebirth in the 
form and formless realms once again fall into the bad migrations. This is because they have 
exhausted their merit and because on their mental consciousness are the seeds of non-virtuous actions created by them in the past. Also like the flame of a butterlamp or candle 
perishes in each moment and will come to an end, these gods experience suffering. In this 
way, the gods of the form or formless realms also experience mental suffering due to seeing, by means of their clairvoyance, their future re birth in the lower realms. While they do not 
have the suffering of suffering related to their bo dy or mind, they have the suffering of change 
(happiness that transforms into suffering) and pe rvasive suffering. For these reasons, we need 
to examine the fact that even if we are reborn  as a god we will still experience suffering. We 
tend to think that the gods do not experience suffering, but since they are not liberated from 
cyclic existence and still abide in it, they do experience suffering.   We see that human beings, ourselves and ot hers, experience problems day and night. 
While we cannot see the gods, they do experien ce mental suffering. Therefore, wherever we 
take rebirth, in the upper or lower realms, th ere will be suffering. There is nowhere about 
which it can be said that there is never any su ffering. Rather, there is suffering everywhere.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B  Thinking about the stages of entering cyclic existence – origins 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
432 1 How the mental afflictions arise 
2 How karma is thereby accumulated  
3 How there is death-transference and rejoining 
The second one has three points: how the mental afflictions arise, how karma is 
thereby accumulated, and how there is death-transference and rejoining.  
 
How do the mental afflictions arise? We ne ed to examine this. Th en, motivated by the 
afflictions, we accumulate karm a. Due to this, there is death- transference and rejoining with 
other aggregates, that is, with a new body.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1 How the mental afflictions arise 
A How the mental afflictions are chief  
B Identification of the mental afflictions  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1A How the mental afflictions are chief  
Although both karma and mental affliction s are necessary as causes that establish 
cyclic existence, the mental afflictions ar e chief. This is because if there are no 
mental afflictions, even though there exists karma beyond counting that was 
accumulated previously, since the cooperat ive conditions for karma are absent, the 
sprout of suffering is not established – just  like a sprout is not established from a 
seed that is lacks humidity, soil, and so fort h. It is also because if there are mental 
afflictions, even though there may be no  karma accumulated previously, it will 
immediately be accumulated anew and future aggregates will be taken. That is also 
how it is set out in the Commentary  [on Valid Cognition ]: 
 
The karma of those who have completely passed beyond  
The craving for existence, cannot project another,  
For the cooperative condition has been exhausted. 
 
And: 
 
Because if craving is present, it will arise once again. 
 Therefore, it is of great importance to rely on the antidotes to the mental 
afflictions, and that again depends on know ing the mental afflictions, which is why 
you should become skilled in them.  
 There are two causes of rebirth in cyclic ex istence, actions and afflictions. Among them, 
which one is the principal cause? It is the afflic tions because if there are no afflictions, even 
though we have created karma it wi ll not be able to bring its result, just like a barley seed will 
not bring the result of a barley sprout if it lacks water, fertilizer, and so forth. Likewise, 
without the cooperative condition that is the afflictions, karma cannot bring the result of 
suffering. On the other hand, if there are afflic tions, even though we have not accumulated 
karma in the past, we will accumulate new karma that will act as the cause for taking another 
body or rebirth.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 11-14 April 2005 (09) 
433  In short, if there are no a fflictions, just as a seed will not produce a result without the 
cooperative conditions of water, heat, and so forth, likewise karma without the cooperative 
condition of the afflictions will not bring the resu lt of rebirth. On the other hand, if there are 
afflictions, even though there is no previous ly created karma, we will accumulate new karma 
that will become the cause of taking another body.  
 When creating karma, the causal motivation is  an affliction. If the simultaneous or time 
motivation is virtuous, we will create a contamin ated virtuous action that will bring the result 
o f  t h e  b o d y  o f  a  h u m a n  b e i n g  o r  g o d  i n  t h e  n e x t  l i f e .  H o w e v e r ,  i t  i s  s t i l l  a  b o d y  o f  c y c l i c  existence. If, on the other hand, there are no a fflictions, we will not take rebirth because the 
cause, the afflictions, is absent. The Buddha taug ht the twelve links, the first being ignorance, 
which is a projecting cause. In addition, in or der to take rebirth the actualizing causes of 
grasping and craving are also necessary. Therefor e, if the afflictions are absent, we will not 
take rebirth.   Those who have attained the state of a foe- destroyer have eliminated the afflictions and 
have become free from cyclic existence. Sinc e they do not create new projecting karma 
because they have exhausted the cooperative condition of the affl ictions, they no longer take 
rebirth in cyclic existence. On the other hand, as long as there is craving, we will create new actions. Therefore, it is very important to apply the antidotes to the mental afflictions. To do 
so, it is necessary to understand the mental afflictions and their ob ject of the mode of 
apprehension. Therefore, by becoming skilled or learned in the mental afflictions, we will be 
able to abandon them.   What are the mental afflictions? There ar e ten subtle-increasers: attachment, hatred, 
ignorance, pride, doubt, the view  of the transitory collection, th e view holding to an extreme, 
the view holding a bad view – such as the view of the transitory collection or an extreme view 
– as supreme, the view holding bad conduct an d morality as supreme, and wrong view (that 
the law of actions and their result s does not exist, that past and future lives do not exist, and 
that the Three Jewels do not exist).   
END 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
434 Monday afternoon, 18 April 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 137) says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B Identification of the mental afflictions 
1 [The actual] identification of the mental afflictions
