2 The stages by which [the mental afflictions ] arise 
3 The faults of the mental afflictions 
Second: This has three points: [The actual] identification of the mental afflictions, 
the stages by which they arise, and th e faults of the mental afflictions. 
 
What is the root of all the sufferings, problems , and difficulties of cyclic existence? It is 
contaminated actions which in turn are motivated by afflictions. For this reason, we need to 
identify the mental afflictions. An affliction is what makes the mind afflicted and unable to 
understand the true nature of all phenomena. What kinds of mental afflictions are there? 
First we need to identify them, then we need to  understand how they arise, and then we need 
to think about their faults or shortcomings. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1 [The actual] identification of the mental afflictions 
A Desirous attachment 
B Anger 
C Pride 
D Ignorance E Doubt 
F The view of the transitory collection 
G View holding on to an extreme [position] 
H [View] holding on to a view as best  
I [View] holding on to an ethical discipline and ascetic practice as best 
J Wrong views  
The first one has ten points. 
 
There are ten main mental afflictions:  
1. attachment, 
2. anger, 
3. pride, 
4. ignorance, 
5. doubt, 
6. the view of the transitory collection, 
7. the view holding to an extreme, 
8. the view holding to a bad view as supreme,  
9. the view holding on to a bad ethical discip line and ascetic practice as supreme, and 
10. wrong views. 
Attachment, through observing a contaminated ob ject and thinking that it is pleasant or 
attractive, wishes to have it. Anger, throug h observing a contaminated object that is 
unpleasant or unattractive, wishes to harm it. Pride is the thought “I am very learned. I am 
from a high class. I am rich. I am very attracti ve,” and so forth. Ignorance is a non-knowing of 
the nature of phenomena or the apprehension of all phenomena as substantially existent or 
inherently existent. Doubt is a mind that is tw o-pointed and unable to decide. The view of the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
435 transitory collection thinks “I ” and “mine.” The view holding on  to an extreme either thinks 
that if things do not exist inherently they do not exist at all, or that if  things exist they must 
exist inherently. The view holding to a bad vi ew as supreme holds either the view of the 
transitory collection or the view holding to  an extreme to be a supreme view. The view 
holding to bad ethical discipline  and ascetic practice as suprem e holds incorrect behavior as 
the best conduct or incorrect morality as the be st morality. Wrong view holds the law of cause 
and effect, the Three Jewels, past and future lives,  and so forth to be no n-existent. In this way 
there are ten main mental afflictions. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1A Desirous attachment 
The first of those, desirous attachment, obse rves an attractive, pleasant, external or 
internal object and is subsequently attached to it. To illustrate: just as oil that has 
penetrated into cloth is hard to remove, [desirous attachment ] also clings to its 
object and pervades it whereby it separa tes from that object with difficulty. 
 
Attachment observes an attractive and pleasant  observed object, external or internal, and 
becomes subsequently attached to it. Attachment is similar to oil that has penetrated a piece 
of cloth and is difficult to remove, as it too pervades the mind and is difficult to eliminate. Attachment arises in our mind every day, at a ll times, and in whatever we do. Hence, it is 
almost always present. For this reason, it is difficult to separate our minds from attachment.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1B Anger 
Anger, {138} through observing a source of suffering such as sentient beings, 
suffering, weapons, thorns, and so forth, is a hostile attitude, a rough mind, 
intending to inflict harm on the object.  
 
Anger takes as its object sentient beings, for ex ample, and then wishes to harm them. It also 
takes as its object suffering, weapons, thorns, and so forth, and then wishes to inflict harm on 
them. It is a rough mind that wishes to ha rm its object. Anger and hatred are synonyms. 
When we are angry, the anger disturbs the happy  and relaxed state of our own mind and also 
disturbs the minds of the people around us, our family, our companion, and so forth. Our 
anger harms others by making them unhappy. Ange r is also dangerous because it destroys our 
virtue. However, unlike attachment, it does not arise at all times, for example, it does not 
arise when we are enjoying eating, drinking, rela xing, and so forth. It only arises in certain 
situations, for example, when we  meet a particular person. Ange r also does not last for long, 
sometimes just an hour or so, or just a few mi nutes. When, on the other hand, we hold anger, 
it becomes belligerence.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1C Pride 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
436 Pride, through observing one’s external or internal level or qualities based on the 
view of the transitory collection, is a pu ffed up mind that engages that having an 
elevated aspect. 
 
Pride, based on the view of the transitory collecti on, discriminates this is high and this is low, 
this is good and this is bad, and then disting uishes oneself to be higher, better, and so forth. 
Thus, it engages a high or elevated aspect and thinks, for example, “I am from a noble family. 
I am from a high caste. I am be tter” and so forth. If we exam ine our mind, we will recognize 
that we have pride. The Treasury of Manifest Dharma  sets out three, seven, and nine types of 
pride, which you can read about.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1D Ignorance 
Ignorance is an afflicted non-knowing, due to an unclear mind, with regard to the 
nature of the four truths, actions and their results, and the Three Jewels.  
 
Ignorance is a non-knowing, due to an unclear mind, with respect to the four truths (true 
sufferings, true origins, true cessations, and tr ue paths), actions and their results (that virtue 
brings happiness and non-virtue brings suffe ring), and the nature of the Three Jewels 
(Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha).  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1E Doubt 
Doubt, through observing those three [objec ts], the four truths, etc. thinks “Do 
they exist or do they not exist?  Are they or are they not?”  
 
Doubt is a wondering with respect to the four noble truths, actions and results, and the Three 
Jewels as to whether they exist or  not, or whether they are true or not. It is a lack of clarity. 
Even thinking “Is this road the right one, or is it that other one?” is a kind of doubt. Due to 
it, we will not even reach our destination. Do ubt is likened wishing to go to India but 
thinking “Maybe I will go to India. Maybe I will  go to the United States. Maybe I will go to 
South America.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1F The view of the transitory collection 
The view of the transitory collection: an afflicted wisdom that, observing the 
appropriated aggregates, views them as a self or of the self thinking ‘I’ or ‘mine.’ 
Here ‘transitory’ means impermanent and ‘collection’ many, therefore the base 
that it views is a mere impermanent and manifold phenomenon. In order to 
indicate “There is no permanent unitary pe rson,” it is given th e name ‘view of the 
transitory collection.’ 
 
The view of the transitory collection is an affl icted wisdom that observes the aggregates, its 
observed object, and thinks “I” and “mine.” Beca use the observed object is transitory it is 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
437 called the view of the transitory  collection, and because the aggreg ates are a collection of many 
parts it is called the view of the transitory collection . Thus, its object is an impermanent 
collection, not a permanent and unitary person . Such a permanent unitary person does not 
exist. Thus, the person or being is empty of being permanent, partless, and unitary. Instead it 
exists in dependence on being imputed on  the aggregates which are an impermanent 
collection. Therefore, in order to indicate that  its basis of imputation is not permanent and 
unitary, it is called “the view of the transitory collection.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1G View holding to an extreme  
The view holding to an extreme is an a fflicted wisdom that, observing that self 
apprehended by the view of the transitory collection, views it to be permanent and 
eternal or views it to cease without join ing this [existence] to a future one.  
 
The view holding to an extreme observes the se lf apprehended by the view of the transitory 
collection to be permanent and unchanging, or vie ws it to not join to a future life, that is, it 
views the consciousness or the being as not conne cting to another rebirth. Thus, it is divided 
into the view of the extreme of permanence an d the view of the extreme of annihilation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1H [The view ] holding to a [bad] view as best  
[The view] holding to a [bad] view as best is an afflicted wisdom that, observing 
any of the three – the view of the transi tory collection, the view holding to an 
extreme, or a wrong view – and the aggregat es of the person holding the view that 
arises in dependence on any of them, holds them to be best. 
 
The view holding a bad view as supreme takes as  its observed object either the view of the 
transitory collection, the view holding to an extr eme, or a wrong view as well as the aggregates 
of the person holding the view that arises in dependence on any of them, and holds them to 
be the best.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1I [The view ] holding to [a bad] ethical discipline and ascetic practice as best 
[The view] holding to [a bad] ethical discipline and ascetic practice as best is an 
afflicted wisdom that, observing an ethical discipline of giving up immorality, an 
ascetic practice that sets a dress code, manners, and bodily and verbal behavior as 
definite, and {139} the aggregates that aris e in dependence on either of them, and 
views them as purifying negativities, as li berating from the mental afflictions, and 
definitely releasing one from cyclic existence. 
 
Morality or ethical discipline is the abando nment of immorality or non-virtue. Ascetic 
practice or conduct refers to external behavi ors of body and speech. The view holding bad 
morality and conduct as supreme think that  immorality and bad conduct can purify 
negativities, liberate from the mental afflictions, and definitely release one from cyclic 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
438 existence. An example of this vi ew is to think that undergoing a lot of hardships is the correct 
practice of morality or good behavior of body  and speech and that it can bring realizations. 
Such views are incorrect.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B1J Wrong view 
A wrong view is an afflicted wisdom that deprecates saying that past and future 
lives, actions and their effects and the like do not exist, or superimposes the 
concept of Ishvara, a primal substance, etc. as the cause of migrating beings. 
 
A wrong view is a view that holds that the law of actions and results, the Three Jewels, past 
and future lives do not exist, or that holds that  the creator of migrating beings is Ishvara, a 
primordial principal, and so forth. Thus, it de precates the existence of that which exists or 
superimposes existence on that which does not ex ist. There is a school that holds that Ishvara 
is the creator and that just by his thinking ab out something, such as good food, it arises. 
Others, such as the Samkhyas, assert that a prim ordial principal is the creator of the entire 
world  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
These are how the mental afflictions are identified from the common perspective 
of the higher and lower tenet systems. Th e Prasangika Madhyamika system will be 
explained below.  
 
This exposition of the mental afflictions is from the point of view that is common to the 
higher and lower systems.   (Oral transmission 7:05-7:25, pp. 139-148)  Tuesday morning, 19 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 139) says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B2 The stages by which [the mental afflictions ] arise 
A The system of the Treatise on the Levels  asserts that the view of the transitory collection and 
ignorance are in opposition to each other 
Second: If the view of the transitory co llection and ignorance are asserted to be 
different [the stages by which the mental afflictions arise must be understood in 
the following manner]. For instance, with regard to a coiled rope, once darkness 
has fallen to some extent, if the mode of ab idance of the rope is not clear, a mind 
apprehending a snake arises. Likewise , through the darkness of ignorance 
obscuring the clear mode of abidance of the aggregates, the aggregates mistakenly 
arise as a self, and from that the other mental afflictions arise.  
 
There are different tenet systems, some that asse rt that the view of the transitory collection 
and ignorance are different and some that assert  that they are synonyms. The Five Treatises 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
439 on the Levels by Asanga assert that they are di fferent saying that, for example, just as in the 
darkness a coiled rope is mistakenly apprehended to be a snake due to not seeing it clearly, 
similarly, due to ignorance obscuring the mode of  abidance of the aggregates, there arises the 
conception that the aggregates are, for example,  a self-supporting substantially existent self. 
Then based on this ignorance, the ot her mental afflictions are produced.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The system of the Madhyamikas and Dharmakirti assert that the view of the transitory collection itself 
is ignorance  
If those two are asserted to be the same, th e view of the transitory collection itself 
is the root of the mental afflictions. 
 
The Prasangika Madhyamikas and Dharmakirti in his Compendium of Valid Cognition  assert 
that the view of the transitory collection and ignorance are the same, me aning that the root of 
all the mental afflictions is the view of the tran sitory collection. How is this? First the view of 
the transitory collection holds to an “I” that  is self-supporting substantially existent or 
inherently existent. On the basi s of this, there arises the view of the transitory collection 
holding to “mine,” and then, from that, attachme nt to what is ours and anger or hatred for 
what is other arise.  
 Asanga instead says that first the ignorance ob scuring the nature of the aggregates or self 
arises, then there arises the apprehension of  I and mine, and then, from this, the other 
mental afflictions are produced. However, si nce in the context of the twelve links of 
dependent-arising it is said that the first link is ignorance, it is from  ignorance that all the 
other afflictions arise.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How the remaining root mental afflictions arise according to the two systems  
Furthermore, when the view of the transi tory collection apprehends a self, an 
arbitrary distinction is made between self and other. Once that is made, 
attachment toward one’s own side and hatr ed toward the other side arise. Also 
conceit arises through observing that self; th at very self is held to be permanent or 
annihilated and is viewed as the self, etc., and the bad activities connected with it 
are held to be the best. Likewise, wrong views that think “The Teacher who taught selflessness, the actions and results taug ht by him, the four truths, the Three 
Jewels, etc. do not exist” are generated,  {140} or, alternatively, the doubt that 
thinks “Do they exist or not? Are they or are they not real?” is  generated. In the 
Commentary  [on Valid Cognition ] it says:  
 
If there is self, there’s awareness of other. Dividing between them brings grasping and anger. Closely connected with those [two] – All the faults will come about. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
440 There are two systems: one that asserts the view of the transitory collection and ignorance to 
be different and one that asserts them to be different. However, according to both systems, 
when the view of the transitory collection ho lds to “I” and “mine,” a distinction is made 
between one’s own side and others’ side. Then, attachment for one’s own side, for example, 
one’s friends and relatives, and hatred or anger for the other side, for example, one’s enemies, 
are produced. Also the pride thinking “I am…” is produced. Then the object of the view of 
the transitory collection, a self-supporting substant ially existent I, is held to either be eternal 
and unchanging or to be non-existent. Then, th e view holding a bad view – the view holding 
the view of the transitory collection, the view of an extreme, or wrong view – as supreme is 
produced. Also, the view holding bad morality or  bad conduct as supreme and as the cause of 
achieving nirvana is produced. Also the wrong vi ew that thinks that the law of actions and 
their results, past and future lives, and the Three Jewels do not exist is produced. 
Alternatively, the doubt wondering whether the law of actions and their results, past and 
future lives, and the Three Jewels exist are not is produced.  
 The Commentary  on Valid Cognition says that first the view of the transitory collection in 
one’s continuum thinking that the I is self-suppo rting substantially existent arises. Then, from 
that, there arises “mine,” after which there arises  attachment to one’s own side and hatred for 
the others’ side. Then, in connection with attachment and hatred, all the other mental 
afflictions or faults arise.  
 Since the mental afflictions arise in this way,  we should not examine what others are doing 
but should examine our own mind to see what ki nd of mind is arising in relation to what 
kind of object. When we see th at attachment, anger, or other mental afflictions have arisen, 
thinking that they disturb us and other people , we should apply their antidotes. If we are 
unable to apply their antidotes, we should at least try to block them so that they do not 
become manifest. All the problems of human beings including disagreements and fighting 
between two friends, which lead to separation, even beating each other, and sometimes even 
killing each other, happen because of the afflicti ons. Also the fact that people try to cheat and 
harm others in order to gain something for themselves comes about due to the mental afflictions. The mental afflictions DO NOT brin g us happiness. If we examine this, we will 
find that it is 100% certain that this does not happen. They only bring us all the sufferings of 
cyclic existence. Therefore, we should not examine others but sh ould examine ourselves. If we 
were government spies and received a salary for spying on others such behavior would be fine, but, since we are not, we should refrain from l ooking at others and instead look inward at our 
own mind.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-1B3 The faults of the mental afflictions 
A How to see the mental afflictions as enemies due to their great shortcomings  
Third: In Ornament of the Sutras  it says:  
 
The mental afflictions destroy yourself, destroy sentient beings, and 
destroy ethical discipline. 
Once that is debased, you become inferior and guardians and teachers 
revile you. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
441 There will be nasty disputes, you wi ll take rebirth elsewhere in unfree 
states. 
Through the debasement of what you’ ve attained or not [yet] attained, 
there is great mental suffering. 
 
The Ornament for the Mahayana Sutras  says that we should see the mental afflictions as our 
enemies in that they destroy us, that is, th ey destroy our mental happiness and may also 
destroy our body by producing sickness in it. The mental afflictions also destroy other 
sentient beings in that they too are overcome by them, due to which they engage in actions 
that lead to the experience of all types of suffe ring. In this way, the mental afflictions destroy 
the happiness of all sentient beings.   The mental afflictions also destroy morality  or ethical discipline. For example, when 
attachment to sexual intercourse arises strong ly in someone who has taken the vows of a fully 
ordained monk or nun or the vows of a novi ce monk or nun, he may break his vows and 
thereby destroy his morality. Due to deteriorating or degenerating our morality, we will become inferior, due to which we will be despised  by our guardians and teachers, that is, they 
will no longer be able to take care of us due to our bad behavior. There will be unpleasant quarrels in this life, and in our next life we wi ll be born in a leisureless state. That which we 
have attained and that which we  wish to attain will degenerate , due to which intense mental 
unhappiness will arise.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And in Engaging in  [Bodhisattva ] Behavior : 
 
Enemies such as hatred and craving, 
Do not have arms or legs nor other [limbs], They have no valor ei ther, so why is it 
That they have reduced me to servitude? 
 
Our enemies, attachment and anger and the other afflictions, lack arms and legs and are not 
intelligent, yet in spite of this we have become  their servants. In other words, we follow them 
without the slightest doubt, for example, un der the power of attachment we follow our 
attachment and under the power of anger we foll ow our anger, and so forth to the point that, 
unable to achieve what we want, we may even commit suicide  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
While residing in my mind they keep 
Inflicting harm on me who lets them be. But the patience that’s not angry at them Is unsuitable patience – the object of blame. 
 Our real enemies, attachment, anger, and so fo rth, abide within our own mind and harm us. 
Therefore, if there exists somethin g that we should get angry at, it  is our mental afflictions. It 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
442 is them that we should strive to eliminate.  On the other hand, if having come under the 
control of the mental afflictions, we bear them with patience, this is not real patience. If we 
want to practice patience, it should be with re spect to other sentient be ings, not with our real 
enemy, the mental afflictions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Even if the gods and demigods 
Were all to rise as enemies against me, They would not be able to lead or put me Into the fires of the Incessant Hell. 
 Even if all the gods and demigods were to aris e as our enemies, they would not be able to 
throw us into the hell realm, hung ry ghost realm, or animal realm. In fact, even if all sentient 
beings were to arise as our enemies, they would not be able to throw us into the bad 
migrations. It is only attachment, hatred, and the other mental afflicti ons that can throw us 
into the hell realm, hungry ghost realm, and an imal realm. Therefore, we need to recognize 
who our real enemies are.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
However, the mental afflictions, these powerful foes, 
Within a single moment they throw me in there, Where whatever meets with it, even Mount Meru, [Is burnt such that] nothing is left – not even ashes. 
 The mental afflictions, our powerful enemies, ar e like the fire at the end of the eon, which 
destroys the entire world such that even ashe s do not remain. Because the mental afflictions 
have this power, we should strive to abandon ev en a single instant of them. In this way we will 
one day be able to eliminate them completely.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Compared to those foes of mine, the mental afflictions, 
Which have been with me without beginning nor end, 
None of all the other enemies Would be able to last for such a long time. 
 While the mental afflictions have existed sinc e beginningless time and are without end, no 
other enemies are like this since they do not last for such a long time. While our external 
enemies change and are not always our enemies,  the mental afflictions are at all times our 
enemies. If we examine our mind even for one week to see whether or not the mental 
afflictions arise, we will find that they aris e constantly. However, while attachment is often 
present, anger is more occasional. We get angr y even if someone says “You have a big nose” 
and we do, in fact, have a big nose!  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
443  We should examine whether attachment, anger,  ignorance, pride, doubt, or an afflicted 
view such as the view of the transitory collection,  the view of an extreme, and so forth arise in 
our mind. We should also examine whether the secondary mental afflictions such as 
belligerence, resentment, spite, jealousy, non-co nscientiousness and so forth arise. When we 
see that one of them has arisen, we should apply its antidote. In this way our mental 
continuum will become softer and clearer, our good qualities will increase, and our faults and 
bad behavior will diminish.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Everyone benefits you and makes you happy, 
When you have acted with re verence and in agreement. 
If you have attended to mental afflictions, They inflict harmful suffering on you in return. 
 You should reflect on the shortcomings stated here. {141} 
 If we try to live in harmony with and help and be nefit others, we will be able to live together 
in mutual comfort and happiness. On the other ha nd, if we rely on the mental afflictions, 
t h e y  w i l l  h a r m  u s  a n d  o n l y  l e a d  t o  s u f f e r i n g.  Therefore, the mental afflictions are our real 
enemies, like fire they destroy our good qualit ies. Hence, we should strive to abandon the 
mental afflictions by meditating on selflessness, emptiness, and so forth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Applying the cultivation of antidotes to the words of former saints  
Gonpapa said:  
 
In order to abandon the mental afflictions it is necessary to know their 
shortcomings, their characteristics, their antidotes, and the causes for 
their arisal. Once you have underst ood their shortcomings, you should 
see them as drawbacks, apprehend them as enemies. If you do not 
understand their shortcomings, you will not understand them to be 
enemies. Therefore, you should cons ider them along the lines of the 
statements from Ornament of the Sutras  and Engaging in [Bodhisattva ] 
Behavior . 
 
In order to abandon the mental afflictions we need to understand their shortcomings, their 
definitions or characteristics, th eir antidotes, and the causes for their arisal. Having seen their 
shortcomings, we should see them to be defect s and hold them to be our real enemies. On 
the other hand, if we do not understand the sh ortcomings of the mental afflictions, we will 
not be able to recognize them as our enemies. We should think about th em as set forth in the 
Ornament of the Mahayana Sutras , which was cited above as saying that the mental afflictions 
destroy us, other sentient beings, an d our morality, and as set forth in Engaging in the 
Bodhisattva Deeds , which was cited above as saying that even though the mental afflictions do 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
444 not have arms and legs and are not intelligent, still we are overcome by them and become 
their servants.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And:  
 
In order to know the characteristic s of the mental afflictions, you 
should also study abhidharma, at least the Differentiation of the Five 
Aggregates . Once you have become acquainted with the root and 
secondary afflictions, you will identify attachment, hatred, and so forth as soon as they arise in your cont inuum, thinking “This is that. Oh no! 
It has arisen,” and will fight the mental afflictions. 
 
This kind of understanding is necessary.  
 
We need to understand the definitions or charac teristics of the mental afflictions by listening 
to teachings on and studying the Abhidharma . If we cannot study all the Abhidharma, we 
should at least listen to teachings on or read and study the short text called Differentiation of 
the Five Aggregates . We need to familiarize with the slight ly different presentations of the root 
and secondary afflictions as set out in the two Abhidharmas, the higher and lower. Asanga’s 
Compendium of Manifest Dharma , the higher Abhidharma, sets out six root afflictions – 
attachment, anger, ignorance, pride, doubt,  and afflicted view – and twenty secondary 
afflictions, such as belligerence and so forth. Having become familiar with them, when any of them arises we will recognize it and will fight it  with our wisdom in order to destroy it. This 
should be our main aim. For example, we shou ld meditate on attachment, examining how it 
thinks of the object as nice and beautiful, th at is, how it exaggerates its beauty, and then 
thinks, “It is beautiful. Oh, it is really beauti ful!” To counteract this attachment, we should 
meditate on ugliness by, for example, imagining the body to be a rotten corpse or a skeleton. 
Various ways to do this in relation to different colors and so forth are taught in the Treasury of 
Manifest Dharma . For example, with respect to the medita tion on skeletons, we imagine that a 
piece of flesh falls from between our eyebrows, revealing the bone below, and then slowly all 
our flesh and skin dissolve until we are only  a skeleton. Then we imagine that the bones 
spread out further and further, until the whole world is covered with skeletons. We should 
imagine both our own and others’ bodies as skel etons without flesh, skin, and so forth. This 
meditation can help to reduce our attachment to our own and others’ bodies. Alternatively, 
Shantideva, in the ei ghth chapter of his Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds , says that we should 
meditate on the fact that our own and others’ bodies are a collection of thirty-six impure 
substances – the skin being like a bag that co ntains many filthy and undesirable substances! 
By meditating on this, our strong a ttachment to bodies will diminish.  
 When we are angry at another sentient being,  we should meditate on love, thinking “May 
he or she have happiness and the causes of ha ppiness.” All sentient beings are similar in 
wanting happiness and the causes of happiness, and in not wanting suffe ring and the causes 
of suffering. We should meditate on all sentient beings as having an attr active aspect and wish 
them happiness and the causes of happiness, ther eby generating love for them. When love is 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
445 manifest in our mind, anger disappears since love is its antidote. Another antidote to anger is 
patience; we also need to develop it.  
 When ignorance arises, we should meditate on dependent-arising or dependent-relation. 
We should think that since all functioning th ings depend on causes and conditions, they 
cannot arise without them. For example, in orde r for smoke to arise, its cause, fire, is 
necessary. In addition, the conditions of wood, fu el, incense, and so fort h are necessary for its 
arisal. Thus, only when the causes and conditions  are complete, does a result arise. Likewise, 
things exist in dependence on th eir parts, for example, a table de pends on four legs and a flat 
support. If one leg is broken, it will not function as a table. Therefore, things depend on their 
parts. Therefore, things are not inherently or  self-supporting substantially existent because 
they arise in dependence. We should try to medi tate on selflessness or emptiness in this way. 
But first we should understand and meditate on dependent-arising.  
 Also, all phenomena depend on being merely imputed by name. In other words, they are 
dependent on being imputed by the mind. In fact, everything is imputed by the mind. 
However, it is important to un derstand that a valid base for the imputation must exist; for 
example, we cannot impute “human being” on th e body of cat, or impute “man” on the body 
of a woman, because they are no t valid bases of imputation fo r those names. In short, all 
phenomena are not inherently, truly, or self-su pporting substantially existent, rather they are 
dependent-relations. Things are not inherently  or self-supporting substantially existent 
because they arise in dependence. We should try to meditate on selflessness or emptiness like 
this.   In this way, we should apply the respective antidotes to the mental afflictions. However, 
the meditation on emptiness is the actual antidote  to all the mental afflictions because it cuts 
the very root of cyclic existenc e – ignorance or the view of the transitory collection; just as, 
only when we cut the roots of a tree, does the tree with all its branches die. Therefore, we 
need to meditate on emptiness. How can we fight the mental afflic tions? We cannot fight 
them with a hammer, nor even with an at omic bomb. While such weapons can destroy 
human beings by separating their mind and body , they cannot destroy the mental afflictions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-2 How karma is accumulated by [the mental afflictions ] 
A Identifying the karma that is accumulated  
B How [karma] is accumulated 
The second one has two points: identifyin g the karma that is accumulated, and 
how it is accumulated. 
 
We need to identify the karma that is accumula ted and to understand how it is accumulated. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-2A Identifying the karma that is accumulated 
1 Action that is intention 
2 Intended actions 
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-2A1 Action that is intention 
First: The action that is intention – the mental factor that makes its concomitant 
mind move and urges it toward an object – is action of mind.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
446  
Intention is action of mind. It is what moves the primary mind that is concomitant with it 
toward an object, for example, toward the side of virtue, toward the side of non-virtue, and 
toward the neutral.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-2A2  Intended actions 
A The actions that have actually been intended 
First: The actions of body and speech th at are motivated by that intention… 
 
Actions of body and speech arise motivated by intention. These actions of body and speech 
are what are called “intended actions.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Their entity 
… are divided into the revelatory and non- revelatory and posited as only having 
form by the Vaibhashikas. Master Vasuba ndhu refutes that and maintains that 
[actions of body and speech ] are the intentions that operate together with the 
revelatory physical and verbal actions, whereby he explains both actions to be 
intention.163  
 
Actions of body can either be revelatory or  non-revelatory, whereas actions of speech are 
always revelatory. Killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct are non-virtuous actions created by the body, whereas the abandonments of killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct are virtuous 
actions created by the body. Lying, divisive spee ch, harsh words, and idle talk are non-virtuous 
actions created by speech, whereas their abando nments are virtuous actions created by the 
speech. These actions of body and speech are moti vated by intention, that is, by action of 
mind.   The Vaibhashikas assert that actions of body  and speech are form, either revelatory or non-
revelatory form. Revelatory form is that whic h reveals the motivation, good or bad, behind 
the action, for example, a nice, pleasant expre ssion of the face reveals a good motivation, 
whereas a red, angry expression  reveals a bad motivation. In other words, the external 
appearance of the face and body reveals the i nner motivation that ca nnot be seen with the 
eyes. Therefore, non-revelatory form is a form  that does not reveal the inner motivation. 
While it cannot be seen with the eyes, accordin g to the Vaibhashikas it exists. For example, 
they say that in the first instant of vows such as those of full or dination, novices, and so forth, 
a revelatory form arises and in the second  instant a non-revelatory form arises.  
 Tuesday afternoon, 19 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
                                             
 
163 He thereby emphasizes that the karmic action itself which is decisive for its effect consists in the intention – 
see also note 24 [*] for the exceptions of accumulated karma 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
447 C Identifying three kinds of action  
In this regard non-virtuous actions are non-meritorious actions. Meritorious 
actions are virtuous actions included in  the level of the desire realm. {142} 
Immovable actions are contaminated virtuous  actions included in the levels of the 
form and formless realms. 
 
There are three kinds of action s: meritorious, non-meritorious, and immovable actions. Non-
virtuous actions are non-meritorious actions.  Meritorious and non-meritorious actions are 
projecting actions that project rebirth in the desire realm. Immovable actions are projecting 
actions that project rebirth in the form and formless realms, thus they are contaminated 
virtuous actions included in the level of the form and formless realms. In other words, non-
meritorious actions are projecting actions that project rebirth in the three lower realms – the 
hell realm, hungry ghost realm, and animal real m. Meritorious actions are projecting actions 
that project rebirth in the three higher realms as a human being of the four continents or as 
one of six types of desire realm gods. Immovabl e actions are projecting  actions that project 
rebirth in the seventeen levels of the form real m and the four levels of the formless realm.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-2B How [karma] is accumulated 
1 Explaining who accumulates the karma to be thrown [into a rebirth] in cyclic existence 
Second: Once you have directly realized selflessness, you can still be reborn in 
cyclic existence under the power of karma and mental afflictions, however you do 
not newly accumulate any throwing karma.  Therefore, those who accumulate the 
karma to be thrown into cyclic existe nce are all ordinary beings up to and 
including those abiding on the great [level of] highest [mundane] qualities of the 
Mahayana path of preparation. 
 
The above statement needs to be examined. Wh ich persons accumulate projecting karma to 
take rebirth in cyclic existenc e? The text says that arya bein gs who have directly realized 
selflessness can take rebirth in cyclic existence by the force of previously created actions and 
afflictions, but they do not newly accumulate pr ojecting karma. It also says that those who 
accumulate the projecting karma for taking rebirt h in cyclic existence are all ordinary beings 
including those abiding on the heat, peak, tolera nce, and supreme mundane qualities levels of 
the path of preparation. Each of these four le vels is further divided into the small, middle, 
and great, due to which there are twelve levels, the last being “the great level of the supreme 
mundane qualities level.” All these beings still cr eate projecting karma to be reborn in cyclic 
existence. However, it is said elsewhere that in the second in stant of the great level of the 
supreme mundane qualities level of the path of preparation, one achieves the path of seeing. 
Since this occurs in the same meditation session , also those who are abiding on the great level 
of the supreme mundane qualities level would no t create projecting karma. Thus, it would 
only be ordinary beings who are abiding on  the middle level of the supreme mundane 
qualities level and below that create projecting  karma. In addition, th ere are also ordinary 
beings who have been reborn in pure lands su ch as Sukhavati who do not create projecting 
karma to be reborn in the places of cyclic existe nce. I think it is like this since in Sukhavati, 
the Blissful Pure Land, not even the word “s uffering” exists, let alone actual suffering.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
448  According to the lower tenets, that is, fr om the Svatantrika Madhyamaka downward, foe-
destroyers with remainder have a contaminated  body made of bones, flesh, and blood, and 
only when they pass away and achieve a nirvana without remainder do they no longer have a 
contaminated flesh body, but attain a mental  body. However, the Prasangika Madhyamikas 
assert a nirvana with remainder and a nirvana without remainder differently. They say that 
those who still have the appearance of true ex istence have a nirvana with remainder, whereas 
those who do not have the appearan ce of true existence, but have a mind that is in meditative 
equipoise on emptiness, like water poured in to water, have a nirvana without remainder 
because they do not have the remainder that  is the appearance of true existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 How [karma] is accumulated by that [person] 
If, with that basis, you create non-virtuous  actions by means of the three doors, for 
instance killing, you will accumulate non-me ritorious karma. If you create virtuous 
actions, such as giving gifts and guarding ethical discipline, you will accumulate 
meritorious karma. If you cultivate the calm abiding and so forth included in the levels of the concentrations and form less absorptions, you will accumulate 
immovable karma. 
 That we create actions by me ans of the three doors, body, speech, and mind, means, for 
example, that we engage in killing, stealing , and sexual misconduct by means of the body; 
lying, divisive speech, harsh words, and idle talk by means of the speech; and covetousness, 
malice, and wrong view by means of mind. Ther eby, we accumulate no n-meritorious actions. 
If, on the other hand, we engage in genero sity, safeguard morality, and so forth, we 
accumulate meritorious actions. If we cultivate the calm abiding and so forth included in the 
levels of the concentrations and formless realm absorptions, we accumulate immovable 
karma. In addition, the special insight included in the level of a preparation also serves as a 
projecting action for taking rebirth in the form or formless realms. In other words, in order to 
accumulate immovable karma, we need to ha ve achieved both calm abiding and special 
insight. This is not easy, whereas even now we can practice generosity, morality, patience, and 
so forth. Therefore, we should put effort into these perfections as they are easier to do than 
concentration and wisdom. In this way, we will accumulate meritorious actions that will act as 
the cause for being reborn as a human being or a god of the desire real m. The best of human 
rebirths is the precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities. In fact, a body born 
from the womb and composed of the six constitu ents is said to be the best body for the 
practice of tantra and therefore for the quic k attainment of buddhah ood. The body of a god 
of the desire realm, on the other hand, is not like this. Therefore, am ong the many types of 
rebirth, that of a human being is the best , and among the human beings of the four 
continents, rebirth as a human be ing of the southern continent is  the best. This is because the 
human beings of the other continents have a fixed lifespan due to which it is not easy for them to generate the mind of renunciation or definite emerge nce, whereas the human beings 
of Jambudvipa experience problems and their lifesp an is not fixed, due to which it is easier to 
generate renunciation of cyclic existence and the wi sh to attain nirvana.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
449 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3 How you die-transfer and join  
A Conditions for death  
B The mind of death 
C From where the heat withdraws 
D How the intermediate state is accomplished after death 
E How you take rebirth in a birth-existence 
The third one has five points: conditions fo r death, the mind of death, from where 
the heat withdraws, how the intermediate state is accomplished after death, and 
how you take rebirth in a birth-existence. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3A Conditions for death 
1 Death following the exhaustion of one’s lifespan 
To die due to the exhaustion of one’s lifesp an is to die when the time has come in 
which one’s entire lifespan projected by previous karma is exhausted. 
2 Death following the exhaustion of one’s merit 
To die following the exhaustion of one’s me rit is the same as dying from a lack of 
basic requirements.  
3 Death due to dangers not being avoided  
To die due to not avoiding danger is the same as the nine causes and conditions 
for death, such as due to overeating, set out in the sutras,.  
 
What are the conditions for death? There are three: 
1. exhaustion of our lifespan, 
2. exhaustion of our merit, and 
3. not avoiding danger. 
Some people have a short lifespan. Why is this? It is because in a previous rebirth they died 
before their lifespan was exhausted and then, having taken rebirth again as a human being, 
they have only the lifespan remaining to live. The second condition for death is that one takes 
rebirth as a human being due to having created the merit to do so, but then dies when this merit is exhausted.   The third condition for death is to die due to dangers that take our life such as car 
accidents, a fall from a great height, hurricanes, fl oods, fire, earthquakes, and so forth. In fact 
there are many such conditions that can take ou r life and that we are unable to abandon or 
avoid. For example, we can also die due to in gesting poison put in our food by someone who 
does not like us! Thus, there are many conditio ns that can take our life including the nine 
causes and conditions that can take life, such as overeating, that are set out in the sutras.  
 It is advised that when traveling in Bhutan to  avoid stopping at houses  with a black flag on 
the roof and that if someone from such a house gives you food or drink to not eat or drink it. 
Such people think that by killing a person who still has merit to live, they can acquire their 
merit for themselves. However, this is a misund erstanding. Such people practice the Deity of 
Poison and believe that if they do not succeed in giving the pois on to someone else, they must 
ingest it themselves.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
450  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3B The mind of death 
1 The mind of death 
2 Their appearances 
3 For all three minds of death there is joining due to craving  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3B1 The mind of death 
A [Virtuous] 
B [Non-virtuous] 
C [Unspecified] 
Both virtuous minds, such as confidence, and non-virtuous minds, such as 
attachment, {143} arise in the mind, in de pendence on one’s own actions or being 
made to recall them by others, as long as gross compositional factors operate. To 
die with an unspecified mind implies th at one did not recall virtuous or non-
virtuous minds by oneself nor was one reminded of them by others. 
 If, when we are dying, we remember by ourselve s or someone else reminds us of our faith in 
the Three Jewels or our spiritual teacher, in a particular practice, and so forth, we will 
generate a coarse virtuous mind. If, on the ot her hand, when we are dying, we remember by 
ourselves or someone else reminds us of an obje ct of attachment, such as our family, wealth, 
and so forth, strong attachment will arise in our mind, whereby the mind of death will be a 
non-virtuous mind. This latter, due to a strong projecting karma, can project us into a rebirth 
in the lower realms.   If we neither remember virtuous or non-vi rtuous minds ourselves and no one reminds us 
of them, our mind of death will be unspecified, that is, neutral. Thus, when we die, although 
the subtle mind of death is always unspecifie d, the coarse mind of death can either be 
virtuous due to generating faith in the Thr ee Jewels and so forth, non-virtuous due to 
generating attachment for our friends, relati ves, wealth, and so forth, or neutral.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3B2 Their appearances 
A [The appearance due to a virtuous mind]  
B [The appearance due to a non-virtuous mind] 
C [The appearance due to an unspecified mind] 
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3B2A [The appearance due to a virtuous mind] 
 For those who have created virtue it is like going from thick darkness into light. 
At the time of death, various pleasant dr eam-like forms appear to them, they die 
happily, and even the interruption of life is of very minor severity.  
 
For those who have created virtue, when they di e it is like going from thick darkness into 
luminous light, like seeing very pleasant forms in a dream, the mind is happy like waking up after a good dream, and the suffering of the interruption of life will be very small.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3B2B  [The appearance due to a non-virtuous mind] 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
451 1 The actual [appearance] 
For those who have created non-virtue, it  is like going from light into thick 
darkness. At the time of death, various dr eam-like repulsive forms appear to them, 
intense suffering feeling arises, and the interruption of life is also very severe. 
 
If we have created non-virtuous ac tions, at the time of death it will be like going from a place 
of light to one of complete darkness. It will also be like seeing forms with a terrible aspect in a dream, due to which our mind is unhappy an d disturbed when we wake up. There will be 
intense suffering feeling but it will be too late to do anything about it . Nowadays, even though 
there are many medical innovations such as inject ions of pain-killers that can relieve our pain, 
still we will experience both physical suffering and great mental suffering at the time of death 
if we have created non-virtuous actions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Clearing up doubts concerning the interruption of life 
The interruption of life exists for all birth-sources excluding the gods and hell 
beings.  
 
The interruption of life (Tib. gnad gcod ) refers to the severe pain that  arises at death. This type 
of suffering does not exist for gods and hell beings. This is because hell beings always 
experience suffering, that is, there is never any interruption to their suffering, whereas the 
gods have a fixed lifespan and therefore thei r lives are not in danger and they do not 
experience this suffering. However, they do have  great mental suffering at the time of death 
due to seeing, by means of their clairvoyance, where they will be reborn. All other sentient 
beings, including animals, hungry ghosts, and human beings,  have this suffering.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3B2C [The appearance due to an unspecified mind] 
1 The actual [appearance] 
In those who have an unspecified mind, the two, happiness and suffering, that 
were explained above do not arise. 
 
At the time of death, those who have an unspec ified or neutral mind do not experience either 
the happiness or the suffering that were explained before.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 What mind becomes manifest at death  
At the time of death, whichever mind, virt uous or non-virtuous, is most familiar 
becomes manifest, whereas other minds do not subsequently operate. If they are 
equally familiar, that which is recalled first will become manifest, whereas others 
do not subsequently operate.  
 
What kinds of mind becomes manifest at the time  of death? Either a virtuous mind or a non-
virtuous mind can become manifest, and whic h one becomes manifest depends on which one 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
452 is more familiar. At that time other minds do no t operate, that is, are not manifest. If one is 
equally familiar with virtuous minds and non- virtuous minds, whiche ver one is remembered 
first will become manifest and other minds will  not operate, that is, will not manifest.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 All the subtle minds of death are unspecified 
At the time when the mind moves to its subtle part, virtuous and non-virtuous 
minds come to a stop and become an unspecified mind. 
 
The subtle mind of death is always an unspecif ied or neutral mind. In other words, it cannot 
be either a virtuous mind or a non-virtuous mind. Why is this? It is because virtuous and non-
virtuous minds are coarse and therefore cannot act as the subtle mind of death. If, at the time 
of death, someone reminds us of our main medi tational deity, for example, the coarse mind 
of death will be virtuous. Due to this it is 100%  certain that we will ta ke a good rebirth. There 
is a story about a brahmin wh o, when dying, happened to see a very nice elephant kitten  and, 
due to thinking that it was very nice , died and was born as an elephant kitten  to elephant 
parents, even though he had created the karma to be reborn as a human being!  
 
We should strive to take a good rebirth. For example, at the time of death we could imagine 
gods or beautiful human beings and think that it  would be nice to be reborn as one of them, 
whereby we will take a good rebirth. It is like  when we see something terrible during the day, 
and then we also have a nightmare that night.  Or like when we see something good during 
the day, and that night we have a good dr eam. This is all due to familiarity.  
 Wednesday morning, 20 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 143) says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3B3 For all three minds of death there is joining due to craving 
For all, at the time of death, as long as [the state of] unclear discrimination has not 
been reached, there arises attachment to a self which has long been familiarized 
with. {144} After that, under the influence of  attachment to a self, thinking “I will 
become non-existent,” delight in a body ar ises. It is the cause for the intermediate 
state to come about. While a ttachment to a self also arises in stream-enterers and 
once-returners, they investigate it with wisdom and abandon it, rather than 
acquiescing to it; just like, for instance, a strong person beating someone weak. In 
non-returners that attachment to a self does not arise. 
 At the time of death, as long as the discriminati on is not unclear, attach ment to the self arises 
due to having familiarized with it for a long time. Then, by the power of attachment to the 
self, thinking “I am dying,” delight in a body  arises and becomes the cause for achieving the 
intermediate state. At the time of death, we ge nerate attachment to the self as well as to our 
body, relatives and friends, and possessions. The thought arises “When I die, I will lose this 
body, my relatives, my belongings, everything,” whereby varying degrees of attachment arise in 
different people. This attachment becomes the ca use for taking the body of the intermediate 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
453 state. The arya beings who are stream-enterers and once-returners also have this attachment to 
the self but it is not strong because they have  the wisdom realizing selflessness and so forth, 
which, for example, is like a strong man beat ing a weak man who is unable to retaliate.  
 Non-returners are those who will not return to  the desire realm, th at is, who will take 
another rebirth in the desire realm. They do not have this attachment to the self. In fact, they 
are not attached to the desirable objects of the desire realm. Because non-returners have 
completely abandoned the afflicti ons of the desire realm, they will not return to it. On the 
other hand, stream-enterers and once-returners wi ll take rebirth again in the desire realm. 
There are stream enterers, for example, who ta ke rebirth seven times as a human being or 
seven times as a god of the desire realm. Once -returners are those who will take one more 
rebirth in the desire realm.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3C From where the heat withdraws  
Third: In those who have created non-virtue the heat  first withdraws from the 
upper part of the corpse and dissipates down to the heart. In those who have 
created virtue the heat first withdraws from the lower part of the corpse and 
dissipates up to the heart. [In both cases ] the consciousness transfers from the 
heart. That which the consciousness fi rst entered into in the middle of the 
[mixture of] semen and [menstrual] blood transforms into the heart, and where it 
eventually transfers from is that very place it first entered into. 
 At the time of death, for those who have ma inly created non-virtuous actions the heat 
withdraws from the upper part of  the corpse down to the hear t, whereas for those who have 
mainly created virtuous actions the heat withdr aws from the lower part of the corpse up to 
the heart. Then in both cases the consciousne ss leaves the body at the heart. However, in 
other texts it says that at the time of death the consciousness can leave through the lower part 
of the body, the upper part of the body, or th e heart. Lama Tsongkhapa says that in both 
cases the consciousness transfers from the heart because at the time of  conception the white 
and red fluids of the father and mother mix and later on are found inside the central channel 
at the heart chakra, for which reason the consci ousness later leaves from this same point.  
 The heat of the body, on the ot her hand, is said to dissipate from either the upper part or 
the lower part of the body. If it leaves from the lower part, one will take rebirth in the bad 
migrations, whereas if it leaves from the u pper part, one will take rebirth in the bad 
migrations.   In the case of the practice of transferring the consciousness, one should have previously 
trained in making the consciousness leave from  the upper opening of the central channel in 
the crown.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3D How the intermediate state is reached after death 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
454 1 From where the bardo is accomplished 
Fourth: From the place where the consci ousness transfers as explained above, 
death and the bardo are accomplished withou t a break, just like the swinging of a 
weighing scale.  
 
When we die, at the place where the cons ciousness leaves the body, death and the 
intermediate state are simultaneously accompli shed, like weighing scales in which one pan 
goes up and the other one simultaneously goes do wn when a weight is put on one. If we are 
to be reborn in the desire realm or form real m, there is necessarily an intermediate state 
(bardo) in between. But if we are to be reborn in the formless realm ther e is no need for an 
intermediate state.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The shape of the intermediate state being, etc. 
The intermediate state being moreover has complete sense faculties, such as the 
eyes, and has the aspect of the body of wh atever migrating being it will be born as. 
As long as it does not take rebirth, it s eye is unobstructed like the divine eye,164 and 
the body is also unobstructed as though possessed of magical powers. It is seen by 
intermediate state beings of similar type and by the flawless divine eye arisen from 
mediation. While it is set out in the Treasury  that once the intermediate state of 
any migrating being has been accomplished , there is no diverting it into some 
other rebirth, the Compendium of All  [the Abhidharma ] also explains reversals. 
 
The intermediate state being has complete sense powers, that is, eye, ear, nose, tongue, and 
body sense powers. Its body is similar in shape to the migrating being it will be born as. For 
example, if it is to be reborn as a human bein g, the intermediate state body will have a shape 
similar to that of a human being; whereas if it is to be reborn as a horse, it will be similar in shape to a horse. It is likened to the shadow of our body, which has a shape similar to that of 
our body. As long as it does no t take rebirth, the intermediate state being has the clairvoyance 
of divine eye. Also its body is not obstruct ed but can pass through walls, mountains, and so 
forth, although it is obstructed  by karma to entering the womb of a mother. Its body is similar 
to a mental body in that it can quickly arrive wh erever it wants as if it has miraculous powers.  
 Intermediate state beings are seen by other in termediate state beings of similar type and by 
those human beings who have achieved the cl airvoyance of the divine eye arisen from 
meditation. On the other hand, human beings wh o lack the clairvoyance of the divine eye 
cannot see intermediate state beings.  
 The Treasury of Manifest Dharma  (Abhidharmakosha ) says that having taken the intermediate 
state of a human being, for example, the rebi rth cannot be changed. On the other hand, the 
Compendium of Manifest Dharma  (Abhidharmasamucchaya ) says that the intermediate state body 
of a human being, for example, having been a ccomplished, while there is no rebirth as an 
animal, having taken the intermediate state body  of a donkey, for example, one can then take 
rebirth as another type of anim al. For example, having taken the intermediate state body of a 
                                             
 
164 The special ability to see through matter and over long distance s without obstruction 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
455 bear, one can take rebirth as a dog. The Treasury  says that this is no t possible, that having 
attained the intermediate state body of a dog, one must take rebirth as a dog.  
 It should also be remembered that is possibl e to become a foe-destroyer in the intermediate 
state and not take rebirth at all,  but that is a different case.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 How the appearance of the intermediate existence appears to those who have created virtue and non-
virtue 
The intermediate existence of those who have created non-virtue appears like a 
black cloth or like a dark night, wherea s the intermediate existence of those who 
have created virtue appears like a wh ite blanket {145} or a moonlit night. 
 
Having created non-virtuous actions, the body of the intermediate state being appears to be 
like a black blanket or like a dark moonless nigh t. On the other hand, if the projecting karma 
is virtuous, the body of the intermediate state being appears to be like a white blanket or a 
bright moonlit night.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 How it sees  
It sees intermediate state beings of similar type, as well as their and its own birth-
source.  
 
The intermediate state being sees intermediate stat e beings of similar type, as well as were it 
will take rebirth. It does not see anything else . The intermediate state being, wishing to take 
rebirth, sees the two organs of a man and woman in sexual intercourse. If it is attached to 
rebirth as a male human being, it becomes angr y at the man, whereas if  it is attached to 
rebirth as a female it becomes angry at the woman.  As a result of this anger, it dies and its 
consciousness enters the mixture of the white and red fluids, the semen and menstrual blood. 
Then the skin, blood, and so forth of the fetu s form from the blood of the mother, and its 
bones and so forth from the semen of the father.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 The color 
In the Entry into the Womb  [Sutra ] it says that the intermediate state of hell beings is 
like [the color] of a burnt log, the intermediate st ate of animals is like smoke, the 
intermediate state of hungry ghosts is like  water, the intermediate state of desire 
realm gods and human beings is like go ld, and the intermediate state of form 
realm gods is white165.  
 
The color of the body of intermediate state beings who will become hell beings is like that of a 
blackened burnt log; that of animals is gray like smoke; that of hungry ghosts is like water; 
                                              
165 T h e  c o l o r s  r e f e r  t o  t h e  c o m p l e x i o n  o f  b e i n g s  i n  a given intermediate state (Geshe Thubten Ngawang,

FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
456 that of desire realm gods and human beings is like gold; and that of form realm beings is 
white. The formless realm is not mentioned here because there is no intermediate state in the 
case of rebirth in the formless realm. Instea d, having died, one is immediately reborn in 
Infinite Space, Infinite Consciousness, No thingness, or the Peak of Existence.  
 Having obtained this precious human rebirt h possessing the eighteen qualities, we can 
achieve higher realizations and no longer have to wander in cyclic existence. However, we 
need to put effort into this now since only with  this particular body can we achieve whatever 
we want.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
6 Distinction of whether there is or is not an intermediate state 
If you are reborn from the formless realm into the two lower realms, there is an 
intermediate state. [However,] if you are born from those two [realms] into the 
formless realm, the aggregates of the formless [realm] are accomplished wherever 
you die, and there is no intermediate state.  
 
Here the text clearly states that when we di e from the formless realm and take rebirth in 
either the form realm or desire realm, there is an intermediate state. On the other hand, if we 
die from the form realm or desire realm and are to be reborn in the formless realm, there is 
no intermediate state because when our consciousn ess leaves the body it the four aggregates of 
the formless realm – the feeling aggregate, disc rimination aggregate, compositional factors 
aggregate, and consciousness aggregate – are immediately accomplished.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
7 How it travels 
It is taught that the intermediate state of gods travels upright, that of humans 
travels straight ahead, and that of those who have done faulty actions, with eyes 
cast downward, travel upside down. The intention is similar for the three bad 
migrations.  
 
How do the intermediate state beings travel? Th e intermediate state beings of gods travel 
upright; the intermediate state beings of humans  travel straight ahead; and the intermediate 
state beings of the beings of lower realm travel  with their eyes looking downward and upside 
down, their legs pointing upward and head down , like people who stand on their heads as a 
form of exercise!   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
8 The lifespan 
The lifespan is that if it does not find th e conditions for rebirth, it abides for as 
many as seven days. If it finds them, [the number of days] is uncertain. Also if it 
does not find them, it exchanges bodies and abides up to seven times seven days. 
However, it does not abide any longer than  that since it will definitely find the 
conditions for rebirth before then.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
457  
The lifespan of intermediate state beings is one week, however if the conditions arise for 
rebirth before this, for example, after just one day,  two days, and so forth, it will take rebirth. 
If the conditions for rebirth have not been fo und within seven days, the intermediate state 
being dies and takes another intermediate state body. This happens over and over again for a 
maximum of seven times, by which time it will definitely have found a place to take rebirth. 
Thus, the lifespan of an intermediate state being is only one week. This refers to a week of a 
human being, for example, from Sunday to Satu rday, not the week of a god, which is much 
longer. Thus, when a human being dies, its inte rmediate state dies after seven days. This 
happens up to a maximum of seven weeks at which time the intermediate state being will 
definitely die and be reborn, for example, in the womb of a human being.   Although the body of the in termediate state is exchanged after seven days, the color and 
shape do not change.   
STUDENT : If the intermediate state being canno t change, what can our prayers do for 
someone in the intermediate state? 
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : The assertions regarding the inte rmediate state being are different 
from the point of view of tantra, whereas here it  is discussed from the point of view of sutra. 
T a n t r a  i s  m o r e  l i k e  m a g i c ,  b y  m e a n s  o f  w h i c h  a  m a g i c i a n  c a n  d o  w h a t e v e r  h e  w a n t s .  F o r  
example, a magician can take a piece of banana, bless it with mantras, give it to Venerable 
Longo to eat, whereby he transforms into… a ni ce horse! Then he ca n mount that horse and 
ride away. There are such stories!  
 Thus, from the point of view of tantra, when  someone is dying a virtuous seed can ripen 
due to our prayers, or someone who is in the intermediate state can change into another 
intermediate state being and take a good rebirt h. On the other hand, from the point of view 
of the Treasury of Manifest Dharma  it is not possible to change re birth, that is, having taken the 
intermediate state of a particular type of rebirth one will necessarily take that rebirth. Perhaps 
from the point of view of the higher Abhidharma, the Compendium of Manifest Dharma , it is 
also possible for the rebirth to change; for example,  to initially take the intermediate state of a 
being of a bad migration, and then, due to others ’ prayers, for a virtuous seed to be activated 
and ripened, whereby the intermediate state bein g dies and takes the intermediate state of a 
human being and is then reborn as a human being. 
 Wednesday afternoon, 20 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 145) says:  
9 The manner of changing into another intermediate state  
Having died and transferred after seven days  in the intermediate state of a god, for 
example, the intermediate state of a god, or  alternatively the intermediate state of a 
human being, is accomplished because th rough the changing activity of other 
karma, the seed of the intermediate st ate changes. It is the same for other 
[intermediate states].  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
458 The intermediate state can change into anothe r intermediate state. For example, having 
stayed for seven days in the intermediate state of a god, one dies and then either the 
intermediate state of a god or that of a human being is accomplished because the seed of that 
other intermediate state ripens due to performi ng other actions. Ther efore, there can be a 
change in the intermediate state after the firs t death, but it could also happen after the 
second, third, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-1B2B-3E How you take rebirth in a birth-existence 
1 How you transfer into an intermediate existence through a mind of attachment and so forth 
Fifth: In Many Levels  it is taught that, when you are born in a womb, a mistaken 
perception arises with regard to the se men and blood of your father and mother: 
in that moment, like an illusion, you s ee your parents to be having intercourse 
even though they are not and you develop attachment to them. {146} However, the 
Commentary  on the Treasury  explains that you see your parents [actually] having 
intercourse. Furthermore, if you are to be born as a female, you desire to be rid of 
the woman, and become attached to the man and want to have intercourse with 
him. If you are to be born as a male, you desire to be rid of the man, and become 
attached to the woman and want to have in tercourse with her. Once this kind of 
desire has arisen, the closer you approach, other body parts of that man and woman not longer appear and you only see the male and female signs. Becoming angry at that, the intermediate existence being dies and transfers and is reborn
166. 
 
How does one take rebirth through generating a mind of attachment? A mistaken perception 
arises with respect to the semen and blood of the father and mother in that they appear to be 
having intercourse although they ar e not. It is like an illusion or  a magical trick, that is, we see 
something that does not exist such as a pebble th at appears to be an elephant or horse. Then 
we generate attachment to them. This is wh at is said in Asanga’s texts, however the 
commentary on the Treasury of Manifest Dharma  says that one actually sees one’s parents 
having intercourse. If we are to be reborn as a woman, we want to be free of the woman and 
develop attachment to the man and wish to have intercourse with him. If we are to be reborn 
as a man, we want to be free of the man and generate attachment to the woman and wish to 
have intercourse with her. As we get closer to our future parents we not longer see other limbs 
or parts of their bodies, such as their head, le gs, and arms, but only see their sexual organs. 
We then become very angry that this has ha ppened, and due to anger we, the intermediate 
state being, die and transfer and are reborn. In  other words, the mind of the intermediate 
state being enters the mixture of the red and white fluids and, from the point of view of 
Buddhism, is reborn, although th is in fact is the moment of conception. At this time the 
mental consciousness, feelings, and discriminati ons are not yet manifest. This first instant is 
called “birth,” whereas the second  instant, in which there is tr ansformation of the two fluids, 
is called “aging.” It is also possible that in th e second instant death occurs, rather than aging. 
                                              
166 According to the Tibetan understanding “birth” signif ies the entry into the maternal womb rather than 
one's exit from it. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
459 If there is death the mind ex its from the union of the egg and sperm and leaves the womb. 
This is in the context of a human rebirth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 What object the consciousness connects with and how  
Furthermore, when there is a situation of intense attachment between father and 
mother at the very end thick fluid emer ges. After that, two drops of semen and 
blood doubtlessly emerge from the two. Through the mixing of the two in the 
birth-source of the mother, [something] li ke, for instance, boiled milk that has 
cooled down and congealed is formed. At  the same time that the intermediate 
existence terminates, by the power of the co nsciousness that joins, the subtle parts 
of the great elements of the sense powers which are other than that [congealed 
mass] and the combination of semen and blood that accord with that mixture arise 
as other along with the sense powers167. The consciousness that enters at that time 
is posited as the basis-of all by those wh o accept a basis-of-all. Those who do not 
accept a basis-of-all assert that it is  the mental consciousness that joins. 
 
When the father and mother have sexual interc ourse with strong attachment, from both of 
them emerges a thick fluid. After that, a drop of semen and a drop of blood arise from the 
two, mix in the womb, and become something li k e  b o i l e d  m i l k  t h a t  h a s  c o o l e d  d o w n  a n d 
congealed. At this time the intermediate st ate being dies and takes rebirth, whereby the 
intermediate state ceases. At this time it is said  “to have joined with its next rebirth,” that is, 
with a new body.   The Chittamatrins, who a ssert eight consciousnesses (an eye consciousness, ear 
consciousness, nose consciousness, tongue cons ciousness, body consciousness, mind-basis-of-
all, and afflicted mentality), say that the mind-bas is-of-all is the basis on which the imprints of 
virtuous, non-virtuous, and unspecified actions ar e deposited. The seed that is deposited can 
be likened to a check which, although it is not real money, functions as though it were money 
to enable tone to buy things. Due to craving and grasping, a seed is activated, ripened, and 
brings rebirth.   At the time of rebirth in the womb, there is only the mental power, then slowly the fetus 
develops and the head and limbs, the inner orga ns, and so forth begin to develop. By thirty-
six or thirty-seven weeks the baby is usually fu lly developed, although sometimes due to inner 
conditions this does not happen. When the ey e consciousness, eye sense power, and form 
meet, the baby finds the womb to be dark, tight, and smelly, due to which it develops the wish 
to leave it. It then changes position, turns up side down, and is then born. The development 
of the fetus is explained in detail in the Nanda’s Entry into the Womb Sutra  as well as in the 
tantric medical texts.   Those who do not assert a mind -basis-of-all say that the mental  consciousness is what enters 
the womb. The eight subtle partic les – the earth element, fire element, water element, wind 
element, form, odor, taste, and tangibility – ar e the basis for the formation of all forms.  
                                             
 
167 According to an oral commentary by Geshe Soepa the consciousness at that point connects with two subtle 
aggregations contained in the mixture of paternal and ma ternal substances: one that will develop into the sense 
faculties and another one that will become the seat of the mental consciousness.
