460  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 You are not born in bad birth-sources unless the bardo being wishes to go there  
If there is no desire to go to a birth-source, [the bardo being] will not go there. And 
if it has not gone there, it will not be bo rn there. Therefore, when the bardo being 
of someone possessing non-vows168 who created and accumulated an action for 
rebirth in hell, such as killing sheep or tr ading in poultry or pigs and the like, sees, 
as if in a dream, sheep and so forth at his birth-source, he rushes there with the 
delight of previous familiarity. {147} Then , having become angry at the form of his 
birth-source, the intermediate existence ends  and he is born in the birth-existence. 
Likewise, rebirth as a hell being, a hungry gh ost with goiter, etc. is similar to this.  
 
Just as when someone is to be reborn in the hi gher realm, there is the desire to be reborn 
there, so too there needs to be desire to be rebo rn in a particular place in order to be reborn 
as a hell being and so forth. Thus, if the interm ediate state being does not desire to go to a 
particular birth-source, it will not go there and therefore will not take rebirth there. 
Therefore, when someone possessing a non-vo w who has created and accumulated the karma 
to be reborn in the hell realm, due to killing sheep, trading in poultry and pigs, and so forth, 
at the time of the intermediate state sees sheep and so forth, like in a dream, at the birth-
source, he will want to quickly go there due to liking this out of previous familiarity. Then, 
seeing the actual birth-source, he becomes angr y and the intermediate state being ceases, that 
is, dies, and he is born in the birth-existence.  In other words, those who, while human beings 
killed sheep, see sheep at the birth-source and wi sh to go there, while those who sold chickens 
or pigs, see these animals and go there, whereupon they are reborn. Rebirth as hell beings, 
hungry ghosts with goiters, and so forth are similar.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 How you are reborn in the bad as an animal etc. 
In the Actual Basis of the Levels  it says that if you are to be born as an animal, 
hungry ghost, human being, or as a god en joying the desire and form [realms], you 
w i l l  s e e  d e l i g h t f u l  b e i n g s  o f  s i m i l a r  t y p e  t o  y o u r s e l f  a t  y o u r  b i r t h - s o u r c e .  T h e n ,  
through generating delight in and desire for that, you go there. Having become 
angry at the birth-source, the intermediate existence ceases and you are born in the 
birth-existence. The way in which some one who has non-vows who does not trade 
in chicken and pigs etc. is born in hell is similar to this. 
 
If we are to be reborn as an animal, we will s ee animals, for example, at the birth-source and, 
thinking they are nice, will go there. We then  become angry at the birth-source, whereupon 
the intermediate state ceases and we are reborn in a birth-existence as an animal. It is the 
same in the case of rebirth as a hungry ghos t, human being, and god. Those possessing non-
vows who do not do business in ch ickens and pigs are also reborn  in hell in the same way, 
that is, they see the bodies of hell beings , go there, get angry, and take rebirth.  
                                              
168 The opposite of the vows “ sdom min ” whereby killing, harming, etc. are given up.

Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 From among the four birth-sources, how you are born from heat and moisture and miraculously born 
In the Treasury  “[Others] strongly desire odors and an abode” is mentioned saying 
that if you are to be born from heat an d moisture you are born through desiring 
odors, and if you are to be born miracu lously you are born through desiring an 
abode. Moreover, the commentary explains th at if you are to be born in the hot 
hells you go to an intermediate-existent through desiring heat, whereas if you are 
to be born in the cold hells you go to an intermediate-existent through desiring 
freshness. The Commentary on the  Treasury  explains birth from an egg to be similar 
to birth from a womb.  
 
There are four birth-sources – birth from a wo mb, birth from an egg,  birth from heat and 
moisture, and miraculous birth – by means of which we can take rebirth. The intermediate 
state being is born from heat and moisture du e to a desire for odors, whereas it is born 
miraculously due to a desire for an abode. If on e is to be reborn in the hot hells there is a 
desire for heat, whereas if one is be reborn in the cold hells there is a desire for cold. Desire 
heat one goes to a hot place and desiring cold one goes to a cold place, whereupon one takes 
rebirth in a hot hell or cold hell. The Commentary on the Treasury  says that birth from an egg 
and birth from a womb are similar. Birth from an egg is called “twice born” because the first 
birth is when a hen, for example, lays the eg g, and the second when the chick hatches from 
the egg. In the past there have also been cases of human beings who were born from an egg. It 
is recounted that when a merchant went looking for jewels on islands in the sea, having been 
ship-wrecked on an island he had intercourse with a female cr ane that then laid two eggs. 
When they hatched, two boys were born who studied the Dharma, became monks, and 
eventually became foe-destroyers.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-2 The measure that those thoughts [c ommon to intermediate beings] have arisen 
A Indicating the measure of their having arisen purely 
Second: If you have come to understand th e characteristics of cyclic existence in 
detail from the point of view of the two, sufferings and their origins, and the desire 
to abandon them and the desire to attain their complete pacification have arisen, 
that is indeed the attitude of renunciation,169 however that alone is not sufficient. 
Therefore, you should generate that mind to  the extent of being like the mind that 
does not want to be [stuck] in a house ablaze with fire or stay [locked up] in prison 
and [to the extent] that it desires libera tion from them, and even then it is 
necessary to still increase it. 
 The measure of generating the pure thought of re nunciation or definite emergence is that it 
arises from having understood the characteristics of cyclic existence from the point of view of 
sufferings and the origins of sufferings. All types of suffering are included in true sufferings. 
                                             
 
169 JN: nges ‘byung , definite emergence 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
462 What are their origins or causes? They are the mental afflictions and actions, that is, true 
origins. We need to understand that due to ac tions and afflictions we continually join with a 
new body, due to which we experience the suffering  of birth, suffering of aging, suffering of 
death, and suffering of illness, as well as ma ny other types of sufferings. Where does this 
suffering come from? It originates from the mental afflictions and actions. The mental 
afflictions cause us to engage in virtuous and non-virtuous actions of body, speech, and mind. 
These two, mental afflictions and actions, are called “true origins.” We need to understand 
them in detail, whereby the desire to pacify tr ue sufferings and true origins will arise. That 
itself is the thought of renunciation or defini te emergence, however it alone is not enough. 
We should want to cyclic existence as if we were stuck inside a burning house due to which 
we would want to immediately extinguish the fire  and get out of the hous e. Also the wish to 
leave cyclic existence should be like the consta nt and strong desire, day and night, to get out 
of prison and to achieve freedom. We need to ge nerate this mind and then increase it more 
and more. In this way we will one day achieve th e state of liberation. This is the measure of 
generating renunciation or definite emergence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The development of nothing but an appearance of striving for liberation if nothing but mediocre 
renunciation has developed  
{148} If this attitude, as taught by Shar awa, is nothing more than mediocre, like 
flour dropped into sour milk, also the vi ew that does not want the causes – the 
origins – of cyclic existence will not be come more than that. Accordingly, one’s 
striving for liberation, the cessation that is the ceasing of sufferings and origins, 
will be similar to that. Because of that , the desire to accomplish the path of 
liberation will be mere words, and also , there being no basis for the compassion 
that cannot bear the suffering of other sentient beings who roam in cyclic 
existence, the uncontrived mind of unsu rpassed enlightenment that has the power 
to spur the mind on will not arise. Co nsequently, you become a Mahayanist only 
in your understanding that follows upon  words. For that reason, you should 
cultivate [these thoughts] again and again.  
 
The development of the attitude of definite  emergence needs to be very strong and 
determined due to being based on an understanding of true sufferings and true origins and 
how we take rebirth in cyclic existence. We have to see true origins as undesirable and 
generate the wish to cease them. If this wish is  not strong, the wish to achieve liberation will 
also not be strong; it will only be mere words. Even if we try to meditate on the path, it will 
o n l y  b e  m e r e  w o r d s .  I t  w i l l  n o t  c o m e  f r o m  the heart and will not be based on a strong 
determination to abandon or cease the two truths, true sufferings and true origins, and to 
achieve true cessations by means of strongly gene rating true paths. Also  the compassion that 
cannot bear the sufferings of the sentient beings  who are wandering in cyclic existence will 
not be strong, but will just be mere words.  Also the uncontrived mind of unsurpassed 
enlightenment will not arise strongly, whereby on e will be a Mahayanist only in mere words. 
Therefore, we need to analytical meditation ag ain and again on true sufferings, that is, on 
how we ourselves and all sentient beings suffer. We also need to meditate on how true origins 
function, that is, how actions are produced by afflictions. Thereby, we will develop a strong 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
463 wish to attain nirvana. Then, thinking that al l sentient beings are tormented by suffering and 
unable to bear this, we will generate strong or great compassion, which will then produce the 
wish to attain enlightenment or buddhahood in or der to be able to take care of all sentient 
beings. We need to develop this attitude from ou r heart. In this way, one day we will generate 
uncontrived definite emergence, whereby we  will enter the firs t path, the path of 
accumulation, followed by the path of preparat ion, path of seeing, path of meditation, and 
path of no-more-learning. This is from the po int of view of the Hinayana, whereas from the 
point of view of the Mahayana we will pass thro ugh the ten grounds until we attain the last 
uninterrupted path of the vajra-like meditative stabilization and then in  the next instant we 
will attain the state in which all abandonm ents have been abandoned – the state of 
omniscience. At this point, we will have achieved all our own aims and will also able to be of 
benefit to all sentient beings.

Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 148) says:  
2B4B-2B2B-3 Eliminating misconceptions about this 
A The manner of misconceptions  
B Their elimination 
2B4B-2B2B-3A The manner of misconceptions  
Third: You may think: “If you cultivate extreme revulsion and disenchantment for 
cyclic existence, like the hearers you will fall into the extreme of peace because of 
your dislike for [re]entering cyclic existe nce. Therefore, while it is becoming for 
Hinayanists to cultivate disenchantment, it  is inappropriate for bodhisattvas to 
cultivate it. That is  explained in [the Sutra of the ] Unfathomable Secret .”  
 
What is the wrong conception that needs to be  eliminated? It is th e thought that if one 
meditates on cyclic existence and generates revu lsion and disenchantment for it and wishes to 
abandon it, one will become like hearers becaus e it is the hearers who do not want to enter 
cyclic existence and fall into the extreme of peace. Therefore, while this is suitable for hearers, 
it is not suitable for those who want to  cultivate the mind of enlightenment.  
 Thus, the wrong conception here is that if Ma hayanists thinking about the fact that cyclic 
existence has many sufferings and problems and generate the wish to leave it and to attain nirvana, they will not think of other sentient  beings. However, this is a mistake because 
Mahayanists actually have a clear understanding of the sufferings of cycl ic existence, of the 
f a c t  t h a t  t h e r e  i s  n o  h a p p i n e s s  i n  c y c l i c  e x istence, and that even that which is called 
“happiness” is in fact the nature of suffering. Due to this, Mahayanists have a strong wish to 
leave this suffering behind.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-3B Their elimination 
1 The meaning of [statements in] scriptures that bodhisattvas are not terrified by cyclic existence 
The meaning of the statement “Thus, a bo dhisattva should not be terrified by 
cyclic existence” is not to point out that he should not be repulsed at the sufferings 
of birth, aging, illness, death, and so on of roaming in existence under the power 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
464 of karma and the mental afflictions. Ra ther, it is that, due to his mind of 
enlightenment, he should not be afraid to  take rebirth in existence for the benefit 
of sentient beings under the power of  aspirational prayers and compassion. 
 
Here this wrong conception is eliminated. Bodhis attvas are afraid of cyclic existence in the 
sense that they are afraid to take rebirth in cyclic existence under the control of actions and 
afflictions and experience the sufferings of birt h, aging, and so forth. On the other hand, 
bodhisattvas are not afraid to take rebirth in cyclic existence under the power of their prayers 
and great compassion in order to be able to ta ke care of and ripen other sentient beings. In 
fact, for this purpose bodhisattvas are fearless and want to take rebirth in  cyclic existence. The 
Lama Chopa  says that even for the sake of a single se ntient being a bodhisattva is willing to go 
to the hell realm and stay there for many eons. Thus, he patiently and willingly bears even 
intense suffering in order to care for other sentient beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 How it is necessary to enjoy being reborn in cyclic existence due to compassion but to be terrified of 
rebirth in cyclic existence due to karma and the afflictions 
In fact, if roaming in existence under the power of karma and the mental 
afflictions, we are tormented by many sufferings and unable to enact even our own welfare, what need is there to mention en acting others’ welfare? {149} Since it is 
the door to all degeneration, having de veloped more disenc hantment than the 
Hinayanists there is a need to block it, whil e it is necessary to enjoy taking rebirth 
in existence under the power of aspi rational prayers and compassion. In 
accordance with that it also  says in the same sutra:  
 
Bodhisattvas, through taking care of thoroughly ripened sentient 
beings, view cyclic existence to be beneficial – unlike the great passage 
[to peace]. 
 
Thus, a bodhisattva fears taking rebirth in cy clic existence under the power of actions and 
afflictions, being tormented by various type s of sufferings and unable to enact his own 
welfare, let alone others’ welfare. Because this is  the door to or source of all degeneration and 
faults, bodhisattvas develop greater fear of cycl ic existence than do Hinayanists. However, they 
still generate the wish to take rebirth in cycl ic existence by the power of prayer and great 
compassion in order to be able to benefit all sentient beings. Since the main aim of 
bodhisattvas is to ripen sentient beings, they  see cyclic existence as being beneficial for 
generating love and compassion. Due to this, they  remain in cyclic existence in order to help 
sentient beings by ripening those who are no t yet ripe, and liberating those who are ripe. 
Therefore, while bodhisattvas fear the suffering of  rebirth in cyclic existence caused by actions 
and afflictions, they are happy to take rebirth in cyclic existence by the force of prayers such as 
“May I be reborn in such-and-such a place in orde r  t o  b e  a b l e  t o  t a k e  c a r e  o f  a l l  s e n t i e n t  
beings.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
465 3 For those with vows of engagement [in bodhisattva behavior] who express themselves in this manner 
without differentiating between rebirths due to karma and mental afflictions and rebirths due to 
aspirational prayers etc. infractions occur 
In the Bodhisattva Levels  it is set out that if someon e who expresses himself in the 
above manner without differentiating [between them ] has bodhisattva vows, he 
commits an afflicted infraction.  
 
There is a difference between taking rebirth by the force of actions and afflictions and taking 
rebirth by the force of prayers. Because of th is, if someone who has the vows of engaging 
bodhichitta (the bodhisattva vows) does not ma ke this distinction and thinks they are the 
same, he commits an infraction.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 Cherishing the way of conduct without falling into the extreme of peace despite great revulsion for 
cyclic existence 
Having developed revulsion for cyclic exis tence, to see those sentient beings as 
one’s relatives and thus generate the mind  of enlightenment for their sake, is the 
intention of the Four Hundred Verses  and is clearly set out by the great master 
Chandrakirti in his commentary on that [text]. 
 
If we have strong revulsion for c y c l i c  e x i s t e n c e ,  w e  h a v e  t o  be careful not to fall into the 
extreme of peace. Therefore, we need to see al l sentient beings as our dear relatives, for 
example, as our dear mother, father, brother, sist er, and so forth, and for their sake strive to 
generate the mind of enlightenment – the attitu de wishing to attain buddhahood in order to 
be able to take care of all sentient beings. This is said by Aryadeva in his Four Hundred Verses  
and clearly explained by Chandrakirti in his commentary on this text.   Hence, we need to strive at all times to thin k of all sentient beings and to generate love, 
compassion, the extraordinary thought, and the conventional mind of enlightenment. In our 
daily lives we should try to avoid thinking only  of ourselves, and instead strive to think of 
others more than we do ourselves. By doing th is, we will accumulate great merit, our Dharma 
practice will be successful, and our next life will be even better.  
 
(Oral transmission 11:45-12:10, pp. 149-160)  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 149) says:  
Explanation of how the nature of the path  leading to liberation is established  
2B4B-2B2B-4 Establishment of the nature of the path leading to liberation  
A Transition 
[B The actual establishment of the nature of the path] 
2B4B-2B2B-4A Transition 
Fourth: Since meditation on the shortcomin gs of existence as explained earlier has 
generated an intense desire to definitely emerge from cyclic existence, there is a 
need to reverse cyclic existence.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
466 Previously the shortcomings of cyclic existence we re explained; for example, as long as we are 
born in cyclic existence we will experience ma ny sufferings. Since the very nature of cyclic 
existence is suffering, we need to give it up or reverse it. To do this, we must generate a strong 
wish to reverse cyclic existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-4B The actual establishment [of the nature of the path ] 
1 By what kind of basis is cyclic existence averted 
2 By cultivating what kind of path is it averted 
Therefore two points [are being made] in this  regard: by what kind of basis is cyclic 
existence averted and by cultivating wh at kind of path is it averted. 
 
By which beings, that is, in dependence on what type of body, can cyclic existence be averted? 
By meditating on what kind of path is it aver ted? The basis or support refers to the body of 
the being. Among the different types of bases, which one is the best? The best one is a human 
body and, among them, a basis or body possessi ng the eight leisures and ten endowments is 
the very best. Then, between lay people and the ordained, the orda ined have the best 
opportunity to avert cyclic existence because l ay people are generally busy and do not have 
much time to practice in that they need to ta ke care of their families, work and make money 
in order to care for their partner and children, and take care of their animals such as horses, 
sheep, and so forth. The ordained are less busy because they do not have to take care of a 
husband or wife, children, domestic animals, and so forth. Instead they can live alone, taking 
care of themselves alone and thereby practicing  the Dharma. In this sense, the body of an 
ordained person is better than that of a lay person. However, maybe times have changed since 
nowadays monks and nuns are also busy! In our modern world everything has changed in that 
monks are also busy wandering around in cyclic existence, trying to make money, taking care 
of businesses, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-4B1 By what kind of basis is cyclic existence averted 
A The need to avert it at this time when you have obtained a basis of leisures and endowments 
From the Letter to a Friend : 
 
Having obtained a birth of leisure that’s free 
From the so-called defective eight unfree states {150} Make an effort to avert rebirths. 
 As is set out [here], you need to avert it at this time when you have obtained the leisures and endowments, for it has alre ady been explained that in the unfree 
[states] there is no opportunity to avert it.  
 
Nagarjuna in his Letter to a Friend  says that at this time, while we are free from the eight 
leisureless states and have the eight freedoms, we need to make an effort to avert rebirth, that 
is, to not take any more rebirths in cyclic exis tence. Since now is the right time to put effort 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
467 into this, we should not wait until we have ni ne or even ten leisures! An example of another 
non-leisure could be to be addicted to playing with one’s computer due to which one goes to 
bed late, sleeps less, has trouble getting up in the morning, and then needs to go to work…! I 
am joking! However, we should try to practice  the Dharma very strongly while keeping our 
mind more relaxed and free from worries. When we have a problem, we tend to worry a lot 
about it, however this worry then becomes our main problem since it makes our mind 
unhappy. It may even lead to physical pain , which makes us then worry about having a 
dangerous illness, due to which we go to hosp ital to undergo many different examinations, 
and so forth. Then, sometimes, even though the doctors do not know what we have, they still 
advise us to do undergo some treatment such as an operation, which then makes us worry 
about dying… We should not allow this to ha ppen, but should keep our mind free of worry 
and relaxed. 
 Thursday afternoon, 21 April 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 150) says:  
B The need to make an effort now 
The Great Yogi [Jangchup Rinchen] said: “N ow is the time to distinguish ourselves 
from cattle.” Potowa also said: “When we were roaming about previously, it did 
not stop by themselves. Since now it will al so not stop by itself, we must avert it. 
The time to avert it is this time wh en we have obtained the leisures and 
endowments.” 
 
The Great Yogi said that we have to differentiate  ourselves from animals, such as cows, sheep, 
goats, and so forth, that is, we have to do something better than animals who only think of 
eating and drinking. Now that we have obtained a human rebirth, we should strive to do this. 
 Potowa also said that in the past we wander ed in cyclic existence but did not do anything 
to put an end to it. However, now that we  have achieved the eight leisures and ten 
endowments we should make an effort to put an end to cyclic existence, that is, to leave it, 
and to gain realizations.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How on that basis it is good to take ordination 
Even more than that, lay people have many  obstacles to the accomplishment of the 
Dharma and have the drawback of numero us faults. Since those who are ordained 
turn away from them, the ordained have the best basis for averting cyclic existence. 
That is why the wise have a strong  liking for ordination. In [the Sutra ] Requested by 
Ugra it says that lay bodhisattv as should aspire for ordinati on. This mainly refers to 
aspiring for full ordination. In the Ornament of the Sutras  it also says:  
 
Those on the side of the ordained  
Have limitless good qualities. Therefore, those exerting themselves in vows Excel over householder bodhisattvas. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
468  
The basis for averting cyclic existence is a huma n body. Why then is it better to be ordained 
to practice the Dharma than to be a lay person or householder? It is because householders or 
lay people have many things th at they need to do and theref ore do not have much time to 
practice the Dharma. The ordained, on the othe r hand, have more leisure to practice the 
Dharma. Therefore, if we take ordination it beco mes easier to practice the Dharma as we will 
have less work and less responsibilities due to not having a wife or husband and children. 
Due to being busy, lay people have less time to practice the Dharma and, for this reason, it is 
better to take ordination. However if we are ordained but continue to work like a lay person it is possible that even more faults will arise. In  general though, the best  basis to avert cyclic 
existence is that of an ordained body. Therefor e, we should enjoy and like ordination. Even 
lay bodhisattvas should think of the benefits of taking ordination and aspire to and make 
prayers to take ordination in the future. To as pire for ordination mainly means to aspire to 
become a fully-ordained monk or nun. Similarly, the Ornament for the Mahayana Sutras  says 
that those on the side of the ordained have ma ny good qualities, due to which, between lay 
bodhisattvas and ordained bodhisattvas, the ordained are superior.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D How it is necessary to respect the [vows of] individual liberation since ordination is also praised by 
both sutra and tantra for the accmplishment of omniscience 
Thus, ordination is not only praised for the attainment of the complete release 
that is liberation from cyclic  existence, but is also presented as being the best basis 
for the accomplishment of omniscie nce by the perfection and tantra [vehicles]. 
The vows of ordination, among the three vo ws, are those of individual liberation. 
Therefore, you should respect [the vows of ] individual liberation, the root of the 
teachings.  
 
From the point of view of both the perfection vehicle and tantra vehicle, ordination is praised 
as being the best basis for both the attain ment of liberation and the attainment of 
omniscience. Among the three vows (the vows of  individual liberation, bodhisattva vows, and 
tantra vows), the vows of ordination refer here to the vows of individual liberation. Among 
the vows of individual liberation there are five  vows of ordained people and three vows of lay 
people, for a total of eight types of vows. Among the three types of lay vows, one is taken for a 
period of twenty-four hours, while the other tw o are to be taken with a strong determination 
to keep them for the rest of one’s life. Even if we do not take vows, we still need to discipline 
ourselves by striving to avoid non-virtuous ac tions and to engage in virtuous actions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-4B2 By cultivating what kind of path is cyclic existence averted 
A Identification of the path – the transition 
B Indication that the other trainings  will be exlained further on  
C In particular how to train in ethical discipline 
2B4B-2B2B-4B2A Identification of the path – the transition 
{151} Second: In the Letter to a Friend  it says: 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
469 Even if your head or clothing were to catch fire 
Give up preventing it; simply make an effort To destroy [the occurrence] of future rebirths. 
There is nothing else that would be better. 
 
Through pure discipline, wisdom, and concentration Attain the immaculate state of peace and taming – 
Nirvana – that’s ageless, deathless, and limitless, 
Free of earth, water, fire, wind, the sun, and the moon. 
 You should train in the three types of prec ious trainings of the path as set out in 
this statement. 
 Nagarjuna says that even if our head or clothing  was to catch fire, we should not do anything 
to put it out but instead we should make an e ffort to put an end to our future rebirths in 
cyclic existence. There is nothin g that is better than this. By means of training in morality, 
wisdom, and concentration, we will attain nirv ana, the state of stainless pacification and 
subjugation, without aging and without death, fr ee from earth, water, fire, wind, the sun, and 
the moon. In other words, having attained liberation, we will be free from all the problems caused by the earth and so forth. Therefore, we  need to train in the three types of precious 
trainings of the path: the higher  training in morality, higher training in concentration, and 
higher training in wisdom.   At the moment we need to practice morality by taking any of the various levels of vows 
and, having taken them, we need to safeguard them. For example, if we have taken the vows 
of a fully-ordained monk, we need to guard the three vows that we possess: the lay vows, 
novice vows, and vows of full ordination. Thus, for a fully-ordained monk to train in morality means to safeguard these vows. In short, we n eed to guard whatever vows – lay vows, novice 
vows, vows of full ordination, or vows of probat ionary nuns – we have taken. For example, a 
fully-ordained monk must guard himself against th e four defeats of the root vows, the thirteen 
remainders, and so forth. Although the numb er of vows differs in the various Buddhist 
traditions, the vows themselves are similar.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-4B2B Indication that the other trainings will be explained further on  
If I were commenting on the path of inte rmediate beings for my own enjoyment, 
an extensive commentary on the three trai nings would be necessary here. However, 
as this is not the case, the training in wi sdom, special insight, and the training in 
the mind, the way to generate calm abiding, will be explained in the context of 
great beings. Therefore, here I will only briefly state the way to train in ethical 
discipline. 
 
With respect to the path of middling beings, it  is necessary to understand the three higher 
trainings in morality, wisdom, and the mind. “The training in the mind” is the training in 
concentration. Since the two latter trainings will  be explained in the context of the path of 
great beings, when calm abiding and special in sight are set out, Lama Tsongkhapa will not 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
470 explain them here. However, he will briefly ex plain how to train in morality or ethical 
discipline.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-4B2C How to train in ethical discipline 
1 The benefits of guarding [ethical discipline]  
2 The shortcomings of not guarding it  
3 How the benefits of generating ethical discipline are great these days  
4 The manner of training 
2B4B-2B2B-4B2C-1 The benefits of guarding [ethical discipline] 
In this regard, initially, by considering the benefits of ethical discipline again and 
again, you should increase your heartfelt enthusiasm for it through thinking about 
it as stated in the Letter to a Friend : 
 
Rules are the base and foundati on of all good qualities, 
So it says; like the earth fo r the animate and inanimate. 
 
And in the Tantra Requested by Subahu : 
 
Just as crops grow faultlessly depending on the ground, 
Likewise depending on ethical discipline, Moistened by the water of compassion, The most superior wholesome qualities grow. 
 
There are many benefits of guarding morality. We need to think about them again and again, 
until we generate a strong joy in morality from  the depths of our heart. Morality or ethical 
discipline is the basis for the development of all qualities, just as the earth is the basis for all 
that is animate and inanimate, i.e., that which moves and does  not move – sentient beings 
and vegetation, buildings, and so forth. We s ee that all sentient beings, for example, human 
b e i n g s ,  d e p e n d  o n  t h e  e a r t h  i n  o r d e r  t o  g r o w  f o o d  s u c h  a s  r i c e  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  I n  f a c t ,  a l l  
v e g e t a t i o n ,  t r e e s ,  p l a n t s ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h ,  a s  w e l l  a s  b u i l d i n g s ,  s u c h  a s  h o u s e s ,  d e p e n d  o n  t h e  earth. All the five sense objects of the desire realm depend on the earth or on the ground. In 
a similar way, all good qualities grow in dependence on morality. Why is this? It is because if w e  w a n t  t o  t a k e  h i g h e r  r e b i r t h ,  t h e  o n e  a n d  o n ly cause is morality or discipline. For this 
reason, we need to strive to safeguard our morality.  
 Also the Tantra Requested by Subahu  says that we need morality as a basis in order for all 
good or white qualities, that is, virtues, to grow, just as crops grow well in a good field. Just as 
seeds planted in the ground need the condition of  water in order to grow , so too do we need 
the moisture of compassion in order for the supe rior qualities of bodhisattvas to grow in our 
mind. Lacking compassion, these qualities will not develop.   In short, we need to guard our morality by doing what is good. We must try not to deceive 
others by pretending that we have good morality while, behind their backs, engaging in bad conduct. It is also not right to  behave well physically while in  our mind thinking bad things, 
such as planning to steal, harm others , and so forth. This is important.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
471 2B4B-2B2B-4B2C-2 The shortcomings of not guarding it 
Just as guarding the ethical discipline you have assumed bring enormous benefits, 
likewise there are great shortcomings to not guarding it. You should consider the 
shortcomings of not guarding it again and again as taught in the scriptures. {152} 
In the Sutra on Cherishing Monks  it says: 
 
Discipline is happiness for some; 
For others, discipline is suffering. 
For those who have it, happiness; 
For those who breach it, suffering. 
 To break our morality or to be immoral means to take vows but then not guard them. If we 
take vows and then strive to keep them purely, there will be great benefit. On the other hand, 
if we take vows but do not guard them well,  there will be enormous shortcomings. The Sutra 
on Cherishing Monks  says that having taken vows, if we make an effort to keep them well the 
result will be happiness, whereas for those wh o take vows of morality but then break them, 
there will only be suffering. Why is this? It is  because having taken vows, the result of not 
guarding them will be an increase in su ffering. Therefore, we must be careful.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In the Manjushri Root Tantra  it says: 
 
If discipline decays in reciters 
There will be no highest attainments, Nor intermediate attainments, Even the least ones will remain absent.  The Capable Lord did not teach  Mantra attainments for the immoral, Nor do they approach the place That goes to the city of nirvana. 
 
How could there be in such bad children  Any attainments of mantra? For those disregarding ethical discipline How could there be happy realms?  If they do not become higher beings, Nor beings bound for highest bliss, What need to mention their attaining The mantras taught by the Jina? 
 Those who recite mantras such as OM MA NI PADME HUM but do not keep morality 
cannot achieve the supreme attainment or siddh i, they also cannot achieve the middling 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
472 attainments, and they cannot even achieve the ve ry least attainments. In fact the Buddha said 
that if we allow our morality to degenerate, we will not achieve any tantra attainments. 
Therefore, while recitation is important, at the same time we also need to strive to maintain 
our pledges and vows. For example, having ta ken the bodhisattva vows, we must keep the 
eighteen root vows as well as the forty-six se condary vows. We must try to do this from the 
depths of our heart. Because we are ordinary  beings, sometimes we have strong mental 
afflictions, sometimes we are confused, and so metimes we do not want to do something. 
However, in spite of this we must try to  keep our vows as much as possible.  
 If we do not keep our vows there is no point in wishing to go to the city of nirvana since 
w e  w i l l  o n l y  r e m a i n  i n  “ t h e  c i t y  o f  c y c l i c  e x istence.” Here “the childish” refer to ordinary 
beings, not to small children. It is they wh o cannot achieve the atta inments of mantra or 
tantra if they do not keep good morality. Also those who degenerate their morality will not be 
reborn in the happy migrations as gods or hu man beings. They also cannot reach the highest 
states of happiness such as buddhahood or nirv ana. The Buddha said that since this is not 
possible, there is no need to mention that they will not attain the result s of mantra or tantra, 
that is, the state of Vajradhara po ssessing the seven unifications.  
 In short, if we do not safeguard our morality , there will be many unpleasant consequences. 
For this reason, we need to think again and ag ain about what will happen if we do not keep 
pure morality. We should do this until we ge nerate fear of what will happen and therefore 
put effort into keeping our morality by not breaking our vows.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B2B-4B2C-3 How there are great benefits of generating ethical discipline nowadays  
In the King of Concentration  [Sutra ] it says: 
 
The trainings that I taught to those 
Who wear the laity’s grey clothes, 
At that time, there will not even be Fully-ordained monks who train in them. 
 
At the time when, according to the statem ent, the thoroughly complete guarding 
of the five bases of training taught to l ay vow-holders is not pr esent even in fully-
ordained monks, since striving in the trai nings is special and has more effect, you 
should make an effort. From the same sutra: 
 
Those who, at the time when  the holy Dharma decays 
And the Sugata’s teachings come to an end, 
Practice a single training day and night, 
Their merit will extraordinarily exceed  That of one who for eons like Ganges’ sand grains And with a mind of faith honours billions of buddhas With food, drink, parasols, banners, and garlands of light. 
 There are many benefits to guarding our morality  in this degenerate time in which sentient 
beings have much attachment, strong mental afflictions, and perform very few virtuous 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
473 actions. The King of Meditative Stabilizations Sutra  says that at the time of degeneration even 
fully-ordained monks will not guard the five vo ws of lay people, even though they need to 
guard not only the five lay vows, but also the vows of novices and the vows of full ordination. 
Because of this, striving at this time to guard even just the five lay precepts brings a great 
result. Consequently, if we take and guard the novice vows or the vows of full ordination 
there will be even more benefit.   If for eons equal to the number of grains of  sand in the Ganges Ri ver, we were to offer 
food, drinks, parasols, banners, and garlands of  butterlamps to the infinite buddhas with a 
mind of faith, the merit accumulated would stil l be greatly exceeded by, in this time of 
degeneration of the Dharma in which the Sugata’s doctrine is coming to an end, the merit 
accumulated by someone guarding a single vow, such as the abandonment of killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, or taking intoxicants for a period of twenty-four hours. This implies 
that in this degenerate time it is very meritorious to keep our vows, therefore we should strive 
to do this. Thus, day and night we should strive  to keep our vows. To do this, in the evening 
before we go to sleep, we should examine what kind of actions of body, speech, and mind we did from the time we woke up that morning. If we find that we created negativities we should 
go for refuge to the Three Jewels, the Buddha , Dharma, and Sangha, and generate the mind 
of enlightenment that wishes to  a t t a i n  b u d d h a h o o d  i n  o r d e r  t o  b e  a b l e  t o  t a k e  c a r e  o f  a l l  
sentient beings; generate regret for the negativi ties created; determine to try not to do them 
again; and recite some mantras, sutras, or a sh ort prayer. Alternatively, meditate a bit on the 
emptiness of the three spheres – the one who di d the action, the action accumulated, and the 
result of the action – and then go to sleep absorbed in that clear light.   Friday morning, 22 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 153) says:  
2B4B-2B2B-4B2C-4 The manner of training 
A Instructions on the four antidotes for abandoning downfalls 
Among the four causes for the occurrenc e of downfalls, the antidote to not 
knowing is to understand the trainings through studying them. 
 The antidote to non-conscientiousness is to train in:  
1. the mindfulness that does not forget the observed objects and aspects of what 
to adopt and what to abandon;  
2. the introspection that individually invest igates the three doors at every moment 
and is aware of the excellent and faulty [actions] being engaged in;  
3. the shame that shuns the faulty from the perspective of oneself or the Dharma;  
4. the embarrassment that shuns the faulty  through thinking that others will 
criticize you; and  
5. the intimidation that fears the full ri pened effects of faulty conduct.  
The antidote to a lack of respect is to  be respectful towards the Teacher, his 
formulated rules, and those wh o perfectly observe the rules. 
 The antidote to many mental afflicti ons is to examine your continuum and 
endeavor to apply the antidote to what ever mental affliction predominates. 
 
There are four causes for the arisal of downfalls: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
474 1. not knowing, 
2. non-conscientiousness, 
3. a lack of respect, 
4. having many mental afflictions. 
They are also called “four doors” because when they are left open they become the cause of 
negative actions. 
 As an antidote to not knowing, we need to  understand the trainings through listening to 
teachings or instructions on them. As an anti dote to non-conscientiousness, we need the 
mindfulness that remembers and does not forget  the objects to be adopted and those to be 
abandoned. We also need the introspection th at examines the three doors of body, speech, 
and mind to see what kind of actions, excellent or faulty, they are engaging in. We also need 
shame which, out of respect for ourselves or th e Dharma, does not engage in faulty behavior. 
We also need embarrassment which, out of re spect and consideration for others, does not 
engage in non-virtuous actions. If both sham e and embarrassment are present, we will engage 
only in virtuous actions. We also need strive  to understand the fully ripened results of our 
negative actions whereby we will develop fear of  their results and thereby want to engage in 
good behavior.   As an antidote to a lack of respect, we should strive to develop respect for the Teacher , Buddha Shakyamuni, his formulated rules, and the companions with whom we practice. As 
an antidote to the many afflictions that aris e in our mind, we should examine at all times 
what kind of mental afflictions are arising in  our mind and then apply the antidote to the 
affliction that tends to predomin ate. Thus, if attachment is the strongest, we should apply the 
antidote [of meditation on ugliness ]; if anger is the strongest, we  should apply the antidote of 
love and patience; and if ignorance is the strong est, we should meditate on dependent-arising 
and try to understand the emptiness of true or  inherent existence. By doing this, we will 
gradually be able to diminish our mental afflic tions and eventually be able to eliminate them 
completely. However, to do this, we first need to examine which mental afflictions arise most 
often in our day to day life. This is importan t because many mental afflictions arise and due 
to them we then create non-virt uous actions that bring the three or four results. Among these 
results, the main one is the fully ripened result  of a bad rebirth, however when we are once 
again reborn as a human being we will also  experience the others. In order to avoid 
experiencing these results we need to apply th e antidotes to our non-virtuous actions – the 
four opponent powers.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Relating this to past events: how it is inappropriate to be careless with respect even to small 
formulated [rules]  
If without putting effort into this you tr ansgress even the minor and think: “It is a 
small mistake,” this engagement in carelessness with respect to the formulated 
rules will only obtain suffering for you because it says in the Classification of  
[Vinaya ] Scriptures : 
 
Those who are unconcerned with  regard to the teachings 
Of the Compassionate Teacher and slightly transgress them 
Will hence come under the influence of suffering, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
475 Just like a mango grove is spoiled through cutting a bamboo grove. 
 Though some here transgress th e royal decrees of the king 
And, if it is not often, do not get punished [at all], 
If they were to transgress the Mu ni’s words in an improper manner 
They would, like the naga El apatra, migrate to the animal [realm]. 
 
We should avoid being non-conscientious or ca reless with respect even to small formulated 
rules, thinking “It does not matter. It is not a mistake.” If we do this and thereby transgress 
them, we will only experience suffering. Buddha Shakyamuni in the Classification of  Vinaya  
Scriptures  said that if we create even small transg ressions through thinking that they do not 
matter, the result will be that we will be overco me by suffering. It is like if a bamboo fence 
surrounding mango trees were to be cut down , whereby the mangoes could be picked and 
stolen by others. Also, while someone who transg resses the rules or decrees of the king or the 
country may not be caught and punished, if we transgress the words of Buddha Shakyamuni 
we will take a bad rebirth like the naga Elapatra . It is recounted that in a previous life, when 
he was the caretaker of a monaster y, he did not take care of th e fruit trees on the monastery’s 
land thinking that they did no t matter. However, due to this he was reborn as Elapatra, a 
naga with a tree growing from its head. One d ay he attended Buddha Shakyamuni’s teachings 
in the form of a magnificent king, but Bu ddha Shakyamuni scolded him and told him to 
come the next day in his real form. The next day a huge snake with a tree growing on its head appeared at the teachings, frightening all the other people there. Buddha Shakyamuni 
explained that he was in fact the magnificent ki ng of the previous day appearing in his real 
form. When Buddha Shakyamuni was asked why he had come to have the form of the naga 
with a tree on its head, the Buddha explained that  it was because he had not taken care of the 
fruit trees but had cut their branches off and damaged them in other ways when he was the 
caretaker of a monastery in his past life.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How there is a need for quick confession if you are stained by faults and downfalls  
{154} Therefore, you should put effort into not being stained by faults and 
downfalls, but if it you do become stained by them, do not leave it at that 
indifferently, but put effort into undoing the transgression or negativity as has 
been taught. 
 
We should strive to not create faults or downfa lls, but if we do create them we should not 
think that it does not matter but instead tr y to purify them immediately by means of 
confessing them. For this reason, every fifteen days monks and nuns perform the Ceremony for Restoring and Purifying (Sojong) to confess any transgression of their vows by applying the 
four opponent powers: generating regret, determ ining not to do them again, going for refuge 
and generating the mind of enlightenment, and a pplying an antidote such as reciting sutras, 
doing prostrations to the thirty-five buddha s, reciting the hundred-syllable mantra of 
Vajrasattva, and so forth. We need to so simila r purification in our every day life, whereby we 
w i l l  b e  a b l e  t o  k e e p  o u r  m i n d  c l e a n .  T h i s  i s  i m p o r t a n t .  J u s t  a s  w h e n  w e  w a k e  u p  i n  t h e  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
476 morning we immediately wash our face and teeth,  take a shower, comb our hair, and so forth, 
so too should we try to clean our mind of the mental afflictions and the non-virtuous actions 
of body, speech, and mind. Unfortunately we cannot clean our non-virtuous actions with 
water, as then it would be very easy! However, there are some traditions which believe that it 
is possible to purify non-virtuous actions of bo dy, speech, and mind by bathing in the Ganges 
R i v e r .  T o  r e f u t e  t h i s  i t  i s  r e c o u n t e d  t h a t  w h e n  A r y a d e v a  a n d  A s h v a g o s h a  w e r e  d e b a t i n g  
together, Aryadeva put some excrement in a golden vase and went to the river where he busily 
washed the outside of the va se. When someone asked him why he was washing the outside 
and not the inside, he replied that he was do ing the same as those people who bath their 
bodies in the river thinking th at they are purifying their inne r negativities! The person who 
had asked the question was unable to argue with this response!  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D The need not just for ordained mantric practitioners but also for lay practitioners of mantra to guard 
the majority of vows  
I f  s u c h  g u a r d i n g  o f  m o r a l i t y  i s  f o r  those who hold the vows of individual 
liberation, it is also similar in the ca se of mantra because it says in the Tantra 
Requested by Subahu : 
 
Of all pure ethical disciplines of pratimoksha 
That I, the Jina, taught, practitioners Of mantra who are householders should abandon 
The signs and rituals while practicing the rest. 
 
If it is necessary for lay practitioners of mantra to behave as set out in the vinaya, except for the signs of ordination, the ritual actions, and some parts of the 
prescribed formulated rules, what need is  there to mention ordained practitioners 
of mantra? 
 
Buddha Shakyamuni taught that the vows or morali ty of individual liberation must be kept 
pure even by lay tantric practitioners with th e exception of wearing monks’ robes, shaving 
their heads, and so forth and the performance of certain rituals specific to the ordained which 
are set out in the Hundred Ritual Vinaya Sutra . However, with these exceptions, they must 
guard all the other vows of the ordained. In ot her words, lay tantric practitioners must guard 
themselves not only from the natural misdeed s but also from most of the formulated 
misdeeds. For example, while lay people can en gage in sexual intercourse, the ordained 
cannot. Since lay tantric practitioners must gu ard these vows, there is no need to mention 
that ordained tantric practitioners must guar d them. However, there are monks who claim “I 
am a tantric practitioner,” grow their hair long, embrace a woman saying that she is a 
qualified consort, and so forth. HA-HA! Such  things happen. I heard of an ex-monk who, 
when he was still ordained, did a retreat in a monastery in Lhadak for one or two years. 
When he came out of retreat, he said to Tri jang Rinpoche that the time had come for him to 
take a consort. Trijang Rinpoche  asked him whether he had atta ined the realizations of the 
completion stage. He said that he had not, but that he had done the re treat well and therefore 
had become a qualified tantric practitioner and so it was time for him to take a consort. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
477 Trijang Rinpoche said that if he  wanted to take a consort it was best for him to first give up 
his vows of ordination! 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
E It is the position of holy former masters to make ethical discipline and all other practices mutually 
supportive of each other 
Khamlungpa also said: “If a famine brea ks out, everything depends on barley. 
Likewise everything hinges on discipline, therefore, ernestly apply yourself to it. 
Pure ethical discipline also does not come about in someone who has not reflected 
on actions and their effects. Therefore, it is an essential instruction to reflect on 
that.” Sharawa also said: “Generally, also  whatever excellent and faulty happens, 
depend on the Dharma. Also in this regard, if you rely on what is suggested in the vinaya, you need not change anything. You will become sincere, withstand 
investigation, enjoy practice, and have a good end.” Geshe [Drom] Tonpa also 
said: “Many people miss out mantra throug h relying on vinaya or miss out vinaya 
through relying on mantra. {155} Only in my teacher’s tradition does the vinaya 
become the companion of mantra an d mantra the companion of vinaya.” 
 Geshe Khamlungpa said that just as when ther e is a famine everyone depends on barley for 
their survival, and likewise whatever actions we do we need to rely on the basis of morality, 
and therefore we need to strive to keep pure morality and to avoid committing negative actions. However, if we do not th ink about actions and their results,  it will be difficult to keep 
pure morality. In other words, in order to keep pure morality it is necessary to reflect on the 
law of cause and effect. It is an essential inst ruction that by understanding the law of actions 
and their results we will be able to keep pure morality. In other words, we need to understand that, since non-virtuous actions bring the result of suffering, due to transgressing our morality 
we will experience suffering. Through understanding this, we will naturally strive to avoid creating non-virtue and downfalls and at the sa me time strive to keep our morality pure.  
 Geshe Sharawa said that whatever excellent and faulty occurs, we should depend on the 
Dharma, that is on the three baskets or pitaka s of the Buddha’s teaching s. Mainly we should 
rely on the vinaya basket whereby our mind will become clear and pure and our life will 
become better and better. On the other hand, if we  do not strive to avoid creating non-virtue, 
the result in this life and in our future lives will be suffering and many problems. Therefore, 
we should try to live in accordance with what is  taught in the vinaya sutra as the result of this 
will be very good.   Geshe Dromtonpa also said that  the practice of tantra helps our practice of sutra, and the 
practice of sutra helps our practice of tantra . In other words, they mutually help each. 
Therefore, it is not that tantric practitioners do  not need sutra, and sutra practitioners do not 
need tantra. In fact it is wron g to think that sutra and tantra contradict each others. The fact 
is that tantric practitioners need sutra as a ba sis, and sutra practitioners eventually need to 
practice tantra because having reached the tenth bodhisattva ground they need to enter tantra 
in order to achieve buddhahood. Hence, to achi eve buddhahood it is necessary to practice 
both sutra and tantra. When the actual or meanin g clear light, the fourth stage, is achieved, 
the bodhisattva receives empowerment from th e buddhas of the ten directions and in the 
second instant enters tantra and attains enli ghtenment, the state of Vajradhara. Dromtonpa 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
478 said that only his teacher, Atisha, had the lineage that holds that sutra and tantra are 
complementary, whereas other teachers say that they are contradictory.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
F How the great beings in the land of aryas put into the practice the requirement that all activities 
accord with the three scriptural collections 
Jowo [Atisha] said:  
 
With us in India, whenever an important matter or temporary activity 
arose, those who upheld the scri ptural collections convened and 
established whether it did not reject  the three scriptural collections170 or 
did not contradict them, and upon that it was decided. We from 
Vikramashila then had the task [to ex amine] whether it was rejected by 
bodhisattva behavior or in contradi ction to it, and the decision was 
upheld by the entire ordained community.  
 
In India, whenever something happened, the monks upholding the three scriptural 
collections – sutra, vinaya, and abhidharma – would assemble together and read and examine 
whether it was in accord with the scriptures or transgressed them. If something transgressed 
or contradicted what was written in the scri ptures, it would not be done. The monks from 
Vikramashila Monastery would also examine whet her or not it rejected or contradicted the 
bodhisattva conduct, that is, the Mahayana sutras. If it did not, all those who upheld the 
vinaya would follow it.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
That concludes the explanation of training  the mind in the stages of the path 
common to intermediate beings. 
 
This concludes the explanation of training th e mind in the stages of the path common to 
middling beings. The path of middling beings co nsists in gaining an understanding of true 
sufferings, that is, the different types of suffering , and how they arise, that is, the true origins 
that are actions and mental afflictions. Then, mi ddling beings strive to understand the entity 
of actions such as the ten non-virtuous an d ten virtuous actions; meritorious, non-
meritorious, and immovable actions; actions th at are created and accumulated, created but 
not accumulated, not created but accumulated, and not created and not accumulated, and so 
forth. They also try to understand the afflicti ons – the six or ten main mental afflictions: 
attachment, anger, ignorance, prid e, doubt, the view of the tran sitory collection, the view of 
an extreme, the view holding a bad view as supreme, the view holding bad morality and 
conduct as supreme, and wrong view. These ar e the ten subtle increasers set out in the 
Treasury of Manifest Dharma  whereas the six root afflictions ar e: attachment, anger, ignorance, 
                                             
 
170 The three scriptural collections (Skt. tripitaka) often referred to more literally as “three baskets” are vinaya 
(words of the Buddha on monastic discipline) sutr a (his teachings proper) and abhidharma (“higher 
knowledge” – a systematic, philosophi cal presentation of his teachings). 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 18-22 April 2005 (10) 
479 pride, doubt, and afflicted view. We need to recognize them and when we see that one of 
them has arisen we need to apply its antidote . We also need to recognize the non-virtuous 
actions of body and speech and apply th e opponent powers to purify them.  
 The path of small beings consists in medita tion on the guru and his qualities, and the 
disciple and his qualities. Small beings al so meditate on the precious human rebirth 
possessing the eighteen qualities, its excellence,  and great meaning. Th ey then meditate on 
the fact that it can easily be lost, that is, on death-impermanence: death is certain, the time of 
death is uncertain, and at the time of death on ly the practice of the Dharma, a spiritual path, 
is of help, whereas our friends, possessions and wealth, and even our body will all be left 
behind. Then small beings contemplate the fact  that due to having created non-virtuous 
actions they will go to the animal, hungry gh ost, or animal realms. They imagine what it 
w o u l d  b e  l i k e  t o  b o r n  i n  o n e  o f  t h e  h e l l s  – the eight hot hells, eight cold hells, the 
neighboring hells, or the occasi onal hells. They try to identi fy them and then meditate on 
them, imagining themselves to be experiencing this suffering, their body the very nature of 
fire, and ask themselves whether they would be ab le to bear this. By means of this meditation, 
there arises a mind of strong dislike for such  suffering. They also imagine experiencing the 
suffering of the hungry ghosts, having a huge body with a tiny neck, unable to swallow food 
or, if it is swallowed, it is never enough to fi ll their bellies. They also imagine being reborn as 
an animal, killing and being killed, being an anim al used by human beings such as horses and 
cows, being beaten, made to work, and so fort h. Even small ants eat other insects and so 
forth. Therefore, animals suffer from eating ea ch other and being stupid. If we were to be 
reborn as a pig, our nose always in the filth, would we be able to bear it? Imagine what it 
would be like to always live in a dirty plac e, searching for food among garbage, and ask 
ourselves whether we could bear it. Since we could not, we need to give up the cause, bad 
actions, and practice good actions. Also we should think about who can help us, whereupon 
we will find that only the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha can help us, can give us refuge. Then 
we examine the law of cause and effect, and try to apply the four opponent powers to purify 
our non-virtuous actions. We need to do this, it is important. Okay! Thank you.  
 END

480 Wednesday morning, 27 April 2005

Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 155) says:  
PART IV: 
GREAT SCOPE UP TO CALM ABIDING 
How to train the mind in the stages  of the path of great beings  
2B4B-2B3  Training the mind in the path of great beings 
A The transition  
B The actual path 
 
The subject of the path of middling beings is fini shed and next there is the subject of the path 
of great beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3A The transition  
1 Even for the achievement of one's own entire welfare it is inappropriate to disregard the welfare of 
others, therefore it is necessary to enter the Great Vehicle from the start 
Through meditating in this manner upon the disadvantages of cyclic existence 
from different perspectives over a long ti me, you will indeed see all existence as 
being like a pit of blazing fire, and your mind will be thoroughly overcome by the 
wish to attain the liberation that complete ly pacifies suffering. If, due to that, you 
train in the three trainings, you will atta in liberation from cyclic existence and, 
moreover, will not turn back again even fo r the glory of high status. Nonetheless, 
since extinguishing [your own] faults and achieving good qualities is limited, one’s 
own welfare is not complete. For that very reason, others’ welfare is also 
temporarily neglected and in the end, havi ng been urged to do so by the buddhas, 
you will have to enter the Great Vehicle.  Therefore, it is appropriate for the 
intelligent to enter the Grea t Vehicle from the start.  
 We need to contemplate the faults of cyclic ex istence from many different and valid points of 
view and to meditate on them for a long time . Thereby, we will come to see that cyclic 
existence is like being in a pit of burning fi re in that, having been born, we continually 
experience suffering. Why is this? We need to understand that the main cause is the mental 
afflictions, which in turn produce actions that bring the result of different types of sufferings 
and problems. Therefore, we should develop the w i s h  t o  p a c i f y  a l l  t h e  s u f f e r i n g s  o f  c y c l i c  
existence as well as the mental afflictions, and to achieve nirvana or liberation. Then, in order 
to achieve liberation, we need to train in  the three higher trainings of morality, 
concentration, and wisdom. On the basis of mora lity, we train in developing a single-pointed 
mind and then use it to analyze the nature or reality of phenomena with wisdom. By training 
in the three higher trainings, we can achiev e the state free from the sufferings of cyclic 
existence – liberation. This state is very stable, whereas a rebirth of high status is not. Even 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
481 though we obtain a high rebirth in which everyt hing is enjoyable and perfect, later on we will 
once again fall into the hell realm, hungry ghos t realm, or animal realm. Therefore, a rebirth 
of high status, for example, the human rebirth th at we have presently obtained, is not stable 
since we do not know what our next rebirth will be. In fact, sometimes we take a high rebirth, 
sometimes we take a low rebirth. On the other ha nd, liberation from cyclic existence is stable, 
in that having obtained it we do not return fr om it. However, while we have eliminated faults 
and developed qualities, we have not yet eliminat ed the obscurations nor have we attained in 
a complete way all excellent qualities. Therefore,  we must not think only of achieving our own 
liberation as this is not enough. We need to do more than that. What do we need to do? We 
need to achieve buddhahood. Thinking about this , we will realize that right now we are only 
able to help others a little bit, just temporar ily, whereas we cannot help them in a complete 
way. To do this, we need to enter the Mahayana path. The door  to the Mahayana is 
bodhichitta or the mind of enli ghtenment, the wish to attain  b u d d h a h o o d  t o  b e  a b l e  t o  
benefit sentient beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
{156} In the Summary of the Perfections it says: 
 
Having abandoned forever those two yanas  
Which are without power to bring about all the world's aims, Those whose nature is of on e taste with helping others 
Enter the Jina’s vehicle taught out of compassion. 
 Even if we wish to fulfill the welfare of the worl d, that is, of all sentient beings, we cannot do 
so by means of either the hearer  or solitary realizer vehicles. Although hearers and solitary 
realizers have abandoned all the mental affliction s, they are unable to help sentient beings. 
Therefore, in order to benefit them, we need to  enter the Mahayana path. In other words, we 
need to develop the great compassion that gives rise to the mind of enlightenment, 
bodhichitta, whose nature is one taste with bene fiting others. When this attitude is very 
strong, in order to help even one sentient being we will be willing to abide even for eons in 
the hell realm. This is the kind of mind that we need to develop.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Since accomplishing the welfare of others is worthy of great beings, it is to be accomplished  
Since it is the happiness and magnificence of  beings and the ability of a being it is 
appropriate to take responsibility for the welfare of others because to observe only 
one’s own welfare is something we also sh are with animals. Therefore, the natural 
disposition of great beings is to categori cally strive for the benefit and happiness of 
others. In the Letter to a Student  it says: 
 
When cattle see easily obtainable  mouthfuls of grass they eat it 
themselves, 
And when tormented by thirst they find water, they happily drink. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
482 The magnificence of beings consis ts in the effort they make  
For the welfare of others; this is the noble ability of happy beings. 
 
Travelling, riding across the sky, the powerful sun illuminates all; 
Regardless of the burden, the earth supports the world with everything 
in it. Those without the least self-interest,  equal in nature to the great, 
Totally strive for that singular taste of the benefit and joy of the world. 
 The work of great beings is to enact the we lfare of other sentient beings. Because human 
beings have strength and good qualities, it is  appropriate for them to take upon themselves 
the responsibility of enacting the welfare of sentient beings. On the other hand, to consider 
our own welfare alone is an attitude that is al so common to or shared with animals. If human 
beings only do this, like animals who search for food and water and so forth for their own 
sake, they will not be any better than animals. He nce, we need to strive to think as do the 
great beings by wishing from our own side to be nefit and help other sentient beings. This is 
important. A Letter to a Student  also says that when animals are hungry they eat any mouthful 
of food that they can easily obtain, and when they are thirst y and find water they happily 
drink it. Hence, such behavior is nothing spec ial. Therefore, human beings need to put as 
much effort as they can into enacting the welf are and taking care of other sentient beings. 
Animals are not able to do this, it is only hu man beings who possess this magnificent quality 
that enables them to become much better than animals.   The sun is powerful and strong, it travels and can be ridden across the sky, and its light goes out to all sentient beings equally, not bene fiting some and harming others. Similarly, we 
too need to ensure that all our actions benefit other sentient beings by ripening the minds of 
those who are not yet ripe and libe rating those who are ripe. Thus, we need to be like the rays 
of the sun which, when they strike the ground, cause plants to grow and fruit to ripen. Thus, whatever we do, we need to try to do it for the benefit of others, and not just for our own 
sake. If, on the other hand, we continue to act only for our own sake there is always the risk 
that we will in the end be of harm to others.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 It is worthy of beings and the wise to toil and strive for the sake of protecting others from suffering  
{157} Thus, seeing how migrating beings are tormented by suffering, those who are 
called “a being” (Sanskrit, parusha ) or “wise” should toil for their welfare. In the 
same text it says:  
 
Those who, having seen how the worl dly, confused by the dense smoke 
clouds of ignorance,  Have helplessly fallen into the blazing fire of suffering,  
Mentally toil and work for them as if fire was spreading on their head,  
Are beings; they are also the wise. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
483 We beings need to strive to quickly protect se ntient beings from suffering. The word “being” 
in Sanskrit, parusha  (Tib. skyes bu ), has the meaning of “being able.” Seeing how all sentient 
beings are tormented by suffering, we should put effort into enacting th eir welfare. It is not 
that we just see this and then not do anything ab out it, rather we must put effort into actually 
helping them.  
 Worldly beings, confused by ignorance which is like dense clouds of smoke obscuring the 
vision, have fallen powerlessly into the blazing fi re of suffering. Just as  if our head were to 
catch fire, we would quickly and vi gorously strive to put it out, in the same way we should put 
effort, quickly and earnestly, into freeing sentie nt beings from their suffering. We should not 
do it slowly or lazily, thinking to do it tomorrow, or the day after, or next week, or next year… W e  n e e d  t o  h e l p  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  r i g h t  n o w  b y  d e v e l o p i n g  g r e a t  c o m p a s s i o n ,  l o v e ,  t h e  extraordinary attitude, and the mind of enligh tenment. Then we should practice the six 
perfections and the four means of gathering disc iples. We should practice the six perfections 
ourselves and also set other sentient beings in th e practice of them. If we are not yet able to 
teach all sentient beings, we should at the very  least try to teach other human beings how to 
meditate in order to eliminate their unhappiness and agitation. In the beginning we can just 
teach them some simple meditation or even some  yoga exercises. Initially we should talk to 
them nicely so that they generate more interest  in what we have to say, after which we can 
slowly begin to teach them the Dharma. It is no t possible to help sentient beings in any other 
way. If we are rich, perhaps we can give others some of our money to help them but still this 
will not relieve their mental suffering. Whatever knowledge we have, we should share it with 
others by teaching them what we know.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 Having generated delight that there exists an entrance to the path that accomplishes all one's own 
and others’ welfare, it is necessary to enter it 
There exists an entrance to the Great Ve hicle – the source of all one’s own and 
others’ excellence, medicine removing all problems, the great path traveled by all 
wise beings that nurtures all migrat ing beings through seeing, hearing, 
remembering, and touching – that posse sses the great skillful means that by 
working for the welfare of others accomplishes, by the way, one’s own welfare 
without any incompleteness. Thinking, “O h, I have found exactly what I was 
looking for!” you should engage in this best  of vehicles with whatever ability of a 
being you have. 
 With delight we should enter th e path that accomplishes both our own and others’ welfare. It 
is the source of all good qualities, it eliminat es all problems, is the path traveled by all the 
wise. It also nurtures all sentient beings through seeing, hearing, remembering, and touching. For example, when a skilled doctor examines a patient, he checks the person’s eyes, ears, 
tongue, and so forth while asking questions abou t his pain or sickness. “Touching” means, for 
example, that the doctor checks the person’s pulse, feels and pats his body, and so forth, 
whereby he is able to diagnose the sickness an d then eliminate it by giving the person the 
right medicine. Unlike the wrong medicine whic h can make an illness worse, the Mahayana 
path is a perfect medicine that is always able  to cure the mental i llness of the afflictive 
obscurations and obscurations to knowledge. In other words, by hearing the Great Vehicle 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
484 teachings taught by the Buddha, by thinking ab out them, and by meditating on them, we will 
generate the Mahayana path and thereby achi eve buddhahood. At this time we will have 
destroyed or abandoned all obscurations and comp leted all qualities, including the ability to 
help others. Therefore, we need to develop the thought to benefit other sentient beings from 
our heart. In this way, we will act to help them and our mind will be happy and rejoice, 
thinking “That I can benefit them in this way is excellent!” Thus , we should be very happy to 
have the possibility to enter the Mahayana path. However much  ability and intelligence we 
have, we should use them to  enter the Mahayana path.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B The actual path 
1 Demonstrating that generating the mind of enlightenment is the only gateway to the Great 
Vehicle 
2 How to generate such a mind  
3 Having generated the mind of enlightenment how to train in the conduct 
Now, there are three [points] concerning th e training the mind in the stages of the 
path of great beings: demonstrating that generating the mind of enlightenment is 
the only gateway to the Great Vehicle; ho w to generate such a mind, and, having 
generated the mind of enlightenmen t, how to train in the conduct. 
 
The actual path of the Great Ve hicle is divided into the three sub-topics: the door to the 
Great Vehicle, how to generate the mind of enli ghtenment, and how to train in the conduct.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
Demonstrating that generating the mind of  enlightenment is the only gateway  
to the Great Vehicle including an  explanation of its benefits 
2B4B-2B3B-1 Demonstrating that generating the mind of enlightenment is the only gateway to the 
Great Vehicle 
A Ascertaining the Great Vehicle as the two – the sutra and mantra vehicles – and generating the mind 
of enlightenment is the only gateway to it 
First: Therefore, as it is necessary to enter the Great Vehicle, you come to think 
about how it is to be entered. There is no Great Vehicle apart from the two – the 
Perfection and Mantra Vehicles – taught by the Conqueror. Whichever of the two 
you may enter, the only gateway is the mind of enlightenment. 
 
The door to the Great Vehicle or the Mahayana  is only the mind of enlightenment. While 
there are two Great Vehicles, the Perfection or Sutra Vehicle and the Tantra or Mantra 
Vehicle, the mind of enlightenment is the d oor to both of them. Here “the mind of 
enlightenment” refers to the conventional mind  of enlightenment, not to the ultimate mind 
of enlightenment. The Ornament for Clear Realizations  sets out the conventional mind of 
enlightenment saying that the mind generation is  the aspiration for the welfare of all sentient 
beings and the aspiration to achieve enlightenmen t. Thus, it consists of two aspirations: one 
wishing to enact the welfare of other sent ient beings and one wishing to achieve 
enlightenment. The primary mind concomitant wi th these two aspirations is called “the mind
