of enlightenment” or “bodhichitta.” Having  generated this mind, we enter the Mahayana 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
485 path and become Mahayanists or Mahayana practi tioners. Similarly, the door to becoming a 
Buddhist is to go for refuge to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, and to not go for final 
refuge to any other objects of refuge. Thus , when we generate this mind, we become 
Buddhists or Buddhist practitioners. Likewise, when we generate the conventional mind of 
enlightenment, we enter the Mahayana path and become Mahayanists. On the other hand, 
the door to tantra is to receive empowerment,  this being the main cause for entering the 
practice of tantra. However, prior to this, in or der to enter tantra it is necessary to generate 
the mind of enlightenment. This is because in  order to practice tantra we need a strong 
determination to achieve enlightenment very quickl y, even in this very life and even within a 
period of three years and three lunar phases (for ty-five days), in order to be able to quickly 
liberate all the sentient beings who are tormented by suffering.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Being posited as a practitioner of the Great Vehicle also depends only on this mind 
Whenever it is generated in the continuu m, even though nothing else has arisen, 
one is posited as a Great Vehicle practiti oner, whereas whenever one is separated 
from it, whatever good qualities one has, such as the realization of emptiness and 
so forth, {158}  one will fall to the level of hearer  and the like and degenerate from 
the Great Vehicle. This is taught in numerous texts of  the Great Vehicle and is 
also established by reasonings. Theref ore, whether one is a Great Vehicle 
practitioner is determined by wh ether one has this mind or not. 
 
When we generate the mind of enlightenment we  become a Mahayanist. Thus, even if we do 
not have a realization of emptiness, just by ge nerating the mind of enlightenment we become 
a Mahayanist. Nothing more than that is necessary . Hence, even if we were to separate from 
the realization of emptiness, we would not fall from the level of Great Vehicle practitioner. 
On the other hand, if we degenerate the mind of  enlightenment, we will fall into the Hearer 
Vehicle and so forth. We should study the many Mahayana texts that say that when we 
degenerate bodhichitta, even though we have the realization of emptiness, we will fall into the 
Lesser Vehicle. Thus, whether we are a Mahayanis t or not depends on having or not having 
the mind of enlightenment. In other words, if we have genera ted the mind of enlightenment 
in our continuum we are a Mahayanist, whereas if we have not generated it, that is, we do not 
wish to attain enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient beings, we are not Mahayanists. In 
this case, if we have generated a path in our continuum, it is necessar ily a Hinayana path. In 
brief, to be a Mahayanist the mind of enligh tenment must be present in our continuum.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Only those who have generated the mind become a son of the conquerors and outshine the hearers 
and solitary realizers  
Thus, in Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior  i t  s a y s  t h a t  a s  s o o n  a s  t h i s  m i n d  h a s  
arisen, you become a son of th e conquerors. Likewise, in the Biography of Maitreya  
it says: 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
486 “Son of the lineage, it is like this. For example, a diamond, even 
broken, outshines gold jewelry, however refined; does not lose its name ‘diamond’; and still averts all povert y. Son of the lineage, likewise the 
diamond that is the mind generated in the omniscient, even free from 
effort, outshines all the golden jewelr y that is the good qualities of the 
hearers and solitary realizers; does not lose the name ‘bodhisattva,’ and 
averts all the misfortune s of cyclic existence.” 
 If that mind exists even in someone untrained in conduct, he will be said to be a bodhisattva.  
 Having generated the mind of enlightenment, we are called “a son of the conqueror” and 
outshine all the hearers and solitary realizers. Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  also says that as 
soon as we generate the mind of enlightenmen t in our continuum, all the buddhas of the ten 
directions immediately say “In such-and-such a place, such-and-such a person has generated 
the mind of enlightenment and become a son of the conquerors.” Thus, even our 
contaminated, appropriated body aggregate, composed of skin, bones, and flesh and the 
nature of suffering, immediately becomes an obje ct of veneration of all the gods and human 
beings when we generate the mind of enligh tenment. We also become a protector of all 
sentient beings. Due to this, having generated the mind of enlightenment, we outshine the 
hearers and solitary realizers even though they are high in terms of their lineage. It is like, for 
example, when a son of a king is born, even th ough still a baby he is respected and venerated 
by all the older and more learned ministers just because of the fact that he has been born in 
the royal lineage. Similarly, when we generate the mind of enlightenment we become a son of 
the conquerors and come to outshine all the hear ers and solitary realizers by the power of our 
lineage, and not by the power of our knowledge since they may have more knowledge than us.  
 Similarly, even a tiny fragment of a diamond outshines all other precious jewels, and, though small, is still of great value and ca n eliminate poverty. Similarly, bodhichitta 
eliminates the poverty or misfortunes of cyclic  existence since, by means of it, we achieve 
higher qualities and thereby become rich in inner qualities. Therefore, we too need to develop the mind of enlightenment in order to be  able to take care of all sentient beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D It is not sufficient for the Dharma that is entered to be Mahayana and it is necessary to generate the 
mind that is more than mere words  
Therefore, it is not sufficient for the Dharma to be the Dharma of the Great 
Vehicle, rather it is important that th e person has entered the Great Vehicle. 
Whether you become a practitioner of th e Great Vehicle depends on the mind of 
enlightenment itself. If that mind is  nothing more than an intellectual 
understanding, you will be a facsimile of a Great Vehicle practitioner. On the 
other hand, if you have a fully qualified mind [of enlightenment ], you will also 
become a completely pure Great Vehicle practitioner. Therefore, you should strive 
in that. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
487 It is not enough to verbally recite Mahayana or tantra sadhan as without generating the mind 
of enlightenment in order to become a Mahayan a practitioner. Thus, in order to become a 
Mahayanist we must develop the mind of enlightenment from deep within our mind. In 
other words, if we wish to enter the Mahayana, we must generate the mind of enlightenment. 
If we just listen to a few Mahayana teaching s and gain a bit of intellectual understanding 
about the fact that if we generate the mind of enlightenment we will enter the Mahayana, but 
do not actually generate the mind of enligh tenment in our continuum, we will only be a 
facsimile of a Mahayanist. On the other hand, if we generate a fully qualified mind of 
enlightenment, we will become a completely pure Mahayanist. Therefore, we need to put 
effort into generating the mind of enlightenmen t in our continuum since it is not enough just 
to gain a bit of intellectual understanding regardin g it without actually generating it. If that is 
a case, we will only be a Mahayanist in mere words and not a real Mahayanist.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
E [The mind of enlightenment] is like a seed since it only gives birth to a buddha  
With respect to this, it says in the Gandavyuha : “Son of the lineage, the mind of 
enlightenment is like the seed of all Dhar ma of the Buddha.” {159} Since certainty 
regarding this must be obtained, it will be explained. 
 
The seed that gives birth to the buddhas is the mind of enlightenment. Thus, this sutra says 
that the mind of enlightenment is the seed of all the Dharma of the Buddha. In other words, 
the mind of enlightenment is the seed that gives rise to buddhahood. Since we must gain 
certainty regarding this, that is, since we mu st understand it, it will be explained. 
 If a seed is health, the sprout that it gives ri se to will also be healthy. On the other hand, if 
the seed is not good, it will give rise to an unhealthy sprout or to no sprout at all. The mind of enlightenment is the seed of all the many g ood qualities of the body, speech, and mind and 
enlightened activities of th e buddhas. Therefore, withou t generating the mind of 
enlightenment we cannot achieve buddhahood , nor will these qualities arise in our 
continuum.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
F The way in which generating the mind [of enlightenment ] is even more fundamental than the view of 
emptiness, etc. for giving birth to a buddha alone 
Since water, manure, heat, soil, etc. act as  a cause for a rice sprout when combined 
with a rice grain, and act as a cause for sp routs of wheat, pulses, and so forth when 
combined with their respective seeds, th ey are common causes. Since a barley seed, 
even though the conditions are gathered to gether, is not suitable as a cause for a 
rice sprout and so forth, it is the un common cause of a barley sprout, while the 
water, manure, and so forth that cooperate with it are [the common cause ] of the 
barley sprout. Similarly, among the causes for a buddha sprout, the mind of 
unsurpassed enlightenment, like the s eed, is the uncommon cause, whereas the 
wisdom realizing emptiness, like the wate r, manure, and so forth, is the common 
cause of the three enlightenments. That  is also why the statement in the  Supreme 
Continuum  “The aspiration to the supreme vehicle is the seed; wisdom is the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
488 mother that gives birth to the Dharma of the Buddha,” says that the aspiration to 
the supreme vehicle is like the father’s s eed and the wisdom realizing selflessness is 
like the mother. For instance, since a Ti betan father cannot beget a son who is 
Indian or Mongolian, the father is the ca use that determines the lineage. On the 
other hand, a Tibetan mother is like th e common cause [presented above] since 
she can give birth to different sons. 
 
Between the mind realizing emptiness and the mind of enlightenment which one is 
fundamental for giving birth to a buddha? It is the mind of enlightenment.  
 Water, manure, heat, and so forth are common causes for sprouts because they are necessary in order for any seed that has been sown in a field to give rise to a sprout. Thus, if water, manure, heat, and so forth are combined  with a wheat seed, a wheat sprout will grow. 
On the other hand, if water, manure, heat, and so forth are combined with a pea, a pea sprout will grow. Therefore, they are common co nditions for the growth of sprouts. On the 
other hand, a barley seed, when it meets these conditions, will bring the unique result of a barley sprout, it will not bring about a rice sp rout, wheat sprout, and so forth. In the same 
way, the mind of enlightenment is the unco mmon cause of buddhahood, whereas the wisdom 
realizing emptiness is the common cause, like wa ter, manure, heat, and so  forth for a seed, of 
the three enlightenments: the small enlightenm ent of hearer foe-destroyers, the middling 
enlightenment of solitary realizer foe-destroye rs, and the great enlightenment of buddhas.  
 The Sublime Continuum  says that the aspiration for the Mahayana is like the father’s seed, 
and the wisdom realizing selflessness is like the mother. For example, the son of a Tibetan 
father and a Mongolian mother is considered to be Tibetan, and likewise the son of a 
Mongolian father and a Tibetan mother is consid ered to be Mongolian. Thus, if a mother has 
five sons with five fathers, one from France, one from Britain, one from China, one from Japan, and one from Italy, then the five sons will respectively be French, British, Chinese, 
Japanese, and Italian! This is because in the pa st the nationality of the mother did not matter 
in determining the nationality of her son. Simila rly it was the custom th at if a couple who had 
two children, one boy and one girl, separated, the mother would take the daughter and the 
father would take the son. Likewise, if th ere was only one daughter, when the couple 
separated the mother would take her, whereas if there was only one son the father would take 
him.  In short, we should strive to generate th e mind of enlightenment! Nothing else matters! 
 Wednesday afternoon, 27 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
G The Great and Lesser Vehicles are also distinguished in terms of conduct, the generation of the mind 
and the like, and are not distinguished in terms of the view 
In praising the perfection of wisdom [with the following verse ], Nagarjuna also 
says that hearers and solitary realizers rely on it: 
 
That definitely relied on by the buddhas, as well as 
The solitary realizers and hearers, The path to liberation, is just one. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
489 Apart from you, there is certainly no other. 
 
That is why the perfection of wisdom is also said to be “the mother.” Since it is the 
mother of the sons of both  the Great and Lesser Vehicles, it is not the wisdom 
realizing emptiness that distinguishes th e Great and Lesser Vehicles. {160} Rather 
it is the mind of enlightenment and the va st conduct that distinguish them. In the 
Precious Garland  they are also taught to not be di fferentiated by the view but to be 
differentiated by the conduct: 
 
How could someone from the Hearers’ Vehicle become a bodhisattva, given that it does not explain the bo dhisattvas’ aspirational prayers and 
their fully dedicated conduct? 
 The Mahayana and Hinayana, th at is, the Great Vehicle and the Lesser Vehicle, are 
differentiated not in terms of the view but in  terms of the mind of enlightenment and the 
bodhisattva conduct. This is because in order to achieve liberation all the buddhas, solitary 
realizers, and hearers rely on the same path, th at is, on the wisdom realizing emptiness. Thus, 
Nagarjuna’s praise to the perfection of wisd om says that the hearers achieve the small 
enlightenment of a hearer foe-destroyer by me ans of the wisdom realizing emptiness, the 
solitary realizers achieve the middling enlighte nment of a solitary realizer foe-destroyer by 
means of the wisdom realizing emptiness, an d the buddhas achieve the great enlightenment 
of buddha by means of the wisdom realizing emptiness. Therefore, the Great Vehicle and 
Lesser Vehicle are distinguished by the genera tion of the mind of enlightenment and the 
bodhisattva conduct of the six perfections and the four means of gathering disciples. Thus, 
they are differentiated by method and not by wisdom.  
 Also Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland  says that the Hinayana script ures do not teach the mind 
of enlightenment nor the bodhisattva conduct of the six perfections and so forth. They only 
contain an explanation of the wisdom realizin g emptiness. Therefore, the Great and Lesser 
Vehicles are not differentiated in terms of the vi ew, the realization of emptiness, but in terms 
of the bodhisattva conduct, that is, the activities of bodhisattv as. What differentiates them is 
the practice or method, that is, the mind of  enlightenment and the practice of the six 
perfections of generosity, morality, patience , joyous effort, concentration, and wisdom. 
Therefore, a Mahayanist or a bodhisattva is on e who engages in practicing them, whereas a 
Hinayanist mainly develops the thought of defi nite emergence and strives to eliminate the 
afflictive obscurations, whereby he abandons the afflictive obscurations and achieves the small 
or middling enlightenment of a foe-destroye r. Thus, generating the wisdom realizing 
emptiness is not sufficient to achieve buddhahood , as the side of method is also necessary. 
Because of this, we need to develop both sides by accumulating the collection of merit and 
the collection of wisdom. With them as a ca use, we will definitely achieve buddhahood.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
H The inappropriateness of not familiarizing oneself mainly with the mind of enlightenment, but rather 
familiarizing oneself with secondary factors other that it 
Therefore, if even the wisdom realizing emptiness is not an uncommon path of the 
Great Vehicle, what need is there to me ntion other paths? For that reason, it 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
490 necessary to train in the mind of enlighte nment, having made instructions on it 
one’s main concern. 
 
Our main object of meditation should be the mind of enlightenment. Thus, if we were to 
focus on other secondary meditations, this wo uld not be right. Even if we meditate on 
emptiness and gain the wisdom realizing emptine ss, this is still not an uncommon path of the 
Mahayana. We need to develop the mind of en lightenment, which is the uncommon path of 
the Mahayana. This should be our main object of  practice, that is, we should train in it. In 
other words, we should transform our mind in to the wish to attain  enlightenment for the 
benefit of all sentient beings. We should generate this mind and then focus on it, whereby we 
will train our mind in the mind of enlightenmen t. Meditation on the mind of enlightenment, 
like meditation on compassion, consists in tran sforming the mind into the entity of the mind 
of enlightenment or compassion. We should stri ve to develop the mind of enlightenment and 
great compassion in our continuum by meditating on them in this way.  
 On the other hand, in the case of meditati on on emptiness, we take emptiness as our 
object of meditation, analyze the lack of inherent  existence, and then focus on this emptiness. 
This meditation is similar to the meditation on deity yoga or impe rmanence. On the other 
hand, when we meditate on definite emergence,  the mind of enlightenment, or compassion, 
we must transform the mind into their very en tity, that is, into an uncontrived mind of 
definite emergence, an uncontrived mind of enlightenment, or uncontrived great compassion. We should train in them in this way and try to generate them in our mind.  
 (Oral transmission 6:30 -7:05 PM, pp. 161-172) 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 160) says:  
The explanation of how to genera te the mind of enlightenment 
2B4B-2B3B-2 How to generate this mind 
A The stages of training in the mind of enlightenment 
B The measure of its arisal  
C The way to take it through a ritual 
The second one has three [points]: the stages of training in the mind of 
enlightenment, the measure of its arisal, an d the way to take it through a ritual.  
 
In terms of how to generate the mind of enligh tenment there is a discussion of the stages of 
training in the mind of enlightenment, the me asure of its arisal, and how to adopt it by 
means of ritual. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A The stages of training in the mind of enlightenment 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
491 1 The sevenfold instruction on cause and effect  transmitted from the great Jowo Atisha 
2 The training in dependence on that which occurs in the texts by Shantideva, son of the 
conquerors 
The first is subdivided in two [points] : the sevenfold instruction on cause and 
effect transmitted from the great Jowo Atisha and the training in dependence on 
that which occurs in the texts by Shantideva, son of the conquerors. 
 
There are two lineages regarding the stages of training in the mind of enlightenment: that 
which stems from Jowa Atisha – the sevenfold ca use and effect instruction, that is, the six 
causes and one effect, and that stemming fr om Shantideva – equali zing and exchanging 
oneself with others. The first way has come do wn to us from Buddha Shakyamuni, Maitreya, 
Asanga, and so forth to Atisha, and the second from Buddha Shakyamuni, Manjushri, Nagarjuna, and so forth to Shantideva.   The six causes are: 1. recognizing all sentient beings  as having been our mother; 
2. remembering their kindness, 
3. wishing to repay their kindness, 
4. attracted love – the wish for all mother sentie nt beings to have happiness and the causes of 
happiness, 
5. compassion – the wish for all mother sentient  beings to be free from suffering and the 
causes of suffering; 
6. the extraordinary attitude – the determination to  take the responsibility upon oneself to set 
all sentient beings in happiness and the causes of happiness and to free them from suffering and the causes of suffering. However, although we have made this promise, we are not yet able to do this. Who can do this? Only a buddha can do this.  
These six causes lead to the seventh point, the result: 7. the mind of enlightenment, the wish to attain enlightenment fo r the benefit of all sentient 
beings. 
 Shantideva’s texts, on the other hand, set ou t the disadvantages of self-cherishing and the 
advantages of cherishing others. We examine th em by thinking about them carefully, whereby 
we will generate the wish to exchange these tw o attitudes, that is, to abandon self-cherishing 
and practice cherishing others. Having generated this mind, we then engage in the meditation 
on taking-and-giving. Motivated by great compassi on we take others sufferings upon ourselves, 
and then motivated by love we give them all our happiness. To gether with the inhalation of 
the breath, we image that all th e sufferings, problems, and so fo rth of sentient beings exit 
from their right nostrils in the aspect of black sm oke. It enters our left nostril and descends to 
our heart where there is a heap of black dust in  the nature of the self -cherishing mind. When 
it comes into contact with the black heap, it completely destro ys it. Next, motivated by love 
we imagine giving others our excellent body, our wealth, the roots of virtue we have created in 
the three times. While exhaling through our righ t nostril, we imagine that white light or air 
exits from our right nostril and enters their left nostrils. It fills their bodies, whereby they experience happiness and receive whatever they n eed. In this way, we should train in giving-
and-taking combined with the inhalation and exhalation of the breath, whereby we abandon 
our self-cherishing and practice cherishing others . This will lead to a co mplete change in our 
present mental attitude, that is, we will give up cherishing ourselves and develop the attitude 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
492 of cherishing others. In this manner we will develop the extraordinary attitude and then, as a 
result, the mind of enlightenment. In this case the mind of enlightenment that is developed is 
said to be even stronger than that developed by means of the sevenfold instruction on cause 
and effect in that we wish to attain enlightenmen t in order to liberate all sentient beings from 
suffering and set them in the state of happiness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
The way to train the mind by means of the sevenfold instruction on cause and effect 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1 The sevenfold instruction on caus e and effect transmitted from the great Jowo 
Atisha 
A Short presentation  
B Extensive explanation 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1A Short presentation  
The sevenfold cause and effect is the seven: 
1. a complete buddha is born from the mind of enlightenment;  
2. this mind from the extraordinary attitude;  
3. this attitude from compassion;  
4. compassion from love;  
5. love from the wish to repay the kindness; 
6. the wish to repay the kindness from remembering the kindness; and  
7. remembering the kindness from seeing them as one's mother. 
 
A complete buddha arises from the mind of enli ghtenment, the mind of enlightenment arises 
from the extraordinary attitude, the extraordinar y attitude arises from great compassion, great 
compassion arises from attracted love, attrac ted love arises from the wish to repay the 
kindness, the wish to repay the kindness arises from remembering the kindness of all sentient 
beings, and remembering their kindness arises from recognizing that all sentient beings have 
been our mother. Here the seven points are set ou t in the reverse order, whereas usually it is 
said that we need to: 
1. recognize all sentient beings as having been our mothers,  
2. remember their kindness,  
3. develop the wish to repay their kindness,  
4. repay their kindness by developing love and compassion,  
5. generate the extraordinary attitude wishing to ta ke the responsibility of setting all sentient 
beings in happiness and the causes of happine ss and freeing them from suffering and the 
causes of suffering,  
6. generate the mind of enlightenment, and  
7. become a buddha.  
 Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B Extensive explanation 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
493 1 Generating certainty concerning the stages  
2 The actual gradual training  
There are two [points]: Generating certai nty concerning the stages and {161} the 
actual gradual training  
 
We need to generate certainty regarding the st ages and then actual train in them gradually. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1 Generating certainty concerning the stages 
A Demonstrating that compassion is the root of the Great Vehicle 
B The way in which the other causes and effects become causes and effects of that 
[compassion ] 
The first one has two [points]: demonstratin g that compassion is the root of the 
Great Vehicle and the way in which the ot her causes and effects become causes 
and effects of that [compassion ].  
 
The root of the Mahayana is grea t compassion. Why is that? It is because in order to become a 
Mahayanist we need to develop the mind of en lightenment (bodhichitta), and in order to 
develop the mind of enlightenment we need to  develop its main cause – great compassion. 
Due to this, compassion is said to  be the root of the Mahayana. It  is also said to be important 
at the beginning, in the middle, and at the end.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1A Demonstrating that compassion is the root of the Great Vehicle 
1 The importance [of compassion] at the beginning 
2 The importance [of compassion] in the middle 
3 The importance [of compassion] at the end 
The first has three [points]: the importan ce [of compassion] at the beginning, the 
importance [of compassion] in the middle,  and the importance [of compassion] at 
the end. 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1A1 The importance [of compassion] at the beginning 
A The responsibility for liberating migrating beings depends upon [compassion ] 
First: When the mind is moved by great compassion, one will definitely resolve to 
get all sentient beings out of cyclic exis t e n c e .  H o w e v e r ,  i f  one’s compassion is 
inferior, deeds that accord with that will  not come about. Therefore, whether you 
take responsibility for liberating all migrating beings without exception depends on 
that, and, if you do not take that re sponsibility, you will not enter the Great 
Vehicle. That is why compassion  is important at the beginning. 
 
The cause for taking the responsibility to libe rate all migrating beings upon ourselves is 
compassion. Thus, when the mind is moved by compassion, we will definitely determine to 
liberate all sentient beings from suffering, that is , to get them out of cycl ic existence. We need 
to make such a promise or determination. On the other hand, if our compassion is weak or 
inferior, our determination to do this will al so be weak and there will be little resulting 
activity. Thus, a strong determination to liberate  all sentient beings from cyclic existence has 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
494 great compassion as its cause. If we do not genera te the wish to take care of all sentient beings, 
we will not enter the Mahayana and we will no t be included among Mahayanists. Therefore, 
compassion is important at the beginning du e to being the main cause of the mind of 
enlightenment. For this reason, we need to ge nerate great compassion at the beginning in 
order to enter the Mahayana path. We need to un derstand this and to strive to generate the 
wish to develop the mind of enlightenment and for this purpose to develop great compassion. 
Hence, we should train in great compassion – the attitude that wishes all sentient beings to be 
free from suffering and the causes of suffering. We must try to develop this attitude in our 
heart and maintain it strongly. To do so, we n eed to transform our mind into the very entity 
of great compassion. At this time the only mind that is manifest is great compassion. We need 
to strive to develop such a mind.  
 Thursday morning, 28 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 161) says:  
B [Compassion ] is the preliminary of all the paths of the Great Vehicle 
In the Akshayamati Nirdesa Sutra  it says:  
 
“Venerable son of Sharadvati, more over the great compassion of the 
bodhisattvas is inexhaustible. Why is that? It is because it is a 
preliminary. Venerable son of Sharadvati, it is like this. For example, 
just as the inward and outward flow of the breath is a preliminary for 
the human life-force faculty, like wise the great compassion of the 
bodhisattvas is a preliminary for perfectly accomplishing the Mahayana.” 
 Great compassion is a preliminary for all the paths of Mahayana. This was explained by the 
Buddha in the Akshayamati Nirdesa Sutra  in which he says that th e great compassion of the 
bodhisattvas is inexhaustible because it is a prel iminary, that is, it is a preliminary for all the 
paths of the Mahayana. For example, when we  develop the mind of enlightenment in our 
continuum we become a Mahayanist or Mahayana  practitioner. However, prior to this we 
need to develop great compassion, its main ca use. Therefore, all th e Mahayana paths – the 
path of accumulation, path of pr eparation, path of seeing, path of meditation, and path of no-
more-learning – must be preceded by the develo pment of compassion. For example, just as in 
order to inhale and exhale it is necessary to firs t have the life-force faculty as without it there 
will be no inhalation and exhalation of the brea th, likewise in order to achieve the qualities of 
the Mahayana we first need to develop great compassion. Great compassion is the basis for 
the Mahayana. Just as there cannot be a child without a mother, since a father alone cannot 
give birth to a child, so too there cannot be the Mahayana without great compassion.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
  
C [Compassion ] is the beginning of the conduct of bodhisattvas 
In the Gavagori [Sutra]  it says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
495  
“Manjushri, what is the beginning of the conduct of bodhisattvas. 
What is its domain?” Manjushri replied: “Son of the gods, the 
beginning of the conduct of bodhis attvas is great compassion. Its 
domain is sentient beings.” 
 
Gavagori is perhaps the name of a mounta in near Bodhgaya. In this sutra Buddha 
Shakyamuni asks Manjushri “What is the beginni ng of all the conduct of the bodhisattvas 
and what is domain?” Manjushri replied that th e beginning is great compassion because it is 
due to great compassion that we engage in the six perfections in order to ripen our own 
continuum and engage in the four means of gathering disciples in order to ripen other 
sentient beings’ continua. The four means of ga thering disciples are giving material things, 
talking nicely, giving teachings, and acting in  accordance with what one teaches. Having 
developed great compassion, we take other sent ient beings as our object or domain. For 
example, in order to practice generosity we n eed other sentient beings to receive our gifts. 
Likewise, the practice of morality also depends on other sentient beings, as does the practice 
of patience. In this way all the bodhisattva cond uct depends on other sentient beings. In this 
sense sentient beings are the domain of grea t compassion. By practicing the bodhisattva 
conduct we develop our inner qualities more and more.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1A2 The importance [of compassion] in the middle 
A If one does not cultivate co mpassion again and again, one will fa ll into the Hinayana  
Second: Although having generated a mind like that one time you enter the Great 
Vehicle, if you become discouraged upon seeing  
1. the great number of sentient beings and their bad actions,{162}  
2. the great difficulty of the trainings,  
3. the need to do them for a boundlessn ess and also limitless time scale,  
you will fall into the Lesser Vehicle.  
 
If we do not continually meditate on great compa ssion there is the risk of that we will fall into 
the Hinayana path. Even thou gh we have generated the mind of enlightenment and are 
engaging in the bodhisattva conduct, there is th e danger that due to th inking about the great 
number of sentient beings, many of whom are bad or evil, thinking that the trainings are 
difficult, and thinking that they  need to be done for a long time, we become discouraged, 
thinking that we are unable to practice the bodhisattva conduct, and give up the mind of 
enlightenment.  
 It is recounted that Buddha Shakyamuni had a disciple who could have achieved the state 
of a foe-destroyer if he had been given teachings on the Hinayana path. However, Manjushri 
gave him teachings on the profound path, after which this disciple criticized the Mahayana. 
Due to this, when he died, he was reborn in th e hell realm. Seeing with his clairvoyance that 
this would happen, Shariputra said to the Buddha, “Today Manjushri committed a very grave 
error. If I had taught this disciple, he woul d have attained the state of a foe-destroyer. 
However, Manjushri taught him profound empt iness due to which he will criticize the 
Mahayana through generating the view of an extr eme that holds that if things do not exist 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
496 inherently they do not exist at  a l l .  B e c a u s e  o f  t h i s ,  h e  w i l l  b e  r e b o r n  i n  t h e  h e l l  r e a l m . ”  
Buddha Shakyamuni replied, “Shariputra, pay atte ntion to me. Manjushri is very skilled in 
teaching others, whereas you are not. Even thou gh that person will take rebirth in the hell 
realm due to Manjushri having taught him th e profound, he will not stay there long and 
when he is once again reborn as a human being he will achieve the higher realizations of the 
Mahayana due to having heard the Mahayana te achings and having these imprints on his 
mind. He will quickly attain the seventh ground  of a bodhisattva. On the other hand, if you 
had taught him, he would have achieved the state of a Hinayana foe-destroyer and remained 
in meditative absorption for eons, whereby he  would have taken much longer to achieve 
buddhahood.” 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B One does not despair with respect to others’  welfare and easily completes the collections 
Due to having familiarized increasingly  with great compa ssion rather than 
generating it just once, you will not look at your own happiness and suffering and 
will not despair with respect to the welfar e of others. Due to that, you will easily 
complete all the collections. 
 Having generated great compassion and the mind of enlightenment, we need to continue to 
familiarize with them in order to make them st ronger and stronger. If we do this, we will not 
look at our own happiness and suffering but will  o n l y  w i s h  t o  t a k e  c a r e  o f  o t h e r  s e n t i e n t  
beings. In that way we will easily complete the two collections of merit and wisdom, whereby we will quickly achieve buddhahood. For this reas on, great compassion is also important in 
the middle of our practice. For example, when we  sow a seed in the ground, at the beginning 
we need a good seed and in the middle we need water, manure, heat, and so forth in order to 
bring the result of crops. Compassion is like the seed as well as the water and so forth since it 
is also necessary in the beginning and in  the middle in order to bring a result. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1A3 The importance [of compassion] at the end 
A The actual importance at the end 
B In particular, the advice to make the mind of enlightenment the focus of the instructions 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1A3A The actual importance at the end 
1 [Difference between buddhas and hearers]  
Third: The fact that, even when the buddhas have achieved their result, they do 
not abide in peace like the Hinayanists but enact the welfare of however many 
sentient beings there are throughout spac e, is also due to the power of their 
compassion, for, without it, they would become like hearers. 
 
Compassion is also important at the end, that  is, even once we have  achieved buddhahood. 
Why is this? It is because when the buddhas attain their result, buddhahood, they do not 
abide absorbed in the state of solitary peac e but instead, by the power of their great 
compassion, they enact the welf are of however many sentient beings there are throughout 
space. If the buddhas, on the other hand, did not possess this great compassion, they would 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
497 be like Hinayana hearers who abide absorbed in solitary peace. The Treasury of Manifest 
Dharma  says that the buddhas out of compassion look upon all sentient beings during the 
three parts of the day and the th ree parts of the night, examining who is ready to be ripened. 
Then, when they see sentient beings who are re ady to be ripened, they send emanations to 
teach them the Dharma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 All three external analogies symbolize compassion alone 
Glorious Chandrakirti said: “For the harves t, what is important in the beginning is 
the seed, in the middle the water, and in the end the maturation. Likewise, for the 
harvest of buddhahood, what is important in the beginning, the end, and in the 
middle is compassion.”  
 
In order to reap a harvest, in the beginning good seeds are necessary, in the middle water is 
necessary, and in the end the mature crops. In  a similar way, for buddhahood, compassion is 
important in the beginning, middle, and end. Th is is was is said by Chandrakirti at the 
beginning of his Supplement to the Middle Way  w h e n  h e  c h o o s e s  t o  p a y  h o m a g e  t o  g r e a t  
compassion although there are many other possi ble objects of veneration. Why does he do 
this? He chooses to prostrate to great comp assion prior to composing his text because 
compassion is important at the beginni ng, in the middle, and at the end. 
 
STUDENT : Is it correct to also have compassion fo r ourselves? I think that it depends on the 
person, whereby it would not be correct for a Mahayana practitioner who is developing 
bodhichitta and guarding the bodhisattva vows.  It also would not be right for a tantric 
practitioner who should have the di vine pride of being the deity.  
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : When we generate ourselves as the deity, we need to have the clear 
appearance of ourselves as the deity and the divine pride that thin ks “I am the deity.” 
However, this does not contradict the Mahayana , as compassion is the basis of all Mahayana 
qualities. When we practice tantra, we need the mind of enlightenment as a motivation, and 
this is produced by great compassion. Theref ore, even tantric practitioners who generate 
divine pride must take care of themselves as well as others. If we do not take care of ourselves, 
we will experience problems such as sickness and may even die, whereupon we will not be 
able to do anything for anyone anymore. While we need to take care of ourselves, we should 
still think to eat healthy food in order to be able  to take care of other sentient beings. If, by 
doing this, we achieve the state of a buddha, for example, Tara , we will then be in a much 
better position to help other sentient  beings. I think it is like this.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1A3B In particular, the advice to make the mind of enlightenment the focus of the instructions 
1 The need to make it the essence of one’s practice 
Shang Nachung Tonpa said: “Although I aske d Jowo [Atisha] for oral instructions, 
nothing came of it other than: ‘Abandon worldly thought. Cultivate the mind of 
enlightenment!’” Geshe Tonpa derided him and said: “You got the central point 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
498 of Jowo’s oral instructions! He unders tands the key point of the teaching, the 
Dharma.”  
 
Atisha told Shang Nachung (Small Nose) to gi ve up the eight worldly dharmas or concerns 
and to cultivate the mind of enlightenment.  Shang Nachung asked Geshe Drom Tonpa why 
Atisha had given him this response. Geshe Drom  Tonpa told him that he had received the 
central point of Atisha’s oral instructions as  Atisha had understood the key point of the 
Dharma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Since it is difficult to gain certainty [about this], it  is necessary to rely on accumulation, purification, 
and the scriptures 
As it is also difficult to gain certainty about this, you must accumulate and purify 
again and again and seek firm certainty th rough studying the scriptures, such as 
the Gandavyuha  and others, and the commentaries on them. It is as Glorious 
Ashvagosha says [referring to the Buddha]: 
 
“Your exalted mind is precious, The seed of complete enlightenment; 
Only you, heroic one, know  it to be the essence. 
{163} Other beings do not guess that.” 
 
Since it is difficult to gain certainty with resp ect to the importance of compassion, we need to 
accumulate merit, purify our negativities and obscurations, and study and read the scriptures 
of the Buddha, such as the Gandavyuha Sutra  and others, and the co mmentaries on them. By 
doing this, we will gain this certainty.  
 Ashvagosha was also called “Trusting Father ” and “Trusting Mother” because he listened 
to whatever his father and mother advised. In  this verse he says that Buddha Shakyamuni’s 
exalted mind, the mind of enlightenment, is a precious jewel and the seed of complete 
enlightenment, and that only the Buddha, the he roic one, understands it  to be the essence, 
whereas other beings do not. As hvagosha is the author of the Jataka Tales , that is, the Life 
Stories of the Buddha , as well as many beautiful poems that contain many analogies.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1B The way in which other caus es and effects become causes and effects of 
compassion  
1 The way in which the understanding as mothers through love are causes 
2 The way in which the extraordinary attitude and generation of the mind [of enlightenment] 
are effects 
With respect to the second there ar e two [points]: the way in which the 
understanding as mothers through love are causes, and the way in which the 
extraordinary attitude and generation of the mind [of enlightenment] are effects. 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1B1 The way in which the understanding as mothers through love are causes 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
499 A How to develop the intense wish to free sentient beings from suffering  
First: If you think again and again about th e suffering of a sentient being, this will 
generally give rise to the mere wish that it be free from suffering. However, for this 
mind to arise easily and for it to arise as ve ry strong and stable, to start with it must 
be a sentient being who has the aspect of being attractive and valuable. For, when 
suffering arises for someone close to us, we cannot bear it; when suffering arises 
for our enemies, we enjoy it; and when su ffering arises for someone who is neither 
a friend nor an enemy mainly an equanimity that neglects him arises.  
 The four steps of recognizing all sentient beings as having been our mother, remembering their kindness, generating the wish to repay their kindness, and repaying their kindness by 
developing love for them are said to be causes , whereas the last two steps – the extraordinary 
attitude and the mind of enlightenment – are sa id to be results. However, usually there are 
said to be six causes and on e effect. The six causes are: 
recognizing all sentient beings as having been our mother,  
remembering their kindness generating the wish to repay their kindness,  developing love for them, developing compassion for them, and developing the extraordinary attitude. The seventh, the effect, is the mind of enligh tenment. However, in this context both the 
extraordinary attitude and the mind of en lightenment are said to be effects.  
 We need to generate a strong determination to free all sentient beings from suffering. To 
do this, we need to think again and again about the fact that all sentient beings are suffering. 
To meditate on this one time is not enough, it  has to be done again and again. However, for 
this mind to arise easily and to be strong and stable, we first need to see all sentient beings as attractive and nice, whereby we will develop love for them and want to take care of them and cherish them. For example, when we see that our friends and relatives are suffering, we too 
suffer and are unable to bear it. On the ot her hand, when we see that our enemies are 
suffering, we are happy and rejoice. when we see that strangers are suffering, we remain 
indifferent or equanimous. Due to this, we firs t need to develop equanimity, not only with 
respect to strangers, but also  with respect to our friends and enemies. By developing 
equanimity, we will come to see all sentient beings  as equal. Then on this basis, we can begin 
to think that all sentient beings have been ou r relatives, such as our mother, father, and so 
forth. We should see all sentient beings as having been like our beloved mother of this life, or our father, sister, brother, grandfather, gr andmother, and so forth. Then, we should 
remember their kindness. Having seen their kindne ss, we should develop the wish to repay it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The difference of generating or not generating attraction toward friends, enemies, and the in between 
Regarding that, since the former is attrac tive to our mind, the more we cherish 
him, the more his suffering will be unbearable to us, and the more [compassion ] 
will arise for him. If we cherish him littl e or middling, our inability to bear [his 
suffering] will also be small. On the other hand, if we cherish someone very much, 
we are not able to bear it at all when he  meets with only slight suffering. When we 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
500 see our enemy suffering, not only does the wi sh that he be free from it not arise, 
but rather the thought occurs to us “May [his suffering ] become greater and may 
he not be free from it.” That is our re action to someone we find unattractive. 
Moreover, Our joy at his suffering will al so be greater or smaller depending on 
how unattractive he is. With respect to the suffering of someone who is neither a 
friend nor an enemy, it is neither that we are unable to bear it nor that we enjoy it. 
That is our reaction when someone is neither attractive nor unattractive.  
 
When we see a dear friend or relative suffering, we are unable to bear it and think about what we can do to help him or her. On the other hand , when they are happy, we rejoice in this and 
are also happy ourselves. On the other hand, when we see our enemies suffering, we enjoy it and wish them even more suffering  and do not wish them to be free from their suffering. This 
happens due to familiarity with hatred for enem ies, whereas for our friends and relatives we 
are familiar with attachment. Due to them, thes e two different attitudes arise. When we see 
strangers suffer, neither are we unable to bear it nor do we rejoice in it. This is because we do 
not have either a feeling of closeness or dist ance with respect to them. We do not care 
whether they are happy or suffering. This is du e to ignorance. Thus, th e three mental poisons 
produce different attitudes in our mind. We need  to recognize them and to understand that 
these three always make problems for us. For exampl e, in this life they make us unable to bear 
the suffering of our relatives and rejoice in the suffering of our enemies. We need to examine these attitudes in our own mind and see how they arise when the three mental poisons, 
attachment, hatred, and ignorance, overcome ou r mind. When they aris e, we need to apply 
their respectively antidotes. For example, when attachment arises we need to meditate on the 
object of attachment as having the aspect of ugliness; when hatred arises we need to meditate on love; and when ignorance arises we need to meditate on dependent-arising. In this way our 
mental poisons will initially diminish and th en, by continuing to meditate on dependent-
arising, we will come to understand the emptine ss of inherent existence we will be able to cut 
the root of the mental poison s. Thus, the meditation on em ptiness is the main antidote. 
Since all the afflictions have ignorance as thei r root, when we cut ignorance we also put an 
end to all the other afflictions, such as attach ment, hatred, and so forth. Therefore, in our 
daily life we need to examine our own mind and not others. We should take care of ourselves 
in this way.  Thursday afternoon, 28 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 163) says:  
C How to generate great love and great compassion by establishing them as greatly attractive by means 
of the three – recognizing them as our mothers, remembering their kindness, and wishing to repay it  
If this is done, meditating on sentient bein gs as our relatives is for the sake of {164} 
making them attractive to us. However, si nce the absolute of our relatives is our 
mother, the three – meditating on them as having been our mother, remembering 
their kindness, and wishing to repay this kindness – establishes them as dear and 
attractive. The love that holds sentient beings as delightful as an only child is the effect of these three. It produces compassion. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
501 In order to generate love we need to see al l sentient beings as having been our mother, 
remember their kindness, and wish to repay this  kindness. When these are strong, all sentient 
beings will appear as attractive, whereby we will generate love for them. Therefore, by 
meditating on all sentient beings as having been  our relatives or friends, we come to see them 
as being attractive and close to us. However, si nce the person closest to us is our mother, we 
try to see all sentient beings as having been  our mother and then remember their kindness 
and wish to repay their kindness. By doing this we  will see them as attractive and thereby wish 
to take care of and cherish them. For example, if a mother has an only child, she will have 
great love for it and only think about how she can take care of it and nourish it. Similarly, we 
too need to see sentient beings as if they were our only child and wish to take care of them. 
Of course, not all parents treat an  only child or their children in the same way, but the point 
is to see all sentient beings as attractive and nice, whereby we generate love for them. In this 
way, love is the result of the three: recognizing all sentient beings as having been our mother, remembering their kindness, and wishing to repay their kindness. 
 Great love produces great compassion – the wish that all sentient beings be free from 
suffering. Due to the arisal of love, we will wish to take care of sentient beings.  
 Middle Lam-Rim says:  
D With respect to mere love and compassion it is not certain which is the cause and which is the effect 
With respect to the love that wishes th em to meet with happiness and compassion, 
there does not seem to be any certainty as to which on e is the cause and which one 
is the effect.  
 
Love is the wish that all sentient beings meet  with happiness and compa ssion is the wish that 
all sentient beings be free from suffering. With re spect to mere love and mere compassion it is 
not certain which one is the cause and which one is the effect. Sometimes we generate 
compassion for someone and this produces love, and sometimes we generate love for 
someone and then this produces compassion. Ther efore, the order is not certain as to which 
one comes before the other.   Middle Lam-Rim says:  
E Whose system are these instructions  
To meditate on sentient beings as our re latives as the cause producing the mind [of 
enlightenment] has been explained by Acharya Chandrakirti, Venerable Chandra, 
and Acharya Kamalashila. 
 
In order to generate compassion we need to me ditate on all sentient beings as having been 
our close relatives. The system of these instructions comes to us from Acharya Chandrakirti, 
Venerable Chandra, and Acharya Kamalashil a. Chandrakirti mentions this in his Supplement 
to the Middle Way , Venerable Chandra in a text whose name I do not remember, and 
Kamalashila in his Stages of Meditation . Chandrakirti says that we need to develop compassion 
for all sentient beings and to do this we need  t o  s e e  a l l  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  a s  c l o s e  f a m i l y  
members. In order to develop the conventional mind of enlightenment we need its causes – 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
502 love and compassion. Love means to see all sentient  beings in an attractive aspect or as a close 
relative or friend.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1B2 The manner in which the extraordinary attitude and the generation of the 
mind [of enlightenment] are effects 
A Doubt 
B Response 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1B2A Doubt 
Second: It should really be sufficient if, compassion having arisen due to having 
gradually trained the mind, the wish arises  to attain enlightenment for the welfare 
of all sentient beings. So why is the extr aordinary attitude inserted between them?  
 
Someone thinks that when we generate compassi on by meditating, after that we will generate 
the mind of enlightenment that wishes to atta in enlightenment for the welfare of sentient 
beings. Based on this he asks why is it necessa ry to generate the extraordinary attitude in 
between compassion and the mind of enlightenmen t. In other words, this person doubts that 
the extraordinary attitude is necessary in that great compassion alone should be sufficient to 
induce the mind of enlightenment.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-1B2B Response 
1 Although there is immeasurable compassion, there may not be the extraordinary attitude that takes 
the responsibility upon oneself 
Although hearers and solitary realizers also have the immeasurable love and 
compassion that thinks, “If only sentient  beings were to meet with happiness and 
were free from suffering!,” still, those who are not Mahayanists do not take the 
responsibility to accomplish happiness for al l sentient beings and to eliminate their 
suffering. Therefore, it is necessary to generate the extraordinary attitude, the 
special attitude of those with courageous hearts. You should understand this from 
a quotation of the Sutra Requested by Sagaramati in the commentary on the 
Uttaratantra.  
 
Although hearers and solitary realizers have i mmeasurable love and compassion they do not 
take upon themselves the responsibility for all sentient beings. For example, although 
immeasurable compassion observes immeasurable sentient beings and wishes them to be free from suffering, it does not think “I myself will accept the responsibility to free them from 
suffering.” Thus, although the lo ve that wishes that sentient beings meet with happiness and 
the compassion that wishes them to be free from  suffering also exist in hearers and solitary 
realizers, they alone are not sufficient. We need to have a much stronger thought, that is, we 
need to not only wish them happiness and freedom  from suffering but in addition we need to 
take the responsibility upon ourselves to acco mplish this. Therefore, in addition to great 
compassion we need to generate the extraordin ary attitude. This is mentioned in a citation 
from the Sutra Requested by Sagaramati  in the commentary on the Uttaratantra . We need the 
strong compassion that takes upon our shoulders the responsibility to set all sentient beings 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
503 in happiness and free them from suffering. When th is attitude is strong, it produces the mind 
of enlightenment that wishes to attain enligh tenment for the benefit of all sentient beings. 
Therefore, between compassion and the mind  of enlightenment, one extra cause, the 
extraordinary attitude, is necessary.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 How the extraordinary attitude induces the generation of the mind [of enlightenment ] 
Once the mind of freeing sentient beings has arisen in this manner, owing to your 
current situation you could not complete even the welfare of one sentient being. 
Not only that, even if you were to atta in the state of the two [kinds of] foe-
destroyer, {165} you would only be able [to complete the welfare ] o f  v e r y  f e w  
beings and their welfare would be to me rely accomplish liberation. Since they 
cannot set them in omniscience, who co uld complete the temporary and final 
welfare of limitless sentient beings? If yo u reflect in this vein, having come to 
understand that only a buddha is able to do this, you will generate the wish to 
attain buddhahood for the we lfare of sentient beings. 
 
How does the extraordinary attitude induce th e mind of enlightenment? Having generated 
the extraordinary attitude, the thought to take the responsibility to free all sentient beings 
from suffering and set them in happiness, we th en realize that at the moment we are unable 
to achieve this. In fact we are not even able to accomplish the complete welfare of a single 
sentient being. Then, even if we were to attain the state of a hearer foe- destroyer or a solitary 
realizer foe-destroyer, still we would only be a b l e  t o  h e l p  a  f e w  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  t o  a c h i e v e  
liberation, but we would not be able to set all sentient beings in the state of omniscience. 
Therefore, who is it that can do this? Who can fulfill the te mporary and final aims of all 
s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s ?  W e  s h o u l d  s e a r c h  f o r  t h i s  p e r s o n  u n t i l  w e  c o m e  t o  r e a l i z e  t h a t  o n l y  a  complete buddha is able to do this. Therefore, th inking that only buddhas are able to do this, 
we should generate the wish to achieve buddhah ood for the benefit of all sentient beings. In 
this way we generate the mind of enlightenmen t. We think “First I will attain enlightenment 
for the benefit of all sentient beings.” Thus, we  generate the mind of enlightenment. By doing 
so, we will be able to help all sentient beings. Therefore, we must strive to generate 
bodhichitta. Chitta  means primary mind, bodhi  means enlightenment. Bodhichitta is a 
primary mind that is concomitant with the caus al aspiration to attain enlightenment and the 
accompanying aspiration to free all sentient be ings from suffering. Therefore, there are two 
aspirations: the aspiration to attain enligh tenment oneself and the aspiration to free all 
sentient beings from suffering and set them in  nirvana, the state gone beyond suffering. The 
final welfare of sentient beings is the achiev ement of enlightenment or non-abiding nirvana, 
in which one does not abide either in cyclic exis tence or in solitary peac e. We need to set all 
sentient beings in this state and thereby fulfill their welfare.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2 The actual gradual training 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
504 A Training the mind to strive for the welfare of others 
B Training the mind to strive for enlightenment 
C The result of the training: identifying the generation of the mind [of enlightenment] 
The second – the actual gradual training – has three [points]: training the mind to 
strive for the welfare of others, training the mind to strive for enlightenment, and 
the result of the training: identify ing the generation of the mind [of 
enlightenment]. 
 
We need to train the mind in striving to enact the welfare of others, train the mind in striving 
for enlightenment, and recognize the result of tr aining which is the mind of enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A Training the mind to strive for the welfare of others 
1 Laying the foundation for generating this mind 
2 Actual generation of that mind 
Concerning the first there are two [points: ] Laying the foundation for generating 
this mind and actual generation of that mind.  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A1 Laying the foundation for generating this mind 
A Producing an equanimous mind toward sentient beings  B Establishing them all in having an attractive aspect 
The first has two [points]: Producing an eq uanimous mind toward sentient beings 
and establishing them all in having an attractive aspect. 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A1A Producing an equanim ous mind toward sentient beings 
1 The preparatory steps 
2 How to meditate on equanimity 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A1A-1 The preparatory steps 
First: The preparatory steps etc. as expl ained before in the context of small and 
middling beings should also be adopted and sustained here. 
 
First we need to train our mind to strive to enac t the welfare of others. To do this we need to 
establish or lay the basis or foundation for genera ting this mind and then we need to actually 
generate this mind. To establish the basis mean s to develop equanimity toward all sentient 
beings by looking at them without attachment and anger. There are many reasons to see all 
sentient beings as equal. There are reasons fr om our own side and reasons from the side of 
others. By seeing all sentient beings as attracti ve, we generate love for them. To do this, there 
are the preparatory steps that which were al ready explained in the context of small and 
middling beings. Since they have already been ex plained, we will not go into detail about 
t h e m  h e r e .  H o w e v e r ,  i n  b r i e f ,  f i r s t  w e  n e e d  t o  r e l y  o n  a  t e a c h e r .  T o  d o  s o ,  w e  n e e d  t o  
understand what kind of qualities he needs and what kind of qualities we, the disciples, need. 
Having found a qualified teacher, we should rely  on him in thought and action by generating 
respect for him and so forth. Then we need to meditate on the precious human rebirth 
possessing eighteen qualities, the fact that we  have obtained it now, that it is highly 
meaningful, and that it can quickly be lost . We then meditate on death-impermanence 
thinking about the fact that death is certain, th e time of death is uncertain, and at the time 
only the Dharma will help us. Having died, we  will have to take rebirth in either the bad 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
505 migrations or the happy migrations. Seeking protec tion from suffering, we go for refuge to the 
Three Jewels, the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. Having done so, we need to abide by the 
respectively trainings and also guard the law of ca use and effect. If, in spite of our efforts, we 
create non-virtuous actions, we need to pu rify them immediately by applying the four 
opponent powers. This is brief overview of the path of small beings. 
  In the context of the path of middling beings , we meditate on true sufferings, for example, 
the eight, six, and three types of sufferings th at occur even in the higher realms. Then we 
meditate on their causes, true origins, which are actions and afflictions. Actions have the 
afflictions as their causal motivation, whereas th e time motivation can be either virtuous or 
non-virtuous whereby our actions of body and speech become respectively virtuous or non-
virtuous. Then we reflect on the fact that we must eliminate the cause of true sufferings and 
achieve true cessations of suffering. How can we achieve them? By meditating on true paths. 
To do so, we need to train in the three high er trainings of morality, concentration, and 
wisdom. Among them, morality is the basis of concentration and wisdom. By training in the 
three higher trainings we can achieve the small enlightenment of a hearer foe-destroyer or the 
middling enlightenment of a solitary realizer foe-destroyer. In both cases we achieve the 
abandonment of the afflictive obscurations wh ereby we ourselves are liberated from cyclic 
existence. However, this alone is not enough in that we have neither accomplished our own 
complete welfare nor have we accomplished others ’ welfare. Therefore, seeing its qualities, we 
must enter the Mahayana path.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A1A-2 How to meditate on equanimity 
A The need for an equanimous mind by stopping attachment and hatred  
To start with, if you do not accomplish an  equanimous mind by stopping the bias 
of attachment toward some sentient beings  and hatred toward others, any love and 
compassion that arise will be generated to gether with partiality. As this does not 
occur if you observe them without partiali ty, you should meditate on equanimity. 
 
We need to equalize sentient beings in our mi nd because at the moment we have attachment 
to some, our friends and relatives, and hatred for others, our enemies. We need to stop the 
attachment and hatred that make  us biased due to which we think “my side” and “the other 
side.” If we do not have an equanimous mind toward sentient beings, we will not be able to generate love and compassion for all of them .  O r ,  e v e n  i f  w e  g e n e r a t e  s o m e  l o v e  a n d  
compassion for them, it will not be actual love and compassion as it will be biased. To have 
love and compassion only for those on our own side is not enough. Since we need to 
completely eliminate our biased attitude, we need to meditate on equanimity.   There are different kinds of equanimity, for example, the equanimity that is a feeling, 
which is between happy feeling and suffering feelin g. This equanimity is not what we need to 
develop here. There is also an equanimity that  is a compositional factor, but the equanimity 
of this context is also not this equanimity. Here  we need to develop the equanimity that sees 
all sentient beings as not different and has the same attitude towa rd all of them. This 
equanimity does not divide people into friends, enemies, and strangers but sees all of them as 
equal. We need this kind of equanimous mi nd. We should meditate on it in order to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
506 eliminate attachment for friends, hatred for en emies, and ignorance for strangers, whereby we 
will come to see all sentient beings in the same way.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B What kind of equanimity needs to be meditated 
Among the two types of equanimous minds that have been taught, one that has 
the aspect of establishing sentient beings  as lacking mental afflictions, such as 
attachment, hatred, and so forth, and one in which oneself is free from attachment 
and hatred towards sentient beings, here it is the latter. 
 
There are two types of equanimity. The first has the aspect of establishing all other sentient beings in freedom from attachm ent and hatred, however this is not the equanimity of this 
context. Rather it is the second type, the equa nimity in which we ourselves have eliminated 
attachment for some sentient beings and hatred fo r others. We need this attitude that is equal 
toward all sentient beings. Hence, the thought “How nice it would be if all sentient beings were to abide in equanimity free from attachment  for the close and hatred  for the distant” is 
not the equanimity here. Rather we ourselves have  to take all sentient beings as our observed 
object and, by freeing our mind from attachment and hatred, come to see all sentient beings as equal whereby we come to have the same a ttitude toward them. This is the attitude in 
which we need to train.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C  The order of the meditation 
Since this is the order of meditation by which it will easily arise, initially you 
should take a person who has neither done you harm or benefit as your object of 
meditation, {166} and, through eliminatin g attachment and anger, accomplish an 
equanimous mind. When you have accomplished an equanimous mind toward 
that [person], you then need to accomplish an equanimous mind with respect to 
friends and relatives. A non-equanimous mind  toward them is to be biased due to 
attachment and hatred or to not be equanimous due to lesser or greater 
attachment. When you are equanimous toward that [person], you should cultivate 
an equanimous mind toward your enemies. The lack of equanimity toward them is 
due to hatred that derives from seeing them as totally incompatible [with yourself]. 
Having become equanimous toward them, you should cultivate an equanimous mind toward all sentient beings.  
 Initially we should meditate on equanimity wi th respect to those for whom we do not have 
either attachment or hatred. In other words, we  should first take a stranger as our observed 
object and try to generate equanimity with respec t to that person. This will make it easier for 
equanimity to arise in our mind. By eliminating attachment and hatred for the stranger, we will develop an equanimous mind toward him or her.   When we are able to do this with strangers,  we should then try to generate equanimity 
toward our friends. We need to develop an eq uanimous mind toward them, if we are unable 
to do this it is because we divide people into the two groups of friends and enemies or 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
507 because we have strong or weaker  attachment toward them. Due to this we need to strive to 
equalize our mind toward our friends, that is, to develop a mind in which there is neither 
attachment or hatred. Having accomplished th is, we should develop an equanimous mind 
toward those people whom we think of as our enemies. Why do we not have an equal mind 
toward enemies? It is because, thinking that th ey are totally incompatible with us, we generate 
hatred or anger toward them. Hence, we also need to develop an equanimous mind toward 
our enemies.  
 In meditation, we should imagine three people , a friend, an enemy, and a stranger, as if 
t h e y  w e r e  s e a t e d  i n  f r o n t  o f  u s .  W e  s h o u l d  first develop an equanimous mind toward the 
stranger, then toward the friend, and then towa rd the enemy. When we are able to generate 
the same attitude toward these three people, we should then extend this attitude and apply it 
to all sentient beings, for example, first to al l the people surrounding us, then to all human 
beings, then to animals, and then to other bein gs. We need to develop this equanimous mind 
toward all sentient beings, rather than having the attitude that holds some close and others 
distant. We should meditate on equanimity in this way.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D By meditating on what are attachment and hatred stopped 
Moreover, the following two thoughts are taught in [Kamalashila’s] Middling Stages 
of Meditation  (Bhavanakrama ):  
1. “Since from their side all sentient beings are the same in wanting happiness 
and not wanting suffering, it would be inappropriate for me to benefit some w h o m  I  c o n s i d e r  c l o s e  a n d  h a r m  o r  f a i l  t o  b e n e f i t  o t h e r s  w h o m  I  c o n s i d e r  
distant.” 
2. “Since from my own side there is not any sentient being who in beginningless 
cyclic existence has not been my relative  hundreds of times, who should I have 
attachment for and who should I have hatred for?” 
Also with regard to attachment to our relatives, the Sutra Requested by the Noble 
Daughter Chandr[ottama]  says: 
 
I have killed all of you in former times, 
I have also been chopped and cut to pieces by you in former times. 
All beings have been enemies and murderers unto each other. 
Therefore, how can a mind of at tachment possibly arise in you? 
 As was explained above in the context of the fault of uncertainty, think about the 
way in which friends and enemies quickly change. Due to this, avert both anger 
and attachment. 
 By means of meditating, we need to stop the a ttachment that holds some close and the hatred 
that holds others distant. From their own side  all sentient beings are the same in wanting 
happiness and not wanting suffering. Because they have the same desire or aim, it is not 
correct to hold some sentient beings close an d then want to benefit and help them and to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
508 hold other sentient beings distant and want to harm them or not want to benefit them. This 
is not at all right since sentient beings from their own side are equal. 
 In addition, from our own side we have been wandering in cyclic existence from 
beginningless time until now and there is no sentient being who has not been our dear friend 
or relative hundreds of times. Therefore, it is  not right to be attached to and want to help 
some while hating and wanting to harm others.  
 The Sutra Requested by the No ble Daughter Chandrottama  also says “In the past I have killed 
all of you and you have also chopped and cut me into pieces. We have killed each other. 
Therefore, why are you attached to some?” In short, since in the past all of us killed and 
chopped each other up why are we now attached to some and not to others? We should think 
about the fact that sometimes friends transf orm into enemies, so metimes enemies into 
friends, and sometimes friends and enemies into st rangers. Cyclic existence is like this, there 
is no certainty at all. Sometimes we kill each other, sometimes we take care of each other. 
This was already explained in the context of the six shortcomings of cyclic existence, one of 
which is the shortcoming of uncertainty, i.e., that friends become enemies, enemies become 
f r i e n d s ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  W e  n e e d  t o  t r y  t o  u n derstand this and to eliminate attachment and 
hatred toward all other sentient beings. We need to strive to do this. This is our 
responsibility! It is our job! 
 Friday morning, 29 April 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 166) says:  
E Ceasing attachment and hatred, although the mind of friend and enemy has not ceased  
{167} Since we need to consider them as  friends or enemies through apprehending 
them to be the basis of respec tive features, what is to be stopped is the mind that is 
biased due to attachment and hatred thro ugh taking as a reason “He/she is an 
enemy or friend” while the mind of friend and enemy does not need to be averted. 
 
In relation to considering someone to be friend or enemy, what we need to do is to change our mind. Due to thinking of someone as a friend  or an enemy there arises attachment to the 
friend and hatred or anger toward the enemy, whereupon we divide people into two distinct 
groups. This is the attitude that we need to st op or block. How can we do this? We should try 
to see all sentient beings as having an attractive aspect.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A1B Establishing them all in having an attractive aspect 
1 Meditating them as one's mother 
2 Remembering their kindness 3 Meditating on repaying their kindness 
The second one has three [points]: medita ting them as one’s mother, remembering 
their kindness, and the meditating on repaying their kindness. 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A1B-1 Meditating them as one's mother 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
509 A The thought that they have been one’s mother in the past 
First: In the sutras it is taught that sinc e cyclic existence is beginningless our births 
are also beginningless. Being incessantly born  and dying, there is no body in cyclic 
existence that we have not taken, no pl ace in which we have not been born, and 
no one who has not been a relative such as our mother and so forth. 
B The thought that they are presently one’s mother 
Moreover, this is not only true of the pa st, they will again be that in the future. 
Therefore reflecting in that way, you shou ld try to gain unshakable certainty that 
they have been your mother, for, once th at certainty arises, it will be easy to 
remember their kindness and so forth. On the other hand, if it does no arise, there 
will be no foundation for remembering their kindness and so forth. 
 We need to first meditate that all sentient be ings have been our mother, then we need to 
remember the kindness of our mother sentient beings, and then we need to wish to repay 
their kindness. In the past each  sentient being has been our mo ther innumerable times and in 
the future they will once again be our mother. Wh y is this? It is because we have been taking 
rebirth since beginningless time in cyclic existe nce whereby even our rebirths as human beings 
alone are infinite. Having died and taken birth again and again, we cannot say that we have 
not been born with a certain body, that we have not been born in a certain place, for 
example, in the west but not in the east, an d that we have not had such a being as our 
mother. Because we have taken so many bodies all sentient beings have been our mothers. 
Because we have taken rebirth in all the ten di rections, all sentient beings have been our 
mothers and relatives in these rebirths. Therefor e, all sentient beings have been our mother.
