There does not exist one sentient being who has not been our mother. No such sentient 
being can be found. Therefore, we need to th ink that all sentient beings have been our 
mother, father, brother, sister, uncle, aunt, grandfather, grandmother, great grandmother, 
great grandfather, son, daughter, and so forth. A l l  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  h a v e  b e e n  j u s t  l i k e  o u r  
relatives of this life. In addition, our mother and father of this life have also been our mother 
and father countless times. Therefore, all sent ient beings have been our mother. We should 
think about this. There is no one about whom we can say “He/she has not been my mother,” 
nor is there anyone who will not become our moth er or father once again. As long as we do 
not become free from cyclic existence we will cont inue to take rebirth and to do this we need 
a mother and father. Therefore, in the future sentient beings will once again be our mother 
and father. Thinking in this w ay, we should develop a stable understanding that all sentient 
beings have been our mother. We need to ga in stable certainty regarding this fact.  
 When we generate the certainty that all sent i e n t  b e i n g s  h a v e  b e e n  o u r  m o t h e r  i t  w i l l  
become easy to remember their kindness and to generate the wish to re pay their kindness. On 
the other hand, if we do not generate this unders tanding, it will also be difficult to remember 
that all sentient beings have been kind and to generate the wish to re pay their kindness. This 
is because the basis, the recognition that all sent ient beings have been our mother, is lacking. 
Therefore, we first need to generate this reco gnition in order to remember their kindness and 
then wish to repay it. In short, we need a strong recognition of the fact that all sentient beings have been our mother.   Even one sentient being has been our mother so  many times that if th e milk that we drunk 
as her child were to be gathered together it wo uld be a huge ocean that would not fit in this 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
510 world. Thus, there is no one that we can poin t to who has not been our mother. Even all the 
people in this room have been our mother, our father, our sister, our brother, our son, or our 
daughter. Our relationship with them is no longer like this, but it was in the past.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A1B-2 Remembering their kindness 
A The thought that it is skillful and so forth to start with one's mother of this life 
Second: [Geshe] Potowa maintained that if, after meditating on all sentient beings 
as having been your mother, you initially me ditate on your mother of this life, [the 
thought remembering their kindness] will  quickly be generated. You should 
meditate like that. Thinking that your moth er in a clear aspect is in front of you, 
repeatedly reflect: “She has been my moth er not only now, but times past counting 
since beginningless cyclic existence. Wh en she was my mother, she protected me 
from all harm and accomplished all happiness and benefit for me.” 
 
We need to try to understand that all sentient  beings have been our mother by meditate on 
this. We should then remember their kindness. We should begin by thinking that our mother 
of this life has been very, very kind to us. Sinc e we can easily recognize her kindness, it will be 
easier to develop the thought of her kindness. We  should visualize our mother clearly in front 
of us, thinking that she has also been our moth er many, many times in the past. By thinking 
in this way, the recognition of her kindness wi ll arise even more easily as we will also 
remember her kindness in other lives. When sh e was our mother, she st rove to protect us 
from harm and tried to benefit us and to make us happy. We should meditate on everything 
that she has tried to do for us. This will make it easier to recognize her kindness. 
 Perhaps there are some exceptions? HA-HA! I am joking. Nowadays some people, thinking 
that their mother of this life has not been ki nd to them and that she was hard on them, do 
not want to think about her kindness. Those peop le tend to think, “She was not kind to me. 
She was very hard one me.” In this case, what  should we do? We should choose whoever has 
been the most close to us and then think that al l sentient beings have been our father, sister, 
uncle, aunt, grandfather, grandmother, and so forth. We can also choose a good friend and 
then think that just as he or she has been very kind to us so too have all sentient beings been our friends and have been very kind to us. Not only has this friend been our friend in this 
life, but he or she has also been our dear friend in many previous lives. We should recognize his or her kindness in this way, and then extend  it to all sentient beings. Maybe we can also 
meditate in this way if we do not have  a good relationship with our mother.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The way in which she also protected one in this life 
In particular, even in this life, she initially carried you in her womb for a long 
time. Then, when you were born, she pressed your yellow tousled hair against the heat of her body, she swung you on her fi ngertips, nursed you with her milk, fed 
you as an infant from her mouth, {168} wiped away your saliva with her mouth, 
cleaned your filth with her hands, caring  for your untiringly in many different 
ways. In addition, when you were hungry or  thirsty, she gave you food and drink. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
511 When you were cold, she gave you clothing. When you were in need, she gave you 
her possessions that she herself had hard ly used. When even the most basic 
necessities where hard to come by, she bo re great hardship and spared herself no 
negativity, suffering, or bad talk to get it and give them to me. 
 
In particular our mother has taken care of us in  this life. She carried us  in her womb for nine 
months, always taking care of us by avoiding eating and drinking certain food and drink 
through not wanting to harm her baby. Then, when  we were born, she continued to take care 
of us. When we were very small, she kept us warm with her body heat, held us in her arms, 
gave us her milk to drink, and made our food soft by chewing it herself so that it was easy for 
us to digest. She wiped away our snot and saliva . She cleaned our urine and feces. Like this, 
she tirelessly took care of us in many ways. Wh en we were hungry or thirsty, she immediately 
gave us her own food and drink. When we were  cold, she dressed us in warm clothing. When 
we were poor, she gave us her possessions th at she has kept apart for us. When basic 
necessities were hard to find, she bore suffer ing, engaged in negativities, accepted a bad 
reputation, and so forth in order to help us. Ther efore, our mother has been very kind to us.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C A mother cherishes her child more than herself 
Whenever her child suffers from a diseas e and so forth, she wishes from the 
depths of her heart to die rather than for he r child to die, to be ill rather than for 
h e r  c h i l d  t o  b e  i l l ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  W i t h  effort, she does whatever she can to 
eliminate [his suffering]. 
 
A mother cherishes her child more than herself. If her child is suffering from an illness, the 
mother thinks it would be better for herself to di e rather than for her child to die. If her child 
is sick, she thinks “It would be better for me to be sick, rather than for my child to be sick.” 
She thinks from the depths of her heart that it would be better for her to be sick rather than 
for her child to be sick, and that it would be be tter for her to die rather than for her child to 
d i e .  A  m o t h e r  i s  a l w a y s  t h i n k i n g  o f  h e r  c h i l d ,  therefore she is very kind. She also tries by 
whatever action possible to reli eve her child’s suffering of sickness, heat, cold, hunger, and 
thirst. She does whatever she possibly can to remove her child’s suffering.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D How to meditate on the essential points separately 
To summarize, by whatever level of know ledge and whatever level of ability she 
had, she thought single-pointedly about the way in which to accomplish your 
happiness and benefit, and to remove any harm and suffering for you.  
 
The essence is that, in brief, she did whatev er she knew and could do, using every possible 
method, to accomplish our benefit and happiness and to remove all our harm and suffering. She had a single-pointed mind thinking only about how to remove our suffering and harm 
and how to make us happy and benefit us. In ot her words, she constantly thinks how she can 
help her child grow up to be a good, learne d, and humble person. Whatever knowledge she 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
512 has, she tries to teach it to her child. In this way, she constant ly thinks single-pointedly about 
her child, not thinking of anything else . Therefore, the mother is very kind.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
E Progressing gradually, how to meditate 
If, by meditating in this way, a mind remembering her kindness, that is not just 
words, arises, then you should meditate on recognizing other friends and relatives, your father and so forth, as also having been your mothers. Then, you should also 
meditate on recognizing neutral [persons] as having been your mothers. When you 
have generated a similar mind toward them as that toward your friends and 
relatives, you should also meditate on recognizing your enemies as also having 
been your mother. When you have generate d a similar mind toward them as that 
toward your mother, you should proceed to  meditate on recognizing all sentient 
beings as having been you mother, and th en gradually extend it more and more. 
 Our understanding of the kindness of mother se ntient beings should not be just words on 
our lips, it should be an understanding that comes from our heart and that moves our mind. When we have developed such an understanding, we should go on to think that all sentient beings have been our father and have been kind  to us, just like our present father who has 
taken care of us. If we were sick, he would chose to be sick, and if we were dying, he would prefer to die himself. He helped us when we were hungry by giving us food, when we were 
cold by giving us clothing, when we were thirsty by giving us something to drink, and so forth. 
He tried to take care of us in whatever way he could, therefore our father is also very kind.  
 We should think about the fact that our dear  friends have also been our mother and have 
been very kind to us. We should remember th eir kindness. Then, we should also recognize 
them as having been our mother in the past. Likewise our present enemy has also been our 
mother in the past and at that time was very kind to us. Also those who are our enemies now 
a r e  k i n d  t o  u s  i n  t h a t  t h e y  g i v e  u s  t h e  o p p o rtunity to practice patience, which is a very 
powerful practice. Our enemies are in reality our precious teachers who teach us how to 
develop patience. Thus, we can also meditate on our enemies in this way, that is, as being our 
teacher who helps us to improve our practice of patience.   Imagining a friend, enemy, and st ranger in front of us, we should meditate on the friend as 
having been our mother, on our enemy as ha ving been our mother, and so forth. Then 
extend this attitude outward until we recognize that all sentient beings have been our mother. 
We begin with the people close to us, then extend  this attitude to the other people around us, 
then to all human beings, and then to all sentient  beings, thinking that they have all been our 
mother, father, and so forth. In this way we recognize the kindness of all sentient beings. Then, what do we need to do? We need to repay their kindness, not their harm! It is like, for example, if we have financial difficulties and so meone helps us by giving us money, we think 
that that person is very kind and remember ev en much later that when  we were poor this 
person helped us. In this way, we should think about the kindness of all sentient beings.   Middle Lam-Rim says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A1B-3 The wish to repay their kindness 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
513 A Even though you do not recognize them due to birth and death-transference, it would be shameless to 
neglect them 
Third: There is nothing le ss shameful than carelessly neglecting your suffering, 
protectorless, kind mothers {169} just because you do not recognize them anymore 
due to birth and death-transference, and being preoccupied with freeing yourself 
from cyclic existence. In the Letter to a Student it says: 
 
If I neglect my relatives who have become a part of the ocean of cyclic 
existence and appear to have fallen into an abyss, whom I do not 
recognize due to birth and death-tran sference, and only liberate myself, 
nothing would be more shameless than that. 
 
We do not presently recognize that sentient beings have been our mothers because of the 
continuous transformation caused by death and rebirth. However, since they have been our 
kind mothers and are now suffering and without a protector, it would no t be right for us to 
neglect them through thinking that  they do not matter and that wh at is important is for us to 
become free from cyclic existence. We should not think only of achieving our own liberation 
and neglect other sentient beings. Likewise, Letter to a Student says that our relatives and 
family members are moving about in the ocean of  cyclic existence, taking rebirth and dying, 
due to which we do not recognize them as havi ng been our mother, father, sister, brother, 
and so forth. However, since they have all been our relatives, we need to take care of them. If we do not do this, but instead only wish to q uickly achieve nirvana for ourselves, this would 
be shameful. We do not recognize that sentie nt beings have been our mother due to the 
strong sufferings of death and rebirth, wh ich overcame our mind and made us forget 
everything. This is the cause that made us forget  this, but in fact they have been our mother 
and have been kind to us, therefore we need to repay their kindness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B  To neglect those who have been kind does not accord with the well-mannered  
Thus, through thinking “If neglecting such kind beings is unacceptable even to the 
ill-mannered, how can I reconcile it with  my own ways?” you should take the 
responsibility to reciprocate their kindness. 
 
To neglect those who have been our kind friends is not acceptable even to people who are ill-
mannered. For example, if someone were to take care of us in this life and then we were to abandon him, this would not be considered good  behavior. Instead it would be considered to 
be ill-mannered, bad, and not right. Since this kind of behavior is unacceptable to everyone, we need to try again and again to think of their kindness. We need to recognize their 
kindness, whereby, through understanding this, the wish to repay their kindness will arise 
naturally. Just as if someone were to do someth ing good for us, it would not be correct to no 
longer want to take care of him or her, likewise since all sentient beings have been kind to us, we need to develop respect for them and have good behavior toward them. We should not act 
disrespectfully toward them, but try at all times to  respect them. If we develop this attitude in 
our mind, it will indicate that our good qu alities are developing. On the other hand, if 
outwardly we act nice toward someone but internal ly we wish to harm him, this would not be 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
514 right. Since our mind is negative, it will harm  us. Although it may not harm others, it will 
definitely harm us! While if our negative mind  leads to actions of body and speech we can 
make others unhappy and harm them, just th inking in a harmful way harms us alone and 
does not harm others.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C As it would be inappropriate to repay the benefit with something contaminated, one should liberate 
them from ignorance 
If someone asks: “Well then, how should  I repay the benefit?” Whatever mere 
happiness and wealth of cyclic existence yo ur mothers obtain, they are deceived by 
all of them. Thus, we should repay the be nefit thinking “I, in the past, by the 
power of conceit of the evil spirits of th e mental afflictions, once again produced 
various suffering in those who were alread y suffering by nature, like putting salt 
and so forth on the injured who were already intensely wounded. I will set all 
those who have benefited me out of love in the happiness of liberation, nirvana.” 
 If someone has benefited us and we thin k to repay this kindness with something 
contaminated, this would not be correct. We need to repay thei r kindness by liberating them 
from ignorance. This is the best thing that we  can do for them. Theref ore, we should think 
about how to benefit and repay the kindness of our mothers who have been so kind to us. 
Our mother sentient beings have experienced a ll the happiness, wealth, and good things of 
cyclic existence in the past, but these things ha ve only deceived them. In the past, due to our 
mind being contaminated by the afflictions, we  did many things to injure those who were 
already suffering by nature in many different ways, like putting salt on a wound, thereby increasing their suffering. It is like if someone were injured and we put salt or chili on their 
wounds, whereby instead of helping them we on ly increased their suffering, this would be 
wrong. Similarly, giving whiskey, beer, and so forth to our mother sentient beings would only 
increase their suffering. We need to make sure  that we make our mother sentient beings 
happy and reduce their suffering. In this way, we  can repay their kindness. Therefore, just as 
our mother sentient beings out of love for us benefited us in the past, so too do we need now need to strive to liberate them from all the su fferings of cyclic existe nce and set them in the 
happiness of the state that has gone beyond cyclic  existence. In other words, we need to help 
our mother sentient beings attain freedom fr om cyclic existence and achieve ever-lasting 
happiness. This is how we can be nefit them. Thus, we need to benefit them, not by repaying 
their kindness with material things, but by  helping them to de velop their own inner 
realizations.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D  How to meditate [on the essential points ] separately 
In brief, if even your own kind mother  who, restless and intoxicated, blind and 
without anyone to guide her, is stumbl ing with each step and approaching a 
fearsome abyss, cannot put her hope in her child, who can she count on? If it is 
not for a child to free its mother from fear, who else should do it? Likewise, the sentient beings who have been our mother s, their minds in a state of confusion 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 27-29 April 2005 (11) 
515 due to the evil spirits of the mental a fflictions, intoxicated and without control 
over their minds, lacking eyes that s ee the path to high status and certain 
goodness, without a blind person’s guide of perfect knowledge, in each instant 
{170} stumble due to being distracted by faulty behavior, and wander toward the 
abyss of cyclic existence in general and the bad migrations in  particular. Seeing 
them, consider how these mothers necessari ly put their hopes in their child, and 
that it is also for the child to get his mothers out of there, you will repay their 
kindness by definitely getting them out of cyclic ex istence. In the Compendium of 
Instructions  (Shiksasammucchaya)  it says: 
 
Intoxicated by mental afflictions, blinded by ignorance, 
Stumbling into many abysses with each step along the path, Myself and others are always the basis of sorrow. 
All migrators are the same in suffering. 
 
In view of this, although it is indeed said that it is inappropriate to look for faults 
in others and when seeing the slightest good  quality to hold it in amazement, here 
it is reasonable to relate these statements to misery. 
 If our mother, who is confused, crazy, blind, an d without a guide, is walking toward an abyss 
and is in danger of falling into it, she would ho pe that her child would be  the one to help her. 
She would think “Only my child can help me. May he help me.” Also from the side of the c h i l d ,  i t  i s  h i s  r e s p o n s i b i l i t y  t o  f r e e  h i s  m o t h e r  f r o m  f e a r .  H e  s h o u l d  t h i n k ,  “ T h i s  i s  m y  responsibility. I must do it.” He should then lead his mother to a place free from the danger 
of falling into the abyss. Th e child must take this resp onsibility upon himself.  
 Like this, all mother sentient beings’ mind s are tormented by the mental afflictions, 
disturbed by spirits, and unable to make decisi ons. They are like craz y people. They are also 
without the eyes to achieve a high rebirth su ch as a human being, and they are without a 
guide. Due to this, they engage in faulty conduct and bad activities, such as creating 
separations, beating others, and so forth. As a result, they are in danger of falling into the 
abyss of the bad migrations. Seeing that our moth er sentient beings are in this situation, we 
must take care of and help them. We should do this as if we were their own child. We should take the responsibility upon ourselves to free ou r mother sentient beings from cyclic existence 
and set them in liberation. In this way, we sh ould make a strong determination to lead all 
sentient beings out of cyclic ex istence. In short, thinking that  our mother sentient beings are 
like crazy people and in the danger of falling in to the bad migrations, we need to extract them 
from cyclic existence and set them  in the state of liberation whic h is beyond cyclic existence.  
 END

516 Monday afternoon, 2 May 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 170) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A2 Actually generation of t he mind striving for the welfare of others 
A Meditating on love 
B Meditating on compassion  
C Meditating on the extraordinary attitude 
The second has three [points]: meditating on love, meditating on compassion, and 
meditating on the extraordinary attitude. 
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A2A Meditating on love 
1 The observed object  
First: The observed object of love is se ntient beings who do not have happiness. 
 
The observed object of love is sentient beings who do not have happiness. Love is the mind 
wishing all sentient beings to have happiness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The aspect [of love] 
Its aspect is the thought: “How wonderful it would be if they were to meet with 
happiness. May they meet with happiness! ” And: “I will make them meet with 
happiness!” 
 
The aspect of love is the thought “How wonderful it would be if sentient beings were to have happiness. May they have happiness. I will make them have happiness.” In general love is the 
wish for sentient beings to have happiness and th e causes of happiness. Here it is the specific 
thoughts “How wonderful it would be if th ey were to have happiness. May they have 
happiness.” This is followed by the determin ation “I will make them have happiness.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The merit is even greater than constantly making offerings to the buddhas 
As regards the benefit, it says in the King of Meditative Stabilizations Sutra :  
 
If one were to constantly offer immeasurable offerings  
Of great variety to the supreme beings in myriads of pure fields,  
This would not compare to a fraction  Of the amount [of merit] of the mind of love. 
 
This is to say that the merit is much greater than that of constantly making 
offerings to the field that is the fi nal object with very vast things. 
 
If we generate love there are many benefits or advantages. For example, although if we were to make offerings to all the buddhas of the three times with various kinds of offerings we would 
accumulate much merit and it would be of great be nefit, if we develop love for sentient beings 
this is of much more benefit and accumulate s much more merit. In other words, through 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
517 offering material things at all times to all th e buddhas, we would create a lot of merit but it 
could not be compared to the merit created by de veloping love for sentient beings. This is the 
benefit of generating love for all sentient beings; we should think about it.  
 However, while it is difficult to make offering s at all times to all the buddhas, we can try to 
develop a mind of love toward all the suffering sentient beings by trying to make those who 
lack happiness meet with happiness. This mind of love is of much more benefit and creates 
much more merit than making offerings like th at, but at the same time not much effort is 
needed to develop it. For example, we can meditate  on love for ten minutes a day, or even just 
five minutes. Of course, we can do even mo re – twenty minutes, thirty minutes, forty 
minutes, or one hour.   In order for sentient beings to have happine ss certain causes and conditions are necessary. 
Without them, happiness will not arise. This is because in order for any functioning thing to 
come into existence, particular causes and cond itions are necessary. This is a law of nature. 
Therefore, we should try to develo p love for all sentient beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 4 If one cultivates love in this field, [the merit ] will exceed even the former 
Also, in Arraying the Buddha Field of Manjushri  it is explained:  
 
In the world sphere called “Thousan d Ornaments” of the great king 
Buddheshvara, at the northeastern bo rder, {171} sentient beings possess 
a happiness similar to the happiness of monks who have entered 
cessation. If a mind of love towards all sentient beings that is generated in this field for as little as the du ration of a finger snap produces far 
more merit than practicing pure co nduct there for a hundred thousand 
millions of years, what need is th ere to mention that of abiding in [that 
mind] day and night?  
 The sentient beings in the field called Thousand Ornaments experience the happiness of fully-ordained-monk foe-destroyers who are abso rbed in self-peace. Thus, they have great 
happiness. However, the pure cond uct of these sentient beings practiced over millions of years 
is exceeded by the merit created in this realm by the love genera ted toward sentient beings for 
the duration of a finger snap. This being so, wh at need is there to mention how much merit is 
created by someone who abides day and night in the meditation on love, wishing all sentient 
beings to have happiness?   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 Achievement of the eight benefits such as being loved by gods and human beings  
In [Nagarjuna’s] Precious Garland (Ratnavali ) we also find: 
 
With respect to merit, just one short moment of love  
Exceeds several times over,  
That produced by giving, three times a day,  Three hundred little bowls of delicious meals. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
518  
You are loved by gods and men  And protected by them. 
You have mental happiness and much happiness, 
Become invulnerable to poison and weapons, 
 Achieve your aims without any effort 
And will be reborn in the world of Brahma. 
Even if you do not attain liberation, You will attain the eight qualities of love. 
 If you have love, you are loved by gods and men and they naturally gather around you. Since even the conquerors defeat the hosts of maras by the force of love, it 
becomes the best of protections and so forth.  
 Eight benefits are obtained by meditating on love. By offering delicious foods three times a 
day, we would accumulate the merit of practici ng generosity. However, the merit created by 
generating love even just for a short moment toward sentient beings is much greater. In addition, due to this, we will be loved by, ta ken care of, and protected by human beings and 
g o d s .  W e  w i l l  h a v e  m e n t a l  a s  w e l l  a s  p h y s i c a l  h a p p i n e s s .  W e  w i l l  n o t  b e  h a r m e d  e i t h e r  b y  poisons or weapons. We will effortlessly achiev e whatever aims we wish to achieve. In our 
next life we will be reborn in the world of Brah ma, that is, in the first concentration. Even if 
we do not attain liberation in this live, we  will attain the eight qualities of love.  
 Thus, if we have love, both gods and human beings will naturally gather around us and remain close to us. Also all the conquerors dest roy the hosts of maras by the power of love, it 
is the best of protections because, due to it, we cannot be harmed by demons or maras. Here 
“mara” refers to the outer maras or demons, not to the inner demons of the mental afflictions 
and so forth.  
 We need to recognize that the mara of the i nner mental afflictions also harms us, since we 
tend to think that the mental afflictions are ve ry nice! However, by th eir very nature they 
harm us. Another inner mara or demon that harms us is our body, this contaminated 
appropriated aggregate, which is called a demon because, based on it, we experience the many 
sufferings of illness, insect bites, wind, fire, and so forth. In addition, there is the inner mara 
of death. However, in this context “mara” refers to external beings who want to harm us. By meditating on love, these beings will not be able  t o  h a rm  us an d we will be able t o  def eat 
them. Since meditation on love is the best of pr otections, there is no need to ask a lama to 
protect us from our problems!   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
6 The stages of meditation on love 
The stages of meditation on love ar e to respectively meditate initially [on love] for 
your friends and relatives, then for neut ral persons, then for your enemies, and 
then for all sentient beings. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
519 How should we meditate on love? First we sh ould meditate on love for our friends and 
relatives. Then we should meditate on love to ward people who are strangers. Then we should 
meditate on it toward the people we consider to  be our enemies. Having done this, we should 
then gradually extend our meditation on love to all sentient beings.  
 We can do this by imagining three people in front of us, a close friend, an unknown 
stranger, and a person who we consider to be  our enemy. Then we generate the thought 
toward our friend “How wonderful it would be if he were to have happiness. May he have 
happiness. I will make him have happiness.” Th en we do the same toward the stranger, 
thinking once again, “How wonderful it would be  if he were to have happiness. May he have 
happiness. I will make him have happiness.” Then we do the same toward our enemy, 
thinking, “How wonderful it would be if he we re to have happiness. May he have happiness. I 
will make him have happiness.” We should medi tate in this way and then gradually extend 
this attitude to the people around us, to the people in the same area, to the people in our 
country, to all the people in the world, to anim als, and then to all other sentient beings. We 
should try to meditate gradually in this way.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
7 The way in which to meditate on [love] 
The way in which to meditate [on love is as follows ]. Just as compassion arises 
when you reflect again and again on how sentient beings have the suffering of 
suffering, likewise you should reflect again and again on how sentient beings lack 
contaminated and uncontaminated happine ss and are devoid of happiness. Once 
you have familiarized yourself with this, the wish for them to meet with happiness 
will arise naturally. In addition, through directing your mind to the various kinds 
of happiness, you will then {172} pr ovide sentient beings with them. 
 
How should we meditate on love? By thinking again and again that all sentient beings are 
tormented by suffering, compassion arises. Likewise, since sentient beings lack even contaminated happiness, there is no need to  mention that they lack uncontaminated 
happiness. In fact, they are devoid of happiness.  By thinking about this again and again, our 
mind will become familiar with this attitude and we will come to generate the wish for all 
sentient beings to have happiness. We shou ld then think about the various kinds of 
happinesses and mentally give such happiness to  sentient beings. For example, to those 
people who become happy when they are given material things such as food, drink, clothing, and so forth, we should mentally give them  such contaminated happinesses and also wish 
them uncontaminated happiness. although at the moment we can only do this in our 
imagination, we should still do this meditation as  much as we can. If we develop this attitude 
very strongly, there will definitely come a result.   (Oral transmission 19:10-19:30, pp. 172-186)  Tuesday morning, 3 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 172) says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
520 2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A2B Meditating on compassion 
1 The observed object [of compassion ] 
Second: The observed object of compassion  is sentient beings who are suffering 
due to any of the three kinds of suffering. 
 
Next the topic of how to meditate on compassion is discussed. The observed object of 
compassion is the sentient beings who are suffer ing due to any of the three kinds of suffering: 
the suffering of suffering, suffering of change, and pervasive suffering. In other words, it is 
they who are the object of our compassion – th e wish that all sentient beings be free from 
suffering.   The suffering of suffering is illustrated by th is contaminated body, which is the very nature 
of suffering. This suffering includes kidney pa in, headache, back pain, stomach ache, and so 
forth. In fact, all kinds of pain, both mental and physical, are the suffering of suffering. In this 
case, on top of that which is already the nature of suffering, our body, there is the addition of 
manifest suffering or pain. The suffering of chan ge, on the other hand, refers to the pleasures 
or happiness of cyclic existence which, when experienced for an extended period of time, 
transform into suffering, either mental or phys ical. An example of a physical suffering of 
change is that when we lie in the sun for too long, we come to experience the suffering of 
heat. Then, in order to relieve it, we go to lie in the shade but, after some time, we begin to 
feel cold and once again want to return to the warmth of the sun. Anot her example is that by 
continuing to indulge in the pleasure of eating  we eventually come to experience a stomach 
ache.   Pervasive suffering refers to the fact that from  the top of our head to the soles of our feet 
our entire body is pervaded by suffering. Alternativ ely, it refers to the fa ct that all of cyclic 
existence from the hell of Unrelenting Torment up to the Peak of Existence is pervaded by 
suffering. Another meaning is that all beings, from the lowest beggar on the streets to the 
president of a nation, are pervaded by suffering. Thus, cyclic existence is  the very nature of 
suffering.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The aspect [of compassion ] 
The aspect is the thought: “How wonderful it would be if they were free from these 
sufferings,” “May they be free from them,”  and “I will free them from them.” 
 
The aspect of compassion is th e thought “How wonderful it would be if all sentient beings 
were free from suffering,” “May they be free from  suffering,” and “I myself will free them from 
suffering.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The sequence of the meditation [on compassion ] 
The sequence of the meditation is to meditate [on compassion ] first for your 
friends and relatives, then for neutral pers ons, then for your enemies, and then for 
all the sentient beings of the ten directions. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
521 In order to develop compassion, we should first meditate on it toward a dear friend. This is 
because when a dear friend is suffering, we naturally wish him to be free from suffering and 
are unable to bear his suffering. Also in this case, we should visualize a friend, a stranger or 
neutral person, and an enemy in front of us, an d then strive to develop compassion first for 
the friend, wishing him to be free from sufferi ng. When we have developed compassion for 
him, we next do the same toward the stranger , wishing him to be free from suffering. When 
this too has been developed, we do the same to ward our enemy, wishing him to also be free 
from suffering. Then we extend this thought to  the sentient beings of the ten directions 
through wishing all the sentient beings of the ten directions to be free from suffering. We 
should think in this way and meditate on co mpassion again and again, whereby we will be 
able to develop great compassion toward all sentient beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 The main point of meditating in this sequence  
This gradual meditation on equanimity, love, and compassion through dividing 
the objects into specific categories was developed by Acharya Kamalashila through 
following the Abhidharma Sutra . This is a very important point because, if from the 
start, you train in observing [sentient beings ] in general without dividing them 
into specific categories, it will a ppear as though you have generated [these 
attitudes], but when you then think about them in dividually it will appear that it 
has not been generated for any of them. On the other hand, if you experience a 
change in your mind toward the individual [persons] as explained above, and then 
extend it to many, when at the end yo u observe them in general and sustain [that 
attitude] it will be generated purely regardless of whom is observed, whether a 
group or an individual.  
 
This order of meditating is set ou t in by Kamalashila following the Abhidharma Sutra . First we 
should develop equanimity, then, having de veloped equanimity, love; and then, having 
developed love, compassion. Love is the mind  wishing someone to have happiness, whereas 
compassion is the mind wishing someone to be  free from suffering. In order for someone to 
have happiness he or she needs to be free from suffering. However, if rather than dividing 
people into friends, stranger, and enemies, we try to develop these attitudes toward all 
sentient beings in general, although it will ap pear that compassion ha ve been generated, in 
actuality it will not have been generated. In ot her words, having developed compassion in this 
way, if we then take an individual sentient be ing as our object, we will see that we have not 
actually developed compassion. Therefore, righ t from the very beginning we need to take 
these three people as our objects and develop compassion for each of them individually. 
Having done so, we then extend it to more and more sentient beings. After that, if we once 
take an individual sentient being as our ob ject, we will find that we have developed 
compassion perfectly. Therefore,  we should meditate on compassion in this way, first with 
respect to individuals, until it is developed in our mind and our mind has changed; then 
toward all sentient beings; and then once again to ward individuals. In this way we will really 
develop great compassion.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
522 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 The way to meditate 
The way to meditate is in terms of the sufferings that were already explained when 
considering how the sentient beings who have been our mothers have fallen into 
existence and are experiencing the general and specific sufferings. 
 
The way in which to meditate is to think abou t the fact that the sentient beings who have 
been our mothers have fallen into cyclic ex istence and are experiencing the general and 
specific sufferings. These sufferings were alread y explained in the context of the four noble 
truths in relation to the path of middling bein gs, and in the context of the bad migrations in 
relation to the path of small be ings. Thus, here we should think about the fact that sentient 
beings have been our mothers, that they have fa llen into the ocean of cyclic existence, that 
they are experiencing the general sufferings, wh ich are the three types of sufferings, and that 
they are experiencing the specific sufferings of their respective realms, for example, those of 
human beings. In this way we should think about the sufferings of sentient beings and generate the wish to free them from their sufferi ng. If we understand how they are suffering, a 
strong wish to free them from suffering will ar ise. Contrarily, if we do not understand how 
they are suffering, our wish to free them  from suffering will not be strong.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
6 The measure of having generated compassion 
The measure of having generated compassion is taught in the First Stages of 
Meditation (Bhavanakrama ) [by Kamalashila]:  
 
At the point where the compassion th at has the aspect of the constant 
d e s i r e  t o  r e m o v e  t h e  s u f f e r i n g  o f  a l l  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  –  a s  i f  y o u r  o w n  
beloved child was unhappy – operates naturally in you and operates in accordance with your own nature, it is complete. Thereby, it obtains the 
name “great compassion.” 
 {173} [Thus,] if compassion towards all sentie nt beings naturally arises to the extent 
of the compassion generate d by a mother when the ch ild she loves with all her 
heart suffers, it is said to be a fully-qual ified great compassion. Hence, the same is 
true for the measure of the generation of “great love.” 
 
The First Stages of Meditation  by Kamalashila sets out the measure of having developed 
compassion. If our only beloved child was un happy and suffering, we would effortlessly and 
spontaneously wish to free him from his suffering. Similarly, by seeing all sentient beings in a 
similar way, we will also come to spontaneously and effortlessly generate compassion for them. In other words, we will come to no longer  need to think about their suffering in that 
just by seeing sentient beings the compassion that wishes them to be free from suffering will 
automatically arise. At this time, we will have generated great compassion in our mind, that is, 
the compassion in our continuum will th en be called “great compassion.”  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
523  When a mother loves her small child very much , she thinks of him as being very beautiful 
and when he suffers she generates strong co mpassion for him. Having this kind of 
compassion for all sentient beings, naturally and spontaneously, is the measure of having 
developed great compassion, that is, it is a fu lly qualified great compassion. The measure of 
great love is similar, that is, we have great love when upon seeing sentient beings the love that 
wishes them to have happiness and the causes of happiness spontaneously and effortlessly 
arises. It is at this point that we ha ve generated great love in our mind.  
 We need to train in love and compassion. Alth ough all sentient beings naturally have some 
love and some compassion, we need to strive to  increase and strengthen it. Even animals have 
a bit of love and compassion, this being evid enced by the way in which cats and dogs, for 
example, take care of their babies. We see that  when their babies are sick, the mother cat or 
dog becomes unhappy, and when its babies are happy  so to is the mother cat or dog. We need 
to develop love and compassion for all sentient  beings, whereby we will establish the basis for 
developing the extraordinary attitude, which th en establishes the basis for generating the 
mind of enlightenment.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 173) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2A2C Meditating on the extraordinary attitude 
1 The actual way to meditate [ on the extraordinary attitude ] 
Third: At the end of having meditated on love and compassion in this manner, 
you will think: “Alas! If these sentient be ings who are attractive and pleasant to me 
are thus deprived of happiness and torm ented by suffering, I must make them 
meet with happiness and I must free th em from suffering?” In taking upon 
yourself, at least in mere words, the responsibility to liberate them, your mind is 
trained. 
 
Having developed love and compassion in our mind, we then need to develop the 
extraordinary attitude. To do this, first we need to see all sentient beings as being attractive 
and pleasant to us. Then we need to think ab out the fact that they lack happiness and are 
tormented by suffering. Because of this, it is  not enough to think, “May they meet with 
happiness. May they be free from suffering.” In addition, we need to take upon ourselves the 
responsibility to make them happy and to free th em from suffering. We need to verbally make 
this determination and to mentally train in ta king this responsibility upon ourselves.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Clearing up doubt 
Although this point has already been made  briefly in the context of repaying the 
kindness, it is shown here that the love and compassion that think “How 
wonderful it would be if they were to m eet with happiness” and “How wonderful it 
would be if they were to be free from suffering” are not sufficient. This is because 
it is shown that it is necessary to gene rate the love and compassion are able to 
induce the thought: “I myself will accomp lish the happiness and benefit of sentient 
beings.” This is not to be done just during meditation sessions, but is to be 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
524 sustained continuously through remembering it during all behaviors after the 
sessions and so forth, as is taught in the Middle Stages of Meditation  (Bhavanakrama ). 
 
Having recognized that sentient beings have been our mother and having remembered their 
kindness, on the occasion of wishing to repay their kindness, the thought also arises wishing 
sentient beings to be happy and to be free from suffering. Through seeing that all sentient 
beings are lacking happiness and experiencing suffering, love and compassion spontaneously 
arise, whereby we come to generate great lo ve and great compassion. However, it is not 
enough to merely wish them happiness and freed om from suffering. In addition, we need to 
take the responsibility upon ourselves to set them in happiness and free them from suffering. 
We must think “I will take this responsibility up on myself!” However, it is also not enough to 
generate this thought one time during a medita tion session, we also need to develop this 
attitude between sessions, when we are walking, eating, talking, and so forth, by constantly 
thinking “I will take the responsibility upon my self to set all sentient beings in happiness and 
free them from suffering. This is my job!” We should not just think this in meditation and 
then not do anything more between meditation sessions. Instead we need to familiarize with 
this thought continually, whereby we will actually generate this attitude. On the other hand, if 
we meditate for a few days and then get tired, or our mind gets distur bed and we do not want 
to meditate anymore, we will not be able to de velop this attitude. Rather, we need to try all 
the time, all day long, to think “Since sentient beings need to have happiness and need to be 
free from suffering, I myself will take th e responsibility to accomplish this.” 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2B Training in the mind striving for enlightenment 
Second: When, inspired by the steps explained so far, you see that it is necessary to 
become a buddha for the welfare of others, yo u will also develop the wish to attain 
it. Nonetheless, since that in itself is not sufficient, at the beginning you should 
increase your faith through thinking abou t the good qualities of the exalted body, 
exalted speech, exalted mind, and enlighte ned activities as they were explained 
before in the context of going for refuge. Th en, since that faith is said to act as the 
basis for aspiration, you will generate the wish to attain thos e qualities from the 
bottom of your heart. That will induce cert ainty that the achievement of an exalted 
knower of all aspects is indispensable also for your own welfare. 
 
How should we train in the mind striving for en lightenment? In order to be able to enact the 
welfare of other sentient beings, we need to  achieve enlightenment or buddhahood. Seeing 
this, we will generate the desire to attain buddhahood. However, this alone is not enough. 
What else needs to be done? We need to increase  our faith in the good qualities of the objects 
of refuge, which were explained before. For exam ple, by contemplating the good qualities of 
the Buddha Jewel in terms of the qualities of his exalted body, exalted speech, exalted mind, 
and enlightened activities, we will generate strong  faith in them. This fa ith will then become 
the basis for us to generate the aspiration  to attain those qualities ourselves.  
 Faith is said to be the basis or the cause of a ll good qualities. Just as a mother gives birth to 
a child, nourishes it, teaches her knowledge to it , and so forth, similarly faith gives birth to 
good qualities. Contrarily, just as it is 100% ce rtain that if there is no mother there is no 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
525 child, similarly if there is no faith there will no t be any good qualities. Thus, faith in the good 
qualities of the buddha gives rise to good qual ities in us. The qualities of the Buddha include 
the 112 qualities of the exalted body, the 64 qualities of the exalted speech, the 21 groups of 
uncontaminated wisdom of the exalted mind, an d the 27 types of enlightened activities. We 
should contemplate all of them, whereby we will develop faith in the Buddha.  
 The Buddha teaches us the Dharma. By studyi ng the scriptures we can develop our own 
inner qualities and generate the Dharma of true  paths, which lead to the cessation of all 
sufferings and obscurations. The Dharma is the actual refuge. The Sang ha are our assistants 
on the path. We should think “I  too want to develop the qualit ies of the exalted body, speech, 
and mind, and the enlightened activities of the buddhas!” Even for our own welfare, we need 
to develop omniscient mind (a knower of all as pects). In other words, with this, not only will 
we be able to achieve others’ welfare, we will also be able to accompli sh our own welfare. The 
Buddha’s omniscient mind, the wisdom truth body, is called “the body that fulfills one’s own 
welfare,” whereas the enjoyment body and emanatio n body are called “the bodies that fulfill 
others’ welfare.” Because other sentient bein gs cannot see omniscient mind, the external 
bodies of the enjoyment body and emanation bo dy are necessary, since they can be seen by 
and teach sentient beings. Therefore, a budd ha has a mental body, the truth body, and 
physical bodies, the enjoyment body and emanat ion body. By achieving these three bodies, we 
can fulfill both our own and others’ welfare.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A1B-2C The result of the training: identifying the mind generation 
1 The general definition 
Third: The general definiti on is as taught in the Ornament for Clear Realizations  
(Abhisamayalamkara ): “The mind generation is th e desire for perfectly complete 
enlightenment for the welfare of others.”  
 
The mind generation or the mind of enlightenment depends on the presence of two 
aspirations. The causal aspiration  is the wish to benefit all sentient beings, which is similar to 
great compassion. The accompanying aspiration  is the wish to attain  enlightenment for the 
benefit of all sentient beings. The primary mind that is concomitant with these two 
aspirations is the mind of enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Divisions 
Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior , following the Gandavyuha [Sutra] , teaches the two – 
the aspiring and engaging [mind generations ] saying:  
 
In the same way that the peculiarities of  
The wish to go and going are to be understood, 
The wise should respectively understand The peculiarities of these two. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
526 If there appear to be a lot of inconsiste ncies in this respect, there is also the 
statement in the First Stages of Meditation  that the mind that thinks: “May I become 
a buddha for the welfare of sentient beings ” is the aspiring mind and that, having 
taken the vow, that mind is the engaging mind generation. 
 
The mind of enlightenment is divided into two fr om the point of view of nature: the aspiring 
mind of enlightenment and the engaging mind  of enlightenment. The aspiring mind of 
enlightenment is likened to the wish to go to  Germany without actually putting the thought 
into action, whereas the engaging mind of enli ghtenment is likened to actually engaging in 
traveling to Germany by car, plane, and so fo rth. Thus, the engaging mind of enlightenment 
is that which is conjoined with the practice of the six perfection s, whereas this in the case for 
the aspiring mind of enlightenment. Thus, the as piring mind of enlightenment is to generate 
the mind of enlightenment but to not engage in th e practice of the perfection of generosity by 
giving material things, protection, and the Dharma ; in the practice of morality; in the practice 
of patience; in the practice of  joyous effort; in the practice of developing single-pointed 
concentration; and in the practice of developi ng wisdom. On the other hand, when the mind 
of enlightenment is conjoined with these practice s, it is the engaging mind of enlightenment. 
Hence, in the case of the aspiring mind of  enlightenment it is not accompanied by the 
engagement in the six perfections.  
 In the Ornament for Clear Realizations  the mind of enlightenment is divided into twenty-two 
types. This text also explains the different ways in which these minds of enlightenment are 
generated. However, in this context, just tw o divisions of the mind of enlightenment – the 
aspiring mind of enlightenment and the enga ging mind of enlightenment – are set out. 
 Kamalashila’s Stages of Meditation  also says that the thought “May I attain buddhahood for 
the welfare of sentient beings” is the aspiring mind of enlightenment, whereas, having taken 
the vow, it becomes the engaging mind of enligh tenment. In this case the vow is the promise 
to take care of sentient beings. In other wo rds, one takes the bodhisattva vows, whereupon 
one makes a promise to take care of each an d every sentient being without any exception 
whatsoever. Having taken the bodhisattva vows and generated the mind of enlightenment, 
the mind of enlightenment becomes an engaging mind of enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 174) says:  
Explanation of how to tr ain the mind through  
equalizing and exchanging oneself and others 
2B4B-2B3B-2A2 The training based on the texts by Shantideva 
A Thinking about the advantages and disadvantages of exchanging and not exchanging 
oneself with others 
B Demonstrating that one can generate this mind if one familiarizes with it 
C The stages of meditating on how to exchange oneself and others 
The second – the training based on the te xts by Shantideva – has three [points]: 
Thinking about the advantages and di sadvantages of exchanging and not 
exchanging oneself with others; demonstrat ing that one can generate this mind if 
one familiarizes with it; and the stages of meditating on how to exchange oneself 
and others. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
527  
We also need to train the mind in accordance  with the explanations of Shantideva. To do 
this, we think about the advantages of exchanging ourselves with others, and the 
disadvantages of not exchanging ourselves with others. Through familiarizing with 
abandoning cherishing ourselves and engaging in cherishing others, we can generate this 
mind. This is achieved through the stages of meditation whereby we exchange ourselves with 
others, that is, we change our present attitude  through giving up cherishing ourselves and 
practicing cherishing others, that is, we give up taking care of ourselves alone and strive to 
take care of others. Thus, this is not a case of actually exchanging places with others.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A2A Thinking about the advantages of exchanging and the disadvantages of not 
exchanging oneself with others 
1  To quickly accomplish the two welfares it is appropriate to exchange oneself with others  
First: In Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior  it says: 
 
Whoever wishes to protect  
Himself and others quickly, Should practice the secret instruction: Exchanging oneself and others. 
 If we want to quickly achieve the two welfares , we need to exchange ourselves with others, 
that is, we need to change our mind. As Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  says, those who wish 
to protect others very quickly should abandon se lf-cherishing and cherish others. This practice 
should be done secretly, that is, it should not be revealed to others. In other words, without telling everyone that we are doing this practice, we should engage in it mentally while trying 
to abandon self-cherishing and develop the though t cherishing others in order to be able to 
quickly protect and benefit other sentient beings.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The advantages of exchanging and the disadvantages of not exchanging oneself with others  
All the happiness there is in the world 
Arises from wanting happiness for others. All the suffering there is in the world, 
Arises from wanting happiness for oneself. 
 
What need is there to say any more? 
The childish work for their own welfare,  
{175} The Munis for the welfare of others.  Look at the difference between the two! 
 If we exchange ourselves with others, what are th e advantages or benefits? If we do not cherish 
others, but continue to cherish ourselves and ne glect others, what are the disadvantages? All 
the happiness that exists in this world comes from the mind wishing that other sentient 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
528 beings be happy. In other words, all happiness ar ises from the cause of cherishing others. On 
the other hand, all the many types of sufferings that exist in the worl d come from the self-
cherishing mind that thinks only of our own happiness. Thus, there is no need to say 
anymore about this.  
 While the childish, that is, ordinary beings, only think of themselves, the Munis, the Able 
Ones, think only of others we lfare. We can see the great difference between these two. 
Although we wandered together with Buddha Sh akyamuni in our previous lives in cyclic 
existence, he generated the stro ng thought wishing to take care  of other sentient beings and 
relieve their suffering, whereby he achieved bu ddhahood and then gave us teachings. We, on 
the other hand, have continued to wander in cycl ic existence, unable to get out. Why is this? 
Because we have not thought about others but ha ve continued to think only of ourselves, “I 
need…,” “My daughter needs…,” “My son needs… ” We only think about ourselves, not about 
others. Therefore, we now need to work to de velop the thought that wa nts to take care of 
others and, at the same time, strive to dimi nish the thought that wa nts to take care of 
ourselves alone. We need to try to gradually exchange these two attitudes whereby we will 
come to be able to cherish others. When this mind is generated, we will have generated the 
mind of enlightenment.  
 How can we actually exchange ourselves with ot hers? It is usually said to do the taking-and-
giving meditation in combination with the brea th. To do this, motivated by compassion we 
imagine taking the sufferings, obscurations, proble ms, and so forth of all sentient beings upon 
ourselves. We visualize all sentient beings in front of us and that from their right nostrils 
comes black smoke that enters our left nostril. It  descends to our heart, where there is a heap 
of black dust in the nature of the self-cherish ing mind. When the sufferings in the aspect of 
black smoke touch the black heap, it is completely  destroyed. It no longer exists. This is the 
way to train in cherishing others and eliminating self-cherishing.  
 Then, motivated by love, we imagine that whit e light in the nature of happiness exits from 
our right nostril and enters the le ft nostrils of the sentient bein gs in front of us. Their bodies 
are completely filled with white light and they  receive all necessities, they become happy and 
relaxed, their minds become intelligent and cl ear, they develop a clear knower, and their 
bodies are transformed into light that is as tr ansparent as crystal. We should try to do the 
taking-and-giving meditation in this w ay. This meditation, referred to in Engaging in the 
Bodhisattva Deeds  as the “secret, holy conduct,” is do ne in order to exchange ourselves and 
others, that is, to diminish the mind of self -cherishing and increase the mind cherishing 
others. We need to try to do this. If we do not try, nothing will happen. On the other hand, 
even if it is difficult, through tr ying to do this meditation even just a little bit every day we will 
gradually improve more and more.  
Tuesday afternoon, 3 May 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 175) says:  
3 Therefore buddhahood etc. are difficult to attain if one does not exchange oneself with others.  
If I do not exchange my happiness 
And the suffering of others correctly, Buddhahood will not be attained and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
529 There will be no happiness in samsara either. 
 
Just as it is expressed here, you should thin k of cherishing oneself as the door to all 
problems and cherishing others as the basis of all excellences. 
 
W e  n e e d  t o  e x c h a n g e  o u r  a t t i t u d e ,  a s  i f  w e  d o  n o t  d o  t h i s  i t  w i l l  b e  d i f f i c u l t  t o  a t t a i n  buddhahood. We should give our happiness to others and take others’ suffering upon 
ourselves. If we do not do this, it will not be  easy to attain buddhah ood and there will be no 
happiness in cyclic existence. In this sense the self-cherishing mind is the door to all 
shortcomings, problems, and sufferings. On the ot her hand, cherishing others is the source of 
all excellences or good qualities. Therefor e, we need to try to change our mind.  
 Until now we have taken care only of ourselves and have not tried to take care of others. 
Because of this, we are still caught in cyclic existence, have not been happy, and have only 
experienced suffering. Therefore, we now need to  try to exchange our attitude by abandoning 
the self-cherishing mind and developing the mind  that cherishes and takes care of others. We 
need to meditate on this in order to gain familiarity with it. Without trying, nothing will 
happen!   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A2B Demonstrating that one can generate this mind if one familiarizes with it 
1 The actual [topic] 
Second: In the same way that fear used to arise in us even upon hearing our 
enemy's name but later on, having come to an agreement, we became friends and 
generated great dislike if he was absent, li kewise, by subsequently familiarizing the 
mind, we can become familiar with seeing  oneself like someone else and others 
like oneself. In the same text it says: 
 
Do not turn away from something because it is difficult. 
Through the power of familiarizing like this, 
We are do not like when someone is not with us Who we used to fear when hearing his name. 
And:  
It is not all that difficult to posit My body as that of others. 
 When someone is our enemy, even  just by hearing his name we can experience fear. However, 
if later on we come to have a harmonious rela tionship with him, he can become such a close 
friend that we are unhappy when he is absent. In a similar way, if we  familiarize our mind 
with exchanging oneself with others, we can co me to think of others as we presently do 
ourselves, whereby we will give up cherishing ourselves and come to ch erish others. We need 
to try to familiarize with this attitude. Even th ough it is difficult to develop, we should not 
give up but strive continually to familiarize ours elves with it very strongly. Due to familiarity, 
even someone whose mere name made us afraid  can later on become a friend whose absence 
makes us unhappy. Our friends can become enem ies, and our enemies can become friends. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
530 Human beings change. Even nations change, sometimes they have a good relationship, 
sometimes a bad one. We ourselves also change , in the morning we can be happy and in the 
afternoon we can be unhappy. Our mind changes in  this way. Therefore,  since it can change, 
we can definitely bring about a change in it. Fo r this reason, everyday we should try to cherish 
others and give up the mind that cherishes ourselves.  
 We can even learn to give up our body. For ex ample, we will become ab le to even give away 
a kidney, an eyeball, and other inner organs wi thout worry. If someone asks us for a part of 
our body, we will be able to give it away. Perhaps we cannot give our heart away as then we will die, but we can give away other parts that do not endanger our life but are needed by other people. Even this becomes easy due to familiarity.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Clearing up doubt 
If you think: “As someone else’s body is not my own body, how could it be 
appropriate to generate the same attitude toward it?” Just as this body is 
accomplished from the semen and blood of  o u r  p a r e n t s  a n d ,  e v e n  t h o u g h  i t  i s  
accomplished from parts of others’ bodies, we hold it to be “I” by the power of previous familiarization; likewise, if we fa miliarize with cherishing others’ bodies as 
if they were our own we will generate [this mind ]. The same text says: 
 
Just as you a drop of semen and blood From someone else  Is held to be ‘”I,” Likewise, we should familiarize regarding others. 
 Through reflecting well on the advantages  and disadvantages along these lines, a 
strong {176} enthusiasm for this meditati on will develop from the depths of the 
heart. Having seen that, if you familiariz e yourself with it, you can generate this 
attitude, you should meditate on it. 
 Someone raises the doubt: “How can we come to think of someone else’s body as our own body?” However, our body, in fact, comes from  our mother’s blood and our father’s semen. 
They are the causes of our body. Therefore, ev en though our body actually comes from parts 
of others’ bodies, we have come to think of  it as “my body.” This has happened out of 
familiarity, whereby we have de veloped the conception of a self  thinking “I” and “ mine” in 
relation to what actually comes from parts of ot hers’ bodies. This shows that we can also come 
to think of others’ bodies as our own body if we familiarize ourselves with this thought.  
 In other words, our mind entered the mixture of blood and semen, which gradually developed into our body. Then, out of familiarit y, we came to think “my body,” “my flesh,” 
“my bones,” and so forth and to ch erish and take care of it. However, in reality, our flesh and 
so forth came from our mother’s blood and ou r bones and so forth came from our father’s 
semen. The mind entered the mixture of the blood  and flesh, and later we came to identify 
the resulting body as “I” and “mine.” Theref ore, through familiarity we have come to 
apprehend what was originally a drop of blood and semen as “I” and “mine.” If this can 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
531 happen, we can also come to think of others’ bodies as “my body.” In other words, just as we 
presently suffer when our body is harmed and re joice when our body experiences comfort, so 
too can we come to suffer when others’ bodies  are harmed and rejoice when their bodies are 
comfortable. At that time we will see our own and others’ bodies as similar and, just as we 
presently take care of our body, we will come to take care of others’ bodies. This is what it 
means to cherish others, including their bodies . It comes about only through familiarity.  
 When the semen and blood mix, six constituen ts – three from the father and three from 
the mother – combine together. The human body composed of these six constituents is the 
ideal basis for the practice of tantra and the achievement of realizations. Likewise, from the 
point of view of sutra this body, composed of the three constituents from the father and the 
three from the mother, is a special basis that ca n be used to develop all good qualities up to 
and including enlightenment. Thus, by means of it, we can achieve omniscience even in this 
life. For this reason, our human body is the best of bodies.   If we think about the many benefits and ad vantages of cherishing others and the many 
disadvantages of cherishing ourselves, and come  to understand them from the depths of our 
heart, we will see that we need to change our attitude. We will then develop enthusiasm 
toward this meditation. Through familiarity we can bring about whatever change we want to 
achieve in our mind and obtain whatever resu lt we want. Our mind can in fact change. 
Therefore, even though initially  meditation may be difficult, by  doing a bit every day it will 
slowly become easier and we will come to want  to meditate more and more. In this way our 
mind will be happy when we can meditate an d unhappy when we canno t meditate. Although 
many of us need to work and take care of a fa mily, we should constantly strive not only to 
care for the few people in our family but also to take care of all sentient beings. We should develop the wish for them to have happiness and the wish for them to be free from suffering, 
and to take upon ourselves the responsibility to ac hieve this. In short, we should strive to give 
up self-cherishing and develop the mind cherishing others.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A2C The stages of meditating on how to exchange oneself and others 
1 Removing obstacles 
2 The actual way to meditate 
The third has two [points]: Removing obstacles and the actual way to meditate. 
2B4B-2B3B-2A2C-1 Removing obstacles 
A Identifying the mind of exchanging oneself and others 
B Removing obstacles to it  
2B4B-2B3B-2A2C-1A Identifying the mind of exchanging oneself and others 
First: To say “exchanging oneself and othe rs,” “making oneself into the other and 
making others into oneself” does not mean  to train in the mind that thinks: “The 
other [person] is me” and “His eyes and so forth are my eyes and so forth.” Rather 
it is to exchange the rank of the two – (1) the mind that cherishes oneself and (2) 
the mind that neglects others, and to gene rate the mind that ch erishes others like 
we do ourselves and neglects ourselves like we do others. That is also why it was 
taught that one’s own happiness and othe rs’ suffering should be exchanged. 
Through looking at self-cherishing as our enemy, we will stop considering our own 
happiness to be a priority. Through looking at the qualities of cherishing others, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
532 we will stop neglecting the suffering of others, and it will become a priority to 
eliminate it. In short, we will act for the sake of removing the suffering of others 
without concern for our own happiness. 
 
When obstacles to exchanging ourselves with others arise, they must be removed. To do so, 
we first need to identify the mind of exchangi n g  s e l f  a n d  o t h e r s .  I t  i s  n o t  t ha t  w e  b e c o m e 
others and others become us, nor is it that others’ bodies become “m y body,” others’ head 
“my head,” others’ eyes “my eyes,” others’ hand s “my hands,” and so fort h. This mind is not 
what we need to train in. What we need to tr ain in is eliminating the mind that cherishes 
ourselves and developing the mind that cheris hes others. Thus, we have to exchange our 
attitude, not our body. Just as we presently ch erish ourselves, we should come to cherish 
others. Just as we presently neglect others, we should come to neglect ourselves. We need to 
develop the attitude that other sentient beings  are more important than us, and generate the 
wish to cherish and take care of them whil e thinking that we ourselves do not matter. 
Between self and others, others are more import ant because they are numberless while we are 
o n l y  o n e .  T h i n k i n g  i n  t h i s  w a y ,  w e  n e e d  t o  c h a n g e  o u r  a t t i t u d e  a n d  g i v e  u p  t h e  m i n d  cherishing ourselves and develop the mind cheris hing others. We need to give our happiness 
to others and take their suffering upon oursel ves. This is the meaning of “exchanging.”  
 By looking at our self-cherishing mind as our real enemy, we should stop the thought that 
our own happiness is most important and develop the thought that it is  more important for 
o t h e r  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  t o  b e  h a p p y .  P r e s e n t l y  when we look at other sentient beings, we 
disregard and do not think about their suffering . This is the attitude that we need to 
eliminate. What do we need to do? Instead of  looking at our own happiness, we should look 
at others who are suffering and develop the wish  to eliminate their suffering and then, based 
on this, do the taking-and-giving meditation. In this way we will be able to change our mind 
and we will become really happy. If we become able to take care of and help other sentient beings by removing their suffering, we too wi ll become happy and content. For example, a 
man does whatever he can in order to take care of his wife and child even though it may be 
difficult, such as working in order to earn mone y so as to provide them with all necessities. 
We should think of all sentient beings like our family and eliminate the attitude that neglects 
them and develop the mind that wishes to take c a r e  o f  t h e m .  I f  w e  c o n t i n u o u s l y  s t r i v e  t o  
develop this attitude, one day we will achieve it  such that it will then arise effortlessly and 
spontaneously. We should try to develop this attitude by not looking at our own short-term 
happiness but instead looking at and meditating on the suffering of others.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A2C-1B Removing obstacles impeding it  
When training in this mind, there are two obstacles. 
 
1 Removing the obstacle of considering oneself and others as distinct individuals 
2 [Removing the obstacle of thinking: “As the suffering of others does not harm me, I need not make an effort to remove it”] 
2B4B-2B3B-2A2C-1B1 Removing the obstacle of considering oneself and others as distinct individuals 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
533 A The actual [topic] 
By separating the two, oneself and othe rs – the basis of one’s own and others’ 
happiness and suffering – into distinct entiti es, like blue and yellow, they are held 
to be established [in this way ]. Then, with respect to the happiness and suffering 
based on them, you think: “Since it is  m i n e ,  i t  s h o u l d  b e  a c c o m p l i s h e d  o r  
removed. Since it is others,’ it is to be neglected.” Therefore, as an antidote to this 
[think]: “Myself and others are not separa te entities by nature. Looking at each 
other, the mind “other” is generated in me, whereas the mind “I” is generated in 
the other [person], ‘the mountain over there’ and ‘the mountain over here.’” For 
instance, although from this perspective wi th respect to the mountain over there, 
the mind “the mountain over there” is generated, when you go to the mountain 
over there, the mind “the mountain over he re” is generated. Therefore, this is not 
the same as the fact that by looking at the color blue {177} the mind of just blue is 
generated, whereas the mind of an other color is not generated.  
 
There are two obstacles to training in the mind of  exchanging self with others. One obstacle is 
to consider ourselves and others as separate or  distinct entities. This thought needs to be 
eliminated.  
 In our mind there can arise the thought that  the basis of our happiness and the basis of 
our suffering, that is, our body, is completely se parate from others, like blue and yellow. Due 
to this, we see our body and others’ bodies as established from their own side. Then when we 
experience happiness in relation to ourselves we want to increase it, and when we experience 
suffering in relation to ourselves we want to  eliminate it. On the other hand, we think of 
others as separate from ourselves and due to th is neglect them. In other words, because their 
bodies are separate from ours we think that it is  fine to neglect them. Th e antidote to this is 
to think that self and other do not exist in this way, that is, they are not separate by nature or 
inherently. Why? Because self and other exist in re lation to each other, that is, they exist in 
dependence on each other. Just as we think of ourselves as “I,” so too do others think of 
themselves as “I.” Therefore, “I” exists in dependence on “other.” For example, we call a 
mountain in the distance “the mountain over  there,” and call a mountain near us “the 
mountain over here,” but when we go to “t he mountain over there” it becomes “the 
mountain over here.” Likewise, a single person can be both father and son because in relation 
to his son he is “father” and in relation to his fa ther he is “son.” Simila rly, tall and short exist
