in relation to each other, for example, a single  person can be tall in relation to someone who 
is shorter and short in relation to someone wh o is taller. Likewise, fat and thin exist in 
relation to each other, whereby a single person  is fat in relation to someone who is thinner 
and is thin in relation to someone who is even fatter. All phenomena are interdependent. 
Thus, big and small, tall and short, long and shor t, and so forth exist in dependence on each 
other. We need to try to develop an understa nding of interdependent relationship – that 
everything depends on something else.   On the other hand, seeing the color blue does  not depend on seeing red, yellow, white, 
green, brown, and so forth. We do not think blue in relation to red, or blue in relation to white, or blue in relation to orange, or blue in  relation to yellow. We only see blue. Thus, this 
is not the same as big and small, tall and shor t, long and short, this mountain and that 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
534 mountain, which exist in dependence on each  other. Blue is just blue. For example, 
sometimes the sky is blue. 
 In short, we need to see that all sentient beings are kind, that we depend on them, that 
they bring us happiness, and that we re main alive in dependence on them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The scriptural source 
In this manner, the Compendium of All Trainings  (Shiksasammucchaya ) also teaches 
that [self and other ] are not established by their own nature, but are merely 
posited in dependence on the point of view: 
 
Through familiarizing with the equality of self and other, The mind of enlightenment becomes stable. ‘Self’ and ‘other’ are relative; 
As fictitious as ‘over there” and ‘over here.’  
 That mountain side is not ‘over there’ in itself; Depending on the perspective it becomes ‘over here.’ If ‘self’ is not established in itself, Depending on the perspective it becomes ‘other.’ 
 
The source of the teaching on exchangi ng self and others is Shantideva’s Compendium of 
Trainings .  T h i s  t e x t  s a y s  t h a t  i f  w e  f a m i l i a r i z e  w i th equalizing ourselves and other sentient 
beings, the mind of enlightenment will become ve ry stable. Self and other exist in dependence 
on each other. When we look at ourselves we think “I” and when we look at others we think 
“other.” However, when others look at themselv es they think “I” and wh en they look at us 
they think “other.” Thus, self and other depend on the point of  view. Similarly, the mountain 
over here and the mountain over there, one on the east and one to the west, exist in relation 
to each other, but if we were go to the mounta in over there, it would become “the mountain 
over here” and the other mountain would become  “the mountain over there.” We therefore 
develop different minds depending on the point of  view. In this same way, self and other can 
change. Everything depends on something el se. Thus, all functioning things are not 
established from their own side, or by their own nature, or by their own entity, or inherently, 
b u t  e x i s t  i n  d e p e n d e n c e  o n  o t h e r ,  s u c h  a s  causes and conditions. Likewise, the “being” 
depends on the five aggregates, that is, it is imputed on the five aggregates. The self depends 
on the body aggregate, feeling aggregate, di scrimination aggregate, compositional factors 
aggregate, and consciousness aggregate. It also  depends on causes and conditions. It also 
depends on parts. It also depends on being imputed by thought and name. In brief, all 
phenomena do not exist from their own side or inherently, but are merely imputed by 
thought, that is, they are merely imputed by  name. Therefore, all phenomena are empty of 
true or inherent existence, existence from their own side, and so forth. These types of 
existence are to be negated.  
 In order to eliminate the conception of a se lf, we need to rely on the wisdom realizing 
emptiness, its main antidote. On the other ha nd, in order to eliminate the self-cherishing 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
535 mind, we need to rely on the mind of enligh tenment, bodhichitta. If we develop these two 
antidotes, we will be able to eliminate all obstacles to the achievement of buddhahood. In 
other words, the obstacles to buddhahood are th e two: the conception of a self and self-
cherishing. However, the latter is the main obst acle. Hence, if we do not eliminate the self-
cherishing mind, we cannot achieve enlightenm ent. To achieve enlightenment we need to 
create its cause – the mind cherishing others. It is  important in daily life to try to do this. It 
does not matter whether or not we actually gain the realization of this, but it is important to 
try. If we try to do this again and again, one d ay we will achieve this realization. Since it does 
not come about immediately, we need to strive to develop it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A2C-1B2 [Removing the obstacle of thinking: “As the suffering of others does not harm me, I 
need not make an effort to remove it”] 
Removing the obstacle of th inking: “As the suffering of others does not harm me, 
I need not make an effort to remove it.” 
A The refutation in terms of the actions of a youth and an elderly person 
If this were the case, ther e would be no need to accumulate wealth when young 
out of concern for the sufferings of old age,  as the sufferings of the elderly person 
would not harm the youth. 
 
The second obstacle that needs to be eliminated is to think that we do not need to eliminate 
others’ suffering because it does not harm us . Although such a thought could arise in our 
mind, it is not a correct way of thinking. If this  were the case, there would be no need to work 
in our youth in order to accumulate money for the sake of eliminating the sufferings of old age, such as no longer being able to work, to  accumulate wealth, and so forth, because the 
suffering of old age does not harm the young person.   When we become old we experience the sufferings of it being difficult even to stand up without relying on someone else, of not being able  to move well, of not being able to see well, 
of not being able to digest food properly, and so forth. Also our physical energy diminishes as the energy of our bones, channels, winds, blood, and so forth degenerate. Seeing these 
sufferings, we work even when we are young to avoid them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The refutation in terms of different parts of the body and different times 
It is said: “Likewise, the hand would not remove the sufferings of the foot because 
they are other.” This is a mere illustration; it can also be applied to [the sufferings ] 
of the morning and those of the evening, and so forth. 
 
When we have suffering in our leg, we reliev e it with our hands by applying medicine and 
oils, massaging it, and so forth. However, this too would not be right si nce the suffering of the 
leg is not the suffering of the hand. This is an other way to refute the thought “Because others’ 
s u f f e r i n g  d o  n o t  h a r m  u s ,  w e  d o  n o t  n e e d  t o  e l i m i n a t e  t h e m . ”  I n  f a c t ,  w e  s h o u l d  r e m o v e  others’ suffering just as the hand eliminates the suffering of the leg.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
536  Likewise, the suffering of the morning and th e suffering of the afternoon are not the same. 
To avoid the suffering of the afternoon, we m ay do something in the morning, even though 
the suffering of the afternoon is different from that of the morning. In  other words, we take 
care of ourselves in the morning in order to av oid experiencing problems in the evening, and 
we take care of ourselves in the evening in order to avoid experiencing problems, such as 
sickness, the next morning. Although the times are different, we still strive to eliminate the 
suffering of another time. Thinking in this w ay, we should also relieve others’ suffering.  
 This is an illustration that we should use to understand why it is necessary to relieve others 
sufferings. For example, when a son suffers fr om a broken leg, his parents become unhappy, 
that is, they suffer mentally, and try to eliminat e his suffering and to do whatever they can to 
take care of him, even though the suffering of  the broken leg does not physically harm them. 
Although they could think that it is not necessa ry for them to eliminate their child’s suffering 
because it does not harm them, this would not be appropriate.  
 
Wednesday morning, 4 May 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 177) says:  
C Abandoning the doubt that [these analogies ] are not the same as self and other since they deal with 
the same continuum or same collection 
If you think: “The two, self and othe r, are not the same since the elderly [person] 
and the youth are the same continuum, whil e the hand and the foot are the same 
collection.” ‘Continuum’ and ‘collection’ are designated on many instants and a 
combination of many parts; they lack an  autonomous entity. Since your own self 
and others’ self must be posited on a continuum or collection, they are not 
established by way of their own entity, which is that self and other are not merely 
posited in relation. 
 Here the doubt is raised that while the yo ung person and elderly person are the same 
continuum, and the hand and foot are parts of th e same collection, self and other are neither 
and are therefore different from these two anal ogies. However, that the young person and 
elderly person are one continuum and that our bo d y  i s  a  c o l l e c t i o n  o f  m a n y  p a r t s  i s  o n l y  
imputed by thought. They do not exist by th eir own power but are dependent. In the same 
way, self and other are imputed by thought as “I ” and “other.” Therefore, they are similar in 
depending on each other. For example, if two pe ople are talking, one says “I” with respect to 
himself and “other” with respect to the other person. However, this “I” and “other” do not 
exist independently; they are dependent-relati ons. Therefore, without doubt self and other 
exist in dependence on each other. They exis t by imputation and do not exist by their own 
nature or from their own side. All phenomena do not exist from their own side, do not exist truly, and do not exist inherently. Such ty pes of existence are objects of negation.  
 Since self and other exist in dependence, we  can change our attitude by abandoning the 
thought that we are more important and deve loping the thought that others are more 
important, and then wish to take care of th em. Because other sentient beings are infinite, 
whereas we are only one, others are more important than us.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
537 D By the power of familiari zation, [the mind] cherishing others can also develop 
{178} Nonetheless, since, by the power of familiarizing with it from beginningless 
time, your own suffering is unbearable, so too if you familiarize with cherishing 
others their suffering will also become unbearable.  
 
If we want to develop the thou ght cherishing others we can do so through familiarizing with 
it. From beginningless time until now we have familiarized ourselves with the thought cherishing ourselves through thinking abou t how unbearable are our own problems and 
sufferings. If we now familiarize with the thought that others’ suffering is also unbearable, just 
by seeing other beings who are suffering the mind that is unable to bear their suffering will arise. This can only be developed by familiarizin g ourselves with this th ought. Therefore, just 
as it is presently easy for us to generate the self-cherishing mind due to having familiarized with it since beginningless time, so too throug h familiarity can we come to find others’ 
suffering unbearable. Based on this, we should  determine to eliminate others’ sufferings and 
accomplish their happiness. This co mes about due to familiarity.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2A2C-2 The actual way to meditate 
A Thinking about the disadvantages of cherishing oneself and the advantages of cherishing others 
Second: By the power of attachment, self -cherishing has produced much that is 
undesirable from beginningless time up to now. Despite the fact that you wanted 
to create excellence for yourself, throug h considering your own welfare paramount 
you engaged in unsuitable means. Due to this, neither your own welfare nor that 
of others was accomplished although a gr eat number of eons passed. Not only did 
you not accomplish that, you were tormente d only by suffering. If you had shifted 
the concern for your own welfare to that  of others, you would have become a 
buddha a long time ago and both your own and others’ welfare would have, 
without doubt, become excellent. But, as  you did not do that, the time has been 
spent in meaningless exertion.  
 The actual way to meditate on exchanging self an d others is divided into five sub-outlines. To 
exchange our present attitude of cherishing ours elves for cherishing others, we first need to 
contemplate the many disadvantages of cheris hing ourselves and the many advantages of 
cherishing others. From beginningless time unti l now, out of attachme nt to ourselves, we 
have continually experienced many undesirable sufferings and difficulties in cyclic existence 
and have not achieved our desired aims. Althou gh we wished for all that is excellent, we 
engaged in inappropriate methods to gain it. Due to this, we experienced suffering in the past 
for a great number of eons and are experiencing  suffering now, without achieving either our 
own or others’ welfare. Instead we were only tormented by suffering. We need to understand 
that up to now we have only been striving to take care of ourselves, due to which we engaged in mistaken methods that did not bring the result  of what is excellent but only more suffering 
and problems. Thus, we now need to change th is habit of thinking of ourselves alone and 
begin to think of others. If we had done this in the past we would now be buddhas. However, 
we did not do this, but only thought of oursel ves and did not think of others. Therefore, now 
we need to think that other sentient beings ar e more important than ourselves. By developing 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
538 this attitude, we will achieve buddhahood wher eby we will be able to excellently accomplish 
the welfare of all other sentient beings. There is  no doubt about this, it is certain. However, 
because we have not yet done this, we have passe d our time in meaningless activities and have 
been unable to eliminate even our own sufferi ng. Due to only thinking of ourselves, we 
engaged in mistaken methods that did not free us from cyclic existe nce and the thought to 
definitely emerge from the pleasures of cyclic  existence has still not arises. However, these 
pleasures deceive and cheat us. They cannot bring a good result, but only lead us to 
experience suffering again and again. For this  r e a s o n ,  w e  s h o u l d  n o t  b e  a t t a c h e d  t o  t h e  
excellences and happiness of cyclic existence. We need to abandon them and to wish to 
achieve the state of liberation for ourselves. To  do so, we need to meditate on emptiness and 
so forth whereby we will achieve the state gone  beyond cyclic existence – nirvana. However, 
since all our mother sentient beings are also experiencing suffering, it is not enough for 
ourselves alone to become liberated from cyclic existence. We need to take upon ourselves the 
responsibility to free all sentient beings. To do this, we must generate the mind of 
enlightenment and practice the six perfections an d the four means of gathering disciples. In 
this way, we can ripen and liberate our own continuum and can ripen those who are not ripe 
and liberate those who are ripe. In this way we will simultaneously achieve our own and 
others’ welfare by achieving buddhahood. Thus, we should not be attached to the pleasures of 
cyclic existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The need to put an end to self-cherishing by regarding it as an enemy 
Think: “Now that I have understood this , through relying on the mindfulness and 
introspection that my self-cherishing is my worst enemy, I will make much effort so 
that [the self-cherishing that ] has not arisen does not arise and that which has 
arisen does not last long.” Having made th is certainty stable, you should familiarize 
with it many times. 
 
Seeing that the self-cherishing atti tude is our real enemy, we need to strive to put an end to it. 
In fact our most powerful and worst enemy is our self-cherishing mind, therefore by relying 
on mindfulness and introspection we need to put effort into ceasing it. Whatever self-
cherishing has not been generated we should s t r i v e  t o  n o t  g e n e r a t e  i t ,  a n d  i f  w e  h a v e  
generated it we should try to stop it. We should achieve stable certainty regarding this and then familiarize with the though t that the self-cherishing mind  is our real enemy and that 
which needs to be eliminated. By familiarizing wi th this over and over again, one day we will 
be able to abandon the self-cherishing mind. On  the other hand, if we do not recognize the 
self-cherishing mind as our enemy, we will not strive to destroy it. Therefore, we must first 
recognize it as our enemy.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Generating the mind cherishing others 
Thinking about the advantages of cherishi ng others again and again in this way, 
you will generate strong enthusiasm to do  this from the bottom of your heart. 
Then, the mind that neglects others that  has not arisen will not arise and that 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
539 which has arisen will not last long. By means of generating [the thought ] that 
others are dear, attractive, and pleasant, you should generate the mind cherishing 
others, just as you previo usly cherished yourself.  
 
We need to develop the mind cherishing others  by thinking about its many advantages and 
benefits. When we develop this understanding from the depths of our heart, we will be 
delight in generating this attitude. We must tr y not to generate the mi nd that neglects other 
sentient beings, and if it has been generated we  must put a stop to it. Thinking that other 
sentient beings are dear, attractive, and pleasant, we should generate this attitude in our mind. In this way, just as in the past we ch erished ourselves, now we will develop the thought 
cherishing others and thinking th at they are important. We need to generate this attitude in 
our mind. We must think that others are very important and that we need to take care of 
them. This attitude will enable us to fulfill ou r own welfare as well as others’ welfare without 
effort. If we sow a rice seed in the ground in the spring, we will reap the result of rice in the 
autumn. While this was the main aim of planting  the rice seed, other grasses and weeds will 
a l s o  g r o w  i n  t h a t  f i e l d.  L i k e w i s e ,  b y  f u l f i l l i n g  t h e  w e l f a r e  o f  o t h e r s  f r o m  t h e  d e p t h s  o f  o u r  heart, we will by the way simultaneously fulfill our own welfare. On the other hand, if we only 
think to take care of ourselves, we will no t fulfill either our own or others’ welfares. 
Therefore, we need to develop th e attitude that cherishes others  and to familiarize with it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D How to meditate through remembering the teachings of earlier holy beings 
{179} Jowo [Atisha] said: “The Tibetans know bodhisattvas who do not know how 
to train in love and compassion. Well then , how is that done? It should be done 
through training sequentially from the beginning.” 
 Langri Thangpa said: “Shawopa and I have eighteen human methods and one 
horse method, nineteen altogether. The method of humans is, having generated 
the mind of supreme enlightenment, to train in doing whatever one does for the welfare of sentient beings. The method of horses is the self-cherishing that 
prevents the mind of enlightenment that has not been generated to arise and that 
which has arisen to remain and increase. Therefore, having taught it in passing, 
train in whatever harms it. Having taught  it as the fundamental point, train in 
whatever benefits sentient beings.”  Khamlungpa said: “Due to our acting deviously towards noble sentient beings, sentient beings will also do the same towards us.” 
 
We need to remember the teachings of past holy beings. For example, Jowo Atisha said that 
there are Tibetans who know bodhisattvas wh o do not know how to train in love and 
compassion. This means that there are so-calle d “bodhisattvas” who do not meditate on love 
and compassion. However, we need to meditate on love and compassion as they become the 
cause for generating the mind of enlightenment.  It is only when we develop an uncontrived 
mind of enlightenment that we become “bod hisattvas.” How is that done? We should 
meditate or train in this sequentially by eq ualizing friends, strangers, and enemies and 
developing equanimity for them; recognizing all sentient beings as having been our mothers; 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
540 remembering their kindness; wishing to repay th eir kindness; developing attracted love and 
compassion; developing the extraordinary attitude ; and, as a result, generating the mind of 
enlightenment. When we have developed an un contrived mind of enlightenment, we become 
bodhisattvas. In short, first we need to cultivate love and compassion and then the 
extraordinary attitude, after which we will  generate the mind of enlightenment.  
 Langri Thangpa said that he and Geshe Sh arawa have eighteen human methods and one 
horse-method, for a total of nineteen. The method  of human beings is to generate the mind 
of enlightenment and then train in doing whatev er one does for the benefit of all sentient 
beings. The method of horses is the self-cherish ing mind. It is that which blocks the mind of 
enlightenment that has not been  generated from arising, and blocks that which has arisen 
from abiding and increasing. Seeing this, we n eed to harm the self-cherishing attitude as 
m u c h  a s  p o s s i b l e .  I n  a d d i t i o n ,  w e  n e e d  t o  t r a i n  a s  m u c h  a s  w e  c a n  i n  t h e  m i n d  o f  
enlightenment and the bo dhisattva conduct.  
 Khamlungpa said that if we do not take care of sentient beings, then all sentient beings will 
do the same toward us. This refers to the fact th at whatever kind of action we create, it will 
bring a similar type of result. In other words, if  we harm other sentient beings, they will harm 
us in return. For example, if a father and mother  do not take care of their child, but instead 
beat him, insult him, and so forth, when that  child grows up he will act against his parents 
wishes, due to which they will experience suffe ring. Also due to this, when the child himself 
grows up and has children, they may treat him ba dly. Although he may then realize that he 
treated his own parents badly and may come to regr et this, it will be too late to do anything 
about it.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
E The appropriateness of putting effort by many approaches into the methods for generating the mind of 
enlightenment 
It is good if a habit develops that constantly checks to what extent this mind has 
been generated since everything stems fr om it: whether or no t the root of the 
Mahayana has been planted and whethe r or not one is included among 
Mahayanists. Even if it has not been generate d, do not leave it at that but, through 
relying on a virtuous spiritual friend who teaches it, always be accompanied by the 
companion that is mind training. Read the scriptures of the Teacher and their 
commentaries. As its cause, accumulate the collections and purify obscurations. 
Since it is certain that if you train your mind in this way, the seed will be fully 
planted, these actions are of no little import ance and a reason for joy. It is as the 
great Jowo [Atisha] said:  
The arisal of the mind of enlighte nment, which like the sun and moon 
Eliminate darkness and pacify the scorching heat, Is worth even an eon of effort 
By those who wish to enter the gateway to the Mahayana Dharma. 
 
We need to try to generate the mind of enli ghtenment by means of many different methods. 
We need to put effort into this because whether or not the root of the Mahayana is 
established and whether or not we become included among Mahayanists depends on the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
541 generation of the mind of en lightenment. Hence, if we do not develop the mind of 
enlightenment, we will not establish the root of the Mahayana and we will not be included 
among Mahayanists. Therefore, at all time s we need try to generate the mind of 
enlightenment and to increase it. If we have not generated it, we should not give up but 
instead should put effort into meditating on it . We should also rely on virtuous spiritual 
friends who teach how to develop the mind of en lightenment by asking them to help us train 
our mind in developing the mind of enlightenm ent such that it will never be lost. Not only 
should we rely on teachers who teach us ho w to develop the mind of enlightenment, we 
should also find friends who are studying an d training in how to develop the mind of 
enlightenment. We should also read Buddha Shakyamuni’s teachings and their 
commentaries. In addition, in order to genera te the mind of enlightenment we need to 
acquire its cause – the collection of merit and the purification of obscurations. If we have 
done all that is necessary, it will definitely aris e. Thus, if we have planted this seed in our 
mind, we should not worry but rejoice as we  will definitely generate the mind of 
enlightenment.   Atisha said that just as sunlight elimin ates the darkness and moonlight pacifies the 
suffering of heat, if we want to enter the Mahayana we need to generate the mind of 
enlightenment and that it is worth putting effort into generating it, even if we have to do so 
f o r  a n  e o n .  T h e r e f o r e ,  i n  o u r  d a i l y  l i f e  w e  s h o u l d  c o n s t a n t l y  t r y  t o  g e n e r a t e  t h e  m i n d  o f  enlightenment. We should not do this only from time to time or just for a short time, as in this way it will not be possible to generate the mind of enlightenment. To generate it, we need 
to make constant effort at it. The mind of en lightenment is the wish to attain enlightenment 
in order to be able to benefit all sentient be ings. we need to generate this mind in our 
continuum and to familiarize with it until we  achieve an uncontrive d and spontaneous mind 
of enlightenment. Right now we only have a we ak and contrived mind of enlightenment that 
arises when we think about it, but then again di sappears. Thus, we need to strive to generate 
an uncontrived mind of enlightenment.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2B The measure of having generated that mind  
The second: {180} that should be understood from what was already explained 
above. 
 
The measure of having generated the mind of  enlightenment should be understood from 
what was explained above. The mind of enligh tenment is the wish to achieve enlightenment 
for the benefit of all sentient beings. When th is mind becomes uncontrived or non-artificial, 
we are said to have generated the mind of enli ghtenment. On the other hand, as long as the 
mind of enlightenment is contrived or artificial, even though it is called “bodhichitta” it is 
not actual  b o d h i c h i t t a .  I t  i s  l i k e  t h e  r e a l  s w e e t n e s s  t h a t  i s  h i d d e n  i n s i d e  t h e  h a r d  b a r k  o f  
sugarcane.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
Explanation of how to assume the mi nd generation by means of ritual 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
542 2B4B-2B3B-2C The way in which to assume it by means of ritual 
1 Receiving that which has not yet been received 
2 Guarding that which has been received without degenerating it 
3 The method for restoring it if it has been degenerated 
The third has three [points]: receiving that which has not yet been received, 
guarding that which has been received without degenerating it, and the method 
for restoring it if it has been degenerated. 
2B4B-2B3B-2C1 Receiving that which has not yet been received 
A From whom: the object from whom it is taken  
B By whom: the basis that takes it 
C How: the ritual by which it is taken 
The first has three [points]: From whom: th e object from whom it is taken; by 
whom: the basis that takes it; how: the ritual by which it is taken. 
 
If the mind generation has not yet been taken, from whom should it be taken? It must be 
taken from a teacher who himself already ha s the bodhisattva vows.  Having taken the 
bodhisattva vows, we must strive to keep them . The basis that takes the bodhisattva vow is 
those who are gods, nagas, and, of course, hu man beings. They can take the aspiring mind 
generation vow. Then the ritual by which the mind generation is to be taken is set forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1A From whom: the object from whom it is taken 
First: The assertions of the earlier masters accord with Master Jetari that it is not 
enough [for the person from whom the vo w is taken] to possess the aspiring mind 
and to be abiding in the trainings, rath er he must possess the engaging vow. 
 
It is not enough to take the mind generation from someone who merely possesses the aspiring 
mind and is abiding in the trainings. This pers on must have the engaging vow, not just the 
aspiring mind. In short, the person from whom it  is received must have the actual bodhisattva 
vows.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1B By whom: the basis that takes it 
Second: All gods, nagas, and so forth who are physically and mentally suitable to 
generate the aspiring mind are also a s uitable basis for it. The commentary on the 
Lamp on the Path  says: 
 
Repulsed by cyclic existence, mindful of death,  
With wisdom and great compassion. 
 
Thus, it should be someone who has gained a little experience of transforming his 
mind into the mind of enlightenment due to  having trained the mind in the stages 
of the path as explained previously. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
543 The bodily basis or support for taking the vow of the aspiring mind generation is that of gods, 
nagas, and human beings. Therefore, they are a suitable basis for it. In the commentary on 
the Lamp on the Path  by Atisha it says that the basis should be someone who has trained the 
mind in renouncing cyclic existence and in remembering death-impermanence, who possesses 
the wisdom understanding that phenomena ar e empty of inherent or self-supporting 
substantial existence, as well as the great compassion that wishes to liberate all sentient beings 
from suffering and the causes of suffering. Ther efore, we too should train as was explained 
before in understanding the sufferings of cyclic  existence whereby we wi ll no longer want to 
abide in cyclic existence and will generate the mi nd wishing to be free from cyclic existence. 
We should also train in remembering death-im permanence: death is certain, the time of 
death is uncertain, and at the time of death on ly our practice of the Dharma will be of help, 
whereas our friends, relatives, and our body will not be able to help us because we will have to 
leave all of them behind and go on alone. We need to remember death and strive to generate 
bodhichitta as much as possible. In this way, we  will become a suitable basis for generating the 
vow.  
 In short, in order to take th e vow of the aspiring mind generation by means of ritual, we 
need to have some slight experience of transforming our mind into the mind of 
enlightenment. Having taken it, we need to tr ain in the mind generation by abandoning the 
four black dharmas and adopting  the four white dharmas.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C How: the ritual by which it is taken 
1 The preparatory ritual 
2 The actual ritual 
3 The concluding ritual  
The third has three [points]: the preparat ory ritual, the actual ritual, and the 
concluding ritual.  
 
The ritual for taking the aspiring mind genera tion is divided into the preparatory ritual, 
actual ritual, and concluding ritual.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-1 The preparatory ritual 
A Special going for refuge  
B Accumulating the collection 
C Training the mind 
The first has three [units]: the special goin g for refuge, accumulating the collection, 
and training the mind. 
 
The preparatory ritual consists in going for refuge, accumulating merit, and training the 
mind. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-1A Special going for refuge 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
544 1 Decorating the place, displaying representations, and setting up offering substances 
2 Making a request and going for refuge 
3 Stating the trainings of having gone for refuge 
The first is divided into three [point s]: Decorating the place, displaying 
representations, and setting up offering substances; making a request and going for 
refuge; and stating the trainings of having gone for refuge. 
 
We need to decorate the place in which we will take the aspiring mind generation by stringing 
garlands of flowers and so forth. We should al so set out representations of the exalted body, 
exalted speech, and exalted mind. The representati on of the exalted body is a statue of the 
Buddha, the representation of  the exalted speech is a volume of scriptures, and the 
representation of the exalted mind is a stupa. Th en we should arrange a set of five offerings: 
water, flowers, incense, light, and food. Then, we should make a request to the master and go 
for refuge. Having gone for refuge, the master explains the trainings that we then need to 
abide by.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-1A1 Decorating the place, displaying representations, and setting up offering 
substances 
A General indication concerning the place, the representations, offerings, etc. 
First: Sweep an isolated place, smear it with the five cow substances171, sprinkle it 
with superb fragrant water such as sand alwood and the like, and scatter fragrant, 
fresh flowers. Set out {181} representation s of the Three Jewels – metal statues and 
so forth, texts and so forth, and images of  bodhisattvas – on a throne, table, or a 
raised platform. Do the preparatory ritual with as valuable offering items such as 
canopies, flowers, and so fo rth as possible, as well as music and food. Arrange a 
throne for the virtuous spiritual friend an d also adorn it with flowers. Accumulate 
the collection [of merit] by paying respect to the fo rmer masters, the sangha, and 
giving a torma to the elemental spirits. 
 We should sweep an isolated place and smear it with the five substances obtained from cows: 
butter, yogurt, milk, dung, and urine. These su bstances are considered to be pure in the 
brahmin tradition. In fact, when I stayed together  with a brahmin family for some years, they 
would clean the ground with these substances every morning.   Then, sprinkle the place with perfume such as sandalwood and scatter fragrant flowers 
about. Then set out representations of the Th ree Jewels. The representation of the Buddha 
Jewel is an image of the Buddha, such as a tsa- tsa or statue. The representation of the Dharma 
Jewel is a volume of scriptures. The representa tion of the Sangha Jewel is images of the 
bodhisattvas. These should be put on a raised platform or altar. Above it, hang a canopy. 
Then set out offerings of flowers and so forth, as much as can be obtained. Offer music in the 
form of cymbals, conch shells, and so forth and set out offerings of food.   Arrange a throne for the virtuous spiritual friend and adorn it with flowers. Make offerings to the sangha of food, robes, money, and so forth and give a torma to the elemental spirits 
                                             
 
171 These are products of a cow that has lived in remote  mountain areas for a certain period of time feeding 
only on the pure grasses and herbs of such pl aces (oral explanation by Geshe Thubten Soepa).

is so-called because a torma composed of three parts is offered to three sets of guests.

Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 181) says:  
B Clearing up doubts about this 
If you do not have any offerings, as described in the Sutra of the Good Eon  it can be 
accomplished even with cloth fringes. If you do have offerings, they should have 
been obtained through great effort without deceit, and should be arrayed in such a 
way that your friends cannot help marveling.  
 When Jowo [Atisha] was requested by Tibetan teachers in Mangyul and Samye for the mind generation, he reportedly said that it was not generated by inferior offerings. He said that also as a repres entation a consecrated statue of the main 
master who taught was imperati ve, and also as a text the Abbreviated Perfection of 
Wisdom  on upward should be used. 
 Here the doubt is eliminated regarding the fact that if you have something good to offer, you 
should offer it. On the other hand, if you do not have any offerings, you can offer even as 
little as a piece of cotton cloth. If you have offerings, they shou ld be obtained without deceit. 
We should offer whatever we can, and arranged in such a way that our friends are made to 
marvel. Thus, with respect to offerings there are the two factors: the motivation and the 
s u b s t a n c e .  F o r  e x a m p l e ,  w e  s h o u l d  n o t  o f f e r  t h i n g s  t h a t  w e r e  o b t a i n e d  b y  h i n t i n g  t o  o u r  
benefactor, saying, for example “Last year you gave me such-and-such offerings,” in order to obtain them again. If we make offerings moti vated by the eight worldly concerns this would 
not be right and the offerings thereby become impure. We should offer only what has been 
obtained honestly.   Atisha was asked by the Tibetan teachers  in Mangyul and Samye to give the mind 
generation by ritual. At this time Atisha explai ned that if the offerings made were inferior it 
would not be possible to generate the mind of  enlightenment, and that only good things 
should be offered. Similarly, the Tibetan nomads say that it is not good  to melt rancid butter 
and offer it in a butter lamp. In other words, we  should not offer things that are no longer 
good to eaten or drunk. Atisha also said that  a consecrated statue of the Teacher, Buddha 
Shakyamuni, as well as a text of the Abbreviated Perfection of Wisdom Sutra  on upward, that is, 
either the 8,000, 25,000, 10, 000 Verse Perfection of Wisdom Sutras , should be set on the altar as 
representations respectively of the Buddha Jewel and Dharma Jewel.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C The steps to be performed by the lama and student  
Then, the assembly of aryas is invoked. The student, washed and well dressed with 
his palms joined, is encouraged by the la ma to generate clear faith in the good 
qualities of the merit field from the bottom of his heart. He should then slowly do 
the seven-limb prayer, imagining that he is in front of the buddhas and 
bodhisattvas. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
546  
The assembly of arya beings should be invited.  The student, clean and well dressed, should 
fold his hands together. The lama then makes him remember the good qualities of the merit 
field and to generate clear faith in them. Then, imagining that he is in front of the buddhas 
and bodhisattvas, the student should, with fo lded hands, do the seven-limb prayer in a 
melodious and slow tune. The seven limbs are: prostrating, offering, confessing, rejoicing, 
requesting to turn the wheel of Dharma, that is, to give teachi ngs, exhorting to not pass away 
but to remain, and dedicating the virtue created by doing the previous six limbs as a cause for 
all sentient beings to achieve buddhahood. Ther e is an extensive vers ion of the seven-limb 
prayer in Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  and the Samantabhadra Prayer  (King of Prayers ) There 
is also a shorter version in the Guru Yoga of Lama Tsongkhapa , as well as the standard very 
short version: 
 
Respectfully I prostrate with my three doors. 
I present offerings, both those actu al arranged and mentally emanated. 
I confess all negativities and downfalls accumulated since beginningless time. 
I rejoice in all the virtues of ordinary and arya beings. Please remain as long as cy clic existence is not empty, 
And turn the wheel of the profound and vast Dharma. I dedicate my own and others’ virtues to great enlightenment. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-1A2 Making a request and going for refuge 
A Making a request 
B The special going for refuge  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-1A2A Making a request 
Second: Then [the student ], having generated the discrimination of his lama as 
the Teacher, prostrates to him and makes offerings, including a mandala, to him. 
With his right knee on the floor, palms jo ined, he makes a request for the purpose 
of the mind of enlightenment: 
 
Just as the previous tathagata, foe-destroyer, perfectly complete buddhas, as well as the great bodhisa ttvas abiding on the great grounds, 
first generated the mind of unsurpassed, perfectly complete enlightenment, likewise so do I, wh ose name is…, request the master to 
generate the mind of unsurpassed, perfectly complete enlightenment. 
 Repeat this three times. 
 The disciple discriminates the te acher who will give him the vow of the aspiring or engaging 
mind generation as the Teacher, Buddha Shakyamun i. He then prostrates to him, either by 
doing long or short prostrations or just putti ng his palms together, and then makes offerings 
including a mandala offering saying:  
This ground anointed with perfume and strewn with flowers,

Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
547 Adorned with Mt. Meru, the four continents, sun, and moon, 
I offer, visualized as a buddha field. May all beings enjoy this completely pure realm. 
 
IDAM GURU RATNA MANDALAKAM NIRYATAYAMI 
 
Then the disciple kneels with his right knee  on the ground, hands folded, and makes the 
request to receive the mind of enlightenment by means of ritual saying the above lines: “ Just 
as the previous tathagata, foe-destroyer, perfectly complete buddhas…” There are ten 
bodhisattva grounds, such as the Joyous, Stainless,  and so forth. We say to the master “Just as 
they perfectly generated the mind of unsurpasse d, perfectly complete enlightenment, likewise 
s o  d o  I ,  w h o  a m  c a l l e d … ,  r e q u e s t  t o  g e n e ra t e  the mind of unsurpassed, perfectly complete 
enlightenment.” In other words, “Just as the buddhas of the past generated the mind of 
enlightenment and just as the bodhisattvas abid ing on the ten grounds generated the mind of 
enlightenment, so too may I generate it. Please  b es t o w i t  u po n m e.” We s ho u ld  m ake t hi s  
request three times, after which we should think that the guru accepts our request.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-1A2B The special going for refuge  
1 The attitude together with the objects 
Then, [the student ] goes for refuge, with the behavior explained before, to the 
objects – the Buddha, the Dharma (true pa ths that mainly bring about cessations), 
and the Sangha (the irreversible arya bo dhisattvas) – with the general attitude 
thinking: “From now until I attain enlighte nment, in order to protect all sentient 
beings [I go for refuge ] to the Buddha, the teacher of refuge; [I go for refuge ] to 
the Dharma, the actual refuge; [I go for refuge ] to the Sangha, those who help to 
achieve refuge”; and with the special attitu de of making the strong aspiration: “I 
will never turn away from this attitude at any time.” 
 
The objects of refuge are the Three Jewels: the Buddha who is an enlightened being; the 
Dharma, mainly true cessations and true path s; and the Sangha, the irreversible arya 
bodhisattvas. The student goes for refuge to them  with the behavior explained before, that is, 
kneeling on his right knee with his hands folded together, while thinking “From now on, as long as I have not achieved enlightenment, for the purpose of protecting all sentient beings I 
go for refuge to the Buddha, th e one who shows refuge or protection; I go for refuge to the 
actual refuge, the Dharma, true paths and true cessations; and I go for refuge to the Sangha, 
those who help me actualize the final refuge, th e Dharma.” This is the general attitude or 
motivation for going for refuge. The special or pa rticular attitude is the strong aspiration: “I 
will not turn away from this attitude at any time.”   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
548 2 The words of the ritual  
“Master, please grant me your attent ion. I, whose name is (…), from 
now until I reach the essence of enli ghtenment, go for refuge to the 
best of men, the Buddha Bhagavans.  
Master, please grant me your attention. I, whose name is (…), from now 
until I reach the essence of enlightenm ent, go for refuge to the best of 
Dharma, the Dharma that is peace, free from attachment.  Master, please grant me your attention. I, whose name is (…), from now 
until I reach the essence of enlightenm ent, go for refuge to the best of 
assemblies, the Sangha of irreve rsible arya bodhisattvas.” {183} 
 
Repeat this three times. This request for a ttention when going for refuge to each of 
the Jewels and the unusual words of going for refuge to the Dharma accord with the ritual composed by Jowo [Atisha]. 
 
Here we begin by asking the “Master” or “Achar ya” to pay attention to us. “I, who am called… 
from now on, as long as I have not reached the essence of enlightenment, go for refuge to the 
best among bipeds, that is, among human be ings who walk on two legs, the Buddha 
Bhagavans.” The Sanskrit word Buddha is sang-gye  (sangs rgyas ) in Tibetan. The syllable, sang, 
meaning purified , indicates that he has completely purified all obscurations. The syllable gye, 
meaning extended , indicates that he has complete increase d his wisdom to the point that he is 
able to see all phenomena directly. The Sanskrit word Bhagavan is translated into Tibetan as 
chom-den-de  (bcom ldan ‘das ) The syllable chom, meaning destroyed , indicates that he has 
destroyed the four maras or demons: the mara of the aggregates, the mara of the mental 
afflictions, the mara of de ath, and the mara of the so ns-of-the-gods. The syllable den, meaning 
possess , indicates that he possesses the six qualities of perfect wisdom and so forth. The syllable 
de, meaning transcended  or passed beyond , is added to the original Sa nskrit word “Bhagavan” in 
order to indicate that a buddha has gone beyond  or transcended the great gods because this 
epithet was used in India to refer to Brahma , Shiva, Mahadeva, and so forth. Because a 
buddha bhagavan possesses these characteristics, we pay homage to him by putting the crown 
of our head to his feet.  
 Again we say “Master, please listen to me. I, who am called…, from now on, as long as I 
have not attained reached the essence of enligh tenment, go for refuge to the Dharma Jewel.” 
The Dharma Jewel is peace or cessation, since it  pacifies or ceases all sufferings and mental 
afflictions, and is free from attachment and so forth.   Then once again we say: “Master, please list en to me. I, who am called…, from now on, as 
long as I have not reached the essence of en lightenment, go for refuge to the best of 
assemblies, the sangha of irreversible arya bodhisattvas.” The Sublime Continuum  sets out the 
eight qualities of the Buddha, Dharma, and Sang ha Jewels. Irreversible arya bodhisattvas who 
are dull-facultied are the bodhisattvas abidin g on the eighth, ninth, and tenth grounds. 
Irreversible arya bodhisattvas who are sharp-fa cultied are those who have achieved the heat 
level of the path of preparation and will never gi ve up bodhichitta and fall to the hearer path. 
In this case “irreversible” means that their re alization of the mind of enlightenment will no 
longer degenerate and that they will never fall to the Hinayana path.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
549  We should repeat these words three times. Th is ritual for taking the vow of the aspiring 
mind generation was composed by Atisha. While the words of going for refuge to the Dharma 
differ from those found in other contexts, they are based upon Atisha’s teachings on taking 
the bodhisattva vows by means of ritual.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-1A3 Stating the trainings of having gone for refuge 
Third: Here too the master should express the trainings that were explained before 
in the context of small beings. 
 
Having gone for refuge, there are precepts or trainings to be followed that were explained 
previously in the context of the path of small beings. These include general precepts and 
specific precepts. The specific precepts regarding what is to be abandoned are: 
1. having gone for refuge to the Buddha, to  not go for refuge to mundane gods;  
2. having gone for refuge to the Dharma, to no t go for refuge to the scriptures of the 
Tirthikas; and  
3. having gone for refuge to the Sangha, to no t associate with misleading friends such as 
Tirthikas. 
The specific precepts regarding what is to be adopted are:  
1. having gone for refuge to the Buddha, to resp ect any representation of the Buddha as if it 
were the actual Buddha;  
2. having gone for refuge to the Dharma, to re spect any Dharma texts as if they were the 
actual Dharma, true paths and true cessations;  
3. having gone for refuge to the Sangha, to resp ect the ordinary sangha of monks and nuns as 
if they were the actual sangha.  
The general precepts are before eating or drinki ng to offer one’s food and drink to the Three 
Jewels, and to recite the refuge formula three times during the day and three times at night. 
These precepts were taught in the context of small beings. We should read and study them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-1B Accumulation of the collection [of merit] 
S e c o n d :  A s  b e f o r e  t h e  s e v e n - l i m b  p r a y e r  s h o u l d  b e  d o n e  i n  t h e  p r e s e n c e  o f  t h e  
direct and lineage gurus and the merit field explained previously. 
 
We also need to accumulate merit. To do so, in front of ourselves we should visualize the 
direct gurus from whom we presently receive teachings and the lineage gurus from Buddha 
Shakyamuni down to our present guru. We shou ld also visualize the merit field that was 
explained previously. Then we should perform th e seven-limb prayer, offer a mandala, and so 
forth as before.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-1C Training in the attitude 
Third: As explained previously, the obse rved objects and aspects of love and 
compassion should be visualized. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
550  
The observed object of love is sentient beings , its aspect is the thought: “How wonderful it 
would be if all sentient beings were to have  happiness and the causes of happiness. May they 
have happiness and the causes of happiness.” Th e observed object of compassion is all 
sentient beings, its aspect is the thought: “How  wonderful it would be if all sentient beings 
were free from suffering and the causes of suffering. May they be free from suffering and the 
causes of suffering.” All sentient beings are tormented by the three types of suffering: the 
suffering of suffering, suffering of change, and pervasive suffering. Unable to bear this, we 
should wish all sentient beings to be free from their suffering and the causes of their suffering. 
We should visualize this clearly.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-2 The actual ritual 
A The behavior 
Second: In front of the master, [the student ] should kneel down on his right knee; 
alternatively, sitting in a crouching position  is also appropriate. With palms joined, 
he should generate the mind [of enlightenment]. 
 
The behavior is, in front of the master, for th e student to kneel with his right knee on the 
ground, or to sit in a crouching position. He should join his palms and generate the mind of enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The distinction of the mind generation 
[The mind generation here ] is not the mere mind generation that thinks “I will 
attain buddhahood for others’ welfare.” Ra ther, it is the promise that, observing 
the mind that has been generated, thinks, “I  shall not give it up as long as I have 
not attained enlightenment.” This is why that attitude should be generated in 
dependence on a ritual. 
 
The distinction or particularity of the mind genera tion here is that it is not just the mind of 
enlightenment wishing to attain buddhahood for th e welfare of all sentient beings. What is it? 
It is the promise or determination to not give up this mind of enlightenment as long as we 
have not attained enlightenment. To make this ki nd of promise, we need to rely on a ritual.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C An exception to that 
If you are unable to train in the traini ngs of the aspiring mind, you should not 
[make this promise ]. On the other hand, if the me re thought “I will become a 
buddha for the welfare of all sentient bein gs” is generated by means of a ritual, 
everyone can do it, regardless of whether they are able or not to train in the 
trainings of the mind generation. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
551 The exception to this is that if we want to take the mind generati on that is the wish to attain 
enlightenment for the welfare of all sentient bein gs by means of ritual but are unable to train 
in the trainings, we should  not make the promise “I will not give up the mind of 
enlightenment as long as I have not attained  enlightenment.” So, wh at should we do? We 
should merely think “I will become a buddha for the welfare of all sentient beings,” and with 
this thought take the mind generation by means of ritual. In this case, whether we can train 
in the trainings or not, we can take the mi nd of enlightenment by means of ritual.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D It is inappropriate to posit two systems of guarding and not guarding the trainings with respect to the 
engaging mind [of enlightenment] 
With respect to the aspiring mind, these two [systems] may be appropriate, 
however, with respect to taking the enga ging mind by means of ritual, it is 
inappropriate to take it without training  in the trainings. Therefore, it is 
unsuitable to assert that there is a diffe rence with respect to it being appropriate 
and inappropriate in the many rituals fo r the vow transmitted from Nagarjuna and 
Asanga {184}. 
 
If we take the aspiring mind of enlightenmen t there are two ways to do so: (1) with the 
promise not to give up the mind generation as  long as one has not attained enlightenment 
and to guard the trainings and (2) with the mere  promise not to give up the mind generation 
as long as one has not attained enlightenment. However, in the case of taking the engaging 
mind of enlightenment by means of a ritual, one must necessarily promise to train in the 
trainings, as otherwise one cannot take the enga ging mind, that is, the bodhisattva vows, by 
means of ritual. While there are two systems rega rding the ritual for taking the vows, that of 
Nagarjuna and that of Asanga, it is inappropriat e to assert that there is a difference between 
them with respect to  this assertion. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
E Although it is appropriate for someone not training in the trainings to generate the aspiring mind, the 
engaging vow is inappropriate 
The Advice to King Prasenajit  says that if one cannot train in the trainings of 
generosity, etc., a great amount of merit is accomplished from merely generating 
the mind [of enlightenment ]. Taking this as its source, the First Stages of Meditation  
says: 
 
Whoever cannot train in the perfection s fully in all their aspects should 
generate the mind of enlightenm ent thoroughly conjoined with 
method because the results will be great. 
 Thus, it is clear that it is appropriate for someone incapable of training in the 
trainings of generosity and so forth to generate the mind of enlightenment, but inappropriate to hold the vow. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
552 Someone who does not guard the trainings can ge nerate the aspiring mind of enlightenment 
by means of ritual but cannot take the engaging mind of enlightenment by means of ritual, 
that is, cannot take the bodhisattva vows. In the Advice to King Prasenajit  it says that someone 
who cannot train in the trainings can still take the vows of aspiring mind of enlightenment. 
In other words, even if such a person cannot keep the trainings he can still generate the mind 
of enlightenment and thereby create  a lot of merit. Likewise, the First Stages of Meditation  says 
that even if someone cannot practice the six perf ections fully and in all ways, if he generates 
the mind of enlightenment it will still bring a great result. In other words, we need to 
generate the mind of enlightenment thoroughly  conjoined with method . Hence, even if we 
are unable to train in the trainings of the six pe rfections, it is still s uitable to generate the 
aspiring mind of enlightenment by means of ritu al. However, if we are unable to train in the 
trainings, we should not take the bodhisattva vows, that is, th e vows of the engaging mind of 
enlightenment.  
 
In short, we should try to generate bodh ichitta. Every morning we should generate 
bodhichitta thinking “May I attain buddhahood in  order to be able to benefit all sentient 
beings.” We should develop the mo tivation to take care of all sentient beings based on great 
love and great compassion, and, with these as a cause, generate the mind of enlightenment 
thinking to attain enlightenment in order to be able to take care of all sentient beings. We 
should try to generate bodhichitta in this way in our every day life. This will make our human 
rebirth useful and meaningful. If we do not do anything special with it, our good human 
rebirth will just go to waste. It will be meaningl ess. If we think only ab out this life, this will 
become the cause for us to be reborn again an d again in cyclic existence and to experience 
suffering. Therefore, we should wish to get out of cyclic existence and to take other sentient 
beings with us.  
 Thursday morning, 5 May 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
F The ritual for assuming the mind generation  
The ritual for assuming the mind generation is: 
 
All buddhas and bodhisattvas residing in the ten directions, please 
grant me your attention. Master, please grant me your attention. I, 
whose name is (…), by means of the roots of virtue of the nature of 
generosity, the nature of morality, an d the nature of meditation that I 
have created, caused to be created, and rejoiced in the creation of in 
this life and in previous lives, just as the previous tathagata, foe-destroyer, perfectly complete buddhas and great bodhisattvas thoroughly abiding on the great grounds, generated the mind of unsurpassed perfectly complete enligh tenment, in the same way also I, 
whose name is (…), from now until I reach the essence of enlightenment, will generate the mind of unsurpassed, perfectly 
complete, great enlightenment, I will  liberate the sentient beings who 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
553 are not liberated, free those who ar e not free, give breath to those 
unable to breathe, and cause thos e who have not completely passed 
beyond sorrow to completely pass beyond sorrow. 
 
Say this three times. Even though it is not clearly mentioned in the refuge 
[ c e r e m o n y ]  a n d  i n  t h i s  r i t u a l ,  i t  i s  n e c e s s a r y  t o  r e p e a t  [ t h e s e  w o r d s ]  a f t e r  t h e  
master. 
 This is the ritual for taking the aspiring mi nd generation. Here we request all the buddhas 
and bodhisattvas abiding in the ten directio ns – the four cardinal directions, four 
intermediate directions, above and below – to p ay attention to us. We also ask the master or 
acharya to pay attention to us. When we say “I, whose name is…, ” we should insert our actual 
name or our bodhisattva name.   There are roots of virtue of th e nature of generosity, the natu re of morality, the nature of 
meditation, that we have created; roots of vi rtue of making others practice generosity, 
morality, and meditation; and roots of virtue of having rejoiced in others’ generosity, morality, and meditation.” By means of these r oots of virtue, we promise that just as the 
previous buddhas and bodhisattvas generated th e mind of unsurpassed, perfectly complete 
great enlightenment, so do we, from now un til we reach the essence of enlightenment, 
generate the mind of enlightenment. Having generated the mind of enlightenment, what 
should we do? We then promise to liberate all the sentient beings who are not liberated, free 
those who are not free, give brea th to those unable to breathe,  and cause those who have not 
completely passed beyond sorrow to completely pass beyond sorrow. By making this promise 
we take the aspiring mind generation by means of ritual.   Although in the ritual of going for refuge an d in this ritual for taking the aspiring mind 
generation it is not clearly said that we need to  repeat these words after the master or acharya, 
this in fact is necessary.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
G The method for taking [the mind generation] by oneself in a ritual  
This is the manner [of taking the mind ge neration] in the presence of a master. 
From Jowo [Atisha]’s Mind Generation  Ritual what to do if we cannot find a master 
is taught: 
 
The ritual for generating the mind  of enlightenment oneself in the 
absence of a master is as follows.  Having brought to mind Tathagata 
Shakyamuni and all the tathagatas of the ten directions, prostrate to 
them and do the rite of offerings an d so forth. Then, according to the 
previous order, make the request, go for refuge while leaving out the 
word “Master,” and so forth.  
 
What was set out above is the system for taking the vow [of the aspiring mind generation ] by 
the ritual from a master. However, if we do no t have a master, we shou ld do as explained in 
the Ritual for the Mind Generation  composed by Jowo Atisha. In th is case we can take the mind 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
554 generation by ritual by visualizing Tathagat a Shakyamuni and all the tathagatas of the ten 
directions in front of us. Then, we prostrate to  them three times, do the rite of offerings, 
make request, and go for refuge while leaving out the words “Master, please grant me your 
attention.” 
 Tathagata  literally means thus gone  or gone thus . In addition, the buddhas are also called 
sugata , literally gone to bliss . There are two types of tathagata or sugata: the tathagata or sugata 
of the complete abandonment of all the afflicti ons, obscurations, and their imprints, and the 
tathagata or sugata of the omniscient mind that directly sees both varieties and modes, that is, 
conventional truths and ultimate truths, with out any exception. One who has achieved this 
realization is called “thus gone.”  
 Shakya  is the name of Buddha’s clan, muni means capable one, that is, able to destroy all 
the afflictions and obscurations. Thinking of the qualities of the tathagatas, we should 
prostrate with our body, speech, and mind, to them , that is, physically we should prostrate to 
them, verbally we should recite praise to th em, and mentally we should generate faith in 
them. Then we can do a short o ffering ritual such as the seve n-limb prayer which consists of 
prostrating, offering, confessing, rejoicing, requ esting to turn the wheel of Dharma, exhorting 
to not pass away, and dedicating all the virtue s created by the previous six limbs that they 
become a cause for all sentient beings to ac hieve enlightenment. Then we should offer a 
mandala, for example, the seven-heap mandala with  the recitation of the usual four-line verse. 
The seven heaps consist of Mount Meru the middl e, surrounded by the four continents in the 
east, south, west, and north, as well as th e sun and the moon. In  the context of the 
Guhyasamaja Tantra and the Tara Chittamani Ta ntra There is also mandala offering of 23 
heaps, however, in this contex t we could over the long mandala of 37 heaps that comes from 
the Sakya tradition.  
 We go for refuge as before, except that we omit the words “Master, please grant me your attention”:   
I, whose name is (…), from now until I re ach the essence of enlightenment, go for 
refuge to the best of men, the Buddha Bhagavans.  I, whose name is (…), from now until I re ach the essence of enlightenment, go for 
refuge to the best of Dharma, the Dharma  that is peace, free from attachment.  
I, whose name is (…), from now until I re ach the essence of enlightenment, go for 
refuge to the best of assemblies, the Sangha of irreversible arya bodhisattvas. 
 The rest of the ritual for taking the mind genera tion is also the same, although once again we 
leave out the words “Master, please grant me your attention”:   
All buddhas and bodhisattvas residing in the ten directions, please grant me your 
attention. I, whose name is (…), by means of the roots of  virtue of the nature of 
generosity, the nature of morality, and the nature of meditation that I have 
created, caused to be created, and rejoiced  in  t h e  c r e a t i o n  o f i n  t h i s  l i f e  a n d  i n  
previous lives, just as the previous tath agata, foe-destroyer, perfectly complete 
buddhas and great bodhisattvas thorou ghly abiding on the great grounds, 
generated the mind of unsurpassed perfectl y complete enlightenment, in the same 
way also I, whose name is (…), from now until I reach the essence of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
555 enlightenment, will generate the mind of  unsurpassed, perfectly complete, great 
enlightenment, I will liberate the sentient  beings who are not liberated, free those 
who are not free, give breath to those unab le to breathe, and cause those who have 
not completely passed beyond sorrow to completely pass beyond sorrow.

2B4B-2B3B-2C1C-3 The concluding ritual

The master should explain to the student the tr ainings of the aspiring mind that he should
