Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2 Guarding that which has been obtained without degeneration 
A Training in the cause that assures the mind generation will not degenerate in this life  
B Training in the cause that assures one will not be separated from [the mind generation ] 
again in future lives 
The second has two [points]: Training in the cause that assures the mind 
generation will not degenerate during this  life and training in the cause that 
assures one will not be separated from [the mind generation ] again in future lives. 
 
We need to train in the causes for not degenera ting the mind of enligh tenment in this life 
and in other lives and for not being separated from it in future lives.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A Training in the cause that assures the mind generation will not degenerate 
during this life 
1 Training in remembering the advantages, so that the strength of one’s delight in the mind generation increases 
2 Training in generating it six times, so that the actual mind generation increases  
3 Training in not mentally abandoning any sentient being for whose welfare the mind has 
been generated 
4 Training in accumulating the collections of merit and wisdom 
The first has four [points]: Training in remembering the advantages, so that the 
strength of one’s delight in the mind genera tion increases, training in generating it 
six times, so that the actual mind genera tion increases, training in not mentally 
abandoning any being, for whose welfare the mind has been generated, and 
training in accumulating the collections of merit and wisdom. 
 We should try at all times to remember the adva ntages or benefits of generating the mind of 
enlightenment, whereby our delight in it will in crease. To increase the strength of the actual 
mind of enlightenment, we need to generate it  six times: three times a day and three times at 
night. In order to take care of all sentient beings, we must never abandon any sentient being and should continually train in the thought to ta ke care of them. If we abandon even a single 
being thinking “I cannot generate the mind of enlightenment toward this man or that 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
556 woman,” our mind of enlightenment degenerates.  Therefore, we should abandon thinking in 
this way.  
 We should also train in accumulating the collections of merit and wisdom. Merit is 
accumulated by making prayers, doing prostratio ns, making offerings, circumambulating holy 
objects, practicing generosity, keeping morality, avoiding anger and practicing patience, 
putting joyous effort into virtue, and developing a single-pointed mind. By means of these practices we accumulate the collection of merit.  The collection of wisdom, on the other hand, 
is accumulated by thinking about the emptiness of inherent or true existence, trying to 
understand it, and doing analytical meditation and single-pointed meditation on it. We need 
to accumulate these two collections.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1 Training in remembering the advantages, so that the strength of one’s delight 
in the mind generation increases 
A The way [the advantages] are explained in the scriptures 
B The way [the mind of enlightenment] is praised in the writings of the holy beings  
C The instruction to exert oneself as it is this very [mind] that the buddhas and their children have seen 
to be the exalted method  
 
The way the benefits are explained in the script ures is set out, followed by how they were 
praised by the holy beings such as Shantideva. We need to put effort into the holy method 
seen by the conquerors and their sons.  
 
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1A The way [the advantages] are explained in the scriptures 
1 The uncommon cause 
2 Showing the advantages 
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1A1 The uncommon cause 
{186} First: You consider the advantages of  the mind of enlightenment by studying 
the sutras or hearing them from the guru. Since they have been taught extensively 
in the Gandhavyuha [Sutra] , they should be studied there. As the quotation above 
from that says “It is the seed of all th e Dharma of the Buddha”  and all the conduct 
and aspiring prayers of the bodhisattvas are summarized in it, it is said to be like a 
brief presentation. Although the extensive explanation of the branches is 
boundless, since the brief presentation co ntains everything, the brief presentation, 
like a synthesis of them, teaches a synthesi s that summarizes the essential points of 
all the paths of bodhisattvas.  
 We need to read the scriptures and listen to teachings from the teacher on the benefits or 
advantages of the mind of enlightenment, an d then think about them . The benefits of the 
mind of enlightenment are ex tensively explained in the Array of Trunks Sutra  (Gandhavyuha ), 
therefore we should read it. Previously a citati on was quoted from this sutra that says “The 
mind of enlightenment is like the seed of all Dh arma of the Buddha.” In other words, it is the 
seed that is the cause of all the qualities of a bu ddha, just as a rice seed is the cause of a rice 
plant. This sutra also sets out the bodhisattva conduct, the six perfections and so forth, which 
have as their root the mind of  enlightenment, that is, which are motivated by the mind of 
enlightenment. It also includes prayers such as “May the mind of enlightenment that has not 
yet been generated, be generated and increase. May that which has been generated, increase 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
557 more and more.” This sutra contains an extens ive explanation of all the branches or parts, 
while this verse is a brief presentation of it. Howe ver, although it is condensed, it contains all 
the essential points of the bodhisattva path.  
 In short, bodhichitta is the root or seed of  all the buddhas’ qualities. Buddhas are said to 
be born from bodhisattvas, and bodhisattvas ar e said to be born from three minds: great 
compassion, the mind of non-duality (the re alization of emptiness), and the mind of 
enlightenment. These are the causes of a bodh isattva. Therefore, if  we wish to attain 
buddhahood, we must generate the mind of enli ghtenment in our continuum and train in it 
so that it becomes stronger and stronger, without ever allowing it to degenerate.  
 
(Oral transmission 12:04-12:26, pp. 186-194)  Thursday afternoon, 5 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1A2 Showing the advantages 
A The transition 
B The actual [advantages] 
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1A2A The transition 
As the advantages taught in [Asanga’s] Bodhisattva Levels  concern the aspiring mind 
[of enlightenment], from among them the two advantages of generating a stable 
mind generation are mentioned first. 
 
The Bodhisattva Levels  by Asanga says that with respect to  the aspiring mind generation, there 
are two advantages of having generated a stable mind generation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 186) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1A2B The actual [advantages] 
1 You become a holy field 
2 You thoroughly hold the merit of non-harm 
You become a holy field and thorou ghly hold the merit of non-harm. 
 
Having generated the aspiring mind of enlighte nment, we become a holy field and thoroughly 
hold the merit to not be harmed, that is, due to  the merit of the mind of enlightenment, we 
can no longer be harmed by other beings and things.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1A2B-1 You become a holy field 
A [One becomes an object of veneration] 
First: Like it says in the scriptures: “The world with its gods and human beings will 
prostrate [to you],” as soon as you generate this mind you become an object of veneration for all sentient beings. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
558 We become a holy field because when we generate the mind of enlightenment, we 
immediately become an object of veneration of  gods and human beings. This is mentioned by 
Shantideva in Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds . As soon as we generate the mind of 
enlightenment in our continuum, not only gods and human beings, but all sentient beings 
respect us as an object of veneration, offeri ngs, and prostration. Thus, due to our having 
generated the mind of enlightenment, when othe r sentient beings venerate us, make offerings 
to us, or prostrate to us, they create much meri t. Likewise, having generated the aspiring mind 
of enlightenment, other sentient beings are bene fited even by their making a small offering to 
us.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B [One becomes an exalted being] 
As it says [in the scriptures], “As soon as  this mind is generated, you outshine the 
foe-destroyer great beings by way of your lineage,” you become a guru or an exalted 
being.  
 
As soon as we generate the mind of enlightenment, we outshine the hearer and solitary realizer foe-destroyers by way of our lineage. This is because, having become a child of the 
buddhas, from the point of view of lineage we become superior to the hearer and solitary 
realizer foe-destroyers. We become Mahayanists, whereas they are Hinayanists. It is like when 
a son is born to a king, he is respected by all th e ministers due to his lineage even though he is 
just a small baby. In a similar way, having gene rated bodhichitta, we come to outshine all the 
foe-destroyers by way of our lineage.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C [One becomes a field of merit] 
You become a field of merit due to yieldi ng limitless results even through doing 
minor meritorious actions.  
 
When we generate the mind of enlightenment, we become a field of merit such that even 
making small offerings will bring a great result, fo r example, if we give even one mouthful of 
food to an animal it will bring a great result. Th erefore, we should try at all times to generate 
the aspiring mind of enlightenment that wishes to attain enlightenment for the welfare of all 
sentient beings. We should do this now, wh ile we have all the necessary conditions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D [One becomes a support for the whole world] 
As is said “Due to supporting the whole wo rld, you are like the earth”; you become 
like a father of the nine beings. 
 
The ground is the support of all that is anim ate and inanimate, for example, human beings 
and their houses. Without the support of the grou nd or earth, nothing could be built and we 
would abide in empty space. Bodhichitta is like the ground or earth. Just as all vegetation 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
559 grows from the earth, similarly all the good qu alities of the bodhisa ttvas and buddhas grow 
from bodhichitta. Therefore, bodhichitta is like ned to the earth. Bodhichitta is also like a 
father who gives rise to a child,  a mother being unable to do th is alone. “The nine beings” are 
the beings who take the nine types of rebirth:  dying from the desire realm and being reborn 
(1-3) in the desire realm, in the form realm, or in the formless realm; dying from the form 
realm and being reborn (4-6) in the form realm, in the formless realm, or in the desire realm; 
and dying from the formless realm and being rebo rn in (7-9) the formless realm, in the form 
realm, or in the desire realm.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1A2B-2 You thoroughly hold the merit of non-harm 
A The way in which one is guarded by the protectors 
Second: {187} Since you are always guarded by twice as many protectors as protect 
a wheel-turning king, yakshas and non-human primordial [local guardians ] cannot 
harm you even when you are asleep, intoxicated, or non-conscientious.  
 
Having generated the mind of enlightenment we  will always be guarded by twice as many 
protectors as a wheel-turning king, whereby ev en when we are asleep, intoxicated, or non-
conscientious we cannot be harmed by yakshas or  non-human guardians of the place. In other 
words, due to having generated the mind of enligh tenment, external beings will not be able to 
harm us. On the other hand, if we do not have bodhichitta, we can be harmed by yakshas, spirits, demons, and so forth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Mantras and the like will succeed more easily 
You pacify calamities, harm, and infectio us diseases with the words of secret 
mantra and knowledge mantra. If even thin gs that cannot be accomplished in the 
hands of sentient beings, will be accompli shed when they come into your hands, 
what need is there to mention that [other things ] will be accomplished? Due to 
this, the collection of activities, pacificati on and so forth, are shown to be easily 
accomplished in dependence on the mind generation, whereby, if that exists, the 
common attainments will also quickly be accomplished.  
 
Having generated the mind of enli ghtenment, if we recite the word s of either secret mantra or 
knowledge mantra, they are effective and can ea sily pacify epidemic illnesses, harm, and 
contagious diseases. The mantras of different de ities perform different functions; for example, 
the mantra of Manjushri slowly diminishes an d eliminates our ignorance whereby our mind 
becomes clearer and our wisdom increases; the mantra of Avalokiteshvara pacifies the mind 
whereby it becomes more compassionate; and th e mantra of Tara eliminates many types of 
harm. Some mantras also pacify particular illnesses.   Even though someone cannot accomplish so mething with their hands, due to having 
generated the mind of enlightenment it can be accomplished, therefore what need is there to 
mention that other things will be accomplished ? Due to the mind gene ration, the collection 
of activities, such as pacification and so forth,  will also be easily ac hieved. Also, due to the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
560 mind generation, we will easily accomplish wh atever common attainments or siddhis we want. 
In other words, when the mind of enlightenment is present, we  will quickly accomplish the 
eight types of common attainments.  The collection of activities ( las tshogs ) refers to the 
activities of pacification; the activities of incr easing for increasing our wealth, lifespan, and so 
forth; the activities of control or power fo r successfully controlling other beings; and the 
activities of wrath, which are to be used when  the previous three fail. Having generated the 
mind of enlightenment, we can achieve all th e common attainments as we wish. We can also 
perform different rituals in order to achieve every success while banging drums, clashing 
cymbals, and so on. REALLY!!! 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Famines and the like will not occur  
Fear, famine, and harm from non-human bein gs will not occur in those places in 
which it has not occurred, and after you di e and transfer you will have little harm, 
will be without illness, and so forth. 
 
There are many benefits of ge nerating the mind of enlighte nment including the fact that 
famines and so forth will not occur. In other word s, in the place in which we live there will be 
a lack of fear. There will also not be famine due to a lack of food, like often happens in some 
of the Third World countries wher e everyday children die of star vation. There will also not be 
harm by non-humans. Also having died, in our ne xt life we will encounter little harm, be free 
from sickness, and so forth. Since there will even be a good result after death, when the 
consciousness has left this body and gone to th e next life, we should strive to develop the 
mind of enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D One is without anger and is patient  
Due to being endowed with patience and a gentle disposition, you will bear harm 
that is done to you and will not return the harm and so forth. 
 
Due to having generated the mind of enlighte nment, we will have less anger and be more 
patient. Due to being endowed with patience, wh en someone harms us we will be able to bear 
it and we will not harm the other person in return , that is, we will not retaliate. Thus, due to 
the mind of enlightenment, we will be more patient, and when someone harms us, due to 
being patient and able to bear it, we  will not think to return the harm.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
E It will be difficult for one to be reborn in the bad migrations and one will quickly be freed  
A l s o  i t  w i l l  b e  d i f f i c u l t  f o r  y o u  t o  b e  r e b o r n  i n  t h e  b a d  m i g r a t i o n s  a n d ,  i f  i t  
happens, you will quickly be freed. While there you will have little suffering and thereby, through taking that as a condition, you will become extremely 
disenchanted with cyclic existence and wi ll also generate compassion for those 
sentient beings. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
561  
Having generated the mind of enlightenment, it wi ll be difficult for us to be reborn in the bad 
migrations, and even if we are born there we will quickly be freed from them. Also if, due to 
our karma, we have to take rebi rth in the lower realms, we will experience very little suffering 
there. In addition, we will develop the mind of definite emergence that wants to quickly 
emerge from cyclic existence, and we will also  generate compassion toward the other sentient 
beings there. Thus, having taken rebirth in the he ll realm, for example, we will not experience 
much suffering and, seeing the many sentient beings tormented by suffering there, we will 
generate strong compassion for them. Ther e is a four-line verse about this in the Sublime 
Continuum  but I can’t remember it now. My head is like an old computer that doesn’t 
function anymore!  
 Having generated bodhichitta, even if due to ka rma that we created in the past we need to 
take rebirth in the lower realms, especially the hell realm, we will stay there only for a short 
time, the suffering we experience will be relatively  little, and during this time we will generate 
compassion for the other suffering hell beings an d will ripen their minds. However, it is also 
possible, without taking rebirth in hell, for this  karma to ripen in intense physical suffering 
like that of hell, for example, Angulimala. It is recounted that Anguli mala was a follower of a 
Hindu teacher whose wife fell in love with Angulimala. When Anguli mala refused to make 
love to her, she scratched her face with her sharp nails and pulled out her hair in anger. 
When her husband asked her what had happened, she told him that Angulimala had tried to 
rape her. The teacher told Angulimala that in order to purify this negative action he would 
have to kill 1000 human beings and, to prove this, cut a finger from each one of them and string it in a garland around his neck. When he  had done this, the teacher promised that he 
would once again give him teachings. Angulima la followed these instructions until he had 
successfully killed 999 people. However, he had difficulty finding the last one as everyone would flee when they saw him appr oaching. He began to think th at he would have to kill his 
mother who would bring food to him each day.  As he thought this, a man appeared, walking 
along the road before him. He ran behind th e man, calling out to him to wait. The man 
turned and said, “I am always waiting. It is  you who are always running.” When Angulimala 
arrived in front of the man, he showed hims elf to be Buddha Shakyamuni. The Buddha gave 
teachings to Angulimala, whereby the thought wi shing to kill his mother was eliminated. The 
Buddha took him to his monastery and gave him a room. Buddha Shakyamuni told a monk 
to go check up on him and to put an iron rod through a hole in the wall of the room. When this was done, the iron rod melted! The monk told Buddha Shakyamuni what had happened. 
Although in that very same life Angulimala  became a monk, subdued his mind, and gained 
higher realizations, whenever he went begging for alms everyone would run away out of fear 
o f  h i m .  I n  t h i s  w a y ,  i n  t h e  s a m e  l i f e  i n  which the heavy karma wa s created, Angulimala 
experienced the suffering of the hot hell, whereb y he purified his negativities and later on 
attained the state of a foe-destroyer.   Thus, there are many benefits of  the mind of enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
562 F  How the advantages are immeasurable 
If the merit of the mind of enlightenmen t were to become form, it could not be 
contained in space itself. Making offerings of material things to the buddhas does 
not compare to it even partially. In [the Sutra] Requested by Viradatta  it says:  
 
If bodhichitta’s merit  
Had physical form, All the realm of space would be filled, 
And still more would remain. 
 Even if one completely filled buddha fields,  Numerous as the sand grains  Of the Ganges River {188} with jewels, And offered them to the world’s Protector, 
 
If someone, palms joined, 
Were to bow to bodhichitta. It would exceed that offering. As for that, it is without limit. 
 If the merit of generating the mi nd of enlightenment were to take form, it could not fit in all 
of space. Even if we were to fill buddha fields, as many as the grains of sand of the Ganges River, with jewels and offer them to the Prot ector of the World, although there would be 
much merit it would be exceeded by the meri t of someone who puts his hands together and 
generates the mind of enlightenm ent. This cannot be measured an d the merit is limitless. In 
short, if we were to offer jewels filling all of  space to all the buddhas of the ten directions, 
much merit would be created but still the meri t of generating the mind of enlightenment, 
even for a short time, would be much greater. Ther efore, we must really try to wish to achieve 
enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient bein gs. We must try to do this all the time, in 
every moment. We must try to move our mind in  the direction of the good heart that wishes 
to take care of all sentient beings, and to  generate love, compassion, and the mind of 
enlightenment toward them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1B  The way [the mind of enlightenment] is praised in the teachings of the holy beings 
When the great Jowo [Atisha] circumambu lated the Vajra Seat and thought about 
what was to be done to attain complete enlightenment quickly, the smaller statues 
stood up and asked the bigger ones: “Wha t should someone who wants to quickly 
become a buddha train in?” whereupon the latter replied: “They should train in 
the mind of enlightenment.” When, in the sky above the Gandhola statue, a 
young woman questioned an older woman,  she received a reply similar to the 
previous. It is said that his mind became very certain with respect to the mind of 
enlightenment due to hearing this. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
563 When Atisha was circumambulating the temple or stupa in Bodhgaya, thinking about how to 
attain complete enlightenment quickly, the small statues stood up and asked the bigger 
statues “What should someone who wants to quic kly become a buddha train in?” The bigger 
statues replied, “They should train in the mind  of enlightenment.” Also, in the space above 
the Gandhola, perhaps the stupa itself, Atisha heard a young woman ask an older woman the 
same question, “What should someone who wants to quickly become a buddha train in?” The 
older woman also replied that it was best fo r such a person to train in the mind of 
enlightenment. When Atisha heard this, he beca me very certain with re spect to the mind of 
enlightenment and afterward he only thou ght about how to generate the mind of 
enlightenment, that is, how to achieve buddhahood  for the welfare of all sentient beings. In 
other words, this became the main practice of his life.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-1C  The instruction to exert oneself as it is this very [mind] that the buddhas and their 
children have seen to be the supreme method 
In that way, you should understand that the mind of enlightenment is: 
1. the condensed essential point of all the Mahayana instructions,  
2. the great treasure of all spiritual attainments,  
3. the special feature distinguishing the Great Vehicle from the Lesser Vehicle,  
4. the exalted basis that exhorts one to the vast conduct of the conquerors’ 
children.  
Through increasing the strength of your de light in meditating on it, you should do 
it like someone thirsty hearing talk of water because, having made it more and 
more subtle by means of their marvelous exalted knowledge over many eons, when 
they analyzed the paths, the buddhas and their children saw it to be the supreme 
method for attaining buddhahood. Thus, it also says in Engaging in Bodhisattva 
Behavior :  
 
The able and powerful [buddhas],  having reflected thoroughly 
For many eons, saw that this above all is beneficial. 
 
The best method for attaining enlightenment is the generation of the mi nd of enlightenment. 
The mind of enlightenment is:  1. the condensed essential point of all the Mahayana instructions,  
2. the treasure of all spiritual attainments,  
3. the special feature that distinguishes the Mahayana from the Hinayana,  
4. the exalted basis that exhorts one to the vast conduct of the conquerors’ sons.  
Understanding this, we should develop more and more delight in meditating on the mind of 
enlightenment, like a very thirsty person who merely upon hearing that there is water in a 
particular place delights in the po ssibility of quenching his thirst.  
 Having thought about the mind generation for many eons and after investigating many 
paths, the conquerors and their sons came to  the conclusion that the supreme method for 
achieving enlightenment is the generation of the mind of enlightenment. Likewise, 
Shantideva’s Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  also says that all the powerful buddhas or 
Munis, having reflected for many eons on what is  of most benefit to se ntient beings, saw that 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
564 bodhichitta was of the greatest be nefit. Therefore, we too need to strive at all times, day and 
night, not to forget bodhichitta. Even though it may be difficult, again and again we should 
generate the thought to achieve enlightenment in or der to be able to take care of all sentient 
beings. To do this, we need to strive to develo p its causes, love and compassion for all sentient 
beings, by wishing them happiness and its caus es and wishing them freedom from suffering 
and its causes, and taking upon ourselves the re sponsibility to give them happiness and its 
causes and to free them from suffering and its ca uses. We need to try to develop this attitude 
in our mind. Although it will not arise immediately, by familiarizing with it our mind will 
gradually change.   Friday morning, 6 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 188) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-2 Training in generating it six times so that the actual mind generation increases 
A Training in not giving up the aspiring mind generation 
B Training in increasing the aspiring mind generation 
The second has two [points]: training in not {189} giving up and increasing the 
aspiring mind generation. 
 
Having generated the aspiring mi nd of enlightenment, we should train in not give it up and 
in increasing it by generating it six times – th ree times a day and three times a night. In order 
to increase it, we need to engage strongly in  the bodhisattva conduct – the six perfections and 
the four means of gathering disciples.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-2A Training in not giving up the aspiring mind generation 
1 Giving it up is heavier than a defeat of the [vow s] of individual liberation and [as a consequence ] one 
must wander in the bad migrations for a long time 
First: In the  Abbreviated [Perfection of Wisdom Sutra ] it was taught:  
 
If you, having taken the buddhas, bodhisattvas, and your spiritual 
friend as your witnesses, in their presence you make the promise to liberate the sentient beings that are not liberated and so forth, and then 
due to the condition of having become discouraged upon seeing  1. the enormous number of sentient beings and their bad actions,  
2. the long time for which it is nece ssary to exert yourself over many 
eons,  
3. that it is necessary to train in  the limitless two collections and 
difficult practices,  
you lay down the burden of the mind  generation, it will be a greater 
negativity than a defeat of individual liberation. 
 
In the Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds it says that if you give up the promised 
mind, you will have to wander in th e bad migrations for a long time,. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
565 A fully-ordained monk commits a defeat of his v o w s  o f  i n d i v i d u a l  l i b e r a t i o n  b y  k i l l i n g  a  
human being, stealing, engaging in sexual intercours e, or telling a great lie, such as saying that 
he has achieved clairvoyance although he has not. However, if a bodhisattva who has made 
the promise of the aspiring mind of enlightenment gives it up, he will be reborn in the bad 
migrations and remain there for a long time. In  other words, having ma de the promise in the 
presence of the buddhas, bodhisattvas, and our sp iritual friend to liberate all the sentient 
beings who have not been liberated, to free those who are not free, and so forth, if later on we 
were to come to think “Oh, there are an enormo us number of sentient beings and by nature 
they are bad,” or “I would have to put effort for many eons into accumulating the collection 
of merit and the collection of wisdom. This is  t o o  d i f f i c u l t ,  I  a m  n o t  a b l e  t o  d o  i t , ”  a n d  
become depressed and give up our promise, the negativity is much greater than breaking a 
root vow of individual liberation. This is mentioned in the Perfection of Wisdom Sutras . Thus, if 
we give up our mind generation in this way, we will have to wander in the lower realms for a 
long time, suffering and experiencing many prob lems. This is also mentioned by Shantideva 
in his Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds .  
 In short, having made the promise of the as piring mind of enlightenment in front of the 
buddhas, bodhisattvas, and other holy beings, if l a t e r  o n  w e  g i v e  i t  u p ,  w e  c r e a t e  a  h e a v y  
negativity and therefore we must try not to do  this. We should try to keep our promise and 
never give it up.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Not giving it up due to delight like that of finding a jewel in a rubbish heap  
Also that same [text] says: 
 
Like a blind person finding  
A jewel in a heap of rubbish, Similar to that, bodhichitta Has been generated in me. 
 Think “What I have found is just as amazin g” and “I will not give it up under any 
circumstances.” Observing this, reinforce the decision to not give it up even for an 
instant. 
 If a blind person were to find a jewel, such as a diamond, in a huge heap  of rubbish, he would 
be very happy, shine it, and take care of it. Si nce we too have presently found this one-time 
opportunity to generate the mind of enlightenmen t, we must generate it, take care of it, and 
increase it. Thinking “It is amazing to have foun d this,” we should dete rmine never to give it 
up but to always guard the vow of the aspiring  mind generation. If, on the other hand, we 
w e r e  t o  g i v e  i t  u p ,  t h i s  w o u l d  b e  v e r y  b a d .  T h e r e f o r e ,  n o w  t h a t  w e  h a v e  t h i s  s p e c i a l  opportunity to generate the mind of enlightenmen t, we should resolve not to give up even for 
an instant. Think “I will not give up the mind of enlightenment even for a single instant. I 
will keep my promise from the bottom of my heart.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

566 2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-2B Training in increasing the aspiring mind generation 
1 It is not enough to merely not give it up, it must be reinforced 
Second: Since it is not enough to merely not give it up, you should reinforce it 
three times a day and three times at night with great effort. 
 
It is not enough merely to not give up the mind  of enlightenment, we should also increase it. 
To do this, we should generate it with effort  three times a day and three times a night. In 
addition, we should practice the six perfections, that is, we should try to increase our practice 
of the bodhisattva conduct day and night by constantly remembering the mind of 
enlightenment – the mind wishing to achieve en lightenment for the welf are of all sentient 
beings. To do this, we should think about the fa ct that all sentient beings need to attain 
enlightenment and that we must help them by leading them to enlightenment. Since this is 
the only solution, we need to think about it  day and night. In ot her words, we should 
remember bodhichitta when we wake up, we should remember it at midday, we should 
remember it in the afternoon, we should rememb er it at night, we should remember it at 
midnight, and we should remember it at dawn. If we cannot do this, we should at the very 
least generate this mind three times in the mo rning and three times in the evening like, for 
example, the Six Session Guru Yoga . This is sufficient, if we cannot do it continually day and 
night due to being busy. However, we shou ld generate it as much as possible.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 How the ritual is performed at the six times 
If the extensive ritual explained above ca n be accomplished, do it according to 
that. If it cannot be accomplished, havi ng visualized the merit field and made 
offerings, you should train in love and compassion. Do this six times [a day]. The 
ritual for that is to say the following three times [in the day ] and three times [at 
night]: 
 
I go for refuge until I am enlightened 
To the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Supreme Assembly. {190} By my practice of generosity and the other [perfections], May I attain buddhahood in order to benefit migrating beings. 
 How should we do the ritual six times? If possib le, we should do the extensive ritual explained 
above. However, if we cannot do this, we should  visualize the merit field, make offerings of 
flowers, light, perfume, water and so forth, as  well as a mandala offering, generate love and 
compassion, and recite the above verse six times. This verse is the short ritual for generating 
the mind of enlightenment. First we go for re fuge to the Three Jewels, the Buddha, Dharma, 
and Sangha, thinking that we will go for refuge to them from now until we attain 
enlightenment. Then we can think about what ever bodhisattva conduct we may have done, 
such as practicing the three types of generosi ty of giving material things, giving Dharma 
teachings, and giving protection to those whose life is in danger or those who are sick. These 
three types of generosity are mentioned in relati on to sutra, whereas in tantra there is the 
additional generosity of giving love. With respec t to the practice of morality, we should guard 
our vows and pledges and strive to benefit othe r sentient beings by setting them in the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
567 practice of morality or ethical discipline, as well  as strive to abandon that which is discordant 
with the other perfections such as hatred, lazi ness, distraction, and distorted wisdom and to 
strive to safeguard them. Morality is the mind  wishing to abandon or the mind wishing to 
safeguard.  
 W e  s h o u l d  a l s o  s t r i v e  t o  d e v e l o p  p a t i e n c e  b y  k e e p i n g  o u r  m i n d  u n d i s t u r b e d ,  b y  n o t  
retaliating when others say something bad to us, and by remaining still like a statue. When we 
experience difficulties, we should try to bear th em with great courage. In addition, we need to 
abandon laziness, which often arises, and instead take delight in the practice of the Dharma 
or virtue. This is the practice of joyous e ffort. When something is difficult and we feel 
discouraged, we should not give up but instea d put effort into continuing it, whereby we will 
eventually achieve success in it.  Because we have many distractions and our mind wanders continually, going here, there, 
and everywhere, we need to eliminate these ob stacles and to develop the mind that abides 
single-pointedly on its object. In other words, we need to develop more and more 
concentration. This will make our mind more stable and we will be able to gain many 
realizations. We also need to develop the wi sdom understanding the real nature of all 
phenomena, that is, how they exist and how they  function. We should do this by means of 
mainly doing analytical meditation but also sometimes doing stabilizing meditation. By means 
of analytical meditation we should examin e the nature of all phenomena and how they 
function.   In this way, we should practice these si x perfections as well as the additional four 
perfections of method, prayer, strength, and ex alted wisdom, for a total of ten perfections. 
When there are six perfections, the last four are included in the perfection of wisdom.  
 We should strive to practice these perfecti ons ourselves and also encourage others to do 
them. In short, we should recite the above formula three times in the morning and three 
times at night.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-3 Training in not mentally abandoning any sentient being for whose welfare the 
mind has been generated 
Third: The measure of mentally abandoning  a sentient being is to generate the 
thought: “I will never enact his welfare”  i n  d e p e n d e n c e  o n  t h e  c o n d i t i o n  o f  
unreasonable behavior and so forth. 
 
Having generated the aspiring mind generation  for the welfare of all sentient beings, we 
should not mentally give up even one single sentient being. The measure of mentally abandoning a sentient being is that, based on th e condition of the sentient being having done 
a negative action, to think “Since he or she is bad, I will not take care of him. I will not enact 
his welfare.” Therefore, to do this , it is not necessary to abandon all sentient beings. It is 
enough that when someone near us acts badly and we become discouraged, to think “I will 
not help him.” Thus, just by mentally abando ning a sentient being from our heart, we 
abandon the aspiring mind of enlightenment.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2A-4 Training in accumulating the two collections 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
568 Fourth: Having assumed the aspiring mind by a ritual, you should strive to 
accumulate the collections through making da ily offerings to the Jewels, etc., as the 
cause for increasing the mind of enlightenment. 
 
Having taken the aspiring mind of enlightenment by means of ritual, we need to train in the 
collection of merit and the collection of wisdom . To do this, as a cause of increasing our 
mind of enlightenment, we should make offering s, such as the usual seven or five types of 
offerings, every day to the Three Jewels. We shou ld try to offer them whatever we can. We can 
also mentally offer them a mandala. In addition , we should purify our negativities and mental 
afflictions and accumulate merit by means of reciting mantras, doing prostrations, 
circumambulating stupas, medita ting, praying, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B Training in the cause of not separating from [the aspiring mind ] in other lives. 
1 Training in abandoning the four black dharmas that cause it to degenerate 
2 Training in adopting the four white dharmas that do not allow it to degenerate 
The second has two [points]: training in abandoning the four black dharmas that 
cause it to degenerate and training in ad opting the four white dharmas that do not 
allow it to degenerate. 
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-1 Training in abandoning the four black dharmas that cause it to degenerate 
A [Deceiving one’s abbot, master, guru, or those worthy of offerings ] 
B [Causing others to regret the virtuous deeds they do not regret] 
C [Saying something infamous [lit. indescribable] about a person who has entered the Great Vehicle] 
D [Actions committed with the intention to deceive or dissimulate, but without the extraordinary attitude] 
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-1A [Deceiving one’s abbot, master, guru, or those worthy of offerings ] 
1 Object and action 
First: Among four black dharmas, with re spect to that of deceiving one’s abbot, 
master, guru, or those worthy of offering s, the objects that are one’s abbot and 
master are easy to understand. The guru is someone who wishes to benefit you, 
and those worthy of offerings are those en dowed with good qualities, even though 
they do not belong to the previous two.  If you wonder about what deed becomes a black dharma with respect to them, to 
knowingly cheat them by any means is a black dharma. 
 In order to not be separated from the mind of enlightenment in our future lives, we need to 
train in abandoning the four black dharmas an d adopting the four white dharmas. The first 
of the four black dharmas is to deceive or ch eat our abbot, master, guru, or those who are 
worthy of offerings. Having generated the mind of enlightenment, such behavior is very bad 
and will become the cause of taking rebirth in the lower realms.   Our abbot is someone who has given us vows, and our master is someone who has given us 
teachings. A guru or lama is someone who wish es to benefit us. Someone who is worthy of 
offerings is someone who is not our abbot or master, but is an object of offering possessing good qualities. If we knowingly try to cheat our abbot, master, guru, or someone possessing 
good qualities by any method, we commit this black dharma.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
569 2 Clearing up doubts 
Nonetheless, as the deceit and dissimulation  that are not lies are indicated below, 
here it has to be cheating thro ugh lying. This is because in [Asanga’s] Compendium 
of Manifest Dharma  it is taught that abandoning a black dharma constitutes a white 
dharma, and that the antidote of this [bla ck dharma] is the first of the four white 
dharmas. 
 
To tell our abbot or master that we need to go somewhere to do something important, such 
as study, whereas in reality we have nothing important to do and instead go to the cinema, or 
play footfall or tennis, it to cheat him by means of  a lie. Likewise, to tell our father or mother 
that we need to go somewhere to do someth ing important, whereupon they are happy and 
praise us, but then we actually go to hang arou nd a street corner and smoke with our friends, 
or go out drinking or playing cards with friends, is to deceive our parents by means of a lie. If we have generated the aspiring mind of enligh tenment, this becomes a black dharma that can 
cause it to degenerate.  
 Deceit and dissimulation will be explained la ter on. For example, it is said that when 
Brahma was asked a question but did not know the answer, rather than saying he did not 
know it, he said “I am Brahma. I am Great Br ahma,” in order to deceive his questioner. The 
mental factors of deceit and dissimulation act to cheat others.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-1B [Causing others to regret virtues that they do not regret] 
1 Object and action 
With respect to causing others to regret [t he virtues] they do not regret, the object 
is another person who does not regret a virtue that he has done. What one does 
toward him is {191} that, with the inte ntion to make him regret, one makes him 
generate regret for that which is not a source of regret. 
 
This black dharma is to make another person regret virtuous actions that he has done. The object is therefore a person who does not regret  a virtuous action that  he has done. We can 
make him regret it by telling hi m that it was not right to do the virtuous action, for example, 
to give something to someone, by saying that  t h e  p e r s o n  i s  b a d  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  T h u s ,  w e  
intentionally wish to make a person, who is with out regret, to regret his virtuous action. This 
is another black dharma. Another example is to  say to someone who has just taken the lay 
vows, novice vows, or vows of fu ll ordination that he has made a very bad mistake in taking 
ordination because he needs to take care of his family. Thus, althou gh the person who has 
taken the vows does not regret his action, we ma ke him generate regret for it by telling him 
that it is a bad action. While it is good to regr et non-virtuous actions such as stealing others’ 
possessions and to try to abandon them in the fu ture, it is not good to make others regret 
their virtuous actions such as entering into retr eat by saying that they may become sick and so 
forth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
570 2 Clearing up doubts 
As regards these two, it is the same whether [the person ] is deceived or not 
deceived and whether [the person ] generated or did not generate regret.  
 
With respect to these two black dharmas, it is the same whether or not we were actually able 
to deceive the other person and whether or not we were actually able to make the person 
regret his actions. In both cases, they are st ill a black dharma. In other words, it is not 
necessary that the other person be deceived or that the other person generate regret, in order 
to commit a black dharma it is enough  that we said tried to do this.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-1C [Saying that which is not praise and so forth to a sentient being who has entered the 
Great Vehicle] 
1 Object and action 
With respect to saying that which is not praise and so forth to a sentient being 
who has entered the Great Vehicle, the ob ject is someone who has generated the 
mind of enlightenment and now possesses it . What one does toward him is to say 
something unpleasant motivated by hatred. 
 
This black dharma is to say something unpl easant about a person who has entered the 
Mahayana. Thus, the object is a bodhisattv a – someone who has generated the mind of 
enlightenment and possesses it. The action is to say something unpleasant motivated by 
hatred.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The measure 
The object to whom it was sa id understands the meaning. 
 
The measure of committing this black dharma is that the obje ct to whom the unpleasant 
words are spoken understands the meaning. On  the other hand, if someone says something 
bad but it is not understood, for example, if  I were to say something bad in Tibetan to 
someone who does not understand Tibetan, it  would not be this black dharma. In this 
particular case, it is necessary that th e other person understand the meaning.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 How it is necessary to abandon this since it easily occurs and is a great fault 
That it is easy for this to occur and it is  also a very great disadvantage has already 
been mentioned previously in brief. Furthermore, it is taught in the Sutra of the 
Miracle of Complete Certainty of Perfect Peace  that if a bodhisattva generates a mind 
of contempt toward a bodhisattva he must remain in hell for that many eons and 
that apart from deprecating a bodhisattva nothing can make a bodhisattva fall into 
the bad migrations. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
571 It is easy for us to say something unpleasa nt motivated by hatred and it is a great 
disadvantage, therefore we need to abandon it. In other words, it is easy to get angry even at a 
bodhisattva, but it is a great fault. This was already explained briefly. In short, for however 
long a bodhisattva generates a mind of deprec ation toward another bodhisattva, one hour, 
half an hour, one minute, and so forth, he wi ll have to remain in hell for a corresponding 
number of eons. Thus, if a bodhisattva disparag es another bodhisattva, it will cause him to 
fall into hell in his next life. There is no other negative action done by a bodhisattva toward a 
bodhisattva will throw him into the he ll realm. This is taught in the Sutra of the Miracle of 
Complete Certainty of Perfect Peace .  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 How this takes you further away from enlightenment 
In the Abbreviated [Perfection of Wisdom Sutra]  it also says that a bodhisattva, who 
has not yet obtained a prediction, who argu es out of anger with a bodhisattva who 
has obtained a prediction, for however long  he generated a mind of anger he will 
have to spend that many eons longer on the path. 
 
If a bodhisattva who has not obtained a pred iction argues with a bodhisattva who has 
obtained a prediction out of anger, he will ha ve to remain on the path for a much longer 
time, continuing to train in the collection s, whereby he will become distant from 
enlightenment. In other words, for however many  instants he generates anger, for that many 
more eons will he have to rema in on the path. Although he do es not fall to a lower path, for 
example, from the path of meditation to the path  of accumulation, he will have to remain on 
that path for a much longer time and will not increase in realizations. Therefore, we should 
also be careful not to generate anger   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 The need to exert oneself in confession and restraint  
Because of that, in the same [t ext] it is also taught that it is necessary to put an end 
to anger i all its aspects and to put effort  into confessing it restraining from it 
immediately whenever it arises. 
 
Therefore, we too should not generate anger.  However, if we do, we should immediately 
confess it by regretting it, determining not to do it anymore, going for refuge to the Buddha, 
Dharma, and Sangha and generating the mind of enlightenment, and reciting the hundred-
syllable mantra of Vajrasattva or other mantras, the names of the thirty-five buddhas, or sutras 
such as the Diamond Cutter Sutra . In short, whenever we create  negative actions we should 
immediately strive to purify them with the four opponent powers.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-1D [An activity done out of deceit and dissimulation but without the extraordinary attitude] 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 2-6 May 2005 (12) 
572 1  Object and action 
With respect to an activity done out of  deceit and dissimulation but without the 
extraordinary attitude, the object is any other sentient being. What one does 
toward him is to engage in deceit and dissimulation. 
 
This is the point where deceit and dissimulati on are now explained. This black dharma is, 
without the extraordinary attitude (a good heart) , to engage in an activity out of deceit or 
dissimulation. The object is another sentient be ing. The action is to engage in deceit or 
dissimulation.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Explanation of the meaning of the word 
{192} Regarding that, deceit and dissimulation are explained in terms of deceiving 
with respect to measures and weights and th e like of what Gyelwa Yechung did: in 
order to send someone off to Ragma, he ca used him to be sent to Tolung first so 
afterwards he would accept Ragma. According to the Compendium of All Trainings  
deceit is to display good qualities that actually do not exist, and dissimulation is to 
take measures for faults not to become evident.  
 
In the past the measure of six handfuls, which filled a pan of a particular size, was used to 
measure quantities of rice, barley, and so forth.  Also a weighing scale with two pans was used. 
However, nowadays we use kilograms and meas ure the weight of things with a digital 
weighing scale! Measures and weights can be used  to deceive or cheat others. For example, in 
India there is a drinking glass used to serve tea that looks large but inside the bottom is raised 
so that it actually does not hold much tea. This would be an example of cheating others with 
wrong measures.  
 Deceit ( sgyu) is to show good qualities that we do not have. Dissimulation ( gyo) is to hide 
our faults by some method in order that they cannot be seen. Deceit, for example, can be to 
say that we know many things and can do many  things whereas in reality we do not know 
them or cannot do them. Dissimulation, on the other hand, is to hide our faults People 
engage in deceit and dissimulation in order to accomplish their own welf are. Although it may 
not directly entail a lie, it is very similar to a lie. 
 
END

Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
573 Monday afternoon, 9 May 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 192) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-2 Adopting the four white dharmas that do not allow it to degenerate 
A [Abandoning lying knowingly] 
B [Abiding with the extraordinary attitude without deceit or dissimulation] 
C [Generating the discrimination that bodhisat tvas are like the Teacher and praising them] 
D [Ripening sentient beings] 
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-2A [Abandoning lying knowingly] 
Second: The object of the first white dharma is all sentient beings. The action is to 
abandon lying to them knowingly even for th e sake of one’s life or for as little a 
joke. If you act in this way, you will be sure not to cheat special objects such as 
your abbot, master, etc. through lies. 
 
The object of the first of the four white dhar ma is all sentient beings. The action is to 
abandon telling a lie knowingly even at the cost of  our life and even to make others laugh. If 
we do this, we will not cheat our abbot,  teacher, and other special objects.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-2B [Abiding with the extraordinary attitude without deceit or dissimulation] 
The object of the second white dharma is al l sentient beings. The action is to abide 
with the extraordinary attitude towards th em without deceit or dissimulation, that 
is, to abide with an honest attitude. It is  the antidote to the fourth black dharma. 
 
The object is all sentient beings. The action is to not deceive or dissimulate while abiding with 
the extraordinary attitude. It is the an tidote to the fourth black dharma.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-2C [Generating the discrimination that bodhisattvas are like the Teacher and praising them] 
1 Object and action 
The object of the third white dharma is al l bodhisattvas. The action is to generate 
the discrimination of them as if they we re the Teacher and to proclaim truthful 
praise about them in the four directions.  
 
The object of the third white dharma is all bodhisattvas. The action is to generate the 
discrimination of all bodhisattvas as being like our Teacher, Buddha Shakyamuni, and to 
praise them in the four directions, east, south, west, and north. In short, we should proclaim 
their good qualities and their abandonments.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 How there is a need to guard against this 
Sharawa says:  
 
Although we are constantly busy scraping together something like 
virtue, there is no sign of its increa se and many signs of its exhaustion. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
574 Virtue is exhausted by hatred toward bodhisattvas and our companions, 
disdain towards them, and through insu lting them. Therefore, if we are 
able to abandon insulting them and bodhisattvas, we will no longer 
experience what is referred to in the Compendium of All Trainings  as 
“harm dependent on persons.” Si nce we do not know who is a 
bodhisattva, through generating th e discrimination of all sentient 
beings as the Teacher, as in the Sutra Requested by Kashyapa , we act from 
the point of view of training in pure appearance. 
 
Sharawa said that although we try to create virtue, there are no signs that our virtue has 
increased and many signs that it is exhausted.  Through generating anger or hatred toward 
bodhisattvas and our companions and by disp araging and insulting them, we exhaust our 
virtue. Therefore, we need to abandon these ac tions. If we are able to do this, as the 
Compendium of Trainings  says, we will not longer experience  harm from other persons. Since 
we do not know who is a bodhisattva, when we encounter sentient beings we should practice 
pure appearance and see them as if they were th e Teacher himself. This is the best way to act 
given that we do not know who is a bodhisattva and who is not.  Some people, although they 
are bodhisattvas, appear outwardly to be very humble and poor, whereas other people who are 
not bodhisattvas appear to be wealthy and pr oud. Because a bodhisattva has the mind of 
enlightenment, his mind is tamed and he does no t show off saying “I am a bodhisattva! I have 
realizations.” In short, we should see sentient beings as if they were the Teacher and train our 
mind in pure appearance.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The way to express the qualities 
{193} If there is an opportunity to expre ss their good qualities and an audience, do 
so. However, if you do not proclaim them ha ving gone to all four directions, this is 
not a fault. 
This [white dharma] is the anti dote to the third black dharma. 
 
When we are teaching we should praise the good  qualities of bodhisattvas. However, if we do 
not express these qualities in all four directions, it is not a fault. When someone asks us to do 
so, we should strive to express all the good qual ities of bodhisattvas such as love, the mind of 
enlightenment, their knowledge of emptiness, and so forth. This is the antidote to the third 
black dharma.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-2D [Ripening sentient beings] 
1 The actual [fourth white dharma]: object and action  
2 The statement in other scriptures as well 
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-2D1 The actual [fourth white dharma]: object and action 
The object of the fourth white dharma is  the sentient beings who are ripened by 
oneself. The action is to make them hold to complete enlightenment without 
wishing for a temporary vehicle. Moreover, while from your side you should cause 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
575 the disciple to make a connection with it , if that thought does not arise in the 
disciple, it does not become a mistake because you were unable to accomplish that. 
 By means of this [white dharma] you ab andon the second black dharma, for, if 
you wish to place others in the final of all happinesses, you do not practice it for 
the purpose of connecting them to the aris al of mental unhappiness for the sake of 
generating the mere regret that is mental unhappiness. 
 
The object of the fourth white dharma is sent ient beings who are ripened by oneself. The 
action is to make them engage in the great enlightenment and to not engage in a temporary 
vehicle. In other words, the action is to ma ke sentient beings wish to attain complete 
buddhahood without generating  the wish to achieve the nirvana of the Hinayana 
enlightenment that is only a personal freedom  from suffering and mental afflictions.  
 Although from our side we need to put effo rt into connecting the disciple to complete 
enlightenment, if the disciple does not generate  this mind, it does not become a fault for us 
because we were unable to acco mplish that. This is because se ntient beings from their own 
side need to put effort into this. Thus, if we pu t effort into helping them generate the mind of 
enlightenment but they do not want to do this, we do not create a fault.  
 This white dharma is the anti dote to the second black dharma  because if we wish to set 
others in the final happiness we do not this fo r the purpose of making others generate the 
regret that is mental unhappiness.  
 In brief, from our side there is a good hear t wishing others to attain enlightenment. Then, 
for others’ sake we try to make them generate  the mind of enlightenm ent and to attain the 
final enlightenment of buddhahood. However, if the other person does not listen to us and 
does not do what we say, there is nothing more  we can do. It is not that we tried to make 
others regret their virtuous actions, that is, we  did not do this for the purpose of making them 
generate the regret that is mental unhappiness.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C2B-2D2 The explanation in three scriptures, the  Compendium of All Trainings  and so forth  
A  The advantage of setting others in the mind generation  
In the [Sutra] Requested by Simha : 
 
“By means of what is the mind of enlightenment 
Not to be given up in all lives, Even while dreaming, much less while not sleeping?” He spoke: “In villages or in cities, in whatever places you abide, Guide [others] correctly to enlightenment. By that, the mind of enlighte nment will not be abandoned.” 
 In the sutra there is the question “How is it po ssible to avoid generating the mind thinking to 
abandon the mind of enlightenment even in  a dream?” The Buddha said “Wherever we 
abide, in a town, city, and so forth, we should strive to set others in enlightenment. By means 
of this, the mind of enlightenment will not be abandoned.”   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
576 B The statement that by abandon ing pride and the like the mind ge neration will not be abandoned 
Furthermore, in Arraying the Buddha Field of Manjushri  it is taught that the aspiring 
mind172 is not abandoned if four [qualities] are possessed: having abandoned pride, 
having abandoned jealousy, having abandoned miserliness, and being mentally 
joyful when seeing others’ wealth.  
 If we have the four – having abandoned pride,  having abandoned jealousy, having abandoned 
miserliness and being mentally joyful when seei ng the wealth of others, we will not give up 
the aspiring mind of enlightenment. The Treasury of Manifest Dharma  sets out nine, seven, and 
three types of pride. Jealousy, on the other ha nd, has many different types of objects, for 
example, if one man has two girlfriends, th e two women may be jealous of each other. 
Miserliness is an inability to give material th ings as well as an inability to share one’s 
knowledge of special painting techniques and s o  f o r t h  w i t h  o t h e r s .  W e  n e e d  t o  a b a n d o n  
pride, jealousy, and miserliness. In addition, when we see that others have wealth, good 
bodies, and other good things, we  should joyfully think “How ni ce! Isn’t that wonderful!” If 
we have these four kinds of minds, we will never give up the aspiring mind of enlightenment.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C  If all actions and conduct are done conjoined with the mind generation, one will not be separated 
from the mind of enlightenment in all lives 
This is clearly taught by the Jewel Cloud Sutra (Ratnamegha Sutra)  {194}: 
 
If you train in the mind of enlightenment in all behaviors and generate 
the mind of enlightenment before starting any virtuous activity, you will 
not be separated from this precious  mind. If you do not investigate 
time and again how... 
 
We must try in all behaviors to generate bo dhichitta, that is, all our actions should be 
conjoined with bodhichitta. By doing this, we  will never be separated from the mind of 
enlightenment in all our future lives. “All behavi ors” refers to the four behaviors of standing, 
sitting, walking, and lying. In all these behavi ors our mind should never be separated from 
bodhichitta and we should constantly be trying to  generate it. In addition, before engaging in 
any virtuous action, we should try to generate bodhichitta. In this way, we will achieve success 
in all our activities and will never be separated from the mind of enlightenment in our future 
lives. For this reason, when we finish a prayer and so forth, we should make a dedication to 
never be separated from the precious mind of enlightenment in all our future lives. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-2C3 The means of restoring [the vow] if it has degenerated 
Third: If you transgress the trainings, ex cluding the two of giving up the aspiring 
mind and mentally giving up sentient be ings, as long as you do not have the 
                                              
172 JN: Although the Tibetan  text actually says smon lam , prayers, it would seem to be more correct for it to read 
smon sems , aspiring mind. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
577 bodhisattva vows there is no downfall fr om the point of view of a bodhisattva. 
However, since it does transgress the tr ainings of the promise of intermediate 
virtue, it becomes faulty conduct and ther efore should be confessed by means of 
the four powers. 
 
If we give up the aspiring mind of enlightenment or mentally give up a sentient being, it is a 
downfall. When the other trainings are transgressed, [as long as you do not have the 
bodhisattva vows ] there is no downfall. However, since th ere is a transgression of the trainings 
of the promise of intermediate virtue, it does  become faulty conduct and therefore must be 
confessed by means of the four opponent powers in order to restore the vows.  
 Among the eighteen root downfalls in relation  to the bodhisattva vows, if we transgress 
sixteen of them, as long as the four factor s – non-shame and non-em barrassment, thinking 
that it does not matter, and so forth – are not complete, we have not broken the vows and 
they still remain. On the other hand, if we gi ve up the aspiring mind of enlightenment or 
mentally give up a sentient being, we dege nerate the trainings of the aspiring mind 
generation. When we transgress any other of th e trainings, although we do not have the 
bodhisattva vows, because we have transgressed the trainings of the promise of intermediate 
virtue, we must confess it with the four opponent powers:  1. the opponent power of regret, 
2. the opponent power of determining not to do the negative action again, 
3. the opponent power of the basis – going for re fuge to the Three Jewels and generating the 
mind of enlightenment, and 
4. the opponent power of applying an actual antidote. 
In this way, we should try to purify our negativi ties. The antidote can be to make tsa-tsas or to 
do any other virtuous action, such as reciting sutras such as the Sutra of Golden Light , with the 
motivation to purify our negativities.   (Oral transmission 7:15-7:30, pp. 194-202)  Tuesday morning, 10 May 2005
