Explanation of how to train in the conduct 
 having generated the mi nd [of enlightenment] 
2B4B-2B3B-3 Having generated the mind of enlightenment, how to train in the conduct 
A The reason it is necessary to train in the trainings, having generated the mind [of 
enlightenment] 
B Demonstration that buddhahood is not achieved  by training in method or wisdom alone 
C Explanation of the actual stages of training in the trainings  
The third has three [points]: The reason why it is necessary to train in the 
trainings, having generated the mind [of enlightenment]; demonstration that 
b u d d h a h o o d  i s  n o t  a c h i e v e d  b y  t r a i n i n g  i n  m e t h o d  o r  w i s d o m  a l o n e ;  a n d  
explanation of the actual stages of training in the trainings  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
578 Having generated the mind of enlightenmen t, we need to train in the conduct of 
bodhisattvas. The reason for this, in brief, is that if, having generated the mind of 
enlightenment, we do not train in the bodhisa ttva conduct it will not be possible to attain 
buddhahood. Likewise, by training in method alone or wisdom alone, buddhahood cannot be 
achieved. In other words, we need to train in  both method (the collection of method) and 
wisdom (the collection of wisdom). Only when the two collections are complete can we attain 
buddhahood. On the other hand, as long as they are not complete buddhahood cannot be 
attained because a buddha’s form body is the re sult of the collection of merit and a buddha’s 
truth body is the result of the collection of wi sdom. Thus, in order to a ttain these two results, 
we must accumulate their two causes – the two collections.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3A The reason it is necessary to train in the trainings, having generated the mind [of 
enlightenment] 
1  Buddhahood is not achieved by not accomplishing [the trainings] 
First: Although, having generated the mind  [of enlightenment], you do not train in 
the trainings of generosity and so forth, it will still be of great benefit as [expressed] 
in the above quotation from the Biography of Maitreya . However, if you do not 
make the bodhisattva trainings your esse ntial practice, it will be impossible to 
become a buddha. Therefore, you shou ld train in the conduct. In the King of 
Concentration  it says:  
 
“One should make it the essential practice. Why? Because, O youthful 
one, for one who makes it his essential practice it will not be difficult to 
obtain unsurpassed, perfectly complete enlightenment.” 
 If we do not train in the trainings – the bo dhisattva conduct of the six perfections of 
generosity and so forth – we will not be able to achieve buddhahood. Although it is still of 
great benefit to generate the mind of enlightenm ent, without training from the bottom of our 
heart in the bodhisattva conduct, we will ne ver achieve buddhahood. Never! Therefore, we 
need to train in the me thod or conduct. The King of Meditative Stabilizations Sutra  also says 
that if we make the bodhisattva conduct our e ssential practice, it will not be difficult to 
achieve unsurpassed, perfectly complete buddhah ood. In other words, if we train, from the 
very depths of our heart, in the bodhisattva co nduct of the six perfecti ons, generosity and so 
forth, and the four means of gathering disciples, it will not be difficult to attain buddhahood. 
O n  t h e  o t h e r  h a n d ,  i f  w e  d o  n o t  d o  t h i s ,  buddhahood cannot be achi eved. Therefore, we 
must try to train and put effort into the bodhisa ttva conduct in our daily life. If we try to do 
this, some result will come. If we  do not try, nothing will come.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 First of all tame oneself since others will not be tamed with out taming oneself 
Also [Kamalashila says] in the First Stages of Meditation : 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
579 {195} A bodhisattva who has genera ted the mind, understanding that 
he cannot tame others without tami ng himself, thoroughly applies 
himself to the practice of generosi ty and so forth. Without them, he 
cannot achieve enlightenment.  
 
Without subduing or taming our own mind, we cannot subdue others’ minds. Thus, if we 
wish to subdue others’ minds, we  must first subdue our own. The First Stages of Meditation  says 
that a bodhisattva, understanding that he cannot subdue others without subduing his own 
mind puts effort into practicing the six perf ections – generosity, morality, patience, joyous 
effort, concentration, and wisdom – or the ten pe rfections. We too must develop this kind of 
attitude in our mental continuum. Since with out this we will not be able to achieve 
buddhahood, we must put effort into practi cing the six perfections and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 What is to be accomplished 
What is to be accomplished once the vow has been taken is to train in those very 
trainings associated with it. 
 
Having taken the vow, we must train in the trai nings, that is, we must guard the bodhisattva 
vows by training in the six perfections so that  they do not degenerate. We must constantly 
train through thinking that we need to do so in order to be able to take  care of other sentient 
beings. We should not become discouraged, bu t instead should make a strong determination 
that, having taken the vows, to  train in them and achieve bu ddhahood. The trainings to be 
trained in are the six perfections of generosity and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B Demonstration that buddhahood is not  achieved by training in method or wisdom 
alone 
1 How to train in the unmistaken and complete method 
2 Refutation of wrong conceptions about this 3 Refutation of the answers to objections about this 
2B4B-2B3B-3B1 How to train in the unmistaken and complete method 
A The need to not be mistaken concerning the complete path  
Second: In the Middle  Stages of Meditation  [Kamalashila ] says:  
 
One who engages in the method fo r accomplishing buddhahood needs 
an unmistaken method, for a mistaken path, however hard you try, will 
not bring about the [desired] result, just as if, wanting milk, one were to pull [a cow] by its horn. If, although faultless, it is incomplete, the result 
will not come about despite your effort, just as a sprout will not grow if either the seed, water, earth, and the like are incomplete. 
 In order to achieve buddhahood, we need to en gage in a method that is unmistaken and 
complete. On the other hand, no matter how much  effort we put into a mistaken path, we 
will not achieve the result of  buddhahood. This would be like  someone who, wanting milk, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
580 pulls the horns of the cow! We must not do li ke this, but must use a correct method. Having 
understood what is the right method, we should  put as much effort as we can into it.  
 Likewise, if the method is not complete, we wi ll also not achieve the result. For example, if 
we have a seed and water but the soil is not good , a sprout will not grow. Also if we sow a seed 
in the soil but there is no water, a sprout will not grow. Likewise, if there is water and soil but 
no seed, as sprout will not grow. In short, Kamalashila, in his Middle Stages of Meditation , says 
that the method must be both complete and unmistaken in order for buddhahood to be 
achieved.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Identifying the complete path 
Well then, what are the complete and unmistaken causes and conditions? In 
Vairochana’s Manifest Enlightenm ent (Vairochana’s Abhisambodhi)  it says: 
 
O Lord of the Secret, the exalted wisdom of omniscience arises from 
the root that is compassion. It arises  from the cause that is the mind of 
enlightenment. It is culminated by method. 
 
I n  t h i s  r e g a r d  “ c o m p a s s i o n ”  h a s  a l r e a d y  b e e n  e x p l a i n e d .  “ T h e  m i n d  o f  
enlightenment” is the two: the convention al mind of enlightenment and ultimate 
mind of enlightenment. “Method” is thorou ghly complete generosity and so forth. 
This was explained by the gr eat trailblazer Kamalashila. 
 
Vairochana’s Manifest Enlightenment  says that the exalted wisdom  of omniscience arises from 
the root that is compassion. It arises from th e cause that is the mind of enlightenment. In 
other words, in order to achieve enlightenment the main cause or root is compassion, from 
which comes the mind of enlightenment, on the basis of which one trains in method, the six 
perfections, whereby one achieves the final result.  
 Compassion has already been explained. The conventional mind of enlightenment is the 
wish to achieve enlightenment for the welfare of all sentient beings. The ultimate mind of enlightenment is the wisdom realizing the emptin ess of inherent existence of all phenomena, 
including buddhahood. We should strive to de velop this wisdom in meditative equipoise. 
Method is thoroughly complete ge nerosity and so forth, that is, generosity, morality, patience, 
joyous effort, concentration, wisdom, method , prayer, strength, and exalted wisdom. These 
six perfections are what we need to practi ce. This was explained by Kamalashila.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B2 Refutation of wrong conceptions about this 
A Wrong modes of conception 
B Their refutation 
2B4B-2B3B-3B2A Wrong modes of conception 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
581 1 Wrong modes of apprehension 
One such mistaken conception regarding the path is held up by the Chinese 
Hashang and others who say: “Since projections173 on upward – there being no 
need to mention bad conceptions as even good conceptions – bind us in cyclic 
existence, their results do not transcend cy clic existence, just as one can be bound 
with fetters of gold or rope {196}, just as both white and black clouds obscure the 
sky, and just as one pain is produced rega rdless of whether one is bitten by a white 
or a black dog. Therefore, just equipoise  without conceptions about anything is 
the path to buddhahood. Generosity, ethica l discipline, and the like were taught 
for the welfare of dull beings incapable of  meditating on the definitive meaning. 
That is why, having found the definitive meaning, to engage in those conducts is 
like a king becoming a commoner or like , having found the elephant, looking for 
its footprints.” 
 “Hashang” is the Chinese name for a fully-ordaine d monk, a bhikshu. It is used by Tibetans 
to refer to those meditators whose strive not to think about anything  at all. They assert that all 
conceptions, even good conceptions such as bo dhichitta and compassion, bind us in cyclic 
existence, and that their results are only cyclic existence. They liken this to being bound with 
g o l d  f e t t e r s  o r  n o r m a l  r o p e ,  t h e  f a c t  b e i n g  t h a t  i n  e i t h e r  c a s e  o n e  i s  b o u n d ;  t h a t  w h e t h e r  clouds are white or black, they  obscure the sky; and that whether we are bitten by a black dog 
or a white dog, suffering is produced. Like this, good thoughts, such as bodhichitta or 
compassion, bind us to cyclic existence in the same way that bad though ts bind us in cyclic 
existence. Therefore, they ho ld that by abiding without conceptions, like a statue, one 
achieves buddhahood. For this reason, the Hashan g say that generosity, morality, and so forth 
were taught by the Buddha for those who are du ll and incapable of meditating on emptiness. 
Thus, having found the definitive meaning, if on e were to train in the bodhisattva conduct of 
generosity and so forth it would be like a king who becomes a commoner or someone who, having found the elephant, continues to looks for its tracks.   King Trisong Detsen and Shilarakshita invite d Kamalashila to debate with a Hashang in 
the presence of many ministers in Samye in the south in Tibet. Before the debate, they made 
a written agreement that whoever lost would beco me the other’s follower. Kamalashila said to 
Hashang “Do you not have any thoughts? When  you walk, do you not think about how to 
walk?” Hashang could not reply to this. Kamalashila asked him other questions but as he was 
unable to reply to them he ran way, leaving be hind a shoe. This has since been taken to be a 
sign that the Hashang’s view – that one should meditate by completely emptying the mind of 
all conceptions – would continue to remain in Tibet.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Wrong explanation of their sources  
The Hashang establish [their position] by quoting eighty passages from sutra that 
praise non-conceptuality.

582 The Hashang base their view on sutra cita tions that praise non-conceptuality.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B2B Their refutation 
1 How scholars refute the worst of wrong views 
2 How some unfortunate beings adhere to it despite those refutations 
3 How non-abiding nirvana has to be accomplished by a path of both wisdom and method 
4 How the emptiness possessing the highest of all aspects is necessary for the attainment of buddhahood 
5 How the position that only accepts wisdom is refuted in the treatises 
2B4B-2B3B-3B2B-1 How scholars refute the worst of wrong views 
The statement by them that “All the fact ors of method are not a genuine path to 
buddhahood” is a grave deprecation, and, by  refuting application in the essence of 
the Conqueror’s teachings – selflessne ss – with the wisdom of individual 
investigation, they abandon the mode of the highest meaning to a great distance. 
Although Kamalashila has refu ted them well with stainless scriptural passages and 
reasonings, and although he has presented in  detail the good path that pleases the 
conquerors… 
 
The Hashangs’ assertion that “All the fact ors of method are not a genuine path to 
buddhahood” is a great deprecatio n. They also negate that the essence of the Buddha’s 
doctrine – emptiness – is realized by the wisdom of individual investigation. Due to this, they 
are unable to achieve anything. Kamalashila re futed them by means of stainless scriptural 
passages and reasonings and extensively presente d the good path that pleases the buddhas.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B2B-2 How some unfortunate beings adhere to it despite those refutations 
…there are still some who scorn the side  o f  c o n d u c t ,  s u c h  a s  g u a r d i n g  a n d  
restraining, and who, acting as has been described above, discard it while 
meditating the path. It appears that so me, excluding deprecating the factor of 
method, discard the way of understandin g the view, while others discard the 
search for the view of suchness by means of  the wisdom of individual investigation, 
and then excellently assert th e meditation of the Chinese [sect] of not thinking 
about anything. 
 
In spite of this, there are still those who, like  the Hashangs, think that  when meditating the 
path it is not necessary to guard vows or practi ce generosity, morality, and so forth. There are 
also people who hold the position that it is not necessary to search for emptiness or 
selflessness by means of analytical wisdom. In fa ct, even nowadays there are Tibetans who still 
think that it is not necessary to study Buddhist  philosophy or learn debate, but that it is 
enough just to sit down and meditate wi thout thinking about anything at all.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B2B-3 How non-abiding nirvana has to be accomplished by a path of both wisdom and method 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
583 A That there is no need for method and wisdom to be complete contradicts all the scriptures and 
reasonings 
This does not seem to even be on the side of meditati on on emptiness. However, 
even if it were permitted to be medita tion on emptiness, the statement “Those 
who meditate well on the meaning of em ptiness should meditate on emptiness 
alone, it not being necessary to cultivate object-possessors of conventionalities, the 
side of conduct” contradicts all the script ures [of the Buddha], and simply seems to 
have gone beyond the path of reasoning. The object to be achi eved by Mahayanists 
is non-abiding nirvana. With respect to that, non-abiding in cyclic existence  is 
achieved by that called “the wisdom re alizing suchness,” “the stage of the path 
relying on the ultimate,” “the path of  the profound,” and that called “the 
collection of exalted wisdom” and “the factor of wisdom,” whereas non-abiding in 
nirvana or peace  i s  n e c e s s a r i l y  a c h i e v e d  b y  t h a t  c a l l e d  “ t h e  w i s d o m  c o g n i z i n g  
varieties,” “the stage of the path relying on conventional truth,” “the path of the 
vast,” “the collection of method,” and “the factor of merit.” 
 
There are people who think that it is enough to meditate on emptine ss without method, that 
is, without conventionalities. However, this contradicts all the teachings of the Buddha and 
has also gone beyond the path of reasoning. Th at to be achieved by Mahayana practitioners is 
non-abiding nirvana – the nirvana that has gone beyond both the extreme of abiding in cyclic 
existence and the extreme of abiding in peace. One does not abide in cyclic existence due to 
the wisdom realizing emptiness, the stage of the path relying on the ulti mate, or the path of 
the profound, the collection of wisdom, and the fa ctor of wisdom. The root of cyclic existence 
is ignorance – the conception of phenomena as in herently or truly existent. This is destroyed 
by the wisdom realizing emptiness, which abandons the wrong consciousness holding 
phenomena to be truly existent. Due to this , one does not abide in cyclic existence. 
 Also one does not abide in nirvana, the pe ace that is the pacification of one’s own 
afflictions and sufferings alone. Due to remainin g in that state, unmovi ng for eons, one will 
not be able to achieve buddhahood. One does not abide in self-peace due to the wisdom 
cognizing varieties, the state of the path relyin g on conventional truth , the path of the vast, 
the collection of method, and the factor of method. In short, the main objective of a 
Mahayanist is non-abiding nirvana, which is to no t abide either in cyclic existence or solitary 
peace.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Establishing by scriptural passages how method and wisdom refute the two extremes 
The following words are taken from the Sutra of the Inconceivable Perfect Secret : 
 
The collection of exalted wisdom is what abandons all mental 
afflictions. The collection of merit is what fully nurtures all sentient 
beings. As this is so, Bhagavan, th e bodhisattva mahasattvas strive in 
the collections of merit and exalted wisdom. 
 
By means of the collection of exalted wisdom, all the mental afflictions are abandoned. By 
means of the collection of merit, all sentient beings are nurtured or taken care of. Merit or 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
584 method includes love, compassion, and the mind of enlightenment, as well as the practices of 
generosity, morality, patience, effort and concen tration, by means of which sentient beings 
are cared for. Therefore, the bodhisattva mahasa ttvas put effort into the collections of merit 
and wisdom. The collection of merit includes th e first five perfections, whereas the collection 
of wisdom includes the sixth perfection.  
 In addition, all six perfections can be included  in the practice of ea ch single perfection. 
Therefore, in the case of the perfection of gene rosity, there is the generosity of generosity, the 
generosity of morality, the generosity of pa tience, the generosity of joyous effort, the 
generosity of concentration, and the genero sity of wisdom. This is explained in the Ornament 
for Clear Realizations . Generosity is, in brief, the mind of  giving. Morality is, in general, the 
mind of abandonment, but in the case of the ge nerosity of morality it is specifically the 
abandonment of miserliness while giving. The gene rosity of patience is an undisturbed mind 
when others’ ask us for something. The generosity of joyous effort is to delight in giving it to 
them. The generosity of concentr ation is to give it to them with a single-pointed mind. The 
generosity of wisdom is to give thinking that  oneself – the giver, the object given, and the 
recipient are empty of inherent existence. At the end when we should  dedicate the virtue 
accumulated for the benefit of all sentient bein gs, this is the generosity of generosity.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Establishing by way of scriptural passages how we are bound to the extremes of existence and peace 
as long as method and wisdom are not complete 
Also, in the  Sutra Taught at the Request of Vimalakirti it says: 
 
In response to the questions “What is the bondage of bodhisattvas?” 
and “What is their liberation?”: 
 Wisdom not conjoined with method is bondage. Wisdom conjoined with method is liberation. Method not conjoined with wisdom is bondage. {198} Method conjoined with wisdom is liberation. 
 As long as both the side of method and the side of wisdom are not complete, we will remain 
bound in cyclic existence. The Sutra Taught at the Request of Vimalakirti  poses the question as 
to what binds bodhisattvas and what liberates them. The reply is that wisdom not conjoined 
with method is bondage (that is, bondage to se lf-peace); wisdom conjoined with method is 
liberation (that is, liberation from self-peace);  method not conjoined with wisdom is bondage 
(that is, bondage to cyclic existence as with out wisdom one cannot become free from cyclic 
existence); and method conjoined with wisdom is  liberation (that is, liberation from cyclic 
existence).   It is necessary to understand that without wisdom, that is, without the wisdom realizing 
selflessness or emptiness, it is not possible to  achieve nirvana or liberation. On the other 
hand, without method, although we can achi eve self-peace, we cannot achieve buddhahood. 
Therefore, we need both method and wisdom.   Tuesday afternoon, 10 May 2005 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
585  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 198) says:  
D The achievement of buddhahood depends on both wisdom and method 
In the Gayagori Sutra : 
 
The bodhisattva path, in brief, is ma de up of two. What are the two? 
They are the following: method and wisdom. 
 
When the path of bodhisattvas is condensed, it  consists of the two: method and wisdom. By 
practicing these two together we can easily achieve buddhahood, whereas if one of them is 
missing we cannot. Therefore, both method and wisdom are necessary. Method includes 
compassion, the mind of enlightenment, and th e practice of the first five perfections. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Their significance is also taught clearly in the Lamp on the Path : 
 
Due being separated from applicatio n in the perfection of wisdom, 
The obscurations will not exhausted. 
In order to abandon all the afflictive obscurations and The obscurations to objects of knowledge without exception, The yoga of the perfection of wisdom should  Always be meditated in combination with method. This is why wisdom separated from method  As well as method separated from wisdom Are called “bondage.”  Therefore, do not abandon either.  
 Without application in the perfection of wisd om, the obscurations cannot be exhausted. To 
abandon the afflictive obscurations and the obscur ations to knowledge, it is necessary to apply 
their antidote – the yoga of the perfection of wisdom, the prajnaparamita, or the wisdom 
gone beyond. However, we need to meditate on emptiness conjoined with the side of method, that is, conjoined with the motivation of love and compassion, and mainly the mind 
of enlightenment. Therefore, we should abando n neither merit or wisdom. If we practice one 
separated from the other, it is called “bondage .” Thus, if we have me thod but not the wisdom 
realizing emptiness, we will remain bound in cycl ic existence. On the other hand, if we have 
the wisdom realizing emptiness but not method, we  will remain bound in self-peace, this also 
being called “bondage” since it prevents us from achieving buddhahood.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And: 
All the collection of virtue, 
The perfection of generosity and the rest, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
586 That have abandoned the perfection of wisdom  
Are explained by the Conquerors to be method.  
A great being who meditates on the wisdom aspect 
With the power of familiarity with method 
Will quickly achieve enlightenment; This cannot be done by meditating on selflessness alone. 
 
The understanding of the emptiness in nature That realizes the aggregates , constituents, and sources  
As without production, Is thoroughly explained to be “wisdom.” 
 All the perfections excluding wisdom, that is, ge nerosity, morality, patience, joyous effort, and 
concentration, are the collection of virtue that  is the side of method. Through familiarizing 
with merit and meditating on the wisdom aspect, the wisdom realizing emptiness, a great being will quickly achieve enlightenment. On th e other hand, by meditating on selflessness 
alone one will not achieve enlightenment.   The realization that the five aggregates, ei ghteen constituents, and twelve sources – all 
phenomena – are not inherently produced is the realization of emptiness; this is thoroughly 
explained to be “wisdom.” The five aggregates  include all compounded phenomena, whereas 
the eighteen constituents and twelve sources include all compounded phenomena as well as all uncompounded phenomena – space, cessations,  emptinesses, and selflessnesses. In this 
way, all phenomena are included in the th ree groups: the five aggregates, eighteen 
constituents, and twelve sources. The form aggr egate includes all forms, that is, colors and 
shapes, as well as revelatory and non-revelato ry form. The feeling a ggregate includes the 
feelings of suffering, happiness, mental happiness, mental unhappiness, and equanimity. The discrimination aggregate includes both correct discriminations and mistaken discriminations. 
All the remaining forty-nine mental factors as well as the non-associated compositional factors 
are included in the compositional factors aggreg ate. The consciousness aggregate includes all 
primary consciousnesses, that is, the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mental consciousnesses. 
All these phenomena are compounded phenomena.  The eighteen constituents include the six 
objects (forms, sounds, odors, tastes, tangible  objects, and phenomena), the six supporting 
powers (the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mental powers), and the six supported 
consciousnesses (the eye, ear, nose, tongue, bo dy, and mental consciousnesses). They include 
all phenomena, that is, both functioning th ings and permanent phenomena. The twelve 
sources are the six object-possessors (the eye so urce, ear source, nose source, tongue source, 
body source, and mental source) and the six ob jects (form source, sound source, odor source, 
taste source, tangible object source, and phen omena source). None of these phenomena exist 
inherently, that is, they are all empty of inherent  existence. We should me ditate on this fact.

587 2B4B-2B3B-3B2B-4 How the emptiness that possesses the best of all aspects is necessary for attaining 
buddhahood174
 
In the Jewel Pinnacle Sutra  it is taught that there it is  necessary to meditate on the 
emptiness that possesses the best of all aspe cts which is complete in all the factors 
of method, such as generosity and so forth.  
 
This sutra says that it is necessary to medita te on emptiness when all the factors of method, 
generosity and so forth, are complete. In other words, together with me ditation on emptiness 
we need to practice generosity and so fort h. In this way, we will attain buddhahood.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B2B-5 How the position that only accepts wisdom is refuted in the treatises 
In the Gathering All the Threads  [Sutra] it says: 
 
Foolish people think to repudiate th e bodhisattvas’ practice of the six 
perfections for the sake of enli ghtenment saying: “Train in the 
perfection of wisdom alone. {199} Wh at is the point in training in the 
remaining?” 
 
And: 
Foolish people also say: “One becomes enlightened by a single means, 
that is, by way of emptiness.” Howe ver, they do not have completely 
pure conduct. 
 Foolish people say that it is enough to train in  the perfection of wisdom  and that bodhisattvas 
do not need to practice the six perfections in order to achieve enlightenment. They say that 
one single means is sufficient to become enli ghtened – meditation on emptiness. However, 
the conduct of such people does not become co mpletely pure because bodhisattvas, in fact, 
need to practice all six perfections, it is no t sufficient for them to meditate on the wisdom 
realizing emptiness alone.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3 Refutation of the answers to objections about this 
A Refutation of the objection that asserts that method is unnecessary if emptiness has been realized 
B Refutation of the objection that asserts that method is unnecessary since in the view of emptiness the 
six perfections are complete 
C Demonstration of contradictions in others’ assertions 
 
First the assertion that if emptiness has been realized, method is not necessary is refuted. 
Then the assertion that since all six perfections are complete in the view of emptiness, 
method is not necessary is refuted. Then the co ntradictions in others’ assertions are shown.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
                                              
174 JN: There is a mistake in the numbering of this ou tline and the next one in the Tibetan text (p. 198). 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
588 2B4B-2B3B-3B3A Refutation of the objection that asserts that method is unnecessary if emptiness has 
been realized 
1 Stating the assertion 
2 Its refutation 
2B4B-2B3B-3B3A-1 Stating the assertion 
Someone thinks: “Training in the conduct of  generosity and so forth is [necessary] 
if one does not have a stable realization of emptiness, but if one has that, it is 
sufficient.” 
 
Someone thinks that those who do not have a st able realization of emptiness should train in 
the bodhisattva conduct, whereas those who have  a stable realization of emptiness do not 
need to practice generosity, morality, pa tience, joyous effort, and concentration. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3A-2 Its refutation 
A Refutation because it is in contradiction to the definitive meaning sutras  
[The refutation is:] “If this were so, the conquerors’ children who have attained the 
first ground and so forth, and especial ly conquerors’ children on the eighth 
ground who have achieved power over non-conceptual wisdom, would not need 
the conduct.” However, this is inappropriate, for in the Sutra of the Ten Grounds  it 
is taught “On each of the ten grounds, one, such as generosity and so forth, also 
becomes paramount. However, it is not th at the remaining ones are not practiced.” 
Therefore, on each of the grounds all six or all ten are said to be practiced.  
 It is incorrect to say that the bodhisattvas who have attained the first seven grounds – the first 
ground, Joyous, the second ground, Stainless, the third ground, Luminous, the fourth ground, Radiant, the fifth ground, Difficult to Overcome, the sixth ground, Approaching, 
and the seventh ground, Gone Af ar – and especially those wh o have attained the eighth 
ground, Immovable, who have attained power ov er non-conceptual wisdom do not need to 
practice the other perfections. This is because on each of the respective grounds, one of the 
perfections is mainly practiced: on the first ground, generosity; on the second, morality; on 
the third, patience; on the fourth, joyous effort ; on the fifth, concentration; on the sixth, 
wisdom; on the seventh, method; on the eighth , prayer; on the ninth, strength; and on the 
tenth, exalted wisdom. Therefore, it is necessary to practice all six or all ten perfections. While 
the Sutra of the Ten Grounds  says that on each ground or bhumi one perfection is mainly 
practiced, it is not that the others are not pr acticed. In this way, the assertion that the 
realization of emptiness alone is enough is refute d on the basis of valid scriptural authority.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B How, even with the highest realization of emptiness, method and wisdom are necessary 
In particular on the occasion of the eigh th ground, when abiding on the ultimate 
that pacifies all elaborations owing to the exhaustion of all the mental afflictions, 
the buddhas [exhort the bodhisattva saying: ] “Buddhahood cannot be achieved by 
the mere realization of emptiness; this [rea lization] is achieved even by hearers and 
solitary realizers.” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
589  
Even with the highest realization of emptine ss, method and wisdom are necessary. On the 
eighth ground, when the bodhisattva, having extinguished all the afflictions, abides in 
meditative equipoise on the ultimate, he is ex horted by the buddhas who say that it is not 
enough to realize emptiness alone in order to achieve buddhahood because even though 
hearer and solitary realizer foe-destroyers have achieved this realization they are not able to 
achieve buddhahood. in other words, when the bo dhisattva abides in meditative equipoise on 
the ultimate in which all elaborations have b een pacified, the buddhas radiate rays of light 
that strike him and make him rise from his me ditation and then tell him that by means of 
this meditation alone he cannot attain buddhah ood, as otherwise even hearer and solitary 
realizer foe-destroyers would have achieved it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How it is therefore inappropriate [to think] that there is no need for wisdom and method right from the 
beginning 
My bodies, my exalted wisdom, my [buddha] fields are immeasurable. 
L o o k  a t  t h e m !  N o r  d o  y o u  h a v e  m y  p o w e r s  a n d  t h e  l i k e .  T h e r e f o r e ,  
start to exert yourselves. Think of the unpacified sentient beings who 
are confused by various mental afflictions. {200} Do not give up 
patience either! 
 If [the buddhas] are so emphatic in explai ning [to eighth ground bodhisattvas] the 
need to train in the bodhisattva conduc t, this obviously applies to others.  
 The buddhas exhort the eighth  ground bodhisattvas saying: “Look at the immeasurable 
qualities of my bodies, exalted wisdom, and pure lands. Also my powers and so forth are without match. Therefore, you too need to put effort into achieving immeasurable bodies, 
immeasurable exalted wisdom, and immeasurable pure lands, as well as the ten powers, the 
four fearlessnesses, and so forth. Think abou t the fact that sentient beings’ minds are 
unpacified, disturbed by the mental afflictions.  You must help them. It is not fitting to 
remain in the meditative absorption of self-peace . Also do not give up patience, but put strong 
effort into taking care of sentient beings. You must do this.” Therefore,  since it is necessary 
for these bodhisattvas to train in the bodhisattva conduct of the six or ten perfections, there is 
no need to mention that ot hers must also do so.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D How even on the high paths of mantra, the way to generate the mind and train in the six perfections is 
the same by and large 
Although on the occasion of the high path of unsu rpassed mantra there are 
differences in this regard, it has already been explained above that, in general, the 
two – the mantra and perfection [vehicles] – share the same overall structure of the 
path of training in generating the two minds of enlightenment and the six 
perfections. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
590 Also the high path of unsurpassed mantra or ta ntra, it is necessary to train in the mind of 
enlightenment and the six perfections. Althou gh there are differences between the tantra 
vehicle and perfection vehicle, in both cases it is necessary to generate the two minds of 
enlightenment, the conventional and ultimate, an d to practice the six perfections. This has 
already been explained before. While there ar e practices that are uncommon to tantra and 
not found in the perfection vehicle, for exampl e, deity yoga, meditation on the channels and 
winds, training the five root winds and five secondary winds, and so forth, everything taught 
in sutra must be practiced by tantric practitioners.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 200) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3B Refutation of the objection stating that method is unnecessary because in the view of 
emptiness all six perfections are complete 
1 The objection thinking “Since the six perfections ar e complete when not thinking about anything, this 
is sufficient” 
2 Its refutation  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3B-1 The objection thinking “Since the six perfections are complete when thinking about nothing at all, this is sufficient”: how they are complete due to appearing according to that way of calculating  
[The objection is:] “We do not assert that  generosity and so forth are unnecessary, 
but they are complete when not thinking about anything because by not adhering 
to the recipient, the act of giving, nor the substance given, non-observing 
generosity is complete, and likewise the remaining [perfections] are complete and 
because it says in sutra that the six perfec tions are contained in each one of them.” 
 Some people think that not thinking about anythi ng includes the six perfections, therefore it 
is enough. They think that meditation on empt iness means to sit motionless while trying to 
stop thoughts, whereby it becomes unnecessary to  practice the six perfec tions. However, this 
would be too easy! Then, even a rabbit, due to  merely sitting still, would quickly achieve 
enlightenment! Hence, this assertion is not ri ght. These people think that by not thinking 
about anything at all, the six perfections are completed because there is no thought adhering 
to the giver, the action of giving, and the subs tance given, whereby non-observing generosity is 
complete. In other words, since one does not th ink about anything, one also does not adhere 
to the three spheres, whereby one complete s non-observing generosity, non-observing 
morality, non-observing patience, non-observing joyous effort, non-observing concentration, 
and non-observing wisdom.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3B-2 Refutation of this together with a digression  
A The absurd consequences [that would ensue] if this were so 
If all the perfections were complete simp ly through this, they would be complete 
for the calm abiding that is a single-point ed mind of a Tirthika at the time of 
meditative equipoise since he does not adhere  to them. In particular, as it says in 
the Sutra of the Ten Grounds , hearers and solitary realizers who have a non-
conceptual exalted wisdom with respect to  reality, when meditatively equipoised 
on it, would become Mahayanists since the entire bodhisattva conduct is complete. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
591 The Sutra on the Ten Grounds  says that since hearers and solita ry realizers have a non-observing 
non-conceptual exalted wisdom with respect to emptiness, they too would have completed the 
bodhisattva conduct of the six perfections. In other words, it would follow that hearers and 
solitary realizers who meditate on emptiness wo uld become Mahayanists, that is, bodhisattvas, 
because all six perfections are complete.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B If it were sufficient that in each one all six are complete, it would follow that a mandala would be 
offered with one single [substance] 
If you assert that this alone is sufficien t, because it is taught that in each 
[perfection] all six are included, then, wh ile offering a mandala with [the verse] 
“Giving cow dung together with water...” it  would also be appropriate to do just 
that since it is said that {201} all six are present. 
 
Is it sufficient to offer a mandala with the verse “Giving cow dung together with water”? 
When we do a mandala offering, we first clean the base with the five  cow substances: dung, 
urine, butter, milk, and yogurt mixed together and made into pills. Since we imagine cleaning 
away the impurities in our mind while simult aneously practicing generosity, morality, 
patience, joyous effort, concentration, and wi sdom, it would be enough just to offer a 
mandala.  Wednesday morning, 11 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 201) says:  
C How inseparable wisdom and method influence each other 
The [following] analogy [is given] in order to illustrate conduct conjoined with the 
view and wisdom conjoined with method. When a mother tormented by sorrow at 
the death of her beloved child engages in talking with other people etc., although 
whatever minds are produced it is not gi ven up due to the power of her sorrow, 
still all her minds are not minds of sorrow. Similarly, if the wisdom realizing 
emptiness is very powerful, when giving gifts, prostrating, circumambulating, and 
so forth, the minds that observe them are not realizing emptiness, but there is no 
contradiction that are performed possessing its strength. If you start off with the 
intense power of the mind of enlighte nment, as you do at beginning of a 
meditation session, although the mind of  enlightenment is not manifest when 
placed in the meditative stabilization of em ptiness, there is no contradiction in it 
being influenced by it. The way in whic h wisdom and method are inseparable is 
also like this. 
 
We need to practice wisdom and method simult aneously, such that one assists the other. Our 
conduct should be conjoined with the view, th e realization of emptiness, and our wisdom 
should be conjoined with method, compassion, the mind of enlightenment, the practice of 
generosity, and so forth. In other words, they  should be inseparable. For example, if a 
mother’s beloved child has died, she is tormen ted by sorrow such that even when she talks 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
592 with other people, although her sorrow or un happiness is not manifest in her mind, its 
strength remains and does not diminish. In othe r words, remembering the death of her child, 
her mind remains conjoined with sorrow or sa dness. Similarly, if our wisdom realizing 
emptiness is strong, even when we practice genero sity, make offerings of water, flowers, light, 
incense, and so forth, do prostrations, circumam bulate holy objects, or do other activities, 
t h e y  w i l l  b e  i n f l u e n c e d  b y  t h e  p o w e r  o f  t h e  w i sdom realizing emptiness, although it is not 
manifest. There is no contradiction that the re alization of emptiness does not decrease in 
strength even when a bodhisattva practices the other perfections. In fact, when a bodhisattva 
practices one of the six perfections, it should be conjoined with all six perfections. He does 
not practice wisdom separate from method and method separate from wisdom, as by doing so 
buddhahood could not be attained.  
 When doing a retreat, at the beginning of ea ch meditation session we generate the strong 
motivation of love, compassion, and the mind of enlightenment. Then, afterward, when we 
meditate on emptiness single-pointedly, even th ough the mind of enlightenment is no longer 
manifest our meditation is still conjoined with  or held by the mind of enlightenment. The 
meaning of inseparable method and wisdom is that while one is manifest, the power of the 
other still remains, that is, they assist each  other. Therefore, every morning we should 
generate the motivation of the mind of enlighte nment that wishes to attain enlightenment for 
the welfare of all sentient beings and then re cite our sadhanas or meditate on emptiness, 
death-impermanence, the breath, and so forth. In this way, the mind of enlightenment 
remains the motivation for all our activities.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C Demonstration of contradictions in others’ assertions 
1 The inappropriateness of the causes for high status and certain goodness being contradictory 
2 [One’s words will be contradictory like the speech of madmen ] 
3 [The reason for its lack of observed objects and its contradictions ] 
4 [Contradiction to bringing about any certainty] 
5 Contradictions with the sutras teaching the absence of an object – has four units 
6 [Clarification of the position that applies equally to sutra and tantra] 
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C-1 The inappropriateness of the causes for high status and certain goodness being 
contradictory 
You should not be misled with regard to  the statement that the results of the 
collection of merit are your [present] body , resources, long life, and so forth of 
cyclic existence. If method is divorced  from wisdom, this is indeed the case. 
However, if it is conjoined with it, it is altogether suitable to be the cause of 
liberation and omniscience. There are infini te scriptural passages for this, like in 
[Nagarjuna’s] Precious  Garland (Ratnavali) : 
 
The form body, in brief, O King, Is born from the collection of merit. 
 It is said that the result of the collection of merit is our body (a high rebirth as a human being 
or god), resources (the result of generosity), long life (the result of the morality of abandoning killing), and so forth of cyclic existence. Howeve r, we should not be mistaken with respect to 
this statement. If merit is separate from wisdom , it is like this, whereas if merit is conjoined 
with the wisdom realizing emptiness it is s uitable to be the cause of liberation and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
593 omniscience. Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland , for instance, says that the form body of a buddha 
is born from the collection of merit. Th ere are many quotations to this effect. 
 The collection of merit from practicing generosity and so forth is necessary since it is the 
cause of the form body of a buddha, and the coll ection of wisdom is necessary since it is the 
cause of the truth body of a buddha, that is, the mind of a buddha. Since these results arise 
simultaneously, we need to accumulate their causes simultaneously. Therefore, it is not 
correct to accumulate merit while leaving as ide wisdom and vice versa. If, when we 
accumulate merit through practicing the first fi ve perfections, we do not have the wisdom 
realizing emptiness, then our practice of morali ty will become the cause of taking a rebirth of 
high status as a human being or god, our prac tice of generosity will become the cause of 
wealth, our practice of patience will become the cause of beauty, and so forth. Thus, when we 
lack wisdom, our merit becomes the cause of high  rebirth in cyclic existence, whereas when 
merit is conjoined with wisdom , it becomes the cause of definite goodness – liberation and 
omniscience.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C-2 One’s words will be contradictory like the speech of madmen 
Furthermore, sometimes you seem to say th at the faulty conduct and all the mental 
afflictions that become the causes of the bad migrations can also become causes for 
buddhahood, and sometimes that virtues like generosity, ethical discipline, and so 
on that become the causes of high status are the causes of cyclic existence, rather 
than the causes of enlightenment. {202} Yo u should set your mind at ease before 
talking! 
 
To sometimes say that faulty conduct and the me ntal afflictions, which are the causes of the 
bad migrations, can become the causes of buddh ahood, and to sometimes say that the virtues 
of generosity, morality, and so forth, which are the causes of high status, are the cause of 
cyclic existence and not cause of enlightenment, is the talk of a crazy person. In other words, 
to say that faulty conduct and the mental affl ictions become the causes of buddhahood, while 
virtues such as generosity and so forth are no t the causes of enlightenment indicates that 
one’s mind is not abiding in its natural state bu t is disturbed. Therefore, before talking, one 
should calm one’s mind and not allow it to fly in the sky! 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C-3 The reason for its lack of observed objects and its contradictions 
A The presentation in the sutras 
It also says in the sutras: 
 
Strong adherence to the six, generosi ty and so forth, is the action of 
Mara. 
 
In the [ Sutra of the] Three Heaps  it also explains: 
 
The giving of gifts due to  falling into observing, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
594 Guarding ethics due to considering it supreme ethics, 
And so forth; these should be confessed individually. 
 
Also from the [Sutra] Requested by Brahma : 
 
However much conduct exists, all of it is conceptual. 
Thorough non-conceptual ity is enlightenment. 
 You should not be mistaken with respect to these statements. 
 In the sutras it says that strong adherence to the six perfections of generosity and so forth as 
inherently existent is the action of  mara. It is also mentioned in the Ornament for Clear 
Realizations  that there are many such ac tions of mara. Thus, engaging in the practice of the six 
perfections while adhering to them as inherent ly existent, truly existent, or self-supporting 
substantially existent is the action of the mara.  
 In the Sutra of the Three Heaps  i t  a l s o  s a y s  t h a t  t h e  g i v i n g  o f  g i f t s  d u e  t o  f a l l i n g  i n t o  
observing, that is, due to holding to true existe nce, and guarding morality due to holding it to 
be supreme morality must be confessed indivi dually, that is, they must be abandoned.  
 The Sutra Requested by Brahma  says that whatever conduct ex ists, it is conceptual, whereas 
thorough non-conceptuality is enlightenment.  
 We should not be mistaken with respect to these citations but think about them and try to 
understand them correctly. For example, when th e sutra says that whatever conduct exists is 
conceptual, the meaning it that enlightenm ent cannot be attained by that which is 
conceptual. In other words, when we ordinary beings practice generosity, guard morality, and 
develop patience, we do so with a conceptual mi nd, not with a direct perceiver. On the other 
hand, when we achieve enlightenment, our mind is  non-conceptual, that is, it is only a direct 
perceiver. However, this does not mean that all conceptions are to be abandoned. While the 
Hashangs assert that all conceptions are to be aban doned, it is not like this in our tradition.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The contradiction to the way in which the first passage is explained 
The meaning of the first one is that genero sity motivated by mistaken adherence to 
the two [types of] self is explained to be an  action of the maras since it is not pure. 
However, generosity and so forth are not pr esented to be the actions of the maras. 
 
The meaning of the first passage is that when generosity and so forth are motivated by the 
adherence to a self of persons and a self of phen omena, that is the adherence to an inherently 
existent or self-supporting substantially existent  self and aggregates, then generosity, morality, 
patience, and so forth are considered to be th e action of the maras since they are not pure. 
However, it is not that the six perfections are taught to be the action of the maras. In other 
words, although generosity and so forth can beco me the action of the ma ras, in general when 
we engage in the practice of the six perfection s with a good motivation, they become perfect 
actions and not actions of the maras. Therefore, we need to  examine our motivation when 
meditating, when practicing generosity and so fo rth. If we find, for ex ample, that one of the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
595 eight worldly concerns, for example, the thought to give so that others can see that we are 
generous whereby we will become famous, has ar isen, it is not pure generosity, but is an 
action of the maras. In other wo rds, if we give motivated by th e wish for fame, for the wish to 
receive something in return, and so forth, it is not pure. When we give, we should do so with 
a pure motivation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C The contradiction to holding the words of the second passage since it actually says “due to falling into 
observing” 
Otherwise, you would have to say “Giving should be confessed in general” without 
the need to fall into observing saying  “Bestowing gifts having fallen into 
observing.” Therefore, it has not been taught like that. In the [Kamalashila’s] Last 
Stages of Meditation  it becomes a very crucial point [for Kamalashila] to give this 
kind of answer, for if this is misunderstood, one will assert the entire side of 
conduct to be together with signs through holding to signs of a self of persons or 
phenomena.  
 
To “fall into observing” means to conceive of inherent existence, true existence, or self-
supporting substantial existence. To give due to falling into ob serving is an action of the 
mara. However, in general generosity does not entail falling into observing, otherwise there 
would have been no need for the Buddha to have  said that it is necessary to confess the 
generosity of falling into observing. The Ha shangs, on the other hand, have the mistaken 
understanding that the entire side of conduct is together with the thought that persons and 
phenomena are truly existent, that is, together with  the signs of a self of persons or a self of 
phenomena. However, this is mistaken, there is  no need for there to be adherence to true 
existence, that is, falling into  observing, when we practice generosity and so forth. The 
adherence to signs of a truly existent self of pe rsons and a truly existent self of phenomena is 
what is called “holding to signs” ( tshen dzin ).  
 
 (Oral transmission, 12:04-12:29, pp. 202-212)  Wednesday afternoon, 11 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 202) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C-4 [Contradiction to bringing about any certainty] 
A It contradicts the teachings that one should rely on abandoning negativities and so forth  
B The mistake that this [view] contradicts any ki nd of logic and makes it impossible to establish 
anything with certainty 
C [Understanding the statements in the context of the two truths] 
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C-4A It contradicts the teachings that one should rely on abandoning negativities and so forth 
If the mind of giving that thinks “I will  give this substance away” and the mind of 
restraint that thinks “I will restrain myse lf from this faulty conduct” and all such 
virtuous conceptions were conceptions of  a self of phenomena apprehending the 
three spheres, it would be suitable for those who have found the view of 
selflessness of phenomena {203} to cease them  in all ways just like hatred, pride, 
etc. and it would be unsuitable to rely on them for that purpose.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
596  
If the mind of giving that thinks “I will give  away this substance” and the mind of restraint 
that thinks “I will restrain myself from this faulty conduct” and all such virtuous conceptions 
were conceptions of a self of phenomena appreh ending the three spheres to be inherently or 
truly existent, then those who have found the view of the selflessness of phenomena (the 
emptiness of all phenomena of true or inherent  existence) would have to cease all virtuous 
conceptions, such as the mind of giving that thinks “I will give away this substance” and the 
mind of restraint that thinks “I will restrain my self from this faulty conduct.” In other words, 
it would be necessary to abandon all virtuous co nceptions in the same way that it is necessary 
to abandon hatred, pride, ignorance, afflicte d doubt, and so forth. However, virtuous 
conceptions such as the mind of giving, that is, generosity, that thinks “I will give this…,” “I 
will make a donation to that…” are not to be abandoned. Therefore, they are not like 
attachment, hatred, pride, and so forth. Vi rtuous minds need to be developed, not 
abandoned.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C-4B The fault that this [assertion] contr adicts all reasonings and also makes it unsuitable to 
gain certainty with respect to anything 
If any conception that thinks “This is this ” is considered to be a conception of a 
self of phenomena that conceives the thr ee spheres, then thinking about the good 
qualities of a spiritual teacher and the great significance of the leisures and 
endowments, etc., remembering death, thinking about the sufferings of the bad 
migrations, going for refuge, and contemplat ing that from this action arises that 
result, training in love, compassion, and the mind of enlightenment, and training 
in the trainings of the engaging mind  of enlightenment would be thoughts 
thinking “This is this,” “From this, that arises,” “This has these qualities and those 
disadvantages.” Since that would necessarily  just induce certainty, the conception 
of a self of phenomena would increase more  and more as certainty with respect to 
those [above-mentioned topics] increases. Moreover, the certainty with respect to 
those paths would decrease as certainty with respect to the selflessness of phenomena increases. Thereby, the side of  conduct and the side of view having 
come to contradict each other like hot and cold, a strong ascertaining 
consciousness with respect to both of them could not be generated continuously. 
 This assertion contradicts logical reasonings and makes it impossible to gain certainty. This is 
because it would follow that all conceptions “Thi s is a cup,” “This is a table,” “This is a man,” 
“This is woman,” would become conceptions of a self of phenomena that conceive the three 
spheres to be inherently existent. Then, thinking  about the qualities of the virtuous spiritual 
friend and the great meaning of the leisures and endowments, remembering death, thinking 
about the sufferings of the bad migrations, going for refuge, thinking about the fact that from 
this action comes that result (i.e., from a virtuous action comes happiness and from a non-
virtuous action comes suffering), and training in  the love that wishes all sentient beings to 
have happiness and its causes, the compassion that  wishes all sentient beings to be free from 
suffering and its causes, and the mind of enlightenment, as well as training in the trainings of the engaging mind of enlightenment would all be thoughts of “This is that,” “This arises from 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
597 that,” “This has this good quality,” and “That has this disadvantage.” Since we must gain 
certainty regarding these thoughts, it would fo llow that the more we think about them and 
the more our certainty increases, the more the conception of a self of phenomena would also 
correspondingly increase. In addition, the more we  gain certainty with respect to these topics, 
the more the certainty with respect to the path would decrease as the certainty with respect to 
the conception of a self of phenomena increases.  As a result the side of conduct and the side 
of view would become contradictory, like hot and cold. Also a strong ascertaining 
consciousness with respect to both of th em would not be generated continuously.  
 This entire discussion is base d on the assertion that all conc eptions, whether good or bad, 
are to be abandoned. Supporting analogies were given saying that whether one is bitten by a 
white dog or a black dog there is pain, whether th ere is a black cloud or white cloud the sky is 
obscured, whether one is bound by a gold rope o r a n o rm al ro pe o n e is  s till bo un d. Suc h  
people thereby conclude that all conceptions ar e  t o  b e  a b a n d o n e d ,  h e n c e  i t  i s  b e s t  n o t  t o  
think about anything. They assert this to be  meditation on emptiness and the definitive 
meaning of ultimate truth, whereas all conception s only become the cause of rebirth in cyclic 
existence. This assertion is what is being refuted here.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C-4C [Understanding the statements in the context of the two truths] 
1 Method and wisdom are not contradictory like hot and cold 
In accordance with the two – on the o ccasion of the result, truth body being 
posited as the object to be obtained an d form body being considered to be the 
object to be obtained – not being contradi ctory, the following two at the time of 
the path must also not be contradictory:  
1. an ascertaining consciousness that is in duced with respect to the freedom from 
elaborations of an observed object, even  the merest particle  without exception, 
that is apprehended as having signs of the two [types of] self, and 
2. an ascertaining consciousness that is in duced with respect to “From this, that 
arises” and “This has that fault or quality.” 
 Method and wisdom are not contradictory as ar e hot and cold. Why? Because just as on the 
occasion of the result, the truth body, the object  to be obtained on the side of wisdom, and 
the form body, the object to be attained on the side of method, are not contradictory, likewise 
the causes, method and wisdom, cannot be contradi ctory. Thus also at the time of the path 
the two ascertaining consciousnesses are not contradictory: 1. an ascertaining consciousness that is in duced with respect to the freedom from 
elaborations of an observed object, even the merest particle without exception, that is 
apprehended as having signs of the two types of self, and 
2. an ascertaining consciousness that is induced with respect to “From this, that arises” and 
“This has that fault or quality.” 
In other words, because the two conceptions of a self, that of persons and that of phenomena, 
that is, the holding to signs of truly exis tent persons and phenomena, are mistaken 
consciousnesses, their objects of the mode of apprehension are destroyed such that not even 
an atom remains. Thus, we need to gain certainty with respect to the fact that all phenomena 
are free from true existence and inherent existenc e. Having gained this certainty, we can say 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
598 “This arises from that,” for example, “Smoke arises from fire.” We can also say “This has 
qualities” and “This has faults”; for example, “V irtuous minds, such as love, have qualities” 
and “The mental afflictions have faults.” We n eed to gain certainty with respect to this, as 
w h e n  t h i s  i s  u n d e r s t o o d  m e t h o d  a n d  w i s d o m  w i l l  n o t  a p p e a r  t o  b e  c o n t r a d i c t o r y .  O n  t h e  
other hand, if one does not understand this, method and wisdom will appear to be 
contradictory. However, they cannot be contra dictory because their final results – the form 
body and truth body – are simultaneously achi eved by a single being. A buddha has many 
qualities such as the qualities of his exal ted body, exalted speech, exalted mind, and 
enlightened activities. Cyclic existence, on th e other hand, has many faults, such as many 
types of suffering. Therefore, cyclic existenc e has many faults while buddhahood has only 
qualities.   If we study the lam-rim, for example, our kn owledge and understanding of the stages of the 
path will grow. In fact, whatever subject we stud y, science, mathematics, and so forth, we will 
come to gain more and more knowledge. On the other hand, if we do not study, we will not 
gain knowledge and may even become more an d more stupid as our ignorance increases! 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 How the two valid cognitions that posit the two truths assist one another 
Moreover, that depends on how {204} the view of the basis, the two truths, is 
settled. Therefore, when, by way of script ures and reasonings, you gain certainty 
that the two: 
1. the valid cognition thoroughly positing the ultimate that has settled the mode 
of subsistence or the manner of being of  all the phenomena of cyclic existence 
and nirvana as without the merest pa rticle of inherent existence; and 
2. the valid cognition of varieties that thor oughly posits causes and results which 
individually ascertains the slightest phenomenon that is a cause or result 
without confusion 
do not harm each other in the least but ra ther assist each other, then you will 
realize the meaning of the two truths and will come to count yourself among 
“those who have found the intention of the Conqueror.” 
 The view of the basis is the two truths. By un derstanding the two truths, we will understand 
that the two valid cognizers realizing them are not mutually contradictory, but instead 
mutually assist each other. By means of vali d scriptural authority and reasonings cyclic 
existence and nirvana are shown to not be esta blished from their own side and to not be 
inherently existent. In fact, they do not have ev en the tiniest particle of inherent existence. 
This emptiness of inhalation and exhalation is their ultimate truth.   On the other hand, causes and results are infa llible: from virtuous actions come happiness 
and not suffering, and from non-virtuous action s come suffering and not happiness. Similarly, 
smoke arises from fire, that is, smoke is the result  of its respective cause, fire. External causes 
function in the same way, for example, a rice s eed does not bring the result of a wheat sprout 
or barley sprout, it only brings the result of rice sprout. Likewise, a mother dog gives birth to 
puppies and not kittens, whereas a mother cat gives birth to kittens and not puppies. Such 
causes and results are established by a valid cognizer of varieties or conventionalities.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
599  The valid cognizer realizing conventional trut hs and the valid cognizer realizing ultimate 
truths do not harm each other, instead they assist each other. Having gained certainty with 
respect to this, we will realize the meaning of  the two truths and find the intention of the 
Buddha. In short, we need to understand that  these two valid cognizers do not contradict 
each other, but assi st each other.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 [Clarification of the misconception expressed in the third quotation] 
The meaning of the third quotation is explained in the Last Stages of Meditation . 
Since the context of this sutra is the cont ext of an analysis of production and so 
forth, with respect to teaching that genero sity and so forth are not really produced, 
they are taught to be merely imputed by  conception saying “They are conceived,” 
however they are not taught to be objects to be discarded and not relied upon.  
 
The third quotation from sutra teaches that gene rosity and so forth are not established from 
their own side or inherently or truly. Rather, ge nerosity and so forth, all six perfections, are 
taught to be merely imputed by conception. Theref ore, it is not that they are not to be relied 
upon or discarded, in fact generosity and so forth are to be practice d. Thus, the sutra only 
teaches that they exist by way of imputation, it does not teach that they are not to be relied 
upon and to be discarded. This is explained by Kamalashila in his Last Stages of Meditation .  
 In short, all phenomena exist by way of impu tation; they do not exis t from their own side. 
We need to understand this. The conception of  phenomena as existing from their own side, 
or inherently, or truly is what is to be aban doned. Phenomena exist by way of being imputed 
by a name on a valid base. Regarding this, it is  recounted that a monk from Sera Monastery, 
J i g m e  W a n g y e l ,  o n c e  s a i d  t o  a n o t h e r  m o n k ,  D o rje Rinchen, “Dorje Rinchen, you are very 
bad.” When Dorje Rinchen aske d why he was bad, Jigme Wang yel explained that it was 
because he called one monk Sengge  (lion) and another Dom (bear), and yet called a dog Tara 
(female liberator)! Dorje Rinchen got a bit angry bu t could not reply since it is true that if we 
give something a name it should be given to a valid basis!   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C-5 It contradicts the many sutras and so forth that teach the lack of observed objects  
A [It contradicts the Compendium of All Sutras ] 
As regards [the statement] that a one-sided pa th is not enough, it is also clearly said 
in the Compendium of All Sutras :  
 
Bodhisattvas who are separated from  skillful means should not make 
strong effort in the profound dharma. 
 
A one-sided path – either the vast alone or th e profound alone – is not enough. Likewise, the 
Compendium of All Sutras  says that bodhisattvas who are wi thout skillful means should not put 
strong effort into understanding the profound dharma, that is, emptiness. In other words, 
bodhisattvas lacking method should not put e ffort into emptiness as there will be little 
benefit. In other words, a unio n of the method side and wisdom side is necessary. It is not 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
600 enough to have one side only, for example, an understanding of emptiness while lacking 
method.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B It contradicts the sutras since omniscience does arise when there is no observed object 
In the [Sutra of] Inconceivable Secrets  it says: 
 
Son of the lineage, it is like this . For instance, a fire burns due to a 
cause; if the cause is absent it will subside. Likewise, a mind blazes due 
to an observed object; if an observed object is absent it will subside. 
 
It contradicts sutra since omniscience does no t arise if there is no observed object. The Sutra 
of Inconceivable Secrets  sets out the example of fire which n eeds a cause, fuel, in order to blaze. 
When there is fuel, the fire will ignite. If there is  no fuel, the fire will extinguish. Similarly, if 
the mind has an observed object, it will blaze up. On the other hand, if the mind does not 
have an observed object, it will disappear.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How it is necessary to individually [investigate ] the meaning of the presence or absence of observed 
objects  
Therefore, the bodhisattva of skillf ul means knows how to completely 
extinguish observed objects by means of the thoroughly pure perfection 
of wisdom, {205} but he does not completely extinguish observed 
objects with respect to the roots of virtue. He does not allow the 
observed objects with respect to th e mental afflictions to arise, but 
settles on the observed objects of the perfections. He also individually 
investigates the observed object that is emptiness, but looks upon the 
observed object – all sentient beings – with great compassion. 
 
The individual statements regarding the w ay in which there is an observed object 
and the way in which there is no observ ed object need to be differentiated. 
 Some minds have observed objects and some do not, this difference must be understood. A bodhisattva who is skilled in method knows how to completely pacify the observed object by 
means of the thoroughly pure pe rfection of wisdom. However, he does not thoroughly pacify 
the observed objects with respect to  roots of virtue, that is, in th is case an observed object is 
necessary. On the other hand, he strives to no t allow the observed objects of the mental 
afflictions to arise, but instead seeks to preven t their arisal. For example, when an object of 
attachment is nearby us, attachme nt arises. When an observed object that we do not like is 
nearby us, hatred arises. Therefore, it is better to try not to observe such objects and to avoid 
them. On the other hand, we should try to bring virtuous objects closer to us, such as images 
of holy beings, Tara, Manjushri, or Avalokites hvara, and to observe them with our mind. For 
example, we should try to focus the mind sing le-pointedly on the observed object of calm 
abiding, whereby we bring this object closer an d closer. In this way, we can develop more and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
601 more concentration. On the other hand, if so meone who is our object of attachment comes 
close when we are doing single-pointed medita tion, immediately our mind will be distracted 
and our concentration interrupted. Therefore, some observed objects need to be brought 
closer while others need to be made distant.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D Although the bonds of the mental afflictions and apprehending signs have been loosened, it is 
inappropriate to grow lax in guarding and restraining 
Likewise, the bonds of the mental afflictions and apprehending signs need to be 
loosened, whereas the ropes of the trai nings need to be tightened. The two 
misdeeds need to destroyed, whereas virtuous  actions do not need to be destroyed. 
Therefore, being restrained by the traini ngs and being bound by the apprehension 
of signs are not the same, nor are loosening guarding and restraining and 
loosening the bonds of the conception of a self the same. Therefore, the meaning 
of destruction and self-liberation etc. need to be examined well. 
 The bondage of the mental afflictions, such as  attachment, and the bondage of apprehending 
signs, that is, apprehending true existence or in herent existence, need to be loosened, whereas 
the bondage of the trainings needs to be tigh tened. Thus, on the one hand, we need to 
abandon the mental afflictions and the apprehensi on of signs, while, on the other hand, we 
need to tighten our morality, that is, we need to guard our vows well, like tightening a rope, with mindfulness and introspection.  
 The two types of misdeeds – the natural and form ulated – need to be destroyed, that is, we 
need to cease them. On the other hand, we shou ld not put an end to virtuous actions, but 
instead strive to increase them by constantly putting more and mo re effort into them in order 
to strengthen them. Having taken vows, we need to constantly ex amine whether we are 
transgressing them; this is the morality of aban donment. For example, in the case of the five 
vows of lay people, the ten vows of novices, and the two hundred fifty-four vows of fully-
ordained monk, we should try to keep them stri ctly by always guarding the doors of our body 
and speech.   Our mind is bound by the apprehension of si gns, that is, the conception of all phenomena 
as existing truly or inherently. However, this bondage is not the same as being bound by our 
vows. Nor is loosening the bondage of apprehen ding signs and loosening the bondage of our 
vows the same. Therefore, we need to analyz e the meaning of the terms “destruction” and 
“self-liberation,” which were used by past scho lars. “Destruction” refers to the destruction of 
mental afflictions, non-virtuous actions, and so forth.   Thursday morning, 12 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 205) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3B3C-6 [Clarification of the position that applies equally to sutra and tantra] 
A [The object of rejection by Kamalashila] 
The way in which Master Kamalashila refu ted mental inattention does not refute 
meditating through initially setting [the mind on ] the view that has settled the 
meaning of the manner of being and then  setting it single-pointedly on that 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
602 without mentally attending to anything else . However, it does refute that the mere 
placement that sets the mind in not concei ving anything without placing it on the 
meaning settled by the view, the manner of being, constitutes meditation on 
emptiness. 
 
Kamalashila refuted the assertion that to no t think about anything is meditation on 
emptiness. While he did not refute meditating single-pointedly on emptiness, he did refute 
meditating single-pointedly on the emptiness th at is a mere lack of conceptuality. When 
meditating on emptiness, the mind must be focu sed single-pointedly on the final reality of all 
phenomena – suchness, thusness, or emptiness. Such meditation on emptiness is not refuted 
by Kamalashila, rather he refutes the assert ion that meditation on ultimate truth is 
meditation on a complete lack of observed obje cts. This is because we need to meditate on 
the observed object that is the emptiness of  all phenomena of true existence, inherent 
existence, or existence from th eir own side. We must focus the mind single-pointedly on this 
observed object. Therefore, this way of meditati ng on emptiness is not refuted. On the other 
hand, meditation without thinking about anyt hing and completely lacking an observed 
object, which is just a cause of cyclic existence, is refuted.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Demonstration that it will be explained that although the trainings in thoroughly complete method and 
wisdom are the same in both sutra and tantra, when individually investigated and so forth there are 
differences 
This is the same for the mantra and the perfection [vehicles]. However, I will 
explain the way in which there are similari ties and differences in the two systems 
regarding sustaining [meditation] throug h analyzing by means of the wisdom of 
individual investigation. 
 
The need to train in thoroughly complete me thod and wisdom is the same in sutra and 
tantra, or in the perfection vehicle and mantra  vehicle. In both cases the two need to be 
unified. However, there are some differences be tween sutra and tantra in terms of analytical 
meditation, which are yet to be explained. For ex ample, from the point of view of sutra, the 
mind that does analytical medita tion on emptiness is a coarse mi nd, whereas in tantra it is the 
subtle mind. In the sutra system there is not even a presentation of a very subtle mind. However, even in tantra, ordinary beings are said to be unable to use the very subtle mind to do analytical meditation on emptiness, that is, to identify and refute the object of negation. 
They too do this with a coarse mi nd, as in the case of sutra.
