 On the other hand, there is no difference in  the emptiness posited by sutra and tantra, and 
likewise there is no difference in the object of  negation. However, there are different objects 
of negation. For example, it is said that in the perspective of the meditative equipoise on 
emptiness of a learner, conventi onal phenomena such as forms an d so forth do not appear. In 
this sense forms and so forth are negated in th is context. However, conventional truths in 
general are not an object of negation. Thus, th ere are these different explanations depending 
on the context.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
603 2B4B-2B3B-3C Explanation of the actual stages of training in the trainings  
1 How to train in the Mahayana in general 
2 In particular how to train in the vajra vehicle 
The third {206} has two [points]: How to train in the Mahayana in general and in 
particular how to train in the vajra vehicle. 
Explanation of how to train in the conduc t of the conquerors’ children in general 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1 How to train in the Mahayana in general 
A Developing the wish to train in the trainings of bodhisattvas  
B Having developed the wish, taking the vows of the conquerors’ children 
C Having taken them, the way in which to train in them  
The first has three [points]: Developing the wish to train in the trainings of 
bodhisattvas; having developed the wish , taking the vows of the conquerors’ 
children; and having taken them, th e way in which to train in them. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1A Developing the wish to train in the trainings of bodhisattvas 
First: With respect to vinaya and tantra it is inappropriate to listen to the trainings 
without taking the vows oneself, but this  is different from them. Through first 
understanding the trainings, the continuum is  purified. Then, if there is delight in 
holding them, the vows are imparted. If yo u take the vows after having understood 
the trainings, taking them to mind as an  object, and having developed a heartfelt 
wish to train in them, they will be very st able. That is why this is a good method. 
 
First we need to develop the wish to train in th e trainings of bodhisattvas. Prior to taking the 
vows of vinaya and the tantra vows it is not perm itted to listen to or read the vows. However, 
in the case of bodhisattva vows, we can listen to and read the trainings prior to taking the 
vow. In other words, we can study the eighteen  root vows and forty-six secondary vows before 
actually taking them. Then, when we generate the wish to take these vows, they should be 
given to us. If we first study the vows, whereb y the mind becomes clear with respect to them 
and we generate the wish to train in them from  our heart, when we take the vows they will be 
very stable. Therefore, this is the best method . On the other hand, if we take them without 
understanding them, there will be difficulties late r on. For this reason it is permitted to study 
the bodhisattva vows prior to taking them. Howe ver, this is not the case of the vows of 
individual liberation and the tantra vows, as we  can only study them after having taken them. 
We need to understand this and to try to train in this way.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1B Having developed the wish, taking the vows of the conquerors’ children 
Second: In the commentary on the Ethics Chapter [of Asanga’s treatise on the 
Bodhisattva Levels ] I already extensively settled how to first take [the vows] and, 
following that, how to guard against the root downfalls and downfalls that are 
infractions, and how to restore [the vows] if they have been degenerated. 
Therefore, before the vows are taken, they  s h o u l d  d e f i n i t e l y  b e  l o o k e d  i n t o  a n d  
understood. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
604 In the commentary on the Ethics Chapter in the Bodhisattva Levels  by Asanga, Lama 
Tsongkhapa has already extensively explained ho w to take the bodhisattva vows, how to guard 
against the root downfalls and infractions, an d how to restore the vo ws if they have been 
degenerated. Therefore, before taking the vows,  one should definitely read about them and 
study them since it is permitted to do so  prior to taking the bodhisattva vows. By 
understanding the vows, we will delight in th e thought to take them and will generate the 
intention to safeguard them. After that, we  should take and then safeguard them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C Having taken them, the way in which to train in them 
1 What the foundation of the trainings is 
2 How the trainings are contained in it 
3 The stages by which to train in them 
The third has three [points]: what the fo undation of the trainings is, how the 
trainings are contained in it, and the stages by which to train in them. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-1 What the foundation of the trainings is  
First: Although there are infinite clear di visions, when grouped together by type, 
all the trainings of bodhisattvas are contai ned in the six perfections. Therefore, the 
six perfections constitute the great synopsis  that condenses all the essential points 
of the bodhisattva path.  
 
The foundation or basis of the trainings is th e six perfections. Although there are infinite 
divisions, when condensed they are all included  in the six perfections – generosity, morality, 
patience, joyous effort, concentr ation, and wisdom. Thus, all the trainings we need to train in 
are included in these six. Therefore, the six pe rfections are the great synopsis that condenses 
the essential points of the bodhisattva path.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2 How the trainings are contained in it 
A The actual meaning: the definite number [of the perfections] 
B In addition to tha t, their definite order 
The second has two [points]: The actual meaning: the definite number [of the 
perfections] and, in addition to that, their definite order. 
 
The perfections are definitely six in number, th ere being generosity, morality, patience, joyous 
effort, concentration, and wisdom. Their order is also definite: first generosity, second 
morality, third patience, and so forth. The re asons for this will be explained later. For 
example, since generosity is easier to practice th an morality, we first engage in the practice of 
generosity. Then, having practiced morality, we need to eliminate anger, short-temperedness, 
by practicing patience. Having developed patience , we may still be lazy and therefore need to 
develop our effort. Then having developed effo rt, we need to set our mind on an object 
whereby we develop concentration. Then, while one part of the mind is set on the object, 
another part of the mind does analytical medita tion on the reality of phenomena, whereby we 
develop wisdom. Perhaps this is the reason for the order, anyway it is what I remember now.   
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
605 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A The actual meaning: the definite number [of the perfections] 
1 The transition 
2 The actual [topic] 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A1 The transition 
First: {207} The Bhagavan has merely made  a total of six perf ections, whereas the 
holy regent of the conquerors, [Maitreya,] having elucidated the essential points of 
the reasons for them according to his intention, generated an ascertaining 
consciousness. The way in which their numb er is definite is those very points. 
Therefore, when you find certainty which ca ptivates the mind wi th respect to this, 
hold the instructions regarding the practi ce of the six perfections as supreme. 
 
Buddha Shakyamuni explained the six perfecti ons in brief. Then, Maitreya Buddha, the 
regent of Tushita who substituted the king, Buddha Shakyamuni, when he left Tushita to 
come to this world, taught how to practi ce the six perfections in detail in the Ornament for the 
Mahayana Sutras  and the Ornament for Clear Realizations , his commentary on the Perfection of 
Wisdom Sutras. We should study the six perfections as they are presented in the Ornament for 
the Mahayana Sutras  in conjunction with the Bodhisattva Levels , as was taught by His Holiness 
the Dalai Lama last year in Sera Monastery. Then, we should hold the instructions on the 
training in the six perfections to be supreme.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A2 The actual [topic] 
A The number is definite from the point of view of high status  
B The number is definite from the point of view of the accomplishment of the two welfares  
C The number is definite from the point of view of the complete accomplishment of the welfare 
of others in all aspects 
D The number is definite from the point of view of comprising the entire Mahayana 
E The number is definite from the point of view of [how the perfections] influence all aspects 
of the path or method 
F The number is definite from the point of view of the three trainings 
With regard to the perfections, the number is definite as six from the point of view 
of high status, the accomplishment of the two welfares, the complete 
accomplishment of the welfare of others in all aspects, comprising the entire 
Mahayana, how they influence all aspects of the path or me thod, and the three 
trainings. 
 
If we want to achieve a rebirth of high status, we need to rely on the six perfections; if we 
want to accomplish our own and others’ welfare, we  need to rely on the six perfections; if we 
want to accomplish the welfare of others, we need  to rely on the six perfections; since all the 
Mahayana practices are included in the six perfec tions, we need to rely on them; since they 
influence all aspects of the path or method, we need to rely on the six perfections; also to 
train in the three higher trainings of morality, concentration, and wisdom, we need to rely on 
the six perfections. Therefore, the perf ections are definitely six in number.  
 For example, we need the support that is a precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen 
qualities – the eight freedoms and ten endowmen ts. To obtain this support, we need to 
practice the six perfections. To obtain a human re birth it is necessary to practice morality. In 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
606 addition, we need wealth, that is , we need to be free from povert y and to achieve this we need 
to practice generosity. We also need an attr active body, therefore we need to practice 
patience. We also need to have a powerful an d magnificent body, due to which we need to 
practice joyous effort. We also need a mind that can remain single-pointed on its object, 
therefore we need to practice concentration. We  also need the intelligence that is able to 
investigate what to do and what not to do, th erefore we need to practice wisdom. If we 
practice all six perfections, then in our ne xt life we will obtain these qualities.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A2A The number is definite from the point of view of high status  
1 How the support has to be excellent in all its parts 
First: In order to bring the vast conduct of a bodhisattva to completion, you need 
many consecutive lives and, in order to progress along the path in them, you need 
a support that is complete in all its feat ures. Without it, even though you practice 
with a mere part like your current suppo rt, there will be no progress whatsoever. 
Thus, a support that is excellent in all its parts is necessary. 
 
To achieve buddhahood we need to be able to co ntinue to practice throughout many lives. To 
progress along the path, we need a body that ha s all characteristics complete. If even one is 
missing, it will be difficult to make progress.  Since we presently have a body with all 
characteristics complete, we need to put more  and more effort into practice whereby our 
inner qualities will develop. This is important.  We need a perfect body and a perfect mind.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Having identified the excellent support, how the causes that are corresponding causes for that are 
achieved 
Furthermore, you need [a support ] that is endowed with the four excellences: (1) 
resources at your disposal, (2) a body with  which to practice, (3) companions with 
whom to practice, (4) the ends of ac tions that are to be undertaken.  
 
Having identified what kind of support we need, that is, what kind of body, we need to create causes that are similar to the result we want to  obtain. We need a support possessing the four 
excellences: excellent resources, an excell ent body, excellent surrounding people, and 
excellent completion of whatever actions we unde rtake. The resources that we use need to be 
excellent as otherwise we will experience proble ms, such as harm to the body or even death. 
For example, if our food is not good it may make us sick and may even cause our death.  
 We also need an excellent body, that is, one th at is healthy. If our body is weak and lacking 
in energy, we will not be able to practice th e Dharma. We also need excellent people with 
whom to live, work, and study because when we do something together with good people the 
result will be good. If, on the other hand, we are surrounded by misleading people they will 
influence us negatively and our behavior will worsen. For example, if we have friends who drink and smoke, we will tend to do the same. Due to them, we might even end up living on 
the streets or in the woods, whereby we may ev entually become ill and die. Therefore, we 
need to ensure that we have good companions.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
607  In addition, our actions should be excellent. For example, if we set up a shop, we should 
avoid cheating others, selling things that are stol en, for example, by the Mafia, and so forth as 
otherwise one day the police will arrive and pu t us in prison. In other words, our actions 
should accord with the laws of the country in which we live.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
However, due merely to these excellences,  many become conditions for the mental 
afflictions. Thus, it is also necessary no t to come under the power of the mental 
afflictions. But that too is  not enough, you need to distinguish well the observed 
objects that are to be unmistakenly enga ged in and turned away from with respect 
to the essential point of what is to be ad opted and what is to be discarded because, 
otherwise, the excellences will destroy themselves, just like bamboo and plantain 
trees are destroyed by bearing fruit and li ke a mule is destroyed by pregnancy.  
 If you have wisdom, you will understand that [the excellences] are the results of previous good actions, and, striving in th e causes once again, they will increase. If 
you do not have wisdom, by using the resu lts you previously accumulated they will 
be exhausted. Since you do not newly increase them, they will become the 
beginning of suffering in future lives. Henc e, the arisal of the six [perfections] in 
other lives does not come about without a cause nor from a cause that does not 
correspond with them. Also the corresp onding causes are sequential. Thus, 
perfections are definite as six. 
 Although we may have these excellences, the ment al afflictions can still arise, therefore we 
need to avoid coming under their power and pr event their arisal. In addition, we need to 
perfectly and clearly distinguish what is to be di scarded and what is to be adopted. If we do 
not do this, the excellences will destroy themselv es, like a mule, which is usually unable to 
become pregnant, is destroyed by pregnancy.   If we have wisdom, we will put effort into crea ting the causes to have a good result. If we 
do not have wisdom, that is, if we are not in telligent, we will exhaust the results that we 
accumulated in the past and will not create new causes, due to which in the next life we will 
experience suffering. If we continue to practice  the six perfections the result will come about 
in other lives. On the other hand, if we do not create the cause of similar type to the result, 
the result will not come about. Therefore, it is important to practice all six perfections in 
order that we obtain a good rebirth in our next life.   Thursday afternoon, 12 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 208) says:  
3 Relating what has been said above with the scriptures and distinguishing the high status of the time 
of the path and the result in detail 
The resources and so forth on the occasion  of the path are temporary high status. 
Culminated high status – an excellent bo dy and so forth – exists on the buddha 
ground. That too is mentioned in  the Ornament for the Sutras (Sutralamkara) : 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
608 Excellent resources and body,  
Excellent retinue and undertakings are high status,  As well as not falling under the sway of the afflictions and 
Being unmistaken with respect to activities.
175 
 
There is a high status of the time of the path and a high status of the time of the result. The 
high status at the time of the path which is a good rebirth with resources and so forth is 
temporary high status. The culminated high status  of an excellent body and so forth exists on 
the buddha ground. For example, although Buddha Shakyamuni had a human body, he had 
completely purified and achieved all qualitie s of the exalted body. Our body, which possesses 
some good qualities, is temporary high status th at can be used to obtain higher realizations 
and eventually buddhahood. Therefore, it too is important.  
 The Ornament for the Mahayana Sutras  says that excellent resource s, excellent body, excellent 
surrounding people, and excellent undertakings are high status. Also by not coming under 
the power of the mental afflictions and not doin g mistaken actions, we make our high rebirth 
useful and in our next life we will improve more and more.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A2B The number is definite from the point of view of the accomplishment of 
the two welfares 
1 The transition 
2 The actual [topic] 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A2B-1 The transition 
Second: When training in the bodhisa ttva conduct with such a support, the 
activities of bodhisattvas are exhausted in the two: those that accomplish one’s 
own welfare and those that accomplish others’ welfare. 
 
Since we have a rebirth possessing excellent resour ces, an excellent body, an excellent retinue, 
and excellent actions, and we do  not come under the control of the mental afflictions and do 
unmistaken actions, we should train in the bo dhisattva conduct. There are only two types of 
bodhisattva conduct: activities for one’s own welfare and activities for others’ welfare. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A2B-2 The actual [topic] 
A How the first three of the six perfections enact others’ welfare  
Moreover, in order to accomplish the welfare of  others it is necessary, first of all, to 
benefit them by means of material gifts. Since generosity accompanied by harm to 
sentient beings does not lead to anything , to excellently turn away from actions 
that harm others together with their bases is itself of great benefit to others; 
                                              
175 Those six characteristics of the higher realms consti tute results related to the six perfections as their 
respective causes: excellent commodities as the result of gi ving, excellent bodies as the result of ethics, excellent 
companions as the result of patience, conditions for th e accomplishment of one’s enterprises as the result of 
joyous effort and the absence of wrong views concerning  actions as the result of wisdom. (cf. Wayman, Alex: 
Ethics of Tibet, Delhi 1992, S. 106) 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
609 therefore, ethics is necessary. [However,] given that pure ethics cannot come about 
if you retaliate once or twice due to bein g unable to bear being harmed, {209} the 
patience that in the face of harm thinks “It does not matter” is necessary in order 
to culminate [ethics]. By not retaliating out of [patience], others are prevented 
from accumulating a lot of negativities and apply themselves to virtue through 
being inspired by that, whereby it is of great benefit to others. 
 
The first three of the six perfec tions – generosity, morality, and patience – enact the welfare of 
others. Initially we need to enact others’ welfare by giving them material things, this being the 
practice of generosity. Then, we need to practice  morality in order to enact others’ welfare by 
turning away from actions that harm others to gether with their bases. Among the ten non-
virtuous actions, the three non-virtuous action s of mind are the bases upon which we engage 
in physical and verbal actions that harm othe rs. We harm others with  our body by killing 
them, stealing from them, and engaging in sexu al misconduct, and we harm others with our 
speech by lying and thereby cheating them, by  engaging in divisive speech that creates 
disharmony between people who were previously  in harmony, by speaking harsh words that 
harm others’ minds, and by means of idle talk which, while it may seem to not be a problem, 
harms others by wasting their time and preven ting them from workin g, practicing Dharma, 
and so forth. Therefore, in order to be of benefit to others we need to engage in the practice 
of morality.   If we cannot bear being harmed by others, wh en someone says or does something bad to us 
we will immediately retaliate with the same kind  of harm, due to which our morality will not 
be pure. Thus, when someone harms us, we n eed to practice the patience that remains 
undisturbed thinking “It doesn’t matter.” If, when others treat us badly, we do not retaliate, 
this will bring about a change in the other pe rson’s mind whereby we will prevent him from 
accumulating negativities and cause him to emulate our good actions and thereby apply himself to virtue. In other words, if we real ly practice patience no matter what the other 
p e r s o n  s a y s ,  h e  t o o  w i l l  c a l m  d o w n  a n d  b e  i n s p i r e d  t o  p r a c t i c e  p a t i e n c e ,  w h e r e b y  h e  w i l l  create virtuous actions that benefit himself. In this way, the other person creates less negative 
actions and cultivates patience, whereby we benefit him, as well as ourselves.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B What enacts one’s own welfare 
If your own welfare is to attain the happine ss of complete liberation by the force of 
wisdom, you should moreover set your mind in equipoise by means of 
concentration through not allowing your mi nd to become distracted. Thus, it is 
necessary to achieve a serviceability of body  that abides on the observed object as 
long as you wish.  
 
Wisdom is what enacts our own welfare. To achi eve it, we need calm abiding. To achieve this, 
we need to set our mind in equipoise by means of concentration. By means of this, we will be 
able to meditate for however long we want on an observed object. The mind will become stable and serviceable, that is, it will be tire less, happy, and pliant. Likewise, our body, no 
matter how long we remain in the meditation posi tion, will also remain tireless and pliant. In 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
610 short, calm abiding leads to pliancy of body an d mind. We need to achieve this as a basis for 
developing wisdom.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C How joyous effort becomes the foundation of both welfares 
Since this does not arise in the lazy, you n eed to undertake it with the joyous effort 
that does not relent day and night. Therefore, this is their foundation. 
 
Joyous effort is necessary for enacting both our own and others’ welfare since the lazy cannot 
achieve calm abiding and special insight. For this reason, we need to put strong effort, 
without laziness, into achieving them day and nigh t. In other words, because joyous effort is 
the foundation or basis of single-pointed medita tion and analytical meditation, it is important 
to develop it in our daily life.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D A scriptural passage to that effect 
Therefore, in order to enact the two welfar es, the perfections are definite in being 
six. [In the Ornament of the Sutras ] it says: 
 
Your commitment for the welfare of sentient beings 
Should be fulfilled through giving, not harming, and patience. By means of abiding and liberation, together with their foundation,  Bring about your own welfare in all aspects. 
 To accomplish the two welfares, one’s own and others, all six perfections are necessary. Those who put effort into enacting the welfare of ot her sentient beings need to put effort into 
giving, that is, generosity; into not harming othe rs, that is, morality; an d into the practice of 
patience. “Abiding” refers to the mind that  abides single-pointedly on its object 
(concentration), and “liberation” to the free dom from the mental afflictions and suffering 
that is obtained by means of wisdom. “Together with their foundation” refers to joyous effort. 
In this way, the six perfections are necessa ry to achieve our own and others’ welfare. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 209) says:  
E One does not set others in complete liberation in dependence on wisdom  
This does not comprise others’ welfare in all aspects.  
 
In order to enact the welfare of others, we need  to rely on wisdom. Without this, we cannot 
set others in liberation from the suffering of cy clic existence. In other words, without wisdom, 
we will not know how to liberate others from cyclic existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
611 F Identifying the respective sequence of the last two [perfections] 
In the statement “abiding and liberation,” the mind abiding  on the observed object 
is said to be the work of meditative concentration and liberation  from cyclic 
existence to be the work of wisdom. Wh en these two are distinguished, calm 
abiding will not be mistaken to be special insight.  
 
“Abiding” refers to the mind abiding on its observed object, which follows concentration. 
This is calm abiding. “Liberation” refers to libe ration from cyclic existence, it follows wisdom. 
This is special insight. If we distinguish thes e two, we will not mistake calm abiding to be 
special insight.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A2C The number is definite from the point of view of the complete 
accomplishment of the welfare of others in all aspects 
Third: At the outset eliminate the poverty of sentient beings with material things. 
Following that, also do not harm to any se ntient being. Not only that, be patient 
when harmed. Due to being your companio n, act with undaunted joyous effort. 
Through relying on concentration, with magical emanations an d so forth provide 
whatever he wishes. When he has beco me a suitable vessel, give him good 
explanations in dependence on wisdom. By severing his doubt, {210} liberate him. 
Because of this, the perfecti ons are definite as six. 
 
Without poverty and without harming Patient with harm and not discouraged by activities, Being joyful and speaking well; Because of this, others’ welfare is one’s own welfare.  
 Initially we should give other sentient beings material things, thereby eliminating the suffering 
of poverty. Then, we should not harm any sentie nt being; this being the practice of morality. 
When someone harms us, we should not retaliat e; this being the practice of patience. We 
should help our companions with undaunted jo yous effort. Relying on concentration, we 
should develop qualities such as the clairvo yance of magical emanat ion and so forth. In 
accordance with others’ minds, dispositions, and so forth, we should give them excellent 
teachings based on wisdom. By severing their do ubts, we should liberate them. In this way, 
the perfections are definite as six.   By being without poverty, not harming, bein g patient with harm, not being discouraged by 
activities, being joyful, and speaking well, we ac hieve others’ welfare and at the same time our 
own. In other words, when we enact others ’ welfare, we simultaneously enact our own 
welfare. On the other hand, if we try to enact our own welfare alone, we will experience many 
problems and interferences, whereas if we strive  to enact others’ welfare they will help us in 
return, whereby there will be mutual benefit. Th erefore, whatever action we do, we should do 
it to help others. In this way we wi ll fulfill our own and others’ welfare.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
612 2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A2D The number is definite from the point of view of comprising the entire 
Mahayana 
Fourth: Thus, you will not be attached to  resources that have been acquired and 
will not view resources that you have no t acquired with interest. When that is 
present, you will be able to guard the trainings, whereby you will take up ethical 
discipline and respect it. Due to patience towards sufferings that arise in 
dependence on sentient beings and non-sentient beings, you will not be 
d i s c o u r a g e d .  D u e  t o  a p p l y i n g  y o u r s e l f  t o  and delighting in any kind of virtuous 
activities, you will not be discouraged by them. You will meditate the non-
conceptual yoga of calm abiding and special insight. These six encompass the 
entire Great Vehicle of migrating beings. They are sequentially accomplished by the six perfections and nothing more than them are necessary.   
Not strongly liking resources and highest respect, Not being discouraged with respect to the two, and The non-conceptual yoga; 
The entire Mahayana is exhausted in this.   [ Sutralamkara  XVI, 5] 
 
If we acquire resources we will not become atta ched to them, and if we do not acquire them 
we will not seek them. Due to this, we will be able to train in morality, that is, to guard and respect morality. When we experience sufferings caused by sentient beings who harm us and 
by inanimate things, such as water, fire, wind , earthquake, snowstorms , flood, and so forth, 
we will be able to bear this patiently. Then, without becoming sad or discouraged, we will put 
effort into and delight in virtue. Then, we will engage in the non-conceptual yoga or union of 
calm abiding and special insight. These six in clude all the qualities of the Mahayana. We 
should train in accomplishing them sequentially  as nothing more than that is necessary.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A2E The number is definite from the point of view of how [the perfections] 
influence all aspects of the path or method: how the six perfections become the method for 
accomplishing any of the individual aspects 
1 The way in which generosity is contained in the path 
Fifth: The path or the meth od of non-attachment to re sources that are objects you 
have already acquired is generosity becaus e, by familiarizing yourself with giving 
them away, you become free from attachment to them. 
 
What method gives rise to what result? The path or method of no n-attachment to the 
resources that are objects we have already acquir ed is generosity because by familiarizing with 
giving them away we will become free from atta chment to them. Therefore, generosity is a 
method or path.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The way in which ethical discipline acts as a method  
The method for restraining the distraction of exerting yourself for the sake of 
acquiring objects that have not yet been ac quired is ethical discipline because in 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 9-12 May 2005 (13) 
613 one with the vows of a fully ordained monk all the distractions of ends of actions 
do not arise. 
 
The method for stopping the distraction of seek ing to obtain something that has not yet been 
obtained is morality because in one with th e vows of a fully-ordained monk there does not 
arise the distractions of wanting wealth and so  forth. In other words, a fully-ordained monk, 
who is trying to abide in accordance with the instructions of the vinaya, strives not to think 
about obtaining that which he does not have, fo r example, a watch, a cell phone with which 
to send messages and take photos, and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The way in which patience acts as a method 
The method for not giving sentient beings  up is patience because of not being 
depressed by all the sufferings of being harmed.  
 
The method for not giving sentient beings up is  patience because we do not become disturbed 
by all the sufferings of harm done to us. Ther e are three types of patience: the patience of 
non-retaliation, the patience of bearing difficu lties, and the patience of bearing suffering. 
With patience, the mind remains undisturbed an d does not give up sentient beings in the 
sense of neglecting them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 The way in which joyous effort acts as a method  
{211} The method for increasing virtue  is joyous effort because through 
undertaking [virtue] with joyous effort it increases. 
 
The method for increasing virtue is joyous e ffort because through undertaking virtue with 
joyous effort our delight in virtue increase s and our qualities increase more and more.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
5 The way in which the last two [perfections] act as a method  
The method for completely purifying the ob scurations is the last two perfections 
because the afflictions are completely  purified by concentration and the 
obscurations to objects of knowledge are completely purified by wisdom.  
 Because of this, the perfec tions are definite as six. 
 
The method for completely purifying the ob scurations is the last two perfections, 
concentration and wisdom, because the affliction s are completely purified by concentration 
and the obscurations to knowledge are complete ly purified by wisdom. In other words, the 
two types of obscurations, the afflictive obscurations and obscurations to knowledge, are 
completely purified by concentration and wisdom . Therefore, the perfecti ons are definitely six 
in number.

615 Monday afternoon, 16 May 2005 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 211) says:  
 
Once you have reached the path of non-attachment to objects, 
Another one is to curb distractions. 
Not to give up sentient beings, to increase, and 
To purify obscurations completely are further paths. [Sutralamkara  XVI, 6] 
 
The last two perfections purify the obscurations , that is, the perfection of concentration and 
the perfection of wisdom respec tively purify the afflictive ob scurations and the obscurations 
to knowledge, that is, to knowing all aspect s of phenomena directly and simultaneously. 
Therefore, the perfections are definitely six in number.  
 Having attained the path of no n-attachment to objects, such as our belongings, we will be 
able to practice the morality that restrains from distraction. To not give up sentient beings is 
the perfection of patience as due to it one stri ves to take care of all sentient beings without 
exception. To increase one’s virtuous actions is  the perfection of joyous effort, the fourth 
perfection. The paths that completely purify th e two obscurations, the afflictive obscurations 
and the obscurations to knowledge, are the perf ection of concentration and the perfection of 
wisdom. In this way, we can achieve all ou r aims by means of the six perfections.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2A2F The number is definite from the point of view of the three trainings 
1 How [the perfections] are contained in the three trainings 
Sixth: The entity of the training in ethical discipline is ethical discipline. 
Moreover, since it is adopted when there is  the generosity that is indifferent to 
resources, [generosity ] is the collection of ethical discipline. Also [when ethical 
discipline ] is adopted, since it is protected by  the patience that does not return 
abuse for abuse, [patience] is in its retinue. Concentration is the training in the 
mind, whereas wisdom is the training in wisdom. Joyous effort is included in all 
three trainings. Therefore, the pe rfections are definite as six. 
 
From the point of view of the three trainings, The Conqueror perfectly explained six perfections: The first is the three, Two are aspects of the last two, 
And one is included in all three.   [ Sutralamkara  XVI, 7] 
 
The three trainings are the higher training in mo rality, the higher training in concentration, 
and the higher training in wisdom. All six perf ections are included in these three trainings. 
The entity of the training in morality is mo rality. Since it is adopted when there is the 
generosity that does not view resources, that is , when we use what we are offered for the sake 
of the donor receiving merit, generosity is includ ed in the collection of morality. The patience 
that does not retaliate when someone says someth ing bad to us is included in the retinue of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
616 morality. In this way, the first three perfec tions, generosity, morality, and patience, are 
included in the higher training of morality. Conc entration is the training in the mind, that is, 
it is the higher training in concentration. Wi sdom is the higher trai ning in wisdom. Joyous 
effort is included in all three higher trainings.  In other words, joyous effort is needed when 
training in morality, when training in the mi nd or concentration, and when training in 
wisdom. Hence, the perfections are definite as six.  
 The Ornament for the Mahayana Sutras  similarly says that from th e point of view of the three 
trainings in morality, concentration, and wisdom, the Conqueror, Buddha Shakyamuni, 
perfectly explained six perfections. The first, th e training in morality, consists of the three, 
generosity, morality, and patience. The last two, concentration and wisdom, are included 
respectively in the training in concentration an d training in wisdom. One, joyous effort, is 
included in all three.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Hence the importance of understanding that the number is definite 
Thus, that which, by means of whatever  excellent support thoroughly completes 
whatever welfare, one’s own or others’, through abiding in either vehicle and 
possessing however many types of methods,  thoroughly completes and synthesizes 
the support that accomplishes any of the tr ainings, the welfare, the great vehicle, 
the method, and the trainings should be  understood to be those very six 
perfections. You should think about this un til you gain great certainty that they are 
the sum of all the essential points  of the bodhisattva practices. 
 
By practicing morality we can obtain the excelle nt support or body that is a precious human 
rebirth possessing the eighteen qualities. Wi th this, we can practice all six perfections 
completely. The six perfections contain all the e ssential points of the bo dhisattva conduct. As 
long as we have not achieved this certainty, we should think about and meditate on this. By 
understanding this, we should practice them. Thus, we should practice the six perfections 
every day. In addition, because all six perfection s are included in each of the perfection, there 
is also the generosity of generosity, the morality  of generosity, and so forth; the generosity of 
morality, the morality of morality, and so forth;  the generosity of patience, the morality of 
patience, and so forth; the generosity of joyous  effort, the morality of joyous effort, and so 
forth; the generosity of concen tration, the morality of concen tration, and so forth; and the 
generosity of wisdom, the morality of wisdom, an d so forth. In this way there are thirty-six 
practices in which to engage. This is explained in the Ornament for Clear Realizations  in the 
context of the armor-like achieving.   (Oral transmission, 18:45-19:00, pp. 212-221)  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-2B In addition to  that, the order is definite 
The second has three [points]: the order in  which they are generated, the order in 
terms of the inferior and superior, and the order in terms of the coarser and 
subtler. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
617 1 The order in which they are generated 
First: If there is generosity that is indi fferent to and unattached to resources, you 
will adopt ethical discipline. If you possess the ethical discipline that refrains from 
faulty conduct, you will be patient with regard to harm. If you have the patience 
that does not despair with respect to hardsh ips, you will be able to act with joyous 
effort since adverse circumstances will be few. Through acting with joyous effort 
day and night, the concentration that is able to set the mind in virtue will be 
generated. When the mind is  in meditative equipoise, th e realization of the perfect 
just as it is will arise.  
 
If there is the generosity that is not attached to resources, th at is, belongings and wealth, we 
will adopt morality. If we possess the morality that refrains from faulty conduct, we will be 
patient with regard to harm from others. If we  have the patience that is not discouraged by 
hardships, we will have joyous effort due to  experiencing few unfavorable conditions. With 
joyous effort day and night, the mind will be come more stable and concentration will arise. 
When the mind is in meditative  equipoise, the wisdom realizing emptiness, the perfect, will 
arise. In short, we first generate generosity, th en morality, then patience, then joyous effort, 
then concentration, and then wisdom in our continuum. In this way, they are gradually 
generated one after the other.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The order in terms of the inferior and superior 
Second: The previous ones are inferi or, and the latter ones superior.  
 
Among generosity and morality, generosity is inferior and morality is superior. In the same way, each of the successive perfections is superior  to the previous ones. Morality is superior to 
generosity because it is more difficult to prac tice that generosity. Then between patience and 
morality, patience is more difficult to practice th an morality. In this way, each of the previous 
perfections is inferior to the successive one.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The order in terms of the coarser and subtler 
Third: Since the previous ones are easier to engage in and do than the latter ones, 
they are coarser. Since the latter ones are more difficult to engage in and do than 
the previous, they are more subtle. In the Ornament of the Sutras  is says: 
 
Because the later are generated in dependence on the previous, 
Because they abide as inferior and superior, Because they are coarser and subtler, 
They were taught in this order. [XVI, 14] 
 
Since, between engaging in generosity and engagi n g  i n  m o r a l i t y ,  i t  i s  e a s i e r  t o  e n g a g e  i n  
generosity than to engage in morality, generosi ty is considered to be coarser then morality. 
Morality, in turn, is more subtle than generosity since it is more difficult to engage in. Since, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
618 between engaging in morality and engaging in pa tience, it is easier to engage in morality, 
morality is coarser and patience is subtler. Th us, in the end engaging in wisdom is subtler 
than engaging in the other perfections, including engaging in concentration. The Ornament of 
the Sutras  says that the later ones are generated in dependence on the previous; thus, first 
there is generosity, then morality, and so forth.  Likewise, generosity is inferior to morality, 
morality is inferior to patience, and so forth, that is, each one is successively superior to the 
previous. In addition, generosity is coarser than morality in that it is  easier than morality, 
morality is coarser than patience in that it is  easier than patience, and so forth. For these 
reasons, the six perfections were taught in a particular order.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3 The stages by which one trains in them

The training in the bodhisattva conduct in genera l is the training in the six perfections. This 
is explained in detail and then summarized. In addition, the training in the last two 
perfections, concentration and wisdom, is then explained in particular.

qualities and training in the four ways of gathering that ripen others’ mental
