2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A Training in the perfections that ripen one’s own buddha qualities 
1 How to train in generosity 
2 How to train in ethical discipline 
3 How to train in patience 
4 How to train in joyous effort 
5 How to train in concentration 
6 How to train in wisdom 
The first has six [points]: how to train in generosity, ethical discipline, patience, 
joyous effort, concentration, and wisdom. 
 
We train in the six perfections of generosity, morality, patience , joyous effort, concentration, 
and wisdom in order to ripen the buddha qualities in our own continuum, whereas we train 
in the four means of gathering disciples in orde r to ripen others’ continua. Generosity is the 
mind of giving. We can give away our body, posse ssions, and roots of vi rtue. Morality is the 
mind of abandonment. Patience is an undisturbe d mind. Joyous effort is the mind delighting 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
619 in virtue. Concentration is a mind single-point edly focused on a virtuous observed object. 
Wisdom is a mind that analyzes its object.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1 How to train in generosity 
A The entity of generosity 
B Subdivisions 
C How to generate [generosity ] in one's mental continuum 
The first has three points: the entity of  generosity, subdivisions, and how to 
generate [generosity ] in one’s mental continuum. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1A The entity of generosity 
1 Entity 
First: [The entity of generosity] is a virt uous intention to give and the actions of 
body and speech motivated by it. {213} It is  the intention at the time the body and 
speech engage in generosity. 
 
The entity of generosity is a virtuous intent ion to give and the actions of body and speech 
motivated by it, thus, it is the intention at the time the body and speech engage in generosity.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The measure of its perfection  
The completion of the perfection of ge nerosity does not depend on having 
eliminated the poverty of sentient beings by giving away substances which are 
objects to be given to others. Rather it  becomes the perfection of generosity 
through destroying the grasping of mi serliness and thoroughly completing 
familiarization with the mind of giving to others, along with the results of giving.  
 
How does generosity become a perfection? The pe rfection of generosity is not completed by 
giving substances to others whereby their povert y is eliminated. Rather it is a mind that has 
eliminated all miserliness and has thoroughly completed familiarity with giving to others, 
along with the result of giving. This  is the perfection of generosity.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1B Subdivisions 
1 Subdivisions from the point of view of individual supports 
2 Subdivisions of the entity of generosity itself 
The second has two [points]: Subdivisions from the point of view of individual 
supports, and subdivisions of the entity of generosity itself. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1B1 Subdivisions from the point of view of individual supports 
A Explanation of the individual generosities for the individual supports  
In the Bodhisattva Pratimoksha  it is taught that generosi ty of material things is 
generally practiced by lay bodhisattvas, whereas ordained bodhisattvas should do 
the generosity of the Dharma rather than generosity of material things. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
620 The Bodhisattva Pratimoksha  says that lay bodhisattvas should practice giving away material 
things, whereas ordained bodhisattvas should practice giving the Dharma. This is because 
ordained bodhisattvas are less busy than lay bo dhisattvas and therefore have more time to 
study and hear the Dharma. If a bodhisattva is wealthy and has many th ings, he should give 
them away. If a bodhisattva is poor, he should not put effort into finding a wealthy benefactor 
as this will become a distra ction to keeping his vows.  
 
Tuesday morning, 17 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 213) says:  
B The achieving and giving of material things that interrupts the hearing and so forth of the ordained is 
prohibited, whereas if they are acquired without harm to [their hearing and so forth ] they should give 
Moreover, in the Compendium of Trainings , intending that which interrupts hearing 
and so forth, it says that the giving of gifts by the ordained through working for the 
sake of material things is prohibited. Ho wever, if they obtain them in plenty 
through the power of previous merit without harm to their virtuous activities, they 
should give material things. Sharawa also  said: “To you I will not talk about the 
advantages of giving, however I will talk ab out the disadvantages of grasping.” This 
is the talk of one who was not pleased wh en he was given gifts that the ordained 
had toiled to obtain and through accumu lating them often damaged their ethical 
discipline.  
 
If the obtaining of material things interrupts the hearing, thinking, and meditating of the 
ordained, they should not practice the generosi ty of giving material things. On the other 
hand, if there is no such interruption, the ordain ed should also give material things to others. 
This is mentioned in the Compendium of Trainings  because if the main purpose of the ordained 
becomes the obtaining of material things, this  becomes a distraction to hearing the Dharma 
and so forth. On the other hand, if the ordained  easily find material things due to having 
created virtuous actions in the past and their givi ng of material things does not interfere with 
their hearing, thinking, and meditating, then the ordained should also practice the generosity 
of giving material things. Sharawa said “I will no t talk about the advantages of generosity but I 
will express the disadvantages of grasping.”  He was not pleased with monks who strove 
tirelessly to gain material things due to which their morality was damaged.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1B2 Subdivisions of  the entity of generosity itself 
A  The generosity of the Dharma 
 Second: The generosity of the Dharma is  to unmistakenly teach the holy Dharma, 
to teach as suitable, without misdeeds, the worldly aims of actions such as crafts 
and so forth, and to make [others] engage  in upholding the basis of the trainings. 
 
The generosity of giving the Dharma is to unmi stakenly teach others the holy Dharma of the 
Buddha. It also consists in teaching worldly actions as long as they are not misdeeds, and 
making others uphold the basis of the trainings.  For example, it includes teaching others the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
621 inner sciences since we should give our knowledg e to others so that they too can gain more 
wisdom. This will help them and also enable them in turn to help others.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The generosity of fearlessness 
The generosity of fearlessness is  {214} to thoroughly protect [others] from the fear 
of humans such as kings, bandits, and so forth, from the fear of non-humans such 
as lions, tigers, and so forth, and fear of  the elements such as water, fire, and so 
forth. 
 
The generosity of fearlessness is to protect othe rs from fear of human beings who harm them, 
such as kings, bandits, and so forth; from the fear of ferocious animals such as lions, tigers, 
bears, and snakes; and the fear of the elements, water, fire, an d so forth. When we protect 
others from such fear, this is the generosity of fearlessness.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C The generosity of material things 
The generosity of material things is to give material things to others. 
 
The generosity of material things is to give othe rs whatever is of benefit to them such as food, 
clothing, houses, cars, telephones, and so forth,.  
 We should try to practice these three types of ge nerosity as much as we can. This is what is 
meant by the practice of generosity. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1C How to generate [generosity ] in one's continuum 
1 How in order to complete the perfection of generosity the mind giving away all one’s possessions is 
necessary in that it is not merely the destruction of miserliness 2 Thinking about the advantages of giving and the disadvantages of not giving  
3 The actual method for engaging in [generosity ] 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1C1 How in order to complete the perfection of generosity the mind giving away all 
one’s possessions is necessary in that it is not just the destruction of miserliness 
Third: By merely destroying all miserline ss without exception wi th respect to one’s 
body and possessions, it does not become the perfection of generosity because, 
since miserliness is included in the fact or of attachment, the two [types of] foe-
destroyers of the Lesser Vehicle have al so abandoned it without exception along 
with all its seeds. Hence, it is not just th e elimination of the interference to giving, 
the holding on to all [things] that is miserliness, but one must generate the 
thought, from the bottom of one’s heart,  to give all one’s possessions away to 
others. 
 
We should give away our body as well as our possessions and wealth to others. By merely destroying miserliness, it does not become the perfection of generosity. Since miserliness is 
included in the factor of attachment, the two Hinayana foe-destroyers, the hearer and solitary 
realizer foe-destroyers, have also abandoned it al ong with its seeds. Ther efore, in addition to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
622 eliminating the interference to giving, that is, holding on to things, we must also develop the 
wish from the bottom of our heart to give away all our possessions in order to benefit others. 
In short, to complete the perfection of ge nerosity is not enough to merely eliminate 
miserliness, we also need to generate the wish , out of love, from the depths of our heart to 
give away all our possessions to ot hers in order to benefit them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1C2 Thinking about the advantages of giving and the disadvantages of not giving  
For that, it is necessary to meditate bo th on the disadvantages of thoroughly 
holding on [to things] and on the advantages  of giving. The former are as taught in 
the Moon Lamp [Sutra] :  
 
Since the body is not clean, the life  force fluctuates like water gushing 
down a steep mountain, and both body  and life force are controlled by 
karma due to which they lack an independent self, you should cease 
attachment to them by seeing them  t o  b e  f a l s e ,  l i k e  a  d r e a m  o r  a  
mirage. If you do not reverse [attachment ], having come under the 
control of attachment, you will a ccumulate great faulty conduct and 
migrate to the bad migrations. 
 
The second, [the advantages of giving,] are as taught in the Compendium of 
Trainings : 
 
Thus, my body and mind  
Pass away in every instant. If through this body, impermanent  And tainted, permanent and  Untainted enlightenment is attained, Is this not invaluable? 
 Holding on to things has many disadvantages, whereas giving has many advantages. We need 
to meditate on these. The Lamp of the Moon Sutra  says that out of miserliness we hold on to 
our body and life force and are unable to give th em away. However, our bo dy is a collection of 
impure substances and our lifespan decreases li ke water cascading down a steep mountain. In 
addition, they are both under the control of ot her, that is, the control of actions, due to 
which they lack an independent self. Due to th is, we should eliminate the attachment to our 
body and life that think “my body” and “my life” by seeing them to be false, like a dream or a mirage. If we do not turn this attachment back, under the power of attachment we will create 
great faulty conduct, that is, negative actions, that will become the caus e for us to migrate to 
the bad migrations, that is, to be reborn in the lower realms in our next life. In short, out of 
attachment to our body and life, we create many non-virtuous acti ons that become the 
projecting karma that will throw us into th e lower realms – the hell realm, hungry ghost 
realm, or animal realm. Therefore, we must stri ve to give up attachment to our body and life, 
as well as our belongings.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
623  The Compendium of Trainings  says that our body and mind change in each instant, whereby 
they quickly pass away. However, by means of  this impermanent and stained body, we can 
achieve the permanent and stainless enlightenment.  A l t h o u g h  i t  i s  d i f f i c u l t  t o  s e e  t h a t  o u r 
body changes in every instant, we can easily see that our mind is constantly changing, for 
example, first wanting something and then no lo nger wanting it, and so forth. Our body ages 
in each day, even though we cannot see the subtle changes. Our body is contaminated and 
impure, as is our mind contaminated and impu re due to the contaminations of attachment, 
anger, jealousy, and so forth. We need to achi eve the permanent body that is the nature body 
of a buddha (svabhavikakaya), the cessation that is the complete abandonment of all 
obscurations and stains. To do this, we need to purify all our mental afflictions, whereby 
eventually we will achieve a pure body and pure mind. When we achieve complete 
enlightenment, we simultaneously achieve the two, three, or four bodies or kayas of a buddha.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1C3 The actual method for engaging in [generosity ] 
A [How to actually generate generosity] 
B [Importance of the inner attitude] 
C Generosity through mere thought 
D How to make [generosity] into [a practice] with [the] six perfections 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1C3A [How to actually generate generosity] 
1 The actual [topic] 
{215} Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior  [describes] how [generosity] should actually be 
generated: 
 
My body and likewise resources, 
All my virtues of the three times, I give away without any sense of loss To accomplish the welfare of all sentient beings.   [III, 11] 
 Like this, through taking the three – body, resources, and roots of virtue – as the 
observed object, you should familiarize yourself again and again with the thought of giving them away to all sentient beings. 
 We should familiarize our mind with the though t that wishes to give away everything – our 
body, resources, and roots of virtue collected in  the past, present, and future – for the welfare 
of all sentient beings. In other words, by taking  our body, resources, and roots of virtue as our 
observed object, we should train in this attitu de again and again, continually wishing to give 
them away.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 How beginners should act 
Since presently this wish has not yet ma tured and it has little strength, even 
though you have already given away your bo dy in thought to sentient beings, you 
should not actually give away your flesh, etc. Nonetheless, since the Compendium of 
Trainings  says that if we do not train in the thought to give away our body and life, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
624 du e t o  lack o f fam i li ar i t y w i t h i t  w e w i ll also  n o t be able t o  do  so  i n  t he fut u r e 
either, it is necessary to trai n in that thought from now on. 
 
Right now we are unable to give away our body since by doing so we would endanger our life 
and thereby would not be able to do anything more. However, we should train in doing this 
in our imagination, striving to develop the strong wish to give away our body and life, without 
actually giving away parts of our body for the ti me being. Since if we cut off an arm in order 
to give it to someone else, we wi ll die and will no longer be able to practice, at the moment we 
should train our mind in giving without actual ly giving away our body and life. We should 
train over and over again in the wish to give away our body, resources, and roots of virtue to 
others. By doing so, when we gain higher realizations in the future we will be able to actually do this. Since it would not be appropriate to  give away parts of our body at the present 
moment, we should not jump off a bridge thinking  to offer our body to the fish living in the 
river!  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1C3B [Importance of the inner attitude] 
1 The disadvantages of forgetting the welfare of others 
Thus, if you use food, clothing, etc. that  you have given away to sentient beings 
with a sincere thought, if, having forgo tten the thought “I will use these for the 
welfare of others,” you instead use them ou t of craving for your own welfare, it is 
an afflicted downfall. If, without this crav ing, you forget the discrimination that 
o b s e r v e s  a l l  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  o r  h a v e  a t t a chment for the sake of another sentient 
being, it is a non-afflicted downfall.  If you use for your own sake those [things] 
dedicated to others while discriminating them to be others’ substances, it becomes 
taking what has not been given (stealing)  and, if it is something valuable, it 
becomes a defeat of the pratimoksha [vow].  [On the other hand,] if you use them 
thinking: “I will use the resources of this  sentient being for his own welfare,” there 
is no fault. This is mentioned in the Compendium of Trainings . 
 If we forget the welfare of other sentient be ings, there are many disa dvantages. If having 
mentally given away our food, clothing, and so forth to others, we then use them out of 
craving for our own welfare while forgetting to th ink “I will use them in the service of others,” 
it is an afflicted downfall. If, having mentally  given them away all. If, without craving for our 
own welfare and forgetting that we have alread y given them away to other sentient beings and 
that they therefore belong to them, we use them, it is a non-afflicted downfall. Also if, 
understanding that they belong to others if , we use them out of attachment to another 
sentient being, it is a non-afflicted downfall, that  is, it is not non-virtuous but it is a fault due 
to the attachment. However, if we use somethin g of value for our own welfare that we have 
already dedicated to others while discriminating  it as belonging to others, it becomes the 
defeat of stealing of the individual liberation vows. On the other hand, the Compendium of 
Trainings  says that if we think to use the belongin gs of others to enact their welfare, for 
example, we eat in order to keep our body healthy so as to use it in the service of others, there 
is no fault.   
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
625 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Clearing up doubt concerning a defeat 
The intention is that the way in which it becomes a defeat is that, having dedicated 
something to a migrating being who is a human being with a sincere thought and 
when he too, having cognized it, holds it to  be his, you take it for your own welfare 
with the discrimination that it belongs to someone else, if it is of some value then 
the factors for it to become defeat are present.  
 
If, having dedicated something such as food, clot hing, wealth, and so forth, from our heart to 
a migrating being who is a human being and he has come to think of it as “mine,” we take it 
for our own welfare with the discrimination that  it belongs to someone else, if it has some 
value then it becomes a defeat. On the other hand, if it is not of a certain value, while it does not become a defeat there is a fault. Since we often recite a verse in which we give away our 
body, possessions, and so forth to others, when we use them we need to think that, having 
given them to others, we will use them for their welfare. If we eat and drink and so forth with 
this motivation, there will be no fault. On the other hand, if we give them away and then use 
them for our own welfare, there will be a fault.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 216) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1C3C  Generosity that is a mere thought 
{216} Moreover, the Bodhisattva Levels  teaches that the traini ng in the aspiration 
that aspires to give to sentient beings through emanating immeasurable varieties of 
phenomena that are objects to be offered wi th clear faith from the depths of one’s 
heart increases immeasurable merit with litt le effort and is the generosity of the 
wise bodhisattva. 
 
If with a clear faith from our heart we mentally  create or visualize various things and give 
them to other sentient beings, we can accu mulate a huge amount of merit with little 
difficulty. It is also the practice of  generosity of wise bodhisattvas. The Samantabhadra Prayer  
also includes verses of prostrating while visu alizing thousands of bodies with thousands of 
heads and thousands of tongues reciting praises,  whereby we create immeasurable merit. In a 
similar way, we should mentally visualize emanating thousands of emanations who make 
offerings, recite praises, do prostrations, practice  generosity, and so forth. If we train in this 
way, we will accumulate great merit very easily  and in a short time. If, on the other hand, our 
motivation is not very good when we do so mething, the merit will be very little.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-1C3D  How to make [generosity] possess the six perfections 
If at the time of training in the perfection  of generosity, it is made to possess the 
six perfections the training that occurs will be very powerful. The six are made 
complete in that, in addition, at that time there is: 
1. the ethical discipline that restrains the mental attention of hearers and solitary 
realizers; 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
626 2. the aspiration for and patience with resp ect to the qualities of a knower of all 
aspects and patience when abused by others; 
3. the joyous effort that generates the aspi ration for the sake of increasing them 
more and more;  
4. the concentration that is a single-poi nted mind not mixed with the Lesser 
Vehicle that dedicates that virtue  to complete enlightenment; and 
5. the wisdom that understands the object given, the giving, and the recipient to 
be like illusions. 
 
When we train in the perfection of generosity , we should also simultaneously possess all six 
perfections as this will make the training very  powerful. If when we train in generosity, we 
have the mental attention of hearers and solitary realizers, thinking of ourselves as being like 
them, we will simultaneously be practicing mo rality. In other words, if, when we practice 
generosity, we also have a mind of abandonment, we will simultaneously practice morality. If 
we aspire for and are patient with respect to  the qualities of omniscience and are patient 
when others abuse us, we will also practice pa tience while training in generosity. If we 
generate the aspiration to increase them more an d more, we will also practice joyous effort. At 
the time of generosity, if we have the concen tration that is a single-pointed mind that 
dedicates our virtue to the attainment of enli ghtenment without mixing it with the Hinayana 
motivation to achieve nirvana for ourselves, we will practice the perfection of concentration. 
If when training in generosity, we understand th e object given, the giving, and the recipient to 
not exist inherently and to be like illusions, we will practice the perfection of wisdom. In this 
way, when we practice generosity, we will at the same time also practice the other five 
perfections, whereby all six perfections will be  complete. Since this makes our practice of 
generosity very powerful, it is important to do so. 
 
STUDENT : Is the practice of wisdom while practicing  generosity – seeing the three spheres as 
empty of inherent existence – only the collectio n of wisdom or also the collection of merit? 
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : It is the collection of both merit and wisdom. This is discussed in the 
commentary on the Ornament for Clear Realizations  in the context of explaining how all the 
perfections are included in the perfection of g e n e r o s i t y  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  B y  p r a c t i c i n g  e a c h  
perfection in combination with all six perfections,  we engage in thirty-six perfections. This is 
also mentioned in the Bodhisattva Grounds  and the Ornament for the Mahayana Sutras , which 
were recently taught by His Holiness the Dalai Lama at Sera Monastery. Since you are still 
young, you should study these texts. An old man like myself can no longer study! Now 
sometimes when I read something,  some memory of it returns but an hour later I forget it 
once again. The mental energy as well as the ph ysical energy of the inner winds and channels 
degenerate with age, due to which the mind is no longer clear.  
 Tuesday afternoon, 17 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 216) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2 Training in ethical discipline 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
627 A The entity of ethical discipline 
B Its subdivisions 
C How to generate [ethical discipline ] in one's mental continuum 
The second has three [points]: The entity of  ethical discipline, its subdivisions, and 
how to generate [ethical discipline ] in one’s mental continuum. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2A The entity of ethical discipline 
1 The entity of ethical discipline 
First: Ethical discipline is the mind of  abandonment that is a mind turned away 
from harming others together with its base . This is mainly from the point of view 
of the ethical discipline of restraint. 
 
The entity of morality is the definition of morality. The entity of morality is a mind of 
abandonment that is a mind turned away from ha rming others together with its base. In other 
words, it is a mind turned away from harming ot hers by means of body and speech, that is, by 
killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisive speech, harsh words, and idle talk. 
“Together with its base” refers to covetous ness, malice, and wrong view, which are called 
“base” because based on them we engage in non-virtuous actions of body and speech. In 
short, the entity of morality or ethical discip line is the mind of abandonment of killing, 
stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisi ve speech, harsh words, and idle talk.  
 Among the three types of morality, “morality” in  this context mainly refers to the morality 
of restraint. In addition, there is the morality of  benefiting sentient beings and the morality of 
accumulating virtuous actions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The entity of the perfection [of morality ] 
Through thoroughly completing the familiar ity with this mind more and more, it 
becomes the perfection of ethical discipline . It is not that you externally set all 
sentient beings in freedom from all harm without exception. In Engaging in 
Bodhisattva Behavior  it says: 
 
The achievement of the mind of abandonment  
Is explained to be the perfection of ethical discipline. 
 By training in morality, we become more an d more familiar with this mind whereby it 
becomes the perfection of morality. It is no t that one sets all sentient beings without 
exception in freedom from all harm. Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  says that the 
achievement of the mind of abandonmen t is the perfection of morality ( shilaparamita ). 
Morality is the mind that abandons harming ot her sentient beings, that is, the mind that 
abandons all harmful actions of body and speec h. It is not that in order to achieve the 
perfection of morality we must free all sentient beings from harm since even a buddha is unable to achieve this.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2B Its subdivisions 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
628 1 The ethical discipline of restraint 
2 [The ethical discipline of accumulating virtuous qualities] 
3 [The ethical discipline of working for the welfare of beings] 
The second has three points.  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2B1 The ethical discipline of restraint 
A General presentation 
The ethical discipline of rest raint, {217} from the point of view of being together 
with the motivations, is the ten abandonm ents that abandon th e ten non-virtues. 
From the point of view of its entity, it is the seven abandonments of body and 
speech that abandon the seven non-virtues.  
 
The morality of restraint from th e point of view of being togeth er with its motivation is the 
ten abandonments that abandon the ten non-virt ues. On the other hand, from the point of 
view of its entity, it is the seven abandonments – the three of body and four of speech – of the 
seven non-virtues. Motivated by covetousness, malice, or wrong view, we engage by means of our body in killing, stealing, and sexual misc onduct, and by means of our speech in lying, 
divisive speech, harsh words, and idle talk. Th us, when these seven non-virtues are together 
with their motivations, there are ten abandonmen ts. However, from the point of view of its 
entity, the morality of restraint is the abandonm ents of the three non-vi rtuous actions of body 
and four non-virtuous actions of speech. This is because it is not possible to take a vow that 
counteracts non-virtuous paths of  actions of mind since the mi nd cannot be stopped. On the 
other hand, if we want to, we can avoid killing , stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisive 
speech, harsh words, and idle talk. For this reason, it is possible to take vows that are 
promises not to engage in them. Therefore, by  way of entity there are seven abandonments, 
whereas when they are together with thei r motivations there are ten abandonments.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B Precise features 
The intention of the Bodhisattva Levels  in stating that the ethical discipline of 
restraint in the continuum of a bodhis attva is the seven types of [vows of] 
individual liberation is [as follows]. In those possessing the vows of individual 
liberation who possess the bodhisattva vows,  the ethical discipline of restraint is 
the actual vows of individual liberation of the class of the laity or the ordained and 
the abandonments and vows that are common to them in their continua. In those 
possessing bodhisattva vows who are uns uitable as a support for the vows of 
individual liberation, the ethical discipli ne of restraint is the abandonments and 
restraints of abandoning either the natu ral misdeeds or formulated misdeeds that 
are common to individual liberation. Although the vows of individual liberation in the continuum of a bodhisattva are vows of ethical discipline in the continuum of 
a bodhisattva, they are not actual bodh isattva vows, whereas the rest have a 
common basis with the bodhisattva vows. 
 
The Bodhisattva Grounds  says that the morality of restrain t in the continuum of a bodhisattva 
is the seven types of vows of individual libe ration. The seven types of vows of individual 
liberation are the male and female lay vows, the male and female no vice vows, the vows of 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
629 probationary nuns, and the male and female vows of full ordination. If a bodhisattva has such 
vows, he possesses the morality of restraint. In  other words, there are lay bodhisattvas who 
possess the lay vows of individual liberation and are upasakas or upasikas, and there are also 
ordained bodhisattvas who are novice monks or  nuns, probationary nuns, or fully-ordained 
monks or nuns. They possess th e morality of restraint.  
 In addition, there are those who possess bodh isattva vows who are unsuitable to take the 
vows of individual liberation, such as gods, anti -gods, and nagas, because they do not have a 
human body of the eastern, southern, or west ern continents. In addition, the human beings 
of the northern continent cannot achieve the vo ws of individual liberation because they are 
unable to generate a mind of renunciation or definite emergence due to their lifespan being 
fixed at 1000 years, due to lacking financial prob lems, and so forth. The morality of restraint 
of these beings is the abandonments of either  the natural misdeeds or formulated misdeeds. 
Although the vows of individual liberation in the continuum of a bodhisattva are the vows of 
morality in the continuum of a bodhisattva, they are not actu al bodhisattva vows. The rest 
have a common basis with the bodhisattva vows. Perhaps “the rest” refers to the 
concentration vows and uncontaminated vows. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2B2 The ethical discipline of gathering virtuous qualities 
The ethical discipline of gathering virtuo us qualities is, through observing virtues 
such as the six perfections, etc., to generate that whic h has not yet been generated 
in one’s own continuum, to not allow that which has b een generated to 
degenerate, and to increase it more and more. 
 
The morality of gathering virtuous qualities is, through observing virtues such as the six 
perfections (these being the observed object), to generate the virtue that has not yet been 
generated in one’s continuum; if it has already b een generated, to not allow it to degenerate; 
and to increase it more and more. For example,  if we have not yet generated bodhichitta we 
should try to generate it; if we have already ge nerated it, we should not allow it to degenerate; 
and we should increase it more and more. In fact, we often make the following dedication: 
 
May the precious mind of enlightenment  Be born in those in whom it has not been generated.  
May that which has been generated  
Not decline, but develop more and more. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2B3 [The ethical discipline of enacting the welfare of sentient beings] 
The ethical discipline of enacting the welf are of sentient beings is to accomplish 
the welfare of sentient beings in this an d future lives through ethical discipline 
without any misdeeds as appropriate.  
 
The morality of enacting the welfare of sentie nt beings is through morality to accomplish 
whatever is suitable for the welfare of sentient beings in this life and future lives without any 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
630 misdeeds. Thus, it is to do whatever we can to  bring temporary benefit to sentient beings in 
this life and to benefit their future lives by causing them to not commit non-virtue. We 
should try to do whatever we can to help sentie nt beings, but if we find that we cannot help 
them we should at least avoid harming them. For example, whenever we encounter sentient 
beings who are suffering, we should do what ever we can to eliminate or reduce their 
sufferings and problems. In addition, we should try to help them refrain from committing 
non-virtuous actions and to set them in engagi ng in virtuous actions. Also when sentient 
beings lack material things such as food, clot hing, a bed to sleep in, a place to stay, and so 
forth, we should do whatever we can to help them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2C How to generate [ethical discipline ] in one's mental continuum 
1 [Preconditions and advantages of ethical discipline] 
2 [The entity of ethical discipline] 3 [Making [ethical discipline] into [a prac tice] [combined] with the six perfections] 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2C1 [Preconditions and advantages of ethical discipline] 
A What is the cause of pure ethical discipline and what does it depend on 
Third: Pure ethical discipline depends on  putting into practice what should be 
engaged in and what should be avoided according to the formulated [rules of the 
Buddha]. Furthermore, it follows the strong  and firm wish to guard [ethical 
discipline]. For it to arise, you must medi tate for a long time on the disadvantages 
of not guarding it and the advantages of  guarding it. {218} This was explained 
above in the context of middling beings. 
 
What are the causes of pure morality and what does it depend on? It depends on thinking 
about the advantages of guarding morality and the disadvantages of not guarding it. Pure morality depends on practicing what is to be  engaged in and what is to be avoided in 
accordance with the formulated rules. In othe r words, one should strive to abide by these 
formulated rules. In addition, it follows from a strong and firm wish to guard our morality. 
For this to arise, we need to meditate for a lo ng time on the advantages of guarding morality 
and the disadvantages of not guarding it. This  was already explained in the context of the 
path of middling beings.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 218) says:  
B Considering the advantages and disadvantages of guarding and not guarding [ethical discipline] 
Moreover, in the Summary of Perfections  [II, 48] it also says: 
 
If by degenerated ethical discipline you cannot achieve your own 
welfare, 
Where would you get the power for the welfare of others? 
Therefore, it is inappropriate for someone striving well  For the welfare of others to loosen his veneration for it. 
 Since, if we degenerate our morality, we cannot achieve our own welfare, how could we 
possibly achieve others’ welfare? In other words, if we do not take care of ourselves, we will 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
631 n o t  b e  a b l e  t o  t a k e  c a r e  o f  o t h e r  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s .  I f  w e  a r e  s t r i v i n g  f o r  o t h e r s ’  w e l f a r e ,  w e  
should not loosen our ve neration of morality.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And [II, 60/61]: 
 
This ethical discipline, the special path of attainment, 
Should be accomplished equal to those who are naturally compassionate. The nature of the excellence of pure exalted wisdom, Free from errors, it is called “the best of ornaments.”  It is the pleasant fragrance guarded in all three realms, 
The unguent that does not contradict ordination. 
One possessing ethical discipline becomes a noble being among humans, Even though he accords with outer appearances. 
 Morality is the special path of  attainment. It should be acco mplished equal to those who are 
naturally compassionate. The nature of superb, pure exalted wisdom, free from errors, it is 
called “the best of ornaments. ” The pleasant fragrance of morality is guarded in all three 
realms. Morality is likened to an ointment or unguent that does not contradict ordination, 
whereas other ointments cannot be worn by the or dained. In fact, it is said that those who 
keep pure morality come to have a naturally fr agrant odor, like sandalwood or lotus flowers, 
without anointing themselves with ointments or  perfumes. Although externally those with 
morality appear to be the same as others in  terms of their outer appearance and behavior, 
they become noble beings among human beings.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
This says that in dependence on [ethical discipline ], one’s mental continuum 
improves more and more; one becomes similar in training to the great beings who 
are naturally compassionate; one attain s the pure exalted wisdom that has 
abandoned all seeds of faulty conduct; al though other ornaments do not beautify 
the very young or very old, the ornament  of ethical discipline is the supreme 
ornament for whoever possesses it, whether young, old, or in between, since it 
pleases everyone; although other fine fragra nces only spread in the direction of the 
wind and do not spread in other directions  whereby they are in but one direction, 
the scent of ethical discipline’s fame spre ads in all directions; although ointments 
such as sandalwood and the like, which cool the torment of heat, contradict 
ordination, the ointment that protects from the torment of the heat of the 
afflictions does not contradict ordination  but rather accords with it; and even 
though you seem to have the signs of ordi nation, to be endowed with the jewel of 
ethical discipline is more excellent than anything else.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
632  
In dependence on morality, our continuum, that is, our realizations, increase more and more. 
We become similar to bodhisattvas who are naturally compassionate and attain the pure 
exalted wisdom that has abandoned all the seeds of faulty conduct. When a very young child 
puts on ornaments, necklaces, earrings, and so forth, she does not look very nice, nor does a 
very old and wrinkled woman who walks bent ov er and with difficulty. On the other hand, 
those who are in between in age are beautified by such ornaments. However, the inner 
ornament of morality suits everyone, old, young, or middle aged, and pleases everyone. 
Therefore, it is the best of ornaments.  
 Whereas other fragrances, such as that of incense, only go where they are carried by the 
wind and do not go to other places, the fragra nce of the fame of morality spreads in all 
directions. Although anointing oneself with ointments of sandalwood that eliminate the 
torment of heat is contradictory for the ordained , the ointment of morali ty that protects from 
the fever of the afflictions does not contradict ordination but accords with it. This is because 
the fragrant odor of morality arises naturally fr om within and is not something that is applied 
externally to the body. While those who merely  wear the robes of monks and nuns can have 
either pure or impure morality in that extern ally they appear the sa me, those who have the 
jewel of morality are superior to the other.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Again from the same text [II, 62-64]: 
 
Without expressing a word and without much effort, The resources and services needed for practice gather. {219} All the world bows low without intimidation;  Power and wealth are gained wi thout effort and without work. 
 Without expressing words and without effort, the resources and service necessary for our 
practice, will arrive. All the world bows down to  those with morality without needing to be 
intimidated. Power and wealth will be obtained without effort and without need to do anything.   Wednesday morning, 18 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 219) says:  
 
All beings from lineages unworthy to be mentioned, 
Even those he did not benefit or help, And those he did not know from former times, Bow to a being who possesses ethical discipline,  Even the dust made auspicious by his feet,  is raised to the head due to being holy, and Gods and men prostrate to them, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
633 Touching them with their heads, and carry them all around. 
 
Thus, one should think about [the advantages] as expressed [here].  
 
Although he has neither helped or benefited ot hers and does not even know them, all beings 
bow to a being who possesses pure and perfect morality. In other words, those who guard 
their vows become objects of respect of others su ch that other people even place the specks of 
dust from their feet on the crown of their head s. Others also bow low to those with perfect, 
pure morality and good behavior. Such signs of  respect are still common in places like Sri 
Lanka and Vietnam when lay people encounter monks. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2C2 [The entity of ethical discipline] 
A Instruction on the importance of the way in which to train in ethical discipline 
Although there are three [kinds of] ethica l discipline, the ethical discipline of 
restraint – the actual formulated rules of individual liberation or the engagements 
and avoidances common to them – are them selves important for bodhisattvas in 
the beginning. If they are guarded, the othe rs will also be guarded, whereas if they 
are not guarded, the others will also  not be guarded. Therefore, the Compendium  
says that if the ethical discipline of th e bodhisattva vows degenerates, all the vows 
are degenerated. Whoever thinks that th e vows of individual liberation are for 
hearers and rejects their formulated rules of engagement and avoidance saying: “I 
must train in the other bodhisattva traini ngs” has thus not grasped the essential 
point of the training in ethical discipline  of bodhisattvas, for it is taught many 
times that the ethical discip line of restraint is the basis and source of the two 
subsequent ethical disciplines. 
 
The training in morality or ethical discipline is  important, therefore we should strive in it. 
There are three types of morality : the morality of restraining from non-virtue, the morality of 
gathering virtuous qualities, and the morality of enacting the welfare of sentient beings. 
Among them, the main one is the morality of rest raint, which is related to vows. It is also 
important for bodhisattvas to trai n in it and to give up whatever  is discordant with it. If one 
guards the vows of individual liberation, one will  also guard the bodhisattva vows. If one does 
not guard the vows of individual liberation, one will also not guard the other vows. Therefore, 
if the morality of vows degenerates in a bodh isattva, also his other vows will degenerate. For 
this reason, bodhisattvas must also train in th e morality of restraint. Thinking “The vows of 
individual liberation are for hearers. I do not need them because I have the bodhisattva vows,” whereby we discard the formulated rules of what to engage in and what to avoid, indicates that we have not understood the bo dhisattva vows. Since the Buddha taught many 
times that he morality of restraint is the foun dation of the other two moralities, we need to 
understand this. In short, the morality of rest raint is the foundation of the other types of 
morality. If we do not guard the morality of vows, also we will not guard the other types of 
morality. Just as a strong and stable founda tion is necessary when building a house as 
otherwise the entire house will collapse, likewise , having taken any of the different kinds of 
vows of individual liberation of lay men or wo men, novice monks or nuns, or fully-ordained 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
634 monks and nuns, we must strive to keep them pu re. When faults arise, we should confess and 
purify them. For this purpose monks and nuns perform the Restoring and Purifying 
Ceremony (so-jong) every fifteen days. Those who have the necessary requisites can also 
perform a self-initiation everyday, such as th at related to Vajrayogini, Tara Chittamani, 
Yamantaka, the Chakrasamvara Body Mandala, and s o  f o r t h .  I n  t h i s  w ay, negativities that 
have committed are purified and vows that have been degenerated are restored.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The main ethical discipline of restraint: how the mind of restraining from natural misdeeds should be 
[generated ] again and again 
The main ethical discipline of restraint is to abandon the natural misdeeds. The 
synthesized essential point of the great disadvantages of the natural [misdeeds] is 
to abandon the ten non-virtues. Therefore,  the mind of restraint that does not 
even move toward their motivations should be [generated ] again and again.  
 
The main point of the morality of restraint is to abandon the natural misdeeds, which are 
synthesized in the ten non-virtuous actions. “Natu ral” indicates that, when it is engaged in, it 
brings a bad result by means of the natural law of cause and result. Although the degeneration of some vows of monks or nuns is  naturally non-virtuous, the degeneration of 
others only becomes non-virtuous because of tr ansgressing a formulated rule such as eating 
after noon, singing, dancing, playing music, and so forth. Thus, when lay people without such 
vows engage in these actions, there is no fault.   We should also strive to avoid even thinki ng about killing someone, stealing something, 
and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
{220} From the Summary of Perfections : 
 
That which becomes the path to th e happiness of high status and 
liberation 
Is to not degenerate from the ten paths of action. Due to abiding in them, there is the special result Of the thought that thinks to benefit migrating beings. 
 
Perfectly restrain speech, body, and mind.  The Conqueror taught “This, in brief, is ethical discipline.” 
Since they are the basis of all ethical discipline  Without exception, thoroughly train in them. 
 The path to the happiness of high rebirth, as a god or human being, and to the happiness of 
the liberation of foe-destroyers and buddhas is to not degenerate from the ten virtuous paths 
of actions. While the th ree abandonments of body (the aban donments of killing, stealing, and 
sexual misconduct) are both actions and paths of  actions, as are the four abandonments of 
speech (the abandonments of lying, divisive sp eech, harsh words, and idle talk), the three 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
635 abandonments of mind are only paths of acti ons, not actions. We should not degenerate 
from these ten virtuous paths of actions. Abiding in them brings the special result of thinking 
to benefit migrating beings. In brief, to perf ectly restrain one’s body, speech, and mind is 
morality; this was taught by the Conqueror. Since they are the ba sis of all morality, we should 
thoroughly train in them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
C Summarized presentation 
In brief, through taking this as the basis,  to train again and again in the mind of 
restraint that trains in the trainings of whatever ethical discipline one has 
promised is the practice of ethical discipline.  
 
We should take this morality as  a basis and then guard whatever  vows of individual liberation, 
bodhisattva vows, or tantra vows we have promis ed to maintain. We should train in the mind 
of restraint again and again through thinking th at having taken these vows we need to keep 
them pure. We must understand the vows we ha ve taken and abandon transgressing them. To 
do so, we need to train in the wish to keep them and to not transgre ss either the natural or 
formulated rules.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-2C Making [ethical discipline] possess the six perfections 
Ethical discipline is made to possess the si x perfections in that, abiding in ethical 
discipline oneself, one sets others in it; this being generosity. The remaining 
[perfections] are as before. 
 
Abiding in morality or ethical discipline oneself, to set others in morality is the practice of the 
generosity of morality. The remaining perfection s are as was explained before in the context 
of generosity. In short, to abide in morality an d set others in morality is the generosity of 
morality. If at this time our mind is patient, this  is the patience of morality. If at that time our 
mind delights in abiding in morality and setting  others in it, this is  the joyous effort of 
morality. If at that time our mind is focused on  what we are doing, this is the concentration 
of morality. If at this time we think that ours elves (the one practicing morality), others, and 
morality all lack inherent existence, this is the wisdom of morality. In this way, when we 
practice morality, it includes the practice of all six perfections.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3 Training in patience 
A The entity of patience 
B Its subdivisions 
C How to generate [patience] in one’s continuum  
The third has three [points]: the entity of  patience, its subdivisions, and how to 
generate [patience] in one’s continuum. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3A The entity of patience 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
636 1 [Explanation of] the entity of patience 
First: The entity of patience is th e mind abiding at ease without being 
overwhelmed by harm and the occurrence of suffering and thoroughly abiding 
aspiring for the Dharma. Its discordant cl ass are hatred, disheartenment, lack of 
appreciation, and lack of desire. 
 
The entity of patience is a mind that abides in its natural state, that is , undisturbed, when we 
are harmed or when suffering occurs. In additi on, it is the mind th at thoroughly abides 
aspiring for the Dharma taught by the Buddha, in particular, for emptiness. The discordant 
c l a s s  o f  p a t i e n c e  i s  h a t r e d  o r  a n g e r ,  t h e  d i s h eartenment that thinks one is unable to be 
patient, a lack of aspiration, and a lack of de sire. When anger arises, we should immediately 
apply its antidote, patience. For example, when someone says something bad to us or even beats us, we should strive to practice patience. Likewise, when we are sick or in pain, instead 
of becoming sad and discouraged thinking that  we are unable to do anything, we should 
p r a c t i c e  p a t i e n c e  b y  w i l l i n g l y  a c c e p t i n g  t h i s  s u f f e r i n g .  T h i s  i s  o n e  k i n d  o f  p a t i e n c e  t h a t  w e  
need to develop. Likewise, when due to conditions it is a bit difficult to stay in a certain place 
and we become discouraged, we should try to a ccept this difficulty and bear it patiently.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The completion of the perfection of patience 
The completion of the perfec tion of patience is the mere complete familiarity of 
the mind with ceasing one’s own belligerence  and so forth; it does not depend on 
freeing [other] sentient beings from their unruliness.  
 
The measure of having completed the perfection of patience is the mere complete familiarity 
of mind with ceasing one’s own belligerence, that  is, anger or hatred. It does not depend on 
freeing sentient beings from unruliness, violen ce, or impatience. Since this can only occur 
when all sentient beings have achieved buddhah ood, this is not what is meant by completing 
the perfection of patience. Rather it is that our mind ceases to have anger, belligerence, 
resentment, spite, and other such  minds. These have all been pacified, whereby our mind is 
quiet and calm. This is the completion  of the perfection of patience.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3B Its subdivisions 
Second: There is the patience of not paying  heed to harm by others, the patience 
of accepting the suffering that arises in  one’s own continuum, and the patience of 
a mind certain with respect to the Dharma.  
 
When others harm us, thinking that it does no t matter we should not ge nerate the thought to 
retaliate but instead keep our mind calm and undisturbed. When suffering arises in our 
continuum, we should voluntarily accept it without sadness and without being disturbed. 
When difficulty arises in understanding th e Dharma, especially the emptiness of all 
phenomena of inherent existenc e, we should be patient.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
637 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C How to generate [patience] in one’s continuum 
1 Meditating on the advantages of patience and the disadvantages of impatience 
2 The actual way of cultivating patience 
It is necessary to meditate on the advant ages of patience and the disadvantages of 
impatience. 
 
In order to generate patience in our continuum,  we should meditate on the many advantages 
of patience and the many disadvantages of anger.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1 Meditating on the advantages of patience and the disadvantages of impatience 
A The advantages of patience 
B The disadvantages of impatience 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1A The advantages of patience 
First: You should think about the fact that later on you will have {221} few 
enemies, you will not be separated from dear ones, you will experience much 
happiness and mental happiness, you will die without regret, and your will be 
reborn among the gods after this body has perished.  
 
The advantages of patience are that later on we  will have few enemies, we will have good 
relationships with those who are dear to us an d will not be separated from them, we will 
experience much physical and mental happiness,  we will die without regret due to being 
certain that we have acted well during our life, and, having given up the body of this life, we will be reborn among the gods. Thus, there are many advantages to being patient.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
The Summary of Perfections  [III, 3-5] says:  
 
For those possessing the mind that  neglects others’ welfare,  
It was taught “The sublime way is patience.” 
The world’s goodness and excellence  Are protected from the faults of anger by patience. 
 For those who possess the mind that neglects ot hers’ welfare, the Buddha taught the sublime 
way of patience. As this statement is a bit un usual, perhaps there is  some mistake in the 
Tibetan text. Or perhaps it means that the mind thinking that one is unable to take care of 
sentient beings is eliminated by patience. All the world’s goodness and excellence comes 
about due to patience and are protected from the faults of anger by patience.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Sublime ornament of the powerful ones, 
Peak of the strength for those upholding the ascetic way, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
638 Torrent engulfing the conflagrat ion of minds wishing to harm, 
 
Patience is the sublime ornament of the powerful one. For ascetics it is the best of strengths 
for bearing difficulties. It is a torrent of wate r extinguishing the fire of the harmful mind.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Patience eliminates manifold harms in this and other lives. 
The arrows of the words of unruly beings Are blunted by the armor of patience of great beings.  They transform into beautiful flowers of praise And become attractive garlands of renown. 
 
Cultivating patience eliminates all the varieties of harm in this and ot her lives. The armor of 
patience dispels the arrows of the words of un ruly beings, whereby they become beautiful 
flowers of praise and attractive garlands of renown. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And [III, 83]: 
 
The source accomplishing the form possessing beautiful qualities and Adorned with the best of signs is also patience. 
 You should meditate on the advantages as taught until a strong and stable 
certainty is reached with regard to them. 
 Due to patience the body becomes very beauti ful, as though adorned with the signs and 
exemplifications. Thus, we should meditate on  the advantages of patience again and again 
until our mind achieves certainty rega rding the need to develop patience.  
 (Oral transmission 12:23-12:33, pp. 221-224)  Wednesday afternoon, 18 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 221) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B The disadvantages of impatience 
1 Invisible disadvantages  
2 Visible disadvantages alone with supplementary remarks 
 
The disadvantages of impatience or a lack of pa tience are of two types: those that cannot be 
seen at the moment and those that can be seen. In other words, the disadvantages of anger 
include disadvantages that can presently be s een and disadvantages that cannot presently be 
seen.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
639  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-1 Invisible disadvantages  
A How anger destroys roots of virtue 
B How they are destroyed even if the object of anger is an ordained person who is not a bodhisattva 
C Clearing up doubts concerning their destruction 
D Details regarding the way in which they are destroyed 
 
The fault or disadvantage that cannot be seen presently is that anger destroys our roots of 
virtue. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-1A How anger destroys roots of virtue 
1 The actual [topic] 
Second: In Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior  [Shantideva says]: 
 
The generosity accumulated  
Over a thousand eons and excellently engaging in Making offerings and the like to the sugatas; All this is also destroyed by one [instant of] anger. 
 
This appears to be set forth in a ccordance with Aryasura’s work [ Summary of 
Perfections ]. The Sutra of the Displays of Manjushri  says that it destroys the virtue 
accumulated over a hundred eons. 
 
The virtue accumulated by the ge nerosity of giving material th ings over a thousand eons and 
that of making offerings to the sugatas, prostr ating to them, and so forth is destroyed by one 
instant of anger. Anger mainly destroys the virt ue that is included in the side of merit, 
whereas it does not destroy the virtue accumulated by me ans of the wisdom realizing 
emptiness. For this reason, we need to be ca reful to avoid anger, especially anger toward 
bodhisattvas. It appears that Shantideva s ays “accumulated over a thousand eons” in 
accordance with the text composed by Aryasura. The Sutra of the Displays of Manjushri , on the 
other hand, says that one instant of ange r destroys the virtue accumulated over a hundred eons . 
That anger destroys our roots of virtue is its invisible disadvantage.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Differences in the object [of anger ] 
With respect to the object, some explain that it must be a bodhisattva, whereas 
some assert it to be a general object. The former accords with the 
[Madhyamak]avatara  [III, 6a-c]: 
 
Through anger toward a child of the conquerors, 
The virtue amassed over one hundred eons, Through generosity and ethical discipline 
{222} Is destroyed in but an instant. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
640  
Some texts say that the anger that destroys our roots of virtue is that directed at a bodhisattva, 
whereas other texts say that it is anger direct ed at any person. However, in general it is 
understood to refer to anger at a bodhisattva.  This accords with wh at is set out in the 
Supplement to the Middle Way , which says that one instant of anger at a conqueror’s child, a 
bodhisattva, destroys the virtue accumulated by generosity and morality over one hundred 
eons.  
 In brief, it is a heavy non-virtue to get angry at a bodhisattva. It is said that even when we 
once again take rebirth as a human being, we will have an ugly body. In fact, we see that when we get angry, our face becomes ugly, our eyes bulge and dart about, our body shakes, and so 
forth. We do not look nice! Likewise, when we  see other people who are angry, we do not 
even want to look at them!  In addition, due to anger we will be led into the unholy, that is, we will not engage in the 
holy (virtuous actions) and will engage in the unholy (non-virtuous actions). In other words, 
we will engage in non-virtuous actions and wi ll not engage in virtuous actions. For this 
reason, we need patience.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The support 
As regards the support of the one who gene rates anger, since even if a bodhisattva 
gets angry it destroys his roots of virtue, th ere is no need to mention that this is the 
case for one who is not a bodhisattva who gets angry at a bodhisattva. The 
Commentary on the Guide [to the Middle Way]  says that whether it is certain that the 
object is a bodhisattva or not and whether the fault seen to be the reason for the 
anger is true or not, the destruction of the virtue like that explained above is 
similar.  
 With respect to the support of the anger, since when a bodhisattva gets angry at a bodhisattva, 
his roots of virtue are destroyed, there is no need to mention that this happens when a non-
bodhisattva gets angry at a bodhisattva. Whethe r or not it is certain that the object is a 
bodhisattva and whether or not the fault that is the reason for getting angry actually exist, the 
roots of virtue from having accumulated genero sity or morality for a thousand eons or a 
hundred eons are destroyed by anger. This  is mentioned by Chandrakirti in his Commentary 
on the  Supplement to the Middle Way . Therefore, we should avoid getting angry at any sentient 
being, even a dog, a cat, or a mosquito! In or der to not get angry we must train in patience. 
We should stop ourselves from showing anger as  much as possible and also apply its antidote. 
If we try to do this, there will be some resu lt. If we do not even try, nothing will happen! 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-1B How they are destroyed even if the object of anger is an ordained person 
who is not a bodhisattva 
In general, anger does not need to be dire cted at a bodhisattva in order for it to 
destroy roots of virtue. A passage quoted from the Sarvastavadin scriptures in the 
Compendium of Trainings  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
641  
If a monk prostrates to a stupa of ha ir and nail [relics] with extended 
limbs and a pure mind, he will come to enjoy as many kingdoms of 
wheel-turning kings as there are partic les of dust in the ground covered 
down to the golden foundation. 
 
And:  
 
Also those roots of virtue will be exhausted if he injures or disparages 
one of pure conduct. Therefore, if one should not even be angry at a 
charred log, how much less so at a body endowed with consciousness! 
 In general, in order for anger to destroy our root s of virtue it is not necessary for it to be 
directed at a bodhisattva. The Compendium of Trainings  says that when a fully-ordained monk 
makes full-length prostrations to  a stupa containing the hair or nails of the Tathagata with a 
mind of lucid faith, he will become a wheel-turning king for as many times as the number of particles of dust he covers with his body down  to the golden foundation. On the other hand, 
if out of anger he insults or hurts a friend of pure conduct with whom he studies and practices, his roots of virtue will be exhausted. The object of anger therefore does not need to 
be a bodhisattva, it can also be another monk  with whom one studies and practices. If we 
should not get angry even with a charred log or stick of wood, what need is there to mention 
that we should not get angry at a body that is endowed with consciousness?  
 
STUDENT : What is the difference between anger and wr ath? Anger seems to me to be the wish 
to harm, whereas I think that there can be wrat h when one is disgusted with the behavior of 
someone while there is no wish to harm him. 
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : When there is wrath, there is compassion inside, although outside 
there is the aspect of wrath. For example, when a child does something bad, sometimes 
parents have to put on a wrathful aspect while in side their intention is virtuous in that they 
wish to benefit and educate their child.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-1C Clearing up doubts concerning their destruction 
1 Doubts raised by scholars 
2 Their refutation 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-1C1 Doubts raised by scholars 
Some scholars say that the meaning of “the  destruction of roots of virtue” is that 
the emergence of their effect is delayed through the potential of earlier virtue to 
bring about its effects quickly being destroye d, whereupon the effects of hatred etc. 
emerge first. However, if they meet with [suitable] conditions afterward, it is not 
that their respective effects do not emerge , for if there is no mundane path that 
can eliminate the seeds of the objects of abandonment, it is not possible for seeds 
to be abandoned by the mental afflictions.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
642 Some scholars say that the destruction of root s of virtue means that the emergence of the 
results of virtuous actions created in the past  that could have quickly brought results is 
delayed, and that the results of hatred will emer ge first. However, it is not that the virtuous 
actions created in the past will not bring ab out a result when they meet the necessary 
conditions; it is just that the emergence of their result will be delayed. They say that just as 
there is no mundane path that can abandon the seeds of the objects of abandonment, 
likewise the seeds of virtuous actions cannot be de stroyed by mental afflictions such as anger.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-1C2 Their refutation 
A The seed is not certain 
B How ‘destruction’ does not just mean postponement  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-1C2A The seed is not certain 
1 How the result does not arise due to conditions even though the seed is present 
That reason that it is not certain is be cause even though ordinary beings purify 
non-virtues by means of the four opponent  powers, their seeds are not abandoned. 
However, although the conditions are met la ter on, {223} it is not possible for their 
fully ripened effects to emerge.  
 
The seed is not certain because even though it exists, the result may not emerge due to conditions. In other words, the reason that it is not certain is that when ordinary beings 
purify their non-virtues by means of applying  the four opponent powers of regret, the 
determination to not do it again, going for refu ge and generating the mind of enlightenment, 
and applying an antidote such as recitation of a sutra such as the Diamond Cutter Sutra  and so 
forth, their seeds are not abandoned, however even though conditions are later met it is 
impossible for these seeds to bring fo rth their fully-ripened results.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Even if the seed has not been abandoned its effect does not emerge once tolerance is attained 
Once the peak and tolerance [levels] of th e path of preparation have been attained, 
although wrong views and the seeds of non- virtues that become the causes of the 
bad migrations have not been abandoned, wrong views and rebirth in the bad 
migrations are not possible even if [the se seeds] meet with their conditions. 
 
Even if the seed has not been abandoned, its re sult will not emerge once one has achieved the 
peak and tolerance levels of the path of preparat ion. In other words, having achieved the peak 
and tolerance levels of the path of preparation,  even though wrong view and the seeds of non-
virtues that become the cause for taking re birth in the bad migrations have not been 
abandoned, wrong view cannot be generated and one cannot take rebirth in the bad migrations even if the co nditions come together.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-1C2B How ‘‘destruction’’ does not just mean postponement  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
643 1 The absurd consequence  
According to the statement “Due to ka rma whatever is heaviest in cyclic 
existence...,” either a virtuo us or non-virtuous karma, due to ripening sooner, can 
temporarily block the occasion for another karma to ripen. However, it cannot be 
posited nor has it been taught that a virt uous or non-virtuous karma is destroyed 
by merely that. The temporary postponement of its ripening is not suitable to be 
the meaning of the destruction of roots of virtue, as otherwise it would have been 
necessary to teach that all strong non-virtuo us actions destroys the roots of virtue.  
 
Whatever actions, whether virtuo us or non-virtuous, are the heaviest bring about their result 
first. When two actions are the same in weight , that which was done most recently ripens 
first. When two actions occurred at the same time , that which is most familiar will ripen first. 
This is mentioned in a sutra. In the case that a virtuous or non-virtuous action ripens first, it 
can temporarily prevent other actions from ripeni ng, however it does not stop their ripening 
forever. Therefore, it cannot be said that it destroys  virtuous or non-virtuous karma. Thus, the 
fact that its ripening is merely postponed is  not the meaning of “destroy,” otherwise the 
Buddha should have taught that any strong non- virtuous action destroys the roots of virtue, 
but he did not.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Analogy for how the effect does not emerge although the seed has not been abandoned 
Therefore, with respect to this , the master Bhavaviveka said:  
 
As explained above, neither non-virtues that have been purified by the 
four powers nor roots of virtue that  have been destroyed by wrong views 
or harmful mind can bring about their effects even if later on they meet 
with the conditions; just like a seed that has been damaged does not 
give rise to a sprout even though the conditions are met. 
 Bhavaviveka said that non-virtues purified by the four opponent powers and roots of virtue 
that have been destroyed by wrong view or anger cannot give rise to their results later on, even 
though their seeds have not been abandoned; ju st like a seed that has been damaged cannot 
give rise to a sprout even when the conditions  are present. Likewise, even though the seeds 
are not abandoned by the four opponent powers or by wrong view or harmful mind, they will 
not give rise to their results.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-1D Details regarding the way in which they are destroyed 
1 How the fully ripened effect and the effect corresponding to the cause may or may not be destroyed 
Moreover, even though negativities that have been accumulated are purified by 
purifying them with the four powers as ex plained above, this does not contradict 
the fact that they delay the arisal  o f  h i g h e r  p a t h s .  T h u s ,  i n  s o m e  [people] the 
results of giving gifts and guarding ethical discipline, such as the arisal of excellent 
resources and a body, are destroyed. However, due to [the result] corresponding to 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
644 the cause (deeds of familiarity with givi ng and the mind of abandonment), the 
roots of virtue of generosity and mora lity are easily generated and cannot be 
destroyed. In others the arisal of [the result ] corresponding to the cause as a 
continuum of similar type (deeds of inner mo rality and so forth) is destroyed, but 
the arisal of an excellent body, resources, and so forth {224} is not destroyed. Again 
others, as described previously, as long  as they do not generate anger at a 
bodhisattva who has attained prophecy, will ha ve some realizations of the path that 
enable them to completely traverse it in one eon. If they generate one thought of 
anger, although the path that exists in their continua is not relinquished, their 
traversal of the path in one eon will be delayed. In brief, just as with respect to 
purifying non-virtues, not everything that was done is necessarily purified, likewise, with respect to the destruction of virtue, no t everything that was done is necessarily 
destroyed.  
 
Even though negativities that have been a ccumulated are purified by means of the four 
opponent powers, this does not contradict the fact that the generation  o f  h i g h e r  p a t h s  i s  
delayed. For some people, while the results of giving gifts and guarding morality, such as the 
arisal of excellent resources and an excellent body, are destroyed, whereas due to the result 
corresponding to the cause that is familiarit y with the mind of giving and the mind of 
abandonment they once again generate the roots of  virtue of generosity and morality and this 
cannot be destroyed. For other people the result  similar to the cause of inner morality is 
destroyed, but the arisal of excellent resour ces, and excellent body, and so forth is not 
destroyed. For still others, as long as they do not become angry at a bodhisattva who has 
received prophesy, their realizations will enab le them to traverse the path in one eon. 
However, if they generate one thought of anger, even though the path is not relinquished, 
their realizations are delayed and they will not ac hieve the result in one eon. In short, if we 
purify non-virtuous actions, not all their resu lts are purified, and likewise, when roots of 
virtue are destroyed, not all their results are destroyed.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 How that depends exclusively on the scriptures 
Nonetheless, this being an important poin t, since it seems to be necessary to 
analyze it in dependence on the Buddha’s scriptures and the reasonings based on 
them, analyze it through depending on the Buddha's excellent teachings.  
 
Nonetheless, we should analyze this impo rtant point in depend ence on the Buddha’s 
scriptures and reasonings by reading and analyz ing well the Buddha’s scriptures, whereby we 
will come to a deeper understanding. Karma is no t visible to a direct perceiver, such as the eye 
or ear consciousness, nor can it be establis hed by reasonings. Therefore, in order to 
understand it, we need to analyze the perfect sc riptures of the Buddha, checking whether they 
are harmed or not by a direct perceiver or by an inferential cognizer based on reasons. 
Examples of valid reasons are: during the day th ere is fire behind the mountain because there 
is smoke; during the night there is no fire on  the water because there is mist; water exists 
there because there are waterfowl overhead; th e body is impermanent because it changes 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
645 momentarily, the body is impermanent because it  is created by causes and conditions. We can 
understand such reasonings by way of an infe rential cognizer. However, the law of cause and 
result cannot be understood by means of such re asons; it can only be understood by relying 
on the Buddha’s scriptures.
