Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 224) says:  
3 How the two activities of the great are done  
Thus, the invisible disadvantages [of anger] ar e that it projects very unpleasant fully 
ripened results and prevents the emergence of other very pleasant fully ripened 
results. 
 
The invisible disadvantages of anger and so fort h is that it projects very unpleasant fully-
ripened or fruitional results and stops the emergence of very pleasant fully-ripened results. These are disadvantages of anger that we are unable to see.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C1B-2 Visible disadvantages alone with supplementary remarks 
A How peace of mind and so forth are not experienced 
The visible disadvantages [of anger] are taught in Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior  
[VI, 3-5]: 
1. one does not experience mental peace and certainty,  
2. previously existing joy and happiness are ruined and later on are not achieved,  
3. sleep does not come and the mind does not abide at ease, 
4. if the hatred is strong, even those who previously nurtured one out of 
kindness, having forgotten their kindness, may kill one, 
5. friends and relatives abandon one out of irritation, 
6. even those you gather with gift s do not stay, and so forth.  
 
There are also visible disadvantages of anger that are results that are visible in this life: we do 
not experience mental happiness and certainty, our happiness and joy decrease both now and 
later, sleep does not come, and our mind does not abide at ease and relaxed. Due to strong 
anger, those people who once cared for us fo rget this and may even kill us. Friends and 
relatives become irritated and unhappy with us and turn away from us. Even if we gather others with gifts, they will not remain with us.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B How to gain certainty regarding the faults 
Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior  [VI, 2] says: 
 
There is no negativity like hatred, 
And no austerity like patience. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso

646 Therefore, in various way 
Earnestly cultivate patience. 
 
Thus, you should consider the advantages and disadvantages and make an effort to 
develop patience in many ways. 
 
Shantideva says that because there is no negativi ty like hatred, and no austerity like patience, 
we should cultivate on patience in a variety of ways. Considering the many advantages of 
patience and the many disadvantages of anger, we  should put effort into cultivating patience.

C Supplementary remarks 
I t  i s  t o  b e  u n d e r s t o o d  f r o m  t h e  Compendium of Trainings  t h a t  h a t r e d  i s  n o t  
ascertained to be the only faulty conduc t that collects both grave fully-ripened 
results and destroys roots of virtue. {225} There is the wrong view that deprecates 
[the law of cause and effect]; the ab andonment of the holy Dharma; and 
contempt, generation of pride, and so forth towards bodhisattvas, gurus, and so 
forth. 
 
Anger or hatred gives rise to unpleasant fully-ripened results and destroys our roots of virtue. 
However, this also occurs due to the wrong view  that deprecates the existence of the law of 
cause and result and so forth, the abandonm ent of the holy Dharma, and not respecting, 
generating strong pride, and so forth toward bo dhisattvas, the holy gurus, one’s parents, and 
so forth. It is said in the Guhyasamaja Tantra  that if we do confessi on well with the four 
opponent powers, we can even purify the five actions of immediate retributions – killing our 
mother, killing our father, killing a foe-destro yer, drawing the blood of a tathagata, and 
creating a schism in the sangha. However, this is not the case for the action of abandoning 
Dharma as this will necessarily bring rebirth in the hell realm. The Treasury of Manifest 
Dharma  says that we must respect and never neglect our parents who gave us this body as well 
as sick people.

2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C2A How to cultivate the patience that is unconcerned with respect to harm 
How to cultivate the patience that is  unconcerned with respect to harm. 
 
When others harm us, we should cultivate patien ce toward that person through thinking that 
the harm does not matter.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
647 1 [The offender’s lack of freedom]  
If you investigate whether or not someon e who harmed you was free to not harm 
you, [you will find that ] he did not harm you while being autonomous, for he 
generated the wish to harm you out of caus es and conditions such as the seeds of 
mental afflictions from previous familiarity and improper mental attention. Also 
due to them, he engaged in activities th at inflicted harm and due to them he 
produced suffering in others, for under the power of other, the mental afflictions, 
he had become as if their slave.  
 Is the person who harms us free or not? If we examine this, we will find that he is not free, rather he is under the control of the mental afflictions and du e to them inflicts harm upon 
us. Due to the seeds of mental afflictions being very strong due to previous familiarity with 
them and due to improper mental attention, the wish to harm us arose in his mind. This in 
turn induced him to engage in actions that ha rm other people and make them suffer. Having 
come under the power of the mental afflictions, he is as if their slave. In other words, he 
became like a soldier who without choice mu st obey the orders of his superiors.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The inappropriateness of anger given that [one harming] has no freedom 
Even if someone, driven by something else without freedom, has harmed you, 
anger is not appropriate. For instance, if  someone, having been possessed by an 
evil spirit, were to come under its power and wish to harm you who would benefit 
him by freeing him from it and strike you,  you would not get even the slightest bit 
angry at him but instead think: “He is acti ng like this now because the evil spirit 
has deprived him of his freedom.” You woul d also put effort into doing whatever 
you could to free him from the demon. A bodhisattva should also act like that. 
 
Since the person harming us has no freedom, that is, he is driven by so mething else, it is not 
right for us to become angry with him. For exam ple, if someone who is possessed by a spirit, 
his mind controlled by it, were to wish to ha rm and beat someone who wants to help him by 
freeing him from the spirit, because he is unde r the power of the spirit his benefactor would 
not become angry at him but instead would strive to help him by performing certain rituals and so forth in order to gradually release him from  the control of the spir it. This is the way in 
which a bodhisattva should act.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The scriptural source for this 
The Four Hundred Verses  says: 
 
Just as a doctor is not disturbed 
By one who, although angry, is gripped by an elemental spirit, 
Likewise, the Muni, who sees the afflictions, Is not contaminated by those with afflictions. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
648 Buddha Shakyamuni sees that the harmful person is  bound by the mental afflictions, that is, 
that he is under control of the mental affliction s and due to this beats others and so forth. All 
faults arise due to the fact that our mind is overpowered by the mental afflictions such as 
anger, jealousy, belligerence, resentment, spite, ignorance, and so forth. They in turn bring 
only suffering, they never bring happiness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Also Master Chandrakirti says: 
 
“This is not a fault of sentient beings, It is a fault of the mental afflictions”; After thorough investigation, the wise ones Are not disturbed by sentient beings. 
 Chandrakirti also says that sentient beings are no t at fault, it is the me ntal afflictions that are 
at fault. Through examining this well, the wi se are not disturbed by sentient beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 How anger is inappropriate if one thinks of one’s own karma 
Moreover, the experience of suffering produced by the harmer {226} is the 
experience of the result of a negative ac tion accumulated by oneself in the past 
and, in dependence on that, the action wi ll be exhausted. If you cultivate patience 
with respect to it, you will not newly accumulate causes for the experience 
suffering later on, whereas, if you get angr y, you will have to experience suffering 
that is even greater than that.  
 
Because the experience of suffering produced by  a harmer is not created by him, we should 
not get angry at him. Instead we should remember that it is the result of non-virtuous actions 
that we created in the past, and that, having now ripened, they will be exhausted. If we 
cultivate patience, we will not accumulate new causes for suffering. If, on the other hand, we 
become angry, we will create causes to ex perience even more suffering in the future. 
Therefore, when we experience suffering, we sh ould remember that it is the result of non-
virtuous actions that we ourselves created in the past and that are now being exhausted, and 
therefore be patient with this suffering. In this  way, we will experience less suffering and will 
also not create new causes for future suffering .  O n  t h e  o t h e r  h a n d ,  i f  w e  d o  n o t  c u l t i v a t e  
patience when someone harms us through thinking  that it is due to our karma, and instead 
retaliate, we will only create more non-virtue  due to which we will once again experience 
suffering in the future.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
649 5 Consequently, the appropriateness of being patient as if it were a medical examination 
Thus, it is very appropriate to be patien t with minor suffering in order to avert 
greater suffering, such as being patient wi th bloodletting, burning, and so forth as 
a method for curing a grave illness. 
 
When someone is sick, he is willing to be ar minor painful treatments as blood-letting, 
burning, and so forth in order to avoid death. Nowadays doctors tend to advise surgery as a 
cure for many problems and people similarly accept such treatment as an alternative to death. 
Thus, people even accept to have a kidney removed, a part of a limb amputated, and so forth 
in order to avoid death, even though they may also risk dying during the surgery itself. Some 
people even accept to have both legs amputate d in order to avoid death. They patiently bear 
such suffering in order to avoid even greater su ffering in the future. Similarly, when others 
harm us, we should patiently bear the suffering in order to avoid creating the cause of even 
greater suffering in the future. We need to us e our intelligence well and try not to get angry 
w i t h  a n y o n e  b y  t h i n k i n g  t h a t  i t  d o e s  n o t matter that someone harms us, and instead 
voluntarily accepting the harm with a calm mind.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C2B How to cultivate the patience that accepts suffering 
How to cultivate the patience that accepts suffering.  
1 Countering the categorical aversion for suffering that arises  
If the suffering that arises is easy to alleviate, aversion is unnecessary. If it is not 
suitable to be alleviated, also dislike fo r it, since without benefit, is useless and 
even disadvantageous, for even tiny suffe ring becomes extremely hard to bear if 
one is very intolerant, whereas even grea t suffering can be endured if one is less 
intolerant.  
 
When suffering arises, we should counteract the mind that does not like it. If the suffering 
that arises is easy to alleviate, there is no need to mentally dislike it. Even if we cannot 
alleviate it, there is no benefit whatsoever in di sliking it and this will only disadvantageous to 
us. If we are very short-tempered or have little to lerance, it will be very difficult to bear even 
the slightest suffering even, for example, someone saying to us “You have a very big nose!” If 
we are less short-tempered and more tolerant, we will be able to bear even great suffering and 
it will not be a the slightest problem if someone tells us that we have a big nose, small eyes, 
and so forth. Thus, those people who are more pa tient and relaxed are able to bear even very 
great suffering.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Considering the good qualities of suffering itself 
The way to mentally take the side of suffe ring is to train the mind again and again 
through thinking “If there was no such su ffering, I would not generate the wish to 
definitely emerge from cyclic existence,  therefore it has the good quality of 
exhorting my mind to liberation; it ha s the good quality of eliminating my 
arrogance since, when I am beset with suffering, my conceited superiority is 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
650 destroyed; it has the good quality of maki ng me shun negativities because when I 
experience intense suffering feeling, since it  arises from non-virtue, if I do not want 
this result, I must turn away from its cause; it has the good quality of making me 
delight in accomplishing virtue since, du e to being tormented by suffering, I want 
happiness and for that purpose I must acco mplish virtue; and, having understood 
that compassion is generated for the bein gs of cyclic existence through thinking 
“In proportion to my own experience, others  also suffer,” this suffering fulfills my 
wishes.”  
 
We should think about the good qualities of su ffering. For example, due to suffering we will 
generate the mind of renunciation that wishes to become free from suffering and to definitely 
emerge from cyclic existence. On the other hand , if we do not experience suffering, the wish 
to emerge from cyclic existence will not arise. Suffering, on the other hand, acts to urge our 
mind to achieve liberation, as if someone were  to tell us “You must attain freedom from 
suffering!”   Also due to experiencing suffering, the pride thinking that we are superior is eliminated. 
Due to experiencing suffering, we will strive to avoid creating its causes – non-virtuous 
actions. If we are tormented by suffering, we wi ll wish to experience happiness and will strive 
to create the causes of happiness – virtuous acti ons. Due to experiencing suffering, we will 
generate the wish from our heart to help ot hers eliminate their sufferings. Therefore, 
suffering has good qualities in that it can he lp us develop our inner realizations. For this 
reason, we should not be sad when we suffer but instead use it as an incentive to practice the 
Dharma.   Thursday afternoon, 19 May 2005

3 It is not difficult if one familiarizes in small steps 
Here again [it says]:  
 
There is nothing that does not get easier, 
If you familiarize with it, therefore, 
{227} By familiarizing yo urself with minor harm, 
You will also be able to bear great harm. 
 
Thus, by putting on the armor of the though t to accept suffering and integrating it 
with suffering in small steps, the stre ngth to accept suffering will increase. 
 
Due to familiarity with something, it becomes ea sy. Thus, by first familiarizing ourselves with 
small harm thinking “It does not matter,” later on when someone does us great harm, we will 
b e  a b l e  t o  b e a r  i t  w i t h  p a t i e n c e .  T h i s  i s  t h e  p a t i e n c e  o f  b e i n g  a b l e  t o  v o l u n t a r i l y  a c c e p t  
suffering. In short, initially we should strive to  accept small sufferings so that our strength to 
accept suffering in the future will increase. On  the other hand, if we continue to think “Poor 
me! I cannot bear this,” our strength will only de crease and we will not be able to bear even 
small sufferings.

651  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-3C2C The way to cultivate the patience that aspires for a certain mind regarding the 
Dharma 
The way to cultivate the patience that aspires to a certain mind regarding the 
Dharma is to train in the aspiration for the following without bias:  
1. the objects of clear faith, the Three Jewels;  
2. the objects to be actualized, the two selflessness; 
3. the object of desire, the great streng th of the buddhas and bodhisattvas; 
4. the objects to be adopted and discarded,  the causes that are excellent conduct 
and fault conduct and their results; 
5. the object of meditation, enlightenm ent, the meaning to be achieved; 
6. the method for attaining that, the pa th of the bodhisattva trainings; 
7. the objects of hearing and thinking, the holy Dharma of the twelve scriptural 
branches and so forth. 
 
We need to develop our aspiration without bias for:  1. the objects of clear faith, the Three Jewels;  
2. the objects to be actualized, the two selflessnesses,  that is, the lack of an inherently existent 
or self-supporting substantially existent person and the lack of inherently existent or self-
supporting substantially existent phenomena;  
3. the object of desire, the great streng th of the buddhas and bodhisattva, 
4. the objects to be adopted and discarded, resp ectively the cause that is excellent conduct 
and its result, happiness, and the cause that is faulty conduct and its result, suffering, 
5. the object of meditation, the enlightenment that is the meaning to be obtained, 
6. the method for attaining that, the path of the tr ainings of bodhisattvas, 
7. the objects of hearing and thinking, the twelve scriptural branches and so forth. 
Our aspiration should not be biased in the se nse of thinking that some of these objects 
belong to the Hinayana and some to the Mahayana and therefore rejecting some and 
adopting others. The objects of hearing and thin king also include the 84,000 aggregates of 
Dharma and the three baskets – the sutra, abhi dharma, and vinaya baskets. We should read 
these texts, think about them, and meditate  on them, whereby we will achieve higher 
realizations. If we think that we can meditate without studying, by merely sitting still like a 
statue with an empty mind, there will be no resu lt. Likewise, sitting in meditation posture but 
thinking about running a business, how to impr ove our painting skills, and so forth will not 
be of any benefit.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4 Training in joyous effort 
A The entity of joyous effort 
B Its subdivisions 
C How to generate [joyous effort] in one’s continuum 
The fourth has three [points]: the entity of  joyous effort, its subdivisions, and how 
to generate it in one’s continuum. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4A The entity of joyous effort 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
652 First: In the Bodhisattva Levels  [joyous effort] is explained to be a mind that delights 
strongly for the sake of gathering virtue  and enacting the welfare of sentient 
beings, as well as the actions of  the three doors motivated by it. 
 
Joyous effort is a mind that delights in virtue. On the other hand, effort in activities that are 
not virtuous is called “laziness” since interferes  with achieving our aims. Here the text says 
that “joyous effort is a mind that delights stro ngly in gathering virtue and enacting the welfare 
of sentient beings and the acti ons of the three doors motivate d by it.” “Gathering virtue” 
refers to engaging in the prac tice of the six perfections.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4B Its subdivisions 
1 Armor-like joyous effort 
Second: From the three, the first is armo r-like joyous effort. When bodhisattvas 
undertake joyous effort, prior to applying themselves, they put on the armor of the 
thought that first delights the mind. Th at is, they delight even in attaining 
buddhahood through abiding only in hell fo r the sake of eliminating the suffering 
of a single sentient being for 100, 000 times 10 million rounds of three 
immeasurable eons. Also with respect to making effort to attain complete 
enlightenment, they wear the armor of th e thought “If I will not give up engaging 
in effort for that length of time , what need is there to mention [that I will not give 
it up] if the time is less than that  and if the suffering is less?” 
 If a bodhisattva wishes to undertake joyous e ffort, prior to applying himself he generates 
delight through thinking that even if it is ne cessary to remain for 100,000 times 10 million 
rounds of three countless eons in hell for the sake of eliminating the suffering of a single 
sentient being, he will do so. A bodhisattva trai ns his mind in such thoughts and puts effort 
into achieving complete enlightenment without ever giving up engaging in joyous effort. 
Therefore, there is no need mention that they do  not give up their effort if they must stay for 
l e s s  t i m e  i n  h e l l  a n d  i f  t h e  s u f f e r i n g  i s  l e s s  t h a n  t h a t  o f  U n r e l e n t i n g  T o r m e n t .  T h i s  i s  t h e  armor-like joyous effort.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The joyous effort of gathering virtuous dharmas 
The joyous effort of gathering virtuous dh armas is to apply ones elf to them for the 
sake of accomplishing the six perfections. 
 
Thus, the joyous effort of gathering virtuous dh armas is to accomplish the six perfections.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The joyous effort of enacting the welfare of sentient beings 
The joyous effort of enacting the welfare of  sentient beings {228} is similar to the 
above. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
653  
The joyous effort of enacting th e welfare of sentient beings is to delight in remaining in the 
hell of Unrelenting Torment even for three countl ess eons for the sake of a single sentient 
being. Thus, it is a strong determination to enac t the welfare of sentient  beings in the eleven 
ways.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C How to generate [joyous effort] in one’s continuum 
1 Advantages to undertaking joyous effort 
2 The disadvantages of not undertaking joyous effort 
3 How to actually generate joyous effort 
4 How joyous effort is made to possess the six perfections 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C1 Advantages to undertaking joyous effort 
Third: how to generate [joyous effort] in one’s continuum: the advantages to 
undertaking joyous effort. 
 
To generate joyous effort in our continuum, we need to contemplate the advantages of joyous 
effort – that one will not be overcome by distractions and that one will achieve the two 
spiritual attainments (siddhis).  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
A One will not be overcome by distraction and so  forth and will achieve the two attainments 
From the Ornament for the Great Vehicle Sutras  [XVI, 65, 66 ,70]: 
 
Among the collection of virtue, joyous effort is the best. 
Thus, in dependence on it, that is subsequently attained. 
Due to joyous effort, immediately ther e is the best source of happiness, 
Including the supramundane and mundane attainments.  
Due to joyous effort, one attains desired worldly resources. 
Due to joyous effort, one possesses thorough purity. Due to joyous effort, one passes beyond the transitory collection and is freed. Due to joyous effort, you become a buddha, the supreme enlightenment. 
 
Among the collection of virtue, joyous effort is the best. Why is this? Be cause by relying on it, 
we achieve all other qualities. Due to joyous e ffort, we will obtain all happinesses, including 
the mundane and supramundane spiritual attainme nts. Due to joyous effort, we will obtain 
the desirable resources of existence. Due to joyous effort, we will come to possess pure 
qualities. Due to joyous effort, we will go beyond and be liberated from the transitory 
collection, that is, from this body. Due to  joyous effort, we will attain the supreme 
enlightenment of buddhahood. In short, ever ything good comes from joyous effort.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
654 B How those endowed with joyous effort not only develop these qualities but also non-humans like them 
In the Summary of Perfections  [IV, 2c-d] it also says: 
 
For him who has great joyous effort and does not dismay, 
There is nothing that cannot be attained or accomplished. 
 
And [IV, 41c-d, 42]:  
Also all non-humans like to benefit one. All types of meditative stabilization will be achieved. Days and nights pass by with results obtained. Due to the collection of good qualities, one will not decline. Due to aims more noble than human activities, One will blossom like an utpala flower. 
 
I f  w e  h a v e  j o y o u s  e f f o r t ,  m a n y  g o o d  qualities will arise. For example, the Supplement to the 
Middle Way  says that if we have joyous effort, we will accumulate the two collections of merit 
and wisdom and thereby attain supreme enlightenm ent. In fact, due to tireless effort, we can 
achieve whatever we want, that is, there is noth ing that we will not attain or accomplish. In 
short, we will achieve success in whatever we do . Due to joyous effort, non-humans will like to 
benefit us. All types of meditative stabilization will be attained. Day and night will pass by in 
good results. Due to our collection of good qualities, we will not decline, but will only 
improve more and more. Due to aims that are su perior to human activities, we will blossom 
like an utpala flower.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C2 The disadvantages of not undertaking joyous effort 
The disadvantages of not undertaking joyous effort are described in the [Sutra] 
Requested by Sagaramati : 
 
For the lazy, enlightenment is very far and far in all ways. 
In the lazy, generosity through wisdom do not exist. 
In the lazy, the welfare of others does not exist. 
 
And the  Close Placements of Mindfulness  also says: 
 
The one foundation of the mental afflictions 
Is laziness, in whomever it exists. He who has the one, laziness, Does not have any qualities whatsoever. 
 
You should think like this. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
655 The Sutra Requested by Sagaramati  says that for those lacking in joyous effort, that is, for the 
lazy, enlightenment is very far away, that is, th ey will not achieve it soon. It is not that 
enlightenment arrives by itself in  our mind, without doing anything  to obtain it. In fact, Lama 
Yeshe used to say, “For the lazy, even wate r cannot reach their mouth,” meaning that water 
does not just fall into the mouth of those who ar e too lazy to pick up a glass of water and put 
it to their mouth!  
 The sutra also says that for the lazy generosity  through wisdom do not exist, that is, they do 
not practice the generosity of giving material things, the generosity of giving the Dharma, or 
the generosity of giving protection, nor do th ey practice morality, pa tience, joyous effort, 
concentration, and wisdom. If someone does  not put any effort into attaining them, 
realizations will not just arise by themselves. It is like the fact that food on the table in front of 
a lazy person does not spontaneously come to hi s mouth. In addition, th e lazy do not think to 
enact the welfare of other sentient beings, and they, in fact, do not do anything for them.  
 The Close Placements of Mindfulness Sutra  says that the foundation of all the afflictions is 
laziness. Those who are lazy do not possess any qual ities on the side of enlightenment, that is, 
the class of the completely pure, which rang e from mindfulness through omniscient mind. 
Instead the lazy possess many a fflictions, which are the class of the thoroughly afflicted  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C3 How to actually generate joyous effort 
A Adverse conditions to joyous effort 
B Relying on favorable conditions for joyous effort 
C How body and mind become serviceable that way  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C3A Adverse conditions to joyous effort 
1 Seeing that one is able to accomplish virtuous dharmas but not engaging in them 
2 The discouragement that thinks “How could I ever accomplish anything like that?” 
There are two adverse circumstances to joyous effort: seeing that one is able to 
accomplish virtuous dharmas but not engaging in them and the discouragement that thinks “How could I ever accomplish anything like that?” 
 
There are two conditions adverse to the generation of joyous effort. The fi rst it to see that one 
can accomplish virtuous dharmas but not to engage in them. The second is the 
discouragement that thinks “P oor me, I cannot do that.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C3A-1 Seeing that one is able to accomplish virtuous dharmas but not 
engaging in them 
{229} The first has two [points]. 
A The actual [topic ] 
One postpones things till later thinking, “The re is still time,” and, if not that, one 
is overcome by attachment to bad activities. 
 
We postpone things through thinking that we ar e still young and therefore there is still time 
and that we will do it later on. This is one type  of laziness. In addition, there is the laziness of 
attachment to bad activities. Due to clinging to the bad or trivial activities of this life, the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
656 attaining of wealth, possessions, and so forth, we busy ourselves with them. As a result of this 
laziness, we focus our whole life on doing acti ons only for the sake of this life, such as 
accumulating wealth so as to buy a house, furnit ure to fill it, a car, then a second and better 
car…, then to find a beautiful wife or a handso me husband, then to have a beautiful child, 
and so forth. Due to this so-ca lled “attachment to bad activities ,” we continue to wander in 
cyclic existence. Such activities are bad in the sense of being trivia l pursuits that make us waste 
our precious human rebirth. Such activities in terfere with our development of joyous effort 
and the thought to obtain enlightenment. Anothe r example of this type of laziness would be 
passing time in chasing votes and so forth in order to become a successful politician. Such 
activities are meaningless from the point of view of the Dharma, although they may be 
considered worthwhile from a worldly point of view.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The antidotes 
The antidote to the first, [the laziness of procrastination, ] is to meditate on the 
three: 
1. this body that has been obtained quickly disintegrates,  
2. after death one will fall into the bad migrations, and  
3. it will be difficult to find such a good support once again. 
This was already taught before. 
[The following considerations are ] the antidote to attachment [to bad activities]:  
1. the holy Dharma is the cause for the aris al of boundless joy in this and future 
lives;  
2. meaningless idle chatter and the distractions of excitement and so forth 
degenerate the great aim in this life, and  
3. through seeing it to be the source that  generates manifold suffering in future 
lives, it should be stopped. 
 
The antidote to the laziness of procrastination, the thought that one still has time to practice 
the Dharma, is to think that this body quickly disintegrates, that after death one will fall into 
the bad migrations, and that it will be diffi cult to find another precious human rebirth 
possessing the eighteen qualities. If instead we  continue to think that we do not need to 
practice the Dharma now but will do it a bit la ter on, perhaps next year, perhaps when we are 
a bit older, and so forth, we will die before we  even begin. This subject was discussed before 
in the context of small beings in which it was explained that while we presently have a 
precious human rebirth possessing the eighteen qual ities, we will soon die, that after death we 
will fall into the bad migrations, and that it is difficult to obtain another precious human 
rebirth again due to it being difficult to create its causes and so forth. 
 The antidote to the attachment  to bad activities, on the other hand, is to think about the 
fact that the holy Dharma is the cause of great ha ppiness in this life and in future lives; that 
due to idle chatter and the distractions of exci tement and so forth, our great aim degenerates 
and we will not achieve anything in this life; and that through seeing this attachment to be 
the source of many sufferings in our future live s, it must be stopped. These considerations 
counteract the attachment to bad activities, that  is, to the mundane activities of this life.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
657  
Friday morning, 20 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 228) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C3A-2 The second adverse condition: the discouragement thinking: “How 
could I ever accomplish anything like that?” 
A The actual [topic] 
B The antidotes 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C3A-2A The actual [topic] 
Discouragement is also of three [types]: 
1. The discouragement that with respect to  the objects to be attained thinks: 
“Since the good qualities of a buddha are limitless, I cannot attain them.” 
2. The discouragement that with respect to  the method thinks: “Since giving away 
my arms, legs, and the like is immensely difficult, I cannot accomplish it.” 
3. The discouragement that with respect to the abode thinks: “Since it is necessary 
to take infinite rebirths in cyclic existenc e, at that time I will be harmed by the 
sufferings of cyclic existence.” 
 
The first type of discouragement is to think “Since the object to be achieved, the good 
qualities of a buddha, are infini te, I cannot attain them.” This type of discouragement is an 
adverse condition to the development of joyous effort.   The second type of discouragement is to th ink “Since the method of giving away my legs, 
arms, and so forth is extremely difficult, I cannot do this. I am not able to give others my eyes 
and so forth. The achievement of buddhahood is  very difficult. I better not think about it. 
Instead I will give it up.” This type of disc ouragement is another adverse condition to the 
development of joyous effort.   The third type of discouragement  is to think “I will have to ta ke rebirth in cyclic existence 
many times, and at that time I will be tormen ted by suffering. I cannot bear this.” On the 
other hand, if we have joyous effort, we will think, “I will remain in the hell realm for eons even to help one sentient being achieve liberation  from cyclic existence. ” Therefore, this type 
of discouragement is another adverse condit ion to the development of joyous effort.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C3A-2B The antidotes 
[Accordingly,] there are also three antidotes. 
1 The antidote to the discouragement with respect to the object to be attained 
The antidote to the first one is the th ought: “Even the buddhas did not already 
attain elevated paths right from the start.  Rather they were like me and became 
buddhas by treading increasingly higher pa ths. Since the Bhagavan also said that 
even those who are greatly inferior to me will attain buddh ahood, why should I 
not attain it too unless I do not make the effort?” 
 
The antidote to thinking that we are unable to achieve the infinite qualities of a buddha is to 
think “The buddhas, for example, the previous  t h r e e  b u d d h a s  w h o  h a v e  c o m e  a s  s u p r e m e  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
658 emanation bodies in the fortun ate eon, as well as the four th, Buddha Shakyamuni, did not 
attain the paths right from the beginning but on ly by training gradually did they achieve the 
various paths and become buddhas. Since Buddha Shakyamuni said that even beings who are 
inferior to me in intelligence can attain buddhahood by constantly making effort in 
meditation without becoming discouraged, why would I too not be able to become a buddha 
i f  I  m a k e  e f f o r t  i n  i t ?  I f  I  t r a i n  i n  t h i s  w a y ,  I  t o o  w i l l  b e  a b l e  t o  a t t a i n  b u d d h a h o o d ! ”  W e  
should encourage ourselves by thinking in this way.  
 Likewise, when we are studying and the though t arises “I cannot understand this. My mind 
i s  c o n f u s e d , ”  w e  s h o u l d  a l s o  t r y  t o  g e n e r a t e  s t r o n g  c o u r a g e  a n d  p u t  m o r e  e f f o r t  i n t o  o u r  
studies, whereby we definitely will be able to  gain a good understanding. Since even animals 
can learn to do something, various tricks an d so forth when taught by human beings, why 
would we not be able to learn new things? Dogs , for example, can be taught to search for 
things that have been lost. Even cats and bird s can learn to do certain things, for example, 
parrots can learn to say some words. There is  a story about a group of people who were 
traveling together to Lhasa with various an imals including a monkey. The owner of the 
monkey gave it some food in a bowl. One youn g boy thought that when  the monkey finished 
eating he would leave the bowl whereupon he  could take it, however when the monkey 
finished eating it put the bowl on its head an d went off. The boy was surprised that even an 
animal could learn to protect its belongings ! Since animals can learn such things, human 
beings are capable of learning much, much more!  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The antidote to discouragement with respect to the method 
The antidote to the second is to think: “As long as I generate the discrimination 
that it is difficult to give my body and so forth away, I will not give them away. 
However, when it is time to give them, it will not at all be difficult but will be like 
giving away cooked vegetables.” 
 
If when we think about the bodh isattva conduct, which includes the generosity of giving away 
our bodily parts, we become discouraged and th ink that we cannot do this, we should think 
that it is not necessary to give  them away at the moment, and th at later on, having trained our 
mind in generosity, we will be able to cut off our leg and give it to others like giving away a plate of cooked vegetables. We need to gradually familiarize with this thought and dispel such discouragement.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The antidote to discouragement with respect to the abode that is to be accomplished 
The antidote to the third {230} is to st op discouragement through thinking: “Due 
to a bodhisattva having abandoned negativities, their results, suffering feelings, do 
not arise. Due to his stable realization that  cyclic existence lacks inherent existence, 
like an illusion, there is no suffering in his mind. If he thrives due to physical and 
mental happiness even though he remains in  cyclic existence, despair is senseless.” 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
659 As an antidote to thinking that we are unable to enact others’ welfare, we should think that 
due to bodhisattva having abandoned negativities , he no longer experiences suffering feelings; 
that due to his stable realization that suffering does not exist inherently but is like an illusion, 
he does not suffer; and that he experiences mental and physical happiness even though he 
remains in cyclic existence so despair is sensele ss. Thinking like this, we will be able to make 
the determination to remain in cy clic existence even in order to help a single sentient being.  
 A bodhisattvas sees that cyclic existence is like an illusion and th at it does not exist 
inherently. Seeing this, he experiences mental  and physical happiness, whereby it is not 
difficult for him to remain in cyclic existenc e for the benefit of other sentient beings. In 
general, there are places or abodes in which it is difficult to stay where there are many 
interferences and distractions. However, we should  think that they are like illusions and that 
they do not exist inherently, whereby they can ch ange. In this way, we should try to keep our 
mind relaxed, whereby noise and so forth will not disturb our mind. Instead we will become 
happy and relaxed, thinking “Now I can do in crease my inner happiness by practicing the 
Dharma, whereby my mind will change and impr ove. This is wonderful.” Thinking in this 
way, it will not be difficult to bear the things that happen to us in our daily life. On the other 
hand, if we continually think, “This is difficult . I cannot do this. I cannot bear this,” we will 
only experience more and more di fficulties. We may think that it would be better to go to stay 
in a cave on a mountain, but then when we  get there we will think “Maybe someone will 
come to kill me.” Due to such thoughts, we wi ll never find a place anywhere in which to stay. 
We will never find any real peace or happiness. Our mind, and no one else, will only create 
more and more problems for us. Thinking in this  way, we should try to be more relaxed.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C3 Relying on favorable conditions for joyous effort 
Relying on favorable conditions for joyous effort has four [types]: 
1 The power of aspiration 
The power of aspiration is that, through me ditating on causes and results, one will 
strive in discarding and adopting. 
 
The power of aspiration is that, through meditati ng on actions and result s, that is, on the law 
of cause and effect, we will strive to discar d non-virtuous actions and to adopt virtuous 
actions. In other words, we need to develop stab le faith in the infallible functioning of the law 
of actions and results, whereby we will develo p a strong aspiration to abandon non-virtuous 
actions in order to avoid suffering and to pr actice virtuous actions in order to achieve 
happiness. This will act as an  antidote to laziness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The power of stability 
The power of stability is to not engage in  anything without investigating it, and, 
having investigated it, to enga ge in it and culminate it.  
 
The power of stability is to not engage in an ything without first investigating it, but then, 
having investigated it, to engage in it and to culminate it, that is, to bring it to a conclusion. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
660 For example, when we undertake to do a certain job, we should make sure that we complete 
it. It is not good to do a bit an d then stop, as in this way we wa ste material things as well as 
our time, whereby there is no benefit. Thus, what ever we want to engage in, such as study and 
meditation, we should first investigate whether we  can do it, then engage in it, and then bring 
it to a conclusion.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The power of joy  
The power of joy makes one’s undertakin g of joyous effort uninterrupted and 
insatiable, like a child engaged in play. 
 
The power of joy makes one want to do more an d more, like a small ch ild who never wants to 
stop playing or the Institute’s dog, Gianni, wh o never wants to stop pulling on a stick. We 
s h o u l d  p u t  s u c h  j o y o u s  e f f o r t  i n t o  o u r  p r actice of the Dharma, thinking to achieve 
buddhahood, and practice happily and continuously, without interruption. Due to such joy, 
our laziness will be eliminated and we will achieve some result.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 The power of suspension 
The power of suspension is to rest when your body and mind are worn out due to 
your joyous effort. As soon as you ha ve recovered, you should start again. 
 
The power of suspension is, for example, having  become physically and mentally tired due to 
meditating, to stop for a bit, relax, and be ca lm. Then, when our body and mind regain their 
power, we should once again make effort in our practice. Likewise, when our body or mind 
become tired from studying, we should stop for a bit, relax our body, relax our mind, lie 
down, sleep a bit, and then later on once again ta ke up our studies. If we do this, we will be 
able to go on and on.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C3C How the body and mind become serviceable through [joyous effort ] 
In this way, in dependence on stopping adverse conditions and relying on 
favorable conditions, you should undertake joyous effort until your body and 
mind become light, like a piece of cotton carried by the wind. 
 
Our body and mind become serviceable throug h joyous effort. Through stopping adverse 
conditions and relying on conducive conditions, we should engage in joyous effort until our 
body and mind become light, like a piece of cotton blown by the wind.  
 We need to develop joyous effort, the armor-li ke joyous effort and so  forth, continuously 
by eliminating conditions adverse to it and relying on conditions, both inner and outer, 
conducive to its development. For example, if we  have companions who put joyous effort into 
doing good things, we will also enjoy doin g the same. Therefore, this is a conducive 
condition. Likewise, eating a moderate amount  good food is a conducive condition for the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
661 development of joyous effort. On the other hand, if we stay together with  lazy people who do 
bad things, for example, who spend their time in smoking and drinking, we too will become 
attached to bad activities and our mind will be come unhappy and we will experience physical 
problems. Therefore, this is an adverse condition to joyous effort. For this reason, we need to 
examine what is happening around us and to give up things that are unfavorable to our 
Dharma practice. We should take care of our body and mind, thinking that we need to be 
healthy in order to be able to practice more strongly. A monk, named Gyeltsen, in Tibet, who 
was from the same class as Khensur Jampa Tegc hog and Geshe Tashi Bum, once sent a short 
letter to a disciple in India saying “If my mind is  quiet, there exists the state of liberation even 
in prison.” Although he had been put in prison  together with other people, because his mind 
was relaxed he did not experience any difficultie s and was able to practi ce and keep his mind 
happy. In this way, the state of liberation exists wherever we are, in prison or in our home, 
and it is not necessary to go to practice in an isolated place.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-4C4 How joyous effort is made to possess the six perfections 
While training in joyous effort, you sh ould possess the six perfections. While 
abiding in joyous effort oneself, to set others  in it is the generosity of joyous effort. 
The remaining are as before. 
 
When we are training in joyous effort, if we al so encourage others to de velop joyous effort, it 
becomes the generosity of joyous effort. The re maining perfections are the same as before. In 
other words, by abandoning the discordant class of joyous effort, that is, laziness, it becomes 
the morality of joyous effort. By practicing joyous effort with an undisturbed mind, it 
becomes the patience of joyous effort. By practi cing joyous effort with a single-pointed mind, 
not allowing it to wander, it becomes the concen tration of joyous effort. By practicing joyous 
effort while thinking that the th ree spheres – the practitioner, we  ourselves, the object of our 
joyous effort, and the joyous e ffort – are empty of inherent ex istence, it becomes the wisdom 
of joyous effort.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-5 Training in concentration 
A The entity of concentration 
B Its subdivisions  
C How to generate [concentration ] in one's continuum 
The fifth has three [points]: the entity of  concentration, its subdivisions, and how 
to generate [concentration ] in one’s continuum. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-5A The entity of concentration 
First: The entity of concentration is a mi nd that abides single-pointedly on any 
suitable virtuous observed object. 
 
The entity of concentration is a mind that abid es single-pointedly on any suitable virtuous 
observed object. Although here  the text says “virtuous observed object,” concentration 
actually can be developed with respect to any observed object whatsoever. This is mentioned 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
662 by Atisha in his Lamp for the Path . Thus, we can choose any object on which the mind is 
inclined to remain. When we meditate on love , compassion, or the mind of enlightenment, 
our mind should become the very entity of lo ve, compassion, or the mind of enlightenment. 
In other words, we should make love and so forth become manifest in our mind and then 
keep our mind focused on it steadfastly, without allowing it to move to any other object. This 
is one way to develop concentration with respec t to a virtuous observed object. Another way 
to meditate on a virtuous observed object is to visualize an image of the Buddha, Manjushri, 
Tara, the merit field, and so forth, making it s aspect appear clearly to our mind and then 
concentrate on it, that is, keep the mind fixed on it. We can also meditate on the four noble truths and their sixteen attributes by, for exampl e taking any of the four attributes of true 
sufferings – impermanence, suffering, empty, or  selfless – as the object of the mode of 
apprehension of our mind. We should generate  its aspect and then concentrate on it.  
 Alternatively, in order to reduce our attachme nt to someone’s body, we can meditate on it 
as having the aspect of ugliness. For example, we can imagine the body of the person to whom 
we are attached as being covered with holes through which worms are going in and out, or 
imagine it to be a rotten corpse or a skelet on. If, on the other hand, we are angry with 
someone, we should meditate on love for him or her, thinking that just as we need happiness and the causes of happiness so too does the othe r person. In this case  we meditate on the 
virtuous observed object that is love and deve lop familiarity with it, whereby we can eliminate 
its discordant class, anger. When our mind is  filled with ignorance,  we can meditate on 
dependent-relationship, thinking that all ph enomena are dependent-relations and therefore 
do not exist inherently. We should try to unders tand this and to meditate single-pointedly on 
this aspect, whereby we will gain a deeper un derstanding of emptiness and thereby eliminate 
our ignorance. When we develop strong pride, we should medita te on the fact that our body 
is composed of the four elements and four deri vatives and examine whether it is in fact so 
pure and beautiful. We can look for the I that is so proud, examining whether it can be found among the four elements, the eighteen constituents , the twelve sources, or the five aggregates. 
In this way, we will come to see that there ar e many things that we do not know, whereby our 
pride will be deflated. We should meditate in  order to gain concentr ation and eliminate our 
laziness.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-5B Its subdivisions 
By way of entity, there are the two: mund ane and supramundane. By way of class, 
there are the three: the class of calm abid ing, the class of special insight, and the 
class that is a union of the two. When it is divided by way of function, there is the 
concentration that causes one to abide in  physical and mental happiness in this 
life, the concentration that manifestly accomplishes good qualities, and the 
concentration that enacts the welfare of sentient beings. 
 
When concentration is divided by way of enti ty, there are the two: mundane concentration 
and supramundane concentration.  When concentration is divide d by way of class, there are 
the three: the concentrat ion that is the class of calm abid ing, the concentration that is the 
class of special insight, and co ncentration that is the class of  the union of calm abiding and 
special insight. When concentration is divided by way of function, th ere are the three: the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
663 concentration that causes one to abide in ha ppiness in this life; the concentration that 
manifestly accomplishes good qualities, for exam ple, the qualities of a buddha of the four legs 
of magical emanation, the clairvoyances, and so  forth; and the concentr ation that enacts the 
welfare of sentient beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
1 The concentration that causes one to abide in physical and mental happiness in this life 
The first is {232} the concentration that generates physical and mental pliancy in 
one who is in meditative equipoise. 
 
The concentration that causes one to abide in mental and physical happiness in this life is the 
concentration that generates physical and me ntal pliancy in one who is in meditative 
equipoise.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The concentration that manifestly accomplishes good qualities 
The second is a concentration that accompli shes the good qualities shared with the 
hearers, such as the clairvoyances, complete  liberations, totalities, masteries, and so 
forth.  
 
The concentration that manifestly accomplishes  good qualities is th e concentration that 
accomplishes the good qualities common to hearer s – the clairvoyances, complete liberations, 
totalities, masteries, and so fo rth. The six clairvoyances, eight complete liberations, ten 
totalities, and eight masteries are discussed in the Ornament for Clear Realizations  and 
Supplement to the Middle Way . They are shared by or common to hearers, bodhisattvas, and 
buddhas.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The concentration that enacts the welfare of sentient beings 
The third is the concentration that accomp lishes the welfares in eleven ways by 
means of concentration. 
 
The third type of concentration is the concentrat ion that accomplishes the welfare of sentient 
beings in eleven ways. The eleven ways in wh ich bodhisattvas enact the welfare of sentient 
beings are: 
1. they enact the welfare by performing action s and alleviating the suffering of sentient 
beings; 
2. they enact the welfare by eliminating the co nfusion of sentient beings with method; 
3. they enact the welfare by benefiting sentient beings; 
4. they enact the welfare of those afflicted by fear; 
5. they enact the welfare of thos e who are tormented by sorrow; 
6. they enact the welfare of those who lack necessities such as food, drink, possessions, and so 
forth; 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
664 7. they enact the welfare of those who ar e unable to find a place to stay; 
8. they enact the welfare through fulfilling thei r wishes in accordance with their minds; 
9. they enact the welfare of thos e who are engaging correctly; 
10. they enact the welfare of those who are en gaging wrongly by making them engage 
correctly; 
11. they enact the welfare of those who ar e to be subdued by emanating magical 
manifestations that fly in the sky, go underg round, and so forth, whereby they generate 
interest in the Dharma. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-5C How to generate [concentration ] in one's continuum 
The thoughts regarding the advantages of cultivating concentration and the 
disadvantages of not cultivating it will be explained in the chapter on calm abiding. 
 
The advantages of cultivating concentrat ion and disadvantages of not cultivating 
concentration will be explained in the context of ca lm abiding. In short, in order to gain calm 
abiding, there are eight antidotes that need to be  applied to the five faults. The first fault, the 
main obstacle to generating concentration, is laziness. The second fault is forgetfulness, for 
example, when we meditate on a deity to forget how to visualize the deta ils of the deity or to 
forget the instructions on how to do this. The third fault is laxity and excitement. Laxity is 
literally a sinking mind, whereupon the mind be comes unclear, dull, and sleepy. Excitement 
is a mind that wanders outside due to remember ing previous enjoyments , for example, events 
with friends and so forth. Thes e two are the main obstacle to developing calm abiding. The 
fourth fault, non-application, is to not apply the appropriate antidote when either laxity or 
excitement arise. The fifth fault, over-applicat ion, is to apply the antidote to laxity or 
excitement even though neither of them have arisen. These five  faults are the obstacles to 
generating calm abiding. They are overcome by ei ght antidotes. The first antidote is faith. Due 
to seeing the qualities of concentration, we develop clear faith in them. Due to this, there 
arises aspiration, the second antidote, which is  the wish to attain them. Due to this, we 
develop effort, the third antidote. Then we gene rate pliancy or suppleness of body and mind, 
the fourth antidote. These are the four antidote s to laziness. The fifth antidote, mindfulness, 
is the antidote to forgetfulness. Mindfulness or  memory is the opposite of forgetfulness; it 
keeps the object clearly in mind. The sixth antido te, introspection, is the antidote to laxity 
and excitement. The seventh antidote, intention,  is the antidote to non-application since 
intention moves the mind toward an antidote . The eighth antidote, equanimity, is the 
antidote to over-application, when neither laxi ty or excitement have arisen. Over-application 
of antidotes distracts the mind, therefore when neither laxity or excitement have arisen we 
should leave the mind in equilibrium. These ar e the eight antidotes that oppose the five 
faults. They will be explained in more detail later on.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
 The generosity of training in concentrat ion while possessing the six perfections is, 
while abiding in concentration oneself, to  set other in it. The remaining are as 
before. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 16-20 May 2005 (14) 
665  
When we practice the perfection of concentration, we should do so in combination with all six perfections. For example, when we abide in concentration and strive to set others in it, 
this is the generosity of concentration. The remaining are as before. 
 
(Oral transmission 12:11-12:23, pp. 231-235)
