666 Wednesday morning, 25 May 2005 
 (Note: There were no teachings 23-24 May 2005  due to the annual Nyung Ne on the full 
moon of Sakadawa.) 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-6 Training in wisdom 
A The entity of wisdom 
B Its subdivisions  
C How to generate [wisdom] in one’s continuum 
The sixth has three [points]: the entity of wisdom, its subdivisions, and how to 
generate [wisdom] in one’s continuum. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-6A The entity of wisdom 
First: In general wisdom is that which very  thoroughly distinguishes the qualities in 
the thing that is being investigated. Here it is the wisdom that is skilled in the five 
sciences and so forth.  
 
In general wisdom is that which very thoroughly distinguishes the qualities in the thing that is 
being investigated in terms of their entity and so forth. However, here it is the wisdom that is 
skilled and so forth in the five sc iences. Among them, inner science ( nang gi rig pa ) is the most 
important. The others are the sciences of dialectics or logic ( gtan tshigs kyi rig pa ), grammar 
(sgra'i rig pa ), medicine ( gso ba'i rig pa ), and arts and crafts ( bzo gnas kyi rig pa ). 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-6B Its subdivisions  
Second: There are three: the wisdom realizing the ultimate, the wisdom realizing 
conventionalities, and the wisdom realiz ing the welfare of sentient beings.  
 
The wisdom realizing the ultimate is the wisd om realizing emptiness. The wisdom realizing 
conventionalities is the wisdom realizing all the varieties of ph enomena. In addition, there is 
the wisdom realizing the welfare of sentient beings.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
1 Wisdom realizing the ultimate 
The first is [the wisdom ] comprehending the suchness that is selflessness by means 
of a meaning generality and [the wisdom ] comprehending the suchness that is 
selflessness by means of a direct perceiver. 
 
The wisdom realizing the ultimate is of two ty pes: the wisdom realizing selflessness by means 
of a meaning generality and the wisdom real izing selflessness directly or nakedly.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
667 2 Wisdom realizing conventionalities  
The second is the wisdom that is  skilled in the five sciences. 
 
The wisdom realizing conventionalities is the wi sdom that is skilled in the five sciences. 
Among the five, the main one is inner sc ience. For example, texts such as the Ornament for 
Clear Realizations , the Ornament for the Middle Way , and the Vinaya Sutra teach how to subdue 
the mind, how phenomena exist and how they func tion, and so forth, whereby they enable us 
to develop wisdom. The Compendium of Valid Cognition  teaches how to develop inner 
realizations by way of a valid cognizer, that is, an inferential cognizer and a direct perceiver. 
There are five sense direct perceivers, a mental di rect perceiver, a yogic direct perceiver, and, 
according to some tenets, a self-knowing direct perceiver. This text also sets out the four 
reasonings and eight reasonings that establis h Buddha Shakyamuni to be a valid being. For 
example, he looks six times during the day and ni ght at sentient beings to see whether or not 
they are ready to be ripened and, if they are re ady, he ripens them directly or indirectly by 
manifesting in different ways to teach them. Because all sentient beings are wandering in 
cyclic existence due to the ignorance conceiving a self, he taught selflessness. At the time of he 
was an ordinary being, he developed compassion  and then gradually developed the realization 
of the selflessness of persons until he attained a direct realization of it on the path of seeing. 
He then continued to meditate on it, whereby he  achieved the state of thus gone (tathagata) 
or gone to bliss (sugata), in which he had gone  beyond cyclic existenc e to enlightenment due 
to having abandoned all objects of abandonm ent and having come to know all phenomena 
directly and simultaneously without exception. He  then acted to benefit all sentient beings by 
turning the first wheel of Dharma of the four noble truths, the second wheel of Dharma of 
emptiness, and the third wheel of Dharma of di fferentiating characteristics. He taught the 
first mainly to disciples who were Vaibhashikas  or Sautrantikas, the se cond to Madhyamikas, 
and the third to Chittamatrins. In  this way he gave teachings to holders of the four tenets of 
Buddhism. This is what is meant by inner science.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 Wisdom realizing the welfare of sentient beings 
The third is to know how to accomplish, without misdeeds, the welfare of sentient 
beings in this and future lives.  
 
The wisdom enacting the welfare of sentient  beings is the wisdom that knows how to 
accomplish the welfare of sentient beings in th is life and future lives without committing any 
misdeeds. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-6C How to generate [wisdom] in one’s continuum 
1 The advantages of generating wisdom 
2 The disadvantages of not generating wisdom 
The third is to think about the advant ages of generating wisdom and the 
disadvantages of not generating it. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
668 In order to generate wisdom we need to unders tand and investigate the benefits of generating 
it in our continuum and the disadvantages of not generating it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-6C1 The advantages of generating wisdom 
A It is the root of all good qualities 
First: In the Hundred Verses on Wisdom  it says: 
 
The root of all good qualities, 
The seen and unseen, is wisdom. Thus, in order to accomplish both,  Thoroughly uphold wisdom. 
 
Whatever good qualities arise, th eir root is wisdom. In fact, the Hundred Verses on Wisdom  says 
that since the root of all good qualities, both  the seen and unseen, is wisdom, in order to 
achieve them it is necessary to uphold or trai n in wisdom. While other sentient beings have 
good qualities that we can see directly, such as the knowledge obtained from studying, there 
are other qualities, such as inner realizations, that remain hidden.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
B The fact that a bodhisattva is not perturbed by good or bad conditions and that he is skilled in 
methods such as generosity etc. depends on wisdom 
Although a bodhisattva gives his own fles h to someone who asks him for it, {232} 
like taking [a leaf] from a medicinal plant it is not done with the 
conceptualizations of pride, discourageme nt, etc., but is done through directly 
perceiving suchness by means of wisdom . By means of the wisdom seeing the 
problems of existence and pe ace, he practices ethical discipline for the welfare of 
others, thereby purifying ethical discipline . By means of the wisdom realizing the 
faults of impatience and the good qualitie s of patience, his mind is subdued, due 
to which he is not captivated by he retical practices and suffering. Through 
understanding, by means of wisdom, the ex cellent basis that is undertaking joyous 
effort, he exerts himself in that, whereb y there is great progress on the path. By 
means of the wisdom relying on a process of reasoning, he achieves the supreme 
joy and happiness of the concentration ab iding on the meaning of suchness. In 
this way, the complete purity of the first five perfections, generosity, etc., depends 
on wisdom. 
 
That a bodhisattva is not moved or perturbed by conducive or adverse conditions and is 
skilled in the methods of generosity, morality , patience, joyous effort, and concentration 
depends on wisdom. For example, when a bodhisa ttva is asked to give away his own flesh, he 
willingly cuts a piece from his body and gives it away. Like taking a leaf from a medicinal 
plant, he does this without any problem whatsoever. He also does so without the 
conceptualizations of pride, discouragement, an d so forth. This is possible only because the 
bodhisattva directly realizes suchness by means of wisdom. Also, by means of the wisdom that 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
669 sees the troubles of existence and peace, his engages in pure morality for the welfare of 
sentient beings. In addition, because he has co mpletely abandoned self-cherishing in order to 
take care of sentient beings, his morality is pure. By means of the wisdom realizing the many 
qualities of patience and the many faults of impatience, his mind is subdued and he is not 
captivated by heretical practices and suffering. An  example of a heretical or wrong practice is, 
for example, due to seeing that in one’s past  life one was a pig and was then reborn as a 
human being, to behave like a pig in order to  take another human rebirth in the future. A 
bodhisattva is free from such mistaken behavior . He also cannot be captivated by suffering 
due to having developed patience through un derstanding the disadvantages of impatience 
and the advantages of patience.  
 Through understanding, by means of wisdom, th at joyous effort is the basis, he exerts 
himself in that and thereby progresses along th e path very quickly. In other words, his 
accomplishment of the path quickly improves. Through relying on wisdom, there arises the 
supreme joy and happiness, physical and mental , of the concentration abiding on suchness. 
In this way, the complete purity of all the fi rst five perfections, generosity and so forth, 
depends on wisdom. For this reason, we too n eed to investigate the nature of phenomena, 
trying to understand their qualities and faults. We need to strive in the practice of the six 
perfections as this has many advantages, wher eas their opposites – miserliness, immorality, 
impatience or anger, laziness, and distraction – have many disadvantages. Although we do not 
recognize their disadvantages, they in fact brin g many problems to us. Therefore, we need to 
understand their disadvantages and then try to eliminate them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 232) says:  
C One possessing wisdom can accomplish without contradiction even those actions that appear 
contradictory to common beings 
Those endowed with wisdom can accomplish two qualities that appear to be 
opposed without any contradiction. If a bodhisattva were to become a wheel-turning king with power over the four co ntinents, that he does not come under 
the power of desirable objects is due to  the force of the minister of wisdom. 
Likewise, although his love that sees sentient beings as attractive is very strong, it is 
not even slightly mixed with attachment . Although his intense compassion that 
cannot bear the suffering of sentient beings lasts for a long time, he is without the 
laziness that does not delight in virtue through being oppressed by sorrow. 
Although he possesses immeasurable joy,  he is without the restlessness that 
distracts the mind from the observed object. Although he possesses great equanimity in his continuum, he does not neglect the welfare of migrating beings even for an instant. All this is done out of wisdom because the obstacles to 
accomplishing them with corresponding strength are ceased by wisdom. 
 
Actions that appear to be contradictory to  ordinary beings can be performed without 
contradiction by those who possess wisdom. In  other words, two apparently contradictory 
qualities, one that appears to ordinary beings  as something to be developed and one that 
appears as something to be avoided, can be accomplished by a bodhisattva. Even if a 
bodhisattva were to become a wheel-turning king  with power over the fo ur continents in the 
four cardinal directions, he would not come un der the control of desirable objects because he 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
670 has the force of the minister of wisdom. On th e other hand, when we see attractive forms we 
come under the power of attachment, when we hear pleasant music we come under the power 
of attachment, when we smell pleasant fragra nces we come under the power of attachment, 
when we taste delicious food we come under the power of attachment, and when we feel 
pleasant tangible objects that are soft and smooth we come unde r the power of attachment. In 
this way our mind is always under the control of  the five desirable sense objects. Contrarily, 
even if a bodhisattva has become a wheel-turning king, he does not come under their control.  
 When a bodhisattva sees sentient beings, he sees them as attractive, whereby he develops 
love for them. However, his love is not mixed even with the slightest attachment. He only 
develops pure love for them. Likewise, when he sees sentient beings who are suffering greatly, 
he develops strong compassion that lasts for a long time. In spite of this, he never develops 
the laziness that dislikes engaging in virtue, bu t due to his wisdom acts with joyous effort. 
Although he possesses immeasurable joy, he is  never distracted when observing sentient 
beings. Although he possesses great equanimity  in his continuum, he never neglects the 
welfare of sentient beings even for an instan t. Instead he constantly seeks to enact their 
welfare due to the power of wisdom. In this way, all the interferences or obstacles to 
accomplishing the good qualities of the si x perfections are eliminated by wisdom. 
 Thus, from all points of view we need wi sdom. Even when doing mundane activities, we 
need wisdom. Without wisdom, we cannot succeed even in mundane activities. Hence, 
wisdom or knowledge is necessary for accomplish ing both the inner, the side of the path, and 
the outer, mundane activities. The more wisdom  we have, the more we will understand the 
final nature of all phenomena, that they do no t exist inherently or truly but exist by way of 
being imputed by thought and name. Some peop le think that if phenomena do not exist 
inherently they do not exist at all, however su ch conclusions are danger ous. Although things 
do not exist inherently, they do exist in depe ndence, that is, in dependence on causes and 
conditions, in dependence on parts, and in dependence on being imputed by a valid conception and a valid name. That phenomen a are empty of inherent existence and yet 
perform their respective functions is not at all contradictory. We need to develop the wisdom 
that understands this.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
D That the two truths are considered to be non-contradictory is also due to wisdom 
The Praise of the Praiseworthy  [v. 23a-b] says: 
 
Without discarding reality, 
{233} It also accords with conventionalities. 
 Thus, that it is not necessary to discard the reality that is the gaining of great 
certainty with respect to the fact that not even a mere atom of the reference object 
that holds the sign is established, and that conventionalities are found through 
deep certainty regarding the fact that indi vidual results arise from their inner and 
outer causes and conditions is not contra dictory and is compatible. Although they 
are extremely contradictory for those with out the power of awareness, for those 
possessing wisdom they are not co ntradictory and are compatible.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
671  
If the two truths are not contradictory, does th is mean that they have a common locus? Since 
the two truths do not have a common locus, in  this sense conventional truth and ultimate 
truth are contradictory. However, there is no need  to discard reality, since it also accords with 
conventionalities. If we investigate the object of comprehension that holds the sign of true 
existence or inherent existence, we will find th at it does not have the slightest atom of true 
existence, or existence from its own side. A tr uly existent object of comprehension does not 
exist, not even the slightest atom. It does not ex ist at all. By gaining ce rtainty with respect to 
this, our mind will not discard thusness or em ptiness because we will understand that this 
does not contradict the fact that all external  and internal phenomena arise from causes and 
conditions, and that actions give rise to results. If we examine this deeply, we will come to see 
that conventionally phenomena perform function s. By understanding the ultimate truth, we 
will also understand that conventionally ph enomena perform their functions and that these 
two are therefore not contradictory.  
 On the other hand, those who do not have st rong wisdom see them to be contradictory. 
They think that, if things do not have an atom of inherent existence, th ey do not exist. They 
hold the view of nihilism. Alternatively, they th ink that if things function, that is, if causes 
and conditions bring results, it is because they  exist truly or inherently. However, for those 
with wisdom the two – the lack of inherent ex istence and the ability to perform a function – 
are not contradictory. Thus, external causes and co nditions give rise to results. For example, if 
we sow a flower seed in a garden, the seed is the cause and the water, heat, fertilizer, and so 
forth are the conditions that give rise to the re sult, a flower. According to  what kind of flower 
seed was sown, a particular type of flower arises. Similarly, when a rice seed is planted it gives 
rise to a rice sprout, and not a barley sprout. The result corresponds to  the cause. Likewise, 
that fire gives rise to smoke also depends on ca uses and conditions. Therefore, external things 
depend upon causes and conditions. In the same way, internally, due to causes and 
conditions our mind becomes happy or unhappy. Due to bad conditions our mind becomes unhappy, and due to good conditions it become s happy. This is how things function. We 
should understand with wisdom that these two – not existing inherently and existing 
conventionality – are not contradictory.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
E That permissions and prohibitions are not contradictory also depends on wisdom 
In the same text [V. 7] it also says: 
 
Regarding permissions and prohibitions  
Some of your words are definite as one. 
While others are not as definite,  
They are also not mutually contradictory. 
 
Thus, there are many different permission s and prohibitions in the higher and 
lower vehicles and in sutra and tantra an d for those seeking the intention of the 
infinite scriptures regarding the two bein g practiced by a single person, to those 
who do not have the strength of intellig ence they are contradictory, whereas for 
the skilled they are not contradictory. This too is due to wisdom. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
672  
That permissions and prohibitions  are not contradictory. For ex ample, while the ordained in 
general are not permitted to eat after midday, perm ission is granted to do so if one’s body is 
not healthy. One can say “Please, Buddha, gran t me permission to ea t after noon,” whereby 
one is permitted to do so. While the permission s and prohibitions may seem to some to be 
contradictory, they are not because they are rela ted to specific occasions. For example, if his 
or her body is not healthy, a mo nk or nun can eat after noon.  
 In short, although there appear to be cont radictions in what is permitted and what is 
prohibited in the higher and lower vehicles an d in sutra and tantra, they are in fact not 
contradictory. Rather, they are to  be practiced by a single pers on. Thus, one first practices the 
common path of sutra, after which one begins to  practice tantra whereby one is permitted to 
do more and more what is prohibited by sutra.  For example, someone with the realization of 
the actual or meaning clear light of the comple tion stage of tantra is permitted to kill a 
sentient being. This is because even though exte rnally it appears that he has killed a sentient 
being, in reality he has stopped that sentient being from creating strong negative karma and 
enabled it to take a high rebirth. On the othe r hand, from the point of view of sutra we are 
prohibited to kill any sentient being.  
 Wednesday afternoon, 25 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 233) says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-6C2 The disadvantages of not generating wisdom 
A The faults of being separated from wisdom 
B Causes of wisdom and so forth 
C How [wisdom] comes to po ssess the six perfections 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-6C2A The faults of being separated from wisdom: that fault that, if wisdom is lacking, 
generosity and so forth will no t become completely pure  
If they are separated from wisdom, the fi ve – generosity and so forth – and the 
view will not be pure. In the Summary of the Perfections  it says: 
 
In those who strive for result s in the absence of wisdom, 
The nature of giving will not be completely pure. 
Giving for others’ welfare is said to be “the best of generosities.” The remaining are applications for the pu rpose of increasing wealth. [VI, 5] 
 If wisdom is lacking, the first five perfections,  generosity, morality, patience, joyous effort, and 
concentration, will not become completely pu re. For example, if we are separated from 
wisdom, our practice of generosity will not be pu re. The best of generosities is to give for the 
welfare of others.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And [VI, 11]: 
 
If the darkness is not dispe lled by the light of wisdom, 
One will not come to possess pure ethical discipline. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
673 Mostly, ethical discipline, due to the absence of wisdom, 
Will be together with the pollution of  the afflictions due to a fault in 
one’s understanding. 
 
Without the light of wisdom, our morality will no t become completely pure, that is, it will be 
impure. This is because without wisdom, our mo rality will be stained by the faults of the 
afflictions, like water is made turbid due to  being mixed with dust. We need to keep our 
morality pure through the wisdom that unders tands that if we commit certain actions our 
morality will become impure. In other words, we  need to apply wisdom in order to keep pure 
morality. If we do not have wisdom, we will no t understand what is the discordant class of 
morality and what is pure morality, that is, wh at actions are to be discarded and what actions 
are to be adopted. Only if we have wisdom, can we keep our morality pure.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And [VI, 13]: 
 
If the mind is polluted by the faults of distorted wisdom, One will not abide in the good qualities of patience; One will sustain dislike for investigating the excellent and the faulty; and  
One will be renowned like a king who is without good qualities. 
 
Distorted wisdom includes the view of the transi tory collection, the view of an extreme, the 
view holding a bad view as supreme, the view holding bad morality and conduct as supreme, 
and wrong view. These views are considered to be wisdoms but they are wrong or distorted wisdoms. Due to them, the mind becomes poll uted and we will not abide in the good 
qualities of patience, we will have dislike for investigating the excellent and faulty, and will be 
renowned like a king without good qualities.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
And [I, 16]: 
 
It is extolled as sublime for the wise. {234} There is nothing more subtle and deep than it. The mind completely unobscured  by the fault of attachment 
Will not take the straight path without wisdom. 
 Wisdom is extolled or praised as sublime for th e wise. There is nothing more subtle and more 
profound than wisdom. Even the mind that is unobscured by attachment will not travel the 
straight path, that is, be honest, without wisdom.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
674 And [VI, 18a]: 
 
If the mind upholds the manner of wisdom without effort, 
The view of that will not become completely pure. 
 
To be renowned as a king implies that for a king who lacks good qualities fame 
may occur one time but, through degenerating, it will pass.  
 The view of whoever does not have effort with  respect to wisdom will not become completely 
pure. “Renowned as a king” means that one wh o is renowned as a king who is without 
qualities will degenerate the name “king” and no one will respect him. In other words, if one 
has the mere name “king” but does not have the good qualities of a king, one will merely 
degenerate this role.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-6C2B Causes of wisdom 
1 The importance of hearing as a cause of wisdom 
Therefore, it being necessary to genera te wisdom, one should listen to the 
completely pure scriptures, its cause, in accordance with one’s mental capacity. In 
the Summary of Perfections  [VI, 48] it says: 
 
Little hearing, blind from birth, not knowing how to meditate; 
With failings like that, how can anyone think? Therefore, make effort in he aring. From that cause,  
There is thinking. By meditating, spacious wisdom arises.  
 In order to generate wisdom, we need to create  its cause – much hearing and studying of the 
Buddha’s scriptures. We should listen to th em, think about them, and meditate on them, 
whereby we will respectively develop the wisdom arisen from hearing, the wisdom arisen from 
thinking, and the wisdom arisen from meditation. If we have little hearing, are blind, and do 
not know how to meditate, how can we think ab out the meaning? Therefore, we need to put 
effort into hearing, then think about the meanin g of what we have hear d, and then meditate 
on the meaning that we have understood. If we  try to meditate without hearing and thinking, 
it would be like someone trying to climb up a cliff without hands. Only having heard a lot of 
teachings and thought about them, should we medi tate on them either by way of stabilizing 
meditation or analytical meditation. All this depends on hearing or study. If, on the other 
hand, we do not study, we will not gain any kn owledge and we will not be able to understand 
anything. Then, even if we try to meditate in a cave or in our room, nothing will happen. It 
will not be any different from sleeping!  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 The wisdom that abandons the two obscurations also depends on hearing 
Venerable Maitreya also wrote [in his Uttaratantra , V, 14-15]: 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
675 Any conceptualization of the three spheres is asserted 
To be an obscuration to objects of knowledge. Any conceptualization of miserliness and the like 
Is asserted to be an afflictive obscuration. 
 
There is nothing apart from wisdom That abandons them; therefore, 
Wisdom is supreme. Likewise, 
Hearing for the sake of that is said to be “supreme hearing.” 
 
And in the Compendium of Trainings  [k.20a-c] it says: 
 
Be patient. Seek hearing.  
Then dwell in a forest. 
Put effort into meditative equipoise. 
 
The two obscurations are the afflictive obscur ations and obscurations to knowledge. The 
wisdom that abandons these two ob scurations depends on hearing.  
 The three spheres, for example, the meditator,  the object of meditation, and the action of 
meditation, do not exist inherently. The concep tualization of them as truly or inherently 
existent is asserted to be an obscuration to objects of knowledge. The conceptualizations of 
miserliness, immorality, impatience or ange r, laziness, and distraction are afflictive 
obscurations. These two obscurations are aban doned by wisdom, nothing else can abandon 
them. Because of this, wisdom is  supreme. Because this wisdom arises from hearing, hearing 
is also said to be supreme. In other words, through the wisdom arisen from hearing and the 
wisdom arisen from thinking, the wisdom arisen  from meditation arises. Therefore, first we 
need to generate the wisdom arisen from hear ing, then the wisdom arisen from thinking 
about the meaning, and then the wisdom arisen from meditation that is equipoised on its 
object. By means of this wisdom, we will come  to realize emptiness by means of a direct 
perceiver rather than by means of a meaning ge nerality or sound generality. In other words, 
we will be able to see emptiness nakedly.  
 The Compendium of Trainings  says that we should be patient,  that we should seek hearing, 
and that we should then dwell in a forest and put effort into meditative equipoise. In other 
words, first we need to be patient with respec t to understanding emptiness. For this purpose, 
we need to hear a lot of teachings and read a lot of books, whereby the wisdom arisen from 
h e a r i n g  w i l l  a r i s e .  T h e n  w e  s h o u l d  t h i n k  a b o u t  t h e  m e a n i n g .  T h e n  w e  s h o u l d  g o  t o  a n  
isolated place, such as a forest, and meditate there, putting effort into meditative equipoise. 
By means of meditative equipoise on emptine ss, we will quickly abandon the afflictive 
obscurations and obscurations to knowledge.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The imprints of training in the instructions 
The holy beings of the past also said: Fi rst you should gather together the Dharma 
of hearing that exists in your mind. Then again and again you should think about 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
676 it, appraise it, and investigate it. Having  forgotten the Dharma, to sometimes train 
in holding and sustaining the mind will not be of help. The great meditators who 
teach the best become the best; the gr eat meditators who teach the middling 
become middling. However much one medi tates, to that same extent one’s 
knowledge of the Dharma should grow. {235 } Once this certainty has become firm 
through such considerations, you will not listen to the words of negative 
companions who say “All virtuous and non- virtuous thoughts are to be abandoned 
because they are conception.” Rather, you will think: “The Dharma does not teach 
anything like that and my virtuous spiritua l friend also does not assert that.” If 
lacking that, someone who has a little bit of faith but no wisdom will burst into 
tears when he sees a face with tears and into burst into laughter when he sees 
someone who is laughing. Considering true wh atever people say, like a river he will 
go wherever he is lead. 
 
The holy beings of the past said that at first we should hear the Dharma, and then we should 
keep it clear in our mind, think about it again and again, and do analytical meditation on it. 
In other words, we should put effort into not forgetting the scriptures and instructions we 
have studied, and instead to keep them in ou r mind without forgetfulness by thinking about 
them again and again. If we forget the Dharma  we have heard and studied and only train in 
holding and sustaining the mind, this will not be of any help, that is, it will not become a 
condition that develops our meditation. In othe r words, forgetting what  we have studied as 
well as the object of meditati on and the instructions, to train in holding the mind in its 
natural state will not be of help or assistance  to us. The great meditators who teach the best 
become like the Teacher. The great meditato rs who teach the middling become middling. 
The more we meditate the more we will unde rstand the Dharma. On the other hand, if we 
meditate alone in an empty cave with an empt y head, an empty mind, and an empty stomach, 
there will not be much benefit!   Once we have gained certainty, we will not listen to negative friends who say that both 
virtuous and non-virtuous thoughts are to be abandoned. If we understand the Dharma very 
well through studying the instructions, we will  understand that this is not taught in the 
scriptures of the Buddha and is not taught by ou r teachers. As a result, we will not listen to 
what others say and will not follow them. On th e other hand, if we lack wisdom but only have 
a bit of faith, when negative companions say th at virtuous thoughts such as bodhichitta are 
objects of abandonment because they are conceptions, we will believe them. We will be like someone who when seeing others cry also begins to cry and when  he sees others laughing he 
also laughs. In other words, like water flowing in  an irrigation ditch, if we are unintelligent 
and have not studied much we will follow whatev er others say. On the other hand, if we 
understand the Dharma deeply, wh en others say something opposite to it we will not follow 
them but will rely on the Buddha’ s teachings and our teachers. We  need to develop this kind 
of wisdom.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1A-6C2C How [wisdom] comes to possess the six perfections 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
677 At the time of training in wisdom, it should possess the six perfections. Through 
abiding in wisdom oneself, to set others in it is the generosity of wisdom. The 
remaining are as above. 
 
At the time of training in wisdom, we need to  simultaneously possess all six perfections. If 
while we ourselves abide in wisdom, we set others  in wisdom, this is the generosity of wisdom. 
While abiding in wisdom, if we strive to aban don distorted wisdom, this is the morality of 
wisdom. If we are patient with respect to meditati ng wisdom, this is the patience of wisdom. If 
we have a mind that delights in  meditating on emptiness, this is the joyous effort of wisdom. 
If we concentrate on wisdom, this is the conc entration of wisdom. The wisdom realizing the 
emptiness of the three spheres is the wisdom of wisdom.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A1B Training in the four means of gathering [disciples ] that ripen others’ 
continua 
1  The entity of the four means of gathering [disciples]  
Second: Generosity is the same as expl ained in the context of the perfection [of 
generosity ]. Pleasant speech is to teach the perfections to disciples. Meaningful 
conduct is to make the disciples engage in  the meanings that were taught or to 
make them engage in assuming them co rrectly. To act in accordance with the 
meaning is, through abiding in accordance with whatev er meaning one has taught 
to others, to train in accordance with it. In Ornament for the Great Vehicle Sutras 
[XVI, 72] it says: 
 
By according with giving, teaching it, making others assume it, And by oneself subsequently engaging in it, There is asserted to be pleasant speech,  Meaningful conduct, and according with the meaning. 
 The generosity of this contex t is explained in the Perfection of Wisdom Sutras and the 
Ornament for Clear Realizations , which say that we should gi ve whatever material things, 
including food, drink, clothing, and a place to stay, are needed to our disciples. This will 
make the mind of the disciple happy. After that we should talk  p l e a s a n t l y  o r  n i c e l y  t o  t h e  
disciple, teaching him the six perfections of generosity, morality, patience, joyous effort, 
concentration, and wisdom. Then we should eith er make the disciple engage in practicing 
them and make one who is already engaging in them, practice them even better. Then, we too 
n e e d  t o  l i v e  i n  a c c o r d a n c e  w i t h  w h a t  w e  t e a c h. If we talk to others but do not act in 
accordance with what we say, it will not be of benefit. It will just be empty words, like an 
echo, that serves no purpose. Therefore, we need  to perfectly practice whatever we teach. The 
Ornament for the Mahayana Sutras  says that we should give to others, talk nicely to them, make 
them practice, and that oneself should engage  in accordance with what one teaches.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
678 2 The reason for the presentation of four means of gathering 
Why are the ways of gathering [disciples] definite as four? In order to gather a 
retinue for the purpose of applying them to virtue, initially they must be joyful. In 
this regard, through giving them gifts of material things, this is done in 
dependence on benefiting their bodies. Ha ving thus become joyful, in order for 
them to engage in the path, initially they  need to know how to do that. For that, 
moreover, by explaining the Dharma to them through speaking pleasantly, they 
will give up unknowing and doubt and wi ll engage in apprehending the meaning 
unmistakenly. When they understand that , make them engage in accomplishing 
virtue through meaningful conduct. {236} Furthermore, if you do not accomplish 
that yourself but explain to others “You should engage like this. You should avoid 
like that,” they will say, “If you do no t accomplish it yourself, why do you tell 
others to accomplish it? You too still need to be corrected by others” and they will 
not listen to what is to be accomplished. However, if you accomplish that yourself, 
they will also newly engage [in the path ] through thinking: “Since he himself also 
abides in the virtue in which he guides me , if I accomplish it, there will definitely 
be benefit and happiness for me.” Also, those who have already engaged [in the 
path] will not turn away from it and will grow firm. Therefore, you should act in 
accordance with the meaning. 
 Why are there definitely four means of gathering di sciples? In order to gath er disciples so as to 
apply them to virtue, we first need to make th em happy. For this purp ose, we should give 
them material things and benefit their bodies by  giving them food if they need food, giving 
them drink if they need drink, giving them clothing if they need clothing, giving them 
medicine if they need medicine, and so forth. Du e to their bodies becoming healthy, they will 
become joyful and will generate interest in th e Dharma. Then, in order for them to engage in 
the path, they need to understand how to do so . For this purpose, we should teach them the 
Dharma by talking to them pleasantly and nice ly. Due to this, their unknowing of ignorance 
and doubts will be eliminated and they will  unmistakenly apprehend the meaning. When 
they understand this, we should encourage them  to engage in accomplishing virtue through 
meditation and so forth. However, if we ourselve s do not engage in abandoning what is to be 
abandoned and adopting what needs to be adopted, others will say “You tell us what to do, but you yourself do not do that! If you do not practice yourself, why do you tell other people 
to practice?” In this way, they will not listen to us. Therefore, it is important that we ourselves 
practice whatever we tell others to do. In this  way, others will think “He practices just as he 
teaches me to practice. Therefore,  if I too practice, I will definitely obtain benefit and become 
happy.” Also those who are already engaged in the path will not turn away from it, but will 
b e c o m e  m o r e  s t a b l e .  O n  t h e  o t h e r  h a n d ,  i f  w e  t e l l  o t h e r s  w h a t  t o  d o  b u t  d o  n o t  d o  i t  
ourselves, it will not be of any benefit. Theref ore, we should act in accordance with what we 
teach.  Thursday morning, 26 May 2005  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 236) says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
679 3 The need to rely on the four means of gathering disciples 
Since the buddhas taught that they accomp lish all the welfare of all disciples and 
that they are a good method, those who gather a retinue should rely on them. 
[Ornament of the Gr eat Vehicle Sutras , XVI, 77] says: 
 
Those who gather a retinue 
Should perfectly rely in this way. They accomplish all the welfare of everyone and 
Are thoroughly praised as being a good method.  
 
Those who wish to gather discip les should rely on the four means of gathering since they 
accomplish their welfare and are a good method. For example, we should talk to them nicely 
and skillfully, using pleasant words and teac hing them the Dharma, whereby they will 
generate interest in it. By relying on such means, we will accomplish the welfare of everyone. 
Thus, these four are praised as a good method. If  we do not fulfill others’ welfare, we will not 
be able to gather disciples around us.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3A2 Their summarized meaning 
The great Jowo [Atisha] taught how to  accomplish them during meditative 
equipoise and subsequent attainment:  
 
The vast conduct of bodhisattvas 
Is the six perfections and so forth. 
The yogi arising from meditative absorption, 
Steadfastly achieves the path of accumulation. 
 With respect to how the six perfections are ac complished during meditative equipoise and 
subsequent attainment, Jowo Atisha said that th e vast conduct of the bodhisattvas is the six 
perfections. Thus, having arisen from medita tive equipoise, we should train in the other 
perfections. Although in meditative equipoise on  emptiness, only the perfection of wisdom is 
manifest and the other perfections are not, wh en one arises from meditative equipoise we 
should engage in the other perfections without forgetting our understand ing of emptiness. In 
this way, we will simultaneously collect both meri t and wisdom. If we practice in this way, the 
path of accumulation will be a ccomplished with stability.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In this way, a beginner bodhisattva, who has taken the vows of a conquerors’ child, 
abiding on the path of accumulation ne ver passes beyond the six perfections, 
whether in meditative equipoise or subseq uent attainment. Ther efore, some of the 
six perfections are sustained in meditative equipoise and some in subsequent 
attainment. Some of the entity of concentr ation, calm abiding, and the entity of 
the perfection of wisdom, special insight,  are cultivated in meditative equipoise. 
The first three perfections and some parts of concentration and wisdom are 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
680 sustained in subsequent attainment. Joyous effort comes in both meditative 
equipoise and subsequent attainment. One class of patience – some minds ascertaining the profound Dharma – also occur during meditative equipoise. 
 
A bodhisattva who has taken vows, whether in meditative equipoise or subsequent 
attainment, does not pass beyond the six perfection s, that is, he practice s them all. Some of 
the perfections are sustained during meditati ve equipoise and some during subsequent 
attainment. The entity of the perf ection of concentration is calm abiding and the entity of the 
perfection of wisdom is special insight. Some  of these two are cultivated in meditative 
equipoise. The first three perfections, generosi ty, morality, and patience, and some parts of 
concentration and wisdom are cultivated in subs equent attainment. Joyous effort is the basis 
for both meditative equipoise and subsequent a ttainment, therefore it is cultivated on both 
occasions. Also one class of patience arises du ring meditative equipoise on emptiness. Which 
one? Among the three types of patience, it is th e patience with respect to the Dharma, that is, 
with respect to the emptiness of inherent or true  existence. Patience is needed with respect to 
this because some people are unable to be ar the thought that phenomena are empty of 
inherent existence due to thinking that this implies that phenomena do not exist at all, 
whereby they fall into the extreme of nihilism. Th erefore, the patience that is able to bear the 
lack of inherent existence, the final nature of phenomena,  is necessary in meditative 
equipoise.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
The great Jowo [Atisha] said: 
 
At the time of rising from meditative equipoise, {237} Familiarize yourself with the view that all phenomena  Are like the eight analogies of  an illusion and so forth. 
Subsequently, purify conceptions and Mainly train in method. At the time of meditative equipoise, Do calm abiding and special insight in equal parts and Familiarize yourself with this continuously. 
 Atisha said that when we arise from medi tative equipoise, we should, in subsequent 
attainment, familiarize ourselves with the eight analogies of an illusion, dream, reflection, 
mirage, and so forth. These eight are analog ies for deceptive phenomena. For example, a 
magician creates an illusion by blessing a subs tance, such as sticks or pebbles, with mantra 
and medicine, whereby the spectators’ eyes are tr icked and they see a horse or a beautiful girl. 
The illusion can be so real that in one of his texts Nagarjuna recounts the story of a young man who seeing such a magically created beautifu l woman, developed the strong desire to be 
with her, marry her, and so forth. However, such  magical tricks are not true in that they do 
not exist in the way that they appear. Another su ch example is the reflec tion of our face in a 
mirror, which appears to be a real  face but is not, there being no  face in the mirror. In this 
case appearances are also deceptive.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
681  There are eight such analogies for the lack of  true existence. Using these analogies of an 
illusion, a dream, and so forth, we should see all phenomena as not existing in the way that 
they appear but as being like an illusion, a dr eam, an echo in an empty room, and so forth. 
We should train our mind in this way in subs equent attainment or post-meditation. Thus, 
during subsequent attainment, we try mainly to  practice the side of method. Among the six 
perfections, the first five are mainly method. Thus, during subsequent attainment we should 
practice the generosity of giving material th ings, giving the Dharma, and giving protection; 
the morality of restraining from non-virtue, the morality of gathering virtue, and the morality 
of enacting the welfare of all sentient beings; and so forth. Then, during meditative equipoise, 
we should strive to develop calm abiding and sp ecial insight in equal parts, that is, without 
one being stronger than the other. We should co ntinuously strive to familiarize ourselves in 
this way, whereby we will eventually achieve their realization.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
If you hear about such wonderful conduct, so hard to do due to the mind being 
untrained, it produces torment in the mind. Although even bodhisattvas are 
unable to practice it right from the start, if, through understanding it, you 
acquaint yourself with it beginning from it being an object to be aspired for, later on you will come to naturally engage in it without depending on effort. Therefore, 
familiarization is important, for if you see it  to not be possible to actually engage in 
them and discard even familiarizing yourse lf with training the mind in them, you 
will be extremely far from the completely pure paths.  
 Even hearing about the bodhisattva conduct, wh ich is special, pure, and good, our mind is 
tormented by the thought “How can I ever do th at?” The bodhisattva conduct is not easy to 
practice from the beginning, however we should  still generate the motivation wishing to 
develop meditative equipoise and to practice the other perfections. By familiarizing with them 
i n  t h i s  w a y ,  l a t e r  o n  w e  w i l l  b e  a b l e  t o  engage in them naturally without much effort. 
Therefore, due to familiarizing ourselves with them, there will come a great result. on the 
other hand, if we think that we cannot actually engage in them and give up training our mind 
in familiarity with them, our achievement of th e pure path will be delayed and become very 
distant.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
In Praise of [the Buddha's] Boundless Qualities  it says: 
 
Even hearing about it, produces harm in the worldly.  
By familiarizing yourself for a long time with those practices  That you have not practiced, in time they will become natural for you.  Henceforth, good qualities are difficult to increase without thorough 
familiarization.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
682 This scripture says that worldly people, having  heard about the bodhisattva conduct which is 
difficult to practice, experience mental sufferi ng due to thinking that they are unable to 
engage in it. Although we canno t practice the conduct right from the beginning, we need to 
familiarize ourselves with it for a long time. No matter how difficult it is to train our mind in 
it, we should continue to do so, whereby it wi ll slowly become more and more familiar, until 
eventually we will no longer need to make e ffort in it, it will arise naturally. Whatever 
qualities we wish to obtain, we need to famili arize ourselves with them. If, on the other hand, 
we do not do this, we can be certain that th ey will not arise. However, if we familiarize 
ourselves with them through continuous effort , all qualities will easily be obtained.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
Therefore, those who have taken the bodhisattva vows bu t, lacking a method, are 
not training in the conduct and also thos e who have not taken the engaging mind 
by means of ritual, should put effort into developing the wish to train in it. Then, 
if, having increased their strength of thei r delight in training, they take the vows, 
[the vows] will be very stable. Theref ore, put effort into this. 
 
If we have taken the bodhisattva vows, we need to train in the bodhisattva conduct. If we do 
not do this, we will not be able  to keep the vow purely. Therefor e, we need to engage in the 
practice of generosity, morality, patience, joyo us effort, concentration, and wisdom. We must 
strive in them. This is the sole method, no other method exists. Those who have not taken 
the engaging mind of enlightenment, that is, the bodhisattva vows, should develop the wish 
to train in this conduct. The more we train in  the bodhisattva conduct,  the more our joy will 
arise, whereby we will want to take the bo dhisattva vows. If we then take them, the 
bodhisattva vows will be very stable. In short, if  we practice the bodhisattva conduct of the six 
perfections and then take the bodh isattva vows, they will be very stable. Therefore, we need to 
put effort into practicing them well.  
  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
 
This concludes the explanation of training in the aspiring mind through the stages 
of the path of great beings and the stages of the path of  training in the conduct of 
the conquerors’ children in general. 
 
This concludes the explanation of the training in the aspiring mind of enlightenment and the 
training in the conduct of the co nquerors’ children in general.

will seem that we have finished the text!   QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
683 STUDENT : If in a future life we do not remember that we have the bodhisattva vows, do we 
commit the fault of breaking the vow even though we are not aware of having it? 
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : If we take the bodhisattva vows in this life and strive to keep them, 
when we die we will relinquish them. Likewise, if we have the vows of individual liberation of 
a lay man or woman, a novice monk or nun, a probationary nun, or a fully-ordained monk or 
nun, they are relinquished when we die. In this case, death becomes the cause for 
relinquishing our vows. Because we are not bodhisattvas, the bodhisattva vows are also 
relinquished at death. However, in the case  of bodhisattvas abiding on the path of 
accumulation, path of preparation, or path of  seeing, when they die they do not relinquish 
the vows. Instead they continue to have bodhic hitta and the path on their continua, due to 
which they also continue to po ssess the bodhisattva vows. On th e other hand, ordinary beings 
like us who take the bodhisattva vows and try to keep them in this life, relinquish the 
bodhisattva vows when we die. I think it is like this.  
 
STUDENT :  C a n  G e s h e - l a  p l e a s e  g i v e  a n  e x a m p l e  f r o m  h i s  o w n  l i f e  o f  h o w  t o  t r a n s f o r m  
suffering into happiness? 
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO : That is easy. I have had many ex periences of something that happened 
that appeared to be suffering, yet my mind di d not suffer. For example, when I experienced 
the physical suffering of being sick, it di d not produce any mental suffering. My mind 
remained quiet and happy. Most people when their body is sick tend to get agitated and worry 
about dying, whereby they add mental suffering to their physical suffering and their physical 
suffering becomes even more difficult.   I also experienced the great suffering of having  to give up my country and leave behind my 
mother, brother, and sister in order to escape  with my uncle and some teachers. However, 
when I arrived in India I was not sad. We sh ould not be too attached to our family, our 
wealth, and so forth, given that they are unst able, impermanent, and constantly changing. If 
we understand this, our mind will not suffer. We  need to try to think like this. Whenever we 
experience suffering, we need to generate mindfu lness of this fact and keep our mind quiet. 
In other words, we need to have less desire and more satisfaction and contentment. In this 
way, we will be able to transform suffering  into a condition for mental happiness.  
 Now I will give the oral transmission of the rest  of the text (Tibetan text page 237-385). The 
remaining part of the text is devoted to a discu ssion of last two perfections – the perfection of 
concentration and the perfection of wisdom. Thes e are first explained in general and then the 
entity of calm abiding and the entity of special insight are explained individually.   In general, concentration is a mind single-poi ntedly focused on its object. Presently it is 
difficult for us to focus on an object even for five or ten minu tes. However, we should choose 
an object of concentration and then strive to concentrate on it, whereby stable concentration 
will gradually arise. An Indian saddhu once told  me that in order to develop concentration, 
one should visualize a familiar object in front of oneself, gain a clear aspect of it, and focus on 
it. He said that if we try to concentrate on an ob ject that is not familiar, it will not be easy for 
our mind to generate a clear aspect of it and our mind will not abide steadfastly on it, rather 
it will wander away from it. When  I asked him what was easiest for him to take as an object of 
concentration, he said that it was his wife! He imagined his wife in front of him, generated a 
clear aspect of her face, and focused on it, whereby his mind could remain on it without 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
684 wandering. He said that if someone wishes to develop calm abiding alone, without wishing to 
attain liberation, this is enough, and that, in th is case, it is not necessary to meditate on a 
deity such as Ram or Krishna.  
 When practicing calm abiding, some people choose a deity as their object of meditation, 
whereas others take even the flame of a candle as the object of meditation and try to focus 
their mind on it. In fact Atisha’s Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment  says that we can take any 
phenomenon as an object of meditation for th e development of concentration. In order to 
develop calm abiding, we need to choose one object and then focus on it. Maitreya Buddha, 
in his Ornament for the Mahayana Sutras , explained the nine stages of mental abiding: setting, 
resetting, close setting, and so forth. We should try to learn these stages.  
 I already mentioned the five faults and thei r eight antidotes, which also explained in 
Maitreya’s Discrimination of the Middle Way and the Extremes . We need to eliminate the five 
faults by means of applying the eight antidote s, the four mental engagements, and the six 
forces, whereby we can attain th e nine mental abidings. The main  obstacle to achieving calm 
abiding is laziness. In fact whenever we do good things, laziness arises. When, on the other hand, we do bad things, such as gossiping for fo ur, five, and six hours, laziness does not arise! 
It is very easy to gossip. On the contrary, even  when we try to do a little bit of meditation, 
laziness arises and we immediately feel tired an d think “Today I am too tired. I will try to 
meditate tomorrow.” Then again tomorrow we th ink tomorrow and so on, until death arrives. 
Hence, we need to give up laziness. Even when  we do our daily commitments, we should keep 
our mind relaxed, clear, virtuous, and joyous, thinking “It is wonderful that I can do such 
practices. I am very fortunate.” We should not th ink, “Oh, I have this commitment. It is very 
difficult to keep.”   We should also try to meditate every day, mainly trying to develop bodhichitta and its 
causes. We should try to develop love and comp assion for all sentient beings. If this is 
difficult to do with respect to all sentient be ings, at the beginning we should generate love 
and compassion for the people around us th rough wishing them to have happiness and the 
causes of happiness and to be free from suffering  and the causes of suffering. In this way, we 
should try to develop love and compassion at al l times and to familiarize with them and with 
bodhichitta, the wish to attain enlightenment fo r the welfare of all sentient beings. In the 
morning we should think, “Whatever I do tod ay, I will do it to benefit and serve other 
sentient beings.” In this way, we will co llect merit and something good will happen.  
 Now I will try to finish the oral transmission of the text. While the FP MT suggested a certain 
amount of time for the study of the Middle Lam-Rim , it has not been sufficient. However, I 
intend to finish this commentary later on  (January-March 2007). While I give the 
transmission, try to meditate on bodhichitta, love , or whatever you wish, or else recite some 
mantras and so forth.  (Oral transmission 12:10-12:30, pp. 237-247)  Thursday afternoon, 26 May 2005  Try to meditate on bodhichitta, developing it in  your mind. Bodhichitta is the mental attitude 
that wishes to attain  buddhahood for the welfare of all sentient beings. Think about and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
685 develop this kind of attitude. We must achiev e buddhahood and to do so we need to develop 
both conventional bodhichitta and ultimate bodhichitta. First we should try to develop 
conventional bodhichitta by mean of the differ ent ways that were taught before. To develop 
ultimate bodhichitta, we need to think abou t the fact that all phenomena do not exist 
inherently or truly. First we should try to reco gnize the object of negation. Once we recognize 
the object of negation then we should try to refu te it. If we do not recognize it, we will not be 
able to clearly understand emptiness. For exampl e, to understand that there are no horns on 
our head, we first need to understand that, if they did exist on our head, they would exist in 
such and such a way. on the ot her hand, if we do not know what horns are, we will not be 
able to understand whether horns exist on our he ad or not. Similarly, if phenomena were to 
exist inherently or from their own side they would have to be concrete, immutable, stable, 
and not depend on parts or causes or anythi ng else. For example, if this table exists 
inherently, it must exist from its own side wi thout depending on causes and conditions or 
parts. It should exist solidly from its own side , independent and without parts. We should try 
to understand the object of negation through imagining what it would be like, trying to 
develop a mental image or a meaning generality or sound generality of it. If it existed 
inherently, it would exist like this…, but it does not. A table exists in dependence on parts. It 
is not unitary, therefore it is empty of inhe rent existence. All phenomena are like this.  
 Likewise, the table does not exist inherently because it does not exist as an inherently existent one or an inherently existent many. It is not an inherently existent one because it has 
parts. It is not partless. Having parts and be ing partless are contradictory. The partless does 
not exist. Therefore, the table does not exist as an inherently existent one because it has parts. 
Then we should think: the table does not exist as  an inherently existent many because it does 
not exist as an inherently existent one; many exists in dependence on one, and it does not 
exist as one. Therefore, the table is not inhere ntly existent because it is not an inherently 
existent one or an inherently existent many. Th erefore, the table is not inherently existent. 
We need to apply these reasonings to establish th e lack of inherent exis tence of the table. We 
should meditate on this, reflec ting on the non-affirming negative that is the emptiness of 
inherent existence. There are no phenomena that  exist inherently. We should try to meditate 
in this way.   Another reasoning that establishes emptine ss is: the table is not inherently produced 
because it is not inherently produced from se lf, not inherently produced from other, not 
inherently produced from both self and other,  and not inherently produced without causes. A 
table is not produced from itself. In fact, no thing is produced from itself. In order for 
something to be produced it must depend on  others, that is, on causes and conditions. 
Without causes and conditions or parts, ph enomena cannot arise. The table is also not 
inherently produced from other because inherent ly existent other does not exist. It is not 
inherently produced from both self and other because neither self nor other are inherently 
existent. Nagarjuna’s Fundamental Wisdom  sets out this fourfold reasoning that proves the lack 
of inherent production from self, from other,  from both, and without causes in order to 
refute the tenet systems that po sit such types of production.  
 We should choose whatever reasoning is easies t for us. Perhaps the easiest is: the table does 
not exist inherently because it is a dependent- arising. We should examine this in our own 
mind, mentally examining what it means. If al l phenomena existed inherently, also my mind 
would have to exist inherently. Does my mind exist inherently? It does not because my mind 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 25-27 May 2005 (15) 
686 is dependent, in each instant it changes. Therefore, it does not exist inherently or 
independently or truly. Therefore, the final na ture of the mind is clear light, that is, 
emptiness. There is the conventional nature of  mind and the ultimate nature of mind. The 
ultimate nature of mind is its emptiness of inhe rent existence. This emptiness is posited from 
the side of the object. From the side of the subject, the mind is a clear knower. It is clear 
because it is not form, it does not have either  color or shape. We need to examine our mind 
i n  t h i s  w a y .  I f  o u r  m i n d  w e r e  i n h e r e n t l y  o r  truly existent, or existent from its own side 
without depending on other, it could not change . Then, for example, ou r mind when we were 
a small child, our mind when we were a teenag er, our mind when we are an adult, and so 
forth could not change. Due to this, we could not learn anything, we could not gain either 
qualities or knowledge. However, the reality is that if we study and tr y to understand, we do 
gain more knowledge. The reason this can happe n is that our mind does not exist inherently. 
It exists in dependence; it depends on causes  and conditions. Due to causes and conditions, 
our mind can improve and our knowledge increase. We should think about this and meditate 
on emptiness. Now I will give the oral transm ission of this text. About 150 pages remain.
