687 Monday afternoon, 22 January 2007 
  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 237) says: 
Explanation how to train in the la st two perfections in particular 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B Explanation how to train in the last two perfections in particular 
1 Transition 
2 The actual explanation 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B1 Transition 
Training in the last two perfections {238 } consists in the meditation methods of 
calm abiding and special insight, as the perfections of concentration and wisdom 
comprise those two in order. 
 
Now there is an explanation of how to train in  the last two perfection s among the six, these 
being respectively the fifth perfection, concentr ation, and the sixth perfection, wisdom. Thus, 
first there is a discussion of calm abiding. In  other words, whereas previously the first four 
perfections of generosity, morality, patience, and joyous effort were discussed, now there is a 
discussion of the last two perfections in particular . In this context, first there is a short section 
that connects the previous outlines with this su bject, called “Transitio n,” which sets out the 
way to meditate on calm abiding and special insigh t, which is also taught in the Perfection of 
Wisdom Sutras.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2 The actual explanation 
A The benefits of developing calm abiding and special insight 
B Demonstration that these two contain all meditative stabilisations C The essence of calm abiding and special insight 
D The reason why both need to be developed 
E How their order is established 
F How to train in each of them 
This has six points: the benefits of developing calm abiding and special insight, 
demonstration that these two contain all me ditative stabilisations, the essence of 
calm abiding and special insight, the reas on why both need to be developed, how 
their order is established and how to train in each of them. 
 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2A The benefits of developing calm abiding and special insight 
1 Demonstration that all the positive qualities of the Mahayana and Hinayana are results of calm 
abiding and special insight  
The first point from among five regarding the first one: In [the Sutra ] Unravelling 
the Thought  it is set out that all the positive qu alities, worldly and transcendental, of 
the Mahayana and Hinayana are results of  calm abiding and special insight. 
 
First the benefits of cultivating calm abiding and special insight are set out. Calm abiding 
refers to a mind that is stable and single po inted. Having achieved calm abiding, one then 
strives to attain special insight by doing analyt ical meditation on an object. Thus, there is 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
688 single-pointed meditation and analytical meditati on, analytical meditation being what leads to 
special insight or lhag-tong ( lhag mthong ), literally “special seeing.” Analytical meditation is 
cultivated in order to understand the nature  of phenomena. For example, one examines 
whether phenomena abide inherently or not, wh ereupon they are found to lack a self of 
persons and a self of phenomena. For example, with respect to the person, one examines how 
the person exists, independently or dependently. By doing so one discovers that the person is 
merely imputed on the bases of the five aggregat es. Thus, since the basis of imputation of the 
person is the aggregates, the person does not exist independently but exists in dependence, 
that is, in reliance on other phenomena. Afte r that, one examines other phenomena such as 
the form aggregate, feeling aggregate, disc rimination aggregate, compositional factors 
aggregate, and consciousness aggregate. Throug h examining them, from the Prasangika point 
of view one will not find these aggregates. For example, if one examines external forms as well 
as the internal sense powers, they cannot be foun d. The physical aggregate of form is not the 
person, nor is it sound, odor, taste, or tactile ob ject. By checking in this way one will find that 
the person does not exist independently, part lessly, or permanently. It is necessary to 
understand this.  
 Hence, first one does single-pointed meditation and then one does analytical meditation, c h e c k i n g  i n  t h i s  w a y .  M e d i t a t i n g  o n  c a l m  abiding and special insight has the benefit of 
bringing about all the hinayana and mahayana qualities as well as all mundane and supramundane qualities. In short, first one n eeds to develop calm abiding, after which one 
needs to develop special insight. This order is mentioned in the Sutra Unraveling the Thought . 
Buddha Shakyamuni taught three wheels of Dharma , the first wheel is called “the four noble 
truths,” the second wheel is called “without characteristics,” and the third wheel is called 
“fine discrimination,” the Sutra Unraveling the Thought being included in the latter. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2 Presentation of a doubt about that 
“Are calm abiding and special insight not positive qualities of mind [that are] 
gained from meditation? How can all those positive qualities be admitted as results 
of those two [then]?” 
 
Someone has the doubt that wonders: “Are calm  abiding and special insight not themselves 
qualities that are obtained from meditation? If so, how can all qualities be the result of these 
two?  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
3 The response to it 
Calm abiding and special insight themselves  are [indeed] positive qualities of mind 
gained from meditation, as will be ex plained [below]. Therefore, not all the 
positive qualities of the Mahayana and Hinayana are results of the two, however, 
[all] meditative stabilisations starting from the mind [remaining] one-pointedly on 
a wholesome object are included in the sphere of calm abiding and [all] the 
wholesome wisdom minds discriminating  in detail what the nature and 
multiplicity of phenomena means are includ ed in the sphere of special insight. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
689 From that understanding there is thus no  contradiction in saying that all the 
positive qualities of the three vehicles are results of calm abiding and special 
insight.  
 
The calm abiding and special insight are them selves qualities achieved from meditation, 
therefore not all qualities of the mahayana an d hinayana are their results. However, all 
meditative stabilizations beginning from single -pointed meditation onward are included in 
calm abiding, and all virtuous wisdoms distin guishing varieties (conventional truths) and 
modes (ultimate truths) are included in special in sight. Therefore, in th is sense there is no 
contradiction in saying that all the qualities of the three vehicles – the hearer, solitary realizer, 
and bodhisattva vehicles – are the results of calm  abiding and special insight. In other words, 
since this is an interpretive meaning statemen t and not a definitive meaning statement, there 
is no contradiction. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
4 How the benefits of giving up what needs to be given up are related to a quotation from [the Sutra ] 
Unravelling the Thought   
In [the Sutra ] Unravelling the Thought  it also says:  
 
Having familiarized themselves 
with calm abiding and special insight, 
beings will gain complete liberation from the fetters of signs and taking bad states. 
 
which means this: “taking bad states” [refer s to] dispositions in the mind capable 
of increasingly generating wrong consciou snesses, and “signs” [are that which] 
nourishes the disposition for attachment to wrong objects arising time and again. 
{239} The Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom  explain that the former is 
abandoned through special insight and the latter through calm abiding. Those are 
the benefits termed calm abiding and special insight. 
 The benefits of abandoning the object s of abandonment are related to the Sutra Unraveling the 
Thought . The citation quoted here says that by fa miliarizing themselves with calm abiding and 
special insight, beings can attain complete  liberation from the bondage of mistaken 
tendencies and the bondage of signs. The latter refers to seeing all phenomena as existing 
inherently and truly. The adherence to all phen omena as truly or inherently existent and its 
imprints in our continuum are bonds that bind us . Bad states or negative tendencies are, for 
example, speaking harsh words automatically with out the thought to do so or swinging from 
tree to tree like monkeys. The bondage of mist aken tendencies and the bondage of signs are 
abandoned by means of the two types of meditation. This is taught in Instructions on the 
Perfection of Wisdom . These benefits are given the name “c alm abiding” and “special insight.”  
 Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
690 5  Through that you should also come to understand the benefits [when] referred to differently 
However, even if they are not termed ca lm abiding and special insight the meaning 
is the same: statements on the benefits of  concentration should be understood as 
the benefits of these two.  
 
Even if they are not given the names “calm abid ing” and “special insight”, there is still the 
result of concentration and wisdom. Therefor e, the benefits of these two should be 
understood.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2B Demonstration that these two contain all meditative stabilisation 
Second: In [the Sutra ] Unraveling the Thought  it says that all the limitless meditative 
stabilisations proclaimed in the Mahayana and the Hinayana can still be subsumed 
under calm abiding and special insight. Therefore, as someone striving for 
meditative stabilisation cannot get to th e root of all their limitless manifest 
divisions, he should skillfully get to th e root of how to sustain calm abiding and 
special insight, the common principle of all meditative stabilisations.  
 
The Sutra  Unraveling the Thought  says that all the limitless meditative stabilizations taught in 
the mahayana and hinayana are included in calm abiding and special insight. Since the 
meditative stabilizations are divided into many types, to examine them all would be difficult, 
therefore one should try to understand how to train in, meditate on, and develop calm 
abiding and special insight. One should do th is by examining them with analysis, whereby 
one will come to understand the benefits of all the  meditative stabilizations. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2C The essence of calm abiding and special insight 
1 The essence of calm abiding 
2 Explaining the essence special insight 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2C-1 The essence of calm abiding 
A Stating the source 
Third: As regards the essence of calm abiding it says in [the Sutra ] Unravelling the 
Thought :  
 
Dwelling alone in solitude, having pl aced [the mind] within, he attends 
to the very phenomena he has thor oughly reflected on, in the same 
manner he has [previously]. He attends to them by directing his mental 
activity inside continuously and attent ively. The [states] he thus enters 
[into] where extreme phys ical and mental suppleness arise, as he dwells 
in them often, are called calm abiding. 
 One should abide in a solitary place and place th e mind inside. In other words, it should not 
be that the body remains inside but the mind remains outside! One should try to hold the 
mind inside, focused single-pointedly on its obje ct and abiding on it. One should meditate on 
the nature of phenomena continuously. In othe r words, one should meditate in this way 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
691 again and again, whereby one will gradually prog ress through the nine stages of calm abiding, 
such as the first called “setting the mind” an d so forth. By doing this, one day a special 
pliancy of body as well as of special pliancy of mind will arise. When this comes to be held by 
concentration, one will have achieved calm ab iding. Due to the special pliancy of body, the 
body feels very light and one can meditate for twenty hours, forty hours, and so on without 
the body ever becoming tired. Also due to the special pliancy of mind, the mind becomes 
tireless but is able to abide peacefully on  its object without tiredness arising.  
 Our mind can be likened to a crazy elephant, me aning that at times it can be very bad! If 
we check our own mind, we will see that this is true! Yes! A crazy elephant is very strong and 
i f  w e  t r y  t o  h a r m  i t ,  i t  w i l l  r e t a l i a t e .  S o m e  B h u t a n e s e  p e o p l e  s a y  t h a t  i f  u p o n  s e e i n g  a n  
elephant, one does prostrations to it, the elep hant will look around and go away whereas if 
one threatens it, it will feel it has to show itself to be strong and come forward. Our mind is like such a crazy elephant. While a crazy dog or crazy cat is not ve ry strong, a crazy elephant is 
indeed very strong. Because of this, one should try to meditate a little bit every day, whereby 
one day the mind will be able to abide on its object.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B The meaning of this [statement] 
The meaning of this: If the mind stays on th e object of meditation by itself, having 
paid attention to it continuously without distraction, and if the bliss of extreme 
physical and mental suppleness arises, th at meditative stabilisation has become 
calm abiding. This will come about from  merely keeping the mind within without 
distraction and it does not depend on  the internalization of suchness.  
 
By meditating on an object a special pliancy ar ises that produces a special bliss of body and 
mind. When this is conjoined with concentration, it is called calm abid ing. At this time the 
mind is able to meditate on its object, abiding stably without any distraction. However, even if 
the mind abides inside on its object with out distraction, this does not bring the 
understanding of the suchness of all phenomena. Therefore, we also need to develop special 
insight.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2C-2 Explanation of special insight 
1 Stating the source 
In the same sutra it says: 
 
Having achieved extreme physical and mental suppleness, he abides in 
it, gives up [what has so far been] the aspect of his mind and {240} takes 
an interest in the discriminating ex amination of the image that is the 
object of meditative stabilisation and [which is] among the very phenomena he had likewise reflecte d on. Thus with regard to the 
images that are objects of meditative  stabilisation any [of the following:] 
discrimination of the meaning of those objects of knowledge, their 
thorough discrimination, consideration, complete investigation, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
692 forbearance, acceptance, differentiation, conceptualization, and view 
are called special insight. In that way the bodhisattva is skilled in special 
insight. 
 
The explanation of the entity of  special insight has six outlines , the first being the setting out 
of the scriptural source. Having achieved a special pliancy of body and mind, one should 
examine how phenomena abide, that  is, their nature, by seeing them to be like reflections. 
Just as the reflection of one’s face in a mirror a ppears to be an actual face but is not, likewise 
all phenomena are similar in that they appear to  exist inherently and truly but in actuality do 
not exist in that way. In shor t, one should analyze how phenomena do not exist inherently or 
truly but are similar to reflections in a mirror. Objects of meditative stabilization, like 
reflections, should be investigated with an alytical meditation. One should check whether 
phenomena can bear investigation because when they are not investigated they are found to 
exist truly, whereas when they are investigated they are not found to exist inherently but are 
found to exist by way of being imputed by though t on their basis. One should examine this by 
means of analytical meditation. This is done on  the basis of having achieved calm abiding, 
that is, a physical and mental pliancy that indu ces a physical and mental bliss, whereupon one 
part of the mind is used to analyze, this be ing special insight. A bo dhisattva is skilled in 
special insight.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 Elucidating the meaning of the sutra [passage] 
In this regard “discrimination” refers to the discrimination of the multiplicity [of 
phenomena] and “thorough discrimination ” to the discrimination of suchness. 
“Consideration” refers to coarse consideration and “investigation” to fine 
investigation.  
 
After the scriptural source, the meaning of the su tra is set out. “Differentiation” refers to the 
differentiation of the multiplicity, va rieties, or conventional truths ( ji snyed pa ) and “thorough 
differentiation” to the differentiation of modes or suchness ( ji lta ba ). Investigation refers to 
investigating in a coarse way, whereas analys is refers to a fine or detailed analysis. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3 The way statements from other sutras and commentaries conform to [the Sutra ] Unravelling the 
Thought   
What is taught in other sutras and commentar ies corresponds with what is taught in the Sutra  
Unravelling the Thought . 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
The Jewel Cloud [Sutra ] says: 
 
Calm abiding is one-pointed mind, 
special insight detailed analysis. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
693  
The Jewel Cloud Sutra  says that the mind abiding single-poi ntedly on its object is called “calm 
abiding,” whereas special insight is the indivi dual investigation that analyzes its object. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
and venerable Maitreya: 
 
The concepts of dharmas – th is summary, too, should be 
known as the path of calm abiding, thorough analysis of their meanings as the path of special insight.” 
 
Maitreya also says that the names of phenomen a, for example, “white,” that is, the mind 
having taken white as an object abiding single-poi ntedly on it without analyzing, is called “the 
path of calm abiding.” Special insight instead investigates, for example, “Why is it the color 
white and not the color red?”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
And: 
 
Something based on abiding thoroughly in order to place the mind on mind, in order to discriminate dharmas is calm abiding and special insight. 
 The mind abiding perfectly on one basis or object is called “calm abiding.” The mind 
examining individual objects or phenomena with investigation and analysis that looks at their 
detailed aspects is called “special insight.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Based on thorough meditative stabilisation,  placing the mind is said to be calm 
abiding and the wisdom discriminating dhar mas [is said to be] special insight. In 
the Bodhisattva Levels  it is set out just like that and in the Middle  Stages of 
Meditation , too, it says:  
 
Once [mental] wandering to external objects has calmed down 
continuous engagement with the internal  object is called ‘abiding in the 
mind itself
176 associated with joy and ex treme suppleness’. That which 
examines suchness at the time of dwe lling in that very [state of] calm 
abiding is special insight. 
                                              
176 The term “sems nyid” (mind itself) is often us ed synonymously with “nature of the mind”. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
694  
The Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom  explain this in the same way. 
 
Setting the mind on its object based on perfec t meditative stabilization is given the name 
“calm abiding.” Examining the entity, characterist ics, divisions, or illustrations of phenomena 
is called “special insigh t.” This is taught in Bodhisattva Levels  as well as in Kamalashila’s Middle 
Stages of Meditation . In the case of calm abiding, the mind that wanders outside to external 
phenomena has been completely pacified and in stead it abides inside on a single object, 
producing pliancy of body and mind. Then, wh en one part of the mind abides single-
pointedly on its object, and one part of the mind  analyzes its object, this is called “special 
insight.” This is taught in the Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom .  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
4 Therefore calm abiding and special insight are not distinguished by way of the meditation object  
{241} Therefore, as each of them, ca lm abiding and special insight, have 
meditation objects among both [things] as  they really are and [things] in their 
variety, calm abiding and special insight are not differentiated by way of their 
meditation objects. There is calm abiding realizing emptiness and there is also 
special insight that does not realize empt iness. Rather it is because [mental] 
wandering to external objects has calm ed down and the mind is abiding on 
internal objects that something is called ca lm abiding and it is because of special, 
distinguished seeing that someth ing is called special insight.  
 Calm abiding and special insight are not different iated in terms of their observed object. In 
other words, it is not because of having a partic ular object that one is called “calm abiding” 
and because of having another ob ject the other is called “special  insight.” Asanga wrote about 
this in his Five Treatises on the Grounds. Bo th calm abiding and special insight can take 
either thusnesses (ultimate truths) and the multip licity (conventional truths) as their observed 
objects. In short, since both calm abiding an d special insight take the two truths as their 
objects, they are not distinguished in terms of their observed objects. Because of this, there 
exists a calm abiding that realizes emptiness as well as a special insight that does not realize 
emptiness. Hence, they are dist inguished by the fact that the mind not wandering to external 
objects but abiding inside is called “calm abidin g,” whereas the special seeing of the divisions 
of phenomena and so forth is  called “special insight.” 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
5 How it is inappropriate to distinguish calm abiding and special insight based on whether or not the 
clarity of the consciousness apprehending its object is intense  
The claim by some that the mind abiding non-conceptually without intense clarity 
is calm abiding and with intense clarity is special insight is inappropriate because it 
contradicts everything explained earlier and because this distinction is only a 
distinction between meditative stabilisat ion with or without mental sinking. 
Because, to be sure, every meditative stabil isation of calm abiding also needs to be 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
695 purified of sinking and in every concentrat ion that is free from  sinking the clarity 
aspect of the mind will surely come.  
 
Nor are calm abiding and special insight diffe rentiated in terms of the clarity of the 
consciousness apprehending the object. In other wo rds, it is not correct, as some people say, 
that a mind that abides non-conceptually with out strong clarity is calm abiding whereas one 
with strong clarity is special insight. If it we re like this, it would contradict that taught 
previously. The distinction of clarity and unclar ity refers only to the respective absence and 
presence of mental sinking or laxity. When ca lm abiding is achieved, sinking is completely 
purified. When the mind is free from sinking or laxity, such a meditative stabilization 
definitely has clarity. Therefore, it cannot be sa id that calm abiding is a lack of clarity and 
special insight is the presence of clarity. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
6 The need to distinguish between the realization of emptiness and [states of] bliss, clarity and non-
discursiveness 
F o r  t h a t  r e a s o n  i t  i s  n e c e s s a r y  t o  i d e n t i f y  w h e t h e r  o r  n o t  [ a n  a w a r e n e s s ]  i s  a  
meditative stabilisation with emptiness as its object and [thus] a wisdom [consciousness] on the basis of whether or not that awareness realizes whichever of 
the two [types of] selflessness as its object. In fact there are countless non-
conceptual meditative stabilisations of bliss and clarity where the mind is not 
directed at that object of suchness
177. As it is proven by direct experience that the 
mind can thus be held in a non-concep tual state without attaining the view 
realizing the mode of being, there is noth ing to contradict the generation of non-
conceptual meditative stabilisations that do not understand emptiness. If the mind 
is held this way for a long time, thro ugh the power of the mind being held a 
workability of inner energies will come ab out. {242} As it is natural for joy and 
bliss to arise in body and mind if that ha s come about, there is no contradiction to 
the development of bliss [without a realization of emptiness]. Once that has 
developed, the clarity of one’s sensation of joy and bliss will brin g on an aspect of 
clarity. Therefore you cannot posit that every blissful, clear, non-conceptual 
meditative stabilisation realizes suchness.  Consequently, since bliss, clarity, and 
non-conceptuality also come on in meditati ve stabilisations realizing emptiness and 
since there are also many meditative stabil isations where the mind is not directed 
at emptiness and where bliss, clarity, and no n-conceptuality occur, it is necessary to 
distinguish between the two.  
 It is necessary to distinguish between the realiz ation of emptiness and the a mind that is non-
conceptual, clear, and blissful. This is beca use there are many non-conceptual meditative 
stabilizations that do not realize emptiness. It  is necessary to examine whether a meditative 
stabilization or wisdom observing emptiness actual ly realizes one of the two selflessnesses: the 
selflessness of persons or the selflessness of ph enomena. In other words, in order to realize 
emptiness one must realize either the selflessne ss of persons or the selflessness of phenomena. 
                                             
 
177 synonym for emptiness 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
696 This is because without realizing one of these clearl y, there exists a mind that is clear, blissful, 
and non-conceptual among non-Buddhists as well  as in the seventeen levels of the four 
concentrations of the form realm. In short, thes e are meditative stabilizations that are clear, 
blissful, and non-conceptual but that do not realize emptiness. However, if it is not a 
meditative stabilization realizing emptiness, it cannot abandon the root of cyclic existence. In 
this case no matter how many years one remained  in meditative stabilization, when one dies 
one can once again fall into the hell realm and so forth.  
 Thus, there is a mind that does not analyze emptiness, the perfect view, and without 
thinking about anything abides very quietly, wh ereby there is no contradiction that there are 
non-conceptual meditative stabilizations that do not realize emptiness. If one concentrates 
without thinking about any object, the winds will gradually become more stable, which produces a bliss of body and mind. This occurs  naturally and there is no contradiction that 
this bliss is generated. Then, by the power of the happiness and joy that arise in the mind, the 
mind will become more clear. In short, not a ll minds that are clear and concentrated are a 
realization of emptiness.   Tuesday morning, 23 January 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 242) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2D The reason for developing both [calm abiding and special insight] 
1 Illustration 
Fourth: You may wonder: Why is it not enough to develop calm abiding and 
special insight alone – why is it necessary to develop both [together]?   To illustrate: if you light a butter lamp at  night in order to look at murals, if the 
butter lamp is both very bright and not ca used to flicker by the wind, you will see 
the painted figures very clearly. However, if  the butter lamp is not very bright or if 
– even though bright – it is blown by the wind, you will not see the forms clearly.  
 
Someone raises the question: “Why is it necessa ry to develop both calm abiding and special 
insight? Why is one of them not enough?” The resp onse to this question is given by way of an 
example. When at night one lights a butter lamp  in order to see the paintings of deities and 
so forth on the wall of a temple, if the butter lamp burns very bright and is not blown by the 
wind, one will be able to see the paintings very clearly. On the other hand, if the butter lamp 
is not very bright or if it is bright but blown by the wind, one will not be able to see the forms 
of the deities clearly. This is an  analogy that signifies that in or der to have a mind that is both 
stable and clear, one needs both calm abiding and special insight.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 Explaining the meaning  
Likewise, if you have both the wisdom  correctly determining the profound 
meaning of suchness and a mind staying on an object immovably at will, you will 
see suchness clearly, but if you do not have the wisdom realizing the mode of 
being, even though you [may] have a mi nd that stays on an object without 
d i f f u s i n g  t o  o t h e r  o b j e c t s ,  y o u  w i l l  n o t  b e  a b l e  t o  r e a l i z e  t h e  m o d e  o f  b e i n g ,  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
697 however much you train in meditative stab ilisation. And if you have the view of 
internalized selflessness but do not have th e firm meditative stabilisation where the 
mind abides one-pointedly, you will not be able to realize clearly the meaning of 
the mode of being. Therefore both calm abiding and special insight are necessary. 
 
It is necessary to examine the view of empt iness with the wisdom that unmistakenly and 
clearly sees emptiness. In addition, it is necessa ry to have the calm abid ing that enables one to 
s e t  o n e ’ s  m i n d  o n  a n  o b j e c t  s u c h  t h a t  i t  a b i d e s  s i n g l e - p o i n t e d l y  o n  i t  w i t h o u t  m o v i n g .  B y  means of these two – calm abiding and special insight – one will be able to clearly see emptiness or suchness without the mind going to  another object. If, on the other hand, one 
does not have the wisdom realizing suchness or thusness, the profound nature, then even 
though one develops firm meditative stabilizat ion one will not realize emptiness. Likewise, 
even if one has realized the view of selflessn ess – either the selflessn ess of persons or the 
selflessness of phenomena – but lacks single-point ed meditative stabilization, one’s realization 
will not be stable. Therefore, both calm abidin g and special insight are necessary in order to 
enable one to see the suchness or final nature of  all phenomena. On the other hand, if one of 
them is missing, one will not see suchness in a stable way.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3 Relating this to scripture 
In the Middle  Stages of Meditation  it says:  
 
Only due to special insight that is  disconnected from calm abiding a 
yogi’s mind wanders away to [other ] objects and rather {243} becomes 
unstable like a butter lamp in the wind. If so the light of wisdom will 
not shine forth very brightly. Therefore you should rely on both alike. 
 
This is the scriptural source that proves th at both calm abiding and special insight are 
necessary. Kamalashila’s Middle Stages of Meditation  says that if one has special insight 
separated from calm abiding, one’s mind will wander and will not be able to abide on its 
object firmly, like a butter lamp blown by the wi nd. Due to this the light of wisdom will not 
shine brightly. Therefore, it is necessary to rely  on both calm abiding and special insight, like 
a butter lamp that is both bright and not blown by the wind.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
And: 
 
Through the power of calm abiding the mind will not be moved by the 
winds of conceptualization – like a butter lamp placed [where] there is 
no wind. Through special insight you leave behind the snares of all inferior views so that others do not shake you [anymore] – it is like it 
has been set out in the words of the Moon Lamp Sutra : “Through the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
698 power of calm abiding you become unshakable; through special insight 
you become like a mountain.” 
 
Due to the strength of calm abiding, the mind will not be moved by the winds of 
conceptualization. Special insight is able to cut through all wrong or bad views, whereby one 
is no longer moved by the disc ordant classes. Similarly, the Moon Lamp Sutra  says that by the 
strength of calm abiding the mind is made unsh akable and by the strength of special insight 
the mind is made like Mount Meru. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
If you investigate [phenomena] with wisd om that is thus conjoined with the 
meditative equipoise of calm abiding free from unevenness through sinking and 
excitement of the mind, you will know the true meaning. With that intention it 
says in the Complete Summary of Teachings : 
 
If the mind is in meditative equipoise you will know reality as it is. 
 
Calm abiding is a mind free from sinking or la xity and distraction and abides on its object 
steadfastly. When it is conjoined with the wisdom that understands the emptiness or the 
profound meaning of phenomena, such a medita tive equipoise will know the perfect view, 
that is, emptiness or ultimate truth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
From the First Stages of Meditation : 
 
Since the mind is mobile like wate r there is no abiding without the 
basis of calm abiding. With a mind that is not in meditative equipoise 
reality cannot be known as it is. The Bhagavat also spoke: “Reality is 
fully known as it is by a mind in meditative equipoise.” 
 
The First Stages of Meditation  says that the mind without calm abiding is like a flowing river in 
that it is constantly moving and never remains on  its object. A mind that is not in meditative 
equipoise cannot completely and perfectly unders tand the perfect view or emptiness. Buddha 
Shakyamuni said that by medita ting perfectly in equipoise, emptiness or selflessness will be 
perfectly realized. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
4 How the power to do anything wholesome is great once calm abiding has been attained 
If you attain calm abiding, not only do you avert the fault of your wisdom 
[consciousness] wandering off while correctly  investigating selflessness. The fault of 
wandering off to [other] objects is even av erted in all analytical meditations you do 
with the wisdom of discriminating aw areness regarding impermanence, actions 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
699 and their effects, the faults of cyclic ex istence, love, compassion, and training in 
bodhichitta and through engaging with each  individual object, whatever it may be, 
without wandering off to something else, anything wholesome you do is very 
powerful. Before, when you have not [yet] gained calm abiding, through wandering 
off to other objects most of the time {244} an y practice of virtue is weak – as it says 
in Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior :  
 
A man whose mind is wandering 
is in the fangs of the afflictions. 
 Having attained calm abiding, whatever virtuo us actions one creates will be more powerful 
and, while correctly investigating selflessne ss, all faults of wisdom wandering off are 
completely eliminated. Likewise, the same applies to the wisdom understanding impermanence, the law of cause and effect, the fa ults of cyclic existence, love, compassion, 
and bodhichitta (the mind of enlightenment) in  that due to calm abiding all wandering and 
distraction are completely overcome. One’s mind  abides unmoving on any observed object, 
whereby whatever virtue one creates becomes very  strong and powerful. If, on the other hand, 
one has not achieved calm abiding, even though one tries to concentrate, the mind will move 
to other objects whereby whatever virtue one practices is weak. Engaging in Bodhisattva Behavior  
also says that someone who has a wandering mind  abides between the fangs of the afflictions. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
And: 
 
The seer taught that recitation, ascetic practice, and the like – even if you practice long – are pointless with a mind wandering off. 
 
Similarly, even though one recites mantras for hours and hours with great effort, goes without 
eating for long periods, and so forth, as long as one has not attained calm abiding, one’s mind 
will continue to wander and be distracted, wh ereby one will not achieve much and will not be 
able to realize suchness or the perfect view.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 244) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2E How to determine the order 
1 The actual [order] 
2 Explanations on how calm abiding is a precondition for special insight 3 Presentation of an objection and the response to it 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2E-1 The actual [order] 
Fifth: In Engaging in  [Bodhisattva ] Behavior  it says: 
 
Having understood that special insight 
conjoined with calm abiding destroys the afflictions 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
700 first seek calm abiding. 
 
Thus you first attain calm abiding and th en develop special insight based on it.  
 
Having attained the quality of calm abiding if  one then achieves sp ecial insight, all the 
afflictions can be completely destroyed. Having  understood this, one should first seek calm 
abiding, that is, one should develop it first. In  short, first it is necessary to develop calm 
abiding and then, having attained it, one should st rive to develop special insight. This sets out 
the order in which they are to be developed. La ter on in the text it will be explained how to 
develop calm abiding by means of the eight antidote s to the five faults, as well as by way of the 
six powers and the four mental engagements, th ereby progressing through the nine stages of 
mental abiding. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 244) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2E-2 [Explanations on how calm abiding is a precondition for special insight] 
A Presentation of an objection 
B The response to it 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2E-2A Presentation of an objection: If you meditate focusing on emptiness from the 
beginning, since you have [ultimate] reality as the meditation object of calm abiding, calm abiding and special 
insight will come about simu ltaneously and therefore th e two do not need to be [p racticed separately as two] 
steps  
In this regard one may wonder: It has been  taught that there is no specification 
with regard to the object of calm abiding – in the First Stages of Meditation , it says: 
“Its object is not specified” – and as ex plained previously there is calm abiding 
with a phenomenon and with [ultimate] re ality as its object. Therefore, having 
understood the meaning of selflessness on e should indeed be able to generate 
simultaneously both calm abiding where through focusing on that [meaning] and 
meditating the mind does not wander o ff to other objects and special insight 
focusing on emptiness. Why first seek calm abiding and then develop special 
insight? 
 
Someone thinks that since the observed object of  calm abiding is not specified, by observing 
emptiness one can simultaneously achieve calm ab iding and special insight. Since it is like 
this, this person asks: “Why is it necessary to first develop calm abiding and then special 
insight?” 
 In fact, Atisha’s Lamp on the Path  says that one can develop calm abiding by taking any 
phenomenon whatsoever as an observed object. In  other words, it is not necessary to take a 
certain object such as emptiness, an image of a buddha, a form, a color,  and so forth as the 
observed object, one can take any observed object whatsoever. In other words, one can take 
either the phenomenon that is the support of emptiness or the supported emptiness itself as 
an object. Thus, if one observes selflessness and meditates on it by means of a mind that is 
not distracted to any other phenomenon, one ca n achieve calm abiding observing emptiness. 
Because of this, this person says that since one can achieve calm abiding and special insight 
on the basis of any object, why is it necessary to first attain calm abiding and then special insight?  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
701 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2E-2B The response to it 
1 An understanding of emptiness and a mere meditational experience does not need to be preceded by 
calm abiding  
Here the manner in which calm abiding pr ecedes special insight [matters]: calm 
abiding does not need to come first for an understanding of the view realizing 
selflessness to arise, because the view has al so been found to arise in the absence of 
calm abiding. Calm abiding also does not need to come first for a transformational 
mental experience concerning that view to arise, because even without calm abiding, there is nothing to contradict th e generation of a transformational mental 
experience through meditating, examinin g again and again with discriminating 
awareness. {245} For, if it were contradi ctory, it would absurdly follow from the 
same reason that the generation of a tran sformational mental experience regarding 
impermanence, the faults of cyclic exis tence, and training in the mind of 
enlightenment also depends on calm abiding.  
 In order to understand and have a slight experience of emptiness it is not necessary to first attain calm abiding. In other words, in order to  understand the view realizing emptiness it is 
not necessary to first attain calm abiding. This  means that even without develop calm abiding, 
one can understand the perfect view or emptin ess or selflessness. Thus, to have a slight 
mental transformational experience with respec t to emptiness it is not necessary to have 
developed calm abiding. In other words, there exists a wisdom that engages in analytical meditation on emptiness without calm abiding. In fact there is no contradiction in this. If it 
were contradictory, it would follow that some  experience regarding impermanence, the faults 
of cyclic existence, and the mind of enlightenm ent would also depend on calm abiding, but it 
is not so.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 The manner in which calm abiding has to precede special insight 
Well then, what about the manner in whic h calm abiding has to precede special 
insight? Here the context of generating sp ecial insight is that of ordinary beings 
where the realization born from meditation has not [yet] arisen, who are 
generating it anew. In this regard there is a method of meditating on selflessness 
with a special consciousness realizing emptiness, which will be explained below. 
[However,] apart from that, in the contex t of the perfection vehicle and the three 
lower classes of tantra, analytical medita tion is necessary because special insight 
which is realization born from meditation  does not arise unless you have examined 
the meaning of selflessness with discrimi nating awareness and then engaged in 
analytical meditation sustaining [the  result from] that [examination].  
 
So why is it necessary to develop calm abiding fi rst and then special insi ght? Special insight is 
a wisdom arisen from meditation among the th ree types of wisdom: the wisdom arisen from 
hearing, wisdom arisen from thinking, and wisdom arisen from meditation which are 
developed even by ordinary beings. In both th e perfection vehicle and the three lower classes 
of tantra – action tantra, performance tantra , and yoga tantra – there exists analytical 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
702 meditation because special insight, which is a wisdom arisen from meditation, does not arise 
without examining the meaning of selflessness and then engaging in analytical meditation on 
it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
If you seek an understanding of selflessne ss before, when you have not yet attained 
calm abiding, and then examine its mean ing again and again, since calm abiding 
has not come about [yet] it is impossible fo r calm abiding to develop based on it. If 
you engage in non-analytical placement meditation, even though calm abiding 
develops based on it, this will only be a method of sustaining calm abiding and you 
will not have a method of sustaining spec ial insight. Therefore it is necessary to 
seek special insight subseque ntly and thus the sequence of seeking calm abiding 
first and then meditating on special insigh t based on it has not been violated. This 
being so, if in this system the method of generating special insight has no effect on 
the generation of pliancy through the an alytical meditation of discriminative 
awareness, there would not be a proper re ason for seeking calm abiding first and 
then meditating on special insight based on it178.  
 If without having achieved calm abiding one seeks an understanding of emptiness by examining it with analytical meditation again and again, it will not be possible to achieve calm 
abiding based on it. If one engages in non-analytical placement meditation, one can develop calm abiding but still this will only be a method for cultivating calm abiding and not special 
insight. Therefore, having attained calm abidin g it is necessary to st rive to achieve special 
insight. This means that the orde r is that first one attains calm  abiding followed by the special 
insight observing emptiness and so forth. Thus, having attained a physical and mental pliancy 
that induces a physical and mental bliss, on e goes on to develop special insight, which 
increases the mental pliancy and bliss even more.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3 How maintaining a sequence other than this contradicts the writings of the great trailblazers 
It would indeed be very unreasonable not to meditate according to that sequence. 
As [mentioned] earlier it says in [the Sutra ] Unravelling the Thought  that you 
meditate on special insight ba sed on the attainment of ca lm abiding. {246} This is 
why: the sequence of the concentration and wisdom [forming part] of the six 
perfections, of which it says: “based on the former the latter comes about” and the 
s e q u e n c e  o f  t h e  h i g h e r  t r a i n i n g  i n  c o ncentration based upon which the higher 
training in wisdom comes about are sequences where you first cultivate calm 
abiding before special insight is cultivated.  
 
                                              
178 The implication here is that analytical meditation generates a level of pliancy exceeding that of calm 
abiding, which, however, does not co me about without it. Cf. A. Wallace, The Bridge of Quiescence , Open Court 
1998, p. 125, and R. Thurman, Life and Teachings of Tsongkhapa , Library of Tibetan Works and Archives, 1993, 
p. 114 and 235. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
703 The assertion that the order is ot her than that of first attaining calm abiding and then special 
insight contradicts the texts of the great trailb lazers. Thus, one should meditate on special 
insight based on first having attained calm abid ing. Likewise, also in the context of the six 
perfections, first there is concentration and then  wisdom. Similarly, the order is also similar 
in the context of the three higher trainings of  morality, concentration, and wisdom, that is, 
first one develops the higher training in morali ty, then the higher trai ning in concentration, 
and then the higher training in wisdom. This indi cates that it is necessary  to first attain calm 
abiding and only after that special insight. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In the Bodhisattva Levels  and the Shravaka Levels  it also says that special insight is 
cultivated based on calm abiding. The Essence of the Middle Way , Engaging in  
[Bodhisattva ] Behavior , and the three [volume] Stages of Meditation  as well as 
Jñanakirti and Shantipa set forth that  one acquires calm abiding and then 
develops special insight. Therefore the po sition of some Indian masters whereby 
you start off developing special insight from an analysis with discriminating 
awareness – without seeking calm abiding se parately – is in contradiction to the 
writings of the great trailblazers which is why it would be inappropriate for the 
intelligent to depend on them. However, the sequence of calm abiding and special 
insight applies in this manner when you first develop them, whereas later on the 
sequence is not specified, calm abiding al so being practiced after special insight has 
been generated. 
 
Also the Bodhisattva Grounds  and Hearer Grounds  say that one first develops calm abiding and 
then based on that, special insight. Bhavaviveka’s Essence of the Middle Way , Shantideva’s 
Engaging in  the Bodhisattva  Behavior , and Kamalashila’s three-volume Stages of Meditation , as 
well as the Indian scholars Jñanakirti and Shanti pa, all say that it is necessary to first seek 
calm abiding and then develop special insight. While some Indian masters say that it is not 
necessary to first seek calm abiding and that  from the beginning one can do analytical 
meditation and develop special insi ght, this is not right in that it contradicts the texts of the 
great trailblazers. Therefore, those who are inte lligent should not assert this. However, even 
though when they are initially developed, on e must first develop calm abiding and then 
special insight, after having de veloped them one can also medita te on calm abiding based on 
special insight. In other words, having attained  both calm abiding and special insight, one can 
first meditate on special insight and then de velop calm abiding base d on it, whereby their 
order is reversed.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 246) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2E-3 Presentation and response to an objection  
A The objection that there is a contradiction to scripture in this 
But how can that be, because in the Compendium of All  [Manifest Knowledge ] it says:  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
704 Some have attained special insight, but not calm abiding. They strive 
for calm abiding based on special insight. 
 
If you wonder how this is [so], 
 
The Compendium of All  Manifest Knowledge  says that some people who have achieved special 
insight but not calm abiding strive to achi eve calm abiding based on special insight. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B The response overcoming that fault [pointing out] the need to distinguish between calm abiding of 
actual level and that which belongs to the preparatory level 
it is not [about] those who have not a ttained calm abiding belonging to the 
preparatory level of the first concentratio n, but rather [about ] those who have not 
attained calm abiding of the actual leve l of the first concentration and above. 
Moreover, upon direct realization of the four truths, calm abiding of the first 
concentration and above are achieved based on that.  
 
Those who first develop special insight and then  seek calm abiding are not those who have 
not attained the calm abiding included in the preparatory level, but those who have not attained the calm abiding included in an actual  concentration. There is calm abiding included 
in a preparation and calm abiding included in an actual concentration. When the four noble 
truths are realized directly, one achieves the calm  abiding of the first concentration and above.  
 
For in the Actual Basis of the Levels  it says:  
 
Apart from that, [it is possible that] someone with perfect understanding of suffering [and so on] up to the path as they really are has not [yet] attained the first concen tration or anything above. As soon 
as {247} that [concentration] is pres ent they place the mind and do not 
analyze. Based on exactly that specia l wisdom they combine it with the 
special state of mind. 
 To simplify the terminology, nine [diffe rent] minds are loosely called calm abiding 
and the four types of investigation
179 are called special insi ght. However, actual 
calm abiding and special insight, as will  be shown [later], must be posited [as 
occurring] after the development of pliancy. 
 There are those who have understood the four no ble truths – true sufferings, true origins, 
true cessations, and true paths – but who have not attained the first concentration. Special 
wisdom needs to be combined with the mind of the first concentrat ion. This means that 
when talking very roughly, ther e are nine mental stages that are loosely called “calm abiding” 
even though they are not actual calm abiding.  These nine stages include setting, continuous 
                                             
 
179 Investigation of phenomena from the point of view  of their nature, the performance of functions, 
dependence and logical establishment. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
705 setting, resetting, close setting, and so forth,  which are at times called “calm abiding.” 
Similarly the four types of thorough differentiation are also loosely called “special insight” 
even though they are not actual  special insight. However, in order to develop actual calm 
abiding and actual special insight, it is necessary to first develop pliancy.
