Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 247) says: 
Explanation of how to train in calm abiding, the essence of concentration 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1 How to train in calm abiding 
A Attending to the prerequisites for calm abiding 
B How to develop calm abiding based on that 
C The measure for the accomplishment of calm abiding through meditation 
The first one has three points: attending to  the prerequisites for calm abiding, how 
to develop calm abiding based on that, an d the measure for the accomplishment of 
calm abiding through meditation. 
 
Calm abiding is the entity of concentration ( bsam gtan ). 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1A Attending to the prerequisites for calm abiding 
The first one has six points.  
1 Staying in a favourable environment 
As regards staying in a favourable enviro nment the five desirable qualities of the 
environment are: easy access to food, clothi ng and the like, in the sense that they 
can be obtained without trouble, a good location in the sense that predators and 
other wild creatures as well as enemies and so  on do not live there, good soil in the 
sense that it does not does not breed dise ases, good company in the sense that one 
has companions of equal ethical discipline  and views, and being well endowed in 
the sense that during the day there are fe w people [around] and at night there is 
little noise.  
 
The place in which one trains in calm abiding sh ould be conducive in that it possesses five 
qualities: 1. the necessities for life such as food and clothing are easily found,  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
706 2. it is free from wild animals as well as  enemies such as bandits and so forth, 
3. it has good earth that does not cause sickness in the body,  
4. there are companions with similar morality  and views who are good friends that do not 
cause problems, and 
5. there are not many people duri ng the day and little noise at ni ght such as that of strongly 
flowing rivers, strongly blowing wind, th e howling of animals, and so forth. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In Ornament of the Sutras  it says: 
 
A place for intelligent people’s attainments 
has good access, a good location,  
good soil as well as good companions 
and comfortable conditions for yoga. 
 
I t  s ho u ld  be a place w her e i t  i s  eas y t o  fi n d  food, such as vegetables, and water, as well as 
clo t hin g. It  s ho uld be  a n ice pl a ce, t h at  h a s good earth, where there are good companions 
who take care of and help one, and all the necessities for one’s comfort are present.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 Few desires 
Few desires: no special attachment to good or numerous robes etc.  
 
In addition, one should have few desires, for ex ample, if one is a monk one should not have 
excessive attachment to possessi ng good and numerous robes. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3 Contentment 
Contentment: always to be satisfied with any [old] robes etc. that serve their 
purpose. 
 
One should be satisfied with what one has, fo r example, if one is a monk one should be 
satisfied with having enough robes to keep out the cold.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
4 Complete abstention from many activities 
{248} Complete abstention from many activities: completely to give up 
unwholesome activities like trading, as well as excessive association with householders and the ordained, practicing medicine and astrology. 
 
One should abandon many activities, meaning that one should give up bad activities such as business, that is, non-Dharma or mundane activities, which involve cheating others, 
bargaining with others, and so forth. One should also give up excessive association with 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
707 householders and the ordained due to which on e engages in meaningless talk, and also give 
up practicing medicine and astrology for money.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
5 Pure ethical discipline 
Pure ethical discipline: with regard to one’s pratimoksha and bodhisattva vows, 
not to breach the basis of the trainings through anything unwholesome either by 
nature or by commitment, and if one care lessly does, to restore it quickly through 
regret in accordance with the teachings.  
 
One should keep pure morality which involves safeguarding the vows of individual liberation 
and bodhisattva vows as well as abandoning th e natural and formulated misdeeds. If one 
engages in these activities out of carelessness, one should imme diately generate regret for it 
and purify it. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
6 Complete abstention from  thoughts of desire 
Complete abstention from thoughts of desi re etc.: you should give up all thoughts 
o f  d e s i r e  e i t h e r  b y  m e d i t a t i n g  o n  b e i n g  k i l l e d  o r  b o u n d  i n  t h i s  l i f e  a n d  o n  
disadvantages of future lives such as going to bad realms, or you should meditate 
thinking ‘All the pleasant and unpleasant things of cyclic existence are subject to 
destruction and impermanent. If I will surely  be separated from all of them before 
long why do I get totally attached and so on?’  
 
One should completely abandon the conceptualizations of desire and so forth. One should 
meditate on the faults of this life, such as being killed, bound, and so forth, and on where 
one will be reborn in the future. In this way,  one should contemplate the many disadvantages 
of negative actions. One should think that ever ything, both the attractive and unattractive, in 
cyclic existence is impermanent and will chan ge. One should ask oneself: “Since I will 
definitely be separated from them because of not remaining in this life for long, why be 
attached to attractive things and so forth?” Thinking in this way one should abandon the 
conceptualizations of desire.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In Lamp on the Path  it says:  
 
Even if you exert yourself greatly, 
even for a thousand years, if calm abiding’s limb is impaired meditative stabilisation will not be achieved. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
708 In this context the “limbs” or “branches” of calm  abiding refer to the causes of calm abiding. 
No matter how much effort one exerts, even for a thousand years, if these causes degenerate 
one will not achieve meditative stabilization. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
For those with the heartfelt wish to achi eve the meditative stabilization of calm 
abiding and special insight it is therefor e essential to strive for the thirteen 
prerequisites set out in the Shravaka Levels  etc.  
 
Those who wish from their heart to achieve the meditative stabilization of calm abiding and 
special insight must strive in the collection of their respective causes, thirteen of which are 
mentioned in the Hearer Grounds .  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 248) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B How to develop calm abiding based on that 
1 The preliminaries 
2 The main part 
The second one has two points: the preliminaries and the main part.  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B1 The preliminaries 
First: You should long cultivate the six preparatory practices explained earlier as 
well as the mind of enlightenment and, as  part of that, also practice the core 
meditation topics shared with lesser and intermediate beings.  
 
One should do the six preparatory practices that were explained earlier on in the text, cultivate the mind of enlightenment, and tr ain in the common paths of small and middling 
beings. The path of small beings consists in re lying on a spiritual teacher, contemplating the 
precious human rebirth endowed with the eighteen  qualities, the difficulty of finding it, its 
meaningfulness, and so forth. One should do a gl ance meditation very briefly on these topics. 
For example, one should think about the eigh t non-leisures and the ten endowments. The 
eight non-leisures include the four non-human st ates of being born as a hell being, a hungry 
g h o s t ,  a n  a n i m a l ,  o r  a  l o n g - l i f e  g o d .  I n  a d d i t i o n ,  o n e  i s  f r e e  f r o m  h a v i n g  b e e n  b o r n  i n  a  barbaric land lacking in culture and religion, one has not been born in a dark eon when a 
buddha has not given teachings, one has not b een born deaf and mute, and one does not 
hold wrong views such as thinking that the law of cause and effect, past and future lives, and 
the Three Jewels do not exist. One should thin k about this and conclude “I am very lucky,” 
thereby lifting up one’s mind with this thought.   Among the ten endowments, five are from a pe rsonal point of view and five are from a 
circumstantial point of view. At the moment we have all ten of them bu t they are difficult to 
find again and, although extremely us eful, they quickly come to an end. 
 One should then do the nine-round medita tion on death-impermanence. All beings who 
are born will definitely die, the time of death is uncertain, and at the time of death all that 
will help one is one’s Dharma practice. At that  time one’s relatives and friends, one’s wealth, 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
709 and even one’s body will not be of any help and will have to be left behind. Thinking about 
this, one should determine to pr actice the Dharma right now.  
 Then one should think about the fact that if  has created non-virtuous actions, one will be 
reborn in the lower realms, for example, in on e of the eight hot or eight cold hells, in a 
neighboring hell, or in a peripheral hell. Right now we are very lucky to not have been born 
there. One can also be born as a hungry ghost with inner or outer obstructions or with both 
inner and outer obstructions. One can also be bo rn as an animal, for example, as one of the 
numerous animals crowding the ocean or as on e of the animals who li ve scattered on the 
earth together with human beings such as a cow, buffalo, and so forth, or as a dog, a cat, a 
bird, and so forth. Animals engage in killing  each other and are unable to practice the 
Dharma due to their stupidity. We are lucky to  have escaped such a rebirth, but one should 
ask oneself: “If I were reborn in the future in  one of these realms how could I bear it?”  
 Thinking like this, one should determine to give up creating non-virtuous karma such as 
the three non-virtuous actions of body, the four of speech, and the three of mind. Even 
though one cannot block the actions of mind physically, one should apply mindfulness and 
introspection in order to do so. In this way on e will be reborn in the good migrations as a 
human or god. However, even if one is reborn as a human or god,  such a rebirth is still within 
cyclic existence. Therefore, one needs to th ink about the path of middling beings which 
includes meditation on the four noble truths and their sixteen attributes. One should begin 
by recognizing sufferings. Then one should th ink about the causes of sufferings, karma and 
afflictions, which are to be abandoned. Ho w are they abandoned? By actualizing true 
cessations. How is this done? By  meditating true paths. Howeve r, this cannot be done right 
from the beginning so one needs to practice the higher training in morality, the higher 
training in concentration, and the higher trai ning in wisdom. By doing so, one will come to 
realize selflessness, whereby one can abandon the afflictions and achieve the state of liberation 
or the state of foe-destroyer. One should de termine to abandon the sufferings of cyclic 
existence in this way. But then  one should think remember that  one’s mother sentient beings 
are also suffering in cyclic existence and, th erefore, generate love and compassion for all 
sentient beings and develop the mind of en lightenment, the mahayana mind. Then one 
should about the fact that if on e follows the sutra path it will ta ke a very long time to attain 
enlightenment. Because of this, one should  engage in the uncommon path of tantra, 
especially highest yoga tantra, in order to be able to quickly achieve buddhahood in order to 
be of benefit to all sentient beings. One should  think about and meditate on these topics in 
this way.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2 The main part 
A What body posture to meditate in  
B Explanation of the stages of meditation themselves 
The second one has two points: what body posture to meditate in and explanation 
of the stages of meditation themselves.  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2A What body posture to meditate in 
F i r s t :  A s  i t  s a y s  i n  Stages of Meditation  you should [settle down] on a very 
comfortable smooth seat [and] {249} ad opt a body posture with the eight 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
710 characteristics: The legs are fully crossed or half-crossed, which is also allowed. 
That is how you should sit. The eyes focu s on the tip of the nose, neither too open 
nor too closed. You sit with the body straig htened up [so it is fully] erect rather 
than too bent and crooked and mindfu lness should be placed inward. The 
shoulders are straight at the same level. The head is not [too] high nor [too] low 
and held without tilting to one side – yo u are erect from the nose [down] to the 
navel. The teeth and lips are held naturall y, as they usually are. The tongue is 
placed near the upper teeth. As regard s the breath, exhalation and inhalation 
should not be allowed to make any sound, be forceful, or uncontrolled. Rather, its 
coming and going always follows effort lessly in a relaxed manner – without any 
sensation. That is the way to do it. In ac cordance with that you should first of all 
achieve the body position with eight [c haracteristics] and especially gentle 
breathing, the way it has been explained.  
 
What should one’s body posture be while training in calm abiding? The Stages of Meditation  
says that one should sit on a soft and co mfortable cushion in the Vairochana posture 
possessing the eight attributes: 
1. one’s legs should be in the fully crossed or half crossed position, like we are sitting here; 
2. one’s eyes should neither be open too wide or  completely closed, but should be half closed 
while looking at the tip of the nose; 3. one’s body should not be either leaning backward or bending forward but should be 
upright with mindfulness placed inward; 
4. one’s shoulders should be at the same leve l, without one being higher than the other; 
5. one’s head should not be held either too high  or too low or tilted to one side, but should 
be very straight, forming a straight line fr om the tip of the nose down to the navel; 
6. one’s teeth and lips should be in the natu ral position (without baring one’s teeth!); 
7. the tip of one’s tongue should rest against th e upper palate so as to prevent saliva forming 
and becoming thirsty; 8. one’s breathing, inhalation and exhalation, sh ould not be strong and noisy (like snoring!), 
rather one should breath in an d out very normally, without fo rce, such that others cannot 
hear it, that is, one should breath slowly and gently, without effort.  
It is important right from the beginning to sit in  this posture with the eight attributes and to 
breath normally.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B Explanation of the stages of meditation themselves 
1 Transition 
2 The actual [explanation] 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-1 Transition 
Second: The majority of Lam-rim [text]s te ach the achievement of calm abiding by 
way of eight [conditional] factors180 [for] giving up five faults, set out in 
[Discrimination of ] the Middle and Extremes . On top of that, instructions from Geshe 
Lagsorwa’s lineage explain the need to achieve the six powers, four attentions, and 
                                              
180 i.e., “antidotes” 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
711 the nine minds explained in the Shravaka Levels . The venerable Maitreya in his 
Ornament of the Sutras  and [ Discrimination of ] the Middle and Extremes  also sets out 
the nine means of stabilizing the mind an d the eight [compositional] factors [for] 
giving up [faults]. Following them Indian  scholars like Haribhadra, Kamalashila, 
and Shantipa have also [written] many co mpositions on the stages of achieving 
meditative stabilisation. In the Mantr ayana too, you need to know them. In 
particular the shortcomings of meditative stabilisation such as the five faults and 
the way to eliminate them is fully illumi nated by scriptures pertaining to the 
collection of sutras.  
 
Most texts on the Stages of the Path teach that  calm abiding is attained through abandoning 
the five faults by means of the eight antido tes. The instructions from Geshe Lagsorwa’s 
lineage say that one needs to achieve the six powers and four attentions or four mental 
engagements as well as the nine mental abidings  in order to attain calm abiding. Maitreya’s 
Ornament of the Sutras  and Discrimination of  the Middle and Extremes  set out nine methods of 
mental abiding and the eight factors for abando ning faults. The Indian scholars who followed 
them also wrote about achieving meditative stabil iz at ion . It  is als o n eces s ary t o  kn o w  t hes e 
subject in the context of tantra. The five fa ults of meditative stabilization and how to 
eliminate them are taught more exte nsively in sutra than in tantra. 
 The five faults are:  1. laziness (we are often lazy!), which has four antidotes; 2. forgetfulness, its antidote is mindfulness; 3. laxity and excitement, their antidote is introspection; 4. non-application of the antidote when these faults arise, its antidote is intention which 
moves the mind to the antidote; and 5. over-application of an antidote when no fa ult has arisen, its antidote is equanimity.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2 The actual [explanation 
A How to generate flawless meditative stabilisation 
B The stages of mental abiding arising on its basis  
This one has two points: {250} How to ge nerate flawless meditative stabilization 
and the stages of mental abiding arising on its basis. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A How to genera te flawless meditative stabilization 
1 What to do before focusing the mind on the meditation object  
2 What to do while focusing the mind on the meditation object 
3 What to do after focusing the mind on the meditation object  
The first one has three points: what to d o  b e f o r e  f o c u s i n g  t h e  m i n d  o n  t h e  
meditation object, what to do while focu sing the mind on the meditation object, 
and what to do after focusing the mind on the meditation object.  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A1 What to do before focusing the mind on the meditation object 
A The need to rely on antidotes to laziness at the outset  
First: If you cannot put an end to lazine ss which does not rejoice in concentration 
but rather delights in its opposite, from th e beginning it will not let you engage in 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
712 concentration and even if you achieve [con centration] once it will not possibly be 
persistent and therefore deteriorate quic kly. For that reason it is of major 
importance to put and end to laziness at the outset.  
 
Initially one needs to strive to eliminate laziness by applying its antidotes. This is because if 
one is not able to stop the laziness that does not enjoy meditation but enjoys its discordant 
classes, right from the beginning one will not en gage in meditative stabilization, and even if 
one engages in meditative stabilization one will not do so continually and it will quickly 
degenerate. Therefore, right from the very be ginning the essential point is to eliminate 
laziness.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B Individual explanation of the four antidotes 
In this regard, once you achieve pliancy where body and mind expand with bliss, 
there will be no [more] weariness day or ni ght with regard to training in what is 
wholesome and therefore laziness will have be en stopped. To generate it there is a 
need for the ability continuously to create  joyous effort with respect to the cause 
for generating pliancy, meditative stab ilization. To generate that, intense, 
continuous aspiration is needed that strive s for meditative stabilization. As a cause 
of that the steady faith of strong fascin ation due to an insight into the positive 
qualities of meditative stabilization is necessary. Therefore you should cultivate 
again and again the faith aware of the positive  qualities of meditative stabilization.  
 There are four antidotes to laziness. If one achi eves the physical and me ntal pliancy that are 
increased by mental happiness and joy, one will be able to train in virtuous actions day and night without becoming tired whereby laziness will be eliminated. However, in order to develop this pliancy, one needs to develop it s cause, effort, with respect to generating 
meditative stabilization. In order to generate this  effort one must first have the aspiration that 
wishes to develop this meditative stabilization.  In order to develop this aspiration, one first 
needs to develop lucid faith in meditative st abilization through seeing its qualities. One 
should cultivate the faith that understands the qualities of meditative stabilization again and 
again.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C The source of this  
In [Discrimination of ] The Middle and Extremes  it says: 
 
The ground and that which rests on it, 
the cause and the result itself. 
 
Here the ground is aspiration, [i.e.] the ground[ing] for endeavour. That which 
rests on it is endeavour or joyous effort. The cause of aspiration is the conviction 
in positive qualities. The result of endeavour is pliancy.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
713 Faith is likened to a mother, in that just as for a child to be born a mother is necessary, 
similarly in order for qualities to grow faith is necessary. Faith is the cause of practicing virtue. 
Just as a mother gives birth to her child and then  nurtures it, likewise the faith that sees the 
qualities of meditative stabilization causes one to aspire to achieve meditative stabilization, 
which then causes one to put effort into achiev ing meditative stabilization, which causes one 
to achieve the result of pliancy or suppleness. Al though the main antidote to laziness is said to 
be pliancy, when pliancy is a ttained laziness has actually alre ady been overcome by means of 
faith, aspiration, and effort. In short, initia lly one needs to contemplate the qualities of 
meditative stabilization.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D How to reflect on the positive qualities of meditative stabilization 
The positive qualities of meditative stabil ization to be developed here: Once calm 
abiding has been accomplished mental happiness and physical comfort increase 
greatly, so that you stay happy in this li fetime. Due to the acquisition of physical 
and mental pliancy the mind is apt to ma ke use of any wholesome object at will. 
As involuntary wandering {251} to wrong ob jects has subsided negative actions do 
not occur a lot while whatever wholesome things you do have great strength. Based 
on calm abiding positive qualities such as supernormal cognitive powers and miraculous powers can be achieved. [Most] specially, based on it the realization of 
special insight knowing emptiness comes abou t so the root of cy clic existence can 
be cut quickly. If you consider any such positive qualities, the power of joy in 
cultivating meditative stabilization will increase. You should get to know them and meditate. Once this has developed, as ther e will be a continuous urge to cultivate 
meditative stabilization, it will be easy to  accomplish meditative stabilization and, 
upon its accomplishment, as you engage in its cultivation again and again, it will 
be very difficult for it to deteriorate.  
 If one achieves calm abiding, the result is that  one’s mind will be joyful and one’s body will 
experience happiness. In other words, this br ings about pliancy which induces mental and 
physical bliss that causes one to abide in ha ppiness in this very life. Due to mental and 
physical pliancy, one will be able to make as  much effort as one wishes with respect to 
virtuous objects due to a tirelessness of body and mind. Due to the mind no longer wandering to wrong objects, one will not commit negative actions and one’s positive actions will become stronger and more powerful. In dependence on calm abiding one can develop 
clairvoyance, such as the clairvoyance knowing others minds, as well as magical powers. In 
addition, based on calm abiding one can generate the special insight realizing emptiness which quickly cuts the root  of cyclic existence.  
 One should think about the many positive qual ities of calm abiding in this way, whereby 
one’s joy in cultivating them will increase. One will then continually strive to develop 
meditative stabilization and will easily achieve it . Having achieved it, one should continue to 
engage in it whereby it will not degenerate. In short, one should strive constantly to develop 
more meditative stabilization.   
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
714 If we have courage, we can even destroy the elephant-shaped mountain that overlooks 
Pomaia! On the other hand, if our mind is lack ing in strength and courage, we will not be 
able to overcome the interferences that arise.  Therefore, we need to develop a good and 
strong mental attitude. If we make effort, we, like the many holy beings of the past, both 
Buddhist and Christian, can develop concentratio n and other high realizations. To do this, 
we need to put effort into meditation. If, on the other hand, we do not make this effort and 
do not meditate but instead only eat, talk, wand er about, watch movies, and so forth, we will 
not achieve anything at all. We might enjoy ourselves for a short time but afterward our mind 
will return to its usual stat e. Perhaps I am mistaken?!  
 Wednesday morning, 24 January 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

B The way to focus the mind on it 
The first one has two points: identifying th e base, the meditation object on which 
to place the mind, and the way to focus the mind on it. 
 
First the observed objects on which to focus the mind are identified and then the way in 
which to focus the mind on them is explained.

Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2A-1A Indication of the meditation objects themselves 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
715 First: The first from among two points, regarding the first one:  
1 Identifying the classifications of meditation objects  
As regards the four meditation objects of yogis taught by the Bhagavat, the 
pervasive objects are four [in number]: (1 -2) [there are] the ones where you place 
[the mind] without analyzing and analytical objects, [which makes] two, (3) the 
limits of phenomena – how they exist and in what variety – and (4) achievement of 
purpose: transformation through medi tation by way of the two previous 
meditation methods with th e focus on the meaning of how phenomena exist and 
in what variety.  
 
There are four types of observed objects, among which pervasive objects are also of four types:  1. setting without analysis,  2. observed object with analysis,  3. the limit of things that  are varieties and modes,  
4. achievement of a purpose. In setting without analysis one sets the mind  single-pointedly on its object, this being 
placement meditation. “Modes” ( ji lta , literally, as it is) refers to ultimate truths or 
emptinesses, whereas “varieties” ( ji snyed , literally whatever is) refers to conventional truths or 
the multiplicity. Observed objects that bring about the achievement of a purpose are those 
that bring about a transformation in the mind by observing their meaning. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 As regards (meditation) objects for puri fying behavior there are five meditation 
objects [serving] as antidotes to one’s pr edominant behavior in previous lives – 
attachment, anger, ignorance, pride, and discursiveness – in that order the 
repulsive, love, dependent arising, the division of elements as well as exhalation 
and inhalation.  
 There are people who have familiarized with attach ment in past lives due to which in this life 
their attachment to objects is very strong. Like wise, there are those who have familiarized with 
anger or hatred and so often become angry, as  well as those who have familiarized with 
ignorance in the past and so are presently confused and ignorant. If one has strong 
attachment in this life, one should take ugline ss or repulsiveness as one’s observed object. To 
do this one should imagine an attractive object  as having a terrible aspect, so bad that one 
does not even want to think ab out it. For example, if one has strong attachment to another 
person’s face, in order to reduce one’s attach ment to it one should imagine worms emerging 
from and entering into the apertures and pores.  If, on the other hand, one has a lot of anger 
for someone, one should strive to develop a mi nd of love toward that person thinking “May 
he have happiness and the causes of happiness” through understanding that that person is 
just like oneself in wanting happi ness and not wanting suffering.  
 If one has a lot of ignorance,  one should meditate on depend ent-relationship, that is, on 
the twelve links of dependent-arising, or on th e dependence of the whole on parts, or on the 
dependence of phenomena being imputed upon a basis. By meditating on dependent-relation 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
716 one will come to understand that all phenomena do not exist inherently or truly, this being 
the antidote to apprehending all phenomena as existing from their own side, as being self-
sufficient substantially existent, or as being in herently and truly existent. If one has very 
strong pride, one should meditate on the eigh teen elements or constituents, the sixty-four 
constituents, and so forth. When one sees that  these are difficult to understand, one’s pride 
will diminish. If one has a lot of strong co nceptualization or discursiveness, one should 
meditate on the inhalation and exhalation of th e breath while letting go of conceptualization. 
In this way, there are these five different ty pes of meditation for five different types of 
problems. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The meditation objects for the skillful ar e also five [in number]: objects of skill 
with regard to the aggregates, elements, se nse fields, the twelve links of dependent 
origination {252} as well as th e definite and the indefinite. 
 
There are also five skillful observed objects: 1. the five aggregates, 2. the eighteen elements or constituents, 3. the twelve sense fields or sources, 4. the twelve links of dependent-arising, and 
5. sources and non-sources.  
In the Treasury of Manifest Dharma  the five aggregates as well as many other aggregates are 
discussed. The form aggregate, for example, is further divided into twenty types, the feeling 
aggregate into five types, the discrimination aggregate into six types, compositional factors 
into the forty-six mental factors and so forth, and the consciousness aggregate into six types. 
the eighteen elements or constitu ents are further divided into he six objects, six sense powers, 
and six object-possessors. The twelve sources or entrances are further divided into form 
source, sound source, odor source, taste source , tangible object source, phenomena source, 
eye source, ear source, nose source, tongue source, body source, and mental source. The 
twelve links of dependent-arising are ignorance,  karmic formations, consciousness, name and 
form, sources, contact, feeling, craving, gras ping, existence, birth, and aging-death. A non-
source is “virtuous actions give rise to suffering” and “non-virtuous actions give rise to happiness,” whereas a source is “virtuous action s give rise to happiness” and “non-virtuous 
actions give rise to suffering.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The objects for purifying mental affliction s are also two: the peaceful and coarse 
[states] of the higher and lower levels and the 16 [aspects] associated with the four 
truths, impermanence etc.  
 
The observed objects for purifying afflictions are of two types:  1. a higher realm as peaceful and the lower realm as rough, that is, looking at the desire realm 
as having many faults and the first concentratio n as being free from them; looking at the first 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
717 concentration as having many faults and the second concentration as being free from them; 
looking at the second concentration as having  many faults and the third concentration as 
being free from them, and so forth; 
2. the four noble truths and their sixteen attr ibutes, which are taught  in the Middle Way – 
true sufferings, true origins, true cessations, tr ue paths. True sufferings have the attributes of 
being impermanent, suffering, empty (empty of a permanent, partless, independent self), and 
selfless (empty of self-sufficient substantially exis tent self); true origins have the attributes of 
being cause, source, strong producer, and condit ion; true cessations have the attributes of 
being cessation, pacification, auspicious highness,  and deliverance; and true paths have that 
attributes of being path, suitability,  achiever, and definite emerger.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 How [all of] these individually serve a special purpose 
In this regard, meditation objects for pu rifying behavior are special in that the 
attachment etc. of those wh o get involved in attachment  etc. more than usual is 
easily stopped and based on that concentration is easily achieved.  
 
As I said before, those who have cultivated  attachment, anger, ignorance, pride, and 
conceptuality in past lives should meditate on their respective antidotes: ugliness, love, the 
dependent-relationship of all phenomena wh ich do not exist inherently, the eighteen 
constituents, and the inhalation and exhalation of the breath. In shor t, these are the best 
objects for such people to atta in meditative stabilization. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The meditation objects for the skilful are good meditation objects of calm 
abiding because [the idea of] a personal  self apart from those phenomena is 
undermined whereby they are in confor mity with the development of special 
insight knowing selflessness.  
 
The five skillful objects are good observed objects for attaining calm abiding because by 
meditating on the selflessness of  persons one can refute a self-sufficient substantially existent 
self, whereby it becomes easier to develop spec ial insight realizing selflessness. Therefore, 
these are the best objects for developing calm abiding.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Meditation objects for purifying mental affl ictions are of great significance in that 
they become antidotes to ment al afflictions in general.  
 
The two observed objects for purifying afflictions – looking at a higher state as peaceful and at 
the lower state as rough and the four noble trut hs – are antidotes to all the afflictions in 
general. Although there are many different types of afflictions, in general there are six root 
afflictions and twenty secondary afflictions that need to be purified, due to which meditation 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
718 on these observed objects is important. The six root afflictions are attachment, anger, 
ignorance, pride, afflicted doubt, and wrong view. In this context doubt is included in 
conceptuality, and the antidote to wrong view s is to examine sources and non-sources.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The pervasive objects do not include an y objects besides the ones [mentioned] 
before. 
 
The pervasion objects are only  those mentioned earlier. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Therefore meditative stabilization should be attained based on meditation objects 
of calm abiding with a special purpose. Fr om that it is obvious that someone who 
practices meditative stabilization based on pebbles, pieces of wood, and the like is 
unaware of the orderly presentation of meditation objects associated with meditative stabilization.  
 One should take one of the four observed objects mentioned above for the development of calm abiding. If one were, on the other hand, to take a stick or pebble as an observed object 
for the development of calm abiding, this wo uld be mistaken and show that one has not 
understood the presentation of observed objects. Brahmins, for example, claim that it is easier 
to develop concentration on the face of one’s wife  because it is easy to visualize and keep in 
mind without wandering to other objects. They say that it is more difficult to visualize a statue 
and so forth. There is a story about this (b am! Geshe-la slaps his forehead). One time a 
Mongolian ex-abbot of Gomang, who lived for a lo ng time in Holland, was invited to teach a 
meditation course to Geshe Rabten’s many We stern students. This lama asked each of the 
students what were there objects of meditation . One replied that it was emptiness, for which 
the lama praised the student. Another student said that he meditated on impermanence, and 
that lama also said that was good. Another stud ent said he meditated on Vajrayogini, another 
on Yamantaka, and another on Guhyasamaja, whereupon the lama asked them: “Do you not 
meditate on your guru?” When they responded that they did not, he told them that they 
should do so and that if they could not visualiz e Geshe Rabten’s entire face they should begin 
by visualizing his nose. He said that when they  could visualize his nose clearly, they should 
gradually add his eyes, ears, and mouth to the visualization, and then slowly add his arms, 
u p p e r  b o d y ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  T h u s ,  h e  s a i d  “ Y o u  s h o u l d  m e d i t a t e  o n  G e s h e  R a b t e n ’ s  n o s e ! ”  
which made everyone laugh! I am joking but, in short, even though we can take any object as 
our observed object it is better not to meditate on a stick and so forth!

object

719 1 How what person should make what his meditation object  
Second: In accordance with what is set out in [the Sutra ] Requested by Revata , it has 
been taught that persons [ranging] from those in whom attachment predominates 
to those in whom discursi veness predominates should rely on the meditation 
objects specified for them individually – from the repulsive to exhalation and 
inhalation, respectively. Those whose behavi or is balanced and those whose mental 
afflictions are small can concentrate on an y one object they are happy with and a 
specific one is not necessary.  
 
Similarly the Sutra  Requested by Revata  says that those people wh o have strong attachment, 
anger, ignorance, pride, or discursiveness shou ld meditate on respectively the topics of 
repulsiveness through the inhalation and exha lation of the breath. With respect to the 
meditation on repulsiveness, nine ways are set out in Treasury of Manifest Dharma , the last of 
which is meditation on a skeleton. When do ing this latter meditation, one begins by 
imagining that at the center of one’s forehead a bit of flesh falls off, revealing the white bone 
beneath. Then slowly more flesh falls off unti l one’s entire body is only bones. Then one 
extends this visualization even further until all the ground is covered with bones and 
skeletons. Then one gradually reabsorbs the visu alization up to one’s forehead and then re-
extends it outward again. This meditation helps to overcome attachment.   If one has familiarized equally with alls thes e afflictions in the past and therefore has less 
afflictions in this life, one can meditate on wh ichever of these objects one likes, there being 
no need for a specific object.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 Differences in the level of predominance etc. of attachment etc.  
I n  t h i s  r e g a r d  p r e d o m i n a n c e  o f  [ o n e  o f ]  the five [mental afflictions], attachment 
and the like, [means that] through habitu ation to attachment and the like in 
previous lives long term attachment and the like arise towards small objects of 
attachment and the like. {253 } As for those with balanc ed behavior: they may not 
have got habituated to attachment and the li ke in previous lives, however, as they 
do not see it as a fault, even though st rong, long term attachment and the other 
[afflictions] may not arise towards their [respective] objects, it is not like they do 
not occur [at all]. Those with small mental  afflictions have not got habituated to 
attachment etc. in previous lives and due to the fact that, among other things, they 
see them as faults, attachment etc. will arise [only] slowly towards great and lots of 
objects of attachment etc. Towards medium  and small objects they will not arise [at 
all]. Moreover, the predominance of attach ment or another one of the five [mental 
afflictions] results in a long term [a ccomplishment of quiescence]; balanced 
behavior in a not too long term [accompli shment of quiescence]; and small mental 
afflictions in a speedy accomplishment of quiescence. 
 Between people there is a difference in terms of the strength of their attachment and so forth. 
Thus, if someone has familiarized with one of th e five, attachment and so forth, in previous 
lives, that particular affliction will arise even  toward small objects of attachment, hatred, and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
720 so forth. This happens because in the past this  person continuously generated this particular 
affliction, due to which in this life it arises strongly and easily. However, even if one did not 
familiarize strongly with any the five (a ttachment, hatred, ignorance, pride, and 
conceptualization) in particular, it is not that  one has seen them as faults. Because of this, 
even if strong and continuous attachment and so forth do not arise toward their respective 
objects, it is not that they do not arise at a ll. Those with small or we ak afflictions are those 
who did not habituate with any of these afflictions in their previous lives and also saw them as 
faults. However, even for them attachment and so  forth slowly arise with respect to many and 
great objects of attachment and so forth. On the other hand, for such people attachment and 
so forth will only arise very slightly or no t at all towards middling and small objects of 
attachment. When attachment and so forth pred ominate it takes a long time to eliminate 
them, whereas when they are middl ing it does not take too long  to eliminate them, and when 
they are small they are quickly eliminated.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 253) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2A-2 Identification of meditation objects for given situations 
A General explanation 
Now, if you wonder about the meditation object based on which calm abiding is 
achieved: Generally speaking, the meditati on objects of various persons are just 
like it has been explained. In particular, the point that those wi th a great deal of 
discursiveness should meditate on  the breath is important.  
 
On what kind of object is calm abiding achi eved? In general there are specific objects for 
d i f f e r e n t  t y p e s  o f  p e r s o n s .  H o w e v e r ,  i t  i s  important that those people with a lot of 
conceptualization meditate on the breath.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B Numerous texts set out how to achieve meditative stabilization taking the Tathagata’s body as one’s 
meditation object  
Apart from that [the Sutra ] Concentration  of the Present Buddha Manifestly Abiding  as 
well as the Middle  and Last Stages of Meditation , following the King of Concentration  
[Sutra ], teach the accomplishment of meditati ve stabilization with the Tathagata’s 
body as the meditation object. The commentary on Lamp on the Path  quotes 
statements by Master Bodhibhadra [to the effect] that meditative stabilization is 
achieved focusing on the Tathagata’s body.  
 
In addition, there is a general object, the body of the Tathagata or the Buddha, that is taught 
in many texts. Since it was Buddha Shakyamuni who visited our world in this fortunate eon, 
it is taught in various sutras as well as in Kamalashila’s Stages of Meditation , that meditative 
stabilization can be achieved by focusing on his body as one’s object of meditation because it 
brings about an increase in virtue. Atisha’s Lamp on the Path  also quotes Master Bodhibhadra 
as saying that meditative stabilization is achieved by observing the Tathagata’s body.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
721 C How it is of great purpose to make such a meditation object one’s basis  
In this regard, with attention to the Budd ha’s body, limitless merit is born through 
recollection of the Buddha. If that body is clear and stable in its aspect, it makes a 
great difference with regard to visualizin g the field for accumulating merit through 
prostrations, offerings, prayers, and the like  a s  w e l l  a s  ( t h e  f i e l d  f o r )  p u r i f y i n g  
obscurations through confession, restraint,  etc. {254} The positive quality that 
recollection of the Buddha does not deteri orate at the time of death, the great 
difference it makes to deity yoga as you cultivate the path of mantra, and other 
things show that it is very purposeful.  
 
It is meaningful to take the Buddha’s body as th e observed object of one’s meditation. This is 
because if one focuses one’s mind on an image of the Buddha, infinite merit is produced due 
to recollecting the Buddha. If one’s visualizat ion of the Buddha’s body is clear and stable, 
when one makes prostrations to him, offerings,  prayers, and so forth one can accumulate a lot 
of merit and when one confesses and so forth to  him one also purifies one’s negativities and 
obscurations. Thus, visualizing the Buddha’s body  is meaningful and powerful in terms of 
creating merit and purifying negative actions,  that is, karmic obscurations. Also due to 
visualizing the Buddha, one will recollect the Bu ddha at the time of death, one’s deity yoga 
when cultivating the path of mantra will be enhanced, and so forth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 254) says: 
D What bodily form to make [one’s meditation object] and how  
In the King of Concentration  it says:  
 
With his body just like the color of gold, 
the Lord of the World is excessively beautiful – whose mind is involved in this object of meditation 
that bodhisattva is said to be equipoised
181. 
 
You should make a likeness [of what is being expressed here] your object of meditation. Furthermore, there are two [types of] visualizations: one [where the object] is newly created by the mind and one [where the object] is [as if] naturally present. The latter [type] has special qual ities with respect to  the generation of 
faith and is consistent with [relevant] sect ions from the general vehicles. Therefore, 
you should [visualize] in the latter way.  
 The body of the Buddha should be visualized as  being very beautiful and golden in color. A 
bodhisattva who sets his or her mind on this object will be called “equipoised.”

722 E How to research the meditation object 
In research of a meditation object, a basi s for the mind to hold on to, you should 
first find a good painted image, statue, etc. of the Teacher’s body, look at it again 
and again and, with a [firm] grasp of the feat ures, get into a habit of projecting it as 
an object of the mind. Or else consider the meaning of what you heard a spiritual 
master say, make it appear in the mind an d pursue [this as] your meditation object. 
However, rather than making  the meditation object appear like a painted image, 
statue, etc., you should learn to make it appear in the form of a real buddha.  
 
One should find a good image of the Buddha, li ke the statue here behind me, and then form 
an image of him in one’s mind. One should im agine the Buddha as though abiding in front 
of oneself by mentally creating an image of the Buddha. However, one should not imagine t h e  B u d d h a  a s  b e i n g  l i k e  a  s t a t u e  o r  p a i n t i n g  o f  t h e  B u d d h a  b u t  a s  a n  a c t u a l  b u d d h a .  I n  short, one should first familiarize oneself with a statue or painting of the Buddha, but then 
when visualizing the Buddha the image should not be  that of a statue or painting but that of a 
real living Buddha. If one meditates in this w ay it will become more effective and one will 
receive the blessings of the buddhas.  Wednesday afternoon, 24 January 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page says: 
F It is unreasonable to practice meditative stabilization looking at the meditati on support with one’s 
eyes 
It makes perfect sense that Master Yeshe De disputes what some [people] do, i.e., 
set up an image vis-à-vis, look at it with the eyes, and meditate by staring at it. The 
reasons for this: meditative stabilization is not achieved in a sense consciousness 
but rather in the mental consciousness,  so the actual meditation object of 
meditative stabilization is a direct object ive basis of the mental consciousness, so 
that is what the mind has to hold on to. In accordance with what has been 
explained before it is taught that [the mi nd] has to focus on the meaning generality 
of the observed object or the appearance of the image.  
 
It is not correct that one takes an object of  meditation of the eyes in order to develop 
meditative stabilization. While some people s ay that one should place an image, such as a 
statue of the Buddha, in front of oneself, look  a t  i t  w it h  w i d e  o p e n  e y e s ,  a n d  m e d i t a t e  b y  
staring at it, this was refuted by Master Yeshe De. This is because meditative stabilization is 
not accomplished in a sense consciousness such  as the eye or ear consciousness, but is 
accomplished in a mental consciousness. Therefor e, the actual or direct observed object of 
meditative stabilization is the direct object of  a mental consciousness. In other words, the 
mind must apprehend such an object, not a ph ysical object. As was explained before, one 
observes a meaning generality of the actual obse rved object or its reflection, not an object 
apprehended by the eye or ear consciousness. For example, one can visualize the Buddha by 
means of a mental image like as if the actual  Buddha were abiding in front of oneself. 
Alternatively, one can think of one’s mind as bein g like a mirror that reflects objects in it and 
visualize the Buddha in this way.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
723  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
G The need to determine just one meditation object in the beginning  
E l s e w h e r e  i t  s a y s  t h a t  p a r t  o f  t h e  f i g u r e  i s  d e t a i l e d  a n d  p a r t  o f  i t  i s  r o u g h ,  
nevertheless you first need to focus on  the rough [outlines] and once [the 
meditation has] stabilized with respect to  that you focus further on the details. 
{255} According to experience rough [featu res] appear very easily so you should 
progress from the image’s rough[er features] making them the support for 
meditation. In particular, if you practice while a fully qualified calm abiding has not come about, it is inappropriate to move between many different meditation 
objects, for, if you develop meditative  stabilization, moving between many 
dissimilar meditation objects becomes a grea t obstacle to the achievement of calm 
abiding.  
 
At the beginning it is necessary to ascertain ju st one single observed object. Some texts say 
that when one first meditates on the body of the Buddha, although one part of the body is 
subtle and one part coarse, at first one should focus on the rough aspect s and only when this 
has become stable should one focu s on the subtle aspects. For ex ample, first one visualizes the 
ushnisha of the Buddha roughly and then graduall y one visualizes it in more detail or more 
subtlety. In this way, one should try to make th e aspect of the ushnisha appear clearly to one’s 
mind. 
 Because it is easier for the coarse or rough aspect to appear to the mind, initially this 
should be taken as the observed object of one’s  meditation. If one wishes to achieve a fully-
qualified calm abiding one must take one sing le object as one’s observed object, without 
changing between many different objects by, for example, one day meditating on ugliness, the 
next day on love, and the next day on dependent- arising. If one does th is, one will not be able 
to attain calm abiding. Therefore, it is nece ssary to choose one object and meditate on it 
single-pointedly. If one moves from one observed  object to another this will become a great 
interference to achieving calm abiding.   
Says Ashvagosha
182: 
 
Relying on one meditation object stabilize the mind’s reflections. Through a series of many objects It will get disturbed by mental afflictions. 
 Ashvagosha says that one should choose one obje ct and stabilize the mind on it, whereas if 
one continually changes objects one’s mind will be disturbed by afflictions. Because of this, it 
is necessary to choose one single object right fr om the beginning and not change it until calm 
abiding is achieved.

724 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
And in the Lamp on the Path :  
 
On any one meditation object – 
let the mind rest on what is wholesome. 
 
[The point] is being made by means of the specifying word “one”.  
 
Atisha’s Lamp on the Path  also says that one should take on e observed object and set the mind 
on that virtuous object. This is emphasized by explicitly saying “one.” When ordinary beings, 
for example, find a job, they do it for a short time and then give it up and look for another 
one, and then do the same again until eventual ly it becomes impossible for them to find a 
stable job with a good salary. Also there are pe ople who constantly change their friends, going 
from one to another, whereupon in the end th ey remain alone and are sad and lonely. Thus, 
we can see that even in the world it is  not good to constantly change things. 
 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
H The criterion for finding the meditation object and, upon finding it, the need for mentally holding on to 
that very object  
The criterion for thus initially finding the me ditation object to hold on to with the 
mind is this: If you visualize the head, (two ) arms, torso, and (two) legs a couple of 
times successively and at the end of that yo u are able to have half the major limbs 
appear in the mind as you consider the ge neral [features of the] body, you should 
be satisfied with that and hold on to it with the mind even if there is no radiant 
clarity. That is because if you do not ho ld on to it, satisfied with as much, but 
rather fancy more clarity, visualizing ag ain and again, the object will become 
clearer, however, not only will you fail to acquire stable meditative stabilization, it 
will become an obstacle to its acquisition.  But if the object is not very clear and 
you still hold on to just this partial object you will acquire meditative stabilization 
quickly and, as clarity will then be enha nced, the clarity aspect will also come 
about easily. This appears from Master Ye she De’s instructions and is of great 
importance.  
 
If one takes the body of the Buddha as one’s ob served object, one should initially visualize the 
head, the arms, the body, and the legs, trying to gradually make the visualization more and 
more clear. However, at the end one should ju st visualize the main limbs – the head, arms, 
and legs – as much as one can and meditate on th at. In other words, it is not necessary to be 
able to visualize the entire body with great cl arity and to strive again and again to make it 
more and more clear. Rather on e should visualize the body in general and then focus on that 
visualization. If instead one keeps striving to make the visualization more and more clear, one 
will not be able to achieve meditative stabilizat ion and this will even become an interference 
to achieving stable meditative stabilization. Thus , even if the entire object is not clear, one 
should focus on just part of the object, whereby one will quickly achieve meditative 
stabilization. Having achieved meditative stabil ization, one should then strive to make the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
725 visualization clearer, whereupon this will quickl y happen. This was taught by Yeshe De and is 
very important.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 That is to say you should {256} mentally hold on to the figure as a whole to the 
best of your abilities and if some parts of  it appear clearly you should hold on to 
those. If they get unclear again you sh ould hold on to the whole again.  
 
In order to develop meditative stabilization on e should observe the body of the Buddha in 
general and, if some parts of it appear clearly, one should apprehend them. If they become unclear, one should visualize the body in gene ral once again, rather than focusing on any 
particular part.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 At that time, if something appears that has not been specified in terms of colour, 
shape, number, and size – you want to  meditate on [something] yellow and 
[something] red appears and the like, or you want to meditate on a sitting figure and a standing one etc. appears, or you want to meditate on one and two appear 
etc., or, for example, you want to medita te on something big and something small 
appears – it is not appropriate to pursue them, so you should only make that which is the original meditation object the support for meditation. 
 While the body of the Buddha is go lden in color, if instead of th e color gold it appears to be 
red like Buddha Amitabha or Amitayus, one shou ld not follow that change and instead make 
a n  e f f o r t  t o  v i s u a l i z e  t h e  b o d y  a s  g o l d  i n  c o l o r .  L i k e w i s e ,  i f  o n e  w a n t s  t o  m e d i t a t e  o n  t h e  
Buddha in the sitting position but instead he appears standing, one should not follow that 
visualization. Similarly, if one wants to medita te on one but two arise to the mind, one should 
not follow it. Also if one wants to visualize a very big body but a small body appears to the 
mind, one should not follow it. One should always  take the original observed object as one’s 
meditation object without changing its color, sh ape, size, and so forth. In short, it should 
always remain the same. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 In the context of performing the deity yo g a  o f  s e c r e t  m a n t r a  i t  i s  n e c e s s a r y  t o  
achieve a clear appearance of the deitie s and therefore various means should be 
used to produce it. However, here, if you have difficulty making the deity’s aspect 
appear, you may place the mind and focus on any one of the objects presented 
above that is suitable or the view ascert aining suchness and accomplish meditative 
stabilization, for the main purpose is to accomplish calm abiding.  
 On the occasion of accomplishing the deity yoga of secret mantra, it is necessary to achieve a 
clear appearance of the deity. Therefore, if it does not arise, one should apply different 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
726 methods to make it clear. For example, wishin g to visualize Green Tara, one should try to 
make her aspect appear clearly to one’s mind . One should do the same whatever deity one 
chooses such as Manjushri or Avalokiteshvara.  This is important. However, if one has 
difficulty making the aspect of the deity appear cl early to one’s mind, it is better to focus on 
any one of the observed objects of meditative  stabilization that were mentioned before. 
Alternatively one can meditate on the view of suchness or emptiness, focusing the mind on 
this in order to achieve meditative stabilizat ion. Since these meditations are done for the 
purpose of accomplishing calm abiding, as lo ng as one has not attained calm abiding one 
should put effort into meditating on one’s object. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 256) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B How to place the mind on it 
1 Presentation of the faultless approach 
2 Eliminating faulty approaches 
3 Indicating the measure of sessions 
The second one has three points: pres entation of the fa ultless approach, 
eliminating faulty approaches and in dicating the measure of sessions. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1 Pres entation of the faultless approach 
A Indication that faultle ss meditative stabilizati on has two distinctions 
B How it is unreasonable that some [people] add bliss and the like, making it three or four distinctions 
C Identification of sinking and excitement and the importance of skill in a method of sustaining 
concentration that puts and end to the two 
D How both mindfulness and introspection are necessary as means for the mind not to wander off from its object 
E How the mind is focused on the meditation object through mindfulness 
F Identification of mindfulness  G How it is shown through an analogy that mindfulness continuously ties the mind to the meditation 
object 
H How concentration will turn out faulty without a firm mode of apprehension of the ascertaining consciousness I Even someone wishing to achieve calm abiding by placing the mind without thinking of anything 
needs to develop the power of mindfulness  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1A  Indication that faultless me ditative stabilization has two 
distinctions 
First: The meditative stabilization that is to be accomplished here has two 
distinctions, i.e., an aspect of intense clarity, the mind being extremely clear, and 
an aspect of non-conceptual stability, [the mind] abiding single-pointedly with the 
meditation object.  
 
The faultless meditative stabilization that is to  be accomplished should have two distinctions 
or qualities:  
1. it should have the intense clarity of a very clear mind, and 
2. it should abide single-pointedly on its observed object without conceptuality.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1B How it is unreasonable that some [people] add bliss and the like, 
making it three or four distinctions 
Here some add bliss and make it three [distinctions], others add limpidity and 
make it four. However, as for limpidity there are two [aspects]: the limpidity of consciousness is felt to be more limpid than even an immaculate crystal bowl full 
of immaculate water being hit by sunlig ht in the absence of clouds. And {257} 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
727 when the aspects of objects like pillars appear, a limpidity emerges that is felt to 
allow one to count even their finest part icles. These two nevertheless also emerge 
from the intensity of the clarity aspect being generated and sustained after subtle 
sinking has been cut. Therefore it is unne cessary to mention them separately in the 
beginning.  
 
Some people say that this meditative stabilizatio n should also have a third distinction of being 
blissful and even a fourth distinction of be ing limpid in addition to the previous two 
distinctions of clarity and single-pointedness. Th ere are two types of limpidity, one of which is 
from the side of the subject or object-possessor which is likened to a crystal bowl filled with 
water and struck by sunlight. The limpidity from th e side of the object is that when a pillar or 
vase appears, it is so clear that one is able to  count even its subtlest particles. However, these 
two are also present when subtle sinking or laxi ty is eliminated. Because of this, it is not 
necessary to make any extra distinctions in that  when one strives to generate calm abiding one 
strives to eliminate subtle laxity.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Although joy and bliss in the form of pl easurable sensations arise as a result of 
the meditative stabilization to be accomplished here, they do not arise in parallel 
to the meditative stabilization belongin g to the preliminary stage of the first 
concentration and therefore are not listed here.  
 Although joy and bliss having the aspect of pleasurable feeling arise as a result of the 
meditative stabilization that is to be accompli shed on this occasion, when calm abiding is 
initially generated it is as a preparation for th e first concentration of which joy and bliss are 
not branches.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The emergence of intensity in the clarity aspect is impeded by sinking, and single-
pointed non-conceptuality [brought to bear  on the meditation object] is impeded 
by excitement. Therefore, these two, sink ing and excitement, are the very reason 
for the main obstacles to pure meditative stabilization coming [into being].  
 
The arisal of clarity is impeded by sinking or laxity, and single-pointed non-conceptuality is 
impeded by excitement. Therefore, the main ob stacles to achieving calm abiding are laxity 
and excitement, which need to be eliminated.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1C Identification of sinking and excitement and the importance of skill 
in a method of sustaining meditative st abilization that puts and end to the two 
Consequently, if you do not know a pure method for identifying subtle and gross 
sinking and excitement [respectively] and for sustaining meditative stabilization 
that puts an end to both of them, even  calm abiding – not to mention special 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
728 insight – cannot develop. Therefore the intelligent striving for meditative 
stabilization should become skilled in this  fashion. That is to say, sinking and 
excitement are adverse conditions to the accomplishment of calm abiding and 
since the identification of adverse conditions as well as the actual methods for 
putting and end to them are taught below, here, mention should [only] be made of 
how to generate meditative stabilization [as] a favourable condition to calm 
abiding.  
 It is necessary to identify both subtle and co arse laxity and exciteme nt and then cease these 
obstacles. If, on the other hand , one does not know how to identify subtle and coarse laxity 
and excitement and how to cease them, it will be  difficult to develop calm abiding. Therefore, 
the intelligent who strive for meditative stabiliz ation should become skilled in these. Because 
laxity and excitement are adverse conditions to  the accomplishment of calm abiding, how to 
recognize and eliminate them will be taught belo w, whereas here it is only explained how to 
generate the meditative stabilization that is a favorable condition to the accomplishment of 
calm abiding.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1D How both mindfulness and introspection are necessary as means 
for the mind not to wander off from its object  
Here meditative stabilization is the aspect of the mind one-pointedly abiding with 
the meditation object, that is to say it ha s to stay with the object continuously. For 
that there are two requirements: a means of making [sure] the mind does not 
wander away from its original object and an awareness of exactly what distraction 
or non-distraction there is. The first on e is mindfulness and the second one is 
introspection. In Commentary on the Ornament of the Sutras  is says:  
 
Mindfulness and introspection cause {258} complete focusing [of the 
mind], for the former causes the mind  not to stray away from its object 
and the latter is fully aware of the mind straying away. 
 In order to develop meditative stabilization bo th mindfulness and introspection are necessary. 
Meditative stabilization is the part of the mind  abiding single-pointedly on its object. That 
which ensures that the mind does not wander aw ay from its original object is mindfulness, 
whereas that which knows whether the mind is distracted or not is introspection. That these 
two are important is mentioned in the Commentary on the Ornament of the Sutras  which says 
that mindfulness and introspection make the mind focus closely because mindfulness makes the mind not stray from its object and in trospection knows when the mind strays. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
If you forget the meditation object, mindfulness having deteriorated, there is 
distraction and you will lose the object immediately. Therefore mindfulness that 
does not forget the object is the root [of meditative stabilization]. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
729 If one’s mindfulness degenerates one will forget  the observed object and the mind will wander 
away, completely losing the observed object. Th erefore, one should try never to forget the 
object by means of mindfulness. For example, even when writing a letter it is necessary to 
have mindfulness as otherwise one will make many mistakes.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1E How the mind is focused on the meditation object through 
mindfulness 
How to completely focus the mind on it s meditation object: You visualize the 
meditation object as explained above. Th en, if at least something appears, you 
s h o u l d  d e v e l o p  a  f o r c e f u l  m a n n e r  o f  a p p r e h e n d i n g  t h a t  [ w h i c h  i s  b e i n g  
apprehended] with your awareness, uplift the mind and place it without newly 
investigating anything. 
 One should focus the mind on its observed obje ct by visualizing one of the observed objects 
mentioned above. One should try to visualize it clearly and whatever appears, one should 
apprehend it mentally in a forceful manner, uplift the mind, and without investigating any 
new object set the mind on its original object.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1F Identification of mindfulness 
1 The source identifying the essence of mindfulness 
2 The distinctive features of mindfulness 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1F1 The source identifying the essence of mindfulness 
In the Compendium of All [Knowledge]  is says: 
 
What is mindfulness? The mind being active with regard to the thing it 
is familiar with, without forgetfulness nor the least distraction. 
 
That is to say it has three distinctive features. 
 
The scriptural source that identifies  the entity of mindfulness is the Compendium of All 
Knowledge . This text says that mindfulness is: (1) a non-forgetting of the mind (2) of a thing 
that is familiar (3) that has the action of be ing completely undistracted. In other words, one 
should choose one of the five observed objects,  then familiarize with it, not forget it, and 
remain undistracted by conceptualizations . This is the entity of mindfulness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1F2 The distinctive features of mindfulness 
A The object feature 
The distinctive feature of the object: As mindfulness does not come about with 
regard to a hitherto unfamiliar object it says : “the thing it is familiar with”. In this 
context [this is] the appearance of the me ditation object having been determined 
before. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
730  
If one is not familiar with an object it is difficult to be mindful of it, therefore one should take something that one is familiar with as an  object. For example, if one takes the body of 
the Buddha as one’s object, one should have ascert ained it previously in order for it to clearly 
appear to the mind. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B The feature of the mode of apprehension 
The distinctive feature of the mode of a pprehension: In this context, the aspect 
that the mind does not forget the object , “the mind ... without forgetfulness” 
implies that it does not forget the medita tion object. As for the manner in which it 
does not forget – the implication is not only  that if others ask and if you are made 
to think about it you can remember your master teaching “the meditation object is 
like this...”, but rather that once the mi nd is tied to the meditation object and 
immediately mindful of it, it does not get distracted even a little. If it does get 
distracted, mindfulness is lost through no thing but the occurrence of distraction. 
 One should not forget the observed object of one’s  meditative stabilization. It is not just that 
if others ask what is one’s observed object and one is made to think about it, one remembers one’s teacher explaining it. Rather it is that the mind is tied to its object, like a ring through 
the nose of a cow, whereupon one does not get distracted from it even slightly. If, on the 
other hand, one’s mind becomes distracted, mindfulness is lost. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Hence as soon as you have placed the mind on the meditation object you should 
give rise to the thought: “That is how I am  tied to the meditation object” and then, 
without renewed conceptual { 259} thought, withou t interrupting the force of this 
very awareness maintain it continuously . That is the essential point about how 
mindfulness is cultivated. 
 
Therefore, having set the mind on the observed  object, one should think “Now I am tied to 
this observed object,” and without any new conc eptuality one should maintain this awareness 
forcefully and continuously. This is the essential point with respect to re lying on mindfulness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C The feature of function 
Not allowing the mind to stray away from the meditation object to something else. 
 
One should not allow one’s mind to be distract ed from one observed object to another but 
should abide on it. 
 
As I said before, our mind is like a crazy elepha nt. Next this analogy is explained in terms of 
how an elephant keeper tames an el ephant with hooks and beatings.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
731  
Thursday morning, 25 January 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 259) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1G How it is shown through an analogy that mindfulness continuously 
ties the mind to the meditation object 
That being so, tying the mind to a meditation object and taming it is taught 
through the analogy of taming an elephant. In [this] analogy, a wild elephant is 
tied to a very firm tree [trunk] or pole with numerous ropes and if it follows the 
instructions of the elephant keeper – all the better; if it does not, it is repeatedly 
punched with a sharp hook and tamed by subjugation. Likewise the mind, 
resembling an untamed elephant, is tied to a firm pillar – the meditation object 
explained earlier – with the rope of mindfu lness and if it cannot stay, it is pierced 
with the hook of introspection and gradually brought under control.  
 How is an elephant subdued? It is subdued by ty ing it to a strong post, pillar, or tree, whereas 
if it is difficult to tame the el ephant keeper also uses sharp hook s to subdue it. Similarly, one’s 
mind, which is like a crazy elephant, needs to be  subdued by tying it to its observed object 
with the rope of mindfulness. In other words,  mindfulness is what keeps the mind on its 
observed object. Then, if the mind wanders, it must be caught with the hook of introspection 
and brought back to its object.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In Essence of the Middle Way  it says:  
 
The elephant of the mind going wrong 
is surely tied with the rope of mindfulness to the sturdy post of the object and slowly controlled with the hook of insight. 
 This text says that the mind, whic h is like an elephant that goes  the wrong way, should be tied 
to its observed object with the rope of mindfulness, and, if it wanders off, it is to be brought under control with the hook of wisd om, that is, with introspection.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
And in the Middle  Stages of Meditation : 
 
By means of the ropes of mindfulne ss and introspection the elephant of 
the mind should be fastened to that very tree trunk of the [meditation] 
object. 
 Similarly, Kamalashila’s text says that by mean s of the ropes of mindfulness and introspection 
the elephant-like mind is tied to the tree trunk of the observed object.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
732  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2 A2B-1H How meditative st abilization will turn out faulty without a firm 
mode of apprehension of the ascertaining consciousness 
[In the teachings] it says that meditative stabilization is accomp lished on the basis 
of mindfulness and that – like a rope – mi ndfulness continuously ties the mind to 
the meditation object. Therefore the princi pal method for sustaining the [gradual] 
accomplishment of meditative stabilizatio n is that of sustaining mindfulness. 
Again mindfulness has a mode of apprehensi on with an aspect of certainty, so 
when [it comes to] sustaining meditative stabilization, as you place [the mind] 
without a firm mode of apprehension  [on the part] of the ascertaining 
consciousness the clarity aspect of mental limpidity may come [about], however, 
the clarity aspect of stark intensity of the ascertaining consciousness does not 
emerge so that powerful mindfulness will not be generated. As subtle sinking does 
not get stopped, the meditative stabilization will become faulty. 
 Without the firm mind holding of its object, faul ts of meditative stabilization will arise. On 
the other hand, by relying on mindfulness medita tive stabilization is achieved. Mindfulness is 
like a rope that ties the mind to its observed  object. Therefore, the method for training in 
meditative stabilization is to guard mindfuln ess. Because mindfulness has the aspect of 
apprehending its object very strongly, if a st r o n g  m o d e  o f  a p p r e h e n s i o n  i s  a b s e n t  w h e n  
training in meditative stabilization or single-p ointed meditation, although there may be clarity
