it will not be strong and the object will quickly be lost, whereby one will be unable to generate strong mindfulness. Since one is unable to el iminate or cease subtle laxity, the meditative 
stabilization will become faulty, that is, imperfect.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-1I Even someone wishing to achieve calm abiding by placing the 
mind without thinking of anything needs to develop the power of mindfulness 
{260} Even someone who sustains mere non-conceptuality without placing the 
mind on any other meditation object such  as a deity’s body reminds himself that 
“The mind should be placed on non-conceptuality with regard to any object 
whatsoever” and should then undistractedly  prevent the mind from scattering. As 
non-distraction is also identical in mean ing with mindfulness not forgetting the 
meditation object, it does not depart from the method of sustaining mindfulness. 
Therefore someone meditating like that also  cultivates mindfulness giving rise to 
the power of ascertainment. 
 Someone who wishes to achieve calm abiding wi thout conceptualizing anything still needs to 
develop the strength of mindfulness. Therefore,  even if one does not place the mind on the 
body of the deity and so forth but cultivates mere non-conceptuality, one must remember “I will place my mind on an object single poin tedly without conceptuality” and prevent one’s 
mind from scattering to anything else. Sinc e “distraction” means that one has lost or 
forgotten one’s original observed object, non-di straction means that one has not forgotten the 
original observed object and is therefore identical to mindfulness. Non-distraction is therefore 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
733 never separated from mindfulness. Because of this, someone who meditates in this way also 
cultivates the mindfulness that gives rise to th e strength of an ascertaining consciousness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-2  Eliminating faulty approaches 
A Identification of a misconception that should be eliminated 
Second: There is this kind of misconception that should be eliminated: “If the 
mind is roused as explained above, held tight and placed [on the meditation object] non-conceptually, there is no si nking, however, [instead] excitement 
predominates, you realize you do not become capable of [achieving] persistent 
stability and your lofty awareness declines. If you loosen your tight grip a lot, you 
will find that stability quickly develops – th is is an excellent method.” With this in 
mind a lot of people also hold and expoun d: “Best relaxation is best meditation.” 
 There is a wrong conception that must be eliminated. What is th is wrong conception? It is the 
thought “If the mind is uplifted, held tight, an d placed non-conceptually on its object, sinking 
or laxity will not arise, whereas there is danger of excitement arising. If excitement arises, I 
will not be able to continually observe the object  as my mind will wander to another object.” 
Excitement occurs when, while focusing on an  o b s e r v e d  o b j e c t  s u c h  a s  t h e  f a c e  o f  t h e  
Buddha, the mind wanders and the face of one’s  lover or friend appears. The person here 
therefore thinks “If I loosen my hold on the observed object, my mind will quickly come to 
abide on its object, therefore this is the best me thod.” In other words, this person thinks that 
one should not hold the object tightly but leav e the mind in a relaxed or loose state, this 
being the best meditation. However, this asse rtion is not correct and must be refuted. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B Its elimination  
However, this [position] does not draw  a distinction between the occurrence of 
sinking and the occurrence of meditation, because, as explained earlier, faultless 
meditative stabilization necessarily has two distinctive features – the stability aspect 
of the non-conceptual mind by itself is not enough. If you think that “In such a 
case, if the mind has gone confused and clou dy, it is sinking whereas, otherwise, it 
is faultless meditative stabilization, beca use there is the clarity aspect of mental 
limpidity”, apparently, you do not distinguish between dullness and sinking, so 
these will explained below. 
 
The previous wrong conception is now eliminated saying that this person does not distinguish 
between the arisal of sinking or laxity and the arisal of meditation. The achievement of 
faultless meditative stabilization necessarily  has the two distinctions mentioned before: 
abiding on the object single-pointedly and wi th clarity, by the power of mindfulness and 
introspection. It is not enough that the mi nd that one part of the mind abides non-
conceptually. If someone thinks “If such a non-conceptual mind becomes clouded with tiredness, this is sinking or laxity, but it is st ill a faultless meditative stabilization because the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
734 mind is clear,” he does not distinguish between dullness and sinking. Ther efore, these will be 
explained below.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 260) says: 
C How it is difficult for faultless meditative stabilization with the two distinctive features to arise 
Consequently, if the awareness has been in tensified and it is being held overly 
tight, the clarity aspect may be present, however, excitement predominates making 
it difficult for the stability aspect to develop. If you relax it a lot while you practice, 
the stability aspect may be there, but si nking predominates and therefore intense 
clarity will be absent. Still, it is difficult to get the right amounts of tightness and 
relaxation balanced, and therefore it is {2 61} difficult for meditative stabilization 
free from sinking and excitement to arise.  
 It is difficult to generate faultless meditative  stabilization possessing the two distinctions. If 
one holds the observed object too strongly, even though the object may be clear there is a risk 
that the mind will not abide on it due to the arisal of exciteme nt as this makes the mind leave 
its object. If, on the other hand, one relaxes to o much, even though the mind abides on its 
object, there is a risk that sinking will arise and clarity lost. Therefore, since it is difficult to 
not hold the object either too tightly or too loosely, it is difficult for a meditative stabilization 
that is free from sinking and excitement to arise. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
With that in mind Chandragomin says: 
 
If I make an effort excitement comes up, if I abandon it dullness is produced. If it’s [so] hard to see and find their balance what am I – confused in mind – to do? 
 And: 
 
If I engage with effort excitement comes up, 
if I relax it dullness is produced. If it’s [so] hard to find the mean between them, what am I – confused in mind – to do? 
 Chandragomin says that if one makes strong effo rt to hold the observed object, there is the 
risk that excitement will arise. If, on the ot her hand, one abandons this effort, the mind will 
become dull, that is, laxity will arise. For this reason, it is difficult to engage the object in a balanced way. So what can one whose mind is disturbed do? In short, it is difficult for one with a disturbed mind to remain in equipoise  as the mind is disturbed by laxity and 
excitement.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
735  Again Chandragomin says that if one puts  effort into holding the observed object, 
excitement will arises and if one gives this effo rt up one will not be able to hold the observed 
object. Since the middle way in wh ich effort is neither too strong  nor too little is difficult to 
find, what can one with a confused mind do? 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[That is to say] if you try hard to concentr ate, making a [real] effort, there will be 
excitement, but if, seeing that, you relax the mind trying hard to engage [its object] 
and give up the zeal, sinking associat ed with the mind turning dull and 
[withdrawing] inward will come about. Therefore it is difficult to find a middle 
[ground] free from the two extremes of sinking and excitement – an equanimity where the mind abides evenly balanced. If the statement were to present relaxation 
as best, there would be no reason whatsoever  for difficulties and, as it is being said 
that sinking arises from it, it would be  unreasonable to cultivate meditative 
stabilization that way.  
 If one makes very strong effort, excitement will arise. If seeing this, one gives up the effort 
completely, then the mind will become dull or weak, and sinking or laxity will arise. Because 
of this, it is difficult to find a middle way free  from the extremes of la xity and excitement in 
which the mind abides in a balanced way. If givi ng up effort were the best, there would be no 
reason for difficulties. However, since laxity or sinking arises from giving up effort, it is not 
suitable to accomplish meditati ve stabilization in this way. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D How to meditate balancing medita tive stabilization and relaxation  
As for the point where meditative stabiliz ation and relaxation are [well] balanced: 
having investigated yourself you should be more relaxed than that level of 
awareness where your think: “If I heighten it just a bit, excitement will surely 
arise,” and you should also raise it above that level where you think: “If I leave it 
just a bit, sinking will surely arise”. 
 
One should engage in meditation neither too ti ghtly or too loosely. When meditating on an 
image of the Buddha, through thinking “If I hold it too tightly, excitement will arise” and “If 
I give up making effort, laxity will arise,” on e  s h o u l d  n o t  l i f t  o n e ’ s  m i n d  u p  t o o  h i g h  n o r  
dampen it down too much, but inst ead try to find the middle way.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
E Presentation of sources for this  
In the context of the first and second [ste p of stabilizing the] mind, Noble Asanga 
also taught: 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
736 That is to say in placing [the mind ] and thoroughly placing [the mind] 
there is mental activity that engages [its object] with meditative 
stabilization.”183 
 
On the occasion of the first two mental abidings , setting and perfectly or continually setting, 
from among the nine mental abidings, one need s the mental attention of forceful engaging. 
This refers to the fact that there are four mental attentions, five powers, and nine mental 
abidings.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In the First Stages of Meditation  it is also explained that:  
 
Sinking is eliminated and the meditation object is grasped tightly. 
 Kamalashila’s First Stages of Meditation  says that one should first eliminate laxity and then hold 
the observed object tightly. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
F The fault of not knowing how to apply mindfulness 
If you practice without knowing the ab ove method of applying mindfulness – 
however much you meditate – only lots of faults will come out: great forgetfulness, 
your intelligence discriminating phenomena growing dull and so forth. 
 
If one does not know the method mentioned previously for applying mindfulness to the observed object, no matter how much one strives to meditate on the object many faults will 
arise, such as great forgetfulness with respect to the object as well as the instructions on calm 
abiding, and also one’s wisdom differentiating  phenomena becoming dull. Therefore, it is 
necessary to perfectly apply mindfulness, wherea s if mindfulness is absent forgetfulness will 
arise and one’s mind will become duller.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
G The need to stand guard with introspection while grasping the meditation object with mindfulness 
{262} One may wonder: “Well then, is it  appropriate to generate conceptual 
thought observing whether or not the medi tation object is grasped well while the 
mind is being tied to the object through mindfulness?”. In the Middle  Stages of 
Meditation it says that this is what needs to be done. Specifically, it is not an 
observation such that you first abandon me ditative stabilization, but rather you 
merely observe from within the lasting state of meditative stabilization whether or 
not the mind is still abiding on the original  meditation object and, if it does not, 
                                              
183 The process of developing calm abiding is divided up into of nine stages associ ated with four types of 
attention. The first two stages, known as "placement " and "thorough placement" (often referred to as 
"continuous placement"), are associated with  the concentrated attention mentioned here. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
737 whether it has become excited or subject to sinking. Once you have established 
meditative stabilization you should do th is now and then, neither at too short nor 
at too long [intervals]. If you do this wh ile the force of the previous awareness has 
not quite run out yet, this will have the [dual] purpose that the intensity of the 
awareness which has [newly] arisen will last  longer and that sinking and excitement 
are recognized quickly. 
 
While holding the observed object with mind fulness, one needs to apply introspection. 
Someone wonders: “While mindfulness is tying the mind to its object, is it appropriate to 
investigate whether or not the mind is holding its object?” In fact it is important to do this. In 
other words, even while mindfulness is holding its object perfectly, on e should examine this 
from time to time. It is not that to do this one abandons meditative stabilization, but rather 
that from within meditative stabilization on e examines whether or not the mind is still 
abiding on its original observed object. If one finds that the mind is no longer abiding on its 
original observed object, one should examine wh ether laxity or excitement has arisen. Having 
accomplished meditative stabilization, one should examine in this way from time to time, but 
neither at too long or too short intervals. If on e does this while the forc e of the previous mind 
has not yet been exhausted, one will quickly re cognize whether laxity or excitement have 
arisen. In short, when meditating, one should  examine in this way from time to time. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
H The need to practice again and again with mindfulness so as not to forget the meditation object  
It is necessary to engage in the practice that way, by just recalling and recalling the 
initial meditation object every so of ten – with powerful mindfulness and 
continuously. That is why this mode of sustaining mindfulness is set out in the 
Shravaka Levels  and why it says in Explanations on the Discrimination of the Middle and 
the Extremes :  
 
As for the statement ‘Mindfulness consists in not forgetting the 
meditation object’ – the instruct ion to make the mind abide is 
expressed through the mind. 
 One must remember the original object from ti me to time by applying strong and continual 
mindfulness. The way to develop meditative stabil ization by means of mindfulness is taught in 
the Hearer Grounds  and Explanations on the Discrimination  of the Middle and the Extremes . The 
latter text says that mindfulness is a non-forg etting of the observed object. Therefore, one 
should instruct the mind to abide on its object.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
The application of mindfulness has the purp ose of preventing one from forgetting 
the meditation object by straying away  from it. Therefore not forgetting the 
meditation object consists in expressing the object mentally, attending to it again 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
738 and again. It is like when , for instance, you are afraid that you might forget 
something you know and recall it again an d again – you will hardly forget it.  
 
Reliance on mindfulness prevents the mind from straying from the observed object and 
forgetting it. To not forget the observed object  is to continually set the mind on its object 
without giving it up and without changing to anothe r object. It is like the fact that in order to 
remember something one knows, one must strive to remember it again and again so as to not 
forget it. For example, if one wishes to memori ze a text, one needs to recite it each day in 
order to not forget it. If, on the other hand, on e memorizes a text but then does not recite it 
regularly, it will soon be forgotten. In a simi lar way, one should recall the observed object of 
meditation again and again. 
 You should try every day to meditate on an obje ct. It is not useful to meditate one day and 
then give it up, and then a few weeks later meditate on it again. By doing this, one’s 
meditation will not improve. Instead one will beco me tired and think that since meditation is 
not useful it is better to give it up and go da ncing, watch a movie, go  car racing, motorcycle 
racing, or bike racing, and so forth. This ca n happen. There are people who first think “I 
want to do this” but then suddenly later on gi ve everything up and become a homeless beggar, 
thinking that it is better not to have any resp onsibilities. However, later on other difficulties 
arise for them. There exist many different types of  mentalities because in our previous lives we 
familiarized with the afflictions and also fami liarized with meditation, virtuous actions, 
Buddhist philosophy or other philosophies. Due to our previous familiarity, we think that it is 
best to follow whatever is easiest for us. For th is reason we need contin uous joyous effort as 
well as mindfulness. You should take one single object as your meditation object, without changing it, and then meditate on it trying to a ttain calm abiding. Although this is not easy, if 
we make effort we can achieve it and then we wi ll even be able to fly without the need to pay 
for an airplane! I am joking, even birds and mosquitoes can fly, it does not require any 
realization! However, if human beings who, having  studied and meditated, become able to fly, 
this shows that they have a ttained some realizations.  
 Thursday afternoon, 25 January 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 262) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A2B-3 The length of sessions 
A How it is taught in Stages of Meditation   
Third: Now, if you wonder whether there is a specified measure [of time] for tying 
the mind to the meditation object [so one co uld] say: ‘place it just this long’ – the 
major sources such as Shravaka Levels  do not seem to maintain a clear length. 
However, {263} in the Last Stages of Meditation  it says:  
 
Like that, gradually, you should sit for 24 minutes184, one and a half 
hours185, three hours,186 or as long as you can. 
                                              
184 One "chu tshod", a unit based on traditiona l Indian time measurement and used in the Abhidharma . It is 
equivalent to one out of 60 parts of a day i.e., 24 minutes.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
739  
Apparently, this was set out in the contex t of the time measure for special insight 
meditation once calm abiding has been ac hieved, but to start with, in the context 
of practicing calm abiding it is obviously the same.  
 
Is there or is there not a certain length of time for which to tie the mind to its observed object 
by means of mindfulness? With re spect to this texts such as the Hearer Grounds , the 
Bodhisattva Grounds , and so forth do not clearly set out the length of sessions. However, 
Kamalashila’s Last Stages of Meditation  says that the length of a session should be one hour, or 
one sixth of a day (by dividing the day in to  six parts dawn, morning, mid-morning and so 
forth as mentioned in the Six Session Guru Yoga ), or for however long one can set the mind on 
its object. This text, however, does not refer to  the length of a sessio n when achieving calm 
abiding but to the length of time of a session of special insight having already achieved calm 
abiding. However, on the occasion of practicing calm abiding the length is similar. In short, 
one should do a session that corresponds wi th how long one can keep the mind on its 
observed object. When one can no longer keep the mind on its object, one should stop the 
meditation session.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B How it is difficult for beginners to cut through [mental] sinking and excitement if the sessions are long  
In fact the method of sustaining mindfu lness and introspection as explained above 
– if you recall the meditation object ever y so often and check every so often – is 
indeed faultless even for long sessions.  H o w e v e r ,  i n  m o s t  b e g i n n e r s  e i t h e r  
forgetfulness arises if the sessions are long  – they wander off distractedly and then, 
even though sinking and excitement occur, they only recognize it after a long time 
and otherwise do not identify them quickly – or even if they do not forget what 
they are mindful of, they easily fall un der the power of sinking and excitement 
without identifying them quic kly. The former of those two [problems] impedes the 
development of strong mindfulness and the latter impedes the development of 
strong introspection, so that it is di fficult to cut sinking and excitement. In 
particular, it is a lot worse to forget the meditation object, let the mind wander 
and then fail to identify the occurrence of sinking and excitement than not to 
identify the occurrence of sinking and ex citement quickly while still remembering 
the meditation object. Therefore,  as the antidote that stops187 a deterioration of 
mindfulness upon mental wandering, the way of sustaining mindfulness explained 
above is very important.  
 It is difficult for beginners to cut laxity and ex citement if the sessions are too long. If the way 
of cultivating the mindfulness that abides  on its object without wandering and the 
introspection that examines whether laxity or excitement has arisen is that one recalls the 
                                                                                                                                                     
 
185 "half a night watch" 
186 "one night watch" – common measur e for a full meditation session  
187 JN: “the means stopping” changed to” as the antidote that stops” (‘ gog pa’i gnyen por ) 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
740 observed object from time to time, the session wi ll be faultless. However, for most beginners if 
the session is too long forgetfu lness arises and the mind wanders, losing its observed object. 
At that time it is difficult to recognize that laxity and excitement has arisen and one does not 
recognize their arisal for a long time. In other words, a beginner is unable to quickly recognize 
that laxity or excitement has arisen. And even if  one does not forget the object one is mindful 
of, one easily comes under the power of laxi ty and excitement and they are not quickly 
identified. Laxity interferes with the develo pment of strong mindfulness, and excitement 
interferes with the development of strong intros pection. Because of this, it is difficult to 
eliminate laxity and excitement.  
 In particular, to forget the observed object or  the instructions, to allow the mind to wander 
and become distracted, and fail to recognize the arisal of laxity and excitement is worse than 
not forgetting the object but allowing laxity an d excitement to arise without identifying them 
quickly. Therefore, one needs to eliminate the degeneration of mindfulness by applying the 
antidote through recalling the obse rved object from time to time. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C The need to proceed in accordance with one’s mental abilities and the like 
If forgetfulness straying here and ther e is great and if introspection quickly 
identifying sinking and excitement is weak, short sessions are needed. However, if 
forgetfulness hardly arises and it seems that sinking and excitement can be known 
quickly there is nothing wrong with slight ly longer sessions either. This is the 
intention behind the statement that the du ration – 24 minutes and so on – is not 
specified. In short, as it needs to be in a ccord with one’s mental abilities it says: “as 
long as you can”. 
 
One should proceed in accordance with one’s me ntal ability. Hence, if one’s mind wanders 
and has strong forgetfulness and if one is unable  to quickly identify laxity and excitement due 
to introspection being weak, one should do shor t sessions. If, on the other hand, forgetfulness 
rarely arises and laxity and excitement are quickl y recognized, there is no fault in doing longer 
sessions. This is why the duration of a session is not specified and can range from one hour to 
three hours and so forth. In short, because the length of the session sh ould accord with one’s 
mental abilities, it is said that it should be as long as possible. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Apart from that {264} rest in meditative equipoise unless you suffer some 
temporary physical or mental harm. If you do, you should not persist in your 
meditation but rather eliminate the consti tutional obstacles and then [continue to] 
meditate. That is the insight of experts, so you should understand this kind of 
activity to be another factor for the length of sessions.  
 Unless one suffers from physical or mental harm, such as bodily pain or mental unhappiness, one should remain in meditative equipoise. If, on the other hand, there arises pain in the 
body or unhappiness in the mind, one should not make effort in medita tion and instead stop 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
741 the session and strive to eliminate the harm. To meditate in this way is the intention of those 
who are skilled in meditation. In other words,  one should adapt the length of the session, 
meaning that if things are going well one should  make the session longer, whereas if there is 
harm to one’s body or mind one should stop  the session, eliminate the problem, and then 
return to meditation. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 264) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3 What to do once  the mind has been placed on the meditation 
object 
A What to do when excitement or sinking occur 
B What to do while free from sinking and excitement 
The third one has two points: what to do  when excitement or sinking occur and 
what to do while free from sinking and excitement. 
 
These points explain what to do when laxity an d excitement occur, and what to do when the 
mind is free from laxity and excitement. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A What to do when excitement or sinking occur 
1 Applying the remedy to non-identification of sinking and excitement 
2 Applying the remedy to a lack of effort at abandoning them even though they have been 
identified 
The first one has two points: applying the remedy to non-identification of sinking 
and excitement, and applying the remedy to  a lack of effort at abandoning them 
even though they have been identified.  
 
In order to identify laxity and excitement one needs to apply the antidote to their non-
identification. Also when there is a lack of e ffort at abandoning laxity and excitement one 
needs to apply the respective antidote.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-1 Applying the remedy to non-identification of sinking and 
excitement 
A Determining the characteristics of sinking and excitement 
B The means of generating introspection that realizes them at the time of meditation 
The first one has two points: determinin g the characteristics of sinking and 
excitement and the means of generating in trospection that realizes these at the 
time of meditation. 
 
First the definitions of laxity and excitement are settled, and then the way to generate the 
introspection that realizes that laxity has arisen  or that excitement has arisen when meditating 
is explained. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-1A Determining the characteristics of sinking and excitement 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
742 1 Determining the characteristics of excitement 
2 Determining the characteristics of sinking 
 
First the respective definitions of excitement and laxity are set out. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-1A1 Determining the characteristics of excitement 
A Presenting the source 
First: As for excitement, it is just like it says in the Compendium of Abhidharma :  
 
“What is excitement? It is a mental restlessness that pursues pleasant 
features and belongs to attachment. It  has the function to disrupt calm 
abiding. 
 
Excitement is defined in the Compendium of Manifest Dharma  (Abhidharmasamucchaya ) as: an 
unpacified mind included in attachment that fo llows pleasant signs that has the function of 
interfering with calm abiding. In other words, when an attractive object appears to the mind 
attachment arises, disturbing one’s ability to remain single pointedly on an object. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B Three particularities – its object, aspect, and function 
There are three [particularities] here: It s object is something attractive and 
pleasant. Its aspect is the mind being restle ss and moving outward. As it is part of 
attachment it gets involved in its objects through the aspect of craving. Its function 
is to disrupt the mind abiding on an object.  
 
Excitement has three distinctions: observed obje ct, aspect, and function. The observed object 
of excitement is an attractive and pleasant obje ct. Its aspect is mind that is unpacified and 
goes outward. In fact it is opposite to calm  abiding which is a mind abiding inward. Because 
excitement is part of attachment, it engages obje cts with the aspect of cr aving. Its function is 
interfere with the mind abiding on its observed object.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C The manner in which it disrupts the mind abiding on an object 
While the mind is tied to a meditation object within, desirous excitement about 
forms, sounds and the like causes the mind to be drawn helplessly towards those 
objects and stray. Just as it is set out in the Praise of Confession :  
 
Regardless of how you are focused on calm abiding 
aiming at this and that again and again, 
the noose of the afflictions helplessly pulls your mind 
away from those things with the rope of attachment to objects. 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
743 How does excitement interfere with the mind abid ing on its object? While the mind is tied to 
its observed object, due to being overcome by th e excitement that is attachment to pleasant 
forms, sounds, odors, tastes, and tangible objects, one’s mind is led to these objects and 
becomes distracted. The Praise to Confession  says that while observing calm abiding and 
focusing the mind on this and that again and ag ain, the noose of the afflictions overcomes the 
mind and pulls it from this to that with  the rope of attachment to objects.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-1A2 Determining the characteristics of sinking 
A Lumping together sinking and dullness – as some do – goes against the scriptures 
{265} In many translations sinking is also rendered as discouragement. Some 
people consider mental dullness without scattering to other objects and without 
lucidity to be sinking, which is incorrect, for it says in the Middle Stages of 
Meditation  and in [the Sutra ] Unravelling the Thought  that sinking is born from 
dullness. In the Compendium of Abhidharma  sinking is indeed explained in the 
context of the secondary affliction dist raction. However, with the distraction 
explained there something positive may also come about, so it is not sure to be 
afflictive 188. 
 
H e r e  t h e  d e f i n i t i o n  o f  l a x i t y  o r  s i n k i n g  i s  t o  be settled. However, first it is explained that 
mixing laxity and dullness contradicts the scriptur es. In many translations laxity is translated 
as “discouragement” ( zhum pa ). Regarding that, some people assert that abiding without 
scattering to another object and a dull mind la cking in clarity is laxity, however this is not 
correct. This is because the Middle Stages of Meditation  and the Sutra Unraveling the Thought  say 
that laxity is produced fr om dullness. Although the Compendium of Manifest Dharma  explains 
laxity in the context of distraction, that distract ion is not necessarily afflicted as it can also be 
virtuous. For example, while meditating, when focusing single-pointedly on an object, the 
thought can arise that one needs to read one’s  sadhana, and although this disturbs one’s 
single-pointed mind it is a virtuous distract ion. Likewise, if when meditating the thought 
suddenly arises that one needs to do prostrat ions, it too is a virtuous distraction.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B How the identification of these two differs and the proof for that 
That is why in the Compendium of Abhidharma  and in the commentary on the 
Treasury  dullness refers to a heaviness, an in adequacy of body and mind that forms 
part of delusion. As for sinking, the mode of apprehension of the mind holding 
on to its object is slack and the object is  not held clearly or tightly. Therefore, 
although there is brightness – if the mode  of apprehending the object does not 
involve clarity there will be sinking. From the Middle Stages of Meditation :  
 
                                              
188 That does not mean distraction is a totally pure state without any mental affliction but rather that it does 
not necessary come under the 20 secondary afflictions,  because there are wholesom e varieties, too. (oral 
explanation by Geshe Thubten Soepa) 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
744 You should understand that at time s when the mind does not see the 
meditation object clearly – like someone blind or someone entering 
darkness or someone closing his eyes – at those times there is sinking. 
 
In the other great texts I have not seen a clear take on the characteristics of 
sinking. 
 
Laxity and dullness are different. The Compendium of Manifest Dharma  and Commentary on the  
Treasury of Manifest Dharma  say that dullness is a part of ignorance or confusion and makes 
the body and mind heavy and unserviceable. In  t h e  c a s e  o f  l a x i t y  o r  s i n k i n g ,  t h e  m i n d  
apprehends its object loosely and the object is not clear or not held tightly. If the mode of 
apprehending the observed object is not clear, it becomes laxity. The Middle Stages of 
Meditation  says that when the mind does not see its object clearly, as though one were blind, 
or like a person entering darkness, or as though  the eyes were closed, there is laxity. In the 
other great texts the definition  of laxity is not clear.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C Moreover the two are not the same, because sinking may also have wholesome aspects 
Sinking may have either a wholesome or a neutral aspect whereas dullness is only 
unwholesome or neutral with respect to the obscurations189 and part of ignorance. 
 
Laxity and dullness are not the same because laxi ty is of two types, virtuous and unpredicted 
or neutral, whereas dullness is either non-virtuous  or obscured, that is, unpredicted, as is the 
case of the afflictions of the upper realm, an d is only part of ignorance or confusion.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D The need for both an aspect of mental clarity and firmness to eliminate sinking 
In the great texts it says that in order to eliminate sinking it is necessary to uplift 
the mind by meditating on an enjoyable physical form or light. Thus you 
counteract the object’s lack of clarity, which is like darkness having fallen within 
the mind, and stop the decline in the mi nd’s mode of apprehension. Then you 
require both the aspect of a clear obje ct and a firm mode of apprehension. 
 
In order to eliminate laxity there is a need fo r both clarity and firmness. The great texts say 
that in order to eliminate laxity, one should visu alize a virtuous object, such as the body of a 
buddha, or a clear light, which uplifts the mind. This eliminates  the lack of clarity of the 
object, which is like darkness that prevents the seeing of an object, and stops the decline of 
the mind’s mode of apprehension. Therefore, is  necessary to have both a clear object and a 
firm mode of apprehension.  
                                             
 
189 Although in the latter case it is not unwholesome it is nevertheless a mental affliction. Another example of 
such a "neutral mental affliction" is the attachment of be ings in higher realms. It also belongs to the afflictive 
obscurations, however, it is not cl assified as unwholesome. (oral ex planation by Geshe Thubten Soepa) 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
745 Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 266) says: 
E The need to determine sinking in accordance with the Stages of Meditation  
{266} Excitement is easy to recognize, bu t sinking is not clearly identified in the 
great original sources which is why it is difficult to understand. Yet it is very 
important, for in it there is much ground  for the illusion that one’s meditative 
stabilization is without fault. Therefore you should examine and identify it well 
with subtle intelligence based on your experience and in accordance with the 
statements in Stages of Meditation . 
 
It is necessary to ascertain laxity in accordance with the Stages of Meditation . Excitement, a 
scattering outward of the mind, is easy to re cognize, whereas because laxity is not clearly 
identified even in the great scriptural sources it is difficult to understand. However, it is 
important to recognize it because when laxity arises one can mistake it for faultless meditative 
stabilization. Therefore, one should examine la xity well using intelligence based on experience 
in accordance with the Stages of Meditation  so that one becomes able to identify it.  
 
Friday morning, 26 January 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 266) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-1B The method for generating introspection that realizes these 
at the time of meditation  
1 The need to generate introspection realizing whether or not sinking or excitement have occurred 
Second: It is not enough to have a mere  understanding of sinking and excitement. 
Rather, the ability to generate intros pection knowing exactly whether or not 
sinking or excitement have occurred during  meditation is needed. In fact through 
the gradual development of powerful introspection you become capable not only 
of generating introspection identifying si nking and excitement as soon as they 
arise. You also need the introspection kn owing them when they have not actually 
arisen, when they are about to arise, for in the latter two Stages of Meditation  it says: 
 
If you suspect or notice mental sinking... 
 and  
If you suspect or notice the mind is excited... 
 
In order to realize whether or not laxity an d excitement have arisen, introspection is 
necessary. However, for this to happen, it is not enough to have a mere understanding of 
laxity, a sinking mind, and excitement, a distract ed mind. In addition, one must be able to 
generate the introspection that knows exactly wh ether laxity or excitement have arisen when 
setting the mind single-pointedly on its observed object. By gradually developing stronger 
introspection, one becomes able to generate the introspection that immediately recognizes 
that laxity or excitement have arisen. After that, one needs to generate introspection that 
knows they are about to arise even when they ha ve not yet actually arisen. This is because the 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
746 Stages of Meditation  says that even if one just suspects that laxity or excitement are about to 
arise, one needs to recognize it by applying introspection.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 If you do not apply introspection your meditation will come under the influence of sinking and 
excitement 
As long as this kind of introspection ha s not developed, even though you conclude 
that “From this point till that point ther e was flawless meditation without sinking 
or excitement”, this is unfounded, for ev en if sinking or excitement occur you will 
not be able ascertain them, given that powerful introspection has not developed. 
Thus it also says in [ Discrimination of ] the Middle and the Extremes : 
 
Realizing sinking and excitement...  
 
because in order to realize sinking and ex citement you need introspection. That 
being so, if introspection which cannot fa il to recognize sinking and excitement 
has not developed, you may even meditate for long, spending years without 
noticing the occurrence of sinking and excitement. 
 
If one does not rely on introspection at the ti me of meditation, one’s mind will be overcome 
by laxity and excitement. As long as one has not generated the introspection described above, 
one may think “From this point to  that, my meditative stabiliz ation was faultless, free from 
laxity and excitement,” where it is  not so in that even if laxity  or excitement arose one was not 
able to ascertain it because one has yet not developed strong enough introspection. Likewise, 
the Discrimination of  the Middle and the Extremes  says that to realize whether laxity and 
excitement have arisen introspection is import ant. Thus, as long as one does not have the 
introspection that unfailing recognizes that laxity and excitement have arisen, one may 
meditate even for years without recognizing that  laxity and excitement have arisen and that 
one is overcome by them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3 The method for sustaining mindfulness is very important as a cause of introspection 
Now how do you generate introspection? Its most important cause is the method 
of sustaining mindfulness presented earlie r. {267} If you can generate continuous 
mindfulness in that manner, you will be ab le to stop forgetting the object and 
wandering off and thereby counteract long spells of insensitivity to the occurrence 
of sinking and excitement. That way you will recognize sinking and excitement 
easily. This is very clear from the perspect ive of personal experience: the long time 
[it takes] to recognize sinking and excitement when mindfulness has decreased and 
the short time it take s when it has not.  
 
In order to generate introspection it is necessa ry to first cultivate mindfulness. Thus, how is 
introspection generated? The gene ration of the mindfulness that  i s  a b l e  t o  r e m a i n  o n  a n  
object is an important cause for the develo pment of introspection. If one generates 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
747 continuous mindfulness, one will not forget th e object and will not wander off, whereby the 
non-recognition of laxity and excitement for long  periods will be eliminated. In other words, 
one will easily think “Now laxity has arisen ” and “Now excitement has arisen.” One can 
understand this from the fact that when mindfu lness has degenerated it takes a long time to 
recognize that laxity and excitement have  arisen, whereas when mindfulness has not 
degenerated they are quickly recognized to have arisen.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
With this understanding Entering [Bodhisattva ] Behavior  says:  
 
Once mindfulness is there to stay at  
the gate of the mind in order to guard it, 
at that point introspection will come. 
 
The Explanatory Commentary on  [Discrimination of ] the Middle and the Extremes  also 
presents mindfulness as the cause of introspection.  
 When mindfulness is guarding the mind , introspection will arise. Also the Explanatory 
Commentary on  Discrimination of  the Middle and the Extremes  says that mindfulness is the cause 
of introspection.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
4 Standing guard continuously over the mind wandering off or not is the essence of sustaining 
introspection 
This one cause consists in focusing on the aspect of the deity’s body or whatever is 
[meant] to be apprehended or else on  the aspect of the apprehender, the 
experience, mere awareness, mere clarity etc. and then, while applying mindfulness 
as explained earlier, standing guard cont inuously over [mental] wandering or the 
absence of it and fixing the mind. This should be held to be the essence of 
sustaining introspection.  
 
To continuously examine whether or the mind is wandering off is the essential point of 
cultivating introspection. The one cause is that  while the mind observes the aspect that is 
apprehended – the deity’s body and so forth – or  observes the apprehended aspect such as the 
experience that is mere cognizing and mere knowing, from within a state of relying on 
mindfulness one examines whether or not the mind has wandered to another object, and 
holds the mind on its object. This is the e ssential point of cultivating introspection. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In accordance with that, Engaging in  [Bodhisattva ] Behavior  also says: 
 
The act of examining time and again 
the current state of body and mind – 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
748 in summary – just that alone is the 
mark of guarding introspection. 
 
Shantideva’s Engaging in the Deeds of a Bodhisattva  says that one should examine one’s body 
and mind again and again, and that just this  in brief is the definition of guarding 
introspection.  
 When the body is tired it becomes more  and more heavy whereupon the mind also 
becomes more and more unclear. When the mind  becomes unclear, laxity arises, and when 
the mind moves to another object, excitement arises. This is why one needs to check one’s 
body and mind, examining again and again what they are doing.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
5 The need to differentiate well between the methods of sustaining mindfulness and introspection 
By doing this you therefore generate in trospection recognizing any sinking and 
excitement about to arise, and – by means of applying mindfulness – forgetfulness 
where the mind wanders and goes off is st opped so that you focus well. You need 
to distinguish them.  
 
By doing as above, one will generate the intr ospection that recognizes whether laxity and 
excitement are about to arise. By means of re lying upon mindfulness, the mind that forgets 
the observed object or the instru ctions and wanders off is ceased. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-2 Applying the antidote to not making an effort at giving up 
sinking and excitement – although one has identified them 
A The need to intend the application of the antidote 
B How to actually apply it 
 
One must rely upon the antidote to not making  an effort to abandon laxity and excitement 
even though they have been identified.  
 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-2A The need to intend the application of the antidote 
Second: As explained earlier working well at the method of sustaining mindfulness 
and introspection will generate strong mindfulness so that even subtle sinking and 
excitement can be recognized by means of  introspection. Hence there will not be 
the fault of not identifying sinking and excitement. However, the failure to try 
hard and commit yourself – not making an e ffort to stop the two as soon as they 
arise or non-application – {268} is a great fault of meditative stabilization, so you 
should cultivate the intention referred to as  application with regard to the antidote 
or endeavour.  
 
As explained previously, by understanding well the way in which to cultivate mindfulness and 
introspection, one will generate strong mind fulness whereby one will be able to recognize 
even subtle laxity and excitement by means of in trospection. Due to this, there will not be the 
fault of not identifying laxity and excitement. Ho wever, if one of them arises but one does not 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
749 strive to eliminate it immediately, it is necessa ry to apply the antidote to this non-application 
of the antidote which is a great fault to developi ng meditative stabilization. In this case, the 
antidote is the intention that is imputed as “a pplication” or “effort” in the antidote. Intention 
is included among the five omnipresent mental fact ors. In this context it is the mental factor 
that moves the mind toward the antidote. In tention is called mental action because it 
produces actions of body and speech. 
 In short, if laxity or excitement arise but one does not apply its antidote, this is a fault and, 
therefore, it is important to immediately strive  to apply it. For this, intention is necessary. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-2B The actual manner of application 
1 Identification of intention and how to stop sinking and excitement 
2 Identifying the causes in dependence on which sinking and excitement arise 
This [one] has two points: identification of intention and how to stop sinking and 
excitement, and identifying the causes in dependence on which sinking and 
excitement arise.  
 
First intention is identified and the way in whic h to stop laxity and excitement are explained, 
and then the causes of laxity and excitement are identified. 
 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-2B1 Identification of intention and how to stop sinking and 
excitement 
A Identification of intention 
B How to stop sinking and excitement 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-2B1A Identification of intention 
First: In the Compendium of Abhidharma  it says:  
 
What is intention? It is the mind  creating compositional factors – 
mental activity with the function of causing the mind to engage in 
wholesome, unwholesome, or neutral things. 
 
That means the mental factor moving th e mind and impelling it to something 
wholesome, unwholesome, or neutral, whatev er it is – like iron moving under the 
power of a magnet without any control – is  intention. Here you should therefore 
appreciate the intention motivating th e mind to get rid of any sinking or 
excitement once they occur.  
 
The Compendium of Manifest Dharma  says that intention is mental  action that has the function 
of making the mind engage in the virtuous , non-virtuous, and unpredicted (that is, the 
neither virtuous nor non-virtuous). In other word s, intention is the mental factor that moves 
the mind toward that which is either virtuous, non-virtuous, or unpredic ted. When laxity and 
excitement arise, intention is what moves the mind toward the antidote that abandons them.  
 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-2B1B How to stop sinking and excitement 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
750 1 How to stop sinking 
2 How to stop excitement 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-2B1B-1 How to stop sinking 
A Counteracting it by considering something joyful or the quality of light etc. 
Now, how do you stop sinking and excitement? A mind that is sinking has 
withdrawn inward too much and lost it s [firm] manner of apprehending the 
object. For that reason it says in the First Stages of Meditation  that you should 
consider a cause for the mind to move ou twards again, something joyful, in fact 
like the Buddha’s body rather than some thing joyful giving rise to mental 
afflictions. Or else if you consider the qu ality of light such as sunshine and cleanse 
the sinking [mind], this will immediately tighten the manner of apprehending the 
object and maintain it. At this point, do  not meditate on a disenchanting object, 
for weariness is a cause for the mind to withdraw inward. If you feel uplifted 
having examined any object you want to examine with discriminating wisdom, this 
will also counter sinking.  
 
How are laxity or sinking stopped? Laxity is coun teracted by mental attention to joyful things 
and to light. A lax mind has withdrawn too strongly inside due to which the mode of 
apprehension of the observed object has loosened . In order to counteract this, one must make 
the mind go a bit outward. To do this one should visualize something that makes one happy and joyful, for example, a beautiful form such as the body of the Buddha, and that does not 
cause afflictions to arise. One should not visu alize an attractive man or woman, or a nice 
horse, car, or telephone, which are causes fo r attachment to arise! Instead, one should 
imagine a virtuous object, and not an object of attachment that one likes very much such as 
delicious food. Such objects are not what are meant here.   Alternatively one can mentally imagine sunlight or moonlight, some kind of clear light, as 
this can also eliminate sinking mind. Just like when the clouds are eliminated from the sky, 
the mind becomes bright again. By doing this , the mode of apprehension of the observed 
object will immediately tighten whereupon on e should resume one’s meditation. This was 
taught by Kamalashila in his First Stages of Meditation .  
 One should not meditate on a disenchanting ob ject as this will make the mind withdraw 
even more inward and will not counter laxity. One can also uplift the mind by means of the 
wisdom of individual investigation, whereby laxity is eliminated.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
From the Summary of Perfections :  
 
Becoming slack uplift the mind through the 
power of striving for special insight. 
 By doing the force of doing analytical meditati on, slackness will be eliminated and the mind 
will be uplifted.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
751 B How to counteract [sinking] by heightening the mind’s mode of apprehension 
Thus, sinking or slackness {269} consists in the mind sinking because its manner of 
apprehending the meditation object ha s gone down, and getting slack upon 
withdrawing inside. Consequently, it is countered by heightening the manner of 
apprehension and expanding the meditation  object so as to uplift the mind.  
 
Laxity is counteracted by heightening the mo de of apprehension of the mind. Laxity or 
slackness ( zhum pa ) is that due to the mode of apprehension of the observed object declining 
there is laxity, and due to the mind withdrawin g inside there is slackness. Therefore, one 
should uplift or heighten the mode of appreh ension and extend the observed object through 
making it more vast. In this way laxity or slackness can be eliminated.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In Essence of the Middle Way  it says: 
 
When slack expand the mind [again] by 
meditating on spacious objects. 
 and  
When slack uplift yourself through seeing the benefits of joyous effort. 
 
The Essence of the Middle Way  says that when the mind is sl ack one should meditate on the 
observed object as being more vast, whereby th e mind will become more spacious and lighter. 
Also when there is slackness, one should contemplate the benefits of joyous effort. 
 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
The most important antidote for putting an end to sinking: if you consider the 
benefits of the Three Jewels and bodhichi tta as well as the great value of the 
leisures you have gained etc. it should have the same effect as cold water if it is splashed into a tired [person’s] face – refr eshing the awareness. It[s effectiveness] 
depends on the personal experience you have  got out of analytical meditation with 
discriminating awareness in the direction of the benefits.  
 The most important antidote to laxity is to co nsider the benefits of the Three Jewels and the 
benefits of bodhichitta, which fulfills one’s  own and others’ purpose, as well as the 
meaningfulness of this precious human rebirth en dowed with the eighteen qualities. Doing so 
is likened to splashing cold water on one’s face  when one is sleepy, in that the mind becomes 
refreshed and clearer. However, this depend s on the experience that comes from doing 
analytical meditation that individually  investigates the side of benefits. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
752 C Use of the idea of light, purifying meditation objec ts, and the like specifically against a vagueness of 
the meditation object such as if darkness had fallen upon the mind 
If you apply the antidote, meditation on light, to the causes that reinforce the 
development of sinking – fogginess, drowsiness, and mental states with an aspect 
of darkness inducing those two – sinkin g which depends on them will also not 
develop and that which has already developed will be countered.  
 
In particular, when darkness befalls the mind, due to which the object becomes unclear, one 
should discriminate light (sunlight or moonlight or  even electrical light!) and take an observed 
object of lucid faith. The causes of developing  laxity are darkness or fogginess, drowsiness or 
sleepiness, and a mind that has the aspect of darkness which induce s these two. It is 
eliminated by meditating on light or illumination . If one relies upon this antidote, the laxity 
that depends on them will not develop and that which has already been developed will be countered.  
 The Shravaka Levels  also talk about having a break from one’s practice, taking to 
mind the characteristics of light and fa miliarizing oneself with them again and 
again, uplifting the mind with any of six [topics]: the Three Jewels, ethical 
discipline, giving and the recollection of deities, or other purifying meditation 
objects, reciting teachings indicati ng the disadvantages of fogginess and 
drowsiness, looking to the sides or at th e moon and the stars, washing one’s face 
[with water] and so forth. 
 When complete darkness and sleepiness arise, on e should take a break and go for a walk, or 
imagine a light pervading one’s body, or recall the six: (1-3) the Three Jewels (the Buddha 
Jewel, Dharma Jewel, and Sangha Jewel), (4) mo rality, (5) the mind of  giving, and (6) one’s 
deity. Alternatively, one should uplift the mind with an observed object of clear faith that is other than them, read texts ab out the disadvantages of darkne ss and drowsiness, look to the 
ten directions or at the moon or stars, wash one’s face with cold water, and so forth. In this 
way it is possible to eliminate darkness and dr owsiness. One should examine which of these 
methods works for oneself. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D How to apply special corrective measures against subtle and gross sinking separately 
Furthermore, if the mental sinking is very slight and does not occur more than 
every so often you should tauten the mode  of apprehension of  the mind as you 
[continue to] meditate. However, if the mind is sinking deeply again and again 
{270} stop your meditation, apply the an tidotes as appropriate and [continue to] 
meditate once sinking has been cleared away . If the object the mind is holding on 
to is unclear and whatever aspect, slight  or strong, of something like darkness 
falling upon the mind emerges, take up the characteristics of lights such as a butter 
lamp, fire, and sunshine. If you familiariz e yourself with them again and again a 
great mental lucidity will develop.  
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
753 I f  l a x i t y  i s  v e r y  s l i g h t  o r  d o e s  n o t  o c c u r  very often, one should tighten the mode of 
apprehension and continue meditating. If, on the other hand, laxity is thick and occurs 
repeatedly, one should stop one’s meditati on and apply an appropriate antidote, and 
continue to meditate only when the laxity has been eliminated. If the apprehended object is 
unclear and the mind is dark, whether slightly or  thickly, one should imagine light, such as 
that of a butter lamp, fire, or sunshine, and familiarize with it, whereby the mind will become 
very clear and lucid.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-2B1B-2 How to stop excitement 
A The need generally to take to mind sobering matters as an antidote to excitement 
When it comes to excitement, the mind ru ns after forms, sounds and the like due 
to attachment, so for that you should take  to mind sobering matters, causes for the 
mind to withdraw inward. However, as s oon as the excitement has thereby been 
calmed, you rest your mind in equipoise.  
 
The antidote to excitement in general is to take to mind something that makes the mind 
despair. Excitement, due to attachment, makes th e mind go after attractive forms (beautiful 
colors and shapes), sounds (pleasant music or chanting), odors, tastes, and tactile objects. 
Since the mind is wandering outward, in order to counteract this one needs to withdraw the 
mind inward by thinking of something that on e does not like. When the excitement has been 
pacified, one should immediately resume one’s meditation.  
In Essence of the Middle Way  it says: 
 
Pacify excitement by considering things like impermanence”  
 and  
You should collect the distracted mind by looking at faults of the marks of distraction. 
 
The Essence of the Middle Way  says that excitement is pacified by thinking about 
impermanence and so forth. For example, one ca n meditate on the fact that an apparently 
beautiful object can change into an ugly obje ct. One can also do the nine-round meditation 
on death-impermanence, or meditate on the vari ous types of sufferings such as the suffering 
of suffering, suffering of change, and pervasive suffering. By meditating on such topics, 
excitement is eliminated. This text also says th at when the mind is distracted one should look 
at the faults of distraction, whereby the mind wi ll once again be withdrawn inside rather than 
wandering outside.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 22-26 January 2007 (16) 
754 B How to apply special corrective measures against subtle and gross excitement separately 
If intense or long lasting excitement occurs, it is effective to relax the meditation 
for a while and meditate on something disi llusioning, rather than to keep drawing 
in and placing the mind because of its tendency to move outward. Once the 
excitement is not as significant [anymore], draw in the distracted mind and tie it to 
the meditation object. In the Summary of Perfections  it says:  
 
Whenever your mind has got excited 
counter this through peaceful calm. 
 With an excited mind, you should not consider inspiring or joyful objects, for they are causes of external distraction. 
 When strong and continuous excitement arises  during meditation, one should give up the 
meditation for a while, that is, take a break fr om the observed object of one’s meditation, and 
meditate on something that makes one despair, such as the fact that everything in cyclic 
existence is imperfect and faulty . In this way one should rein force the thought of definite 
emergence by thinking about the fact that although there seem to be objects of enjoyment in 
cyclic existence, in fact they only deceive us in that they bring only suffering and problems.  
 When the excitement is no longer strong, one should once again tie the mind to its 
observed object. The Summary of Perfections  says that when excitement arises, one should 
counteract it by means of calm abiding. In short, when the mind is excited, one should not take a pleasant and enjoyable object as one’s observed object, since this will only cause the 
mind to wander outward even more.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3A-2B2 Identification of causes which the development of sinking 
and excitement depend on 
A Common causes of sinking and excitement 
Second: The common causes of both sink ing and excitement are not to guard 
one’s sense doors, not to eat judiciousl y, not to make an effort at one’s 
[meditation] practice without sleeping duri ng the first and last part of the night, 
and to remain without introspection.  
 
Next the cause for the arisal of laxity and ex citement are identified. Common causes for the 
arisal of laxity and excitement are to not guar d the doors of the senses, the eyes, ears, nose, 
tongue, and body; to not eat the right amount bu t instead too overeat; to not put effort into 
not sleeping in the first and last parts of the night; and to not have introspection. Therefore, 
one should guard the senses, eat the right amou nt, and sleep at the right times. Although 
some people think that a lot of sleep, even ten or  eleven hours, is good for the body, this is 
not the case. For example, Choden Rinpoche’s attendant does not sleep at night but when 
everyone else is asleep he does his mantras an d so forth, and then goes to bed about two in 
the morning. He then sleeps until eleven the ne xt morning, has breakfast at eleven thirty, 
lunch at five or six in the evening, and then di nner after midnight! This is not a good habit to 
get into.         END
