Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
755 Monday afternoon, 29 January 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 270) says: 
B Causes of sinking 
The causes of sinking are great sleepiness, {271} excessive looseness with regard to 
the mind holding on to its meditation object, not getting the balance of calm 
abiding and special insight right and cult ivating calm abiding too much, the mind 
remaining as though in darkness, and no t enjoying placement on the meditation 
object.  
 
The causes of sinking or laxity are: 
1. very strong sleepiness,  
2. excessive looseness with respect to the mind holding its observed object,  
3. strong reliance on calm abiding without ca lm abiding and special insight being balanced,  
4. the mind abiding as though dark, and 5. not liking to focus on the observed object.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C Causes of excitement 
The causes of excitement were taught to be [too] little disillusionment, the mind 
being to high-strung with regard to the me ditation object, a lack of effort, and the 
mind being distracted by thoughts of close ones etc. 
 
The causes of excitement are: 
1. little disillusionment or renunciation, 
2. excessive tightness of the mind wi th respect to its observed object, 
3. a lack of familiarity with effort, 4. the mind being distracted by conceptions about one’s loved ones.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D If you have not persevered in getting rid of even su btle sinking and excitement you will not realize the 
achievement of perfect m editative stabilization  
Therefore you need to be[come] aware, through introspection, of even subtle 
aspects of sinking and excitement and stop them all entirely. If you give up, 
thinking: “In the beginning, although I cut through excitement, distraction and 
the like, they were never cut off [completel y], so I shall not cut through them [any 
longer]” or if you think “Unless they are intense, continued, and long-lasting, there 
is no need to cut through them, because th ey are brief and weak, so that no karma 
will be accumulated”, you will not realiz e the achievement of perfect meditative 
stabilization. That is because you will be  departing from the conclusion Venerable 
Maitreya and others have reached on how to achieve meditative stabilization. 
 
Without putting effort into abandoning subtle laxity and excitement it is not possible to 
achieve meditative stabilization. Therefore, it is  necessary to recognize even subtle laxity and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
756 excitement by means of introspection and put a st op to them entirely. If one gives up through 
thinking “If I do not eliminate excitement, distra ction, and so forth at the beginning, later on 
it will be difficult to eliminate them, therefore I will not strive to eliminate them,” and thinks 
“Unless they are intense and a long-lasting co ntinuum there is no need to eliminate them, 
whereas if they are short lasting and weak there is a need to eliminate them,” this shows that 
one does not understand how to achieve pure meditative stabilization. Thus, one will be far 
from understanding how Venerable Maitreya and others have settled the way in meditative 
stabilization is accomplished.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
E When you only have the factors of st ability and the limpidity,  that of brilliance be ing weak, there is no 
hope for meditati ve stabilization 
Therefore, lure your mind away from distra ction and excitement so as to tie it to 
the meditation object within and seek th e stability factor. Whenever the stability 
factor has emerged beware of sinking and br ing out the brilliance factor. Intent on 
both of these in turn, you will achieve im peccable meditative stabilization, but do 
not trust mere limpidity without the factor  of brilliance which has an intensity 
about its mode of apprehension. 
 
When one merely has the factors of abiding and li mpidity but lacks the factor of clarity, it is 
not possible to achieve meditative stabilization. One should pull the mind away from 
scattering and excitement, tying the mind to its observed object, and seek the factor of 
abiding. Whenever the factor of abiding arises ,  o n e  s h o u l d  b e w a r e  o f  l a x i t y  a n d  m a k e  t h e  
factor of clarity strong. Throug h alternating these two, one will achieve faultless meditative 
stabilization. However, one should not trust in mere limpidity that is without the factor of 
clarity. Instead, it is necessary to see the object  clearly and for the mind to be very lucid. If 
both factors – abiding and limpidity – are not pr esent one’s meditative stabilization will be 
faulty.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A3B What to do at times without sinking or excitement 
1 As it is a mistake to keep applying antidotes although sinking and excitement have been cut off, there 
is a need to resort to the antidote of that, the equanimity of natural composure 
Second: when even subtle sinking and exci tement have been cut off as explained 
earlier and the mind enters into [a st ate of] perfect equa nimity without the 
imbalance of either sinking or excitement  developing through meditation, at that 
time it would be a mistake if you applie d antidotes or made an[y] effort, so you 
should practice equanimity as the antidote. {272}  
 
If one applies an antidote when the mind is free from laxity and excitement, it is a fault. In 
this case one needs to rely on equanimity as th e antidote to this over  application. If when 
subtle laxity and excitement have been cut off and neither laxity or excitement have arisen, that is, the mind is neither sinking or wanderin g off, one applies an antidote or makes effort, 
this becomes a fault with respect to attaining single-pointed meditative stabilization. The 
antidote to this is equanimity.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
757  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
This is how you go wrong through applicat ion and effort: as you keep bringing the 
mind back inward and uplifting it whenever  it is sinking, you grow confident that 
sinking and excitement will not [continue to] come on in every session. At that 
stage it would be a mistake to worry about sinking or worry greatly about 
excitement. If you do, your mind is goin g to wander, so you will need to know 
[how to] relax then. That is to say rela x the effort rather than let go off the 
intensity of your mode of apprehension.  
 
In this case application and making effort become  a fault. In other words, if the mind is free 
from laxity and excitement but still one makes an effort, it becomes a fault with respect to attaining meditative stabilization. One should  bring the mind inside and uplift it when 
sinking, whereby one becomes confident that laxity  and excitement will not arise. At that time 
worrying “Perhaps laxity will arise” and “Perhaps  excitement will arise” will cause the mind to 
become distracted, therefore one needs to keep the mind relaxed and quiet. In other words, 
one should relax one’s effort but without givi ng up holding the observed object strongly. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 That holds once you are past the peak of sinking and excitement 
Therefore this cultivation of equanimity applies once you are past the peak of 
sinking and excitement, it is not practiced every time sinking or excitement do not 
occur190, because when you have not yet passed the peak for sinking and 
excitement there is no equanimity.  
 
The cultivation of equanimity is not done on a ll occasions on which laxity and excitement do 
not occur, but only after having passed the peak of laxity and excitement as before this there 
is no equanimity. In other words, one needs to  cultivate equanimity when one has passed the 
peak of laxity and excitement.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3 How to identify that very equanimity 
Now, what is this equanimity? Among the three kinds of equanimity – equanimity 
of feelings, immeasurable equanimity, and eq uanimity of application – it is the last 
one [we are dealing with here]. Its essence is just as explained in the Shravaka 
Levels : it is the mind in equipoise on its obje ct from the sphere of calm abiding or 
special insight, falling into its natural stat e, engaging [in its object] spontaneously, 
and attaining pliability. Once you have achieved this kind of equanimity you 
should fully actualize it, not striving too hard, as long as there is no sinking or 
excitement.  
 
                                              
190 JN “occur” changed to “do not occur” ( ma byung ba ) 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
758 What type of equanimity is this equanimity? Equanimity that is a feeling, immeasurable 
equanimity, or the equanimity that is a composit ional factor? It is the equanimity that is a 
compositional factor ( ‘du byed btang snyoms ). The Hearer Grounds  says that its entity is a mind 
equipoised on its observed object that is in th e direction of calm abiding and special insight, 
rests in its natural state, engages naturally, and has attained serviceability. Having achieved the 
equanimity that is a compositional factor, wh en cultivating meditative stabilization and 
neither laxity or excitement has arisen, one sh ould manifest that equanimity but not make 
strong effort.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
4 Presentation of the sour ces for these explanations  on how to develop perfec t meditative stabilization 
like that 
Like it says in [ Discriminating Between ] the Middle and the Extremes : 
 
On that basis, pliancy, 
all purposes will be attained. 
They spring from the causes relied upon: 
the eight applications removing five faults – 
laziness, forgetfulness 
of the instructions, excitement and sinking, 
non-application and application, these are held to be the five faults. The base and what is based on it, 
the cause and the effect of it
191, 
not to forget the object perceived, 
realizing excitement and sinking, application to remove them, when stilled {273} getting into a state of ease. 
 By means of serviceability of body and mind, all purposes are achieved. By abandoning the five faults and relying on the eight applications , meditative stabilization is achieved. The five 
faults are (1) laziness, (2) forgetting the observ ed object or the instructions, (3) laxity and 
excitement, (4) the non-applicatio n of the antidote when either laxity or excitement have 
arisen, and (5) the application of the antidote wh en neither laxity or excitement have arisen.  
 The antidotes to laziness are set out saying “t he base and what is based on it, the cause and 
the effect of it.” The antidotes to the other four faults are: (1) to not forget the observed 
object, (2) to investigate whether laxity or excitement have aris en, (3) to manifestly apply the 
antidote that abandons them, and (4) to set the mind in its natural stat e when they have been 
pacified.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
                                             
 
191 cf. 2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2A1B: Individual explanations on the four  antidotes to laziness 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
759 5 Explaining the meaning of the quotation in detail 
Here “on that basis” is on the basis of mustering up joyous effort in order to 
eliminate unfavourable conditions – from  that the meditative stabilization of 
mental pliancy is born. Furthermore, as it  is the footing or basis of miraculous 
powers bringing about all purposes such as clairvoyance, it achieves those 
purposes. Such meditative stabilization is born from the cause of giving up five 
faults and relying on eight applications.  
 
“Based on that” means that based on genera ting joyous effort in order to eliminate 
unfavorable conditions, this being laziness, the meditative stabilization that is a serviceable 
mind arises. Since this meditative stabilization is the cause of achieving the legs of magical 
emanation that accomplish all purposes, such as  the six clairvoyances, the five eyes, and so 
forth; it is the basis or source, whereby all purposes are achieved. Such meditative 
stabilization, which is mainly calm abiding, co mes from the cause that is abandoning the five 
faults and relying on the eight applications.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
The five faults: when it is time for you to  apply yourself, laziness is a fault, because 
you will not apply yourself to meditative st abilization. When you make an effort at 
meditative stabilization, forgetting the inst ructions is a fault, for if you forget the 
meditation object there will be no medita tive equipoise of the mind on the object. 
With regard to equipoise, sinking and ex citement are faults, because they cause 
mental pliancy to be lacking. When there is  sinking or excitement, it is a fault not 
to try hard, for that way these two will no t be pacified. Once you have got rid of 
sinking and excitement, the intention to a pply remedies is a fault. If you count 
sinking and excitement as one they come to five, if divided up there is six as set out 
in Stages of Meditation . 
 
At the time of application, laziness is a fault because due to it one does not apply oneself to 
meditative stabilization. Instead one thinks “Tod ay I am too tired, I cannot concentrate. It is 
better for me to sleep. It is better for me to watch a movie. It is better for me to go for a walk, 
to go dancing, and so forth,” due to which one does not meditate in order to develop 
meditative stabilization.   Then even when one puts effort into meditati ve stabilization, when setting the mind on its 
observed object one forgets the instructions one received regarding meditation. This is a fault 
because if one forgets the observed object, ther e is no meditative equipoise on the observed 
object. When equipoised, if laxity or excitement  arise, these two are faults because they make 
the mind unserviceable. When laxity and exciteme nt arise, if one does not make an effort to 
apply the antidote this is a fault because these tw o will not be pacified. Therefore, at this time 
the intention the moves the mind toward the anti dote is necessary. On the other hand, if the 
mind is free from laxity and excitement, the intention to apply the an tidote is a fault.  
 When laxity and excitement are counted toge ther as one fault there are a total of five 
faults, but when they are counted separate ly there are six faults as mentioned in Stages of 
Meditation . 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
760  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Among their eight antidotes, applications  for abandoning them, laziness has four. 
They are faith, aspiration, effort, an d suppleness. Then the antidotes to 
forgetfulness, sinking and excitement , non-application [of antidotes], and 
application [of antidotes] are mindfulness,  introspection, the intention to apply 
antidotes and [settling the mind into] the equanimity of natural ease, respectively. 
They have already been explained above. 
 
The five faults are laziness, forgetfulness, la xity and excitement, non-application, and over-
application. There are four antidotes to laziness:  (1) faith, (2) aspiration, (3) effort, and (4) 
pliancy. The antidote to forgetfulness is mindfuln ess, the antidote to la xity and excitement is 
introspection, the antidote to non-application is the intention to apply the antidote, and the 
antidote to over-application is th e equanimity in which the mind is  set in its natural state (this 
being the equanimity of compositional factors from among the three types of equanimity).  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
6 How this manner of  sustaining [meditative stabilization] is  common to all of sutra and mantra 
This is common to all the instructions on  sustaining the mind in single-pointed 
meditative stabilization having freed it  from sinking and excitement through 
mindfulness and introspection. Therefore, you should not consider it a specific 
detail of the sutra system which, however, is  not required in mantra. {274} It is also 
taught in many highest yoga tantras.  
 
This way of cultivating meditative stabilization is common to both sutra and tantra. In other 
words, the instructions to cultivate meditative stabilization by freeing the mind from laxity 
and excitement by means of mindfulness and introspection are common to sutra and tantra. 
In other words, one should not make the mist ake of thinking that these instructions are 
specific to the perfection vehicle and do not a pply to mantra since they are also taught in 
many highest yoga tantras. In ot her words, these instructions ar e applicable to both sutra and 
tantra. Whereas some people like sutra and othe rs like tantra, there are some people who do 
not like either sutra or tantra. Instead they sit in the meditation posture with their eyes closed 
and empty their mind of all thoughts, thinking  that this is the BEST meditation. Other 
people want to meditate on mahamudra. Once someone asked me to teach mahamudra 
meditation but I responded saying that I did no t know this practice! Someone else asked me 
for instructions on dream yoga, and I told him that if he went to sleep, dream yoga would 
arise! Someone else asked me for instructions on  the Six Yogas of Naropa but I told him that 
I did not remember anything about them! 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 274) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2B The stages in which mental abiding arises based on it

The second one has three points: The actual  stages of mental abiding, the method 
for achieving them by means of the six powers and how it has four types of 
attention. 
 
Next the stages of the nine mental abidings are explained, as well as ho w they are achieved by 
the six powers and the four types of mental attention.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2B1 The actual stages of mental abiding 
[A Placement of the mind 
B Continuous placement 
C Patchy placement 
D Close placement 
E Taming 
F Pacification 
G Complete pacification 
H Making single-pointed 
I Even placement] 
First: There are nine [of them]: 
[A] Placing the mind on such and such  consists in gathering the mind [, 
withdrawing it] from all external objects and fixing it on th e meditation object 
within. In Ornament of the Sutras  it says:  
 
Having fixed the mind on the object...192 
 
One should place or set the mind on its observ e d  o b j e c t  b y  n o t  a l l o w i n g  i t  t o  g o  o u t  t o  
external objects such as forms, sounds, and so  forth and instead withdrawing it inward and 
focusing it on the observed object. Likewise, the Ornament  of Sutras  says to focus the mind on 
the observed object.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[B] Placing the mind continuously: Having  initially fixed the mind you keep it 
placed on the meditation object over a period of time without wandering off to 
anything else.  
 
Do not let it wander at all in that period. 
 Having focused the mind, one should not allow it to be distracted to other objects but set it 
continuously on its observed object. Likewise, the Ornament  of Sutras  says not to be distracted 
to other objects. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
                                             
 
192 Unless identified otherwise al l the following quotations are taken from the same text.

762  
[C] Patchy placement: If you get distract ed through forgetfulness and your mind 
wanders outward, you notice and tie it to the true meditation object again. 
 
Knowing mental wandering quickly, 
you should patch it up again. 
 
Resetting or patchy placement is that when the mind is distracted by forgetfulness and 
wanders outward, one should recognize this and once again set the mind on its observed 
object. The Ornament  of Sutras says that having quickly realized  that the mind is distracted, 
one should once again set it on its object.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[D] Close placement is explained as follows in the Instructions on th e Perfection of 
Wisdom : The mind, extensive by nature, is ga thered again and again and thus made 
more subtle, so that placement gets be tter and better – in agreement with the 
statement: 
 
Intelligent people should more and more gather the mind internally. 
 
Close setting or close placement is, as explained in the Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom , 
that the mind which is very vast by nature is withdrawn again and again, thus making it more 
subtle, whereby setting improves. The Ornament  of Sutras  also says that the intelligent, 
improving more and more, should gather the mind inside.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[E] Taming: Having considered the positive  traits of meditative stabilization you 
rejoice in it. 
 
Then, as you see the positive traits, you tame the mind in meditative stabilization. 
 Taming or subduing is that through thinking ab out the qualities of meditative stabilization, 
one comes to enjoy or like it. The Ornament  of Sutras  says that by seeing its qualities, the mind 
becomes subdued with respect to meditative stabilization.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[F] Pacification: Having recognized distract ion as a fault, you pacify aversion to 
meditative stabilization. 
 
Seeing distraction as a fault 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
763 you should still aversion to it. 
 
Pacification means that by seeing distraction as  a fault, one pacifies dislike for meditative 
stabilization. The Ornament  of Sutras  also says that seeing distractio n as a fault, dislike for it is 
pacified. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[G] Complete pacification: completely pacifying instances of desire, mental 
unhappiness, dullness, sleepiness etc.  
Desire, unhappiness and the like should be pacified as they appear. 
 
Complete pacification is the pacification of attachment, mental unhappiness, darkness, sleep, 
and so forth. In other words, when there is attachment to pleasant forms, sounds, odors, 
tastes, and tactile objects, as well as when th ere is mental unhappiness, they are immediately 
pacified. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[H] Making single-pointed: making an effort for one’s mental engagement to 
become effortless.  
Then, by means of restraint and effort, {275} along with actual application you get it to come about naturally. 
 M a k i n g  t h e  m i n d  s i n g l e - p o i n t e d  i s  t o  m a k e  e f f o r t  i n  o r d e r  t o  e n g a g e  e f f o r t l e s s l y .  T h e  
Ornament  of Sutras  says that by means of restraint with  effort, together with application in 
intention it will arise naturally. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[I] ‘Even placement’ is what the Stages of Meditation  says for establishing equanimity 
once the mind has become even. In the Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom  it is 
the expression [used] for spontaneous, natural engagement due to the habit of 
unifying the mental continuum. In accordance with that it also says [in Ornament 
of the Sutras ]:  
 
As it’s habitual, you do not apply means. 
 
Even placement or equipoise is that when the mind  is balanced there is the equanimity that is 
a compositional factor. By means of continuous familiarity one will engage spontaneously and 
naturally, whereupon one will be overcome by the power of meditative stabilization.

Tuesday morning, 30 January 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 275) says: 
 
The terms for these nine mental states are as quoted from the First Stages of 
Meditation  and elsewhere: 
 
This path of calm abiding is expl ained in the Perfection of Wisdom 
[sutras] etc. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2B2 The method for achieving them by means of the six powers 
A Identifying the six powers 
B What minds are achieved through them in what manner 
C How during the ninth mental  abiding you effortlessly en ter meditative stabilization 
D How the ninth mental abiding has to be preceded by the earlier ones in turn 
E The summarized meaning of all that 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2B2A Identifying the six powers 
Second: The six powers are those of hearin g, thinking, mindfulness, introspection, 
effort, and acquaintance. 
 
The power of hearing refers to listening to  teachings on how to develop meditative 
stabilization, that is, a single-pointed mind. Th e other five powers are: the power of thinking 
or contemplation, the power of mindfulness, th e power of introspection, the power of effort, 
and the power of acquaintance or familiarity.  The power of thinking means to think about 
what one has heard and read. Mindfulness is ne cessary in order to no t forget the observed 
object and the instructions on developing medi tative stabilization. Introspection counters 
laxity and excitement which are discordant to meditative stabilization. Effort is also necessary 
in order to achieve meditative stabilizatio n, as is acquaintance or familiarity.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says:  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2B2B What minds are achieved through them in what manner 
1 The power of hearing 
Through the power of hearing, placement of the mind is achieved, for, as you 
follow instructions you have heard from  someone on placing the mind on an 
object, it is initially a mere tying to the meditation object rather than something 
that has become a habit through your own repeated reflections.  
 
What minds are achieved by the six powers? The nine mental abidings are achieved by means of the six powers. The first mental abiding is placement or setting the mind on its observed 
object, which is achieved by the power of hear ing. By receiving and following instructions 
from others on how to set or place the mind on  its object, initially one merely ties the mind 
to its observed object, whereas there is not ye t the familiarity that arises from repeatedly 
thinking about it.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
765 2 The power of thinking 
Through the power of thinking, continuous  placement of the mind is achieved, 
for, having first tied it to the medita tion object, [placement] is sustained by 
thinking of its continuity again and again.  From that you will achieve an initial 
capacity for a somewhat connected continuity.  
 
The second mental abiding is continuous plac ement or setting, which strives to continually 
think about the observed object, even though one is not yet able to abide on the it for much 
time. This is achieved by the power of thinking.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3 The power of mindfulness 
Through the power of mindfulness, both patchy placement and close placement of 
the mind are achieved, for, if the mind gets distracted and wanders off from the 
meditation object, you are mindful of the earlier object and gather the mind 
within. Developing the strength of your mindfulness from the start, you will not 
give rise to distraction.  
 
The third and fourth mental abid ings, patchy or repeated placement and close placement, are 
achieved by the power of mindfulness. When  the mind becomes distracted and loses the 
observed object, one brings the mind back to it through remembering it and gathers the mind 
inside. By generating strong mindfulness ri ght from the beginning, the mind will not be 
distracted from its observed object.  STUDENT: What is the meaning of “inside”? Does it mean to focus inside the body or to focus within the mind itself? 
GESHE JAMPA GYATSO: It means to not allow the mind to go outside to other objects, but 
to keep it abiding inside the heart or brain! In  short, it means that the mind is set on its 
object and does not wander off to other objects.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
4 The power of introspection 
Through the power of introspection bo th a tamed and a pacified mind are 
achieved, {276} for, through introspection recognizing the fault of moving towards 
discursive thought and towards the marks of  secondary afflictions, through seeing 
them as faults, you do not allow the mind  to move towards those two [sources of 
distraction]. 
 
The fifth and sixth stages of me ntal abiding – tamed or subdued and pacified – are attained 
by means of the power of introspection. Th is is because when discursive thoughts 
(conceptualizations) or secondary afflictions aris e, introspection sees and recognizes them as 
faults, whereby it does not allow th e mind to wander toward them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
766 5 Effort 
Through the power of effort you achieve bo th a fully pacified and a single-pointed 
mind, for you diligently abandon even subtle occurrences of conceptual thought 
and secondary afflictions, thereby not acq uiescing to them. As you do so, sinking 
and excitement etc. cannot obstruct your meditative stabilization [any more] and 
you achieve meditative stabilization arising continuously.  
 
The seventh and eighth mental abidings – a th oroughly pacified mind and a single-pointed 
mind – are achieved by the power of effort. This is because with diligence one abandons 
subtle discursive thoughts and secondary affl ictions without acquiesc ing to them. Thereby, 
laxity and excitement, as well as conceptualiz ations and the secondary afflictions, can no 
longer interfere with the development of single -pointed meditative stabilization, whereupon 
the meditative stabilization that ar ises continuously is achieved.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
6 The power of acquaintance 
Through the power of acquaintance you achieve a mind placed in equipoise, for 
through the power of intense familiariz ation with the foregoing, meditative 
stabilization is born that oper ates naturally and effortlessly. 
 
The ninth mental abiding – a mind of equipoise – is achieved by the power of acquaintance 
or familiarity. By means of familiarity with the previous stages, a meditative stabilization that 
engages naturally and effortlessly arises. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
This is all in conformity with  the intended meaning of the Shravaka Levels , so you 
should not rely on any other explanations.  
 Since this explanation concords with the explanation found in Hearer Grounds , one should 
not trust in other explanations. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2B2C How during the ninth mental abiding you effortlessly enter meditative 
stabilization 
Now when you achieve the ninth mental abiding it is like in this analogy: if you 
form a very strong habit of reciting a text or something like that then, when you 
recite it with the initial motivation to recite, even though your mind may wander 
off to something else in the meantime, the recitation itself will come to be continuous and effortless. Similarly, once you have placed your mind in equipoise 
through being mindful of having initially di rected it towards the meditation object, 
if you are then alert, even though you may not keep it up each time, you will become capable of entering a meditative stabilization continuously over a long 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
767 period without distraction disrupting it. [This stage where] there is no need for 
effort at keeping it up is said to be without application or exertion.  
 
On the occasion of the ninth mental abiding, one effortlessly engages in meditative 
stabilization. An analogy that illustrates this is that it is similar to having a strong familiarity 
with a certain text, sadhana, or prayer, due to which, when one begins to recite it with the 
initial motivation to do so, even though the mind  then wanders off, the recitation will remain 
continuous and effortless. Similarly, having set the mind in equipoise on its observed object, 
without the need to apply mindfulness and intr ospection one will abide on the object for a 
long time. At this point it is  said that there is no longer  any need to make effort.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2B2D How the ninth mental abiding has to be preceded by the earlier ones in 
turn 
For it to arise, long-lasting meditative  stabilization that cannot be impeded by 
unfavourable conditions like sinking and excitement has to arise first through 
mindfulness, introspection and effort applie d each time [they are needed]. Yet that 
is the eighth one. {277} It is like the nint h one in that unfavourable conditions like 
sinking and excitement cannot disrupt your  meditative stabilization. As it is 
nevertheless necessary here to rely on mindfulness and introspection constantly, it 
is said to be associated with application or exertion.  
 In order to achieve the ninth mental abiding, the previous mental abidings must have been 
successively generated. In other words, in orde r to generate the ninth mental abiding, one 
must first rely on mindfulness, introspection, and effort whereby one’s meditative stabilization will no longer be impeded by the ar isal of laxity and excitement, this being the 
eighth mental abiding. It is similar to the nint h mental abiding in that the discordant classes 
of mindfulness and introspection can no longer  obstruct one’s meditative stabilization. 
However, since during the eight mental abidin g it is necessary to rely constantly on 
mindfulness and introspection,  effort is still needed.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 For that to arise there is a need to stop even subtle si nking, excitement, and so on 
as soon as they arise without acquiesc ing in them which is why you need the 
seventh [abiding of the] mind. For that to arise there is a need for powerful 
introspection standing guard so that there is no outward movement towards 
conceptual thought and secondary afflicti ons, mental wandering brought on by 
those two having been understood to be faults – therefore you need the fifth and 
sixth [abiding of the] mind, because thos e two are achieved through the power of 
introspection.  
 For the eighth mental abiding to arise, one must generate the seventh mental abiding in 
which subtle laxity and excitement are immedi ately stopped upon their arisal. Prior to the 
seventh, it is necessary to generate the intr ospection that sees discursive thoughts and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
768 secondary afflictions as faults, whereby the fi fth and sixth mental abidings are necessary 
because they are achieved by the power of introspection. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
For something like that to arise, again, there is a need for mindfulness that soon 
remembers the meditation object even if one’s mind has been distracted [away] 
from it and [there is a need for] mindfu lness that does not allow any distraction 
from the meditation object from the star t – therefore you need the third and forth 
[abiding of the] mind, beca use those two [minds] are achi eved by the two [types of] 
mindfulness. For that to arise, again, there is a need first of all to tie the mind to 
the meditation object and not to wander off while it is tied, which means prior 
generation of the first and se cond [abiding of the] mind. 
 
For the fifth and sixth mental abidings to be gene rated, it is necessary to previously generate 
the mindfulness that immediately remembers the ob served object when the mind is distracted 
from it as well as the mindfulness that from  the beginning does not allow the mind to be 
distracted. Therefore, one needs the third an d fourth mental abidings because they are 
achieved by the power of mindfulness. Then, for the third and fourth mental abidings to 
arise, it is necessary to first generate the first and second mental abidings – setting and 
continuous setting – that tie the mind to the observed object an d do not allow the mind to be 
distracted. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2B2E The summarized meaning of all that 
Thus in short you should first follow the instructions you have heard and do your 
best at the method of plac ing the mind in equipoise. Then think again and again 
of the placement sustaining the continuit y by way of the ability to piece short 
periods together. Then, if mindfulness deteriorates and your mind wanders, you 
should gather it quickly, and quickly become mindful of having forgotten the 
meditation object. Then you should ge nerate even stronger mindfulness and 
generate a power of mindfulness [such that] the mind does not wander off from 
the meditation object from the start.  
 
In short, one must first follow the instructions that one has heard and strive to set the mind 
on its object. Then, at the time of continuo us setting, one needs the power of thinking 
whereby one becomes able to set the mind for short periods of time on its observed object. If one’s mindfulness degenerates and the mind wa nders, one should quic kly gather the mind 
inward without forgetting the observed obje ct. Then one should generate even strong 
mindfulness such that the mind is not distra cted from its object right from the start. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Accomplishing powerful mindfulness, seeing  the fault of sinking, excitement, and 
so on where you wander off from the meditation object to something else, you 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
769 should develop intense introspection stan ding guard [over it]. Then you should 
generate the strong endeavour that immediately recognizes distraction brought on 
by forgetfulness – however subtle – that cuts  it short and, once it is cut, lengthens 
the time where it is not impeded by  unfavourable conditions. {278}  
 
By means of generating introspection one shou ld stop laxity and excitement. Even if the 
distraction is subtle one should immediately reco gnize this and generate the strong effort that 
cuts it off.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Once that has arisen, with effort, having perfected the habit through 
familiarization, you will achieve the ninth [abiding of the] mind that moves into 
meditative stabilization without any exerti on. In fact before, when you have not 
achieved the ninth mind, it is necessary to make an outward effort [at] putting the mind into meditative stabilization by me ans of yoga. But once you have attained 
the ninth mind, even though you do not make an effort [at] putting the mind into meditative equipoise deliberately, it will move into meditative stabilization completely.  
 By means of familiarity, one then achieves the ninth mental abiding whereby remains in 
single-pointed meditation without effort. As lo ng as one has not achieved the ninth mental 
abiding, the yogi needs to make effort, whereas when the ninth mental abiding has been achieved effort is no longer needed.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 When despite the achievement of the ni nth mind, which is like that, you have 
not achieved pliancy, you cannot claim to have achieved calm abiding yet, either, 
let alone special insight, as will be explained later. 
 
Even though one has achieved the ninth mental  abiding, as long as one has not achieved 
pliancy one has not yet achieved calm abiding. Only when one’s meditative stabilization is 
conjoined with a pliancy that produces physic al and mental bliss, has calm abiding been 
achieved. Since prior to this one has not achieved  calm abiding, there is no need to mention 
that special insight has not been achieved.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 278) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1B2B-2B3 How there are four types of attention 
A The actual [types] 
The Shravaka Levels  explain that the nine mental abidings are associated with four 
types of attention. Explained in accordance with that there is forced engagement  at 
the time of the first and second minds, be cause there is a need to force it with 
effort. Then, at the time of [the following] five minds you cannot sustain 
meditation sessions for long [yet] due to  interruptions caused by sinking and 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
770 excitement, so at that time there is interrupted engagement . Then, the eighth mind 
can sustain meditation sessions for long, interruptions by sinking and excitement 
having become impossible, so at that time there is uninterrupted engagement . Then, 
the ninth mind is not interrupted and do es not require constant effort, so the 
attention of effortless engagement  is established. 
 
The Hearer Grounds  says that there are four types of mental attention that assist the 
attainment of the nine mental abidings. At the time of the first and second mental abidings, 
there is forced engagement. At the time of the third through the seventh mental abidings – 
resetting, closely setting, disciplining or taming , pacifying, and thorough ly pacifying – there is 
interrupted engagement, meaning that the engage ment is interrupted from time to time. At 
the time of the eighth mental abiding, there is uninterrupted engagement, and at the time of 
the ninth mental abiding there is effortless en gagement. These four types of engagement are 
necessary in order to achieve the nine mental abidings. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B Presenting a doubt 
Now, there is interrupted engagement at th e time of the first and second [abidings 
of the] mind and there is also a need to force it at the time of the five intervening 
minds. Why is it that the first and second are not said have interrupted 
engagement and that the intervening five ar e not said to have forced engagement?  
 
A doubt is raised here: since at the time of setting and continuous setting there is also 
interrupted engagement, and at the time of th e middle five mental abidings there is also 
forced engagement, why are the first and second mental abidings not said to have interrupted engagement and the next five to have forced engagement?  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C The response to that 
Between the [spells of] meditative stabiliz ation and non-meditative stabilization you 
go through, the latter are far longer with  the first and second [abidings of the] 
mind. With the intervening five, abiding in meditative stabilization is far longer. 
Therefore, the term “interru ption of meditative stabiliz ation” has been applied to 
them rather than to the fo rmer. {279} That is why al though the two [sets] are 
indeed the same in having forced engageme nt they are not the same in the context 
of having or not having interrupted enga gement. The five intervening minds have 
not been categorized as attentions  that have forced engagement.  
 
During the first and second mental abidings, th e periods of non-meditative stabilization are 
longer than the periods of meditative stabiliz ation, whereas during the middle five mental 
abidings, the periods of meditative stabilization,  in which the mind abides on its object, are 
longer than the periods of non-meditative stab ilization. Because of this, the five mental 
abidings are said to be “interrupted,” whereas the first and second mental abidings are so 
short that they cannot be said to be “interrupt ed.” Thus the first two mental abidings require 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
771 forced engagement but are not said to requir e interrupted engagement, whereas the middle 
five mental engagement are not said to need  forced engagement because the periods of 
meditative stabilization are longer but still have periods of interruption. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D The need for continuous efforts along those lines 
In the Summary of Perfections  it says: 
 
Through uninterrupted yoga try hard 
to realize stable concentration. If you rest again and again, you will not get fire by means of friction. The method of yoga is like that: Do not give up till you gain the distinction. 
 You should achieve it in accordance with that statement. 
 
As is mentioned in the Summary of Perfections , continuous effort is necessary. By means of an 
uninterrupted yoga, one should put effort into  accomplishing concentration. If one relaxes 
the mind, that is, does not put much effo rt into equipoise, one will not accomplish 
concentration, just like fire will not arise with out constant friction. Therefore, one should not 
give this yoga up. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C The measure of having achieved calm abiding 
1 Indication of the dividing line between having achieved it or not 
2 General indication of how to tread the path in reliance on calm abiding 
3 Indication of how to tread a worldly path in particular 
The third one has three points: indication  of the dividing line between having 
achieved it or not, general indication of how to tread the path in reliance on calm 
abiding, and indication of how to tr ead a worldly path in particular.  
 
First the boundary between having achieved or not having achieved calm abiding is set out. 
Then it is explained in general how to progress on  the path in reliance on calm abiding. This 
is followed by an explanation of how to progre ss on the worldly or mundane path of the four 
concentrations and so forth in particular. Becaus e the latter is not a path to liberation, it is 
called a mundane path.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1 Indication of the dividing line between having achieved it or not 
A Indication of the actual point 
B The signs of having [gained] attention along with a clarification of doubts 
The first one has two points: indication of the actual point and the signs of having 
[gained] attention along with a clarification of doubts. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
772 2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1A Indication of the actual point 
1 Indicating whether or not calm abiding has been achieved in terms of whether or not pliancy 
has been perfectly achieved 
2 Indicating the method by which calm abiding is accomplished once pliancy has been 
achieved 
The first one has two points: indicating whether or not calm abiding has been 
achieved in terms of whether or not pl iancy has been perfectly achieved and 
indicating the method by which calm ab iding is accomplished once pliancy has 
been achieved. 
 
When pliancy is perfectly achieved, calm abidin g is achieved, whereas as long as pliancy has 
not been achieved, even though one has a single -pointed mind, one has not yet achieved calm 
abiding. Then the way in which calm abiding is  achieved once pliancy has been achieved is 
explained.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1A-1 Indicating whether or not calm abiding has been achieved in terms 
of whether or not pliancy has been perfectly achieved 
A Presenting a doubt 
First: With the ninth [abidi ng of the] mind – if you can sustain long meditation 
sessions free from subtle sinking and excitement as has been explained earlier, and 
if you attain meditative stabilization th at operates spontaneously regardless of 
efforts at applying mindfulness and intr ospection continuously, has calm abiding 
been achieved or not?  
 
Here the question is raised: when with the ni nth mental abiding one is able to remain in 
meditation for long periods free from subtle la xity and excitement and one has achieved the 
meditative stabilization that no longer needs effort in applying mindfulness and introspection, has calm abiding been achieved or not?  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B The response to that 
As regards the attainment of meditative stabilization there are two [types], [one] 
where pliancy has been achieved and [one ] where it has not. If pliancy has not 
been achieved it is a similitude of ca lm abiding rather than calm abiding.  
 
The response to the question is that as long as  one has not achieved pliancy, it is not actual 
calm abiding but is only a similitude of calm ab iding. In order to achieve actual calm abiding 
the pliancy that induces physical and mental  bliss is necessary, and only when one’s 
meditative stabilization is conjoined with  these has calm abiding been achieved. 
 Tuesday afternoon, 30 January 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 279) says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
773 C The reason  
In [the Sutra ] Unravelling the Intent  it says: 
 
‘Bhagavat, if the bodhisattva directs his mental activity inwards at the 
mind [itself] while physical and mental pliancy have not been achieved, 
what do you call that mental activity ?’ {280} ‘Maitreya, it is not calm 
abiding, but should be referred to as  attention that is a similitude of 
calm abiding and of the same kind.’ 
 The sutra says: “’Bhagavan, however much the bodhisattva sets his mind on the observed 
object, focusing inside, as long as pliancy of bo dy and pliancy of mind have not been attained 
what kind of mind is that?’ Ma itreya, that is not calm abiding,  but is said to be concomitant 
with the aspiration that is a similitude of calm abiding.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In Ornament of the Sutras , too, it says:  
 
Thanks to habit ther e’s non-application. 
Then his body and mind obtain 
extreme, overwhelming pliancy and he is said to possess attention. 
 
In this context “attention” is calm abiding. 
 
This sutra says that through fami liarity with meditative stabilizat ion there is no need to apply 
oneself, and that having attained physical and mental pliancy one attains that called “together 
with mental attention.” This single-pointed me ditative stabilization which is effortless and 
held by pliancy is calm abiding.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In the Middle  Stages of Meditation  it also clearly says: 
 
When body and mind of the one who has thus formed a habit of calm 
abiding become extremely pliable and [when] his mind becomes 
subservient with regard to meditation objects at will, he should understand that to be the point wh ere calm abiding is accomplished. 
 When one who has familiarized with calm abid ing obtains pliancy of body and mind and is 
able to meditate on an observed object for as long as is wished with a subdued mind free from 
distraction, one has achieved calm abiding.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
774 And from the Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom : 
 
Here, the bodhisattva dwelling alone in seclusion should direct his 
attention to the object just like it  is meant to be thought of. Having 
abandoned the mind’s discourse he should oftentimes direct his attention to the mind itself as it a ppears. As long as extreme pliancy of 
body and mind has not arisen, it is an  attention that is a similitude of 
calm abiding, while, once it has, it is calm abiding. 
 This text says that a bodhisattva who abides alon e in solitude should take to mind his desired 
purpose. Having abandoned mental talk, that is, conceptualizations, he should try as much as 
possible to mentally attend to the mind itself. However, as long as pliancy of body and mind 
have not arisen, this remains a mental attention that is a similitude of calm abiding. Only 
when pliancy of body and mind have arisen does one achieve calm abiding.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D Although the meditative stabilization before the achi evement of extreme pliancy belongs to the desire 
[realm] level, it is not a level of meditative equipoise in the desire [realm]  
Now, what level does meditative stabiliz ation before the achievement of extreme 
pliancy belong to? It belongs to the desire [realm] level. Although it has such single-
pointedness of mind, it is not a level of equipoise and it is not included within the 
so-called level of equipoise. The Actual Basis of the Levels  e x p l a i n s  t h i s  o n  t h e  
grounds that there is no regret and that it is not achieved through supreme bliss 
and pliancy.  
 
The meditative stabilization that arises prior to  the achievement of pliancy is included in the 
level of the desire realm, but it is not a leve l of meditative equipoise of the desire realm. 
Although it is a single-pointed mind included in the level of the desire realm, it is a level of 
non-equipoise and is not called “a level of equipo ise.” This is because this single-pointed mind 
is not accomplished by a lack of regret , supreme joy and happiness, and pliancy.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
E That meditative stabilization is the meditative stabilizat ion of a single-pointed mind of the desire 
[realm] 
If pliancy has not been achieved, meditati ve stabilization that does not rely on 
constant mindfulness and where the mind moves of its own accord without 
conceptual thought, still is {281} referred to as single-pointedness of the desire 
realm; it is not actual calm abiding. Appa rently, it can in fact be combined with 
any physical activity: moving, wa lking, lying, and sitting.  
 
That meditative stabilization is a meditative stab ilization of a single-pointed mind of the desire 
realm. If pliancy has not been achieved, the meditative stabiliz ation that is the mind moving 
by its own strength without conceptuality even  without relying on continuous mindfulness 
and that appears to be able to  be combined with all the cond ucts of walking moving, lying, 
and sitting, is a single-pointed mind of the de sire realm; it is not actual calm abiding.

Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
775  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 281) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1A-2 Indication of how calm abiding is achieved once pliancy has been 
fully accomplished 
A Explanation of how pliancy is identified and generated 
B Actual indication of how – once it has been fully accomplished – calm abiding is achieved  
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1A-2A Explanation of how pliancy is identified and generated 
1 Identification 
Now what is the method for attaining plianc y? Once it has been attained, what is 
the method for proceeding to calm abiding? This is what the Compendium of 
Abhidharma  says with regard to pliancy: 
 
What is pliancy? It is that work ability of body and mind due to 
breaking the continuity of taking on bad physical and mental states. Its 
function is to eliminate all obscurations. 
 
First pliancy is identified. What is the method for attaining pliancy? Having attained it, what 
is the method for progressing to calm abiding? The Compendium of Manifest Dharma  says that 
pliancy is a serviceability of body and mind be cause of having cut the continuity of negative 
physical and mental states, and that it has th e function of eliminating all obscurations.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 The meaning of the quotation from Compendium of Abhidharma  
Taking on bad physical and mental states [implies] that body and mind are unfit to 
be employed for any wholesome activity at will. Its antidote, physical and mental 
pliancy, [implies] an extreme workabilit y with regard to body and mind being 
employed for wholesome activities, which is due to freedom from taking on bad 
states, both physical and mental. Specific ally, if you strive to give up mental 
afflictions and bad physical states that tend towards mental afflictions and which 
are an obstacle to the joy of giving up me ntal afflictions, you will get rid of [the 
aspects of] unworkability such as heaviness of the body and then what will emerge 
is workability of the body.  
 
Negative states of body and mind arise when the body is tired the mi nd does not wish to 
engage in the virtuous action of meditating. Th eir antidotes, physical and mental pliancy, are 
free from all negative states of body and mind, whereby one’s body and mind become 
serviceable with respect to virt uous actions. When striving to  abandon the mental afflictions 
and the negative states of body that interfere with wanting to abandon the afflictions and that 
are the afflicted class, one must become free from an unserviceability of body, such as physical 
heaviness. Thereby the body becomes as light as cotton.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Similarly, if you strive to give up mental afflictions and bad mental states that 
tend towards mental afflictions and which are an obstacle to the joy of giving up 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
776 mental afflictions, you will get rid of the un suitability of the mind for being tied to 
the meditation object and engaging [in it ] with pleasure. Then the mind engaging 
in the meditation object unobstructed ly constitutes mental workability.  
 
Similarly, when striving to abandon the afflic tions and negative states of mind, one becomes 
free from the unsuitability of the mind to enga ge as one wishes in focusing on a virtuous 
object. The mind engaging its observed object without obstruction is serviceability of mind. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3 The reason for that  
In accordance with that Acharya Sthiramati says: 
 
In this regard physical workability is what gives rise to lightness and 
mobility with regard to activities of  the body itself. Workability of the 
mind is the other factor, produced from mind, the cause of the mind 
becoming happy after entering into [a state of] perfect attention. {282} 
If you are possessed of it, you will engage in objects unobstructedly, 
which is why it is called workability of the mind. 
 Serviceability of body is a lightness with respect to activities of the body. Serviceability of mind 
is a freshness and lightness of mind which engage s in perfect mental atte ntion. If one has it, 
one will engage the observed object withou t obstruction, due to which it is called 
“serviceability of mind.”   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In brief, once pliancy has been achieved, the unworkability of body and mind – 
the difficulty engaging in something out of concern as one engages in one’s own 
unworthy affairs despite the desire to strive at giving up mental afflictions – stops, 
and body and mind become extremely serviceable. In the Shravaka Levels  it says 
that such perfect workability of body and mind emerges in subtle parts right from 
the beginning when you [first] gain meditative  stabilization. So, as it increases, it 
eventually turns into pliant, single-pointed ca lm abiding. In fact at first it is subtle 
and therefore hard to recognize, but later on it becomes easy to recognize.  
 
In brief, once pliancy has been achieved, thro ugh averting the unserviceability of body and 
mind that makes it difficult to engage out of  concern for one’s own ba d activities, one comes 
to possess a body and mind that are easily employ ed, that is, one is able to engage as much as 
one wants in virtuous actions without any he aviness of body and mind. Such complete 
serviceability of body and mind arises righ t from the beginning when one first attains 
meditative stabilization. It gradually increases,  eventually becoming pliancy and single-pointed 
calm abiding. However, because at first it is subt le, it difficult to recognize, whereas later on it 
is easily recognized.  
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
777 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
4 How, among the two types of pliancy, mental pliancy is the first one to arise 
An early sign of such pliancy about to ar ise, complete with all its characteristics 
and easy to perceive, is a feeling of heav iness at the skullcap, experienced by the 
person striving for concentrative meditation . It is not an unco mfortable [sense of] 
heaviness. As soon as it arises you are rid of negative mental tendencies causing 
obstacles to the joy of giving up mental afflictions and its antidote, pliancy arises 
first193.  
 From among physical pliancy and mental pliancy, mental pliancy is the first one to arise. An 
early sign of having generated a fully qualified and easily perceived plia ncy is that the person 
striving to cultivate meditative stabilization ha s a heaviness in the brain, but it is not an 
unpleasant heaviness. As soon as it is generate d, one becomes free from the negative states of 
mind that interfere with wanting to abando n the afflictions and their antidote, mental 
pliancy, arises.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In Shravaka Levels  it says:  
 
As an early sign of the emergence of  gross and easily perceptible single-
pointedness of mind as well as pliancy of mind and body not being 
delayed much longer, his skullcap will seem heavy. In fact this symptom 
is not painful. As soon as it arises whatever negative mental tendencies belonging to the [sphere of] mental afflictions and causing obstacles to 
the joy of giving them up are abandoned and furthermore their antidote, workability of mind and mental pliancy, arise. 
 The sign that a single-pointed mind as well as pliancy of mind and body are about to arise is 
that a heaviness in the brain is produced, how it is not harmful. As soon as it has arisen, one 
abandons the negative mental states included in the afflicted class that interfere with wanting to abandon them and their antidotes, serviceabi lity of mind and mental pliancy, arise.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
5 How physical pliancy is born from that 
Then, based on the force of that pliancy of  the workable mind having arisen, {283} 
the energies that will cause physical plianc y to arise circulate within the body. If 
those energies pass through your body parts and pervade them you will get rid of 
                                              
193 Getting rid of negative mental tendencies and the deve lopment of the antidote are in direct opposition to 
one another: the more you have of one, the less the ot her one will be present. Otherwise the obvious question 
would be why the antidote develops if you are already rid of negative states. The rela tionship should rather be 
understood in these terms: at the point where the negative  states have come to an end, the antidote is fully 
developed – according to Geshe Thubten Soepa: "Like ho t and cold. One comes [as] the other one goes away." 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
778 negative physical states and the antidote to taking on negative states, physical 
plian cy, w il l aris e. F urt herm o re, aft er t hey h av e s pread in  t he en t ire bo dy it  will 
seem as though filled with the power of workable energy.  
 
From mental pliancy comes physical pliancy. By  the force of mental pliancy, the winds that 
are the cause that produces physical pliancy move in the body. When the winds pervade the 
parts of the body, one becomes free from negative states of body, such as physical heaviness, 
and their antidote, physical pliancy, arises. When t he body is filled with the winds, it is as 
though filled with the power of serviceable wind s. At this time there is physical pliancy. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In the Shravaka Levels  it says:  
 
Through its occurrence, the energies of the great elements arising in 
harmony with the occurrence of ph ysical pliancy move through the 
body. As they move, one is freed from  any negative states of the body 
that belong to the sphere of afflicti ons and cause obstructions to the joy 
of abandoning [them]. Their antidote, physical pliancy, spreads in the entire body so that it seems as  though it is full [of it]. 
 The winds of the great elements that accord  with generating physical pliancy move 
throughout the body without blockage. When th ey move, one becomes free from the negative 
states of body included in the afflicted class that interfere with wanting to abandon them and 
one’s body becomes completely filled wi th their antidote, physical pliancy. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
6 The difference between those two kinds of pliancy 
In this regard physical pliancy is a physic al, internal, extremely pleasant sensation, 
it is not a mental event. Says Master Sthiramati: 
 
If you are seized by the distinctive joy of the physical sensation, you 
should know it to be physical pliancy, for in the sutras it says that if the 
mind rejoices, the body will become pliant. 
 
What is the difference between mental pliancy an d physical pliancy? Physical pliancy is an 
extremely pleasant tactile object inside the body; it  is not a mental factor. If one is held by the 
special joy that is a tactile object of the body, it should be understood to be physical pliancy 
because the sutras say that a joyful  mind becomes physical pliancy. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
779 7 How among the two kinds of bliss, [that] of physical and [that] of mental pliancy, the bliss of physical 
pliancy arises first 
Thus when physical pliancy first arises , through the force of energies, a great 
experience of bliss is born within the bo dy based on which within the mind, too, a 
very exquisite experience of joyful bliss is produced.  
 
The bliss of physical pliancy arises before the bliss of mental pliancy.  When physical pliancy 
first arises, by the force of the winds moving in  the body, a great bliss arises in the body. In 
dependence on that, a great experience of joy and bliss also arises in the mind.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 283) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1A-2A7B Indicating the actual method of accomplishing calm abiding once pliancy 
has been fully achieved 
1 The actual [method] 
Then the force of [sensations associated with] that pliancy having occurred for the 
first time gradually diminishes. In fact this  process is not due to an exhaustion of 
pliancy. Rather the gross kind of plianc y has moved the mind excessively, so that 
once it has neutralized unshakable pliancy that is delicate like a shadow and in 
accord with meditative stabilization aris es. As the disappearance of joy reverses 
again, the mind {284} stays firmly on it s meditation object and calm abiding free 
from the disquiet of agitation caused by great joy is achieved. 
 
Having achieved pliancy, what is the actual method for accomplishing calm abiding? After 
that, the strength of the initially generated pl iancy gradually diminishes. It is not that the 
pliancy is exhausted, but due to that coarse pliancy moving the mind excessively it is averted 
and the unmovable pliancy that is thin like a shadow arises. As the vanishing of mental joy 
reverses, the mind abides firmly on its observed object and the calm abiding that is free from the disturbance of being agitated by great joy is achieved. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2 The reason for that 
From the Shravaka Levels : 
 
When that first arises the mind is joyful, at ease and supremely 
delighted and even the meditation  object appears endowed with 
manifest joy. Then that very force of pliancy which first develops, little 
by little turns extremely subtle and the body becomes possessed of 
shadow-like pliancy. The mind ab andons joy, and through calm 
abiding, becoming completely firm, engages in the meditation object utterly peaceful. 
 When that happens “attention” and calm abiding included in the preparatory 
stage of the first [level of] concen tration has been achieved. In the Shravaka Levels  it 
is explained that thereby the lesser a ttention of someone settling the mind in 
equipoise is achieved.

Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
780  
When that is first generated, the mind is joyful , the mind experiences much bliss, the mind is 
supremely joyful, and one manifestly enjoys the observed object. The strength of that pliancy 
that initially developed slowly becomes more an d more subtle, and the body comes to possess 
shadow-like pliancy. The mind th at was joyful abandons that, abides firmly on the object due 
to calm abiding, and engages the observed obje ct with the aspect of complete pacification. 
When this happens, one attains me ntal attention and the calm abid ing that is included in the 
preparation of the first concentration, whereby one attains the small mental attention of the 
level of meditative equipoise. There are seven le vels of preparation of the first concentration, 
such as the mental attention or  contemplation of a mere beginner and so forth, on which the 
afflictions of the desire realm have been abandoned.

Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 284) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1B The signs of being endowed with attention and clearing up doubts 
[about this] 
1 The actual signs of being endowed with attention 
2 Clearing up doubts [about them] 
The second one has two points: explaining the actual signs of being endowed with 
attention and clearing up doubts [about them]. 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1B-1 The actual signs of being endowed with attention 
A How this is set out in the Shravaka Levels  
First: The marks revealing whether oneself or someone else has attained 
“attention” are set out in the Shravaka Levels : through this attainment four minor 
ones are achieved: a mind belonging to the form realm, physical and mental 
pliancy as well as single pointedness. [F urthermore,] one is enabled to purify 
mental afflictions by means of calm and coarse194 kinds of paths or paths associated 
with aspects of the [four noble] truths, physical and mental pliancy arise very 
quickly when[ever] the mind settles in eq uipoise, the five impediments, craving for 
sense pleasures, sleepiness and the like do not occur for the most part, and even 
after rising from equipoise one is [still] imbued with some physical and mental pliancy.  
 The marks that make it understood that oneself or others have achieved the levels of mental 
attention are that the four are attained: (1) a mind included in the level of the form realm,  (2-3) physical pliancy and mental pliancy, and (4) single-pointedness. In addition, one is able to puri fy the afflictions by the paths having the as pect of peacefulness 
and coarseness (that is, seeing the lower realm, for example, the desire realm, as coarse and seeing the upper realm, for example, the firs t concentration, as peaceful) and having the 
                                             
 
194 A so-called worldly special insight that consists in lo oking at lower levels as coarse and higher levels as 
peaceful (cf. 2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C2 B). 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
781 aspect of the four noble truths,. When setting the mind in equipoise on the observed object, 
physical and mental pliancy arise very quickly. The five obscurations – aspiration for desirable 
objects, sleep, and so forth – do not occur for the most part. Having arisen from meditative 
equipoise into post-meditation or subsequent  attainment, the body and mind still possess 
some physical and mental pliancy.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B Once this attention has been achieved calm abiding easily becomes pure 
Once attention with these characteristics is  achieved {285} the path of calm abiding 
easily becomes pure195. After meditative equipoise of the mind in single-pointed 
calm abiding, physical and mental plianc y can quickly be induced so that pliancy 
increases. The Shravaka Levels  explain that the more pliancy increases, the more 
calm abiding also increases, whereby they cause one another to increase 
reciprocally. In brief, if the mind is workable, the energies become workable, at 
that stage special physical pliancy em erges and upon its emergence special 
meditative stabilization is born. Through that, again, special workability of the 
energies is achieved whereby physical and mental pliancy is induced. 
 Having achieved the mental attention that po ssesses such signs, the path of calm abiding 
easily becomes pure. At the conclusion of the meditative equipoise of single-pointed calm 
abiding, physical and mental pliancy can be quickly induced, whereby pliancy increases. 
However much pliancy increases, so too does ca lm abiding, whereby they  cause each other to 
increase. In brief, if the mind is serviceable, th e winds also become serviceable. At that time a 
special physical pliancy arises, and upon its arisal a special meditative stabilization is 
produced. Due to that, a special serviceability of the winds is achieved, whereby physical and 
mental pliancy are induced.   Although this is not easy to achieve, we shou ld try to achieve it. However, there is no point 
to doing such meditation for one or  two days and then giving it up. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C Statements from the Shravaka Levels  about how other positive qualities are also gained  
Furthermore the Shravaka Levels  explains that at the time the mind is first placed 
single-pointedly, it is placed without mind fulness or attention of anything else at 
all: 
 
In order not to turn to [any] marks and for the sake of non-distraction, 
you cause mindfulness and attention to be absent from the start by 
means of firm placement. 
 In order to not turn to any signs and to remain  without distraction, right from the beginning, 
by means of focusing firmly, one should not be mindful of or pay attention to anything else. 
                                             
 
195 In this context "becomes pure" means ‘becomes a hi gher level path’ (oral explanation by Geshe Thubten 
Soepa). 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
782 In other words, initially when setting the mind  single-pointedly on its observed object, one 
should not be mindful of or pay attention to anything else.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Once you have got used to that, the Shravaka Levels  go on to say:  
 
Through fixing closely on single-poi ntedness itself, come what may, and 
by gradually fixing closely on that calm abiding of the mind within, the 
mental continuum and all the continua of mind become free from marks, free from discursive though t and absolutely calm – in that 
manner, in just that manner, fix the mind.  
 
One should focus one’s mind single-pointedly in side on the observed object of calm abiding 
without allowing it to wander outside, whereb y the mental continuum and all the continua of 
mind will become free from marks, free from conceptualization or discursive thought, and 
completely pacified. In that way one should fo cus the mind single-pointedly on its object. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Once you have achieved the mental  state of calm abiding in that 
manner, if marks arise due to the fa ults of forgetfulness and lack of 
familiarity or if appearances arise through secondary mental afflictions, 
if holes start showing, if conceptual ization arises, you should not mind 
such occurrences nor attend to them  from the perspective of having 
seen them as faults earlier. Throug h not minding them nor attending to 
them in this manner those objects completely fall to pieces, and {286} when they have been cleared away placement will be on the very absence of appearances. “Sir, this object of meditation is subtle and hard to comprehend, therefore aspire and try hard in order to 
comprehend it.” 
 
The way meditative stabilization develops is set out in these terms. 
 Having achieved the mind of calm abiding in th is way, if marks and conceptualizations arise 
due to the faults of forgetfulness and lack of familiarity, if appearances arise due to secondary afflictions, if the doors of the senses are revealed, through immediately seeing them as faults, 
one should not be mindful of and pay attentio n to them. Due to not being mindful of or 
paying attention to them, these observed objects will be completely destroyed, and, when they 
have been eliminated, they will not appear at all, whereupon one should once again set the 
mind in equipoise. Oh handsome face, the observed  object is subtle and difficult to realize, 
therefore aspire and strive intensely for the sa ke of realizing it. An example of a subtle 
observed object is emptiness and the imperman ence of changing momentarily, these being 
difficult to realize.
