Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
783 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D Explaining the meaning of that [in detail] 
Up to “fix the mind,” it is being show n how the absence of marks and the other 
two develop gradually from sustaining [meditation] as before. Then up to “you 
should not [mind such occurrences],” it is being explained that in case marks etc. 
do appear in the mind even though calm abiding is present – because there is no 
great familiarity with it [yet] – you should remember the disadvantages of 
submitting to such mental states, not pu rsue them, and place [the mind] without 
any thoughts. 
 The citation up to “fix the mi nd” shows how the three, the abse nce of marks and so forth, are 
g r a d u a l l y  g e n e r a t e d .  T h e n  u p  t o  “ y o u  s h o u l d  n o t  [ m i n d  s u c h  o c c u r r e n c e s ] ”  s h o w s  t h a t  although one has developed calm abiding, if th e marks and so forth still appear to the mind 
i n  d e p e n d e n c e  o n  a  l a c k  o f  f a m i l i a r i t y  w i t h  i t  a n d  s o  f o r t h ,  o n e  s h o u l d  r e m e m b e r  t h e  
disadvantages of coming under the control of su ch minds, and without following them set the 
mind without thoughts. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Then the section up to “[placement]  will be ...,” explains that through 
familiarizing oneself in that way, whicheve r of the three, marks and so on, emerges, 
through the force of familiarity with not th inking anything at all it will subside by 
itself regardless of deliberate fixation of the mind. Then you abide in the non-
appearance of the three and you will not be ravished by them. The remaining [bit] 
indicates that this calm abiding is subtle and that it is difficult to comprehend the 
explanation of it. 
 Then up to “[placement] will be ...” indicates that whenever any of the three – marks and so 
forth – arise due to having familiarized thus , it will be pacified without depending on 
deliberately focusing the mind due to strong fa miliarity with not thinking about anything at 
all. Then one will abide in the non-appearance of the three and one will not be captivated by 
these distracted minds.  
 The rest of the citation shows that calm ab iding is subtle and difficult to realize the 
explanation of it. For example, if one puts a candy on one’s tongue, while oneself experiences 
its taste one is unable to descri be it to others, whereas one can show visible forms such as a 
pen, book, and so forth to others. Like this, th e experience of calm abiding is difficult to 
explain to others.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
E How awareness is not directed at marks  
In this regard “marks” are explained in te rms of ten marks – those associated with 
the five [sense] objects, forms etc., those a ssociated with the three poisons, as well 
as those associated with male and female . How they disappear: first a multiplicity 
of marks associated with forms etc. appear and as soon as they appear they subside 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
784 of their own accord getting purified. At la st, once equipoise has been established, 
the marks of forms, sounds, etc. do no t appear, only the mental aspects of 
knowing, clarity, and vibrant happiness do.  
 
One should not direct the mind to marks. Marks or signs are also explained in the Treasury of 
Manifest Dharma , but here they are explained to be the ten marks: (1-5) the marks of the five 
objects, forms (colors and shape), sounds (m usic and other sounds both pleasant and 
unpleasant), and so forth; (6-8) the marks of  the three poisons, attachment, hatred, and 
ignorance; and (9-10) the marks of males and females, that is, the bodily charac teristics that 
identify someone to be a man or a woman.  
 The way in which they become non-existent is that at first a variety of marks of objects, 
such as forms and so forth, appear to the mind  when meditating on calm abiding. Then as 
soon as they appear, they are naturally pa cified and purified. Then, at the end when 
equipoised, the marks of the five sense objects, forms and so forth, do not appear, and all that 
appears is the aspects of the mind of mere knowing, clarity, and vibrant happiness. In other words, having accomplished calm abiding, all that  appears is the nature of the mind itself of 
being knowing and clear.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
F How all conceptual thoughts also subside 
How they disappear: since you do not mind them nor attend to them as 
[described] before, conceptual thoughts th at have arisen, like bubbles emerging 
from water, cannot proliferate greatly or conjoin {287} and subside by themselves. 
Then, through sustaining [meditation] as  [explained] before, awareness of the 
experience and the appearance of bliss, without being stopped deliberately, subside 
by themselves and get purified as soon as they arise like a husk unable to withstand 
the pressure. Bliss and awareness of the experience become more subtle.  
 
Having achieved calm abiding, all concep tualizations are naturally pacified. How 
conceptualizations become non-existent is th at due to not being mindful of and paying 
attention to other objects, when conceptualizat ions arise they do not abide but are naturally 
pacified, like bubbles that arise from water an d quickly disappear again without remaining for 
long. Then, through cultivating such medita tion, the experience of knowing and the 
appearance of bliss, without being deliberately stopped, are naturally pacified and purified as 
soon as they arise, like a husk of rice unab le to withstand pressure, whereupon the bliss and 
the experience of knowing become subtle.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
G Differences between the time of meditative equipoise and thereafter 
At that time, when equipoise has been esta blished, there is no appearance of one’s 
body etc. and there is a sense that the mind  is no longer separate from space. As 
one arises [from meditation], it feels as if one’s body had suddenly emerged [again]. 
Afterwards, even afflictive conceptual thoughts associated with anger and the like 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
785 that arise are also different from before or originally: they are weak and incapable 
of conjoining over long periods of time.  
 
There are differences between the occasion of  meditative equipoise and the occasion of 
subsequent attainment. At the time of meditative  equipoise, that is, calm abiding, there is no 
appearance of the body and so forth, and the mind is experienced as though it has become 
inseparable from space. When one rises from that meditative equipoise into subsequent 
attainment or post-meditation, one’s body is  suddenly experienced again. At the time of 
subsequent attainment, although the conceptual izations of the afflictions such as hatred, 
attachment, pride, jealousy, excitement, and so forth arise, they are not the same as before, 
but are weak and unable to last for long. In stead they cease as soon as they arise. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
H Experiences of great clarity and the like also occur 
These periods are periods of what is ca lled “complete pacification”: in the 
experience of great clarity you feel you ca n count the minute particles of pillars and 
walls of a house. Due to strong stability the kind of sleep from the time before 
achieving meditative stabilization does not even come during the hours of sleep; it 
is as if mixed with meditative stabil ization and there are many pure dream 
appearances and the like. 
 
These occasions are called “complete pacification.” There is the experience of great clarity, as 
though one could count the subtle particles of th e pillars, walls, and so forth of one’s house. 
Due to the factor of abiding being dense, at th e time of sleep, sleep like that prior to the 
attainment of meditative stabilization does not come. Rather it is as if it were mixed with 
concentration and many pure dream appearances and the like arise. In other words, one has 
very clear dreams, whereas presently we have ma ny different types of dreams, including those 
that are bad. For example, some people dream of being dragged off by another person and so 
forth, some dream of flying though in actualit y they do not fly, and some dream that while 
their coarse body remains on the bed, their subtle body leaves it and travels to other places. 
Someone once asked me whether I could do that  but I said that I could not travel in my 
dreams. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 287) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1B-2 Eliminating a doubt 
A A doubt 
When the kind of meditative stabilizatio n explained above is achieved, which of 
the five paths is the context to place it in?  
 
Which of the five paths – the path  of accumulation, path of prep aration, path of seeing, path 
of meditation, and path of no-more-learning – is the meditative stabilization that was explained above?  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
786 B Its elimination  
If it is the meditative stabilization explai ned above, if it was cultivated after the 
view of selflessness had been uncontrovertibly ascertained and if it was established 
within the view196, it can be placed in the contex t of the path of liberation of 
ordinary beings. However, if it is a medita tion that has been cultivated otherwise, 
the Shravaka Levels  says that worldly paths associ ated with the view which has 
coarse and subtle aspects and which accomplishes the actual level of the first 
concentration, those paths are also accompli shed in dependence on this meditative 
stabilization. Therefore non-Buddhist sa ges {288} who free themselves from 
attachment by means of worldly paths up to that of Nothingn ess and those below, 
also need to move on to higher paths in dependence on it. It is thus a meditative 
stabilization common to non-Buddhists and Buddhists.  
 If it is the meditative stabilization explained ab ove that is cultivated after having unmistakenly 
ascertained the view of selflessness, it can be posited as a path of liberation of ordinary beings, that is, the path of accumulation. However, if  not such a meditation, it is a meditative 
stabilization that is a mundane path, for example,  an actual first concentration, that sees the 
upper level as peaceful and the lower level as coarse. Therefore, non-Buddhist sages, through 
meditating on the mundane path of the four co ncentrations and the first three of the four 
formless absorptions, can separate from the atta chment of these levels but cannot become free 
from the attachment of the Peak  of Existence. Thus, the first concentration and so forth are 
paths that are shared by both  Buddhists and non-Buddhists.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C If it is also seized by any of the three principal aspects of the path it becomes a path of liberation and 
so on 
Furthermore if it is seized by revulsion wi th samsara [born] from the view correctly 
realizing selflessness and from realizing the fa ults of limited existe nce, and if it is 
seized by the thought of re nunciation that seeks libera tion, it becomes a path of 
liberation. If it is seized by bodhichitta  it also becomes a Mahayana path. Likewise, 
for instance, if it is seized by the genero sity of giving [as little as] one small food 
offering to an animal and the intentio n of guarding any one type of ethical 
discipline, it will gradually turn into th e accumulations of the paths of liberation 
and of omniscience.  
 
If one’s calm abiding is held by any of the three principal aspects of the path – definite 
emergence, bodhichitta, or correct  view, it becomes a path to liberation and so forth, meaning 
t h a t  d u e  t o  i t  o n e  c a n  b e c o m e  f r e e  f r o m  c y c l i c  e x i s t e n c e  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  I f  i t  i s  h e l d  b y  a  repulsion for cyclic existence through the view  that unmistakenly realizes selflessness and 
through excellently realizing the fa ults of cyclic existence, and if  i t  i s  h e l d  b y  t h e  d e f i n i t e  
emergence that seeks liberation, it becomes a path  of liberation. If it is held by bodhichitta, 
the mind of enlightenment, it also becomes a ma hayana path. For example, if it is held by the 
                                             
 
196 A given meditation does not need to have this view as its object. Still, it remains linked with in so far as it is 
shaped by it, deriving its particular taste from  it (oral explanation by Geshe Thubten Soepa). 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
787 generosity of giving one morsel of food to an animal and the guarding of  any type of morality, 
it becomes a collection of the path to liberation and omniscience. In other words, if it is held 
by definite emergence it becomes a path to libera tion, whereas if it is held by bodhichitta it 
becomes a path to omniscience.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 However, here, what is being examined is not whether or not it becomes a path 
of liberation and omniscience when seized  b y  o t h e r  p a t h s .  R a t h e r  w h a t  i s  b e i n g  
examined is what the path becomes thro ugh the own essence of this meditative 
stabilization.  
 However, what is being examined here is not whether it becomes a path to liberation or a path to omniscience by means of being held by other paths. Rather what  is being examined is 
whether meditative stabilization becomes a path by means of its own entity.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D The consequent need to differentiate with regard to happiness, clarity, and non-conceptuality whether 
or not the meditation is associated with emptiness 
When it comes to meditations without mi ndfulness and mental activity which are 
called “unfabricated by the mind” and “wit hout grasping,” there are two [types] 
with respect to happiness, clarity, and no n-conceptuality: that which is and that 
which is not an emptiness meditation wi th equipoise established on the meaning 
of suchness. That is why it is absolutely crucial to differentiate them well, for there 
is much ground for mistaking that which is not a realization of suchness as [its] 
realization. If you do not differentiate them  as explained earlier, you will even take 
the meditative stabilization which is commo n to this Dharma and other [teaching]s 
to be the essence of completion stage of highest yoga tantra, so you should 
examine this at depth. 
 
It is necessary to differentiate  between what is and what is not a meditation on emptiness. 
The happiness, clarity, and non-conceptuality that are imputed “unfabricated by mind” and 
“without apprehension” with respect to medita tion that is without mindfulness and without 
mental attention are of two type s: that which is meditation on  emptiness which is equipoised 
on the meaning of suchness and that which is not.  It is very important to differentiate them 
since it is possible to mistake that which is no t a realization of emptiness for a realization of 
emptiness. If one does not diffe rentiate them, one will consider the meditative stabilization 
that is common to the Dharma and non-Buddh ist teachings to be the main point of the 
completion stage of highest yoga tantra. Because of this, it is necessary to investigate them 
well. In short, it is important not to mist akenly think that one has accomplished the 
completion stage of highest yoga tantra and that  therefore one no longer needs to do anything 
else.     
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
788 Wednesday afternoon, 31 January 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 288) says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C2 General indication of how to travel along the path relying on calm 
abiding 
A How important it is to cultivat e special insight relying on it 
Second: [Now] Should someone who has th us {289} achieved attention, the non-
conceptual meditative stabilization explained above, sustain just that non-
conceptuality characterized by clarity, non-conceptuality, and so on? Such 
meditative stabilization having been prod uced in the mental continuum is for the 
sake of producing special insight which dest roys the [mental] afflictions. Therefore, 
if special insight does not arise in depend ence on it, however much that meditative 
stabilization is trained it will not ev en be possible to abandon the mental 
afflictions of the desire realm, and if so no need to speak of all the mental 
afflictions. For that reason it is ne cessary to cultivate special insight.  
 
First there is a general indication of how to progress on the path in dependence on calm 
abiding, the first point being that it is importan t to cultivate special insight in dependence on 
calm abiding.   Having achieved the non-conceptual meditative  stabilization, mental attention, explained 
above, should one cultivate that mere non-conc eptuality that has the feature of clarity, non-
conceptuality, and so forth? Such meditative st abilization is produced in the continuum is for 
the purpose of generating the spec ial insight that destroys the affl ictions. Therefore, if special 
insight is not generated in dependence on that single-pointed meditative stabilization, no matter how much one familiarizes with this medi tative stabilization it will not be possible to 
abandon even the afflictions of the desire realm.  This being so, there is no need to mention 
that it will not be possible to abandon all the affl ictions. For this reason it is also necessary to 
meditate on and develop special insight.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B How there are two special insights to be cultivated 
In this regard there are two special insights, one that progresses along worldly 
paths abandoning manifest afflicti ons and one that progresses along 
transcendental paths abandoning the se eds of afflictions from the root. The 
former is a meditation with the coarse and peaceful aspects of seeing lower levels as 
coarser and higher ones as more peaceful and the latter is a view meditation with 
the sixteen aspects such as impermanence re lative to the four truths. The principal 
one [of these] set out in the Shravaka Levels  is the view realizing the selflessness of 
persons197.  
 
There are two types of special insight or higher seeing: 
                                              
197 "Selflessness" forms part of the 16 aspects of the four  noble truths. It is the most important one in this 
context. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
789 1. the special insight that prog resses by means of the mundan e paths that abandon manifest 
afflictions, and 
2. the special insight that progresses by mean s of the supramundane paths that abandon the 
seeds of the afflictions from the root. 
The mundane paths have the aspect of peaceful ness and coarseness that view the lower level 
as coarse and the higher level as peaceful. By means of them, one can temporarily abandon 
the manifest afflictions up to and includin g those of Nothingness, whereas one cannot 
abandon those of Peak of Existence. The supr amundane paths are meditations that view the 
four noble truths as having the aspects of th e sixteen, impermanence and so forth. However, 
the main one, as taught in Hearer Grounds , the view realizing the selflessness of persons, by 
which all the afflictions are abandoned. 
 The four noble truths are: true sufferings, tr ue origins, true cessations, and true paths. 
Each one of them has four attributes. For exampl e, the four attributes of true sufferings are 
impermanence, suffering, empty (empty of bein g a permanent, independent, and partless 
self), and selfless (empty of a self-sufficient subs tantially existent self). However, according to 
the Prasangika Madhyamikas the realization of  the emptiness of in herent existence is 
necessary in order to abandon th e root of the afflictions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C How calm abiding constitutes the basis for the progress of all non-Buddhist and Buddhist yogis on the 
path  
That being so, everyone – non-Buddhists  abandoning manifest afflictions by 
meditating on the path with coarse  and peaceful aspects and Buddhists 
abandoning afflictions from their root by meditating on the meaning of 
selflessness – requires the calm abiding explained above as the basis for 
abandoning afflictions of both non-Buddh ist and Buddhist yogis. Not only that, 
any Mahayana or Hinayana yogi also needs to accomplish that meditative 
stabilization, and as for Mahayana prac titioners, again, all Mantrayana and 
Paramitayana yogis need to accomplish th at calm abiding. Therefore this calm 
abiding is crucial as the basis of progress on the paths of all yogis. 
 Calm abiding is the basis for progressing on th e paths of all non-Buddhist and Buddhist yogis. 
Non-Buddhists abandon the manifest afflictions by  meditating the path that has the aspect of 
peacefulness and coarseness, and Buddhists abandon the afflictions from the root by 
meditating on the meaning of selflessness. Howe ver, both of these require the calm abiding 
explained above as a basis for abandoning the a fflictions, both the coarse and the subtle. In 
addition, mahayana and hinayana yogis must also  accomplish that meditative stabilization. 
Also all practitioners of both the mantra vehi cle and the perfection vehicle must accomplish 
calm abiding. Therefore, this calm abiding is  important as the basis for progressing on the 
paths of all yogis.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
790 D How it is unacceptable for us Buddhists not to have special insight realizing selflessness  
For Buddhists {290} it is not unacceptable to lack the former of those two special 
insights, but the latter special insight real izing selflessness is an element [of the 
path] whose lack is unacceptable. In fact if you attain the calm abiding explained 
above which is part of the level of the preparation [stage] for the first 
concentration, you can achieve the libe ration which is freedom from all the 
various fetters of cyclic existence by cult ivating special insight based on it – even 
without achieving the calm abiding of higher concentrations or formlessness. If 
you do not realize the very essence of self lessness and fail to meditate on it, you 
will not escape from cyclic existence even if, based on calm abiding explained 
above, you abandon all manifest afflic tions up to [the level of] “complete 
nothingness” by means of worldly special in sight and thus attain [the mental state] 
“peak of existence”. 
 Buddhists need the special insight realizing se lflessness as without th is they cannot progress 
on the path. Thus, although amon g the two types of special insi ght it is acceptable to not 
develop the mundane special insight that views th e lower level as coarse and the upper level as 
peaceful, it is not acceptable to lack the spec ial insight realizing selflessness, the branch or 
cause without which one cannot trav el the path to liberation. If one attains the calm abiding 
included in the level of the preparation of the first concentration, even without achieving the 
calm abiding of the higher concentrations or formless absorptions up to the Peak of 
Existence, one can achieve the liberation that  is a freedom from all the bonds of cyclic 
existence by cultivating special insight in de pendence on that. However, if one does not 
realize and does not meditate on the thusness that  is selflessness, even if one attains the mind 
of the Peak of Existence by abandoning all the manifest afflictions up to and including those 
of Nothingness, the third level of the formless absorptions, by means of calm abiding and the 
mundane special insight that depends on it, si nce one has not abandoned the afflictions from 
the root one will not become free from cyclic existence. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
E The reason for that 
From Praise of the Praiseworthy : 
 
Beings who fail to turn to your teachings, 
who are blinded by ignorance, 
having gone to the Peak of Existence still gain lives again where there is pain. Those who follow your  teachings, without 
attaining the basis of stabilization still avert existences, while being looked over by the Maras. 
 Those beings who do not turn to the Dharma bu t are blinded by ignorance due to lacking the 
eye of wisdom, even though they go up to the Pe ak of Existence, they are unable to completely 
abandon suffering and later on, having exhausted th e virtue of their past meditation, they will 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
791 once again fall to the lower realms as animals, hungry ghosts, and hell beings. In other words, 
suffering will arise for them once again and th ey will accomplish exis tence. Those, on the 
other hand, who follow the teachings of the Buddha will avert existence even without 
achieving an actual concentration while being watched by the maras. This will come about 
due to their realization of realizing selflessness which abandons the afflictions by the root and 
makes it impossible for the maras to harm th em. Among the four maras – the mara of the 
aggregates, the mara of the afflic tions, the mara of death, and th e mara of the sons-of-the-gods 
– no matter what harm the sons-o f-the-gods try to inflict on th em, they will not be able to 
harm them.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
F Yogis of highest yoga tantra also need to journey along the path based on calm abiding 
For that reason even yogis of highest yo ga tantra who do not develop the special 
insight directed at the multiplicity of peaceful and coarse aspects and the calm 
abiding accomplished by means of it, those yogis still need to develop one kind of 
calm abiding. In fact the point where it firs t arises is during the generation stage.  
 Even practitioners of highest yoga tantra need to  rely on calm abiding in order to travel their 
path. Because of that, even those yogis of highes t yoga tantra who do no t generate the special 
insight observing the varieties, that is, conv entional truths, that have the aspect of 
peacefulness and coarseness as well as the calm abiding accomplished by that, must generate 
one calm abiding. In fact during the generati on stage when one meditates on carrying death 
into the path to the truth body, the intermedia te state into the path to the enjoyment body, 
and rebirth into the path to the emanation bo dy, calm abiding is necessary in order to 
visualize, for example, the thirty-two deities of  Guhyasamaja, the sixty-four deities of Heruka 
Chakrasamvara, the thirteen de ities of Yamantaka, and the many deities of Kalachakra. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C3 Indication of how to progress on worldly paths in particular 
A How the distinguishing features of two [kinds of] beginners are explained in the Shravaka Levels   
Here the Shravaka Levels  explain that from the ninth mental abiding onward,198 you 
are a beginner with regard to attention as long as you have not achieved 
attention,199 while someone who has achieved atte ntion and wants to purify mental 
afflictions cultivating the attention th at knows the various characteristics200 is a 
beginner with regard to purifying afflictions.  
 
                                              
198 JN: “from [the point of view of] the nine stages of  mental abiding” changed to “from the ninth mental 
abiding onward” ( sems dgu pa nas bzung ). 
199 This term for the stage where calm abiding has b een accomplished was mentioned in 2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-
3B2F-1C1A-1C and 2B4B-2B3B-3C1C-3B2F-1C1A-2A7B-2.

note 20. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
792 There are two different kinds of beginners explained in the Hearer Grounds . From the ninth 
mental abiding onward, as long as one has not attained a mental attention ( yid la byed pa , also 
translated as ‘mental contemplation’), one is a be ginner at mental attention. This first level of 
mental attention, a mere beginner, is not able to abandon any affliction s, whereas the first of 
the six mental attentions purifying affliction s is the mental attention of the individual 
knowledge of the character. The afflictions of the desire realm are divided into three main 
divisions: the great, middling, and small, which are each further divided into great, middling, 
small. Thus, there are the great of the great, th e middling of the great, the small of the great, 
the great of the middling, middling of the middlin g, small of the middling, great of the small, 
middling of the small, and small of the small afflictions, due to which their respective 
antidotes are also divided into nine. 
 [Having attained mental attention, one who meditates the mental attention of individual 
knowledge of the character out of the wish to purify the affliction s, is a beginner at completely 
purifying the afflictions.] 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B The need to differentiate those two properly 
{291} If this point explained in the Shravaka Levels  is not ascertained properly, the 
false impression will arise that the lo west of the paths of concentration and 
formlessness is the access stage of the fi rst concentration, and that the first 
instance of mind gathered by means of th at access stage [concentration] constitutes 
realization of the various characteristics,  as it is the first of six attentions201 that are 
explained, realizing the various characteristics.  
 
It is important to differentiate these two well as otherwise it will erroneously seem that the 
lowest of the paths of the concentrations and formless absorptions being the preparation of 
the first concentration, that sinc e the first of the six mental atte ntions explained is individual 
knowledge of the character, the first mind gene rated that is included in a preparation is 
individual knowledge of character. In other words,  it is mistaken to think that when the first 
of the six mental attentions – the mental a ttention of the individual knowledge of the 
character – is generated, that one has achieved a preparation of the first concentration. This is 
because the previous mind, that of calm abid ing, is also included in the level of the 
preparation of the first concentration. This mi nd of calm abiding is called “mere beginner.” 
The six mental attentions are: (1) individual  knowledge of the character, (2) belief, (3) 
isolation, (4) joy-withdrawal, (5) analysis, and (6) final training. They are discussed in detail in 
the text Concentrations and Formless Absorptions . 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
                                              
201 Those six attentions are not equivalent to the four  attentions described above (p. 43). Rather they are 
another set of attentions associated with special co ntemplations which, howeve r, Tsongkhapa does not 
elaborate on here. Cf. also notes 25 and 20. 
FPMT Basic Program  Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path    ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
793 C Establishing that this is well founded  
This position is inadmissible because there is no developing the access stage of the 
first concentration without thus achieving calm abiding, and if that access stage 
has not been achieved, calm abiding is not achieved202. Also, since realization of 
the various characteristics is an analytical meditation, it is impossible by means of 
that meditation to newly accomplish the calm abiding that has not been achieved 
before.  
 
This position is incorrect. Without achieving ca lm abiding, it is not possible to generate the 
preparation of the first concentration. If the preparation of the first concentration is not 
achieved, calm abiding is not achieved. Since the individual knowledge of the character is 
analytical meditation, it is not possible to newl y achieve calm abiding by means of meditating 
it. In other words, first it is ne cessary to achieve calm abiding. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D The need for beginners to achieve calm abiding from the attention which is the access stage to the 
first [concentration] before the attention that realizes the various characteristics,  access stage of the first 
concentration 
Therefore the first of six attentions of  the access stage is the beginning of 
cultivating the special insight belonging to the access stage. However, it is not at 
the beginning of the mere first [level] a ccess stage [concentration] – calm abiding 
belonging to the access stage has to go be fore. All high meditative stabilizations 
prior to achievement of the meditative st abilization belonging to the first access 
stage are single-pointed minds of the desire realm203, which is also why, according 
to the great texts, those who achieve calm abiding seem to be very few. 
 The way to get rid of attachment to th e desire realm by means of the six access 
stage attentions is not written down here for fear of wordiness. 
 
In order to achieve the mental attention of in dividual knowledge of the character, it is 
necessary to first achieve calm abiding. Ther efore, the mental attention of individual 
knowledge of the characte r, the first of the six mental attentions, is included in special 
insight. It is not the beginning of the mere fi rst preparation because it must be preceded by 
the calm abiding that is included in the prepar ation. All meditative stabilizations prior to 
attaining the meditative stabilization included in  the preparation of the first concentration are 
single-pointed minds of the desire realm. For th is reason, according to the great texts very few 
people achieve calm abiding, meaning that al though there are many people who think that 
they have achieved calm abiding, in fact they have not actually achieved it. Because it would 
                                              
202 In other words calm abiding and th e access stage to the first concentrat ion are attained simultaneously and 
may be thought of as synonymous. This access stage is not the first concentration pr oper and therefore not its 
lowest path. Between the realization of calm abiding and the attainment of the actual first concentration there 
is a phase of investigative meditation – the so-called six attentions. It follows that the first of those is not the 
first mental state of the access stage, as the latter is pr eceded by actual calm abidin g which is the first mental 
state of the access stage. (Expla nation by Geshe Thubten Soepa)

Special Insight 
From Dzong-ka-ba’s 
Medium Exposition of the Stages of the Path to Enlightenment  
Practiced by Persons of Three Capacities  
Translated by Jeffrey Hopkins 
 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 291) says: 
 
PART ONE: IGNORANCE 
 
The presentation of the mode of training  in special insight has four parts: its 
prerequisites, divisions, mode of cultivatio n, and the measure of having established 
it through cultivation. 
 
In order to train in special insight it is necessa r y  t o  r e l y  o n  t h e  c o l l e c t i o n  o r  c a u s e s  ( t h e  
prerequisites) of special insight.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Introduction 
The presentation of the prerequisites for special insight has two parts: a general 
exposition of the prerequisites and ho w to settle the view in particular. 
General exposition of the prer equisites for special insight 
Kamalashila’s second [o f three works on the] Stages of Meditation1 says that relying 
on an excellent being, seeking hearing of  the doctrine from him/her, and proper 
contemplation are the three prer equisites for special insight. 
 
In general in order to develop special insight it is  necessary to rely on holy beings, to seek to 
hear the Dharma, that is, receive teachings, from them, and then, having received their 
teachings, to reflect on them properly. These are the three collections  o r  c a u s e s  t h a t  a r e  
necessary for the development of spec ial insight which are set out in the Middle Stages of 
Meditation . 
 
                                              
1 bhavanakrama 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
795 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Need for hearing and thinking about the scriptures: 
 The generation of the view realizing suchness by the wisdoms of hearing and 
thinking—having heard the stainless te xts in dependence on a scholar who 
unmistakenly knows the essentials of the scriptures—is an essential prerequisite for 
special insight. For, if you do not have  the view penetrating the meaning of the 
mode of being, it is impossible to gene rate a realization of  the special insight 
cognizing the mode [of being of phenomena, that is, emptiness]. 
 
First it is necessary to hear or listen to teachi ngs. The causes of specia l insight are relying on 
the wise who understand the essential points of  the Buddha’s teachings correctly, to hear or 
listen to stainless texts, and to develop the view realizing suchness by means of the wisdom 
arisen from hearing and the wisdom arisen from thinking. If one of these causes is missing it 
will not be possible to develop special insight, therefore all three are in dispensable. This is 
because if one does not have the view that has come to a decisive conclusion about the final 
nature of phenomena, that is, if one does no t understand emptiness or suchness perfectly, 
one will not be able to generate the special insi ght realizing suchness, that is, realizing things 
as they are. In short, due to lacking one of th ese causes, it will not be possible to generate 
special insight.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Need to follow any of the great  chariots: 
 F u r t h e r m o r e ,  s u c h  a  v i e w  m u s t  b e  s o u g h t  b y  o n e  w h o  i s  r e l y i n g  n o t  o n  t h e  
interpretable meaning but on the definitive  meaning. Hence, it is necessary to 
understand the meaning of scriptures of  definitive meaning within knowing the 
difference between the interpretable and the definitive. 
 Concerning this, if you do not rely on a treatise by one of the great valid openers 
of a chariot-way who commented on [Buddh a’s] thought, you will be like a blind 
person without a guide going in a directio n of fright. Thus, you should rely on a 
non-mistaken commentary [of Buddha’s thought]. 
 It is necessary to this view by relying on defi nitive meaning teachings and not on interpretive 
meaning teachings. Therefore, through clearly understanding the difference between interpretive meaning and definitive meaning, one must comprehend the Buddha’s teachings 
or scriptures that are definitive meaning. For this one needs to rely on the treatises by the 
great trail-blazers who are valid. Without rely ing on them, one will be like a blind person 
without a guide who is unable to arrive at any destination but wanders off in the wrong 
direction. In short, one must rely on commentaries that teach the unmistaken meaning of the 
Buddha’s scriptures.   
Thursday morning, 1 February 2007 
 It is necessary to understand the difference between definitive meaning and interpretive 
meaning, teachings and then to rely on the de finitive meaning teachings given by the Buddha. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
796 It is also necessary to rely on the treatises of valid beings, as otherwise one will be like a blind 
person without a guide who goes  to the wrong destination.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 292) says: 
Need to seek view in dependence on Nagarjuna’s system: 
 On whom should you rely? He is the Superior Nagarjuna widely renowned in the 
three levels [below, above, and on the ground] whom the Supramundane Victor himself prophesied very clearly in many  sutras and tantras as unraveling the 
essence of his teaching—the profound meaning [of emptiness] free from all 
extremes of [inherent] existence and no [n ominal] existence. Therefore, you should 
seek the view realizing emptiness in dependence on his texts. 
 
It is necessary to seek the view that relies  on Nagarjuna’s system . The Buddha Bhagavan 
prophesied that one called Nagarjuna, renowned  throughout the three levels (above, on, and 
under the ground) would explain the essence of  the teachings, the profound meaning free 
from all extremes of existing and not exis ting. Therefore, through depending on his 
scriptures, one should seek th e view realizing emptiness. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Identification of the model Madhyamikas: 
 S i n c e  A r y a d e v a  i s  t a k e n  t o  b e  a s  v a l i d as the master [Nagarjuna] by the great 
Madhyamikas such as the masters Buddh apalita, Bhavaviveka, Chandrakirti, 
Shantarakshita, and so forth, both the father [Nagarjuna] and his spiritual son 
[Aryadeva] are sources for the other Madh yamikas. Therefore, earlier [Tibetans 
rightly] called those two the “Madhyamik as of the model texts” and called the 
others “partisan Madhyamikas”. 
 
Aryadeva is considered by Buddhapalita, Bhav aviveka, Chandrakirti , Shantarakshita, and 
others to be similar to the master, Nagarjun a (Buddhapalita is a Prasangika Madhyamaka, 
Bhavaviveka a Svatantrika Madhyamaka, Chan drakirti a Prasangika Madhyamaka, and 
Shantarakshita a Yogachara Svatantrika Madhyam aka). Therefore, the father, Nagarjuna, and 
his son, Aryadeva are the source of other Madh yamikas. Earlier scholars called Nagarjuna and 
Aryadeva “past Madhyamikas” and called the ot hers “partisan Madhyamikas” because they 
claim that their tenets are right wh ereas other tenets are mistaken.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Designation of names of Madhyamikas by how conventionalities are posited: 
 Some earlier [Tibetan] spiritual guides [wrongly] said that from the viewpoint of 
how Madhyamikas posit conventionalities they are designated as of two types—
Sautrantika-Madhyamikas who assert that ex ternal objects exist conventionally and 
Yogachara-Madhyamikas who assert that conventionally there are no external 
objects.  
 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
797 The names designated by earlier scholars are examined. Some spiritual friends of earlier 
generations said that there are two kinds of  Madhyamikas who are na med in terms of how 
they posit conventionalities. Th ose that assert that external objects exist conventionally are 
the Sautrantika Madhyamikas, wher eas those that assert that ex ternal objects do not exist 
conventionally are the Yogachara Madhyamika s, so-called because they resemble the 
Chittamatrins.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Designation of names of Madhyamikas by how the ultimate is asserted: 
 They also [wrongly] said that from the viewpoint of how they assert the ultimate 
Madhyamikas are designated as of two types—(1) those propounding an 
establishment of illusion by a reasoning consciousness who assert that a composite of the appearances of the subject, such as a sprout, and of its absence of true 
existence is an ultimate truth and (2) those propounding a thorough non-abiding 
who assert that the affirmative eliminativ e phenomenon which is  an elimination of 
the elaborations [of thing, non-thing, both , neither, and so forth] with respect to 
appearances is an ultimate truth. Th ey identified those propounding an 
establishment of illusion by a reas oning consciousness as the masters 
Shantarakshita, Kamalashila, and so forth. Some Indians also used the 
designations “like illusion” and “thoroughly non-abiding”. 
 Also from the point of view of how the ultimate  truth is asserted ther e are two designations. 
Those who assert that the collect ion of the substratum, such as a sprout, and the appearance 
of non-true existence as ultimate truth are the exponents who establish the illusory by 
reasoning. Those who assert the determinations that have completely cut elaborations with 
respect to appearances as ultimate truths are th e exponents of complete non-abiding, that is, 
of the non-abiding of appearances. It is assert ed that Master Shantarakshita, Kamalashila, and 
others are the first of these two, whereas some Indians also assert the terms illusory-like and 
completely non-abiding. In Tibet, on the othe r hand, there are the Jonangpas; for example, 
there is one well-known lama from Drepung Go mang who gave up his vows when he arrived 
in India, married, and had children.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
The names and meanings of this assertion are obscured: 
 The great translator [Lo-den-shay-rap2 rightly] says [in his letter, the Drop of 
Ambrosia ], “The positing of the two from th e viewpoint of how the ultimate is 
asserted is an obscured presentation generating amazement.”3 
 
                                              
2rngog lo chen po blo ldan shes rab. 
3 In the mchan bzhi this is interpreted differently as  “generating amazement in the obscured but not in the 
wise”. However, Tri-jang Rin- bo-chay, author of the subheadings, clearl y affixes “obscured” to the presentation. 
Those are two types of amazement, at the good and at the bad. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
798 The Great Translator Loden Sherab said that this  way of naming in terms of how the ultimate 
truth is asserted is the cause of confusion. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Ye-shay-day is correct on author of Treatise and history of founders of S autrantika-Madhyamika and 
Yogachara-Madhyamika but wrong in excluding Prasangika division: 
 Concerning this, the master Ye-shay-day4 explains: 
In the Madhyamika treatises by the father, the Superior [Nagarjuna], 
and his spiritual son [Aryadeva], whether external objects exist or not is 
not clear. After them, the master Bhavaviveka refuted the system of 
Mere Knowledge [Mind-Only, Chittamatra] and established a system in 
which external objects exist co nventionally. Then, the master 
Shantarakshita made a different Ma dhyamika system, teaching—based 
on Yogachara treatises—that conventionally external objects do not exist and that ultimately the mind is wi thout inherent existence. Thereby, 
two forms of Madhyamika arose; the former is called Sautrantika-Madhyamika and the latter,  Yogachara-Madhyamika. 
The history of the clarification [of the mo del texts by those masters] through great 
treatises is as he says. 
 The way in which Yeshe De explained the author s of the Madhyamaka tr eatises and the stages 
of development of the systems of Svatantr ika Madhyamaka and Yo gachara Madhyamaka, 
which are respectively similar to the positions of the Svatantrikas and the Chittamatrins, is 
correct. Yeshe De said that Arya Nagarjuna and hi s spiritual son Aryadeva did not clearly state 
in their treatises whether external objects are established or not. After them, Master 
Bhavaviveka refuted the Chittamatra system and asserted a system in which external 
phenomena exist conventionally. Master Shanta rakshita, based on the Chittamatra system, 
said that phenomena are not established from ex ternal particles and that ultimately the mind 
does not exist. Thereby, two kinds of Madhya mikas appeared: the Saut rantika Madhyamikas, 
who base their assertions on the sutras, and the Yogachara Svatantrikas, who assert that 
phenomena are the nature of the mind and aris e from imprints. This presentation accords 
with the stages of development set out in the great treatises. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Unsuitability of those designations  for the master Chandrakirti: 
 However, although the master Chandrakir ti asserts that external objects exist 
conventionally, he does not do so in comparison with another tenet system. 
Therefore, it is not suitable to call him a Sautrantika[-Madhyamika]. That he 
asserts external objects in accordance with  the Vaibhashikas is also not feasible. 
 
                                              
4 Ye shes sde.

799 Although Chandrakirti posits th at external phenomena exist conventionally, it is not suitable 
to call him a Sautrantika, nor is it suitable  to say that his assertions accord with the 
Vaibhashikas, even though they also assert the existence of external phenomena that are 
c o m p o s e d  o f  e i g h t  s u b t l e  p a r t i c l e s .  T h i s  i s  because Chandrakirti makes the additional 
assertion that such external phenomena do not exist inherently or truly.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Correctness of designation of Svatantri ka and Prasangika by [Ga-dam-ba] scholars of later dissemination: 
 The terminology of Svatantrika and Pr asangika was used with respect to 
Madhyamikas by scholars of the later disse mination [of Buddhism] into the land of 
snowy mountains [Tibet]; this accords with Chandrakirti’s Clear Words5 [in which 
he refutes the usage of autonomous syllogisms6 and shows that using only 
consequences7 is sufficient for generating in another the Madhyamika view]. 
 Thus, Madhyamikas are definite as of tw o types—those assertin g and not asserting 
external objects conventionally. Furthermor e, when names are designated from the 
viewpoint of how the view ascertaining emptiness is generated in the mental 
continuum, they are definite as of tw o types—Svatantrikas and Prasangikas. 
 
That the terms “Svatantrika” and “Prasangika” we re given by scholars of the later propagation 
accords with Chandrakirti’s Clear Words , a commentary on Nagarjuna’s Fundamental Wisdom . 
Thus, there are those who assert conventionally existent extern al phenomena, such as forms 
and so forth, and those who do not assert them. When designated  from the point of view of 
how the view ascertaining emptiness is generate d in the mental continuum, they are given the 
names “Prasangika Madhyamaka” an d “Svatantrika Madhyamaka.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Ga-dam-bas, in accordance with Atisha, sought view following Chandrakirti 
 Then, following whom did those master s seek the thought of the father, the 
Superior [Nagarjuna], and his spiritual son [Aryadeva]? Following the Great Elder 
[Atisha] who took the master Chandrakirti’s  system to be chief, the earlier great 
lamas of these precepts also he ld his system to be chief. 
 
That the earlier Kadampas sought the view thro ugh following Chandrakirti is the intention of 
Atisha. If one is seeking the intention of Na garjuna and Aryadeva, which master should one 
follow? Through following Atisha who took Chan drakirti’s system to be the main one, the 
great lamas of the past of these instructions al so held Chandrakirti’s system to be the main 
one.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

800 Assertion of system of Buddhapa lita and Chandrakirti as chief and importance of Bhavaviveka: 
 The master Chandrakirti saw that among the commentators on Nagarjuna’s 
Treatise on the Middle8 the master Buddhapalita commented in a complete way on 
the Superior’s thought. Thus, putting Budd hapalita’s system as his basis, taking 
many good explanations even from Bh avaviveka, and refuting those [within 
Bhavaviveka’s works] that appeared to be  a little incorrect, he commented on the 
Superior’s thought. 
 
Lama Tsongkhapa himself also asserted the sy stems of Buddhapalita and Chandrakirti to be 
the main systems, and after them asserted Bhavav iveka’s system to be im portant. Chandrakirti 
saw that among the commentaries on Fundamental Wisdom , Buddhapalita explained the 
intention of Arya Nagarjuna completely and then posited this system as a basis. Chandrakirti 
also took some of Bhavaviveka’s explanations  but corrected them and thereby clarified the 
intention of Nagarjuna.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Importance of these two mast ers for the profound meaning 
 Because the commentaries by these two masters [Buddhapalita and Chandrakirti] 
are seen to be very highly developed with  regard to explaining the texts of the 
Superior—the father—and his spiritual son,  here their thought will be delineated 
following the masters Buddhapalita and Chandrakirti. 
 
These two masters – Chandrakirti and Buddhapa lita – are important for the explanation of 
the profound meaning. Since th ey excellently explained the texts of Nagarjuna and Aryadeva, 
the intentions of the arya father and his spirit ual sons are settled by following Buddhapalita 
and Chandrakirti.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 295) says: 
1 Root of Cyclic Existence 
The presentation of how to settle the view in particular has three parts: identifying 
afflicted ignorance, showing that it is the root of revolving in cyclic existence, and 
that one wishing to abandon the concepti on of self should seek the view of 
selflessness. 
 
First afflicted ignorance is identified and then it is shown to be the root of cyclic existence. It 
is said that the root of all the afflictions is ignorance. Also th e first of the twelve links that 
explain how one takes rebirth in cyclic existence is ignorance. Therefore,  ignorance is the root 
of cyclic existence. What kind  of ignorance is this? Because there are different types of 
ignorance, this particular igno rance first needs to be identified. This ignorance is the non-
knowing of suchness, selflessness, or emptin ess, that is, the nature of phenomena. 
Alternatively it is the ignorance not knowing th e selflessness of persons or the selflessness of 
                                             
 
8 madhyamakashastra . 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
801 phenomena. The particular ignoranc e that is the root of cyclic ex istence is this ignorance. In 
our daily life the root of all our problems is ig norance, in the sense that due to it we create 
actions of body and speech that are non-virt uous and bring the result of suffering and 
problems, without ever bringing the result of happiness and well-being. 
 Ignorance is the grasping at a self, it need s to be abandoned. Wishing to abandon it, one 
must seek the view of selflessness. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Identifying afflicted ignorance 
(This has five parts, the first of which is: that although desire and so forth are in a class contrary to knowledge they are 
not chief:) 
The antidotes to the other afflictive emot ions—desire and so forth—set forth by the 
Conqueror are partial ones, but the antidote  to ignorance that he described serves 
as an antidote to all [afflictive emotions].  Therefore, ignorance is the basis of all 
faults and defects.  
 
Although attachment, hatred, pride, doubt, wr ong view, and the secondary afflictions are the 
discordant class of knowledge, they are not the root of cyclic  existence. Thus , although the 
Conqueror, Buddha Shakyamuni, taught particular  antidotes to the various afflictions, they 
are only partial antidotes, whereas the antidote to ignorance is a complete antidote because it 
is the antidote to all the afflic tions. Hence, ignorance is the ba sis of all faults and defects. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Chandrakirti’s Clear Words  says: 
In the nine forms of the Buddha’s teachings based on the two truths—
the sets of sutras and so forth— 
Proclaimed with respect to the extensiv e forms of behavior of worldly beings, 
Those spoken for the sake of removi ng desire do not remove hatred, 
Those spoken for the sake of removing hatred do not remove desire, 
Those spoken for the sake of removing pride and so forth do not overcome the 
other defilements. 
Therefore, they are not very pervasiv e and those teachings are not of great 
meaning. 
Those spoken for the sake of removing ignorance overcome all afflictive emotions. 
All afflictive emotions thoroughly depe nd on ignorance; this is what the 
Conquerors said. 
 
The buddhas taught twelve branches of scripture as well as nine types of teachings based on 
the two truths – the conventional truth and ulti mate truth. In these whatever is vast with 
respect to the conduct of worldly beings is pe rfectly renowned. However, the teachings that 
Buddha Shakyamuni gave for the purpose of elim inating attachment cannot eliminate hatred. 
For example, he taught that the antidote to attachment is to meditate on ugliness, such as 
skeletons; but though this meditation can reduce attachment it cannot reduce hatred. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
802 Likewise the antidote to hatred taught by th e Buddha – meditation on love and compassion – 
can eliminate hatred and anger but cannot exhaus t attachment. Therefore,  these antidotes do 
not exhaust all afflictions, wherea s the antidote that exhausts igno rance or delusion is also the 
antidote that destroys all afflictions, that is, the six root afflictions as well as the twenty 
secondary afflictions. This is because the Conq ueror, Buddha Shakyamuni, taught that all the 
afflictions are dependent on delusion or  ignorance, this being their root.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, you must meditate on suchness as an  antidote to ignorance. Moreover, if 
ignorance is not identified, you will no t know how to cultivate its antidote; 
therefore, identification of ignorance is extremely important. 
 
Therefore, it is necessary to meditate on such ness or emptiness as the antidote to ignorance. 
However, since without first identifying ignorance,  it is not possible to meditate its antidote, 
initially it is important to identify ignorance. Because of this, one should strive to recognize 
the root of cyclic existence an d the root all the problems th at arise in our daily life – 
ignorance. Having identified th is ignorance, one should then apply its antidote – meditation 
on emptiness or selflessness.  Thursday afternoon, 1 February 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 295) says: 
(Second, though in general ignorance is posited as the mere  opposite of knowledge, here ignorance is to be taken as 
the opposite of knowledge realizing the absence of true existence:)  
Ignorance is the opposite of knowledge, bu t knowledge should not be taken as just 
any [knowledge]; rather, it is the wisd om knowing suchness —selflessness. Its 
o p p o s i t e  i s  n o t  s u i t a b l e  t o  b e  j u s t  t h e  n o n - e x i s t e n c e  o f  i t  o r  j u s t  o t h e r  t h a n  i t ;  
hence, its opposite is its contradictory equivalent. 
 
In general ignorance ( ma rig pa ) is the discordant class of knowing ( rig pa ), but in this 
particular context it is the discordant class of the knowing that is the re alization of emptiness. 
Ignorance (literally, non-knowing) does not know its object, and is therefore the exact 
opposite of knowing. Here knowing is the wisdom  realizing selflessness. Its discordant class or 
opposite class is not the mere absence of wisdom  nor is it that which is other than wisdom. 
The ignorance apprehending self-sufficient substa ntial existence or inherent existence and the 
wisdom realizing thusness or selflessness have completely opposite modes of apprehension. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, describing the two subtle conceptions  of self as afflictive emotions:)  
That [ignorance] is a superimposition of self; furthermore, it is the two 
superimpositions of a self of phenomen a and of persons. Therefore, both the 
conception of a self of phenomena and th e conception of a self of persons are 
ignorance. 
 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
803 The two subtle conceptions of a self – the concep tion of a self of persons and the conception 
of a self of phenomena – are afflictions. They are a superimposition of a self. In the case of 
the conception of a self of phenomena the basis of imputation is the aggregates of a being – 
the form aggregate, feeling aggregate, disc rimination aggregate, compositional factors 
aggregate, and consciousness aggregate – upon which one superimposes a self. The 
conception of a self of persons,  on the other hand, apprehends th e person to be self-sufficient 
substantially existent or inherently existent. Therefore, both the conc eption of a self of 
phenomena and the conception of a self of pe rsons are ignorance. Ig norance is the main 
discordant class to the re alization of emptiness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, how ignorance superimposes what sort of inherent existence:)  
The manner of superimposition by ignoranc e is to conceive that phenomena exist 
by way of their own entity, by way of th eir own nature, or by way of their own 
being. The  Questions of Upali Sutra9 says that phenomena are posited through the 
power of conceptuality: 
Here the various mind-pleasing blossoming flowers 
And attractive shining supreme golden houses 
Have no [inherently existent] maker at all. They are posited through the power of conceptuality.  Through the power of conceptuality the world is imputed. 
 What kind of nature does ignorance superimp ose? The way in which ignorance superimposes 
is that it apprehends persons or phenomena to  exist by way of their own entity, by way of 
their own characteristics, or inhe rently. This sutra says that bo th the blossoming flowers that 
make the mind happy and attractive golden mansions are not created by anyone. This 
statement refutes that the assertion that they  are the creations of a God, a primordial 
principle, Brahma, Vishnu, and so forth. Instea d they are posited by the power of conception. 
In fact the entire world is imputed by the power of conception, whereby all phenomena are 
imputed by the power of conception. This indi cates that phenomena do not exist from their 
own side but are merely imputed. For example, just as a pregnant woman gives a name to her 
unborn child and then thinks “Cristina” with re spect to it, in a similar way we impute a name 
to a phenomenon and then think of  the phenomenon as being that.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, Nagarjuna’s Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning
10 says: 
Because the perfect Buddha said 
The world is caused by ignorance, 
Why is it not correct that  
This world is [posited by] conceptuality?

10 yuktisastika .

804 In commentary on the meaning of this, Chan drakirti explains that the worlds are 
not established by way of their own entity  and are only imputed by conceptuality. 
 
Also the Sixty Verses of Reasons  by Nagarjuna says that the entire  world, that is, all phenomena, 
has ignorance as a condition. There are two kinds of ignorance, one that is a causal 
motivation and one that is a time motivation which is present simultaneous with the action. 
These two ignorances produce actions that give  rise to the world. For example, the many 
buildings in our cities come about as the result of an architect drawing the building plan of 
where the kitchen, bathroom, windows, doors, an d so forth will be located, after which the 
building plan is given to the builders who then construct the house. Since similarly all 
phenomena arise from thought, it is not incorre c t  t o  s a y  “ T h e  w o r l d  i s  c o n c e p t i o n . ”  T h i s  
means that all the world – the mountains, lakes, rivers, and so forth – do not exist by way of 
their own entity but are merely imputed by conception. If phenomena existed truly or 
inherently there would be no need to build or  create them. For example, it would not be 
necessary for a cook to make food as the food  would be established inherently without the 
need for anyone to make it.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Aryadeva’s Four Hundred
11 says: 
If without conceptuality the existence 
Of desire and so forth does not exist 
Who with intelligence would hold [that things] 
Are ultimate realities and [imputed by] conceptuality? 
Chandrakirti’s commentary on this says: 
Those which exist only when the co nceptuality [imputing them] exists 
and do not exist when conceptualit y does not are without question 
definite as not established by way of their own entities, like a snake imputed to a coiled rope. 
Thus, he explains that desire and so fort h are like a snake imputed to a rope in 
terms of the factor of being imputed to be established by way of their own entities 
whereas they are not. [However] a rope-s nake and desire and so forth are not 
similar in terms of whether they exist co nventionally or not [since a rope-snake 
does not exist conventionally whereas desire and so forth do]. 
 
Aryadeva’s Four Hundred Verses  says that attachment and so  forth do not exist without 
conception. For example, upon seeing something attractive we think “O h, how beautiful” and 
develop attachment to it. Similarly upon seeing an unattractive object we think “How awful” 
and develop hatred for it. If, for example, a 100 people were to look at the same object, such 
as a particular man or woman, each person would have a different idea about him or her, 
with some of them liking him or her and others not liking him or her.  
                                             
 
11 catuhshataka. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
805  Another example is that upon seeing a stri ped rope coiled in a dim corner, we become 
frightened thinking “Oh, there is a snake,” alth ough in reality a snake does not exist there. 
Similarly, although phenomena do not exist from  their own side or by their own entity, they 
appear to us to exist from their own side and inherently. Whereas in the case of the striped, 
coiled rope which appears to be a snake, the ob ject ‘snake’ does not exist at all, attachment 
and hatred, although imputed, do exist conventi onally, as do pride, jealousy, resentment, and 
so forth. However, they do not exist inherent ly or from their own side but exist by being 
merely imputed by name and conception. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The fifth, a detailed description of the conception of self, has two parts, an explanation of the conception of self as 
twofold and the difference between a conception of self and a view of the transitory.  
 
The two types of conception of a self are th e conception of a self of persons and the 
conception of a self of phenomena. There is a difference between the conc eption of a self and 
the view of the transitory collection.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
The former has two parts, the first of which is: how even though the two conceptions  of self have the same subjective 
aspect, they are differentiated by wa y of their object of observation:) 
For those reasons—the mode of conceiving true existence—the object of negation—
is to conceive [that object s] are not posited through the force of beginningless 
conceptuality but are established object ively by way of their own entity. The 
referent object of that conception is ca lled self or inherent existence. The non-
existence of that with a person as the subs tratum is said to be a selflessness of a 
person, and the non-existence of that with [other] phenomena such as an eye, ear, 
and so forth as the substratum  is said to be a selflessnes s of phenomena. Therefore, 
we can implicitly understand that the conceptions12 of inherent existence with 
respect to persons and [other] phenomena are the conceptions of the two selves. 
 
The two conceptions of a self are similar in te rms of their aspects but are differentiated in 
terms of their observed objects.  The object of negation, the wa y of apprehending true existe nce, is the apprehension of 
objects as established by way of their own en tity without being imputed by the power of 
beginningless conceptions. For example, it is to think that a table is established by way of its 
own entity without being imputed by the power of beginningless conceptions. Its determined 
object is said to be “self” or “nature.” The abse nce of this on top of a person is the selflessness 
of persons, and the absence of this on top of  a phenomenon, such as an eye, ear, nose, 
tongue, or body, is the selflessness of phenomen a. Apprehending that na ture to exist on top 
of persons and phenomena is to be understand as being the apprehension or conception of 
the two selves.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
                                             
 
12 “Conception” (’dzin  pa) means a conscious conceiving or holding or apprehending, in this case, inherent 
existence.

806 (Second, a source for the differentiation of t he two conceptions of self in that way:)  
Chandrakirti’s Commentary on (Aryadeva’s) “Four Hundred”13 says: 
Concerning that, “self” is an entity of things that does not depend on 
others—inherent existence. Its non-ex istence is selflessness. Through the 
division of phenomena and persons it is understood as twofold, “a 
selflessness of phenomena and a selflessness of persons.” 
 The two types of apprehension of a self – the se lf of persons and self of phenomena – are to 
be distinguished. The commentary on the Four Hundred Verses  says that that called “self” is an 
entity or nature that does not depend on an y other phenomena; its absence is selflessness. 
Selflessness is divided into the selflessness of persons and selflessness of phenomena. In other 
words, a person’s absence of existing inhere ntly or independently is the selflessness of 
persons, whereas a phenomenon’s absence of ex isting inherently or independently is the 
selflessness of phenomena.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The second, the difference between the conception of a self of  persons and the view of the transitory, has three parts, 
the first of which is: that the object of  observation of a view of the transitory  conceiving an inherently existent I is 
necessarily a person:) 
With respect to the object of observation of a conception of a self of persons, 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”14 explains that 
one Sammitiya sect asserts that all five aggregates are the basis or object of 
observation of a view of self whereas anot her asserts that just the mind is. Also 
with respect to the mind, some Chitta matrins and Madhyamikas who assert a 
mind-basis-of-all15 assert that the mind-basis-of-al l is the object of observation, 
whereas other Madhyamikas such as Bhavav iveka who do not assert a mind-basis-
of-all and many Hearer sects assert that the mental consciousness is the object of 
observation.  
 With respect to the observed object of the concep tion of a self of persons, there are different 
positions. The Supplement to the Middle Way  says that some Sammitiyas , for example, assert all 
five aggregates and others assert only the mind to be the support or observed object of the view of a self. The Chittamatrins and some Ma dhyamikas assert the mind-basis-of-all, the 
mind that is the basis on which all imprints ar e deposited, to be the observed object of the 
conception of a self of persons. On the othe r hand, some Madhyamikas, such as Bhavaviveka 
and so forth, who do not accept it as well as many exponents of the he arer schools assert the 
mental consciousness to be the observed object of the conception of a self of persons. In short, some say that all five aggregates, the mind-basis-of-all, and only the mental 
consciousness is the observed object of the conc eption of a self of persons. The Chittamatrins 
assert eight consciousnesses – the usual six plus the mind-basis-of-all and an afflicted 
                                             
 
13catuhshatakarika. 
14 madhyamakavatara. 
15 alayavijñana . 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
807 consciousness. In addition, ther e is a presentation of nine co nsciousnesses in which case the 
ninth is a stainless consciousness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
With respect to the meaning—in these syst ems—of the designation “person”, as in 
the cultivator of a path and the revolver in  cyclic existence, you need to know the 
two modes of [the Prasangikas’] positing  the mere I and [the other schools’] 
positing the mind-basis-of-all, and so forth, as  an illustration of that I [that is to say, 
as something that is the I].  
 
It is necessary to understand how these system s posit the person – the one meditating paths 
and the one circling in cyclic existence. There are two different ways of positing the person. 
The Prasangika Madhyamikas say that the person  is the mere I, whereas other tenets posit 
either the mind-basis-of-all, the sixth consciousn ess, that is, the mental consciousness, or the 
five aggregates as the illustration of that I.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 With respect to the innate view of the transitory which is a conception of self, 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s ) “Treatise on the Middle”  refutes that the 
aggregates are the object of observation,  and in his commentary he says that the 
dependently imputed self is the object of ob servation. Since he says that even just 
the collection of the aggregates is not th e conventional self, neither the collection 
at one time nor the collection which is th e continuum of the aggregates over time 
are the object of observation. Rather, the mere I which is the object observed in 
generating the mere thought, “I”, or the me re person, is the object of observation. 
Neither one from among the aggregates no r the collection of the aggregates is 
posited as an illustration of that I [in other words, none of these can be said to be 
the I]. I have explained at length el sewhere that this is an unsurpassed 
distinguishing feature of this  Prasangika-Madhyamika system. 
 
The Supplement to the Middle Way  refutes that the innate view of  the transitory collection that 
apprehends a self has the aggregates as its obse rved object. The commentary to this text says 
t h a t  i t s  o b s e r v e d  o b j e c t  i s  t h e  s e l f  t h a t  i s  imputed on the basis of the aggregates. In other 
words, the mere I is the observed object of th e innate view of the transitory collection that 
apprehends a self. Also the mere collection of aggregates is not the conventionally existent 
self, nor is the collection of aggregates of the pr esent being, the collection of aggregates of the 
past being, or the collection of aggregates of th e future being. None of these are the observed 
object of the innate view of th e transitory collection that appr ehends a self. So what is its 
observed object? The merely imputed I is the observed object of the innate view of the 
transitory collection that apprehends a self. Th us, none of the individual aggregates nor the 
collection of the aggregates are posited as an illu stration of I. This spec ial presentation of the 
Prasangika Madhyamaka system is ex plained in other texts such as the Ocean of Reasoning  and 
the Great Stages of the Path to Enlightenment .  
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
808  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the difference between the objects of obs ervation of the two conceptions of self:) 
 The object of observation of an innate view of the transitory must be something 
that naturally generates an awareness thinki ng, “I”. Therefore, although [your own] 
innate conception of a person whose continuum is other [than your own as 
inherently existent] is an innate conception of a self of persons, it is not an innate 
view of the transitory of that person. 
 
With respect to the observed object of the inna te view of the transitory collection, the mind 
that thinks “I” necessary arises naturally. Theref ore, although the innate  apprehension of the 
person of another continuum, such as Laura, as established by way of its own characteristics is 
the innate conception of a self of  persons, it is not an innate vi ew of the transitory collection 
of that person.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 With regard to the object of observation of an innate view of the transitory that 
conceives of an [inherently existent] mine, the object of observation of an innate 
awareness thinking, “mine,” is just mine; it  should not be held that one’s own eyes 
and so forth are the object of observation.  
 
The view of the transitory collection apprehends  both I and mine. The observed object of the 
innate view of the transitory collection apprehen ding mine is only mine-ness, it is not “my 
eyes,” “my nose,” “my body,” an d so forth. The apprehending of “my eyes” and so forth is the 
conception of a self of phenomen a, not the view of the transitory  collection or the conception 
of a self of persons. In short,  the view of the transitory coll ection does not observe my hand, 
my house, and so forth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
The subjective aspect [of such an innate misconception] is that within observing 
the mind it conceives the mine to be established by way of its own nature. 
 
The aspect of the view of the transitory collec tion is that through observing that observed 
object, it apprehends mine as establis hed by way of its own characteristics. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The objects of observation of the innate  conception of a self of phenomena are 
the form aggregate, and so forth, and the eyes, ears, and so fort h, in one’s own and 
others’ continuums as well as the envi ronments and so fo rth which are not 
included within the personal continuum . The subjective aspect [of such a 
misconception] is as explained before [tha t is, within observing these to conceive 
them to exist by way of their own nature].
