Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
809  
The observed object of the innate conception of  a self of phenomena can be either the form 
aggregate of one’s own continuum  or the form aggregate of an other person’s continuum and 
so forth, as well as eyes, ears, and so forth,  and phenomena of the environmental world that 
are not included in anyone’s continuum such as ho uses and so forth. Its aspect is the same at 
that mentioned before, that is, through observin g such observed objects, it apprehends them 
to exist by way of their own ch aracteristics, by way of their own entity, or inherently.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) ‘Treatise on the 
Middle’”16 says: 
Stupidity or ignorance is a superimpos ition of an inherent existence of 
things [even though such] does no t exist; it has the nature of 
obstructing perception of the nature [emptiness]—a concealer.17  
 
The Commentary on the  Supplement to the Middle Way  says that delusion ( gti mug ) is ignorance 
that superimposes that which does not exist – existence by way of it own characteristics or 
inherent existence. Its nature is  to obscure the seeing of the final nature of phenomena, due 
to which it is called “all concealing”) ( kun rdzob ). 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Also: 
Thus, through the force of the afflic ted ignorance included within the 
links [of the dependent-arising] of a cyclic existence … 
 
Afflicted ignorance is fully included in the br anches or links of existence, meaning in the 
twelve links, the first one being ignorance. Unde r the power of this igno rance, the first link, 
one is obscured from seeing the nature of phenomena. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Hence, he asserts that [a cons ciousness] conceiving an obje ct as truly existent is an 
ignorance and that this is an afflicte d ignorance. Therefore, between the two 
modes of positing the conception of a self  of phenomena as an afflictive emotion 
or as an obstruction to omniscience, he re [in the Prasangika system] it is the 
former.

Stages of the Path         ILTK – 29 January-1 February 2007 (17) 
810 The ignorance that apprehends objects as truly existent is afflicted ignorance. There are two 
ways of positing the conception of a self of ph enomena. From point of view of the Prasangika 
Madhyamaka the conception of a self of phenomenon is an affl ictive obscuration and not an 
obscuration to knowledge, whereas from the poin t of view of the Svat antrika Madhyamaka it 
is an obscuration to knowledge and not an afflictive obscuration. An obscuration to 
knowledge is an obscuration that prevents th e simultaneous and direct seeing of the two 
truths – conventional truth and ultimate truth.

811 Monday afternoon, 5 February 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (translation by Jeffrey Hopkins, Tibetan text page 299) says: 
(Third, showing that this identification of the ignorance conceiving true existence is asserted by the Superior father and 
his spiritual son:)  
This was set forth by the Superior—the father [Nagarjuna]—and his spiritual son 
[Aryadeva]. Nagarjuna’s Seventy Stanzas on Emptiness18 says: 
That which conceives things produced 
From causes and conditions to be real 
Was said by the Teacher to be ignorance. 
From it the twelve links arise. Through knowing well that things are empty Due to seeing reality, ignorance does not arise. That is the cessation of ignorance, Whereby the twelve links cease. 
 The way in which to identify the ignorance a pprehending true existence was taught by the 
Arya father, Nagarjuna, and his spiritual son, Aryadeva. Nagarjuna’s Seventy Verses on 
Emptiness  says that the Teacher Buddha taught that  that it is ignorance that apprehends 
functioning things – that is, results which ar e produced when causes and conditions are 
complete – to be real, that is, to be truly existent. In other words,  that which apprehend 
phenomena to be truly or inherently existent is ignorance, then from it arise the other links of 
the twelve links.   The twelve links are divided into two sets: (1) a set of projecting causes and projected 
results and (2) a set of actualizing causes and ac tualized results. The first link is ignorance, 
which is the main cause. This is the ignorance th at apprehends the person to be inherently or 
truly existent, or according to other tenets to be  self-sufficient substantially existent. It is the 
causal motivation due to which one creates ac tions, which are of two types – virtuous and 
non-virtuous. However, it is the simultaneous time motivation, which can be either virtuous 
or non-virtuous, that makes the action respec tively virtuous or non-virtuous. In general 
virtuous actions are of ten types, likewise no n-virtuous actions are also ten. The three non-
virtuous actions created by body are killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct. The four non-
virtuous actions created by speec h are lying, divisive speech, ha rsh words, and idle talk. The 
three non-virtuous actions of mind are: cove tousness, harmful mind or malice, and wrong 
view. The abandonment these actions are the vi rtuous actions of body, speech, and mind. 
While we can stop ourselves from doing negati ve actions of body and speech, like a dam 
blocking the flow of a river, we are not able to stop those of mind. These virtuous and non-
virtuous actions are the second link of karmic formations. These actions or karmic formations 
deposit an imprint on the consciousness, the th ird link. This can be likened to putting the 
money that we have earned as a result of workin g in the bank, whereby we ensure that it does 
not get lost. Then comes the fourth  link of name and form, the fi fth link of the six sources or 
entrances, the sixth link of contact, and the seve nth link of feeling. These belong to one set of 
links. Then when we are dying the eighth link of craving and the ninth link of grasping cause 
                                             
 
18 shunyatasaptati . 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
812 an imprint left on the consciousness to ripen,  this being the tenth link of existence or 
becoming. The result of this link is the eleventh  link, birth, and then, having taken birth, the 
next link of aging-death arises. Having been co nceived, we immediately begin to age, that is, 
our body immediately begins to change. Then co mes death. We can die at any time, even in 
the womb. This is due to impermanence.  
 Through understanding dependent-relation we will understand well that all phenomena 
are empty of inherent existence, due to which ig norance will no longer arise. In other words, 
ignorance ceases. Having ceased ignorance, wh at happens? All the other links cease because 
the main cause, ignorance, has ceased.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
To conceive things to be real [means] to  apprehend them as established as [their 
own] reality or as truly established. 
 This is similar in meaning to Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland,
19 which says that until 
the conception of the aggregates as trul y existent is overcome, the view of the 
transitory cannot be overcome: 
As long as conception of the aggregates exists, 
So long is there conception of an I in them. 
 We apprehend all phenomena to exist truly or inhe rently or to exist from their own side, that 
is, to be established as real or as true. This a pprehension is the root of  ignorance, and is what 
needs to be overcome. Likewise, the Precious Garland  says that as long as we think “my body,” 
“my feelings,” “my discrimination,” “my compos itional factors,” and “m y consciousness,” that 
is, as long as we apprehend our body and so forth to be truly existent, for that long will the conception of I with respect to th em exist; this is the view of the transitory collection of I and 
mine. That is, for that long we will conceive ou rselves to exist inherently  and truly. Hence, as 
long as we continue to apprehend the aggregates to be truly existent, for that long we will not 
be able to counteract the view of the transi tory collection. The view of the transitory 
collection means to apprehend the I imputed on the body, which is the transitory collection.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, Aryadeva’s Four Hundred  says: 
Just as the body sense power [pervades] the body, 
Stupidity abides in all [afflicti ve emotions] as their basis. 
Therefore, all afflictive emotions are overcome Through overcoming stupidity. 
When dependent-arising is seen, 
Stupidity does not arise; Therefore, with all endeavor here [in this text] 
                                             
 
19 ratnavali . 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
813 I will set forth just talk on this.20 
 
Aryadeva’s Four Hundred Verses  says that our body – from the cr own of our head to the soles of 
our feet – is pervaded by the body sense powe r, meaning that the body sense power is found 
everywhere in our body. Similarly as long as  there exists delusion or ignorance or the 
conception of true existence th at apprehends all phenomena to exist truly and inherently, for 
that long other afflictions will be produced, that  is, for that long there will be attachment to 
“my friend,” “my wife,” “my husband,” “my son, ” “my daughter,” and so forth, as well as 
hatred, jealousy, and so forth toward “my enemy. ” Just as if the body is destroyed, all the 
other organs will cease to function, similarly if ignorance is destroyed, all the other afflictions 
will be destroyed. Hence, if we see dependent- arising or dependent-relation, all delusion be 
stopped. Therefore, we should put as much effort as possible into realizing dependent-
relation. By understanding dependent-arising directly, we will understand that no 
phenomenon whatsoever exists inherently, bu t that all phenomena exist in dependence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Concerning the stupidity so described, sinc e this is at a poin t of identifying the 
stupidity which is one of the three poison s [desire, hatred, and stupidity], it is 
afflicted ignorance. 
 Also, Aryadeva says that in order to over come this ignorance it is necessary to 
realize the meaning of the profound depe ndent-arising such that the meaning of 
emptiness appears as the meaning of de pendent-arising. Therefore, afflicted 
stupidity should be taken in accordance with the explanation of it—in Chandrakirti’s commentary on that passage —as a superimposition of true existence 
upon things. The honorable Chandrakir ti, following Buddhapalita’s commentary 
on the Superior [Nagarjuna’s] though t, clearly speaks to this point. 
 
Stupidity or delusion or ignorance ( gti mug ) is one of the three mental poisons of attachment, 
hatred, and ignorance. Just as poison harm us and never benefits us, so too are these mental 
poisons so-called in that they only harm us an d never benefit us. Just as even a small amount 
of poison in our food can harm and even kill us , so too does ignorance harm us. This type of 
ignorance is afflicted ignorance. In order to cease it, it is nece ssary to realize the meaning of 
profound dependent-arising, that is, that the meaning of emptiness is the meaning of 
dependent-arising. The commentary to the Four Hundred Verses  explain that afflicted 
ignorance is a superimposition of true ex istence upon things. In accordance with 
Buddhapalita’s explanation, given in his text which is called Buddhapalita , Chandrakirti 
clearly set out this meaning.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Second, showing that afflicted ignorance is th e root of revolving in cyclic existence 
(This has six parts, the first of which is: that the innate conception of self must be posited as the root of cyclic existence 
and that the artificial one is not suitable to be posited so:) 
                                              
20 The translation of the last two lines is confirme d by Chandra. 113b.2 (blue) and Ren-da-wa 172.12-13 
(Sarnath: Sakya Students' Union, 1974; and Gyel chap. 6, 8 2-4 Sarnath: 1971). 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
814 Also, the ignorance explained above which co nceives of the two selves in that way 
is not an artificial conception of a se lf or persons and phenomena as in the 
assertions of the truth21 of (1) a permanent, unitary, and independent person, (2) 
external objects of apprehension (a) whic h are partless in terms of directions—east 
and so forth—or (b) which are gross object s that are composites of directionally 
partless particles, (3) internal appreh ending consciousnesses which are (a) 
temporarily partless—there being no earlier or later portions, and so forth—or (b) a 
continuum of consciousness which is a se ries [of temporally partless moments], 
and (4) self-consciousness which is non-du alistic in the sense of being empty of 
such object and subject, these being im puted by the uncommon assertions of the 
non-Buddhist and [non-Prasangika] Buddhis t systems of tenets. Rather, ignorance 
is the two innate conceptions of self, wh ich all those whose minds are and are not 
affected by tenets have in common and which have operated beginninglessly 
without depending on the mind’s being affected by tenets.  Just that [ignorance] is here held to  be the root of cyclic existence,  
 In order to demonstrate that igno rance is the root of cyclic existence there are six sections, the 
first being that the innate  conception of a self must be posite d as the root of cyclic existence, 
the artificial or acquired conception of a self no t being suitable as the root of cyclic existence. 
Just as there are two selves, a self of persons and a self of phenomena, likewise, there are two 
selflessnesses, the selflessness of persons and th e selflessness of phenomena. There is also an 
innate ignorance and an artificial or acquired ignorance.   There are systems of tenets, non-Buddhist and Buddhist that assert: 1. a person that is permanent, unitary, and independent, 2. external forms that are composed of subtle pa rticles that are partless, that is, that do not 
have eastern, western, and so fort h sides in the various directions, 
3. inner consciousnesses that are temporally partless, that is, which are not composed of 
moments, and that are a continuum of consciousn ess composed of a series of such partless 
moments, 
4. a self-knower that is non-dual in the sense of apprehenders (consciousness) and 
apprehendeds (objects) being empty (a self-kno wer does not apprehend external objects, but 
only apprehends consciousness). 
These are artificial conceptions of a self of persons and a self of phenomena and therefore 
exist only in those whose mind has been influe nced by tenets. However,  the conception of a 
self or ignorance referred to here is not that which arises due to tenets. Instead it is the 
ignorance that does not rely on tenets but arises due to familiarity, that is, it is the ignorance 
that has operated since beginningless time.   In short, the conception of a self of phenomena and the concep tion of a self of persons are 
the two conceptions of a self. It is the innate conception of a se lf that arises without studying 
tenets that is the root of cyclic existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
                                             
 
21 “The truth” could be interpreted as going with just number 4.

815 for Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
This non-produced permanent [self imputed by false systems] is not 
perceived 
By those spending many eons as animals, 
Yet the [mis]conception of I is  seen to operate in them. 
Through this reasoning [it can be understo od that] what binds sentient beings in 
cyclic existence is innate ignorance, and since artificial ignorance occurs only in 
proponents of tenets, it is not feas ibly the root of cyclic existence. 
 
Those who wander for eons as animals do not perceive a permanent, partless, and 
independent self, however they still apprehend a self. For example, while cats recognize their 
own babies and even birds take care of their young, the recognition of “my baby” does not 
arise from having studied tenets. Therefore, th at which binds all sentient beings in cyclic 
existence is innate ignorance, whereas since arti ficial or acquired ignorance exists only in 
those who are proponents of tenets, it is not suitable as the root of cyclic existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, since the innate mode of apprehensi on does not involve the artificial one, stopping [only] the artificial mode of 
apprehension does not help:) 
Gaining specific ascertainment with respect to  this is very import ant. If you do not 
understand this, you will not know—when settling [emptiness] through the view—to 
hold as chief the ascertainment that objects as apprehended by the innate 
ignorance are non-existent and you will no t know that the objects of artificial 
conceptions are refuted as a branch of this [process]. If the two selves are negated 
within neglecting to refute the mode of apprehension by the innate ignorance, you 
will ascertain only a selflessness which negates [the objects] imputed by only the 
proponents of tenets mentioned earlier. Thereby, since ascertainment though the 
view is for the sake of meditation, even during meditation you will have to 
meditate only on such. 
 I n  s h o r t ,  s i n c e  i t  i s  n o t  e n o u g h  t o  s t o p  t h e  a p p r e h e n s i o n  o f  a  p e r m a n e n t ,  u n i t a r y ,  a n d  independent self, it is important to distinguis h between these two – the innate and acquired 
conceptions of a self. In other words, it is very  important to ascertain wh at is innate ignorance 
and what is acquired ignorance. If one does not ascertain this difference, at the time of 
settling the correct view one will not unders tand the way in which innate ignorance 
apprehends the object. If, while wanting to re fute the two selves, one does not understand 
how to negate innate ignorance, one will only refute the mere self imputed by other tenets. 
Therefore, at the time of medi tation one should meditate on ly on this, that is, having 
established the correct view one must put effort into familiarizing with this, otherwise one will 
not be able to realize selflessness.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, it would be very absurd if through merely refuting the referent object of the ar tificial conception the innate 
afflictive emotions were overcome:) 
Therefore, even if [the emptiness of such an artificially conceived self] were made 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
816 manifest through meditating on it and ev en if the familiarization with it were 
brought to completion, it would be exhauste d as only that. Thus, it would be very 
absurd to assert that through seeing the non-existence of the two selves merely as 
imputed by artificial conceptions the inna te afflictive emotions are overcome.  
 
It is absurd to think that by merely refuting the determined object of the acquired conception 
of a self the innate afflictions are overcome. In  other words, even if one were to familiarize 
with refuting the artificial conception of a self, nothing more than that would happen. 
Therefore, to say that by seeing the non-existenc e of the two selves that are merely imputed by 
the innate conception of a self, all innate a fflictions are overcome is completely incorrect. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
[You propound] that when selflessness is realized 
One abandons [only] the permanent self, [but] it is not asserted  
As the base of the conception of self. Hence, it is fantasy to propound That through knowing [such] selflessne ss the view of self is eradicated. 
 While the realization of the lack of a permanen t, unitary, and independent self abandons the 
grasping at a permanent, unitary, and independent self, such as self is not asserted to be the 
base of the conception of a self , that is, the view of the transi tory collection. Therefore, to 
think that knowing this selflessness elimin ates the view of a self is absurd.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Chandrakirti’s commentary says, “In order to clarify by way of an example this 
meaning of mutual unrelatedness [that is, senselessness] the text says: 
That while seeing a snake living in  a hole in a wall of your house, 
Your fears can be removed and the fright of a snake abandoned 
By [someone’s saying] “There is no elephant here,” Is, alas, laughable to others! 
 An analogy of this absurdity is then set out. If  upon seeing a snake in one’s house, although 
in reality it is a coiled rope, one were to say “T here is no elephant here!” in order to eliminate 
one’s fear of the snake, since this cannot abandon the fear of the snake, it would make one 
the object of others’ laughter. In other words, la ughing at one, they would say “Why does he – 
worried about the presence of a snake – say that there is no elephant in  his house? That is 
ridiculous!”   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Although this is said with respect to the se lflessness of persons, it is the same also 
with respect to the selflessn ess of phenomena. [The stanza] could be put together 
this way:

817 [You propound] that when selflessness is realized  
One abandons [that is, refutes only] the artificial self, [but] it is not asserted  As the base of ignorance. Henc e it is fantasy to propound 
That through knowing [such] selflessness ignorance is eradicated. 
 The verse above sets out the selflessness of pe r s o n s ,  b u t  i t  c a n  a l s o  b e  a p p l i e d  t o  t h e  
selflessness of phenomena. In other words, it is also absurd to say that ignorance is eliminated 
by seeing the selflessness of phenomena that is  the non-existence of a partless particle that 
cannot be divided into different directions. Like wise, to say that ignorance is eliminated by 
seeing the lack of a consciousness that is comp osed of temporally partless particles is equally 
absurd.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, although the view of the transitory and ignorance are the root of cyclic existence, there are not two roots of 
cyclic existence:) 
Objection : Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland  says: 
As long as there is a conception of the aggregates  
So long does one have a conception of I. 
When the conception of an I exists, 
There is action; from action there is birth. 
Thus, he explains that the conception of  a self of phenomen a—the conception of 
the aggregates as truly existent—is the root of cyclic existence.  
 Although both the view of the transitory co llection and ignorance are the root of cyclic 
existence, it is not that there are two roots of  cyclic existence. Someone makes an objection 
based on Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland  which says that as long as  one there is the conception 
of aggregates, for that long there will be the conc eption of I; that if the conception of I exists, 
actions are created; and that from those actions comes birth. This person says that this means 
that the conception of a self of phenomena that apprehends the aggregates to be truly existent 
is the root of cyclic existence.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
[However] Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
Seeing with his mind that all afflictive emotions and defects 
Arise from the view of the transitory collection ... 
Thus, he explains that the view of the tran sitory is the root of cyclic existence.  
These two explanations are contradictory be cause it is not feasible to have two 
discordant roots of cyclic existence. 
 
On the other hand, the Supplement to the Middle Way  says that all the faults of the afflictions 
come from the view of the transitory collection , meaning that the root of cyclic existence is 
the view of the transitory collec tion. Therefore, this person says  that it would seem that these 
two texts are contradictory. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
818  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Answer:  There is no fallacy. For this Pras angika-Madhyamika system differentiates 
the two conceptions of self [only] by way of object of observation, and the two do 
not have different aspects in their mode of apprehension because they both have 
the aspect of conceiving [their object] as being established by way of its own nature. 
A contradiction of two roots of cyclic ex istence occurs when two [consciousnesses] 
having discordant modes of apprehension  in their operation on an object are 
posited as roots of cyclic existence. 
 Therefore, when it is taught that the conception of a self of phenomena is the 
cause of the view of the transitory, it is being shown that the two inner divisions of 
ignorance are cause and effect [the conc eption of the inherent existence of 
phenomena causing the conception of such wi th respect to the person]. When it is 
taught that both of those ar e the root of the afflictive emotions, it is being shown 
that they are the root of all other afflicti ve emotions the mode of apprehension of 
which is discordant with them. Since this fact  is [true] for both of them, there is no 
contradiction, just as there is not cont radiction in both the former and later 
[moments] of a similar type of ignoranc e being the root of cyclic existence. 
 However, there is not the fault that there are two roots of cyclic exis tence. The Prasangika 
Madhyamikas distinguish the conception of a se lf of persons and conception of a self of 
phenomena in terms of their observed objects, no t in terms of their mode of apprehension in 
that both of them have the aspect of conceivi ng existence by way of its own characteristics. 
There would only be two roots of cyclic ex istence if there were posited to be two 
consciousnesses with different modes of apprehensi on as the root of cycl ic existence. When it 
is said that the conception of a self of phenomenon is the caus e of the view of the transitory 
collection, this shows that ther e are two internal divisions type s of ignorance, one that is a 
cause and one that is a result. When both are said  to be the root of the afflictions, it means 
that all other afflictions do not have the same mode of apprehension as them. Thus there is 
no contradiction, just as it is not contradictor y for earlier and later instances of ignorance to 
be the root of cyclic existence.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Although the honorable Chandrakirti does  not clearly and specifically describe 
the view of the transitory as ignorance, he does say—without differentiating persons 
and phenomena—that the conception of th ings in general as truly existent is 
afflicted ignorance, and he does assert that  the conception of a self of persons is a 
conception of persons as established by way of their own nature. He also 
frequently describes the innate view of the tr ansitory as the root of cyclic existence. 
Furthermore, if he did assert [the view of  the transitory] as another thing from the 
ignorance which is the conception of true existence, there would be the 
contradiction of positing two roots of cyclic existence which have discordant 
modes of apprehension. Therefore, both [conceptions of the true existence of 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
819 persons and phenomena] should be taken as ignorance. 
 
Chandrakirti did not clearly expl ain the view of the transitory collection as being ignorance. 
Without distinguishing persons and phenomena, he  says that the conception of true existence 
is in general afflicted ignorance. He also says that the conception of a self of person is the conception of persons as being established by way of their own characteristics, and he often 
explains the innate view of the transitory collection  to be the root of cyclic existence. If he had 
asserted the them to be different from the ig norance apprehending true  existence, he would 
have posited the contradiction of there being two roots of cyclic existence with different 
modes of apprehension. Therefore, both the conc eption of persons as truly existent and the 
conception of phenomena as truly existent are ignorance.  Tuesday morning, 6 February 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 302) says: 
(Fifth, showing that the Superior father [Nagarjuna] and his spiritual son [Aryadeva] assert that the conception of true 
existence is the root of cyclic existence:) 
All other afflictive emotions—innate and artificial—operate within apprehending 
individual features of just that object which has been superimposed [with a sense 
of inherent existence] by the innate igno rance explained above. Therefore, just as 
the other four sense powers—the eye and so forth—abide in dependence on the 
body sense power and are not located in their own independen t place other than 
[where the body sense power is], so a ll other afflictive emotions operate in 
dependence on innate ignorance;  thus, stupidity is chief.  
 
That the conception of all phenomena as truly or  inherently existent is the root of cyclic 
existence was taught by the father, Arya Nagarj una, and his spiritual son, Aryadeva. Innate 
ignorance superimposes true existence on obje cts, and then all other afflictions operate 
through apprehending their indivi dual features. Just as the eye,  ear, nose, and tongue sense 
powers rely on the body sense power and do not have their own independent locations, so 
too do all other afflictions operate in depend ence on the innate ignorance apprehending a 
self of persons and a self of phenomena, that is, apprehending true or inherent existence. 
Therefore, stupidity or delusion  is the main affliction.   
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[Aryadeva’s Four Hundred ] says: 
Just as the body sense power [pervades] the body 
Stupidity abides in all [afflictive emotions]. 
 Just as the body sense power pervades the enti re body, so too does stupidity or ignorance 
abide as the root of all the afflictions.

820 Also, desire and so forth engage in superimposing features, such as 
beauty and ugliness, on just the inherent nature of things imputed by stupidity. Therefore, they operate in a manner that is not different from stupidity and depend on stupidity, for stupidity is chief. 
 Attachment and so forth, that is, hatred, je alousy, miserliness, resentment, and the other 
afflictions, superimpose beauty, ugliness, and so forth on just the nature of things imputed by 
stupidity. Therefore, they do not engage their object in a way that differs from ignorance and 
they depend on it, since ignorance is the princi pal affliction. In other words, it is ignorance 
that gives rise to all other afflictions, the si x root afflictions as well as twenty secondary 
afflictions. Ignorance, in fact, is their root.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, when stupidity holds objects to be established by way of their own nature, if 
the apprehended object is agreeable with one’s mind, desire is generated observing 
it. If it appears to be disagreeable with one’s mind, anger is generated with respect 
to it. If the object does not appear to be either agreeable or disagreeable with one’s mind but abides as an ordinary thing in the middle, even though desire and hatred 
are not generated observing it, a continuati on of the same type of stupidity is.  
 Ignorance – the conception of true existence or the conception of inherent existence – 
apprehends objects to be established by way of their own characteristics.  Then, if the observed 
object is agreeable to one’s mind, through observ ing it attachment is ge nerated. For example, 
upon observing an observed object that one like s, such as one’s girlfriend, boyfriend, house, 
or car, attachment arises. If, on the other hand , the observed object is something that is not 
agreeable to one’s mind, through observing it ange r or hatred arises. If one observes an object 
that is neither agreeable to disagreeable, but in between, through observing it neither 
attachment or hatred are generated, whereas a later similar type of ignorance is generated. 
Similarly, it is often taught that one generates attachment for friends and strives to take care 
of them, one generates anger toward enemies and strives to harm them, and one generate 
ignorance for strangers and ignores them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Nagarjuna’s Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning  says: 
Why would the great poisonous a fflictive emotions not arise 
In those whose minds have a basis [an inherently existent object]? 
Even when [the object] is ordinary, [their minds] 
Are grasped by the snake of the afflictive emotions. 
Chandrakirti’s commentary explains it as above. 
 Just as a poisonous snake is dangerous to our we ll-being, so too are the afflictions that abide 
in our mind dangerous in that they destroy our happiness. Although we tend to point to 
external beings as our main enemies, in reality our main enemy lies within our own mind. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
821 What is it? It is the mental poisons of a ttachment, hatred, and ignorance. These mental 
poisons destroy all our happiness and bring much unhappiness, suffering, and problems.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 That the view of the transitory is produc ed from the conception of the aggregates 
as truly existent appears to be the thought also of Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland . 
How the remaining afflictive emotions are produced should be known through 
inferring it from the explanation at the point of beings of middling capacity 
[earlier in the book]. 
 
First there arises the conception  of the aggregates as truly or inherently existent, and then 
from it comes the view of the transitory coll ection. For example, when we first see someone 
who we do not know, we see his body and to our mind appears a man, whereupon we apprehend his body to be truly existent. Then wh en that person introduces himself as George, 
we also apprehend a self of persons. In this way, first the aggregates  appears and then the 
person or being appears. Due to this, first we apprehend the aggregates to be truly existent, 
and then we apprehend the person to be truly existent. In addition, we also apprehend our 
own body as I, this being the view of the transito ry collection of I, and then from this arises 
the view of the transitory collection of mine . Both of these views are included in the 
conception of a self of persons. When one ap prehends “my body,” “my head,” and so forth, 
this is the conception of a self  of phenomena, it is not the conception of a self of persons. 
 How the other afflictions are produced was explained earlier in the context of middling 
beings.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 304) says: 
(Sixth, how to understand the stages of the produc tion of afflictive emotions from Dharmakirti’s Commentary on 
(Dignaga’s) “Compendium of Prime Cognition ”22
:)  
How the afflictive emotions are produced  should also be known in accordance 
with Dharmakirti’s Commentary on (Dignaga’s) “Compendium of Prime Cognition ”:  
In one who sees self, 
There is always adherence to “I.” Through that adherence, there is attachment to pleasure. Through attachment, faults are obscured 
And advantages seen, whereby there is strong attachment. 
The “mine” are taken up as means of achieving [pleasure]. Thus, as long as there is attachment to self, So long does one revolve in cyclic existence. 
 Upon seeing oneself, there is adherence or clin ging to “I.” Due to that adherence, there is 
craving for happiness. Due to that  craving, all faults are obscur ed, that is, they are not seen. 
Due to seeing qualities, there is thorough craving.  At that time “mine” arises. As long as there 
is manifest attachment to a self, one will continue to circle in cyclic existence. 
                                             
 
22 Pramanavarttika . 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
822  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Although the system of this [passage] and the mode of positing two conceptions of 
self [in the Prasangika system] as explai ned above are different, the stages of how 
afflictive emotions are produced can be understood in dependence on it. 
 
Although the way of positing the two conception s of a self – the conception of a self of 
persons and conception of a self of phenomena – in the Prasangika system and in the above 
explanation are different, the order of how th e afflictions arise can be understood in 
dependence on it.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Initially, when the I which is the object  of observation of the thought “I” is 
conceived to exist by way of its own nature , attachment to the self is generated. 
That generates attachment to the self’s pleasure. Also, since the self’s pleasure is 
not independent but depends on the mine, there is attachment to the mine. That 
causes obscuration of the faults of the mi ne and perception of its advantages. Then, 
the mine are taken up as means of achievin g the self’s pleasure. Out of afflictive 
emotions produced in this way, actions are done. Through these actions, in turn, 
the connection is made to cyclic existence.  
 
One thinks “I, I, I.” Through observing this I,  it is conceived to ex ist by way of its own 
characteristics, whereby attachment  to the self is produced. This attachment to an I that exists 
by way of its own characteristics or inherent ly produces craving for one’s own happiness. 
Since the self’s happiness depends on mine, th ere is craving for mine (my belongings, my 
things, my face, and so forth). This obscures the faults of mine and causes one to see its good 
qualities, which in reality do not exist. Due to this, one collects many things, a house, jewelry, 
and so forth, as a means of achieving happiness.  For example, when rich women go shopping, 
immediately upon seeing a nice piece of jewelry th ey buy it even though they have no need of 
it.   In short, first there arises the thought “I,” and then the thought “min e.” Due to afflictions, 
actions are performed. Due to these actions, one joins with cyclic existence, that is, one takes 
birth again and again, continuing to wander in cyclic existence without being able to get out. 
We are just like pigs who walk with their head down even in very dirty places, thinking that they are very pleasant. Instead we must try to l ook upward and to get out of cyclic existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Nagarjuna’s Seventy Stanzas on Emptiness  says: 
Compositional activity is caused by afflictive emotions. 
The afflictive emotions that put together [or motivate actions] have a nature of 
[contaminated] actions. 
The body is caused by [contaminated] actions. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
823 All three are empty of inherent existence. 
You should train in the stages of how one revolves in cyclic existence in this way 
until ascertainment is gained. 
 
All actions or karmic formations are caused by  afflictions, that is, they are motivated by 
afflictions. In other words, they arise from ig norance. The body is caused by actions, for 
example, the human body is produced by contam inated virtuous actions. However, all three 
are empty of inherent existence, true exis tence, or existence by way of their own 
characteristics. One should train in gaining ce rtainty with respect to to how one circles in 
cyclic existence.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Third, showing that one wishing to abandon the concept ion of self should seek the view of selflessness 
This has two parts: the reason why, if yo u wish to abandon ignorance, you should 
seek the view realizing selflessness an d how to generate the view realizing 
selflessness. 
 
If one wishes to abandon ignorance it is necessa ry to seek the view realizing selflessness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
First, the reason why, if you wish to abandon ignoranc e, you should seek the view realizing selflessness 
(This section has four parts, the first of  which is: first, that not striving to gener ate the pure view in your own continuum 
despite wanting to abandon ignorance is dumb:) 
A state of removal in which the two conc eptions of self—the ignorance explained 
before—have been abandoned should certainl y be wanted and appears indeed to be 
wanted. Nevertheless, despite its being want ed, there are those who do not strive at 
understanding how the conception of self co mes to be the root of cyclic existence 
or, although they see a portion of that, do not strive for the sake of generating the 
pure view of selflessness in their contin uum, having refuted well, with reasoning 
and scriptures of definitive meaning, the ob ject conceived by the conception of self. 
They have very dull faculties, for althou gh they have lost th e life of the path 
progressing to liberation and omniscie nce, they are not concerned about it. 
 
If one wishes to abandon ignorance it would be foolish and dull not to strive to generate pure view. Although it is necessary to want to  abandon the two conceptions of a self, the 
conception of a self of persons and the concep tion of a self of phenomena, there are those 
who wish to abandon ignorance or the conception  of a self but do not strive to understand 
how the conception of a self becomes the root of cyclic existence. Th ere are also those who 
see a portion of that, but do not strive to ge nerate the pure view of selflessness in their 
continuum through refuting – by means of reason ings and definitive meaning scriptures – the 
object apprehended by the conception of true  existence of persons and phenomena. Such 
persons have very dull faculties because even thou gh they have lost the life of the path that 
proceeds to liberation and omniscience, they are not concerned about it. In other words, 
although if someone were to try to take our li f e  w e  w o u l d  b e  v e r y  c o n c e r n e d ,  i n  s p i t e  o f  
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
824 having lost the life of liberation and buddhah ood we are not concerned. Therefore, because 
this is very foolish and stupid, we should not act like this but instead strive to abandon the 
conception of a self of persons and the conception of a self of phenomena. In other words, we 
must strive to apply the antidote to the view of  the transitory collection of the I and mine in 
order to abandon it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The second, showing that the conception of self must be abandoned, has two parts: how such is taught in treatises and 
in sutras. The former has five parts, the first of which is : how the glorious Dharmakirti shows that the conception of 
self must be abandoned:) 
The glorious Dharmakirti’s Commentary on (Dignaga’s) “Compendium of Prime 
Cognition ” says: 
Without disbelieving its object, 
It cannot be abandoned. The abandonment of desire , hatred, and so forth, 
Which are related with [perceiving] advantages and disadvantages, Is through not seeing those in [their] objects; Not through external ways. 
 
In his Commentary on (Dignaga’s) “Compendium of Prime Cognition” , Dharmakirti shows that the 
conception of a self must be abandoned. Withou t refuting the object of the conception of a 
self, the conception of a self cannot be aban doned. Due to the conception of a self, some 
phenomena appear to have qualities whereas ot hers appear to have faults, due to which 
attachment, hatred, and so forth arise. In order to abandon them, one must not see the 
qualities and faults as abiding inside the object , but see them as abiding in our mind. In other 
words, due to thinking  beauty, attachment arises, whereas due to thinking  ugly, hatred arises. 
These minds cannot be taken away by external means. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
When an external object of abandonment such as a thorn that has pierced oneself 
is removed, it can be removed from the root with a needle, for instance, without 
depending on [a process of] coming to di sbelieve the object that it conceives. 
However, when an internal mental object of abandonment is abandoned, it is not 
done in this way. Rather, it must be abandoned through seeing the non-existence 
of the object as it is conceived by, for instance, the conception of self. 
 
Whereas an external object such as a thorn can be removed by a needle and so forth, unlike 
this, in order to remove the conception of a se lf, one has to realize that the object of the 
conception of a self does not exist. Thus, th e ignorance that apprehends true existence or 
inherent existence or existence from its own side is abandoned by abandoning its apprehended object through realizing that it do es not exist. As a result of this, the other 
afflictions are abandoned. Hence, their ab andonment is not like removing a thorn. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, how the glorious Chandrakirti shows that the conception of self must be abandoned:) 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
825 Also, the glorious Chandrakirti says th at when one sees that all afflictive 
emotions—desire and so forth—and all defe cts—birth, death, and so forth—arise 
from the conception of self, a wish to cease and abandon it arises, whereupon a 
yogi refutes with reasoning the self of persons, the object superimposed by the 
conception of self.  
 
Chandrakirti also shows how the conception of a self must be abandoned. He says that upon 
seeing that all afflictions, attachment, anger, igno rance, and so forth, as well as all faults, such 
as birth, aging, death, and so forth, arise from the conception of a self, one will wish to 
abandon and cease it. Due to this, one refutes by  means of reasons the existence of a self – 
true or inherent existence.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
His Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  clearly says: 
Seeing with his mind that all afflictive emotions and defects 
Arise from the view of transitory collection And realizing that the self is the object of this, A yogi refutes self. 
This must be done by a meditator on suchness; hence, he says “a yogi”. 
 Upon seeing with intelligence that all afflic tions and defects arise from the view of the 
transitory collection, and realizing that the self – the conventional self – is the observed object 
of the view of the transitory collection, the yogi  refutes the self. It should be noted that there 
is a conventional self and a self that is an object of negation; the latter one being the self that 
needs to be refuted. It is due to observing th e conventional self that one thinks “I” and the 
conception of a self arises. Since it is a medita tor who must refute this self, Chandrakirti calls 
him or her “yogi.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, this is the thought also of the protector Nagarjuna:) 
This procedure is the excellent thought al so of the protector Nagarjuna, for his 
Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning  says: 
That is the cause of all [bad] views. 
Without it, afflictive emotions are not produced. Therefore, when that is thoroughly known, 
[Bad] view and afflictive emotions are thoroughly purified. 
By what is that known? 
It is said to be by the supreme knowledge of suchness, The seeing of dependent-arising, that what Is dependently produced is not [inherently] produced. 
 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
826 Nagarjuna also says that view of the transitory  collection arises from viewing the observed 
object that is the conventional self and adheri ng to it as truly existent or self-sufficient 
substantially existent. It is this truly existent or self-sufficient substantially existent self that is 
to be abandoned. 
 Nagarjuna’s Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning  says that the ignorance that apprehends true existence 
is the cause of all views. If it does not exist, the afflictions will  not arise. Therefore, when that 
is thoroughly understood, views and affliction s are thoroughly purified. By what is that 
known? It is known by realizing dependent-arisin g, that is, by seeing all phenomena as being 
dependent-arisings, that is, as dependently produced. This is the supreme knowledge of 
suchness. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, he indicates that the conception of  true existence—called the assertion of 
inherent existence,23 which is the cause of all afflic ted views and all other afflictive 
emotions—is abandoned through realizing the suchness of thin gs, the absence of 
inherently existent production, by means of the reason of dependent-arising. The 
perception that inherent existence does not exist does not occur without coming 
to disbelieve the object of the conception  of things as inherently established. 
 
There are five afflicted views, such as the view of the transitory collection and so forth. The 
conception of true existence is the cause of al l these afflicted views as well as all the other 
afflictions, such as attachment, anger, pride, and so on. The co nception of true existence is 
abandoned through realizing the lack of inhere ntly existent production by means of the 
reason of dependent-arising, whereby all afflicted views and all other afflictions are also abandoned. The seeing that inherent existe nce does not exist does not arise without 
repudiating the object of the conception of thin gs as inherently existent. Therefore, it is 
necessary to realize the selflessness of pe rsons and the selflessness of phenomena.

on, 6 February 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 306) says: 
(Fourth, how Aryadeva shows that the conception of self must be abandoned:) 
A statement by Aryadeva that accords with this was quoted earlier; also, his Four 
Hundred  says: 
When selflessness is seen in objects, 
The seed of cyclic existence ceases. 
He says that through seeing the non-existenc e of the self [inherent existence] of the 
object of observation conceived by the co nception of self, the root of cyclic 
existence—ignorance—is cut. 
 Aryadeva says that when selflessness is seen with respect to objects, the seed of cyclic existence 
will cease. Seeing the selflessness of persons direct ly is the antidote to th e afflictions or to the 
                                             
 
23 dngos  po, bhava.  
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
827 afflictive obscurations. A seed is what has the potential to produce an affliction in the 
continuum. Through seeing that the self that is observed object – a truly existent, inherently 
existent, or self-sufficient substantially existent person – of the conception of a self does not 
exist, the root of cyclic existe nce, ignorance, is cut. Thereby,  due to their root having been 
eradicated, the sufferings of birth, aging, de ath, and so forth will no longer arise.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, how Shantideva shows that the conception of self must be abandoned:) 
Also, the venerable Shantideva says: 
Thus, the emptiness of the person is thoroughly established. Therefore, 
by cutting their root all afflictive emotions do not arise in any way. 
 
Shantideva also says that through establishing  the selflessness of persons, the root of the 
afflictions is cut whereby they will no longer ar ise again in any way. In other words, since the 
root of all the afflictions is th e ignorance apprehending persons to  be truly existent, when it is 
eliminated the afflictions no longer arise.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, how it is shown in sutra that the conception of self must be abandoned:) 
It is as the Sutra on the Secrecies of the Tathagatas  says:  
Shantimati, it is like this. For exampl e, when the roots of a tree are cut, 
all the branches, leaves, and twigs dr y. Shantimati, similarly, when the 
view of the transitory collection is pacified, all afflictive emotions and 
secondary afflictive emotions are pacified. 
 
This sutra says that just as when the roots of a tree are cut, all its branches, leaves, and twigs 
dry up, similarly when the view of the transitory  collection is pacified, all root and secondary 
afflictions are also pacified.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, it is being said that through cultivating realization of the person as empty of 
inherent existence the view of the transi tory is overcome and that when it is 
overcome, all other afflictive emotions are overcome. Furthermore, without 
coming to disbelieve the object of the conc eption of a self of persons, a realization 
of selflessness cannot occur. The passage fr om sutra indicates that the view of the 
transitory is the root of all other afflicti ve emotions; if it were another thing from 
ignorance, there would be two discordant ro ots of cyclic existence; thus, it should 
be taken as ignorance. 
 Through familiarizing with the realization that th e person is empty of inherent existence, the 
view of the transitory collection is overcome. When it is overcome, all other afflictions are 
also overcome, that is, they cease. However, wi thout repudiating the object of the conception 
of a self of persons, a realization of selflessn ess is not possible. The citation from the sutra 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
828 above indicates that the root of all other afflic tions is the view of the transitory collection. 
Therefore, if the view of the transitory collect ion were something other than ignorance, there 
would be two discordant roots of cyclic existe nce. Therefore, the view of the transitory 
collection is ignorance, that is, it is the ig norance not knowing selflessness or emptiness. In 
short, since the view of the tran sitory collection is the root of  cyclic existence, it is the 
ignorance that is a non-knowing of  the nature of all phenomena.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, analysis by way of scripture and reasoning is important for abandoning the conception of self:) 
In brief, when the many supreme schola rs who commented on the meaning of the 
scriptures on the profound [emptiness] settled the meaning of suchness, they 
analyzed by way of scripture and reasoning. They did this from having perceived 
that selflessness and emptiness cannot be re alized without seeing that the self as 
conceived by erroneous conception do es not exist and without seeing the 
emptiness of that self. It is important to  gain ascertainment with respect to this. 
 
In order to abandon the conception of a self it is necessary to analyze it by way of scripture 
and reasoning. In brief, when many supreme scholars who commented on the meaning of the 
scriptures on the profound settled the meaning of suchness, they anal yzed it by means of 
many scriptures and reasonings. Without seeing th at the self that is apprehended by mistaken 
conception, the object of negation, does not exis t, it is not possible to realize selflessness or 
emptiness. therefore, it is important to understand  this and gain certainty with respect to it by 
analyzing this in the same way as the past scholars. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The fourth has two parts, the first of which is: that in order to abandon the conception of self it is necessary to come to 
disbelieve its referent object:) 
For, if you do not meditate on the me aning of the negation of the erroneous 
object which is the root of being bound in cyclic existence, even though you 
meditate on the meaning of something else which you consider to be profound, it 
w i l l  n o t  h a r m  t h e  c o n c e p t i o n  o f  s e l f  a t  a l l .  T h i s  i s  b e c a u s e ,  u n l e s s  t h e  m i n d  becomes absorbed in the suchness of selfle ssness and emptiness, an overcoming of 
the conception of self cannot occur. Also, without coming to disbelieve the object 
of the conception of self, although you perform the mere withdrawal of the mind 
that moves there to its object, this cannot constitute being absorbed in selflessness. 
 
It is necessary to repudiate the referent or determined object ( zhen yul ) of the conception of a 
self in order to abandon the conception of a self. If one does not meditate on the meaning 
that negates the mistaken object which is the root  of bondage in cyclic existence, even though 
one meditates on something else that is asse r t e d  t o  b e  p r o f o u n d ,  i t  w i l l  n o t  h a r m  t h e  
conception of a self at all. Fo r example, although it is good to meditate on the impermanence 
that is momentary change, this meditation ca nnot harm the conception of a self. This is 
because without engaging the suchness of selfle ssness or emptiness, it is not possible to 
eliminate the conception of a self. Also, without repudiating the object of the conception of a 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
829 self, even though the mind goes out and retu rns inward, it cannot be posited to engage 
selflessness. 
 In short, in order to cut the root of cyclic ex istence, the conception of a self, it is necessary 
to realize selflessness or emptiness. By medita ting on this continually , the root of cyclic 
existence can be cut and one can abandon cycl ic existence and achieve the state of foe-
destroyer or nirvana.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The second, the reason for that, has three parts, the first of which is: the existence in general of three modes of 
conceiving I:)  
The reason for this is that there are th ree modes of the mind’s operating on an 
object—(1) apprehending the object of obse rvation to exist truly, (2) apprehending 
it not to exist truly, and (3) apprehending it without qualifying it with either of 
those. Therefore, although one is not conceiving [an ob ject] as not truly existent, 
one is not necessarily conceiving it as tr uly existent. Just so, although one is not 
involved in the two selves, it is not necessary that one is involved in the two 
selflessnesses. For, there are limitles s minds abiding in the third category. 
 There are three modes in which the mind engages an observed object: 
1. it apprehends the observed object to be truly existent, 
2. it apprehends the observed object  to be empty of true existence, 
3. it apprehends it without it being qualified by  either true existence or non-true existence. 
Thus, although one is not conceiving an object to be non-truly existent, one is not necessarily 
conceiving it to be truly existent since there is  also a third mode. Likewise, even if one is not 
engaging the two selves, it is not necessar ily the case that one is engaging the two 
selflessnesses. This is because there are limitle ss minds in the third category, that is, there are 
many minds that apprehend an object without conc eiving it to be either truly existent or non-
truly existent. For example, there is a mind appr ehending its object to be impermanent rather 
than to be truly existent or non-truly existent.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 308) says: 
(Second, the need to ascertain just that basis—with res pect to which a consciousness conceiving true existence is 
mistaken—as not existing in the way that it is conceived:) 
Furthermore, because the two conceptions of  self operate mainly within observing 
persons and phenomena, you need to asce rtain just those bases—with respect to 
which the error is made—as not existing in the way that they are conceived. 
Otherwise, it like searching for robber on the plain after he  has gone to the 
woods.24 
 
The conception of true existenc e is of two types: a conception  of a self of persons and a 
conception of a self of phenomena. The concepti on of a self of persons observes persons and 
the conception of a self of phenomena observ es the aggregates. Therefore, one needs to

Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Since error is removed by meditating on  the meaning which has been ascertained 
in that way, such an empt iness is the supreme meaning of suchness. Ascertainment 
of the meaning of suchness as something else that forsakes this is exhausted as only 
something imputed by one’s own wish. Th us, hold [in mind] that this is a 
deviating from the meaning of the scriptures. 
 
Having established the meaning of emptiness on e must meditate on it continually, whereby 
mistaken conceptions will be eliminated. This  meaning of emptiness is the supreme meaning 
of suchness. If, on the other hand, one fors akes this and looks for another meaning of 
suchness, one will deviate from the meaning of th e Buddha’s scriptures. Thus, it is necessary 
to study the scriptures and treatises that expl ain the meaning of suchness and try to realize 
this very meaning. In short, one should not se arch for another type of emptiness invented by 
oneself as this will only be a waste of time and will not fulfill any aim whatsoever.    
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, the need to value realization of the view of suc hness upon seeing that when the conc eption of true existence is 
overcome, cyclic existence is overcome:) 
 Therefore, the ignorance which conceives the elaborat ions of persons—such as 
man and woman—and of phenomena—such as form and feeling—as truly existent is 
overcome by finding the view realizing empt iness, selflessness, and cultivating it in 
meditation. When that ignorance is ov ercome, one overcomes the conceptuality 
which is the improper mental application superimposing signs of beauty, ugliness, 
and so forth upon observing the objects of the conception of true existence. When 
that is overcome, the other afflictive em otions—desire and so forth—which have the 
view of the transitory as their root, are overcome. When they are overcome, actions motivated by them are overcome. When they are overcome, powerless birth in cyclic existence impelled by acti ons is overcome, whereby liberation is 
attained. 
 
Since cyclic existence is overco me when the conception of true  existence is overcome, it is 
necessary to cherish the realization of the view  of suchness. The ignorance that conceives the 
elaborations of persons, such as men and wome n, and phenomena, such as forms, feelings, 
and so forth, as truly existent is overcome th rough finding the view realizing selflessness and 
emptiness and meditating on that. When that ignorance is overcome, the conceptualization 
that is improper mental attention which superi mposes signs of beauty, ugliness, and so forth 
through observing the object of the conception of true existence is ov ercome. When that is 
overcome, other afflictions, such as attachment , anger, pride, and so forth which have the 
view of the transitory collection  as their root are also overcome. When they are overcome, the 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
831 actions motivated by them are overcome. When they are overcome, the powerless taking of 
rebirth in cyclic existence which is  projected by them is overcome. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Thinking on that [process], you should generate firm ascertainment and then unerringly seek the view of suchness. Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  says: 
By removing actions and afflictive emotions, there is liberation. 
Actions and afflictive emotions [arise] from conception [improper mental 
application]. 
It arises from fictional elaborations  [conceptions of true existence]. 
Fictional elaborations cease by [cul tivating realization of] emptiness. 
You need to value understanding the stages of how one enters into and disengages from cyclic existence in this way and th ereupon realizes the meaning of suchness. 
It will not come through vague invo lvement in which the points are not 
differentiated well.  
 Therefore, one should generate firm ascertainm ent thinking “I will attain liberation,” and 
then seek the view of suchness. Nagarjuna says that by exhausting the afflictions and actions, 
there is liberation. In short, by realizing em ptiness the afflictions cease, and due to their 
ceasing actions also cease.   It is important to understand the stages of how one enters into and reverses from cyclic 
existence and realize the meaning of suchne ss. (I have never come across the word tom ‘jug  – 
here translated as “vague involvement.”) In  short, this will not happen without clearly 
distinguishing the observed object. In other wo rds, we need to understand the exact meaning 
of selflessness and emptiness, distinguish it clea rly, and then meditate on it, as this will 
definitely bring a result.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 309) says: 
 
PART TWO: THE VIEW 
 
The presentation of how to generate the vi ew realizing selflessness has three parts: 
the stages of generating the two views of selflessness, the actual generation of the 
two views in stages, and a presentation of conventional and ultimate truths. 
 
First the order of generating the two views of se lflessness is explained, then the generation of 
the two views is explained to have an order, an d then conventional truths and ultimate truths 
are presented.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
832 2 Order of Realization 
Stages of generating the two views of selflessness 
(This has two parts: the act ual explanation and the reason. 
First, the actual explanation:) 
With respect to the stages  of generating the two conceptions of self, the 
conception of a self of phenomena generate s the conception of a self of persons. 
However, when entering into the suchness of  selflessness, you should first generate 
the view of the selflessness of persons an d then generate the view realizing the 
selflessness of phenomena.  
 
With respect to the order of generating the two conceptions of a self, first the conception of a 
self of phenomena is generated and then the co nception of a self of persons. On the other 
hand, with respect to the order of generating th e two selflessnesses, first the realization of the 
self of persons is generated and then the re alization of the selflessness of phenomena.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
It is as Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland  says: 
If the persons is not earth, not water, 
Not fire, not wind, not space, Not consciousness, and not all [of them] What person is there other than those? 
Just as a person is not [established in] reality 
Because of being [designated in dependence upon] an aggregation of the six 
constituents, 
So each constituent also is not [established 
In] reality because of being [designated in dependence upon an aggregation. 
Thus, he speaks first of the absence of in herent existence of persons and then of 
the constituents—earth and so forth—wh ich are the bases of designation of a 
person. 
 The person or being is not the collection of the six elements or constitu ents, nor is the person 
the earth element, water element, fire el ement, wind element, space element, and 
consciousness element. So what is the person? Th e person or being is imputed on the basis of 
the collection or aggregation of the six elemen ts, therefore the person is not established in 
reality. Just as the being is not established as  the individual elements but is imputed on the 
collection of elements, so too are the individual  elements or constituents also not established 
in reality. In this way, Nagarjuna first sets  out the selflessness of  persons and then the 
selflessness of the six constituents which ar e the basis of designation of the person.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Also, Chandrakirti’s Clear Words  and Buddhapalita’s Commentary on (Nagarjuna’s) 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
833 “Treatise on the Middle”25 explain that when entering into suchness, one initially 
enters by way of the selflessness of pe rsons. Shantideva also says the same. 
 
Both Chandrakirti and Buddhapalita explain th at when engaging suchness, one does so 
initially by way of the selflessness of persons. Shantideva also says this. Why is it this so? It is 
because it is easier to realize the selflessness of persons than the selflessness of phenomena. 
Because the person or being is im puted on the basis of the five a ggregates, it depends or relies 
on the aggregates. Since it is relatively easy to  understand how the person exists in terms of 
being imputed on the aggregates, it is easier to realize the selflessness of persons. However, the 
conception of a self of phenomena arises before the conception of a self of persons. As I said 
before, when someone we do not know enters  the room, we initially see his body and 
apprehend it to be truly existent. It is only af ter that, when the person introduces himself to 
us, that we come to apprehend a truly existent person.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 309) says: 
(The second, the reason for this, has two parts, th e first of which is: a proof through reasoning:) 
The reason why it must be done this way is  that, although there is no difference in 
subtlety with regard to the selflessness to be ascertained in terms of the persons or 
phenomena that are its base, selflessness is easier to ascertain in terms of a person 
due to essentials of the substratum,26 w h e r e a s  i t  i s  m o r e  d i f f i c u l t  t o  a s c e r t a i n  i n  
terms of [other] phenomena. For example, si nce it is more difficult to ascertain the 
selflessness of phenomena in terms of an eye, ear, and so forth and easier to 
ascertain it in terms of a reflection and so  forth, the latter are posited as examples 
for settling selflessness in terms of the former. 
 The selflessness of persons and the selflessness of phenomena are not di fferent in terms of 
their subtlety. However, it is ea sier to realize the selflessness of  persons than the selflessness of 
phenomena in terms of the base. The emptiness of inherent existence of phenomena such as 
the eye, ear, nose, tongue, and body is more di fficult to ascertain, whereas it is easier to 
ascertain it in terms of a reflection in a mirro r, an echo, and so forth which are examples of 
things that do not exist from their own side. This is because we all understand that the 
reflection of our face in a mirror appears to be our face but is not. Th erefore, the selflessness 
of phenomena such as eyes and so forth is se ttled by means of positing such examples.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 310) says: 
(Second, a proof through scripture:) 
In consideration of this fact, the King of Meditative Stabilization Sutra27 says: 
Just as you have known the discrimination of self, 
Apply this mentally to all [phenomena]. 
All phenomena are completely devoid
