834 Of their own entityness, like space. 
Through one all are known. Through one all are also seen. 
 This sutra says having understood the discrimina tion of the self, one should apply this to all 
other phenomena. All phenomena are the nature of selflessness, that is, purified of true 
existence, like space. By one, all ar e known. By one, all are also seen.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
It says that when you know well the mo de of subsistence of I—with respect to 
which the discrimination of self, thinki ng “I”, operates—you should apply its 
reasoning to all phenomena, internal such as eye and ear and external such as pot, 
and understand them in the same way. Thereby, through knowing and seeing the 
natural mode of one phenomenon, the na ture of all other phenomena is known 
and seen. 
 
When one knows the mode of abidance of the I with respect to which the discrimination of 
self thinking I engages, one should apply th is reasoning to all other phenomena including 
inner phenomena such as the eye, ear, nose, and so forth as well as external phenomena such 
as pots, pillars, houses, mountains, lakes, and so forth. Through knowing and seeing the 
mode of abidance of one phenomenon, one will know and see the nature of all other phenomena.   In this way, we should try to understand  that all phenomena are empty of true and 
inherent existence, that is, that they do not ex ist truly, inherently, or independently but exist 
dependently, that is, by relying on other.  Wednesday morning, 7 February 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 310) says: 
3  Selflessness of Persons 
The presentation of the actual generation of the two views in st ages has two parts: 
settling the selflessness of persons and settling the selflessness of phenomena. 
Settling the selflessness of persons 
This section has two parts: identifying pe rsons and settling them as not inherently 
existent. 
 
The two views of the self are ge nerated in a particular order in  that first the selflessness of 
persons is settled and then the selflessness of phenomena is settled. To  settle the selflessness 
of persons it is necessary to first identify persons or beings, after which it is necessary to settle 
them as not inherently existent.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
835 IDENTIFYING PERSONS  
(This section has four parts, the first of wh ich is: the actual ident ification of persons:) 
“Persons” are the persons of the six ty pes—gods, demi-gods, humans, animals, 
hungry ghosts, and hell-beings—as well as persons who are common beings and 
Superiors, and so forth, They are the accumulators of wholesome and 
unwholesome actions, the experiencers of the fruits of those actions, the revolvers 
in cyclic existence, the cultivators of path s for the sake of liberation, the attainers 
of liberation, and so forth. 
 
Persons are identified as the persons of the si x classes, that is, as gods, demigods, human 
beings, animals, hungry ghosts, and hell beings. They are also persons who are ordinary beings 
and persons who are aryas or superiors. They ar e the accumulators of white actions, that is, 
virtuous actions, and black actions, that is , non-virtuous actions. They are also the 
experiencers of the results of white or vi rtuous actions, that is, happiness, and the 
experiencers of the results of black or non-virt uous actions, that is, suffering. They are also 
those who circle in cyclic exis tence, those who meditate paths in order to achieve liberation, 
and those who attain liberation and omniscie nce. In this way the actual persons are 
identified. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, commenting on the meaning of a sutra teachi ng that persons are designated to the aggregates:) 
A sutra cited in Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) 
‘Treatise on the Middle’” and so forth says: 
Self is a devilish mind. 
You are under the control of a [bad] view. These conditioned aggregates are empty. There is no sentient being here. 
Just as a chariot is designated 
In dependence on collections of parts So conventionally a sentient being [Is designated] depending on the aggregates. 
 It is taught in sutra that the person or being is merely imputed on the aggregates. 
Chandrakirti says: 
• that which is called “self,” that is, the concepti on of a self, is the mind of a mara or demon, 
• we are under the control of views such as th e view of the transitory collection, extreme 
view, and so forth, 
• the compounded aggregates are empty, that  is, empty of inherent existence, and  
• a sentient being does not exist on them, that is, a truly exis tent sentient being does not 
exist on the aggregates, meaning that if one l ooks for a sentient being in the aggregates, it 
will not be found.  
The next verse says that although “chariot” is designated in depend ence on its parts, if each of 
the individual parts is taken away none of them are found to be the chariot. In a similar way, 
conventionally a sentient being exists in dependence on the aggregates.   
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
836  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
The first stanza indicates the selflessness of  persons—that ultimately persons do not 
exist. The first line says that  the conception of a self of  persons is a devilish mind. 
The second, that one who conceives such is under the control of a bad view. The 
third and fourth, that the aggregates are empty of a self of persons. 
 The second stanza indicates that person s exist conventionally. The first two lines 
of it give an example; the last two conne ct it to the meaning, indicating that 
persons are only designated in dependence on the aggregates. 
 The first stanza shows the selfle ssness of persons which is that persons do not exist ultimately 
. The first line says that the conception of a self of person, that is , the conception of the 
pers o n  as  in heren t ly exis t en t  o r self-s ufficien t  substantially existent, is a mind of a mara or 
demon. Just as a demon or mara only harms and never helps, so too does the conception of a 
self only harm and never help. The second line shows that one who conceives this is under the control of a bad view. The third and fourth line show that the aggregates are empty of a 
self of persons. It is also often said that the a ggregates do not exist as the object of utilization 
of a self-sufficient substantially existent person or inherently existent person, that is, they are 
empty of being such an object of utilization.  The second stanza shows that persons exist conventionally. The first two lines show an 
example and the last two lines connect the ex ample to the meaning, thereby showing that 
persons are merely imputed in dependence on th e aggregates and do not exist inherently. In 
other words, the person is merely imputed by name and conception on the basis of 
imputation, the aggregates.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, how the person—the phenomenon designated—is posited as the object of observati on of the innate conception 
of an [inherently existent] I:) 
This sutra speaks of the collection of the ag gregates as the basis of designation of a 
person. Also, since a basis of designation is not feasible to be the object designated 
and since the collection of the aggregates mu st be taken as both a collection at one 
time and a collection over former and later [moments], it is not feasible to posit 
the continuum of the collection of the aggr egates as a person. When the collection 
is posited as the basis of designation, th e possessors of the collection [that is, the 
parts] are also posited as the bases of de signation, and thus bo th of those are not 
fit to be a person.  
 
The person is posited as the observed object of the innate conception of I or the innate view 
of the transitory collection. This sutra shows that  the collection of the aggregates is the basis 
of imputation of the person. The basis of imputa tion, the aggregates, is not suitable to be the 
imputed phenomenon. Since the collection of aggr egates is necessarily both the collection at 
one particular time and the collection of former and later moments, it is not suitable to posit 
the continuum of the collection of the aggregates  as the person. If the collection is posited as 
the basis of imputation, the possessor of the collection is also posited as the basis of 
imputation. Therefore, both are not suitable to be the person. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
837  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
Sutra says it is in dependence on the aggregates. 
Thus the mere composite of the aggregate is not the self. 
Also: 
Objection:  The Teacher asserts the aggregates as the self 
Because he said, “The a ggregates are the self.” 
[Answer: ] That refutes a self other than the aggregates, 
For other sutras say that form is not the self, and so forth. 
 Sutra says that the person depends on the aggreg ates, therefore the composite of aggregates is 
not the self, that is, not the person. Here this is  not discussing the self that is the object of 
negation.   Chandrakirti’s text says that someone makes the objection that Buddha Shakyamuni 
asserts the aggregates to be the self given that he said “The aggregates are the self.” However, 
the answer is that this does no t mean that the aggregates are the self, but rather means that 
the aggregates are one entity with  the self whereby it refutes a se lf that is a different entity 
from the aggregates, because other sutras say that form is not the self and so forth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, even the statement [in sutra], “The view of I and mine by practitioners of 
virtue and Brahmins is a viewing only of these five aggregates,” refutes through the 
term “only” that a self—which is a different  entity from the aggregates—exists as the 
object of observation of an innate concepti on of an [inherently existent] I. Having 
refuted such, it does not indicate that th e aggregates are the object of observation 
of a conception of [an inherently existent ] I. If it did, it would contradict the 
refutation in other sutras that the five a ggregates are the self. For, the object of 
observation—from within the object of observation and subjective aspect of a 
conception of [an inherently existent] I—is necessarily posited as the self. Therefore, 
the meaning of the statement in sutra [abo ve] about viewing the aggregates must be 
explained as referring to observing the self that is imputed to the aggregates. 
 
Sutra says: “Trainees in virtue and brahmins, the view of I and mine views only the five 
aggregates.” I is the being and mine is the a ggregates such as my head, my feeling, my 
discrimination, and so forth. By means of the te rm “only” this statement refutes that a self 
which is a different entity from the aggregates exists as the observed object of the innate 
conception of I. Having refuted that, it does no t indicate that the aggregates are the observed 
object of the conception of I. If it were so, it  would contradict the sutras that refute the five 
aggregates as being the self. From among the two – the observed object and the aspect – of the 
conception of I, the object that is observed is necessary posited as the self. This self refers to the conventionally existent self and not to the ul timately existent self. In short, the observed 
object of the conception of I is the se lf that is imputed on the aggregates.  
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
838  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, the need, therefore, to differentiate between the self that exists and the self that does not:) 
Hence, you should differentiate between th e references to the mere I, called self, 
which exists conventionally and the inherently existent person, called self, which 
does not exist even conventionally. You shou ld not say that this system asserts that 
a self [inherent existence] of persons exis ts  co n v en t io n ally [ev en  t ho ugh it  is the  
case that self, meaning person, exists conventionally]. 
 Such an identification of persons is a distinguishing feature of this unsurpassed system. Ascertaining this well is a g ood technique for realizing the uncommon 
selflessness of persons. 
 Since there are two selves, one that exists and one that does not exist, it is necessary to 
differentiate between them. The mere I, considered  to be a self, exists conventionally, whereas 
the inherently existent person, considered to be a self, does not exist even conventionally. 
One should not say that the Prasangika Madhyamaka  system asserts that a self of persons, that 
is, an inherently existent person, exists conv entionally. This particular identification of 
persons is a distinguishing feature of this unsu rpassed system. To ascertain the identification 
of the persons in the Prasangika Madhyamaka system as being the mere I imputed on the 
basis of imputation, the aggregates, is th e best method for realizing the uncommon 
selflessness of persons.  The common selflessness of persons is the em ptiness of the person of self-sufficient 
substantial existence in that th is selflessness is common to all four Buddhist tenet systems as 
well as to both the Svatantrika Madhyamaka an d Prasangika Madhyamaka. However, the view 
of the emptiness of the person of inherent existence is unique only to the Prasangika 
Madhyamaka. This is because Svat antrika Madhyamaka asserts that if something exists it must 
exist inherently, and if it does not exist inherently  it does not exist at all, like the horns of a 
rabbit or a sky-flower. Prasangika Madhyamaka, on the other hand, says that if something 
exists inherently it cannot change and cannot co me into being due to ca uses and conditions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
SETTLING PERSONS AS NOT INHERENTLY EXISTENT 
This has three parts: settling I as not in herently existent, settling mine as not 
inherently existent, and showing how, in dependence on this, persons appear as 
like illusions. 
Settling I as not inherently existent 
(This has two parts, how to generate the view realizing the selflessness of persons by way of the four essentials in 
complete form and an extensive explanation of the third and fourth essentials in particular. The former has four 
parts, the first which is: 
The essential of ascertaining the object of negation:) 
Concerning this, the first of the four e ssentials is to identify the mode of 
conceiving a self of persons through analyzing your own continuum. It was 
explained earlier. 
 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
839 The first essential point is to ascertain the object  of negation. This is because if the object of 
negation is not recognized, it cannot be refuted.  For example, when figh ting a war if one does 
not recognize who is a friend and who is an en emy, one will shoot randomly, perhaps killing 
even those who are friends. Similarly in this case, one first has to recognize the object of 
negation, that is, one has to identify the way in  which one conceives a se lf of persons through 
analyzing one’s own mental continuum. This has already been explained before.  
 I n  s h o r t ,  f i r s t  o f  a l l  i t  i s  n e c e s s a r y  t o  r e cognize the object of negation. The object of 
negation is not the conventionally existent self, ra ther it is the self that is conceived to exist 
from its own side, to exist inherently, or exist se lf-sufficiently and substantially. This is what 
has to be recognized. Therefore, the first the essential point is to re cognize the object of 
negation.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The second, the essential of ascertaining pervasion, has two pa rts, the first of which is: that if a person is inherently 
established, it does not pass beyond being either one or different:) 
The second essential is the decision that if  a person is inherently established, it 
must be established as either one entity  with or a different entity from the 
aggregates and that there is no mode of establishment other than those two. 
 
Then one must analyze the fact that if the person  is inherently established, it must be either 
inherently established as one or as many. In othe r words, the self is either one or many, there 
being no third alternative. Thus, the second esse ntial point is that if the person or self is 
inherently existent, it must be either an inhe rently existent one or an inherently existent 
many. It cannot be established in another way.  One must come to a strong determination 
regarding this, after which one will be able to de cide that the self does not exist inherently 
because it does not exist as an inherently exis tent one or as an inherently existent many.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, since in general not being single and single are explicitly contradictory [a  dichotomy], a third category which is 
not either one or many is excluded:)  
It is established through experience that  when “pot and pillar,” for instance, are 
positively set off as not single,28 it is eliminated that they are single. [Similarly] 
when “pot,” for instance, is positively set off as single, it is eliminated that it is not 
single. Hence, there is no third category  which is not either one or many. Thus 
[the second essential] is to ascertain th at something which is not one entity or 
different entities does not occur.  
 
Not being single and being single are directly co ntradictory, and there is no third category of 
neither being one or many. When “pot and pillar, ” for example, are established as not single, 
that is, as many, it is automatically eliminat ed that they are single. Also when “pot” is 
established to be one, it is eliminated as bein g many. Therefore, there is no third category of 
being neither one or many. Therefore, there is no phenomena that is neither one entity or 
different entities. This means that the person or being, for example, is either one entity with 
the aggregates or a different entity from the a ggregates, there being no third category of that 
                                              
28 zla bcas = having an equivalent, having a mate; zl a med = not having an equivalent, not having a mate. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
840 which is neither one entity or a different entity  from the aggregates. Such a third category is 
impossible. Therefore, the imputed person is one entity with the basis of imputation, the 
aggregates, and a not different entity.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The third essential is to see the damage to [the position that] a person and the 
aggregates are one inherently existent enti ty. The fourth essential is to see well the 
damage [to the position that] those two are inherently different. 
 
The third essential point is to see that there is harm to the position that the person and 
aggregates are one inherently existent entity. If  they are one entity, they are just one. They 
cannot be one entity but different. If they are established as one entity, they must be one. 
Thus, if the aggregates and the person are one en tity, they must be one. Since it is not like 
this, there is harm to the asse rtion that the person and the aggregates are one inherently 
existent entity.   The fourth essential point is to see the harm to the position  that the two, the person and 
the aggregates (its basis of imputation), are inherently different. If the aggregates and the 
person were inherently different, they woul d not depend on each other and would be 
completely separate. However, it can be seen that  they are not like this in that the person and 
the aggregates are commonly seen to be differe nt. For example, when we  have a headache, we 
say “I have a headache.” This is  because we think of ourselves, the I, as the owner of our head. 
We could also say “My head hurts” but usually we  say “I have a headache.” Therefore, it is 
clear to us that the person depends on the a ggregates, we can see this directly. Therefore, 
there is harm to the assertio n that the person and aggregates are inherently different.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 When in that way the four essentials ar e complete, then the pure view realizing 
the suchness which is the selfle ssness of a person is produced. 
 
When these four essential points are complete, one will realize the selflessness of persons. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 312) says: 
(The second, an extensive explanation of the third and fourth essentials has two parts, the fi rst of which is: a refutation 
that the two—the self and the aggregates—are inherently one. It has ten parts, the first of which is:) 
Concerning that, if the two—the self and the aggregates—are one inherently 
established entity, from among three fallacie s the first damage to this position is 
that the assertion of self would be sensele ss. If the sameness of entity of those two 
were inherently established, then they would become an utterly indivisible one. 
This is because if a sameness of entity were  ultimately established, then to whatever 
awareness those two appeared they would necessarily not appear to be different. The reason for this is that although am ong falsities—conventionalities—it is not 
contradictory for the mode of appearance and mode of being to be in disagreement, such is necessarily contradictor y in what is truly established. For, the 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
841 mode of being of what truly exists must appe ar just as it is to any mind to which it 
appears. 
 
If the person and the aggregates are one inherent ly existent entity, among the three faults the 
first harm to this position is that it would be meaningless to assert a self. If the person and the 
aggregates were one inherently existent entity , they could not be distinguished but would be 
an utterly indivisible one. If they are one inhere ntly existent entity, they could not appear to 
an awareness as different. In other words, if they are one inherently existent entity, they 
would be just one and so could not appear to an y awareness as two. Although among falsities, 
that is, conventionalities, it is  not contradictory for their mode  of appearance and their mode 
of abidance to be different, these are necessarily  contradictory for what is truly existent. For 
example, there are people who externally appear to  be very kind while internally their mind is 
quite malicious and harmful. Thus, such contra dictions between the mode of appearance and 
the mode of abidance can exist conventionally, wh ereas if things were inherently existent this 
could not happen. In other words, if something is truly existent its mode of appearance and 
its mode of abidance must be the same, they canno t be contradictory. In short, if it is truly 
existent, when it appears to the mind, the way in which it appears and the way in which it 
exists must be exactly the same.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The assertion of an inherently established self is for the sake of establishing an 
assumer and discarder of the aggregates; howe ver, if it is one with the aggregates, 
those are not feasible. About that, Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  says: 
When it is taken that there is no self 
Separate from the appropriated [aggregates], 
Then if just the appropriated [aggregates] are the self, Your self does not exist. 
 Someone says that an inherently existent self is asserted for the purpose of establishing one 
who discards and one who appropriates the aggr egates, that is, one who dies and one who is 
reborn. However, if the person and the aggreg ates are one, such an assumer and discarder 
would not be feasible. Regarding that, Nagarjuna’s Fundamental Wisdom  or Treatise on the 
Middle  says that a self that is separate from the appropriator of the aggregates does not exist, 
because that which takes rebirth is the self or the person. Since the appropriator of the 
aggregates is the self, an inherently existent self does not exist. While it is said that the self or 
person takes rebirth, it is not said that the aggr egates take rebirth, for example, it is not said 
that the hand takes rebirth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 The second [damage to the position that the self and the aggregates are one] is 
that the self would be many. If the self we re one with the aggregates, then just as 
one person has many aggregates, so even  one person would have many selves.  
 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
842 If the self and the aggregates were one it would also be that case that, because the aggregates 
are many, the self would also be many. In short, if  each of the five aggregates exists inherently, 
each of them would be one enti ty with the self, whereby the se lf and the form aggregate would 
be one entity; the self and the feeling aggr egate would be one entity; the self and the 
discrimination aggregate would be one entity, th e self and the compositional factors aggregate 
would be one entity, and the self and the cons ciousness aggregate would be one entity. This 
would have the consequence that one single person  or being would have to be five persons or 
beings. In that case when putting on a pair of pants, one would have to put on five pants! 
Also when one goes to sleep, one would need a ve ry big bed since it would have to hold five 
persons!   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
[Or] just as there is no more than one self , so the aggregates would have to be one. 
Those are the fallacies. Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the 
Middle”  says: 
If the aggregates were the self, then since 
They are many, the selves also would be many. 
 On the other hand, if the self is only one, then  the aggregates would also have to be one. But 
do the five aggregates become on e aggregate? They do not. A single being has five aggregates. 
However, if the person and the aggregates ar e one inherently existent entity, then the 
aggregates would also only be one, that is, the five aggregates would have to be a single one. 
Chandrakirti says that if the aggregates were th e self, that is, if the aggregates and self were 
one inherently existent entity, because the aggreg ates are many it would follow that the selves 
would also have to be many. This is the conseque nce of asserting the self and the aggregates to 
be one inherently existent entity. If, on the othe r hand, the self and aggregates are asserted to 
be one entity conventionally , there is not this absurd consequence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 The third [damage to the position of oneness] is that the self would have 
production and disintegration. Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  says: 
If the aggregates were the self, 
It would have production and disintegration. 
Just as the aggregates are produced and disintegrate, the self also would be produced and disintegrate because those two are one. [This is now explained at length in points four through ten.] 
 If the self and the aggregates are asserted to be  one inherently existent entity, it would also 
follow that just as the aggregates are produc ed and disintegrate, so too would the self be 
produced and disintegrate. Nagarjuna also says that if the aggregates were one inherently 
existent entity with the self, the self would have production and disintegration. This would 
mean, for example, that just as the aggregates grow bigger and stronger as a baby’s body grows 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
843 into a teenager and so forth, so  too would the self have to grow . Also just as the aggregates 
disintegrate and become unhealthy and old, so too would the self have to become sick and 
old. Similarly, if the aggregates and the self were one inherently existent entity, when the 
person dies and the form aggregate is buried in a cemetery or cremated in a fire so too would 
the self be buried in a cemetery or cremated in a fire.  
 These are the faults associat ed with the self and the aggregates being one inherently 
existent entity.  Wednesday afternoon, 7 February 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 314) says: 
(Fourth, one is forced to assert that the self is pr oduced and disintegrates inherently , not just conventionally:) 
Objection:  It is accepted that the self or person is produced and disintegrates each 
moment. 
Answer:  There is no fault in asserting such conventionally, but the opponent asserts 
that persons are established by way of thei r own nature and thus has to assert that 
persons are produced and disintegrate inherently. 
 
S o m e o n e  s a y s  t h a t  i t  i s  a s s e r t e d  t h a t  t h e  s e l f  o r  p e r s o n  i s  p r o d u c e d  a n d  d i s i n t e g r a t e s  e a c h  
moment. Although there is no fault in assertin g that conventionally the self or person is 
produced and disintegrates each moment, howe ver this opponent asse rts that persons are 
established by way of their own characteristics and therefore asserts that inherently existent 
persons are produced and disintegrate inherently. This is not acceptable because if the person 
exists inherently and truly, it cannot be produced and cannot disintegrate.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, thus it follows that memory of former lives would impossible:) 
That has three fallacies as set out in Chandrakirti’s Commentary to the “Supplement 
to (Nagarjuna’s) ‘Treatise on the Middle’” . With respect to th e first fallacy, his 
Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
Those which are separate by way of their own nature 
Are not feasible to be included in one continuum. 
 I f  i t  w e r e  a s  s t a t e d  a b o v e ,  i t  w o u l d  f o l l o w  t h a t  t h e  m e m o r y  o f  f o r m e r  l i v e s  w o u l d  b e  impossible, but this too is incorrect and cannot be accepted. There are three faults, the first 
one being that if the person exis ts inherently or by way of its own characteristics then the past 
and present person would not be suitable to be  included in one continuum. In other words, a 
past person who exists inherently cannot beco me the present person who exists inherently. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, it is not feasible for former and later individuals which are established by 
way of their own entityness to have the re lationship of the latter depending on the 
former. Since the former and later are establ ished in the mode of being able to set 
themselves up under their own power, it is not suitable for them to depend on 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
844 others. 
 
Former and later individuals who are establishe d by way of their own entity cannot have a 
relationship of the later depending on the fo rmer. Since the former and later continuum are 
established as able to set themselves up under th eir own power, that is, are established to exist 
inherently and truly, they cannot depend on other.   For example, the Tashi of yesterday and the Tashi of today would be completely different 
because Tashi exists inherently. Also if a smal l child existed inherently it could not grow 
bigger. There are many such absurd consequences  that follow from the assertion of inherent 
existence.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 When in that way a sameness of continuum  is not feasible, memory of a former 
life—“At such and such a time, in that life , I was such and such”— is not possible. It 
is like the fact that when Devadatta reme mbers a [former] life, he does not think, 
“I was Yajñadatta,” who [is his contem porary and] has a separate continuum. 
 
If being one continuum is not feasible, the memory  of a former life thinking “I was such-and-
such a man or woman” is not possible. It like the fact that Jonathan does not remember that 
in a previous life he was George, who is a separate continuum.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, the correctness of our own system:) 
In our own system, although there is [con ventionally existent] disintegration in 
each moment, it is not contradictory for the former and later to be one continuum. 
Due to this, memory of lives is feasible. 
 
In our own system, there is disintegration in each moment but it is not contradictory for 
former and later to be one continuum. Therefore,  it is possible to remember previous lives. 
Buddha, for example, was able to remember and say “In the past I was such-and-such a king.” 
However, if the person were inherently existent  this would not be possible in that the former 
person and later person would be completely independent and therefore could not remember 
past lives.    
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Seventh, how it is an extreme view to hold that a Buddha and a sentient being are one:)  
Those who do not understand the meaning of the above [think] that because in 
the sets of discourses29 Buddha frequently says, “In the past I was such and such [a 
person],” the two persons—the one after en lightenment and the one at an earlier 
time—are one. Furthermore, [they think that ] it would not be suitable for them to 
be one if they were caused phenomena, since they would disintegrate moment by 
moment, and therefore they say that both are permanent. In this way the first of 
                                              
29 sutranta.  
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
845 the four bad views based on a former extreme is generated in them. So that we 
might not fall to such [a view], when Buddha remembered a life, [he spoke of] 
remembering in general, saying “I” without qualifying it with a place, time, or specific nature. You need to understand well this feature of his mode of remembering. 
 It is an extreme view to assert that a buddh a and a sentient being are one. In the sutras 
Buddha often said “In the past I was such and such…” due to which those who do not 
understand the meaning of the above think th at the person before enlightenment and the 
person after enlightenment are one. However, this  is not correct. These pe ople also think that 
if they are compounded phenomena it would not be suitable for them to be one since they 
disintegrate in each moment, and therefore say that both are permanent. In this way they 
generate the first of the four bad views based on an earlier end which is related to the self and 
the world. These four are included among the four teen unspecified views that are the result of 
the Buddha not replying to particular question s posed to him. Wrong view is one of the ten 
non-virtuous actions. In order to not fall in to this view, when the Buddha remembered a 
previous rebirth he said “I” in general without qualifying it with a sp ecific place, time, or 
nature. In other words, he did not say “I lived at  this time in this pl ace as this particular 
being.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Eighth, the consequence that actions done would be wasted:) 
The second fallacy [of an inherently produced and disintegrating person] is that 
actions done would be wasted. The tw o—the agent of the action and the 
experiencer of the effect—could not be incl uded within the one base of the mere I. 
 
There would also be the consequence or second fault that actions done would be wasted. This 
is because the creator of the action and the expe riencer of the result would not be included in 
one base, the mere I. It is usually said that fi rst there is the creator of the action and later 
there is the experiencer of the result, but this  would not be possible if the person were 
inherently existent, due to which acti ons done would not be experienced. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Ninth, the consequence of meeting with [t he effects of] actions not done [by oneself]:) 
The fallacy of meeting with actions not do ne [by oneself] is the greatly absurd 
consequence that all the effects of acti ons accumulated by other [persons] of 
different continuums would have to  be experienced by a person. 
 
There would also be the consequence or fault of experiencing the results of actions not 
created by oneself. In other words, one woul d experience the results of actions done by 
another beings of different continua. Thus even  though one particular person created many 
actions, their results would be experienced by other persons. However, this is very absurd. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Tenth, the unsuitability of the former and later being one continuum if they are inherently established:) 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
846 These two fallacies come by way of the e ssential—as in the earlier explanation of 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle” —that if a person 
is established inherently, the former and later moments are not suitable to be of 
the same continuum. Furthermore, Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  says: 
If the god and human were other, 
A continuum would not be feasible. 
 If the former and later are inherently establis hed they could not be one continuum. In other 
words, if the person exists inherently, former  and later moments of the person could not be 
of the same continuum because they would not depend on each other at all but would be 
completely unrelated. For example, if the past  god and the present human were separate, they 
could not be one continuum.    
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The second, the fourth essential, which is the refutation of  inherently different self and aggregates, has five parts, the 
first of which is: an i ndication of a source:) 
Question : What fallacy is there if the self and the aggregates are asserted as 
inherently established other? 
Answer:  There is the fallacy described in Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle : 
If it is other than the aggregates, 
It would not have the character of the aggregates. 
 What is the fault of asserting that the self an d aggregates are inherently different? Nagarjuna’s 
Fundamental Wisdom  says that if the self is other than the aggregates, it would not have the 
characteristics of the aggregates  because the self would be inherently established as different 
from the aggregates. Thus, if the self were inhe rently different from the aggregates, it would 
not possess the characteristics of the aggregates such as having parts and so forth. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the consequence that if those two are inherently different, the self would not have the character of the 
aggregates:) 
Concerning that, if the self were inherent ly different from the aggregates, it would 
not possess the characteristics that ch aracterize the aggregates as caused 
phenomena—production, abiding, and di sintegration. For example, because a 
horse is established as a thing factually o t h e r  t h a n  a n  o x ,  i t  d o e s  n o t  h a v e  t h e  
characteristics of an ox. 
 
If the self and aggregates were different by w ay of their entity the se lf would not possess the 
characteristics of the aggregates. Whil e compounded phenomena have the three 
characteristics of being produced, abiding, and disintegrating, if the self were inherently 
different from the aggregates, the self would no t possess the characteristics that characterize 
the aggregates as being compounded phenomena. For example, since a horse is established as 
being completely different from an ox, it does no t have the characteristics of an ox. Also an ox 
does not have the characteristics of a horse. Similarly, if the self and the aggregates are 
inherently different, there would be the cons equence that the self does not have the 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
847 characteristics that characterize the aggregates  as being compounded phenomena, these being 
production, abidance, and disintegration. Howe ver, the self is a functioning thing or a 
compounded phenomenon that arises from caus es and conditions. A baby, for example, is 
conceived, born, and grows in dependence on  causes and conditions, it does not arise 
independently. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, an other-approved syllogism [showing] by the sign of being an uncaused phenomenon that [this so-called self] is 
not suitable as the basis of the designati on self or to be conceived as a self:) 
If you think, “That indeed is so but” [that is, if you are not entirely convinced], 
then it [this so-called self] is not feasible as the object which is the base of 
designating the verbal convention “self”  by innate conception because it is 
uncaused, like a flower in the sky or nirvana. 
 
If it were like this, the self would not suitable to be the observed object  that is the basis of 
conventionally imputing a self by the innate  conception because it is an uncompounded 
phenomena, like a sky-flower or nirvana. Be cause nirvana is a true cessation, it is an 
uncompounded phenomenon that is not prod uced by causes and conditions, whereas 
because a sky-flower does not exist, it is not produced.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, from the consequence that the self would be appr ehended as other than the character of the aggregates [we 
can understand] that it is not apprehended in this way:) 
Moreover, if [the self] exists as inherently  different from the ch aracteristics of the 
aggregates and so forth—such as being s uitable [to be called] form—it must be 
apprehended in that way, just as, for example, form and mind are apprehended as 
different. Further, since there is no such apprehension, the self does not exist as a 
thing factually other [than the aggregates]. Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  says: 
It is not feasible that the self 
Be other than the appropriated [aggregates]. 
If it were other, then it would be apprehendable 
Without the aggregates, whereas it is not. 
 If the self were inherently different from the ch aracteristics of the aggregates (such as “suitable 
to be form” which is the definition or characte ristic of the form aggregate), it should be 
observed to be different from them, for exampl e, like form and mind are observed to be 
completely different. Form and mind are obse rved to be different because mind has the 
nature of clarity and knowing whereas form is established from the eight particles. Since the 
self and the aggregates are not observed to exis t as different, like the mind and form, the self 
does not exist as a different entity from the aggregates. Nagarjuna’s Fundamental Wisdom  also 
says that the self and the appropriated aggregates are not feasible as other,  that is, as different 
entities. If the self and aggregates were inhere ntly other, the self could not appropriate the 
aggregates, that is, it could not take rebirth.  This, however, is not apprehended by a valid 
cognizer.   
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
848 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
Thus, there is no self ot her than the aggregates 
Because apprehension of it aside from  the aggregates is not established. 
 
There is no self that is other than the aggregat es because there is no self apprehended separate 
from the aggregates. Thus, the self necessarily depends on the aggregates. In other words, the 
self appearing to the mind depends upon the aggregates appearing to the mind. Without the 
aggregates appearing, the self does not appear to the mind.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, the importance of gaining ascertainm ent with respect to such reasonings:) 
You should train until gaining firm ascert ainment, seeing through such reasonings 
the damage to [the position that] the self  exists as a different entity from the 
aggregates. For, if you do not induce pu re ascertainment of the damage to these 
two positions of sameness and difference, although you might de cide that persons 
do not inherently exist, it would be just a thesis; the pure view  would not be found. 
 
It is important to gain certainty with respect to such reasonings. Having seen, through such 
reasonings, the harm to the position that the self exists as a different entity from the 
aggregates, one should train until one gains firm  certainty or understanding that the self and 
aggregates are not different entities but are on e entity. If one does not gain certainty with 
respect to the harm done to th e two positions of the self and aggregates being inherently one 
or being inherently different, even though on e might think that persons do not inherently 
existent it would be just a thesis. Although the self and aggregates are conventionally one 
entity, they are not inherently one entity. If ultimately they were one entity, the self and 
aggregates would become one single phenomenon. Bu t this is not so. Theref ore, it is necessary 
to analyze this based on good reasonings.  
 Some people say that the creator of the world is God or a primordial principle but without a clear understanding of how such a creator woul d be if it existed. When asked where such a 
creator exists, they might just answer “In the sk y.” These kind of assertions show that they 
have not analyzed how God or a primordial princi ple, if it existed, would have to exist.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Settling mine as not inherently existent 
(This has two parts, the first of which is: that the reasoning refuting an inherently existent self also refutes inherently 
existent mine, together with sources:) 
When whether the self has or does not ha ve inherent establishment is sought in 
this way through reasoning, it is not foun d to be either one or many; thereby, the 
inherent existence of the self is negated. At that time, inherently established mine 
will not be found by the reasoning analyz ing suchness, just as, for example, when 
the son of a barren woman is not observed, the mine  of the son of a barren 
woman—eyes and so forth—are not observed.  
 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
849 The reasoning that refutes an inherently existent self also refutes an inherently existent mine. 
Having analyzed whether or not th e self is inherently existent, it  is not found to be either one 
or many, whereby an inherently existent self is  refuted. At that time, also an inherently 
existent mine is not found by the reasoning anal yzing suchness. It is like, for example, when 
the son of a barren woman is not observed (giv en that a barren woman does not have a son!), 
also the mine of the son of a barren woman, hi s eyes, ears, nose, tongue, legs, and so forth, 
are also not observed.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  says: 
If the self does not [inherently] exist, 
How could the mine [inherently] exist? 
 Nagarjuna says that if the self does not exist, the mine does no t exist. Without the I existing, 
the mine cannot exist.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
Because the object of a non-ex istent agent does not exist, 
Without [an inherently existent] self, [inh erently existent] mine does not exist. 
Thus, through the view of th e emptiness of I and mine, 
A yogi will be released. 
 
Also Chandrakirti says that just as without an agent, actions do not exist, so too without an 
inherently existent self, an inherently existent  mine does not exist. Through the view of the 
emptiness of I and mine, a yogi is liberated fr om cyclic existence and achieves nirvana. In 
other words, by realizing the lack of inherent existence of I and mine, one achieves liberation 
by abandoning the conception of a self – ignora nce or the view of the transitory collection.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 317) says: 
(Second, the need to know how those r easonings establish all other persons and t heir mine as not inherently existent:) 
Those reasonings settle that the I of the conception thinking “I” in your own 
continuum—or self, or person—is not esta blished by way of its own entityness. 
Through them you should also realize all the meaning of the suchness of the 
selflessness of persons— that all persons, ranging from hell-beings through Buddhas, 
and all their mine, are not inherently established as the same entity with or 
different entities from the contaminated  and non-contaminated aggregates which 
are the bases of [their] designation. Yo u should also know how, through that 
reasoning, all their mine30 is established as without inherent existence.
