Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 317) says: 
4 Illusion 
The presentation of how, in dependence on [settling I and mine as not inherently 
existent,] persons appear as like illusi ons has two parts: the meaning of setting 
forth illusory-like appearance and the meth od in dependence on which illusory-like 
appearance occurs. 
The meaning of setting forth illusory-like appearance 
This section has two parts: the unerring mode of illusory-like appearance and a 
fallacious mode of illusory-like appearance. 
 Having settled I and mine as no t inherently existent, persons appear as being like illusions. 
Thus, the meaning of illusory-like is now expl ained and then the method in dependence on 
on which phenomena appear as like illusi ons. Then the unmistaken way in which 
phenomena appear as like illusions  and mist aken illusory-like appearances are set out. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
THE UNERRING MODE OF ILLUSORY -LIKE APPEARANCE  
(This section has nine parts, the first of  which is: that all phenomena, like illusi ons, ultimately have no signs [such as 
color and so forth]:) 
The King of Meditative Stabilizations Sutra  says; 
Like a mirage, city of gandharvas, 
A magician’s illusions, and dreams, 
Meditation on signs is em pty of inherent existence 
Know all phenomena as like that. 
A Mother of the Conquerors [ Perfection of Wisdom Sutra ] says that all phenomena 
from forms through exalted knowers of all aspects are like a magician’s illusions 
and dreams. 
 
All phenomena are ultimately without signs like illusions. This sutra says that one should 
meditate on all phenomena as being empty of in herent existence and like a mirage, a city of 
gandharvas, an illusion, and a dream. The Mo ther of the Conquerors, that is, the 80,000, 
20,000, and 100,000 Stanza Perf ection of Wisdom Sutras, say that all 108 phenomena, from 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
851 forms through an exalted knower of all aspects, are like illusions and dreams. Although 
dreams appear to be real at the time of dr eaming, when one wakes up nothing solid remains 
of them. Likewise, the illusions created by a magi cian appear to be real but are not. Yesterday 
I saw a program on television in which a man wa s put in a box and cut in two, but still he 
continued to talk and his legs continued to move.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, between the [two] of being illusory-like this one is appearance:) 
There are two meanings of the teaching of being like an illusion. In the description 
of an ultimate truth as being like an illu sion, it means that although [an ultimate 
truth or emptiness] is established as merely  existent, [its] true existence is negated.31 
The second is an illusory-like appearance in which [an object] appears while being 
empty. Between these two, here [being li ke an illusion refers to] the latter. 
 There are two meanings of illusory -like. When the ultimate truth is  said to be like an illusion, 
it means that it does not exist inherently. In ot her words, the fact that ultimate truths exist 
but do not exist truly is what is negated when saying that ultimate truths are like illusions. The second case of illusory-like is that of an illusory-like appearance which appears while 
being empty, that is, which in reality does not exist. Here illusory-like refers to the latter. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, the need for a composite of two [factors], a convent ional awareness of appearance—it not being that there is no 
appearance at all—and a reasoning consciousness to which [t he object] appears to be empty of existing in the way 
it appears:) 
Concerning this, the two—appearance as th at and emptiness of existing the way it 
appears—are needed for a person trying to comprehend this view.  
 
It is not that there are no appearances at all.  In fact there are conventional awarenesses to 
which object appear, for example, there is an eye consciousness to which forms appear, an ear 
consciousness to which sounds appear, and so  forth. In addition, there is a reasoning 
consciousness, one that knows emptiness or thusness, to which all phenomena appear as 
empty of existing as they appear. Whereas things  appear to a conventional awareness, such as 
eye consciousness and so forth th ings, it is a reasoning consciou sness that realizes their final 
mode of abidance, emptiness.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Also, if [objects seem] to be utterly non- existent even as mere appearances like a 
horn of a rabbit or the son of a barren woman, and do not dawn as empty of 
existing the way they appear, the meanin g of illusory-like appearance has not 
appeared to the mind. 
 
                                             
 
31 Perhaps, just as an illusory horse ex ists but is not established as horse, so an ultimate truth exists but is not 
inherently established. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
852 There is the appearance of true or inherent existence to our mind, although phenomena do 
not exist in this way. In other words, they appear to be truly existent but in reality they are 
empty of inherent existence. Thus, if phenomena seem to be utterly non- existent even as mere 
appearances, like the horns of a rabbit and th e son of a barren woman, and if they do not 
dawn as empty of existing in th e way that they appear (that is, as empty of true existence), the 
meaning of illusory-like appearance has not yet dawned to the mind, that is, it has not yet 
been understood. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, the mode of knowing other phenomena as like the example of an illusion:) 
Therefore, the mode of knowing other phenomena as like the example of an 
illusion is as follows. For example, alth ough the illusions emanated [or conjured 
up] by a magician are from the start empty of being horses and elephants, 
appearances of horses and elephants are un deniably cast. Just so, you should know 
that although phenomena, persons and so  forth, are from the start empty of 
inherent existence—that is, of their bein g objectively established by way of their 
own entities—it is undeniable that they appear as if established that way. 
 
The way in which other phenomena are also known to be like the example of an illusion is 
that even though illusions, su ch as horses, elephants, beauti ful woman, and so forth, are 
created on the basis of a pebble or stick by applyi ng medicine to the eyes of the spectators and 
reciting mantras, they appear to be real ac tual horses, elephants, and so forth but never 
existed in that way right from the beginning. In  other words, while all that exists is the 
pebbles or stones, still horses and elephants undeni ably appear, that is, th ey cannot be said to 
not appear. Similarly, while all phenomena have been empty of inherent existence right from 
the very beginning, still they undeniably appear  as if they were established from their own 
side, solidly and inherently. In other words, it cannot be said that they do not appear as if 
they exist from their own side.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, such an emptiness is not an annihilatory emptiness or a mentally fabricated, or partial emptiness, and so forth:) 
The appearances as a god, human, and so  forth are posited as persons and the 
objects which are the appearances as form s, sounds, and so forth are posited as 
phenomena. Therefore, although person s and phenomena do not have even a 
mere particle of inherent existence—an establishment by way of their own nature—
all the dependently arisen actions, agen ts, and objects—accumulator of actions32 
and so forth, viewing, object of hearin g, and so forth—are feasible. Because all 
actions, agents, and objects are feasible, th is is not an annihilatory emptiness.  
 
The emptiness of persons and phenomena, wh ich range from forms through the exalted 
knower of all aspects, of existing by way of their own characteristics is not an annihilatory 
emptiness or a mentally fabricated emptiness or a partial emptiness.  
                                              
32 las, karma.  
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
853  The appearances as gods, humans, animals an d so forth are considered to be persons. 
Although not all these beings, such as gods an d hell beings, appear to us human beings, these 
beings appear to each other (that is, hungry ghos ts appear to hungry ghosts, hell beings to hell 
beings, and so forth). The appearances as forms,  sounds, odors, tastes, tactile objects, and 
other phenomena such as nirvana, uncompou nded phenomena, space, and so forth are 
considered to be phenomena. Although pers ons and phenomena do not have even a mere 
particle of inherent existence or existenc e by way of their own characteristics, the 
accumulators of actions such as making paper, books, cotton cloth, and so forth, the actions 
of looking, listening, and so forth, and the ob jects of looking (forms), the objects of hearing 
(sounds), and so forth are still feasible, that is , they still exist. Because actions, agents, and 
objects are perfectly feasible, this is not an annihilatory emptiness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Also, because phenomena, which from the primordial start have been empty in 
this way, are just being known as empty, th is is not a mentally fabricated emptiness. 
Furthermore, since all objects of knowledge are asserted  this way, it is not a partial 
emptiness. Therefore, meditati on on it goes as the anti dote to all the exaggerated 
adherences of the concepti on of true existence. 
 
Also because all phenomena have primordially b een empty of inherent existence, it is not a 
mentally fabricated emptiness. The emptiness that  is being discussed here is not the emptiness 
of a house of people, nor is it the emptiness of the cup of tea, or the emptiness of the stomach 
of food, and so forth, rather it is the emptiness of inherent existence. 
 In addition, because all objects of knowle dge are asserted to be empty of inherent 
existence, it is not a partial emptiness. Theref ore, meditation on the emptiness of inherent 
existence of persons and phenomena is the antido te to all the adherenc es of the conception 
of true existence.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 It is not that the meaning of the profou nd [emptiness] is not suitable to be the 
object of any awareness. Through settlin g it by means of the correct view and 
through meditating on the meaning of the re ality [that has been settled], it can be 
taken as an object [of the mind]. Therefor e, it is not that it cannot be practiced 
during the time of path, and it is also not a vacuousness in which there is nothing 
to be known and nothing to be realized.  
 
It is not that emptiness is not suitable to be the object of an awareness. Through settling the 
emptiness of inherent existence or true existenc e by means of the correct view and meditating 
on the perfect meaning, it can be taken as an object. Therefore, it is  not that it cannot be 
practiced at the time of the path and it is al so not a vacuousness in which there is nothing 
known and nothing realized. In other words, at th e time of the path it is possible to meditate 
on and realize emptiness and it is not an em ptiness like the emptiness that is the non-
existence of the horns of a rabbit.  
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
854  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, stating the qualm that ascertaining a reflection as empty of what it appears to be involves ascertaining that it is 
empty of inherent existence:) 
Objection:  If this ascertainment that reflection s and so forth are empty of what they 
appear to be is a realization of their abse nce of inherent existence, then the direct 
perceivers of common beings would realize the absence of inherent existence, then 
the direct perceivers of common beings would realize the absence of inherent 
existence, and due to that, they would be Superiors. If the ascertainment that 
reflections and so forth are empty of what th ey appear to be is not a realization of 
their absence of inherent existence, how co uld these be suitable as examples of the 
absence of inherent existence? 
 
Here the doubt is raised that ascertaining a refl ection in a mirror or in still water as empty of 
what it appears to be is an ascertainment of its emptiness of inherent existence. When one 
looks at one’s face in the mirror,  although the reflection appear s to be one’s actual face in 
reality it is empty of being one’s face. If this  ascertainment that reflections and so forth are 
empty of what they appear to be is a realizatio n of their absence of in herent existence, then 
the direct perceivers of ordinary beings woul d realize the emptiness of inherent existence 
(because they realize that the reflection of a fa ce is empty of being an actual face) and they 
would therefore be aryas. However, ordinary bein gs are not arya beings in that they have not 
attained the path of seeing, th e path of meditation, or the pa th of no-more-learning, and in 
most cases have not even attained the path of accumulation or path of preparation. Based on 
this, the doubt arises: “If the as certainment that reflections an d so forth are empty of what 
they appear to be is not a realization of th eir absence of inherent existence, how could 
reflections and so forth be suitable as exam ples of the lack of inherent existence?”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Seventh, though one has realized that a reflection is empty of being a face, si nce one has the conception of the true 
existence of the reflection this is not a realization of its suchness:) 
Answer : Aryadeva’s Four Hundred  says: 
That which is the viewer of one thing 
Is explained to be the viewer of all. 
That which is the emptiness of one Is the emptiness of all. 
 Although one has realized that the reflection of a face in a mirror is em pty of being an actual 
face, because one has the conception of true existe nce of this reflection, it is not a realization 
of suchness. Because the reflection in a mirror is a visual form, upon seeing it the innate 
conception of true existence arises. Therefore, th e understanding that it is not an actual face 
is not a realization of emptiness. In other words, just realizing that the reflection of a face is 
empty of being a face is not a realization of  the emptiness of inherent existence of the 
reflection of a face.  
 With respect to this, Aryadeva s ays that the viewer of one thin g is the viewer of all things. 
For example, if one realizes the emptiness of on e thing, such as a vase, of inherent or true 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
855 existence, one will also realize that pillars do not exist truly, that human beings do not exist 
inherently, and that all other phenomena do not exist inherently. If one sees the emptiness of 
one phenomena, one will also see the emptiness of all other phenomena. For example, if one 
sees the emptiness of inherent ex istence of this table, one will gradually come to see that this 
emptiness of inherent existence also applies to all other phenomena. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, he explains that the viewer, or re alizer, of the emptiness of one thing can 
realize the emptiness of other things. Howeve r, the realization that a reflection of a 
f a c e  i s  e m p t y  o f  b e i n g  a  f a c e  d o e s  n o t do any damage to the object of the 
conception33 of true existence which conceives th e reflection to be established by 
way of its own entity. Furthermore, without coming to disbelieve [that is to say, 
without realizing as non-existent] the obje ct of that conception, the emptiness of 
inherent establishment of the reflection cannot be realized. Hence, that awareness 
does not realize the suchness of the reflecti on. Though one realizes illusions to be 
empty of being horses and elephants and realizes dream appearances and so forth 
as empty of what they appear to be , one has not found the Madhyamika view 
realizing [objects] as like illusions and dreams. 
 The viewer of the emptiness of one thing can al so realize the emptiness of all other things. 
However, the realization that the reflection of  a face is empty of being a face is not a 
realization of true existence and does not harm the object of the concep tion of true existence 
which apprehends the reflection to exist truly. This is because without repudiating the object 
of that conception, one cannot realize the emptin ess of inherent existe nce. Therefore, that 
awareness does not realize the emptiness of true existence of the reflection. Likewise, although 
one realizes illusory horses and elephants to be  empty of being horses and elephants, one has 
not yet found the Madhyamaka view realizing that all phenomena are like illusions and 
dreams in the sense of their not existing inherently.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Eighth, the reason, nevertheless, for stating reflections and illusory horses and el ephants, and so forth, as examples:) 
However, these are used as examples due to  the fact that the realization that these 
do not inherently exist is easier than th e realization that other phenomena such as 
f o r m s ,  s o u n d s ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h  d o  n o t .  I f  a n  o b j e c t  i s  t r u l y  e s t a b l i s h e d ,  i t  i s  n o t  
suitable for it to appear to a mind in any way other than just what its mode of 
subsistence is. Therefore, that these [refle ctions and magical illusions] are empty of 
inherent existence in the sense of being es tablished by way of their own entities is 
proven through showing the contradiction in  those two [i.e., a reflection appearing 
to be a face or an illusory horse’s appear ing to be a horse constitutes its appearing 
in a way other than its mode  of subsistence and this contradicts its being truly 
established]. 
 
                                              
33 “conception”: consciousness conceiving. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
856 The reason that reflections and illusory horses an d elephants are used as examples is that it is 
easier to realize that they do not exist inherent ly than it is to realize that other phenomena 
such as forms and so forth do not exist inhe rently. If objects were truly or inherently 
established, it would not be feasible for them to appear in any other way than their actual 
mode of abidance. However, although form, for example, appears to exist truly it does not 
exist truly, due to which its mode of appearance  and its mode of abidance are contradictory. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Initially, one must enter into realizing that these examples, which are renowned 
as false in the world, are without inhe rent existence, and then generate the 
realization that other phenomena which are not renowned as false in the world are 
without inherent existence. Because thes e have a temporal order, it is not 
[Aryadeva’s] thought [in the passage cite d on the previous page] that when the 
emptiness of one phenomenon is realized , the emptinesses of all other phenomena 
are realized, the emptinesses of all other phenomena are explicitly realized. Rather, 
his meaning is that when [after realizin g the emptiness of on e phenomenon] one’s 
awareness is directed toward whether anot her phenomenon truly exists or not, it 
can realize [its emptiness through the functioning of the former reasoning]. 
 Reflections and so forth are renowned in the wo rld to as untrue or false. One must first 
realize that they lack inherent  existence, after which one shou ld realize that those phenomena 
that are not renowned as false in the world ar e also without inherent  existence. These two 
realizations do not arise simultaneously but must  be generated one after the other. Therefore, 
it not the intention of the prev ious citation that when the em ptiness of one phenomenon is 
realized, all other phenomena are simultaneously realized to be empty of inherent existence. 
Rather it means that having realized one phen omena to be empty of in herent existence, one 
can gradually come to realize that all other ph enomena are also empty of true existence. For 
example, it is like having invented one thing, one can also invent other similar things just by 
changing it in some small way.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Ninth, the need to distinguish between the realization of appearances, such as reflections , as empty of what they 
appear to be and the realization of appearances  as empty of inherent existence:) 
Thus, (1) within knowing a dream as a drea m, to realize the appearances as men, 
women, and so forth [in the dream] as em pty of being those and (2) to view in 
dreams all phenomena as like dreams as described in Maitreya’s Ornament for Clear 
Realization34—”Viewing all phenomena even in dreams as like dreams, and so 
forth”—do not have the same meaning [for the latter involves realization of their 
emptiness whereas the former does not]. 
 
It is necessary to distinguish between (1) the re alization of appearances as being empty of what 
they appear to be and (2) the realization of ap pearances as empty of inherent existence. Thus, 
                                              
34 abhisamayalamkara . 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
857 to realize in a dream that the appearances in a dream of men, women, and so forth do not 
exist and to view all phenomena in dreams as li ke dreams, illusions, mirages, reflections, and 
so forth are not the same.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Similarly, to realize the pots, woolen cloth, and so forth which appear in the 
visions of one who has cultivated meditative stabilization as empty of being what 
they appear to be and to realize them as like non-inherently existent illusions and 
dreams do not have the same meaning. Thus, you should investigate well the 
uncommon mode of illusory-like appearance in the prescriptions in scriptures and 
treatises of definitive meaning to realize [phenomena] as like illusions and dreams. 
 
Likewise, to realize the visions of vases, woolen  cloth, and so forth th at appear to those who 
have cultivated meditative stabilization as empty of being what they appear to be, that is, as 
appearing in one way but not existi ng in that way, is different fr om realizing them to not exist 
inherently. Therefore, one should determin e the uncommon mode of appearing like an 
illusion according to the definitive meaning scriptures and treatises.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 (1) Whether a mirror-image is conceived to be a face, and so forth, by a child not 
versed in terminology, (2) whether magica l appearances are conceived to be horses, 
elephants, and so forth by a member of the audience unfamiliar with magic, (3) 
whether dream appearances of mountains, houses, and so forth are conceived as 
real by one who does not recognize dreams as such, or whether those appearances 
are known as untrue by (1) mature pe rsons trained in terminology, (2) by 
magicians, (3) and by those who know dreams  as dreams, it is similar; both have 
not found the view of suchness. 
 A child not versed in terminology conceives the refl ection of a face in a mirror to be an actual 
face. Members of an audience unfamiliar with  magic conceive the magical appearances of 
horses and elephants to be actual horses and elephants. Those who do  not recognize dreams 
and so forth as dreams conceive the dream appe arances of mountains, houses, and so forth to 
be actual mountains, houses, and so forth. On the other hand, mature persons trained in 
terminology, magicians who create illusions, and those who know dreams as dreams know 
that these appearances are untrue. However, th ese two sets of persons are similar in not 
having found the view of suchness, that is, they have not found the correct view. 
 Thursday afternoon, 8 February 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 321) says: 
FALLACIOUS MODE OF ILLUSORY -LIKE APPEARANCE  
(This has eight parts, the first of whic h is: that unidentified chaotic appearance, in which there is no [discrimination], “It is 
this, not that,” is a view of annihilation and not the m eaning of illusion since the measure of the object of negation 
has been mistaken and the object analyzed and the analyzer [are considered to be] non-existent:) 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
858 When the measure of the object of negati on explained before is not grasped well 
and an object is analyzed with reasoning,  breaking it down, initially the thought 
arises, “The object does not exist.” Then , seeing the same wi th respect to the 
analyzer also, there is no ascertainer of non-existence, whereby it comes that there 
is nothing to ascertain as, “It is this, no t that.” The dawning th en of unidentified 
chaotic appearance arises in dependence on not differentiating inherent  existence 
and non-existence from mere existence and non-existe nce. Hence, such an 
emptiness is an emptiness destroying depe ndent-arising. Therefore, the dawning of 
unidentified chaotic appearance induced by realizing this is not at all the meaning 
of being like an illusion. 
 Since someone who does not unders tand the correct measure of th e object of negation thinks 
that the object of analysis and the analyzer do no t exist, he does not know “It is this. It is not 
that.” This chaotic or vague appearance is no t the meaning of illusion but is a view of 
annihilation. When the measure of the object of  negation explained before is not identified 
well and one analyzes an object with reasoning,  breaking it into parts, initially the thought 
arises “This object does not exis t.” Then, seeing oneself, the anal yzer, to be similar, one thinks 
that there is no ascertainer of non-existence. Th en one comes to think th at there is nothing to 
be ascertained as “It is this. It is not that. It is hot. It is cold” and so forth. The dawning of 
appearances that have become unidentified chao tic appearances arises in dependence on not 
distinguishing between (1) inherent existenc e and inherent non-existence and (2) mere 
existence and mere non-existence.  Hence, this is not actual emptiness and is an emptiness 
that destroys dependent-arisings, that is, it makes dependent-arisings non-existent. Thus, the 
dawning of unidentified chaotic a ppearance which is induced by re alizing this is not at all the 
meaning of illusory-like. “Illusory-like” does not mean not existing truly or inherently or from 
its own side, but means that things do not exist in the way that they a ppear. Nor also it refer 
to the illusions that arise due to a collection of causes and conditions. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, although there are many cases of generating such  unidentified chaotic appearance when analyzing the view, it 
is not the Madhyamika [understanding of] illusion:) 
Therefore, it is not difficult when analyz ing with reasoning to think, “Persons and 
so forth do not in the least have an obje ctive mode of subsis tence which is their 
being established by way of their own enti ties,” and in dependence on that for 
these appearances to shine forth unasso ciatedly. Such happens to all who are 
interested in Madhyamika tenets and have heard a few scattered doctrines showing 
the mode of the absence of inherent existence. 
 
Although when analyzing the vi ew there are many cases of generating such unidentified 
chaotic appearance that does not correctly identi fy inherent existence and the lack of inherent 
existence, it is not th e Madhyamaka meaning of illusory-lik e. Therefore, when analyzing with 
reasoning it is not difficult to come to thin k “Persons and so forth are not in the least 
established as objects from their own side or by  way of their own entities” and, in dependence 
on that, for those appearances to merely dawn va guely or without clarity. This happens to all 
those who are interested in the Madhyamaka tenets and have heard some occasional 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
859 teachings on the emptiness that is the lack of inherent existence. However, there is not much 
benefit to this in that such people have not correctly identified the object of negation and 
therefore have not correctly identified the emptiness of that object of negation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, the difficult part about the correct illusory appearanc e is the composite of the two, an absence of inherent 
existence and the existence of  merely nominal objects:) 
However, the difficult point is that  one must, from the depths, induce 
ascertainment with respect to refuting  without residue inherent existence—
establishment by way of its [the object’s] own entity—and positing just non-
inherently existent persons and so forth as the accumulators of actions, 
experiencers of effects, and so forth. A co mposite of these two in which there is an 
ability to posit these hardly occurs; hence,  the Madhyamaka view is very difficult to 
find.  
 To understand correct illusory appearance is difficult because a comb ination of the two are 
necessary: the absence of inherent existence and the existence of merely nominal objects. The 
difficult point is that one must induce asce rtainment with respect to refuting inherent 
existence without anything remaining and yet si multaneously posit that a person who does 
not exist inherently is the accu mulator of actions, the experien cer of results, and so forth. 
Because it is very rare that these two are able  to be combined, the Madhyamaka view is very 
difficult to find. In short, it is valid or correct that because persons do not exist inherently  they can 
accumulate actions, experience their results, and so forth. On the other hand, if persons 
existed inherently they would not be able to accumulate actions, experience the results of 
actions, and so forth; in that, they would no t be able to perform any function at all.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 322) says: 
(Fourth, if, mistaking the measure of the object of negation, one refutes all, then [phenomena] could only be similar to 
the horns of a rabbit and could not be like illusions:) 
Therefore, when analyzed with the reas oning analyzing suchness, production and 
so forth are not found, whereby inherently existent production and so forth are 
refuted, but all whatsoever [phenomena]— production, cessation, and so forth—are 
not refuted. If they were, it is said that there would be the fallacy that [everything] 
would be like the horns of a rabbit or the son of a barren woman, empty of all 
performance of functions. Furthermore, ther e would be the fallacy that it would be 
that all dependently-arisen agents, actions,  and objects are not feasible within the 
mere illusory-like appearance that is left over.  
 
If, mistaking the measure of the object of negation, one refutes all phenomena, these 
phenomena would be similar to the horns of a rabbit and could not be like illusions. When 
production, abidance, and disintegration are not found through analyzing them with the 
reasoning analyzing suchness, inherent production and so forth are refuted. However, it is not 
that production, cessation, and so forth are refuted. In other words, conventionally 
phenomena are produced, cease, abide and so fort h, and this is not refuted. If, on the other 
hand, production, cessation, and so forth were refuted, there would be the fault that compounded phenomena would be empty of being able to perform a function and would 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
860 instead be like the horns of a rabbit or the so n of a barren women, that is, non-existent. In 
addition, there would be the fault that all de pendently-arisen agents, actions, and objects 
would not be feasible within the mere il lusory-like appearance that remains.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Aryadeva’s Four Hundred  says: 
If that were the case, cyclic existence 
Would not be like an illusion. 
Chandrakirti’s commentary says: 
When dependent-arisings are seen as th ey are, they become like illusory 
creations, not like the son of a barren woman. If it is asserted that this 
analysis refutes production in all respects and that thereby the non-production of caused phenomena is indicated, then they would not be 
like illusions but would be comprehended by way of [examples] such as the son of a barren woman. Therefore,  fearing that this would entail 
the consequence that dependent-aris ings do not exist, [we] do not 
compare [phenomena] with those. Use magical illusions and so forth which do not contradict dependent-arisings. 
 Aryadeva says that if it were like that, cyclic existence would not be like an illusion but would 
be like the horns of a rabbit. Sometimes from a distance the long ears of a rabbit can be 
mistaken for horns, although such horns do not in  reality exist. An illusion is something that 
appears and performs a function but does not exist inherently.  Chandrakirti’s commentary says that when depe ndent-arisings are seen exactly as they are, 
they become like illusory creations such as illu sory horses and elephant s, and not like the son 
of a barren women. If it is asserted that such  analysis refutes the production of compounded 
phenomena in all ways and thereby shows th at compounded phenomena are not produced, 
then such phenomena would not be like illu sions but would instead be understood by 
examples such as the son of a barren women. Since this woul d entail the consequence that 
dependent-arising would not exist, phenomena are not likened to the son of a barren women, but are likened to magical illusions, dreams, mi rages, moons in water, and so forth which do 
not contradict dependent-arisings but show that they lack solidity. While compounded 
phenomena depend on causes and conditions , uncompounded phenomena depend on being 
imputed by name and conception. Thus , neither compounded phenomena or 
uncompounded phenomena exist inherently or truly.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, it would be a fault if the subject [the phenomenon being realized as empty] existed in the face of a reasoning 
consciousness, but there is no fault for an illusory-like appearance to be left over as a remainder after the reasoned 
negation:)  
Chandrakirti also says: 
Therefore, when thoroughly analyzed in this way, an inherent existence 
of things is not established. Just an illusory-like [appearance] remains 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
861 left over with respect to things individually. 
 
There would be a fault if a phenomenon were  analyzed and found to not exist truly but 
existed in the perspective of a reasoning consci ousness. On the other hand, there is no fault 
in illusory-like appearance remaining after true  existence has been negation by reasoning. 
Chandrakirti says that when thoroughly analyzed in this way, an inhere nt existence of things 
is not established. All that remains with respect to  things is an illusory-l ike appearance. In this 
way all phenomena are like illusions.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, therefore, although it is a fault to conceive illusory appearances as trul y having their own entityness, there is no 
fault in conceiving them as only illusory:) 
Thus, conceiving [objects] as merely existe nt dependent-arisings which are illusory-
like appearances is not a faulty conception  of illusion. If illusory-like appearances 
were conceived to be established by way of their own entityness or were conceived 
to be truly existent, it would be a fault. 
 
It is a fault to apprehend illusory appearances as truly existent in their own entityness, but 
there is no fault in apprehending them to be mere illusions. Therefore, apprehending merely 
existent dependent-arisings which are illusory-l ike appearances is not a faulty conception of 
illusion but is correct. If illusory-like appearance s are conceived to exist from their own side or 
truly, this is a fault and needs to be abandoned.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 323) says: 
(Seventh, a source for those points:) 
The King of Meditative Stabilizations Sutra  says: 
Migrators in cyclic existence are like dreams. 
No one is [inherently] born here and no one [inherently] dies. 
No [inherently existent] sentient being,  human, or living being is found. 
These phenomena are like bubbles, banana trees, 
Magician’s illusions, lightning in the sky, Moons in water, and mirages. In this world no one [inherently] dies and goes, Passing over to another life. But actions done are not wasted, they ripen As wholesome and unwholesome effects in cyclic existence. 
 The migrating beings who wander in existence, th at is, in cyclic existence, are like dreams. 
Since there is no one who is born, there is no one who dies. No sentient being, human being, 
or living is found – that is, no inherently existe nt sentient being, human being, or living being 
is found – whereas a conventiona lly existent sentient being, hu man being, and living being is 
found. Such phenomena are like banana trees, which, when their bark is peeled away, no 
essence is found. They are also like bubbles, like  illusions, like a flash of lightning in the sky 
that quickly finishes, like the moon reflected in  pools of water which appear to be the actual 
moon, and like mirages. There is no one who havi ng died in this world transfers and migrates 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
862 to another world, that is, there is no inherent ly existent person who dies and migrates to 
another world. Actions done are never wasted bu t ripen as white and black results in cyclic 
existence. White results are the results of vi rtue, happiness and good rebirths. Black results 
are the results of non-virtue, suffering and bad re births as cats, birds, and other animals or as 
hell beings or hungry ghosts.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
When sought with the reasoning analyz ing suchness, persons—who are born and 
transmigrate—and so forth, able to with stand analysis, are no t found, not even a 
particle. Nevertheless, phenomena give  rise to wholesome and unwholesome 
effects within being illusory-like. You need to form understanding of such 
statements. 
 When sought with the reasoning analyzing suchne ss, not even a small particle of persons who 
are born and migrate that are able to bear analysis are found. Although phenomena are 
illusory-like they give rise to white and black resu lts, that is, white or virtuous actions give rise 
to happiness and black or non-virtuous actions gi ve rise to suffering. It is necessary to 
understand the meaning of such statements.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Eighth, furthermore, although [phenomena] appear as empt y of gross obstructiveness, this is not illusory-like 
appearance:) 
Moreover, instead of sustaining in meditative equipoise a setting within the view 
penetrating the mode of being, [some] gain  a stability of a mere one-pointedness in 
which the mind does not conceive anythi ng. Through its power, when they rise 
from it, appearances of mountains and so forth—rather than appearing as they did 
before as hard and obstructive—appear vagu ely, like rainbows or light smoke. Even 
this is not the illusory-like appearance mentioned in scripture because it is an 
appearance [of objects] as empty of gro ss obstructiveness whereas those objects are 
not appearing as empty of inherent establishment.  
 
Appearing as empty of coarse ob structiveness is not the meaning of illusory-like appearance. 
For example, while a very small particle is em pty of coarse obstructiveness this is not the 
meaning of it appearing like an illusion. Ther e are people who cultivat e meditative equipoise 
without really understanding the meaning of em ptiness or correct view and, thinking that 
they are meditating on emptiness, abide sing le-pointedly without their mind apprehending 
anything. When such people, who think that th ey have been equipoised firmly on emptiness 
for a long time, rise from that equipoise, mounta ins and so forth, instead of appearing to be 
solid and obstructive like before, appear to them like rainbows or fine smoke. However, this is 
not the illusory-like appearance taught in the Buddha’s scriptures but just an appearance of 
things as empty of coarse obstructiveness, ther efore they do not appear as empty of inherent 
existence.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
863 Also, the non-existence of obstructive hard ness is not at all suitable to be the 
meaning of the emptiness which is the abse nce of inherent existence. Otherwise, 
there would be the fallacies that when rain bows and so forth are taken as substrata, 
it would not be possible to generate a conc eption of true existence [with respect to 
them] and that, when obstruct ive objects are taken as su bstrata, it would not be 
possible to generate a consciousness real izing the absence of  true existence. 
 
The lack of obstructive hardness is not at all th e meaning of the emptiness that is the lack of 
inherent existence. If it were, there would be th at fault that if one we re to take rainbows and 
so forth as a basis, it would no t be possible to generate a conception of true existence with 
respect to them. There would also be the fault that  if one were to take obstructive objects as a 
basis, it would not be possible to generate a wi sdom realizing the lack of inherent existence 
with respect to them.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 324) says: 
The method in dependence on which illusory-like appearance occurs 
(This section has five parts, the first of whic h is: how to generate appearance as like illusions:) 
Question : What has to be done for the meanin g of illusion to appear unerringly? 
Answer:  For example, the eye consciousness sees  illusory horses and elephants, and 
the mental consciousness ascertains the non-existence of those horses and 
elephants in accordance with how they  appear. In dependence on those, 
ascertainment that the appearance of horses  and elephants is an illusory or false 
appearance is generated. 
 
What is to be done for the meaning of illusion to appear unmistakenly? The eye 
consciousness, for example, sees illusory horses and elephants, whereas the mental 
consciousness ascertains that those horses and elephants do not exist in the way that they 
appear. In short, it is the eye consciousness that sees illusory horses and elephants, but it is 
the mental consciousness that understands that th ey do not exist in the way that they appear. 
In this way the appearances of such horses and el ephants are ascertained to be illusory or false 
appearances. 
 
Friday morning, 9 February 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 324) says: 
(Second, a composite of the two—a reasoning consci ousness and a conventional consciousness—simultaneously 
appear:) 
Similarly, there is undeniable appearance of persons and so forth to a 
conventional consciousness, and a reason ing consciousness ascertains them as 
empty of inherent existence—establishm ent by way of their own entities. In 
dependence on those, ascertainment of persons as illusory or false appearances is 
generated. 
 A composite of a reasoning consciousness and a consciousness of conventionalities can arise 
simultaneously. A reasoning consciousness ( rigs shes ) is a knower of suchness, thusness, or 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
864 emptiness, whereas an awareness of conventionalities ( tha snyad pa’i blo ) or conventional 
consciousness is any ordinary consciousness such  as an eye consciousness, ear consciousness, 
and so forth. These two can arise simultaneous ly. That persons and so forth appear to a 
conventional consciousness cannot be denied s aying “They do not appear” in that houses, 
mountains, the aggregates of people, and so fo rth are seen by an ordinary conventional eye 
consciousness. At the same time a reasoning consciousness can know them to be empty of 
inherent or true existence. In dependence on these two consciousnesses, the ascertainment of 
persons and so forth as being illusory  or false appearances is generated. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Due to this fact, in meditative equipois e one meditates on space-like emptiness, 
[realizing that] the target aimed at by th e conception of signs [inherent existence] 
does not exist—not even a particle. When  that has gotten down to the essential 
point35 and one rises from it viewing the appe arance of objects, an illusory-like 
emptiness [which is a composite of appe arance and emptiness] subsequent to 
meditative equipoise appears. 
 
At the time of meditative equipoise there is no  appearance of true existence as well as no 
appearance of conventionalities, and all that appears is space-like emptiness. That is the 
essential point of this meditation. Then, when  one arises from meditative equipoise into 
subsequent attainment, one views all appearances as being like illusions. Just as an illusion is 
something that appears but does not exist fr om its own side, similarly in subsequent 
attainment all phenomena appear – that is, form s appear to the eye consciousness, sounds to 
the ear consciousness, odors to the nose consci ousness, tastes to the tongue consciousness, 
and tactile objects to the body co nsciousness – as truly exist, but mentally one knows that they 
are not truly existent and therefore s ees them as being like illusions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, therefore, a separate reasoning for sett ling [appearances] as illusory-like is not needed:)  
In that way, through analyz ing phenomena frequently with the reasoning analyzing 
whether they are established by way of  their own entities or not, strong 
ascertainment with respect to the absenc e of inherent existence is generated. 
When, after that, one views the dawning of  appearances, they appear as like 
illusions; there is no separate way of  settling the illusory-like emptiness. 
 
There is no need for another reasoning to settle appearances as being like illusions. In other 
words, through analyzing phenomena with th e reasoning analyzing whether they exist 
inherently or not, one generates the strong as certainment that they do not exist inherently, 
and, after that, when appearances dawn they a ppear as illusory-like, due to which there no 
need to separately settle illusory-like emptiness.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
                                             
 
35 That is, when the space-like meditative equipoise has been successful.

865 (Fourth, everything should be done within the illuso ry-like state subsequent to meditative equipoise:) 
Therefore, even when engaging in the cl ass of deeds such as bowing down and 
circumambulating, you should do them wi thin being affected by the force of 
analysis as [explained] earlier and thereby train in illusory-like appearance. 
Activities should be done within this. Through having trained in it, merely by 
becoming mindful of the view those will appear as like illusions. 
 
One should do everything – prostrations, circum ambulating, making offerings, tsa-tsas, and so 
forth – within illusory-like subsequent attainment . In other words, having previously analyzed 
the illusory-like, when doing actions as such prostrating, circumambulating, making offerings 
or tsa-tsas, going for refuge, confessing, reciti ng the Samayavajra practice, doing the Vajradaka 
burnt offering ritual, and so forth, one should cons tantly strive to train in seeing everything as 
illusory-like appearances.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The fifth, the instructions with regard to  seeking ascertainment of the illusory-like,  has three parts, the first of which is:  
to train in emptiness since one needs to induce ascert ainment with respect to [a composite of] appearance and 
emptiness:)  
Let us express how to seek that ascertai nment in terms easy to understand: As 
explained earlier, cause the generality of the object of negation by reasoning to 
appear well; contemplate well inherent existence as it is superimposed by the 
ignorance in your own continuum and identify it.  
 
With respect to the instructions on seeking asce rtainment of the illusory-like, one should first 
train in emptiness since it is necessary to in duce ascertainment with respect to appearance 
and emptiness. An easily understandable way in which to seek that asce rtainment is that, as 
explained earlier, one should first make the me ntal image of the object of negation appear 
clearly by means of reasoning. This means th at one should clearly imagine the object of 
negation thinking, “If the object of negation we re to exist, it would exist in such-and-such a 
way.” For example, just like imagining, “If th ere were horns on my head, they would point 
upward and not downward,” or “If there was a ta il on my bottom, it would be like this and 
that,” similarly one should imagine “If things were to exist truly, they would be stable, 
unchanging, and independent” and so forth. Afte r that, one should strive to contemplate and 
identify the inherent existence superimposed on all phenomena by the ignorance in one’s 
own continuum.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Then, with particular emphasis contempl ate the fact that if such inherent 
establishment exists, it does not pass beyo nd oneness and difference, and similarly 
contemplate the mode of the presence of da mage to the assertions [of the self and 
aggregates] as both of those. Inducing as certainment that sees the damage, finally 
make firm the ascertainment thinking, “A  person does not in the least have 
inherent establishment.” In this way train frequently in the factor of emptiness. 
 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
866 Then, contemplate that fact that if there was such true existence, things could not pass 
beyond being one or different, and that if th ings are asserted to be inherently one or 
different, how this assertion is harmed by reas oning. Having induced the understanding that 
sees this harm, one should make this ascertai nment firm thinking, “A person is not in the 
least inherently existent.” In this way, one should  try to arrive at a stable understanding of this 
and train in the factor of emptiness. one can also ask “Does emptiness itself exist truly and 
inherently?” It does not. In fact among the tw enty and sixteen types of emptinesses, such as 
the emptiness of the external, the emptiness of the internal, the emptiness of both, and so 
forth, there is one type of emptiness called “the emptiness of emptiness.” These emptinesses 
are taught in the Ornament for Clear Realizations  and its commentaries, as well as in the 
Supplement to the Middle Way  and its commentaries. You should try to understand them.  
  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, without forgetting emptiness, the illusory-like appearance of persons:)  
Then, let the undeniable appearances of the conventions of persons appear as 
objects of your awareness. Take to mind the dependently-arisen factors of positing 
the person as the accumulator of action s and experiencer of effects, finding 
ascertainment with respect to how dependent-arisings are feasible within the 
absence of inherent existence. 
 Then, how is it that the illusory-like person a ppears without forgetting emptiness? One should 
make the conventional appearance of the person arise as the object of one’s awareness. Then 
one should take to mind the factor of dependent- arising which is that that person is posited as 
the accumulator of actions and the experiencer of  results. In other wo rds, one should think 
about the fact that it is the person who collects  actions and experiences their results, just like 
a farmer who sows seed in the field in spring and gathers the resulting harvest in autumn. Then it is necessary to gain ascertainment with  respect to how dependent-arisings are feasible 
within the lack of inherent existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, the need for the example of a mirror image w hen appearance and emptiness seem to be contradictory:) 
When those two seem to be contradictory, taking as examples [mirror] images and 
so forth, contemplate the mode of non- contradiction as follows. The image of a 
face is an aggregate in one common base  (1) of undeniably being produced in 
dependence on a face and a mirror even thou gh it is empty of the eyes, ears, and so 
forth it appears to have, and (2) of di sintegrating when ei ther of those two 
conditions [face and mirror] is absent. Just  so, even though a person does not have 
inherent establishment—not even a particle—i t is not contradictory for it to be the 
accumulator of actions, the experiencer o f  e f f e c t s ,  a n d  t o  b e  p r o d u c e d  i n  
dependence on former contaminated action s, afflictive emotions, and so forth. 
Contemplating this, you should train [in the non-contradiction of appearance and 
emptiness]. Such should be known in all 36 
 
                                              
36 That is, not just with respect to the person bu t also with respect to phenomena, and so forth. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
867 When appearances and emptiness seem to be contradictory, one should apply the example of 
the reflection of a face in a mirror. One should  think that although one’s face appears in a 
mirror, the appearance is empty of being an actual  face. That is, there is no actual face inside 
the mirror. The reflection of a face together with  eyes, ears, nose and so forth is seen in the 
mirror, even though there are no eyes, ears, nose , and so forth inside th e mirror, that is, even 
though it is empty of the appearances which ar e eyes, ears, nose, and so forth. However, in 
dependence on the face and the mirror, the refl ection of a face is undeniably produced. On 
the other hand, when one of those conditions , for example, the mirror, is absent, the 
reflection of the face disintegrates, that is, it does not exist. In other words, without the 
presence of the two conditions of  the face and mirror, the reflection of a face will not be seen. 
Similarly, although the person does not have the sm allest particle of inherent existence, still it 
is not contradictory for it to be the accumula tor of actions and the experiencer of results 
produced by previous actions and afflictions. We, for example, presently experience happiness 
and pleasure, as well as some unhappiness and suffering, but none of these can occur without 
a previous cause. Happiness and well-being arise due to virtuous actions created in past lives, 
whereas suffering, unhappiness, pain, mental illn ess, and so forth arise due to non-virtuous 
actions created in past lives. Therefore, all of them arise due to the coming together of causes 
and conditions. On the other hand, even though the cause exists, as long as the conditions 
have not arisen the result will not arise. For exampl e, although a rice seed is good, if it is not 
put in good soil it will not give rise to a result, and even if it is planted in good soil but is not 
watered and so forth it will not give rise to it s result because the necessary conditions for its 
growth are absent. Similarly, other phenomena al so come about as a result of the gathering 
together of their causes and conditions. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 325) says: 
5 Selflessness of Phenomena 
(The presentation of settling the selflessness of phenomena has two parts, the first of which is that although the objects 
of negation [in the selflessnesses of persons and phenomena] do not differ in subtlety, the two selflessnesses are 
differentiated by way of the subjects:) 
The bases of the designation of a person—the  five aggregates, six constituents [earth, 
water, fire, wind, space, and consciousness], six sense spheres [eye, ear, nose tongue, 
body, and mental sense powers], and so forth are phenomena. Their emptiness of 
inherent existence—establishment by way of  their own entities—is the selflessness of 
those phenomena. 
 
According to the Prasangika Madhyamaka the objects of negation in the case of the 
selflessness of persons and the selflessness of phenomena are not different in terms of one 
being more coarse and one being more subtle. However, in the case of Svatantrika Madhyamaka the two objects of negation are completely different.   The basis of designation of the person, such as Annamaria, Jonathan, and so forth, is the 
five aggregates – the form aggregate, feeling ag gregate, discrimination aggregate, compositional 
factors aggregate, and consciousness aggregate. Th e person is also designated on the basis of 
the six constituents or elements : the earth constituent, water constituent, fire constituent, 
wind constituent, space constituent, and consciou sness constituent. It is also designated on 
the basis of the six spheres: the eye sphere, ear sphere, nose sphere, tongue sphere, body 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
868 sphere, and mental sphere. All of these – the five aggregates, six constituents, and six spheres 
– are phenomena. The emptiness of inherent ex istence or emptiness of establishment by way 
of their own entity of all these phenomena is the selflessness of those phenomena.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the mode of settling that selflessness:) 
The mode of settling that selflessness has two parts: a refutation through moving 
over the reasoning explained earlier and a refutation through another reasoning 
not explained earlier. 
 
One way of settling selflessness is by way of th e reasonings explained above, for example, the 
reasoning of one and many or the reasoning of  one and different. For example, one analyzes 
whether the aggregates, constituents, and spheres ex ist as an inherently existent one or as an 
inherently existent many. Selflessness is also se ttled by means of other reasonings that were 
not explained above.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 326) says: 
Refutation through moving over the reasoning explained earlier 
There are two types within the phenomen a of the aggregates, constituents, and 
sources [the physical and the mental. Their inherent existence] is to be refuted as 
before through analyzing with respect to  the physical whether the parts—their 
directional parts such as the eastern part, and so forth—and the whole are 
established as inherently one or different,  and analyzing with respect to the mental 
whether the parts—their own temporal part s such as earlier and later [portions]—
and the whole are established as inherently one or different. 
 
There are two types of phenomena included in the aggregates, constituents, and spheres: (1) 
those that have form, the physic al, and (2) those that are cons ciousnesses. One investigates 
those that have form as to whether their dire ctional parts and their whole are inherently one 
or inherently different. For example, one can inve stigate a pen in terms of whether its eastern, 
southern, western, northern, upper, and lower parts and the whole pen are inherently one or 
inherently different. Likewise, one investigates those that are consciousnesses as to whether 
their earlier and later temporal parts and the whole consciousness are established as 
inherently one or inherently different.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 This is the meaning of the sutra quoted earlier: 
Just as you have known the discrimination of self, 
Apply this mentally to all [phenomena]. 
 One should similarly apply the knowledge that  discriminates the self as not existing 
independently, solidly, partlessly, and so forth to all phenomena, such as the form aggregate. 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
869 For example, one can think: this table does not ex ist inherently because it does not exist as an 
inherently existent one or as an inherently existent many.  
 STUDENT: There are two reasonings – (1) not ex isting as an inherently existent one and 
inherently existent many and (2) not existing as an inherently existent one and inherently 
existent different – that seem to be used interc hangeably here. However, they seem to be quite 
different in that it does not mean the same to say (1) that something is either single or many 
and (2) that something is iden tical with its basis of imputation or a separate entity. 
G E S H E  J A M P A  G Y A T S O :  T w o  o f  s o m e t h i n g  i s  a l r e a d y  c o n s i d e r e d  t o  b e  m a n y  o r  a  multitude. In both Sanskrit and Tibetan there are the three: the singular tense, the dual tense, 
and the plural tense. Therefore, two are already a plurality.  STUDENT: But it seems to be different to say that the aggregates abide separately in a different place, and to say that they abide as many but in the same place. GESHE JAMPA GYATSO: The aggregates are ma ny, therefore they exist separately. For 
example, the form aggregate and discrimination  aggregate are different, as are the feeling 
aggregate and discrimination aggregate. Some texts mention “one and many” but here this 
text also says “one and different.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Refutation through another reasoning not explained earlier 
This section has two parts: showing the reasoning of dependent-arising and 
establishing uncaused phenomena also as not truly existent through that and the 
former reasoning. 
 
The reasoning of dependent-arising and the reason ing of one and many can also be applied to 
establish that uncompounded phenomena are empty of inherent existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
SHOWING THE REASONING OF DEPENDENT -ARISING  
(This section has ten parts, the first of which is: a citation of sources:) 
With regard to the reasoning of dependent-arising, the Questions of Sagaramati 
Sutra37 clearly describes a refutation of inherent establishment by the sign of 
dependent-arising: 
Those which arise dependently 
Do not exist by way of their own entityness. 
 The reasoning of dependent-arising refutes in herent existence by the sign “dependent-
arising.” An example of this reasoning is: the tabl e is empty of inherent existence because it is 
a dependent-arising. The reason that is applied here is “because it is a dependent-arising.” 
This is clearly stated in the Questions of Sagaramati Sutra . 
 
                                             
 
37 sagaramatipariprccha . 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
870 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 Also, the Questions of the King of Nagas, Anavatapta, Sutra38 clearly says: 
Those which are produced from conditions are not produced; 
They have no inherently  existent production. 
Those which depend on conditions are said to be empty. 
One who knows emptiness is aware. 
This is [said] very frequently in the precious scriptures. 
 Whatever is produced from conditions is not produced inherently. Thus, there is no 
production by entityness. Whatever depends on conditions is said to be empty of inherent 
existence. Whoever knows emptiness is conscien tious. Such statements often occur in the 
Buddha’s teachings.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, commenting on the meaning of the sutra:) 
The meaning of the “not produced” mentioned in the first line is explained by the 
second, “...no inherently existent producti on.” Thus, a qualification of the object 
of negation is affixed to the refutation of production. 
 
“Whatever is produced from conditions is  not produced” means that they are not inherently  
produced. Therefore, the qualificatio n of the object of negation, “not  inherently  produced,” is 
necessary affixed to the refutation of production.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Chandrakirti’s Clear Words  cites the Descent into Lanka Sutra,39 “Thinking of no 
inherently existent production I said, ‘A ll phenomena are not produced.’” Fearing 
that his statements of no production with out a qualification affixed might be held 
[as indicating that] all production wh atsoever was non-existent, the Teacher 
himself identified the thought of the sutra,  explaining that it means the absence of 
inherently existent production. 
 The Buddha said in this sutra “Intending that they are not inherently  produced, I said ‘All 
phenomena are not produced.’” Fearing that with out qualifying his statement with the word 
“inherently” that all phenomena might be held to  be non-existent, the Buddha said that “not 
produced” means empty of inherently existent production. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
                                             
 
38 anavataptanagarajapariprccha . 
39 lankavatara . 
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
871  Then, the third line says that depending40 and relying on conditions is the 
meaning of being empty of inherent establis hment. Thereby, it indicates that being 
empty of inherent establishment is the me aning of dependent-arising, and not an 
emptiness of the ability to perform a func tion—a negative of mere production.  
 
The third line explains that depending and re lying on conditions is the meaning of being 
empty of inherent existence. This shows that  being empty of inherent existence is the 
meaning of dependent-arising, and that depend ent-arising is the meaning of being empty of 
inherent existence. This is because if phenomen a were inherently existent they could not arise 
in dependence on anything. This is not an empt iness of the ability to perform a function, that 
is, it is not a refutation of mere production.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  says that by reason of being dependent-arisings 
[phenomena] are pacified of or empty of inherent existence: 
That which is a dependent-arising 
Is pacified of inherent existence. 
Therefore, you should know that those who propound any and all creations of 
darkness, such as that the Madhyamika syst em must propound that by reason of 
dependent production there is no production, are refuted. 
 Nagarjuna also says that whatever is a dependent- arising is pacified of inherent existence, that 
is, is without inherent existence. By the reas on of being dependent-arisings, phenomena are 
pacified of or empty of inherent existence. Among the three doors of liberation, signlessness 
r e f e r s  t o  c a u s e s  b e i n g  p a c i f i e d  o f  s i g n s  o f  inherent existence; emptiness refers to all 
phenomena being pacified or empty of inherent existence; and wishlessness refers to results 
being pacified of being inherently existent wished for results. Thus, those with mental 
darkness who say that the Madhyamaka system propounds that by the reason of dependent 
production there is no production at all are refuted, in that the Ma dhyamaka system only 
refutes inherent  production.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 327) says: 
(Third, praising the reasoning of dependent-arisi ng as the eliminator of all bad views:) 
Such reasoning of dependent-arising is greatly praised; the Questions of the King of 
Nagas, Anavatapta, Sutra  says: 
Realizing the doctrines of dependent-arising, 
The wise do not at all rely on extreme views. 
Thus, it says that through realizing dependen t-arising as it is one does not rely on 
views holding to extremes.  
 
                                              
40 rag las .  
FPMT Basic Program          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path          ILTK – 5-9 February 2007 (18) 
872 The reason of dependent-arising is greatly praise d as being the best. This sutra says that the 
wise or skilled, through realizing dependentl y-arising phenomena, do not rely on extreme 
views, that is, on the view of annihilation and the view of permanence. In other words, 
through realizing dependent-arising exactly as it is, one does not rely on the view holding to 
the extreme of annihilation and the view holdin g to the view of permanence. Extreme view is 
one of the five views: (1) the view of the tran sitory collection, (2) extreme view, (3) the view 
holding a bad view as supreme, (4) the view holding bad morality and conduct as supreme 
morality and conduct, and (5) wrong view (den ying the existence of the law of cause and 
effect, past and future lives, and so forth). 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s ) “Treatise on the Middle”  sets forth the 
meaning of that: 
Since things are dependently arisen 
They cannot sustain these conceptions [of be ing produced from self, other, and so 
forth]. 
Therefore, this reasoning of dependent-arising 
Cuts through all the nets41 of bad views. 
This is an unsurpassed feature of the gr eat beings, the father Nagarjuna and his 
spiritual sons. Therefore, from among th e reasonings I will describe here the 
reasoning of dependent-arising. 
 
The Supplement to the Middle Way  says that since things arise in dependence, conceptions such 
as “If they exist, they must ex ist inherently, and if they do no t exist inherently, they do not 
exist at all” are not sustained. Thus, the reas oning of dependent-arising cuts through all the 
nets of bad views. This is the unsurpassed fe ature of the father Nagarjuna and his spiritual 
sons, Aryadeva and so forth. Therefore, here  among the many different reasonings, Lama 
Tsongkhapa explains the reasoning of dependent-arising. 
 We too should try to understand and realize de pendent-arising so as to cut through all wrong 
views and gain the correct view. Together wi th correct view, we should also develop 
renunciation and bodhichitta whereby we will co me to have all three principle aspects of the 
path. Practicing in this way becomes very meaningful.
