873 Monday afternoon, 12 February 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (translation by Jeffrey Hopkins, Tibetan text page 327) says: 
(Fourth, identifying the extremes of superimposition and dep recation, the places where one can go wrong with respect 
to the view:) 
Here, the principal places of possible error that serve as obstacles to the pure view 
are two. One is the view of permanence or superimposition, which involves the 
mode of [mis]apprehension of the conception  of true establishment, this being to 
conceive phenomena as truly established. Th e second is the view of annihilation or 
denial, in which the measure of the object of negation has not been delimited and 
one has gone too far, whereby it is impo ssible to induce ascertainment in one’s 
own system with respect to the dependent- arising of cause and effect, without any 
way of identifying, “It is this, not that.” 
 The opposite of pure view are the extreme view  that all phenomena exist inherently, and the 
extreme view that if phenomena do not exist in herently they do not exist at all. In other 
words, there are two extremes that are obstac les to pure view, the first is the view of 
permanence or the view of superimposition wh ich conceives phenomena to be truly existent. 
The second is the view of anni hilation or the view of deprec ation which denies, for example, 
the existence of a relationship be tween causes and their results.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, the simultaneous avoidance of the two ex tremes through the reasoning of dependent-arising:) 
Those two are abandoned without residue when inherent establishment is refuted 
based on a reason inducing ascertainment with respect to the arising of such and 
such an effect from such and such causes and conditions. For, through 
ascertaining the reason, the view of a nnihilation is eradicated, and through 
ascertaining the meaning of the thesis, the view of permanence is eradicated. 
 
Through the reasoning of dependent-arising,  which abandons true existence, the two 
extremes of superimposition and deprecation ar e simultaneously abandoned. With respect to 
the arising of a specific result, for example, a table, from specific ca uses and conditions, one 
can say: the table does not exist inherently  because it is a dependent-arising. The 
ascertainment of the reason “because it is a dependent-arising” eliminates the extreme of 
annihilation. The ascertainment of the meanin g of the thesis “the table is a dependent-
arising” the view of permanence is eradicated. In “dependent-relation” or “dependent-arising” 
the word “dependent” eliminates the extreme of superimposition,” and the word “relation” or 
“arising” eliminates the extreme of deprecation or non-existence by indicating that there is 
something arises.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
a. (The sixth, the presentation of dependent-arising, has tw o parts, the first of which is: that shared with the 
Chittamatrins:) 
Therefore, external things such as sprouts and internal things such as 
compositional activity arise in dependence respectively on seeds, and so forth, and 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
874 ignorance, and so forth. 
 
One presentation of dependent-arising is common to both the Chittamatrins and the 
Madhyamikas, whereas another is unique to the Madhyamikas . External things, such as 
sprouts, are dependent-arisings because they ar ise from seeds and so forth. Also internal 
things such as the second of the twelve links, karmic formations (compositional activity), are 
dependent-arisings because they arise from the first link of ignorance and so forth. Such 
karmic formations are also called projecting actions since they project a rebirth in cyclic 
existence. Both Chittamatrin s and Madhyamikas assert these external and internal 
phenomena to be dependent-arisings.    
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the uncommon mode of positing dependent-arising by the Madhyamikas:) 
If that is so, that those [sprouts, compositional activity, and so forth] are 
established by way of their own nature is not feasible because whatever is established by way of its own entityness must  be inherently established—that is, be 
able to set itself up under its own power—whereby it is contradictory for it to rely 
on causes and conditions.  
 The Madhyamikas’ uncommon mode of positing dependent-arising is that they say that 
external phenomena such as sprouts and seeds,  and internal phenomena, such as karmic 
formations and so forth, are not established by way of their own characteristics because if they 
were established by way of their own characterist ics they would be able to set themselves up 
without depending on anything else and would ther efore be inherently existent. If it were like 
this, it would be contradictory for them  to rely on causes and conditions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Aryadeva’s Four Hundred  says: 
That which has a dependent arising 
Is not under its own power. All these are not under their own power; Therefore, self [inherent ex istence] does not exist. 
Through this you should understand that persons, pots, and so forth are without inherent establishment because of being imputed in dependence on their own 
collection [of parts]. Those are the tw o presentations of the reasoning of 
dependent-arising. 
 
Aryadeva says that that which arises in depend ence is not under its own power. Because all 
functioning things are not under their own powe r, they do not exist inherently. Because of 
that, a self, the object of negation, does not exis t. By means of these citations, it should be 
understood that persons, pots, and so fort h are imputed in dependence on their own 
collection. For example, the person is imputed in dependence on the aggregates, and pot is 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
875 imputed in dependence on its parts or on the ba sis of the collection of eight or nine subtle 
particles. Therefore, these phenomena do not exist inherently.  
 The two presentations of the reasoning of dependent-arising are: 1. the reasoning common to the Chittamatrins and Madhyamikas, and 
2. the reasoning uncommon to the Madhyamikas,  specifically the Pras angika Madhyamikas. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Seventh, how these refute truly existent oneness and difference:) 
What is dependently produced or dependently imputed is not an inherently 
existent one with that upon which it depends; [for] if it  were, all agents and objects 
would be one. Those two are also not inhe rently existent others because if they 
were, a relationship [between them] would be refuted, whereby dependence on 
[causes or a basis of imputation] would be contradictory. 
 How do these reasonings refute being truly existe nt one and being truly existent different? If it 
is dependently produced and dependently imputed,  it is not an inherently existent one with 
that upon which it depends. If it were an inherently existent one, all agents and actions would 
be one. These two are also not inherently other or inherently different because if they were 
inherently different a relationship between them  could be refuted, whereby reliance would be 
contradictory. In other words, if two things we re inherently different, they could not rely on 
each other and could not have any kind of relationship whatsoever.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 329) says: 
(Eighth, statements of this in Nagarjuna’s texts:) 
Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  says: 
That which arises dependently, 
Respectively is not one [with what it depends on,] 
And is also not [inherently] other than that. 
Hence, it is not annihilated and not permanent. 
 
Nagarjuna’s Fundamental Wisdom  also says that that which aris es in dependence on something, 
for example, a son who arises in dependence on a mother, is not that itself. Also that son is 
not inherently other than or different from his mo ther as if he were inherently different from 
her, he would not need to depend on his mother  having given birth to him. Hence, this is 
neither annihilation nor permanence. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, Nagarjuna’s Praise of the Supramundane42 says: 
The philosophers assert that suffering 
Is created by itself, created by another, 
Created by both, and causelessly. 
You said it is dependently arisen. 
                                              
42 lokatitastava . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
876 That which arises dependently 
You asserted as empty. That things do not exist under their own power Is the lion’s roar by the unequalled you. 
Thus, he says that conceptions of oneness,  difference, the extremes of permanence 
and annihilation, as well as the four extreme types of production are refuted by the reasoning of dependent-arising. 
 The logicians – that is, those who accept other tr eatises – who assert that suffering is created 
by s elf, t ha t  it  is  creat ed by o t her, t hat  it  is  created by both, and that it is created without 
causes. For example, the Samkyas assert that ph enomena arise from their own nature, that is, 
from themselves. Those who assert that phenom ena are produced by other are most of the 
Buddhist schools, all those from the Svatan trika Madhyamaka downward, who assert that 
from an inherently existent cause is produced an inherently existent result. Those who assert 
that phenomena are created by both self an d other are the Nigranthas, the Naked Ones. 
Those who assert that phenomena, results, ar e produced without causes are the Charvakas, 
for example, they say that the roundness of peas , the sharpness of thorns, and so forth are not 
made by anyone but arise naturally. They say that  if there were a cause that makes peas round, 
it should be possible to see it. Some years ago there was a meeting in Sarnath in which a 
Charvaka propounded his views to which anothe r Indian responded: “It follows that you do 
not have a liver, lungs, kidneys, and so forth inside your body because you do not see them. 
Also there is no hair on the back of your he ad because you cannot see it.” The Charvaka was 
not able to reply and brushed aside the debate. In  fact, all these tenets still exist nowadays in 
India.   The Buddha taught that ph enomena that arise in dependence are empty of existing 
inherently, that is, existing from their own side. His speech teaching dependent-arising is 
likened to the roar of a lion, which is able to  frighten all other anim als. The reasoning of 
dependent-arising refutes being inherently one and inherently different, the extremes of annihilation and permanence, and the four extr emes of production from self, other, both, 
and without a cause.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Ninth, praising the view in which appearance and emptiness dawn as mutually compatible:) 
Inducing ascertainment in that way of the emptiness of all the targets aimed at by 
conception of signs [inherent existence], one does not forsake ascertainment of the 
relationship of actions and their effects.  The consequent reliance on discarding 
[non-virtues] and assuming [virtues] is greatly praised.  
 
The ascertainment of the emptiness of inherent  existence does not fo rsake the ascertainment 
of a relationship between actions and results. Th erefore, to rely on discarding the object of 
negation, that is, discarding the conception of true existence, and adopting the correct view 
that sees all phenomena as dependent-arisings, is greatly praised.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says:

Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
877 Nagarjuna’s Essay on the Mind of Enlightenment43 says: 
Reliance on actions and their fruits 
Within knowing this emptiness of phenomena 
Is more wonderful than the wonderful, 
More fantastic than the fantastic. 
 Through knowing all phenomena to  be empty of inherent existence to rely on actions and 
their results, that is, the law of cause and ef fect, is more wonderful than wonderful, more 
amazing than amazing. His Holiness gave the tr ansmission of this text by Nagarjuna in 
Sarnath in December 2006 and gave some explanation on it.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The tenth, advice on the need to differentiate the four, inherent existence and absence of inherent existence and in 
general existence and non-existence, has six parts, the first of which is : the actual advice:) 
I n  o r d e r  f o r  s u c h  t o  h a p p e n ,  i t  i s  n e c e s s a r y  t o  d i f f e r e n t i a t e  b e t w e e n  i n h e r e n t  
existence and mere existence as well as between the absence of inherent existence 
and non-existence. It is as Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Supplement to 
(Nagarjuna’s)  ‘Treatise on the Middle’”  says: 
What wise person, while knowing the presentation of non-inherently 
existent cause and effect which are reflections would—through observing as merely existent forms, feelings, and so forth which do not 
abide separately from causes and effects—ascertain them as having 
inherent existence? Therefore, although  observed to exist, they have no 
inherently existent production. 
 It is necessary to distinguish between (1) inhe rent existence and mere existence and between 
(2) non-existence by way of its own characteri stics and non-existence. While knowing that 
causes and results, like the reflection of a face  in a mirror, do not exist inherently what wise 
person, through observing the form aggregate, feeling aggregate, discrimination aggregate, 
compositional factors aggregates, and consciou sness which do not exist inherently but are 
observed to be merely existent, would ascertai n them to be inherently existent? Therefore, 
although these phenomena are observed to exist by a valid cognizer, they are not observed to 
have inherently existent production.  Existence in general and inherent existence ar e different, as are not existent in general and 
not inherently existent. For example, the horns of  a rabbit do not exist in general, but while 
the head of the rabbit does not exist inherently it  does exist. Also while horns do not exist on 
my head, my head does exist (in fact, it is ve ry hard like the head of George W. Bush!).  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 330) says: 
(Second, together with a source, how one falls to th e two extremes when those are not differentiated:) 
When those are not differentiated, one do es not pass beyond the two extremes of 
superimposition and deprecation, since then once something exists it exists by way 
                                              
43 bodhicittavivarana . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
878 of its own entity and once something does not exist by way of its own entity it is 
totally non-existent.  
 
If one does not differentiate be tween them – (1) existence in general – mere existence – and 
inherent existence, and (2) non-existence in ge neral and non-inherent existence – one will not 
pass beyond the extremes of superimposition an d deprecation which think, respectively, that 
if something exists it exists by way of its own en tity, and if it does not exist by way of its own 
entity it does not exist at all.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Chandrakirti’s Commentary on (Aryadeva’s) “Four Hundred”  says: 
According to the proponents of things as inherently existent, as long as 
a thing’s existence occurs, its own enti tyness also exists and when it is 
devoid of its own entityness, that th ing does not exist in all ways, like 
the horn of a donkey. Thus, si nce they do not pass beyond 
propounding the two [extremes], it is difficult to fit together all their 
assertions. 
 The proponents of things, that is, those who propound things to  be inherently existent, say 
that if things exist, they must exist by way of their own entity or from their own side, and that 
if they do not exist by way of their own entity, th at is, if they do not exist inherently, they do 
not exist at all, like the horns of a rabbit. Si nce they do not pass be yond propounding the two 
extremes, it is difficult to fi t all their assertions together. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, the means by which t hose two extremes are avoided:) 
Therefore, one is freed fr om all extremes of existe nce through the absence of 
inherent existence, and one is freed from  all extremes of non-existence through 
being able to posit non-inherently existent  causes and effects within what does not 
inherently exist. 
 
The lack of inherent existence frees one from th e extreme of existence, and the ability to posit 
non-inherently existent causes and results frees one from the extreme of annihilation. In this 
way, one is freed from both extremes.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, the mere poles of existenc e and non-existence and the poles [or extrem es] which are places of error with 
respect to the view do not have the same meaning:) 
Concerning anta [end, extreme, limit, and so forth] Vasubandhu’s Reasonings for 
Explanation44 says: 
Anta is used for finish, end, 
                                              
44 vyakhyayukti . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
879 Nearness, direction, and lowliness. 
 
The mere extremes of existence and non-existenc e and the extremes that are a deviation from 
or the opposite of correct view do no t mean the same. The word “extreme” ( mtha’ ) has the 
meaning of exhausted ( zad), conclusion (‘ jug cha ), near ( nye), direction ( phyogs ), and depreciate 
(smad pa ).  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Although the antas mentioned here are accepted in our own system also, with 
respect to the anta (extreme) which is a place of erro r with respect to the view as in 
“free from the extremes” Kamalashila’s Illumination of the Middle45 says: 
If in Madhyamaka there were any ultimate nature of self-entity to the 
mind, then since it had that, how could conceptions of permanence 
and impermanence be extremes? It is not reasonable to say that proper 
mental application in accordance with the suchness of things is a 
situation of falling [to an extreme]. 
 The extremes mentioned here are accepted in  our own system. The extreme that is the 
opposite of the view indicated in “free from ex tremes” is set out in Kamalashila’s text saying 
that if in Madhyamaka the mi nd existed ultimately, how could conceptions of permanence 
and impermanence be extremes? The extreme of ex istence or the extreme of permanence is to 
hold that something is inherently permanen t and therefore cannot perform a function, 
whereas the extreme of impermanence is to hold that the aggregates are inherently 
impermanent. Thus, it is not correct to says th at proper mental attention in accordance with 
the suchness of things is a place into which to fall.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
As he says, mental application in accordance  with how an object actually is, is not 
a situation of falling [to an extreme]. Hence,  just as in the world an abyss is called 
an extreme and falling into it is called fa lling into an extreme, the conception of 
phenomena as truly existent and the concep tion that nothing at all is established 
or that nothing exists are [cases of] falling to extremes of permanence and 
annihilation—the opposite of the correct  fact. Conceptions that phenomena do 
not ultimately exist and that actions and th eir effects, and so forth, conventionally 
exist, and so forth, are not conceptions of  extremes because the objects subsist in 
the way that they are conceived. 
 
Mental attention to the way in which an object abides, that, is as not existing inherently but 
existing conventionally, is not a place into which to fall, like an abyss, and is not a holding to 
an extreme. In the world an abyss is called “an ex treme” and falling into an abyss is said to be 
“falling into an extreme.” Similarly, the co nception of phenomena as truly existent or 
inherently existent and the conception that no  phenomenon at all are established or exists 
                                             
 
45 madhyamakaloka . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
880 (this is, if phenomena are not inhe rently existent they do not exis t at all) is a falling into the 
extremes of permanence and annihilation. Th erefore, conceptions that phenomena do not 
exist inherently and that actions and their resu lts exist conventionally, are not conceptions of 
an extreme because these objects exist in exactl y the way that they are conceived to exist. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, sources:) 
For, Nagarjuna’s Refutation of Objections46 says: 
If the absence of inherent existence were disposed of, 
Then [phenomena] would be established as inherently existent. 
Thus he says that if [something] is not ul timately non-existent, then it ultimately 
exists. 
 
Nagarjuna says that if the lack of inherent existence is averted, inherent existence is 
established. In other words, if something does no t exist non-ultimately, it exists ultimately or 
inherently.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also: 
We do not propound 
A non-assertion of conventionalities. 
 Nagarjuna also says that we do not explain co nventionalities as not being asserted (that is, 
conventionalities are asserted).   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Nagarjuna’s Seventy Stanzas on Emptiness says: 
Do not refute this worldly mode 
Of “Depending on that, this arises.” 
 
Nagarjuna also says that one sh ould not refute the worldly mode of saying “In dependence on 
this, that arises.” Here “the worldly” refers to  people who are not trained in tenets, that is, 
those who merely see the fact th at vegetables arise from planting vegetable seeds, that rice 
arises from planting rice seeds, and so on.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, the distinction of not falling to extremes through [usi ng] not existent, not non-existent, and so forth is a fixation o n 
just words:) 
Therefore, differentiating between the two— is not existent and does not exist—and 
[between the two] is not non-existent and exists [in order to propound that an 
object is not existent, is not non-existe nt, is not both, and is not either] with 
                                              
46 vigrahavyavartani . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
881 respect to a base [i.e., an object] is exha usted as merely a difference in the mode of 
expression. No matter how much one analyz es how the meanings of both appear 
to the mind there is no difference at all. Therefore, to propound that one falls or 
does not fall to extremes through [how] th ose words [are used] is exhausted as an 
unfounded fixation on mere words. 
 
Differentiating between the two “not existent” and “existent” and between “not non-existent” 
and “not existent” with respect to a base, is me rely a different way of expressing them and no 
matter how much one analyzes them there is not found to be any difference in how they 
appear to the mind. Therefore, to propound that  one falls or does not fall to extremes due to 
these words is an unfounded fixation on mere words.  Tuesday morning, 13 February 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 331) says: 
ESTABLISHING UNCAUSED PHEN OMENA ALSO AS NOT TRULY EXISTENT THROUGH THAT AND THE FORMER REASONING  
(This section has twelve parts, the first of which is: to show that  it is the thought of Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  
that when caused phenomena are establis hed by reasoning as not truly existen t, it is easy to establish uncaused 
phenomena as not truly existent:) 
When in this way caused things—persons  and other phenomena—are established as 
not truly existent by way of the reasonings  described earlier, it can be established 
with little difficulty that uncaused phenomen a such as spaces, analytical cessations, 
non-analytical cessations, and suchnesses ar e not truly existent. Thinking of this, 
Nagarjuna says in his Treatise on the Middle : 
Since caused phenomena are thoroughly not established, 
How could the uncaused be established? 
 By this and the previous reasons uncompounded  phenomena are also established to not be 
truly existent. When compounded phenomena are established by reasonings to not be truly 
existent, it becomes easy to also establish un compounded phenomena as not truly existent. In 
fact, this is the intention of Nagarjuna’s Fundamental Wisdom . In other words, when 
compounded phenomena (phenomena that arise due to causes) such as persons and phenomena (for example, the aggregates) are es tablished by way of the reasonings mentioned 
earlier to not be truly existent, one can also easily establish that uncompounded phenomena 
such as space, analytical cessations (a cessati on that is an abandonm ent of an object of 
abandonment by means of wisdom), and non-an alytical cessations (a non-production of a 
phenomena ever again due to the absence of its causes and conditions) are empty of inherent 
existence. Nagarjuna, with th is intention in mind, says th at since compounded phenomena 
are not established, how can uncompounded phenomena be established?  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 332) says: 
(Second, the reason for this:) 
With regard to how it is easy to es tablish [uncaused phenomena as not truly 
existent], when the inherent establishm ent of caused phenomena is refuted as 
before, it is established that even though [phenomena] do not inherently exist, 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
882 agents, activities, and objects—bondage an d liberation, cause and effect, object 
comprehended and comprehender, and so forth—may be posited with respect to 
them. When that is established, even though uncaused phenomena such as the 
nature of phenomena and analytical cessa tions do not truly exist, one can posit 
well the presentations of the objects of attainment and objects of comprehension 
of the path as well as presenting the Jewels , and so forth, as the sources of refuge 
for trainees. When those are not asserted to  exist truly, there is no saying that 
these presentations of the need for positi ng them are incorrect; hence there is no 
point in asserting them to be truly existent. 
 
The way in which uncompounded phenomena are es tablished to be not tr uly existent is that 
when the inherent existence of compounded phenomena is refuted,  it is established that even 
though they do not exist inherently, still bondage in cyclic existence and liberation from cyclic 
existence, causes and results, objects comprehe nded and comprehenders, and so forth can be 
posited. When that is established, even thou gh uncompounded phenomena, such as reality 
(chos nyid , here translated as “the nature of phenom ena”) or thusness and analytical cessations, 
do not truly exist, one can still posit that true cessations are actualized by meditating the path, 
that there are objects of comprehension of th e path, and that there are the Three Jewels, 
among which true cessations and true paths are the Dharma Jewel. When they are not 
asserted to exist truly, the pr esentations that posit them ca nnot be said to be incorrect. 
Therefore, one should not asse rt them to exist truly, and instead assert them to exist 
conventionally and that, therefore, by meditating  the path one can attain true cessations, and 
by meditating on emptiness the root of cycl ic existence, the igno rance conceiving true 
existence, can be eliminated.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, refuting through the reasoning of dependent-arising even the assertion of uncaused phenomena, such as the 
nature of phenomena and cessations, as inherently true:) 
Even those who assert [the nature of phen omena, cessations, and so forth] as truly 
existent must indeed assert and do indeed  assert presentations of definiendum and 
definition, cause of separation and effect  of separation, comprehension by such 
and such a valid cognition, an d so forth with respect to th ose. Then, if [the nature 
of phenomena, cessations, and so forth]  are not related with one’s object of 
attainment, definition, comprehender, and so forth, it could not be refuted that all 
unrelated [phenomena] are definition and de finiendum, and so forth. If a relation 
is asserted, then since dependence on anothe r is not suitable in what truly exists or 
is inherently established, a relationship cannot be posited. 
 
The assertion that uncompounded phenomena, su ch as reality and cessations, are inherently 
true is also refuted by the reasoning of de pendent-arising. Even those who assert true 
existence, the Svatantrika Madhyamaka school an d below, must also accept the presentations 
of definiendums and definitions, the cause of se paration and result of separation (that is, by 
means of meditating the path, the cause, one attains the result that is a separation or 
cessation), and comprehension by a valid cognizer . If there is no relation to the object of 
attainment, the definition, the comprehender, and so forth, it could not be refuted that all 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
883 those unrelated phenomena are definition and de f i n i e n d u m ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  I f  a  r e l a t i o n  i s  
accepted, since relying on another is not suitable for that which is truly or inherently existent, 
a relationship could not be posited. In other word s, if something is truly existent it cannot be 
related to or depend on anything else, but would exist solidly and unchanged by anything. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, a refutation by way of the lack of being one or many:) 
Similarly, [the inherent existence of an uncaused phenomenon] can be refuted 
through analyzing whether [it and its basis of designation] are one or different. If 
the assertion of these as truly existent could not be refuted by this reasoned 
analysis, then since it would also be the sa me in all respects with regard to caused 
phenomena, true existence could not in the least be refuted. 
 
The inherent existence of uncompounded phenom ena is also refuted by  the reasoning of the 
lack of being one or many. If, on the ot her hand, the assertion of uncompounded 
phenomena as truly existent could not be refuted by this reasoning, since it would also be the 
same with respect to compounded phenomena,  true existence could not be refuted even 
slightly by the reasoning of freedom from be ing one and many. For ex ample, the Svatantrika 
Madhyamikas say that while all ph enomena do not exist truly they  do exist inherently or as 
established by way of their own characteristics,  whereas the Prasangika Madhyamikas say that 
true existence, inherent existence, and establ ishment by way of their own characteristics are 
all exactly the same. The Svatantrika Madhyamika s say that phenomena exist inherently or by 
way of their own characteristics because when th ey are sought they are found, for example, 
when the person is sought among the five aggreg ates, an illustration of the person is found, 
this being the mental consciousness. Similarl y, the Chittamatrins a ssert that because the 
mind-basis-of-all is the illustration of the pers on or being, the person or being can be found 
when sought. For these schools, if something is  not found when sought, it does not exist. 
However, according to the Prasangikas when some thing is sought ultimately and not found it 
indicates that it does not exist inherently, wher eas when it is sought conventionally it is 
found, for example, the mere I is found wh en the person is sought conventionally.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, to assert that a caused phenomenon’s emptiness of inherently existing in its own right is an annihilatory 
emptiness and to assert that suchness is trul y existent are wrong perspectives on emptiness:) 
[Wrong position :] The meaning of the statement that caused phenomena are empty 
of inherently existing by way of their ow n entities is that th ose phenomena do not 
have their own entities, whereby [empti ness] is an annihilatory emptiness. 
However, since suchness has its own entity, it truly exists.  
 
Asserting that the emptiness of inherent ex istence of a compounded phenomenon is an 
annihilatory emptiness and asserting that suchne ss is truly existent are wrong understandings 
of emptiness. Here someone wrongly asserts th at the meaning of saying that compounded 
phenomena are empty by way of their own entities  of inherent existenc e means that they do 
not have their own entities, which is an annihilato ry emptiness, that is, a view of annihilation. 
This person asserts, on the other hand, that si nce thusness has its own entity, it truly exists.  
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
884  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Answer:  [The first part of that assertion] is the final place of going wrong with 
respect to settling caused phenomena as em pty of inherent establishment, a view 
deprecating the dependent-arising of caus ed phenomena. The latter is an awful 
view of permanence superimposing true ex istence on whatever has its own entity. 
Thus, [the proponents of these] are wron gly involved with respect to the correct 
meaning of emptiness. 
 
This first assertion is a final deviation with respect to settling compounded phenomena as being empty of inherent existence, and is a view deprecating the dependent-relation of 
compounded phenomena. This second assertio n is an awful view of permanence that 
superimposes true existence on that which has its own entity. This is a wrong understanding 
of the correct meaning of emptiness.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, if to be empty of existing by way of its own entity meant  that the object did not exist in itself, then it would not be  
feasible for there to be a holder of a thesis that so me phenomena truly exist who posits that some phenomena truly 
exist, since [he also would be] empty of inher ent existence [in this annihilatory sense]:) 
If [an object’s] emptiness of its own inherent ly established entity [meant that] it did 
not exist in itself, then sinc e not existing in itself [mea ns] that existence would not 
occur anywhere, a holder of the thesis that  some phenomena truly exist as well as 
the scriptures and reasonings proving that , and so forth, would not be established 
bases [that is, would not exist] due to  being empty of their own inherently 
established entity. Therefore, the positing of a tenet that some phenomena 
[namely, suchnesses] truly exist is an unexamined propounding of whatever appears to mind. 
 If being empty of existing by w ay of its own entity means that the object does not exist (for 
example, if the table being empty of existing by way of its own entity means that the table does 
not exist), it would also not be feasible for a holder of thesis who posits phenomena as truly existing to exist (that is, since he is empty of existing by way of his own entity he too would 
not exist). In other words, the holder of this thesis as well as the scriptures and reasonings 
proving this thesis would not be  established bases (that is, exis tents) due to being empty of 
inherent existence. Therefore, to posit a tenet that some phen omena truly exist is to merely 
say whatever comes to mind (that is, it is a lot of blah-blah). The Prasangika Madhyamikas 
assert that the lack of true existence of all phenomena is the ultimate truth. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 333) says: 
(Seventh, the two—(1) asserting the absence of true existenc e having refuted through reasoning a true existence in all 
phenomena and (2) propounding the truthlessness of all phenomena with a faulty mode of understanding 
emptiness—are in all ways not the same:) 
Seeing well the implications of this mo de of reasoning, all our own [Buddhist] 
sects in the country of Superiors [India] who propound that phenomena truly exist 
are called proponents of things since they  definitely propound that things truly 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
885 exist. Once things are propounded as not truly existent, not to assert any 
phenomenon [as truly existent] appears to be  a sign of greatly surpassing those who 
propound the foolishness of this position. 
 The two – (1) to assert all phenomena as non-tr uly existent having refuted true existence by 
reasoning and (2) to propound all phenomena as non-truly existent by a faulty way of 
understanding emptiness – are not at all simila r. All the Buddhist sects – the Vaibhashikas, 
Sautrantikas, Chittamatrins, and Svatantrika Madhyamikas – who definitely propound that 
all compounded phenomena or functioning thin gs truly exist are called “proponents of 
things.” Those who propound things as not truly existent, that is, those who do not assert any 
phenomena at all to be truly established, greatl y surpass those who talk foolishly, that is, the 
Vaibhashikas, Sautrantikas, Chittamatr ins, and Svatantrika Madhyamikas. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Also, with respect to the propounding of two discordant [positions] regarding 
suchness [some saying that emptiness truly exis ts and others saying that it is not an 
object of knowledge] you should—through  the earlier explanation—understand well 
the status of their mode of debate on whet her the ultimate is ultimately established 
or not within their affinity for the form er mode of conventionalities being empty 
of their own inherently established entity. For the two—(1) not asserting true existence with respect to all things and all phenomena, having [properly] refuted 
with reasoning a true existence in phenom ena, and (2) propounding that all things 
and all phenomena do not truly exist based on an annihilatory emptiness in which 
the way of understanding emptiness is faulty—are dissimilar in all respects. 
 Also those who propound two discordant such nesses, through having an affinity for the 
former mode of conventionalities being empty by way of their own entity of inherent establishment, should understand well by mean s of the earlier explanation how these become 
ways of debating whether the ultimate is ultimate ly established or not. This is because the two 
– (1) not asserting true existence with respect to all things and all ph enomena through having 
refuted true existence with respect to phenomen a by reasoning and (2) propounding all things 
and all phenomena as not being truly existent in  dependence on an annihilatory emptiness, 
which is a faulty way of understanding emptine ss – are different in all ways. In other words, 
there are people who understand that although things do not ex ist inherently they do exist 
conventionally and people who think that things  do not exist inherently and do not exist at 
all, but these two are very different. The latter asserts emptiness but does not understand it 
correctly. The former, on the other hand, clearl y understand that while all phenomena do not 
exist inherently or truly they do exist conventi onally and are able to perform a function. They 
understand that while from the ultimate poin t of view things do not exist, from the 
conventional point of view they do exist.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 334) says: 
(Eighth, the qualm that if all uncaused phenomena are without tr ue existence, this would contradict scripture that 
teaches nirvana to be true:)

886 Objection:  [As quoted before , Nagarjuna said:] 
Since caused phenomena are thoroughly not established, 
How could the uncaused be established? 
If the meaning of that is as you explaine d earlier, does it not contradict other 
statements by him? For, his Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning  says: 
When the Conqueror said 
That only nirvana is a truth, 
What wise person would think, “The rest are not unreal”? 
Thus he says that only nirvana is true and that the others are not.  
 Here the doubt is raised that if all uncomp ounded phenomena are non-truly existent, this 
would contradict the scripture that shows that nirvana is true or truly existent. Although 
Nagarjuna was previously cited as saying that since compounded phenomena are not 
established, how could uncompounded phenomena be truly established, this person here points out that this seems to contradict other statements made by him. To support this he 
cites Nagarjuna’s Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning  which says that nirvana is a truth or true. In short, 
in the previous citation Nagarjuna says that  just as compounded phenomena are not truly 
existent, so too are uncompounded phenomena no t truly established, whereas in another text 
he says that since the Buddha said that only one phenomenon, nirvana, is true, what wise 
person would say “The rest are not unreal?”   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, his Praise of the Element of Qualities
47 says:  
All the sutras teaching emptiness 
Set forth by the Conqueror 
Overcome the afflictive emotions; 
They do not diminish this realm. 
He explains that the sutras teaching s emptiness—the absence of inherent 
existence—are for the sake of abandoning th e afflictive emotions and that they do 
not teach the non-existence of the realm of the pure nature. 
 Also this text by Nagarjuna says that whil e all the sutras teaching emptiness eliminate 
afflictions, that is, they were taught for the purpose of disciples abandoning the afflictions, 
they do not degenerate this realm. Thus, Na garjuna explains that the sutras that teach 
emptiness, the lack of inherent  existence, are for the purpose of abandoning the afflictions 
and that they do not teach the non-existence of the realm, that is, of the buddha lineage that 
exists in the continua of all sentient beings wh ich has the potential to transform into the body 
of a buddha.  
                                             
 
47 dharmadhatustotra. 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
887 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Ninth, the answer is that although nirvana is  undeceiving to a mind that directly percei ves it, it is not a truth in the sense  
of being able to bear [ultimate] analysis; thus, ther e is no contradiction with the former scripture:) 
Answer:  Those [who say such] are wrongl y approaching the meaning of the 
scriptures. For, the meaning of the fo rmer scripture is expressed in the 
Supramundane Victor’s statement, “Monks, this ultimate truth is one, the 
undeceiving nirvana. All composed things have the attribute of falsity, 
deceptiveness.” This sutra says that nirvana is a truth whereas all composed things are false. The early part of the passage very clearly explains that truth means 
undeceiving, and the latter part very clearl y explains that falsity means deceiving.  
 The response to the objection is  that although in the perspective of a mind directly seeing 
nirvana it is undeceptive, since nirvana is not a tr uth that is able to bear analysis it too does 
not exist truly and therefore ther e is no contradiction with the previous scriptural passages. 
Those who say that there is contradiction engage  the meaning of the scriptures wrongly. The 
Buddha said that the ultimate truth is on ly one, undeceiving nirvana, whereas all 
compounded phenomena are false and deceptive. This means that nirvan a is a truth, whereas 
all compounded phenomena are not truths but ar e falsities. The first part of the passage 
clearly explains that “truth” means undeceptiv e and the latter part clearly explains that 
“falsity” means deceptive.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Furthermore, a nirvana [here refers] to an  ultimate truth, as is explained in 
Chandrakirti’s Commentary on (Nagarjuna’s) “Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning”.
48 Hence, an 
ultimate truth lacks the deception of appear ing—to the mind that directly perceives 
it—as if being established inherently wherea s it is not. The re maining [phenomena], 
conditioned things, have the deceptiveness of appearing—to the mind that directly 
perceives them—to be established inherently  whereas they are not. Therefore, when 
[an ultimate truth or natural nirvana] is analyzed with the reasoning investigating 
whether it is truly established or not, [it is  found that] it is not truly established in 
the sense of being able to bear analysis. He nce, what point is th ere in not thinking 
about the meaning in detail and being attached to mere names! 
 
Here a nirvana is an ultimate truth. In the pers pective of the mind directly perceiving ultimate 
truth, ultimate truth does not appear as if inherently existent while not being inherently 
existent. Thus, it is undeceptive. On the ot her hand, when compounded phenomena appear 
to a direct perceiver they appear to be established inherently but are not and because of this are said to be deceptive. When a nirvana is an alyzed with the reasoning investigating whether 
it is truly existent or not, it is found to not be  truly existent in the sense of being able to bear 
analysis. Therefore, there is no point to being attached to mere names.  
 In short, if one analyzes nirvana with reasoning, it will be found to not be able to bear analysis. If, on the other hand, it were found to be able to bear analysis, it would exist 
                                             
 
48 yuktisastikavrtti. 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
888 ultimately, whereupon no one would be able to attain nirvana and all practices done for the 
purpose of attaining it would be meaningless. Thus , that nirvana is a truth means that it is an 
ultimate truth and that all compounded phenom ena are not truths means that they are not 
ultimate truths, although they are deceptive or  conventional truths. This difference must be 
understood and distinguished well.  
 Tuesday afternoon, 13 February 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 335) says: 
(Tenth, establishing the correctness of that:) 
Moreover, Nagarjuna’s Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning  says: 
These two, cyclic existence 
And nirvana, do not [inherently] exist. 
The thorough knowledge of cyclic existence 
Is called “nirvana”. 
Thus, he explains that both cyclic existence and nirvana are not inherently existent 
and that [emptiness which is] just the obje ct of the knowledge that cyclic existence 
is not inherently established is posited as nirvana. Thus, how could this be the 
position of asserting emptiness—the non-tr ue existence of cyclic existence—as an 
annihilatory emptiness? 
 
Nagarjuna’s text says that the two, cyclic exis tence and nirvana, do no t exist, meaning that 
they do not exist truly or inherently. When there is the thorough knowledge of cyclic 
existence, there is also the th orough knowledge of nirvana, the state gone beyond cyclic 
existence, because both existence and peace ar e not inherently existent. The object of the 
wisdom understanding that cyclic existence does not exist inherently is also called “nirvana.” 
There are four types of nirvana, one of which is  called “natural nirvana”; this is emptiness. 
True existence or inherent existence can be li kened to suffering, in which case passed beyond 
suffering or nirvana is the lack of true existe nce or the emptiness of true existence. How can 
the assertion of cyclic existence as  not truly existent, that is, as empty of true existence, be an 
annihilatory emptiness? It is not, because it is not a view that if cyclic existence does not exist 
inherently, it does not exist at all. This view is completely unacceptable. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Eleventh, although emptiness exists, it need not be truly ex istent, and thereby the [suggested] contradiction with the 
quote from the Praise of the Element of Qualities  is dispelled:) 
The passage from Nagarjuna’s Praise of the Element of Qualities  means: 
For the sake of overcoming the concep tion of things as truly existent—
the root of all other afflictive emot ions—the sutras teaching emptiness, 
the absence of inherent establishment,  teach that the referent object of 
the conception of true existence does  not exist. They do not teach that 
emptiness—the realm of the pure natu re, a negative of the two selves 
which are the objects of the conception  of true existence—does not exist. 
 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
889 Although emptiness exists, it does not need to  be truly established, therefore there is no 
contradiction with the citation from Naga rjuna’s text. The meaning of Nagarjuna’s Praise  is 
that the sutras teaching the em ptiness that is a lack of inhe rent existence, teach that the 
determined object of the conception of true  existence does not ex ist for the purpose of 
overcoming the conception of things as truly exis tent – the root of all other afflictions. They 
do not teach that emptiness, the realm of pure  nature (the buddha essence or buddha lineage) 
that is a negation of the two selves, the self of persons and self of phenomena, which are the 
objects of the conception of true existence, does not exist. In short, emptiness does exist but it 
does not exist truly. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Since although emptiness exists  it is not truly established, that passage serves as a 
source for refuting the proposition that th e emptiness which is a negative of true 
existence—its object of negation—does not ex ist. It also refutes the proposition that 
it is not necessary to realize emptine ss, the ultimate suchness, in order to 
exhaustively abandon the afflictive emotions. 
 
Since although that emptiness exists it is not tr uly established, this passage acts to refute the 
statement “Also the emptiness that is a negation of  true existence, the object of negation, does 
not exist” and the statement “It is not necessary  to realize emptiness in order to completely 
abandon the afflictions.” In other words, it is  necessary to realize emptiness in order to 
completely exhaust the afflictions.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Twelfth, through taking a truly existent nature of phenom ena as your object of medita tion the conception of true 
existence cannot be overcome:) 
Therefore, Nagarjuna’s Praise of the Element of Qualities  says: 
Through the three called imperm anence, [coarse] emptiness, 
And suffering the mind is purified. 
The doctrine supremely purifying the mind 
Is the absence of inherent existence. 
 This section shows that if one takes a truly established reality as one’s object of meditation, 
the conception of true existence cannot be over come. Nagarjuna’s text says that by the three 
called “impermanence, empty, and suffering” the mind is purified and that the best Dharma 
for purifying the mind is the lack of inherent exis tence. This verses sets out the four attributes 
of true sufferings: impermanent, suffering, empty,  and selfless. The last, selfless, is the absence 
of inherent existence; meditation on this is the best means for purifying the mind. In 
addition, meditation on the attribute of imperm anence, meditation on the attribute of empty 
– empty of being permanent, partless, and inde pendent, and meditation on the attribute of 
suffering, the misconception that holds suffering s as pleasure, can purify the mind of the 
afflictions.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
890 Also: 
The absence of inherent existence of phenomena 
Should be meditated upon as  the element of qualities. 
 Nagarjuna also says that the absence of inhere nt existence of phenomena should be meditated 
as the dharmadhatu or element of qu alities or sphere of phenomena ( chos kyi dbyings ).  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus he says that the absence of an inherently established nature in these 
phenomena is the element of qualities whic h is the object of meditation, and he 
says that just meditation on it is the su preme purifier of the mind. Therefore, how 
could it be suitable to interpret this as the position that the emptiness which is the 
absence of inherent establishment of ph enomena appearing in this way is an 
annihilatory emptiness and that, therefore, a truly existent emptiness separate from 
it is to be posited as the emptiness which is the object of meditation! This is like 
propounding that in order to remove th e suffering of fright [arising] from 
apprehending a snake in the east de spite there being none there, the 
demonstration that there is no snake in the east will not serve as an antidote to it, 
but that rather one should indicate, “The re is a tree in the west.” For, one is 
propounding that in order to remove the suffering [arising] from adhering to the 
true existence of what appears in this w ay to sentient beings, realization of those 
bases—which are conceived to exist truly—as not truly existent will not serve as an 
antidote, but that rather one must indica te some other senseless base as truly 
existent. 
 
The absence of inherent existence is the dhar madhatu or sphere of phenomena which is the 
object of meditation. Meditation on it is the be st means to purify the mind of the afflictions 
that presently cover or obscure it. How can this be an annihilatory emptiness that thinks that 
if phenomena do not exist inherently they do  not exist at all and that therefore a truly 
existent emptiness separate from it is to be posited as the em ptiness which is the object of 
meditation? This would be like propounding that  in order to eliminate the suffering of fear 
caused by apprehending a snake in the east even  though there is no such snake, it would be 
useless to point out that the apparent snake is a coiled rope, and instead one should say 
“There is a tree in the west.” In other words, th is would be like propounding that in order to 
remove the suffering of sentient beings who ad here to true existence there is no need to 
realize that the bases – persons or phenomena – do not exist truly, and instead one should 
i n d i c a t e  s o m e  o t h e r  b a s e  s u c h  a s  a  p i l l a r  o r  v a se as truly existent saying “There is a truly 
existent pillar” or “There is a truly existent vase.” However, since such declarations are not 
correct, it is necessary to understand that objects do not exist inherently.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 336) says: 
 
PART THREE: TWO TRUTHS 
 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
891 The presentation of conventional and ulti mate truths has four parts: the basis 
from which the two truths are divided, number of divisions, meaning of dividing 
them that way, and meaning of the individual divisions. 
6 Basis 
Basis from which the two truths are divided 
With respect to the basis of division of  the two truths there are many modes of 
assertion among earlier [scholars]; however, Shantideva’s Compendium of 
Instructions49 says, “Objects of knowledge are exhausted as conventional and 
ultimate truths.” Thus, here objects of  knowledge are the basis of division. 
 
With respect to the basis of divi sion of the two truths there are different ways of asserting it. 
However, because Shantideva says that objects of knowledge are divided into just the two, 
conventional truths and ultimate truths, the basis of division is objects of knowledge. Collected 
Topics , on the other hand, posits various divisions of objects of knowledge but we will not go 
into them here.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Number of divisions 
Nagarjuna’s Treatise on the Middle  says:  
Worldly truths for a concealer 
And ultimate truths 
Accordingly, [objects of knowledge] are divided into the two truths—truths for a 
concealer and ultimate truths. 
 
The number of divisions refers to the number of divisions of objects of knowledge, these 
being the two: truths for a concealer or conventional truths ( kun rdzob bden pa ) and ultimate 
truths ( don dam bden pa ).  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Meaning of dividing them that way 
(This section has two parts, the first of which is : to state the assertion of earlier [scholars]:) 
Question : Since the two of the divi sion into two must be different, what kind of 
difference is this? 
Answer:  With respect to this, many earlier [scholars] propounded: 
Pot and woolen cloth, for instance, are different entities. Product and 
impermanent thing, for instance, are one entity and different isolates. 
In these two cases, the two that ar e different are both [functioning] 
things; however, in cases of difference when either is a non-[functioning] thing [that is, a permanent phenomenon] they have a 
                                             
 
49 shiksasamuccaya

892 difference which negates sameness. Among these three [modes of] 
difference, the two truths are different in the sense of negating sameness. 
Some [correctly] assert that the two trut hs are one entity and different isolates. 
 Given that there is a division of objects of knowledge into two, the two divisions must be 
different, so how are they different? Many earlie r scholars said that just as pot and woolen 
cloth, for example, are different entities, so t oo are conventional truths and ultimate truths 
different entities. Others said that just as product and impermanent thing are one entity but 
different isolates, so too are conventional truths  and ultimate truths one entity but different 
isolates. In the case of product and imperman ent thing both are functioning things, in the 
case that one of the pair is a non-functioning thing, there would be th e difference that negates 
oneness. Thus, among the three ways of being di fferent, the two truths are different in terms 
of negating oneness. Some scholars assert th at the two truths are one entity but different 
isolates, which is correct accordin g to the Prasangika Madhyamikas. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, proving the correctness of our own system:) 
I t  i s  g o o d  t o  t a k e  t h i s  i n  a c c o r d a n ce with the statement in Kamalashila’s 
Illumination of the Middle that the relationship of one entity is not contradictory 
even in non-[functioning] things [that is, permanent phenomena]. Therefore, a 
oneness of entity and difference of isolates  is not contradictory even when both of 
the different phenomena are non-[fun ctioning] things or when one is. 
 
Kamalashila says that the relationship of on e entity is not contradictory even for non-
functioning things. Therefore, being one entity but different isolates is not contradictory when both phenomena are non-functioning thin gs and also when one of them is a non-
functioning thing.   Conventional truth and ultimate truth are one entity but different isolates even with respect to non-functioning things. For exampl e, an uncompounded phenomenon such as 
space is itself a conventional truth while its em ptiness of inherent existence is its ultimate 
truth. Thus, conventional truth and ultimate truth are not contradictory even when their 
basis is an uncompounded phenomenon. Likewise , when their basis is a functioning thing, 
the base and its emptiness are one entity but different isolat es. In this case one of the 
phenomenon is a functioning thing and one,  emptiness, is a non-functioning thing. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 For, Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Supplement to  (Nagarjuna’s) ‘Treatise on the 
Middle’”  says, “Two types of natures of all this are taught, conventional and 
ultimate.” Thus, with regard to the na tures of each subject there are two, 
conventional and ultimate. Also, if the tw o truths were not one entity, then since 
it would be very unreasonable for them to be different entities, the two truths 
would have to be entitiless, whereby they  would not exist, for whatever exists 
necessarily exists as one entity or many entities.

893  
All things have two types of entities, the co nventional and the ultimate. Therefore, each 
substratum has these two entities. If the two trut hs were not one entity, they would have to be 
different entities, but this would be very unreasonable, therefore the two truths would have to be entitiless, that is, they would have to be no n-existent, because whatever exists must exist as 
one entity or many entities. There is no third possibility.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Nagarjuna’s Essay on the Mind of Enlightenment  says: 
Conventionalities are explained as emptiness 
[And] just emptiness is the conventional Because of the definiteness that [the on e] would not occur without [the other], 
Like product and impermanent thing. 
 Conventionalities are explained to be empty of inherent existence, and emptiness is explained 
to be conventionally existent because it is defini te that if one does not exist the other does not 
arise, like product and impermanent thing.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
If a sprout, for instance, were a different en tity from its own ultimate [entity], then 
since it would be a different entity also fr om its own emptiness of  true existence, it 
would be truly established. Thus, since it  is not a different entity [from its own 
emptiness of true existence], it is the same  entity. A sprout is empty of its own true 
existence, but is not its own ultimate truth. 
 
If a sprout were a different entity from its ulti mate, it would be a different entity from its own 
emptiness of true existence, whereby it would fo llow as being truly existent. Since it is not a 
different entity from its ultimate, it is one enti ty. A sprout is empty of its own true existence, 
but it is not its own ultimate truth.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Some texts say that the two truths ar e neither one nor different. Among these, 
some are referring to inherently establ ished oneness and difference; others are 
referring to their not being either di fferent entities or one isolate.  
 
Some treatises say that the truths are neither on e nor different. In some cases this means that 
they are not an inherently existent one or an inherently existent different, whereas in other 
cases it means that they are not both different entities and one isolate.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page338) says:

894 7 Truths for a Concealer 
The presentation of the meaning of the individual divisions has three parts: 
conventional truths [or truths for a concealer], ultimate truths, and the 
definiteness of the number of the trut hs as two. The first has three parts: 
etymologies of concealer ( samvrti ) and truth ( satya), definition of a truth for a 
concealer, and divisions of conventionalities. 
Etymologies of concealer ( samvrti ) and truth ( satya ) 
(This section has two parts, an etymology of samvrti  and an etymology of satya . The etymology of samvrti  has two 
parts, the first of which is : the actual etymology:) 
Chandrakirti’s Clear Words  explains samvrti  as having three [usages]—(1) concealer 
of suchness, (2) mutually dependent objects, and (3) worldly conventions. He 
describes the latter as having the characte r of object of expression and means of 
expression, knower and object of knowledge, and so forth; therefore, these are not 
just subjective conventions—that is, co nsciousnesses and expressions [that is, 
words]. Also, all objects of knowledge and objects of expression should not be held t o  b e  c o n v e n t i o n a l  t r u t h s  [ s i n c e  a n  e m p t i n e s s  i s  a n  o b j e c t  o f  k n o w l e d g e  a n d  
expression but is an ultimate truth]. 
 
Svamrti  or kun rdzob , literally all-concealing or all-obsc uring, has the meaning of that which 
conceals suchness. It also has the meaning of  mutually dependent bases, as well as the 
meaning of worldly conventions. Since the latter is explain ed t o hav e the characteristics of 
object of expression and means of expression, as well as knower and object of knowledge, 
conventional truths are not just conventions,  knowers, and expressions that are object-
possessors. Also whatever is an object of know ledge and object of expression should not be 
held to necessarily be a conventional truth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The samvrti  which is the concealing consciou sness with respect to which forms 
and so forth are posited as truths [in the sense that ignorance takes them to exist 
the way they appear] is the first among the three [usages]. It is the ignorance which 
superimposes on phenomena the existenc e of their own inherently established 
entity whereas they do not have such. For,  since in fact true establishment does 
not occur, a positing of [objects that appe ar to be truly existent] as truths is in 
terms of a mind and there is no positing [o f objects that appear to be truly existent] 
as truths for a mind that is not a conceiver of true existence.  
 
The concealer that is the concea ling consciousnesses in the pers pective of which forms and so 
forth are posited as truths is the first of the three. It is the ignora nce that superimposes 
inherent existence on phenomena that do not exist inherently. Since it is not possible for 
objects to be truly existent, the positing of them as truths is in the perspective of an 
awareness, but they are not posited as truths in  the perspective of an awareness that does not 
conceive true existence. In other words, conven tional truth are truths only in the perspective 
of a conceiver of true existence,  they are not truths in the perspective of minds that are not 
conceivers of true existence.  
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
895  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In that way Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
The Subduer said that because obscuration 
Obscures the nature, it is the concealer; Those fabrications which it perceives As true are truths for a concealer. Things that are fabrications [exist] conventionally. 
 The Subduer said that obscuration or ignorance is a concealer because it obscures or covers 
the final nature of phenomena, that is, their em ptiness. The fabrications that appear to be 
true to the ignorance that conceives true existe nce are truths for a concealer. The things that 
arise from causes and conditions  and change momentarily are co nventional truths or truths 
for a concealer.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, eliminating error with respect to that:) 
Concerning this, Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Suppl ement to (Nagarjuna’s) 
‘Treatise on the Middle’”  says: 
In that way, truths for a concealer are posited through the force of the 
afflictive ignorance which is includ ed within the [twelve] links [of a 
dependent-arising] of cyclic existenc e. For Hearers, Solitary Realizers, 
and Bodhisattvas who have abandone d afflictive ignorance and who see 
compositional phenomena as like th e existence of reflections and so 
forth, [these] have a nature of fa brication and are not truths because 
they do not exaggerate [phenomena] as  truly existent. To children these 
are deceptive, but to the others they  are just conventionalities due to 
being dependent-arisings, like a ma gician’s illusions and so forth. 
 
Truths for a concealer or conventional truths  are posited by the force of the afflicted 
ignorance included in the twelve links of dependen t-arising. For hearers, solitary realizers, and 
bodhisattvas who have abandoned afflicted ignorance and who see compounded phenomena 
as being like the reflection of a face in a mirror and so forth, they have a nature of fabrication 
and are not truths, that is, ultimate truths, beca use they do not strongly adhere to true or 
inherent existence. For ordinary childish bein gs, who have ignorance, things are deceptive, 
whereas for arya beings they are mere conven tionalities due to arising dependently and 
relatedly, like illusions, mirages, reflections, echoes, moon reflected in water, and so forth. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
This passage does not teach that the positing  of truths for a conc ealer is a positing 
of [their] existence by ignorance, and it does not teach that truths for a concealer 
are not posited in the perspective of Hearer s, Solitary Realizers, and Bodhisattvas 
who have abandoned the afflictive ignoranc e. The reason for th e first is that, as 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                    ILTK – 12-13 February 2007 (19) 
896 explained before, afflictive ignorance is a conceiver of true existence and thus the 
object conceived by it does not exist even  conventionally and whatever is a truth 
for a concealer necessarily exists conventionally. Therefore, whatever is the 
conventional50 in terms of which phenomena are posited as existing 
conventionally51 must be something that is not the afflictive ignorance which is 
taken as the concealer52 [in “truth for a concealer”53]. 
 
This does not teach that the positing of truths fo r a concealer as existing is a positing of their 
existence by ignorance, nor does it teach that truths for a concealer, such as forms, are not 
posited in the perspective of hearers, solitary realizers, and bodhisattvas who have abandoned 
afflicted ignorance. The reason for the first is that since afflicted ignorance is a conceiver of 
true existence, the object appr ehended by it does not exist ev en conventionally, whereas that 
which is a truth for a concealer is pervaded by  existing conventionally. When phenomena are 
posited as existing conventionally, afflicted ig norance is not taken to be the concealer. In 
other words, it is not in the perspective of  afflicted ignorance that all phenomena exist 
conventionally.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The reason for the second point is that Chandrakirti is establishing that because 
those who have abandoned the concealer— afflictive ignorance—do not have the 
concealer—a consciousness adhering to true existence—in the face of which [objects 
appearing to exist inherently] are posite d as truths, compositional phenomena are 
not truths for them. It does not establish that compositional phenomena are not 
truths for a concealer [for them]. Chan drakirti’s statement that compositional 
phenomena are only conventionalit ies for them means that between 
conventionality and truth those are not posi table as truths for them, and thus the 
term “only” [in “only conventionalities”] el iminates truth, not truth for a concealer. 
Chandrakirti’s thought in speaking of th e two—mere conventionality and truth for 
a concealer—should be un derstood in that way. 
 The reason for the second is that since thos e who have abandoned the concealer, afflicted 
ignorance, do not have a concea ler adhering to true existence which posits them as truths, 
compounded phenomena in their perspective are established as not being truths, but they do 
not establish that compounded phenomena ar e not truths for a concealer (conventional 
truths). In other words, in the perspective of hearers, solita ry realizers, and bodhisattvas who 
have abandoned afflicted ignorance, compound ed phenomena such as forms are not truths, 
but are mere conventionalities. The word “m ere” eliminates compounded phenomena being 
truths, but not their being truths for a concea ler or conventional truths. The intention in

897 saying “mere conventionality” and “conventional truth” or “truth for a concealer” should be 
understood to be just this.

898 Tuesday morning, 6 March 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 339) says: 
(The second, the etymology of satya , has two parts, the first of wh ich is: the actual etymology:) 
Chandrakirti’s Clear Words  says, “Truths for a worldly concealer are worldly 
concealer truths” [which might be mistaken to mean “ samvrtisatya  are truly 
established conventionally”]. With respect to this, Chandrakirti’s Commentary on 
the “Supplement to (Nagarjuna ’s) ‘Treatise on the Middle’”  says: 
Those which are perceived as true by the concealer and are individually 
perceived as having inherent existenc e, whereas they do not, are truths 
for a worldly erroneous concealer; hence, they are worldly concealer 
truths. 
 
Truths for a concealer are so-called because they appear to be truly existent and are adhered to 
as truly existent, although in fact they are no t truly existent but appear in this way due to 
ignorance. In other words, due to ignorance a ll conventional phenomena appear to be true, 
that is, truly existent. Thus, even  though they are not truly exis tent, they are conceived to be 
truly existent by a mistaken conception. For this  reason they are called “truths for a worldly 
concealer.”   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, the statement in the Clear Words  s ho uld be t aken  in  acco rda n ce w it h t h is  
clear description of truths for that co ncealer which is the ignorance explained 
earlier. It should not be taken as true establishment conventionally. For, otherwise 
it would contradict the system of the no n-occurrence of establishment by way of 
[the object’s] own character even conventionally. Also, the refutation of true 
establishment and the proof of the ab sence of true existence is done 
conventionally. Through this way, you should also understand the master 
Jñanagarbha’s explanation of abiding as trut hs for a concealer [not mistaking it for 
“abiding as truths conventionally”]. 
 That concealer is the ignorance that was expl ained earlier, that is , the ignorance that 
conceives all phenomena to be truly or inherently  existent. Thus, it is not that phenomena are 
conventionally established truly or inherently. Otherwise this would cont radict the Prasangika 
Madhyamaka point of view that establishment by  way of its own characteristics does not exist 
even conventionally. The refutation of true establishment and the establishment of non-true existence is done nominally or conventionally, that is, by way of words. In this way, one 
should understand the master Jñanagarbha’s expl anation of abiding as truths for a concealer, 
that is, that all conventional phenomena such as  forms and so forth are truths for a concealer 
or conventional truths. The meaning of this is that while they are not established truly, they 
are established for a concealer.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, dispelling a qualm:)
