Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
899 Objection:  Then, since the nature of phenomen a [which actually is an ultimate 
truth] and the two selves [which do not exis t at all] are truths for a concealer—that 
is, for a consciousness conceiving true existence—they would be truths for a 
concealer. 
 
Someone thinks that reality ( chos nyid ) or thusness and the two selves  – the self of persons and 
the self of phenomena – are truths for a concea ler. Emptiness or thus ness is not actually a 
conventional truth or a truth for a concealer. The conception  of a self of persons and the 
conception  of a self, on the other hand, of phenomena do exist conventionally.  
 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Answer:  If that which is merely true for th e concealer, the conception of true 
existence, were posited as a truth for a concealer, such would indeed be so, but 
that is not propounded. This is merely  an explanation of the concealer with 
respect to which the truths of “tru ths for a concealer” are truths.  
 
If that which is merely true in the perspect ive of the conception of  true existence, the 
concealer, were posited as truth for a conceale r it would indeed be like that. This is an 
explanation of the concealer in the context of  the etymology of the word “truth” in the 
context of “truth for a conceale r,” as in actuality truths for a concealer are not truths, the 
truth being just one. Although suchness or th usness is not a truth for a concealer, in the 
perspective of a conception of truly existent em ptiness, emptiness is a truth for a concealer. 
On the other hand, in the perspective of the exalted wisdom of meditative equipoise on 
emptiness, thusness is an ultimate truth.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Definition of a truth for a concealer 
(This section has six parts, the firs t of which is: how all phenomena have two natures, together with a source:) 
Each of these external and internal things has two natures, ultimate and 
conventional. If this is illustrated with a sprout, for instance, there is the nature of 
the sprout found by a reasoning consci ousness which perceives the meaning of 
suchness, a true object of knowledge, and there is the nature of the sprout found 
by a conventional consciousness comprehe nding a deceptive object, a false object 
of knowledge. The former is the nature of the sprout’s ultimate truth; the latter is 
the nature of the sprout’s truth for a concealer.  
 All phenomena have two entities, an ultimate entity and a conventional entity. Thus, both 
external phenomena such as forms and so fo rth and internal phenomena such as sense 
powers and consciousnesses have two entities, an ultimate entity and a conventional entity. For example, a sprout has two entities: 
• the entity of the sprout found by a reasonin g consciousness (that is, that exists in the 
perspective of the meditative equipoise of an  arya) which sees the meaning of suchness, a 
real object of knowledge is its ultimate entity, and 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
900 • the entity of the sprout found by a consci ousness of conventionalities comprehending a 
deceptive object, a false object of kn owledge, is its conventional entity. 
In other words, the sprout seen with the eyes is its conventional entity.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In that way Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
[Buddha] said that all phenomena have two natures, 
Those found by perceivers of the true and of the false; Objects of perceivers of the true are suchness, Objects of perceivers of the false are truths for concealers. 
 
All functioning things have two natures, one found by that seeing the true or real ( yang dag ) 
and one found by that seeing the false ( rdzun pa ). Objects of perceivers of the real are suchness 
or thusness, whereas objects of perceivers of the false are truths for a concealer or 
conventional truths. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, showing that those are found by separate valid cognitions:) 
This teaches that with regard to the na tures of a sprout there are two—the two 
truths—and that the ultimate is found by the former consciousness whereas the 
conventional is found by the latter consciousness. Chandrakirti’s Commentary on 
the Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s ) “Treatise on the Middle”  says, “Two types of natures of 
all things are taught, conventional and ul timate.” Thus, differentiating two natures 
for each thing, he says that the ultimate is found by a consciousness perceiving the 
meaning of reality and the conventional is found by a consciousness perceiving 
falsities. 
 
The two truths are found by different valid cogn izers. The ultimate entity of the sprout is 
found by the former consciousness, whereas it s conventional entity is found by the latter 
consciousness. However, it is to be noted that this does not mean that a sprout is determined 
to be ultimate or conventional based on a former and later consciousness.   Chandrakirti says that there are two types of entity, conventional entity and ultimate 
entity. Thus, he differentiates two entities for each thing, an ultimate entity and a 
conventional entity. In the perspective of a cons ciousness seeing the meaning of reality, that 
is, an exalted wisdom of meditative equipoise of an arya meditating on emptiness, ultimate truth is found. On the other hand, a consci ousness perceiving falsities, that is, a 
consciousness of an ordinary being, finds conv entional truths or truths for a concealer.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, the need to find the Madhyamika view in order to ascertain a base [a phenomenon] as  a truth for a concealer:) 
Since a truth for a concealer is not a trut h in fact but only for a consciousness 
conceiving true existence, it is necessary to ascertain it as a falsity in order to 
ascertain the very meaning [of truth for a concealer]. In order to ascertain an 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
901 illustration [of a truth for a concealer] such  as a pot as a false object of knowledge, 
a deceptive object, it is necessary to find the view which through a reasoning 
consciousness causes disbelief of the referent object of the conception of [its] true 
existence. For, without refuting truene ss falsity cannot be established by valid 
cognition. 
 In order to ascertain a base as a truth for a co ncealer it is necessary to find the Madhyamaka 
view of emptiness. A truth for a concealer is no t actually a truth but is only a truth in the 
perspective of a consciousness conceiving true exis tence. Therefore, it is necessary to ascertain 
the opposite meaning of that. In order to ascertain, for example, a pot, cup, table, eyeglasses, 
and so forth as a false or deceptive object of kn owledge, that is, in order to understand that 
although it appears to be truly existent it is not truly existent, it is necessary to find the 
Madhyamaka view of emptiness. For example, in the case of a pot that is apprehended to be 
truly existent, it is necessary to understand that the referent  or determined object of the 
conception of its true existence does not exist.  This is because without refuting trueness by 
reasoning, it is not possible for falsity to be established by a valid cognizer.  
 For example, taking this cup as an example, on e can ask whether it is truly existent or not. 
Although it appears to be truly existent, it does not exist truly or inherently. Thus, this conception of true existence is mistaken, and it s determined object does not exist. This must 
be understood, whereby the emptiness or ultima te truth that eliminat es true existence is 
understood.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, although the meaning of truth for a concealer has not  been established for one’s mind, it is not contradictory [to 
be able] to ascertain an illustration of a truth for a concealer, such as a pot:) 
A l t h o u g h  p o t s ,  w o o l e n  c l o t h ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h  a r e  t r u t h s  f o r  a  c o n c e a l e r ,  i t  i s  n o t  
necessary when they are established [as ex isting] by a mind that the meaning of 
truth for a concealer be established by the mind. For instance, although pots, 
woolen cloth, and so forth are illusory-lik e appearances as if inherently existent, 
whereas they are not, a mind that establis hes them [as existing] does not need to 
establish the meaning of being illusory-like. 
 
Even though the meaning of truth for a conceale r has not been established for one’s mind, it 
is not contradictory to be able to  ascertain an illustration of a truth for a concealer, such as a 
pot. In other words, pot still appears to  the mind and can be used even without 
understanding how it is established. Pots, woolen  cloth, and so forth, which are truths for a 
concealer or conventional trut hs, are ascertained but are not necessarily ascertained to be 
conventional truths. Thus, it is not necessary to  establish that pots, woolen clots, cushions, 
tables, lights, and so forth are illusory-like appear ances in order to establish that they exist.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, it is not feasible to posit pots and so forth as conventionalities in relation to  a worldly consciousness and as 
ultimates in relation to Superiors:) 
Therefore, it is not feasible  to propound that in this system pots, woolen cloth, 
and so forth are posited as truths for a co ncealer in relation to the consciousness 
of a common being who does not have th e Madhyamika view and that the same 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
902 are posited as ultimate truths in relation to a Superior.  
 
It is not suitable to posit pots, woolen clot hs, and so forth as co nventionalities in the 
perspective of worldly consciousness and to posit them as ultimates in the perspective of aryas. 
In other words, in this system it is not feasib le to posit pots, woolen cloths, and so forth as 
conventional truths or truths for a conceale r in the perspective of the consciousness of 
ordinary beings who have not realized emptiness,  and to posit them as ultimate truths in the 
perspective of a superior or arya being.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
For, that would be propounded opposite  to what Chandrakirti says in his 
Commentary on the “Supplement to (N agarjuna’s) ‘Treatise on the Middle ’”: 
Those which are ultimates for common beings are mere 
conventionalities for [the consciou snesses of] Superiors acting on 
objects involving appearance. That  which is the nature of those 
objects—emptiness—is the ultimate for them. 
 Chandrakirti says that those th at are ultimates for common beings are mere conventionalities 
for superiors who have the appearance of co nventionalities, that is, for superiors in 
subsequent attainment. on the other hand, the nature of those phenomena of lacking true 
existence – emptiness – is the ultimate for supe riors. Therefore, to propound the above would 
be to propound the opposite to this.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Common beings conceive pots and so forth to be true, and since just that is a 
conception [of pots and so forth] as existing ultimately, in relation to their 
consciousness pots and so forth are ultimately established and are not 
conventional objects. The bases—pots and so forth—which for them are ultimately 
established are conventionalities in rela tion to the perception by the exalted 
wisdom in the continuum of a Superior comprehending illusory-like appearance. 
Chandrakirti says that in relation to th is consciousness these cannot be posited as 
true, whereby they are mere conventionalities. 
 Ordinary beings conceive pots and so forth to be  truly existent. Therefore, in their perspective 
conventional truths such as pots, cups, human be ings, horses, dogs, cats, and so on appear to 
be ultimately established. Thus, they do not s ee the meaning of existing conventionally, that 
is, that although they do not ex ist truly they appear to be trul y existent. The pots and so forth 
that appear to be ultimately established in the perspective of ordinary beings are 
conventionalities in the perspective of the exal ted wisdom of an arya comprehending illusory-
like appearance. Since they cannot be posited as true in relation to that consciousness, they 
are mere conventionalities. Hence, it is necessary  to differentiate between a conventionality 
and a mere conventionality. Sometimes it is said  that one first realizes conventional truth and 
that this becomes a method for realizing ultimate truth. Sometimes it is said that one first 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
903 realizes ultimate truth and that this becomes a method for realizing conventional truth. In the 
latter case this refers to mere conventional truth. The word  “mere” indicates that while 
superiors see all phenomena as empty of inherent  existence, they see that the phenomena that 
do not exist inherently do exis t as mere conventionalities.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, the need to differentiate between pots, and so forth, and their nature:) 
Nevertheless, since their nature is said to  be an ultimate truth, a differentiation 
should be made, saying, “Pots and so fort h are conventionalities, and their nature 
is the ultimate of Superiors.” It should not be propounded that pots and so forth 
are ultimates for Superiors; for, their reasoning consciousness seeing the meaning 
of reality does not find pots and so fort h and that which is found by a reasoning 
consciousness seeing the meaning of real ity is said to be the meaning of an 
ultimate truth. 
 It is necessary to differentiate between pots an d so forth and their nature. Pots and so forth 
are conventionalities, whereas their final nature , emptiness is the ultimate for aryas. In other 
words, the final nature seen by aryas is ultimate truth; which is not seen by ordinary beings. It 
should not be said that pots and so forth are ultimates in the perspective of superiors because 
their reasoning consciousness seeing the meaning of reality, that is, their exalted wisdom of 
meditative equipoise seeing empt i n e s s ,  d o e s  n o t  f i n d  p o t s  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  I n  o t h e r  w o r d s ,  
conventional truths such as pots and so fort h do not appear to this exalted wisdom, only 
emptiness or ultimate truth appears to it. In this  context “to find” means “to realize,” it is not 
like the case that having lost on e’s flashlight one finds it again,  or having lost one’s head one 
finds it again! That which is found by a reasonin g consciousness seeing the meaning of reality, 
that is, an exalted wisdom realizing emptiness, is the meaning of ultimate truth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Divisions of conventionalities 
(This section has six parts, the first of which is: that since Prasangika and Svatant rika differ with regard to positing the 
object of negation, their differentiation of real  and unreal conventionalities is not the same:) 
The Svatantrika-Madhyamikas assert that since a consciousness which appears to 
be established by way of its own characte r necessarily exists as it appears, a 
differentiation of real and unreal is not made with respect to subjects [that is, 
consciousnesses]. Rather, appearances of ob jects are differentiated by whether they 
exist or not by way of their own character in  accordance with how they appear. It is 
as Jñanagarbha’s Differentiation of the Two Truths54 says: 
A division of real and unreal 
Conventionalities is made 
Because, though similar in appearing,  
[The one] is able and [the other] unable to perform functions. 
[However] this [Prasangika] system asserts that all appearances [of objects] as being 
                                              
54 satyadvayavibhanga . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
904 established by way of their own charac ter to those who possess ignorance are 
appearances to a consciousness polluted by ignorance. Therefore, they do not 
divide conventional objects into the two—real and unreal. 
 
Since the object of negation is not the same for the Prasangika Madhyamikas and Svatantrika 
Madhyamikas, also the way in which they differ entiate real or perfect conventionalities and 
unreal or mistaken conventionalities is no t the same. From the Svatantrika Madhyamaka 
point of view, consciousness is established by  way of its own characteristics and therefore 
necessarily exists in the way that it appear s. Therefore, the differentiation of real 
conventionalities and unreal conventionalities is not made with respect to subjects or object-
possessors, that is, with respect to knowers or consciousnesses. Rather appearing in one way 
and existing in that way, and appearing in one way but not existing in this way, are 
respectively the ways in which real and unreal conventionalities are posited.   Jñanagarbha also says that although all ph enomena are similar in appearing, not all are 
able to function in the way in which they a ppear. Those that appear in one way and also 
function in that way are real conventionalities,  whereas those that appear in one way but do 
not function in that way are posited as unreal co nventionalities. A table, for example, is a real 
conventionality because it appears as a table an d functions as a table. On the other hand, the 
reflection of a face in a mirror appears as a face but does not function as a face and is 
therefore posited as an unreal conventionality.  Likewise, the moon reflected in water appears 
to be the moon but does not function as the m oon and is therefore also posited as an unreal 
or mistaken conventionality.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, though a mirror image of a face is not a truth fo r a concealer in relation to a worldly person versed in 
terminology, it is in general  a truth for a concealer:) 
With respect to this, Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Suppl ement to (Nagarjuna’s) 
‘Treatise on the Middle’”  says, “That which is false even  conventionally is not a truth 
for a concealer.” [This means that] since an  image of a face, for instance, is not 
true as a face for a worldly conventional [consciousness] of one versed in 
terminology, it is not a truth for a conceale r in relation to it. Nonetheless, because 
it is an object found by a [consciousness]  perceiving a false object of knowledge—a 
deceptive object—it is a truth for a concea ler. Just as a consciousness to which a 
reflection appears is mistaken with respec t to its appearing object, so among those 
who possess ignorance [their consciousnesses to which] blue and so forth appear as 
established by way of their own character are similarly mistaken with respect to 
their appearing object. 
 In the perspective of those who are well versed in terminology, that is, who have studied 
philosophy, a reflection of a face in a mirror is not held to be a truth for a concealer, although 
in general it is a truth for a concealer. This is be cause if it is an existent but is not an ultimate 
truth, it is necessarily a truth for a concealer. Thus, because a reflection, for example, is a type 
of form that exists but is not an ultimate tr uth, it is necessarily a truth for a concealer. 
Chandrakirti says that that which is false even conventionally, such  as a reflection of a face, is 
not a truth for the concealer of one who is well versed in terminology. Since the reflection of 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
905 a face is not true as a face for a worldly conventional consciousness of one versed in 
terminology, it is not a truth for a concealer in  relation to that conventional consciousness. 
However, because a reflection of a face is an object found by a consciousness seeing a false 
object of knowledge, a deceptive truth, it is a truth for a concealer or a conventional truth. 
Just as the consciousness to which a reflection of  a face appears is mistaken with respect to its 
appearing object, similarly the consciousnesses of those who possess igno rance to which blue 
and so forth appear to be established by way of their own characterist ics are also mistaken 
with respect to their appearing objects. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, although a mistaken consciousness cannot posit a true object of comprehens ion, this mistakenness acts as an 
aid in positing a false object of comprehension:) 
When a true object of comprehension is posited, it would be contradictory for [a 
consciousness] mistaken in that way to po sit it. However, this acts as an aid in 
positing a false object of comprehensio n. Otherwise, whatever was not truly 
established conventionally could not be posited as a truth for a concealer, and thus 
when falsities such as illusory-like [appe arances] are posited conventionally, they 
could not be posited as truths for a concealer.  
 
Although a mistaken consciousness cannot posit a true object of comprehe nsion, it acts as a 
assisting condition for positing a false object of  comprehension. In othe r words, when a true 
object of comprehension is posited, it would be  contradictory for a mistaken consciousness to 
posit it. However, the mistaken condition acts as  an assistant in positing a false object of 
comprehension. Otherwise, whatever is not trul y existent conventionally could not be posited 
as a truth for a concealer, and when falsities such as illusory-like appearances are posited 
conventionally, they could not be posited as trut hs for a concealer. In other words, although 
conventional truths are illusory-like appearances, they do exist. Similarly the illusions such as 
a horse or elephant created by a magician on the base of a stick or pebble are a type of form 
and therefore are existents.   Tuesday afternoon, 6 March 2007  There are two types of conventionalities, the real and unreal.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 343) says: 
(Fourth, together with a source, how real and unreal conv entionalities are posited in relation just to the world:) 
The Prasangika system posits the six cons ciousnesses not affected by superficial 
causes of error and the six objects apprehended by those consciousnesses as real 
conventionalities. It posits  the six consciousnesses affected by superficial causes of 
error and the six objects apprehended by those consciousnesses as unreal 
conventionalities. Furthermore, it posits  real and unreal conventionalities in 
relation to just worldly or conventional valid cognitions, not in relation to 
reasoning consciousnesses that accord with a Superior’s perception.  
 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
906 “To the world” means in the perspective of ordi nary beings, in relation to which real and 
unreal conventionalities are posited. From the point of view of the Prasangika Madhyamikas 
there are six consciousnesses – eye, ear, nose , tongue, body, and mental consciousnesses – 
which are affected by superficial causes of error and six consciousnesses which are their 
opposites. Then there are six objects – forms,  sounds, odors, tastes, tactile objects, and 
phenomena – which are apprehended by the six co nsciousnesses affected by superficial causes 
of error and six objects which are apprehended by the six consciousnesses opposite to them, 
those that are not affected by superficial causes of error. Th e unreal or wrong objects and 
object-possessors are unreal conventionalities wh ereas the real or correct objects and object-
possessors are real conventionalities. Moreover, real and unreal conventionalities are posited 
in relation to worldly valid cognizers or valid cognizers of conventionalities, and not in 
relation to reasoning consciousnesses that understand the final nature, thusness, of 
phenomena.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 344) says: 
 
Therefore, since in the Madhyamikas’ own system the two appearances of 
reflections, and so forth, and blue, and so  forth, to those who possess ignorance do 
not differ with respect to whether they ar e mistaken or not in relation to their 
appearing object, they do not make a di vision into the two—real and unreal 
conventionalities.  
 
In this system the two appearances of reflecti ons and blue to those who possess ignorance are 
not different in terms of being mistaken or no t in relation to their appearing objects. For 
example, when an eye consciousness sees the re flection of a face in a mirror, although it 
appears to be a real face, a real face does no t exist in the mirror. Similarly, when an echo 
appears to the ear consciousness in an empty room, it appears to be a real voice although it is 
not a real voice. Hence, the Madhyamikas do not make a division into real and unreal 
conventionalities.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  (6.25) says: 
Objects realized by the world and apprehended 
By the six non-defective senses are true from only A worldly point of view; the rest are presented As unreal from the viewpoint of only the world. 
 Objects apprehended by the six non-defective sens e powers and realized by the world are true 
only from the worldly point of view. The rest  – those phenomena that  are apprehended by 
the six sense powers which are affected by a su perficial cause of error (such as reflections, 
echoes, and so forth) – are pr esented as unreal from the po int of view of the world.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 344) says: 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
907 (Fifth, the exception that a conventional  valid cognition is not able to posit the opposite of the mode of apprehension by 
[a mind] affected by the superficial c ondition of a [bad] system of tenets:) 
With respect to the conception of person s and phenomena as being established by 
way of their own character there are two ty pes, [the innate and artificial]. The 
opposite of a mode of apprehension aris ing, for instance, from the mind’s being 
superficially affected by one’s own bad system of tenets is not established by a 
conventional valid cognition, [that is, the em ptiness which is the o pposite of this is 
established by an ultimate valid cognition]. 
 A conventional valid cognizer affected by the superficial or temporary condition of a tenet 
system is not able to posit th e opposite of the mode of a pprehension. The conception of 
persons and phenomena as established by way of  their own characteristics are of two types, 
that which is innate and that which is due to tenets. The o pposite mode of apprehension 
arising from the mind being superficially affected  by a bad system of tenets is not established 
by a valid cognizer of conventionalities;  therefore, this is an exception.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 344) says: 
(Sixth, although [a consciousness] has dualistic ap pearance, it need not be a mistaken consciousness:) 
Furthermore, although dualistic appearance  occurs for an exalted wisdom knowing 
the varieties which has separated from all causes of pollution due to the 
predispositions of ignorance, it is not mist aken with respect to its appearing object. 
I have explained the reason elsewhere. 
 
Even though there is a dualistic appearance, that is, the appearance of two things, the 
consciousness is not necessarily mistaken. Alth ough there is dualistic appearance for an 
exalted wisdom knowing varieties which is separa ted from all causes of pollution due to the 
predispositions or latencies of ignorance, it is not mistaken with respect to its appearing 
object. The reason has already been explained el sewhere. In other words, the exalted wisdom 
knowing varieties can have many appearances, two, three, four, and so on, but it is not 
necessarily mistaken. In this context dualisti c appearance does not refer to a difference 
between object and object-possessor but only re fers to having two appearances and more, for 
example, the seeing of many people at the sa me time. An omniscient being’s exalted wisdom 
knowing varieties sees all phenomen a as they exist without mistake. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
8 Ultimate Truths 
This section has three parts: meaning of paramartha  (ultimate) and satya (truth), 
definition of an ultimate truth, and divisions of ultimate truths. 
Meaning of paramartha  and satya  
(This section, has three parts, the fi rst of which is: the actual meaning:) 
Chandrakirti’s Clear Words  says, “Because it is an object55 and is ultimate,56 [it is 
                                              
55 artha . 
56 parama . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
908 called] the ultimate object.57 Because just it is a truth,58 [it is called] the ultimate 
object truth.”59 Hence, he asserts both object and ultimate as [applying to 
emptiness] the ultimate object truth [o r truth which is the ultimate object.]. 
 
Chandrakirti says that be cause it is a meaning ( don) and because it is highest or supreme ( dam 
pa), it is the highest meaning or ultimate ( don dam pa ). Because it is truth,  it is called highest 
meaning truth or ultimate truth. Therefore, he  asserts both “meaning” and “highest” to be 
the highest meaning truth or ultimate truth. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, how it is a truth:) 
The mode of truth of an ultimate truth is  [its] non-deceptivene ss. Because it does 
not deceive the world through its mode of subsistence abiding one way and its 
appearing another way, Chandrakirti’s Commentary on (Nagarjuna’s) “Sixty Stanzas of 
Reasoning”  says that an ultimate truth is merely posited as existing through the 
force of worldly conventions. 
 
The mode of truth of an ultimate truth is that it is non-deceptive in that it does not deceive 
the world, that is, ordinary beings, through ex isting or abiding in one way but appearing in 
another way. Ultimate truth is merely posited as existing by the force of  worldly conventions.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, the difference in the meaning of the term “truth” in the two truths:) 
Therefore, “truth” in truth for a conc ealer means a truth for a consciousness 
conceiving true existence, and thus di ffers from [the meaning of “truth” in] 
ultimate truth [which means non-deceptive]. 
 
The word “truth” in “truth for a concealer” or “conventional truth” means a truth in the 
perspective of a conception of true existence. Ultimate truth is not like this, in that an 
ultimate truth is a truth for a superior’s exal ted wisdom directly realizing the emptiness of 
true existence. Hence, ultimate truth does not exist in the perspective of the conception of 
true existence, but exists only in the perspectiv e of the exalted wisdom of a superior directly 
realizing emptiness.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 345) says: 
Definition of an ultimate truth 
This section has two parts: the actual definition and dispelling objections. 
ACTUAL DEFINITION OF AN ULTIMATE TRUTH  
(This section has nine parts, the first of which is: the identification of t he definition of an ultimate truth:) 
                                              
57 paramartha.  
58 satya.  
59 paramarthasatya . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
909 The definition of an ultimate truth is that given in Chandrakirti’s Supplement as 
explained before—[an object] found by a perceiver of that object which is a true 
object of knowledge. Chandrakirti’s commentary on that says: 
Concerning that, the ultimate gain s its own entity through being the 
object of the special exalted wisdom of those perceiving the real. It is 
not established by way of its own se lfhood. This is one nature [of an 
object, the other being its conventional nature]. 
 The definition of an ultimate truth is: that wh ich is found by seeing the object of knowledge 
that is the real meaning, that is, suchness or em ptiness. That ultimate finds its own entity by 
being the object of a special exalted wisdom seei ng the real. It is not established by its own 
nature.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
His saying that it is found by the uncontaminated exalted wisdom comprehending 
suchness and that it is not established by way of its own entity refutes the 
proposition that if there is anything  found by uncontaminated meditative 
equipoise, it is truly established. His speaking of a “ special  exalted wisdom” 
indicates that what is found by any exal ted wisdom of Superiors is not sufficient 
but rather what is found by a special ex alted wisdom—the exalted wisdom knowing 
the mode—is an ultimate truth. An object  found [by such a wisdom] is an object 
established thus [as an ultimate truth] by th at consciousness; it is the same also for 
conventionalities. 
 That it is found by the unco ntaminated exalted wisdom comprehending suchness and that it 
is not established by way of its own entity refu tes the assertion that if it is found by an 
uncontaminated exalted wisdom of meditative equip oise it is truly existent, that is, ultimately 
established. Saying a “ special  exalted wisdom” indicates that ulti mate truth is not found by just 
any exalted wisdom of a superior  but is only found by an exal ted wisdom seeing modes, that 
is, seeing suchness or thusness. That which is fo und by this wisdom is established to be an 
ultimate truth by that consciousness. Likewi se that found by a valid consciousness of 
conventionalities – a valid eye consciousness, ea r consciousness, and so forth – is established 
to be a conventional truth by that consciousness.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the manner in which a Superior’s exalted wisdom of  uncontaminated meditative equipoise finds [its object]:) 
With respect to its mode of finding, when  the eyes of one with cataracts see hairs 
falling—the appearance of intermediate sp ace serving as its basis—the eyes of one 
without cataracts does not see even an appe arance of falling ha irs with respect to 
that base. In the same way, those damage d by the cataracts of ignorance apprehend 
an inherent existence of the aggregates and so forth; [however] in terms of the 
mode of perception of suchness by those who have removed all predispositions of 
ignorance and by a Learner Superior’s exalted wisdom of uncontaminated 
meditative equipoise like the eyes of one without cataracts, not even subtle 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
910 dualistic appearance is perceived. The nature perceived in this manner is an 
ultimate truth. 
 
How does a superior’s uncontaminated exalte d wisdom of meditative equipoise seeing 
thusness find ultimate truth? For example, the ey es of someone with cataracts see falling hairs 
in the space before him, whereas the eyes of some one without cataracts do not see even a 
single piece of falling hair with respect to that  same base. Similarly, those whose consciousness 
is harmed by the cataracts of ignorance appreh end inherently existent aggregates, whereas for 
those who have completely abando ned the predispositions of ignorance, like those whose eyes 
are without cataracts, the uncontaminated ex alted wisdom of meditative equipoise of a 
learner superior perceives suchness without even the slightest subtle dualistic appearance of true existence. The nature seen by this exalted wisdom of a superior is an ultimate truth.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, a source:) 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  (4.29) says: 
One with pure eyes sees the nature—suchness— 
Of the falling hairs and so forth in the place 
Where these unreal entities are imputed 
Through the force of cataracts. Know it is similar here. 
 
The scriptural source for the above is Chandrakirti’s Supplement to the Middle Way  which says 
that one with pure eyes does not see falling hairs in the plac e where they are imputed by the 
force of cataracts but understands them ex actly in the way in which they exist.  
 Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, Chandrakirti’s commentary on that s ays, “The nature of the aggregates and 
so forth perceived by the supramundane buddhas free from the predispositions of 
ignorance in the manner of those without cataracts seeing falling hairs is their 
ultimate truth.” 
 
Chandrakirti’s commentary on that says that th e buddhas see the nature of the aggregates and 
so forth free from the predisposi tions of ignorance in the way th at those without cataracts do 
not see falling hairs. In other words, just as those without cataracts do not see falling hairs, 
likewise the buddhas see the final nature of the a ggregates and so forth, that is, their ultimate 
truth.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, a nirvana is an ultimate truth:) 
The ultimate perceived in that way is the ultimate nature from among the two 
natures of each phenomenon. Furthermore, it is the naturally pure nirvana which 
is the emptiness of inherent existence of subjects, and it is the nirvana which is a 
true cessation—just that [natural emptiness]  separated from any of the seeds of the 
defilements. 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
911  
Nirvana or cessation is an ultimate truth. The ul timate truth or nirvana that is perfectly seen 
by exalted wisdom is the ultimate entity. The emptiness of inherent existence of all 
phenomena is called “naturally pure  nirvana,” this being a terminolog ical type of nirvana. It is 
a nirvana that is a true cessation  that is free from any seeds of  the stains. From the point of 
view of the Prasangika Madhyamikas a true cessation is an ultimate truth and not a 
conventional truth, whereas from the point of  view of the Svatantrika Madhyamikas a true 
cessation is a conventional truth because the ob ject of abandonment is a conventional truth. 
In other words, the Svatantrika Madhyamikas s ay that because the objects of abandonment, 
the afflictions, are conventional truths, their cessations are also conventional truths and not 
ultimate truths.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, it is not the thought of Chandrakirti’s Commentary on (Nagarjuna’s) “Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning”  that a nirvana is 
a truth for a concealer:) 
Chandrakirti’s Commentary on (Nagarjuna’s) “Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning”  says, “Is a 
nirvana a truth for a concealer? It is so .” Also, “Therefore, a nirvana is only 
imputed as a truth for a concealer.” Those statements mean that with regard to 
positing a nirvana or ultimate truth as existing , it is posited as merely existent for a 
conventional consciousness; this system does  not assert that a nirvana is a truth for 
a concealer.  
 
It is not Chandrakirti’s inte ntion that a nirvana is a conv entional truth or truth for a 
concealer. His text sets out the question “Is nirvana a truth for a conc ealer?”, to which the 
reply is “It is so.” This would be said from th e point of view of the Svatantrika Madhyamikas. 
However, Chandrakirti’s text also says that a nirvana is only imputed as a truth for a 
concealer. These statements mean with respec t to positing nirvana or ultimate truth as 
existing, it is posited as merely existing  in the perspective of a consciousness of 
conventionalities. This system does not asse rt nirvana to be a tr uth for a concealer.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
The same commentary explains that the thr ee truths [true sufferings, sources, and 
paths] are truths for a concealer and a nirv ana is an ultimate truth. Chandrakirti’s 
Commentary on the “Supplement to (N agarjuna’s) ‘Treatise on the Middle’”  also speaks of 
the other three truths as conventional and true cessations as ultimate truths. Also, 
as an answer to the objection that if a nirv ana is posited as existing conventionally, 
this would contradict the statement that it is  an ultimate truth, he says, “It is said 
to be an ultimate truth only by way of worldly conventions.” 
 The same commentary explains that  the three truths, true sufferings, true origins, and true 
paths are truths for a concealer or conventional truths, whereas nirvanas or true cessations are 
ultimate truths. To those who say that if a nirv ana is posited as existing conventionally this 
would contradict the statement that it is an ultima te truth, the answer is that it is an ultimate 
truth only by way of worldly conventions. 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
912  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, a source for positing all phenom ena by the force of conventions:) 
Therefore, all that is posited as existing  is posited through the force of worldly 
conventions. The One Hundred Thousand Stanza Perfection of Wisdom Sutra60 says, 
“All these phenomena are designated in dependence on worldly conventions.”  
 
All phenomena are posited by the force of worl dly conventions or terms. In other words, 
everything that is posited as existing is posi ted through the force of worldly conventions. The 
Perfection of Wisdom Sutra says that all phenomena are designated in dependence on 
worldly conventions, but they are not ultimate.   Wednesday morning, 7 March 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 347) says: 
 
Also, Nagarjuna’s Seventy Stanzas on Emptiness  says: 
Through the force of worldly expressions 
Buddha spoke of abiding, production, or disintegration, Existence or non-existence, low, equal, or Special—not through th e force of reality. 
 The Buddha taught abiding, production, or disi ntegration, existence or non-existence, and 
low, equal, or special by the force of worldly ex pressions, not by the force of what is real.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
He says that all the various presenta tions of the three— low, middling, and 
supreme—as well as, “This exists, that do es not exist” were only posited by the 
Conqueror through the force of worldly co nventions. He says that they are not 
posited through the force of a real status  which is not just posited through the 
force of conventions. 
 
All functioning things are first produced, th en abide, and then disintegrate. All those 
phenomena that are low, equal, and special we re all taught by the Buddha by the force of 
worldly expressions or conventions. They were not posited through the force of a real status 
which is not posited by the force of conventions.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Seventh, establishing the correctness of the fact that a truth for a reasoning consciousness and a truth able to bear 
analysis must be differentiated and, therefore, it is not nece ssary that whatever is true as an ultimate necessarily be 
truly established:) 
With respect to the master Jñanagarbha’s statement, “Because of being a truth for 
the ultimate [consciousness], it is an ul timate truth,” he describes a reasoning 
                                              
60 shatasahasrikaprajñaparamita . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
913 consciousness as the ultimate and thus says  that what is non-deceptive for it is a 
truth. His thought is not that [an ultimate truth] is truly established in the sense of 
being able to withstand analysis because in his text the true establishment of all 
phenomena is refuted.  
 A truth in the perspective of a reasoning co nsciousness – an exalted wisdom realizing 
emptiness – and a truth that is able to bear an alysis must be differentiated. Because of this, 
whatever is true as an ultimate or is an ultimate truth is not necessarily truly established. Master Jñanagarbha says that a truth for the ulti mate is an ultimate truth. Since there exists 
an ultimate with respect to both objects and ob ject-possessors, Jñanagarbha says that in this 
case the ultimate refers to a reasoning consciou sness, an object-possessor, and that therefore 
that which is non-deceptive in the perspective of this reasonin g consciousness is a truth. He 
does not intend that it an ultimate is truly established in the sense of being able to bear 
analysis. This refers to the fa ct that when phenomena are anal yzed by wisdom, they are found 
to not be able to bear analysis in that no ph enomenon is found to exis t truly or from its own 
side or without relying upon other. Thus, Jñanag arbha’s text refutes the true establishment of 
all phenomena.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Therefore, we assert the proposition that “i f an ultimate is not true ultimately [that 
is, for a reasoning consciousness called the ultimate], then a conventionality is not 
a truth conventionally [for a conventi onal valid cognition].” However, to 
propound that “if the ultimate is not esta blished ultimately, then a conventionality 
is not established conventionally,” is to say [absurdly] that if a negative of truth is 
not truly established, then the subjects which are the bases of the negation would 
be truly established. This is because an ul timate truth is posited as just a negative 
of truth in the subject whic h is the basis of negation and because the implication 
that subjects are not established conven tionally is that they are not falsely 
established. Thus, [to propound such] is extremely senseless because the bases of 
negation have to be established as false due to just the non-true establishment of 
the non-true existence of appearances—the subjects. [Only by showing that ultimate 
truths are not truly existent—that is, falsely established—can it be said that all bases 
of negation are falsely established.] 
 If an ultimate is not true ultimately, then a conventionality is not a truth conventionally. 
Ho w ev er, to  pro po und t hat  “If the fo rm er is  n o t established  as the former, the latter is not 
established  as the latter,” is to say that if the ne gation of the true existence is not truly 
established, then the basis of negation, the su bstrata, would be truly established. This is 
because ultimate truth is a mere negation of  true existence on a basis of negation, a 
substratum and because it is implied that if the substratum is not established conventionally it 
is not falsely established. This reason is comp letely incorrect because the bases of negation 
must be established as false by establishing no n-truly existent substrata or appearances as non-
truly established.   
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
914 Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 348) says: 
(Eighth, to posit [something] as existi ng conventionally it is necessary that there be no damage by any valid cognition:) 
Therefore, although to posit something as existing conventionally it is not 
necessary that it be established by a re asoning consciousness of suchness, it is 
necessary that there be no damage by any valid cognition, conventional or 
reasoning. Chandrakirti’s Commentary on (Nagarjuna’s) “Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning”  
explains that since the appropriated aggr egates conventionally abide as the four—
impermanent and so forth—the conception  of those four is non-erroneous in 
relation to that, and since the aggregates do not abide even conventionally as the 
four—permanent and so forth—the concep tion of those four is erroneous in 
relation to that.  
 
In order to posit something as existing conven tionally, it must not be harmed by any valid 
cognizer. Although in order to posit something as  existing conventionally it is not necessary 
that it be established by a reasoning consciou sness of suchness, it must not be harmed by 
either a conventional valid cognizer or a reason ing valid cognizer. Chandrakirti says that since 
the appropriated aggregates abide convention ally as impermanent, suffering, empty, and 
selfless, the conception of these four is not mistaken. For example, to apprehend the body 
aggregate as impermanent, suffering, empty of being permanent, unitary, and independent, 
and empty of being self-sufficient substantial exis tent is not erroneous. On the other hand, to 
apprehend the body aggregate as permanent, as  happiness, as not empty of being permanent, 
unitary, and independent, or as not empty of being self-sufficient substantially existent is 
erroneous.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Also, Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
The selfhood imputed by the Forders 
Affected by the sleep of ignorance 
And those [real horses, elephants, water, and so forth] which are imputed to 
illusions, mirages, and so forth 
Just do not exist from th e viewpoint of the world. 
Since he says that the self, principal, and so forth imputed by the Forders as well as 
the objects conceived as the horses, elephant s, and so forth of illusions do not exist 
even conventionally, the proposition that it is the Prasangika system that what 
merely exist for a mistaken mind are posite d as existing conventionally is pithless 
talk; no great Madhyamika asserts such 
 The Forders who are affected by the sleep of ignorance impute a self and so forth. For 
example, the Samkyas assert a primordial principa l that is the creator of all phenomena, and a 
self, knower, and being that is neither a cause or result but is a permanent phenomenon. The 
Vaisheshikas, on the other hand, assert that al l phenomena are created by Ishvara’s thought, 
such that just by his thinking of pizza, a pizza appears. Also the illusi ons, such as a horse, 
elephant, handsome boy, beautiful girl, fine hous e, and so forth, created by a magician on the 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
915 basis of a stick or stone are conceived to exis t, whereas in actuality there does not exist a 
horse, elephant, and so forth. Such objects of the mode of a pprehension do not exist even 
conventionally, although in the perspective of a mistaken consciousness they appear to exist. 
Thus, to say that the Prasangikas assert that that which exists for a mistaken mind exists 
conventionally is senseless talk in that  no great Madhyamika would assert this. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 In Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s ) “Treatise on the Middle”  object and 
subject are taken to be similarly existent or  non-existent; this is done in reference 
not to mere existence or non-existence in  general but to inherently existent and 
inherently non-existent object and subject. 
 
Chandrakirti says that object and object-posse ssor are similar in existing and not existing, 
however this does not refer to merely existent  and merely non-existent in general, but to 
inherently existent and non-inherently existe nt objects and object-possessors. For example, 
when traveling by car due to an error in the eye it appears that houses, mountains, and so 
forth are moving whereas in reality they ar e not moving. Although there is such an 
appearance to the consciousness, these moving ob jects do not in fact exist. Similarly, objects 
as well as object-possessors do not exist inherent ly. In this sense objects and object-possessors 
are the same in not existing inherently.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Ninth, though [phenomena] are merely posited through the forc e of conventions, this does  not eliminate their being 
established by valid cognition, and so forth:) 
Therefore, although what is posited as exis ting conventionally is posited as existing 
through the force of nominal conventions, all that is posited through the force of 
nominal conventions is not asserted as ex isting conventionally [such as the horns 
of a rabbit]. 
 
Although phenomena are merely posited by the fo rce of conventions, it is not refuted that 
they are established by valid cognizers. Alth ough phenomena that are posited as existing 
conventionally are posited as existing by the force of nominal conventions, not everything 
that is posited through the force of nominal co nventions is asserted to exist conventionally. 
For example, the horns of a rabbit are posited by the force of conven tions or names but do 
not exist conventionally.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Although [phenomena] are asserted as only posited through the force of 
conventions, the term “only” eliminates  anything that is not a subjective 
convention; it does not at all eliminate th at the object posited is established by 
valid cognition. For, in this system it is not that one is not to be satisfied with a 
positing through the force of the imputa tion of a convention and instead posit 
[something] as existing if it is found upon investigating how the object imputed—
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
916 which is not just posited through the forc e of imputation—exists in fact and posit 
[something] as not existing if not found. [T he Prasangikas] assert that if something 
were found upon searching with that mo de of searching, it would be truly 
established. Therefore, they do not assert  even conventionally that anything is 
found as existing upon such analysis.  
 
Although phenomena are asserted to be only po sited by the force of conventions, the term 
“only” eliminates those objects which are not co nventions of an object-possessor; it does not 
at all eliminate that the object is establis hed by a valid cognizer. In the Prasangika 
Madhyamaka system when it is analyzed whethe r something is found or not found, it is not 
found. In other words, this system does not asse rt that if something is found when sought it 
exists, and if it is not found when sought it does not exist. The Svatantrika Madhyamikas, on 
the other hand, say that when analyzed, phenom ena must be found as otherwise they would 
not exist. Therefore, according to them if a house is analyzed it is found to exist there, and if a 
door is analyzed it is found to exist there. If these phenomena were not found they would not 
exist. However, the Prasangika Madhyamaka point of view is that if phenomena are analyzed 
they are not found. This is because they posit th is from the point of vi ew of analyzing for an 
ultimate, whereas when the Svatantrika Madhyamikas  posit this they do so from the point of 
view of not analyzing for an ultimate but analyzing for a conventionality.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Since the boundary of analyzing and not an alyzing with respect to suchness is put 
there, they see that if [something] that was established by way of its own character 
existed, it would have to exist by way of its own entity as an object which was not 
just posited by the force of subjective co nventions, and thus they do not assert 
even conventionally inherent existence, ex istence by way of its [the object’s] own 
character, or existence by way of its own en tityness. I have explained this at length 
elsewhere. 
 
Because the Prasangika Madhyamikas posit th is as the boundary of analyzing and not 
analyzing suchness, they say that if someth ing that is established by way of its own 
characteristics existed, it would exist by way of its own entity without being posited by the 
force of conventions. Therefore, they do no asse rt inherent existence, existence by way of its 
own characteristics, and existence by way of its own entity even conventionally. This has 
already been explained in detail by Lama Tsongkhapa in other texts, for example, in his Great 
Stages of the Path to Enlightenment .  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 349) says: 
DISPELLING OBJECTIONS  
(This has two parts, the first of whic h is: the statement of an objection:) 
Objection:  If the ultimate truth is found by a buddha’s exalted wisdom knowing the 
mode [of being of phenomena], then how do es this not contradict Chandrakirti’s 
Commentary to the “Supplement to (N agarjuna’s) ‘Treatise on the Middle’” ? That text 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
917 says: 
Question: Is such a nature not un perceivable? Therefore, how could 
they perceive it? 
Answer:  That indeed is true, but it is sa id that they perceive it in the 
manner of non-perception. 
Thus, he explains that not to perceive an ything is to perceive suchness, and as 
source he quotes an explanation that the ultimate truth is beyond even the objects 
of omniscient wisdom. Furthermore, Chan drakirti explains that on the buddha 
ground the movement of mind and ment al factors has utterly stopped, and 
explains at the point of the ten powers  that when a buddha does not see the 
aggregates and so forth he knows all ph enomena. How are you not contradicting 
these? 
 Someone thinks that if ultimate truth is foun d by a buddha’s exalted wisdom knowing modes, 
it would contradict Chandrakirti’s text. This te xt says “Is such a nature not seen?” to which 
the reply is that it is seen in the manner of not seeing. In other words, when suchness or 
emptiness is seen, it is not seen  in the manner of existing truly or inherently. Because of this, 
the non-seeing of a buddha is the seeing of su chness. The non-seeing of a buddha is the non-
seeing of ultimate or true existence, which is the seeing of suchness, thusness, or emptiness. 
Because of this ultimate truth has gone beyond being the object of exalted wisdom. That minds and mental factors have ceased on th e buddha ground means that in a buddha the 
minds and mental factors that are conceptualizatio ns have ceased, in that only non-conceptual 
yogic direct perceivers exist in the continuum of a buddha.   This person asks: “If the buddh as do not see the aggregates, ho w can they be said to know 
all phenomena? These two are contradictory.” However, that a buddha does not see the 
aggregates means that the does not see truly exis tent aggregates. A buddha  does in fact see all 
phenomena. However, in the perspective of the exalted wisdom of meditative equipoise, there 
is no seeing of the aggregates because they do no t exist in its perspective; all that exists in its 
perspective is ultimate truth or emptiness.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
a. (The second, the answer, has fifteen parts, the firs t of which is: that because the passage means that an 
exalted wisdom knowing the mode does not appreh end conventionalities from the viewpoint of its 
perceiving the ultimate, ther e is no contradiction:) 
Answer:  “They perceive it in the manner of non-perception” does not refer to not 
seeing any and all objects. Rather, it indicates that if these which are observed 
through the power of the cata racts of ignorance existed in  reality, they would have 
to be observed by the exalted wisdom of  uncontaminated meditative equipoise of 
Superiors, and thus their perception of su c h  i s  b y  w a y  o f  n o t  p e r c e i v i n g  a n y  o f  
those. For, due to non-observation of the ob ject of negation despite the fact that if 
it did exist, it would be suitable to be observed, realization of the negative of the 
object of negation is posited. The meanin g of “Non-seeing is the excellent seeing” 
is to be understood similarly. 
 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
918 The answer to this objection has fifteen parts.  The exalted wisdom knowing modes does not 
observe conventionalities, it only sees ultimate s. Thus, conventionalities do not exist in the 
perspective of that exalted wisdom,. However, th ere is no contradiction in this. The statement 
“It is seen in the manner of not seeing” does  not mean that nothing whatsoever is seen. 
Rather it indicates that if phenomena observed  by the power of the cataracts of ignorance 
existed truly, they would have to be observed  by the uncontaminated exalted wisdom of the 
meditative equipoise of superiors. However, such phenomena are not seen, rather what is 
seen is suchness. In short, if phenomena were truly existent, they would have to be seen by 
the uncontaminated exalted wisdom of medita tive equipoise, wherea s they are not. The 
seeing of the negation of the object of negati on is the seeing of emptiness. One sutra says 
“Not seeing is holy seeing,” meaning that the non- seeing of true existenc e is correct seeing or 
the best seeing.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, a source for that:) 
The Condensed Perfection  of Wisdom Sutra61 says: 
The Tathagata teaches that on e who does not see forms, 
Does not see feelings, does not see discriminations, 
Does not see intentions, do es not see consciousness, 
Mind, or sentience, sees reality. 
 Analyze how space is seen as in the expression By sentient beings in words, “Space is seen.” The Tathagata teaches that seeing  reality is also like that. 
The seeing cannot be expressed by another example.
62 
The unseen is said to be the aggregates, and the seen, reality,63 which means 
suchness, as in the statement, “Whoever  sees dependent-arising sees reality.”64 
 This sutra says that the Tathagata says that on e who does not see forms, does not see feelings, 
does not see discriminations, do es not see intentions, does not see consciousness, mind, and 
mentality, sees the Dharma or reality. In other words, one who does not see the five 
aggregates, which are conventional  truths, sees the Dharma or reality, that is, emptiness or 
ultimate truth.   Upon seeing space, sentient beings express th at in words saying “Space is seen.” But how 
space is seen must be investigated. Like the s eeing of space, also th e Dharma, emptiness, is 
seen. Just as space is a negation or absence of obstructive contact, so too is emptiness a 
negation of the object of negation, true existenc e. Seeing this was said by the Tathagata to be 
the seeing of the Dharma. This seeing cannot be  expressed by other examples. The unseen is 
the five aggregates – forms, feelings, di scriminations, compositional factors, and
