Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 351) says: 
(Third, unraveling the meaning of the sutra:) 
It is like, for example, the fact that spac e is a mere eliminatio n of an obstructive 
object of touch, and that seeing or realiz ing it refers to not seeing the preventive 
obstruction that is the object of negati on and that would be suitable to be 
observed if it were present. In that [examp le], the seen is spac e, and the unseen is 
preventive obstructive. 
 
The example or analogy set out here is that of sp ace. In this example wh at is seen is space, 
whereas what is not seen is obscuring obstruction.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The last two lines refute a seeing of such ness while seeing blue [for instance], not 
seeing in accordance with the example [o f seeing space]. The statement that the 
five aggregates are not seen indicates that  the subjects [these phenomena] are not 
seen in the face of perception of suchne ss by uncontaminated meditative equipoise 
in its aspect of perceiving suchness. 
 
The last two lines refute a seeing of suchness wh ile seeing blue. That the five aggregates are 
not seen indicates that the substrata, the five aggregates, are not seen in the perspective of 
seeing suchness by uncontaminated exalted wisdom of meditative equipoise.  Wednesday afternoon, 7 March 2007  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 351) says: 
(Fourth, the Introduction to the  Two Truths Sutra65
 explains that dualis tic appearance has vanished in the face of 
perception of the ultimate by an exalted knower of all aspects; it does not stat e that the ultimate is not realized:) 
The Introduction to th e Two Truths Sutra  says: 
Devaputra, the ultimate truth is passed beyond [the objects of all 
consciousnesses] ranging right through the objects of omniscient 
wisdoms possessing the supreme of all as pects; it is not as is expressed 
in the phrase “ultimate truth”. 
It explains that [the ultimate truth] is not seen in accordance with the individual 
appearance to the mind of the two—subject and object—when one says “ultimate 
truth”. Therefore, it is a so urce for the disappearance of dualistic appearance, not a 
source for a buddha’s not realizing the ultimate. 
 
                                              
65 satydvayavatara . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
920 This sutra explains that dualistic appearance has vanished in the perspective of an exalted 
knower of all aspects seeing the ultimate, but it does not say that the ultimate is not realized. 
It says that the ultimate trut h has passed beyond all through th e objects of the exalted wisdom 
of an omniscient being, which possesses the supreme in all ways, and is not expressed stating 
“ultimate truth.” When one says “ultimate truth,” one states that  one does not see the object 
and object-possessor as though appearing to th e mind separately. In other words, in the 
perspective of the exalted wisdom of meditative equipoise of an omniscient being, there is no 
separate appearance of object-possessor and object . Therefore this is a scriptural source that 
sets out the vanishing of dualistic appearance and not one that says that a buddha does not 
realize the ultimate. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, a description of the mode of perception in that way by Chandrakirti:) 
Moreover, Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) ‘Treatise on 
the Middle’” says: 
Through making manifest only the nature without contacting [or being 
obstructed by] produced things, he understands suchness; hence, he is 
called “buddha”. 
Chandrakirti says that in terms of th e perception of suchness by a buddha’s 
exalted wisdom knowing the mode, only the nature of phenomena is cognized, without contacting othe r-powered phenomena. 
 Because he directly perceives the nature, su chness, without contacti ng things that are 
produced, he is called “buddha.” This mean s that functioning things or other-powered 
phenomena do not appear in the perspective of  a buddha’s exalted wisdom realizing suchness. 
Other-powered phenomena are those that are pr oduced by causes and conditions. “Without 
contacting produced things” means without s eeing the other-powered phenomena that range 
from form through the exalted knower of all aspe cts. This is because all that is seen by a 
buddha’s exalted wisdom knowing modes is suchne ss or thusness or emptiness. Thus, there is 
a difference between “seen” and “seen in the pe rspective of”; for example, although eye 
medicine can be seen when it is in the hand, when  it is applied to the eye it can no longer be 
seen.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, the statement in sutra that during meditative equipoise there is no movement of the mind refers to there not being 
any movement of conceptuality:) 
The disappearance of the movement of mi nds and mental factors means that when 
suchness is actualized, the movement of co nceptuality stops; it does not indicate 
that there are no minds and mental fa ctors [at that time]. Chandrakirti’s Clear 
Words  says: 
If conceptuality is the movement of the mind, due to being free from 
that, suchness is non-conceptual. Sutra says, “What is ultimate truth? If there is no movement of the mind there, what need is there to say 
anything about letters?!” 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
921 Thus he explains that the statement that there is no movement of the mind refers 
to the absence of the movement of  conceptuality. Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the 
“Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s ) ‘Treatise on the Middle’”  says that it does not stop forever 
during a learner superior’s meditative eq uipoise but at buddhahood it does stop 
forever. 
 
That there is no movement of the mind during meditative equipoise means that there is no 
movement of conceptuality at that time. Thus, it is not that there do not exist minds and 
mental factors in a buddha’s continuum, but ra ther that during meditative equipoise on 
emptiness there is no movement of conceptual minds and mental factors. Chandrakirti says 
that conceptuality is the movement of the mind, and that since a buddha is free from this, the 
seeing of suchness of a buddha in meditative equipoise is non-conceptual. He cites a sutra that says “What is ultimate truth?” and then gi ves the response that if  there is no movement 
of the mind, that is, no conceptual ity, what need is there to ment ion that at that time there is 
no expression of letters? Chandrakirti explains  that the statement sayi ng that there is no 
movement of the mind means that there is  no movement of conceptuality. While 
conceptuality is not utterly stopped at the ti me of the meditative equipoise of a learner 
superior, upon achieving buddhahood all conceptuality ceases forever.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Seventh, propounding that an ultimate truth is not  an object of knowledge and so forth are only wrong:) 
Furthermore, Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) 
‘Treatise on the Middle’” proves with a source that if the nature were not existent, 
the bodhisattva’s hard work in order to realize it would be senseless: 
Question : What is their nature, [that is, what is the nature realized by 
bodhisattvas]? 
Answer:  It is that which is unfabricat ed and which has no reliance on 
another, the entity realized by a mind free from the darkness of 
ignorance. 
Question: Does it exist or not? 
Answer:  If it did not exist, for what pu rpose would bodhisattvas cultivate 
the paths of the perfection? For, in  order to realize that nature of 
phenomena bodhisattvas underg o hundreds of hardships. 
 
To propound or state that “An ultimate truth is not an object of knowledge” is only incorrect. 
Chandrakirti says that if the na ture, that is, the ultimate nature , did not exist, the effort and 
hardships of bodhisattvas in order to realize it  would be meaningless. The scriptural source 
for this says that the nature, th at is, the ultimate truth, is unfabricated or uncontrived, does 
not rely on other, and is the entity realized by  a knower free from the cataracts of ignorance. 
Thus it has three characteristics or  features: (1) it is unfabricated, (2) it does not rely on other, 
and (3) it is realized by a knower free from th e cataracts of ignorance. In response to the 
question of whether or not ultimate truth exists, this text says that if the final or ultimate 
nature did not exist, the effort of bodhisattvas  to practice the path of perfections would be 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
922 meaningless. On the other hand, because it do es exist, bodhisattvas undergo hundreds of 
difficulties in order to realize reality or suchness. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
As a source he quotes a sutra: 
Child of good lineage, if the ultimate  did not exist, behavior for purity 
would be senseless, and the arising of tathagatas would be senseless. 
Because the ultimate exists, bodhisa ttvas are said to be skilled with 
respect to the ultimate. 
 As proof of this, Chandrakirti cites a sutra that says that if the ultimate did not exist, pure 
conduct – non-abiding nirvana – would be meanin gless and that the coming of a tathagata to 
the world would be meaningless. However, be cause the ultimate exists, bodhisattvas are 
skilled in the ultimate.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, he quotes a sutra proving that the ultimate truth exists because: 
1. if the ultimate truth did not exist, perf orming practice for the sake of the purity 
of the final nirvana would be senseless,  
2. since realization of the ultimate by tr ainees would not occur, a buddha’s coming 
to the world in order that trainees might realize the ultimate would be senseless, 
and  3 .the great conqueror children would not be skilled in ultimate truths. 
Therefore, that the system of this great ma ster propounds that the ultimate truth is 
not an object of knowledge and that there is no exalted wisdom realizing suchness 
in a superior’s meditative equipoise are only wrong propositions. 
 
Chandrakirti cites a sutra that prov es ultimate truth exists saying: 
1. if the ultimate truth did not exist, practicing  the conduct for the sake  of the purity of the 
final nirvana, that is, practicing the six perfections, would be meaningless, 
2. if the ultimate truth were not an object of  knowledge its realization would not exist and a 
buddha’s coming to the world in order for di sciples to realize the ultimate would be 
meaningless, and 3. the bodhisattvas would not be skilled in ultimate truths. Therefore, it is only wrong to say that this ma ster’s system propounds that the ultimate truth 
is not an object of knowledge and that there is no exalted wisdom realizing suchness in a 
superior’s meditative equipoise. In other words, ultimate truth exists. This proof refutes those 
Tibetan scholars of the past who propounded that ultimate truth is not an object of 
knowledge, that is, who said that it does no t exist and that only conventional truths are 
objects of knowledge.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Eighth, object and subject are not posited separately for an uncontam inated meditative equipoise:) 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
923 Furthermore, Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) 
‘Treatise on the Middle’”  says: 
Therefore, that “suchness is realized” is posited from imputation; 
actually there is no knowing of so mething by something because both 
knower and object known are non-produced. 
 Object and object-possessor are not posite d separately in the perspective of an 
uncontaminated meditative equipoise. Chandrak irti says that “suchness is realized” is 
presented through imputation. In reality, ther e is no knowing of something by something 
because both knower and object known are not produced.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
The meaning of the first part is that a posi ting of a realization of suchness with the 
two—the exalted wisdom and suchness—being taken as separate subject and object 
i s  a  p o s i t i n g  i n  t e r m s  o n l y  o f  a  c o n v e n tional consciousness, not for the exalted 
wisdom. That the knower is non-produced means that it has become like water put 
in water with respect to the meaning of  the absence of inherently existent 
production. 
 
The meaning of the first part of this statemen t is that to posit the realization of suchness 
through taking the object (suchness) and the ob ject-possessor (exalted wisdom) to be separate 
is to posit it merely in the perspective of a know er of conventionalities, and not posit it in the 
perspective of that exalted wisdom. That “a kn ower is not produced” means that with respect 
to the meaning of not being produced inherently it has become like water poured into water. 
In other words, the exalted wisdom of meditati ve equipoise realizing emptiness and its object, 
emptiness, are no longer seen to be separate  but are like water poured into water, which 
cannot be differentiated. This analogy is ma de because water poured into water becomes 
undifferentiable, whereas when milk is poured  into water there are certain animals who can 
drink the milk but leave the water. In short,  the object-possessor, the exalted wisdom of 
meditative equipoise realizing emptiness, and it s object, emptiness, have become like water 
poured into water.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Ninth, actualization of suchness in the manner of the stoppage of the movement of conceptuality:) 
Chandrakirti says, “Because minds and mental factors do not operate with respect 
to suchness—the object of exalted wisdom—it is actualized only by the body.” About 
that, the object to be actualized is suchne ss; the subject, the wisdom consciousness, 
is the means of actualization, and the co mplete enjoyment body is the agent of 
actualization, the knower. 
 
Suchness is actualized in the manner of st opping the movement of conceptuality. 
Chandrakirti says that because conceptual mi nds and mental factors do not operate with 
respect to the object of exalted wisdom of the me ditative equipoise of a buddha, it is directly 
perceived by the body, meaning that it is direct ly perceived by the enjoyment body. The object 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
924 to be actualized is suchness; the means of actual ization is the exalted wisd om that is an object-
possessor; and the agent of actualizat ion is the complete enjoyment body. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Its mode of actualizing suchness is in the manner of the stoppage of conceptual 
minds and mental factors as explained before. Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the 
“Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s ) ‘Treatise on the Middle’”  says, “The body through which 
suchness is actualized can be known thro ugh the explanation of it as having a 
nature of peace due to being free from minds and mental factors.” 
 
The mode of actualizing suchness is in the ma nner of stopping conceptual minds and mental 
factors. The body that actualizes suchness is th at body that has the nature of being pacified 
due to being free conceptual minds and mental factors.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 354) says: 
(Tenth, if a buddha did not perceive the aggregates and so fo rth, it would be a deprecation of the exalted knowledge of 
the varieties, and so forth:) 
If a buddha did not perceive the aggregates  and so forth, it would be a deprecation 
of the exalted knowledge of the varieties and of all the various objects because the 
existent and what is not known by  a buddha are mutually exclusive. 
 
If it were said that a buddha does not see th e aggregates and so fo rth, this would be a 
deprecation of the exalted knower of varieties and of all the objects that are the varieties 
because a buddha simultaneously sees all phenom ena, both conventional truths and ultimate 
truths. A buddha has an exalted knower of variet ies, that is, of conventional truths, and an 
exalted knower of modes, that is, of ultimate  truths. Although a buddha sees both truths 
simultaneously, in the perspective of his exalte d wisdom of meditative equipoise on suchness 
only suchness appears, conventionalities do not appear.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Eleventh, because [the various objects] are known by an exalted knower of all aspects through appearing to the exalted 
knower of the varieties, there ar e two types of appearing objects:) 
Consequently, the various objects must appear to an exalted knower of the 
varieties. Since knowledge without the aspe ct [of the object appearing] is not the 
Prasangika system, [objects are known] upon the appearance of their aspect. 
 
Because the objects that are the varieties are known by an exalted knower of all aspects 
through appearing to it, there must be two ty pes of appearing objects. Consequently, the 
objects that are the varieties must appear to an ex alted knower of varieties. It is the Prasangika 
system that there is knowing thro ugh the dawning of an aspect.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Also, with respect to the varieties whic h are the appearing objects, there are two 
[types]—(1) the major and minor marks of  a buddha and so forth which are not 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
925 polluted by the latencies of ignorance and (2) the impure environments and 
inhabitants, and so forth, which are pollu ted by the latencies of ignorance. There 
is no point in the non-appearance of th e first of those on the buddha ground, 
whereas the second have disappeared due to  the stoppage of their causes on that 
ground. 
 
With respect to the varieties which are th e appearing objects, there are two types: 
1. the signs and exemplifications of a buddha which are not polluted by the latencies of ignorance, and 2. the impure environments and impure inhabita nts or sentient beings which are polluted by 
the latencies of ignorance. It would be meaningless for the first to ceas e on the buddha ground, and the second cease 
due to the stopping of their causes on that ground.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Twelfth, the difference between the mode of appearance to an exalted knower of all aspects  cognizing the varieties and 
to other persons:) 
With respect to the mode of appearance , when the major and minor marks of a 
buddha appear to persons who have not abandoned ignorance, they appear to be 
established by way of their own character whereas they are not. This is not by 
reason of the object’s having arisen throug h the force of the latencies of ignorance; 
rather, it is an appearance due to the su bject’s being polluted by the latencies of 
ignorance. For, they do not appear to that subject [that is, a buddha’s 
consciousness] from the viewpoint of me rely appearing in that way to other 
persons but appear thus from their own side. 
 
There is a difference between th e mode of appearance to an exalted knower of all aspects 
realizing varieties and the mode of appearance to other persons. When the signs and exemplifications appear to pers ons who have not abandoned igno rance, they appear to exist 
inherently although they do not exist inherently . In other words, although from the object’s 
side there is no establishment by way of its own characteristics, it appear s to exist in that way 
by the power of ignorance to those who have not abandoned ignorance. This is not because 
the object itself has arisen thro ugh the force of the la tencies of ignorance, but rather it is an 
appearance due to that object-possessor of an ordinary person being polluted by the latencies 
o f  i g n o r a n c e .  T h i s  i s  b e c a u s e  i t  d o e s  n o t  a p p e a r  t o  t h a t  o b j e c t - p o s s e s s o r  f r o m  t h e  s i d e  o f  merely appearing like that to other persons but appears like that from its own side.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Objects such as forms and sounds—which appear to those who have not 
abandoned ignorance to be established by  way of their own nature whereas they 
are not—appear to a buddha’s exalted wi sdom knowing the varieties [but] they 
appear to a buddha only from the viewpo int of their appearing to persons who 
have the pollutions of ignorance. Withou t depending on their appearing that way 
to others, they do not appear from a buddha’s own side. Therefore, a buddha’s

926 knowing forms and so forth—which appear as established inherently whereas they 
are not—is from the viewpoint of their appearing that way to those who possess 
ignorance. Without depending on the appe arance of them to persons, a buddha 
from his own viewpoint does not know them in the manner of their appearing 
t h a t  w a y ;  h e n c e ,  t h e r e  i s  n o  s e n s e  o f  [ a  b u d d h a ’ s  c o n s c i o u s n e s s ]  b e c o m i n g  
mistaken through their appearing. This is  because, although they do not appear 
within the context of the exalted wisdom’s having pollution, they appear by way of 
the fact that the exalted wisdom mu st know all objects of knowledge. 
 Forms, sounds, odors, tastes, and tactile objects appear as though established by way of their 
own characteristics, although they are not, to a buddha’s exalted wisdom knowing varieties 
due to appearing in that way to persons who ar e polluted by ignorance. Because they appear 
to be established by way of their own characte ristics to those polluted by ignorance, they 
appear in that way to a buddha, but they do not appear in that way from the side of the 
buddha himself. In other words, because the form  aggregate appears to be truly existent to 
other persons, although it is not, a buddha sees it in this way. However, without depending 
on them appearing in that way to other person s, a buddha does not know them in that way. 
Therefore, a buddha does not have any mistaken appearances. Therefore, they do not appear 
through a buddha’s exalted wisdom being pollute d, but appear because his exalted wisdom 
necessarily knows all objects of knowledge.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 From the viewpoint of an ex alted knower of the varieties, all things appear in the 
aspect of selflessness and the absence of in herent existence, whereby they appear as 
falsities like illusions; they do not appe ar as truths. When they appear to that 
exalted wisdom by way of appearing to th ose who have ignorance, there is a mere 
appearance of the appearance [of those] as true to other persons. 
 
From the side of a buddha’s exalted knower of va rieties, all things appear in the entity of 
selflessness and non-inherent exis tence. Therefore, all things a ppear to a buddha as falsities 
like illusions and do not appear as truths. When forms and so forth appear to be truly 
existent to that exalted wisdom by way of appe aring to those polluted by ignorance, there is 
the mere appearance of that appearance of true  existence to other persons. In other words, 
because they appear to other beings as truly existent, a buddha sees that in their perspective they appear to be truly existent, but they do not appear to him to be truly existent.   Thursday morning, 8 March 20 07 (Happy Woman’s Day!) 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 355) says: 
(Thirteenth, the statement of such by Nagarjuna:) 
Nagarjuna’s Sixty Stanzas of Reasoning  says: 
Those skilled with respect to things 
See things as impermanent, deceptive phenomena, 
Composites, empty, 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
927 Selfless, and void. 
Chandrakirti’s commentary says that one who has completed the deeds [of 
practice—a buddha—] perceives in that way. 
 
Nagarjuna also taught like that. In this text he says that those skilled in functioning things see 
them as impermanent, as deceptive phenomena, as composites, as empty, as selfless, and as 
thoroughly devoid. They see all compounded phenomena to be impermanent in that they 
change momentarily; to be deceptive in that they  do not exist in the w ay that they appear; to 
be composites in that they are a gathering toge ther; to be empty, the third attribute of true 
sufferings, in that they are empty of self-suffi cient substantial existence; to be selfless, the 
fourth attribute of true sufferings, in that th ey are empty of inherent existence; and to be 
thoroughly devoid of true existence. Those wh o have culminated the deeds successfully, that 
is, the buddhas, see all phenomena in this w ay, whereas we ordinary beings do not see 
phenomena as changing momentarily and so forth.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourteenth, although the two truths are one entity, it is not c ontradictory to posit individual exalted knowers in relation to  
the two truths—the object:) 
Jñanagarbha’s Differentiation of the Two Truths  says: 
An omniscient knower directly perceives 
All the dependently produced, 
Just these [phenomena] as they appear 
Devoid of the superimposed entity. 
Thus he explains that it vividly perceives directly all the varieties.  
 Although the two truths are one entity, it is not contradictory to posit two separate exalted 
knowers in relation to them. Jñanagarbha says  t h a t  a n  o m n i s c i e n t  b e i n g  d i r e c t l y  s e e s  
phenomena that appear as though inherently existent while devoid of an imputed entity 
(imputation by thought), that is, all the ph enomena that are dependently produced. Thus, 
omniscient beings directly see or realize all the varieties.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Furthermore: 
Because that which does not see knowers, 
Objects known, and selfhood has a stable abiding Due to the non-arising of signs, They do not rise [from meditative stabilization]. 
 The buddhas do not arise from meditative equip oise into subsequent attainment, but from 
within a state of meditative equipoise are able to perform actions. They can do this because 
their meditative equipoise and subsequent a ttainment are not contra dictory, whereas for 
others they are contradictory. They do not se e knowers, the objects known, and the self. For 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
928 them signs do not arise. Because they abide firmly, they do not rise from meditative 
equipoise.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus he also explains that [buddhas] never rise from the meditative stabilization in 
which dualistic appearance has been pa cified. Although to those who do not 
properly understand the modes of thos e two explanations it seems to be 
contradictory to assert both—rather than just  one of the two—it is not contradictory. 
This is because, although the exalted wi sdom perceiving suchness and the exalted 
wisdom perceiving the varieties are on e entity, there is not the slightest 
contradiction that in relation to the in dividual objects there are two—a reasoning 
consciousness and a conventional consciousness. 
 
The buddhas never arise from the meditative st abilization in which dualistic appearance has 
been pacified. To those who do not understand these two explanations clearly it appears that 
they are contradictory. However, that a buddha has both an exalted knower knowing varieties 
and an exalted knower knowing modes, and that they are one entity but posited as individual 
or separate in dependence on their respective objects, is not at all contradictory. While it is difficult for us to understand that a wisdom realizing emptiness can also understand other 
phenomena, in the case of a buddha there is no contradiction in that they are able to 
simultaneously see both varieties and modes.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifteenth, when the two valid cognitions comprehend objects individually on the occasion of the view of the bases and 
on the occasion of the fruit, it is necessary to know  which of the two valid cognitions something is:) 
This depends on knowing well that there is  not the slightest contradiction in there 
being two different modes of finding [o bjects] by the two valid cognitions—
reasoning and conventional—in terms of on e subject at the time of the view of 
bases, [that is, viewing phenomena as empty of inherent existence]. If you know 
well not only that fact about the time of  the fruit when the two exalted wisdoms 
comprehend objects but also which of th e two valid cognitions  it becomes with 
regard to the objects, you can know that the two subjects [con sciousnesses] do not 
become a common locus [that is, one thing that is both] even though the objects 
are not ascertained separately. Through th is you can also understand the fine 
points of the definitions of the two truths. 
 
Since on the occasion of the view of the bases and on the occasion of the result the two valid 
cognizers comprehend their objects individually , it is necessary to know which of the two 
valid cognizers it is. There is no contradiction th at at the time of the vi ew of the bases, with 
respect to one single phenomena there exist two different ways of finding suchness or 
emptiness, that is, ultimate truth, and finding the conventional truth by the two types of valid 
cognizers. At the time of the result the two exalted wisdoms of the omniscient mind 
respectively see thusness and varieties. Because of  this, it is necessary to know which of the 
two valid cognizers it becomes with respect to its respectively object, as well as to know that 
the two object-possessors do not become a commo n locus. Thereby one will be able to also 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
929 understand the details of the definitions of th e two truths. For example, since the omniscient 
mind knowing suchness also realizes all varietie s, and the omniscient mind knowing varieties 
also realizes suchness, it can be asked wh ether the omniscient mind realizing suchness 
becomes a valid cognizer of varieties, and wh ether the omniscient mind realizing varieties 
becomes a valid cognizers of suchness. This disc ussion occurs in the teachings on Tenets and 
in the Supplement to the Middle Way . 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 356) says: 
Divisions of ultimate truths 
(This section has eleven parts, the fi rst of which is: the different mode s of dividing ultimate truths:) 
When ultimate truths are divided, it is as Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the 
“Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s ) ‘Treatise on the Middle’”  says—an extensive division into 
the sixteen emptiness, a middling division  into the four emptinesses of things, 
non-things, self entity, and other enti ty, and a brief division into the two 
selflessnesses of persons and phenomena. 
 
There are different modes of dividing ultimate truths. Chandrakirti says that when emptiness 
is extensively divided, there are sixteen emptin esses. These are also set out in Haribhadra’s 
Clarifying the Meaning  and include the emptiness of the external, emptiness of the inner, 
emptiness of both, emptiness of the great, emptin ess of emptiness, and so forth. For example, 
the emptiness of the external is the emptiness of inherent existence of external phenomena – 
forms, sounds, odors, tastes, tactile objects, an d phenomena. The emptine ss of the internal is 
the emptiness of the internal sense powers – th e eye sense power, ear sense power, nose sense 
power, tongue sense power, body sense power,  and mental sense power. The emptiness of 
both external and internal is the emptiness of th e loci of the eye, ear, nose, tongue, and body 
sense powers. They are so-called because th ey are external with respect to inner 
consciousnesses, and internal in the sense of be ing held by feelings. Emptiness of the great is 
the emptiness of the ten directio ns – four cardinal directions, four intermediate directions, 
the zenith, and nadir. The emptiness of empt iness refers to the emptiness of inherent 
existence of emptiness itself.   There is also a middling division of emptin ess into the four: emptiness of functioning 
things, emptiness of non-functioning things, empt iness of self entity, and emptiness of other 
entity. There is also a brief division of emptin ess into the two: the se lflessness of persons and 
the selflessness of phenomena. The emptiness of  inherent existence of the person is the 
selflessness of persons, whereas the emptiness of  inherent existence of the aggregates is the 
selflessness of phenomena.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Other texts speak of two—actual ultimates and concordant ultimates. Kamalashila’s Illumination of the Middle  says: 
Because this absence of production accords with the ultimate, it is 
called an “ultimate” but it is not so in fact because actually the ultimate 
is beyond all elaborations. 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
930  
Other texts mention two types of ultimates: actual ultimates ( don dam pa dngos ) and 
concordant ultimates or ulti mates that are similitudes ( mthun pa’i don dam ). Kamalashila says 
that because the lack of production is concorda nt with the ultimate, it is called “ultimate.” 
However, in reality the ultimate has passed beyond all elaborations, 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, Shantarakshita’s Ornament for the Middle66 says: 
Because of according with the ultimate, 
This is called an “ultimate.” 
Actually [the ultimate] is free from all 
The collections of elaborations. 
 Because of according with the ultimate, it is called “ultimate.” Actually the ultimate is free 
from all collections of elaborations, that is, it  is not a collection of elaborations. In other 
words, an actual ultimate is not a collection of elaborations.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, the same is said in Jñanagarbha’s Differentiation of the Two Truths,  and 
Jñanagarbha’s own commentary on that text as well as Maitreya’s Ornament  speak 
of the negative of ultimately [existent] production and so forth as conventional.
67 
 
These texts say that the negation of ultimate pr oduction is a conventionality. However, since 
this is not asserted from the point of view of  the Prasangika Madhyamikas, perhaps it accords 
with other tenets such as those of the Svatantrika Madhyamikas.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 357) says: 
(Second, earlier Tibetans’ explanation of the entit y of the ultimate described in those texts:) 
With respect to the meaning of those, many earlier [Tibetans] took the two 
ultimate truths—metaphoric and non-metaphoric—and [identified] the emptiness 
which is a negative of the ultimately exis tent production and so forth of forms and 
so forth as the metaphoric ultimate, [saying that] it is an imputed ultimate truth 
and a fully qualified truth for a concealer.  They asserted that  the non-metaphoric 
ultimate truth cannot be taken as an object  of any mind and thus is not an object 
of knowledge. 
 Some Tibetan scholars of the past explained the en tity of the ultimate as  taught in those texts 
through taking into consideration two ultimate  truths – the metaphoric and non-metaphoric 
(or the categorized and uncategorized). They t ook the emptiness that is the negation of 
ultimate production and so forth to be the first type, a metaphoric ultimate, and said that it is 
                                             
 
66 madhyamakalamkara . 
67  This is misinterpreted as meaning that it is a co nventionality, whereas the me aning is that it exists 
conventionally.

931 an imputed ultimate truth and not an actual ultimate truth but instead is a fully-qualified 
conventional truth. They said that the second , a non-metaphoric ultimate truth, cannot be 
realized by any mind and therefore is not an ob j e c t  o f  k n o w l e d g e .  I n  t h i s  w a y ,  s o m e  p a s t  
Tibetan scholars propounded that the emptiness th at is an ultimate truth is not an object of 
knowledge given that only conventional truths ar e objects of knowledge. Because of this, they 
claimed that the ultimate truth cannot be realized by anyone.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, the two ultimates explained in thos e texts do not refer just to objects:) 
Since that is not the meaning of those [tex ts], they are to be explained this way: 
T h o u g h  i n d e e d  j u s t  t h e  o b j e c t — t h e  n a t u r e  o f  p h e n o m e n a — i s  t o  b e  t a k e n  a s  t h e  
ultimate, there are many explanations of  the subject—the reasoning consciousness—
as an ultimate. Jñanagarbha’s Differentiation of the Two Truths  says, “Because of 
being undeceiving, a reasoning [consciousness] is an ultimate.” 
 
The two ultimates explained in those texts do no t refer only to objects, but also refer to 
object-possessors. Although the object, reality or emptiness or suchness, is to be taken as the 
ultimate, also the object-possessors, reasoning co nsciousnesses, are explained in many texts to 
be ultimates. For example, Jñanagarbha’s text s ays that because it is undeceiving, a reasoning 
consciousness is ultimate.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Kamalashila’s Illumination of the Middle  says: 
The statements that there is no ulti mately existent production and so 
forth are asserted to mean the fo llowing: All consciousnesses arising 
from correct hearing, thinking, and meditating are non-erroneous 
subjects; hence, they are called “ultimates” because of being the 
ultimate among them, [that is, among consciousnesses]. 
 
Kamalashila says that the meaning of sayi ng that phenomena do not have ultimate 
production is as follows. The wisdom arisen fr om hearing, the wisdom arisen from thinking, 
and the wisdom arisen from meditation are non- erroneous object-possessors. Due to this, they 
are called “ultimates” because among all cons ciousnesses these three consciousnesses of 
hearing, thinking, and meditating are ultimate.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
There are two types of reasoning consciou snesses—(1) a superior’s non-conceptual 
exalted wisdom of non-conceptual medi tative equipoise and (2) a conceptual 
reasoning consciousness comprehending suchness in dependence on a sign, and so 
forth The thought of Bhavaviveka’s Blaze of Reasoning
68 in explaining two—a non-
conceptual exalted wisdom with respect to  the ultimate and a wisdom concordant 
                                              
68 tarkajvala . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
932 with that—and the thou ght of Kamalashila’s Illumination of the Middle  in explaining 
two ultimates are the same. Therefore, it is  not the meaning of those texts that the 
explanation of two ultimates should be take n as only ultimates in terms of objects 
and not subjects. 
 
There are two types of reasoning consciousnesses: 1. a superior’s non-conceptual exalted wisdom of meditative equipoise, and 2. an ordinary being’s conceptual reason ing consciousness comprehending suchness in 
dependence on a sign. 
When Bhavaviveka explains a non-conceptual ex alted wisdom with respect to the ultimate 
and a wisdom concordant with that and when Kamalashila explains two ultimates, their 
intention is the same. Therefore, the explanation of two ultimates in thes e texts should not be 
taken to refer only to objects, but should also be  taken to refer to object-possessors or subjects. 
In other words, there are two ultimates from th e side of objects and two ultimates from the 
side of object-possessors.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 358) says: 
(Fourth, the two reasoning consciousnesses are posit ed separately as actual and concordant ultimates:) 
Concerning that, when the non-conceptual  exalted wisdom understands suchness, 
it is able to eliminate with respect to it s object simultaneously the elaborations of 
[the conception of] true [existence] and dual istic appearance; hence, it is an actual 
ultimate; that is the meaning of being “beyond all elaborations.” Although a 
conceptual reasoning consciousness is ab le to cease the elaborations [of the 
conception] of true [existence] with resp ect to its own object, it cannot eliminate 
the elaboration of dualistic appearance; henc e, it is an ultimate that accords in 
aspect with the supramundane ultimate. 
 The two reasoning consciousnesses, those that kn ow suchness, are posited separately as actual 
ultimates and concordant ultimates. When th e first, a non-conceptual exalted wisdom, 
engages suchness, it is able to simultaneously  eliminate or cut the elaborations of true 
existence and dualistic appearance with respec t to its object and th erefore is an actual 
ultimate. This is the meaning of stating that it “has passed beyond all elaborations.” The 
second, a conceptual reasoning consciousness, is able to cease the elaborations of true 
existence with respect to its object but cannot cut the elaborations of dualistic appearance, 
and therefore is a concordant ultimate that  accords in aspect with the supramundane 
ultimate, nirvana. Here the distinction of actu al and concordant ultimates is made from the 
side of object-possessors.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, it is necessary to explain two modes also with respect to the ultimate  as object in relation to these minds:) 
It is necessary to set forth two modes also with respect to the ultimate in the sense 
of the object which is the negative of th e ultimately [existent] production and so 
forth of forms and so forth. That emptiness of the object is the actual ultimate free 
from both elaborations for a non-concep tual reasoning consciousness and is not 
the actual ultimate free from both el aborations for a conceptual reasoning 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
933 consciousness since [in the latter case] it is free from only one class of elaborations. 
However, this is not to say that in gene ral it is not an actual ultimate truth. 
Therefore, it is not the meaning of those texts that except for being free from all 
elaborations of dualistic appearance for some minds, it does not happen that an 
emptiness of true existence is free from  all elaborations of appearance, whereby 
whatever is an ultimate truth is necessa rily free from all the elaborations of 
dualistic appearance. 
 It is also necessary to explain two modes with re spect to ultimates as objects in the perspective 
of these minds. In other words, it is also necessary to explain two modes with respect to 
ultimates that are objects – the negations of ulti mate production and so forth of forms and so 
forth. The emptiness of the object is the actual  ultimate which is free from both elaborations 
in the perspective of a non-conceptual reasoning consciousness. However, it is not the actual 
ultimate which is free from both elaborations  in the perspective of a conceptual reasoning 
consciousness because in this case it is free from  only one type of elaboration. But this does 
not say that in general it is not an actual ultima te truth. Thus, it is not that an emptiness of 
true existence is free from all elaborations of appearance. For example, in the perspective of a 
conceptual consciousness realizing the emptine ss of inherent existence, other phenomena can 
also appear, for example, the meaning generality  can also appear because it is a conceptual 
mind. This is because if it is a conceptual mind, the actual object and the meaning generality appear as though mixed. Therefore, whatever is  an ultimate truth is not necessarily free from 
all the elaborations of dualistic appearance.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, the ultimate asserted by [those propounding] an estab lishment of illusion by a reasoning [consciousness] is not 
an actual ultimate:) 
[The proponents of] an establishment of illusion by a reasoning [consciousness] 
assert that the composite of the two, a base such as an aggregate and the 
appearance of its emptiness of true ex istence—[this composite] being the mere 
object established by an inferential reasoning consciousness—is an ultimate truth. 
It is a concordant ultimate, not an ultimate truth. 
 An inferential cognizer realizing emptiness aris es due to reasonings. This is not an actual 
ultimate, but is a concordant ultimate or an ulti mate that is a similitude. This is because the 
collection or composite of the aggregates an d the appearance of their emptiness of true 
existence is the mere object established by an inferential reasoning consciousness. In short, a 
conceptual mind understands emptiness by means of reasonings.   There is a story about a person who had clai rvoyance and another person who had studied 
much philosophy. One time when they were talking together, th e clairvoyant said that there 
was a farmer on the other side of the mounta in who had a pregnant cow, whose calf has a 
white patch on its forehead. The scholar said that  this was not the case and that it was the tip 
of the calf’s tail that was white and that it wa s resting on its forehead. After the calf was born, 
they sent a trustworthy monk to  check what had happened. It was found that the tip of the 
calf’s tail was white, and not its forehead!  
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
934 Thursday afternoon, 8 March 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 358) says: 
(Seventh, it is not suitable to prove the appearance of the non-true existence of sprouts and so forth through the 
appearance of the lack of [its being] one or many:) 
With respect to [their] “proving” the a ppearance of the non-true existence of 
sprouts and so forth through the appearance  of the lack of being a true one or 
many, there is no point in proving such  t o  a n  i n t e l l i g e n t  p e r s o n  w h o  h a s  n o t  
eliminated doubt with respect to whether or not the base truly exis ts [in that it will 
not help in realizing the absence of in herent existence]. Also, for one who has 
eliminated such doubt, the si gn is not a correct sign [sin ce he would have no doubt 
that objects appear as non-inherently ex istent and thus would have no need to 
realize the thesis].  
 
It is not suitable to prove th e appearance of non-true existence of sprouts and so forth 
through the appearance of its being free from be ing one or many. That is, it is senseless to 
prove this to someone who has no t eliminated doubt with respect to whether the base exists 
truly or not. Also for one who has eliminated su ch a doubt, that sign is not a correct sign. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Also Kamalashila’s Illumination of the Middle  says that both the sign of lacking being 
one or many and the predicate [of non-t rue existence] are mere eliminations. 
Furthermore, he says that whatever is ta ken as the sign—“is not one or many,” or 
“does not exist as one or many”—it is the same. That he is not referring to 
affirming negatives can be known from the examples given there. Hence, it is not 
at all the assertion of the masters, the fa ther Shantarakshita and his spiritual son 
[Kamalashila], or the master Haribhadra [t hat the appearance of non-true existence 
of sprouts and so forth is proven throug h the appearance of the lack of being a 
true one or many]. 
 
Kamalashila’s text says that both the sign or reason of “being free from being one or many” 
and the predicate are mere eliminations ( rnam bcad tsam ). Whether the sign is “is not one or 
many” or “does not exist as one or many” it is  similar. Neither the reason or the predicate 
refer to an affirming negative. There are two ty pes of negatives, a non-affirming negative and 
an affirming negative. An example of this reasonin g is: a sprout is not truly existent because it 
is free from being a truly existent one or many . It is free from bein g a truly existent one 
because it has parts. That wi th parts and that without part s are directly contradictory. 
Without parts does not exist. The sprout is not a truly existent many because it is not a truly 
existent one. Many – two, thr ee, and so forth – depends on one. Both the reason and the 
predicate are non-affirming negatives.  
 Therefore, that the appearance  of the non-true existence of sprouts and so forth is proved 
through the appearance of the lack of being a tr uly existent one or many is not the assertion 
of either Shantarakshita and Kamalash ila, nor is it that of Haribhadra. 
 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
935 Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 359) says: 
(Eighth, the assertion of the ultimate by the thoroughly non-abiding Madhyamikas is not as it is explained by earlier 
[scholars]:) 
Also, there is no great Madhyamika who asserts that the mere object 
comprehended by an inferential consciousness—the latter of the two, a [mere] 
elimination and an affirmative elimination,  with respect to an elimination of the 
elaborations which are the object of negation in relation to appearances—is an 
ultimate truth.69 Through this mode you can unders tand in detail my explanation 
of their presentation in the Great Exposition of the Stages of the Path.  
 
What great Madhyamika would asse rt as an ultimate truth the mere object comprehended by 
an inferential consciousness which is an a ffirmative elimination with respect to an 
elimination of the elaborations which are the object of negation  in relation to appearances? 
No one would. This can be understood from st udying the presentation in Lama Tsongkhapa’s 
Great Exposition of th e Stages of the Path . 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Ninth, although a negative of ultimately [e xistent] production and so forth is not a co nventionality, it is not contradictory 
to indicate that it exists conventionally:) 
In connection with the explanation of the ne gative of production and so forth as a 
concordant ultimate, Jñanagarbha’s Commentary on the “Differentiation of the Two 
Truths”70 says: 
Others [Chittamatrins] hold [that em ptiness is] only real; thus, “also” 
[in the root text] has the meaning of a conjunction. [However] when 
analyzed with reasoning it is only conventional. Why? Since the object 
of negation [a self of phenomena] do es not exist, it is clear that the 
negative does not exist in reality. 
 
Although the negation of ultimate production an d so forth is not a conv entionality, it exists 
conventionally. These two are not contradictory. For example, emptiness exists conventionally 
and exists nominally, that is, it can be expresse d by words, but it is not a conventional truth. 
Likewise, the negation of ultimate production also exists conventionally. The negation of 
production and so forth is a concordant ulti mate or an ultimate that is a similitude. 
Jñanagarbha’s text says that others hold or a pprehend emptiness as only real. Saying “also” 
means to include or condense two or more thin gs. When analyzed with reasoning, emptiness 
is conventional, that is, it exists conventiona lly. Why? Since the object of negation does not 
exist, also its negation or refutati on does not exist in actuality.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
                                              
69 In lhag mchan 170.5: 
The elimination of the elaborations of  the object of negation has two fact ors, the negative exclusion and the 
positive inclusion from among these two, the latter is the mere object comprehended by an inferential cognition, 
and there is no great Madhyamika who a sserts that this was ultimate truth. 
70 satyadvayavibhangavrtti . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
936 Thus, he says that others—Chittamatrins— assert that an emptiness which is a 
negative of the self of phenomena in a ba se of negation is established in [as its 
own] reality, whereas his own system [asse rts] that since the self of phenomena 
which is the object of negation does not ex ist, the negation which is the negative of 
that is not established in reality. Theref ore, the explanation that a negative of 
ultimately existent production and so fort h is conventional means that it exists 
conventionally; it does not indicate  that such is a conventionality. 
 
From the point of view of the Chittamatrins the emptiness that is a negation of a self of 
phenomena on a basis of negation is established as real, that is, emptiness exists truly. The 
Chittamatrins assert three char acteristics: imputational natu res, other-powered phenomena, 
and thoroughly established phenomena. While imputational natures are not truly existent, 
other-powered phenomena and thoroughly es tablished phenomena are both truly or 
inherently established. In this context the obje ct of negation of a self of phenomena is the 
form and the eye consciousness apprehending fo rm being established as different substances. 
This is because they assert form and the ey e consciousness apprehending form to be one 
entity. However, this is not th e object of negation of a self of phenomena according to the 
Prasangika Madhyamikas who asse rt that form and the eye consciousness apprehending form 
are different substances and assert externally exis tent objects. The Chitta matrins, on the other 
hand, assert that form and the eye consciousness apprehending form are not  different 
substances and do not accept ex ternally existent objects.  
 Jñanagarbha’s own system asserts that since th e self of phenomena which is the object of 
negation does not exist, the nega tion which is the negative of th at is not established as real. 
Therefore, the explanation that a negation of ultimately existent production and so forth is 
conventional means that it exists convention ally; it does not indi cate that it is a 
conventionality or a conventional truth. In ot her words, emptiness exists conventionally or 
nominally (in that, it can be talked about and ca n be mentally conceived of), but it is not a 
conventional truth.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Tenth, it is said that when blue appears, its emptiness of  true existence appears to an ey e consciousness; since this 
refers to an affirming negative, it is not contradictory t hat its emptiness of true existence is an ultimate truth:) 
Also, Jñanagarbha’s Commentary on the “Differe ntiation of the Two Truths” says: 
[Objection: ] Because when a thing appears, its really existent production 
and so forth do not appear, such ar e unreal conventionalities. In the 
same way, a negative of re ally existent production and so forth also is an 
unreal conventionality, for when the thing that is the basis of negation 
appears, it does not appear. 
Answer:  It is not that it does not appear because it is non-different from 
the entity of the thing. 
 
To say that when blue appears to  an eye consciousness, the emptiness of true existence of blue 
that appears to an eye consciousness refers to an affirming negative ( ma yin dgag ). Therefore, it 
is not contradictory that its emptiness of true existence is an ultimate truth. An objection is set out in Jñanagarbha’s text that says that be cause real production, real abiding, and real 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
937 disintegration do not appear when a thing appear s, they are unreal conventionalities, like, for 
example, the reflection of a face in a mirror, an echo, a mirage, and so forth. Similarly, the 
negations of real production and so forth are al so unreal conventionalities because when that 
thing that is the basis of negation  appears, they do not appear. The response to this is that it 
is not that they do not appear because they are not different from  the entity of the thing. In 
other words, the negations of truly existent production, truly existent abiding, and truly 
existent disintegration are not differen t entities from the basis of negation. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
With respect to his explanation that when blue, for instance, appears, its 
emptiness of true existence appears, it is  not that the mere el imination which is a 
negative of true existence appears to an eye consciousness and so forth; rather, he 
is referring to an affirming negative. Thus, even though such is a fully qualified 
conventionality, it is not contradictor y that an emptiness which is a mere 
elimination of truth is an ultimate truth. 
 Jñanagarbha’s says that when blue appears, it s emptiness of true existence also appears. 
However, it is not that the mere elimination that  is a negation of true existence appears to an 
eye consciousness, rather he refers to an affirm ing negative. Therefore, although this is a fully 
qualified conventionality, it is not contradictor y that the emptiness that  is a mere elimination 
of true existence is an ultimate truth. For exam ple, the emptiness of inherent existence of this 
table is an ultimate truth, but it is  a fully qualified conventionality.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Eleventh, the positing of the two reasoning consciousnesses—non-conceptual and conceptual—as well their objects as 
actual ultimates and concordant ultimates is as before:) 
Shantarakshita’s Ornament for the Middle  explains that, although a negative of 
ultimately existent production and so forth is included among real 
conventionalities, it is a concordant ulti mate since it accords with the ultimate. 
The ultimate is said to have abandoned all the nets of elaboratio ns such as existent 
thing, non-existent thing, and so fort h; the nets of elaborations are as 
Jñanagarbha’s Commentary on the “Differentiation of the Two Truths”  says: 
Therefore, the Blessed One said 
It is not empty, not non-empty, 
Not existent and non- existent, Not non-produced and not produced. 
 The individual positing of conceptual an d non-conceptual reasoning consciousnesses 
(consciousnesses to which emptiness appears) as well as their objects as actual ultimates and 
concordant ultimates is as before. A non-concep tual reasoning consciousness is a yogic direct 
perceiver realizing emptiness, whereas a conceptu al reasoning consciousness is an inferential 
cognizer realizing emptiness. A non-conceptual reasoning consciousness is an actual ultimate, 
while a conceptual reasoning consciousness is a concordant ultimate.

938  Although a negation of ultimate production and so forth is included among real 
conventionalities, it is a concordant ultimate an d not an actual ultimate, since it accords with 
the ultimate. The ultimate has abandoned all the ne ts of elaborations such as existent thing, 
non-existent thing, and so forth. The nets of elaborations are explained in Jñanagarbha’s text 
saying “It is not empty and it is not non-empty. It  is not existent and it is not non-existent. It 
is not non-produced and it is not produced.”   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also:  
Why? That is without elaborations; suchness is free from all nets of 
conceptuality. 
Thus, he describes the nets of concep tuality as the nets of elaborations. 
Furthermore, because those have vanished in the face of direct realization of 
suchness, it is the actual ultimate, and th e reasoning consciousness as well as its 
object, which are not like that, are concor dant with the former, and so forth, as 
[explained] before. 
 Suchness is without elaborations, that is, it is free from all nets of conceptuality. Thus, the 
nets of conceptuality are the nets of elabor ations. Because those conceptualizations or 
elaborations have vanished in the perspective of a direct realization of suchness, such a direct 
realization of suchness is an actual ultimate . On the other hand, the reasoning consciousness 
which is not a direct perceiver realizing emptin ess but is an inferential cognizer realizing 
emptiness, as well as its obje ct, are concordant ultimates.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Also, with respect to the negative of rea lly existent production and so forth, there 
are two—the reasoning consciousness by which it is negated and its object of 
comprehension. Thus, the mode of inclusio n as a real conventionality should be 
known in terms of those. This way of ex plaining freedom from the nets of all 
whatsoever elaborations with respect to  the two truths is important on many 
occasions. 
 With respect to the negation of really exis tent production, there is (1) the reasoning 
consciousness by which it is negated and (2) its object of comprehension. The mode of 
inclusion as real conventionality should be understood in relation to them. It is important to understand this way of explaining freedom from  the nets of elaborations or conceptuality 
with respect to the two truths.   
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 361) says: 
Definiteness of the number of the truths as two 
(This section has three parts, the firs t of which is: the actual exposition:) 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
939 If a base is positively set off71 as being a false deceptive object, it is necessarily 
eliminated as being a non-deceptive obje ct. Therefore, deceptive object and non-
deceptive object are mutually exclusive co ntradictories. Because they apply to all 
objects of knowledge in the manner of mu tual abandonment, a third category is 
also excluded. Therefore, you should know that with respect to objects of 
knowledge the enumeration is definite as the two truths. 
 I f  a  b a s e  i s  e s t a b l i s h e d  a s  o r  d e t e r m i n e d  t o  be a false deceptive object, it is necessarily 
eliminated as being a non-deceptive object. Therefore, deceptive and non-deceptive are 
mutually exclusive, meaning that there is no  third possibility. Thus when, on one hand, 
something is established and on the other si de something is eliminated, these two are 
mutually contradictory without any third possibi lity existing. For example, a phenomena must 
be either a conventional truth or an ultimate tr uth, it cannot be anything else. However, there 
do exist three possibilities, for example, in the case of functioning things which are definitely 
either one of the three: material form, cons ciousnesses, and non-associated compositional 
factors (which are neither material form nor cons ciousness). In this case there is no fourth 
possibility. In short, objects of knowledge ar e definitely two in number – the two truths. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, sources for that:) 
The Meeting of the Father and Son Sutra72 says: 
It is like this: The Tathagata comp rehends the two truths, conventional 
and ultimate. Furthermore, objects of  knowledge are exhausted in these 
conventional and ultimate truths. 
Thus, [Buddha] said that all objects of knowledge are exhausted in the two truths. 
 
These are the scriptural sources for the fact th at objects of knowledge are definitely two in 
number as the two truths. The Meeting of the Father and Son Sutra  says that the Tathagata, the 
One Gone Thus, comprehends two truths, the ul timate truth and conventional truth, and 
that objects of knowledge are only the two – ulti mate truths and conventional truths. In other 
words, there are no other objects of knowledge in that they are exhausted in the two truths. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 The Meditative Stabilization Definitely Revealing Suchness  says: 
The conventional and likewise the ultimate— 
There is not at all a third truth. 
Thus, it is clearly said that the enum eration is definite as the two truths.

940 This text also says that there is the convention al truth and the ultimate truth, and that there

Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, establishing the correctness of the need to become skilled in the two truths:) 
It is said that if the divisi on of the two truths is know n, one is not obscured with 
regard to the Subduer’s word, whereas if the division of the two truths is not 
known, one will not know the essence of the teaching. Furthermore, it must be 
known in accordance with how Nagarjuna settled it.  
 
It is necessary to become skilled in the two truths. This is because if one knows the divisions of the two truths, ultimate truth and conventional truth, one will not be obscured or ignorant 
with respect to the teachings of the Buddha. If, on the other hand, one does not know the 
division of the two truths into the convention al and ultimate, one will not know the essence 
of the Buddha’s teachings. How should the tw o truths be understood? They should be 
understood in accordance with how the protecto r Nagarjuna settled them in his six treatises 
that include Fundamental Wisdom . 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
There is no method of pacification in those outside 
The path of the honorable master Nagarjuna. They fall from the truths of the conventional and suchness. By falling from those liberation is not achieved. One who does not know the division of the two— Conventional truths as the method 
And ultimate truths as arisen from the method— 
Abides on a bad path through wrong conceptions. 
Hence, it is very important for those who wish liberation to become skilled in the 
two truths. 
 Chandrakirti says that for those who wander ou tside the path of Nagarjuna there will be no 
method for achieving peace. They degenerate  from the conventional truth and suchness, 
whereby they cannot achieve liberation. Therefor e, one who does not know the division of 
the two truths, that is, who does not know the conventional truth to be the method and does 
not know that ultimate truth arises from that  method, abides on a bad path through wrong 
conceptions. Hence, for those who wish to attain liberation it is important to become skilled 
in the two truths.  Friday morning, 9 March 2007
