PART FOUR: SPECIAL INSIGHT 
9 Divisions of Special Insight 
(The presentation has three parts, the first of which is : [a summary of] the prerequisites for special insight:) 
When from observing the prerequisites of special insight as explained before the 
view realizing the two selflessnesses has been found, you should cultivate special 
insight. 
 
In order to achieve special insight it is necessa ry to collect its causes, which were explained 
before. First one must study special insight in de tail, rely on a teacher, and have wisdom. In 
particular, one must safeguard the three higher trainings of morality, concentration, and 
wisdom. One should also engage in collecting me rit, and find a solitary place for meditation 
where it is easy to obtain necessities such as  food, where there is access to a doctor, and so 
f o r t h .  O n e  m u s t  f i n d  a  p l a c e  s u c h  a s  t h a t  described previously free from danger, with 
attendants or helpers who have a subdued mind. Moreover, in the case of a nun or lay woman 
it would be best if the helper were a woman, and in the case of a monk or layman it would be 
best if the helper were a man.   These are the causes or prerequisites for at taining special insight, in dependence upon 
which one should study the subject of the se lflessness of persons and the selflessness of 
phenomena. Since this is not an easy topic, one should first study it well and then establish it 
by reasons. For example one reason is: the person or being does not exist truly because it does not exist as a truly existent one or many. Anot her reason is: the person does not exist truly 
because it is a dependent-arising. Likewise, an other reason is: the aggregates do not exist 
inherently because they are dependent-arisings. By means of such reasonings, an inferential 
cognizer realizing selflessness will arise in on e’s mind. Having found this view realizing the 
two selflessnesses, one should cultivate special insight. Thus, in this context the object of 
special insight is specifically the selflessness of persons or th e selflessness of phenomena.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the special insight to be cultivate by common beings will mainly be explained:) 
Question : How many [types of] special insight are there? 
Answer:  Here I will mainly indicate not th e special insights of those on high 
grounds but rather those to be cultivated while a common being. 
 
In this context the main expl anation given will be the cultiv ation of special insight by 
ordinary beings, rather than the special insights of superiors. In other words, here the special 
insights of those on the higher grounds, that is , arya beings, is not what is mainly explained, 
rather the special insight that is to be cultiv ated by ordinary beings is what is mainly 
explained.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The third, the actual divisions, has three parts, the first of which is: a brief indication of the divisions:) 
All inclusively, the divisions of the spec ial insights to be cultivated while a 
common being are those of the four natu res, the three appr oaches, and the six 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
942 searches. 
 
Through considering the complete divisions of special insight, from the point of view of 
entity there are four, from the point of view of  approach there are thr ee, and from the point 
of view of searching there are six.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The second, the extensive explanation has three parts, the first of which is: an explanation of the four natures:) 
The four are those described in the Sutra Unraveling the Thought73—thorough 
differentiation and so forth. Concerning th em, thorough differentiation is done in 
observation of the varieties whereas intens e differentiation is done in observation 
of the mode, [emptiness]. The first has two types, thorough investigation and 
thorough analysis; the second also has two types, investigation and analysis—
[respectively] analyses of coarse and subtle  objects. Identifications of those four are 
given in Asanga’s Grounds of Hearers74 and Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential 
Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom.  
 
With respect to the four entities mentioned in the Sutra Unraveling the Thought , a sutra that is 
included in the third wheel of Dharma, there are:  
(1) thorough differentiation ( rnam par ‘byed pa ) – special insight observing varieties, and  
(2) intense differentiation ( rab tu rnam par ‘byed pa ) – special insight obse rving modes, that is, 
emptinesses,  
each of which is further divided into two. The special insight observing varieties has two 
types: thorough investigation an d thorough analysis. The specia l insight observing modes also 
has two types: investigation and analysis, which ar e respectively analysis of coarser and subtler 
objects, for example, the coarse form that is th e form aggregate and the subtle form that is a 
particle or atom. These four entities are identi fied in Asanga’s and Ratnakarashanti’s texts as 
well as in other texts.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the explanation of the three from the viewpoint of approach:) 
The three approaches are those described in the Sutra Unraveling the Thought —
arisen from a sign [an imagistic remembranc e of experience gained earlier], arisen 
from thorough searching, and arisen from individual investigation. To illustrate 
identifications of these with respect, for instance, to the meaning of selflessness, 
the special insight arisen from a sign is a case of observing the selflessness already 
ascertained and taking to mind its sign; one does not perform much [additional] 
settling [of the meaning]. The special insigh t arisen from thorough searching is a 
settling [of the meaning] for the sake of ascertaining what was not ascertained earlier. The special insight arisen from in dividual investigation is a performing of 
analysis on a meaning already ascertai ned in the way [it was done] earlier. 
                                             
 
73 samdhinirmocana . 
74 shravakabhumi . 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
943  
The three doors or three approa ches of special insight are: 
1. special insight arisen from a sign, 2. special insight arisen from  thorough searching, and  
3. special insight arisen from individual investigation. In relation to the meaning of selflessness, the special insight arisen from a sign observes the 
selflessness already ascertained and pays attentio n to its sign, without doing much to settle it. 
In other words, one does not investigate it in de tail in order to settle it. The special insight 
arisen from thorough searching settles the mean ing of selflessness in order to ascertain that 
not previously ascertained. The special insight ar isen from individual investigation analyzes as 
before the meaning of selflessne ss that was already ascertained. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The third, the explanation of the six investigations, has three parts, the first of which is: a brief exposition that 
the six are by way of the object of observation of special insight:) 
The six investigations are a thorough  researching of meanings, things, 
characteristics, class, time, and reasoning, as well as individual investigation after 
searching.  
 
The six searches are: 
1. thorough researching of meanings, 2. thorough researching of things, 
3. thorough researchin g of characteristics, 
4. thorough researching of classes or sides, 
5. thorough researching of time, and 6. thorough researching of reasoning. Also having sought them, one individually investigates them,   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, individual explanations of the six:) 
Concerning those, to research the meaning is to make the search, “The meaning of 
this word is such and such.” To research things is to make the search, “This is an 
internal thing; that is an external thing.” To research characteri stics is to make the 
search, “This is an exclusive characteristic; that is a general characteristic,” or the 
common and the uncommon. To research cla sses is to search from the viewpoint 
of the unwholesome class—faults and disadvantages—and the wholesome class—
good qualities and advantages. To resear ch times is to make  the search, “Such 
occurred in the past; such will occur in the future, such is occurring in the 
present.” 
 
1. The researching of meanings is to, for exampl e, to investigate the meaning of a word such 
as “abhi,” which means higher and so forth. Anothe r example is to ask: “What is the meaning 
of the word “pillar”? A pillar is a support of beams.”   
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
944 2. The researching of things is, for example, to think “This is an  internal thing” with respect 
to, for example, the six consci ousnesses or the six sense powe rs, and to think “This is an 
external thing” with respect to houses, mountains, rivers, trees, and so forth.  
 
3. The researching of characteristics is to think “This is a specific characteristic” or “This is a 
general characteristic.” These terms usually re fer to specifically characterized phenomena 
(which according to the lower tenets are establ ished by way of their own characteristics) and 
generally characterized phenomena (which in th is context refers to imputed phenomena that 
are not established by way of their own characte ristics). There is also the researching of the 
common and the uncommon, for example, common paths and uncommon paths. For 
example, meditating on impermanence, sufferi ng, and so forth are common paths, whereas 
meditating on the mind of enlightenment is an uncommon path of the mahayana. There are 
also common attainments and uncommon a ttainments. Likewise, there are common 
perceptions and uncommon perceptions in th at this room, for example, is a common 
perception in that it is perceived by all of us , whereas there is also an uncommon hall that is 
the specific hall perceived by each one of us . In addition, there is common property which 
can be utilized by everyone, and uncommon proper ty which can only be used by its respective 
owner.  4. The researching of classes or sides refers to  researching the black side, that is, faults and 
disadvantages, and researching the white side, good qualities and benefits. The black side 
refers to the ten non-virtuous actions – killing,  stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisive 
speech, harsh words, idle talk, covetousness, ma lice, and wrong view – which have many faults 
and disadvantages in that they bring the result of suffering. The white side, on the other hand 
is the ten virtuous actions of abandoning kill ing, abandoning stealing, abandoning sexual 
misconduct, abandoning lying, abandoning divisive speech, abandoning harsh words, 
abandoning idle talk, abandoning covetousne ss, abandoning malice, and abandoning wrong 
view. Another way of positing them is that th e white side is nirvana and the black side is 
samsara or cyclic existence, whic h is so-called because having been  born in cyclic existence one 
will always experience suffering. Perhaps today there is a bit of happiness, but tomorrow there 
will be suffering again. It is like the fact that finding oneself in a mire or a swamp, one cannot easily get out   5. The researching of times is to make the sear ch, “Such occurred in the past; such will occur 
in the future, such is occurring in the pres ent.” For example, acco rding to the Buddhist 
teachings a 1,000 buddhas will visit this world in the fortunate eon, among which three have 
already passed, Buddha Shakyamuni has presen tly come, and other 996 buddhas will come in 
the future. Similarly, there were kings of the past who were very  powerful, in the future there 
will be such kings, and in the present there are such kings (although nowadays most kings do not have much power!). Another example is th e twenty-five kings of Shambala, twenty of 
whom have gone, one who is present, and four wh o have yet to come. There are also wars that 
have passed, for example, the First and Second World Wars, there are presently wars in this 
world, and perhaps in the future there will be a third and fourth world war.  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
945  
 The researching of reasoning is of four ty pes. The reasoning of relativity is from 
the viewpoint of the arising of effects in dependence on causes and conditions. It 
is also a researching from the viewpoin t of the conventional, the ultimate, and 
their bases individually. The reasoning of the performance of function is from the 
viewpoint of the performance by phenomena of their own functions, such as fire 
performing the function of burning. One searches out, “This is the phenomenon; 
this is the functioning; this phen omenon performs that function.”  
 
6. The researching of reasoning is of four types: (1) the reasoning of relativity or dependence – this research is from the point of view of 
results arising in dependence on causes and conditions. For example, a baby arises from 
intercourse between its father and mother, a harv est arises from seeds sown by a farmer in a 
field, and so forth. In the latter case the ca use is the seeds, while the conditions are the 
farmer’s work, the field, water, sun, and so fort h. This is also a researching from the point of 
view of the conventional, the ultimate, and their individual bases. All phenomena, that is, all 
bases, have two natures: a conventional trut h and an ultimate truth. For example, smoke 
arises from fire, this smoke is a conventional trut h, but is it established inherently? It is empty 
of inherent establishment, this being its ultimate truth.  (2) the reasoning of the performa nce of function – this is from the point of view of, for 
example, fire performing the function of bu rning wood, fuel, houses , and so forth, water 
performing the function of destruction by flood i n g ,  a n d  s o  f o r t h .  I n  f a c t  a l l  c o m p o u n d e d  
phenomena have their specific functions. For example, what is the function of the five 
fingers? The Tibetans say that the index finger  functions to point, th e middle to show that 
someone is bad, the thumb to show  that something is very good (and it can also be used for 
hitchhiking!). Thus, one searches out, “This is the phenomenon; this is the functioning; this 
phenomenon performs that function.”  
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
The reasoning of proof by means of a co rrectness is to prove a meaning without 
contradicting valid cognitions. It is a se arching out within wondering whether such 
and such has a valid cognition—direct, infe rential, or believable scripture. The 
reasoning of nature is to research from the viewpoint of (1) natures renowned in 
the world, such as heat bein g the nature of fire and moisture being the nature of 
water, (2) inconceivable natures, and (3) su bsisting natures. It is done by way of 
believing in them and not contemplating other “reasons” for their not being like 
that.  
 
(3) the reasoning of proof by means of a co rrectness – this establishes a meaning without 
contradicting valid cognition. This is because if  something is not realized by a valid cognizer, 
it does not exist. For example, that a pen exis t s  i n  f r o n t  o f  o n e  i s  s e e n  b y  a  v a l i d  d i r e c t  
perceiver without any need for reasoning. An in ferential cognizer, on the other hand, is able 
to realize that which is not seen  directly, for example, seeing waterfowl soaring at a distance 
one can infer the existence of water, seeing fire on a mountain side at a distance one can infer 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
946 the existence of fire, and so forth. These phenomena are understood by means of valid 
inferential cognizers. In addition, there are phenomena that are understood based on valid 
scriptures. For example, one scripture says  “From generosity, wealth; from morality, 
happiness” and that this is so is only established by valid texts since it cannot be directly 
realized. In other words, that by practicing  generosity one obtains wealth, and that by 
practicing morality one obtains a good rebirth, can only be understood by citations from valid 
scriptures.  
 
(4) The reasoning of nature or reality ( chos nyid ) – this is to research from the viewpoint of: 
(1) natures renowned in the world, that is, those known to worldly beings, such as that heat is 
the nature of fire, moisture is the nature of water, heaviness is the nature of earth, and 
lightness is the nature of wind, 
(2) inconceivable natures, such as  the qualities of a buddha, and  
(3) subsisting natures, thos e that exist naturally.  
This is done by way of believing in them an d not contemplating other reasons for their being 
like that.  
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 363) says: 
(Third, those six are explained as of three types by way of their object of observation:) 
Through the positing of six types in that w ay, it can be ascertained that the objects 
to be known by a yogi are threefold—meanin gs of terms, the varieties of objects of 
knowledge, and the mode. The first research  is posited in terms of the first. The 
researching of things and of exclusive ch aracteristics is posited in terms of the 
second. The remaining three as well as researching general characteristics are 
posited in terms of the third. 
 
These six searches are of three types by way of their observed object. through positing the six 
searches it can be ascertained that a yogi has three objects of knowledge: 
1. meanings of terms or meanings expressed,  2. the objects of knowledge that are varieties, and  3. the objects of knowledge that are modes. The first research among the six – the researchin g of meanings – is posited in terms of the 
meanings of terms or the meanings expressed. The researching of things and the researching 
of specific characteristics are po sited in terms of the objects of knowledge that are varieties. 
The remaining three – the researching of classes,  the researching of time, and the researching 
of reasoning – as well as the researching of gene ral characteristics are po sited in terms of the 
objects of knowledge that are modes.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The third, condensing the divisions, has two parts, the first of which is: that the three approaches and the six 
researches are included in the four natures:) 
The approaches of the four special insights  explained first are three; the modes of 
searching are said to be six. Thus, the three approaches and six researches are 
included in the former four. 
FPMT Basic Program                        Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim  by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
Stages of the Path                            ILTK – 6-9 March 2007 (20) 
947  
The three approaches or three doors of the four special insi ghts and the six researches – 
researching of meanings, researching of things, researching of characteristics the researching 
of classes, researching of time, and researching of  reasoning – are included in the four entities 
of special insight.   
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, special insight involves the four mental activities:) 
Asanga’s Grounds of Hearers  says that the four mental activities explained earlier—
forcible engagement and so forth [i nterrupted engagement, uninterrupted 
engagement, and spontaneous engagement ]—are common to both calm abiding 
and special insight. Thus special insight al so involves the four mental activities. 
 
Special insight has the four ment al attentions of engagement: 
1. forcible engagement, 
2. interrupted engagement,  
3. uninterrupted engagement, and  4. spontaneous engagement: These four were explained in the context of developing calm abiding by way of the nine 
stages, six powers, and four mental attentions. Thus, they are common to both calm abiding 
and special insight. In other words, special insight also has the four mental attentions.

                                             Monday afternoon, 12 March 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 364) says: 
10 How to Cultivate Special Insight 
The presentation has three pa rts: (1) showing the meanin g of the statements that 
special insight is cultivated in dependence  on calm abiding, (2) from the viewpoint 
of the path of which vehicle—great and smal l—this is the system and (3) the actual 
mode of cultivating special insight in dependence on calm abiding. 
The meaning of the statements that  special insight is cultivated in dependence on calm abiding 
(This section has eight parts, the first of which is: the statement in many texts that they are to be cultivated in that order: ) 
The Sutra Unraveling the Thought says that having first ac hieved calm abiding, one 
should afterward cultivate special insight. In the same fashion many texts such as 
those of Maitreya, Asanga’s Grounds of Bodhisattvas75 and Grounds of Hearers,  as well 
as Bhavaviveka, Shantideva, Ka malashila’s three works on the Stages of Meditation,  
and Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom speak of 
[this procedure]. 
 
That the order is that first calm abiding is de veloped and then special insight is developed is 
taught in many texts. The Sutra Unraveling the Thought , for example, says that first one must 
accomplish calm abiding and after that one shou ld cultivate special in sight. This was also 
taught by Maitreya in his treatises, by Asanga in his treatises such as Grounds of Bodhisattvas  
and Grounds of Hearers,  by Bhavaviveka and Shantideva, by Kamalashila in his three works on 
the Stages of Meditation,  and by Ratnakarashanti in his Quintessential Instructions on the 
Perfection of Wisdom . 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, those texts do not say that first a calm abiding observing one of the varieties is generated and then the mere 
sustaining [of the mind] within observi ng selflessness is special insight:) 
The thought of these texts is not that first, without observing the meaning of 
selflessness, one generates a calm abiding observing any object of observation and 
then the later sustaining [of the mind] within observing selflessness is special 
insight. This is because the two—calm  abiding and special insight—are not 
differentiated by way of object of observation.  
 
These texts do not say that firs t one achieves a calm abiding observing the varieties, that is, 
conventional truths, and then to merely observe selflessness from within it is special insight. 
In other words, special insight is not that having generated a calm abiding observing any observed object whatsoever, one observes selflessness. This is so because calm abiding and 
special insight are not differentiated in terms of their observed objects, rather they are 
differentiated in terms of how they are develo ped. Thus, in the case of calm abiding one 
 
75 bodhisattvabhumi . 
948 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
engages in single-pointed or stabilizing meditation, whereas in the case of special insight one 
engages in analytical meditation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Also, Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom  
explains that first one generates a calm abiding within observing suchness—the emptiness of duality between object and su bject—and later generates special insight 
through analytical meditation within observ ing that same object of observation.  
 
Ratnakarashanti says that one generates calm  abiding observing the emptiness of duality 
between object and object-possessor and then generates special insight through analytical meditation observing the same observed object . Therefore, calm abiding and special insight 
are not differentiated in terms of their obse rved objects, but in terms of how they are 
meditated or cultivated. In order to develop calm abiding one meditates single-pointedly 
without doing analysis, whereas in the case of special insight one meditates while doing 
analysis.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Also, the superior Asanga describes a sp ecial insight observing the varieties and 
says that after having first generated calm  abiding, in dependence on it a special 
insight is cultivated having the aspect of [viewing of the lower level as] gross and 
[the upper level as] peaceful. Furthermor e ,  A s a n g a  s p e a k s  o f  t h i s  a s  a  p a t h  
common to both non-Buddhist and Buddhist as well as to common being and 
superior. 
 
Also Asanga says that there is a special insi ght observing varieties. He says that after 
generating calm abiding, in dependence on it on e cultivates a special insight having the aspect 
of coarseness and peacefulness. One analyzes the desire realm as being coarse due to its many 
sufferings, and analyzes the first concentration as being more peaceful, whereby one abandons 
mundane coarse attachment to the five qualities or sense objects of the desire realm – pleasant forms, sounds, odors, tastes, and tactil e objects. Asanga says that the meditation on 
coarseness-peacefulness is common to both Buddhists and non-Buddhists and also common 
to ordinary beings and superiors, meaning that everyone does this type of meditation. Thus, 
because one is a Buddhist one cannot say that it  is not necessary to meditate on coarseness-
peacefulness, and likewise because one is a superi or one cannot say that it is not necessary to 
meditate on it. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 365) says: 
(Third, when calm abiding is initially being achieved, exc ept for setting [the mind] one-pointedly on the object of 
observation it is not suitable to analyze in many ways:) 
Therefore, when one who has not achieved calm abiding before is newly achieving 
it, except for setting [the mind] one- pointedly on whatever the object of 
observation is, it is not achievable within  analyzing the object of observation in 
949 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
many ways. For, if done the former way, calm abiding is achieved whereas if the 
latter, achievement is impossible. 
 
When one is first meditating calm abiding, one sets the mind single-pointedly on the 
observed object without investigating and analyz ing it in many ways. In other words, those 
who have not previously achieved calm abiding an d are striving to achieve it should only set 
their mind single-pointedly on the observed ob ject and should not analyze it in many ways, 
such as in terms of its being impermanent, sufferi ng, empty, and so forth. If one first strives to 
meditate single-pointedly on the observed object without analyzing it, one will achieve calm 
abiding, whereas if one analyzes it in many w ays one will never achieve it. In short, if one 
wishes to achieve calm abiding, one must me ditate single-pointedly and not engage in 
analytical meditation. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, when one who has first achieved calm abiding per forms analytical meditation instead of just stabilizing 
meditation, a powerful one -pointed meditative stabilization is achieved:) 
When one who has first achieved calm  abiding does not just extend the 
conditioning to mere stabilizing meditation as before but performs analytical 
meditation in which the object appropri ate to the occasion—the mode or the 
varieties—is individually analyzed with  wisdom, finally a special one-pointed 
meditative stabilization can be induced. Since through the former [mode of mere 
stabilization] a very powerful one-pointed meditative stabilization such as is achieved through analytical meditation ca nnot [be induced], analytical meditation 
is praised. 
 
If, on the other hand, those who have already ac hieved calm abiding, instead of just doing 
stabilizing meditation, also do analytical medita tion, their meditative stabilization will become 
more powerful. In other words, if one who has achieved calm abiding does not just cultivate 
single-pointed meditation but also does analytic al meditation on the mo des or varieties with 
wisdom, at the end a special single-pointed medita tive stabilization will be induced. Since the 
meditative stabilization that is achieved throug h analytical meditation is more powerful than 
that achieved by stabilizing meditation alone, it is praised as being superior.  
 In short, although initially one should not do  analytical meditation when cultivating calm 
abiding, if upon having achieved calm abidin g one engages in analytical meditation, one’s 
single-pointed meditative stabilization will become stronger. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, this fact of achievement is the reason for there being the two different  modes of procedure in calm abiding and 
special insight even with respec t to one object of observation:) 
Just that mode of achievement is how, in dependence on first having sought calm 
abiding, to cultivate special insight afterwards. Therefore, it is the general reason 
for there being the two different modes of  procedure in calm abiding and special 
insight even though the object of observation—selflessness, for instance—might be 
the same. 
 
950 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
Thus, there are two different modes of procedur e in relation to calm abiding and special 
insight. Because of this, one first achieves calm  abiding, and then, in dependence on it, one 
achieves special insight. Although the observed  object is the same, selflessness, there is a 
different way of cultivating calm abiding and special insight. By meditating on the observed 
object single-pointedly one achieves calm abidin g, whereas by taking the same observed object 
and analyzing it, one achieves special insight. Th erefore, with respect to one object, there are 
two different procedures, one for developing calm abiding and one for developing special 
insight. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, the great power of cultivating the special insight which has the aspect of gross ness/peacefulness and the special 
insight which has the aspect of selflessness:) 
In particular, the cultivation of the special insight having the aspect of 
grossness/peacefulness—individually analyzing the faults of a lower realm and 
advantages of an upper realm—and the cult ivation of the special insight having the 
aspect of selflessness—in which the meaning of selflessness is analyzed and 
sustained through the wisdom of indivi dual investigation—necessarily involve 
generation of strong and steady ascertai nment. Therefore, they have great power 
with respect to abandoning their individual objects of abandonment. 
 
There is special insight that has the aspect of coarseness-peacefulness and special insight that 
has the aspect of selflessness. Th e latter, for example, meditates on the seven-point analysis of 
the self of persons, which is found in the Supplement to the Middle Way . It also meditates on 
the reasonings found in Fundamental Wisdom  i n  r e l a t i o n  t o  t h e  s e l f  o f  p h e n o m e n a :  
phenomena are not produced from self, from other, from both, and without cause, and 
therefore are not generated ultimately. There are five types of reasonings that prove the 
selflessness of phenomena, the main one be ing the reasoning of dependent-arising: 
phenomena are not truly existent because they are dependent-arisings. One does analytical 
meditation on these reasonings in order to de velop special insight, wh ereby one’s meditation 
becomes more and more powerful.  
 One can also meditate on coarseness-peac efulness, whereby one abandons the mundane 
objects of abandonment but without being able to  abandon all afflictions. The special insight 
observing selflessness, on the other hand, can abandon all the objects of abandonment – the 
afflictive obscurations, obscurations to kn owledge, fruitional obscurations, and karmic 
obscurations.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 366) says: 
(Seventh, there are yet other types of special insight observing the varieties:) 
Not only is there cultivation of special in sight observing the varieties which has the 
aspect of grossness/peacefulness for the sake  of abandoning the manifest afflictive 
emotions, but also, as is explained in Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential Instructions on 
the Perfection of Wisdom,  there is analytical meditation thoroughly differentiating 
the character of the eighteen spheres. Thus, using this as an illustration you should 
understand that there are other specia l insights which involve meditation 
differentiating the objects comprising the varieties. 
951 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
 
There exist other kinds of specia l insight observing the varieties.  Thus, there is not only the 
special insight observing the varieties that ha s the aspect of coarseness-peacefulness which 
abandons the manifest afflictions, but as expl ained by Ratnakarashanti, there is also an 
analytical meditation that thoroughly different iates the characteristics or definitions of the 
eighteen constituents, whereby one develops spec ial insight. The eighteen constituents are: 
the six objects – the form constituent, sound cons tituent, odor constituent, taste constituent, 
tactile object constituent, and phenomena cons tituent; the six sense powers – the eye sense 
power constituent, the ear sense power constitu ent, the nose sense power constituent, the 
tongue sense power constituent, the body sense power constituent, and the mental sense 
power constituent; and the six consciousnesse s – the eye consciousness constituent, ear 
consciousness constituent, nose consciousness co nstituent, tongue consciousness constituent, 
body consciousness constituent, and mental co nsciousness constituent. Each of these has a 
different definition and different characteristic s. Thus, there exists a special insight that 
meditates on these eighteen constituents. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Eighth, unlike the order expl ained in Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom,  the 
order here accords with that asserted by Shantideva and so forth:) 
Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom  explains that 
prior to generating calm abiding and special insight observing the mode 
[emptiness], calm abiding and special insigh t on the level of a yoga observing the 
varieties is to be generated. However, ac cording to the assertions of Shantideva, 
Kamalashila, and so forth, first any calm  abiding is generated and then special 
insight is. Just the special insight observing the mode will be taught [here]. 
 
The order mentioned in Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential Instructions on  the Perfection of Wisdom  
is different from the order mentioned here, which accords with the order explained by 
Shantideva and so forth. Ratnakarashanti explai ns that before developing calm abiding and 
special insight observing modes, one generates a calm abiding and special insight which are 
the level of a yoga observing varieties. Howeve r, according to Shantideva, Kamalashila, and 
others, having first generated calm abiding ob serving any observed object, that is, either 
varieties or modes, one then generates specia l insight. However, here the special insight 
observing modes or thusness is shown in particular.  
 For example, initially one could meditate on the tip of one’s nose. Having achieved calm abiding on the tip of one’s nose, one can go on to examine how the tip of  one’s nose exists by 
asking: Is it permanent or impermanent? Is it  the nature of suffering or the nature of 
happiness? Is it empty or not? Does it exist inherently or not? Is it matter or consciousness or 
a non-associated compositional factor? One should  analyze the tip of one’s  nose in this way. 
By doing so, based on one little object, one can develop both calm abiding and special insight. 
In fact, Atisha, in his Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment , says that one can take any object 
whatsoever as the basis for developing single-p ointed meditation through the nine stages by 
applying the six powers and four mental attentions. After that one can meditate on the same object, examining whether it exists as an inherent ly existent one or as an inherently existent 
many using the reasoning: It is not an inherently  existent one because it has parts. With parts 
952 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
and without parts are contradictory. That withou t parts does not exist. It is also not an 
inherently existent many because it is not an inherently existent one. This is because for it to 
be many there must be one. One should engage  in such analytical meditation whereby one 
will develop special insight. 
 There are people who say that one should medi tate on the nature of the mind. The nature 
of the mind of being clear and knowing is the conventional nature of the mind, whereas the 
ultimate nature of mind is its emptiness of inherent existence.  
 Then, there are people who want to meditate  on mahamudra, the great seal, and others 
who want to meditate on dzog-chen , the great perfection. What is the great perfection? What 
does one meditate on? Some people say that it is  not necessary to do analytical meditation but 
that without taking any object of meditation at  all, one should just remain like a statue 
without thinking, while trying to recognize the nature of the mind (of course, without falling 
asleep!). His Holiness the Dalai Lama often advi ses the study of a text by the Nyingma lama, 
Do Drubchen, whose present incarnation lives in Sikkim and is completely against the Dalai 
Lama and the Gelugpas. Although this lama’s text discusses dzog-chen, it sometimes seems 
that he is describing the primordial princi pal asserted by the Samkyas, which upon being 
discovered disappears, implying a type of nihili sm. The Samkyas say that if the self or being 
examines from where the many results come, it  will find that they are created by the 
primordial principal. The primordial principal, upon seeing that the being has recognized it 
as the cause of all creations, becomes ashamed an d withdraws all its manifestations, that is, all 
results, whereby all that remains is the self or  knower or consciousness, which is permanent 
and neither a result or a cause. At this time th e self achieves nirvana. These are the assertions 
of the Samkyas and if you wish to verify them you can go to Varanasi to study with them. I 
myself have read many of their texts. The text  that His Holiness refers to is a Nyingma text, 
not a Gelugpa text. Therefore, it would be best to follow the texts of the many Indian pandits 
who explain the need to do analysis, rather than  just doing dzog-chen, dzog-chen, dzog-chen. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 366) says: 
From the viewpoint of the pat hs of which vehicles—great and small—this is the system 
(This has three parts, the first of wh ich is: an explanation that this procedure is common to all vehicles—great and 
small—and is similar even for the three lower tantra sets:) 
Question : From the viewpoint of what vehicles—great or small—and sutra or mantra 
is this the mode of generating calm abiding and special insight? 
Answer:  It is common to the two vehicles—that of the hearers and solitary realizers 
and that of the perfections—and is common to the four schools of tenets. In my 
Great Exposition of Secret Mantra  I have explained that  the assertion of the 
individual tantras and their great commentat ors is that it is similar also for the 
three lower tantra sets with in the mantra [vehicle]. 
 
This system is from the point of  view of which vehicle –the grea t or the small? This procedure 
is common to all vehicles and is similar also in the three lower tantra classes or sets – action 
tantra, performance tantra, and yoga tantra.  
 First the question is asked: from the point of view of which vehicles – the great or the small 
vehicles and the sutra or the mantra vehicles – is this the procedure for generating calm 
abiding and special insight? The re sponse is that it is common to  the two vehicles, that of the 
953 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
                                             hearers and solitary realizers, that is, the hin ayana, and that of the pe rfections, the mahayana. 
It is also common to all four tenet systems: the Vaibhashika, Sautrantika, Chittamatra, and 
Madhyamaka. It is also common to the three lowe r tantra sets of the mantra vehicle – action 
tantra, performance tantra, and yoga tantra.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 367) says: 
(Second, [according to Ratnakarashanti ,] highest yoga tantra asserts a mode of  achieving calm abiding and special 
insight through analytical and stabilizing yogas ob serving mind-only, suchness, and so forth:) 
With respect to highest yoga tantra, Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential Instructions on 
the Perfection of Wisdom  says: 
The Guhyasamaja Tantra76 says: 
When one’s own mind is analyzed, 
All phenomena dwell in one’s mind. 
These phenomena dwell as vajras of space. 
Phenomena and their nature do not exist. 
Also, the Descent into Lanka Sutra says: 
Based on mind-only 
External objects are not imputed. 
 
According to Ratnakarashanti, highest yoga tant ra asserts a way to achieve calm abiding and 
special insight through analytical and stabilizin g yogas observing mind-only, suchness, and so 
forth. Ratnakarashanti’s text cites the Guhy asamaja Tantra as saying that all phenomena 
abide in the nature of the mind. They abide as  space vajras. Phenomena and their realities or 
natures, that is, their emptinesses, do not exis t, meaning that they do not exist inherently. 
The Descent into Lanka Sutra  says that in dependence on mere mind, or mind-only, external 
objects are not imputed. This indicates that th e objects of calm abiding are merely mind and 
not external phenomena. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Through such Ratnakarashanti explains that  three levels of yoga—observing mind-
only, observing suchness, and without appearance—are taught [in highest yoga 
tantra]. It appears that he explains how to achieve calm abiding and special insight 
through stabilizing and analytical meditati on on the first two levels in the manner 
explained above. Therefore, he asserts that the mode of generating in the mental 
continuum the special insight observing th e mode is similar [also in those two 
levels of highest yoga tantra]. 
 
There are three levels of yoga – observing mind-only, observing suchness, and without 
appearance. Ratnakarashanti explains how to achieve calm abiding and special insight through respectively stabilizing meditation and an alytical meditation on the first two levels – 
 
76 guhyasamaja . 
954 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
observing mind-only and observing suchness. Theref ore, he says that the way to generate the 
special insight observing modes is similar in these two.  
 
Tuesday morning, 13 March 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 367) says: 
(Third, even in highest yoga tantra the mode of generating an understanding of the view must be in accordance with the 
Madhyamika texts, and there is the fine point that al though one on the stage of completion must, during meditative 
equipoise, initially set [the mind] within t he view, analytical meditation is not done:) 
Our own system is as follows: Even in highest yoga tantra the mode of generating 
an understanding of the view must be done in accordance with what appears in the Madhyamika texts. With respect to how it is sustained, at some time during 
states subsequent to meditative equipoise on the stages of generation and 
completion, one does take suchness to mind within analyzing it.  
 
Also on the occasion of meditating on highes t yoga tantra, one must do so according to 
generate the correct view of emptiness of the Madhyamaka system. However, during the 
meditative equipoise of the completion stage, one must set the mind within the correct view 
but without actually doing analytical medita tion. In other words, according to our own 
system, the Prasangika Madhyamaka, even in highest yoga tantra one must generate an 
understanding of the correct view in accordan ce with the Madhyamaka texts. This is then 
sustained in the subsequent attainment of the generation stage and completion stage by at 
times doing some analytical meditation on suchness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
However, when those on the stage of comple tion who have attained the capacity to 
concentrate on essential points in the body sustains suchness in meditative 
equipoise, although they  definitely must meditate with in setting [the mind] in the 
context of the view, they do not perfor m the analytical meditation of special 
insight as it appears in other texts. Theref ore, with respect to highest yoga tantra 
do not posit analytical meditation as one-pointed meditation on suchness from 
within the context of the view subsidiary to stabilizing [meditation]. Since to show 
clearly the reasons why doing such is sufficient is not appropriate here, I will explain here the reasons for what is  to be done on the other paths. 
 
Those on the completion stage who have attained  the ability to penetrate the essential points 
of the body, that is, to dissolve the winds into the various chakras (the six or four or eight 
according to the different tantras), should not do  the analytical meditati on of special insight 
as it appears in other texts but should just sustain suchness in meditative equipoise. In other words, on the stages of the completion stage – (1) the isolation of speech, (2) the isolation of 
mind, (3) illusory body, (4) clear light, and (5) union – one does not do analytical meditation. In the context of highest yoga tantra do not posit a single-pointed meditation on suchness 
that concerns the view that is incidentally posited as analytical meditation. There are many reasons for such but it would not be approp riate to mention them here since they are 
mentioned in other texts.

FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 337) says: 
The actual mode of cultiv ating special insight in dependence on calm abiding 
(This section has two parts, the first of which is: the need to find the view:) 
If the view of selflessness is not found, no matter what system  of meditation you 
perform, that meditation will not dwell in the meaning of suchness; therefore, it is 
necessary to find the view. 
 
Now the actual way in which to cultivate specia l insight in dependence on having previously 
developed calm abiding is explained. Initially one has to find the correct view of emptiness. 
This is because without finding the correct view  of selflessness, no ma tter what system of 
meditation one uses it will not ab ide in the meaning of suchness. Therefore, it is necessary to 
first understand or find the view – the correct view of the emptiness of inherent existence of 
all phenomena. This is the view that needs to be found or understood and then relied upon.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 368) says: 
(The second, a summary of the essentials of how to sustain [the view], has three parts, the fi rst of which is: the actual 
mode of sustaining, which in tu rn has six parts; the first is: 
The need for both analytical and stabilizing meditation:) 
If although one has the view, one does not become mindful of it during 
meditation on suchness and does not meditate  within being set in its context, such 
will not serve as meditation on suchness.  Therefore, to set [the mind] without 
contemplating anything at the end of first doing a little analysis of the view is not a 
sustaining of suchness. Even to become accustomed to a mere placement within 
the view upon becoming mindful of it is exhausted as just the mode of sustaining 
[a session] in calm abiding explained in th e previous [section]. Thus, such is not 
the meaning of the texts describing a mode of sustaining special insight which is different from that. 
 
The way in which to sustain the view concerns th e actual way to meditate on it, which is in 
addition to the preparatory practices and the concluding activities. When one does 
meditation in general, one first engages in the preparatory practices that include cleaning the 
room, setting out a cushion, arranging offerings on  an altar, and so fort h, as well as that of 
developing a correct motivation. The concluding  activities of a medi tation session include 
those activities that are usually done during a retreat. With resp ect to the activity of eating 
food, for example, one first has to do the prep aration of cutting vegetables, putting them in 
the pot, cooking them, setting the table, and so  forth. After that, one actually engages in 
eating the food – the actual activity. Having eaten, there are the conclu ding activities to do 
such as washing the dishes and pots, rinsing one’s mouth, and so forth.  
 During the actual meditation session, both an alytical meditation and stabilizing meditation 
are needed. Having understood  emptiness correctly, one then  needs to remember or be 
mindful of it during meditation on suchness. If  one does not meditate like this, it will not 
become meditation on suchness. In other words,  if one does not meditate from within the 
correct view of emptiness, it will not become a meditation on suchness. Thus, to first do a 
956 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
little bit of analysis of the co rrect view and then to abide wi thout contemplating anything at 
all, is not meditation on emptin ess or suchness, but is similar to  sleeping. Also to understand 
emptiness a little bit and, having become mindfu l of it, to meditate merely setting the mind 
on it, is nothing more than meditation on ca lm abiding; it is not meditation on special 
insight. This is not the meaning of the scriptures that describe  a mode of sustaining special 
insight that is different from the mode of sustaining calm abiding. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the need for alternating analysis and stabilization:) 
Therefore, within individually analyzing the meaning of selflessness by means of 
wisdom as explained before, you should sustain [the view]. If analytical meditation 
is solely done, the calm abiding generate d earlier will degenerate. Therefore, you 
should sustain analysis having mounted the horse of calm abiding and then in 
between alternate it with stabilizing meditation. 
 
It is necessary to alternate analysis and stabiliz ation. Therefore, through individually analyzing 
the meaning of the selflessness of persons or  the selflessness of phenomena by means of 
wisdom, one should sustain specia l insight. If one does analytic al meditation alone, the calm 
abiding that was developed previously will de generate. Therefore, having mounted the horse 
of calm abiding, one should do analysis, alte rnating analytical meditation with stabilizing 
meditation. Just as a horse is a vehicle by means of which one travels more easily and without becoming tired, so too does calm abiding make  analytical meditation easier and less tiring. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, if through too much analysis the factor of stability lessens, stabilizing meditation is needed, and if too 
much stabilization harms [the ability] to analyze, great analysis is needed:) 
Furthermore, if through too much analytic al meditation the factor of stability 
lessens, you should perform much stabilizin g meditation and reinstate the factor of 
stability. If through too much stabilizing meditation you do not want to analyze or, 
though analyzing, it is unworkable and th e mind entirely goes into the factor of 
stability, you should perform much analytical meditation. 
 
If too much analytical meditation is done, the fa ctor of abiding on the object single-pointedly 
will diminish, whereupon stabilizing meditation will become necessary. If, on the other hand, 
too much stabilizing meditation is done, it will harms analytical meditation, whereupon 
analysis becomes necessary. In other words, if too much analytical meditation of the observed 
object is done, the ability to remain single-p ointed on the object will diminish, whereupon 
one should do more stabilizing meditation, conc entrating on the object, and thereby reinstate 
the factor of abiding, that is, reinstat e the ability to abide on the object.  
 If, on the other hand, one does too much st abilizing meditation whereby the mind does 
not want to analyze or, even though one analyz es, the analysis does not go well and the mind 
tends to want to abide single-pointedly on the object, one should do more analytical 
meditation.  
 
957 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 If the two—calm abiding and special insight—are cultivated in continuous equality, 
it is very powerful; therefore, you should do it that way. The last of Kamalashila’s 
[three works on the] Stages of Meditation  says: 
When, due to having cultivated special insight, wisdom becomes too 
dominant, calm abiding is lessened. Thereby, like a butterlamp set in a 
breeze, the mind wavers, due to which suchness is not seen very clearly. 
Therefore, at that time you should cultivate calm abiding. Also, when 
calm abiding is predominant, you will, like a person asleep, not see 
suchness very clearly. Therefore, at that time you should cultivate wisdom. 
 
Therefore, it is necessary to cultivate calm abiding and special insight in equal parts, for 
example, if one does two hours of meditation, one should do one hour of stabilizing 
meditation and one hour of analytical meditati on, whereby both will become very powerful. 
Kamalashila likewise says that if one cultiv ates special insight whereby wisdom becomes 
predominant, one’s calm abiding will diminish. Like the flame of a butterlamp wavering in 
the wind whereby forms are not clearly seen, similarly the mind will waver and one will not be 
able to clearly see suchness. At that time it is  necessary to cultivate ca lm abiding more. If, on 
the other hand, calm abiding is predominant it wi ll be like a person who has fallen asleep and 
does not see or remember anything , in that suchness will not be s een clearly. At that time one 
should cultivate wisdom or special insight or analytical meditation on emptiness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 369) says: 
(Fourth, it is not correct to hold that all thoughts are conceptions of signs, that is, conceptions of true 
existence, and hence stop analytical meditation:) 
With respect to sustained analysis in that way, it is not correct to hold all 
conceptions whatsoever to be conceptions of signs—that is, conceptions of true 
existence. For earlier it was frequently pr oven that thoughts which are conceptions 
of true existence are just one class of conceptions. 
 
It is not correct to think that all conceptions are conceptions of signs, that is, conceptions of true existence, and therefore not do analytical  meditation. In fact there are people who say 
that conceptions such as the mind of enlightenment or compassion are the same as 
conceptions of true existence or inherent existenc e. They say that the reason for this is that 
conceptions such as the mind of enlightenment also  bind us in cyclic existence, and liken this 
to the fact that whether one is bitten by a wh ite dog or bitten by a black dog, one has been 
bitten. Thus, they say that because all concepti ons are conceptions, they must be abandoned. 
In reality there do exist such st range views or strange ideas.  
 In short, it is not correct to hold that  all conceptions are conceptions of signs or 
conception of true existence, there being many types of conceptions that are good.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
958 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
                                             (Fifth, also to think that when the ultimate is taken as a subject all conceptions by the mind are conceptions of 
signs—even though such is not asserted when a conventionality is taken as the subject—is not correct:) 
To view that whatever conceptuality conceives incurs the damage of reasoning is 
established to be a deprecation in whic h the object of reasoned negation is 
absurdly excessive; it is not the meaning of  the scriptures. If, on account of this, 
one does not assert such with regard to ot her subjects but thinks that whatever the 
mind conceives with respect to the nature  of phenomena is a conception of signs 
which is an adherence to true existence, th is also is a fallacy in which the mode of 
conception is faulty. All conceptions wh atsoever are not [conceptions of true 
existence] because it is said that one l ooking nearby [that is, one whose mind has 
not turned toward emptiness] who is seek ing release must make a determination of 
suchness through multiple approaches of scripture and reasoning. 
 
Also to think that although this  is not asserted with respect to  apprehending a basis that is a 
conventionality, all conceptions apprehending the ultimate are conceptions of signs is not 
correct. To think that whatever conceptuality apprehends is harmed by reasoning is not a 
correct recognition of the object of negation and is a deprecation. This is not the meaning of 
the scriptures and is not right. If one does not assert all conceptions with  respect to a table to 
be conceptions of true existence, but instead asserts that all concep tions with respect to 
suchness or thusness are conceptions of signs whic h are an adherence true existence, this too 
is a fallacy in which the mode of apprehension is faulty. Those who look nearby (that is, those 
who see only this life) and who are seeking liberation must determine suchness through many doors of scriptures and reasonings. In short, the conceptions that establish suchness by reasoning are not conceptions of true existence that are to be abandoned.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, a dispelling of an objection, has two parts, the first of which is: an objection:) 
Objection:  If meditation on suchness is fo r the sake of generating the non-
conceptual, it will not be generated from individual analysis because cause and 
effect must accord. 
 
First an objection is set out and then it is dispelled by means of the response. The objection is 
that if meditation on suchness is for the sake of generating non-conceptuality, it cannot be 
generated by doing analysis because cause and result must be similar. This refers to the fact 
these two are not similar in that the cause is analytical meditation and the result is a non-
conceptuality. However, this answer would imply, for example, that fr om a father who is a 
donkey and a mother who is a horse, a son that is a mule could not arise! 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The second, the reply, has ten parts, the first of which is: that the non-conceptual exalted wisdom is generated 
through individual analysis:) 
Answer:  Concerning this, the Supramundane Victor himself gave a clear answer; 
the Kashyapa Chapter77 says: 
 
77 kashyapaparivarta . 
959 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
Kashyapa, it is like this: For exampl e, from the rubbing together of two 
branches by the wind fire arises, and once arisen the two branches are 
burned up. Similarly, Kashyapa, if one has the correct analytical 
intellect, a superior’s faculty of wisdom is generated. Through its 
generation the correct analytical intellect is consumed. 
Thus, Buddha said that a superior’s wisdom  is generated from individual analysis. 
 
The first part of the response is th at the non-conceptual exalted wisdom is generated through 
individual analysis. The Buddha himself said that, for example, just as if one rubs two sticks 
together, a fire will be generated that then burns the two sticks, so to if one has correct 
individual investigation, a superior’s faculty of wisdom will be generated and, having generated it, it will consume the correct indivi dual investigation. In other words, having 
generated a superior’s non-conceptual exalted wisdom, the individual investigation ceases. 
Therefore, a superior’s wisdom is gene rated from individual investigation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 370) says: 
(Second, if you analyze this way, non-conceptual wisdom will be generated; by just abandoning mental 
application it will not:) 
Kamalashila’s second [of three works on the] Stages of Meditation  says: 
They analyze in that way with wisd om. Then, when yogis definitely do 
not apprehend an entityness of anything ultimately, they enter into the 
non-conceptual meditative stabilization. They also realize the non-
entityness of all phenomena. The co nceptuality of those who do not 
cultivate individual analysis on the en tityness of things with wisdom but 
cultivate only just an abandonment of mental application will never be 
reversed. Also, they will never real ize non-entityness due to not having 
the illumination of wisdom. For, the Supramundane Victor himself s a i d  t h a t  w h e n  t h e  f i r e  o f  k n o w i n g  t h e  r e a l  j u s t  a s  i t  i s  a r i s e s  f r o m  c o r r e c t  i n d i v i d u a l  a n a l y s i s  l i k e  t h e  f i r e  o f  s t i c k s  r u b b e d  t o g e t h e r ,  t h e  wood of conceptuality is burned. 
 
If one analyzes in this way, one will generate  a non-conceptual exalted wisdom, whereas one 
will not generate it by abandoning mental a ttention. Kamalashila says that when, through 
thoroughly analyzing with wisdom, yogis definite ly do not apprehend an entitiness of things 
ultimately, they enter into a non-conceptual medita tive stabilization. At that time they realize 
the non-entitiness of all phenomena, that is, they realize that all phenomena do not exist 
inherently or truly. However, for those who do not meditate through individually 
investigating with wisdom the entitiness of th ings, but instead cultivate the abandonment of 
mental attention, their conceptuality will never be eliminated. Also they will never realize the emptiness of inherent or true existence due to not having the illumination of wisdom. This is 
because the Buddha himself said that correct individual investigation brings about the non-conceptual knowing of reality. Just like fire arises from rubbing two sticks together and then burns the same two sticks, so too does the non-conceptual mind of an arya’s meditative equipoise arise from conceptuality and then burn it up.

ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
 
Tuesday afternoon, 13 March 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 370) says: 
(Third, it would be very absurd if cause and effect had to be similar in all respects:) 
If such were not the case, then the ar ising of the uncontaminated from the 
contaminated, of the supramundane fr om the mundane, of a buddha from a 
sentient being, or a superior from a common being, and so forth, would not occur 
because cause and effect would be dissimilar. 
 
If cause and result had to be similar in all resp ects it would be very absurd. For example, if 
cause and result had to be similar in all respec ts, then the arising of an uncontaminated path 
from a contaminated path, the arising of a supramundane path from a mundane path, the 
arising of a buddha from a sentient being, the ar ising of a superior from an ordinary being, 
and so forth would be impossible because these causes and their result are dissimilar. In other 
words, the contaminated and th e uncontaminated are dissimilar, as are the supramundane 
and mundane, a buddha and a sentient being, an ordinary being and a superior. Likewise, 
also an infant becoming a youth, becoming an adult, and becoming an old man or woman 
could not happen because they are dissimilar in that , for example, the baby of five of six days 
and the 100 year old man or woman he or  she becomes are completely dissimilar. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, the deriding of adhering to object of analysis, analyzer, and so forth in Nagarjuna’s Essay on the Mind 
of Enlightenment  and so forth indicate a refutation of adherence to true existence; they do not refute the 
wisdom of individual analysis:) 
Nagarjuna’s Essay on the Mind of Enlightenment  says: 
How could emptiness be where 
Conceptuality has appeared? 
The tathagatas do not perceive 
Minds having the aspect of object analyzed and analyzer. 
Enlightenment is not present where 
Object of analysis and analyzer exist. 
 
How can emptiness appear to a mind that has th e appearance of true existence? The object 
analyzed and the mind that has the aspect of an analyzer are not seen by the tathagatas, 
meaning that a truly existent object of analysis  and a truly existent analyzer are not seen by 
them. There is no enlightenment as long as obje ct of analysis and anal yzer are apprehended to 
be truly existent. All phenomena do not exist inherently, therefore the object analyzed and 
the analyzer also do not exist inherently. This la ck of true existence needs to be analyzed with 
wisdom.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
He is indicating that one wh o has the conception of true  existence with respect to 
an object analyzed and an analyzer has no attainment of enlightenment. If he were 
961 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
refuting the wisdom of individual analysis and were refuting mere object analyzed 
and analyzer, it would be contradictory for him to settle suchness in that text 
through many approaches of investigation by way of individual analysis. Also, if 
buddhas did not perceive those tw o, they would be non-existent. 
 
The verse from Nagarjuna’s text indicates that  one who has a conception of true existence 
with respect to an object analyzed and an anal yzer cannot attain enlightenment. He does not 
refute the wisdom of individual investigatio ns nor the mere object analyzed and mere 
analyzer, otherwise it would be contradictory fo r him to settle suchness in that same text by 
many doors of the analysis of individual inve stigation. If buddhas did not see the object 
analyzed and the analyzer, they would be non-ex istent, however buddhas see them so they do 
exist; what they do not see is that they are truly existent. The object analyzed and the analyzer 
are not like the horns of a rabbit which are not seen by anyone. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Also, the same text says: 
One is not to meditate on the emptiness 
Called “non-production,” “emptiness,” 
And “selflessness” which is meditated 
On as having a low nature. 
He is not refuting meditation observin g selflessness—the emptiness which is the 
non-existence of inherently existent produc tion; rather he is refuting meditation 
on a low emptiness having the low nature of being conceived to exist truly.  
 
Nagarjuna is not refuting meditation on th e emptiness that is called “non-production.” 
“emptiness,” or “selflessness,” which is the empt iness that is a lack of inherently existent 
production, but is refuting meditation on an inferior emptin ess that is conceived to be 
inherently existent – that is, on an inherently existent non-production, an inherently existent 
emptiness, and an inherently existent selflessness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
It is as Nagarjuna’s Praise of the Supramundane  says: 
Since in order to remove all conceptuality 
The ambrosia of emptiness was taught, 
One who adheres to it [as truly existent] 
Is greatly derided by you. 
 
As Nagarjuna says, in order to eliminate all co nceptuality, that is, the conception of true 
existence of all phenomena, the Buddha taught the nectar or ambrosia of emptiness. Those 
who adhere to it as truly existent, that is, who think that emptiness is truly existent, are greatly 
derided by him.  
 
962 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
                                             Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Similarly, Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland78 also says: 
In that way self and selflessness 
Are not properly apprehendable as reals. 
Therefore the Great Subduer rejected 
Views of self and selflessness. 
 
Nagarjuna’s Precious Garland  also says that since self and selflessness are not observed to be 
truly or inherently existent, the Great Muni reje cted the views of self and selflessness. Neither 
the self or selflessness exist truly or inherently . For example, just as the table does not exist 
inherently, neither does the emptiness or se lflessness of the table exist inherently. The 
apprehension of such true existenc e must therefore be eliminated.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
This means that since both self and self lessness are not established in reality, 
[Buddha] rejected the views that those two really exist; he did not refute the view 
of selflessness. For, like the passage from Nagarjuna’s Refutation of Objections 
quoted earlier, if [phenomena] were not without inherent existe nce in the sense of 
being inherently established, inherent establishment would exist. 
 
Therefore, both self and selflessness are not esta blished in reality, that is, they do not exist 
inherently. Because of that, the Buddha rejected the views that the self and selflessness really 
exist, but he did not refute the view of selflessness. As is said in Nagarjuna’s text, if 
phenomena were not without inherent existence then inherent existenc e would exist, whereas 
this is not the case. All phenomena have two en tities, a conventional entity and an ultimate 
entity, but these entities are the same in not existing truly. Therefore, also emptiness does not 
exist truly, that is, emptiness is empty of inhe rent existence. This is what is called “the 
emptiness of emptiness” from among the sixt een types of emptiness. If emptiness were 
established inherently or truly, then meditati on on emptiness would not cease ignorance, the 
root of cyclic existence, and would not bring about the achievement of nirvana. Therefore, 
also emptiness does not exist truly or inherently.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, similarly the statement in sutra about acting in signs refers to the signs of true establishment:) 
The Condensed Perfection of Wisdom Sutra  says: 
Even if a bodhisattva conceives, “Thi s aggregate is empty,” he is acting 
in signs and does not have faith in the topic of non-production. 
The great mother [a Perfection of Wisdom Sutra ] says, “If one considers, ‘Form is 
empty and selfless,’ one is acting in signs and is not acting in the perfection of 
wisdom.” The meaning of these statements  refers to holding emptiness and so 
 
78 Stanza 103. 
963 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
                                             forth to exist truly. 
 
The statement in sutra about acting in signs or utilizing signs refers to having signs of true 
establishment. The Condensed Perfection of Wisdom Sutra says that if a bodhisattva conceives 
“This hollow is empty,79” he is acting in signs or utilizing signs and does not have faith in the 
topic of non-production . The Great Mother, the 100,000 Stanza Perfection of Wisdom Sutra , 
says that if one conceives “Form is empty and selfless,” one is acting in or utilizing signs, that 
is, one is apprehending the emptin ess of form to be inherently ex istent. Because of this, one is 
not acting in or utilizing the perfection of wisdom. Thus, these statements refer to apprehending emptiness to exist truly. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 372) says: 
(Sixth, if that were not so, it would contradict the praise in sutras for analyzing emptiness with wisdom:) 
If that were not the case, it would not be right for Buddha to say “does not have 
faith in the topic of non-production,” becaus e to have faith in that would be to act 
in signs. Also, it would contradict many sutras; the same sutra says, “One who 
thoroughly knows all phenomena as without inherent existence is acting in the 
supreme perfection of wisdom,” and: 
When wisdom breaks down caused and uncaused phenomena 
As well as the wholesome and unwholesome,  not even particles are apprehended. 
At that time in the world this is counted as the perfection of wisdom. 
 
If it were not like that, it would not be suitab le for the Buddha to say “Does not have faith in 
the topic of non-production,” that is, does not have faith in the lack of inherent existence, 
because to have faith in that would be to act in signs, that is, signs of inherent existence. The 
same sutras say that that one who thorough ly knows all phenomena as without inherent 
existence is utilizing the supreme perfection of  wisdom. It also says that when analytical 
meditation dissects compounded phenomena and uncompounded phenomena, as well as white phenomena, that is, virtuous actions, and black phenomena, that is, non-virtuous 
actions, not even a particle of true existence is  observed. In the world that is counted as the 
perfection of wisdom.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, the King of Meditative Stabilizations Sutra  says: 
If phenomena are individually analyzed as selfless 
And what has been analyzed is meditated upon, 
That is the cause for attaining the fruit, nirvana. 
Through any other cause one does not go to peace. 
 
 
79 JN: The Tibetan text we are following reads phug, meaning hollow or cavern, whereas Hopkins translates 
“aggregates” ( phung ).

FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso
