                                             If a phenomenon is individually investigated  as selfless, and having been individually 
investigated is meditated upon, this becomes the cause for attaining the result that is nirvana. 
By means of other causes, the conception of tr ue existence is not pacified, and nirvana, the 
result, cannot be attained.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, in the Essence of the Perfec tion of Wisdom Sutra80 Shariputra asks, “How does a 
bodhisattva who wants to practice the prof ound perfection of wisdom train?” In 
answer Avalokiteshvara says, “He should th oroughly and correctly view even these 
five aggregates as empty of inherent existence” and so forth. 
 
In the Heart Sutra  Shariputra asks Avalokiteshvara, “H ow should a bodhisattva who wants to 
practice the profound perfection of wisdom train?” To this, Avalokiteshvara replies: “He 
should thoroughly and correctly view even th ese five aggregates as empty of inherent 
existence. Form is empty, emptiness is form. Emptiness is not other than form, and form is 
not other than emptiness.” If, on the other hand, one thinks that to act in or utilize inherent 
existence is the perfection of wisdom, this would contradict such sutras. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Seventh, Nagarjuna and Chandrakirti praise individual investigation and analysis of the meaning of 
selflessness as the cause of release:) 
Therefore, Nagarjuna’s Praise of the Element of Qualities  says: 
The doctrine supremely purifying the mind 
Is the absence of inherent existence. 
 
Nagarjuna and Chandrakirti praise individual investigation and analysis of the meaning of 
emptiness as the cause of liberation. Nagarjuna says that the Dharma that supremely purifies 
or trains the mind is the la ck of inherent existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also: 
As long as “self” and “mine” are conceived 
So long is there [false] imputation of the external. 
When the two types of selflessness are seen, 
The seeds of cyclic existence cease. 
 
Due to apprehending “self” and “mine,” for that long is there imputation of the external. If 
the two types of selflessness – the selflessness of persons and the selflessness of phenomena – 
are seen, the seeds of existence will cease. The main seed of cyclic existence is ignorance 
together with its imprints. A seed is that wh ich acts as a cause connecting a past cyclic 
existence with another cyclic ex istence. To see the selflessness of persons and selflessness of 
 
80 prajñaparamitahrdaya . 
965 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
phenomena is to realize them. In  this context “phenomena” refe rs to the aggregates. To see 
the selflessness of persons is to realize that the being does not exist inherently or truly, and to 
see the selflessness of phenomena is to realize that the aggregates that belong to that being do 
not exist inherently or truly. By seeing or re alizing them, the seeds of cyclic existence are 
ceased and one is freed from cyclic existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Chandrakirti’s Supplement to (Nagarjuna’s) “Treatise on the Middle”  says: 
Therefore, through the view of the emptiness 
Of self and mine a yogi will be released. 
You should understand this in accordance  with those statements and sustain the 
continuum of ascertainment of selflessness and the absence of inherent existence. 
 
Chandrakirti also says that by the view of the emptiness of self and mine, or I and mine, a 
yogi will be liberated. In other words, by seeing that the self and mine, the aggregates, are empty of inherent existence through a meditati on or yoga conjoined with wisdom, one will 
attain liberation. One should understand selfle ssness in accordance with  such statements and 
sustain the continuum of the ascertainment of se lflessness and the lack of inherent existence. 
In other words, one should meditate on them continuously, as if one meditates on them for a 
brief time, then gives it up, and then resumes meditating, one will not be able to become free 
from the conception of true existence. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 373) says: 
(Eighth, also the meaning of the Non-Conceptual Retention Sutra  is not just to abandon mental application; 
rather, its thought is to analyze with wisdom and set [the mind] in that which is free from the target of the 
conception of true existence:) 
The first of Kamalashila’s [three works on the] Stages of Meditation  says: 
The Non-Conceptual Retention Sutra  says, “Through not doing mental 
application the signs of forms an d so forth are abandoned.” This 
statement refers to the fact that if  one analyzes with wisdom, one will 
not apply to mind that which is unob servable [as truly existent] in that 
manner [that is, as truly existent]. It does not mean that there is [to be] 
no mental application. This is not an abandoning through the mere 
abandonment of mental application like that of one in the meditative absorption of non-discrimination who forsakes taking to mind the 
beginningless attachment to  forms and so forth. 
 
Kamalashila cites a sutra that says “By not mentally attending, the signs of forms and so forth 
are abandoned.” This means that if one invest igates with wisdom, one will not mentally 
a t t e n d  t o  t h a t  w h i c h  i s  u n o b s e r v a b l e .  I t  d o e s  n o t  m e a n  t h a t  t h e r e  i s  n o  m e r e  m e n t a l  
attention. It is also not an abandonment by merely abandoning the mentally attention that 
strongly adheres to forms and so forth from be ginningless time, like the meditative absorption 
of without discrimination.

                                             Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Thus, the descriptions in the scriptures  to abandon the conception of signs 
through cultivating the absence of mental application refer to setting in equipoise 
on the meaning of realizing the non-apprehension of the target of the conception 
of true existence—even a particle—through analysis by way of the proper analytical wisdom. 
 
This means that although the scriptures say to abandon the conception of signs through 
cultivating a lack of mental attention, they re fer to equipoising on the meaning of realizing 
that the observed object of the conception of true existence is unobservable, even a particle, 
through investigating with the wisdom that analyzes properly. 
 
Wednesday morning, 14 March 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 373) says: 
(Ninth, the Stages of Meditation  quotes the Cloud of Jewels Sutra81
 and says that its meaning is not just to 
abandon mental application but to enter into signlessness just through meditation analyzing the meaning of 
emptiness:) 
The second [of Kamalashil a’s three works on the] Stages of Meditation  says: 
[The Cloud of Jewels Sutra  says,] “...When he analyzes what the mind is, 
he realizes it as empty. When he exam ines by what mind that is realized, 
he realizes it as empty. Through realizing such he enters into the yoga of 
signlessness.” This indicates that wh oever does not analyze in this way 
will not enter into signlessness. It very clearly indicates that through 
merely abandoning mental applic ation and without analyzing the 
entityness of things with wisdom, it  is not possible to enter into non-
conceptuality. 
 
Kamalashila says that when one searches for the what the mind is, one realizes it is empty. 
When one searches for the entity of that mind that realizes it is empty, one realizes that it too 
is empty. Thereby, one enters into the yoga  of signlessness. For example, having asked 
whether the mind exists inherently or not, one fi nds that it does not, rather it is empty of 
inherent existence. also having asked whether the mind that realizes that exists inherently or 
not, one finds that it does not exist inherent ly and cannot be found. By means of this, one 
enters the yoga of signlessness. 
 Therefore, this citation clearly shows that th rough thoroughly investigating in this way, one 
enters signlessness and that through merely  abandoning mental attention and without 
analyzing the entity of things with wisdom, it is  not possible to enter non-conceptuality. Thus, 
in short, one must first understand and invest igate emptiness, after which one can enter the 
non-conceptual meditative equipoise of a superior. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
81 ratnamegha . 
967 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
                                             Thus, the statement in the Cloud of Jewels Sutra  explains that if one has not found 
the view of suchness through prior analysis in the proper way, it is impossible to 
understand non-conceptually  the meaning of suchness. 
 
This sutra explains that if one has not foun d the view of suchness by prior and proper 
analysis, it will not be possible to enter non- conceptuality on the meaning of suchness.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 374) says: 
(Tenth, an exhortation about the need to understand the refutation in the Stages of Meditation  of the bad 
propositions of the Chinese Ho-shang82
 with regard to asserting that the non-conceptual is entered through 
abandoning mental application:) 
The last [of Kamalashila’s three works on the] Stages of Meditation  explains that:  
In order to refute claims that the profound meaning [of emptiness] can 
be realized merely through hearing and thinking, [the sutras] speak of the inconceivable, that which is beyond the mind, and so forth, 
indicating that because those are objects known by a superior’s own 
individual [meditative equipoise] they  are inconceivable by others, and 
so forth. Also, these were taught for the sake of refuting improper 
contemplation upon conceiving the meaning of the profound 
[emptiness] to exist truly. They do not refute proper mental application 
by means of the wisdom of individual analysis. It is said that if such were refuted, it would contradict a great many reasonings and scriptures. 
Also, although it is an entity of conceptuality, it is an entity of proper mental application, whereby the non- conceptual exalted wisdom arises, 
and thus since one wants that exalte d wisdom, one should rely on it. 
 
The Chinese Hvashang, who was a mahayanist, says that without any mental attention at all, 
that is, without thinking about anything at a ll, it is possible to enter non-conceptuality. 
However, Kamalashila says that the scriptures  teach the inconceivable, that passed beyond 
mind, and so forth in order to refute those wh o say that it is possible to realize emptiness 
merely through hearing and thinking. Thereby th ese sutras indicate that because they are 
objects known only by a superior’s meditative equipoise, they are inconceivable by others and 
so forth. Thus, they were taught to refute th e improper thought that emptiness exists truly. 
They do not refute proper analysis by means of the wisdom of individual investigation. If such 
were refuted, it would contradict many reason ings and many scriptural citations. Although 
mental attention is the entity of conceptuality, it is the entity  of proper mental attention. 
Therefore, from it arises a non-conceptual exalted wisdom. If one wishes to achieve this non-
conceptual exalted wisdom, one should rely on su ch mental attention. In other words, by first 
doing analytical meditation that investigates the entity of all phenomena, how they abide, and 
so forth, one can develop a non-conceptual exalted wisdom. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
82 hva shang . 
968 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
                                             It is very important to understand th rough Kamalashila’s explanations how to 
refute the Chinese abbot who propounded that although the view ascertaining 
suchness cannot be gained in dependence on scripture and reasoning, suchness 
can be realized through a setting in meditative equipoise in which nothing at all is 
taken to mind. 
 
It is important to understand how to refute the Chinese abbot who prop ounded that in order 
to gain the view ascertaining suchness, that is , the exalted wisdom of meditative equipoise of 
suchness, it is not necessary to depend on script ures or reasonings, but that it is enough to 
remain without thinking anything at all. This tradition is that of Zen Buddhism, which says 
that it is enough to sit in meditation with out thinking. There is a story regarding this 
assertion that took place in Samye Monastery in  Tibet. Kamalashila was to debate there with 
Hvashang, and they agree beforehand that th e loser would give up his own tenet system 
f o l l o w  t h a t  o f  t h e  w i n n e r .  H o w e v e r ,  H v a s h a n g  having lost the debate, flew the monastery, 
leaving behind one of his shoes. This was taken to be a sign that his view would continue to 
flourish in Tibet and in fact it  still exists nowadays. This sch ool says that by stopping all 
mental attention, realizations will come. This would make the attainment of realizations very 
easy! 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(The second, that these modes of sustaining [the view] are the word of earlier scholars and adepts such as 
Atisha, has two parts, the first of which is: the earlier Ga-dam-bas’ way of explaining the thought of the Elder 
[Atisha]:) 
These ways of meditating have arisen from  earlier precepts on the stages of the 
path. Bo-do-wa’s83 Small Vessel  says: 
Some say at the time of hearing and thinking 
To settle the absence of inherent existence through reasoning 
And when meditating to cultiv ate only the non-conceptual. 
Thus, because a separate unrelated emptiness 
Is meditated, it will not serve as an antidote. 
Therefore, even at th e time of meditation 
Analyze individually with whatever you are familiar— 
The lack of being one or many, dependent-arising, and so forth 
And stay a little also in the non-conceptual. 
 
Theses ways of meditating have come from ea rlier instructions on the stages of the path. 
Geshe Potowa, for example, says that some people say that at the time of hearing and 
thinking, one should settle the lack of inherent existence by reasoning. Then, at the time of 
meditation, one should only medi tate without thinking, abiding as though one were a statue. 
If it were like this, the emptiness that is unde rstood by analysis and the emptiness that is a 
lack of thought at the time of  meditation would be completely unrelated. Instead one would 
meditate on something other than emptiness an d it would not become an antidote to the 
afflictions or to the root of cyclic existence, th e conception of true existence. Therefore, also 
 
83 po to ba . 
969 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
at the time of meditation one should individually investigate phenomena by means of 
whatever reasoning one is most familiar with, such as the reasoning of being free from being 
one or many, the reasoning of dependent-arisin g, and so forth. Thus, if one takes, for 
example, the conventional nature of the mind of being clear and knowing as one’s object of 
meditation , one should analyze its lack of true or  inherent existence by thinking that it is not 
inherently existent because it is not an inherent ly existent one and because it is also not an 
inherently existent many. The conventional nature  of the mind is not an inherently existent 
one because it has parts. That with parts and that without parts are mutually contradictory. 
That without parts does not exist. By applying such reasons, one should  understand that the 
c l e a r  a n d  k n o w i n g  n a t u r e  o f  t h e  m i n d  i s  n o t  a n  i n h e r e n t l y  e x i s t e n t  o n e .  I t  i s  a l s o  n o t  a n  
inherently existent many because it is not an in herently existent one. This is because in order 
for there to be many, there must  be one. Therefore, the conclu sion is that the conventional 
nature of the mind of being clear and knowing do es not exist inherently because it is not an 
inherently existent one or an inherently existe nt many. One should medi tate in this way on 
the emptiness of the conventional nature of th e mind. This is an example of one type of 
reasoning but if it is easier to understand, on e can apply the reasoning that the mind does not 
exist inherently or truly because it is a depend ent-arising. By such reasonings one can cut the 
extremes of permanence and annihilation. Ha ving become familiar with one of these 
reasonings, one should abid e in non-conceptuality.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Those following the sole deity [A tisha] assert that meditation 
In this way serves as the antidote to the afflictive emotions. 
This is the system of cultivating wisdom 
By those wishing to practice th e system of the perfections. 
Further, one familiarizing with the selflessness of persons 
Should then enter into su ch [non-conceptuality]. 
 
If one meditates in this way, one’s meditation wi ll become an antidote to the afflictions. This 
is the Prasangika Madhyamaka system of cultivat ing wisdom by those who wish to practice the 
perfection system. Those who have familiariz ed with the selflessness of persons through 
reasonings – such as that the person is not inhe rently existent because it is not an inherently 
existent one and not an inherent ly existent many, that it is not self-sufficient substantially 
existent because it is not a self-sufficient su bstantially existent one or a self-sufficient 
substantially existent many, that it is not inherently existent because it is a dependent-arising, 
and so forth – should enter into non-conceptuality.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 375) says: 
(Second, between the two, Madhyamika and Chittamatra, the Elder praised the father Nagarjuna and his 
spiritual sons and the Elder’s style of quintessential instructions accords with the master Kamalashila:) 
Furthermore, the Elder [Atisha] said: 
Through whom should emptiness be realized?  
Through Chandrakirti, the student of Nagarjuna 
970 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
Who was prophesied by the Tathagata  
And saw reality, the truth. 
Through the quintessential instructions transmitted 
From them, reality, the truth, will be realized. 
Atisha set forth their mode of instructions as Madhyamika precepts, and [I] have 
explained those and the thought of the ma ster Kamalashila which appear to be 
similar. 
 
There are two schools of mahayana tenets, the Chittamatra and Madhyamaka, but Atisha followed Nagarjuna and his spiritual sons, that  is, the Madhyamaka. Th e Chittamatra tenets, 
on the other hand, come through Asanga, Vasubandhu, and others. Kamalashila’s thought 
resembles that of Atisha.  
 Atisha’s text poses the question: through wh om is emptiness realized? It then responds 
saying that it is through th e instructions transmitted from  Chandrakirti, as student of 
Nagarjuna was prophesied by the Tathagata and who saw reality, the truth. In other words, 
through realizing their instructions, one will re alize reality. Atisha set forth their way of 
explaining as Madhyamaka instructions. Their thoughts and Kamalashila’s thought appear to 
be similar. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, the features of the preparation, end, and so forth of how to sustain [the view]:) 
For sustaining special insight you should  know the mode of observing the six 
preparatory practices, how to sustain the actual session and its conclusion, how to 
act between sessions, and especially how to sustain the session free from laxity and 
excitement as explained before [in the section on calm abiding]. 
 
First there are the preparatory practices for medi tation, then the actual meditation, and then 
the concluding activities. For example, one who wi shes to cultivate special insight should first 
rely on the six preparatory practices, as well as perform the practice that combines the Offering 
to the Spiritual Master (Lama Chopa ) with the Preparatory Practice ( Jor Cho ) as suggested by 
Kyabje Thubten Zopa Rinpoche. Then, during the actual meditation session one should 
meditation on the various points of the stages of the path (lam-rim). At the end, one should 
do some concluding prayers and dedications. In between sessions, one should relax but 
without allowing oneself to be overcome by laxity and excitement. In other words, one should keep the mind quiet and relaxed without letti ng it wander to too many objects due to 
thinking “This is nice. This is beautiful. I wa nt it” or “Today the food is no good. I want 
better food.” Nor should one think “It would be  nice to go into town, to watch a movie…” 
One should also not allow oneself to come unde r the power of sinking or laxity, a lack of 
clarity. Between sessions one should do on e’s other daily commitments, including the 
recitation of mantras and sadhanas.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 375) says: 
971 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
11 Harmony 
The measure of having established sp ecial insight through cultivation 
(This has five parts, the first of whic h is: that special insight has been achieved when analytical meditation generates 
pliancy:) 
When you have meditated in that way analyzing by way of the wisdom of 
individual analysis, there are similitudes of  special insight [which arise] prior to 
generating the pliancy described earlier [in the section on calm abiding]. Then 
when pliancy is generated, there is a full y qualified special insight. The entity and 
mode of generating pliancy are as explained earlier. 
 
When one meditates through analyz ing with the wisdom of individu al investigation, there is a 
similitude of special insight that occurs prior to generating pliancy of body and mind. Having 
generated that pliancy, there is a fully-qualified  special insight. The en tity of pliancy and how 
to generate it were explained earlier in the text . Pliancy is a lightness of mind that produces a 
lightness of body such that it no  longer feels heavy. This produces a bliss or happiness of body 
that then induces mental bliss. When the pliancy that induces bliss is held by wisdom, it is an 
actual special insight or a full y-qualified special insight.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the mere pliancy of calm abiding is not sufficient:) 
Because one also has the pliancy induced by the calm abiding—already achieved 
and not deteriorated—[the achievement of sp ecial insight] is not the mere presence 
of pliancy. 
 
The mere pliancy of calm abiding is not en ough. Although one has achieved the pliancy 
induced by achieving calm abiding and its not ha ving deteriorated, special insight is not just 
the presence of this pliancy. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Question : Then, what is it? 
Answer:  When the power of having performed anal ytical meditation itself is able to 
induce pliancy, there is special insight. It  is the same for both  the special insight 
observing the varieties and the special insight observing the mode. 
 
When analytical meditation is able to induce  pliancy by its own power, one has achieved 
special insight. It is the same  for both the special insight ob serving varieties and the special 
insight observing modes. In other words, in bo th cases pliancy is induced by the power of 
analytical meditation.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 376) says: 
(Third, a source for the measure of havi ng achieved special insi ght as being thus:) 
In that way the Sutra Unraveling the Thought  says: 
“Supramundane Victor, until a bodhisattva has attained physical and 
972 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
mental pliancy what is the name fo r a mental contemplation which is 
an internal taking to mind of an image—an object of meditative 
stabilization—of those phenomena which have been contemplated 
well?” 
“Maitreya, it is not special insight; it  should be said that it accompanies 
an imitation which is a similitude of special insight.” 
 
The scriptural source for the measure of having achieved special insight is the Sutra Unraveling 
the Thought . This sutra sets out a question pose d by Maitreya to the Buddha, “Until a 
bodhisattva has attained physical and mental pliancy, what is the meditative stabilization of 
emptiness or suchness that is not an actual specia l insight but is like a reflection of it?” The 
Buddha answers Maitreya, one of the eight close bo dhisattvas, saying, “It is not actual special 
insight. It is concomitant with the aspiration  that is a similitude of special insight.”  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Also, Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom  says: 
He abides in just that attainment of mental and physical pliancy. He 
individually analyzes the meaning of just what was contemplated 
through intense interest in the object which is an internal image of 
meditative stabilization. As long as  mental and physical pliancy are not 
generated, it is a mental contemplation which is a similitude of special 
insight. When those are generated, it is special insight. 
 
Also Ratnakarashanti says that one abides in th e attainment of mental and physical pliancy. 
As long as the mental and physical pliancy is not induced by analytical meditation on 
suchness, it is only a similitude of special insight,  like a reflection of it. In other words, as long 
as one has not generated mental and physical pliancy by means of analysis, one has not yet 
attained an actual special insight but has attain ed only a similitude of it. When mental and 
physical pliancy are generated by the power of an alysis, it becomes an actual special insight.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
Fourth, when pliancy is induced by analysis, st eady meditative stabilization is induced:) 
When the power of [analysis] itself is able  to induce pliancy, it is also able to 
induce a one-pointedness of mind. Thus, this inducing of calm abiding through 
the power of the analytical meditation of individual analysis is a quality [arising 
from] having established calm abiding previously. 
 
When pliancy is induced by analysis, at this ti me a steady meditative stabilization is also 
induced. In other words, when analysis is able to induce pliancy by its own power, one is also able to induce a single-pointed mind. The calm abiding induced by the power of an analytical 
meditation of individual investigation is a quality that comes from the previously 
accomplished calm abiding. In other words, due to the pliancy induced by analysis, one’s calm 
abiding becomes more stable.  
973 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, therefore wrong ideas are unsuitable:) 
Since in that way calm abiding becomes far more developed through the analytical 
meditation performed by one who has achieved calm abiding well, you should not hold that if the analytical meditation of individual analysis is done, the factors of 
stability will be lessened. 
 
Having perfectly attained calm abiding, one th en engages in analytical meditation, whereby 
one’s calm abiding becomes even stronger. Therefore, although some people think that by 
doing analytical meditation the factor of abiding of the mind on its object will diminish, this 
is incorrect. 
 In short, having attained the pliancy of ca lm abiding, one should engage in analytical 
meditation whereby the pliancy of body and mind induced by analysis make the pliancy and 
single-pointedness of calm abiding even stronger. 
 
Wednesday afternoon, 14 March 2007 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 376) says: 
The mode of union of calm abiding and special insight 
(This section has two parts, the first of which is: the actual explanation, which itself has six parts. The first is: 
The need to attain calm abiding and specia l insight in order to have their union:) 
If calm abiding and special insight are not attained as explained at the point of the 
measure of their establishment, there will  be nothing to be unified. Therefore, 
those two must definitely be attained for [there to be] a union. 
 
If the two, calm abiding and special insight, are not attained as was explained on the occasion 
of explaining the measure of their achievement, then as long as neither of them or one of 
them is not attained there canno t be their union. Therefore, for there to be union, both 
definitely need to be attained. When talking ab out the union of two things, as long as only 
one of a pair is present there cannot be a discussion of union.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the attainment of special insight and the attainment of a union are simultaneous:) 
Furthermore, from the beginning of attaining special insight a union is attained. With regard to how this is done: when , through the force of  having performed 
analytical meditation in dependence on the earlier calm abiding, one attains the 
mental activity of natural engagement without the activity [of applying the 
antidotes to laxity and excitement] as ex plained earlier in the section on calm 
abiding, it becomes a union. 
 
When special insight is first attained, a union of calm abiding and special insight is also 
simultaneously attained. Therefore, right from the beginning when special insight is attained, 
a union is also attained. How this is done is th at when, by the force of analytical meditation 
974 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
in dependence on the previously attained calm abiding, one attains the mental attention that 
engages naturally without the need fo r exertion, they become unified.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, a scripture on the need for harmonizing calm  abiding and special insight in equal strength:) 
Asanga’s Grounds of Hearers says: 
Question : At what point are calm abiding and special insight mixed and 
equalized and through what is it called the path of unification? 
Answer:  It is through the nine mental abidings. It is like this: One 
attains the ninth, that of equipoise and, in dependence on that 
thorough establishment of meditative stabilization, makes intense effort 
at the differentiation of phenom ena—the higher wisdom. Then, the 
path of the differentiation of phenomena will operate naturally and 
without exertion; also, like the path of calm abiding there will be no 
activity [of applying the antidotes to laxity and excitement]. Thereby, 
[one’s mind] will be conjoined with  thoroughly pure special insight, 
thorough purity, subsequence to calm  abiding, and a sense of bliss. 
Thereby, one’s calm abiding and special insight become mixed and 
equalized. This is called the path of unification of calm abiding and special insight. 
 
With respect to the union of calm abiding an d special insight, they must be similar in 
strength. Asanga’s text sets out the question: At what point when calm abiding and special 
insight are mixed and equalized, that is, when both are engaging the same object equally, and 
through what, is it called “the path of unific ation”? There are nine mental abidings including 
setting through equipoise. Having attained the ninth, equipoise, in dependence on 
thoroughly accomplishing meditative stabilization, one makes intense effort in special wisdom differentiating phenomena. At th is time, the path of the differe ntiation of phenomena, like 
calm abiding, is without effort, that is, one meditates single-pointedly without effort. Thereby one’s mind becomes thoroughly conjoined with a special insight that is thoroughly pure, 
thoroughly trained, a follower of calm abidin g, and a blissful experience. Because of that, 
one’s calm abiding and special insight are mixed, meaning that they engage simultaneously, and engage equally. This is called “the path  of unification of calm abiding and special 
insight.”  
 Therefore, first it is necessary to meditate on the nine stages successively, whereby pliancy 
of mind is produced. This in turn produces a special physical pliancy, which when held with 
single-pointed mind becomes calm abiding. Wi th one part of the mind engaging in calm 
abiding, the other part engages in the analytic al meditation of individual investigation, 
whereby the pliancy of body and mind increase  in strength, producing an even stronger 
physical and mental bliss conjoined with wisdom; this is called special insight and a union of 
calm abiding and special insight . 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fourth, a source for the time of achieving a union:) 
975 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
The last [of Kamalashila’s three works on the] Stages of Meditation  says: 
When due to being devoid of laxity and excitement [your mind] is set in 
equipoise and is naturally operating, whereby a very clear mind with 
respect to suchness arises, you should abide equanimously through 
loosening the exertion [involved in applying the antidotes to laxity and excitement]. You should know that at  that time you have achieved the 
path of a union of calm abiding and special insight. 
Thus, [the point of attaining a union of calm abiding and special insight] is from 
the point of attaining a fully  qualified special insight. 
 
The time at which a union is achieved is set out in Kamalashila’s text. When due to being 
devoid of laxity and excitement, the mind is equipoised and engages naturally, whereby a very 
clear mind with respect to suchness arises, on e should loosen one’s effort and abide in 
equanimity (this being the equanimity that is a compositional factor). At this time one has 
achieved the path of the union of calm abiding and special insight. This  is the boundary of 
achieving a fully-qualified special insight. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Fifth, a source for the union of simultaneous and mutual harmony of calm abiding and special insight when analysis 
induces calm abiding:) 
Ratnakarashanti’s Quintessential Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom  says: 
After that, observe just the image which is being analyzed. When 
through the continuum of continuous and uninterrupted mental 
engagement both are experienced in th e mind, that is called a path of a 
union [or joining of the pair] of calm abiding and special insight. 
Concerning that, calm abiding and special insight are the pair; joining means mutual embracement. 
 
When analysis induces calm abiding, there is a union of simultaneous and mutually 
concomitant calm abiding and special insight.  
 One observes that very reflection that is together with conceptualization When, by the 
continuum of continuous and uninterrupted me ntal attention, one experiences both calm 
abiding and special insight in that mind, it said to be “the path of unification of calm abiding 
and special insight.” Calm abiding and special in sight are unified as a pair, that is, possessed 
as a pair, meaning that they engage through being bound to each other.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
In that, “uninterrupted” means that within leaving the run of just that analytical 
meditation—it not being necessary to set in non-conceptuality—analytical 
meditation itself induces non-conceptuality. 
 
“Uninterrupted” means that without the need to set the mind in non-conceptuality to 
through halting analytical meditation, that analytical meditation itself induces non-
conceptuality. In other words, first one sets the mind in calm abiding, which is non-
976 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
conceptual, then one does a conceptual analys is of the object, which then produces a non-
conceptual mind, a yogic direct perceiver. Th us, a conceptual mind can induce a direct 
perceiver, in which case they have the relationship  of cause and result.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Sixth, though formerly analysis and stabilization were not si multaneous, they operate in harmony in the union of calm 
abiding and special insight:) 
“Both are experienced” means that both the calm abiding observing the 
unanalyzed image and the special insight observing the image with analysis are 
experienced. 
 “Through the continuum” is said in term s of the non-arising at one time of the 
analytical special insight and the calm ab iding which is a placement at the end of 
analysis. Then at the time of the calm abiding which is the actual one induced 
through the power of analysis, special insight—the intense differentiation of 
phenomena observing the mode—and calm abiding—the meditative stabilization 
which is a steady one-pointed placement on the mode—operate in harmony. At 
such a time, calm abiding and special insi ght are mixed; they operate in equality. 
 
Although the two, analytical me ditation and stabilizing meditati on, do not occur at the same 
time, there being first one and then the other, they engage mutually in the union of calm 
abiding and special insight. “Both are experienced” means that both the calm abiding which 
observes the reflection without conception and th e special insight that observes the reflection 
together with conceptuality are experienced.  “Through the continuum” means that special 
insight, which is analytical, and calm abiding, which is an abiding at the end of analysis, do 
not occur simultaneously, that is, first one occurs and then the other. Then, at the time of an 
actual calm abiding induced by the power of analysis, special insight (a wisdom observing 
modes) and calm abiding (a single-pointed medi tation on modes) engage concomitantly. At 
that time, calm abiding and special insigh t are mixed, meaning that they engage 
simultaneously. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 With this, realization whic h is a state arisen from meditation must be attained. 
Therefore, a composite of the two—the suit ability of (1) individually analyzing the 
meaning of selflessness within the non-de struction of the influence of (2) non-
conceptuality which is a steady factor of st ability, these being like the small fish [of 
analysis] moving about while staying in the unmoving water [mental stability]—is 
posited as a similitude of calm abiding an d special insight. Except for that, nothing 
has the meaning of a union of actual calm abiding and special insight. 
 
Therefore, it is necessary to at tain the realization which is a wisdom arisen from meditation. 
Like a small fish moving about in unmoving water without making waves, while the mind 
abides steadily there is the individual analysis  of selflessness. If this were posited as a 
similitude of calm abiding and special insight,  there would be nothing that is a union of 
actual calm abiding and special insight.  
977 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, advice on the need to abandon error and understand [the t opics] according to reliable texts as well as on the 
need to know the extensive explanation of the stages of the path from the Great Exposition of the Stages of the 
Path:) 
You should understand the mode of the un ion of calm abiding and special insight 
in that way according to what appears in reliable texts and should not put 
confidence in explanations that fabricate it  in other ways. Reasoned final decisions, 
scriptural sources, and the mode of cult ivation of the stages of the path to 
enlightenment can be known in extensive form from the Great Exposition of the 
Stages of the Path. 
 
It is necessary to understand the extensive ex planation of the stages of the path from the Great 
Exposition of the Stages of the Path , meaning that one should study this text. One should 
understand the way in which calm abiding and special insight are unified in dependence on 
reliable sources and one should not place one’s  trust in fabricated explanations. Reasoned 
final decisions, scriptural sources, and the way to meditate of the stages of the path are to be 
understood from the extensive ex planation of them found in the Great Exposition of the Stages 
of the Path . 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 379) says: 
12 Summary of the Stages of Path 
(The summation of the general path has three parts, the first of which is: the mode of procedure of the general path:) 
Now I will set forth a brief summation of the general path. The root of the path 
meets back to the mode of relying on a spiritual guide; hence, it should be 
carefully taken to heart. Then, if a non-ar tificial wish to extract the essence with 
respect to leisure is generated, it will urge you on to practice from within. Hence, 
in order to generate it you should medita te on the topics concerning leisure and 
fortune. 
 
Since the root of the path is relying on a guru, you should emphasize meditation on guru 
devotion, as taught in various texts, by develo ping faith in him and so forth. Then, if you 
generate a non-artificial wish to take the essenc e of this human rebirth with its eight leisures 
and ten endowments, it will urge you from the inside to practice. Therefore, in order to 
generate this wish, you should meditate on  the eight leisures and ten endowments, the 
eighteen qualities of a precious human rebirth, as well as th e difficulty of finding them, the 
fact that when found they are ve ry useful, and so forth. You should meditate on the difficulty 
of finding a precious human rebirth in terms of the difficulty of creating its causes, the rarity 
of finding it in terms of numbers, and so forth. For example, the many beings who go to the bad migrations of hell beings, hungry ghosts, and animals are like the number of grains of 
sand in the Ganges River, whereas the number of beings who are reborn as human beings and gods are like the number of grains of sa nd that can sit on a fingernail. The cause of 
attaining such a good rebirth is the practice of morality of abandoning killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisive speech, harsh word s, idle talk, covetousness, malice, and wrong 
978 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
view. However, morality is difficult to practice as our mind tends to engage in non-virtue and 
not in virtue. You should think about this fact. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Then, since if you do not tu rn around the attitude of seeking [the affairs of] this 
lifetime, a strong seeking of [the purposes ] of future lives [as well as deeper aims] 
will not arise, you should strive at medi tating on impermanence —the fact that the

transmigrations upon dying. Since at that time a natural attitude mindful of fright 
will be generated, you should, from th e depths, generate ascertainment with 
respect to the qualities of the Three Refuge s as well as dwell in the vow of common 
refuge and train in its points of training. 
 
If you do not turn away from interest in this li fe, a strong wish for a good future rebirth will 
not arise. Therefore, it is necessary to meditate on impermanence, the fact that the body with 
the eighteen qualities that we presently have do es not last long. You should meditate on the 
nine-round meditation on death-impermanence, th inking that death is ce rtain, that the time 
of death is uncertain, and so forth. You should  also think that if you were to die now, you 
cannot be sure that you will not be reborn in the bad migrations. If you take rebirth in a hot 
hell, cold hell, neighboring hell, or occasional hell , if you take rebirth as a hungry ghost, or if 
you are reborn as an animal (you can see the su fferings of animals directly), what would it be 
like? Think about this. By doing so, a strong fe ar will be developed, whereupon you should 
ascertain from your heart the qualities of the three objects of refuge  – the Buddha, Dharma, 
and Sangha. Each of their eight qualities are taught in the Sublime Continuum , therefore you 
should study them. All that can protect us is pu tting into practice the Dharma taught by the 
Buddha. Therefore, having taken refuge, you should keep the refuge precepts and train in the 
respective trainings. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Then, you should generate from many viewpoints the faith of conviction in 
actions and their effects—this being the ba sis for all wholesome practices. Having 
made that faith firm, you should strive at  engaging in virtues and disengaging from 
non-virtues, abiding continuously in the path of the four powers. 
 
Then you should generate faith or confidence in  the law of cause and effect – the basis of all 
white or virtuous actions. You should then strive  to do virtuous actions and give up engaging 
in non-virtuous actions. However, if you do commit a non-virtuous action, you should purify 
it by means of applying the four opponent powers. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 When in that way the topics of practice by a being of small capacity have been internalized, you should contemplate frequently the faults of cyclic existence in

general and in particular, thereby turning your mind away from cyclic existence in 
general as much as possible. Then, identi fying contaminated actions and afflictive 
emotions as the causes from which cyclic existence arises, generate a non-artificial 
wish to abandon them. Work hard at the three trainings in general—the paths of 
release from cyclic existence—and in pa rticular at the [vow of] individual

In this way you should meditate on and understand the topics of a small being. After that, 
you should contemplate the general and specific fa ults of cyclic existence, thereby generating 
the wish to be free from cyclic existence in general. Then you should identify the causes of 
taking rebirth in cyclic existe nce – contaminated actions and a fflictions – and generate a non-

Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 When in that way the topics of practice by a being of middling capacity have been 
internalized, you should take to mind ma ny who have fallen into the ocean of 
cyclic existence just as you have and trai n in the altruistic mind of enlightenment

whatever you can, for without it the six perfections, the two stages, and so forth are

In this way you should try to understand clea rly the topics of practice of a middle being. 
 
Thursday morning, 15 March 2007 
 
Then, you should think that just as you have fa llen into the ocean of cyclic existence, so too 
have all other sentient beings and therefore trai n in the mind of enlightenment that has as its 
root love and compassion. Love has the aspect thinking “How wonderful it would be if all 
sentient beings were to have happiness and the causes of happiness.” Then one takes the 
responsibility to accomplish this upon onesel f. Compassion has the aspect thinking “How 
wonderful it would be all sentient beings were free from suffering and the causes of 
suffering.” On this basis, you should generate as much as you can the mind of enlightenment 
wishing to benefit all sentient beings. If this bo dhichitta does not exist,  then the practice of 
the six perfections and the two stages – the ge neration stage and completion stage – will be 
without a foundation. It will be like a building  that lacks a foundation but, having built the 
walls and roof, one day will collapse. When you have generated some experience of the mind 
of enlightenment, you should take the mind of enlightenment by means of the rite or ritual. 
You first take the wishing or aspirational mind of enlightenment in a ri te with the wish to 
980 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
attain enlightenment. Having done so, you should give up the four black dharmas and 
practice the four white dharmas. In this way you should train in the trainings and make the 
mind of enlightenment as stable as you can. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Then, hear about the great waves of  [bodhisattva] deeds and generate 
understanding of the boundaries of enga gement and disengagement as well as a 
wish to train in them. When  that is generated, take the vow of practice through 
the rite. Train in the six perfections wh ich ripen your own continuum and in the 
four means of gathering [trainees] and so forth which ripen others’ continuums. In 
particular risk even your life [to keep] from  the root infractions. Strive at not being 
polluted by the lesser and middling contamin ations and faults; even if polluted by 
such, work at restoration. 
 
After that, you should hear teachings on and study the extensive deeds of the bodhisattvas 
and generate an understanding of what should be practiced and what should be discarded with respect to them, as well as generate the wish to train in them. When this wish has been 
developed, you should take the vows of engagi ng bodhichitta. Then yo u should train in the 
six perfections in order to ripen your own co ntinuum and in the four means of gathering 
disciples in order to ripen others’ continua. Th e six perfections, as you know, are generosity, 
morality, patience, joyous effort, concentratio n, and wisdom. The four means of gathering 
disciples are to give material things, to talk ni cely, to teach what should be practiced, and to 
act in accordance with what you teach. The last one means that it is not enough to tell others 
what to do, you should also do that yourself otherwise your advice will be useless.  
 In particular, even at the risk of your life you should not commit the eighteen root 
downfalls of the bodhisattva vows, which are relate d to the practice of the six perfections. You 
should also strive not to be polluted by the small and middling contaminations as well as by 
faults. If you commit a downfall and so forth, you should confess it by means of the General 
Confession composed by Ashvagosha. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 Then, since you must train in particular  in the latter two perfections, become 
skilled in the mode of sustaining concentration and achieve meditative 
stabilization. As much as you can, generate  in your continuum the pure view of the 
two selflessnesses. Having understood how to gain the view and how to sustain it 
within setting [the mind] in the context of  the view, sustain [its continuum]. Such 
concentration and wisdom are designated with the names of calm abiding and 
special insight, which since they are not se parate from those two, come within the 
points of training of the bodhisa ttva vow once it has been taken. 
 
You should train, in particular, in the last two perfections – concentration and wisdom. 
Because of this, these two are extensively explaine d in all the texts on the stages of the path. 
Among the six perfections, the prac tice of morality is the basis. If your morality is good, you 
981 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
can take rebirth as a human being. If you have  practiced generosity, you will obtain all the 
necessities of life. Patience, in addition, is also very important because if you lack patience, 
and instead easily become angry, this anger, which is the worst of negativities, will destroy 
your virtue. In order to practice the first th ree perfections, the fourth, joyous effort, is 
necessary. This is because it is necessary to put e ffort into virtue with a joyful mind. It should 
not be like putting effort into something wi th an unhappy mind because you have been 
ordered by someone else to do so; this is not th e practice of joyous effort. Also making effort 
to make money and the like, even if it is done  for eighteen or sixteen hours a day, is not 
considered to be effort but to be a type of laziness. This is explained in the Ornament for Clear 
Realizations .  
 As much as possible you should generate th e correct view of the two selflessnesses – the 
selflessness of persons and the selflessness of phenomena. Havi ng understood this view, you 
should set your mind on the view of selflessne ss and strive to concentrate on it. Having taken 
selflessness as the observed object, you should at  times engage in stabilizing meditation with 
respect to it and at other times engage in anal ytical meditation. If you always do analytical 
meditation without doing any stabilizing medi tation, your meditative stabilization will 
degenerate. The basis of your me ditation should be single-point ed meditative stabilization, 
upon which another part of your mind engages in analysis. By meditating in this way, both 
calm abiding and special insight will become stable. 
 The perfections of concentration and wi sdom are given the names “calm abiding” 
(samatha) and “special insight” (vipassana). Ther efore, since calm abiding and special insight 
are not other than concentration and wisdom, having taken the bodhisattva vow you should 
train in its trainings whereby these two minds will arise.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Second, the difference between successful and unsuccessful  meditation and how to bring out special emphasis 
through fine thought:) 
It should be such that when cultivatin g the lower paths the wish to attain the 
higher paths increases and when hearing ab out the higher, the wish to achieve the 
lower increases. Also, when those are cultivated in meditation, you need an 
equalized mind training in those thoughts. 
 
This outline discusses the feature of whether yo ur meditation goes to the essential point or 
not and how to penetrate the essential point by fine conception. Your meditation should be 
such that when you meditate on the lower paths,  you generate an increasingly stronger wish 
to attain the higher paths. Likewise, when you hear teachings on and read books about the 
higher paths, your wish to achieve the lower pa th should become increasingly stronger. Then, 
at the time of meditation you should have a mind that trains in eq ual parts in these two 
thoughts.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 If it appears that you have little respec t for the spiritual guide leading you on the 
path, since the root of the co llections of goodness is severed, you should strive at 
the mode of reliance [on a spiritual guide].  
982 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
 
If you have little respect for your teacher, you should strive in meditating on and practicing 
reliance on your guru, as otherwise the root of the path will be cut and it will be difficult to 
generate realizations. In order to cultivate this  devotion, you should think about how to rely 
on the your guru in thought and action. For example, there are nine attitudes that need to be 
generated in relation to the guru. You should always remember that all your knowledge arises from the guru. There is a story about a tulk u who had an old teacher who, when he was 
young, taught him to read and write. This te acher had not studied much philosophy, but the 
tulku often requested him for teachings. However, the teacher only gave him the 
transmissions of texts and so forth. Once so meone asked the tulku why he went to receive 
teachings from the old teacher, since he was more intelligent th an his teacher. The tulku said 
that, although he was able to quickly learn things he also quickly forgot them, but by taking teachings from his old teacher he received bl essings from him and was able to keep the 
teachings in his mind and without ever forgetti ng them. The other person asked him, rather 
skeptically, how he received blessings from such a teacher, to which the tulku replied that his 
receiving blessings came about because of his fa ith, and that therefore those who did not have 
faith in the teacher would not receive blessings from him.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
Similarly, if you have little force of enthusiasm for practice, work at the topics of 
leisure and fortune.  
 
Similarly, if your joy or enthusiasm for practice  is little or decreases, you should meditate on 
the eight leisures and ten endowments, the difficu lty of finding them, their usefulness, and so 
on. You should generate the th ought that you are very lucky to have obtained a precious 
human rebirth and to not have been born as a he ll being, a hungry ghost, an animal or as 
hungry ghost. You should also consider yourself lucky to have been born as a human being in 
a central land, to not be stupid, and so forth. Thinking in this way, you should rejoice in your good fortune. Then you should meditate on the ten endowments, the five from a personal 
point of view and the five from a circumstanti al point of view. Again you should think that 
you are very lucky and determine to practice the Dharma so as to not lo se this opportunity in 
the future. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
If you come to have great attachment to this life, mainly work at meditating on 
impermanence and the faults of bad transmigrations.  
 
If at the time of practice you come to have a lo t of clinging to this life, you should think about 
death-impermanence and the sufferings of the lower realms. Thinking that this precious 
human rebirth that you have now is impermanent, you should do the nine-round meditation 
on death-impermanence. You should also think about the beings in the eight hot hells, eight 
cold hells, neighboring hells, and occasional he lls, as well as the hungry ghosts who have 
internal, external, and internal and external obscurations, asking yourself whether you could 
983 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
bear to be reborn like them. You should also think about how it would be to be reborn as a 
handicapped human being. Presently you have not taken such rebirths, but it is also necessary 
to ensure that in the future you will also  be able to enjoy the practice of Dharma. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
If it appears that you are deviating from  the [ethical] formulation that you have 
accepted, mainly work at meditating on actions and their effects.  
 
If you have taken the lay vows, the novice vows,  the vows of full ordination, the bodhisattva 
vows, or the tantra vows but are not taking care of them, you should take the law of cause and 
effect as your main object of meditation. Ther e are many points regarding this subject which 
we will not go into now. However, having created a particular type of action it will definitely 
bring a particular type of result. You should  study this subject and meditate on it.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
If you have little discouragement with resp ect to cyclic existence, then since your 
seeking of liberation has become merely verbal, contemplate the faults of cyclic 
existence.  
 
If you have little discouragement or weariness of cyclic existence, you should think about the 
faults of cyclic existence in general as otherwis e your seeking of liberation will only be words. 
You should think about true sufferings – the suffering of suffering, the suffering of change, 
pervasive suffering, and so forth. In other word s, you should contemplat e the three, six, and 
eight kinds of suffering. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
If it appears that you do not have a strong  force of mind turning everything you do 
for the sake of sentient beings, then since the root of the Mahayana has been 
severed, train in the aspirational mind of enlightenment as well as its causes. 
 
If your attitude wishing to enact the welfare of other sentient beings is weak, since the root of 
the mahayana has been severed, you should tr ain in the wishing or aspirational mind of 
enlightenment and its causes. The causes of this mind are love and compassion. It is said that 
f i r s t  i t  i s  n e c e s s a r y  t o  r e c o g n i z e  a l l  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s  a s  h a v i n g  b e e n  o u r  m o t h e r s ,  t h e n  t o  
recognize their kindness, then to generate the wi sh to repay it, then to develop love followed 
by compassion, and then to generate the extrao rdinary attitude. These are the causes of the 
mind of enlightenment. Alternatively, you can think about the fact th at the self-cherishing 
mind is the root of all problems and diffic ulties, whereas all good qualities come from 
cherishing others. Then you should engage in th e practice of exchanging yourself and others, 
that is, exchanging your present attitude of cherishing yourself for the attitude of cherishing 
others. These too are causes for generating the mind of enlightenment. In short, you should 
train in its causes.  
984 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
 If upon taking the bodhisattva vow you train in the [bodhisattva] deeds and it 
appears that you have the fetters of the co nceptions of signs [inherent existence] in 
strong force, break down the target of th e conception of signs through a reasoning 
consciousness and train in the emptin ess like space and like illusion.  
 
If, having taken the bodhisattva vow, you train in the bodhisattva deeds but it seems that the 
fetters or bondage of the conceptions of signs, that is, the conceptions of true existence, are 
strong, you should destroy the observed object of the conceptions of signs with a reasoning 
consciousness. In other words, you should th ink about the fact that phenomena not exist 
inherently or truly by applying various types of  reasonings. You should also train in the space-
like emptiness and illusion-like emptiness. For example, in a session you should do single-
pointed meditation on space-like emptiness, th inking that just as space is the absence of 
obstructive contact, so too is emptiness an absence of inherent ex istence. When, during 
subsequent attainment, things appear to you to exist truly or inherently, you should think 
that they are like illusions. Just as an elepha nt appears due to the power of a magician, so too 
do phenomena appear to exist inherently due to the imprints of th e conception of true 
existence.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
If it appears that your mind is not stayi ng on its object of observation and has 
become a servant of distraction, mainly train in the factor of one-pointed stability. 
This is what the earlier [great masters] said. 
 
If your mind wanders or is distracted from the observed object and does not abide on the 
object, you should not do analytical meditation but instead should train in abiding on the object single-pointedly. This is what the earlier scholars taught. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
(Third, advice not to let your practice become partial:) 
Using those as illustrations you should also understand [what to do in] other [situations] which have not been explaine d. In brief, without becoming partial, 
your continuum should be serviceable in all virtuous directions. 
 
You should not allow your practice to become on e-sided or partial. Base d on this explanation, 
you should understand how to act and train in other situations. In brief, you should not practice one thing while giving up another, for example, practicing calm abiding alone 
without cultivating special insight. You should make your continuum serviceable in 
everything virtuous.

FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
                                              
Thursday morning, 15 March 2007 (cont’d) 
 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (translated by Philip Quarcoo, Tibetan text page 381) says: 
Explanation of how to train in the uncommon vehicle, the vajrayana 
2B4B-2B3B-3C2 Explanation of how to train in the uncommon vehicle, the vajrayana 
A The advice to make sure and engage in mantra 
Second: In particular how to  train in the Vajrayana:  
Having thus trained in what sutra and mantra have in common, {382}  you should 
doubtlessly engage in mantra84, for that path is extremely rare compared with other 
Dharma teachings and lets you comple te the two accumulations quickly.  
 
Next come some brief instructions on how to  train in the vajrayana. The path common to 
sutra and tantra includes the paths of small, middling, and great beings. Having trained in 
these paths, one should definitely engage in  the path of tantra. Among the teachings of 
Buddha this path is very rare and enables one to quickly complete the two collections. In 
short, it is important to first train in the co mmon path to sutra and tantra and then enter the 
tantra path.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 382) says: 
B How essential it is to rely on a spiritual teacher when you engage in it 
If you engage in it, you should first of all make your teacher happy in excess of 
what has been explained above, according to the statements in Lamp on the Path, 
and this should be someone who has at least all the characteristics discussed.  
 
When engaging in the path of tantra, it is nece ssary to rely on a teacher, a virtuous spiritual 
friend. As is taught in the Lamp on the Path , the lam-rim text by Atisha, you should rely on a 
teacher and delight him even more than when prac ticing sutra. The guru or teacher should be 
one who is qualified in the sense of possessing al l ten qualities, or a minimum of five of the 
ten, or even just three – having a good heart, possessing the lineage, and taking care of his 
disciples. This is the type of guru upon whom one should rely. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C The entryway, receiving an empowerment, and the importance of keeping one’s commitments and 
vows intact 
Then you should first have your ment al continuum ripene d by means of an 
empowerment explained in authentic tantra  texts. Then you should hear and get 
to know the vows and commitments taken on  that occasion and keep them. If a 
root infraction occurs, even if the vow is  taken again the development of positive 
qualities of the path will be greatly delaye d in one’s mind, so try hard to not be 
 
84 cf. chap. I, note 1***. 
986 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
                                             tainted by that. Also try not to be tainte d by serious contraventions and also apply 
the means of restoring the 100 [lesser] ones if you become tainted [by them] 
because they are the root for cultivating th e paths. Without them, it would be like 
a house with rotten foundations collapsing. 
 
The door or entryway to tantra is an empowerm ent. During an empowerment it is necessary 
to assume certain vows and pledges which must then be safeguarded. Therefore, your mind 
should be ripened by receiving an empowerment from an authentic scriptural source. This is 
important because there are tantras that were ta ught by the Buddha and others that occur in 
other Indian traditions. Having taken an em powerment, you should study the vows and 
pledges that you have received by, for example, studying texts such as the Fifty Verses of Guru 
Devotion . If a root downfall occurs, you should take  the vows again. However, it would be best 
not to commit such root downfalls at all beca use even if you take the vows again it the 
achievement of the results of the tantra path will still be delayed. Therefore, it is better not to 
commit them at all. You should also try not to  be stained by serious contraventions because 
they are the basis for cultivating the path. Withou t them, it would be like the collapsing of a 
house with a rotten foundations. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
 
In the Manjushri Root Tantra  it says: 
 
The Capable One never talked about yogis 
with discipline lost accomplishing mantra. 
 
Statements such as this indicate th at [in that case] there will be no 
accomplishments at all, neither great, medium, nor small. In the highest yoga 
tantras it also says that practitioners who do not keep thei r sacred commitments, 
who had inferior empowerments,85 or who do not unders tand suchness will not 
attain any accomplishments despite their practice. Therefore someone claiming to 
meditate on the path without keeping his sacred commitments and vows is 
straying far from the established way of mantra. 
 
Those whose morality is corrupt or unclean will not be able to achieve the results of mantra, 
that is, they will not be able to attain the real izations of the yogas with and without sign of 
action tantra, performance tantra, and yoga tantra, and the realizations of the generation and 
completion stages of highest yoga tantra. This indicates that they will not be able to attain the 
great, middling, and small attainments.  
 In highest yoga tantra it is also said that if  one does not keep one’s pledges, if one receives 
an inferior empowerment, or if one does not understand the meaning of tantra, one will not 
attain the attainments no matte r how much one practices. Ther efore, someone who claims to 
 
85 An inferior empowerment is one where either the one gi ving it or the one receiving it does not meet all the 
necessary requirements. (Explana tion by Geshe Thubten Soepa) 
987 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
practice tantra but at the same time does not k eep his or her vows and pledges, has strayed far 
from tantra.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
D How to train in the paths of ripening and freeing 
Those who keep their sacred vows and commitments should thus {383}  train in 
the two levels of yoga with and without signs according to the three lower tantras 
and in the two levels of yoga according to  the highest one in order to cultivate the 
paths of mantra. 
 Here only the aspect of how you get invo lved in mantra is presented nominally, 
so you must get to know them more in detail from the Stages of Mantra .  
 
It is necessary to train in the paths that ripen and the paths that liberate. Those who 
safeguard their vows and pledges should train in  the yoga with signs and yoga without signs 
according to the three lower tantras, and in the generation and completion stages in the case 
of highest yoga tantra. Here these stages are only presented in name, so you should 
understand them from the Great Exposition of the Stages of Mantra .  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
E How to make your leisures and endowments meaningful in that manner
If you train in such a way, you will completely perfect the [entire] body of the path 
comprising all the key points of sutra an d tantra, and make the leisures you have 
achieved meaningful through [sustained] practice. You will be able to make the 
jewel of the Conqueror’s teachings develop in your own mind and that of others.  
 
Having obtained the leisures and endowments you must make them meaningful. If you train 
in this way, you will complete the paths of su tra and tantra, thereby making this precious 
human rebirth that you presently have very meaningful. Thus, you will be able to make the Conqueror’s teachings flourish in your own and others’ continua.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  (Tibetan text page 383) says: 
3 Conclusion of the explanations 
A How the treatise was composed 
B Dedicating the virtue of its composition 
C The author who composed the text and how he did it 
3A How the treatise was composed 
1 Explaining the greatness of the instructions 
2 The purpose of a writing a composition that has such great qualities 
3 Indication of the sources: the instructions from which the subject matter of this treatise was handed 
down 
3A1 Explaining the greatness of the instructions  
A Generally speaking: The greatness that all the excellent sutra teachings come to be seen as [practical] instructions 
The Conqueror has set out the sutra and tantra approaches – 
two aspects of the path. The excellent texts 
on paths of all sutra teachings and commentaries, 
988 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
easily come to be seen as direct instructions 
through the fearless confidence one is given. 
 
This is the first greatness of the instructions : the greatness that all the teachings of sutra 
appear as personal instructions. Buddha Sh akyamuni taught both sutra and tantra. The 
excellent texts come to be seen as personal inst ructions due to fearless confidence in the paths 
of all the scriptures and treatises of the sutra class. 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
B In particular: The greatness that all key points of the path shared by sutra and mantra are [made] clear 
The shared great vehicle path s explained in the tantras 
are all made clear, which supremely cleanses the mind. 
 
Then there is the second greatnes s that all the essential points of  sutra and tantra are clarified. 
The paths of the common great vehicle, that  is, those common to bo th sutra and tantra, 
which are explained in tantras are clar ified and the continuum is purified.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
C The greatness of correcting errors about the concentration of yogis through a clear explanation of how the 
concentration common to non-Buddhists and Buddhists is achieved
Moreover you properly realize errors of paths  
sought by yogis and correctly explained  
according to very clear texts by many scholars 
on concentration common to Buddhists, non-Buddhists, 
Hinayana, both kinds of Mahayana, 
higher and lower tantras and both stages. 
 
Then there is the greatness of correcting errors regarding the meditative stabilizations 
common to Buddhists and non-Buddhists.  
 By means of the excellent explanations according to the very clear texts by many scholars on the meditative stabilizations common to Bu ddhists and non-Buddhists, hinayana and both 
types of mahayana, the higher tantra (highest yo ga tantra) and the lower tantras (action tantra, 
performance tantra, and yoga tantra) and the two stages, one realizes well the errors of paths 
sought be yogis.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3A2 The purpose of a writing a composition that has such great qualities 
This summary of the path to enlightenment 
I rearranged for those who want liberation 
 
This is a summary of the path to enlightenm ent that Lama Tsongkha pa composed for the 
purpose of those who wish to attain liberation.
