Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3A3 Indication of the sources: the instructions from which the subject matter of this treatise was handed 
down 
The path of subtle view and extensive behavior 
passed from the regent, Maitreya, and noble Manjushri 
to the protector Nagarjuna, Asanga, 
and Shantideva – three rivers that were merged  
in the supreme instructions of Shri Atisha. 
 
The profound view and the path of vast cond uct were passed down from Maitreya to Asanga 
and from Manjushri to the protector Nagarjuna. In addition, there is a third lineage held by 
Shantideva. These three lineages were merged in  the supreme instructions of Shri Atisha.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3B Dedicating the virtue of composing the treatise as a cause for the propagation of the teachings 
Through the merit gained here by my effort 
may the Conqueror’s teachings, root of all welfare  
prosper, uncorrupted, for a long time. 
 
At the end, Lama Tsongkhapa dedicates the virt ue of having composed this text. He says 
“Through the virtues due to my effort may the Conqueror’s teachings flourish, uncorrupted, for a long time for the benefit of all sentient beings.” 
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
3C The author who composed the text and how he did it 
This summary of all the excellent teachings of the Buddha condenses, yet again, the tradition of the great trail-blazers Nagarjuna and Asanga, the supreme Dharma 
system of beings progressing towards th e level of omniscience, the lam-rim of 
enlightenment teaching in full all the stag es to be practiced by the three types of 
beings, the meaning of instructions passed from Gönpawa to Neuzurpa as well as 
down from Jenngawa and those passed from  Potowa to Sharawa as well as from 
Potowa to Tölpa. It was arranged in the glorious mountain monastery of Geden by 
the learned and renounced monk Tsongkhapa Losang Dragpa based on his 
extensive Lam-rim.  
 
This summary, which includes all the teachings of the Buddha, as well as the tradition of 
Nagarjuna and Asanga, for all those wishing to progress to enlightenment, a complete 
teaching on the stages of the path to enligh tenment to be practiced by the three types of 
beings, came down as instru ctions from Gönpawa to Neuz urpa, as well as down from 
Jenngawa, and passed from Potowa to Sharawa as well as from Potowa to Tölpa. These names 
are based on the names of small monasteries th at existed in the past, many of which have 
become nunneries with good practitioners, this being said to be due to the Kadampas having 
tended to denigrate women. Potowa is a well -known Kadam lama whose present incarnation 
990 
FPMT Basic Program Homestudy          Commentary to Middle Lam-Rim by Geshe Jampa Gyatso 
ILTK – 12-16 March 2007 (21) 
 
                                             lives in India. This particular text was co mposed by the meditator or renounced monk 
Tsongkhapa Losang Dragpa based on his Extensive Exposition of the Stages of the Path  in Gaden 
Monastery (also called Geden).  
 
Here I have finished a short explanation of this text as I was unable to go into much detail.  
 
Middle Lam-Rim  says: 
This Middling Exposition of the Stages of the Path to Enlightenment  composed by the Lord of 
the teachings of the three realms, the gr eat Tsongkhapa, is also known as the Lesser Lam-
rim. Its outlines are not restricted to the ones found in the original text. Since I had never 
seen nor heard about a thorough explanatory division, I thought ‘If only there were a 
thorough division along the lines of the Four Annotations86 on the Great Exposition of the 
Stages of the Path to Enlightenment’ . With that intention I started off from the points that 
clearly match in the outlines of Four Annotations  as my basis. Where the sequence is 
different and where the divisions are more cond ensed or more detailed I left things out 
and made insertions as appropriate in a give n context. The outlines  of new divisions that 
have become necessary, for instance in the se ctions on calm abiding and the two truths, 
were put in wherever the argument could be  broken up. I have received profound and 
extensive explanations on this Middling Exposition of the Stages of the Path  from both the 
exalted master possessed of inconceivable great compassion, Büldü Dorje Chang Losang 
Yeshe Tenpe Pel Sangpo of Dre[pung] Gomang  and the sovereign holding the essence of 
kindness that comprises all the refuge object s of the three times till the end of samsara, 
Dorje Chang Pabongka, in that order. It wa s written down by the indolent lazy bone, 
lying around and lacking practice, in the guise of a teacher, the tutor named Ganden 
Trijang in this life [and known as] Lobsang Yeshe Tenzin Gyatso, in the Tibetan year of 2903 and the Western year of 1966 in Dharamsa la, in the state of Himachal Pradesh, 
India. I kindly request impartia l scholars to correct any distortions – additions, omissions, 
and errors – that I have made.  
 
Through this virtuous effort may all lives 
be undivorced from this full, unmistaken, 
ultimate path. Having grasped the Conqueror’s teachings, 
may the power to guide all beings be gained.   
Prayer for the purity of publications: 
Meant for anything good, either present or fu ture, such as the complete propagation of 
the Buddha’s long-standing tradition which po ints out the path of non-harm and peace 
all over the world, the following lines were composed as a dedication prayer that may be 
appended to any publication or reproduction of Buddhist scriptures associated with non-
partisan schools of thought by the Shakya’s  monk, the exponent of Dharma, the Lord of 
speech Lobsang Tenzin Gyatso. 
 
Of the same taste as that oc ean-like peace – liberation: 
the slow-moving river of limitless Dharma one strives at 
whose every word component removes some stains

of the three poisons – this ne ctar of good explanations 
 
Through the massive virtue of its publication 
may hundreds of thousands of doors of pure Dharma open.
